### The Chapter on Two Things (Numbered Discourses 2.87–97) ^an2-87-97-0

87 ^an2-87-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-87-1-1

What two? ^an2-87-1-2

Freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom. ^an2-87-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-87-1-4

88 ^an2-88-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-88-1-1

What two? ^an2-88-1-2

Exertion, and not being scattered. ^an2-88-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-88-1-4

89 ^an2-89-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-89-1-1

What two? ^an2-89-1-2

Name and form. ^an2-89-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-89-1-4

90 ^an2-90-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-90-1-1

What two? ^an2-90-1-2

Knowledge and freedom. ^an2-90-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-90-1-4

91 ^an2-91-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-91-1-1

What two? ^an2-91-1-2

Views favoring existence and views favoring nonexistence. ^an2-91-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-91-1-4

92 ^an2-92-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-92-1-1

What two? ^an2-92-1-2

Lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-92-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-92-1-4

93 ^an2-93-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-93-1-1

What two? ^an2-93-1-2

Conscience and prudence. ^an2-93-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-93-1-4

94 ^an2-94-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-94-1-1

What two? ^an2-94-1-2

Being hard to admonish and having bad friends. ^an2-94-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-94-1-4

95 ^an2-95-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-95-1-1

What two? ^an2-95-1-2

Being easy to admonish and having good friends. ^an2-95-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-95-1-4

96 ^an2-96-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-96-1-1

What two? ^an2-96-1-2

Skill in the elements and skill in application of mind. ^an2-96-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-96-1-4

97 ^an2-97-1-0

“There are, mendicants, these two things. ^an2-97-1-1

What two? ^an2-97-1-2

Skill in offenses and skill in resolving offenses. ^an2-97-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-97-1-4

### The Chapter on Fools (Numbered Discourses 2.98–117) ^an2-98-117-0

98 ^an2-98-1-0

“Mendicants, there are two fools. ^an2-98-1-1

What two? ^an2-98-1-2

One who carries the burden of what has not come to pass, and one who doesn’t carry the burden of what has come to pass. ^an2-98-1-3

These are the two fools.” ^an2-98-1-4

99 ^an2-99-1-0

“There are two who are astute. ^an2-99-1-1

What two? ^an2-99-1-2

One who doesn’t carry the burden of what has not come to pass, and one who carries the burden of what has come to pass. ^an2-99-1-3

These are the two who are astute.” ^an2-99-1-4

100 ^an2-100-1-0

“Mendicants, there are two fools. ^an2-100-1-1

What two? ^an2-100-1-2

One who perceives what is unallowable as allowable, and one who perceives what is allowable as unallowable. ^an2-100-1-3

These are the two fools.” ^an2-100-1-4

101 ^an2-101-1-0

“There are two who are astute. ^an2-101-1-1

What two? ^an2-101-1-2

One who perceives what is unallowable as unallowable, and one who perceives what is allowable as allowable. ^an2-101-1-3

These are the two who are astute.” ^an2-101-1-4

102 ^an2-102-1-0

“Mendicants, there are two fools. ^an2-102-1-1

What two? ^an2-102-1-2

One who perceives a non-offense as an offense, and one who perceives an offense as a non-offense. ^an2-102-1-3

These are the two fools.” ^an2-102-1-4

103 ^an2-103-1-0

“There are two who are astute. ^an2-103-1-1

What two? ^an2-103-1-2

One who perceives a non-offense as a non-offense, and one who perceives an offense as an offense. ^an2-103-1-3

These are the two who are astute.” ^an2-103-1-4

104 ^an2-104-1-0

“Mendicants, there are two fools. ^an2-104-1-1

What two? ^an2-104-1-2

One who perceives what is not the teaching as the teaching, and one who perceives the teaching as not the teaching. ^an2-104-1-3

These are the two fools.” ^an2-104-1-4

105 ^an2-105-1-0

“There are two who are astute. ^an2-105-1-1

What two? ^an2-105-1-2

One who perceives the teaching as the teaching, and one who perceives what is not the teaching as not the teaching. ^an2-105-1-3

These are the two who are astute.” ^an2-105-1-4

106 ^an2-106-1-0

“Mendicants, there are two fools. ^an2-106-1-1

What two? ^an2-106-1-2

One who perceives what is not the training as the training, and one who perceives what is the training as not the training. ^an2-106-1-3

These are the two fools.” ^an2-106-1-4

107 ^an2-107-1-0

“There are two who are astute. ^an2-107-1-1

What two? ^an2-107-1-2

One who perceives what is not the training as not the training, and one who perceives what is the training as the training. ^an2-107-1-3

These are the two who are astute.” ^an2-107-1-4

108 ^an2-108-1-0

“For these two, defilements grow. ^an2-108-1-1

What two? ^an2-108-1-2

One who is remorseful over something they shouldn’t be, and one who isn’t remorseful over something they should be. ^an2-108-1-3

These are the two whose defilements grow.” ^an2-108-1-4

109 ^an2-109-1-0

“For these two, defilements don’t grow. ^an2-109-1-1

What two? ^an2-109-1-2

One who isn’t remorseful over something they shouldn’t be, and one who is remorseful over something they should be. ^an2-109-1-3

These are the two whose defilements don’t grow.” ^an2-109-1-4

110 ^an2-110-1-0

“For these two, defilements grow. ^an2-110-1-1

What two? ^an2-110-1-2

One who perceives what is unallowable as allowable, and one who perceives what is allowable as unallowable. ^an2-110-1-3

These are the two whose defilements grow.” ^an2-110-1-4

111 ^an2-111-1-0

“For these two, defilements don’t grow. ^an2-111-1-1

What two? ^an2-111-1-2

One who perceives what is unallowable as unallowable, and one who perceives what is allowable as allowable. ^an2-111-1-3

These are the two whose defilements don’t grow.” ^an2-111-1-4

112 ^an2-112-1-0

“For these two, defilements grow. ^an2-112-1-1

What two? ^an2-112-1-2

One who perceives an offense as a non-offense, and one who perceives a non-offense as an offense. ^an2-112-1-3

These are the two whose defilements grow.” ^an2-112-1-4

113 ^an2-113-1-0

“For these two, defilements don’t grow. ^an2-113-1-1

What two? ^an2-113-1-2

One who perceives an offense as an offense, and one who perceives a non-offense as a non-offense. ^an2-113-1-3

These are the two whose defilements don’t grow.” ^an2-113-1-4

114 ^an2-114-1-0

“For these two, defilements grow. ^an2-114-1-1

What two? ^an2-114-1-2

One who perceives what is not the teaching as the teaching, and one who perceives the teaching as not the teaching. ^an2-114-1-3

These are the two whose defilements grow.” ^an2-114-1-4

115 ^an2-115-1-0

“For these two, defilements don’t grow. ^an2-115-1-1

What two? ^an2-115-1-2

One who perceives the teaching as the teaching, and one who perceives what is not the teaching as not the teaching. ^an2-115-1-3

These are the two whose defilements don’t grow.” ^an2-115-1-4

116 ^an2-116-1-0

“For these two, defilements grow. ^an2-116-1-1

What two? ^an2-116-1-2

One who perceives what is not the training as the training, and one who perceives what is the training as not the training. ^an2-116-1-3

These are the two whose defilements grow.” ^an2-116-1-4

117 ^an2-117-1-0

“For these two, defilements don’t grow. ^an2-117-1-1

What two? ^an2-117-1-2

One who perceives what is not the training as not the training, and one who perceives what is the training as the training. ^an2-117-1-3

These are the two whose defilements don’t grow.” ^an2-117-1-4

### The Chapter on Hopes That Are Hard to Give Up (Numbered Discourses 2.118–129) ^an2-118-129-0

118 ^an2-118-1-0

“These two hopes are hard to give up. ^an2-118-1-1

What two? ^an2-118-1-2

The hope for wealth and the hope for long life. ^an2-118-1-3

These are two hopes that are hard to give up.” ^an2-118-1-4

119 ^an2-119-1-0

“These two individuals are rare in the world. ^an2-119-1-1

What two? ^an2-119-1-2

One who takes the initiative, and one who is grateful and thankful. ^an2-119-1-3

These are the two individuals who are rare in the world.” ^an2-119-1-4

120 ^an2-120-1-0

“These two individuals are rare in the world. ^an2-120-1-1

What two? ^an2-120-1-2

One who is satisfied, and one who satisfies others. ^an2-120-1-3

These are the two individuals who are rare in the world.” ^an2-120-1-4

121 ^an2-121-1-0

“These two individuals are hard to satisfy in the world. ^an2-121-1-1

What two? ^an2-121-1-2

One who continually hoards wealth, and one who continually wastes wealth. ^an2-121-1-3

These are the two individuals who are hard to satisfy in the world.” ^an2-121-1-4

122 ^an2-122-1-0

“These two individuals are easy to satisfy in the world. ^an2-122-1-1

What two? ^an2-122-1-2

One who does not continually hoard wealth, and one who does not continually waste wealth. ^an2-122-1-3

These are the two individuals who are easy to satisfy in the world.” ^an2-122-1-4

123 ^an2-123-1-0

“There are two conditions for the arising of greed. ^an2-123-1-1

What two? ^an2-123-1-2

The feature of beauty and irrational application of mind. ^an2-123-1-3

These are the two conditions for the arising of greed.” ^an2-123-1-4

124 ^an2-124-1-0

“There are two conditions for the arising of hate. ^an2-124-1-1

What two? ^an2-124-1-2

The feature of harshness and irrational application of mind. ^an2-124-1-3

These are the two conditions for the arising of hate.” ^an2-124-1-4

125 ^an2-125-1-0

“There are two conditions for the arising of wrong view. ^an2-125-1-1

What two? ^an2-125-1-2

The voice of another and irrational application of mind. ^an2-125-1-3

These are the two conditions for the arising of wrong view.” ^an2-125-1-4

126 ^an2-126-1-0

“There are two conditions for the arising of right view. ^an2-126-1-1

What two? ^an2-126-1-2

The voice of another and rational application of mind. ^an2-126-1-3

These are the two conditions for the arising of right view.” ^an2-126-1-4

127 ^an2-127-1-0

“There are these two offenses. ^an2-127-1-1

What two? ^an2-127-1-2

A light offense and a serious offense. ^an2-127-1-3

These are the two offenses.” ^an2-127-1-4

128 ^an2-128-1-0

“There are these two offenses. ^an2-128-1-1

What two? ^an2-128-1-2

An offense with corrupt intention and an offense without corrupt intention. ^an2-128-1-3

These are the two offenses.” ^an2-128-1-4

129 ^an2-129-1-0

“There are these two offenses. ^an2-129-1-1

What two? ^an2-129-1-2

An offense requiring rehabilitation and an offense not requiring rehabilitation. ^an2-129-1-3

These are the two offenses.” ^an2-129-1-4

### The Chapter on Aspiration (Numbered Discourses 2.130–140) ^an2-130-140-0

130 ^an2-130-1-0

“A faithful monk would rightly aspire: ^an2-130-1-1

‘May I be like Sāriputta and Moggallāna!’ ^an2-130-1-2

These are a standard and a measure for my monk disciples, that is, Sāriputta and Moggallāna.” ^an2-130-1-3

131 ^an2-131-1-0

“A faithful nun would rightly aspire: ^an2-131-1-1

‘May I be like the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā!’ ^an2-131-1-2

These are a standard and a measure for my nun disciples, that is, the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā.” ^an2-131-1-3

132 ^an2-132-1-0

“A faithful layman would rightly aspire: ^an2-132-1-1

‘May I be like the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī!’ ^an2-132-1-2

These are a standard and a measure for my male lay followers, that is, the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī.” ^an2-132-1-3

133 ^an2-133-1-0

“A faithful laywoman would rightly aspire: ^an2-133-1-1

‘May I be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother!’ ^an2-133-1-2

These are a standard and a measure for my female lay disciples, that is, the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother.” ^an2-133-1-3

134 ^an2-134-1-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has two qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-134-1-1

What two? ^an2-134-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism ^an2-134-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an2-134-1-4

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these two qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness. ^an2-134-1-5

When an astute, competent true person has two qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an2-134-2-1

What two? ^an2-134-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism ^an2-134-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an2-134-2-4

When an astute, competent true person possesses these two qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an2-134-2-5

135 ^an2-135-1-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has two qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-135-1-1

What two? ^an2-135-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an2-135-1-3

and they don’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an2-135-1-4

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these two qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness. ^an2-135-1-5

When an astute, competent true person has two qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an2-135-2-1

What two? ^an2-135-2-2

After examining or scrutinizing, they don’t arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an2-135-2-3

and they do arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an2-135-2-4

When an astute, competent true person possesses these two qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an2-135-2-5

136 ^an2-136-1-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward two individuals they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-136-1-1

What two? ^an2-136-1-2

Mother and father. ^an2-136-1-3

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward these two individuals they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-136-1-4

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward two individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an2-136-2-1

What two? ^an2-136-2-2

Mother and father. ^an2-136-2-3

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward these two individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit.” ^an2-136-2-4

137 ^an2-137-1-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward two individuals they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-137-1-1

What two? ^an2-137-1-2

The Realized One and a disciple of the Realized One. ^an2-137-1-3

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward these people they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an2-137-1-4

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward two individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an2-137-2-1

What two? ^an2-137-2-2

The Realized One and a disciple of the Realized One. ^an2-137-2-3

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward these two individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit.” ^an2-137-2-4

138 ^an2-138-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-138-1-1

What two? ^an2-138-1-2

Cleaning your own mind, and not grasping at anything in the world. ^an2-138-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-138-1-4

139 ^an2-139-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-139-1-1

What two? ^an2-139-1-2

Anger and acrimony. ^an2-139-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-139-1-4

140 ^an2-140-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-140-1-1

What two? ^an2-140-1-2

Dispelling anger and dispelling acrimony. ^an2-140-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-140-1-4

163 ^an2-163-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-163-1-1

What two? ^an2-163-1-2

Skill in meditative attainments and skill in emerging from those attainments. ^an2-163-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-163-1-4

164 ^an2-164-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-164-1-1

What two? ^an2-164-1-2

Integrity and tenderness. ^an2-164-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-164-1-4

165 ^an2-165-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-165-1-1

What two? ^an2-165-1-2

Patience and gentleness. ^an2-165-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-165-1-4

166 ^an2-166-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-166-1-1

What two? ^an2-166-1-2

Camaraderie and hospitality. ^an2-166-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-166-1-4

167 ^an2-167-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-167-1-1

What two? ^an2-167-1-2

Harmlessness and purity. ^an2-167-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-167-1-4

168 ^an2-168-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-168-1-1

What two? ^an2-168-1-2

Not guarding the sense doors and eating too much. ^an2-168-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-168-1-4

169 ^an2-169-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-169-1-1

What two? ^an2-169-1-2

Guarding the sense doors and moderation in eating. ^an2-169-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-169-1-4

170 ^an2-170-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-170-1-1

What two? ^an2-170-1-2

The power of reflection and the power of development. ^an2-170-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-170-1-4

171 ^an2-171-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-171-1-1

What two? ^an2-171-1-2

The power of mindfulness and the power of immersion. ^an2-171-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-171-1-4

172 ^an2-172-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-172-1-1

What two? ^an2-172-1-2

Serenity and discernment. ^an2-172-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-172-1-4

173 ^an2-173-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-173-1-1

What two? ^an2-173-1-2

Failure in ethical conduct and failure in view. ^an2-173-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-173-1-4

174 ^an2-174-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-174-1-1

What two? ^an2-174-1-2

Accomplishment in ethical conduct and accomplishment in view. ^an2-174-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-174-1-4

175 ^an2-175-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-175-1-1

What two? ^an2-175-1-2

Purification of ethics and purification of view. ^an2-175-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-175-1-4

176 ^an2-176-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-176-1-1

What two? ^an2-176-1-2

Purification of view and making an effort in line with that view. ^an2-176-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-176-1-4

177 ^an2-177-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-177-1-1

What two? ^an2-177-1-2

To never be content with skillful qualities, and to never stop trying. ^an2-177-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-177-1-4

178 ^an2-178-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-178-1-1

What two? ^an2-178-1-2

Lack of mindfulness and lack of situational awareness. ^an2-178-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-178-1-4

179 ^an2-179-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-179-1-1

What two? ^an2-179-1-2

Mindfulness and situational awareness. ^an2-179-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-179-1-4

### The Chapter of Abbreviated Texts Beginning With Anger (Numbered Discourses 2.180–229) ^an2-180-229-0

180–184 ^an2-180-184-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-180-184-1-1

What two? ^an2-180-184-1-2

Anger and acrimony … disdain and contempt … jealousy and stinginess … deceit and deviousness … lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-180-184-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-180-184-1-4

185–189 ^an2-185-189-1-0

“There are these two things. ^an2-185-189-1-1

What two? ^an2-185-189-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-185-189-1-3

These are the two things.” ^an2-185-189-1-4

190–194 ^an2-190-194-1-0

“Anyone who has two things lives in suffering. ^an2-190-194-1-1

What two? ^an2-190-194-1-2

Anger and acrimony … disdain and contempt … jealousy and stinginess … deceit and deviousness … lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-190-194-1-3

Anyone who has these two things lives in suffering.” ^an2-190-194-1-4

195–199 ^an2-195-199-1-0

“Anyone who has these two things lives happily. ^an2-195-199-1-1

What two? ^an2-195-199-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-195-199-1-3

Anyone who has these two things lives happily.” ^an2-195-199-1-4

200–204 ^an2-200-204-1-0

“These two things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an2-200-204-1-1

What two? ^an2-200-204-1-2

Anger and acrimony … disdain and contempt … jealousy and stinginess … deceit and deviousness … lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-200-204-1-3

These two things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an2-200-204-1-4

205–209 ^an2-205-209-1-0

“These two things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an2-205-209-1-1

What two? ^an2-205-209-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-205-209-1-3

These two things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an2-205-209-1-4

210–214 ^an2-210-214-1-0

“Anyone who has two things is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an2-210-214-1-1

What two? ^an2-210-214-1-2

Anger and acrimony … disdain and contempt … jealousy and stinginess … deceit and deviousness … lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-210-214-1-3

Anyone who has these two things is placed in hell as if delivered there.” ^an2-210-214-1-4

215–219 ^an2-215-219-1-0

“Anyone who has two things is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an2-215-219-1-1

What two? ^an2-215-219-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-215-219-1-3

Anyone who has these two things is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an2-215-219-1-4

220–224 ^an2-220-224-1-0

“When they have two things, some people, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an2-220-224-1-1

What two? ^an2-220-224-1-2

Anger and acrimony … disdain and contempt … jealousy and stinginess … deceit and deviousness … lack of conscience and prudence. ^an2-220-224-1-3

When they have two things, some people, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.” ^an2-220-224-1-4

225–229 ^an2-225-229-1-0

“When they have two things, some people—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an2-225-229-1-1

What two? ^an2-225-229-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-225-229-1-3

When they have these two things, some people—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^an2-225-229-1-4

### The Chapter of Abbreviated Texts Beginning With the Unskillful (Numbered Discourses 2) ^an2-230-279-0

“These two things are unskillful … are skillful … are blameworthy … are blameless … have suffering as outcome … have happiness as outcome … result in suffering … result in happiness … are hurtful … are not hurtful. ^an2-230-279-1-1

What two? ^an2-230-279-1-2

Freedom from anger and acrimony … freedom from disdain and contempt … freedom from jealousy and stinginess … freedom from deceit and deviousness … conscience and prudence. ^an2-230-279-1-3

These are the two things that are not hurtful.” ^an2-230-279-1-4

### The Chapter of Abbreviated Texts on Monastic Law (Numbered Discourses 2.280–309) ^an2-280-309-0

280 ^an2-280-1-0

“For two reasons the Realized One laid down training rules for his disciples. ^an2-280-1-1

What two? ^an2-280-1-2

For the well-being and comfort of the Saṅgha … For keeping difficult individuals in check and for the comfort of good-hearted mendicants … For restraining defilements that affect this life and protecting against defilements that affect lives to come … For restraining threats to this life and protecting against threats to lives to come … For restraining faults that affect this life and protecting against faults that affect lives to come … For restraining hazards that affect this life and protecting against hazards that affect lives to come … For restraining unskillful qualities that affect this life and protecting against unskillful qualities that affect lives to come … Out of sympathy for laypeople and for breaking up factions of mendicants with corrupt wishes … For inspiring confidence in those without it, and increasing confidence in those who have it … For the continuation of the true teaching and the support of the training. ^an2-280-1-3

These are the two reasons why the Realized One laid down training rules for his disciples.” ^an2-280-1-4

281–309 ^an2-281-309-1-0

“For two reasons the Realized One laid down for his disciples the monastic code … the recitation of the monastic code … the suspension of the recitation of the monastic code … the invitation to admonish … the setting aside of the invitation to admonish … the disciplinary act of condemnation … placing under dependence … banishment … reconciliation … debarment … probation … being sent back to the beginning … penance … reinstatement … restoration … removal … ordination … an act with a motion … an act with a motion and one announcement … an act with a motion and three announcements … laying down what was not previously laid down … amending what was laid down … the settling of a disciplinary matter by the following methods: face-to-face … through recollection … because of past insanity … acting according to what has been admitted … majority decision … further penalty…  covering over as if with grass. ^an2-281-309-1-1

What two? ^an2-281-309-1-2

For the well-being and comfort of the Saṅgha … For keeping difficult individuals in check and for the comfort of good-hearted mendicants … For restraining defilements that affect this life and protecting against defilements that affect lives to come … For restraining threats to this life and protecting against threats to lives to come … For restraining faults that affect this life and protecting against faults that affect lives to come … For restraining hazards that affect this life and protecting against hazards that affect lives to come … For restraining unskillful qualities that affect this life and protecting against unskillful qualities that affect lives to come … Out of sympathy for laypeople and for breaking up factions of mendicants with corrupt wishes … For inspiring confidence in those without it, and increasing confidence in those who have it … For the continuation of the true teaching and the support of the training. ^an2-281-309-1-3

These are the two reasons why the Realized One laid down the settlement of a disciplinary matter by covering over with grass for his disciples.” ^an2-281-309-1-4

### The Chapter of Abbreviated Texts Beginning with Greed (Numbered Discourses 2) ^an2-310-479-0

310–319 ^an2-310-319-1-0

“For insight into greed, two things should be developed. ^an2-310-319-1-1

What two? ^an2-310-319-1-2

Serenity and discernment. ^an2-310-319-1-3

For insight into greed, these two things should be developed.” ^an2-310-319-1-4

“For the complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go of greed, two things should be developed.” ^an2-310-319-2-1

320–479 ^an2-320-479-1-0

“Of hate … delusion … anger … acrimony … disdain … contempt … jealousy … stinginess … deceit … deviousness … obstinacy … aggression … conceit … arrogance … vanity … negligence … for insight … complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go … two things should be developed. ^an2-320-479-1-1

What two? ^an2-320-479-1-2

Serenity and discernment. ^an2-320-479-1-3

For the letting go of negligence, these two things should be developed.” ^an2-320-479-1-4

That is what the Buddha said. ^an2-320-479-2-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an2-320-479-2-2

The Book of the Twos is finished. ^an2-320-479-2-4

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 3 ^an3-0

### Perils (Numbered Discourses 3.1) ^an3-1-0

So I have heard. ^an3-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an3-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an3-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an3-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an3-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an3-1-1-6

“Whatever dangers there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-1

Whatever perils there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-2

Whatever hazards there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-3

It’s like a fire that spreads from a hut made of reeds or grass, and burns down even a bungalow, plastered inside and out, draft-free, with doors fastened and windows shuttered. ^an3-1-2-4

In the same way, whatever dangers there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-5

Whatever perils there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-6

Whatever hazards there are, all come from the foolish, not from the astute. ^an3-1-2-7

So, the fool is dangerous, but the astute person is safe. ^an3-1-3-1

The fool is perilous, but the astute person is not. ^an3-1-3-2

The fool is hazardous, but the astute person is not. ^an3-1-3-3

There’s no danger, peril, or hazard that comes from the astute. ^an3-1-3-4

So you should train like this: ^an3-1-4-1

‘We will shun the three things by which a fool is known, and we will undertake and follow the three things by which an astute person is known.’ ^an3-1-4-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-1-4-3

### Characteristics (Numbered Discourses 3.2) ^an3-2-0

“A fool is characterized by their deeds, and an astute person is characterized by their deeds, for wisdom shines in its traces. ^an3-2-1-1

A fool is known by three things. ^an3-2-1-2

What three? ^an3-2-1-3

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-2-1-4

These are the three things by which a fool is known. ^an3-2-1-5

An astute person is known by three things. ^an3-2-2-1

What three? ^an3-2-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-2-2-3

These are the three things by which an astute person is known. ^an3-2-2-4

So you should train like this: ^an3-2-3-1

‘We will shun the three things by which a fool is known, and we will undertake and follow the three things by which an astute person is known.’ ^an3-2-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-2-3-3

### Thinking (Numbered Discourses 3.3) ^an3-3-0

“There are these three characteristics, signs, and manifestations of a fool. ^an3-3-1-1

What three? ^an3-3-1-2

A fool thinks poorly, speaks poorly, and acts poorly. ^an3-3-1-3

If a fool didn’t think poorly, speak poorly, and act poorly, then how would the astute know of them: ^an3-3-1-4

‘This worthy one is a fool, an untrue person’? ^an3-3-1-5

But since a fool does think poorly, speak poorly, and act poorly, then the astute do know of them: ^an3-3-1-6

‘This worthy one is a fool, an untrue person’. ^an3-3-1-7

These are the three characteristics, signs, and manifestations of a fool. ^an3-3-1-8

There are these three characteristics, signs, and manifestations of an astute person. ^an3-3-2-1

What three? ^an3-3-2-2

An astute person thinks well, speaks well, and acts well. ^an3-3-2-3

If an astute person didn’t think well, speak well, and act well, then how would the astute know of them: ^an3-3-2-4

‘This worthy one is astute, a true person’? ^an3-3-2-5

But since an astute person does think well, speak well, and act well, then the astute do know of them: ^an3-3-2-6

‘This worthy one is astute, a true person’. ^an3-3-2-7

These are the three characteristics, signs, and manifestations of an astute person. ^an3-3-2-8

So you should train …” ^an3-3-2-9

### Mistakes (Numbered Discourses 3.4) ^an3-4-0

“A fool is known by three things. ^an3-4-1-1

What three? ^an3-4-1-2

They don’t recognize when they’ve made a mistake. When they do recognize it they don’t deal with it properly. And when someone else confesses a mistake to them, they don’t accept it properly. ^an3-4-1-3

These are the three things by which a fool is known. ^an3-4-1-4

An astute person is known by three things. ^an3-4-2-1

What three? ^an3-4-2-2

They recognize when they’ve made a mistake. When they recognize it they deal with it properly. And when someone else confesses a mistake to them, they accept it properly. ^an3-4-2-3

These are the three things by which an astute person is known. ^an3-4-2-4

So you should train …” ^an3-4-2-5

### Irrational (Numbered Discourses 3.5) ^an3-5-0

“A fool is known by three things. ^an3-5-1-1

What three? ^an3-5-1-2

They ask a question irrationally. They answer a question irrationally. And when someone else answers a question rationally—with well-rounded, coherent, and relevant words and phrases—they disagree with it. ^an3-5-1-3

These are the three things by which a fool is known. ^an3-5-1-4

An astute person is known by three things. ^an3-5-2-1

What three? ^an3-5-2-2

They ask a question rationally. They answer a question rationally. And when someone else answers a question rationally—with well-rounded, coherent, and relevant words and phrases—they agree with it. ^an3-5-2-3

These are the three things by which an astute person is known. ^an3-5-2-4

So you should train …” ^an3-5-2-5

### Unskillful (Numbered Discourses 3.6) ^an3-6-0

“A fool is known by three things. ^an3-6-1-1

What three? ^an3-6-1-2

Unskillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-6-1-3

These are the three things by which a fool is known. ^an3-6-1-4

An astute person is known by three things. ^an3-6-2-1

What three? ^an3-6-2-2

Skillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-6-2-3

These are the three things by which an astute person is known. ^an3-6-2-4

So you should train …” ^an3-6-2-5

### Blameworthy (Numbered Discourses 3.7) ^an3-7-0

“A fool is known by three things. ^an3-7-1-1

What three? ^an3-7-1-2

Blameworthy deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … An astute person is known by blameless deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-7-1-3

### Hurtful (Numbered Discourses 3.8) ^an3-8-0

“A fool is known by three things. ^an3-8-1-1

What three? ^an3-8-1-2

Hurtful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … An astute person is known by kind deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-8-1-3

These are the three things by which an astute person is known. ^an3-8-1-4

So you should train like this: ^an3-8-2-1

‘We will shun the three qualities by which a fool is known, and we will undertake and follow the three qualities by which an astute person is known.’ ^an3-8-2-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-8-2-3

### Broken (Numbered Discourses 3.9) ^an3-9-0

“When a foolish, incompetent, untrue person has three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an3-9-1-1

What three? ^an3-9-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-9-1-3

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness. ^an3-9-1-4

When an astute, competent true person has three qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an3-9-2-1

What three? ^an3-9-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-9-2-3

When an astute, competent true person possesses these three qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an3-9-2-4

### Stains (Numbered Discourses 3.10) ^an3-10-0

“Anyone who has three qualities, and has not given up three stains, is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-10-1-1

What three? ^an3-10-1-2

They’re unethical, and haven’t given up the stain of immorality. ^an3-10-1-3

They’re jealous, and haven’t given up the stain of jealousy. ^an3-10-1-4

They’re stingy, and haven’t given up the stain of stinginess. ^an3-10-1-5

Anyone who possesses these three qualities, and has not given up these three stains, is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-10-1-6

Anyone who has three qualities, and has given up three stains, is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an3-10-2-1

What three? ^an3-10-2-2

They’re ethical, and have given up the stain of immorality. ^an3-10-2-3

They’re not jealous, and have given up the stain of jealousy. ^an3-10-2-4

They’re not stingy, and have given up the stain of stinginess. ^an3-10-2-5

Anyone who possesses these three qualities, and has given up these three stains, is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an3-10-2-6

### Well-known (Numbered Discourses 3.11) ^an3-11-0

“Mendicants, a well-known mendicant who has three qualities is acting for the detriment and suffering of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of gods and humans. ^an3-11-1-1

What three? ^an3-11-1-2

They encourage deeds of body and speech, as well as principles, that aren’t in line with good qualities. ^an3-11-1-3

A well-known mendicant who possesses these three qualities is acting for the detriment and suffering of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of gods and humans. ^an3-11-1-4

A well-known mendicant who has three qualities is acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an3-11-2-1

What three? ^an3-11-2-2

They encourage deeds of body and speech, as well as principles, that are in line with good qualities. ^an3-11-2-3

A well-known mendicant who possesses these three qualities is acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.” ^an3-11-2-4

### Commemoration (Numbered Discourses 3.12) ^an3-12-0

“An anointed aristocratic king should commemorate three places as long as he lives. ^an3-12-1-1

What three? ^an3-12-1-2

The place he was born. ^an3-12-1-3

This is the first place. ^an3-12-1-4

The place he was anointed as king. ^an3-12-2-1

This is the second place. ^an3-12-2-2

The place where he won victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an3-12-3-1

This is the third place. ^an3-12-3-2

These are the three places an anointed king should commemorate as long as he lives. ^an3-12-3-3

In the same way, a mendicant should commemorate three places as long as they live. ^an3-12-4-1

What three? ^an3-12-4-2

The place where the mendicant shaved off their hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an3-12-4-3

This is the first place. ^an3-12-4-4

The place where the mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-12-5-1

This is the second place. ^an3-12-5-2

The place where the mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-12-6-1

This is the third place. ^an3-12-6-2

These are the three places a mendicant should commemorate as long as they live.” ^an3-12-6-3

### Hopes (Numbered Discourses 3.13) ^an3-13-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-13-1-1

What three? ^an3-13-1-2

The hopeless, the hopeful, and the one who has done away with hope. ^an3-13-1-3

And what, mendicants, is a hopeless individual? ^an3-13-1-4

It’s when an individual is reborn in a low family—a family of corpse-workers, bamboo-workers, hunters, chariot-makers, or scavengers—poor, with little to eat or drink, where life is tough, and food and shelter are hard to find. ^an3-13-1-5

And they’re ugly, unsightly, deformed, sickly—one-eyed, crippled, lame, or half-paralyzed. They don’t get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrances, and makeup; or bed, house, and lighting. ^an3-13-1-6

They hear this: ^an3-13-1-7

‘They say the aristocrats have anointed the aristocrat named so-and-so as king.’ ^an3-13-1-8

It never occurs to them: ^an3-13-1-9

‘Oh, when will the aristocrats anoint me too as king?’ ^an3-13-1-10

This is called a hopeless individual. ^an3-13-1-11

And what, mendicants, is a hopeful individual? ^an3-13-2-1

It’s when someone is the eldest son of an anointed aristocratic king. He has not yet been anointed, but is eligible, and has been confirmed in the succession. ^an3-13-2-2

He hears this: ^an3-13-2-3

‘They say that the aristocrats have anointed the aristocrat named so-and-so as king.’ ^an3-13-2-4

It occurs to him: ^an3-13-2-5

‘Oh, when will the aristocrats anoint me too as king?’ ^an3-13-2-6

This is called a hopeful individual. ^an3-13-2-7

And what, mendicants, is an individual who has done away with hope? ^an3-13-3-1

It’s when a king has been anointed. ^an3-13-3-2

He hears this: ^an3-13-3-3

‘They say that the aristocrats have anointed the aristocrat named so-and-so as king.’ ^an3-13-3-4

It never occurs to him: ^an3-13-3-5

‘Oh, when will the aristocrats anoint me too as king?’ ^an3-13-3-6

Why is that? ^an3-13-3-7

Because the former hope he had to be anointed has now died down. ^an3-13-3-8

This is called an individual who has done away with hope. ^an3-13-3-9

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-13-3-10

In the same way, these three individuals are found among the mendicants. ^an3-13-4-1

What three? ^an3-13-4-2

The hopeless, the hopeful, and the one who has done away with hope. ^an3-13-4-3

And what, mendicants, is a hopeless individual? ^an3-13-4-4

It’s when an individual is unethical, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved. ^an3-13-4-5

They hear this: ^an3-13-4-6

‘They say that the mendicant named so-and-so has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-4-7

It never occurs to them: ^an3-13-4-8

‘Oh, when will I too realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-4-9

This is called a hopeless individual. ^an3-13-4-10

And what, mendicants, is a hopeful individual? ^an3-13-5-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, of good character. ^an3-13-5-2

They hear this: ^an3-13-5-3

‘They say that the mendicant named so-and-so has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-5-4

It occurs to them: ^an3-13-5-5

‘Oh, when will I too realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-5-6

This is called a hopeful individual. ^an3-13-5-7

And what, mendicants, is an individual who has done away with hope? ^an3-13-6-1

It’s when a mendicant is a perfected one, who has ended all defilements. ^an3-13-6-2

They hear this: ^an3-13-6-3

‘They say that the mendicant named so-and-so has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-6-4

It never occurs to them: ^an3-13-6-5

‘Oh, when will I too realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-13-6-6

Why is that? ^an3-13-6-7

Because the former hope they had to be freed has now died down. ^an3-13-6-8

This is called an individual who has done away with hope. ^an3-13-6-9

These are the three individuals found among the mendicants.” ^an3-13-6-10

### The Wheel-Turning Monarch (Numbered Discourses 3.14) ^an3-14-0

“Mendicants, even a wheel-turning monarch, a just and principled king, does not wield power without having their own king.” ^an3-14-1-1

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha: ^an3-14-1-2

“But who is the king of the wheel-turning monarch, the just and principled king?” ^an3-14-1-3

“It is principle, monk,” said the Buddha. ^an3-14-1-4

“Monk, a wheel-turning monarch provides just protection and security for his court, relying only on principle—honoring, respecting, and venerating principle, having principle as his flag, banner, and authority. ^an3-14-1-5

He provides just protection and security for his aristocrats, vassals, troops, brahmins and householders, people of town and country, ascetics and brahmins, beasts and birds. ^an3-14-2-1

When he has done this, he wields power only in a principled manner. ^an3-14-2-2

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an3-14-2-3

In the same way, monk, a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, a just and principled king, provides just protection and security regarding bodily actions, relying only on principle—honoring, respecting, and venerating principle, having principle as his flag, banner, and authority. ^an3-14-3-1

‘This kind of bodily action should be cultivated. This kind of bodily action should not be cultivated.’ ^an3-14-3-2

Furthermore, a Realized One … provides just protection and security regarding verbal actions, saying: ^an3-14-4-1

‘This kind of verbal action should be cultivated. This kind of verbal action should not be cultivated.’ … And regarding mental actions: ^an3-14-4-2

‘This kind of mental action should be cultivated. This kind of mental action should not be cultivated.’ ^an3-14-4-3

And when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has provided just protection and security regarding actions of body, speech, and mind, he rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma. ^an3-14-5-1

And that wheel cannot be rolled back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world.” ^an3-14-5-2

### About Pacetana (Numbered Discourses 3.15) ^an3-15-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^an3-15-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an3-15-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an3-15-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an3-15-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an3-15-1-5

“Once upon a time there was a king named Pacetana. ^an3-15-2-1

Then King Pacetana addressed his chariot-maker, ^an3-15-2-2

‘In six months’ time, my good chariot-maker, there will be a battle. ^an3-15-2-3

Are you able to make me a new pair of wheels?’ ^an3-15-2-4

‘I can, Your Majesty,’ replied the chariot-maker. ^an3-15-2-5

Then, when it was six days less than six months later, the chariot-maker had finished one wheel. ^an3-15-2-6

Then King Pacetana addressed his chariot-maker, ^an3-15-2-7

‘In six days’ time there will be a battle. Is my new pair of wheels finished?’ ^an3-15-2-8

‘Now that it is six days less than six months, Your Majesty, I have finished one wheel.’ ^an3-15-2-9

‘Are you able to finish the second wheel in these six days?’ ^an3-15-2-10

Saying, ‘I can, Your Majesty,’ the chariot-maker finished the second wheel in six days. Taking the pair of wheels he went up to King Pacetana, and said this to the king, ^an3-15-2-11

‘Your Majesty, these are your two new wheels, finished.’ ^an3-15-2-12

‘But, my good chariot-maker, what is the difference between the wheel that was finished in six days less than six months, and the wheel finished in just six days? ^an3-15-2-13

Because I can’t see any difference between them.’ ^an3-15-2-14

‘But, Your Majesty, there is a difference. ^an3-15-2-15

See now what it is.’ ^an3-15-2-16

Then the chariot-maker rolled forth the wheel that had been finished in six days. ^an3-15-3-1

It rolled as far as the original impetus took it, then wobbled and fell down. ^an3-15-3-2

Then he rolled forth the wheel that had been finished in six days less than six months. ^an3-15-3-3

It rolled as far as the original impetus took it, then stood still as if fixed to an axle. ^an3-15-3-4

‘But what is the cause, my good chariot-maker, what is the reason why the wheel that was finished in six days wobbled and fell, ^an3-15-4-1

while the one that was finished in six days less than six months stood still as if fixed to an axle?’ ^an3-15-4-2

‘The wheel that was finished in six days, Your Majesty, is crooked, flawed, and defective in rim, spoke, and hub. ^an3-15-4-3

That’s why it wobbled and fell. ^an3-15-4-4

The wheel that was finished in six days less than six months, Your Majesty, is not crooked, flawed, and defective in rim, spoke, and hub. ^an3-15-4-5

That’s why it stood still as if fixed to an axle.’ ^an3-15-4-6

Now, mendicants, you might think: ^an3-15-5-1

‘Surely that chariot-maker must have been someone else at that time?’ ^an3-15-5-2

But you should not see it like that. ^an3-15-5-3

I myself was the chariot-maker at that time. ^an3-15-5-4

Then I was skilled in the crooks, flaws, and defects of wood. ^an3-15-5-5

Now that I am a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, I am skilled in the crooks, flaws, and defects of actions by body, speech, and mind. ^an3-15-5-6

Whatever monk or nun has not given up the crooks, flaws, and defects of body, speech, and mind has fallen from the teaching and training, just like the wheel that was finished in six days. ^an3-15-5-7

Whatever monk or nun has given up the crooks, flaws, and defects of body, speech, and mind is established in the teaching and training, just like the wheel that was finished in six days less than six months. ^an3-15-6-1

So you should train like this: ^an3-15-7-1

‘We will give up the crooks, flaws, and defects of body, speech, and mind.’ ^an3-15-7-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-15-7-3

### A Sure Bet (Numbered Discourses 3.16) ^an3-16-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has three things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements. ^an3-16-1-1

What three? ^an3-16-1-2

It’s when a mendicant guards the sense doors, eats in moderation, and is dedicated to wakefulness. ^an3-16-1-3

And how does a mendicant guard the sense doors? ^an3-16-2-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an3-16-2-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^an3-16-2-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an3-16-2-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an3-16-2-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an3-16-2-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an3-16-2-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an3-16-2-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^an3-16-2-9

That’s how a mendicant guards the sense doors. ^an3-16-2-10

And how does a mendicant eat in moderation? ^an3-16-3-1

It’s when a mendicant reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^an3-16-3-2

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^an3-16-3-3

That’s how a mendicant eats in moderation. ^an3-16-3-4

And how is a mendicant dedicated to wakefulness? ^an3-16-4-1

It’s when a mendicant practices walking and sitting meditation by day, purifying their mind from obstacles. In the first watch of the night, they continue to practice walking and sitting meditation. In the middle watch, they lie down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. In the last watch, they get up and continue to practice walking and sitting meditation, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^an3-16-4-2

This is how a mendicant is dedicated to wakefulness. ^an3-16-4-3

When a mendicant possesses these three things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements.” ^an3-16-4-4

### Hurting Yourself (Numbered Discourses 3.17) ^an3-17-0

“These three things, mendicants, lead to hurting yourself, hurting others, and hurting both. ^an3-17-1-1

What three? ^an3-17-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-17-1-3

These are three things that lead to hurting yourself, hurting others, and hurting both. ^an3-17-1-4

These three things, mendicants, don’t lead to hurting yourself, hurting others, or hurting both. ^an3-17-2-1

What three? ^an3-17-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-17-2-3

These are three things that don’t lead to hurting yourself, hurting others, or hurting both.” ^an3-17-2-4

### The Realm of the Gods (Numbered Discourses 3.18) ^an3-18-0

“Mendicants, if wanderers who follow another religion were to ask you: ^an3-18-1-1

‘Reverend, do you lead the spiritual life with the ascetic Gotama so that you can be reborn in the realm of the gods?’ ^an3-18-1-2

Being questioned like this, wouldn’t you be horrified, repelled, and disgusted?” ^an3-18-1-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-18-1-4

“So it seems that you are horrified, repelled, and disgusted by heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, and sovereignty. ^an3-18-1-5

How much more then should you be horrified, embarrassed, and disgusted by bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind.” ^an3-18-1-6

### A Shopkeeper (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.19) ^an3-19-0

“Mendicants, a shopkeeper who has three factors is unable to acquire more wealth or to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-1-1

What three? ^an3-19-1-2

It’s when a shopkeeper doesn’t carefully focus on their work in the morning, at midday, and in the afternoon. ^an3-19-1-3

A shopkeeper who has these three factors is unable to acquire more wealth or to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant who has three qualities is unable to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-2-1

What three? ^an3-19-2-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t carefully focus on a meditation subject as a basis of immersion in the morning, at midday, and in the afternoon. ^an3-19-2-3

A mendicant who possesses these three qualities is unable to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-2-4

A shopkeeper who has three factors is able to acquire more wealth or to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-3-1

What three? ^an3-19-3-2

It’s when a shopkeeper carefully focuses on their work in the morning, at midday, and in the afternoon. ^an3-19-3-3

A shopkeeper who has these three factors is able to acquire more wealth or to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-3-4

In the same way, a mendicant who has three qualities is able to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an3-19-4-1

What three? ^an3-19-4-2

It’s when a mendicant carefully focuses on a meditation subject as a basis of immersion in the morning, at midday, and in the afternoon. ^an3-19-4-3

A mendicant who possesses these three qualities is able to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired.” ^an3-19-4-4

### A Shopkeeper (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.20) ^an3-20-0

“Mendicants, a shopkeeper who has three factors soon acquires great and abundant wealth. ^an3-20-1-1

What three? ^an3-20-1-2

It’s when a shopkeeper sees clearly, is indefatigable, and has supporters. ^an3-20-1-3

And how does a shopkeeper see clearly? ^an3-20-1-4

It’s when a shopkeeper knows of a product: ^an3-20-1-5

‘This product is bought at this price and is selling at this price. With this much investment, it’ll bring this much profit.’ ^an3-20-1-6

That’s how a shopkeeper sees clearly. ^an3-20-1-7

And how is a shopkeeper indefatigable? ^an3-20-2-1

It’s when a shopkeeper is skilled in buying and selling products. ^an3-20-2-2

That’s how a shopkeeper is indefatigable. ^an3-20-2-3

And how does a shopkeeper have supporters? ^an3-20-3-1

It’s when rich, affluent, and wealthy householders or householders’ children know of him: ^an3-20-3-2

‘This good shopkeeper sees clearly and is indefatigable. They are capable of providing for their wives and children, and paying us back from time to time.’ ^an3-20-3-3

They deposit money with the shopkeeper, saying: ^an3-20-3-4

‘With this, my good shopkeeper, earn money to provide for your wives and children, and pay us back from time to time.’ ^an3-20-3-5

That’s how a shopkeeper has supporters. ^an3-20-3-6

A shopkeeper who has these three factors soon acquires great and abundant wealth. ^an3-20-3-7

In the same way, a mendicant who has three qualities soon acquires great and abundant skillful qualities. ^an3-20-4-1

What three? ^an3-20-4-2

It’s when a mendicant sees clearly, is indefatigable, and has supporters. ^an3-20-4-3

And how does a mendicant see clearly? ^an3-20-4-4

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-20-4-5

That’s how a mendicant sees clearly. ^an3-20-4-6

And how is a mendicant indefatigable? ^an3-20-5-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an3-20-5-2

That’s how a mendicant is indefatigable. ^an3-20-5-3

And how does a mendicant have supporters? ^an3-20-6-1

It’s when from time to time a mendicant goes up to those mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—and asks them questions: ^an3-20-6-2

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an3-20-6-3

Those venerables reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an3-20-6-4

That’s how a mendicant has supporters. ^an3-20-6-5

A mendicant who possesses these three qualities soon acquires great and abundant skillful qualities.” ^an3-20-6-6

### With Saviṭṭha (Numbered Discourses 3.21) ^an3-21-0

So I have heard. ^an3-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an3-21-1-2

Then Venerable Saviṭṭha and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-21-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and Venerable Sāriputta said to Venerable Saviṭṭha, ^an3-21-1-4

“Reverend Saviṭṭha, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-2-1

What three? ^an3-21-2-2

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-2-3

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-2-4

Of these three individuals, who do you believe to be the finest?” ^an3-21-2-5

“Reverend Sāriputta, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-3-1

What three? ^an3-21-3-2

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-3-3

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-3-4

Of these three individuals, I believe the individual freed by faith to be finest. ^an3-21-3-5

Why is that? ^an3-21-3-6

Because this individual’s faculty of faith is extraordinary.” ^an3-21-3-7

Then Sāriputta said to Mahākoṭṭhita, ^an3-21-4-1

“Reverend Koṭṭhika, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-4-2

What three? ^an3-21-4-3

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-4-4

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-4-5

Of these three individuals, who do you believe to be the finest?” ^an3-21-4-6

“Reverend Sāriputta, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-5-1

What three? ^an3-21-5-2

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-5-3

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-5-4

Of these three individuals, I believe the direct witness to be finest. ^an3-21-5-5

Why is that? ^an3-21-5-6

Because this individuals’s faculty of immersion is extraordinary.” ^an3-21-5-7

Then Mahākoṭṭhita said to Sāriputta, ^an3-21-6-1

“Reverend Sāriputta, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-6-2

What three? ^an3-21-6-3

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-6-4

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-6-5

Of these three individuals, who do you believe to be the finest?” ^an3-21-6-6

“Reverend Koṭṭhika, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-21-7-1

What three? ^an3-21-7-2

The direct witness, the one attained to view, and the one freed by faith. ^an3-21-7-3

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-21-7-4

Of these three individuals, I believe the individual attained to view to be finest. ^an3-21-7-5

Why is that? ^an3-21-7-6

Because this individual’s faculty of wisdom is extraordinary.” ^an3-21-7-7

Then Sāriputta said to Saviṭṭha and Mahākoṭṭhita, ^an3-21-8-1

“Each of us has spoken from the heart. ^an3-21-8-2

Come, reverends, let’s go to the Buddha, and tell him about this. ^an3-21-8-3

As he answers, so we’ll remember it.” ^an3-21-8-4

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^an3-21-8-5

Then Sāriputta, Saviṭṭha, and Mahākoṭṭhita went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an3-21-8-6

Then Sāriputta told the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^an3-21-8-7

“In this matter, Sāriputta, it’s not easy to categorically declare that ^an3-21-9-1

one of these three individuals is finest. ^an3-21-9-2

In some cases, an individual freed by faith is practicing for perfection, while the direct witness and the one attained to view are once-returners or non-returners. ^an3-21-9-3

In this matter, it’s not easy to categorically declare that ^an3-21-10-1

one of these three individuals is finest. ^an3-21-10-2

In some cases, a direct witness is practicing for perfection, while the one freed by faith and the one attained to view are once-returners or non-returners. ^an3-21-10-3

In this matter, it’s not easy to categorically declare that ^an3-21-11-1

one of these three individuals is finest. ^an3-21-11-2

In some cases, one attained to view is practicing for perfection, while the one freed by faith and the direct witness are once-returners or non-returners. ^an3-21-11-3

In this matter, it’s not easy to categorically declare that ^an3-21-12-1

one of these three individuals is finest.” ^an3-21-12-2

### Patients (Numbered Discourses 3.22) ^an3-22-0

“These three patients are found in the world. ^an3-22-1-1

What three? ^an3-22-1-2

In some cases a patient won’t recover from an illness, regardless of whether or not they get suitable food and medicines, and a capable carer. ^an3-22-1-3

In some cases a patient will recover from an illness, regardless of whether or not they get suitable food and medicines, and a capable carer. ^an3-22-2-1

In some cases a patient can recover from an illness, but only if they get suitable food and medicines, and a capable carer, and not if they don’t get these things. ^an3-22-3-1

Now, it’s for the sake of the last patient—who will recover only if they get suitable food and medicines, and a capable carer—that food, medicines, and a carer are prescribed. ^an3-22-4-1

But also, for the sake of this patient, the other patients should be looked after. ^an3-22-4-2

These are the three kinds of patients found in the world. ^an3-22-4-3

In the same way, these three individuals similar to patients are found in the world. ^an3-22-5-1

What three? ^an3-22-5-2

Some people don’t step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, regardless of whether or not they get to see a Realized One, and to hear the teaching and training that he proclaims. ^an3-22-5-3

Some people do step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, regardless of whether or not they get to see a Realized One, and to hear the teaching and training that he proclaims. ^an3-22-6-1

Some people can step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, but only if they get to see a Realized One, and to hear the teaching and training that he proclaims, and not when they don’t get those things. ^an3-22-7-1

Now, it’s for the sake of this last individual that teaching the Dhamma is prescribed, that is, the one who can step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, but only if they get to see a Realized One, and to hear the teaching and training that he proclaims. ^an3-22-8-1

But also, for the sake of this individual, the other people should be taught Dhamma. ^an3-22-8-2

These are the three individuals similar to patients found in the world.” ^an3-22-8-3

### Choices (Numbered Discourses 3.23) ^an3-23-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-23-1-1

What three? ^an3-23-1-2

Firstly, a certain individual makes hurtful choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-23-1-3

Having made these choices, they’re reborn in a hurtful world, ^an3-23-1-4

where hurtful contacts strike them. ^an3-23-1-5

Touched by hurtful contacts, they experience hurtful feelings that are exclusively painful—like the beings in hell. ^an3-23-1-6

Furthermore, another individual makes pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-23-2-1

Having made these choices, they are reborn in a pleasing world, ^an3-23-2-2

where pleasing contacts strike them. ^an3-23-2-3

Touched by pleasing contacts, they experience pleasing feelings of perfect happiness—like the gods of universal beauty. ^an3-23-2-4

Furthermore, another individual makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-23-3-1

Having made these choices, they are reborn in a world that is both hurtful and pleasing, ^an3-23-3-2

where hurtful and pleasing contacts strike them. ^an3-23-3-3

Touched by both hurtful and pleasing contacts, they experience both hurtful and pleasing feelings that are a mixture of pleasure and pain—like humans, some gods, and some beings in the underworld. ^an3-23-3-4

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-23-3-5

### Very Helpful (Numbered Discourses 3.24) ^an3-24-0

“Mendicants, these three individuals are very helpful to another. ^an3-24-1-1

What three? ^an3-24-1-2

The individual who has enabled you to go for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an3-24-1-3

This individual is very helpful to another. ^an3-24-1-4

Furthermore, the individual who has enabled you to truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-24-2-1

This individual is very helpful to another. ^an3-24-2-2

Furthermore, the individual who has enabled you to realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with your own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-24-3-1

This individual is very helpful to another. ^an3-24-3-2

These are the three individuals who are very helpful to another individual. ^an3-24-3-3

And I say that there is no-one more helpful to another than these three individuals. ^an3-24-4-1

And I also say that it’s not easy to repay these three individuals by bowing down to them, rising up for them, greeting them with cupped palms, and observing proper etiquette for them; or by providing them with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.” ^an3-24-4-2

### Like Diamond (Numbered Discourses 3.25) ^an3-25-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-25-1-1

What three? ^an3-25-1-2

An individual with a mind like an open sore, an individual with a mind like lightning, and an individual with a mind like diamond. ^an3-25-1-3

And who has a mind like an open sore? ^an3-25-1-4

It’s an individual who is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-25-1-5

Even when lightly criticized they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and they display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an3-25-1-6

They’re like a festering sore, which, when you hit it with a stick or a stone, discharges even more. ^an3-25-1-7

In the same way, an individual is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-25-1-8

Even when lightly criticized they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and they display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an3-25-1-9

This is called an individual with a mind like an open sore. ^an3-25-1-10

And who has a mind like lightning? ^an3-25-2-1

It’s an individual who truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-25-2-2

They’re like a person with keen eyes in the dark of the night, who sees by a flash of lightning. ^an3-25-2-3

In the same way, an individual truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-25-2-4

This is called an individual with a mind like lightning. ^an3-25-2-5

And who has a mind like diamond? ^an3-25-3-1

It’s an individual who realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-25-3-2

It’s like a diamond, which can’t be cut by anything at all, not even a gem or a stone. ^an3-25-3-3

In the same way, an individual realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. … ^an3-25-3-4

This is called an individual with a mind like diamond. ^an3-25-3-5

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-25-3-6

### Associates (Numbered Discourses 3.26) ^an3-26-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-26-1-1

What three? ^an3-26-1-2

There is an individual you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend. ^an3-26-1-3

There is an individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend. ^an3-26-1-4

There is an individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend with honor and respect. ^an3-26-1-5

Who is the individual you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend? ^an3-26-1-6

It’s an individual who is inferior in terms of ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-26-1-7

You shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend such an individual, except out of kindness and sympathy. ^an3-26-1-8

Who is the individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend? ^an3-26-2-1

It’s an individual who is similar in terms of ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-26-2-2

You should associate with, accompany, and attend such an individual. ^an3-26-2-3

Why is that? ^an3-26-2-4

Thinking, ‘Since our ethical conduct is similar, we can discuss ethics, the conversation will flow, and we’ll both be at ease. ^an3-26-2-5

Since our immersion is similar, we can discuss immersion, the conversation will flow, and we’ll both be at ease. ^an3-26-2-6

Since our wisdom is similar, we can discuss wisdom, the conversation will flow, and we’ll both be at ease.’ ^an3-26-2-7

That’s why you should associate with, accompany, and attend such an individual. ^an3-26-2-8

Who is the individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend with honor and respect? ^an3-26-3-1

It’s an individual who is superior in terms of ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-26-3-2

You should associate with, accompany, and attend such an individual with honor and respect. ^an3-26-3-3

Why is that? ^an3-26-3-4

Thinking, ‘I’ll fulfill the entire spectrum of ethical conduct I haven’t yet fulfilled, or support with wisdom in every situation the ethical conduct I’ve already fulfilled. ^an3-26-3-5

I’ll fulfill the entire spectrum of immersion I haven’t yet fulfilled, or support with wisdom in every situation the immersion I’ve already fulfilled. ^an3-26-3-6

I’ll fulfill the entire spectrum of wisdom I haven’t yet fulfilled, or support with wisdom in every situation the wisdom I’ve already fulfilled.’ ^an3-26-3-7

That’s why you should associate with, accompany, and attend this individual with honor and respect. ^an3-26-3-8

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-26-3-9

A man who associates with an inferior goes downhill, ^an3-26-4-1

but associating with an equal, you’ll never decline; ^an3-26-4-2

following the best, you’ll quickly rise up, ^an3-26-4-3

so you should keep company <j>with people better than you.” ^an3-26-4-4

### You Should be Disgusted (Numbered Discourses 3.27) ^an3-27-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-27-1-1

What three? ^an3-27-1-2

There is an individual you should be disgusted by, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-1-3

There is an individual you should regard with equanimity, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-1-4

There is an individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend. ^an3-27-1-5

Who is the individual you should be disgusted by, and not associate with, accompany, or attend? ^an3-27-1-6

It’s an individual who is unethical, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved. ^an3-27-1-7

You should be disgusted by such an individual, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-1-8

Why is that? ^an3-27-1-9

Even if you don’t follow the example of such an individual, you still get a bad reputation: ^an3-27-1-10

‘That individual has bad friends, companions, and associates.’ ^an3-27-1-11

They’re like a snake that’s been living in a pile of dung. Even if it doesn’t bite, it’ll still rub off on you. ^an3-27-1-12

In the same way, even if you don’t follow the example of such an individual, you still get a bad reputation: ^an3-27-1-13

‘That individual has bad friends, companions, and associates.’ ^an3-27-1-14

That’s why you should be disgusted by such an individual, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-1-15

Who is the individual you should regard with equanimity, and not associate with, accompany, or attend? ^an3-27-2-1

It’s an individual who is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-27-2-2

Even when lightly criticized they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and they display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an3-27-2-3

They’re like a festering sore, which, when you hit it with a stick or a stone, discharges even more. ^an3-27-2-4

In the same way, someone is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-27-2-5

They’re like a firebrand of pale-moon ebony, which, when you hit it with a stick or a stone, sizzles and crackles even more. ^an3-27-2-6

In the same way, someone is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-27-2-7

They’re like a sewer, which, when you stir it with a stick or a stone, stinks even more. ^an3-27-2-8

In the same way, an individual is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an3-27-2-9

Even when lightly criticized they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and they display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an3-27-2-10

You should regard such an individual with equanimity, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-2-11

Why is that? ^an3-27-2-12

Thinking, ‘They might abuse or insult me, or do me harm.’ ^an3-27-2-13

That’s why you should regard such an individual with equanimity, and you shouldn’t associate with, accompany, or attend them. ^an3-27-2-14

Who is the individual you should associate with, accompany, and attend? ^an3-27-3-1

It’s an individual who is ethical, of good character. ^an3-27-3-2

You should associate with, accompany, and attend such an individual. ^an3-27-3-3

Why is that? ^an3-27-3-4

Even if you don’t follow the example of such an individual, you still get a good reputation: ^an3-27-3-5

‘That individual has good friends, companions, and associates.’ ^an3-27-3-6

That’s why you should associate with, accompany, and attend such an individual. ^an3-27-3-7

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-27-3-8

A man who associates with an inferior goes downhill, ^an3-27-4-1

but associating with an equal, you’ll never decline; ^an3-27-4-2

following the best, you’ll quickly rise up, ^an3-27-4-3

so you should keep company <j>with people better than you.” ^an3-27-4-4

### Speech like Dung (Numbered Discourses 3.28) ^an3-28-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-28-1-1

What three? ^an3-28-1-2

One with speech like dung, one with speech like flowers, and one with speech like honey. ^an3-28-1-3

And who has speech like dung? ^an3-28-1-4

It’s an individual who is summoned to a council, an assembly, a family meeting, a guild, or to the royal court, and asked to bear witness: ‘Please, my man, say what you know.’ Not knowing, they say ‘I know.’ Knowing, they say ‘I don’t know.’ Not seeing, they say ‘I see.’ And seeing, they say ‘I don’t see.’ So they deliberately lie for the sake of themselves or another, or for some trivial worldly reason. ^an3-28-1-5

This is called an individual with speech like dung. ^an3-28-1-6

And who has speech like flowers? ^an3-28-2-1
