It’s an individual who is summoned to a council, an assembly, a family meeting, a guild, or to the royal court, and asked to bear witness: ‘Please, my man, say what you know.’ Not knowing, they say ‘I don’t know.’ Knowing, they say ‘I know.’ Not seeing, they say ‘I don’t see.’ And seeing, they say ‘I see.’ So they don’t deliberately lie for the sake of themselves or another, or for some trivial worldly reason. ^an3-28-2-2

This is called an individual with speech like flowers. ^an3-28-2-3

And who has speech like honey? ^an3-28-3-1

It’s an individual who gives up harsh speech. ^an3-28-3-2

They speak in a way that’s mellow, pleasing to the ear, endearing, going to the heart, polite, likable and agreeable to the people. ^an3-28-3-3

This is called an individual with speech like honey. ^an3-28-3-4

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-28-3-5

### Blind (Numbered Discourses 3.29) ^an3-29-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-29-1-1

What three? ^an3-29-1-2

The blind, the one-eyed, and the two-eyed. ^an3-29-1-3

Who is the blind individual? ^an3-29-1-4

It’s someone who doesn’t have the kind of vision that’s needed to acquire more wealth or to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-29-1-5

Nor do they have the kind of vision that’s needed to know the difference between qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^an3-29-1-6

This is called a blind individual. ^an3-29-1-7

Who is the individual with one eye? ^an3-29-2-1

It’s someone who has the kind of vision that’s needed to acquire more wealth and to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-29-2-2

But they don’t have the kind of vision that’s needed to know the difference between qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^an3-29-2-3

This is called a one-eyed individual. ^an3-29-2-4

Who is the individual with two eyes? ^an3-29-3-1

It’s someone who has the kind of vision that’s needed to acquire more wealth and to increase the wealth they’ve already acquired. ^an3-29-3-2

And they have the kind of vision that’s needed to know the difference between skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, or qualities on the side of dark and bright. ^an3-29-3-3

This is called a two-eyed individual. ^an3-29-3-5

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-29-3-6

Neither suitable wealth, ^an3-29-4-1

nor merit do they make. ^an3-29-4-2

They hold a losing hand on both counts, ^an3-29-4-3

those who are blind, with ruined eyes. ^an3-29-4-4

And now the one-eyed ^an3-29-5-1

individual is explained. ^an3-29-5-2

By methods good and bad, ^an3-29-5-3

that devious person seeks wealth. ^an3-29-5-4

Both by fraudulent, thieving deeds, ^an3-29-6-1

and also by lies, ^an3-29-6-2

the young man’s skilled at piling up money, ^an3-29-6-3

and enjoying sensual pleasures. ^an3-29-6-4

From here they go to hell— ^an3-29-6-5

the one-eyed person is ruined. ^an3-29-6-6

And now the two-eyed is explained, ^an3-29-7-1

the best individual. ^an3-29-7-2

Their wealth is earned legitimately, ^an3-29-7-3

money acquired by their own hard work. ^an3-29-7-4

They give with best of intentions, ^an3-29-8-1

that peaceful-hearted man. ^an3-29-8-2

They go to a good place, ^an3-29-8-3

where there is no sorrow. ^an3-29-8-4

The blind and the one-eyed, ^an3-29-9-1

you should avoid from afar. ^an3-29-9-2

But you should keep the two-eyed close, ^an3-29-9-3

the best individual.” ^an3-29-9-4

### Upside-down (Numbered Discourses 3.30) ^an3-30-0

“These three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-30-1-1

What three? ^an3-30-1-2

One with upside-down wisdom, one with wisdom on their lap, and one with widespread wisdom. ^an3-30-1-3

And who is the individual with upside-down wisdom? ^an3-30-1-4

It’s an individual who often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-1-5

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-1-6

But even while sitting there, that person doesn’t apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-1-7

And when they get up from their seat, they don’t apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-1-8

It’s like when a pot full of water is tipped over, so the water drains out and doesn’t stay. ^an3-30-1-9

In the same way, an individual often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-1-10

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-1-11

But even while sitting there, that person doesn’t apply the mind to the discussion in the beginning, middle, or end. ^an3-30-1-12

And when they get up from their seat, they don’t apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-1-13

This is called an individual with upside-down wisdom. ^an3-30-1-14

And who is the individual with wisdom on their lap? ^an3-30-2-1

It’s an individual who often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-2-2

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-2-3

While sitting there, that person applies the mind to the discussion in the beginning, middle, and end. ^an3-30-2-4

But when they get up from their seat, they don’t apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-2-5

It’s like a person who has different kinds of food crammed on their lap— ^an3-30-2-6

such as sesame, rice, sweets, or jujube— ^an3-30-2-7

so that if they get up from the seat without mindfulness, everything gets scattered. ^an3-30-2-8

In the same way, an individual often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-2-9

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-2-10

While sitting there, that person applies the mind to the discussion in the beginning, middle, and end. ^an3-30-2-11

But when they get up from their seat, they don’t apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-2-12

This is called an individual with wisdom on their lap. ^an3-30-2-13

And who is the individual with widespread wisdom? ^an3-30-3-1

It’s an individual who often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-3-2

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-3-3

While sitting there, they apply the mind to the discussion in the beginning, middle, and end. ^an3-30-3-4

And when they get up from their seat, they continue to apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-3-5

It’s like when a pot full of water is set straight, so the water stays and doesn’t drain out. ^an3-30-3-6

In the same way, an individual often goes to the monastery to hear the teaching in the presence of the mendicants. ^an3-30-3-7

The mendicants teach them Dhamma that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-30-3-8

While sitting there, they apply the mind to the discussion in the beginning, middle, and end. ^an3-30-3-9

And when they get up from their seat, they continue to apply the mind to the beginning, middle, or end of the discussion. ^an3-30-3-10

This is called an individual with widespread wisdom. ^an3-30-3-11

These are the three individuals found in the world. ^an3-30-3-12

A person with upside-down wisdom, ^an3-30-4-1

is a simpleton and cannot see, ^an3-30-4-2

and even if they frequently ^an3-30-4-3

go into the mendicants’ presence, ^an3-30-4-4

such a person can’t learn ^an3-30-5-1

the beginning, middle, or end ^an3-30-5-2

of the discussion, ^an3-30-5-3

for their wisdom is lacking. ^an3-30-5-4

the individual with wisdom on their lap ^an3-30-6-1

is better than that, it’s said; ^an3-30-6-2

but even if they frequently ^an3-30-6-3

go into the mendicants’ presence, ^an3-30-6-4

such a person can only learn ^an3-30-7-1

the beginning, middle, and end ^an3-30-7-2

while sitting in that seat; ^an3-30-7-3

but they’ve only grasped the phrasing, ^an3-30-7-4

for when they get up their understanding fails, ^an3-30-7-5

and what they’ve learned is lost. ^an3-30-7-6

The person with widespread wisdom ^an3-30-8-1

is better than that, it’s said; ^an3-30-8-2

and if they, too, frequently ^an3-30-8-3

go into the mendicants’ presence, ^an3-30-8-4

one such can learn ^an3-30-9-1

the beginning, middle, and end ^an3-30-9-2

while sitting in that seat; ^an3-30-9-3

and when they’ve grasped the phrasing, ^an3-30-9-4

they remember it with the best of intentions. ^an3-30-10-1

That peaceful-hearted man, ^an3-30-10-2

practicing in line with the teaching, ^an3-30-10-3

would make an end of suffering.” ^an3-30-10-4

### With Divinity (Numbered Discourses 3.31) ^an3-31-0

“Mendicants, a family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with divinity. ^an3-31-1-1

A family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with the first tutors. ^an3-31-1-2

A family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods. ^an3-31-1-3

‘Divinity’ is a term for your parents. ^an3-31-1-4

‘First tutors’ is a term for your parents. ^an3-31-1-5

‘Worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods’ is a term for your parents. ^an3-31-1-6

Why is that? ^an3-31-1-7

Parents are very helpful to their children, they raise them, nurture them, and show them the world. ^an3-31-1-8

Parents are said to be ‘divinity’ ^an3-31-2-1

and ‘first tutors’, it’s said. ^an3-31-2-2

They’re worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods <j>from their children, ^an3-31-2-3

for they love their offspring. ^an3-31-2-4

Therefore an astute person ^an3-31-3-1

would revere them and honor them ^an3-31-3-2

with food and drink, ^an3-31-3-3

clothes and bedding, ^an3-31-3-4

anointing and bathing, ^an3-31-3-5

and by washing their feet. ^an3-31-3-6

Because they look after ^an3-31-4-1

their parents like this, ^an3-31-4-2

in this life they’re praised by the astute, ^an3-31-4-3

and they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an3-31-4-4

### With Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 3.32) ^an3-32-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an3-32-1-1

“Could it be, sir, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion such that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body; and no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for externally for all signs; ^an3-32-2-1

and that they’d live having attained the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit are no more?” ^an3-32-2-2

“It could be, Ānanda, that a mendicant gains a state of immersion such that they have no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body; and no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for externally for all signs; ^an3-32-2-3

and that they’d live having attained the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit are no more.” ^an3-32-2-4

“But how could this be, sir?” ^an3-32-3-1

“Ānanda, it’s when a mendicant thinks: ^an3-32-4-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an3-32-4-2

That’s how, Ānanda, a mendicant might gain a state of immersion such that there’s no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body; and no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for externally for all signs; ^an3-32-4-3

and that they’d live having achieved the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit are no more. ^an3-32-4-4

And Ānanda, this is what I was referring to in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Puṇṇaka’ when I said: ^an3-32-5-1

‘Having appraised the world high and low, ^an3-32-6-1

there is nothing in the world that disturbs them. ^an3-32-6-2

Peaceful, unclouded, untroubled, <j>with no need for hope— ^an3-32-6-3

they’ve crossed over rebirth and old age, I declare.’” ^an3-32-6-4

### With Sāriputta (Numbered Discourses 3.33) ^an3-33-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an3-33-1-1

“Maybe I’ll teach Dhamma in brief, ^an3-33-1-2

maybe in detail, ^an3-33-1-3

maybe both in brief and in detail. ^an3-33-1-4

But it’s hard to find anyone who understands.” ^an3-33-1-5

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^an3-33-1-6

Let the Buddha teach Dhamma in brief, in detail, and both in brief and in detail. ^an3-33-1-7

There will be those who understand the teaching!” ^an3-33-1-8

“So, Sāriputta, you should train like this: ^an3-33-2-1

‘There’ll be no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body; and no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit externally for all signs; and we’ll live having achieved the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit are no more.’ ^an3-33-2-2

That’s how you should train. ^an3-33-2-3

When a mendicant has no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit for this conscious body; and no I-making, mine-making, or underlying tendency to conceit externally for all signs; and they live having attained the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit are no more— ^an3-33-3-1

they’re called ^an3-33-3-2

a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering. ^an3-33-3-3

And Sāriputta, this is what I was referring to in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Udaya’ when I said: ^an3-33-3-4

‘The giving up of both ^an3-33-4-1

sensual desires and displeasures, ^an3-33-4-2

the casting aside of dullness, ^an3-33-4-3

and the prevention of remorse; ^an3-33-4-4

pure equanimity and mindfulness, ^an3-33-5-1

preceded by investigation of principles— ^an3-33-5-2

this, I declare, is liberation by enlightenment, ^an3-33-5-3

the smashing of ignorance.’” ^an3-33-5-4

### Sources (Numbered Discourses 3.34) ^an3-34-0

“Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-1-1

What three? ^an3-34-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-1-3

Any deed that emerges from greed—born, sourced, and originated from greed—ripens where that new incarnation is born. ^an3-34-2-1

And wherever that deed ripens, its result is experienced—either in the present life, or in the next life, or in some subsequent period. ^an3-34-2-2

Any deed that emerges from hate—born, sourced, and originated from hate—ripens where that new incarnation is born. ^an3-34-3-1

And wherever that deed ripens, its result is experienced—either in the present life, or in the next life, or in some subsequent period. ^an3-34-3-2

Any deed that emerges from delusion—born, sourced, and originated from delusion—ripens where that new incarnation is born. ^an3-34-4-1

And wherever that deed ripens, its result is experienced—either in the present life, or in the next life, or in some subsequent period. ^an3-34-4-2

Suppose some seeds were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. They’re sown in a well-prepared, productive field, ^an3-34-5-1

and the heavens provide plenty of rain. ^an3-34-5-2

Then those seeds would grow, increase, and mature. ^an3-34-5-3

In the same way, any deed that emerges from greed—born, sourced, and originated from greed—ripens where that new incarnation is born. ^an3-34-5-4

And wherever that deed ripens, its result is experienced—either in the present life, or in the next life, or in some subsequent period. ^an3-34-5-5

Any deed that emerges from hate … ^an3-34-6-1

Any deed that emerges from delusion—born, sourced, and originated from delusion—ripens where that new incarnation is born. ^an3-34-6-2

And wherever that deed ripens, its result is experienced—either in the present life, or in the next life, or in some subsequent period. ^an3-34-6-3

These are three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-6-4

Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-7-1

What three? ^an3-34-7-2

Contentment, love, and understanding are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-7-3

Any deed that emerges from contentment—born, sourced, and originated from contentment—is given up when greed is done away with. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-8-1

Any deed that emerges from love—born, sourced, and originated from love—is abandoned when hate is done away with. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-9-1

Any deed that emerges from understanding—born, sourced, and originated from understanding—is abandoned when delusion is done away with. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-10-1

Suppose some seeds were intact, unspoiled, not damaged by wind and sun, fertile, and well-kept. ^an3-34-11-1

But someone would burn them with fire, ^an3-34-11-2

reduce them to ashes, ^an3-34-11-3

and whisk away the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^an3-34-11-4

Then those seeds would be cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-11-5

In the same way, any deed that emerges from contentment—born, sourced, and originated from contentment—is abandoned when greed is done away with. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-11-6

Any deed that emerges from love … ^an3-34-12-1

Any deed that emerges from understanding—born, sourced, and originated from understanding—is abandoned when delusion is done away with. ^an3-34-12-2

It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-34-12-3

These are three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-34-12-4

When an ignorant person acts ^an3-34-13-1

out of greed, hate, or delusion, ^an3-34-13-2

any deeds they have done, ^an3-34-13-3

a little or a lot, ^an3-34-13-4

are to be experienced right here, ^an3-34-13-5

not in any other place. ^an3-34-13-6

So a wise person, ^an3-34-14-1

a mendicant arousing knowledge ^an3-34-14-2

of the outcome of greed, hate, and delusion, ^an3-34-14-3

would abandon all bad destinies.” ^an3-34-14-4

### With Hatthaka (Numbered Discourses 3.35) ^an3-35-0

So I have heard. ^an3-35-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Āḷavī, on a mat of leaves by a cow-path in a rosewood grove. ^an3-35-1-2

Then as Hatthaka of Āḷavī was going for a walk he saw the Buddha sitting on that mat of leaves. ^an3-35-1-3

He went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said, ^an3-35-1-4

“Sir, I trust the Buddha slept well?” ^an3-35-1-5

“Yes, prince, I slept well. ^an3-35-1-6

I am one of those who sleep at ease in the world.” ^an3-35-1-7

“The winter nights are cold, sir, and it’s the week of mid-winter, when the snow falls. Rough is the ground trampled under the cows’ hooves, and thin is the mat of leaves. The leaves are sparse on the trees, the ocher robes are cold, and cold blows the north wind. ^an3-35-2-1

And yet the Buddha says, ^an3-35-2-2

‘Yes, prince, I slept well. ^an3-35-2-3

I am one of those who sleep at ease in the world.’” ^an3-35-2-4

“Well then, prince, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an3-35-3-1

What do you think? Take the case of a householder or his son, who lives in a bungalow, plastered inside and out, draft-free, with door fastened and window shuttered. ^an3-35-3-2

His couch is spread with woolen covers—shag-piled, pure white, or embroidered with flowers—and spread with a fine deer hide. It has a canopy above and red pillows at both ends. ^an3-35-3-3

An oil lamp is burning there, ^an3-35-3-4

while his four wives attend to him in all manner of agreeable ways. ^an3-35-3-5

What do you think, prince, would he sleep at ease, or not? ^an3-35-3-6

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-35-3-7

“He would sleep at ease, sir. ^an3-35-3-8

Of those who sleep at ease in the world, he would be one.” ^an3-35-3-9

“What do you think, prince? ^an3-35-4-1

Is it not possible that a fever born of greed—physical or mental—might arise in that householder or householder’s son, burning him so he sleeps badly?” ^an3-35-4-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-35-4-3

“The greed that burns that householder or householder’s son, making them sleep badly, has been cut off at the root by the Realized One, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-35-5-1

That’s why I sleep at ease. ^an3-35-5-2

What do you think, prince? ^an3-35-6-1

Is it not possible that a fever born of hate … ^an3-35-6-2

or a fever born of delusion—physical or mental—might arise in that householder or householder’s son, burning him so he sleeps badly?” ^an3-35-6-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-35-6-4

“The delusion that burns that householder or householder’s son, making them sleep badly, has been cut off at the root by the Realized One, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-35-7-1

That’s why I sleep at ease. ^an3-35-7-2

A brahmin who is fully quenched ^an3-35-8-1

always sleeps at ease. ^an3-35-8-2

Sensual pleasures slip off them, ^an3-35-8-3

they’re cooled, free of attachments. ^an3-35-8-4

Since they’ve cut off all clinging, ^an3-35-9-1

and removed the stress from the heart, ^an3-35-9-2

the peaceful sleep at ease, ^an3-35-9-3

having found peace of mind.” ^an3-35-9-4

### Messengers of the Gods (Numbered Discourses 3.36) ^an3-36-0

“There are, mendicants, these three messengers of the gods. ^an3-36-1-1

What three? ^an3-36-1-2

Firstly, someone does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-36-1-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an3-36-1-4

The wardens of hell take them by the arms and present them to King Yama, saying: ^an3-36-1-5

‘Your Majesty, this person did not pay due respect to their mother and father, ascetics and brahmins, or honor the elders in the family. ^an3-36-1-6

May Your Majesty punish them!’ ^an3-36-1-7

Then King Yama pursues, presses, and grills them about the first messenger of the gods: ^an3-36-2-1

‘My man, did you not see the first messenger of the gods that appeared among human beings?’ ^an3-36-2-2

They say, ^an3-36-2-3

‘I saw nothing, sir.’ ^an3-36-2-4

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-3-1

‘My man, did you not see among human beings an elderly woman or a man—eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old—bent double, crooked, leaning on a staff, trembling as they walk, ailing, past their prime, with teeth broken, hair grey and scanty or bald, skin wrinkled, and limbs blotchy?’ ^an3-36-3-2

They say, ^an3-36-3-3

‘I saw that, sir.’ ^an3-36-3-4

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-4-1

‘My man, did it not occur to you—being sensible and mature— ^an3-36-4-2

“I, too, am liable to grow old. I’m not exempt from old age. I’d better do good by way of body, speech, and mind”?’ ^an3-36-4-3

They say, ^an3-36-4-4

‘I couldn’t, sir. I was negligent.’ ^an3-36-4-5

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-5-1

‘My man, because you were negligent, you didn’t do good by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-36-5-2

Indeed, they’ll definitely punish you to fit your negligence. ^an3-36-5-3

That bad deed wasn’t done by your mother, father, brother, or sister. It wasn’t done by friends and colleagues, by relatives and kin, by the deities, or by ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-36-5-4

That bad deed was done by you alone, and you alone will experience the result.’ ^an3-36-5-5

Then King Yama grills them about the second messenger of the gods: ^an3-36-6-1

‘My man, did you not see the second messenger of the gods that appeared among human beings?’ ^an3-36-6-2

They say, ^an3-36-6-3

‘I saw nothing, sir.’ ^an3-36-6-4

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-6-5

‘My man, did you not see among human beings a woman or a man, sick, suffering, gravely ill, collapsed in their own urine and feces, being picked up by some and put down by others?’ ^an3-36-6-6

They say, ^an3-36-6-7

‘I saw that, sir.’ ^an3-36-6-8

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-7-1

‘My man, did it not occur to you—being sensible and mature— ^an3-36-7-2

“I, too, am liable to become sick. I’m not exempt from sickness. I’d better do good by way of body, speech, and mind”?’ ^an3-36-7-3

They say, ^an3-36-7-4

‘I couldn’t, sir. I was negligent.’ ^an3-36-7-5

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-8-1

‘My man, because you were negligent, you didn’t do good by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-36-8-2

Well, they’ll definitely punish you to fit your negligence. ^an3-36-8-3

That bad deed wasn’t done by your mother, father, brother, or sister. It wasn’t done by friends and colleagues, by relatives and kin, by the deities, or by ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-36-8-4

That bad deed was done by you alone, and you alone will experience the result.’ ^an3-36-8-5

Then King Yama grills them about the third messenger of the gods: ^an3-36-9-1

‘My man, did you not see the third messenger of the gods that appeared among human beings?’ ^an3-36-9-2

They say, ^an3-36-9-3

‘I saw nothing, sir.’ ^an3-36-9-4

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-10-1

‘My man, did you not see among human beings a woman or a man, dead for one, two, or three days, bloated, livid, and festering?’ ^an3-36-10-2

They say, ^an3-36-10-3

‘I saw that, sir.’ ^an3-36-10-4

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-11-1

‘My man, did it not occur to you—being sensible and mature— ^an3-36-11-2

“I, too, am liable to die. I’m not exempt from death. I’d better do good by way of body, speech, and mind”?’ ^an3-36-11-3

They say, ^an3-36-11-4

‘I couldn’t, sir. I was negligent.’ ^an3-36-11-5

King Yama says to them, ^an3-36-12-1

‘My man, because you were negligent, you didn’t do good by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-36-12-2

Well, they’ll definitely punish you to fit your negligence. ^an3-36-12-3

That bad deed wasn’t done by your mother, father, brother, or sister. It wasn’t done by friends and colleagues, by relatives and kin, by the deities, or by ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-36-12-4

That bad deed was done by you alone, and you alone will experience the result.’ ^an3-36-12-5

After grilling them about the third messenger of the gods, King Yama falls silent. ^an3-36-13-1

The wardens of hell punish them with the five-fold crucifixion. ^an3-36-13-2

They drive red-hot stakes through the hands and feet, and another in the middle of the chest. ^an3-36-13-3

And there they suffer painful, sharp, severe, acute feelings—but they don’t die until that bad deed is eliminated. ^an3-36-13-4

The wardens of hell throw them down and hack them with axes. … ^an3-36-14-1

They hang them upside-down and hack them with hatchets. … ^an3-36-15-1

They harness them to a chariot, and drive them back and forth across burning ground, blazing and glowing. … ^an3-36-15-2

They make them climb up and down a huge mountain of burning coals, blazing and glowing. … ^an3-36-15-3

The wardens of hell turn them upside down and throw them in a red-hot copper pot, burning, blazing, and glowing. ^an3-36-15-4

There they’re seared in boiling scum, and they’re swept up and down and round and round. ^an3-36-15-5

And there they suffer painful, sharp, severe, acute feelings—but they don’t die until that bad deed is eliminated. ^an3-36-15-6

The wardens of hell toss them into the Great Hell. ^an3-36-15-7

Now, about that Great Hell: ^an3-36-15-8

‘Four are its corners, four its doors, ^an3-36-16-1

neatly divided section by section. ^an3-36-16-2

Surrounded by an iron wall, ^an3-36-16-3

of iron is its roof. ^an3-36-16-4

The ground is even made of iron, ^an3-36-17-1

it burns with fierce fire. ^an3-36-17-2

The heat forever radiates ^an3-36-17-3

a hundred leagues around.’ ^an3-36-17-4

Once upon a time, King Yama thought, ^an3-36-18-1

‘Those who do such bad deeds in the world receive these many different punishments. ^an3-36-18-2

Oh, I hope I may be reborn as a human being! And that a Realized One—a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha—arises in the world! And that I may pay homage to the Buddha! ^an3-36-18-3

Then the Buddha can teach me Dhamma, so that I may understand his teaching.’ ^an3-36-18-4

Now, I don’t say this because I’ve heard it from some other ascetic or brahmin. I only say it because I’ve known, seen, and realized it for myself. ^an3-36-18-5

When warned by the gods’ messengers, ^an3-36-19-1

those sons of Manu who are negligent ^an3-36-19-2

sorrow for a long time ^an3-36-19-3

when they go to that wretched place. ^an3-36-19-4

When warned by the gods’ messengers, ^an3-36-20-1

the good and true persons here, ^an3-36-20-2

never neglect ^an3-36-20-3

the teaching of the Noble One. ^an3-36-20-4

Seeing the peril in grasping, ^an3-36-21-1

the origin of birth and death, ^an3-36-21-2

the unattached are freed ^an3-36-21-3

with the ending of birth and death. ^an3-36-21-4

Happy, they’ve come to a safe place, ^an3-36-22-1

quenched in this very life. ^an3-36-22-2

They’ve gone beyond all threats and perils, ^an3-36-22-3

and risen above all suffering.” ^an3-36-22-4

### The Four Great Kings (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.37) ^an3-37-0

“On the eighth day of the fortnight, mendicants, the ministers and councillors of the four great kings wander about the world, thinking: ^an3-37-1-1

‘Hopefully most humans are paying due respect to their parents, ascetics and brahmins, honoring the elders in their families, observing and keeping vigil on the sabbath, and making merit.’ ^an3-37-1-2

And on the fourteenth day of the fortnight, the sons of the four great kings wander about the world, thinking: ^an3-37-1-3

‘Hopefully most humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit.’ ^an3-37-1-4

And on the fifteenth day sabbath, the four great kings themselves wander about the world, thinking: ^an3-37-1-5

‘Hopefully most humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit.’ ^an3-37-1-6

If only a few humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit, ^an3-37-2-1

the four great kings address the gods of the thirty-three, seated together in the Hall of Clear Right: ^an3-37-2-2

‘Only a few humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit.’ ^an3-37-2-3

Then the gods of the thirty-three are disappointed, thinking, ^an3-37-2-4

‘The heavenly hosts will dwindle, while the titan hosts will swell!’ ^an3-37-2-5

But if many humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit, ^an3-37-3-1

the four great kings address the gods of the thirty-three, seated together in the Hall of Clear Right: ^an3-37-3-2

‘Many humans are paying due respect to their parents … and making merit.’ ^an3-37-3-3

Then the gods of the thirty-three are pleased, thinking, ^an3-37-3-4

‘The heavenly hosts will swell, while the titan hosts will dwindle!’ ^an3-37-3-5

Once upon a time, Sakka, lord of gods, guiding the gods of the thirty-three, spoke this verse: ^an3-37-4-1

‘Whoever wants to be like me ^an3-37-5-1

would observe the sabbath ^an3-37-5-2

complete in all eight factors, ^an3-37-5-3

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^an3-37-5-4

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^an3-37-5-5

as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’ ^an3-37-5-6

But that verse was poorly sung by Sakka, lord of gods, not well sung; poorly spoken, not well spoken. ^an3-37-6-1

Why is that? ^an3-37-6-2

Sakka, lord of gods, is not free of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-37-6-3

But for a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—it is appropriate to say: ^an3-37-7-1

‘Whoever wants to be like me ^an3-37-8-1

would observe the sabbath, ^an3-37-8-2

complete in all eight factors, ^an3-37-8-3

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^an3-37-8-4

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^an3-37-8-5

as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’ ^an3-37-8-6

Why is that? ^an3-37-9-1

Because that mendicant is free of greed, hate, and delusion.” ^an3-37-9-2

### The Four Great Kings (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.38) ^an3-38-0

“Once upon a time, mendicants, Sakka, lord of gods, guiding the gods of the thirty-three, spoke this verse: ^an3-38-1-1

‘Whoever wants to be like me ^an3-38-2-1

would observe the sabbath ^an3-38-2-2

complete in all eight factors, ^an3-38-2-3

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^an3-38-2-4

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^an3-38-2-5

as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’ ^an3-38-2-6

But that verse was poorly sung by Sakka, lord of gods, not well sung; poorly spoken, not well spoken. ^an3-38-3-1

Why is that? ^an3-38-3-2

Because Sakka, lord of gods, is not exempt from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. He is not exempt from suffering, I say. ^an3-38-3-3

But for a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—it is appropriate to say: ^an3-38-4-1

‘Whoever wants to be like me ^an3-38-5-1

would observe the sabbath, ^an3-38-5-2

complete in all eight factors, ^an3-38-5-3

on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days, ^an3-38-5-4

and the eighth day of the fortnight, ^an3-38-5-5

as well as on the fortnightly special displays.’ ^an3-38-5-6

Why is that? ^an3-38-6-1

Because that mendicant is exempt from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. He is exempt from suffering, I say.” ^an3-38-6-2

### A Delicate Lifestyle (Numbered Discourses 3.39) ^an3-39-0

“My lifestyle was delicate, mendicants, most delicate, extremely delicate. ^an3-39-1-1

In my father’s home, lotus ponds were made just for me. ^an3-39-1-2

In some, blue water lilies blossomed, while in others, there were pink or white lotuses, just for my benefit. ^an3-39-1-3

I only used sandalwood from Kāsi, and my turbans, jackets, sarongs, and upper robes also came from Kāsi. ^an3-39-1-4

And a white parasol was held over me night and day, with the thought: ^an3-39-1-5

‘Don’t let cold, heat, grass, dust, or damp bother him.’ ^an3-39-1-6

I had three stilt longhouses—one for the winter, one for the summer, and one for the rainy season. ^an3-39-2-1

I stayed in a stilt longhouse without coming downstairs for the four months of the rainy season, where I was entertained by musicians—none of them men. ^an3-39-2-2

While the bondservants, workers, and staff in other houses are given rough gruel with false black pepper to eat, in my father’s home they are given fine rice with meat. ^an3-39-2-3

Amid such prosperity and such a delicate lifestyle, I thought: ^an3-39-3-1

‘When an unlearned ordinary person—who is liable to grow old, not being exempt from old age—sees someone else who is old, they’re horrified, repelled, and disgusted, overlooking the fact that they themselves are in the same situation. ^an3-39-3-2

But since I, too, am liable to grow old, it would not be appropriate for me to be horrified, embarrassed, and disgusted, when I see someone else who is old.’ ^an3-39-3-3

Reflecting like this, I entirely gave up the vanity of youth. ^an3-39-3-4

‘When an unlearned ordinary person—who is liable to get sick, not being exempt from sickness—sees someone else who is sick, they’re horrified, repelled, and disgusted, overlooking the fact that they themselves are in the same situation. ^an3-39-4-1

But since I, too, am liable to get sick, it would not be appropriate for me to be horrified, embarrassed, and disgusted, when I see someone else who is sick.’ ^an3-39-4-2

Reflecting like this, I entirely gave up the vanity of health. ^an3-39-4-3

‘When an unlearned ordinary person—who is liable to die, not being exempt from death—sees someone else who is dead, they’re horrified, repelled, and disgusted, overlooking the fact that they themselves are in the same situation. ^an3-39-5-1

But since I, too, am liable to die, it would not be appropriate for me to be horrified, embarrassed, and disgusted, when I see someone else who is dead.’ ^an3-39-5-2

Reflecting like this, I entirely gave up the vanity of life. ^an3-39-5-3

There are these three vanities. ^an3-39-6-1

What three? ^an3-39-6-2

The vanity of youth, of health, and of life. ^an3-39-6-3

Intoxicated with the vanity of youth, an unlearned ordinary person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-39-6-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an3-39-6-5

Intoxicated with the vanity of health … ^an3-39-6-6

Intoxicated with the vanity of life, an unlearned ordinary person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-39-6-7

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an3-39-6-8

Intoxicated with the vanity of youth, health, or life, a mendicant disavows the training and returns to a lesser life. ^an3-39-7-1

For others, sickness is natural, ^an3-39-8-1

and so are old age and death. ^an3-39-8-2

Though this is how their nature is, ^an3-39-8-3

ordinary people feel disgusted. ^an3-39-8-4

If I were to be disgusted ^an3-39-9-1

with creatures whose nature is such, ^an3-39-9-2

it would not be appropriate for me, ^an3-39-9-3

since my life is just the same. ^an3-39-9-4

Living in such a way, ^an3-39-10-1

I understood the truth without attachments. ^an3-39-10-2

I mastered all vanities— ^an3-39-10-3

of health, of youth, ^an3-39-10-4

and even of life— ^an3-39-11-1

seeing renunciation as sanctuary. ^an3-39-11-2

Zeal sprang up in me ^an3-39-11-3

as I looked to extinguishment. ^an3-39-11-4

Now I’m unable ^an3-39-12-1

to indulge in sensual pleasures; ^an3-39-12-2

there’s no turning back, ^an3-39-12-3

I’m committed to the spiritual life.” ^an3-39-12-4

### In Charge (Numbered Discourses 3.40) ^an3-40-0

“There are, mendicants, these three things to put in charge. ^an3-40-1-1

What three? ^an3-40-1-2

Putting oneself, the world, or the teaching in charge. ^an3-40-1-3

And what, mendicants, is putting oneself in charge? ^an3-40-1-4

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an3-40-1-5

‘I didn’t go forth from the lay life to homelessness for the sake of a robe, ^an3-40-1-6

almsfood, lodgings, or rebirth in this or that state. ^an3-40-1-7

But I was swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I was swamped by suffering, mired in suffering. ^an3-40-1-8

And I thought, “Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.” ^an3-40-1-9

But it would not be appropriate for me to seek sensual pleasures like those I abandoned when I went forth, or even worse.’ ^an3-40-1-10

Then they reflect: ^an3-40-1-11

‘My energy shall be roused up and unflagging, mindfulness shall be established and lucid, my body shall be tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind shall be immersed in samādhi.’ ^an3-40-1-12

Putting themselves in charge, they give up the unskillful and develop the skillful, they give up the blameworthy and develop the blameless, and they keep themselves pure. ^an3-40-1-13

This is called putting oneself in charge. ^an3-40-1-14

And what, mendicants, is putting the world in charge? ^an3-40-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an3-40-2-2

‘I didn’t go forth from the lay life to homelessness for the sake of a robe, ^an3-40-2-3

almsfood, lodgings, or rebirth in this or that state. ^an3-40-2-4

But I was swamped by rebirth, old age, and death, by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I was swamped by suffering, mired in suffering. ^an3-40-2-5

And I thought, “Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.” ^an3-40-2-6

And now, since I’ve now gone forth, I might have sensual, malicious, or cruel thoughts. But the population of the world is large, ^an3-40-2-7

and there are ascetics and brahmins who have psychic power—they’re clairvoyant, and can read the minds of others. ^an3-40-2-8

They see far without being seen, even by those close; and they understand the minds of others. ^an3-40-2-9

They would know me: ^an3-40-2-10

“Look at this gentleman; he’s gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, but he’s living mixed up with bad, unskillful qualities.” ^an3-40-2-11

And there are deities, too, who have psychic power—they’re clairvoyant, and can read the minds of others. ^an3-40-2-12

They see far without being seen, even by those close; and they understand the minds of others. ^an3-40-2-13

They would know me: ^an3-40-2-14

“Look at this gentleman; he’s gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, but he’s living mixed up with bad, unskillful qualities.”’ ^an3-40-2-15

Then they reflect: ^an3-40-2-16

‘My energy shall be roused up and unflagging, mindfulness shall be established and lucid, my body shall be tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind shall be immersed in samādhi.’ ^an3-40-2-17

Putting the world in charge, they give up the unskillful and develop the skillful, they give up the blameworthy and develop the blameless, and they keep themselves pure. ^an3-40-2-18

This is called putting the world in charge. ^an3-40-2-19

And what, mendicants, is putting the teaching in charge? ^an3-40-3-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an3-40-3-2

‘I didn’t go forth from the lay life to homelessness for the sake of a robe, ^an3-40-3-3

almsfood, lodgings, or rebirth in this or that state. ^an3-40-3-4

But I was swamped by rebirth, old age, and death, by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I was swamped by suffering, mired in suffering. ^an3-40-3-5

And I thought, “Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.” ^an3-40-3-6

The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-40-3-7

I have spiritual companions who live knowing and seeing. ^an3-40-3-8

Now that I’ve gone forth in this well explained teaching and training, it would not be appropriate for me to live lazy and heedless.’ ^an3-40-3-9

Then they reflect: ^an3-40-3-10

‘My energy shall be roused up and unflagging, mindfulness shall be established and lucid, my body shall be tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind shall be immersed in samādhi.’ ^an3-40-3-11

Putting the teaching in charge, they give up the unskillful and develop the skillful, they give up the blameworthy and develop the blameless, and they keep themselves pure. ^an3-40-3-12

This is called putting the teaching in charge. ^an3-40-3-13

These are the three things to put in charge. ^an3-40-3-14

There’s no privacy in the world, ^an3-40-4-1

for someone who does bad deeds. ^an3-40-4-2

You’ll know for yourself, ^an3-40-4-3

whether you’ve lied or told the truth. ^an3-40-4-4

When you witness your good self, ^an3-40-5-1

you despise it; ^an3-40-5-2

while you disguise ^an3-40-5-3

your bad self inside yourself. ^an3-40-5-4

The gods and the Realized One see ^an3-40-6-1

the fool who lives unjustly in the world. ^an3-40-6-2

So with yourself in charge, live mindfully; ^an3-40-6-3

with the world in charge, <j>be alert and practice absorption; ^an3-40-6-4

with the teaching in charge, <j>live in line with that teaching: ^an3-40-6-5

a sage who tries for the truth doesn’t deteriorate. ^an3-40-6-6

Māra’s conquered; the terminator’s overcome: ^an3-40-7-1

one who strives reaches the end of rebirth. ^an3-40-7-2

Unaffected, intelligent, knowing the world— ^an3-40-7-3

that sage is determined by nothing at all.” ^an3-40-7-4

### Present (Numbered Discourses 3.41) ^an3-41-0

“Mendicants, when three things are present, a faithful gentleman brims with much merit. ^an3-41-1-1

What three? ^an3-41-1-2

When faith is present, ^an3-41-1-3

when a gift to give is present, ^an3-41-1-4

and when those worthy of a religious donation are present. ^an3-41-1-5

When these three things are present, a faithful gentleman brims with much merit.” ^an3-41-1-6

### Three Grounds (Numbered Discourses 3.42) ^an3-42-0

“There are three grounds, mendicants, by which a person with faith and confidence can be known. ^an3-42-1-1

What three? ^an3-42-1-2

They like to see ethical people. They like to hear the true teaching. And they live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an3-42-1-3

These are the three grounds by which a person with faith and confidence can be known. ^an3-42-1-4

They like to see ethical people; ^an3-42-2-1

they want to hear the true teaching; ^an3-42-2-2

they’ve driven out the stain of stinginess: ^an3-42-2-3

that’s who’s called an individual of faith.” ^an3-42-2-4

### Good Reasons (Numbered Discourses 3.43) ^an3-43-0

“Mendicants, taking three reasons into consideration provides quite enough motivation to teach Dhamma to another. ^an3-43-1-1

What three? ^an3-43-1-2

When the teacher understands the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-43-1-3

When the audience understands the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-43-1-4

When both the teacher and the audience understand the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-43-1-5

Taking these three reasons into consideration provides quite enough motivation to teach Dhamma to another.” ^an3-43-1-6

### When Conversation Flows (Numbered Discourses 3.44) ^an3-44-0

“In three situations, mendicants, conversation flows. ^an3-44-1-1

What three? ^an3-44-1-2

When the teacher understands the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-44-1-3

When the audience understands the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-44-1-4

When both the teacher and the audience understand the meaning and the teaching. ^an3-44-1-5

These are the three situations in which conversation flows.” ^an3-44-1-6

### Recommended by the Astute (Numbered Discourses 3.45) ^an3-45-0

“Mendicants, these three things are recommended by astute true persons. ^an3-45-1-1

What three? ^an3-45-1-2

Giving, ^an3-45-1-3

going forth, ^an3-45-1-4

and taking care of your mother and father. ^an3-45-1-5

These are the three things recommended by astute true persons. ^an3-45-1-6

The virtuous recommend giving, ^an3-45-2-1

harmlessness, restraint, and self-control; ^an3-45-2-2

caring for mother and father, ^an3-45-2-3

and peaceful spiritual practitioners. ^an3-45-2-4

These are the things recommended by the good, ^an3-45-3-1

which the astute should cultivate. ^an3-45-3-2

A noble one, having vision, ^an3-45-3-3

will enjoy a world of grace.” ^an3-45-3-4

### Ethical (Numbered Discourses 3.46) ^an3-46-0

“Mendicants, when ethical renunciates are supported by a town or village, ^an3-46-1-1

the people there brim with much merit in three ways. ^an3-46-1-2

What three? ^an3-46-1-3

By way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-46-1-4

When ethical renunciates are supported by a town or village, ^an3-46-1-5

the people there brim with much merit in these three ways.” ^an3-46-1-6

### Characteristics of the Conditioned (Numbered Discourses 3.47) ^an3-47-0

“Mendicants, the conditioned has these three characteristics. ^an3-47-1-1

What three? ^an3-47-1-2

Arising is evident, vanishing is evident, and change while persisting is evident. ^an3-47-1-3

These are the three characteristics of the conditioned.” ^an3-47-1-4

Characteristics of the Unconditioned ^an3-47-2-0

“The unconditioned has these three characteristics. ^an3-47-2-1

What three? ^an3-47-2-2

No arising is evident, no vanishing is evident, and no change while persisting is evident. ^an3-47-2-3

These are the three characteristics of the unconditioned.” ^an3-47-2-4

### The King of Mountains (Numbered Discourses 3.48) ^an3-48-0

“Mendicants, great sal trees grow in three ways supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^an3-48-1-1

What three? ^an3-48-1-2

The branches, leaves, and foliage; the bark and shoots; and the softwood and heartwood. ^an3-48-1-3

Great sal trees grow in these three ways supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^an3-48-1-4

In the same way, a family grows in three ways supported by a family head with faith. ^an3-48-2-1

What three? ^an3-48-2-2

Faith, ethics, and wisdom. ^an3-48-2-3

A family grows in these three ways supported by a family head with faith. ^an3-48-2-4

Supported by the rocky mountain ^an3-48-3-1

in the wilds, the formidable forest, ^an3-48-3-2

the tree grows ^an3-48-3-3

to become lord of the forest. ^an3-48-3-4

So too, when the family head ^an3-48-4-1

is ethical and faithful, ^an3-48-4-2

supported by them, they grow: ^an3-48-4-3

children, partners, and kin, ^an3-48-4-4

colleagues, relatives, ^an3-48-4-5

and those dependent for their livelihood. ^an3-48-4-6

Seeing the ethical conduct of the virtuous, ^an3-48-5-1

the generosity and good deeds, ^an3-48-5-2

those who see clearly ^an3-48-5-3

do likewise. ^an3-48-5-4

Having practiced the teaching here, ^an3-48-6-1

the path that goes to a good place, ^an3-48-6-2

they delight in the heavenly realm, ^an3-48-6-3

enjoying all the pleasures they desire.” ^an3-48-6-4

### Keen (Numbered Discourses 3.49) ^an3-49-0

“In three situations, mendicants, you should be keen. ^an3-49-1-1

What three? ^an3-49-1-2

You should be keen to prevent bad, unskillful qualities from arising. You should be keen to give rise to skillful qualities. And you should be keen to endure physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, life-threatening. ^an3-49-1-3

In these three situations, you should be keen. ^an3-49-1-4

It’s a mendicant who is keen to prevent bad, unskillful qualities from arising. They’re keen to give rise to skillful qualities. And they’re keen to endure physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, life-threatening. ^an3-49-2-1

This is called a mendicant who is keen, alert, and mindful so as to rightly make an end of suffering.” ^an3-49-2-2

### A Master Thief (Numbered Discourses 3.50) ^an3-50-0

“Mendicants, a master thief with three factors breaks into houses, plunders wealth, steals from isolated buildings, and commits highway robbery. ^an3-50-1-1

What three? ^an3-50-1-2

A master thief relies on uneven ground, on thick cover, and on the powerful. ^an3-50-1-3

And how does a master thief rely on uneven ground? ^an3-50-1-4

It’s when a master thief relies on inaccessible riverlands or rugged mountains. ^an3-50-1-5

That’s how a master thief relies on uneven ground. ^an3-50-1-6

And how does a master thief rely on thick cover? ^an3-50-2-1

It’s when a master thief relies on thick grass, thick trees, a blind spot, or a large forest grove. ^an3-50-2-2

That’s how a master thief relies on thick cover. ^an3-50-2-3

And how does a master thief rely on the powerful? ^an3-50-3-1

It’s when a master thief relies on rulers or their chief ministers. ^an3-50-3-2

They think: ^an3-50-3-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, these rulers or their chief ministers will speak in my defense in the case.’ ^an3-50-3-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an3-50-3-5

That’s how a master thief relies on the powerful. ^an3-50-3-6

A master thief with these three factors breaks into houses, plunders wealth, steals from isolated buildings, and commits highway robbery. ^an3-50-3-7

In the same way, when a bad mendicant has three factors, they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an3-50-4-1

What three? ^an3-50-4-2

A bad mendicant relies on uneven ground, on thick cover, and on the powerful. ^an3-50-4-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on uneven ground? ^an3-50-5-1

It’s when a bad mendicant has unethical conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-50-5-2

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on uneven ground. ^an3-50-5-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on thick cover? ^an3-50-6-1

It’s when a bad mendicant has wrong view, he’s attached to an extremist view. ^an3-50-6-2

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on thick cover. ^an3-50-6-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on the powerful? ^an3-50-7-1

It’s when a bad mendicant relies on rulers or their chief ministers. ^an3-50-7-2

They think: ^an3-50-7-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, these rulers or their chief ministers will speak in my defense in the case.’ ^an3-50-7-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an3-50-7-5

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on the powerful. ^an3-50-7-6

When a bad mendicant possesses these three qualities, they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness.” ^an3-50-7-7

### Two Brahmins (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.51) ^an3-51-0

Then two old brahmins—elderly and senior, who were advanced in years and had reached the final stage of life, a hundred and twenty years old—went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-51-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an3-51-1-2

“We brahmins, worthy Gotama, are old, elderly and senior, we’re advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; we’re a hundred and twenty years old. ^an3-51-1-3

And we haven’t done what is good and skillful, nor have we made a shelter from fear. ^an3-51-1-4

Advise us, worthy Gotama, instruct us! It will be for our lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an3-51-1-5

“Indeed, brahmins, you’re old, elderly and senior. ^an3-51-2-1

And you haven’t done what is good and skillful, nor have you made a shelter from fear. ^an3-51-2-2

This world is led onward by old age, sickness, and death. ^an3-51-2-3

But restraint here by way of body, speech, and mind is the shelter, asylum, island, refuge, and final haven for the departed. ^an3-51-2-4

This life, so very short, is led onward. ^an3-51-3-1

One led on to old age has no shelter. ^an3-51-3-2

Seeing this peril in death, ^an3-51-3-3

do good deeds that bring you joy. ^an3-51-3-4

The restraint practiced here— ^an3-51-4-1

of body, speech, and mind— ^an3-51-4-2

leads the departed to happiness, ^an3-51-4-3

as the good deeds done while living.” ^an3-51-4-4

### Two Brahmins (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.52) ^an3-52-0

Then two old brahmins—elderly and senior, who were advanced in years and had reached the final stage of life, being a hundred and twenty years old—went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an3-52-1-1

“We brahmins, worthy Gotama, are old, elderly and senior, we’re advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; we’re a hundred and twenty years old. ^an3-52-1-2

And we haven’t done what is good and skillful, nor have we made a shelter from fear. ^an3-52-1-3

Advise us, worthy Gotama, instruct us! It will be for our lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an3-52-1-4

“Indeed, brahmins, you’re old, elderly and senior. ^an3-52-2-1

And you haven’t done what is good and skillful, nor have you made a shelter from fear. ^an3-52-2-2

This world is burning with old age, sickness, and death. ^an3-52-2-3

But restraint here by way of body, speech, and mind is the shelter, asylum, island, refuge, and final haven for the departed. ^an3-52-2-4

When your house is on fire, ^an3-52-3-1

you rescue the pot ^an3-52-3-2

that’s useful, ^an3-52-3-3

not the one that’s burnt. ^an3-52-3-4

And as the world is on fire ^an3-52-4-1

with old age and death, ^an3-52-4-2

you should rescue by giving, ^an3-52-4-3

for what’s given is rescued. ^an3-52-4-4

The restraint practiced here— ^an3-52-5-1

of body, speech, and mind— ^an3-52-5-2

leads the departed to happiness, ^an3-52-5-3

as the good deeds done while living.” ^an3-52-5-4

### A Certain Brahmin (Numbered Discourses 3.53) ^an3-53-0

Then a brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. Seated to one side he said to the Buddha: ^an3-53-1-1

“Worthy Gotama, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^an3-53-1-2

In what way is the teaching apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^an3-53-1-3

“A greedy person, overcome and overwhelmed by greed, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-2-1

When greed has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-2-2

This is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-53-2-7

A hateful person, overcome by hate, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-3-1

When hate has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-3-2

This, too, is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-53-3-3

A deluded person, overcome by delusion, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-4-1

When delusion has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-53-4-2

This, too, is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^an3-53-4-3

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^an3-53-5-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an3-53-5-2

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an3-53-5-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-53-5-4

### A Wanderer (Numbered Discourses 3.54) ^an3-54-0

Then a brahmin wanderer went up to the Buddha … Seated to one side he said to the Buddha: ^an3-54-1-1

“Worthy Gotama, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^an3-54-1-2

In what way is the teaching apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^an3-54-1-3

“A greedy person, overcome by greed, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-54-2-1

When greed has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-54-2-2

A greedy person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-54-3-1

When greed has been given up, they don’t do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-54-3-2

A greedy person doesn’t truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-54-4-1

When greed has been given up, they truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-54-4-2

This is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-54-4-3

A hateful person … ^an3-54-5-1

A deluded person, overcome by delusion, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-54-5-2

When delusion has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-54-5-3

A deluded person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-54-6-1

When delusion has been given up, they don’t do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-54-6-2

A deluded person doesn’t truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-54-7-1

When delusion has been given up, they truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-54-7-2

This, too, is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^an3-54-7-3

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an3-54-8-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-54-8-2

### Quenched (Numbered Discourses 3.55) ^an3-55-0

Then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an3-55-1-1

“Worthy Gotama, they say that ‘extinguishment is apparent in the present life’. ^an3-55-1-2

In what way is extinguishment apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^an3-55-1-3

“A greedy person, overcome by greed, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-55-2-1

When greed has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-55-2-2

This is how extinguishment is apparent in the present life. ^an3-55-2-3

A hateful person … ^an3-55-3-1

A deluded person, overcome by delusion, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-55-3-2

When delusion has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-55-3-3

This, too, is how extinguishment is apparent in the present life. ^an3-55-3-4

When you experience the ending of greed, hate, and delusion without remainder, ^an3-55-4-1

that’s how extinguishment is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^an3-55-4-2

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an3-55-5-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-55-5-2

### Breaking Apart (Numbered Discourses 3.56) ^an3-56-0

Then a well-to-do Brahmin went up to the Buddha, and seated to one side he said to him: ^an3-56-1-1

“Worthy Gotama, I have heard that brahmins of the past who were elderly and senior, the tutors of tutors, said: ^an3-56-1-2

‘In the old days this world was so full of humans you’d think they were squashed together. The villages, towns and capital cities were no more than a chicken’s flight apart.’ ^an3-56-1-3

What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why these days human numbers have dwindled, a decline in population is evident, and whole villages, towns, cities, and countries have disappeared?” ^an3-56-1-4

“These days, brahmin, humans just love illicit desire. They’re overcome by immoral greed, and mired in wrong custom. ^an3-56-2-1

Taking up sharp knives, they murder each other. And so many people perish. ^an3-56-2-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why these days human numbers have dwindled. ^an3-56-2-3

Furthermore, because these days humans just love illicit desire … ^an3-56-3-1

the heavens don’t provide enough rain, ^an3-56-3-2

so there’s famine, a bad harvest, with crops blighted and turned to straw. ^an3-56-3-3

And so many people perish. ^an3-56-3-4

This is the cause, this is the reason why these days human numbers have dwindled. ^an3-56-3-5

Furthermore, because these days humans just love illicit desire … ^an3-56-4-1

native spirits let vicious monsters loose. And so many humans perish. ^an3-56-4-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why these days human numbers have dwindled.” ^an3-56-4-3

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an3-56-5-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-56-5-2

### With Vacchagotta (Numbered Discourses 3.57) ^an3-57-0

Then the wanderer Vacchagotta went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-57-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an3-57-1-2

“I have heard, worthy Gotama, that the ascetic Gotama says this: ^an3-57-1-3

‘Gifts should only be given to me, not to others. ^an3-57-1-4

Gifts should only be given to my disciples, not to the disciples of others. ^an3-57-1-5

Only what is given to me is very fruitful, not what is given to others. ^an3-57-1-6

Only what is given to my disciples is very fruitful, not what is given to the disciples of others.’ ^an3-57-1-7

I trust that those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and do not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism? ^an3-57-1-13

For we don’t want to misrepresent the worthy Gotama.” ^an3-57-1-14

“Vaccha, those who say this ^an3-57-2-1

do not repeat what I have said. They misrepresent me with what is false and untrue. ^an3-57-2-5

Anyone who prevents another from giving makes an obstacle and a roadblock for three individuals. ^an3-57-2-6

What three? ^an3-57-2-7

The giver is obstructed from making merit. The receiver is obstructed from getting what is offered. And they’ve already broken and damaged themselves. ^an3-57-2-8

Anyone who prevents another from giving makes an obstacle and a roadblock for these three individuals. ^an3-57-2-9

Vaccha, this is what I say: ^an3-57-3-1

‘You even make merit by tipping out dish-washing water in a cesspool or a sump with living creatures in it, thinking, ^an3-57-3-2

“May any creatures here be nourished!”’ ^an3-57-3-3

How much more then for human beings! ^an3-57-3-4

However, I also say that a gift to an ethical person is more fruitful than one to an unethical person. They’ve given up five factors, and possess five factors. ^an3-57-3-5

What are the five factors they’ve given up? ^an3-57-4-1

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an3-57-4-2

These are the five factors they’ve given up. ^an3-57-4-3

What are the five factors they possess? ^an3-57-5-1

The entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an3-57-5-2

These are the five factors they possess. ^an3-57-5-3

I say that a gift to anyone who has given up these five factors and possesses these five factors is very fruitful. ^an3-57-5-4

Cows may be black or white, ^an3-57-6-1

red or tawny, ^an3-57-6-2

mottled or uniform, ^an3-57-6-3

or pigeon-colored. ^an3-57-6-4

But when one is born among them, ^an3-57-7-1

the bull that’s tamed— ^an3-57-7-2

a behemoth, powerful, ^an3-57-7-3

well-paced in pulling forward— ^an3-57-7-4

they yoke the load just to him, ^an3-57-7-5

regardless of his color. ^an3-57-7-6

So it is for humans, ^an3-57-8-1

wherever they may be born— ^an3-57-8-2

among aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, ^an3-57-8-3

menials, or corpse-workers and scavengers— ^an3-57-8-4

one is born among them, ^an3-57-9-1

tamed, true to their vows. ^an3-57-9-2

Firm in principle, accomplished in ethical conduct, ^an3-57-9-3

truthful, conscientious, ^an3-57-9-4

they’ve given up birth and death. ^an3-57-10-1

Complete in the spiritual journey, ^an3-57-10-2

with burden put down, detached, ^an3-57-10-3

they’ve completed the task <j>and are free of defilements. ^an3-57-10-4

Gone beyond all things, ^an3-57-11-1

they’re quenched by not grasping. ^an3-57-11-2

In that flawless field, ^an3-57-11-3

a religious donation is abundant. ^an3-57-11-4

Fools who don’t understand— ^an3-57-12-1

unlearned simpletons— ^an3-57-12-2

give their gifts to those outside, ^an3-57-12-3

and don’t attend the peaceful ones. ^an3-57-12-4

But those who do attend the peaceful ones— ^an3-57-13-1

wise, esteemed as attentive— ^an3-57-13-2

and whose faith in the Holy One ^an3-57-13-3

has roots planted deep, ^an3-57-13-4

they go to the realm of the gods, ^an3-57-14-1

or are born here in a good family. ^an3-57-14-2

Gradually those astute ones ^an3-57-14-3

reach extinguishment.” ^an3-57-14-4

### With Tikaṇṇa (Numbered Discourses 3.58) ^an3-58-0

Then Tikaṇṇa the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-58-1-1

Seated to one side, in front of the Buddha, Tikaṇṇa praised the brahmins who were proficient in the three Vedas, ^an3-58-1-2

“Such are the brahmins, masters of the three Vedic knowledges! Thus are the brahmins, masters of the three Vedic knowledges!” ^an3-58-1-3

“But brahmin, how do the brahmins describe a brahmin who is master of the three Vedic knowledges?” ^an3-58-2-1

“Mister Gotama, it’s when a brahmin is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. He recites and remembers the hymns, and has mastered the three Vedas, together with their vocabularies and ritual performance, their phonology and word classification, and the testaments as fifth. He knows them word-by-word, and their grammar. He is well versed in cosmology and the marks of a great man. ^an3-58-2-2

That’s how the brahmins describe a brahmin who is master of the three Vedic knowledges.” ^an3-58-2-3

“Brahmin, a master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is quite different from a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One.” ^an3-58-3-1

“But worthy Gotama, how is one a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One? ^an3-58-3-2

Worthy Gotama, please teach me this.” ^an3-58-3-3

“Well then, brahmin, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-58-3-4

“Yes, worthy sir,” Tikaṇṇa replied. ^an3-58-3-5

The Buddha said this: ^an3-58-3-6

“Brahmin, it’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an3-58-4-1

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an3-58-4-2

And with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an3-58-4-3

With the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an3-58-4-4

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward recollection of past lives. ^an3-58-5-1

They recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so they recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an3-58-5-2

This is the first knowledge that they attain. ^an3-58-5-3

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-58-5-4

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward knowledge of the death and rebirth of sentient beings. ^an3-58-6-1

With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds: ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an3-58-6-2

This is the second knowledge that they attain. ^an3-58-6-3

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-58-6-4

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an3-58-7-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-58-7-2

They truly understand: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. ^an3-58-7-3

Knowing and seeing like this, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an3-58-7-4

When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^an3-58-7-5

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an3-58-7-6

This is the third knowledge that they attain. ^an3-58-7-7

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished, and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-58-7-8

For someone whose ethical conduct doesn’t waver, ^an3-58-8-1

who is alert, practicing absorption; ^an3-58-8-2

whose mind is mastered, ^an3-58-8-3

unified, serene. ^an3-58-8-4

That attentive one dispels the darkness, ^an3-58-9-1

master of the three knowledges, conqueror of death. ^an3-58-9-2

For the welfare of gods and humans, ^an3-58-9-3

he has given up everything, they say. ^an3-58-9-4

Accomplished in the three knowledges, ^an3-58-10-1

living without confusion, ^an3-58-10-2

bearing the final body, ^an3-58-10-3

they revere the awakened Gotama. ^an3-58-10-4

One who knows their past lives, ^an3-58-11-1

sees heaven and places of loss, ^an3-58-11-2

and has attained the ending of rebirth, ^an3-58-11-3

a sage of perfect insight— ^an3-58-11-4

it’s because of these three knowledges ^an3-58-12-1

that a brahmin is a master of the three knowledges. ^an3-58-12-2

That’s who I call a three-knowledge master, ^an3-58-12-3

and not the other <j>who repeats what they are told. ^an3-58-12-4

This, brahmin, is a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One.” ^an3-58-13-1

“Worthy Gotama, a master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is quite different from a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One. ^an3-58-13-2

And, worthy Gotama, a master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is not worth a sixteenth part of a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One. ^an3-58-13-3

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an3-58-14-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-58-14-2

### With Jānussoṇi (Numbered Discourses 3.59) ^an3-59-0

Then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. Seated to one side he said to the Buddha: ^an3-59-1-1

“Worthy Gotama, whoever has a sacrifice, an offering of food for ancestors, a dish of milk-rice prepared for an auspicious ceremony, or a gift to give, should give it to the brahmins who have mastered the three Vedic knowledges.” ^an3-59-1-2

“But brahmin, how do the brahmins describe a brahmin who is proficient in the three Vedic knowledges?” ^an3-59-1-3

“Mister Gotama, it’s when a brahmin is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. He recites and remembers the hymns, and has mastered the three Vedas, together with their vocabularies and ritual performance, their phonology and word classification, and the testaments as fifth. He knows them word-by-word, and their grammar. He is well versed in cosmology and the marks of a great man. ^an3-59-1-4

That’s how the brahmins describe a brahmin who is proficient in the three Vedic knowledges.” ^an3-59-1-5

“Brahmin, a master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is quite different from a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One.” ^an3-59-2-1
