“But worthy Gotama, how is one a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One? ^an3-59-2-2

Worthy Gotama, please teach me this.” ^an3-59-2-3

“Well then, brahmin, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-59-2-4

“Yes, worthy sir,” Jānussoṇi replied. ^an3-59-2-5

The Buddha said this: ^an3-59-2-6

“Brahmin, it’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … ^an3-59-3-1

enters and remains in the fourth absorption. ^an3-59-3-2

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward recollection of past lives. ^an3-59-4-1

They recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an3-59-4-2

This is the first knowledge that they attain. ^an3-59-4-3

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-59-4-4

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward knowledge of the death and rebirth of sentient beings. ^an3-59-5-1

With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, they understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an3-59-5-2

This is the second knowledge that they attain. ^an3-59-5-3

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-59-5-4

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an3-59-6-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-59-6-2

They truly understand: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. ^an3-59-6-3

Knowing and seeing like this, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an3-59-6-4

When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^an3-59-6-5

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an3-59-6-6

This is the third knowledge that they attain. ^an3-59-6-7

Ignorance is banished and knowledge has arisen; darkness is banished and light has arisen, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an3-59-6-8

One who is perfect in precepts and observances, ^an3-59-7-1

resolute and serene, ^an3-59-7-2

whose mind is mastered, ^an3-59-7-3

unified, serene; ^an3-59-7-4

who knows their past lives, ^an3-59-8-1

sees heaven and places of loss, ^an3-59-8-2

and has attained the end of rebirth, ^an3-59-8-3

such a sage has perfect insight. ^an3-59-8-4

Because of these three knowledges ^an3-59-9-1

a brahmin is a master of the three knowledges. ^an3-59-9-2

That’s who I call a three-knowledge master, ^an3-59-9-3

and not the other <j>who repeats what they are told. ^an3-59-9-4

This, brahmin, is a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One.” ^an3-59-10-1

“Worthy Gotama, the master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is quite different from a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One. ^an3-59-10-2

And, worthy Gotama, a master of three knowledges according to the brahmins is not worth a sixteenth part of a master of the three knowledges in the training of the Noble One. ^an3-59-10-3

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an3-59-11-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-59-11-2

### With Saṅgārava (Numbered Discourses 3.60) ^an3-60-0

Then Saṅgārava the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-60-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an3-60-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, we who are called brahmins ^an3-60-1-3

make sacrifices and encourage others to make sacrifices. ^an3-60-1-4

Now, worthy Gotama, both of these people—the one who sacrifices and the one who encourages others to sacrifice—are doing good for many people on account of that sacrifice. ^an3-60-1-5

But, worthy Gotama, when someone has gone forth from the lay life to homelessness, they tame, calm, and extinguish themselves alone. That being so, they are doing good for just one person on account of that going forth.” ^an3-60-1-6

“Well then, brahmin, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an3-60-2-1

What do you think, brahmin? ^an3-60-2-2

A Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^an3-60-2-3

He says, ^an3-60-2-4

‘Come, this is the path, this is the practice. Practicing like this, I realized the supreme objective of the spiritual life with my own insight, and I make it known. ^an3-60-2-5

Please, all of you, practice like this, and you too will realize the supreme objective of the spiritual life, and will live having realized it with your own insight.’ ^an3-60-2-6

So the teacher teaches Dhamma, and others practice accordingly, in their hundreds and thousands, and hundreds of thousands. ^an3-60-2-7

What do you think, brahmin? ^an3-60-3-1

This being so, are they doing good for just one person or for many people on account of going forth?” ^an3-60-3-2

“This being so, worthy Gotama, they are doing good for many people on account of going forth.” ^an3-60-3-3

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to Saṅgārava, ^an3-60-4-1

“Brahmin, which of these two practices do you believe has fewer obligations and undertakings, yet is more fruitful and beneficial?” ^an3-60-4-2

Saṅgārava said to Ānanda, ^an3-60-4-3

“Those such as the worthies Gotama and Ānanda ^an3-60-4-4

are honored and praised by me!” ^an3-60-4-5

For a second time, Ānanda said to Saṅgārava, ^an3-60-5-1

“Brahmin, I didn’t ask you ^an3-60-5-2

who you honor and praise. ^an3-60-5-3

I asked you ^an3-60-5-4

which of these two practices do you believe has fewer obligations and undertakings, yet is more fruitful and beneficial?” ^an3-60-5-5

For a second time Saṅgārava said to Ānanda, ^an3-60-5-6

“Those such as the worthies Gotama and Ānanda ^an3-60-5-7

are honored and praised by me!” ^an3-60-5-8

For a third time, Ānanda said to Saṅgārava, ^an3-60-6-1

“Brahmin, I didn’t ask you ^an3-60-6-2

who you honor and praise. ^an3-60-6-3

I asked you ^an3-60-6-4

which of these two practices do you believe has fewer obligations and undertakings, yet is more fruitful and beneficial?” ^an3-60-6-5

For a third time Saṅgārava said to Ānanda, ^an3-60-6-6

“Those such as the worthies Gotama and Ānanda ^an3-60-6-7

are honored and praised by me!” ^an3-60-6-8

Then it occurred to the Buddha, ^an3-60-7-1

“Though Ānanda asked him a sensible question three times, Saṅgārava falters without answering. ^an3-60-7-2

Why don’t I give him a way out?” ^an3-60-7-3

So the Buddha said to Saṅgārava, ^an3-60-7-4

“Brahmin, what came up in the conversation among the king’s retinue today, sitting together in the royal compound?” ^an3-60-7-5

“Worthy Gotama, this came up: ^an3-60-7-6

‘Formerly, it seems, there were fewer mendicants, but more of them displayed superhuman demonstrations of psychic power; ^an3-60-7-7

while these days, there are more mendicants, but fewer display superhuman demonstrations of psychic power.’ ^an3-60-7-8

This is what came up in the conversation among the king’s retinue today, while sitting together in the royal compound.” ^an3-60-7-9

“Brahmin, there are three kinds of demonstration. ^an3-60-8-1

What three? ^an3-60-8-2

A demonstration of psychic power, a demonstration of revealing, and a demonstration of instruction. ^an3-60-8-3

And what is the demonstration of psychic power? ^an3-60-8-4

It’s when someone wields the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. They control the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an3-60-8-5

This is called the demonstration of psychic power. ^an3-60-8-6

And what is the demonstration of revealing? ^an3-60-9-1

In one case, someone reveals by means of a sign: ^an3-60-9-2

‘This is what you’re thinking, such is your thought, and thus is your state of mind.’ ^an3-60-9-3

And even if they reveal this many times, it turns out exactly so, not otherwise. ^an3-60-9-4

In another case, someone reveals after hearing it from humans or non-humans or deities: ^an3-60-10-1

‘This is what you’re thinking, such is your thought, and thus is your state of mind.’ ^an3-60-10-2

And even if they reveal this many times, it turns out exactly so, not otherwise. ^an3-60-10-3

In another case, someone reveals by hearing the sound of thought spreading as someone thinks and considers: ^an3-60-11-1

‘This is what you’re thinking, such is your thought, and thus is your state of mind.’ ^an3-60-11-2

And even if they reveal this many times, it turns out exactly so, not otherwise. ^an3-60-11-3

In another case, someone comprehends the mind of one who has attained the immersion that’s free of placing the mind and keeping it connected. They understand: ^an3-60-12-1

‘Judging by the way this worthy’s intentions are directed, immediately after this mind state, they’ll think this thought.’ ^an3-60-12-2

And even if they reveal this many times, it turns out exactly so, not otherwise. ^an3-60-12-3

This is called the demonstration of revealing. ^an3-60-12-4

And what is a demonstration of instruction? ^an3-60-13-1

It’s when someone instructs others like this: ^an3-60-13-2

‘Think like this, not like that. Focus your mind like this, not like that. Give up this, and live having achieved that.’ ^an3-60-13-3

This is called a demonstration of instruction. ^an3-60-13-4

These are the three kinds of demonstration. ^an3-60-13-5

Of these three kinds of demonstration, which do you consider to be the finest?” ^an3-60-13-6

“Regarding this, worthy Gotama, a demonstration of psychic power ^an3-60-14-1

is experienced only by the one who performs it, occurring only to them. ^an3-60-14-3

This seems to me like an illusion. ^an3-60-14-4

And the demonstration where someone reveals something by means of a sign, ^an3-60-15-1

or after hearing it from humans, non-humans, or deities, ^an3-60-15-3

or by hearing the sound of thought spreading as someone thinks and considers, ^an3-60-15-4

or by comprehending the mind of another, ^an3-60-15-5

is also experienced only by the one who performs it, occurring only to them. ^an3-60-15-7

This also seems to me like an illusion. ^an3-60-15-8

But as to the demonstration where someone instructs others: ^an3-60-16-1

‘Think like this, not like that. Focus your mind like this, not like that. Give up this, and live having achieved that.’ ^an3-60-16-2

I prefer this demonstration, worthy Gotama. It’s the finest of the three kinds of demonstration. ^an3-60-16-3

It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing, ^an3-60-17-1

how well this was said by the worthy Gotama. We regard the worthy Gotama as someone who possesses these three kinds of demonstration. ^an3-60-17-2

For the worthy Gotama wields the many kinds of psychic power … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an3-60-17-3

And Mister Gotama comprehends the mind of another who has attained the immersion that is free of placing the mind and keeping it connected. He understands: ^an3-60-17-4

‘Judging by the way this worthy’s intentions are directed, immediately after this mind state they’ll think this thought.’ ^an3-60-17-5

And the worthy Gotama instructs others like this: ^an3-60-17-6

‘Think like this, not like that. Focus your mind like this, not like that. Give up this, and live having achieved that.’” ^an3-60-17-7

“Your words are clearly invasive and intrusive, brahmin. ^an3-60-18-1

Nevertheless, I will answer you. ^an3-60-18-2

For I do wield the many kinds of psychic power … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an3-60-18-3

And I do comprehend the mind of another individual who has attained the immersion that is free of placing the mind and keeping it connected. I understand: ^an3-60-18-4

‘Judging by the way this worthy’s intentions are directed, immediately after this mind state they’ll think this thought.’ ^an3-60-18-5

And I do instruct others like this: ^an3-60-18-6

‘Think like this, not like that. Focus your mind like this, not like that. Give up this, and live having achieved that.’” ^an3-60-18-7

“But worthy Gotama, is there even one other mendicant who possesses these three kinds of demonstration, apart from the worthy Gotama?” ^an3-60-19-1

“There’s not just one hundred mendicants, brahmin, who possess these three kinds of demonstration, nor two, three, four, or five hundred, but many more than that.” ^an3-60-19-2

“But where are these mendicants now staying?” ^an3-60-19-3

“Right here, brahmin, in this Saṅgha of mendicants.” ^an3-60-19-4

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^an3-60-20-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an3-60-20-2

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an3-60-20-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-60-20-4

### Sectarian Tenets (Numbered Discourses 3.61) ^an3-61-0

“Mendicants, these three sectarian tenets—as pursued, pressed, and grilled by the astute—when taken to their conclusion, end with inaction. ^an3-61-1-1

What three? ^an3-61-1-2

There are some ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view: ^an3-61-1-3

‘Everything this individual person experiences—pleasurable, painful, or neutral—is because of past deeds.’ ^an3-61-1-4

There are some ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view: ^an3-61-1-5

‘Everything this individual person experiences—pleasurable, painful, or neutral—is because of God Almighty’s creation.’ ^an3-61-1-6

There are some ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view: ^an3-61-1-7

‘Everything this individual person experiences—pleasurable, painful, or neutral—has no cause or reason.’ ^an3-61-1-8

Regarding this, I went up to the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^an3-61-2-1

everything an individual person experiences is because of past deeds, and I said to them: ^an3-61-2-2

‘Is it really true that this is the venerables’ view?’ ^an3-61-2-3

And they answered, ‘Yes’. ^an3-61-2-5

I said to them: ^an3-61-2-6

‘In that case, you might kill living creatures, steal, be unchaste; use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical; be covetous, malicious, or have wrong view, all because of past deeds.’ ^an3-61-2-7

Those who believe that past deeds are the most important thing have no enthusiasm or effort, no idea that there are things that should and should not be done. ^an3-61-3-1

Not finding as a genuine fact that there are things that should and should not be done, they’re unmindful and careless, and can’t rightly be called ascetics. ^an3-61-3-2

This is my first legitimate refutation of the ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view. ^an3-61-3-3

Regarding this, I went up to the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^an3-61-4-1

everything that an individual person experiences is because of God Almighty’s creation, and I said to them: ^an3-61-4-2

‘Is it really true that this is the venerables’ view?’ ^an3-61-4-3

And they answered, ‘Yes’. ^an3-61-4-5

I said to them: ^an3-61-4-6

‘In that case, you might kill living creatures, steal, be unchaste; use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical; be covetous, malicious, or have wrong view, all because of God Almighty’s creation.’ ^an3-61-4-7

Those who believe that God Almighty’s creative power is the most important thing have no enthusiasm, no effort, no idea that there are things that should and should not be done. ^an3-61-5-1

Not finding as a genuine fact that there are things that should and should not be done, they’re unmindful and careless, and can’t rightly be called ascetics. ^an3-61-5-2

This is my second legitimate refutation of the ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view. ^an3-61-5-3

Regarding this, I went up to the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that ^an3-61-6-1

everything that an individual person experiences has no cause or reason, and I said to them: ^an3-61-6-2

‘Is it really true that this is the venerables’ view?’ ^an3-61-6-3

And they answered, ‘Yes’. ^an3-61-6-5

I said to them: ^an3-61-6-6

‘In that case, you might kill living creatures, steal, be unchaste; use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical; be covetous, malicious, or have wrong view, all without cause or reason.’ ^an3-61-6-7

Those who believe that the absence of cause or reason is the most important thing have no enthusiasm, no effort, no idea that there are things that should and should not be done. ^an3-61-7-1

Not finding as a genuine fact that there are things that should and should not be done, they’re unmindful and careless, and can’t rightly be called ascetics. ^an3-61-7-2

This is my third legitimate refutation of the ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view. ^an3-61-7-3

These are the three sectarian tenets—as pursued, pressed, and grilled by the astute—which, when taken to their conclusion, end with inaction. ^an3-61-8-1

But the Dhamma that I’ve taught is irrefutable, uncorrupted, beyond reproach, and not scorned by sensible ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-61-9-1

What is the Dhamma that I’ve taught? ^an3-61-9-2

‘These are the six elements’: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught … ^an3-61-9-3

‘These are the six fields of contact’: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught … ^an3-61-9-4

‘These are the eighteen mental preoccupations’: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught … ^an3-61-9-5

‘These are the four noble truths’: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught that is irrefutable, uncorrupted, beyond reproach, and is not scorned by sensible ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-61-9-6

‘“These are the six elements”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-10-1

That’s what I said, ^an3-61-10-2

but why did I say it? ^an3-61-10-3

There are these six elements: ^an3-61-10-4

the elements of earth, water, fire, air, space, and consciousness. ^an3-61-10-5

‘“These are the six elements”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-10-6

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-61-10-7

‘“These are the six fields of contact”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-11-1

That’s what I said, ^an3-61-11-2

but why did I say it? ^an3-61-11-3

There are these six fields of contact: ^an3-61-11-4

eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind contact. ^an3-61-11-5

‘“These are the six fields of contact”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-11-6

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-61-11-7

‘“These are the eighteen mental preoccupations”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-12-1

This is what I said, ^an3-61-12-2

but why did I say it? ^an3-61-12-3

Seeing a sight with the eye, one is preoccupied with a sight that’s a basis for happiness or sadness or equanimity. ^an3-61-12-4

Hearing a sound with the ear … ^an3-61-12-5

Smelling an odor with the nose … ^an3-61-12-6

Tasting a flavor with the tongue … ^an3-61-12-7

Encountering a touch with the body … ^an3-61-12-8

Becoming conscious of an idea with the mind, one is preoccupied with an idea that’s a basis for happiness or sadness or equanimity. ^an3-61-12-9

‘“These are the eighteen mental preoccupations”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-12-10

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-61-12-11

‘“These are the four noble truths”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught …’ ^an3-61-13-1

That’s what I said, ^an3-61-13-2

but why did I say it? ^an3-61-13-3

Supported by the six elements, an embryo is conceived. ^an3-61-13-4

When it is conceived, there are name and form. Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. The six sense fields are requirements for contact. Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^an3-61-13-5

It’s for one who feels that I declare: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-61-13-6

And what is the noble truth of suffering? ^an3-61-14-1

Rebirth is suffering; old age is suffering; death is suffering; sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are suffering; being coupled with the disliked is suffering; separation from the liked is suffering; not getting what you wish for is suffering. In brief, the five grasping aggregates are suffering. ^an3-61-14-2

This is called the noble truth of suffering. ^an3-61-14-3

And what is the noble truth of the origin of suffering? ^an3-61-15-1

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^an3-61-15-2

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. ^an3-61-15-3

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. ^an3-61-15-4

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. ^an3-61-15-5

The six sense fields are requirements for contact. ^an3-61-15-6

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^an3-61-15-7

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^an3-61-15-8

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^an3-61-15-9

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^an3-61-15-10

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^an3-61-15-11

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^an3-61-15-12

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^an3-61-15-13

This is called the noble truth of the origin of suffering. ^an3-61-15-14

And what is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering? ^an3-61-16-1

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. ^an3-61-16-2

When choices cease, consciousness ceases. ^an3-61-16-3

When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. ^an3-61-16-4

When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. ^an3-61-16-5

When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. ^an3-61-16-6

When contact ceases, feeling ceases. ^an3-61-16-7

When feeling ceases, craving ceases. ^an3-61-16-8

When craving ceases, grasping ceases. ^an3-61-16-9

When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. ^an3-61-16-10

When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. ^an3-61-16-11

When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^an3-61-16-12

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases. ^an3-61-16-13

This is called the noble truth of the cessation of suffering. ^an3-61-16-14

And what is the noble truth of the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering? ^an3-61-17-1

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an3-61-17-2

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an3-61-17-3

This is called the noble truth of the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering. ^an3-61-17-4

‘“These are the four noble truths”: this is the Dhamma I’ve taught that is irrefutable, uncorrupted, beyond reproach, and is not scorned by sensible ascetics and brahmins.’ ^an3-61-17-5

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an3-61-17-6

### Perils (Numbered Discourses 3.62) ^an3-62-0

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person speaks of three perils that tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-1-1

What three? ^an3-62-1-2

There comes a time when a great fire flares up, ^an3-62-1-3

and it burns villages, towns, and cities. ^an3-62-1-4

When this happens, a mother can’t find her child, and a child can’t find their mother. ^an3-62-1-5

This is the first peril that tears mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-1-6

Furthermore, there comes a time when a great storm gathers, ^an3-62-2-1

and it unleashes a mighty flood ^an3-62-2-2

that sweeps away villages, towns, and cities. ^an3-62-2-3

When this happens, a mother can’t find her child, and a child can’t find their mother. ^an3-62-2-4

This is the second peril that tears mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-2-5

Furthermore, there comes a time of peril due to turmoil in the wilds, so the countryfolk mount their vehicles and flee everywhere. ^an3-62-3-1

When this happens, a mother can’t find her child, and a child can’t find their mother. ^an3-62-3-2

This is the third peril that tears mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-3-3

These are the three perils an unlearned ordinary person speaks of that tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-3-4

Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person speaks of three perils that don’t tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-4-1

What three? ^an3-62-4-2

There comes a time when a great fire flares up, ^an3-62-4-3

and it burns villages, towns, and cities. ^an3-62-4-4

When this happens, sometimes a mother can find her child, and a child can find their mother. ^an3-62-4-5

This is the first peril that doesn’t tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-4-6

Furthermore, there comes a time when a great storm gathers, ^an3-62-5-1

and it unleashes a mighty flood ^an3-62-5-2

that sweeps away villages, towns, and cities. ^an3-62-5-3

When this happens, sometimes a mother can find her child, and a child can find their mother. ^an3-62-5-4

This is the second peril that doesn’t tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-5-5

Furthermore, there comes a time of peril due to turmoil in the wilds, so the countryfolk mount their vehicles and flee everywhere. ^an3-62-6-1

When this happens, sometimes a mother can find her child, and a child can find their mother. ^an3-62-6-2

This is the third peril that doesn’t tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-6-3

These are the three perils an unlearned ordinary person speaks of that don’t tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-6-4

There are three perils that tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-7-1

What three? ^an3-62-7-2

The perils of old age, sickness, and death. ^an3-62-7-3

When a child is growing old, a mother doesn’t get her wish: ^an3-62-7-4

‘Let me grow old, may my child not grow old!’ ^an3-62-7-5

When a mother is growing old, a child doesn’t get their wish: ^an3-62-7-6

‘Let me grow old, may my mother not grow old!’ ^an3-62-7-7

When a child is sick, a mother doesn’t get her wish: ^an3-62-8-1

‘Let me be sick, may my child not be sick!’ ^an3-62-8-2

When a mother is sick, a child doesn’t get their wish: ^an3-62-8-3

‘Let me be sick, may my mother not be sick!’ ^an3-62-8-4

When a child is dying, a mother doesn’t get her wish: ^an3-62-9-1

‘Let me die, may my child not die!’ ^an3-62-9-2

When a mother is dying, a child doesn’t get their wish: ^an3-62-9-3

‘Let me die, may my mother not die!’ ^an3-62-9-4

These are the three perils that tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-9-5

There is a path and a practice that leads to giving up and going beyond the three perils that don’t tear mothers and children apart, and the three perils that do tear mothers and children apart. ^an3-62-10-1

What is that path and practice? ^an3-62-10-2

It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an3-62-10-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an3-62-10-4

This is the path, this is the practice that leads to giving up and going beyond the three perils that don’t tear mothers and children apart, and the three perils that do tear mothers and children apart.” ^an3-62-10-5

### At Venāgapura (Numbered Discourses 3.63) ^an3-63-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a village of the Kosalan brahmins named Venāgapura. ^an3-63-1-1

The brahmins and householders of Venāgapura heard: ^an3-63-1-2

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Venāgapura. ^an3-63-1-3

He has this good reputation: ^an3-63-1-4

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an3-63-1-5

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. ^an3-63-1-6

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an3-63-1-7

It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^an3-63-1-8

Then the brahmins and householders of Venāgapura went up to the Buddha. Before sitting down to one side, some bowed, some exchanged greetings and polite conversation, some held up their cupped palms toward the Buddha, some announced their name and clan, while some kept silent. Then the brahmin Vacchagotta of Venāgapura said to the Buddha: ^an3-63-2-1

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing, ^an3-63-3-1

how your faculties are so very clear, and the complexion of your skin is pure and bright. ^an3-63-3-2

It’s like a golden brown jujube in the autumn, ^an3-63-3-3

or a ripe palmyra fruit freshly plucked from the stalk, ^an3-63-3-5

or a pendant of Black Plum River gold, fashioned by a deft smith, well-wrought in the forge, and placed on a cream rug where it shines and glows and radiates. ^an3-63-3-7

In the same way, your faculties are so very clear, and the complexion of your skin is pure and bright. ^an3-63-3-8

Surely the worthy Gotama gets when he wants, without trouble or difficulty, various kinds of high and luxurious bedding, such as: ^an3-63-3-9

sofas, couches, woolen covers—shag-piled, colorful, white, embroidered with flowers, quilted, embroidered with animals, double-or single-fringed—and silk covers studded with gems, as well as silken sheets, woven carpets, rugs for elephants, horses, or chariots, antelope hide rugs, and spreads of fine deer hide, with a canopy above and red cushions at both ends.” ^an3-63-3-10

“Brahmin, these various kinds of high and luxurious bedding ^an3-63-4-1

are hard for renunciates to find. And even if they do get them, they’re not allowed. ^an3-63-4-3

There are, brahmin, these three high and luxurious beds that I get these days when I want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an3-63-5-1

What three? ^an3-63-5-2

The heavenly high and luxurious bed, the divine high and luxurious bed, and the noble high and luxurious bed. ^an3-63-5-3

These are the three high and luxurious beds that I get these days when I want, without trouble or difficulty.” ^an3-63-5-4

“But what, worthy Gotama, is the heavenly high and luxurious bed?” ^an3-63-6-1

“Brahmin, when I am living supported by a village or town, I robe up in the morning and, taking my bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms. ^an3-63-6-2

After the meal, on my return from almsround, I enter within a forest. ^an3-63-6-3

I gather up some grass or leaves into a pile and sit down cross-legged, setting my body straight, and bringing mindfulness to the present. ^an3-63-6-4

Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an3-63-6-5

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, I enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an3-63-6-6

And with the fading away of rapture, I enter and remain in the third absorption, where I meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an3-63-6-7

With the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, I enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an3-63-6-8

When I’m practicing like this, if I walk, at that time my walking is heavenly. ^an3-63-6-9

When I’m practicing like this, if I stand, at that time my standing is heavenly. ^an3-63-6-10

When I’m practicing like this, if I sit, at that time my sitting is heavenly. ^an3-63-6-11

When I’m practicing like this, if I lie down, at that time my lying is heavenly. ^an3-63-6-12

This is the heavenly high and luxurious bed that I get these days when I want, without trouble or difficulty.” ^an3-63-6-13

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing! ^an3-63-7-1

Who but the worthy Gotama could get such a heavenly high and luxurious bed when he wants, without trouble or difficulty? ^an3-63-7-2

But what, worthy Gotama, is the divine high and luxurious bed?” ^an3-63-8-1

“Brahmin, when I am living supported by a village or town, I robe up in the morning and, taking my bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms. ^an3-63-8-2

After the meal, on my return from almsround, I enter within a forest. ^an3-63-8-3

I gather up some grass or leaves into a pile and sit down cross-legged, setting my body straight, and bringing mindfulness to the present. ^an3-63-8-4

I meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, I spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-63-8-5

I meditate spreading a heart full of compassion to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, I spread a heart full of compassion to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-63-8-6

I meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, I spread a heart full of rejoicing to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-63-8-7

I meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, I spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-63-8-8

When I’m practicing like this, if I walk, at that time my walking is divine. ^an3-63-8-9

… my standing is divine. ^an3-63-8-10

… my sitting is divine. ^an3-63-8-11

When I’m practicing like this, if I lie down, at that time my lying is divine. ^an3-63-8-12

This is the divine high and luxurious bed that I get these days when I want, without trouble or difficulty.” ^an3-63-8-13

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing! ^an3-63-9-1

Who but the worthy Gotama could get such a divine high and luxurious bed when he wants, without trouble or difficulty? ^an3-63-9-2

But what, worthy Gotama, is the noble high and luxurious bed?” ^an3-63-10-1

“Brahmin, when I am living supported by a village or town, I robe up in the morning and, taking my bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms. ^an3-63-10-2

After the meal, on my return from almsround, I enter within a forest. ^an3-63-10-3

I gather up some grass or leaves into a pile and sit down cross-legged, setting my body straight, and bringing mindfulness to the present. ^an3-63-10-4

I know this: ^an3-63-10-5

‘I’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they’re unable to arise in the future.’ ^an3-63-10-6

When I’m practicing like this, if I walk, at that time my walking is noble. ^an3-63-10-9

… my standing is noble. ^an3-63-10-10

… my sitting is noble. ^an3-63-10-11

When I’m practicing like this, if I lie down, at that time my lying is noble. ^an3-63-10-12

This is the noble high and luxurious bed that I get these days when I want, without trouble or difficulty.” ^an3-63-10-13

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing! ^an3-63-11-1

Who but the worthy Gotama could get such a noble high and luxurious bed when he wants, without trouble or difficulty? ^an3-63-11-2

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^an3-63-12-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an3-63-12-2

We go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an3-63-12-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-63-12-4

### With Sarabha (Numbered Discourses 3.64) ^an3-64-0

So I have heard. ^an3-64-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an3-64-1-2

Now at that time a wanderer named Sarabha had recently left this teaching and training. ^an3-64-1-3

He was telling a crowd in Rājagaha, ^an3-64-1-4

“I learned the teaching of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan, ^an3-64-1-5

then I left their teaching and training.” ^an3-64-1-6

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Rājagaha for alms. ^an3-64-1-7

They heard what Sarabha was saying. ^an3-64-1-8

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an3-64-2-1

“The wanderer named Sarabha has recently left this teaching and training. ^an3-64-2-2

He was telling a crowd in Rājagaha: ^an3-64-2-3

‘I learned the teaching of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan, ^an3-64-2-4

then I left their teaching and training.’ ^an3-64-2-5

Worthy sir, please go to the wanderers’ monastery on the banks of the Serpentine river to see Sarabha the wanderer out of sympathy.” ^an3-64-2-6

The Buddha consented with silence. ^an3-64-2-7

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the wanderers’ monastery on the banks of the Serpentine river to visit Sarabha the wanderer. He sat on the seat spread out, and said to the wanderer Sarabha, ^an3-64-3-1

“Is it really true, Sarabha, that you’ve been saying: ^an3-64-3-2

‘I learned the teaching of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan, ^an3-64-3-3

then I left their teaching and training.’” ^an3-64-3-4

When he said this, Sarabha kept silent. ^an3-64-3-5

For a second time, the Buddha said to Sarabha, ^an3-64-4-1

“Tell me, Sarabha, what exactly have you learned of the teachings of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan? ^an3-64-4-2

If you’ve not learned it fully, I’ll fill you in. ^an3-64-4-3

But if you have learned it fully, I’ll agree.” ^an3-64-4-4

For a second time, Sarabha kept silent. ^an3-64-4-5

For a third time, the Buddha said to Sarabha, ^an3-64-5-1

“Sarabha, the teachings of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan are clear to me. What exactly have you learned of the teachings of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan? ^an3-64-5-2

If you’ve not learned it fully, I’ll fill you in. ^an3-64-5-3

But if you have learned it fully, I’ll agree.” ^an3-64-5-4

For a third time, Sarabha kept silent. ^an3-64-5-5

Then those wanderers said to Sarabha, ^an3-64-6-1

“The ascetic Gotama has offered to tell you anything you ask for. ^an3-64-6-2

Speak, reverend Sarabha, what exactly have you learned of the teachings of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan? ^an3-64-6-3

If you’ve not learned it fully, he’ll fill you in. ^an3-64-6-4

But if you have learned it fully, he’ll agree.” ^an3-64-6-5

When this was said, Sarabha sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say. ^an3-64-6-6

Knowing this, the Buddha said to the wanderers: ^an3-64-7-1

“Wanderers, someone might say to me: ^an3-64-8-1

‘You claim to be a fully awakened Buddha, but you don’t understand these things.’ Then I’d carefully pursue, press, and grill them on that point. ^an3-64-8-2

When grilled by me, they would, without a doubt, fall into one of these three categories. They’d dodge the issue, distracting the discussion with irrelevant points. They’d display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. Or they’d sit silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say, like Sarabha. ^an3-64-8-3

Wanderers, someone might say to me: ^an3-64-9-1

‘You claim to have ended all defilements, but you still have these defilements.’ Then I’d carefully pursue, press, and grill them on that point. ^an3-64-9-2

When grilled by me, they would, without a doubt, fall into one of these three categories. They’d dodge the issue, distracting the discussion with irrelevant points. They’d display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. Or they’d sit silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say, like Sarabha. ^an3-64-9-3

Wanderers, someone might say to me: ^an3-64-10-1

‘Your teaching does not lead someone who practices it to the complete ending of suffering, the goal for which it is taught.’ Then I’d carefully pursue, press, and grill them on that point. ^an3-64-10-2

When grilled by me, they would, without a doubt, fall into one of these three categories. They’d dodge the issue, distracting the discussion with irrelevant points. They’d display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. Or they’d sit silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say, like Sarabha.” ^an3-64-10-3

Then the Buddha, having roared his lion’s roar three times in the wanderers’ monastery on the bank of the Serpentine river, rose into the air and flew away. ^an3-64-10-4

Soon after the Buddha left, those wanderers beset Sarabha on all sides with sneering and jeering. ^an3-64-11-1

“Reverend Sarabha, you’re just like an old jackal in the formidable wilderness who thinks, ‘I’ll roar a lion’s roar!’ but they still only manage to squeal and yelp like a jackal. ^an3-64-11-2

In the same way, when the ascetic Gotama wasn’t here you said ‘I’ll roar a lion’s roar!’ but you only managed to squeal and yelp like a jackal. ^an3-64-11-3

You’re just like a marsh hen who thinks, ‘I’ll cry like a cuckoo!’ but they still only manage to cry like a marsh hen. ^an3-64-11-4

In the same way, when the ascetic Gotama wasn’t here you said ‘I’ll cry like a cuckoo!’ but you still only managed to cry like a marsh hen. ^an3-64-11-5

You’re just like a bull that thinks to bellow only when the cowstall is empty. ^an3-64-11-6

In the same way, you only thought to bellow when the ascetic Gotama wasn’t here.” ^an3-64-11-7

That’s how those wanderers beset Sarabha on all sides with sneering and jeering. ^an3-64-11-8

### With the Kālāmas of Kesamutta (Numbered Discourses 3.65) ^an3-65-0

So I have heard. ^an3-65-1-1

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a town of the Kālāmas named Kesamutta. ^an3-65-1-2

The Kālāmas of Kesamutta heard: ^an3-65-1-3

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Kesamutta. ^an3-65-1-4

He has this good reputation: ^an3-65-1-5

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha …’ ^an3-65-1-6

It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^an3-65-1-7

Then the Kālāmas went up to the Buddha. Before sitting down to one side, some bowed, some exchanged greetings and polite conversation, some held up their cupped palms toward the Buddha, some announced their name and clan, while some kept silent. Seated to one side the Kālāmas said to the Buddha: ^an3-65-2-1

“There are, sir, some ascetics and brahmins who come to Kesamutta. ^an3-65-3-1

They explain and promote only their own doctrine, while they attack, badmouth, disparage, and smear the doctrines of others. ^an3-65-3-2

Then some other ascetics and brahmins come to Kesamutta. ^an3-65-3-3

They too explain and promote only their own doctrine, while they attack, badmouth, disparage, and smear the doctrines of others. ^an3-65-3-4

So, sir, we’re doubting and uncertain: ^an3-65-3-5

‘I wonder who of these respected ascetics and brahmins speaks the truth, and who speaks falsehood?’” ^an3-65-3-6

“It is enough, Kālāmas, for you to be doubting and uncertain. ^an3-65-3-7

Doubt has come up in you about an uncertain matter. ^an3-65-3-8

Please, Kālāmas, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’ ^an3-65-4-1

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-65-4-2

‘These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering’, then you should give them up. ^an3-65-4-3

What do you think, Kālāmas? ^an3-65-5-1

Does greed come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an3-65-5-2

“Harm, sir.” ^an3-65-6-1

“A greedy individual, overcome by greed, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-65-7-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-65-8-1

“What do you think, Kālāmas? ^an3-65-9-1

Does hate come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an3-65-9-2

“Harm, sir.” ^an3-65-10-1

“A hateful individual, overcome by hate, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-65-11-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-65-12-1

“What do you think, Kālāmas? ^an3-65-13-1

Does delusion come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an3-65-13-2

“Harm, sir.” ^an3-65-14-1

“A deluded individual, overcome by delusion, kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-65-15-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-65-16-1

“What do you think, Kālāmas, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an3-65-17-1

“Unskillful, sir.” ^an3-65-18-1

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an3-65-19-1

“Blameworthy, sir.” ^an3-65-20-1

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an3-65-21-1

“Criticized by sensible people, sir.” ^an3-65-22-1

“When you undertake them, do they lead to harm and suffering, or not? ^an3-65-23-1

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-65-23-2

“When you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering. ^an3-65-24-1

That’s how we see it.” ^an3-65-24-2

“So, Kālāmas, when I said: ^an3-65-25-1

‘Please, ^an3-65-25-2

don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an3-65-25-3

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-65-25-4

“These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering”, then you should give them up.’ ^an3-65-25-5

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-65-25-6

Please, Kālāmas, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’ ^an3-65-26-1

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-65-26-2

‘These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness’, then you should acquire them and keep them. ^an3-65-26-3

What do you think, Kālāmas? ^an3-65-27-1

Does contentment come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an3-65-27-2

“Welfare, sir.” ^an3-65-28-1

“An individual who is content, not overcome by greed, doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an3-65-29-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-65-30-1

“What do you think, Kālāmas? ^an3-65-31-1

Does love come up in a person for their welfare or harm? … ^an3-65-31-2

Does understanding come up in a person for their welfare or harm? … ^an3-65-31-3

Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an3-65-31-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-65-32-1

“What do you think, Kālāmas, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an3-65-33-1

“Skillful, sir.” ^an3-65-34-1

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an3-65-35-1

“Blameless, sir.” ^an3-65-36-1

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an3-65-37-1

“Praised by sensible people, sir.” ^an3-65-38-1

“When you undertake them, do they lead to welfare and happiness, or not? ^an3-65-39-1

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-65-39-2

“When you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness. ^an3-65-40-1

That’s how we see it.” ^an3-65-40-2

“So, Kālāmas, when I said: ^an3-65-41-1

‘Please, ^an3-65-41-2

don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an3-65-41-3

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-65-41-4

“These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness”, then you should acquire them and keep them.’ ^an3-65-41-5

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-65-41-6

Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-65-42-1

They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of compassion to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-65-42-2

They meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of rejoicing to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-65-42-3

They meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-65-42-4

When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified, ^an3-65-43-1

they’ve won four consolations in this very life. ^an3-65-43-2

‘If it turns out there is another world, and good and bad deeds have a result, then—when the body breaks up, after death—I’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ This is the first consolation they’ve won. ^an3-65-43-3

‘If it turns out there is no other world, and good and bad deeds don’t have a result, then in this very life I’ll keep myself free of enmity and ill will, untroubled and happy.’ This is the second consolation they’ve won. ^an3-65-44-1

‘If it turns out that bad things happen to people who do bad things, then since I have no bad intentions, ^an3-65-45-1

and since I’m not doing anything bad, how can suffering touch me?’ This is the third consolation they’ve won. ^an3-65-45-2

‘If it turns out that bad things don’t happen to people who do bad things, then I still see myself pure on both sides.’ This is the fourth consolation they’ve won. ^an3-65-46-1

When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified, ^an3-65-47-1

they’ve won these four consolations in this very life.” ^an3-65-47-2

“That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One! ^an3-65-48-1

When that noble disciple has a mind that’s free of enmity and ill will, uncorrupted and purified, ^an3-65-48-2

they’ve won these four consolations in this very life. … ^an3-65-48-3

Excellent, sir! Excellent! … ^an3-65-53-1

We go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an3-65-53-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-65-53-3

### With Sāḷha and His Friend (Numbered Discourses 3.66) ^an3-66-0

So I have heard. ^an3-66-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Nandaka was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an3-66-1-2

Then Sāḷha, Migāra’s grandson, and Rohaṇa, Pekhuṇiya’s grandson went up to Venerable Nandaka, bowed, and sat down to one side. Then Venerable Nandaka said to Sāḷha: ^an3-66-1-3

“Please, Sāḷha and friend, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’ ^an3-66-2-1

But when you know for yourselves: ‘These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering’, then you should give them up. ^an3-66-2-2

What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an3-66-3-1

Is greed real?” ^an3-66-3-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-4-1

“‘Covetousness’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-5-1

A person who is greedy and covetous kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-66-5-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-6-1

“What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an3-66-7-1

Is hate real?” ^an3-66-7-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-8-1

“‘Malice’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-9-1

A hateful and malicious person kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-66-9-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-10-1

“What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an3-66-11-1

Is delusion real?” ^an3-66-11-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-12-1

“‘Ignorance’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-13-1

A person who is deluded and ignorant kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an3-66-13-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-14-1

“What do you think, Sāḷha, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an3-66-15-1

“Unskillful, sir.” ^an3-66-16-1

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an3-66-17-1

“Blameworthy, sir.” ^an3-66-18-1

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an3-66-19-1

“Criticized by sensible people, sir.” ^an3-66-20-1

“When you undertake them, do they lead to harm and suffering, or not? ^an3-66-21-1

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-66-21-2

“When you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering. ^an3-66-22-1

That’s how we see it.” ^an3-66-22-2

“So, Sāḷha and friend, when I said: ^an3-66-23-1

‘Please, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an3-66-23-2

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-66-23-3

“These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering”, then you should give them up.’ ^an3-66-23-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-66-23-5

Please, Sāḷha and friend, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’ ^an3-66-24-1

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-66-24-2

‘These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness’, then you should acquire them and keep them. ^an3-66-24-3

What do you think? Is contentment real?” ^an3-66-25-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-26-1

“‘Satisfaction’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-27-1

A person who is content and satisfied doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an3-66-27-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-28-1

“What do you think? Is love real?” ^an3-66-29-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-30-1

“‘Kindness’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-31-1

A loving and kind-hearted person doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an3-66-31-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-32-1

“What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an3-66-33-1

Is understanding real?” ^an3-66-33-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-34-1

“‘Knowledge’ is what I mean by this. ^an3-66-35-1

A person who understands and knows doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an3-66-35-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an3-66-36-1

“What do you think, Sāḷha, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an3-66-37-1

“Skillful, sir.” ^an3-66-38-1

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an3-66-39-1

“Blameless, sir.” ^an3-66-40-1

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an3-66-41-1

“Praised by sensible people, sir.” ^an3-66-42-1

“When you undertake them, do they lead to welfare and happiness, or not? ^an3-66-43-1

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-66-43-2

“When you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness. ^an3-66-44-1

That’s how we see it.” ^an3-66-44-2

“So, Sāḷha and friend, when I said: ^an3-66-45-1

‘Please, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an3-66-45-2

But when you know for yourselves: ^an3-66-45-3

“These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness”, then you should acquire them and keep them.’ ^an3-66-45-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an3-66-45-5

Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love … ^an3-66-46-1

compassion … ^an3-66-46-2

rejoicing … ^an3-66-46-3

equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an3-66-46-4

They understand: ^an3-66-46-5

‘There is this, there is what is worse than this, there is what is better than this, and there is an escape beyond the scope of perception.’ ^an3-66-46-6

Knowing and seeing like this, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an3-66-46-7

When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^an3-66-46-8

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an3-66-46-9

They understand: ^an3-66-47-1

‘Formerly there was greed, which was unskillful. Now there is none, so that’s skillful. ^an3-66-47-2

Formerly there was hate, which was unskillful. Now there is none, so that’s skillful. ^an3-66-47-3

Formerly there was delusion, which was unskillful. Now there is none, so that’s skillful.’ ^an3-66-47-4

So they live without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine.” ^an3-66-47-5

### Topics of Discussion (Numbered Discourses 3.67) ^an3-67-0

“There are, mendicants, these three topics of discussion. ^an3-67-1-1

What three? ^an3-67-1-2

You might discuss the past: ^an3-67-1-3

‘That is how it was in the past.’ ^an3-67-1-4

You might discuss the future: ^an3-67-1-5

‘That is how it will be in the future.’ ^an3-67-1-6

Or you might discuss the present: ^an3-67-1-7

‘This is how it is at present.’ ^an3-67-1-8

You can know whether or not an individual is competent to hold a discussion by seeing how they take part in a discussion. ^an3-67-2-1

When an individual is asked a question, if it needs to be answered categorically and they don’t answer it categorically; or if it needs analysis and they answer without analyzing it; or if it needs a counter-question and they answer without a counter-question; or if it should be set aside and they don’t set it aside, then that individual is not competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-2-2

When an individual is asked a question, if it needs to be answered categorically and they answer it categorically; or if it needs analysis and they answer after analyzing it; or if it needs a counter-question and they answer with a counter-question; or if it should be set aside and they set it aside, then that individual is competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-2-3

You can know whether or not an individual is competent to hold a discussion by seeing how they take part in a discussion. ^an3-67-3-1

When an individual is asked a question, if they’re not consistent about what their position is and what it isn’t; about what they propose; about speaking from what they know; and about the appropriate procedure, then that individual is not competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-3-2

When an individual is asked a question, if they are consistent about what their position is and what it isn’t; about what they propose; about speaking from what they know; and about the appropriate procedure, then that individual is competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-3-3

You can know whether or not an individual is competent to hold a discussion by seeing how they take part in a discussion. ^an3-67-4-1

When an individual is asked a question, if they dodge the issue; distract the discussion with irrelevant points; or display annoyance, hate, and bitterness, then that individual is not competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-4-2

When an individual is asked a question, if they don’t dodge the issue; distract the discussion with irrelevant points; or display annoyance, hate, and bitterness, then that individual is competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-4-3

You can know whether or not an individual is competent to hold a discussion by seeing how they take part in a discussion. ^an3-67-5-1

When an individual is asked a question, if they intimidate, crush, mock, or seize on trivial mistakes, then that individual is not competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-5-2

When an individual is asked a question, if they don’t intimidate, crush, mock, or seize on trivial mistakes, then that individual is competent to hold a discussion. ^an3-67-5-3

You can know whether or not an individual has what’s required by seeing how they take part in a discussion. ^an3-67-6-1

If they actively listen they have what’s required; if they don’t actively listen they don’t have what’s required. ^an3-67-6-2

Someone who has what’s required directly knows one thing, completely understands one thing, gives up one thing, and realizes one thing— ^an3-67-6-3

and then they experience complete freedom. ^an3-67-6-4

This is the purpose of discussion, ^an3-67-6-5

consultation, ^an3-67-6-6

sitting close, ^an3-67-6-7

and listening well, that is, the liberation of the mind by not grasping. ^an3-67-6-8

Those who converse with hostility, ^an3-67-7-1

too sure of themselves, arrogant, ^an3-67-7-2

ignoble, attacking virtues, ^an3-67-7-3

they look for flaws in each other. ^an3-67-7-4

They rejoice together when their opponent ^an3-67-8-1

speaks poorly and makes a mistake, ^an3-67-8-2

becoming confused and defeated— ^an3-67-8-3

but the noble ones don’t discuss like this. ^an3-67-8-4

If an astute person wants to hold a discussion ^an3-67-9-1

connected with the teaching and its meaning— ^an3-67-9-2

the kind of discussion that noble ones hold— ^an3-67-9-3

then that wise one should start the discussion, ^an3-67-9-4

knowing when the time is right, ^an3-67-10-1

neither hostile nor arrogant. ^an3-67-10-2

Not over-excited, ^an3-67-10-3

contemptuous, or aggressive, ^an3-67-10-4

or with a mind full of jealousy, ^an3-67-11-1

they’d speak from what they rightly know. ^an3-67-11-2

They agree with what was well spoken, ^an3-67-11-3

without criticizing what was poorly said. ^an3-67-11-4

They’d not persist in finding faults, ^an3-67-12-1

nor seize on trivial mistakes, ^an3-67-12-2

neither intimidating nor crushing the other, ^an3-67-12-3

nor would they speak suggestively. ^an3-67-12-4

Good people consult ^an3-67-13-1

for the sake of knowledge and clarity. ^an3-67-13-2

That’s how the noble ones consult, ^an3-67-13-3

this is a noble consultation. ^an3-67-13-4

Knowing this, an intelligent person ^an3-67-13-5

would consult without arrogance.” ^an3-67-13-6

### Followers of Other Religions (Numbered Discourses 3.68) ^an3-68-0

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an3-68-1-1

‘There are these three things. ^an3-68-1-2

What three? ^an3-68-1-3

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-68-1-4

These are the three things. ^an3-68-1-5

What’s the difference between them?’ ^an3-68-1-6

How would you answer them?” ^an3-68-1-7

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an3-68-1-8

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-68-1-9

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an3-68-1-10

The Buddha said this: ^an3-68-1-11

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an3-68-2-1

‘There are these three things. ^an3-68-2-2

What three? ^an3-68-2-3

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-68-2-4

These are the three things. ^an3-68-2-5

What’s the difference between them?’ ^an3-68-2-6

You should answer them: ^an3-68-2-7

‘Greed, reverends, is mildly blameworthy, but slow to fade away. Hate is very blameworthy, but quick to fade away. Delusion is very blameworthy, and slow to fade away.’ ^an3-68-2-8

And if they ask: ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why greed arises, and once arisen it increases and grows?’ ^an3-68-3-1

You should say: ‘The beautiful feature of things. ^an3-68-3-2

When you apply the mind irrationally to the beautiful feature of things, greed arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^an3-68-3-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why greed arises, and once arisen it increases and grows.’ ^an3-68-3-4

And if they ask: ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why hate arises, and once arisen it increases and grows?’ ^an3-68-4-1

You should say: ‘The feature of aversion. ^an3-68-4-2

When you apply the mind irrationally to the feature of aversion, hate arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^an3-68-4-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why hate arises, and once arisen it increases and grows.’ ^an3-68-4-4

And if they ask: ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why delusion arises, and once arisen it increases and grows?’ ^an3-68-5-1

You should say: ‘Irrational application of mind. ^an3-68-5-2

When you apply the mind irrationally, delusion arises, and once arisen it increases and grows. ^an3-68-5-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why delusion arises, and once arisen it increases and grows.’ ^an3-68-5-4

And if they ask, ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why greed doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up?’ ^an3-68-6-1

You should say: ‘The ugly feature of things. ^an3-68-6-2

When you apply the mind rationally on the ugly feature of things, greed doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up. ^an3-68-6-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why greed doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up.’ ^an3-68-6-4

And if they ask, ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why hate doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up?’ ^an3-68-7-1

You should say: ‘The heart’s release by love.’ ^an3-68-7-2

When you apply the mind rationally on the heart’s release by love, hate doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up. ^an3-68-7-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why hate doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up.’ ^an3-68-7-4

And if they ask, ‘What is the cause, what is the reason why delusion doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up?’ ^an3-68-8-1

You should say: ‘Rational application of mind. ^an3-68-8-2

When you apply the mind rationally, delusion doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up. ^an3-68-8-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why delusion doesn’t arise, or if it’s already arisen it’s given up.’” ^an3-68-8-4

### Unskillful Roots (Numbered Discourses 3.69) ^an3-69-0

“Mendicants, there are these three unskillful roots. ^an3-69-1-1

What three? ^an3-69-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-69-1-3

Greed is a root of the unskillful. ^an3-69-2-1

When a greedy person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-2-2

When a greedy person, overcome by greed, causes another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-2-3

And so these many bad, unskillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by greed. ^an3-69-2-4

Hate is a root of the unskillful. ^an3-69-3-1

When a hateful person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-3-2

When a hateful person, overcome by hate, causes another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-3-3

And so these many bad, unskillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by hate. ^an3-69-3-4

Delusion is a root of the unskillful. ^an3-69-4-1

When a deluded person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-4-2

When a deluded person, overcome by delusion, causes another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is unskillful. ^an3-69-4-3

And so these many bad, unskillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by delusion. ^an3-69-4-4

Such an individual is said to have speech that’s ill-timed, false, meaningless, not in line with the teaching and training. ^an3-69-4-5

Why is this? ^an3-69-5-1

This individual causes another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’. ^an3-69-5-2

So when someone makes a valid criticism, they’re scornful and admit nothing. ^an3-69-5-3

When someone makes a baseless criticism, they make no effort to explain, ‘This is why that’s untrue, this is why that’s false.’ ^an3-69-5-4

That’s why such an individual is said to have speech that’s ill-timed, false, meaningless, not in line with the teaching and training. ^an3-69-5-5

Such an individual—overcome with bad, unskillful qualities born of greed, hate, and delusion—suffers in this very life, with distress, anguish, and fever. ^an3-69-6-1

And when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect to be reborn in a bad place. ^an3-69-6-2

Suppose a sal, axlewood, or boxwood tree was choked and enveloped by three camel’s foot creepers. It would fall to ruin and disaster. ^an3-69-7-4

In the same way, such an individual—overcome with bad, unskillful qualities born of greed, hate, and delusion—suffers in this very life, with distress, anguish, and fever. ^an3-69-7-5

And when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect to be reborn in a bad place. ^an3-69-7-6

These are the three unskillful roots. ^an3-69-8-4

There are these three skillful roots. ^an3-69-9-1

What three? ^an3-69-9-2

Contentment, love, and understanding. ^an3-69-9-3

Contentment is a root of the skillful. ^an3-69-10-1

When a contented person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-10-2

When a contented person, not overcome by greed, doesn’t cause another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-10-3

And so these many skillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by contentment. ^an3-69-10-4

Love is a root of the skillful. ^an3-69-11-1

When a loving person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-11-2

When a loving person, not overcome by hate, doesn’t cause another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-11-3

And so these many skillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by love. ^an3-69-11-4

Understanding is a root of the skillful. ^an3-69-12-1

When an understanding person chooses to act by way of body, speech, or mind, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-12-2

When an understanding person, not overcome by delusion, doesn’t cause another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’, that too is skillful. ^an3-69-12-3

And so these many skillful things are produced in them, born, sourced, originated, and conditioned by understanding. ^an3-69-12-4

Such an individual is said to have speech that’s well-timed, true, meaningful, in line with the teaching and training. ^an3-69-12-5

Why is this? ^an3-69-13-1

This individual doesn’t cause another to suffer under a false pretext—by execution or imprisonment or confiscation or condemnation or banishment—thinking ‘I’m powerful, I want power’. ^an3-69-13-2

So when someone makes a valid criticism, they admit it and aren’t scornful. ^an3-69-13-3

When someone makes a baseless criticism, they make an effort to explain, ^an3-69-13-4

‘This is why that’s untrue, this is why that’s false.’ ^an3-69-13-5

That’s why such an individual is said to have speech that’s well-timed, true, meaningful, in line with the teaching and training. ^an3-69-13-6

For such an individual, bad unskillful qualities born of greed, hate, and delusion are cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-69-14-1

In this very life they’re happy, free of distress, anguish, and fever, ^an3-69-14-2

and they’re also extinguished in this very life. ^an3-69-14-3

Suppose a sal, axlewood, or boxwood tree was choked and enveloped by three camel’s foot creepers. ^an3-69-15-5

Then along comes a person with a spade and basket. ^an3-69-15-6

They’d cut the creeper out by the roots, dig them up, and pull them out, down to the fibers and stems. ^an3-69-15-7

Then they’d split the creeper apart, cut up the parts, and chop it into splinters. They’d dry the splinters in the wind and sun, burn them with fire, and reduce them to ashes. Then they’d whisk away the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^an3-69-15-8

So those camel’s foot creepers would be cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, and obliterated, unable to arise in the future. ^an3-69-15-9

In the same way, for such an individual, bad unskillful qualities born of greed, hate, and delusion are cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an3-69-15-10

In this very life they’re happy, free of  distress, anguish, and fever, ^an3-69-15-11

and they’re also extinguished in this very life. ^an3-69-15-12

These are the three skillful roots.” ^an3-69-16-5

### Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 3.70) ^an3-70-0

So I have heard. ^an3-70-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an3-70-1-2

Then Visākhā, Migāra’s mother, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to her, ^an3-70-1-3

“So, Visākhā, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^an3-70-1-4

“Today, sir, I’m observing the sabbath.” ^an3-70-1-5

“There are, Visākhā, these three sabbaths. ^an3-70-2-1

What three? ^an3-70-2-2

The sabbath of the cowherds, the sabbath of the Jains, and the sabbath of the noble ones. ^an3-70-2-3

And what is the sabbath of the cowherds? ^an3-70-2-4

It’s just like a cowherd who, in the late afternoon, takes the cows back to their owners. They reflect: ^an3-70-2-5

‘Today the cows grazed in this spot and that, and they drank in this spot and that. ^an3-70-2-6

Tomorrow the cows will graze in this spot and that, and drink in this spot and that.’ ^an3-70-2-7

In the same way, someone keeping the sabbath reflects: ^an3-70-2-8

‘Today I ate this and that, and had a meal of this and that. ^an3-70-2-9

Tomorrow I’ll eat this and that, and have a meal of this and that.’ ^an3-70-2-10

And so they spend their day with a mind full of covetousness. ^an3-70-2-11

That’s the sabbath of the cowherds. ^an3-70-2-12

When the cowherd’s sabbath is observed like this it’s not very fruitful or beneficial or dazzling or bountiful. ^an3-70-2-13

And what is the sabbath of the Jains? ^an3-70-3-1

There’s a kind of ascetic belonging to a group called the Jains. ^an3-70-3-2

They encourage their disciple: ^an3-70-3-3

‘Please, my man, don’t hurt any living creatures more than a hundred leagues away to the east. ^an3-70-3-4
