Don’t hurt any living creatures more than a hundred leagues away to the west. ^an3-70-3-5

Don’t hurt any living creatures more than a hundred leagues away to the north. ^an3-70-3-6

Don’t hurt any living creatures more than a hundred leagues away to the south.’ ^an3-70-3-7

So they encourage kindness and sympathy for some creatures and not others. ^an3-70-3-8

On the sabbath, they encourage their disciple: ^an3-70-3-9

‘Please, my man, take off all your clothes and say: ^an3-70-3-10

“I don’t belong to anyone anywhere! And nothing belongs to me anywhere!”’ ^an3-70-3-11

But their mother and father still know, ^an3-70-3-12

‘This is our child.’ ^an3-70-3-13

And they know, ^an3-70-3-14

‘This is my mother and father.’ ^an3-70-3-15

Partner and child still know, ^an3-70-3-16

‘This is our supporter.’ ^an3-70-3-17

And they know, ^an3-70-3-18

‘This is my partner and child.’ ^an3-70-3-19

Bondservants, workers, and staff still know: ^an3-70-3-20

‘This is our master.’ ^an3-70-3-21

And they know, ^an3-70-3-22

‘These are my bondservants, workers, and staff.’ ^an3-70-3-23

So, at a time when they should be encouraged to speak the truth, the Jains encourage them to lie. ^an3-70-3-24

This, I say, is lying. ^an3-70-3-25

When the night has passed they use their possessions once more, though they’ve not been given back to them. ^an3-70-3-26

This, I say, is stealing. ^an3-70-3-27

That’s the sabbath of the Jains. ^an3-70-3-28

When the Jain’s sabbath is observed like this it’s not very fruitful or beneficial or dazzling or bountiful. ^an3-70-3-29

And what is the sabbath of the noble ones? ^an3-70-4-1

A corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-4-2

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-4-3

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the Realized One: ^an3-70-4-4

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an3-70-4-5

As they recollect the Realized One, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. It’s just like cleaning a dirty head by applying effort. ^an3-70-4-6

And how is a dirty head cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-5-1

With cleansing paste, clay, and water, and by applying the appropriate effort. ^an3-70-5-2

In the same way, a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-5-3

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the Realized One: ^an3-70-6-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an3-70-6-3

As they recollect the Realized One, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. ^an3-70-6-4

This is called: ^an3-70-6-5

‘A noble disciple who observes the sabbath of the divinity, living together with the divinity. And because they think of the divinity their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up.’ ^an3-70-6-6

That’s how a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-6-7

A corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-7-1

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-7-2

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the teaching: ^an3-70-7-3

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an3-70-7-4

As they recollect the teaching, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. It’s just like cleaning a dirty body by applying effort. ^an3-70-7-5

And how is a dirty body cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-8-1

With cleanser and powder, water, and by applying the appropriate effort. ^an3-70-8-2

That’s how a dirty body is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-8-3

In the same way, a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-8-4

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-9-1

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the teaching: ^an3-70-9-2

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an3-70-9-3

As they recollect the teaching, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. ^an3-70-9-4

This is called: ‘A noble disciple who observes the sabbath of Dhamma, living together with Dhamma. And because they think of the Dhamma their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up.’ ^an3-70-9-5

That’s how a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-9-6

A corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-10-1

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-10-2

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha: ^an3-70-10-3

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an3-70-10-4

As they recollect the Saṅgha, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. It’s just like cleaning a dirty cloth by applying effort. ^an3-70-10-5

And how is a dirty cloth cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-11-1

With salt, lye, cow dung, and water, and by applying the appropriate effort. ^an3-70-11-2

That’s how a dirty cloth is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-11-3

In the same way, a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-11-4

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-12-1

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha: ^an3-70-12-2

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, and worthy of veneration with cupped palms. It is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an3-70-12-3

As they recollect the Saṅgha, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. ^an3-70-12-4

This is called: ‘A noble disciple who observes the sabbath of the Saṅgha, living together with the Saṅgha. And because they think of the Saṅgha their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up.’ ^an3-70-12-5

That’s how a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-12-6

A corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-13-1

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-13-2

It’s when a noble disciple recollects their own ethical conduct, which is intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an3-70-13-3

As they recollect their ethical conduct, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. It’s just like cleaning a dirty mirror by applying effort. ^an3-70-13-4

And how is a dirty mirror cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-14-1

With oil, ash, a rolled-up cloth, and by applying the appropriate effort. ^an3-70-14-2

That’s how a dirty mirror is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-14-3

In the same way, a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-14-4

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-15-1

It’s when a noble disciple recollects their own ethical conduct, which is intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an3-70-15-2

As they recollect their ethical conduct, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. ^an3-70-15-3

This is called: ‘A noble disciple who observes the sabbath of ethical conduct, living together with ethics. And because they think of their ethical conduct their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up.’ ^an3-70-15-4

That’s how a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-15-5

A corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-16-1

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-16-2

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the deities: ^an3-70-16-3

‘There are the gods of the four great kings, the gods of the thirty-three, the gods of Yama, the joyful gods, the gods who love to create, the gods who control what is created by others, the gods of the Divinity’s host, and gods even higher than these. ^an3-70-16-4

When those deities passed away from here, they were reborn there because of their faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. I, too, have the same kind of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.’ ^an3-70-16-5

As they recollect the faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom of both themselves and those deities, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. It’s just like cleansing corrupt native gold by applying effort. ^an3-70-16-10

And how is corrupt native gold cleansed by applying effort? ^an3-70-17-1

With a furnace, flux, a blowpipe, and tongs, and by applying the appropriate effort. ^an3-70-17-2

That’s how corrupt native gold is cleansed by applying effort. ^an3-70-17-3

In the same way, a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-17-4

And how is a corrupt mind cleaned by applying effort? ^an3-70-18-1

It’s when a noble disciple recollects the deities: ^an3-70-18-2

‘There are the gods of the four great kings, the gods of the thirty-three, the gods of Yama, the joyful gods, the gods who love to imagine, the gods who control what is imagined by others, the gods of the Divinity’s host, and gods even higher than these. ^an3-70-18-3

When those deities passed away from here, they were reborn there because of their faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. I, too, have the same kind of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.’ ^an3-70-18-4

As they recollect the faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom of both themselves and those deities, their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up. ^an3-70-18-9

This is called: ‘A noble disciple who observes the sabbath of the deities, living together with the deities. And because they think of the deities their mind becomes clear, joy arises, and mental corruptions are given up.’ ^an3-70-18-10

That’s how a corrupt mind is cleaned by applying effort. ^an3-70-18-11

Then that noble disciple reflects: ^an3-70-19-1

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an3-70-19-2

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an3-70-19-3

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-19-4

As long as they live, the perfected ones give up stealing. They take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. They keep themselves clean by not thieving. ^an3-70-20-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up stealing. I’ll take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. I’ll keep myself clean by not thieving. ^an3-70-20-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-20-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones give up unchastity. They are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an3-70-21-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up unchastity. I will be chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an3-70-21-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-21-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones give up lying. They speak the truth and stick to the truth. They’re honest and dependable, and don’t trick the world with their words. ^an3-70-22-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up lying. I’ll speak the truth and stick to the truth. I’ll be honest and dependable, and won’t trick the world with my words. ^an3-70-22-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-22-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones give up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an3-70-23-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an3-70-23-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-23-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and from food at the wrong time. ^an3-70-24-1

I, too, for this day and night will eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and food at the wrong time. ^an3-70-24-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-24-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones refrain from seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music; and attiring and adorning themselves with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an3-70-25-1

I, too, for this day and night will refrain from seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music ; and attiring and adorning myself with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an3-70-25-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect. ^an3-70-25-3

As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an3-70-26-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an3-70-26-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an3-70-26-3

That’s the sabbath of the noble ones. ^an3-70-27-1

When the sabbath of the noble ones is observed like this it’s very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an3-70-27-2

How much so? ^an3-70-28-1

Suppose you were to rule as sovereign lord over these sixteen great countries—Aṅga, Magadha, Kāsi, Kosala, Vajji, Malla, Cetī, Vaccha, Kuru, Pañcāla, Maccha, Sūrasena, Assaka, Avanti, Gandhāra, and Kamboja—full of the seven treasures. ^an3-70-28-2

This wouldn’t be worth a sixteenth part of the sabbath with its eight factors. ^an3-70-28-3

Why is that? ^an3-70-28-4

Because human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods. ^an3-70-28-5

Fifty years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the four great kings. ^an3-70-29-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-29-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-29-3

The lifespan of the gods of the four great kings is five hundred of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-29-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an3-70-29-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-29-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-29-7

A hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the thirty-three. ^an3-70-30-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-30-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-30-3

The lifespan of the gods of the thirty-three is a thousand of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-30-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the thirty-three. ^an3-70-30-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-30-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-30-7

Two hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of Yama. ^an3-70-31-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-31-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-31-3

The lifespan of the gods of Yama is two thousand of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-31-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of Yama. ^an3-70-31-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-31-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-31-7

Four hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the joyful gods. ^an3-70-32-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-32-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-32-3

The lifespan of the joyful gods is four thousand of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-32-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the joyful gods. ^an3-70-32-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-32-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-32-7

Eight hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who love to create. ^an3-70-33-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-33-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-33-3

The life span of the gods who love to create is eight thousand of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-33-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who love to create. ^an3-70-33-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-33-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-33-7

Sixteen hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who control what is created by others. ^an3-70-34-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an3-70-34-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an3-70-34-3

The life span of the gods who control what is created by others is sixteen thousand of these heavenly years. ^an3-70-34-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an3-70-34-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an3-70-34-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an3-70-34-7

You shouldn’t kill living creatures, or steal, ^an3-70-35-1

or lie, or drink liquor. ^an3-70-35-2

Be chaste, refraining from sex, ^an3-70-35-3

and don’t eat at night, the wrong time. ^an3-70-35-4

Not wearing garlands or applying fragrance, ^an3-70-36-1

you should sleep on a low bed, <j>or a mat on the ground. ^an3-70-36-2

This is the eight-factored sabbath, they say, ^an3-70-36-3

explained by the Buddha, <j>who has gone to suffering’s end. ^an3-70-36-4

The moon and sun are both fair to see, ^an3-70-37-1

radiating as far as they revolve. ^an3-70-37-2

Those dispellers of darkness, travellers in midair, ^an3-70-37-3

shine in the firmament, lighting up the quarters. ^an3-70-37-4

All of the wealth that’s found in this realm— ^an3-70-38-1

pearls, gems, fine beryl too, ^an3-70-38-2

mountain gold or lustrous gold, ^an3-70-38-3

or the native gold called ‘coruscant’— ^an3-70-38-4

they’re not worth a sixteenth part ^an3-70-39-1

of the sabbath with its eight factors, ^an3-70-39-2

as starlight cannot rival the moon. ^an3-70-39-3

So an ethical woman or man, ^an3-70-40-1

who has observed the eight-factored sabbath, ^an3-70-40-2

having made merit whose outcome is happiness, ^an3-70-40-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an3-70-40-4

### With Channa (Numbered Discourses 3.71) ^an3-71-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an3-71-1-1

Then the wanderer Channa went up to Venerable Ānanda and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-71-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda: ^an3-71-1-3

“Reverend Ānanda, do you advocate giving up greed, hate, and delusion?” ^an3-71-1-4

“We do, reverend.” ^an3-71-1-5

“But what drawbacks have you seen, Reverend Ānanda, that you advocate giving up greed, hate, and delusion?” ^an3-71-2-1

“A greedy person, overcome by greed, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-71-3-1

When greed has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-71-3-2

A greedy person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-71-3-3

When greed has been given up, they don’t do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-71-3-4

A greedy person doesn’t truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-71-3-5

When greed has been given up, they truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-71-3-6

Greed is a destroyer of sight, vision, and knowledge. It blocks wisdom, it’s on the side of distress, and it doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an3-71-3-7

A hateful person, overcome by hate, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. … ^an3-71-4-1

A deluded person, overcome by delusion, intends to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-71-4-2

When delusion has been given up, they don’t intend to hurt themselves, hurt others, and hurt both. They don’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an3-71-4-3

A deluded person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-71-4-4

When delusion has been given up, they don’t do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-71-4-5

A deluded person doesn’t truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-71-4-6

When delusion has been given up, they truly understand what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both. ^an3-71-4-7

Delusion is a destroyer of sight, vision, and knowledge; it blocks wisdom, it’s on the side of distress, and it doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an3-71-4-8

This is the drawback we’ve seen in greed, hate, and delusion, and this is why we advocate giving them up.” ^an3-71-4-9

“But, reverend, is there a path and a practice for giving up that greed, hate, and delusion?” ^an3-71-5-1

“There is, reverend, a path and a practice for giving up that greed, hate, and delusion.” ^an3-71-5-2

“Well, what is it?” ^an3-71-5-3

“It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an3-71-5-4

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an3-71-5-5

This is the path, this is the practice for giving up that greed, hate, and delusion.” ^an3-71-5-6

“This is a fine path, a fine practice, for giving up that greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-71-5-7

Just this much is enough for diligence.” ^an3-71-5-8

### A Disciple of the Ājīvakas (Numbered Discourses 3.72) ^an3-72-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an3-72-1-1

Then a householder who was a disciple of the Ājīvaka ascetics went up to Venerable Ānanda, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to Ānanda: ^an3-72-1-2

“Honorable Ānanda, whose teaching is well explained? ^an3-72-2-1

Who in the world is practicing well? ^an3-72-2-2

Who in the world has done well?” ^an3-72-2-3

“Well then, householder, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an3-72-2-4

What do you think, householder? ^an3-72-2-5

Is the teaching of those who teach for giving up greed, hate, and delusion well explained or not? ^an3-72-2-6

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-72-2-7

“The teaching of those who teach for giving up greed, hate, and delusion is well explained. ^an3-72-2-8

That’s how I see it.” ^an3-72-2-9

“What do you think, householder? ^an3-72-3-1

Are those who practice for giving up greed, hate, and delusion practicing well or not? ^an3-72-3-2

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-72-3-3

“Those who practice for giving up greed, hate, and delusion are practicing well. ^an3-72-3-4

That’s how I see it.” ^an3-72-3-5

“What do you think, householder? ^an3-72-4-1

Have those who’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion—so they’re cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future—done well in the world, or not? ^an3-72-4-2

Or how do you see this?” ^an3-72-4-3

“Those who’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion have done well in the world. ^an3-72-4-4

That’s how I see it.” ^an3-72-4-5

“So, householder, you’ve declared: ^an3-72-5-1

‘The teaching of those who teach for giving up greed, hate, and delusion is well explained.’ ^an3-72-5-2

And you’ve declared: ^an3-72-5-3

‘Those who practice for giving up greed, hate, and delusion are practicing well.’ ^an3-72-5-4

And you’ve declared: ^an3-72-5-5

‘Those who’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion have done well in the world.’” ^an3-72-5-6

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an3-72-6-1

There’s no acclaiming your own teaching or disrespecting someone else’s, ^an3-72-6-2

just teaching what’s relevant in that context. The goal is spoken of, but the self is not involved. ^an3-72-6-3

You, sir, teach Dhamma for giving up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-72-6-4

Your teaching is well explained. ^an3-72-6-5

You, sir, practice for giving up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-72-6-6

You in the world are practicing well. ^an3-72-6-7

You’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-72-6-8

You in the world have done well. ^an3-72-6-9

Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^an3-72-7-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, Master Ānanda has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an3-72-7-2

I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an3-72-7-3

From this day forth, may Master Ānanda remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an3-72-7-4

### With Mahānāma the Sakyan (Numbered Discourses 3.73) ^an3-73-0

So I have heard. ^an3-73-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an3-73-1-2

Now at that time the Buddha had recently recovered from an illness. ^an3-73-1-3

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-73-1-4

“For a long time, sir, I have understood your teaching like this: ^an3-73-1-5

‘Knowledge is for those with immersion, not those without immersion.’ ^an3-73-1-6

But, sir, does immersion come first, then knowledge? ^an3-73-1-7

Or does knowledge come first, then immersion?” ^an3-73-1-8

Then Venerable Ānanda thought, ^an3-73-1-9

“The Buddha has recently recovered from an illness, ^an3-73-1-10

and this Mahānāma asks him a question that’s too deep. ^an3-73-1-11

Why don’t I take him off to one side and teach him the Dhamma?” ^an3-73-1-12

Then Ānanda took Mahānāma by the arm, led him off to one side, and said to him, ^an3-73-2-1

“Mahānāma, the Buddha has spoken of the ethics, immersion, and wisdom of a trainee; and the ethics, immersion, and wisdom of an adept. ^an3-73-2-2

What is the ethics of a trainee? ^an3-73-2-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-73-2-4

This is called the ethics of a trainee. ^an3-73-2-5

And what is the immersion of a trainee? ^an3-73-3-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an3-73-3-2

This is called the immersion of a trainee. ^an3-73-3-3

And what is the wisdom of a trainee? ^an3-73-4-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-73-4-2

This is called the wisdom of a trainee. ^an3-73-4-3

Then a noble disciple—accomplished in ethics, immersion, and wisdom—realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-73-5-1

In this way the Buddha has spoken of the ethics, immersion, and wisdom of both a trainee and an adept.” ^an3-73-5-2

### Jains (Numbered Discourses 3.74) ^an3-74-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an3-74-1-1

Then the Licchavis Abhaya and Paṇḍitakumāra went up to Venerable Ānanda, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-74-1-2

“Sir, the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan claims to be all-knowing and all-seeing, to know and see everything without exception, thus: ^an3-74-1-3

‘Knowledge and vision are constantly and continually present to me, while walking, standing, sleeping, and waking.’ ^an3-74-1-4

He advocates the elimination of past deeds by fervent mortification, and breaking the bridge by not doing any new deeds. ^an3-74-1-5

So with the ending of deeds, suffering ends; with the ending of suffering, feeling ends; and with the ending of feeling, all suffering will have been worn away. ^an3-74-1-6

This is how to go beyond suffering by means of this purification by wearing away in the present life. ^an3-74-1-7

What, sir, does the Buddha say about this?” ^an3-74-1-8

“Abhaya, these three kinds of purification by wearing away have been rightly explained by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment. ^an3-74-2-1

What three? ^an3-74-2-2

It’s when, Abhaya, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-74-2-3

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an3-74-2-4

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-74-2-5

Then a mendicant accomplished in ethics, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an3-74-3-1

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an3-74-3-2

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-74-3-3

Then a mendicant accomplished in immersion realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-74-4-1

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an3-74-4-2

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an3-74-4-3

These are the three kinds of purification by wearing away that have been rightly explained by the Buddha … in order to realize extinguishment.” ^an3-74-4-4

When he said this, Paṇḍitakumāra said to Abhaya, ^an3-74-5-1

“Dear Abhaya, is there anything in what Ānanda has said so well that you would disagree with?” ^an3-74-5-2

“How could I not agree with what was said so well by Ānanda? ^an3-74-5-3

If anyone didn’t agree with him, their head would explode!” ^an3-74-5-4

### Support (Numbered Discourses 3.75) ^an3-75-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an3-75-1-1

“Ānanda, those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established in three things. ^an3-75-2-1

What three? ^an3-75-2-2

Experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^an3-75-2-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an3-75-2-4

Experiential confidence in the teaching: ^an3-75-2-5

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an3-75-2-6

Experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^an3-75-2-7

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an3-75-2-8

There might be change in the four principal states— ^an3-75-3-1

earth, water, air, and fire—but a noble disciple with experiential confidence in the Buddha would never change. ^an3-75-3-2

In this context, ‘change’ means that such a noble disciple will be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm: this is quite impossible. ^an3-75-3-3

There might be change in the four principal states— ^an3-75-4-1

earth, water, air, and fire—but a noble disciple with experiential confidence in the teaching … ^an3-75-4-2

or the Saṅgha would never change. ^an3-75-4-3

In this context, ‘change’ means that such a noble disciple will be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm: this is quite impossible. ^an3-75-4-4

Those who you have sympathy for, and those worth listening to—friends and colleagues, relatives and family—should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in these three things.” ^an3-75-5-1

### Continued Existence (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.76) ^an3-76-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-76-1-1

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘continued existence’. ^an3-76-1-2

How is continued existence defined?” ^an3-76-1-3

“If, Ānanda, there were no deeds to result in the sensual realm, would continued existence in the sensual realm still come about?” ^an3-76-2-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-76-2-2

“So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-76-2-3

The consciousness of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a lower realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-76-2-4

If there were no deeds to result in the realm of luminous form, would continued existence in the realm of luminous form still come about?” ^an3-76-3-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-76-3-2

“So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-76-3-3

The consciousness of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a middle realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-76-3-4

If there were no deeds to result in the formless realm, would continued existence in the formless realm still come about?” ^an3-76-4-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-76-4-2

“So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-76-4-3

The consciousness of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a higher realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-76-4-4

That’s how continued existence is defined.” ^an3-76-4-5

### Continued Existence (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.77) ^an3-77-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-77-1-1

“Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘continued existence’. ^an3-77-1-2

How is continued existence defined?” ^an3-77-1-3

“If, Ānanda, there were no deeds to result in the sensual realm, would continued existence in the sensual realm still come about?” ^an3-77-2-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-77-2-2

“So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-77-2-3

The intention and aim of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a lower realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-77-2-4

If there were no deeds to result in the realm of luminous form, would continued existence in the realm of luminous form still come about?” ^an3-77-3-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-77-3-2

“So, Ānanda, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-77-3-3

The intention and aim of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a middle realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-77-3-4

If there were no deeds to result in the formless realm, would continued existence in the formless realm still come about?” ^an3-77-4-1

“No, sir.” ^an3-77-4-2

“So, deeds are the field, consciousness is the seed, and craving is the moisture. ^an3-77-4-3

The intention and aim of sentient beings—shrouded by ignorance and fettered by craving—is established in a higher realm. That’s how there is regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. ^an3-77-4-4

That’s how continued existence is defined.” ^an3-77-4-5

### Precepts and Observances (Numbered Discourses 3.78) ^an3-78-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an3-78-1-1

“Ānanda, are all precepts and observances, lifestyles, and spiritual paths fruitful when taken as the essence?” ^an3-78-1-2

“This matter is not categorical, sir.” ^an3-78-1-3

“Well then, Ānanda, break it down.” ^an3-78-1-4

“Take the case of someone who cultivates precepts and observances, a lifestyle, and a spiritual path, taking this as the essence. If unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline, that’s not fruitful. ^an3-78-2-1

However, if unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow, that is fruitful.” ^an3-78-2-2

That’s what Ānanda said, ^an3-78-2-3

and the teacher approved. ^an3-78-2-4

Then Ānanda, knowing that the teacher approved, rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an3-78-3-1

Then, not long after Ānanda had left, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an3-78-3-2

“Mendicants, Ānanda is a trainee, ^an3-78-3-3

but it’s not easy to find his equal in wisdom.” ^an3-78-3-4

### Fragrances (Numbered Discourses 3.79) ^an3-79-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-79-1-1

“Sir, there are these three kinds of fragrance that spread only with the wind, not against it. ^an3-79-2-1

What three? ^an3-79-2-2

The fragrance of roots, heartwood, and flowers. ^an3-79-2-3

These are the three kinds of fragrance that spread only with the wind, not against it. ^an3-79-2-4

Is there a kind of fragrance that spreads with the wind, and against it, and both ways?” ^an3-79-2-5

“There is, Ānanda, such a kind of fragrance.” ^an3-79-3-1

“So what, sir, is that kind of fragrance?” ^an3-79-3-2

“It’s when, Ānanda, in some village or town, a woman or man has gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an3-79-4-1

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. They’re ethical, of good character. ^an3-79-4-2

They live at home with a heart rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an3-79-4-3

Ascetics and brahmins everywhere praise them for these good qualities; ^an3-79-5-1

even the deities praise them. ^an3-79-6-1

This is the kind of fragrance that spreads with the wind, and against it, and both. ^an3-79-6-5

The fragrance of flowers <j>doesn’t spread against the wind, ^an3-79-7-1

nor sandalwood, pinwheel flowers, or jasmine; ^an3-79-7-2

but the fragrance of the good <j>spreads against the wind; ^an3-79-7-3

a true person’s virtue spreads in every direction.” ^an3-79-7-4

### Lesser (Numbered Discourses 3.80) ^an3-80-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-80-1-1

“Sir, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an3-80-1-2

‘Ānanda, the Buddha Sikhī had a disciple called Abhibhū. Standing in the realm of divinity, he could make his voice heard throughout the thousandfold galaxy.’ ^an3-80-1-3

I wonder how far a Blessed One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha can make their voice heard?” ^an3-80-1-4

“He was a disciple, Ānanda. Realized Ones are immeasurable.” ^an3-80-1-5

For a second time … ^an3-80-2-1

For a third time, Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^an3-80-3-1

“… I wonder how far a Blessed One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha can make their voice heard?” ^an3-80-3-4

“Ānanda, have you heard of a thousandfold lesser world system, a galaxy?” ^an3-80-3-5

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^an3-80-3-6

Let the Buddha speak. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an3-80-3-7

“Well then, Ānanda, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-80-3-8

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an3-80-3-9

The Buddha said this: ^an3-80-3-10

“Ānanda, a galaxy extends a thousand times as far as the moon and sun revolve and the shining ones light up the quarters. ^an3-80-4-1

In that galaxy there are a thousand moons, a thousand suns, a thousand Sinerus king of mountains, a thousand Black Plum Tree Lands, a thousand lands west of Godāvarī, a thousand lands north of Kuru, a thousand lands east of Videha, four thousand oceans, four thousand great kings, a thousand realms of the gods of the four great kings, a thousand realms of the gods of the thirty-three, of the gods of Yama, of the joyful gods, of the gods who love to create, of the gods who control what is created by others, and a thousand realms of divinity. ^an3-80-4-2

This is called a thousandfold lesser world system, a ‘galaxy’. ^an3-80-4-3

A world system that extends for a thousand galaxies ^an3-80-5-1

is called a millionfold middling world system, a ‘galactic cluster’. ^an3-80-5-2

A world system that extends for a thousand galactic clusters ^an3-80-6-1

is called a billionfold great world system, a ‘galactic supercluster’. ^an3-80-6-2

If he wished, Ānanda, a Realized One could make his voice heard throughout a galactic supercluster, or as far as he wants.” ^an3-80-7-1

“But how would the Buddha make his voice heard so far?” ^an3-80-8-1

“First, Ānanda, a Realized One would fill the galactic supercluster with light. ^an3-80-8-2

When sentient beings saw the light, the Realized One would project his call so that they’d hear the sound. ^an3-80-8-3

That’s how a Realized One could make his voice heard throughout a galactic supercluster, or as far as he wants.” ^an3-80-8-4

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said, ^an3-80-9-1

“I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate, ^an3-80-9-2

to have a teacher with such power and might!” ^an3-80-9-3

When he said this, Venerable Udāyī said to Venerable Ānanda, ^an3-80-9-4

“What is it to you, Reverend Ānanda, if your teacher has such power and might?” ^an3-80-9-5

When he said this, the Buddha said to Venerable Udāyī, ^an3-80-9-6

“Not so, Udāyī, not so! ^an3-80-9-7

If Ānanda were to die while still not free of greed, he would rule as king of the gods for seven lifetimes, or as king of the Black Plum Tree Land for seven lifetimes, because of the confidence of his heart. ^an3-80-9-8

However, Ānanda will be fully extinguished in this very life.” ^an3-80-9-9

### Ascetics (Numbered Discourses 3.81) ^an3-81-0

“Mendicants, there are three duties of an ascetic. ^an3-81-1-1

What three? ^an3-81-1-2

Undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-81-1-3

These are the three duties of an ascetic. ^an3-81-1-4

So you should train like this: ^an3-81-2-1

‘We will have keen enthusiasm for undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom.’ ^an3-81-2-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-81-2-3

### The Donkey (Numbered Discourses 3.82) ^an3-82-0

“Suppose, mendicants, a donkey followed behind a herd of cattle, thinking: ^an3-82-1-1

‘I can moo too! I can moo too!’ ^an3-82-1-2

But it doesn’t look like a cow, or sound like a cow, or leave a footprint like a cow. ^an3-82-1-3

Still it follows behind a herd of cattle, thinking: ^an3-82-1-4

‘I can moo too! I can moo too!’ ^an3-82-1-5

In the same way, some mendicant follows behind the mendicant Saṅgha, thinking: ^an3-82-2-1

‘I’m a monk too! I’m a monk too!’ ^an3-82-2-2

But they don’t have the same enthusiasm for undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom as the other mendicants. ^an3-82-2-3

Still they follow behind the mendicant Saṇgha, thinking: ^an3-82-2-4

‘I’m a monk too! I’m a monk too!’ ^an3-82-2-5

So you should train like this: ^an3-82-3-1

‘We will have keen enthusiasm for undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom.’ ^an3-82-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-82-3-3

### Fields (Numbered Discourses 3.83) ^an3-83-0

“Mendicants, a farmer has three primary duties. ^an3-83-1-1

What three? ^an3-83-1-2

A farmer first of all makes sure the field is well ploughed and tilled. ^an3-83-1-3

Next they plant seeds in season. ^an3-83-1-4

When the time is right, they irrigate the field and then drain it. ^an3-83-1-5

These are the three primary duties of a farmer. ^an3-83-1-6

In the same way, a mendicant has three primary duties. ^an3-83-2-1

What three? ^an3-83-2-2

Undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-83-2-3

These are the three primary duties of a mendicant. ^an3-83-2-4

So you should train like this: ^an3-83-3-1

‘We will have keen enthusiasm for undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom.’ ^an3-83-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-83-3-3

### The Vajji (Numbered Discourses 3.84) ^an3-84-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an3-84-1-1

Then a certain Vajji monk went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-84-1-2

“Sir, each fortnight over a hundred and fifty training rules are recited. ^an3-84-1-3

I’m not able to train in them.” ^an3-84-1-4

“But monk, are you able to train in three trainings: ^an3-84-1-5

the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom?” ^an3-84-1-6

“I am, sir.” ^an3-84-1-7

“So, monk, you should train in these three trainings: ^an3-84-1-9

the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-84-1-10

As you train in these, you will give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-84-2-1

Then you won’t do anything unskillful, or practice anything bad.” ^an3-84-2-2

After some time that monk trained in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-84-3-1

He gave up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an3-84-3-2

Then he didn’t do anything unskillful, or practice anything bad. ^an3-84-3-3

### A Trainee (Numbered Discourses 3.85) ^an3-85-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-85-1-1

“Sir, they speak of the one called ‘a trainee’. ^an3-85-2-1

How is a trainee defined?” ^an3-85-2-2

“They train, mendicant, that’s why they’re called ‘a trainee’. ^an3-85-2-3

What is their training? ^an3-85-2-4

They train in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-85-2-5

They train, that’s why they’re called ‘a trainee’. ^an3-85-2-6

As a trainee trains, ^an3-85-3-1

following the straight road, ^an3-85-3-2

first they know about ending; ^an3-85-3-3

enlightenment follows in the same lifetime. ^an3-85-3-4

Then the knowledge comes ^an3-85-4-1

such a one, freed through enlightenment, ^an3-85-4-2

with the end of the fetters of continued existence: ^an3-85-4-3

‘My freedom is unshakable.’” ^an3-85-4-4

### Training (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.86) ^an3-86-0

“Mendicants, each fortnight over a hundred and fifty training rules come up for recitation, in which gentlemen who desire self-knowledge train. ^an3-86-1-1

These are all included in the three trainings. ^an3-86-1-2

What three? ^an3-86-1-3

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-86-1-4

These are the three trainings that include them all. ^an3-86-1-5

Take the case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an3-86-2-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-86-2-2

Why is that? ^an3-86-2-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-86-2-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-86-2-5

With the ending of three fetters they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^an3-86-2-6

Take another case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an3-86-3-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-86-3-2

Why is that? ^an3-86-3-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-86-3-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-86-3-5

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an3-86-3-6

Take another case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics and immersion, but has limited wisdom. ^an3-86-4-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-86-4-2

Why is that? ^an3-86-4-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-86-4-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-86-4-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an3-86-4-6

Take another case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-86-5-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-86-5-2

Why is that? ^an3-86-5-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-86-5-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-86-5-5

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-86-5-6

So, mendicants, if you practice partially you succeed partially. If you practice fully you succeed fully. ^an3-86-6-1

But in any case, these training rules are not barren, I say.” ^an3-86-6-2

### Training (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.87) ^an3-87-0

“Mendicants, each fortnight over a hundred and fifty training rules come up for recitation, in which gentlemen who desire self-knowledge train. ^an3-87-1-1

These are all included in the three trainings. ^an3-87-1-2

What three? ^an3-87-1-3

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-87-1-4

These are the three trainings that include them all. ^an3-87-1-5

Take the case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an3-87-2-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-87-2-2

Why is that? ^an3-87-2-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-87-2-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual life. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-87-2-5

With the ending of three fetters they have at most seven rebirths. ^an3-87-2-6

They will transmigrate at most seven times among gods and humans and then make an end of suffering. ^an3-87-2-7

With the ending of three fetters, they go from family to family. They will transmigrate between two or three families and then make an end of suffering. ^an3-87-2-8

With the ending of three fetters, they’re a one-seeder. They will be reborn just one time in a human existence, then make an end of suffering. ^an3-87-2-9

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an3-87-2-10

Take another case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics and immersion, but has limited wisdom. ^an3-87-3-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-87-3-2

Why is that? ^an3-87-3-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-87-3-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-87-3-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an3-87-3-6

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^an3-87-3-7

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^an3-87-3-8

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. ^an3-87-3-9

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an3-87-3-10

Take another case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-87-4-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-87-4-2

Why is that? ^an3-87-4-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-87-4-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-87-4-5

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-87-4-6

So, mendicants, if you practice partially you succeed partially. If you practice fully you succeed fully. But in any case, these training rules are not barren, I say.” ^an3-87-5-1

### Training (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 3.88) ^an3-88-0

“Mendicants, each fortnight over a hundred and fifty training rules come up for recitation, in which gentlemen who desire self-knowledge train. ^an3-88-1-1

These are all included in the three trainings. ^an3-88-1-2

What three? ^an3-88-1-3

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-88-1-4

These are the three trainings that include them all. ^an3-88-1-5

Take the case of a mendicant who has fulfilled their ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-88-2-1

They break some lesser and minor training rules, but are restored. ^an3-88-2-2

Why is that? ^an3-88-2-3

Because I don’t say they’re incapable of that. ^an3-88-2-4

But they’re constant and steady in their precepts regarding the training rules that are fundamental, befitting the spiritual path. They keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-88-2-5

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-88-2-6

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an3-88-2-7

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. ^an3-88-2-8

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^an3-88-2-9

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^an3-88-2-10

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an3-88-2-11

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an3-88-2-12

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of three fetters, they’re a one-seeder. They will be reborn just one time in a human existence, then make an end of suffering. ^an3-88-2-13

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of three fetters, they go from family to family. They will transmigrate between two or three families and then make an end of suffering. ^an3-88-2-14

If they don’t penetrate so far, with the ending of three fetters, they have at most seven rebirths. They will transmigrate at most seven times among gods and humans and then make an end of suffering. ^an3-88-2-15

So, mendicants, if you practice fully you succeed fully. If you practice partially you succeed partially. ^an3-88-3-1

But in any case, these training rules are not barren, I say.” ^an3-88-3-2

### Three Trainings (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.89) ^an3-89-0

“Mendicants, these are the three trainings. ^an3-89-1-1

What three? ^an3-89-1-2

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-89-1-3

And what is the training in the higher ethics? ^an3-89-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-89-2-2

This is called the training in the higher ethics. ^an3-89-2-3

And what is the training in the higher mind? ^an3-89-3-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an3-89-3-2

This is called the training in the higher mind. ^an3-89-3-3

And what is the training in the higher wisdom? ^an3-89-4-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-89-4-2

This is called the training in the higher wisdom. ^an3-89-4-3

These are the three trainings.” ^an3-89-4-4

### Three Trainings (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.90) ^an3-90-0

“Mendicants, these are the three trainings. ^an3-90-1-1

What three? ^an3-90-1-2

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-90-1-3

And what is the training in the higher ethics? ^an3-90-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-90-2-2

This is called the training in the higher ethics. ^an3-90-2-3

And what is the training in the higher mind? ^an3-90-3-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an3-90-3-2

This is called the training in the higher mind. ^an3-90-3-3

And what is the training in the higher wisdom? ^an3-90-4-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-90-4-2

This is called the training in the higher wisdom. ^an3-90-4-3

These are the three trainings. ^an3-90-4-4

The higher ethics, the higher mind, ^an3-90-5-1

and the higher wisdom should be practiced ^an3-90-5-2

by those energetic, steadfast, and resolute, ^an3-90-5-3

practicing absorption, mindful, with guarded senses. ^an3-90-5-4

As before, so behind; ^an3-90-6-1

as behind, so before. ^an3-90-6-2

As below, so above; ^an3-90-6-3

as above, so below. ^an3-90-6-4

As by day, so by night; ^an3-90-7-1

as by night, so by day. ^an3-90-7-2

Having mastered every direction ^an3-90-7-3

with limitless immersion, ^an3-90-7-4

they call them a ‘trainee on the path’, ^an3-90-8-1

and ‘one living a pure life’. ^an3-90-8-2

But a wise one who has gone to the end of the path ^an3-90-8-3

they call a ‘Buddha’ in the world. ^an3-90-8-4

With the cessation of consciousness, ^an3-90-9-1

freed by the ending of craving, ^an3-90-9-2

the liberation of their heart ^an3-90-9-3

is like a lamp going out.” ^an3-90-9-4

### At Paṅkadhā (Numbered Discourses 3.91) ^an3-91-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. He arrived at a town of the Kosalans named Paṅkadhā, ^an3-91-1-1

and stayed there. ^an3-91-1-2

Now, at that time a monk named Kassapagotta was resident at Paṅkadhā. ^an3-91-1-3

There the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about the training rules. ^an3-91-1-4

Kassapagotta became quite impatient and bitter, thinking, ^an3-91-1-5

“This ascetic is much too strict.” ^an3-91-1-6

When the Buddha had stayed in Paṅkadhā as long as he pleased, he set out for Rājagaha. ^an3-91-1-7

Traveling stage by stage, he arrived at Rājagaha, ^an3-91-1-8

and stayed there. ^an3-91-1-9

Soon after the Buddha left, Kassapagotta became quite remorseful and regretful, thinking, ^an3-91-2-1

“It’s my loss, my misfortune, ^an3-91-2-2

that when the Buddha was talking about the training rules I became quite impatient and bitter, thinking ^an3-91-2-3

he was much too strict. ^an3-91-2-4

Why don’t I go to the Buddha and confess my mistake to him?” ^an3-91-2-5

Then Kassapagotta set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, set out for Rājagaha. ^an3-91-2-6

Eventually he came to Rājagaha and the Vulture’s Peak. He went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened, saying: ^an3-91-2-7

“I made a mistake, sir. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of me to become impatient and bitter when the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk about the training rules, and to think, ^an3-91-3-11

‘This ascetic is much too strict.’ ^an3-91-3-12

Please, sir, accept my mistake for what it is, so I will restrain myself in future.” ^an3-91-3-13

“Indeed, Kassapa, you made a mistake. ^an3-91-4-1

But since you have recognized your mistake for what it is, and have dealt with it properly, I accept it. ^an3-91-4-3

For it is growth in the training of the Noble One to recognize a mistake for what it is, deal with it properly, and commit to restraint in the future. ^an3-91-4-4

Kassapa, take the case of a senior mendicant who doesn’t want to train and doesn’t praise taking up the training. They don’t encourage other mendicants who don’t want to train to take up the training. And they don’t truthfully and correctly praise at the right time those mendicants who do want to train. I don’t praise that kind of senior mendicant. ^an3-91-5-1

Why is that? ^an3-91-5-2

Because, hearing that I praised that mendicant, other mendicants might want to keep company with them. Then they might follow their example, which would be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an3-91-5-3

That’s why I don’t praise that kind of senior mendicant. ^an3-91-5-4

Take the case of a middle mendicant who doesn’t want to train … ^an3-91-6-1

Take the case of a junior mendicant who doesn’t want to train … ^an3-91-6-2

That’s why I don’t praise that kind of junior mendicant. ^an3-91-6-5

Kassapa, take the case of a senior mendicant who does want to train and praises taking up the training. They encourage other mendicants who don’t want to train to take up the training. And they truthfully and correctly praise at the right time those mendicants who do want to train. I praise that kind of senior mendicant. ^an3-91-7-1

Why is that? ^an3-91-7-2

Because, hearing that I praised that mendicant, other mendicants might want to keep company with them. Then they might follow their example, which would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an3-91-7-3

That’s why I praise that kind of senior mendicant. ^an3-91-7-4

Take the case of a middle mendicant who wants to train … ^an3-91-8-1

Take the case of a junior mendicant who wants to train … ^an3-91-8-2

That’s why I praise that kind of junior mendicant.” ^an3-91-8-5

### Urgent (Numbered Discourses 3.92) ^an3-92-0

“Mendicants, a farmer has three urgent duties. ^an3-92-1-1

What three? ^an3-92-1-2

A farmer swiftly makes sure the field is well ploughed and tilled. ^an3-92-1-3

Next they swiftly plant seeds in season. ^an3-92-1-4

When the time is right, they swiftly irrigate or drain the field. ^an3-92-1-5

These are the three urgent duties of a farmer. ^an3-92-1-6

That farmer has no special power or ability to say: ^an3-92-1-7

‘Let the crops germinate today! Let them flower tomorrow! Let them ripen the day after!’ ^an3-92-1-8

But there comes a time when that farmer’s crops germinate, flower, and ripen as the seasons change. ^an3-92-1-9

In the same way, a mendicant has three urgent duties. ^an3-92-2-1

What three? ^an3-92-2-2

Undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an3-92-2-3

These are the three urgent duties of a mendicant. ^an3-92-2-4

That mendicant has no special power or ability to say: ^an3-92-2-5

‘Let my mind be freed from defilements by not grasping today! Or tomorrow! Or the day after!’ ^an3-92-2-6

But there comes a time—as that mendicant trains in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom—that their mind is freed from defilements by not grasping. ^an3-92-2-7

So you should train like this: ^an3-92-3-1

‘We will have keen enthusiasm for undertaking the training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom.’ ^an3-92-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-92-3-3

### Seclusion (Numbered Discourses 3.93) ^an3-93-0

“Mendicants, wanderers of other religions advocate three kinds of seclusion. ^an3-93-1-1

What three? ^an3-93-1-2

Seclusion in robes, almsfood, and lodgings. ^an3-93-1-3

Wanderers of other religions advocate this kind of seclusion in robes. They wear robes of sunn hemp, mixed hemp, corpse-wrapping cloth, rags, lodh tree bark, antelope hide (whole or in strips), kusa grass, bark, wood-chips, human hair, horse-tail hair, or owls’ wings. ^an3-93-2-1

This is what wanderers of other religions advocate for seclusion in robes. ^an3-93-2-2

Wanderers of other religions advocate this kind of seclusion in almsfood. ^an3-93-3-1

They eat herbs, millet, wild rice, poor rice, water lettuce, rice bran, scum from boiling rice, sesame flour, grass, or cow dung. They survive on forest roots and fruits, or eating fallen fruit. ^an3-93-3-2

This is what the wanderers of other religions advocate for seclusion in almsfood. ^an3-93-3-3

Wanderers of other religions advocate this kind of seclusion in lodgings. They stay in a wilderness, at the root of a tree, in a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw, or a threshing-hut. ^an3-93-4-1

This is what wanderers of other religions advocate for seclusion in lodgings. ^an3-93-4-2

These are the three kinds of seclusion that wanderers of other religions advocate. ^an3-93-4-3

In this teaching and training, there are three kinds of seclusion for a mendicant. ^an3-93-5-1

What three? ^an3-93-5-2

Firstly, a mendicant is ethical, giving up unethical conduct, being secluded from it. ^an3-93-5-3

They have right view, giving up wrong view, being secluded from it. ^an3-93-5-4

They’ve ended defilements, giving up defilements, being secluded from them. ^an3-93-5-5

When a mendicant has these three kinds of seclusion, ^an3-93-5-6

they’re called a mendicant who has reached the peak and the pith, being pure and consolidated in the core. ^an3-93-5-9

When a farmer’s rice field is ripe, ^an3-93-6-1

they’d have the rice cut swiftly, ^an3-93-6-2

gathered swiftly, ^an3-93-6-3

transported swiftly, ^an3-93-6-4

made into heaps swiftly, ^an3-93-6-5

threshed swiftly, ^an3-93-6-6

the straw removed swiftly, ^an3-93-6-7

winnowed swiftly, ^an3-93-6-9

brought over swiftly, ^an3-93-6-10

pounded swiftly, ^an3-93-6-11

and have the husks removed swiftly. ^an3-93-6-12

In this way that farmer’s crop would reach the peak and the pith, being pure and consolidated in the core. ^an3-93-6-13

In the same way, when a mendicant is ethical, giving up unethical conduct, being secluded from it; ^an3-93-7-1

when they have right view, giving up wrong view, being secluded from it; ^an3-93-7-2

when they’ve ended defilements, giving up defilements, being secluded from them: ^an3-93-7-3

they’re called a mendicant who has reached the peak and the pith, being pure and consolidated in the core.” ^an3-93-7-4

### Autumn (Numbered Discourses 3.94) ^an3-94-0

“In the autumn, the heavens are sunny and cloudless. And as the sun is rising to the firmament, having dispelled all the darkness of space, it shines and glows and radiates. ^an3-94-1-1

In the same way, when the stainless, immaculate vision of the teaching arises in a noble disciple, three fetters are given up: ^an3-94-2-1

substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an3-94-2-2

Afterwards they get rid of two things: covetousness and ill will. ^an3-94-3-1

Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, they enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an3-94-3-2

If that noble disciple passed away at that time, they’re bound by no fetter that might return them to this world.” ^an3-94-3-3

### Assemblies (Numbered Discourses 3.95) ^an3-95-0

“Mendicants, these are the three assemblies. ^an3-95-1-1

What three? ^an3-95-1-2

An assembly of the best, a divided assembly, and a harmonious assembly. ^an3-95-1-3

And what is an assembly of the best? ^an3-95-2-1

An assembly where the senior mendicants are not indulgent or slack, nor are they backsliders. Instead, they take the lead in seclusion, rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. And those who come afterwards follow their example. ^an3-95-2-2

They too are not indulgent or slack, nor are they backsliders. Instead, they take the lead in seclusion, rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an3-95-2-3

This is called an assembly of the best. ^an3-95-2-4

And what is a divided assembly? ^an3-95-3-1

An assembly where the mendicants fight, quarrel, and dispute, continually wounding each other with barbed words. This is called a divided assembly. ^an3-95-3-2

And what is a harmonious assembly? ^an3-95-4-1

An assembly where the mendicants live in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, blending like milk and water, and regarding each other with kindly eyes. This is called a harmonious assembly. ^an3-95-4-2

When the mendicants live in harmony like this, they brim with much merit. ^an3-95-5-1

At that time the mendicants live in a divine dwelling, that is, the heart’s release by rejoicing. ^an3-95-5-2

When they’re joyful, rapture springs up. When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. When the body is tranquil, they feel bliss. And when they’re blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an3-95-5-3

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an3-95-6-1

In the same way, when the mendicants are in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, blending like milk and water, and regarding each other with kindly eyes, they create much merit. ^an3-95-7-1

At that time the mendicants live in a divine dwelling, that is, the heart’s release by rejoicing. ^an3-95-7-2

When they’re joyful, rapture springs up. When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. When the body is tranquil, they feel bliss. And when they’re blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an3-95-7-3

These are the three assemblies.” ^an3-95-7-4

### The Thoroughbred (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.96) ^an3-96-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned as a factor of kingship. ^an3-96-1-1

What three? ^an3-96-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-96-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these three factors is worthy of a king. … ^an3-96-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an3-96-1-5

What three? ^an3-96-1-6

It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-96-1-7

And how is a mendicant beautiful? ^an3-96-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-96-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is beautiful. ^an3-96-2-3

And how is a mendicant strong? ^an3-96-3-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an3-96-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is strong. ^an3-96-3-3

And how is a mendicant fast? ^an3-96-4-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an3-96-4-2

‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ^an3-96-4-3

‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ^an3-96-4-4

‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-96-4-5

That’s how a mendicant is fast. ^an3-96-4-6

A mendicant with these three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an3-96-4-7

### The Thoroughbred (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.97) ^an3-97-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and considered a factor of kingship. ^an3-97-1-1

What three? ^an3-97-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-97-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these three factors is worthy of a king, … ^an3-97-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an3-97-1-5

What three? ^an3-97-1-6

It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-97-1-7

And how is a mendicant beautiful? ^an3-97-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-97-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is beautiful. ^an3-97-2-3

And how is a mendicant strong? ^an3-97-3-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an3-97-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is strong. ^an3-97-3-3

And how is a mendicant fast? ^an3-97-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an3-97-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is fast. ^an3-97-4-3

A mendicant with these three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an3-97-4-4

### The Thoroughbred (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 3.98) ^an3-98-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and considered a factor of kingship. ^an3-98-1-1

What three? ^an3-98-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-98-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these three factors is worthy of a king. … ^an3-98-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an3-98-1-5

What three? ^an3-98-1-6

It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, and fast. ^an3-98-1-7

And how is a mendicant beautiful? ^an3-98-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an3-98-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is beautiful. ^an3-98-2-3

And how is a mendicant strong? ^an3-98-3-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an3-98-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is strong. ^an3-98-3-3

And how is a mendicant fast? ^an3-98-4-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-98-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is fast. ^an3-98-4-3

A mendicant with these three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an3-98-4-4

### Jute (Numbered Discourses 3.99) ^an3-99-0

“Jute canvas is ugly, unpleasant to touch, and worthless whether it’s new, worn in, or worn out. ^an3-99-1-1
