They use worn out jute canvas for scrubbing cauldrons, or else they just throw it away on the rubbish heap. ^an3-99-1-4

In the same way, if a junior mendicant is unethical, of bad character, ^an3-99-2-1

this is how they’re ugly, I say. ^an3-99-2-2

That individual is just as ugly as jute canvas. ^an3-99-2-3

If you associate with, accompany, and attend to them, following their example, it’ll be for your lasting harm and suffering. ^an3-99-2-4

This is how they’re unpleasant to touch, I say. ^an3-99-2-5

That individual is just as unpleasant to touch as jute canvas. ^an3-99-2-6

Any robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick that they receive are not very fruitful or beneficial for the donor. ^an3-99-2-7

This is how they’re worthless, I say. ^an3-99-2-8

That individual is just as worthless as jute canvas. ^an3-99-2-9

If a middle mendicant is unethical, of bad character, this is how they’re ugly, I say. … ^an3-99-2-10

If a senior mendicant is unethical, of bad character, this is how they’re ugly, I say. … ^an3-99-2-11

If you associate with, accompany, and attend to them, following their example, it’ll be for your lasting harm and suffering. … ^an3-99-2-13

If such a senior mendicant speaks among the Saṅgha, ^an3-99-3-1

the mendicants say: ^an3-99-3-2

‘What’s an incompetent fool like you got to say? ^an3-99-3-3

How on earth could you imagine you’ve got something worth saying!’ ^an3-99-3-4

They become angry and upset, and blurt out things that make the Saṅgha throw them out, as if they were throwing jute canvas away on the rubbish heap. ^an3-99-3-5

Cloth from Kāsi is beautiful, pleasant to touch, and valuable whether it’s new, worn in, or worn out. ^an3-99-4-1

They use worn out cloth from Kāsi for wrapping, or else they place it in a fragrant casket. ^an3-99-4-2

In the same way, if a junior mendicant is ethical, of good character, this is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-99-5-1

That individual is just as beautiful as cloth from Kāsi. ^an3-99-5-2

If you associate with, accompany, and attend to such an individual, following their example, it will be for your lasting welfare and happiness. ^an3-99-5-3

This is how they’re pleasant to touch, I say. ^an3-99-5-4

That individual is just as pleasant to touch as cloth from Kāsi. ^an3-99-5-5

Any robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick that they receive are very fruitful and beneficial for the donor. ^an3-99-5-6

This is how they’re valuable, I say. ^an3-99-5-7

That individual is just as valuable as cloth from Kāsi. ^an3-99-5-8

If a middle mendicant is ethical, of good character, this is how they’re beautiful, I say. … ^an3-99-5-9

If a senior mendicant is ethical, of good character, this is how they’re beautiful, I say. … ^an3-99-5-10

If such a senior mendicant speaks in the midst of the Saṅgha, ^an3-99-6-1

the mendicants say: ^an3-99-6-2

‘Venerables, be quiet! The senior mendicant is speaking on the teaching and training.’ ^an3-99-6-3

So you should train like this: ^an3-99-6-4

‘We will be like cloth from Kāsi, not like jute canvas.’ ^an3-99-6-5

That’s how you should train.” ^an3-99-6-6

### A Lump of Salt (Numbered Discourses 3.100) ^an3-100-0

“Mendicants, suppose you say: ^an3-100-1-1

‘No matter how this person performs a deed, they experience it the same way.’ This being so, the spiritual life could not be lived, and there’d be no chance of making a complete end of suffering. ^an3-100-1-2

Suppose you say: ^an3-100-1-3

‘No matter how this person performs a deed, they experience the result as it should be experienced.’ This being so, the spiritual life can be lived, and there is a chance of making a complete end of suffering. ^an3-100-1-4

Take the case of the individual who does a trivial bad deed, but it lands them in hell. ^an3-100-1-5

Meanwhile, another individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot. ^an3-100-1-6

What kind of individual does a trivial bad deed, but it lands them in hell? ^an3-100-2-1

An individual who hasn’t developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, or wisdom. They’re small-minded and mean-spirited, living in suffering. ^an3-100-2-2

That kind of individual does a trivial bad deed, but it lands them in hell. ^an3-100-2-3

What kind of individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot? ^an3-100-3-1

An individual who has developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. They’re not small-minded, but are big-hearted, meditating on the immeasurables. ^an3-100-3-2

That kind of individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot. ^an3-100-3-3

Suppose a person was to drop a lump of salt into a mug of water. ^an3-100-4-1

What do you think, mendicants? ^an3-100-4-2

Would that mug of water become salty and undrinkable?” ^an3-100-4-3

“Yes, sir. ^an3-100-4-4

Why is that? ^an3-100-4-5

Because there is only a little water in the bowl.” ^an3-100-4-6

“Suppose a person was to drop a lump of salt into the Ganges river. ^an3-100-4-7

What do you think, mendicants? ^an3-100-4-8

Would the Ganges river become salty and undrinkable?” ^an3-100-4-9

“No, sir. ^an3-100-4-10

Why is that? ^an3-100-4-11

Because the Ganges river is a vast mass of water.” ^an3-100-4-12

“This is how it is in the case of an individual who does a trivial bad deed, but it lands them in hell. ^an3-100-5-1

Meanwhile, another individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot. … ^an3-100-5-2

Take the case of someone who is thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars. ^an3-100-8-1

While someone else isn’t thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars. ^an3-100-8-2

What sort of person is thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars? ^an3-100-9-1

Someone who is poor, with few possessions and little wealth. ^an3-100-9-2

That kind of person is thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars. ^an3-100-9-3

What sort of person isn’t thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars? ^an3-100-10-1

Someone who is rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an3-100-10-2

That kind of person isn’t thrown in jail for stealing half a dollar, a dollar, or a hundred dollars. ^an3-100-10-3

This is how it is in the case of an individual who does a trivial bad deed, but they go to hell. ^an3-100-10-4

Meanwhile, another individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot. … ^an3-100-10-6

It’s like a sheep dealer or butcher. They can execute, jail, fine, or otherwise punish one person who steals from them, but not another. ^an3-100-13-3

What sort of person can they punish? ^an3-100-14-1

Someone who is poor, with few possessions and little wealth. ^an3-100-14-2

That’s the kind of person they can punish. ^an3-100-14-3

What sort of person can’t they punish? ^an3-100-15-1

A ruler or their chief minister who is rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an3-100-15-2

That’s the kind of person they can’t punish. ^an3-100-15-3

In fact, all they can do is raise their cupped palms and ask: ^an3-100-15-4

‘Please, good fellow, give me my sheep or pay me for it.’ ^an3-100-15-5

This is how it is in the case of an individual who does a trivial bad deed, but it lands them in hell. ^an3-100-15-6

Meanwhile, another individual does the same trivial bad deed, but experiences it in this life, so that not even a little remains, let alone a lot. … ^an3-100-15-7

Mendicants, suppose you say: ^an3-100-18-1

‘No matter how this person performs a deed, they experience it the same way.’ This being so, the spiritual life could not be lived, and there’d be no chance of making a complete end of suffering. ^an3-100-18-2

Suppose you say: ^an3-100-18-3

‘No matter how this person performs a deed, they experience the result as it should be experienced.’ This being so, the spiritual life can be lived, and there is a chance of making a complete end of suffering.” ^an3-100-18-4

### A Panner (Numbered Discourses 3.101) ^an3-101-0

“Native gold has coarse corruptions: sand, soil, and gravel. ^an3-101-1-1

A panner or their apprentice pours it into a pan, where they wash, rinse, and clean it. ^an3-101-1-2

When that’s been eliminated, there are medium corruptions in the native gold: fine grit and coarse sand. ^an3-101-1-3

The panner washes it again. ^an3-101-1-4

When that’s been eliminated, there are fine corruptions in the native gold: fine sand and black grime. ^an3-101-1-5

The panner washes it again. ^an3-101-1-6

When that’s been eliminated, only golden flecks remain. ^an3-101-1-7

A goldsmith or their apprentice places the native gold in a crucible where they blow, melt, and smelt it. ^an3-101-1-8

Still the native gold is not settled and the dross is not totally gone. It’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working. ^an3-101-1-9

But the goldsmith keeps on blowing, melting, and smelting it. ^an3-101-1-10

The native gold becomes pliable, workable, and radiant, not brittle, and ready to be worked. ^an3-101-1-11

Then the goldsmith can successfully create any kind of ornament they want, whether a bracelet, earrings, a necklace, or a golden garland. ^an3-101-1-12

In the same way, a mendicant who is committed to the higher mind has coarse corruptions: bad bodily, verbal, and mental conduct. A sincere, capable mendicant gives these up, gets rid of, eliminates, and obliterates them. ^an3-101-2-1

When they’ve been given up and eliminated, there are middling corruptions: sensual, malicious, or cruel thoughts. A sincere, capable mendicant gives these up, gets rid of, eliminates, and obliterates them. ^an3-101-2-2

When they’ve been given up and eliminated, there are fine corruptions: thoughts of family, country, and status. A sincere, capable mendicant gives these up, gets rid of, eliminates, and obliterates them. ^an3-101-2-3

When they’ve been given up and eliminated, only thoughts about the teaching are left. ^an3-101-2-4

That immersion is not peaceful or sublime or tranquil or unified, but is held in place by forceful suppression. ^an3-101-2-5

But there comes a time when that mind is stilled internally; it settles, unifies, and becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an3-101-2-6

That immersion is peaceful and sublime and tranquil and unified, not held in place by forceful suppression. ^an3-101-2-7

They extend the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-2-8

If they wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with my hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful; controlling my body as far as the realm of divinity.’ ^an3-101-3-1

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-3-2

If they wish: ‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.’ ^an3-101-4-1

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-4-2

If they wish: ‘May I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^an3-101-5-1

May I understand mind with greed as “mind with greed”, ^an3-101-5-2

and mind without greed as “mind without greed”; ^an3-101-5-3

mind with hate as “mind with hate”, ^an3-101-5-4

and mind without hate as “mind without hate”; ^an3-101-5-5

mind with delusion as “mind with delusion”, ^an3-101-5-6

and mind without delusion as “mind without delusion”; ^an3-101-5-7

constricted mind as “constricted mind”, ^an3-101-5-8

and scattered mind as “scattered mind”; ^an3-101-5-9

expansive mind as “expansive mind”, ^an3-101-5-10

and unexpansive mind as “unexpansive mind”; ^an3-101-5-11

mind that is not supreme as “mind that is not supreme”, ^an3-101-5-12

and mind that is supreme as “mind that is supreme”; ^an3-101-5-13

mind immersed in samādhi as “mind immersed in samādhi”, ^an3-101-5-14

and mind not immersed in samādhi as “mind not immersed in samādhi”; ^an3-101-5-15

freed mind as “freed mind”, ^an3-101-5-16

and unfreed mind as “unfreed mind”.’ ^an3-101-5-17

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-5-18

If they wish: ‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. May I remember: “There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.” May I recollect my many past lives, with features and details.’ ^an3-101-6-1

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-6-2

If they wish: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place—and understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds: “These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. And may I understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds.’ ^an3-101-7-1

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-101-7-2

If they wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-101-8-1

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range.” ^an3-101-8-2

### Basis (Numbered Discourses 3.102) ^an3-102-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant committed to the higher mind should focus on three bases from time to time: ^an3-102-1-1

the basis of immersion, the basis of exertion, and the basis of equanimity. ^an3-102-1-2

If a mendicant dedicated to the higher mind focuses solely on the basis of immersion, it’s likely their mind will incline to laziness. ^an3-102-1-3

If they focus solely on the basis of exertion, it’s likely their mind will incline to restlessness. ^an3-102-1-4

If they focus solely on the basis of equanimity, it’s likely their mind won’t properly become immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an3-102-1-5

But when a mendicant dedicated to the higher mind focuses from time to time on the basis of immersion, the basis of exertion, and the basis of equanimity, their mind becomes pliable, workable, and radiant, not brittle, and has become rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an3-102-1-6

It’s like when a goldsmith or a goldsmith’s apprentice prepares a forge, fires the crucible, picks up some native gold with tongs and puts it in the crucible. From time to time they fan it, from time to time they sprinkle water on it, and from time to time they just watch over it. ^an3-102-2-1

If they solely fanned it, the native gold would likely be scorched. ^an3-102-2-2

If they solely sprinkled water on it, the native gold would likely cool down. ^an3-102-2-3

If they solely watched over it, the native gold would likely not be properly processed. ^an3-102-2-4

But when that goldsmith fans it from time to time, sprinkles water on it from time to time, and watches over it from time to time, that native gold becomes pliable, workable, and radiant, not brittle, and is ready to be worked. ^an3-102-2-5

Then the goldsmith can successfully create any kind of ornament they want, whether a bracelet, earrings, a necklace, or a golden garland. ^an3-102-2-6

In the same way, a mendicant committed to the higher mind should focus on three bases from time to time: ^an3-102-3-1

the basis of immersion, the basis of exertion, and the basis of equanimity. … ^an3-102-3-2

When a mendicant dedicated to the higher mind focuses from time to time on the basis of immersion, the basis of exertion, and the basis of equanimity, their mind becomes pliable, workable, and radiant, not brittle, and has become rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an3-102-3-6

They extend the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an3-102-3-7

If they wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power’ … ^an3-102-4-1

‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and divine, whether near or far.’ … ^an3-102-4-2

‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives.’ … ^an3-102-4-3

‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn.’ … ^an3-102-4-4

‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an3-102-4-5

They are capable of realizing these things, since each and every one is within range.” ^an3-102-4-6

### Before Awakening (Numbered Discourses 3.103) ^an3-103-0

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought: ^an3-103-1-1

‘What’s the gratification in the world? What’s the drawback? What’s the escape?’ ^an3-103-1-2

Then it occurred to me: ^an3-103-1-3

‘The pleasure and happiness that arise from the world: this is its gratification. ^an3-103-1-4

That the world is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback. ^an3-103-1-5

Removing and giving up desire and greed for the world: this is its escape.’ ^an3-103-1-6

As long as I didn’t truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an3-103-1-7

But when I did truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape in this way for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an3-103-1-8

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^an3-103-1-9

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^an3-103-1-10

### Gratification (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.104) ^an3-104-0

“Mendicants, I went in search of the world’s gratification, and I found it. ^an3-104-1-1

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of gratification in the world. ^an3-104-1-2

I went in search of the world’s drawbacks, and I found them. ^an3-104-1-3

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of the drawbacks in the world. ^an3-104-1-4

I went in search of escape from the world, and I found it. ^an3-104-1-5

I’ve seen clearly with wisdom the full extent of escape from the world. ^an3-104-1-6

As long as I didn’t truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an3-104-1-7

But when I did truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an3-104-1-8

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^an3-104-1-9

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^an3-104-1-10

### Gratification (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.105) ^an3-105-0

“Mendicants, if there were no gratification in the world, sentient beings wouldn’t be aroused by it. ^an3-105-1-1

But because there is gratification in the world, sentient beings are aroused by it. ^an3-105-1-2

If the world had no drawback, sentient beings wouldn’t grow disillusioned with it. ^an3-105-1-3

But since the world has a drawback, sentient beings do grow disillusioned with it. ^an3-105-1-4

If there were no escape from the world, sentient beings wouldn’t escape from it. ^an3-105-1-5

But since there is an escape from the world, sentient beings do escape from it. ^an3-105-1-6

As long as sentient beings don’t truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they haven’t escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they don’t live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries. ^an3-105-1-7

But when sentient beings truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are, they’ve escaped from this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—and they live detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries.” ^an3-105-1-8

### Ascetics and Brahmins (Numbered Discourses 3.106) ^an3-106-0

“Mendicants, there are ascetics and brahmins who don’t truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are. ^an3-106-1-1

I don’t deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables don’t realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and don’t live having realized it with their own insight. ^an3-106-1-2

There are ascetics and brahmins who do truly understand the world’s gratification, drawback, and escape for what they are. ^an3-106-1-3

I deem them as true ascetics and brahmins. Those venerables realize the goal of life as an ascetic or brahmin, and live having realized it with their own insight.” ^an3-106-1-4

### Wailing (Numbered Discourses 3.107) ^an3-107-0

“Singing is regarded as wailing in the training of the Noble One. ^an3-107-1-1

Dancing is regarded as madness. ^an3-107-1-2

Too much laughter, showing the teeth, is regarded as childish. ^an3-107-1-3

So break off singing and dancing; and when you’re appropriately pleased, it’s enough to simply smile.” ^an3-107-1-4

### Satisfaction (Numbered Discourses 3.108) ^an3-108-0

“Mendicants, there are three indulgences that never satisfy. ^an3-108-1-1

What three? ^an3-108-1-2

Sleep, ^an3-108-1-3

drinking beer and wine, ^an3-108-1-4

and sexual intercourse. ^an3-108-1-5

These are the three indulgences that never satisfy.” ^an3-108-1-6

### Unprotected (Numbered Discourses 3.109) ^an3-109-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an3-109-1-1

“Householder, when the mind is unprotected, deeds of body, speech, and mind are unprotected. ^an3-109-1-2

When deeds are unprotected, they fester. ^an3-109-1-3

When deeds fester, they become rotten. ^an3-109-1-4

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are rotten will not have a good death. ^an3-109-1-5

It’s like a bungalow with a bad roof. The roof peak, rafters, and walls are unprotected. ^an3-109-2-1

They fester, ^an3-109-2-2

and become rotten. ^an3-109-2-3

In the same way, when the mind is unprotected, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are unprotected. … ^an3-109-3-1

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are rotten will not have a good death. ^an3-109-3-4

When the mind is protected, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are protected. ^an3-109-4-1

When deeds are protected, they don’t fester. ^an3-109-4-2

When deeds don’t fester, they don’t become rotten. ^an3-109-4-3

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind aren’t rotten will have a good death. ^an3-109-4-4

It’s like a bungalow with a good roof. The roof peak, rafters, and walls are protected. ^an3-109-5-1

They don’t get soaked, ^an3-109-5-2

and they don’t become rotten. ^an3-109-5-3

In the same way, when the mind is protected, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are protected. … ^an3-109-6-1

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind aren’t rotten will have a good death.” ^an3-109-6-4

### Fallen (Numbered Discourses 3.110) ^an3-110-0

Seated to one side, the Buddha said to the householder Anāthapiṇḍika: ^an3-110-1-1

“Householder, when the mind is fallen, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are fallen. ^an3-110-1-2

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are fallen will not have a good death. ^an3-110-1-3

It’s like a bungalow with a bad roof. The roof peak, rafters, and walls fall in. ^an3-110-1-4

In the same way, when the mind is fallen, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are fallen. ^an3-110-1-5

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are fallen will not have a good death. ^an3-110-1-6

When the mind is not fallen, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are not fallen. ^an3-110-2-1

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are not fallen will have a good death. ^an3-110-2-2

It’s like a bungalow with a good roof. The roof peak, rafters, and walls are not fallen in. ^an3-110-2-3

In the same way, when the mind is not fallen, bodily, verbal, and mental deeds are not fallen. ^an3-110-2-4

Someone whose deeds of body, speech, and mind are not fallen will have a good death.” ^an3-110-2-5

### Sources (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.111) ^an3-111-0

“Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-111-1-1

What three? ^an3-111-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-111-1-3

Any deed that emerges from greed, hate, or delusion—born, sourced, and originated from greed, hate, or delusion—is unskillful, blameworthy, results in suffering, and leads to the creation of more deeds, not their cessation. ^an3-111-1-4

These are three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-111-1-7

There are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-111-2-1

What three? ^an3-111-2-2

Contentment, love, and understanding are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-111-2-3

Any deed that emerges from contentment, love, or understanding—born, sourced, and originated from contentment, love, or understanding—is skillful, blameless, results in happiness, and leads to the cessation of more deeds, not their creation. ^an3-111-2-4

These are three sources that give rise to deeds.” ^an3-111-2-7

### Sources (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.112) ^an3-112-0

“Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-112-1-1

What three? ^an3-112-1-2

Desire comes up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-1-3

And how does desire come up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present? ^an3-112-1-6

In your heart you think about and consider things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-1-7

When you do this, desire comes up, ^an3-112-1-8

and you get attached to those things. ^an3-112-1-9

This lust in the heart is what I call a fetter. ^an3-112-1-10

That’s how desire comes up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-1-11

These are three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-112-3-7

There are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an3-112-4-1

What three? ^an3-112-4-2

Desire doesn’t come up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-4-3

And how does desire not come up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present? ^an3-112-5-1

You understand the future result of things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-5-2

When you know this, you grow disillusioned, ^an3-112-5-3

your heart becomes dispassionate, and you see it with penetrating wisdom. ^an3-112-5-4

That’s how desire doesn’t come up for things that stimulate desire and greed in the past, future, or present. ^an3-112-5-5

These are three sources that give rise to deeds.” ^an3-112-7-4

### Bound for Loss (Numbered Discourses 3.113) ^an3-113-0

“Mendicants, these three are bound for a place of loss, bound for hell, if they don’t give up this fault. ^an3-113-1-1

What three? ^an3-113-1-2

Someone who is unchaste, but claims to be chaste; ^an3-113-1-3

someone who makes a groundless accusation of unchastity against a person whose chastity is pure; ^an3-113-1-4

and someone who has the view, ‘There is nothing wrong with sensual pleasures,’ so they throw themselves into sensual pleasures. ^an3-113-1-5

These are the three who are bound for a place of loss, bound for hell, if they don’t give up this fault.” ^an3-113-1-6

### Rare (Numbered Discourses 3.114) ^an3-114-0

“Mendicants, the appearance of three individuals is rare in the world. ^an3-114-1-1

What three? ^an3-114-1-2

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. A teacher of the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. An individual who is grateful and thankful. ^an3-114-1-3

The appearance of these three individuals is rare in the world.” ^an3-114-1-4

### Immeasurable (Numbered Discourses 3.115) ^an3-115-0

“Mendicants, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-115-1-1

What three? ^an3-115-1-2

Someone easy to measure, someone hard to measure, and someone who is immeasurable. ^an3-115-1-3

And who is the individual easy to measure? ^an3-115-1-4

It’s an individual who is restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties. ^an3-115-1-5

This is called ‘an individual easy to measure’. ^an3-115-1-6

And who is the individual hard to measure? ^an3-115-2-1

It’s an individual who is not restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, or loose-tongued. They have established mindfulness, situational awareness and immersion, with unified mind and restrained faculties. ^an3-115-2-2

This is called ‘an individual hard to measure’. ^an3-115-2-3

And who is the immeasurable individual? ^an3-115-3-1

It’s a mendicant who is perfected, and has ended defilements. ^an3-115-3-2

This is called ‘an immeasurable individual’. ^an3-115-3-3

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-115-3-4

### Imperturbable (Numbered Discourses 3.116) ^an3-116-0

“Mendicants, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-116-1-1

What three? ^an3-116-1-2

First, an individual, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an3-116-1-3

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. If they’re set on that, committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the dimension of infinite space. ^an3-116-1-4

The lifespan of the gods of infinite space is twenty thousand eons. ^an3-116-1-5

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an3-116-1-6

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an3-116-1-7

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person as regards their place of rebirth. ^an3-116-1-8

Furthermore, an individual, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an3-116-2-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. If they’re set on that, committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an3-116-2-2

The lifespan of the gods of infinite consciousness is forty thousand eons. ^an3-116-2-3

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an3-116-2-4

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an3-116-2-5

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person as regards their place of rebirth. ^an3-116-2-6

Furthermore, an individual, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an3-116-3-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. If they’re set on that, committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the dimension of nothingness. ^an3-116-3-2

The lifespan of the gods of nothingness is sixty thousand eons. ^an3-116-3-3

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an3-116-3-4

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an3-116-3-5

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person as regards their place of rebirth. ^an3-116-3-6

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-116-3-7

### Failures and Accomplishments (Numbered Discourses 3.117) ^an3-117-0

“Mendicants, there are three failures. ^an3-117-1-1

What three? ^an3-117-1-2

Failure in ethics, mind, and view. ^an3-117-1-3

And what is failure in ethics? ^an3-117-1-4

It’s when someone kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, and uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-117-1-5

This is called ‘failure in ethics’. ^an3-117-1-6

And what is failure in mind? ^an3-117-2-1

It’s when someone is covetous and malicious. ^an3-117-2-2

This is called ‘failure in mind’. ^an3-117-2-3

And what is failure in view? ^an3-117-3-1

It’s when someone has wrong view, a distorted perspective, such as: ^an3-117-3-2

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-117-3-3

This is called ‘failure in view’. ^an3-117-3-4

Some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell because of failure in ethics, ^an3-117-3-5

mind, ^an3-117-3-6

or view. ^an3-117-3-7

These are the three failures. ^an3-117-3-8

There are three accomplishments. ^an3-117-4-1

What three? ^an3-117-4-2

Accomplishment in ethics, mind, and view. ^an3-117-4-3

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an3-117-5-1

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-117-5-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an3-117-5-3

And what is accomplishment in mind? ^an3-117-6-1

It’s when someone is content and kind-hearted. ^an3-117-6-2

This is called accomplishment in mind. ^an3-117-6-3

And what is accomplishment in view? ^an3-117-7-1

It’s when someone has right view, an undistorted perspective, such as: ^an3-117-7-2

‘There is meaning in giving, sacrifice, and offerings. There are fruits and results of good and bad deeds. There is an afterlife. There are such things as mother and father, and beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-117-7-3

This is called accomplishment in view. ^an3-117-7-4

Some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm because of accomplishment in ethics, ^an3-117-7-5

mind, ^an3-117-7-6

or view. ^an3-117-7-7

These are the three accomplishments.” ^an3-117-7-8

### Sure-bet Dice (Numbered Discourses 3.118) ^an3-118-0

“Mendicants, there are three failures. ^an3-118-1-1

What three? ^an3-118-1-2

Failure in ethics, mind, and view. ^an3-118-1-3

And what is failure in ethics? ^an3-118-2-1

It’s when someone kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, and uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-118-2-2

This is called ‘failure in ethics’. ^an3-118-2-3

And what is failure in mind? ^an3-118-3-1

It’s when someone is covetous and malicious. ^an3-118-3-2

This is called ‘failure in mind’. ^an3-118-3-3

And what is failure in view? ^an3-118-4-1

It’s when someone has wrong view, a distorted perspective, such as: ^an3-118-4-2

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-118-4-3

This is called ‘failure in view’. ^an3-118-4-4

Some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell because of failure in ethics, mind, or view. ^an3-118-4-5

It’s like throwing sure-bet dice: they always fall the right side up. ^an3-118-4-6

In the same way, some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell because of failure in ethics, mind, or view. ^an3-118-4-7

These are the three failures. ^an3-118-4-8

There are three accomplishments. ^an3-118-5-1

What three? ^an3-118-5-2

Accomplishment in ethics, mind, and view. ^an3-118-5-3

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an3-118-6-1

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-118-6-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an3-118-6-3

And what is accomplishment in mind? ^an3-118-7-1

It’s when someone is content and kind-hearted. ^an3-118-7-2

This is called accomplishment in mind. ^an3-118-7-3

And what is accomplishment in view? ^an3-118-8-1

It’s when someone has right view, an undistorted perspective, such as: ^an3-118-8-2

‘There is meaning in giving, sacrifice, and offerings. There are fruits and results of good and bad deeds. There is an afterlife. There are such things as mother and father, and beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-118-8-3

This is called accomplishment in view. ^an3-118-8-4

Some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm because of accomplishment in ethics, mind, or view. ^an3-118-8-5

It’s like throwing sure-bet dice: they always fall the right side up. ^an3-118-8-6

In the same way, some sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm because of accomplishment in ethics, mind, or view. ^an3-118-8-7

These are the three accomplishments.” ^an3-118-8-8

### Action (Numbered Discourses 3.119) ^an3-119-0

“Mendicants, there are three failures. ^an3-119-1-1

What three? ^an3-119-1-2

Failure in action, livelihood, and view. ^an3-119-1-3

And what is failure in action? ^an3-119-1-4

It’s when someone kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, and uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-119-1-5

This is called ‘failure in action’. ^an3-119-1-6

And what is failure in livelihood? ^an3-119-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has wrong livelihood and earns a living by wrong livelihood. ^an3-119-2-2

This is called ‘failure in livelihood’. ^an3-119-2-3

And what is failure in view? ^an3-119-3-1

It’s when someone has wrong view, a distorted perspective, such as: ^an3-119-3-2

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-119-3-3

This is called ‘failure in view’. ^an3-119-3-4

These are the three failures. ^an3-119-3-5

There are three accomplishments. ^an3-119-4-1

What three? ^an3-119-4-2

Accomplishment in action, livelihood, and view. ^an3-119-4-3

And what is accomplishment in action? ^an3-119-4-4

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-119-4-5

This is called ‘accomplishment in action’. ^an3-119-4-6

And what is accomplishment in livelihood? ^an3-119-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple has right livelihood and earns a living by right livelihood. ^an3-119-5-2

This is called ‘accomplishment in livelihood’. ^an3-119-5-3

And what is accomplishment in view? ^an3-119-6-1

It’s when someone has right view, an undistorted perspective, such as: ^an3-119-6-2

‘There is meaning in giving, sacrifice, and offerings. There are fruits and results of good and bad deeds. There is an afterlife. There are such things as mother and father, and beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ ^an3-119-6-3

This is called ‘accomplishment in view’. ^an3-119-6-4

These are the three accomplishments.” ^an3-119-6-5

### Purity (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.120) ^an3-120-0

“Mendicants, there are these three kinds of purity. ^an3-120-1-1

What three? ^an3-120-1-2

Purity of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-120-1-3

And what is purity of body? ^an3-120-1-4

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. ^an3-120-1-5

This is called ‘purity of body’. ^an3-120-1-6

And what is purity of speech? ^an3-120-2-1

It’s when someone doesn’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-120-2-2

This is called ‘purity of speech’. ^an3-120-2-3

And what is purity of mind? ^an3-120-3-1

It’s when someone is content, kind-hearted, and has right view. ^an3-120-3-2

This is called ‘purity of mind’. ^an3-120-3-3

These are the three kinds of purity.” ^an3-120-3-4

### Purity (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.121) ^an3-121-0

“Mendicants, there are these three kinds of purity. ^an3-121-1-1

What three? ^an3-121-1-2

Purity of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-121-1-3

And what is purity of body? ^an3-121-1-4

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or have sex. ^an3-121-1-5

This is called ‘purity of body’. ^an3-121-1-6

And what is purity of speech? ^an3-121-2-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-121-2-2

This is called ‘purity of speech’. ^an3-121-2-3

And what is purity of mind? ^an3-121-3-1

It’s when a mendicant who has sensual desire in them understands ‘I have sensual desire in me.’ When they don’t have sensual desire in them, they understand ‘I don’t have sensual desire in me.’ They understand how sensual desire arises; how, when it’s already arisen, it’s given up; and how, once it’s given up, it doesn’t arise again in the future. ^an3-121-3-2

When they have ill will in them they understand ‘I have ill will in me’; and when they don’t have ill will in them they understand ‘I don’t have ill will in me’. They understand how ill will arises; how, when it’s already arisen, it’s given up; and how, once it’s given up, it doesn’t arise again in the future. ^an3-121-3-3

When they have dullness and drowsiness in them they understand ‘I have dullness and drowsiness in me’; and when they don’t have dullness and drowsiness in them they understand ‘I don’t have dullness and drowsiness in me’. They understand how dullness and drowsiness arise; how, when they’ve already arisen, they’re given up; and how, once they’re given up, they don’t arise again in the future. ^an3-121-3-4

When they have restlessness and remorse in them they understand ‘I have restlessness and remorse in me’; and when they don’t have restlessness and remorse in them they understand ‘I don’t have restlessness and remorse in me’. They understand how restlessness and remorse arise; how, when they’ve already arisen, they’re given up; and how, once they’re given up, they don’t arise again in the future. ^an3-121-3-5

When they have doubt in them they understand ‘I have doubt in me’; and when they don’t have doubt in them they understand ‘I don’t have doubt in me’. They understand how doubt arises; how, when it’s already arisen, it’s given up; and how, once it’s given up, it doesn’t arise again in the future. ^an3-121-3-6

This is called ‘purity of mind’. ^an3-121-3-7

These are the three kinds of purity. ^an3-121-3-8

Purity of body, purity of speech, ^an3-121-4-1

and undefiled purity of heart. ^an3-121-4-2

A pure one, blessed with purity, ^an3-121-4-3

has washed off all bad things, they say.” ^an3-121-4-4

### Sagacity (Numbered Discourses 3.122) ^an3-122-0

“Mendicants, there are these three kinds of sagacity. ^an3-122-1-1

What three? ^an3-122-1-2

Sagacity of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-122-1-3

And what is sagacity of body? ^an3-122-1-4

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or have sex. ^an3-122-1-5

This is called ‘sagacity of body’. ^an3-122-1-6

And what is sagacity of speech? ^an3-122-2-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an3-122-2-2

This is called ‘sagacity of speech’. ^an3-122-2-3

And what is sagacity of mind? ^an3-122-3-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-122-3-2

This is called ‘sagacity of mind’. ^an3-122-3-3

These are the three kinds of sagacity. ^an3-122-3-4

A sage in body, a sage in speech, ^an3-122-4-1

a sage undefiled in heart; ^an3-122-4-2

a sage, blessed with sagacity, ^an3-122-4-3

has given up everything, they say.” ^an3-122-4-4

### At Kusinārā (Numbered Discourses 3.123) ^an3-123-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kusinārā, in the Forest of Offerings. ^an3-123-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an3-123-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an3-123-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an3-123-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an3-123-1-5

“Mendicants, take the case of a mendicant living supported by a town or village. ^an3-123-2-1

A householder or their child approaches and invites them for the next day’s meal. ^an3-123-2-2

The mendicant accepts if they want. ^an3-123-2-3

When the night has passed, they robe up in the morning, take their bowl and robe, and approach that householder’s home, where they sit on the seat spread out. ^an3-123-2-4

The householder or their child serves and satisfies them with their own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an3-123-2-5

The mendicant thinks: ^an3-123-3-1

‘It’s so good that this householder serves me with delicious fresh and cooked foods.’ ^an3-123-3-2

Then they think: ^an3-123-3-3

‘I really hope this householder serves me with delicious fresh and cooked foods in the future, too.’ ^an3-123-3-4

They eat that food tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. ^an3-123-3-5

They think about it with sensual, malicious, or cruel thoughts. ^an3-123-3-6

A gift to such a mendicant is not very fruitful, I say. ^an3-123-3-7

Why is that? ^an3-123-3-8

Because that mendicant is negligent. ^an3-123-3-9

Take another case of a mendicant living supported by a town or village. ^an3-123-4-1

A householder or their child approaches and invites them for the next day’s meal. ^an3-123-4-2

The mendicant accepts if they want. ^an3-123-4-3

When the night has passed, they robe up in the morning, take their bowl and robe, and approach that householder’s home, where they sit on the seat spread out. ^an3-123-4-4

The householder or their child serves and satisfies them with their own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an3-123-4-5

It never occurs to them: ^an3-123-5-1

‘It’s so good that the householder or their child serves and satisfies me with their own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods.’ ^an3-123-5-2

They don’t think: ^an3-123-5-3

‘I really hope this householder serves me with delicious fresh and cooked foods in the future, too.’ ^an3-123-5-4

They eat that almsfood untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawback, and understanding the escape. ^an3-123-5-5

They think about it with thoughts of renunciation, good will, or harmlessness. ^an3-123-5-6

A gift to such a mendicant is very fruitful, I say. ^an3-123-5-7

Why is that? ^an3-123-5-8

Because that mendicant is diligent.” ^an3-123-5-9

### Arguments (Numbered Discourses 3.124) ^an3-124-0

“Mendicants, I’m not even comfortable thinking about a place where mendicants fight—quarreling and disputing, continually wounding each other with barbed words—let alone going there. ^an3-124-1-1

I come to a conclusion about them: ^an3-124-1-2

‘Clearly those venerables have given up three things and cultivated three things.’ ^an3-124-1-3

What three things have they given up? ^an3-124-1-4

Thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. ^an3-124-1-5

What three things have they cultivated? ^an3-124-1-7

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. … ^an3-124-1-8

I come to a conclusion about them: ^an3-124-1-11

‘Clearly those venerables have given up three things and cultivated three things.’ ^an3-124-1-12

I feel comfortable going to a place where the mendicants live in harmony—appreciating each other, without quarreling, blending like milk and water, and regarding each other with kindly eyes—let alone thinking about it. ^an3-124-2-1

I come to a conclusion about them: ^an3-124-2-2

‘Clearly those venerables have given up three things and cultivated three things.’ ^an3-124-2-3

What three things have they given up? ^an3-124-2-4

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^an3-124-2-5

What three things have they cultivated? ^an3-124-2-7

Thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. … ^an3-124-2-8

I come to a conclusion about them: ^an3-124-2-11

‘Clearly those venerables have given up three things and cultivated three things.’” ^an3-124-2-12

### The Gotamaka Shrine (Numbered Discourses 3.125) ^an3-125-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Gotamaka Shrine. ^an3-125-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an3-125-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an3-125-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an3-125-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an3-125-1-5

“Mendicants, I teach based on direct knowledge, not without direct knowledge. ^an3-125-2-1

I teach with reasons, not without them. ^an3-125-2-2

I teach with a demonstrable basis, not without it. ^an3-125-2-3

Since this is so, you should follow my advice and instruction. ^an3-125-2-4

This is enough for you to feel joyful, delighted, and happy: ^an3-125-2-5

‘The Blessed One is a fully awakened Buddha! The teaching is well explained! The Saṅgha is practicing well!’” ^an3-125-2-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^an3-125-3-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an3-125-3-2

And while this discourse was being spoken, the thousandfold galaxy shook. ^an3-125-3-3

### Bharaṇḍu Kālāma (Numbered Discourses 3.126) ^an3-126-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans when he arrived at Kapilavatthu. ^an3-126-1-1

Mahānāma the Sakyan heard that he had arrived. ^an3-126-1-2

He went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an3-126-1-3

“Go into Kapilavatthu, Mahānāma, and check if there’s a suitable guest house where I can spend the night.” ^an3-126-2-1

“Yes, sir,” replied Mahānāma. He returned to Kapilavatthu and searched all over the city, but he couldn’t see a suitable guest house for the Buddha to spend the night. ^an3-126-2-2

Then Mahānāma went up to the Buddha, and said to him, ^an3-126-3-1

“Sir, there’s no suitable guest house in Kapilavatthu for you to spend the night. ^an3-126-3-2

But there is this Bharaṇḍu the Kālāma, who used to be the Buddha’s spiritual companion. ^an3-126-3-3

Why don’t you spend the night at his hermitage?” ^an3-126-3-4

“Go, Mahānāma, and set out a mat.” ^an3-126-3-5

“Yes, sir,” replied Mahānāma. He went to Bharaṇḍu’s hermitage, where he set out a mat, and got foot-washing water ready. Then he went back to the Buddha and said to him, ^an3-126-3-6

“The mat and foot-washing water are set out. ^an3-126-3-7

Please, sir, go at your convenience.” ^an3-126-3-8

Then the Buddha went to Bharaṇḍu’s hermitage, sat down on the seat spread out, ^an3-126-4-1

and washed his feet. ^an3-126-4-2

Then it occurred to Mahānāma, ^an3-126-4-3

“It’s too late to pay homage to the Buddha today. ^an3-126-4-4

He’s tired. ^an3-126-4-5

Tomorrow I’ll pay homage to the Buddha.” He bowed to the Buddha and respectfully circled him, keeping him on his right, then he left. ^an3-126-4-6

Then, when the night had passed, Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an3-126-5-1

“Mahānāma, there are these three teachers found in the world. ^an3-126-5-2

What three? ^an3-126-5-3

One teacher advocates the complete understanding of sensual pleasures, ^an3-126-5-4

but not of forms or feelings. ^an3-126-5-5

One teacher advocates the complete understanding of sensual pleasures and forms, ^an3-126-5-6

but not of feelings. ^an3-126-5-7

One teacher advocates the complete understanding of sensual pleasures, forms, and feelings. ^an3-126-5-8

These are the three teachers found in the world. ^an3-126-5-9

Do these three teachers have the same end or different ends?” ^an3-126-5-10

When he said this, Bharaṇḍu said to Mahānāma, ^an3-126-6-1

“Say they’re the same, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-2

The Buddha said, ^an3-126-6-3

“Say they’re different, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-4

For a second time, Bharaṇḍu said, ^an3-126-6-5

“Say they’re the same, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-6

The Buddha said, ^an3-126-6-7

“Say they’re different, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-8

For a third time, Bharaṇḍu said, ^an3-126-6-9

“Say they’re the same, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-10

The Buddha said, ^an3-126-6-11

“Say they’re different, Mahānāma!” ^an3-126-6-12

Then it occurred to Bharaṇḍu, ^an3-126-7-1

“The Buddha has rebuked me three times in front of this illustrious Mahānāma. ^an3-126-7-2

Why don’t I leave Kapilavatthu?” ^an3-126-7-3

Then Bharaṇḍu the Kālāma left Kapilavatthu, ^an3-126-7-4

never to return. ^an3-126-7-5

### With Hatthaka (Numbered Discourses 3.127) ^an3-127-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an3-127-1-1

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Hatthaka, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha. Thinking, ^an3-127-1-2

“I will stand before the Buddha,” he sank and melted down, and wasn’t able to stay still. ^an3-127-1-3

It’s like when ghee or oil is poured on sand, it sinks and melts down, and can’t remain stable. ^an3-127-1-4

Then the Buddha said to Hatthaka, ^an3-127-2-1

“Hatthaka, manifest a solid incarnation.” ^an3-127-2-2

“Yes, sir,” replied Hatthaka. He manifested a solid incarnation, bowed to the Buddha, and stood to one side. ^an3-127-2-3

The Buddha said to him, ^an3-127-2-4

“Hatthaka, I wonder whether you still promulgate now the teachings that you promulgated when you were a human being?” ^an3-127-3-1

“I still promulgate the teachings that I promulgated as a human being. ^an3-127-3-2

And I also promulgate teachings that I didn’t promulgate as a human being. ^an3-127-3-3

Just as the Buddha these days lives crowded by monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; by rulers and their chief ministers, and monastics of other religions and their disciples, ^an3-127-3-4

so I live crowded by the godlings. ^an3-127-3-5

The godlings come from far away, thinking, ‘We’ll hear the teaching in the presence of Hatthaka.’ ^an3-127-3-6

Sir, I passed away without getting enough of three things. ^an3-127-3-7

What three? ^an3-127-3-8

Seeing the Buddha; ^an3-127-3-9

hearing the true teaching; ^an3-127-3-10

and serving the Saṅgha. ^an3-127-3-11

I passed away without getting enough of these three things. ^an3-127-3-12

I could never get enough ^an3-127-4-1

of seeing the Buddha, ^an3-127-4-2

serving the Saṅgha, ^an3-127-4-3

or hearing the teaching. ^an3-127-4-4

Training in the higher ethics, ^an3-127-5-1

loving to hear the true teaching, ^an3-127-5-2

Hatthaka has gone to the Aviha realm ^an3-127-5-3

without getting enough of these three things.” ^an3-127-5-4

### Bitter (Numbered Discourses 3.128) ^an3-128-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^an3-128-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Varanasi for alms. ^an3-128-1-2

While the Buddha was walking for alms near the cow-hitching place at the wavy leaf fig, he saw a disgruntled monk who was looking for pleasure in external things, unmindful, without situational awareness or immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties. ^an3-128-1-3

The Buddha said to him, ^an3-128-1-4

“Monk, don’t be bitter. ^an3-128-2-1

If you’re bitter, festering with putrefaction, flies will, without a doubt, plague and infest you.” ^an3-128-2-2

Hearing this advice of the Buddha, that monk was struck with a sense of urgency. ^an3-128-2-3

Then, after the meal, on his return from almsround, the Buddha told the mendicants what had happened. … ^an3-128-2-4

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha: ^an3-128-4-4

“Sir, what is this ‘bitterness’? ^an3-128-4-5

What is ‘putrefaction’? ^an3-128-4-6

And what are the ‘flies’?” ^an3-128-4-7

“Desire is bitterness; ^an3-128-5-1

ill will is the putrefaction; ^an3-128-5-2

and bad, unskillful thoughts are the flies. ^an3-128-5-3

If you’re bitter, festering with putrefaction, flies will, without a doubt, plague and infest you. ^an3-128-5-4

When your eyes and ears are unguarded, ^an3-128-6-1

and you’re not restrained in your sense faculties, ^an3-128-6-2

flies—those lustful thoughts— ^an3-128-6-3

will plague you. ^an3-128-6-4

A mendicant who’s bitter, ^an3-128-7-1

festering with putrefaction, ^an3-128-7-2

is far from being extinguished, ^an3-128-7-3

distress is their lot. ^an3-128-7-4

Whether in village or wilderness, ^an3-128-8-1

if they don’t find serenity in themselves, ^an3-128-8-2

the fool, the simpleton departs, ^an3-128-8-3

honored only by flies. ^an3-128-8-4

But those who have ethics, ^an3-128-9-1

lovers of wisdom and peace, ^an3-128-9-2

they, being peaceful, sleep at ease, ^an3-128-9-3

since they’ve got rid of the flies.” ^an3-128-9-4

### With Anuruddha (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.129) ^an3-129-0

Then Venerable Anuruddha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an3-129-1-1

“Sometimes, sir, with my clairvoyance that’s purified and superhuman, I see that ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are mostly reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an3-129-1-2

How many qualities do ladies have so that they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell?” ^an3-129-1-3

“When ladies have three qualities, when their body breaks up, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an3-129-2-1

What three? ^an3-129-2-2

A lady lives at home with a heart full of the stain of stinginess in the morning, jealousy at midday, and sexual desire in the evening. ^an3-129-2-3

When ladies possess these three qualities, when their body breaks up, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.” ^an3-129-2-4

### With Anuruddha (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.130) ^an3-130-0

Then Venerable Anuruddha went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an3-130-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him: ^an3-130-1-2

“Here’s the thing, Reverend Sāriputta. With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, I survey the thousandfold galaxy. ^an3-130-1-3

My energy is roused up and unflagging, my mindfulness is established and lucid, my body is tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind is immersed in samādhi and unified. ^an3-130-1-4

But my mind is not freed from the defilements by not grasping.” ^an3-130-1-5

“Well, Reverend Anuruddha, when you say: ^an3-130-2-1

‘With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, I survey the thousandfold galaxy,’ that’s your conceit. ^an3-130-2-2

And when you say: ^an3-130-2-3

‘My energy is roused up and unflagging, my mindfulness is established and lucid, my body is tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind is immersed in samādhi,’ that’s your restlessness. ^an3-130-2-4

And when you say: ^an3-130-2-5

‘But my mind is not freed from the defilements by not grasping,’ that’s your remorse. ^an3-130-2-6

It would be good to give up these three things. Ignore them and apply your mind to freedom from death.” ^an3-130-2-7

After some time Anuruddha gave up these three things. Ignoring them, he applied his mind to freedom from death. ^an3-130-3-1

Then Anuruddha, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an3-130-3-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an3-130-3-3

And Venerable Anuruddha became one of the perfected. ^an3-130-3-4

### Under Cover (Numbered Discourses 3.131) ^an3-131-0

“Mendicants, three things are conveyed under cover, not in the open. ^an3-131-1-1

What three? ^an3-131-1-2

Ladies are conveyed for marriage with a veil, not unveiled. ^an3-131-1-3

Hymns of the brahmins are conveyed under cover, not openly. ^an3-131-1-4

Wrong view is conveyed under cover, not in the open. ^an3-131-1-5

These three things are conveyed under cover, not in the open. ^an3-131-1-6

Three things shine in the open, not under cover. ^an3-131-2-1

What three? ^an3-131-2-2

The moon shines in the open, not under cover. ^an3-131-2-3

The sun shines in the open, not under cover. ^an3-131-2-4

The teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One shine in the open, not under cover. ^an3-131-2-5

These three things shine in the open, not under cover.” ^an3-131-2-6

### Etchings (Numbered Discourses 3.132) ^an3-132-0

“Mendicants, these three individuals are found in the world. ^an3-132-1-1

What three? ^an3-132-1-2

An individual like a line drawn in stone, an individual like a line drawn in sand, and an individual like a line drawn in water. ^an3-132-1-3

And who is the individual like a line drawn in stone? ^an3-132-1-4

It’s an individual who is often angry, ^an3-132-1-5

and their anger lingers for a long time. ^an3-132-1-6

It’s like a line drawn in stone, which isn’t quickly worn away by wind and water, but lasts for a long time. ^an3-132-1-7

In the same way, an individual is often angry, ^an3-132-1-8

and their anger lingers for a long time. ^an3-132-1-9

This is called an individual like a line drawn in stone. ^an3-132-1-10

And who is the individual like a line drawn in sand? ^an3-132-2-1

It’s an individual who is often angry, ^an3-132-2-2

but their anger doesn’t linger long. ^an3-132-2-3

It’s like a line drawn in sand, which is quickly worn away by wind and water, and doesn’t last long. ^an3-132-2-4

In the same way, an individual is often angry, ^an3-132-2-5

but their anger doesn’t linger long. ^an3-132-2-6

This is called an individual like a line drawn in sand. ^an3-132-2-7

And who is the individual like a line drawn in water? ^an3-132-3-1

It’s an individual who, though spoken to by someone in a rough, harsh, and disagreeable manner, still stays in touch, interacts with, and greets them. ^an3-132-3-2

It’s like a line drawn in water, which vanishes right away, and doesn’t last long. ^an3-132-3-3

In the same way, an individual, though spoken to by someone in a rough, harsh, and disagreeable manner, still stays in touch, interacts with, and greets them. ^an3-132-3-4

This is called an individual like a line drawn in water. ^an3-132-3-5

These are the three individuals found in the world.” ^an3-132-3-6

### A Warrior (Numbered Discourses 3.133) ^an3-133-0

“Mendicants, a warrior with three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned as a factor of kingship. ^an3-133-1-1

What three? ^an3-133-1-2

He’s a long-distance shooter, a marksman, one who shatters large objects. ^an3-133-1-3

A warrior with these three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned as a factor of kingship. ^an3-133-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with three factors is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an3-133-1-5

What three? ^an3-133-1-6

They’re a long-distance shooter, a marksman, and one who shatters large objects. ^an3-133-1-7

And how is a mendicant a long-distance shooter? ^an3-133-2-1

It’s when a mendicant truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an3-133-2-2

They truly see any kind of feeling at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> feeling—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an3-133-2-3

They truly see any kind of perception at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> perception—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an3-133-2-4

They truly see any kind of choices at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> choices—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an3-133-2-5

They truly see any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near, <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an3-133-2-6

That’s how a mendicant is a long-distance shooter. ^an3-133-2-7

And how is a mendicant a marksman? ^an3-133-3-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-133-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is a marksman. ^an3-133-3-3

And how does a mendicant shatter large objects? ^an3-133-4-1

It’s when a mendicant shatters the great mass of ignorance. ^an3-133-4-2

That’s how a mendicant shatters large objects. ^an3-133-4-3

A mendicant with these three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an3-133-4-4

### Assemblies (Numbered Discourses 3.134) ^an3-134-0

“Mendicants, there are these three assemblies. ^an3-134-1-1

What three? ^an3-134-1-2

An assembly educated in fancy talk, an assembly educated in questioning, and an assembly educated to the fullest extent. ^an3-134-1-3

These are the three assemblies.” ^an3-134-1-4

### A Friend (Numbered Discourses 3.135) ^an3-135-0

“Mendicants, you should associate with a friend who has three factors. ^an3-135-1-1

What three? ^an3-135-1-2

They give what is hard to give, they do what is hard to do, and they bear what is hard to bear. ^an3-135-1-3

You should associate with a friend who has these three factors.” ^an3-135-1-4

### Arising (Numbered Discourses 3.136) ^an3-136-0

“Mendicants, whether Realized Ones arise or not, this law of nature persists, this regularity of natural principles, this surety of natural principles: ^an3-136-1-1

all conditions are impermanent. ^an3-136-1-2

A Realized One awakens to this and comprehends it, ^an3-136-1-3

then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it: ^an3-136-1-4

‘All conditions are impermanent.’ ^an3-136-1-5

Whether Realized Ones arise or not, this law of nature persists, this regularity of natural principles, this surety of natural principles: ^an3-136-1-6

all conditions are suffering. ^an3-136-1-7

A Realized One awakens to this and comprehends it, ^an3-136-1-8

then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it: ^an3-136-1-9

‘All conditions are suffering.’ ^an3-136-1-10

Whether Realized Ones arise or not, this law of nature persists, this regularity of natural principles, this surety of natural principles: ^an3-136-1-11

all things are not-self. ^an3-136-1-12

A Realized One awakens to this and comprehends it, ^an3-136-1-13

then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it: ^an3-136-1-14

‘All things are not-self.’” ^an3-136-1-15

### A Hair Blanket (Numbered Discourses 3.137) ^an3-137-0

“Mendicants, a hair blanket is said to be the worst kind of woven cloth. ^an3-137-1-1

It’s cold in the cold, hot in the heat, ugly, smelly, and unpleasant to touch. ^an3-137-1-2

In the same way, the teaching of the bamboo-staffed ascetic is said to be the worst of all the doctrines of the various ascetics and brahmins. ^an3-137-1-3

The bamboo-staffed ascetic, that futile man, has this doctrine and view: ^an3-137-2-1

‘There is no power in deeds, action, or energy.’ ^an3-137-2-2

Those who were perfected ones, fully awakened Buddhas in the past also taught the efficacy of deeds, action, and energy. ^an3-137-2-3

But the bamboo-staffed ascetic opposes them by saying: ^an3-137-2-4

‘There is no power in deeds, action, or energy.’ ^an3-137-2-5

Those who will be perfected ones, fully awakened Buddhas in the future will also teach the efficacy of deeds, action, and energy. ^an3-137-2-6

But the bamboo-staffed ascetic opposes them by saying: ^an3-137-2-7

‘There is no power in deeds, action, or energy.’ ^an3-137-2-8

I too, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha in the present, teach the efficacy of deeds, action, and energy. ^an3-137-2-9

But the bamboo-staffed ascetic opposes me by saying: ^an3-137-2-10

‘There is no power in deeds, action, or energy.’ ^an3-137-2-11

It’s like a trap set at the mouth of a river, which would bring harm, suffering, calamity, and disaster for many fish. ^an3-137-3-1

In the same way that futile man the bamboo-staffed ascetic is a trap for humans, it seems to me. He has come into the world for the harm, suffering, calamity, and disaster of many beings.” ^an3-137-3-2

### Accomplishment (Numbered Discourses 3.138) ^an3-138-0

“Mendicants, there are three accomplishments. ^an3-138-1-1

What three? ^an3-138-1-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, and wisdom. ^an3-138-1-3

These are the three accomplishments.” ^an3-138-1-4

### Growth (Numbered Discourses 3.139) ^an3-139-0

“Mendicants, there are three kinds of growth. ^an3-139-1-1

What three? ^an3-139-1-2

Growth in faith, ethics, and wisdom. ^an3-139-1-3

These are the three kinds of growth.” ^an3-139-1-4

### A Wild Colt (Numbered Discourses 3.140) ^an3-140-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you about three wild colts and three wild people. ^an3-140-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-140-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an3-140-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an3-140-1-4

“What are the three wild colts? ^an3-140-2-1

One wild colt is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-140-2-2

Another wild colt is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-140-2-3

While another wild colt is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-140-2-4

These are the three wild colts. ^an3-140-2-5

And what are the three wild people? ^an3-140-3-1

One wild person is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-140-3-2

Another wild person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-140-3-3

While another wild person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-140-3-4

And how is a wild person fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned? ^an3-140-4-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-140-4-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-140-4-3

But when asked a question about the teaching or training, they falter without answering. ^an3-140-4-4

This is how they’re not beautiful, I say. ^an3-140-4-5

And they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-140-4-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. ^an3-140-4-7

This is how a wild person is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-140-4-8

And how is a wild person fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned? ^an3-140-5-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-140-5-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-140-5-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an3-140-5-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-140-5-5

But they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-140-5-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. ^an3-140-5-7

This is how a wild person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-140-5-8

And how is a wild person fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an3-140-6-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an3-140-6-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-140-6-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an3-140-6-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-140-6-5

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-140-6-6

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an3-140-6-7

This is how a wild person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-140-6-8

These are the three wild people.” ^an3-140-6-9

### Excellent Horses (Numbered Discourses 3.141) ^an3-141-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the three excellent horses and the three excellent people. ^an3-141-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-141-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an3-141-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an3-141-1-4

“What are the three excellent horses? ^an3-141-2-1

One excellent horse is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-141-2-2

Another excellent horse is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-141-2-3

While another excellent horse is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-141-2-4

These are the three excellent horses. ^an3-141-2-5

What are the three excellent people? ^an3-141-3-1

One excellent person is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-141-3-2

Another excellent person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-141-3-3

While another excellent person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-141-3-4

And how is an excellent person fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned? ^an3-141-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an3-141-4-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-141-4-3

But when asked a question about the teaching or training, they falter without answering. ^an3-141-4-4

This is how they’re not beautiful, I say. ^an3-141-4-5

And they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-141-4-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. This is how an excellent person is fast, ^an3-141-4-7

but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an3-141-4-8

And how is an excellent person fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned? ^an3-141-5-1

It’s when a mendicant, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an3-141-5-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-141-5-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an3-141-5-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-141-5-5

But they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-141-5-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. ^an3-141-5-7

This is how an excellent person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an3-141-5-8

And how is an excellent person fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an3-141-6-1

It’s when a mendicant, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an3-141-6-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-141-6-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an3-141-6-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-141-6-5
