They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-141-6-6

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an3-141-6-7

This is how an excellent person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-141-6-8

These are the three excellent people.” ^an3-141-6-9

### The Thoroughbred (Numbered Discourses 3.142) ^an3-142-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the three fine thoroughbred horses, and the three fine thoroughbred people. ^an3-142-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an3-142-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an3-142-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an3-142-1-4

“What are the three fine thoroughbred horses? ^an3-142-2-1

One fine thoroughbred horse … ^an3-142-2-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-142-2-3

These are the three fine thoroughbred horses. ^an3-142-2-4

And what are the three fine thoroughbred people? ^an3-142-3-1

One fine thoroughbred person … ^an3-142-3-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-142-3-3

And how is a fine thoroughbred person … ^an3-142-4-1

fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an3-142-4-2

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an3-142-4-3

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an3-142-4-4

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an3-142-4-5

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an3-142-4-6

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an3-142-4-7

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an3-142-4-8

This is how a fine thoroughbred person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an3-142-4-9

These are the three fine thoroughbred people.” ^an3-142-4-10

### At the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.143) ^an3-143-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, at the monastery of the wanderers in the peacocks’ feeding ground. ^an3-143-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an3-143-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an3-143-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an3-143-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an3-143-1-5

“Mendicants, a mendicant with three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans. ^an3-143-2-1

What three? ^an3-143-2-2

The entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an3-143-2-3

A mendicant with these three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans.” ^an3-143-2-4

### At the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.144) ^an3-144-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans. ^an3-144-1-1

What three? ^an3-144-1-2

A demonstration of psychic power, a demonstration of revealing, and a demonstration of instruction. ^an3-144-1-3

A mendicant with these three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans.” ^an3-144-1-4

### At the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 3.145) ^an3-145-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans. ^an3-145-1-1

What three? ^an3-145-1-2

Right view, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an3-145-1-3

A mendicant with these three qualities has reached the ultimate end, the ultimate sanctuary from the yoke, the ultimate spiritual life, the ultimate goal. They are the best among gods and humans.” ^an3-145-1-4

### Unskillful (Numbered Discourses 3.146) ^an3-146-0

“Someone with three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-146-1-1

What three? ^an3-146-1-2

Unskillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-146-1-3

Someone with these three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-146-1-4

Someone with three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an3-146-2-1

What three? ^an3-146-2-2

Skillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-146-2-3

Someone with these three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an3-146-2-4

### Blameworthy (Numbered Discourses 3.147) ^an3-147-0

“Someone with three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-147-1-1

What three? ^an3-147-1-2

Blameworthy deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-147-1-3

Someone with these three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-147-1-4

Someone with three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an3-147-2-1

What three? ^an3-147-2-2

Blameless deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-147-2-3

Someone with these three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an3-147-2-4

### Unethical (Numbered Discourses 3.148) ^an3-148-0

“Someone with three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … ^an3-148-1-1

Unethical deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-148-1-2

Someone with three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … ^an3-148-2-1

Ethical deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-148-2-2

### Impure (Numbered Discourses 3.149) ^an3-149-0

“Someone with three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … ^an3-149-1-1

Impure deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-149-1-2

Someone with three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … ^an3-149-2-1

Pure deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-149-2-2

### Broken (1st) (Numbered Discourses 3.150) ^an3-150-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and reproved by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an3-150-1-1

What three? ^an3-150-1-2

Unskillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-150-1-3

When an astute, competent true person has three qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an3-150-2-1

What three? ^an3-150-2-2

Skillful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-150-2-3

### Broken (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 3.151) ^an3-151-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. … ^an3-151-1-1

Blameworthy deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-151-1-2

When an astute, competent true person has three qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. … ^an3-151-2-1

Blameless deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-151-2-2

### Broken (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 3.152) ^an3-152-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. … ^an3-152-1-1

Unethical deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-152-1-2

When an astute, competent true person has three qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. … ^an3-152-2-1

Ethical deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-152-2-2

### Broken (4th) (Numbered Discourses 3.153) ^an3-153-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has three qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. … ^an3-153-1-1

Impure deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an3-153-1-2

When an astute, competent true person has three qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. … ^an3-153-2-1

Pure deeds by way of body, speech, and mind. …” ^an3-153-2-2

### Homage (Numbered Discourses 3.154) ^an3-154-0

“Mendicants, there are three kinds of homage. ^an3-154-1-1

What three? ^an3-154-1-2

By way of body, speech, and mind. ^an3-154-1-3

These are the three kinds of homage.” ^an3-154-1-4

### Morning (Numbered Discourses 3.155) ^an3-155-0

“Mendicants, those sentient beings who do good things in the morning by way of body, speech, and mind have a good morning. ^an3-155-1-1

Those sentient beings who do good things at midday by way of body, speech, and mind have a good midday. ^an3-155-2-1

Those sentient beings who do good things in the evening by way of body, speech, and mind have a good evening. ^an3-155-3-1

A good star, a good fortune, ^an3-155-4-1

a good dawn, a good rising, ^an3-155-4-2

a good moment, a good hour: ^an3-155-4-3

these come with good gifts to spiritual practitioners. ^an3-155-4-4

Auspicious deeds of body, ^an3-155-5-1

verbal auspicious deeds, ^an3-155-5-2

auspicious deeds of mind, ^an3-155-5-3

auspicious resolutions: ^an3-155-5-4

when your deeds have been auspicious, ^an3-155-5-5

you get auspicious benefits. ^an3-155-5-6

Those happy with these benefits ^an3-155-6-1

flourish in the Buddha’s teaching. ^an3-155-6-2

May you and all your relatives ^an3-155-6-3

be healthy and happy!” ^an3-155-6-4

### Untitled Discourses on Three Practices (Numbered Discourses 3) ^an3-156-162-0

“Mendicants, there are three practices. ^an3-156-1-1

What three? ^an3-156-1-2

The addicted practice, the scorching practice, and the middle practice. ^an3-156-1-3

And what’s the addicted practice? ^an3-156-1-4

It’s when someone has this doctrine and view: ^an3-156-1-5

‘There’s nothing wrong with sensual pleasures’; ^an3-156-1-6

so they throw themselves into sensual pleasures. ^an3-156-1-7

This is called the addicted practice. ^an3-156-1-8

And what’s the scorching practice? ^an3-156-2-1

It’s when someone goes naked, ignoring conventions. They lick their hands, and don’t come or wait when called. They don’t consent to food brought to them, or food prepared on their behalf, or an invitation for a meal. ^an3-156-2-2

They don’t receive anything from a pot or bowl; or from someone who keeps sheep, or who has a weapon or a shovel in their home; or where a couple is eating; or where there is a woman who is pregnant, breastfeeding, or who lives with a man; or where there’s a hound waiting or flies buzzing. They accept no fish or meat or beer or wine, and drink no fermented gruel. ^an3-156-2-3

They go to just one house for alms, taking just one mouthful, or two houses and two mouthfuls, up to seven houses and seven mouthfuls. ^an3-156-2-4

They feed on one saucer a day, two saucers a day, up to seven saucers a day. ^an3-156-2-5

They eat once a day, once every second day, up to once a week, and so on, even up to once a fortnight. They live pursuing the practice of eating food at set intervals. ^an3-156-2-6

They eat herbs, millet, wild rice, poor rice, water lettuce, rice bran, scum from boiling rice, sesame flour, grass, or cow dung. They survive on forest roots and fruits, or eating fallen fruit. ^an3-156-3-1

They wear robes of sunn hemp, mixed hemp, corpse-wrapping cloth, rags, lodh tree bark, antelope hide (whole or in strips), kusa grass, bark, wood-chips, human hair, horse-tail hair, or owls’ wings. ^an3-156-4-1

They tear out their hair and beard, committed to this practice. ^an3-156-4-2

They constantly stand, turning down seats. ^an3-156-4-3

They squat, committed to the endeavor of squatting. ^an3-156-4-4

They lie on a mat of thorns, making a mat of thorns their bed. ^an3-156-4-5

They’re devoted to ritual bathing three times a day, including at dusk. ^an3-156-4-6

And so they live pursuing these various ways of mortifying and tormenting the body. ^an3-156-4-7

This is called the scorching practice. ^an3-156-4-8

And what’s the middle practice? ^an3-156-5-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an3-156-5-2

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^an3-156-5-3

They meditate observing an aspect of the mind … ^an3-156-5-4

They meditate observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an3-156-5-5

This is called the middle practice. ^an3-156-5-6

These are the three practices. ^an3-156-5-7

Mendicants, there are three practices. ^an3-157-1-1

What three? ^an3-157-1-2

The addicted practice, the scorching practice, the middle practice. ^an3-157-1-3

And what’s the addicted practice? … ^an3-157-1-4

This is called the addicted practice. ^an3-157-1-5

And what is the scorching practice? … ^an3-157-2-1

This is called the scorching practice. ^an3-157-2-2

And what’s the middle practice? ^an3-157-3-1

It’s when a mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. ^an3-157-3-2

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen are given up. ^an3-157-3-3

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities arise. ^an3-157-3-4

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are completed by development. … ^an3-157-3-5

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^an3-158-1-1

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort. ^an3-158-1-2

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort. ^an3-158-1-3

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. … ^an3-158-1-4

They develop the faculty of faith … ^an3-159-1-1

energy … ^an3-159-1-2

mindfulness … ^an3-159-1-3

immersion … ^an3-159-1-4

wisdom … ^an3-159-1-5

They develop the power of faith … ^an3-160-1-1

energy … ^an3-160-1-2

mindfulness … ^an3-160-1-3

immersion … ^an3-160-1-4

wisdom … ^an3-160-1-5

They develop the awakening factor of mindfulness … ^an3-161-1-1

investigation of principles … ^an3-161-1-2

energy … ^an3-161-1-3

rapture … ^an3-161-1-4

tranquility … ^an3-161-1-5

immersion … ^an3-161-1-6

equanimity … ^an3-161-1-7

They develop right view … ^an3-162-1-1

right purpose … ^an3-162-1-2

right speech … ^an3-162-1-3

right action … ^an3-162-1-4

right livelihood … ^an3-162-1-5

right effort … ^an3-162-1-6

right mindfulness … ^an3-162-1-7

right immersion … ^an3-162-1-8

This is called the middle practice. ^an3-162-1-9

These are the three practices.” ^an3-162-1-10

### Untitled Discourses on Three Qualities (Numbered Discourses 3) ^an3-163-182-0

“Someone with three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-163-1-1

What three? ^an3-163-1-2

They themselves kill living creatures. They encourage others to kill living creatures. And they approve of killing living creatures. ^an3-163-1-3

Someone with these three qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an3-163-1-4

Someone with three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an3-164-1-1

What three? ^an3-164-1-2

They don’t themselves kill living creatures. They encourage others to not kill living creatures. And they approve of not killing living creatures. … ^an3-164-1-3

They themselves steal. They encourage others to steal. And they approve of stealing. … ^an3-165-1-1

They don’t themselves steal. They encourage others to not steal. And they approve of not stealing. … ^an3-166-1-1

They themselves commit sexual misconduct. They encourage others to commit sexual misconduct. And they approve of sexual misconduct. … ^an3-167-1-1

They don’t themselves commit sexual misconduct. They encourage others to not commit sexual misconduct. And they approve of refraining from sexual misconduct. … ^an3-168-1-1

They themselves lie. They encourage others to lie. And they approve of lying. … ^an3-169-1-1

They don’t themselves lie. They encourage others to not lie. And they approve of not lying. … ^an3-170-1-1

They themselves backbite. They encourage others to backbite. And they approve of backbiting. … ^an3-171-1-1

They don’t themselves backbite. They encourage others to not backbite. And they approve of not backbiting. … ^an3-172-1-1

They themselves speak harshly. They encourage others to speak harshly. And they approve of speaking harshly. … ^an3-173-1-1

They don’t themselves speak harshly. They encourage others to not speak harshly. And they approve of not speaking harshly. … ^an3-174-1-1

They themselves talk nonsense. They encourage others to talk nonsense. And they approve of talking nonsense. … ^an3-175-1-1

They don’t themselves talk nonsense. They encourage others to not talk nonsense. And they approve of not talking nonsense. … ^an3-176-1-1

They themselves are covetous. They encourage others to be covetous. And they approve of covetousness. … ^an3-177-1-1

They themselves are content. They encourage others to be contented. And they approve of being contented. … ^an3-178-1-1

They themselves have ill will. They encourage others to have ill will. And they approve of having ill will. … ^an3-179-1-1

They themselves are kind-hearted. They encourage others to be kind-hearted. And they approve of kind-heartedness. … ^an3-180-1-1

They themselves have wrong view. They encourage others to have wrong view. And they approve of wrong view. … ^an3-181-1-1

They themselves have right view. They encourage others to have right view. And they approve of right view. ^an3-182-1-1

Someone with these three qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an3-182-1-2

### Untitled Discourses on Greed, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 3) ^an3-183-352-0

“For insight into greed, three things should be developed. ^an3-183-352-1-1

What three? ^an3-183-352-1-2

Emptiness immersion; signless immersion; and undirected immersion. ^an3-183-352-1-3

For insight into greed, these three things should be developed. ^an3-183-352-1-4

For the complete understanding of greed … ^an3-183-352-2-1

complete ending … ^an3-183-352-2-2

giving up … ^an3-183-352-2-3

ending … ^an3-183-352-2-4

vanishing … ^an3-183-352-2-5

fading away … ^an3-183-352-2-6

cessation … ^an3-183-352-2-7

giving away … ^an3-183-352-2-8

letting go … ^an3-183-352-2-9

hate … ^an3-183-352-3-1

delusion … ^an3-183-352-3-2

anger … ^an3-183-352-3-3

acrimony … ^an3-183-352-3-4

disdain … ^an3-183-352-3-5

contempt … ^an3-183-352-3-6

jealousy … ^an3-183-352-3-7

stinginess … ^an3-183-352-3-8

deceitfulness … ^an3-183-352-3-9

deviousness … ^an3-183-352-3-10

obstinacy … ^an3-183-352-3-11

aggression … ^an3-183-352-3-12

conceit … ^an3-183-352-3-13

arrogance … ^an3-183-352-3-14

vanity … ^an3-183-352-3-15

negligence … ^an3-183-352-3-16

insight … ^an3-183-352-3-17

complete understanding … ^an3-183-352-3-18

complete ending … ^an3-183-352-3-19

giving up … ^an3-183-352-3-20

ending … ^an3-183-352-3-21

vanishing … ^an3-183-352-3-22

fading away … ^an3-183-352-3-23

cessation … ^an3-183-352-3-24

giving away … ^an3-183-352-3-25

For the letting go of negligence, these three things should be developed.” ^an3-183-352-3-26

That is what the Buddha said. ^an3-183-352-4-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an3-183-352-4-2

The Book of the Threes is finished. ^an3-183-352-8-6

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 4 ^an4-0

### Understood (Numbered Discourses 4.1) ^an4-1-0

So I have heard. ^an4-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Vajjis at Wares Village. ^an4-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an4-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an4-1-1-6

“Mendicants, due to not understanding and not penetrating four things, both you and I have wandered and transmigrated for such a very long time. ^an4-1-2-1

What four? ^an4-1-2-2

Noble ethics, ^an4-1-2-3

immersion, ^an4-1-2-4

wisdom, ^an4-1-2-5

and freedom. ^an4-1-2-6

These noble ethics, immersion, wisdom, and freedom have been understood and comprehended. Craving for continued existence has been cut off; the leash to existence is ended; now there’ll be no more future lives.” ^an4-1-2-7

That is what the Buddha said. ^an4-1-3-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^an4-1-3-2

“Ethics, immersion, and wisdom, ^an4-1-4-1

and the supreme freedom: ^an4-1-4-2

these things have been understood ^an4-1-4-3

by Gotama the renowned. ^an4-1-4-4

And so the Buddha, having insight, ^an4-1-5-1

explained this teaching to the mendicants. ^an4-1-5-2

The teacher made an end of suffering, ^an4-1-5-3

seeing clearly, he is fully quenched.” ^an4-1-5-4

### Fallen (Numbered Discourses 4.2) ^an4-2-0

“Someone without four things is said to have ‘fallen from this teaching and training’. ^an4-2-1-1

What four? ^an4-2-1-2

Noble ethics, ^an4-2-1-3

immersion, ^an4-2-1-4

wisdom, ^an4-2-1-5

and freedom. ^an4-2-1-6

Someone without these four things is said to have ‘fallen from this teaching and training’. ^an4-2-1-7

Someone with four things is said to be ‘secure in this teaching and training’. ^an4-2-2-1

What four? ^an4-2-2-2

Noble ethics, ^an4-2-2-3

immersion, ^an4-2-2-4

wisdom, ^an4-2-2-5

and freedom. ^an4-2-2-6

Someone with these four things is said to be ‘secure in this teaching and training’. ^an4-2-2-7

They fall, collapsed and fallen; ^an4-2-3-1

greedy, they return. ^an4-2-3-2

The work is done, the joyful is enjoyed, ^an4-2-3-3

happiness is found through happiness.” ^an4-2-3-4

### Broken (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.3) ^an4-3-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-3-1-1

What four? ^an4-3-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism, ^an4-3-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an4-3-1-4

They arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an4-3-1-5

and they don’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an4-3-1-6

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-3-1-7

When an astute, competent true person has four qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-3-2-1

What four? ^an4-3-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism, ^an4-3-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an4-3-2-4

They don’t arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an4-3-2-5

and they do arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an4-3-2-6

When an astute, competent true person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-3-2-7

When you praise someone worthy of criticism, ^an4-3-3-1

or criticize someone worthy of praise, ^an4-3-3-2

you choose a losing hand with your own mouth: ^an4-3-3-3

you’ll never find happiness that way. ^an4-3-3-4

A losing hand at dice is a trivial thing, ^an4-3-4-1

if all you lose is your money ^an4-3-4-2

and all you own, even yourself. ^an4-3-4-3

What’s a really terrible hand ^an4-3-4-4

is to hate the holy ones. ^an4-3-4-5

For a hundred thousand times a hundred million, ^an4-3-5-1

times five hundred and thirty-six times <j>a thousand times ten million years ^an4-3-5-2

a slanderer of noble ones goes to hell, ^an4-3-5-3

having aimed bad words and thoughts at them.” ^an4-3-5-4

### Broken (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.4) ^an4-4-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward four individuals they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-4-1-1

What four? ^an4-4-1-2

Mother … ^an4-4-1-3

father … a Realized One … and a disciple of a Realized One. ^an4-4-1-4

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person acts wrongly toward these four individuals they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-4-1-5

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward four individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-4-2-1

What four? ^an4-4-2-2

Mother … ^an4-4-2-3

father … a Realized One … and a disciple of a Realized One. ^an4-4-2-4

When an astute, competent true person acts rightly toward these four individuals they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-4-2-5

A person who does wrong ^an4-4-3-1

by their mother or father, ^an4-4-3-2

or a Realized One, a Buddha, ^an4-4-3-3

or one of their disciples, ^an4-4-3-4

brims with much wickedness. ^an4-4-3-5

Because of their unprincipled conduct ^an4-4-4-1

toward their parents, ^an4-4-4-2

they’re criticized in this life by the astute, ^an4-4-4-3

and they depart to be reborn in a place of loss. ^an4-4-4-4

A person who does right ^an4-4-5-1

by their mother and father, ^an4-4-5-2

or a Realized One, a Buddha, ^an4-4-5-3

or one of their disciples, ^an4-4-5-4

brims with much merit. ^an4-4-5-5

Because of their principled conduct ^an4-4-6-1

toward their parents, ^an4-4-6-2

they’re praised in this life by the astute, ^an4-4-6-3

and they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an4-4-6-4

### With the Stream (Numbered Discourses 4.5) ^an4-5-0

“These four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-5-1-1

What four? ^an4-5-1-2

An individual who goes with the stream; an individual who goes against the stream; a steadfast individual; and a brahmin who has crossed over, gone to the far shore, and stands on solid ground. ^an4-5-1-3

And who is the individual who goes with the stream? ^an4-5-1-4

It’s an individual who indulges in sensual pleasures and does bad deeds. ^an4-5-1-5

This is called an individual who goes with the stream. ^an4-5-1-6

And who is the individual who goes against the stream? ^an4-5-2-1

It’s an individual who doesn’t indulge in sensual pleasures or do bad deeds. They live the full and pure spiritual life in pain and sadness, weeping, with tearful faces. ^an4-5-2-2

This is called an individual who goes against the stream. ^an4-5-2-3

And who is the steadfast individual? ^an4-5-3-1

It’s an individual who, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an4-5-3-2

This is called a steadfast individual. ^an4-5-3-3

And who is a brahmin who has crossed over, gone to the far shore, and stands on solid ground? ^an4-5-4-1

It’s an individual who realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-5-4-2

This is called a brahmin who has crossed over, gone to the far shore, and stands on solid ground. ^an4-5-4-3

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-5-4-4

All those people with unbridled sensuality, ^an4-5-5-1

not free of lust, enjoying sensual pleasures in this life: ^an4-5-5-2

again and again, they return to birth and old age; ^an4-5-5-3

those who go with the stream are sunk in craving. ^an4-5-5-4

So the attentive in this life, <j>with mindfulness established, ^an4-5-6-1

don’t take part in sensual pleasures and bad deeds. ^an4-5-6-2

In pain they’d give up sensual pleasures: ^an4-5-6-3

they call that individual ‘one who goes against the stream’. ^an4-5-6-4

Someone who’s given up five corruptions, ^an4-5-7-1

a perfect trainee, not liable to decline, ^an4-5-7-2

who’s mastered their mind, <j>with faculties immersed in samādhi, ^an4-5-7-3

that man is called ‘steadfast’. ^an4-5-7-4

The sage who has comprehended all things, <j>high and low, ^an4-5-8-1

cleared them and ended them, so they are no more; ^an4-5-8-2

they’ve completed the spiritual journey, <j>and gone to the end of the world, ^an4-5-8-3

they’re called ‘one who has gone beyond’.” ^an4-5-8-4

### A Little Learning (Numbered Discourses 4.6) ^an4-6-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-6-1-1

What four? They may have: ^an4-6-1-2

Little learning and not get the point of learning. ^an4-6-1-3

Little learning but get the point of learning. ^an4-6-1-4

Much learning but not get the point of learning. ^an4-6-1-5

Much learning and get the point of learning. ^an4-6-1-6

And how has an individual learned little and not got the point of learning? ^an4-6-1-7

It’s when an individual has learned little of the ^an4-6-1-8

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-6-1-9

And with the little they’ve learned, they understand neither the meaning nor the teaching, nor do they practice in line with the teaching. ^an4-6-1-10

That’s how an individual has learned little and not got the point of learning. ^an4-6-1-11

And how has an individual learned little but has got the point of learning? ^an4-6-2-1

It’s when an individual has learned little of the ^an4-6-2-2

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-6-2-3

But with the little they’ve learned, they understand the meaning and the teaching, and they practice in line with the teaching. ^an4-6-2-4

That’s how an individual has learned little but has got the point of learning. ^an4-6-2-5

And how has an individual learned much but hasn't got the point of learning? ^an4-6-3-1

It’s when an individual has learned much of the ^an4-6-3-2

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-6-3-3

But even though they’ve learned much, they understand neither the meaning nor the teaching, nor do they practice in line with the teaching. ^an4-6-3-4

That’s how an individual has learned much but hasn't got the point of learning. ^an4-6-3-5

And how has an individual learned much and has got the point of learning? ^an4-6-4-1

It’s when an individual has learned much of the ^an4-6-4-2

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-6-4-3

And with the large amount they’ve learned, they understand the meaning and the teaching, and they practice in line with the teaching. ^an4-6-4-4

That’s how an individual has learned much and has got the point of learning. ^an4-6-4-5

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-6-4-6

If you don’t learn much, ^an4-6-5-1

and aren’t steady in ethics, ^an4-6-5-2

they’ll criticize you on both counts, ^an4-6-5-3

for your ethics and your learning. ^an4-6-5-4

If you don’t learn much, ^an4-6-6-1

and you are steady in ethics, ^an4-6-6-2

they’ll praise your ethical conduct, ^an4-6-6-3

since your learning has succeeded. ^an4-6-6-4

If you learn much, ^an4-6-7-1

but aren’t steady in ethics, ^an4-6-7-2

they’ll criticize your ethical conduct, ^an4-6-7-3

for your learning hasn’t succeeded. ^an4-6-7-4

If you learn much, ^an4-6-8-1

and you are steady in ethics, ^an4-6-8-2

they’ll praise you on both counts, ^an4-6-8-3

for your ethics and your learning. ^an4-6-8-4

A wise disciple of the Buddha ^an4-6-9-1

who is learned and has memorized the teachings; ^an4-6-9-2

like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, ^an4-6-9-3

who is worthy to criticize them? ^an4-6-9-4

Even the gods praise them, ^an4-6-9-5

and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.” ^an4-6-9-6

### Grace (Numbered Discourses 4.7) ^an4-7-0

“Mendicants, these four who are competent, educated, self-assured, and learned, who have memorized the teachings and practice in line with the teaching, grace the Saṅgha. ^an4-7-1-1

What four? ^an4-7-1-2

A monk, ^an4-7-1-3

a nun, ^an4-7-1-4

a layman, ^an4-7-1-5

and a laywoman. ^an4-7-1-6

These four who are competent, educated, self-assured, and learned, who have memorized the teachings and practice in line with the teaching, grace the Saṅgha. ^an4-7-1-7

Whoever is competent and self-assured, ^an4-7-2-1

learned, a memorizer of the teachings, ^an4-7-2-2

who lives in line with the teaching— ^an4-7-2-3

such a one is said to grace the Saṅgha. ^an4-7-2-4

A monk accomplished in ethics, ^an4-7-3-1

and a learned nun, ^an4-7-3-2

a faithful layman, ^an4-7-3-3

and a faithful laywoman, too: ^an4-7-3-4

these grace the Saṅgha, ^an4-7-3-5

they are the graces of the Saṅgha.” ^an4-7-3-6

### Self-assured (Numbered Discourses 4.8) ^an4-8-0

“Mendicants, a Realized One has four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an4-8-1-1

What four? ^an4-8-1-2

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ‘You claim to be a fully awakened Buddha, but you don’t understand these things.’ ^an4-8-1-3

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an4-8-1-4

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ‘You claim to have ended all defilements, but you still have these defilements.’ ^an4-8-2-1

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an4-8-2-2

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ‘The acts that you say are obstructions are not really obstructions for the one who performs them.’ ^an4-8-3-1

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an4-8-3-2

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ‘Though you teach that this teaching leads to the goal of the complete ending of suffering, it doesn’t lead there for one who practices it.’ ^an4-8-4-1

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an4-8-4-2

A Realized One possesses these four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an4-8-4-3

The various grounds for criticism ^an4-8-5-1

that ascetics and brahmins rely on ^an4-8-5-2

vanish on reaching a Realized One, ^an4-8-5-3

self-assured, gone beyond grounds for criticism. ^an4-8-5-4

He rolls forth the Wheel of Dhamma <j>as a consummate one, ^an4-8-6-1

complete, sympathetic for all living creatures. ^an4-8-6-2

Sentient beings revere him, <j>first among gods and humans, ^an4-8-6-3

who has gone beyond rebirth.” ^an4-8-6-4

### The Arising of Craving (Numbered Discourses 4.9) ^an4-9-0

“Mendicants, there are four things that give rise to craving in a mendicant. ^an4-9-1-1

What four? ^an4-9-1-2

For the sake of robes, ^an4-9-1-3

almsfood, ^an4-9-1-4

lodgings, ^an4-9-1-5

or rebirth in this or that state. ^an4-9-1-6

These are the four things that give rise to craving in a mendicant. ^an4-9-1-7

Craving is a person’s partner ^an4-9-2-1

as they transmigrate on this long journey. ^an4-9-2-2

They go from this state to another, ^an4-9-2-3

but don’t escape transmigration. ^an4-9-2-4

Knowing this drawback— ^an4-9-3-1

that craving is the cause of suffering— ^an4-9-3-2

rid of craving, free of grasping, ^an4-9-3-3

a mendicant would wander mindful.” ^an4-9-3-4

### Yokes (Numbered Discourses 4.10) ^an4-10-0

“Mendicants, there are these four yokes. ^an4-10-1-1

What four? ^an4-10-1-2

The yokes of sensual pleasures, future lives, views, and ignorance. ^an4-10-1-3

And what is the yoke of sensual pleasures? ^an4-10-1-4

It’s when you don’t truly understand sensual pleasures’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-1-5

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for sensual pleasures linger on inside. ^an4-10-1-6

This is called the yoke of sensual pleasures. ^an4-10-1-7

Such is the yoke of sensual pleasures. ^an4-10-1-8

And what is the yoke of future lives? ^an4-10-2-1

It’s when you don’t truly understand future lives’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-2-2

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for continued existence linger on inside. ^an4-10-2-3

This is called the yoke of future lives. ^an4-10-2-4

Such are the yokes of sensual pleasures and future lives. ^an4-10-2-5

And what is the yoke of views? ^an4-10-3-1

It’s when you don’t truly understand views’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-3-2

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for views linger on inside. ^an4-10-3-3

This is called the yoke of views. ^an4-10-3-4

Such are the yokes of sensual pleasures, future lives, and views. ^an4-10-3-5

And what is the yoke of ignorance? ^an4-10-4-1

It’s when you don’t truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape, ^an4-10-4-2

so ignorance and unknowing of the six fields of contact linger on inside. ^an4-10-4-3

This is called the yoke of ignorance. ^an4-10-4-4

Such are the yokes of sensual pleasures, future lives, views, and ignorance. ^an4-10-4-5

These are bad, unskillful qualities that are corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death. That’s why someone yoked to them is called: ‘one who has not found sanctuary from the yoke’. ^an4-10-4-6

These are the four yokes. ^an4-10-4-7

There are these four kinds of unyoking. ^an4-10-5-1

What four? ^an4-10-5-2

Unyoking from sensual pleasures, future lives, views, and ignorance. ^an4-10-5-3

And what is unyoking from sensual pleasures? ^an4-10-5-4

It’s when you truly understand sensual pleasures’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-5-5

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for sensual pleasures don’t linger on inside. ^an4-10-5-6

This is called unyoking from sensual pleasures. ^an4-10-5-7

Such is unyoking from sensual pleasures. ^an4-10-5-8

And what is unyoking from future lives? ^an4-10-6-1

It’s when you truly understand future lives’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-6-2

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for continued existence don’t linger on inside. ^an4-10-6-3

This is called unyoking from future lives. ^an4-10-6-4

Such is unyoking from sensual pleasures and future lives. ^an4-10-6-5

And what is unyoking from views? ^an4-10-7-1

It’s when you truly understand views’ origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape. ^an4-10-7-2

So greed, relishing, affection, infatuation, thirst, passion, attachment, and craving for views don’t linger on inside. ^an4-10-7-3

This is called unyoking from views. ^an4-10-7-4

Such is unyoking from sensual pleasures, future lives, and views. ^an4-10-7-5

And what is unyoking from ignorance? ^an4-10-8-1

It’s when you truly understand the six fields of contact’s origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape, ^an4-10-8-2

so ignorance and unknowing of the six fields of contact don’t linger on inside. ^an4-10-8-3

This is called unyoking from ignorance. ^an4-10-8-4

Such is unyoking from sensual pleasures, future lives, views, and ignorance. ^an4-10-8-5

These are bad, unskillful qualities that are corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death. That’s why someone unyoked from them is called: ‘one who has found sanctuary from the yoke’. ^an4-10-8-6

These are the four kinds of unyoking. ^an4-10-8-7

Yoked to both sensual pleasures ^an4-10-9-1

and the desire to be reborn in a future life; ^an4-10-9-2

yoked also to views, ^an4-10-9-3

and governed by ignorance, ^an4-10-9-4

sentient beings continue to transmigrate, ^an4-10-10-1

with ongoing birth and death. ^an4-10-10-2

But those who fully understand sensual pleasures, ^an4-10-10-3

and the yoke to all future lives; ^an4-10-10-4

with the yoke of views eradicated, ^an4-10-11-1

and ignorance dispelled, ^an4-10-11-2

unyoked from all yokes, ^an4-10-11-3

truly those sages have slipped their yoke.” ^an4-10-11-4

### Walking (Numbered Discourses 4.11) ^an4-11-0

“Mendicants, suppose a mendicant has a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought while walking. ^an4-11-1-1

They tolerate it and don’t give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it. ^an4-11-1-2

Such a mendicant is said to be ‘not keen or prudent, always lazy, and lacking energy’ when walking. ^an4-11-1-3

Suppose a mendicant has a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought while standing … ^an4-11-2-1

sitting … ^an4-11-3-1

or when lying down while awake. ^an4-11-4-1

They tolerate it and don’t give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it. ^an4-11-4-2

Such a mendicant is said to be ‘not keen or prudent, always lazy, and lacking energy’ when lying down while awake. ^an4-11-4-3

Suppose a mendicant has a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought while walking. ^an4-11-5-1

They don’t tolerate it, but give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it. ^an4-11-5-2

Such a mendicant is said to be ‘keen and prudent, always energetic and resolute’ when walking. ^an4-11-5-3

Suppose a mendicant has a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought while standing … ^an4-11-6-1

sitting … ^an4-11-7-1

or when lying down while awake. ^an4-11-8-1

They don’t tolerate it, but give it up, get rid of it, eliminate it, and obliterate it. ^an4-11-8-2

Such a mendicant is said to be ‘keen and prudent, always energetic and resolute’ when lying down while awake. ^an4-11-8-3

Whether walking or standing, ^an4-11-9-1

sitting or lying down, ^an4-11-9-2

if you think a bad thought ^an4-11-9-3

to do with the domestic life, ^an4-11-9-4

you’re on the wrong path, ^an4-11-10-1

lost among things that delude. ^an4-11-10-2

Such a mendicant is incapable ^an4-11-10-3

of experiencing the highest awakening. ^an4-11-10-4

But one who, whether standing or walking, ^an4-11-11-1

sitting or lying down, ^an4-11-11-2

has calmed their thoughts, ^an4-11-11-3

loving peace of mind; ^an4-11-11-4

such a mendicant is capable ^an4-11-11-5

of experiencing the highest awakening.” ^an4-11-11-6

### Ethics (Numbered Discourses 4.12) ^an4-12-0

“Mendicants, live by the ethical precepts and the monastic code. Live restrained in the monastic code, conducting yourselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, keep the rules you’ve undertaken. ^an4-12-1-1

When you’ve done this, what more is there to do? ^an4-12-1-2

Suppose a mendicant has got rid of desire and ill will while walking, and has given up dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. Their energy is roused up and unflagging, their mindfulness is established and lucid, their body is tranquil and undisturbed, and their mind is immersed in samādhi. Such a mendicant is said to be ‘keen and prudent, always energetic and resolute’ when walking. ^an4-12-2-1

Suppose a mendicant has got rid of desire and ill will while standing … ^an4-12-3-1

sitting … ^an4-12-4-1

and when lying down while awake, and has given up dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. Their energy is roused up and unflagging, their mindfulness is established and lucid, their body is tranquil and undisturbed, and their mind is immersed in samādhi and unified. Such a mendicant is said to be ‘keen and prudent, always energetic and resolute’ when lying down while awake. ^an4-12-5-1

Carefully walking, carefully standing, ^an4-12-6-1

carefully sitting, carefully lying; ^an4-12-6-2

a mendicant carefully bends their limbs, ^an4-12-6-3

and carefully extends them. ^an4-12-6-4

Above, below, all round, ^an4-12-7-1

as far as the planet extends; ^an4-12-7-2

they scrutinize the rise and fall ^an4-12-7-3

of phenomena such as the aggregates. ^an4-12-7-4

Training in what leads to serenity of heart, ^an4-12-8-1

always staying mindful; ^an4-12-8-2

they call such a mendicant ^an4-12-8-3

‘always determined’.” ^an4-12-8-4

### Effort (Numbered Discourses 4.13) ^an4-13-0

“Mendicants, there are these four right efforts. ^an4-13-1-1

What four? ^an4-13-1-2

A mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. ^an4-13-1-3

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen are given up. ^an4-13-1-4

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities arise. ^an4-13-1-5

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are fulfilled by development. ^an4-13-1-6

These are the four right efforts. ^an4-13-1-7

By rightly striving, <j>they’ve crushed Māra’s sovereignty; ^an4-13-2-1

unattached, they’ve gone beyond  <j>the peril of birth and death. ^an4-13-2-2

Contented and unstirred, they’ve vanquished Māra with his legions; ^an4-13-2-3

now they’ve gone beyond all Namuci’s forces, <j>they’re happy.” ^an4-13-2-4

### Restraint (Numbered Discourses 4.14) ^an4-14-0

“Mendicants, there are these four efforts. ^an4-14-1-1

What four? ^an4-14-1-2

The efforts to restrain, to give up, to develop, and to preserve. ^an4-14-1-3

And what, mendicants, is the effort to restrain? ^an4-14-1-4

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-14-1-5

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^an4-14-1-6

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an4-14-1-7

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an4-14-1-8

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an4-14-1-9

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an4-14-1-10

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-14-1-11

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^an4-14-1-12

This is called the effort to restrain. ^an4-14-1-13

And what, mendicants, is the effort to give up? ^an4-14-2-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, ^an4-14-2-2

malicious, ^an4-14-2-3

or cruel thought that’s arisen, but gives it up, gets rid of it, eliminates it, and obliterates it. ^an4-14-2-4

They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an4-14-2-5

This is called the effort to give up. ^an4-14-2-6

And what, mendicants, is the effort to develop? ^an4-14-3-1

It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, ^an4-14-3-2

investigation of principles, ^an4-14-3-3

energy, ^an4-14-3-4

rapture, ^an4-14-3-5

tranquility, ^an4-14-3-6

immersion, ^an4-14-3-7

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^an4-14-3-8

This is called the effort to develop. ^an4-14-3-9

And what, mendicants, is the effort to preserve? ^an4-14-4-1

It’s when a mendicant preserves a meditation subject that’s a fine basis of immersion: the perception of a skeleton, a worm-infested corpse, a livid corpse, a split open corpse, or a bloated corpse. ^an4-14-4-2

This is called the effort to preserve. ^an4-14-4-3

These are the four efforts. ^an4-14-4-4

Restraint and giving up, ^an4-14-5-1

development and preservation: ^an4-14-5-2

these are the four efforts ^an4-14-5-3

taught by the Kinsman of the Sun. ^an4-14-5-4

Any mendicant who keenly applies these ^an4-14-5-5

may attain the ending of suffering.” ^an4-14-5-6

### Regarded as Foremost (Numbered Discourses 4.15) ^an4-15-0

“Mendicants, these four are regarded as foremost. ^an4-15-1-1

What four? ^an4-15-1-2

The foremost in size of incarnation is ^an4-15-1-3

Rāhu, lord of titans. ^an4-15-1-4

The foremost pleasure seeker is ^an4-15-1-5

King Mandhātā. ^an4-15-1-6

The foremost in sovereignty is ^an4-15-1-7

Māra the Wicked. ^an4-15-1-8

In this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—a Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha is said to be the best. ^an4-15-1-9

These are the four regarded as foremost. ^an4-15-1-10

Rāhu is foremost in size of incarnation, ^an4-15-2-1

Mandhātā in enjoying sensual pleasures, ^an4-15-2-2

Māra in sovereignty, ^an4-15-2-3

shining with power and glory. ^an4-15-2-4

Above, below, all round, ^an4-15-3-1

as far as the planet extends; ^an4-15-3-2

in all the world with its gods, ^an4-15-3-3

the Buddha is declared foremost.” ^an4-15-3-4

### Subtlety (Numbered Discourses 4.16) ^an4-16-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of subtlety. ^an4-16-1-1

What four? ^an4-16-1-2

A mendicant has ultimate subtlety of form. ^an4-16-1-3

They don’t see any other subtlety of form that’s better or finer than that, ^an4-16-1-4

nor do they aim for it. ^an4-16-1-5

A mendicant has ultimate subtlety of feeling. ^an4-16-1-6

They don’t see any other subtlety of feeling that’s better or finer than that, ^an4-16-1-7

nor do they aim for it. ^an4-16-1-8

A mendicant has ultimate subtlety of perception. ^an4-16-1-9

They don’t see any other subtlety of perception that’s better or finer than that, ^an4-16-1-10

nor do they aim for it. ^an4-16-1-11

A mendicant has ultimate subtlety of choices. ^an4-16-1-12

They don’t see any other subtlety of choices that’s better or finer than that, ^an4-16-1-13

nor do they aim for it. ^an4-16-1-14

These are the four kinds of subtlety. ^an4-16-1-15

Knowing the subtlety of form, ^an4-16-2-1

the cause of feelings, ^an4-16-2-2

where perception comes from, ^an4-16-2-3

and where it ends; ^an4-16-2-4

and knowing choices as alien, ^an4-16-2-5

as suffering and as not-self, ^an4-16-2-6

that mendicant sees rightly, ^an4-16-3-1

peaceful, in love with the state of peace. ^an4-16-3-2

They bear their final body, ^an4-16-3-3

having vanquished Māra with his legions.” ^an4-16-3-4

### Prejudice (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.17) ^an4-17-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of making prejudiced decisions. ^an4-17-1-1

What four? ^an4-17-1-2

Making decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-17-1-3

These are the four ways of making prejudiced decisions. ^an4-17-1-4

If you act against the teaching ^an4-17-2-1

out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, or stupidity, ^an4-17-2-2

your fame fades away, ^an4-17-2-3

like the moon in the waning fortnight.” ^an4-17-2-4

### Prejudice (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.18) ^an4-18-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of making unprejudiced decisions. ^an4-18-1-1

What four? ^an4-18-1-2

Making decisions unprejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-18-1-3

These are the four ways of making unprejudiced decisions. ^an4-18-1-4

If you don’t act against the teaching ^an4-18-2-1

out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, and stupidity, ^an4-18-2-2

your fame swells, ^an4-18-2-3

like the moon in the waxing fortnight.” ^an4-18-2-4

### Prejudice (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 4.19) ^an4-19-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of making prejudiced decisions. ^an4-19-1-1

What four? ^an4-19-1-2

Making decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-19-1-3

These are the four ways of making prejudiced decisions. ^an4-19-1-4

There are these four ways of making unprejudiced decisions. ^an4-19-2-1

What four? ^an4-19-2-2

Making decisions unprejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-19-2-3

These are the four ways of making unprejudiced decisions. ^an4-19-2-4

If you act against the teaching ^an4-19-3-1

out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, or stupidity, ^an4-19-3-2

your fame fades away, ^an4-19-3-3

like the moon in the waning fortnight. ^an4-19-3-4

If you don’t act against the teaching ^an4-19-4-1

out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, and stupidity, ^an4-19-4-2

your fame swells, ^an4-19-4-3

like the moon in the waxing fortnight.” ^an4-19-4-4

### A Meal Assigner (Numbered Discourses 4.20) ^an4-20-0

“Mendicants, a meal assigner who has four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-20-1-1

What four? ^an4-20-1-2

They make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-20-1-3

A meal assigner who has these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-20-1-4

A meal designator who has four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-20-2-1

What four? ^an4-20-2-2

They make decisions unprejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. ^an4-20-2-3

A meal assigner who has these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-20-2-4

All those people with unbridled sensuality, ^an4-20-3-1

unprincipled, with no respect for principle, ^an4-20-3-2

led astray <j>by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, or cowardice, ^an4-20-3-3

are called ‘an assembly of the dregs’: ^an4-20-3-4

that’s what was said by the ascetic who knows. ^an4-20-4-1

And so those praiseworthy true persons, ^an4-20-4-2

standing on principle, doing nothing wrong, ^an4-20-4-3

not led astray <j>by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, or cowardice, ^an4-20-4-4

are called ‘an assembly of the cream’: ^an4-20-4-5

that’s what was said by the ascetic who knows.” ^an4-20-4-6

### At Uruvelā (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.21) ^an4-21-0

So I have heard. ^an4-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-21-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-21-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an4-21-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-21-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an4-21-1-6

“Mendicants, this one time, when I was first awakened, I was staying in Uruvelā at the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^an4-21-2-1

As I was in private retreat this thought came to mind: ^an4-21-2-2

‘One without respect and reverence lives in suffering. ^an4-21-2-3

What ascetic or brahmin should I honor and respect and rely on?’ ^an4-21-2-4

Then it occurred to me: ^an4-21-3-1

‘I would honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of ethics, if it were incomplete. ^an4-21-3-2

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who is more accomplished than myself in ethics, who I should honor and respect and rely on. ^an4-21-3-3

I would honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of immersion, if it were incomplete. ^an4-21-4-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin … who is more accomplished than myself in immersion … ^an4-21-4-2

I would honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entrie spectrum of wisdom, if it were incomplete. ^an4-21-5-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin in this world … who is more accomplished than myself in wisdom … ^an4-21-5-2

I would honor and respect and rely on another ascetic or brahmin so as to complete the entire spectrum of freedom, if it were incomplete. ^an4-21-6-1

But I don’t see any other ascetic or brahmin in this world … who is more accomplished than myself in freedom …’ ^an4-21-6-2

Then it occurred to me: ^an4-21-7-1

‘Why don’t I honor and respect and rely on the same teaching to which I was awakened?’ ^an4-21-7-2

And then the divinity Sahampati, knowing my train of thought, vanished from the realm of divinity and appeared in front of me, as easily as a strong man would extend or contract his arm. ^an4-21-8-1

He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward me, and said: ^an4-21-8-2

‘That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One! ^an4-21-8-3

All the perfected ones, the fully awakened Buddhas who lived in the past honored and respected and relied on this same teaching. ^an4-21-8-4

All the perfected ones, the fully awakened Buddhas who will live in the future will honor and respect and rely on this same teaching. ^an4-21-8-5

May the Blessed One, who is the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha at present, also honor and respect and rely on this same teaching.’ ^an4-21-8-6

That’s what the divinity Sahampati said. ^an4-21-8-7

Then he went on to say: ^an4-21-8-8

‘All Buddhas, whether in the past, ^an4-21-9-1

the Buddhas of the future, ^an4-21-9-2

and the Buddha at present— ^an4-21-9-3

destroyer of the sorrows of many— ^an4-21-9-4

respecting the true teaching ^an4-21-10-1

they did live, they do live, ^an4-21-10-2

and they also will live. ^an4-21-10-3

This is the nature of the Buddhas. ^an4-21-10-4

Therefore someone who desires self-knowledge, ^an4-21-11-1

and aspires to greatness themselves, ^an4-21-11-2

should respect the true teaching, ^an4-21-11-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.’ ^an4-21-11-4

That’s what the divinity Sahampati said. ^an4-21-12-1

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an4-21-12-2

Then, knowing the request of the Divinity and what was suitable for myself, I honored and respected and relied on the same teaching to which I was awakened. ^an4-21-12-3

And since the Saṅgha has also achieved greatness, I also respect the Saṅgha.” ^an4-21-12-4

### At Uruvelā (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.22) ^an4-22-0

“Mendicants, this one time, when I was first awakened, I was staying in Uruvelā at the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River. ^an4-22-1-1

Then several old brahmins—elderly and senior, who were advanced in years and had reached the final stage of life—came up to me, and exchanged greetings with me. ^an4-22-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side, and said to me: ^an4-22-1-3

‘Worthy Gotama, , we have heard this: ^an4-22-1-4

“The ascetic Gotama does not bow to old brahmins, elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat.” ^an4-22-1-5

And this is indeed the case, ^an4-22-1-6

for the worthy Gotama does not bow to old brahmins, elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat. ^an4-22-1-7

This is not appropriate, worthy Gotama.’ ^an4-22-1-8

Then it occurred to me, ^an4-22-2-1

‘These venerables don’t know what a senior is, or what qualities make you a senior.’ ^an4-22-2-2

Mendicants, suppose you’re eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old. ^an4-22-2-3

But your speech is untimely, false, meaningless, and against the teaching or training. You say things at the wrong time which are worthless, unreasonable, rambling, and unbeneficial. ^an4-22-2-4

Then you’ll be reckoned a ‘childish senior’. ^an4-22-2-5

Now suppose you’re a youth, young, with pristine black hair, blessed with youth, in the prime of life. ^an4-22-3-1

But your speech is timely, true, meaningful, and in line with the teaching and training. You say things at the right time which are valuable, reasonable, succinct, and beneficial. ^an4-22-3-2

Then you’ll be reckoned an ‘astute senior’. ^an4-22-3-3

There are these four qualities that make a senior. ^an4-22-4-1

What four? ^an4-22-4-2

A mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-22-4-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an4-22-4-4

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-22-4-5

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-22-4-6

These are the four qualities that make a senior. ^an4-22-4-7

The creature with a restless mind ^an4-22-5-1

speaks a lot of nonsense. ^an4-22-5-2

Their thoughts are unsettled, ^an4-22-5-3

and they don’t like the true teaching. ^an4-22-5-4

They’re far from seniority, with their bad views ^an4-22-5-5

and their lack of regard for others. ^an4-22-5-6

But one accomplished in ethics, ^an4-22-6-1

learned and eloquent, that wise one ^an4-22-6-2

is restrained when experiencing phenomena, ^an4-22-6-3

discerning the meaning with wisdom. ^an4-22-6-4

Gone beyond all things, ^an4-22-7-1

kind, eloquent, ^an4-22-7-2

they’ve given up birth and death, ^an4-22-7-3

and have completed the spiritual journey. ^an4-22-7-4

That’s who I call a senior, ^an4-22-8-1

who has no defilements. ^an4-22-8-2

With the ending of defilements, a mendicant ^an4-22-8-3

is declared a ‘senior’.” ^an4-22-8-4

### The World (Numbered Discourses 4.23) ^an4-23-0

“Mendicants, the world has been understood by the Realized One; ^an4-23-1-1

and he is detached from the world. ^an4-23-1-2

The origin of the world has been understood by the Realized One; ^an4-23-1-3

and he has given up the origin of the world. ^an4-23-1-4

The cessation of the world has been understood by the Realized One; ^an4-23-1-5

and he has realized the cessation of the world. ^an4-23-1-6

The practice that leads to the cessation of the world has been understood by the Realized One; ^an4-23-1-7

and he has developed the practice that leads to the cessation of the world. ^an4-23-1-8

In this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—whatever is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind, all that has been understood by the Realized One. ^an4-23-2-1

That’s why he’s called the ‘Realized One’. ^an4-23-2-2

From the night when the Realized One awakens to the supreme perfect awakening until the night he becomes fully extinguished—in the element of extinguishment with no residue—everything he speaks, says, and expresses is real, not otherwise. ^an4-23-3-1

That’s why he’s called the ‘Realized One’. ^an4-23-3-2

The Realized One does as he says, and says as he does. ^an4-23-4-1

Since this is so, ^an4-23-4-2

that’s why he’s called the ‘Realized One’. ^an4-23-4-3

In this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—the Realized One is the vanquisher, the unvanquished, the universal seer, the wielder of power. ^an4-23-5-1

That’s why he’s called the ‘Realized One’. ^an4-23-5-2

Directly knowing the whole world as it is, ^an4-23-6-1

and everything in it, ^an4-23-6-2

he is detached from the whole world, ^an4-23-6-3

uninvolved with the whole world. ^an4-23-6-4

That attentive one is the vanquisher of all ^an4-23-7-1

who is released from all ties. ^an4-23-7-2

He has reached ultimate peace: ^an4-23-7-3

extinguishment, fearing nothing from any quarter. ^an4-23-7-4

He is the Buddha, with defilements ended, ^an4-23-8-1

untroubled, with doubts cut off. ^an4-23-8-2

He has attained the end of all deeds, ^an4-23-8-3

freed with the end of attachments. ^an4-23-8-4

That Blessed One is the Buddha, ^an4-23-9-1

he is the supreme lion, ^an4-23-9-2

in all the world with its gods, ^an4-23-9-3

he turns the divine wheel. ^an4-23-9-4

And so those gods and humans, ^an4-23-10-1

who have gone to the Buddha for refuge, ^an4-23-10-2

come together and revere him, ^an4-23-10-3

great of heart and intrepid: ^an4-23-10-4

‘Tamed, he is the best of tamers, ^an4-23-11-1

peaceful, he is the seer among the peaceful, ^an4-23-11-2

liberated, he is the foremost of liberators, ^an4-23-11-3

crossed over, he is the most excellent of guides across.’ ^an4-23-11-4

And so they revere him, ^an4-23-12-1

great of heart and intrepid. ^an4-23-12-2

In the world with its gods, ^an4-23-12-3

he has no rival.” ^an4-23-12-4

### At Kāḷaka’s Monastery (Numbered Discourses 4.24) ^an4-24-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāketa, in Kāḷaka’s monastery. ^an4-24-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-24-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an4-24-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-24-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an4-24-1-5

“In this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—whatever is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: that I know. ^an4-24-2-1

In this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—whatever is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: that I have insight into. ^an4-24-3-1

That has been known by a Realized One, but a Realized One is not subject to it. ^an4-24-3-2

If I were to say that ‘I do not know … the world with its gods’, I would be lying. ^an4-24-4-1

If I were to say that ‘I both know and do not know … the world with its gods’, that would be just the same. ^an4-24-5-1

If I were to say that ‘I neither know nor do not know … the world with its gods’, that would be my fault. ^an4-24-6-1

So a Realized One sees what is to be seen, but does not conceive what is seen, does not conceive what is unseen, does not conceive what is to be seen, and does not conceive a seer. ^an4-24-7-1

He hears what is to be heard, but does not conceive what is heard, does not conceive what is unheard, does not conceive what is to be heard, and does not conceive a hearer. ^an4-24-7-2

He thinks what is to be thought, but does not conceive what is thought, does not conceive what is not thought, does not conceive what is to be thought, and does not conceive a thinker. ^an4-24-7-3

He knows what is to be known, but does not conceive what is known, does not conceive what is unknown, does not conceive what is to be known, and does not conceive a knower. ^an4-24-7-4

Since a Realized One is unaffected in the midst of things that ought be seen, heard, thought, and known, he is the unaffected one. ^an4-24-7-5

And I say that there is no better or finer poise than this. ^an4-24-7-6

Such a one does not take anything ^an4-24-8-1

seen, heard, or thought to be ultimately true or false. ^an4-24-8-2

But others get attached, thinking it’s the truth, ^an4-24-8-3

limited by their preconceptions. ^an4-24-8-4

Since they’ve seen this dart ^an4-24-9-1

to which people are attached and cling, ^an4-24-9-2

saying, ‘I know, I see, that’s how it is’, ^an4-24-9-3

the Realized Ones have no attachments.” ^an4-24-9-4

### The Spiritual Life (Numbered Discourses 4.25) ^an4-25-0

“Mendicants, this spiritual life is not lived for the sake of fawning or flattering people, nor for the benefit of possessions, honor, or popularity, nor for the benefit of winning debates, nor thinking, ‘So let people know about me!’ ^an4-25-1-1

This spiritual life is lived for the sake of restraint, giving up, fading away, and cessation. ^an4-25-1-2

The Buddha taught the spiritual life ^an4-25-2-1

not because of tradition, ^an4-25-2-2

but for the sake of restraint and giving up, ^an4-25-2-3

and because its objective is extinguishment. ^an4-25-2-4

This is the path followed by the big-hearted, ^an4-25-2-5

the great seers. ^an4-25-2-6

Those who practice it ^an4-25-3-1

as it was taught by the Buddha, ^an4-25-3-2

doing the teacher’s bidding, ^an4-25-3-3

make an end of suffering.” ^an4-25-3-4

### Deceivers (Numbered Discourses 4.26) ^an4-26-0

“Mendicants, those mendicants who are deceivers and flatterers, pompous and fake, insolent, and scattered: those mendicants are no followers of mine. ^an4-26-1-1

They’ve left this teaching and training, and they don’t achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an4-26-1-2

But those mendicants who are genuine, not flatterers, attentive, amenable, and serene: those mendicants are followers of mine. ^an4-26-1-3

They haven’t left this teaching and training, and they achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an4-26-1-4

Those who are deceivers and flatterers, <j>pompous and fake, ^an4-26-2-1

insolent and scattered: ^an4-26-2-2

these don’t grow in the teaching ^an4-26-2-3

that was taught by the perfected Buddha. ^an4-26-2-4

But those who are genuine, not flatterers, attentive, ^an4-26-3-1

amenable, and serene: ^an4-26-3-2

these do grow in the teaching ^an4-26-3-3

that was taught by the perfected Buddha.” ^an4-26-3-4

### Contentment (Numbered Discourses 4.27) ^an4-27-0

“Mendicants, these four trifles are easy to find and are blameless. ^an4-27-1-1

What four? ^an4-27-1-2

Rag-robes … ^an4-27-1-3

A lump of almsfood … ^an4-27-1-4

Lodgings at the root of a tree … ^an4-27-1-5

Rancid urine as medicine … ^an4-27-1-6

These four trifles are easy to find and are blameless. ^an4-27-1-7

When a mendicant is content with trifles that are easy to find, they have one of the factors of the ascetic life, I say. ^an4-27-1-8

When you’re content with what’s blameless, ^an4-27-2-1

trifling, and easy to find, ^an4-27-2-2

you don’t get upset ^an4-27-2-3

about lodgings, robes, ^an4-27-2-4

food, and drink, ^an4-27-2-5

and you’re not obstructed anywhere. ^an4-27-2-6

These qualities are said to be ^an4-27-3-1

integral to the ascetic life. ^an4-27-3-2

They’re mastered by one who trains, ^an4-27-3-3

content and diligent.” ^an4-27-3-4

### The Noble Traditions (Numbered Discourses 4.28) ^an4-28-0

“Mendicants, these four noble traditions are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now, nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-28-1-1

What four? ^an4-28-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant is content with any kind of robe, and praises such contentment. They don’t employ improper solicitation for the sake of a robe. They don’t worry if they don’t get a robe. And if they do get a robe, they use it untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawback, and understanding the escape. ^an4-28-1-3

But they don’t glorify themselves or put others down on account of their contentment. ^an4-28-1-4

A mendicant who is deft, tireless, aware, and mindful in this is said to stand in the ancient, primordial noble tradition. ^an4-28-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant is content with any kind of almsfood … ^an4-28-2-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is content with any kind of lodgings … ^an4-28-3-1

Furthermore, a mendicant enjoys meditation and loves to meditate. They enjoy giving up and love to give up. ^an4-28-4-1

But they don’t glorify themselves or put down others on account of their love for meditation and giving up. ^an4-28-4-2

A mendicant who is deft, tireless, aware, and mindful in this is said to stand in the ancient, primordial noble tradition. ^an4-28-4-3

These four noble traditions are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-28-4-4

When a mendicant possesses these four noble traditions, if they live in the east they prevail over discontent, and discontent doesn’t prevail over them. ^an4-28-5-1

If they live in the west … ^an4-28-5-2

the north … ^an4-28-5-3

the south, they prevail over discontent, and discontent doesn’t prevail over them. ^an4-28-5-4

Why is that? ^an4-28-5-5

Because the attentive prevail over desire and discontent. ^an4-28-5-6

Discontent cannot prevail over the attentive; ^an4-28-6-1

the attentive are not prevailed over by discontent. ^an4-28-6-2

The attentive can prevail over discontent, ^an4-28-6-3

for the attentive are those who prevail over discontent. ^an4-28-6-4

Who can hold back the dispeller, ^an4-28-7-1

who’s cast aside all deeds? ^an4-28-7-2

Like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, ^an4-28-7-3

who is worthy to criticize them? ^an4-28-7-4

Even the gods praise them, ^an4-28-7-5

and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.” ^an4-28-7-6

### Footprints of the Dhamma (Numbered Discourses 4.29) ^an4-29-0

“Mendicants, these four footprints of the Dhamma are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-29-1-1

What four? ^an4-29-1-2

Contentment, good will, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an4-29-1-3

These four footprints of the Dhamma are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-29-4-2

You should live with contentment, ^an4-29-5-1

and a heart of good will, ^an4-29-5-2

mindful, with unified mind, ^an4-29-5-3

serene within.” ^an4-29-5-4

### Wanderers (Numbered Discourses 4.30) ^an4-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an4-30-1-1

Now at that time several very well-known wanderers were residing in the monastery of the wanderers on the bank of the Serpentine river. They included Annabhāra, Varadhara, Sakuludāyī, and other very well-known wanderers. ^an4-30-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the wanderer’s monastery on the banks of the Serpentine river, He sat down on the seat spread out, and said to the wanderers: ^an4-30-1-3

“Wanderers, these four footprints of the Dhamma are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-30-2-1

What four? ^an4-30-2-2

Contentment … ^an4-30-2-3

Good will … Right mindfulness … Right immersion … ^an4-30-2-4

These four footprints of the Dhamma are primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an4-30-2-5

Wanderers, if someone should say: ^an4-30-3-1

‘I’ll deny this Dhamma footprint of contentment, and describe a true ascetic or brahmin who covets sensual pleasures with acute lust.’ Then I’d say to them: ^an4-30-3-2

‘Let them come, speak, and discuss. We’ll see how powerful they are.’ ^an4-30-3-3

It is quite impossible to deny this Dhamma footprint of contentment, and point out a true ascetic or brahmin who covets sensual pleasures with acute lust. ^an4-30-3-4

If someone should say: ^an4-30-4-1

‘I’ll deny this Dhamma footprint of good will, and describe a true ascetic or brahmin who has ill will and malicious intent.’ Then I’d say to them: ^an4-30-4-2

‘Let them come, speak, and discuss. We’ll see how powerful they are.’ ^an4-30-4-3

It is quite impossible to deny this Dhamma footprint of good will, and point out a true ascetic or brahmin who has ill will and malicious intent. ^an4-30-4-4

If someone should say: ^an4-30-5-1

‘I’ll deny this Dhamma footprint of right mindfulness, and describe a true ascetic or brahmin who is unmindful, with no situational awareness.’ Then I’d say to them: ^an4-30-5-2

‘Let them come, speak, and discuss. We’ll see how powerful they are.’ ^an4-30-5-3

It is quite impossible to deny this Dhamma footprint of right mindfulness, and point out a true ascetic or brahmin who is unmindful, with no situational awareness. ^an4-30-5-4

If someone should say: ^an4-30-6-1

‘I’ll deny this Dhamma footprint of right immersion, and describe a true ascetic or brahmin who is scattered, with straying mind.’ Then I’d say to them: ^an4-30-6-2

‘Let them come, speak, and discuss. We’ll see how powerful they are.’ ^an4-30-6-3

It is quite impossible to deny this Dhamma footprint of right immersion, and point out a true ascetic or brahmin who is scattered, with straying mind. ^an4-30-6-4

If anyone imagines they can criticize and reject these four footprints of the Dhamma, they deserve rebuttal and criticism on four legitimate grounds in this very life. ^an4-30-7-1

What four? ^an4-30-7-2

If you reject the Dhamma footprint of contentment, then you must honor and praise those ascetics and brahmins who covet sensual pleasures with acute lust. ^an4-30-7-3

If you reject the Dhamma footprint of good will, you must honor and praise those ascetics and brahmins who have ill will and malicious intent. ^an4-30-7-4

If you reject the Dhamma footprint of right mindfulness, then you must honor and praise those ascetics and brahmins who are unmindful, with no situational awareness. ^an4-30-7-5

If you reject the Dhamma footprint of right immersion, you must honor and praise those ascetics and brahmins who are scattered, with straying minds. ^an4-30-7-6

If anyone imagines they can criticize and reject these four footprints of the Dhamma, they deserve rebuttal and criticism on four legitimate grounds in this very life. ^an4-30-8-1

Even those who were loud and bold advocates of the doctrines of no-cause, inaction, and nihilism didn’t imagine that these four footprints of the Dhamma should be criticized or rejected. ^an4-30-8-2

Why is that? ^an4-30-8-3

For fear of being blamed, provoked, and faulted. ^an4-30-8-4

One who has good will, ever mindful, ^an4-30-9-1

serene within, ^an4-30-9-2

training to remove desire, ^an4-30-9-3

is called ‘a diligent one’.” ^an4-30-9-4

### Situations (Numbered Discourses 4.31) ^an4-31-0

“Mendicants, there are these four situations. When these situations come about, any god or human who takes advantage of them will soon acquire great and abundant wealth. ^an4-31-1-1

What four? ^an4-31-1-2

Living in a suitable region, relying on true persons, being rightly resolved in oneself, and past merit. ^an4-31-1-3

These are the four situations. When these situations come about, any god or human who takes advantage of them will soon acquire great and abundant wealth. ^an4-31-1-4

When a man lives in a suitable region, ^an4-31-2-1

making friends with noble ones, ^an4-31-2-2

possessing right resolve, ^an4-31-2-3

and having merit from the past, ^an4-31-2-4

grain, riches, fame, reputation, ^an4-31-2-5

and happiness come to them.” ^an4-31-2-6
