### Inclusion (Numbered Discourses 4.32) ^an4-32-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of being inclusive. ^an4-32-1-1

What four? ^an4-32-1-2

Giving, kindly words, taking care, and equality. ^an4-32-1-3

These are the four ways of being inclusive. ^an4-32-1-4

Giving and kindly words, ^an4-32-2-1

taking care here, ^an4-32-2-2

and treating equally in worldly conditions, ^an4-32-2-3

as they deserve in each case. ^an4-32-2-4

These ways of being inclusive in the world ^an4-32-2-5

are like a moving chariot’s linchpin. ^an4-32-2-6

If there were no such ways of being inclusive, ^an4-32-3-1

neither mother nor father ^an4-32-3-2

would be respected and honored ^an4-32-3-3

for what they’ve done for their children. ^an4-32-3-4

But since these ways of being inclusive do exist, ^an4-32-4-1

the astute do regard them well, ^an4-32-4-2

so they achieve greatness ^an4-32-4-3

and are praised.” ^an4-32-4-4

### The Lion (Numbered Discourses 4.33) ^an4-33-0

“Mendicants, towards evening the lion, king of beasts, emerges from his den, ^an4-33-1-1

yawns, ^an4-33-1-2

surveys the four quarters, ^an4-33-1-3

and roars his lion’s roar three times. ^an4-33-1-4

Then he sets out on the hunt. ^an4-33-1-5

And the animals who hear the roar of the lion, king of beasts, are typically filled with fear, awe, and terror. ^an4-33-1-6

They return to their lairs, be they in a hole, the water, or a wood; and the birds take to the air. ^an4-33-1-7

Even the royal elephants, bound with strong harnesses in the villages, towns, and capital cities, break apart their bonds, and urinate and defecate in terror as they flee here and there. ^an4-33-1-8

That’s how powerful is the lion, king of beasts, over animals, how illustrious and mighty. ^an4-33-1-9

In the same way, when a Realized One arises in the world—perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed—he teaches the Dhamma: ^an4-33-2-1

‘Such is substantial reality, such is the origin of substantial reality, such is the cessation of substantial reality, such is the practice that leads to the cessation of substantial reality.’ ^an4-33-2-2

Now, there are gods who are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy, lasting long in their divine palaces. When they hear this teaching by the Realized One, they’re typically filled with fear, awe, and terror. ^an4-33-2-3

‘Oh no! It turns out we’re impermanent, though we thought we were permanent! ^an4-33-2-4

It turns out we don’t last, though we thought we were everlasting! ^an4-33-2-5

It turns out we’re transient, though we thought we were eternal! ^an4-33-2-6

It turns out that we’re impermanent, not lasting, transient, and included within substantial reality.’ ^an4-33-2-7

That’s how powerful is the Realized One in the world with its gods, how illustrious and mighty. ^an4-33-2-8

The Buddha, the unrivaled teacher ^an4-33-3-1

in all the world with its gods, ^an4-33-3-2

rolls forth the Wheel of Dhamma ^an4-33-3-3

from his own insight: ^an4-33-3-4

substantial reality, its cessation, ^an4-33-4-1

the origin of substantial reality, ^an4-33-4-2

and the noble eightfold path ^an4-33-4-3

that leads to the stilling of suffering. ^an4-33-4-4

And then the long-lived gods, ^an4-33-5-1

so beautiful and famous, ^an4-33-5-2

are afraid and full of terror, ^an4-33-5-3

like the other beasts when they hear a lion. ^an4-33-5-4

‘We haven’t passed beyond substantial reality! ^an4-33-6-1

It turns out we’re impermanent!’ ^an4-33-6-2

So they say when they hear the word ^an4-33-6-3

of the perfected one, free and unaffected.” ^an4-33-6-4

### The Best Kinds of Confidence (Numbered Discourses 4.34) ^an4-34-0

“Mendicants, these four kinds of confidence are the best. ^an4-34-1-1

What four? ^an4-34-1-2

The Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, is said to be the best of all sentient beings—be they footless, with two feet, four feet, or many feet; with form or formless; with perception or without perception or with neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-34-1-3

Those who have confidence in the Buddha have confidence in the best. ^an4-34-1-4

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an4-34-1-5

The noble eightfold path is said to be the best of all conditioned things. ^an4-34-2-1

Those who have confidence in the noble eightfold path have confidence in the best. ^an4-34-2-2

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an4-34-2-3

Fading away is said to be the best of all things whether conditioned or unconditioned. That is, the quelling of vanity, the removing of thirst, the abolishing of clinging, the breaking of the round, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^an4-34-3-1

Those who have confidence in the teaching of fading away have confidence in the best. ^an4-34-3-2

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an4-34-3-3

The Saṅgha of the Realized One’s disciples is said to be the best of all communities and groups. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-34-4-1

Those who have confidence in the Saṅgha have confidence in the best. ^an4-34-4-2

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an4-34-4-3

These are the four best kinds of confidence. ^an4-34-4-4

For those who, knowing the best teaching, ^an4-34-5-1

base their confidence on the best— ^an4-34-5-2

confident in the best Awakened One, ^an4-34-5-3

supremely worthy of a religious donation; ^an4-34-5-4

confident in the best teaching, ^an4-34-6-1

the bliss of fading and stilling; ^an4-34-6-2

confident in the best Saṅgha, ^an4-34-6-3

the supreme field of merit— ^an4-34-6-4

giving gifts to the best, ^an4-34-7-1

the best of merit grows: ^an4-34-7-2

the best lifespan, beauty, ^an4-34-7-3

fame, reputation, happiness, and strength. ^an4-34-7-4

An intelligent person gives to the best, ^an4-34-8-1

settled on the best teaching. ^an4-34-8-2

When they become a god or human, ^an4-34-8-3

they rejoice at reaching the best.” ^an4-34-8-4

### With Vassakāra (Numbered Discourses 4.35) ^an4-35-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an4-35-1-1

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, a chief minister of Magadha, went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-35-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an4-35-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, when someone has four qualities we describe him as a great man with great wisdom. ^an4-35-2-1

What four? ^an4-35-2-2

They are very learned in diverse fields of learning. ^an4-35-2-3

They understand the meaning of diverse statements, saying: ‘This is what that statement means; that is what this statement means.’ ^an4-35-2-4

They are mindful, able to remember and recollect what was said and done long ago. ^an4-35-2-5

They are deft and tireless in household duties, understanding how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an4-35-2-6

When someone possesses these four qualities we describe him as a great man with great wisdom. ^an4-35-2-7

If the worthy Gotama agrees with me, please say so. ^an4-35-2-8

If he disagrees, please say so.” ^an4-35-2-9

“Brahmin, I neither agree nor disagree with you, ^an4-35-3-1

but when someone has four qualities I describe him as a great man with great wisdom. ^an4-35-3-2

What four? ^an4-35-3-3

It’s when someone practices for the welfare and happiness of the people. ^an4-35-3-4

They’ve established many people in the noble system, that is, the principles of goodness and skillfulness. ^an4-35-3-5

They think what they want to think, and don’t think what they don’t want to think. ^an4-35-3-6

They consider what they want to consider, and don’t consider what they don’t want to consider. ^an4-35-3-7

Thus they have achieved mental mastery of the paths of thought. ^an4-35-3-8

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-35-3-9

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-35-3-10

Brahmin, I neither agree nor disagree with you, ^an4-35-3-11

but when someone possesses these four qualities I describe him as a great man with great wisdom.” ^an4-35-3-12

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing! ^an4-35-4-1

How well said this was by the worthy Gotama! ^an4-35-4-2

And we will remember the worthy Gotama as someone who possesses these four qualities. ^an4-35-4-3

For the worthy Gotama practices for the welfare and happiness of the people … ^an4-35-4-4

The worthy Gotama has achieved mental mastery of the paths of thought. ^an4-35-4-7

The worthy Gotama gets the four absorptions … when he wants, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-35-4-8

The worthy Gotama has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. He lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^an4-35-4-9

“Your words are clearly invasive and intrusive, brahmin. ^an4-35-5-1

Nevertheless, I will answer you. ^an4-35-5-2

For I do practice for the welfare and happiness of the people … ^an4-35-5-3

I have achieved mental mastery of the paths of thought. ^an4-35-5-6

I do get the four absorptions … when I want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-35-5-7

I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-35-5-8

He discovered release from the snare of death ^an4-35-6-1

for all beings, ^an4-35-6-2

and explained the system of the teaching ^an4-35-6-3

for the welfare of gods and humans. ^an4-35-6-4

When they see him or hear him, ^an4-35-6-5

many people become confident. ^an4-35-6-6

He is skilled in what is the path <j>and what is not the path, ^an4-35-7-1

he has completed the task <j>and is free of defilements. ^an4-35-7-2

The Buddha, bearing his final body, ^an4-35-7-3

is called ‘a great man, of great wisdom’.” ^an4-35-7-4

### Doṇa (Numbered Discourses 4.36) ^an4-36-0

At one time the Buddha was traveling along the road between Ukkaṭṭhā and Setavyā, ^an4-36-1-1

as was the brahmin Doṇa. ^an4-36-1-2

Doṇa saw that the Buddha’s footprints had thousand-spoked wheels, with rims and hubs, complete in every detail. ^an4-36-1-3

It occurred to him, ^an4-36-1-4

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^an4-36-1-5

Surely these couldn’t be the footprints of a human being?” ^an4-36-1-6

The Buddha had left the road and sat at the root of a tree cross-legged, setting his body straight, and bringing mindfulness to the present. ^an4-36-1-7

Then Doṇa, following the Buddha’s footprints, saw him sitting at the tree root—impressive and inspiring, with peaceful faculties and heart, attained to the highest self-control and serenity, like an elephant with tamed, guarded, and controlled faculties. ^an4-36-1-8

He went up to the Buddha and said to him: ^an4-36-1-9

“Sir, might you be a god?” ^an4-36-2-1

“I will not be a god, brahmin.” ^an4-36-2-2

“Might you be a centaur?” ^an4-36-2-3

“I will not be a centaur.” ^an4-36-2-4

“Might you be a spirit?” ^an4-36-2-5

“I will not be a spirit.” ^an4-36-2-6

“Might you be a human?” ^an4-36-2-7

“I will not be a human.” ^an4-36-2-8

“When asked whether you might be a god, centaur, spirit, or human, ^an4-36-3-1

you answer that you will not be any of these. ^an4-36-3-2

So what then might you be?” ^an4-36-3-9

“Brahmin, if I had not given up defilements I might have become a god … a centaur … a spirit … or a human. But I have given up those defilements, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them so they are unable to arise in the future. ^an4-36-4-1

Suppose there was a blue water lily, or a pink or white lotus. Though it sprouted and grew in the water, it would rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to it. ^an4-36-4-3

In the same way, though I was born and grew up in the world, I live having mastered the world, unsullied by the world. ^an4-36-4-4

Remember me, brahmin, as a Buddha. ^an4-36-4-5

I could have been reborn as a god, ^an4-36-5-1

or as a centaur flying through the sky. ^an4-36-5-2

I could have become a spirit, ^an4-36-5-3

or returned as a human. ^an4-36-5-4

But I’ve ended those defilements, ^an4-36-5-5

they’re blown away and mown down. ^an4-36-5-6

Like a graceful lotus, ^an4-36-6-1

to which water does not cling, ^an4-36-6-2

the world doesn’t cling to me, ^an4-36-6-3

and so, brahmin, I am a Buddha.” ^an4-36-6-4

### Non-decline (Numbered Discourses 4.37) ^an4-37-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has four qualities can’t decline, and has drawn near to extinguishment. ^an4-37-1-1

What four? ^an4-37-1-2

A mendicant is accomplished in ethics, guards the sense doors, eats in moderation, and is dedicated to wakefulness. ^an4-37-1-3

And how is a mendicant accomplished in ethics? ^an4-37-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-37-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is accomplished in ethics. ^an4-37-2-3

And how does a mendicant guard the sense doors? ^an4-37-3-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-37-3-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-37-3-3

Hearing a sound with their ears … ^an4-37-3-4

Smelling an odor with their nose … ^an4-37-3-5

Tasting a flavor with their tongue … ^an4-37-3-6

Encountering a touch with their body … ^an4-37-3-7

Knowing an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-37-3-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-37-3-9

That’s how a mendicant guards the sense doors. ^an4-37-3-10

And how does a mendicant eat in moderation? ^an4-37-4-1

It’s when a mendicant reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^an4-37-4-2

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^an4-37-4-3

That’s how a mendicant eats in moderation. ^an4-37-4-4

And how is a mendicant dedicated to wakefulness? ^an4-37-5-1

It’s when a mendicant practices walking and sitting meditation by day, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^an4-37-5-2

In the first watch of the night, they continue to practice walking and sitting meditation. ^an4-37-5-3

In the middle watch, they lie down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^an4-37-5-4

In the last watch, they get up and continue to practice walking and sitting meditation, purifying their mind from obstacles. ^an4-37-5-5

This is how a mendicant is dedicated to wakefulness. ^an4-37-5-6

A mendicant who possesses these four qualities can’t decline, and has drawn near to extinguishment. ^an4-37-5-7

Established in ethics, ^an4-37-6-1

restrained in the sense faculties, ^an4-37-6-2

eating in moderation, ^an4-37-6-3

and dedicated to wakefulness; ^an4-37-6-4

a mendicant lives like this, with keen energy, ^an4-37-7-1

tireless all night and day, ^an4-37-7-2

developing skillful qualities, ^an4-37-7-3

for the sake of sanctuary from the yoke. ^an4-37-7-4

A mendicant who loves diligence, ^an4-37-8-1

seeing fear in negligence, ^an4-37-8-2

can’t decline, ^an4-37-8-3

and has drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an4-37-8-4

### Withdrawn (Numbered Discourses 4.38) ^an4-38-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth, has totally given up searching, has stilled the physical process, and is said to be ‘withdrawn’. ^an4-38-1-1

And how has a mendicant cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth? ^an4-38-1-2

Different ascetics and brahmins have different idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth. For example: ^an4-38-1-3

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists. ^an4-38-1-4

A mendicant has cast out, cast aside, thrown out, discarded, let go of, given up, and relinquished all these. ^an4-38-1-5

That’s how a mendicant has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth. ^an4-38-1-6

And how has a mendicant totally given up searching? ^an4-38-2-1

It’s when they’ve given up searching for sensual pleasures, for continued existence, and for a spiritual path. ^an4-38-2-2

That’s how a mendicant has totally given up searching. ^an4-38-2-3

And how has a mendicant stilled the physical process? ^an4-38-3-1

It’s when, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an4-38-3-2

That’s how a mendicant has stilled the physical process. ^an4-38-3-3

And how is a mendicant withdrawn? ^an4-38-4-1

It’s when they’ve given up the conceit ‘I am’, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an4-38-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is withdrawn. ^an4-38-4-3

A mendicant has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth, has totally given up searching, has stilled the physical process, and is said to be ‘withdrawn’. ^an4-38-4-4

The search for sensual pleasures, for a good rebirth, ^an4-38-5-1

and the search for a spiritual path; ^an4-38-5-2

the holding tight to the thought, ‘this is the truth’, ^an4-38-5-3

and the mass of grounds for views— ^an4-38-5-4

for one detached from all lusts, ^an4-38-6-1

freed by the ending of craving, ^an4-38-6-2

that searching has been relinquished, ^an4-38-6-3

and those grounds for views eradicated. ^an4-38-6-4

That mendicant is peaceful and mindful, ^an4-38-7-1

tranquil and undefeated. ^an4-38-7-2

And when they’re awakened <j>by comprehending conceit, ^an4-38-7-3

they’re called ‘withdrawn’.” ^an4-38-7-4

### With Ujjaya (Numbered Discourses 4.39) ^an4-39-0

Then Ujjaya the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-39-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an4-39-1-2

“Does the worthy Gotama praise sacrifice?” ^an4-39-1-3

“Brahmin, I don’t praise all sacrifices. ^an4-39-1-4

Nor do I criticize all sacrifices. ^an4-39-1-5

Take the kind of sacrifice where cattle, goats and sheep, chickens and pigs, and various kinds of creatures are slaughtered. ^an4-39-1-6

I criticize that kind of violent sacrifice. ^an4-39-1-7

Why is that? ^an4-39-1-8

Because neither perfected ones nor those who are on the path to perfection will attend such a violent sacrifice. ^an4-39-1-9

But take the kind of sacrifice where cattle, goats and sheep, chickens and pigs, and various kinds of creatures are not slaughtered. ^an4-39-2-1

I praise that kind of non-violent sacrifice; for example, a regular gift as an propitious sacrifice. ^an4-39-2-2

Why is that? ^an4-39-2-3

Because perfected ones and those who are on the path to perfection will attend such a non-violent sacrifice. ^an4-39-2-4

Horse sacrifice, human sacrifice, ^an4-39-3-1

the sacrifices of the ‘casting of the yoke-pin’, ^an4-39-3-2

the ‘royal soma drinking’, and the ‘unbarred’— ^an4-39-3-3

these huge violent sacrifices yield no great fruit. ^an4-39-3-4

The great sages of right comportment ^an4-39-4-1

don’t attend sacrifices ^an4-39-4-2

where goats, sheep, and cattle, ^an4-39-4-3

and various creatures are killed. ^an4-39-4-4

But the great sages of right comportment ^an4-39-5-1

do attend those sacrifices ^an4-39-5-2

that are non-violent and propitious, ^an4-39-5-3

where goats, sheep, and cattle, ^an4-39-5-4

and various creatures are not killed. ^an4-39-5-5

An intelligent person should sacrifice like this, ^an4-39-6-1

for this sacrifice is very fruitful. ^an4-39-6-2

For a sponsor of sacrifices like this, ^an4-39-6-3

things get better, not worse. ^an4-39-6-4

Such a sacrifice is truly abundant, ^an4-39-6-5

and even the deities are pleased.” ^an4-39-6-6

### With Udāyī (Numbered Discourses 4.40) ^an4-40-0

Then Udāyī the brahmin went up to the Buddha, … and asked him, ^an4-40-1-1

“Does the worthy Gotama praise sacrifice?” ^an4-40-1-2

“Brahmin, I don’t praise all sacrifices. ^an4-40-1-3

Nor do I criticize all sacrifices. … ^an4-40-1-4

Take the kind of sacrifice where cattle, goats and sheep, chickens and pigs, and various kinds of animals are slaughtered. ^an4-40-1-5

I don’t praise that kind of violent sacrifice. ^an4-40-1-6

But take the kind of sacrifice where cattle, goats and sheep, chickens and pigs, and various kinds of animals are not slaughtered. ^an4-40-2-1

I do praise that kind of non-violent sacrifice; for example, a regular gift as an propitious sacrifice. ^an4-40-2-2

Well-prepared and non-violent, ^an4-40-3-1

a sacrifice that’s allowable and timely, ^an4-40-3-2

is attended by ^an4-40-3-3

disciplined spiritual practitioners. ^an4-40-3-4

The Buddhas—who have cast off the veil from the world, ^an4-40-4-1

passing beyond time and rebirth— ^an4-40-4-2

praise this sacrifice, ^an4-40-4-3

as they are expert in sacrifice. ^an4-40-4-4

When you’ve prepared a suitable offering, ^an4-40-5-1

whether as sacrifice or for ancestors, ^an4-40-5-2

sacrifice it with confident heart, ^an4-40-5-3

in the fertile field of spiritual practitioners. ^an4-40-5-4

When it’s well-gotten, well-offered, <j>and well-sacrificed, ^an4-40-6-1

to those worthy of a religious donation, ^an4-40-6-2

a sacrifice is truly abundant, ^an4-40-6-3

and even the deities are pleased. ^an4-40-6-4

When an intelligent, faithful person, ^an4-40-7-1

sacrifices like this, with a mind of letting go, ^an4-40-7-2

that astute one is reborn ^an4-40-7-3

in a happy, pleasing world.” ^an4-40-7-4

### Ways of Developing Immersion Further (Numbered Discourses 4.41) ^an4-41-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of developing immersion further. ^an4-41-1-1

What four? ^an4-41-1-2

There is a way of developing immersion further that leads to blissful meditation in this life. ^an4-41-1-3

There is a way of developing immersion further that leads to gaining knowledge and vision. ^an4-41-1-4

There is a way of developing immersion further that leads to mindfulness and awareness. ^an4-41-1-5

There is a way of developing immersion further that leads to the ending of defilements. ^an4-41-1-6

And what is the way of developing immersion further that leads to blissful meditation in this life? ^an4-41-2-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an4-41-2-2

This is the way of developing immersion further that leads to blissful meditation in this life. ^an4-41-2-3

And what is the way of developing immersion further that leads to gaining knowledge and vision? ^an4-41-3-1

It’s when a mendicant applies their mind to the perception of light, focusing on the perception of day: ^an4-41-3-2

as by day, so by night; as by night, so by day. ^an4-41-3-3

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, they develop a mind that’s full of radiance. ^an4-41-3-4

This is the way of developing immersion further that leads to gaining knowledge and vision. ^an4-41-3-5

And what is the way of developing immersion further that leads to mindfulness and awareness? ^an4-41-4-1

It’s when a mendicant knows feelings as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an4-41-4-2

They know perceptions as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. They know thoughts as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an4-41-4-3

This is the way of developing immersion further that leads to mindfulness and awareness. ^an4-41-4-4

And what is the way of developing immersion further that leads to the ending of defilements? ^an4-41-5-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^an4-41-5-2

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^an4-41-5-3

Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the ending of feeling. ^an4-41-5-4

Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the ending of perception. ^an4-41-5-5

Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the ending of choices. ^an4-41-5-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^an4-41-5-7

This is the way of developing immersion further that leads to the ending of defilements. ^an4-41-5-8

These are the four ways of developing immersion further. ^an4-41-5-9

And it was in this connection that I said in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Puṇṇaka’: ^an4-41-5-10

‘Having appraised the world high and low, ^an4-41-6-1

there is nothing in the world that disturbs them. ^an4-41-6-2

Peaceful, unclouded, untroubled, <j>with no need for hope, ^an4-41-6-3

they’ve crossed over rebirth and old age, I declare.’” ^an4-41-6-4

### Ways of Answering Questions (Numbered Discourses 4.42) ^an4-42-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of answering questions. ^an4-42-1-1

What four? ^an4-42-1-2

There is a question that should be answered categorically. ^an4-42-1-3

There is a question that should be answered analytically. ^an4-42-1-4

There is a question that should be answered with a counter-question. ^an4-42-1-5

There is a question that should be set aside. ^an4-42-1-6

These are the four ways of answering questions. ^an4-42-1-7

One is stated categorically, ^an4-42-2-1

another analytically, ^an4-42-2-2

a third with a counter-question, ^an4-42-2-3

while a fourth is set aside. ^an4-42-2-4

That sort of mendicant, <j>who knows each of these, ^an4-42-3-1

in line with good principles, ^an4-42-3-2

is said to be skilled ^an4-42-3-3

in the four kinds of questions. ^an4-42-3-4

They’re intimidating, hard to defeat, ^an4-42-4-1

deep, and hard to crush. ^an4-42-4-2

They’re expert in both ^an4-42-4-3

what the meaning is and what it isn’t. ^an4-42-4-4

Shunning what is not the meaning, ^an4-42-5-1

an astute person grasps the meaning. ^an4-42-5-2

An attentive one, comprehending the meaning, ^an4-42-5-3

is said to be astute.” ^an4-42-5-4

### Valuing Anger (Numbered Discourses 4.43) ^an4-43-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-43-1-1

What four? ^an4-43-1-2

People who value anger, or denigration, or material things, or honor rather than the true teaching. ^an4-43-1-3

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-43-1-4

These four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-43-2-1

What four? ^an4-43-2-2

People who value the true teaching rather than anger, or denigration, or material things, or honor. ^an4-43-2-3

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-43-2-4

Mendicants who value anger and denigration, ^an4-43-3-1

possessions and honor, ^an4-43-3-2

don’t grow in the teaching ^an4-43-3-3

that was taught by the perfected Buddha. ^an4-43-3-4

But those who value the true teaching, ^an4-43-4-1

who have lived it, and are living it now, ^an4-43-4-2

these do grow in the teaching ^an4-43-4-3

that was taught by the perfected Buddha.” ^an4-43-4-4

### Valuing Anger (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.44) ^an4-44-0

“Mendicants, these four things oppose the true teaching. ^an4-44-1-1

What four? ^an4-44-1-2

Valuing anger, denigration, material things, and honor rather than the true teaching. ^an4-44-1-3

These are the four things that oppose the true teaching. ^an4-44-1-4

These four things are the true teaching. ^an4-44-2-1

What four? ^an4-44-2-2

Valuing the true teaching rather than anger, denigration, material things, and honor. ^an4-44-2-3

These are the four things that are the true teaching. ^an4-44-2-4

A mendicant who values anger and denigration, ^an4-44-3-1

possessions and honor, ^an4-44-3-2

doesn’t grow in the true teaching, ^an4-44-3-3

like a rotten seed in a good field. ^an4-44-3-4

But those who value the true teaching, ^an4-44-4-1

who have lived it, and are living it now, ^an4-44-4-2

these do grow in the teaching, ^an4-44-4-3

like well-watered herbs.” ^an4-44-4-4

### With Rohitassa (Numbered Discourses 4.45) ^an4-45-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-45-1-1

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Rohitassa, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an4-45-1-2

“Sir, is it possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn?” ^an4-45-2-1

“Reverend, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn.” ^an4-45-2-2

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^an4-45-3-1

how well said this was by the Buddha. ^an4-45-3-2

Once upon a time, I was a seer called Rohitassa, son of the benefactors. I was a sky-walker with psychic powers. ^an4-45-4-1

I was as fast as a light arrow easily shot across the shadow of a palm tree by a well-trained expert archer with a strong bow. ^an4-45-4-2

My stride was such that it could span from the eastern ocean to the western ocean. ^an4-45-4-3

Having such speed and stride, this wish came to me: ^an4-45-4-4

‘I will reach the end of the world by traveling.’ ^an4-45-4-5

I traveled for my whole lifespan of a hundred years—pausing only to eat and drink, go to the toilet, and sleep to dispel weariness—and I passed away along the way, never reaching the end of the world. ^an4-45-4-6

It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing, ^an4-45-5-1

how well said this was by the Buddha.” ^an4-45-5-2

“Reverend, I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn. ^an4-45-6-1

But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world. ^an4-45-6-2

For it is in this fathom-long carcass with its perception and mind that I describe the world, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation. ^an4-45-6-3

The end of the world can never ^an4-45-7-1

be reached by traveling. ^an4-45-7-2

But without reaching the end of the world, ^an4-45-7-3

there’s no release from suffering. ^an4-45-7-4

So an intelligent person, understanding the world, ^an4-45-8-1

has completed the spiritual journey, <j>and gone to the end of the world. ^an4-45-8-2

Assuaged, knowing the end of the world, ^an4-45-8-3

they do not hope for this world or the next.” ^an4-45-8-4

### With Rohitassa (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.46) ^an4-46-0

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an4-46-1-1

“Tonight, the glorious godling Rohitassa, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an4-46-1-2

‘Sir, is it possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by traveling to a place where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn?’ … ^an4-46-1-3

<em>(The rest is the same as the previous discourse, AN 4.45.)</em> ^an4-46-1-4

### Very Far Apart (Numbered Discourses 4.47) ^an4-47-0

“Mendicants, these four things are very far apart. ^an4-47-1-1

What four? ^an4-47-1-2

The firmament and the earth. … ^an4-47-1-3

The near and the far shore of the ocean. … ^an4-47-1-5

Where the sun rises and where it sets. … ^an4-47-1-7

The teaching of the virtuous and the teaching of the wicked. … ^an4-47-1-9

These are the four things very far apart. ^an4-47-1-11

The firmament is far from the earth; ^an4-47-2-1

they say the other shore of the ocean is far; ^an4-47-2-2

and where the sun rises is far ^an4-47-2-3

from where that beacon sets. ^an4-47-2-4

But even further apart than that, they say, ^an4-47-2-5

is the teaching of the virtuous from the wicked. ^an4-47-2-6

The company of the virtuous is reliable; ^an4-47-3-1

as long as it remains, it stays the same. ^an4-47-3-2

But the company of the wicked is fickle, ^an4-47-3-3

and so the teaching of the virtuous <j>is far from the wicked.” ^an4-47-3-4

### With Visākha, Pañcāli’s Son (Numbered Discourses 4.48) ^an4-48-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-48-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Visākha, Pañcāli’s son, was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk. His words were polished, clear, articulate, expressing the meaning, comprehensive, and independent. ^an4-48-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall. He sat down on the seat spread out, ^an4-48-1-3

and addressed the mendicants, ^an4-48-1-4

“Mendicants, who was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk?” ^an4-48-2-1

“Sir, it was Venerable Visākha, Pañcāli’s son.” ^an4-48-2-2

Then the Buddha said to Visākha, ^an4-48-3-1

“Good, good, Visākha! ^an4-48-3-2

It’s good that you educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk, with words that are polished, clear, articulate, expressing the meaning, comprehensive, and independent. ^an4-48-3-3

Though an astute person is mixed up with fools, ^an4-48-4-1

they don’t know unless he speaks. ^an4-48-4-2

But when he speaks they know, ^an4-48-4-3

he’s teaching the state free of death. ^an4-48-4-4

He should speak and illustrate the teaching, ^an4-48-5-1

holding up the banner of the seers. ^an4-48-5-2

Words well spoken are the seers’ banner, ^an4-48-5-3

for the teaching is the banner of the seers.” ^an4-48-5-4

### Perversions (Numbered Discourses 4.49) ^an4-49-0

“Mendicants, there are these four perversions of perception, thought, and view. ^an4-49-1-1

What four? ^an4-49-1-2

Taking impermanence as permanence. ^an4-49-1-3

Taking suffering as happiness. ^an4-49-1-4

Taking not-self as self. ^an4-49-1-5

Taking ugliness as beauty. ^an4-49-1-6

These are the four perversions of perception, thought, and view. ^an4-49-1-7

There are these four corrections of perception, thought, and view. ^an4-49-2-1

What four? ^an4-49-2-2

Taking impermanence as impermanence. ^an4-49-2-3

Taking suffering as suffering. ^an4-49-2-4

Taking not-self as not-self. ^an4-49-2-5

Taking ugliness as ugliness. ^an4-49-2-6

These are the four corrections of perception, thought, and view. ^an4-49-2-7

Perceiving impermanence as permanence, ^an4-49-3-1

suffering as happiness, ^an4-49-3-2

not-self as self, ^an4-49-3-3

and ugliness as beauty— ^an4-49-3-4

sentient beings are ruined by wrong view, ^an4-49-3-5

deranged, out of their minds. ^an4-49-3-6

Yoked by Māra’s yoke, these people ^an4-49-4-1

find no sanctuary from the yoke. ^an4-49-4-2

Sentient beings continue to transmigrate, ^an4-49-4-3

with ongoing birth and death. ^an4-49-4-4

But when the Buddhas arise ^an4-49-5-1

in the world, those beacons ^an4-49-5-2

reveal this teaching, ^an4-49-5-3

that leads to the stilling of suffering. ^an4-49-5-4

When a wise person hears them, ^an4-49-6-1

they get their mind back. ^an4-49-6-2

Seeing impermanence as impermanence, ^an4-49-6-3

suffering as suffering, ^an4-49-6-4

not-self as not-self, ^an4-49-7-1

and ugliness as ugliness— ^an4-49-7-2

taking up right view, ^an4-49-7-3

they’ve risen above all suffering.” ^an4-49-7-4

### Corruptions (Numbered Discourses 4.50) ^an4-50-0

“Mendicants, these four corruptions obscure the sun and moon, so they don’t shine and glow and radiate. ^an4-50-1-1

What four? ^an4-50-1-2

Stormclouds … ^an4-50-1-3

Fog … ^an4-50-2-1

Smoke … ^an4-50-3-1

An eclipse of Rāhu, lord of titans … ^an4-50-4-1

These are four corruptions that obscure the sun and moon, so they don’t shine and glow and radiate. ^an4-50-4-2

In the same way, these four things corrupt ascetics and brahmins, so they don’t shine and glow and radiate. ^an4-50-5-1

What four? ^an4-50-5-2

There are some ascetics and brahmins who drink beer and wine, not refraining from drinking beer and wine. ^an4-50-5-3

This is the first thing that corrupts ascetics and brahmins … ^an4-50-5-4

There are some ascetics and brahmins who have sex, not refraining from sex. ^an4-50-6-1

This is the second thing that corrupts ascetics and brahmins … ^an4-50-6-2

There are some ascetics and brahmins who accept gold and currency, not refraining from receiving gold and currency. ^an4-50-7-1

This is the third thing that corrupts ascetics and brahmins … ^an4-50-7-2

There are some ascetics and brahmins who make a living the wrong way, not refraining from wrong livelihood. ^an4-50-8-1

This is the fourth thing that corrupts ascetics and brahmins … ^an4-50-8-2

These are four things that corrupt ascetics and brahmins, so they don’t shine and glow and radiate. ^an4-50-8-3

Some ascetics and brahmins ^an4-50-9-1

are plagued by greed and hate; ^an4-50-9-2

men shrouded by ignorance, ^an4-50-9-3

enjoying things that seem pleasant. ^an4-50-9-4

Drinking beer and wine, ^an4-50-10-1

having sex, ^an4-50-10-2

accepting currency and gold: ^an4-50-10-3

they’re ignorant. ^an4-50-10-4

Some ascetics and brahmins ^an4-50-10-5

make a living the wrong way. ^an4-50-10-6

These corruptions were spoken of ^an4-50-11-1

by the Buddha, Kinsman of the Sun. ^an4-50-11-2

When corrupted by these, ^an4-50-11-3

some ascetics and brahmins ^an4-50-11-4

don’t shine or glow. ^an4-50-11-5

Impure, dirty creatures, ^an4-50-11-6

swathed in darkness, ^an4-50-12-1

bondservants of craving, full of attachments, ^an4-50-12-2

swell the horrors of the charnel ground, ^an4-50-12-3

taking up future lives.” ^an4-50-12-4

### Overflowing Merit (Numbered Discourses 4.51) ^an4-51-0

At Sāvatthī. ^an4-51-1-1

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an4-51-1-2

What four? ^an4-51-1-3

When a mendicant enters and remains in a limitless immersion of heart while using a robe, the overflowing of merit for the donor is limitless … ^an4-51-1-4

When a mendicant enters and remains in a limitless immersion of heart while eating almsfood, the overflowing of merit for the donor is limitless … ^an4-51-2-1

When a mendicant enters and remains in a limitless immersion of heart while using lodgings, the overflowing of merit for the donor is limitless … ^an4-51-3-1

When a mendicant enters and remains in a limitless immersion of heart while using medicines and supplies for the sick, the overflowing of merit for the donor is limitless … ^an4-51-4-1

These are the four kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an4-51-4-2

When a noble disciple has these four kinds of overflowing merit and goodness, it’s not easy to grasp how much merit they have by saying that this is the extent of their overflowing merit … that leads to happiness. It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of merit. ^an4-51-5-1

It’s like trying to grasp how much water is in the ocean. It’s not easy to say how many gallons, how many hundreds, thousands, hundreds of thousands of gallons there are. It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of water. ^an4-51-6-1

In the same way, when a noble disciple has these four kinds of overflowing merit it’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of merit. ^an4-51-6-2

Hosts of people use the rivers, ^an4-51-7-1

and though the rivers are many, ^an4-51-7-2

all reach the great deep, the boundless ocean, ^an4-51-7-3

the cruel sea that’s home to precious gems. ^an4-51-7-4

In the same way, when a person gives <j>food, drink, and clothes; ^an4-51-8-1

and they’re a giver of beds, seats, and mats— ^an4-51-8-2

the streams of merit reach that astute person, ^an4-51-8-3

as the rivers bring their waters to the sea.” ^an4-51-8-4

### Overflowing Merit (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.52) ^an4-52-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an4-52-1-1

What four? ^an4-52-1-2

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^an4-52-1-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an4-52-1-4

This is the first kind of overflowing merit … ^an4-52-1-5

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the teaching: ^an4-52-2-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an4-52-2-2

This is the second kind of overflowing merit … ^an4-52-2-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^an4-52-3-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an4-52-3-2

This is the third kind of overflowing merit … ^an4-52-3-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an4-52-4-1

This is the fourth kind of overflowing merit … ^an4-52-4-2

These are the four kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an4-52-4-3

Whoever has faith in the Realized One, ^an4-52-5-1

unwavering and well grounded; ^an4-52-5-2

whose ethical conduct is good, ^an4-52-5-3

praised and loved by the noble ones; ^an4-52-5-4

who has confidence in the Saṅgha, ^an4-52-6-1

and correct view: ^an4-52-6-2

they’re said to be prosperous, ^an4-52-6-3

their life is not in vain. ^an4-52-6-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^an4-52-7-1

to faith, ethical behavior, ^an4-52-7-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^an4-52-7-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an4-52-7-4

### Living Together (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.53) ^an4-53-0

At one time the Buddha was traveling along the road between Madhurā and Verañjā, ^an4-53-1-1

as were several householders, both women and men. ^an4-53-1-2

The Buddha left the road and sat at the root of a tree, ^an4-53-1-3

where the householders saw him. ^an4-53-1-4

They went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^an4-53-1-5

“Householders, there are four ways of living together. ^an4-53-2-1

What four? ^an4-53-2-2

A male zombie living with a female zombie; ^an4-53-2-3

a male zombie living with a goddess; ^an4-53-2-4

a god living with a female zombie; ^an4-53-2-5

a god living with a goddess. ^an4-53-2-6

And how does a male zombie live with a female zombie? ^an4-53-3-1

It’s when the husband kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. He’s unethical, of bad character, living at home with his heart full of the stain of stinginess, abusing and insulting ascetics and brahmins. ^an4-53-3-2

And the wife is also … unethical, of bad character … ^an4-53-3-3

That’s how a male zombie lives with a female zombie. ^an4-53-3-4

And how does a male zombie live with a goddess? ^an4-53-4-1

It’s when the husband … is unethical, of bad character … ^an4-53-4-2

But the wife doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. She’s ethical, of good character, living at home with her heart rid of the stain of stinginess, not abusing and insulting ascetics and brahmins. ^an4-53-4-3

That’s how a male zombie lives with a goddess. ^an4-53-4-4

And how does a god live with a female zombie? ^an4-53-5-1

It’s when the husband … is ethical, of good character … ^an4-53-5-2

But the wife … is unethical, of bad character … ^an4-53-5-3

That’s how a god lives with a female zombie. ^an4-53-5-4

And how does a god live with a goddess? ^an4-53-6-1

It’s when the husband … is ethical, of good character … ^an4-53-6-2

And the wife is also … ethical, of good character … ^an4-53-6-3

That’s how a god lives with a goddess. ^an4-53-6-4

These are the four ways of living together. ^an4-53-6-5

When both are unethical, ^an4-53-7-1

miserly and abusive, ^an4-53-7-2

then wife and husband ^an4-53-7-3

live together as zombies. ^an4-53-7-4

When the husband is unethical, ^an4-53-8-1

miserly and abusive, ^an4-53-8-2

but the wife is ethical, ^an4-53-8-3

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an4-53-8-4

she’s a goddess living ^an4-53-8-5

with a zombie for a husband. ^an4-53-8-6

When the husband is ethical, ^an4-53-9-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an4-53-9-2

but the wife is unethical, ^an4-53-9-3

miserly and abusive, ^an4-53-9-4

she’s a zombie living ^an4-53-9-5

with a god for a husband. ^an4-53-9-6

When both are faithful and bountiful, ^an4-53-10-1

disciplined, living righteously, ^an4-53-10-2

then wife and husband ^an4-53-10-3

say nice things to each other. ^an4-53-10-4

Their needs are amply satisfied, ^an4-53-11-1

so they live at ease. ^an4-53-11-2

Their enemies are downhearted, ^an4-53-11-3

when both are equal in ethics. ^an4-53-11-4

Having practiced the teaching here, ^an4-53-12-1

both equal in precepts and observances, ^an4-53-12-2

they delight in the heavenly realm, ^an4-53-12-3

enjoying all the pleasures they desire.” ^an4-53-12-4

### Living Together (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.54) ^an4-54-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of living together. ^an4-54-1-1

What four? ^an4-54-1-2

A male zombie living with a female zombie; ^an4-54-1-3

a male zombie living with a goddess; ^an4-54-1-4

a god living with a female zombie; ^an4-54-1-5

a god living with a goddess. ^an4-54-1-6

And how does a male zombie live with a female zombie? ^an4-54-2-1

It’s when the husband kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct; he uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical; and he’s covetous, malicious, and has wrong view. He’s unethical, of bad character, living at home with his heart full of the stain of stinginess, abusing and insulting ascetics and brahmins. ^an4-54-2-2

And the wife is also … unethical, of bad character … ^an4-54-2-3

That’s how a male zombie lives with a female zombie. ^an4-54-2-4

And how does a male zombie live with a goddess? ^an4-54-3-1

It’s when the husband … is unethical, of bad character … ^an4-54-3-2

But the wife doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. She doesn’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And she’s contented, kind-hearted, with right view. She’s ethical, of good character, living at home with her heart rid of the stain of stinginess, not abusing and insulting ascetics and brahmins. ^an4-54-3-3

That’s how a male zombie lives with a goddess. ^an4-54-3-4

And how does a god live with a female zombie? ^an4-54-4-1

It’s when the husband … is ethical, of good character … ^an4-54-4-2

But the wife … is unethical, of bad character … ^an4-54-4-3

That’s how a god lives with a female zombie. ^an4-54-4-4

And how does a god live with a goddess? ^an4-54-5-1

It’s when the husband … is ethical, of good character … ^an4-54-5-2

And the wife is also … ethical, of good character … ^an4-54-5-3

That’s how a god lives with a goddess. ^an4-54-5-4

These are the four ways of living together.” … ^an4-54-5-5

### Equality (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.55) ^an4-55-0

So I have heard. ^an4-55-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^an4-55-1-2

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Nakula’s father, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an4-55-1-3

Then the householder Nakula’s father and the housewife Nakula’s mother went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Nakula’s father said to the Buddha, ^an4-55-1-4

“Sir, ever since we were both young, and Nakula’s mother was given to me in marriage, I can’t recall betraying her even in thought, still less in deed. ^an4-55-2-1

We want to see each other in both this life and the next.” ^an4-55-2-2

Then Nakula’s mother said to the Buddha, ^an4-55-2-3

“Sir, ever since we were both young, and I was given in marriage to Nakula’s father, I can’t recall betraying him even in thought, still less in deed. ^an4-55-2-4

We want to see each other in both this life and the next.” ^an4-55-2-5

“Householders, if wife and husband want to see each other in both this life and the next, they should be equals in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an4-55-3-1

When both are faithful and bountiful, ^an4-55-4-1

disciplined, living righteously, ^an4-55-4-2

then wife and husband ^an4-55-4-3

say nice things to each other. ^an4-55-4-4

Their needs are amply satisfied, ^an4-55-5-1

so they live at ease. ^an4-55-5-2

Their enemies are downhearted, ^an4-55-5-3

when both are equal in ethics. ^an4-55-5-4

Having practiced the teaching here, ^an4-55-6-1

both equal in precepts and observances, ^an4-55-6-2

they delight in the heavenly realm, ^an4-55-6-3

enjoying all the pleasures they desire.” ^an4-55-6-4

### Equality (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.56) ^an4-56-0

“Mendicants, if wife and husband want to see each other in both this life and the next, they should be equals in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. …” ^an4-56-1-1

### Suppavāsā (Numbered Discourses 4.57) ^an4-57-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Pajjanika. ^an4-57-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of Suppavāsā the Koliyan, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an4-57-1-2

Then Suppavāsā served and satisfied the Buddha with her own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an4-57-1-3

When the Buddha had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, she sat down to one side. ^an4-57-1-4

The Buddha said to her: ^an4-57-1-5

“Suppavāsā, when a noble disciple gives food, she gives the recipients four things. ^an4-57-2-1

What four? ^an4-57-2-2

Long life, beauty, happiness, and strength. ^an4-57-2-3

Giving long life, she has long life as a god or human. ^an4-57-2-4

Giving beauty, she has beauty as a god or human. ^an4-57-2-5

Giving happiness, she has happiness as a god or human. ^an4-57-2-6

Giving strength, she has strength as a god or human. ^an4-57-2-7

When a noble disciple gives food, she gives the recipients these four things. ^an4-57-2-8

When she gives well-prepared food, ^an4-57-3-1

pure, fine, and full of flavor, ^an4-57-3-2

that offering—given to the sincere, ^an4-57-3-3

who have good conduct, and are big-hearted— ^an4-57-3-4

joins merit to merit. It’s very fruitful, ^an4-57-3-5

and is praised by those who know the world. ^an4-57-3-6

Those who recall such sacrifices, ^an4-57-4-1

live in the world full of inspiration. ^an4-57-4-2

They’ve driven out the stain of stinginess, <j>root and all, ^an4-57-4-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an4-57-4-4

### Sudatta (Numbered Discourses 4.58) ^an4-58-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an4-58-1-1

“Householder, when a noble disciple gives food, they give the recipients four things. ^an4-58-2-1

What four? ^an4-58-2-2

Long life, beauty, happiness, and strength. ^an4-58-2-3

Giving long life, they have long life as a god or human. … ^an4-58-2-4

Giving beauty … happiness … strength … ^an4-58-2-5

When a noble disciple gives food, they give the recipients these four things. ^an4-58-2-6

Carefully giving food at the right time, ^an4-58-3-1

to those who are disciplined, <j>eating only what others give, ^an4-58-3-2

you provide them with four things: ^an4-58-3-3

long life, beauty, happiness, and strength. ^an4-58-3-4

A person who gives long life, beauty, ^an4-58-4-1

happiness, and strength, ^an4-58-4-2

has long life and fame ^an4-58-4-3

wherever they’re reborn.” ^an4-58-4-4

### Food (Numbered Discourses 4.59) ^an4-59-0

“Mendicants, when a donor gives food, they give the recipients four things. ^an4-59-1-1

What four? ^an4-59-1-2

Long life, beauty, happiness, and strength. …” ^an4-59-1-3

### Lay Practice (Numbered Discourses 4.60) ^an4-60-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an4-60-1-1

“Householder, when a noble disciple does four things they are practicing appropriately for a layperson, which brings fame and leads to heaven. ^an4-60-2-1

What four? ^an4-60-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple serves the mendicant Saṅgha with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an4-60-2-3

When a noble disciple does these four things they are practicing appropriately for a layperson, which brings fame and leads to heaven. ^an4-60-2-4

Those who are astute practice the way ^an4-60-3-1

that’s appropriate for laypeople. ^an4-60-3-2

They provide those who are ethical ^an4-60-3-3

and rightly comported with robes, ^an4-60-3-4

almsfood, lodgings, ^an4-60-4-1

and supplies for the sick. ^an4-60-4-2

Their merit always grows ^an4-60-4-3

by day and by night. ^an4-60-4-4

They pass on to a place in heaven, ^an4-60-4-5

having done excellent deeds.” ^an4-60-4-6

### Fitting Deeds (Numbered Discourses 4.61) ^an4-61-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an4-61-1-1

“Householder, these four things, which are likable, desirable, and agreeable are hard to get in the world. ^an4-61-2-1

What four? ^an4-61-2-2

The first thing is the wish: ‘May wealth come to me by legitimate means!’ ^an4-61-2-3

The second thing, having got wealth by legitimate means, is the wish: ‘May fame come to me, together with my family and teachers.’ ^an4-61-3-1

The third thing, having got wealth and fame, is the wish: ‘May I live long, keeping alive for a long time!’ ^an4-61-4-1

The fourth thing, having got wealth, fame, and long life, is the wish: ‘When my body breaks up, after death, may I be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm!’ ^an4-61-5-1

These are the four things, which are likable, desirable, and agreeable, but hard to get in the world. ^an4-61-5-2

These next four things lead to the getting of those four things. ^an4-61-6-1

What four? ^an4-61-6-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an4-61-6-3

And what is accomplishment in faith? ^an4-61-7-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an4-61-7-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an4-61-7-3

This is called accomplishment in faith. ^an4-61-7-4

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an4-61-8-1

It’s when a noble disciple doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an4-61-8-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an4-61-8-3

And what is accomplishment in generosity? ^an4-61-9-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an4-61-9-2

This is called accomplishment in generosity. ^an4-61-9-3

And what is accomplishment in wisdom? ^an4-61-10-1

When your heart is mastered by covetousness and immoral greed, you do what you shouldn’t, and fail to do what you should. ^an4-61-10-2

Your fame and happiness fall to dust. ^an4-61-10-3

When your heart is mastered by ill will … ^an4-61-10-4

dullness and drowsiness … ^an4-61-10-6

restlessness and remorse … ^an4-61-10-8

doubt, you do what you shouldn’t, and fail to do what you should. ^an4-61-10-10

Your fame and happiness fall to dust. ^an4-61-10-11

Knowing that ‘covetousness and immoral greed are corruptions of the mind’, that noble disciple gives them up. ^an4-61-11-1

Knowing that ‘ill will …’ … ^an4-61-11-2

‘dullness and drowsiness …’ … ^an4-61-11-3

‘restlessness and remorse …’ … ^an4-61-11-4

‘doubt is a corruption of the mind’, that noble disciple gives it up. ^an4-61-11-5

When a noble disciple has given up these things, ^an4-61-12-1

they’re called ‘a noble disciple of great wisdom, of widespread wisdom, who sees what matters, and is accomplished in wisdom’. ^an4-61-12-6

This is called accomplishment in wisdom. ^an4-61-12-7

These are the four things that lead to the getting of the four things, which are likable, desirable, and agreeable, but hard to get in the world. ^an4-61-12-8

There are four fitting deeds that a noble disciple does with the legitimate wealth he has earned by his efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. ^an4-61-13-1

What four? ^an4-61-13-2

To start with, with his legitimate wealth he makes himself happy and pleased, keeping himself properly happy. ^an4-61-13-3

He makes his mother and father happy … ^an4-61-13-4

He makes his children, partners, bondservants, workers, and staff happy … ^an4-61-13-5

He makes his friends and colleagues happy … ^an4-61-13-6

This is his first expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-13-7

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he defends himself against threats from such things as fire, flood, rulers, bandits, or unloved heirs. ^an4-61-14-1

He keeps himself safe. ^an4-61-14-2

This is his second expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-14-3

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he makes five spirit-offerings: ^an4-61-15-1

to relatives, guests, ancestors, king, and deities. ^an4-61-15-2

This is his third expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-15-3

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he establishes an uplifting religious donation for ascetics and brahmins—those who refrain from intoxication and negligence, are settled in patience and gentleness, and who tame, calm, and extinguish themselves—that’s conducive to heaven, ripens in happiness, and leads to heaven. ^an4-61-16-1

This is his fourth expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-16-2

’these are the four fitting deeds that a noble disciple does with the legitimate wealth he has earned by his efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. ^an4-61-17-1

Whatever wealth is spent on something other than these four fitting deeds is said to not be expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-17-2

But whatever wealth is spent on these four fitting deeds is said to be expenditure in an appropriate sphere on a deserved and fitting cause. ^an4-61-17-3

‘I’ve enjoyed my wealth, <j>supporting those who depend on me; ^an4-61-18-1

I’ve overcome losses; ^an4-61-18-2

I’ve given uplifting religious donations; ^an4-61-18-3

and made the five spirit-offerings. ^an4-61-18-4

I have served the ethical and ^an4-61-18-5

disciplined spiritual practitioners. ^an4-61-18-6

I’ve achieved the goal ^an4-61-19-1

for which an astute layperson ^an4-61-19-2

wishes to gain wealth. ^an4-61-19-3

I don’t regret what I’ve done.’ ^an4-61-19-4

A mortal man who recollects this ^an4-61-20-1

stands firm in the teaching of the noble ones. ^an4-61-20-2

They’re praised in this life, ^an4-61-20-3

and they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an4-61-20-4

### Debtlessness (Numbered Discourses 4.62) ^an4-62-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an4-62-1-1

“Householder, these four kinds of happiness can be earned by a layperson who enjoys sensual pleasures, depending on time and occasion. ^an4-62-2-1

What four? ^an4-62-2-2

The happiness of ownership, using wealth, debtlessness, and blamelessness. ^an4-62-2-3

And what is the happiness of ownership? ^an4-62-3-1

It’s when a gentleman owns legitimate wealth that he has earned by his own efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. ^an4-62-3-2

When he reflects on this, he’s filled with pleasure and happiness. ^an4-62-3-3

This is called ‘the happiness of ownership’. ^an4-62-3-4

And what is the happiness of using wealth? ^an4-62-4-1

It’s when a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth, and makes merit with it. ^an4-62-4-2

When he reflects on this, he’s filled with pleasure and happiness. ^an4-62-4-3

This is called ‘the happiness of using wealth’. ^an4-62-4-4

And what is the happiness of debtlessness? ^an4-62-5-1

It’s when a gentleman owes no debt, large or small, to anyone. ^an4-62-5-2

When he reflects on this, he’s filled with pleasure and happiness. ^an4-62-5-3

This is called ‘the happiness of debtlessness’. ^an4-62-5-4

And what is the happiness of blamelessness? ^an4-62-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple has blameless conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-62-6-2

When he reflects on this, he’s filled with pleasure and happiness. ^an4-62-6-3

This is called ‘the happiness of blamelessness’. ^an4-62-6-4

These four kinds of happiness can be earned by a layperson who enjoys sensual pleasures, depending on time and occasion. ^an4-62-6-5

Knowing the happiness of debtlessness, ^an4-62-7-1

and the extra happiness of possession, ^an4-62-7-2

a mortal enjoying the happiness of using wealth, ^an4-62-7-3

then discerns clearly with wisdom. ^an4-62-7-4

Discerning clearly, a clever person knows ^an4-62-8-1

both kinds of happiness: ^an4-62-8-2

the other kind is not worth a sixteenth part ^an4-62-8-3

of the happiness of blamelessness.” ^an4-62-8-4

### Living with Divinity (Numbered Discourses 4.63) ^an4-63-0

“Mendicants, a family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with divinity. ^an4-63-1-1

A family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with the first tutors. ^an4-63-1-2

A family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with the elder gods. ^an4-63-1-3

A family where the children honor their parents in their home is said to live with those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods. ^an4-63-1-4

‘Divinity’ is a term for your parents. ^an4-63-2-1

‘First tutors’ is a term for your parents. ^an4-63-2-2

‘Elder gods’ is a term for your parents. ^an4-63-2-3

‘Worthy of an offering dedicated to the gods’ is a term for your parents. ^an4-63-2-4

Why is that? ^an4-63-2-5

Parents are very helpful to their children, they raise them, nurture them, and show them the world. ^an4-63-2-6

Parents are said to be ‘Divinity’ ^an4-63-3-1

and ‘first tutors’. ^an4-63-3-2

They’re worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods <j>from their children, ^an4-63-3-3

for they love their offspring. ^an4-63-3-4

Therefore an astute person ^an4-63-4-1

would revere them and honor them ^an4-63-4-2

with food and drink, ^an4-63-4-3

clothes and bedding, ^an4-63-4-4

by anointing and bathing, ^an4-63-4-5

and by washing their feet. ^an4-63-4-6

Because they look after ^an4-63-5-1

their parents like this, ^an4-63-5-2

they’re praised in this life by the astute, ^an4-63-5-3

and they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an4-63-5-4

### Hell (Numbered Discourses 4.64) ^an4-64-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-64-1-1

What four? ^an4-64-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. ^an4-64-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-64-1-4

Killing, stealing, ^an4-64-2-1

telling lies, ^an4-64-2-2

and visiting others’ wives: ^an4-64-2-3

astute people don’t praise these things.” ^an4-64-2-4

### Appearance (Numbered Discourses 4.65) ^an4-65-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-65-1-1

What four? ^an4-65-1-2

There are those whose estimation of and confidence in others is based on their appearance, on their voice, on their mortification, and on principle. ^an4-65-1-3

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-65-1-4

Those who judge on appearance, ^an4-65-2-1

and those swayed by a voice, ^an4-65-2-2

are full of desire and greed; ^an4-65-2-3

those people just don’t understand. ^an4-65-2-4

Not knowing what’s inside, ^an4-65-3-1

nor seeing what’s outside, ^an4-65-3-2

the fool shut in on every side, ^an4-65-3-3

gets carried away by a voice. ^an4-65-3-4

Not knowing what’s inside, ^an4-65-4-1

but discerning what’s outside, ^an4-65-4-2

seeing the fruit outside, ^an4-65-4-3

they’re also carried away by a voice. ^an4-65-4-4

Understanding what’s inside, ^an4-65-5-1

and discerning what’s outside, ^an4-65-5-2

seeing without hindrances, ^an4-65-5-3

they don’t get carried away by a voice.” ^an4-65-5-4

### Greedy (Numbered Discourses 4.66) ^an4-66-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-66-1-1

What four? ^an4-66-1-2

The greedy, the hateful, the delusional, and the conceited. ^an4-66-1-3

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-66-1-4

Full of desire for desirable things, ^an4-66-2-1

enjoying things that seem pleasant, ^an4-66-2-2

beings veiled by delusion ^an4-66-2-3

only tighten their bonds. ^an4-66-2-4

Born of greed, born of hate, ^an4-66-3-1

born of delusion: the ignorant ^an4-66-3-2

do unskillful deeds ^an4-66-3-3

that afflict and produce pain. ^an4-66-3-4

If you act out of these qualities, <j>that’s what you become. ^an4-66-4-1

But men shrouded by ignorance, ^an4-66-4-2

are blind, with no eyes to see, ^an4-66-4-3

and they never imagine that this could be so.” ^an4-66-4-4

### The Snake King (Numbered Discourses 4.67) ^an4-67-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-67-1-1

Now, at that time a monk in Sāvatthī passed away due to a snake bite. ^an4-67-1-2

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an4-67-1-3

“Sir, a monk in Sāvatthī has passed away due to a snake bite.” ^an4-67-1-4

“Mendicants, that monk mustn’t have spread a mind of love to the four royal snake families. ^an4-67-2-1

If he had, he wouldn’t have died due to a snake bite. ^an4-67-2-2

What four? ^an4-67-3-1

The royal snake families of Virūpakkha, Erāpatha, Chabyāputta, and Kaṇhāgotamaka. … ^an4-67-3-2

Mendicants, I urge you to spread a mind of love to the four royal snake families, for your own safety, security, and protection. ^an4-67-4-1

I love the Virūpakkhas, ^an4-67-5-1

the Erāpathas I love, ^an4-67-5-2

I love the Chabyāputtas, ^an4-67-5-3

the Kaṇhāgotamakas I love. ^an4-67-5-4

I love the footless creatures, ^an4-67-6-1

the two-footed I love, ^an4-67-6-2

I love the four-footed, ^an4-67-6-3

the many-footed I love. ^an4-67-6-4

May the footless not harm me! ^an4-67-7-1

May I not be harmed by the two-footed! ^an4-67-7-2

May the four-footed not harm me! ^an4-67-7-3

May I not be harmed by the many-footed! ^an4-67-7-4

All sentient beings, all living things, ^an4-67-8-1

all creatures, every one: ^an4-67-8-2

may they see only nice things, ^an4-67-8-3

may bad not come to anyone. ^an4-67-8-4

The Buddha is immeasurable, ^an4-67-9-1

the teaching is immeasurable, ^an4-67-9-2

the Saṅgha is immeasurable. ^an4-67-9-3

But limited are reptiles, ^an4-67-9-4

snakes and scorpions, centipedes, ^an4-67-10-1

spiders and lizards and mice. ^an4-67-10-2

I’ve made this safeguard, I’ve made this protection: ^an4-67-10-3

go away, creatures! ^an4-67-10-4

And so I revere the Blessed One, ^an4-67-10-5

I revere the seven perfectly awakened Buddhas.” ^an4-67-10-6

### Devadatta (Numbered Discourses 4.68) ^an4-68-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had left. ^an4-68-1-1

There the Buddha spoke to the mendicants about Devadatta: ^an4-68-1-2

“Possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall. ^an4-68-1-3

It’s like a banana plant, ^an4-68-2-1

or a bamboo, ^an4-68-3-1

or a reed, all of which bear fruit to their own ruin and downfall … ^an4-68-4-1

It’s like a mule, which becomes pregnant to its own ruin and downfall. ^an4-68-5-1

In the same way, possessions, honor, and popularity came to Devadatta for his own ruin and downfall. ^an4-68-5-2

The banana plant is destroyed by its own fruit, ^an4-68-6-1

as are the bamboo and the reed. ^an4-68-6-2

Honor destroys a reprobate, ^an4-68-6-3

as pregnancy destroys a mule.” ^an4-68-6-4

### Effort (Numbered Discourses 4.69) ^an4-69-0

“Mendicants, there are these four efforts. ^an4-69-1-1

What four? ^an4-69-1-2

The efforts to restrain, to give up, to develop, and to preserve. ^an4-69-1-3

And what, mendicants, is the effort to restrain? ^an4-69-1-4

It’s when you generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. ^an4-69-1-5

This is called the effort to restrain. ^an4-69-1-6

And what, mendicants, is the effort to give up? ^an4-69-2-1

It’s when you generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities are given up. ^an4-69-2-2

This is called the effort to give up. ^an4-69-2-3

And what, mendicants, is the effort to develop? ^an4-69-3-1

It’s when you generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities arise. ^an4-69-3-2

This is called the effort to develop. ^an4-69-3-3

And what, mendicants, is the effort to preserve? ^an4-69-4-1

It’s when you generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are fulfilled by development. ^an4-69-4-2

This is called the effort to preserve. ^an4-69-4-3

These are the four efforts. ^an4-69-4-4

Restraint and giving up, ^an4-69-5-1

development and preservation: ^an4-69-5-2

these are the four efforts ^an4-69-5-3

taught by the Kinsman of the Sun. ^an4-69-5-4

Any mendicant who keenly applies these ^an4-69-5-5

may attain the ending of suffering.” ^an4-69-5-6

### Unprincipled (Numbered Discourses 4.70) ^an4-70-0

“At a time when kings are unprincipled, royal officials become unprincipled. ^an4-70-1-1

When royal officials are unprincipled, brahmins and householders become unprincipled. ^an4-70-1-2

When brahmins and householders are unprincipled, the people of town and country become unprincipled. ^an4-70-1-3

When the people of town and country are unprincipled, the courses of the moon and sun become erratic. ^an4-70-1-4

… the courses of the stars and constellations … ^an4-70-1-5

the days and nights … ^an4-70-1-6

the months and fortnights … ^an4-70-1-7

the seasons and years become erratic. ^an4-70-1-8

… the blowing of the winds becomes erratic and chaotic. ^an4-70-1-9

… the deities are angered. ^an4-70-1-10

… the heavens don’t provide enough rain. ^an4-70-1-11

… the crops ripen erratically. ^an4-70-1-12

When people eat crops that have ripened erratically, they become short-lived, ugly, weak, and sickly. ^an4-70-1-13

At a time when kings are principled, royal officials become principled. ^an4-70-2-1

… brahmins and householders … ^an4-70-2-2

people of town and country become principled. ^an4-70-2-3

When the people of town and country are principled, the courses of the sun and moon become regular. ^an4-70-2-4

… the stars and constellations … ^an4-70-2-5

the days and nights … ^an4-70-2-6

the months and fortnights … ^an4-70-2-7

the seasons and years become regular. ^an4-70-2-8

… the blowing of the winds becomes regular and orderly. ^an4-70-2-9

… the deities are not angered … ^an4-70-2-10

… the heavens provide plenty of rain. ^an4-70-2-11

When the heavens provide plenty of rain, the crops ripen well. ^an4-70-2-12

When people eat crops that have ripened well, they become long-lived, beautiful, strong, and healthy. ^an4-70-2-13

When cattle cross a river, ^an4-70-3-1

if the bull goes off course, ^an4-70-3-2

they all go off course, ^an4-70-3-3

because their leader is off course. ^an4-70-3-4

So it is for humans: ^an4-70-4-1

when the one deemed chief ^an4-70-4-2

behaves badly, ^an4-70-4-3

what do you expect the rest to do? ^an4-70-4-4

The whole country sleeps badly, ^an4-70-4-5

when the king is unprincipled. ^an4-70-4-6

When cattle cross a river, ^an4-70-5-1

if the bull goes straight, ^an4-70-5-2

they all go straight, ^an4-70-5-3

because their leader is straight. ^an4-70-5-4

So it is for humans: ^an4-70-6-1

when the one deemed chief ^an4-70-6-2

does the right thing, ^an4-70-6-3

what do you expect the rest to do? ^an4-70-6-4

The whole country sleeps at ease, ^an4-70-6-5

when the king is just.” ^an4-70-6-6

### Effort (Numbered Discourses 4.71) ^an4-71-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has four things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements. ^an4-71-1-1

What four? ^an4-71-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, learned, energetic, and wise. ^an4-71-1-3

When a mendicant possesses these four things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements.” ^an4-71-1-4

### Right View (Numbered Discourses 4.72) ^an4-72-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has four things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements. ^an4-72-1-1

What four? ^an4-72-1-2

Thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness; and right view. ^an4-72-1-3

When a mendicant possesses these four things their practice is a sure bet, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements.” ^an4-72-1-4

### A True Person (Numbered Discourses 4.73) ^an4-73-0

“Mendicants, an untrue person can be known by four qualities. ^an4-73-1-1

What four? ^an4-73-1-2

To start with, an untrue person speaks ill of another even when not asked, let alone when asked. ^an4-73-1-3

But when summoned for questioning they speak ill of another in full detail, not leaving anything out. ^an4-73-1-4

That’s how to know that this is an untrue person. ^an4-73-1-5

Furthermore, an untrue person doesn’t speak well of another even when asked, let alone when not asked. ^an4-73-2-1

But when summoned for questioning they speak well of another without giving the full details, leaving many things out. ^an4-73-2-2

That’s how to know that this is an untrue person. ^an4-73-2-3

Furthermore, an untrue person doesn’t speak ill of themselves even when asked, let alone when not asked. ^an4-73-3-1

But when summoned for questioning they speak ill of themselves without giving the full details, leaving many things out. ^an4-73-3-2

That’s how to know that this is an untrue person. ^an4-73-3-3

Furthermore, an untrue person speaks well of themselves even when not asked, let alone when asked. ^an4-73-4-1

But when summoned for questioning they speak well of themselves in full detail, not leaving anything out. ^an4-73-4-2

That’s how to know that this is an untrue person. ^an4-73-4-3

An untrue person can be known by these four qualities. ^an4-73-4-4

A true person can be known by four qualities. ^an4-73-5-1

What four? ^an4-73-5-2

To start with, a true person doesn’t speak ill of another even when asked, let alone when not asked. ^an4-73-5-3

But when summoned for questioning they speak ill of another without giving the full details, leaving many things out. ^an4-73-5-4

That’s how to know that this is a true person. ^an4-73-5-5

Furthermore, a true person speaks well of another even when not asked, let alone when asked. ^an4-73-6-1

But when summoned for questioning they speak well of another in full detail, not leaving anything out. ^an4-73-6-2

That’s how to know that this is a true person. ^an4-73-6-3

Furthermore, a true person speaks ill of themselves even when not asked, let alone when asked. ^an4-73-7-1

But when led on by questions they speak ill of themselves in full detail, not leaving anything out. ^an4-73-7-2

That’s how to know that this is a true person. ^an4-73-7-3

Furthermore, a true person doesn’t speak well of themselves even when asked, let alone when not asked. ^an4-73-8-1

But when summoned for questioning they speak well of themselves without giving the full details, leaving many things out. ^an4-73-8-2

That’s how to know that this is a true person. ^an4-73-8-3

A true person can be known by these four qualities. ^an4-73-8-4

It’s like a bride on the day or night she’s first brought to her husband’s home. Right away she sets up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for her mother and father in law, her husband, and even the bondservants, workers, and staff. ^an4-73-9-1

But after some time, because of living together and familiarity, she’ll even say to her mother and father in law, or to her husband: ^an4-73-9-2

‘Go away! What would you know?’ ^an4-73-9-3

In the same way, on the day or night a mendicant first goes forth from the lay life to homelessness, right away they set up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen, and even the monastery workers and novices. ^an4-73-9-4

But after some time, because of living together and familiarity, they’ll even say to their tutor or mentor: ^an4-73-9-5

‘Go away! What would you know?’ ^an4-73-9-6

So you should train like this: ^an4-73-9-7
