‘We will live with hearts like that of a newly wedded bride.’ ^an4-73-9-8

That’s how you should train.” ^an4-73-9-9

### Best (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.74) ^an4-74-0

“Mendicants, these four things are the best. ^an4-74-1-1

What four? ^an4-74-1-2

The best ethics, immersion, wisdom, and freedom. ^an4-74-1-3

These are the four things that are the best.” ^an4-74-1-4

### Best (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.75) ^an4-75-0

“Mendicants, these four things are the best. ^an4-75-1-1

What four? ^an4-75-1-2

The best form, feeling, perception, and existence. ^an4-75-1-3

These are the four things that are the best.” ^an4-75-1-4

### At Kusinārā (Numbered Discourses 4.76) ^an4-76-0

At one time the Buddha was staying between a pair of sal trees in the sal forest of the Mallas at Upavattana near Kusinārā at the time of his full extinguishment. ^an4-76-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-76-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an4-76-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-76-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an4-76-1-5

“Perhaps even a single mendicant has doubt or uncertainty regarding the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the path, or the practice. So ask, mendicants! Don’t regret it later, thinking: ^an4-76-2-1

‘We were in the Teacher’s presence and we weren’t able to ask the Buddha a question.’” ^an4-76-2-2

When this was said, the mendicants kept silent. ^an4-76-2-3

For a second time the Buddha addressed the mendicants: … ^an4-76-3-1

For a second time, the mendicants kept silent. ^an4-76-3-4

For a third time the Buddha addressed the mendicants: … ^an4-76-4-1

For a third time, the mendicants kept silent. ^an4-76-4-4

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants: ^an4-76-5-1

“Mendicants, perhaps you don’t ask out of respect for the Teacher. So let a friend tell a friend.” ^an4-76-5-2

When this was said, the mendicants kept silent. ^an4-76-5-3

Then Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^an4-76-5-4

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an4-76-5-5

I am quite confident that there’s not even a single mendicant in this Saṅgha who has doubt or uncertainty regarding the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the path, or the practice.” ^an4-76-5-6

“Ānanda, you speak from faith. But the Realized One knows that there’s not even a single mendicant in this Saṅgha who has doubt or uncertainty regarding the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the path, or the practice. ^an4-76-6-1

Even the last of these five hundred mendicants is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening.” ^an4-76-6-2

### Unthinkable (Numbered Discourses 4.77) ^an4-77-0

“Mendicants, these four things are unthinkable. They should not be thought about, and anyone who tries to think about them will go mad or get frustrated. ^an4-77-1-1

What four? ^an4-77-1-2

The domain of the Buddhas … ^an4-77-1-3

The domain of one in absorption … ^an4-77-1-5

The results of deeds … ^an4-77-1-7

Speculation about the world … ^an4-77-1-9

These are the four unthinkable things. They should not be thought about, and anyone who tries to think about them will go mad or get frustrated.” ^an4-77-1-11

### A Religious Donation (Numbered Discourses 4.78) ^an4-78-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of purifying a religious donation. ^an4-78-1-1

What four? ^an4-78-1-2

There’s a religious donation that’s purified by the giver, not the recipient. ^an4-78-1-3

There’s a religious donation that’s purified by the recipient, not the giver. ^an4-78-1-4

There’s a religious donation that’s purified by neither the giver nor the recipient. ^an4-78-1-5

There’s a religious donation that’s purified by both the giver and the recipient. ^an4-78-1-6

And how is a religious donation purified by the giver, not the recipient? ^an4-78-2-1

It’s when the giver is ethical, of good character, but the recipient is unethical, of bad character. ^an4-78-2-2

And how is a religious donation purified by the recipient, not the giver? ^an4-78-3-1

It’s when the giver is unethical, of bad character, but the recipient is ethical, of good character. ^an4-78-3-2

And how is a religious donation purified by neither the giver nor the recipient? ^an4-78-4-1

It’s when both the giver and the recipient are unethical, of bad character. ^an4-78-4-2

And how is a religious donation purified by both the giver and the recipient? ^an4-78-5-1

It’s when both the giver and the recipient are ethical, of good character. ^an4-78-5-2

These are the four ways of purifying a religious donation.” ^an4-78-5-4

### Business (Numbered Discourses 4.79) ^an4-79-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-79-1-1

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why for different people the same kind of business undertaking might fail, ^an4-79-1-2

while another doesn’t meet expectations, ^an4-79-1-3

another meets expectations, ^an4-79-1-4

and another exceeds expectations?” ^an4-79-1-5

“Sāriputta, take a case where someone goes to an ascetic or brahmin and invites them to ask for what they need. But they fail to give what’s requested. ^an4-79-2-1

When they’ve passed away from that life, if they’re reborn in this place, whatever business they undertake fails. ^an4-79-2-2

Take a case where someone goes to an ascetic or brahmin and invites them to ask for what they need. They give what’s requested, but don’t meet expectations. ^an4-79-3-1

When they’ve passed away from that life, if they’re reborn in this place, whatever business they undertake doesn’t meet expectations. ^an4-79-3-2

Take a case where someone goes to an ascetic or brahmin and invites them to ask for what they need. They give what’s requested, meeting expectations. ^an4-79-4-1

When they’ve passed away from that life, if they’re reborn in this place, whatever business they undertake meets expectations. ^an4-79-4-2

Take a case where someone goes to an ascetic or brahmin and invites them to ask for what they need. They give what’s requested, exceeding expectations. ^an4-79-5-1

When they’ve passed away from that life, if they’re reborn in this place, whatever business they undertake exceeds expectations. ^an4-79-5-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why for different people the same kind of business undertaking might fail, ^an4-79-6-1

while another doesn’t meet expectations, ^an4-79-6-2

another meets expectations, ^an4-79-6-3

and another exceeds expectations.” ^an4-79-6-4

### Persia (Numbered Discourses 4.80) ^an4-80-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an4-80-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-80-1-2

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why ladies don’t attend council meetings, work for a living, or travel to Persia?” ^an4-80-2-1

“Ānanda, ladies are irritable, ^an4-80-2-2

jealous, ^an4-80-2-3

stingy, ^an4-80-2-4

and unintelligent. ^an4-80-2-5

This is the cause, this is the reason why ladies don’t attend council meetings, work for a living, or travel to Persia.” ^an4-80-2-6

### Killing Living Creatures (Numbered Discourses 4.81) ^an4-81-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-81-1-1

What four? ^an4-81-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. ^an4-81-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-81-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-81-2-1

What four? ^an4-81-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or lie. ^an4-81-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-81-2-4

### Lying (Numbered Discourses 4.82) ^an4-82-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-82-1-1

What four? ^an4-82-1-2

They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-82-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-82-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-82-2-1

What four? ^an4-82-2-2

They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-82-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-82-2-4

### Where Criticism Takes You (Numbered Discourses 4.83) ^an4-83-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-83-1-1

What four? ^an4-83-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism, ^an4-83-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an4-83-1-4

They arouse faith in things that are dubious, and they don’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an4-83-1-5

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-83-1-6

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-83-2-1

What four? ^an4-83-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism, ^an4-83-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an4-83-2-4

They don’t arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an4-83-2-5

and they do arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an4-83-2-6

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-83-2-7

### Valuing Anger (Numbered Discourses 4.84) ^an4-84-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-84-1-1

What four? ^an4-84-1-2

They value anger, or denigration, or material things, or honor rather than the true teaching. ^an4-84-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-84-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-84-2-1

What four? ^an4-84-2-2

They value the true teaching rather than anger, or denigration, or material things, or honor. ^an4-84-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-84-2-4

### From Darkness to Darkness (Numbered Discourses 4.85) ^an4-85-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-85-1-1

What four? ^an4-85-1-2

The dark bound for darkness, ^an4-85-1-3

the dark bound for light, ^an4-85-1-4

the light bound for darkness, and ^an4-85-1-5

the light bound for light. ^an4-85-1-6

And how is an individual dark and bound for darkness? ^an4-85-2-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in a low family—a family of corpse-workers, bamboo-workers, hunters, chariot-makers, or scavengers—poor, with little to eat or drink, where life is tough, and food and shelter are hard to find. ^an4-85-2-2

And they’re ugly, unsightly, deformed, sickly—one-eyed, crippled, lame, or half-paralyzed. They don’t get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; or bed, house, and lighting. ^an4-85-2-3

And they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-85-2-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an4-85-2-5

That’s how an individual is dark and bound for darkness. ^an4-85-2-6

And how is an individual dark and bound for light? ^an4-85-3-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in a low family … ^an4-85-3-2

But they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-85-3-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an4-85-3-5

That’s how an individual is dark and bound for light. ^an4-85-3-6

And how is an individual light and bound for darkness? ^an4-85-4-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in an eminent family—a well-to-do family of aristocrats, brahmins, or householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. ^an4-85-4-2

And they’re attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. They get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an4-85-4-3

But they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-85-4-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an4-85-4-5

That’s how an individual is light and bound for darkness. ^an4-85-4-6

And how is an individual light and bound for light? ^an4-85-5-1

It’s when an individual is reborn in an eminent family … ^an4-85-5-2

And they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-85-5-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an4-85-5-5

That’s how an individual is light and bound for light. ^an4-85-5-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-85-5-7

### Sunk Low (Numbered Discourses 4.86) ^an4-86-0

“These four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-86-1-1

What four? ^an4-86-1-2

One sunk low who sinks lower, ^an4-86-1-3

one sunk low who rises high, ^an4-86-1-4

one risen high who sinks low, and ^an4-86-1-5

one risen high who rises higher. ^an4-86-1-6

… These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-86-1-7

### The Son (Numbered Discourses 4.87) ^an4-87-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-87-1-1

What four? ^an4-87-1-2

The confirmed ascetic, the white lotus ascetic, the pink lotus ascetic, and the delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-87-1-3

And how is an individual a confirmed ascetic? ^an4-87-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is a practicing trainee, ^an4-87-2-2

who lives aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an4-87-2-3

It’s like the eldest son of an anointed aristocratic king. He has not yet been anointed, but is eligible, and has been confirmed in the succession. ^an4-87-2-4

In the same way, a mendicant is a practicing trainee, who lives aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an4-87-2-5

That’s how an individual is a confirmed ascetic. ^an4-87-2-6

And how is an individual a white lotus ascetic? ^an4-87-3-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. But they don’t have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-87-3-2

That’s how an individual is a white lotus ascetic. ^an4-87-3-3

And how is an individual a pink lotus ascetic? ^an4-87-4-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. … And they have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-87-4-2

That’s how an individual is a pink lotus ascetic. ^an4-87-4-3

And how is an individual a delicate ascetic of ascetics? ^an4-87-5-1

It’s when a mendicant usually uses only what they’ve been invited to accept—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—rarely using them without invitation. ^an4-87-5-2

When living with other spiritual practitioners, they usually find themselves treated agreeably by them by way of body, speech, and mind, and rarely disagreeably. ^an4-87-5-3

And they are usually presented with agreeable things by them, rarely with disagreeable ones. ^an4-87-5-4

They’re healthy, so the various unpleasant feelings—stemming from disorders of bile, phlegm, wind, or their conjunction; or caused by change in weather, by not taking care of yourself, by overexertion, or as the result of past deeds—usually don’t come up. ^an4-87-5-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-87-5-6

And they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. … ^an4-87-5-7

That’s how an individual is a delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-87-5-8

And if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me. ^an4-87-6-1

For I usually use only what I’ve been invited to accept … ^an4-87-6-2

When living with other mendicants, I am usually treated agreeably by them.  … ^an4-87-6-3

I’m healthy … ^an4-87-6-4

I get the four absorptions when I want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an4-87-6-5

And I’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. … ^an4-87-6-6

So if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me. ^an4-87-7-1

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-87-7-2

### Fetters (Numbered Discourses 4.88) ^an4-88-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-88-1-1

What four? ^an4-88-1-2

The confirmed ascetic, the white lotus ascetic, the pink lotus ascetic, and the delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-88-1-3

And how is an individual a confirmed ascetic? ^an4-88-2-1

It’s when a mendicant—with the ending of three fetters—is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^an4-88-2-2

That’s how an individual is a confirmed ascetic. ^an4-88-2-3

And how is an individual a white lotus ascetic? ^an4-88-3-1

It’s when a mendicant—with the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion—is a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an4-88-3-2

That’s how an individual is a white lotus ascetic. ^an4-88-3-3

And how is an individual a pink lotus ascetic? ^an4-88-4-1

It’s when a mendicant—with the ending of the five lower fetters—is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an4-88-4-2

That’s how an individual is a pink lotus ascetic. ^an4-88-4-3

And how is an individual a delicate ascetic of ascetics? ^an4-88-5-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-88-5-2

That’s how an individual is a delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-88-5-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-88-5-4

### Right View (Numbered Discourses 4.89) ^an4-89-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-89-1-1

What four? ^an4-89-1-2

The confirmed ascetic, the white lotus ascetic, the pink lotus ascetic, and the delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-89-1-3

And how is an individual a confirmed ascetic? ^an4-89-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an4-89-2-2

That’s how an individual is a confirmed ascetic. ^an4-89-2-3

And how is an individual a white lotus ascetic? ^an4-89-3-1

It’s when they have right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. But they don’t have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-89-3-2

That’s how an individual is a white lotus ascetic. ^an4-89-3-3

And how is an individual a pink lotus ascetic? ^an4-89-4-1

It’s when they have right view … and right freedom. And they do have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-89-4-2

That’s how an individual is a pink lotus ascetic. ^an4-89-4-3

And how is an individual a delicate ascetic of ascetics? ^an4-89-5-1

It’s when a mendicant usually uses only what they’ve been invited to accept … And if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me. ^an4-89-5-2

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-89-5-3

### Aggregates (Numbered Discourses 4.90) ^an4-90-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-90-1-1

What four? ^an4-90-1-2

The confirmed ascetic, the white lotus ascetic, the pink lotus ascetic, and the delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an4-90-1-3

And how is an individual a confirmed ascetic? ^an4-90-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire, but lives aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an4-90-2-2

That’s how an individual is a confirmed ascetic. ^an4-90-2-3

And how is an individual a white lotus ascetic? ^an4-90-3-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^an4-90-3-2

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^an4-90-3-3

Such is feeling … ^an4-90-3-4

Such is perception … ^an4-90-3-5

Such are choices … ^an4-90-3-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^an4-90-3-7

But they don’t have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-90-3-8

That’s how an individual is a white lotus ascetic. ^an4-90-3-9

And how is an individual a pink lotus ascetic? ^an4-90-4-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^an4-90-4-2

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form. ^an4-90-4-3

Such is feeling … ^an4-90-4-4

Such is perception … ^an4-90-4-5

Such are choices … ^an4-90-4-6

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’ ^an4-90-4-7

And they have direct meditative experience of the eight liberations. ^an4-90-4-8

That’s how an individual is a pink lotus ascetic. ^an4-90-4-9

And how is an individual a delicate ascetic of ascetics? ^an4-90-5-1

It’s when a mendicant usually uses only what they’ve been invited to accept … And if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me. ^an4-90-5-2

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-90-5-3

### Titans (Numbered Discourses 4.91) ^an4-91-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-91-1-1

What four? ^an4-91-1-2

A titan surrounded by titans, ^an4-91-1-3

a titan surrounded by gods, ^an4-91-1-4

a god surrounded by titans, and ^an4-91-1-5

a god surrounded by gods. ^an4-91-1-6

And how is an individual a titan surrounded by titans? ^an4-91-2-1

It’s when an individual is unethical, of bad character, and their followers are the same. ^an4-91-2-2

That’s how an individual is a titan surrounded by titans. ^an4-91-2-3

And how is an individual a titan surrounded by gods? ^an4-91-3-1

It’s when an individual is unethical, of bad character, but their followers are ethical, of good character. ^an4-91-3-2

That’s how an individual is a titan surrounded by gods. ^an4-91-3-3

And how is an individual a god surrounded by titans? ^an4-91-4-1

It’s when an individual is ethical, of good character, but their followers are unethical, of bad character. ^an4-91-4-2

That’s how an individual is a god surrounded by titans. ^an4-91-4-3

And how is an individual a god surrounded by gods? ^an4-91-5-1

It’s when an individual is ethical, of good character, and their followers are the same. ^an4-91-5-2

That’s how an individual is a god surrounded by gods. ^an4-91-5-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-91-5-4

### Immersion (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.92) ^an4-92-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-92-1-1

What four? ^an4-92-1-2

One individual has internal serenity of heart, but not the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-92-1-3

One individual has the higher wisdom of discernment of principles, but not internal serenity of heart. ^an4-92-1-4

One individual has neither internal serenity of heart, nor the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-92-1-5

One individual has both internal serenity of heart, and the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-92-1-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-92-1-7

### Immersion (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.93) ^an4-93-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-93-1-1

What four? ^an4-93-1-2

One individual has internal serenity of heart, but not the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-93-1-3

One individual has the higher wisdom of discernment of principles, but not internal serenity of heart. ^an4-93-1-4

One individual has neither internal serenity of heart, nor the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-93-1-5

One individual has both internal serenity of heart, and the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-93-1-6

As for the individual who has serenity but not discernment: grounded on serenity, they should practice meditation to get discernment. ^an4-93-2-1

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an4-93-2-2

As for the individual who has discernment but not serenity: grounded on discernment, they should practice meditation to get serenity. ^an4-93-3-1

After some time they have both discernment and serenity. ^an4-93-3-2

As for the individual who has neither serenity nor discernment: in order to get those skillful qualities, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an4-93-4-1

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an4-93-4-2

In the same way, in order to get those skillful qualities, that individual should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an4-93-4-3

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an4-93-4-4

As for the individual who has both serenity and discernment: grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements. ^an4-93-5-1

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-93-5-2

### Immersion (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 4.94) ^an4-94-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-94-1-1

What four? ^an4-94-1-2

One individual has internal serenity of heart, but not the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-94-1-3

One individual has the higher wisdom of discernment of principles, but not internal serenity of heart. ^an4-94-1-4

One individual has neither internal serenity of heart, nor the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-94-1-5

One individual has both internal serenity of heart, and the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an4-94-1-6

As for the individual who has serenity but not discernment: they should approach someone who has discernment and ask: ^an4-94-2-1

‘Reverend, how should conditions be seen? ^an4-94-2-2

How should they be comprehended? ^an4-94-2-3

How should they be discerned?’ ^an4-94-2-4

They would answer from their own experience: ^an4-94-2-5

‘This is how conditions should be seen, comprehended, and discerned.’ ^an4-94-2-6

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an4-94-2-7

As for the individual who has discernment but not serenity: they should approach someone who has serenity and ask: ^an4-94-3-1

‘Reverend, how should the mind be stilled? ^an4-94-3-2

How should it be settled? ^an4-94-3-3

How should it be unified? ^an4-94-3-4

How should it be immersed in samādhi?’ ^an4-94-3-5

They would answer from their own experience: ^an4-94-3-6

‘Reverend, this is how the mind should be stilled, settled, unified, and immersed in samādhi.’ ^an4-94-3-7

After some time they have both discernment and serenity. ^an4-94-3-8

As for the individual who has neither serenity nor discernment: they should approach someone who has serenity and discernment and ask: ^an4-94-4-1

‘Reverend, how should the mind be stilled? ^an4-94-4-2

How should it be settled? ^an4-94-4-3

How should it be unified? ^an4-94-4-4

How should it be immersed in samādhi?’ ^an4-94-4-5

How should conditions be seen? ^an4-94-4-6

How should they be comprehended? ^an4-94-4-7

How should they be discerned?’ ^an4-94-4-8

They would answer as they’ve seen and known: ^an4-94-4-9

‘Reverend, this is how the mind should be stilled, settled, unified, and immersed in samādhi. And this is how conditions should be seen, comprehended, and discerned.’ ^an4-94-4-10

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an4-94-4-11

As for the individual who has both serenity and discernment: grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements. ^an4-94-5-1

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-94-5-2

### A Firebrand (Numbered Discourses 4.95) ^an4-95-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-95-1-1

What four? ^an4-95-1-2

One who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others; ^an4-95-1-3

one who practices to benefit others, but not themselves; ^an4-95-1-4

one who practices to benefit themselves, but not others; and ^an4-95-1-5

one who practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-95-1-6

Suppose there was a firebrand for lighting a funeral pyre, burning at both ends, and smeared with dung in the middle. It couldn’t be used as timber either in the village or the wilderness. ^an4-95-2-1

The individual who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others is like this, I say. ^an4-95-2-2

The individual who practices to benefit others, but not themselves, is better than that. ^an4-95-3-1

The individual who practices to benefit themselves, but not others, is better than both of those. ^an4-95-3-2

But the individual who practices to benefit both themselves and others is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the four. ^an4-95-3-3

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^an4-95-4-1

In the same way, the individual who practices to benefit both themselves and others is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the four. ^an4-95-4-2

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-95-4-3

### Removing Greed (Numbered Discourses 4.96) ^an4-96-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-96-1-1

What four? ^an4-96-1-2

One who practices to benefit themselves, but not others; ^an4-96-1-3

one who practices to benefit others, but not themselves; ^an4-96-1-4

one who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others; and ^an4-96-1-5

one who practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-96-1-6

And how does an individual practice to benefit themselves, but not others? ^an4-96-2-1

It’s when an individual practices to remove their own greed, hate, and delusion, but doesn’t encourage others to do the same. ^an4-96-2-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit themselves, but not others. ^an4-96-2-5

And how does an individual practice to benefit others, but not themselves? ^an4-96-3-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t practice to remove their own greed, hate, and delusion, but encourages others to remove theirs. ^an4-96-3-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit others, but not themselves. ^an4-96-3-5

And how does an individual practice to benefit neither themselves nor others? ^an4-96-4-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t practice to remove their own greed, hate, and delusion, nor do they encourage others to remove theirs. ^an4-96-4-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit neither themselves nor others. ^an4-96-4-5

And how does an individual practice to benefit both themselves and others? ^an4-96-5-1

It’s when an individual practices to remove their own greed, hate, and delusion, and encourages others to remove theirs. ^an4-96-5-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-96-5-5

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-96-5-6

### Quick-witted (Numbered Discourses 4.97) ^an4-97-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-97-1-1

What four? ^an4-97-1-2

One who practices to benefit themselves, but not others; ^an4-97-1-3

one who practices to benefit others, but not themselves; ^an4-97-1-4

one who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others; and ^an4-97-1-5

one who practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-97-1-6

And how does an individual practice to benefit themselves, but not others? ^an4-97-2-1

It’s when an individual is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an4-97-2-2

But they’re not a good speaker and do not enunciate well. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an4-97-2-3

That’s how an individual practices to benefit themselves, but not others. ^an4-97-2-4

And how does an individual practice to benefit others, but not themselves? ^an4-97-3-1

It’s when an individual is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. … ^an4-97-3-2

But they’re a good speaker. … ^an4-97-3-3

That’s how an individual practices to benefit others, but not themselves. ^an4-97-3-4

And how does an individual practice to benefit neither themselves nor others? ^an4-97-4-1

It’s when an individual is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. … ^an4-97-4-2

Nor are they a good speaker. … ^an4-97-4-3

That’s how an individual practices to benefit neither themselves nor others. ^an4-97-4-4

And how does an individual practice to benefit both themselves and others? ^an4-97-5-1

It’s when an individual is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. … ^an4-97-5-2

And they’re a good speaker. … ^an4-97-5-3

That’s how an individual practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-97-5-4

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-97-5-5

### To Benefit Oneself (Numbered Discourses 4.98) ^an4-98-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-98-1-1

What four? ^an4-98-1-2

One who practices to benefit themselves, but not others; ^an4-98-1-3

one who practices to benefit others, but not themselves; ^an4-98-1-4

one who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others; and ^an4-98-1-5

one who practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-98-1-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-98-1-7

### Training Rules (Numbered Discourses 4.99) ^an4-99-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-99-1-1

What four? ^an4-99-1-2

One who practices to benefit themselves, but not others; ^an4-99-1-3

one who practices to benefit others, but not themselves; ^an4-99-1-4

one who practices to benefit neither themselves nor others; and ^an4-99-1-5

one who practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-99-1-6

And how does an individual practice to benefit themselves, but not others? ^an4-99-2-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. But they don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an4-99-2-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit themselves, but not others. ^an4-99-2-7

And how does an individual practice to benefit others, but not themselves? ^an4-99-3-1

It’s when an individual kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. But they encourage others to not do these things. ^an4-99-3-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit others, but not themselves. ^an4-99-3-7

And how does a person practice to benefit neither themselves nor others? ^an4-99-4-1

It’s when an individual kills, etc. … and doesn’t encourage others to not do these things. ^an4-99-4-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit neither themselves nor others. ^an4-99-4-3

And how does an individual practice to benefit both themselves and others? ^an4-99-5-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t kill, etc. … and encourages others to do the same. ^an4-99-5-2

That’s how an individual practices to benefit both themselves and others. ^an4-99-5-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-99-5-4

### With Potaliya the Wanderer (Numbered Discourses 4.100) ^an4-100-0

Then the wanderer Potaliya went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-100-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side, and the Buddha said to him: ^an4-100-1-2

“Potaliya, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-100-2-1

What four? ^an4-100-2-2

One individual criticizes those deserving of criticism at the right time, truthfully and correctly. But they don’t praise those deserving of praise at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an4-100-2-3

Another individual praises those deserving of praise … But they don’t criticize those deserving of criticism … ^an4-100-2-4

Another individual doesn’t praise those deserving of praise … Nor do they criticize those deserving of criticism … ^an4-100-2-5

Another individual criticizes those deserving of criticism at the right time, truthfully and correctly. And they praise those deserving of praise at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an4-100-2-6

These are the four individuals found in the world. ^an4-100-2-7

Of these four individuals, who do you believe to be the finest?” ^an4-100-2-8

“Mister Gotama, of these four individuals, it is the individual who neither praises those deserving of praise at the right time, truthfully and correctly; nor criticizes those deserving of criticism at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an4-100-3-8

That is the individual I believe to be the finest. ^an4-100-3-9

Why is that? ^an4-100-3-10

Because, worthy Gotama, equanimity is the best.” ^an4-100-3-11

“Potaliya, of these four individuals, it is the individual who criticizes those deserving of criticism at the right time, truthfully and correctly; and praises those deserving of praise at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an4-100-4-3

That is the individual I consider to be the finest. ^an4-100-4-4

Why is that? ^an4-100-4-5

Because, Potaliya, understanding of time and context is the best.” ^an4-100-4-6

“Mister Gotama, of these four individuals, it is the individual who criticizes those deserving of criticism at the right time, truthfully and correctly; and praises those deserving of praise at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an4-100-5-3

That is the individual I believe to be the finest. ^an4-100-5-4

Why is that? ^an4-100-5-5

Because, worthy Gotama, understanding of time and context is the best. ^an4-100-5-6

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^an4-100-6-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an4-100-6-2

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an4-100-6-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-100-6-4

### Clouds (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.101) ^an4-101-0

So I have heard. ^an4-101-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-101-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-101-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an4-101-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-101-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an4-101-1-6

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of clouds. ^an4-101-2-1

What four? ^an4-101-2-2

One thunders but doesn’t rain, ^an4-101-2-3

one rains but doesn’t thunder, ^an4-101-2-4

one neither thunders nor rains, and ^an4-101-2-5

one both rains and thunders. ^an4-101-2-6

These are the four kinds of clouds. ^an4-101-2-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to clouds are found in the world. ^an4-101-2-8

What four? ^an4-101-2-9

One thunders but doesn’t rain, ^an4-101-2-10

one rains but doesn’t thunder, ^an4-101-2-11

one neither thunders nor rains, and ^an4-101-2-12

one both rains and thunders. ^an4-101-2-13

And how does an individual thunder but not rain? ^an4-101-3-1

It’s when an individual is a talker, not a doer. ^an4-101-3-2

That’s how an individual thunders but doesn’t rain. ^an4-101-3-3

That individual is like a cloud that thunders but doesn’t rain, I say. ^an4-101-3-4

And how does an individual rain but not thunder? ^an4-101-4-1

It’s when an individual is a doer, not a talker. … ^an4-101-4-2

And how does an individual neither thunder nor rain? ^an4-101-5-1

It’s when an individual is neither a talker nor a doer. … ^an4-101-5-2

And how does an individual both thunder and rain? ^an4-101-6-1

It’s when an individual is both a talker and a doer. … ^an4-101-6-2

These four individuals similar to clouds are found in the world.” ^an4-101-6-6

### Clouds (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.102) ^an4-102-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of clouds. ^an4-102-1-1

What four? ^an4-102-1-2

One thunders but doesn’t rain, ^an4-102-1-3

one rains but doesn’t thunder, ^an4-102-1-4

one neither thunders nor rains, and ^an4-102-1-5

one both rains and thunders. ^an4-102-1-6

These are the four kinds of clouds. ^an4-102-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to clouds are found in the world. ^an4-102-1-8

What four? ^an4-102-1-9

One thunders but doesn’t rain, ^an4-102-1-10

one rains but doesn’t thunder, ^an4-102-1-11

one neither thunders nor rains, and ^an4-102-1-12

one both rains and thunders. ^an4-102-1-13

And how does an individual thunder but not rain? ^an4-102-2-1

It’s when an individual memorizes the teaching— ^an4-102-2-2

statements, mixed prose and verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-102-2-3

But they don’t truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-102-2-4

That’s how an individual thunders but doesn’t rain. ^an4-102-2-5

That individual is like a cloud that thunders but doesn’t rain, I say. ^an4-102-2-6

And how does an individual rain but not thunder? ^an4-102-3-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t memorize the teaching … ^an4-102-3-2

But they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-102-3-4

And how does an individual neither thunder nor rain? ^an4-102-4-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t memorize the teaching … ^an4-102-4-2

Nor do they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-102-4-4

And how does an individual both thunder and rain? ^an4-102-5-1

It’s when an individual memorizes the teaching … ^an4-102-5-2

And they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-102-5-4

These four individuals similar to clouds are found in the world.” ^an4-102-5-8

### Pots (Numbered Discourses 4.103) ^an4-103-0

“Mendicants, there are these four pots. ^an4-103-1-1

What four? ^an4-103-1-2

Covered but hollow, ^an4-103-1-3

uncovered but full, ^an4-103-1-4

uncovered and hollow, and ^an4-103-1-5

covered and full. ^an4-103-1-6

These are the four pots. ^an4-103-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to pots are found in the world. ^an4-103-1-8

What four? ^an4-103-1-9

Covered but hollow, ^an4-103-1-10

uncovered but full, ^an4-103-1-11

uncovered and hollow, and ^an4-103-1-12

covered and full. ^an4-103-1-13

And how is a individual covered but hollow? ^an4-103-2-1

It’s when an individual is impressive when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an4-103-2-2

But they don’t truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-103-2-3

That’s how an individual is covered but hollow. ^an4-103-2-4

That individual is like a pot that’s covered but hollow, I say. ^an4-103-2-5

And how is a individual uncovered but full? ^an4-103-3-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-103-3-2

But they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-103-3-3

And how is a individual uncovered and hollow? ^an4-103-4-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-103-4-2

Nor do they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-103-4-3

And how is a individual covered and full? ^an4-103-5-1

It’s when an individual is impressive … ^an4-103-5-2

And they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-103-5-3

These four individuals similar to pots are found in the world.” ^an4-103-5-7

### Lakes (Numbered Discourses 4.104) ^an4-104-0

“Mendicants, there are these four lakes. ^an4-104-1-1

What four? ^an4-104-1-2

One is shallow but appears deep, ^an4-104-1-3

one is deep but appears shallow, ^an4-104-1-4

one is shallow and appears shallow, and ^an4-104-1-5

one is deep and appears deep. ^an4-104-1-6

These are the four lakes. ^an4-104-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to lakes are found in the world. ^an4-104-1-8

What four? ^an4-104-1-9

One is shallow but appears deep, ^an4-104-1-10

one is deep but appears shallow, ^an4-104-1-11

one is shallow and appears shallow, and ^an4-104-1-12

one is deep and appears deep. ^an4-104-1-13

And how is an individual shallow but appears deep? ^an4-104-2-1

It’s when an individual is impressive when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an4-104-2-2

But they don’t really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-104-2-3

That’s how an individual is shallow but appears deep. ^an4-104-2-4

That individual is like a lake that’s shallow but appears deep, I say. ^an4-104-2-5

And how is an individual deep but appears shallow? ^an4-104-3-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-104-3-2

But they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-104-3-3

And how is an individual shallow and appears shallow? ^an4-104-4-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-104-4-2

Nor do they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-104-4-3

And how is an individual deep and appears deep? ^an4-104-5-1

It’s when an individual is impressive … ^an4-104-5-2

And they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-104-5-3

These four individuals similar to lakes are found in the world.” ^an4-104-5-7

### Mangoes (Numbered Discourses 4.105) ^an4-105-0

“Mendicants, there are these four mangoes. ^an4-105-1-1

What four? ^an4-105-1-2

One is unripe but seems ripe, ^an4-105-1-3

one is ripe but seems unripe, ^an4-105-1-4

one is unripe and seems unripe, and ^an4-105-1-5

one is ripe and seems ripe. ^an4-105-1-6

These are the four mangoes. ^an4-105-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to mangoes are found in the world. ^an4-105-1-8

What four? ^an4-105-1-9

One is unripe but seems ripe, ^an4-105-1-10

one is ripe but seems unripe, ^an4-105-1-11

one is unripe and seems unripe, and ^an4-105-1-12

one is ripe and seems ripe. ^an4-105-1-13

And how is an individual unripe but seems ripe? ^an4-105-2-1

It’s when an individual is impressive when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an4-105-2-2

But they don’t really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-105-2-3

That’s how an individual is unripe but seems ripe. ^an4-105-2-4

That individual is like a mango that’s unripe but seems ripe, I say. ^an4-105-2-5

And how is an individual ripe but seems unripe? ^an4-105-3-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-105-3-2

But they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-105-3-3

And how is an individual unripe and seems unripe? ^an4-105-4-1

It’s when an individual is not impressive … ^an4-105-4-2

Nor do they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-105-4-3

And how is an individual ripe and seems ripe? ^an4-105-5-1

It’s when an individual is impressive … ^an4-105-5-2

And they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-105-5-3

These four individuals similar to mangoes are found in the world.” ^an4-105-5-7

### Mangoes (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.106) ^an4-106-0

(This is a ghost sutta: there is no text for it in any available editions.) ^an4-106-1-1

### Mice (Numbered Discourses 4.107) ^an4-107-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of mice. ^an4-107-1-1

What four? ^an4-107-1-2

One makes a hole but doesn’t live in it, ^an4-107-1-3

one lives in a hole but doesn’t make it, ^an4-107-1-4

one neither makes a hole nor lives in it, and ^an4-107-1-5

one both makes a hole and lives in it. ^an4-107-1-6

These are the four kinds of mice. ^an4-107-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to mice are found in the world. ^an4-107-1-8

What four? ^an4-107-1-9

One makes a hole but doesn’t live in it, ^an4-107-1-10

one lives in a hole but doesn’t make it, ^an4-107-1-11

one neither makes a hole nor lives in it, and ^an4-107-1-12

one both makes a hole and lives in it. ^an4-107-1-13

And how does an individual make a hole but not live in it? ^an4-107-2-1

It’s when an individual memorizes the teaching— ^an4-107-2-2

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-107-2-3

But they don’t really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-107-2-4

That’s how an individual makes a hole but doesn’t live in it. ^an4-107-2-5

That individual is like a mouse that makes a hole but doesn’t live in it, I say. ^an4-107-2-6

And how does an individual live in a hole but not make it? ^an4-107-3-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t memorize the teaching … ^an4-107-3-2

But they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-107-3-4

And how does an individual neither make a hole nor live in it? ^an4-107-4-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t memorize the teaching … ^an4-107-4-2

Nor do they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-107-4-4

And how does an individual both make a hole and live in it? ^an4-107-5-1

It’s when an individual memorizes the teaching … ^an4-107-5-2

And they really understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ^an4-107-5-4

These four individuals similar to mice are found in the world.” ^an4-107-5-8

### Oxen (Numbered Discourses 4.108) ^an4-108-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of oxen. ^an4-108-1-1

What four? ^an4-108-1-2

One hostile to its own herd, not others; ^an4-108-1-3

one hostile to other herds, not its own; ^an4-108-1-4

one hostile to both its own herd and others; and ^an4-108-1-5

one hostile to neither its own herd nor others. ^an4-108-1-6

These are the four kinds of oxen. ^an4-108-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to oxen are found in the world. ^an4-108-1-8

What four? ^an4-108-1-9

One hostile to their own herd, not others; ^an4-108-1-10

one hostile to other herds, not their own; ^an4-108-1-11

one hostile to both their own herd and others; and ^an4-108-1-12

one hostile to neither their own herd nor others. ^an4-108-1-13

And how is an individual hostile to their own herd, not others? ^an4-108-2-1

It’s when an individual intimidates their own followers, not the followers of others. ^an4-108-2-2

That’s how an individual is hostile to their own herd, not others. ^an4-108-2-3

That individual is like an ox that’s hostile to its own herd, not others. ^an4-108-2-4

And how is an individual hostile to other herds, not their own? ^an4-108-3-1

It’s when an individual intimidates the followers of others, not their own. … ^an4-108-3-2

And how is an individual hostile to both their own herd and others? ^an4-108-4-1

It’s when an individual intimidates their own followers and the followers of others. … ^an4-108-4-2

And how is an individual hostile to neither their own herd nor others? ^an4-108-5-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t intimidate their own followers or the followers of others. ^an4-108-5-2

These four individuals similar to oxen are found in the world.” ^an4-108-5-6

### Trees (Numbered Discourses 4.109) ^an4-109-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of tree. ^an4-109-1-1

What four? ^an4-109-1-2

One is a softwood surrounded by softwoods, ^an4-109-1-3

one is a softwood surrounded by hardwoods, ^an4-109-1-4

one is a hardwood surrounded by softwoods, and ^an4-109-1-5

one is a hardwood surrounded by hardwoods. ^an4-109-1-6

These are the four kinds of tree. ^an4-109-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to trees are found in the world. ^an4-109-1-8

What four? ^an4-109-1-9

One is a softwood surrounded by softwoods, ^an4-109-1-10

one is a softwood surrounded by hardwoods, ^an4-109-1-11

one is a hardwood surrounded by softwoods, and ^an4-109-1-12

one is a hardwood surrounded by hardwoods. ^an4-109-1-13

And how is an individual a softwood surrounded by softwoods? ^an4-109-2-1

It’s when an individual is unethical, of bad character, ^an4-109-2-2

and their followers are the same. ^an4-109-2-3

That’s how an individual is a softwood surrounded by softwoods. ^an4-109-2-4

That individual is like a softwood tree that’s surrounded by softwoods, I say. ^an4-109-2-5

And how is an individual a softwood surrounded by hardwoods? ^an4-109-3-1

It’s when an individual is unethical, of bad character, ^an4-109-3-2

but their followers are ethical, of good character. … ^an4-109-3-3

And how is an individual a hardwood surrounded by softwoods? ^an4-109-4-1

It’s when an individual is ethical, of good character, ^an4-109-4-2

but their followers are unethical, of bad character. … ^an4-109-4-3

And how is an individual a hardwood surrounded by hardwoods? ^an4-109-5-1

It’s when an individual is ethical, of good character, ^an4-109-5-2

and their followers are the same. ^an4-109-5-3

These four individuals similar to trees are found in the world.” ^an4-109-5-7

### Vipers (Numbered Discourses 4.110) ^an4-110-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of viper. ^an4-110-1-1

What four? ^an4-110-1-2

One whose venom is fast-acting but not lethal, ^an4-110-1-3

one whose venom is lethal but not fast-acting, ^an4-110-1-4

one whose venom is both fast-acting and lethal, and ^an4-110-1-5

one whose venom is neither fast-acting nor lethal. ^an4-110-1-6

These are the four kinds of viper. ^an4-110-1-7

In the same way, these four individuals similar to vipers are found in the world. ^an4-110-1-8

What four? ^an4-110-1-9

One whose venom is fast-acting but not lethal, ^an4-110-1-10

one whose venom is lethal but not fast-acting, ^an4-110-1-11

one whose venom is both fast-acting and lethal, and ^an4-110-1-12

one whose venom is neither fast-acting nor lethal. ^an4-110-1-13

And how is an individual’s venom fast-acting but not lethal? ^an4-110-2-1

It’s when an individual is often angry, ^an4-110-2-2

but their anger doesn’t linger long. ^an4-110-2-3

That’s how an individual’s venom is fast-acting but not lethal. ^an4-110-2-4

That individual is like a viper whose venom is fast-acting but not lethal. ^an4-110-2-5

And how is an individual’s venom lethal but not fast-acting? ^an4-110-3-1

It’s when an individual is not often angry, ^an4-110-3-2

but their anger lingers for a long time. ^an4-110-3-3

And how is an individual’s venom both fast-acting and lethal? ^an4-110-4-1

It’s when an individual is often angry, ^an4-110-4-2

and their anger lingers for a long time. ^an4-110-4-3

And how is an individual’s venom neither fast-acting nor lethal? ^an4-110-5-1

It’s when an individual is not often angry, ^an4-110-5-2

and their anger doesn’t linger long. ^an4-110-5-3

These four individuals similar to vipers are found in the world.” ^an4-110-5-7

### With Kesi (Numbered Discourses 4.111) ^an4-111-0

Then Kesi the horse trainer went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an4-111-1-1

“Kesi, you’re known as a horse trainer. ^an4-111-1-2

Just how do you guide a horse in training?” ^an4-111-1-3

“Sir, I guide a horse in training sometimes gently, sometimes harshly, and sometimes both gently and harshly.” ^an4-111-1-4

“Kesi, what do you do with a horse in training that doesn’t follow these forms of training?” ^an4-111-1-5

“In that case, sir, I kill it. ^an4-111-1-7

Why is that? ^an4-111-1-8

So that I don’t disgrace my tradition. ^an4-111-1-9

But sir, the Buddha is the supreme guide for those fit for training. ^an4-111-2-1

Just how do you guide a person in training?” ^an4-111-2-2

“Kesi, I guide a person in training sometimes gently, sometimes harshly, and sometimes both gently and harshly. ^an4-111-2-3

The gentle way is this: ^an4-111-2-4

‘This is good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. This is the result of good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. This is life as a god. This is life as a human.’ ^an4-111-2-5

The harsh way is this: ^an4-111-2-6

‘This is bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. This is the result of bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. This is life in hell. This is life as an animal. This is life as a ghost.’ ^an4-111-2-7

The both gentle and harsh way is this: ^an4-111-3-1

‘This is good conduct … this is bad conduct …’” ^an4-111-3-2

“Sir, what do you do with a person in training who doesn’t follow these forms of training?” ^an4-111-4-1

“In that case, Kesi, I kill them.” ^an4-111-4-2

“Sir, it’s not appropriate for the Buddha to kill living creatures. ^an4-111-4-3

And yet you say ^an4-111-4-4

you kill them.” ^an4-111-4-5

“It’s true, Kesi, ^an4-111-4-6

it’s not appropriate for a Realized One to kill living creatures. ^an4-111-4-7

But when a person in training doesn’t follow any of these forms of training, the Realized One doesn’t think they’re worth advising or instructing, and neither do their sensible spiritual companions. ^an4-111-4-8

For it is killing in the training of the Noble One ^an4-111-4-9

when the Realized One doesn’t think they’re worth advising or instructing, and neither do their sensible spiritual companions.” ^an4-111-4-10

“Well, they’re definitely dead ^an4-111-5-1

when the Realized One doesn’t think they’re worth advising or instructing, and neither do their sensible spiritual companions. ^an4-111-5-2

Excellent, sir! … From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-111-5-3

### Speed (Numbered Discourses 4.112) ^an4-112-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-112-1-1

What four? ^an4-112-1-2

Integrity, speed, patience, and gentleness. ^an4-112-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these four factors is worthy of a king. … ^an4-112-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-112-2-1

What four? ^an4-112-2-2

Integrity, speed, patience, and gentleness. ^an4-112-2-3

A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an4-112-2-4

### The Goad (Numbered Discourses 4.113) ^an4-113-0

“Mendicants, these four fine thoroughbreds are found in the world. ^an4-113-1-1

What four? ^an4-113-1-2

One fine thoroughbred is moved to act when it sees the shadow of the goad, thinking: ^an4-113-1-3

‘What task will the horse trainer have me do today? How should I respond?’ ^an4-113-1-4

Some fine thoroughbreds are like that. ^an4-113-1-5

This is the first fine thoroughbred found in the world. ^an4-113-1-6

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred isn’t moved to act when it sees the shadow of the goad, but only when its hairs are struck, thinking: ^an4-113-2-1

‘What task will the horse trainer have me do today? How should I respond?’ ^an4-113-2-2

Some fine thoroughbreds are like that. ^an4-113-2-3

This is the second fine thoroughbred found in the world. ^an4-113-2-4

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred isn’t moved to act when it sees the shadow of the goad, nor when its hairs are struck, but only when its hide is struck, thinking: ^an4-113-3-1

‘What task will the horse trainer have me do today? How should I respond?’ ^an4-113-3-2

Some fine thoroughbreds are like that. ^an4-113-3-3

This is the third fine thoroughbred found in the world. ^an4-113-3-4

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred isn’t moved to act when it sees the shadow of the goad, nor when its hairs are struck, nor when its hide is struck, but only when its bone is struck, thinking: ^an4-113-4-1

‘What task will the horse trainer have me do today? How should I respond?’ ^an4-113-4-2

Some fine thoroughbreds are like that. ^an4-113-4-3

This is the fourth fine thoroughbred found in the world. ^an4-113-4-4

These are the four fine thoroughbreds found in the world. ^an4-113-4-5

In the same way, these four fine thoroughbred people are found in the world. ^an4-113-5-1

What four? ^an4-113-5-2

One fine thoroughbred person hears about ^an4-113-5-3

the suffering or death of a woman or man in such and such village or town. ^an4-113-5-4

They’re moved to act by this, ^an4-113-5-5

and strive effectively. ^an4-113-5-6

Applying themselves, they directly realize the ultimate truth, and see it with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-113-5-7

This individual is like the fine thoroughbred ^an4-113-5-8

that’s shaken when it sees the shadow of the goad. ^an4-113-5-9

Some fine thoroughbred people are like that. ^an4-113-5-10

This is the first fine thoroughbred person found in the world. ^an4-113-5-11

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred person doesn’t hear about ^an4-113-6-1

the suffering or death of a woman or man in such and such village or town, but they see it themselves. ^an4-113-6-2

They’re moved to act by this, ^an4-113-6-3

and strive effectively. ^an4-113-6-4

Applying themselves, they directly realize the ultimate truth, and see it with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-113-6-5

This person is like the fine thoroughbred that’s moved to act ^an4-113-6-6

when its hairs are struck. ^an4-113-6-7

Some fine thoroughbred people are like that. ^an4-113-6-8

This is the second fine thoroughbred person found in the world. ^an4-113-6-9

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred person doesn’t hear about ^an4-113-7-1

the suffering or death of a woman or man in such and such village or town, nor do they see it themselves, but it happens to their own relative or family member. ^an4-113-7-2

They’re moved to act by this, ^an4-113-7-3

and strive effectively. ^an4-113-7-4

Applying themselves, they directly realize the ultimate truth, and see it with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-113-7-5

This person is like the fine thoroughbred that’s moved to act ^an4-113-7-6

when its skin is struck. ^an4-113-7-7

Some fine thoroughbred people are like that. ^an4-113-7-8

This is the third fine thoroughbred person found in the world. ^an4-113-7-9

Furthermore, one fine thoroughbred person doesn’t hear about ^an4-113-8-1

the suffering or death of a woman or man in such and such village or town, nor do they see it themselves, nor does it happen to their own relative or family member, but they themselves are afflicted with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an4-113-8-2

They’re moved to act by this, ^an4-113-8-3

and strive effectively. ^an4-113-8-4

Applying themselves, they directly realize the ultimate truth, and see it with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-113-8-5

This person is like the fine thoroughbred that’s moved to act ^an4-113-8-6

when its bone is struck. ^an4-113-8-7

Some fine thoroughbred people are like that. ^an4-113-8-8

This is the fourth fine thoroughbred person found in the world. ^an4-113-8-9

These are the four fine thoroughbred people found in the world.” ^an4-113-8-10

### A Royal Elephant (Numbered Discourses 4.114) ^an4-114-0

“Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-114-1-1

What four? ^an4-114-1-2

A royal bull elephant listens, destroys, endures, and goes fast. ^an4-114-1-3

And how does a royal bull elephant listen? ^an4-114-2-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens to whatever task the elephant trainer has it do, ^an4-114-2-2

whether or not it has done it before. ^an4-114-2-3

That’s how a royal bull elephant listens. ^an4-114-2-5

And how does a royal bull elephant destroy? ^an4-114-3-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant in battle destroys elephants with their riders, horses with their riders, chariots and charioteers, and foot soldiers. ^an4-114-3-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant destroys. ^an4-114-3-3

And how does a royal bull elephant endure? ^an4-114-4-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant in battle endures being struck by spears, swords, arrows, and axes; it endures the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. ^an4-114-4-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant endures. ^an4-114-4-3

And how does a royal bull elephant go fast? ^an4-114-5-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant swiftly goes in whatever direction the elephant trainer sends it, ^an4-114-5-2

whether or not it has been there before. ^an4-114-5-3

That’s how a royal bull elephant goes fast. ^an4-114-5-5

A royal bull elephant with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-114-5-6

In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-114-6-1

What four? ^an4-114-6-2

A mendicant listens, destroys, endures, and goes fast. ^an4-114-6-3

And how does a mendicant listen? ^an4-114-7-1

It’s when a mendicant pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens when the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One is being taught. ^an4-114-7-2

That’s how a mendicant listens. ^an4-114-7-3

And how does a mendicant destroy? ^an4-114-8-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an4-114-8-2

That’s how a mendicant destroys. ^an4-114-8-3

And how does a mendicant endure? ^an4-114-9-1

It’s when a mendicant endures cold, heat, hunger, and thirst; the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles; rude and unwelcome criticism; and they put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an4-114-9-2

That’s how a mendicant endures. ^an4-114-9-3

And how does a mendicant go fast? ^an4-114-10-1

It’s when a mendicant swiftly goes in the direction they’ve never gone before in all this long time; that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^an4-114-10-2

That’s how a mendicant goes fast. ^an4-114-10-3

A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an4-114-10-4

### Things (Numbered Discourses 4.115) ^an4-115-0

“Mendicants, there are these four things. ^an4-115-1-1

What four? ^an4-115-1-2

There is a thing that’s unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-1-3

and doing it proves harmful. ^an4-115-1-4

There is a thing that’s unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-1-5

but doing it proves beneficial. ^an4-115-1-6

There is a thing that’s pleasant to do, ^an4-115-1-7

but doing it proves harmful. ^an4-115-1-8

There is a thing that’s pleasant to do, ^an4-115-1-9

and doing it proves beneficial. ^an4-115-1-10

Take the thing that’s unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-2-1

and doing it proves harmful. ^an4-115-2-2

This is regarded as a thing that shouldn’t be done on both grounds: ^an4-115-2-3

because it’s unpleasant, ^an4-115-2-4

and because doing it proves harmful. ^an4-115-2-6

This is regarded as a thing that shouldn’t be done on both grounds. ^an4-115-2-8

Next, take the thing that’s unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-3-1

but doing it proves beneficial. ^an4-115-3-2

It is here that you can tell who is foolish and who is astute in regard to human strength, energy, and vigor. ^an4-115-3-3

A fool doesn’t reflect: ^an4-115-3-4

‘Despite the fact that this thing is unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-3-5

doing it still proves beneficial.’ ^an4-115-3-6

They don’t do that thing, ^an4-115-3-7

so that proves harmful. ^an4-115-3-8

An astute person does reflect: ^an4-115-3-9

‘Even though this thing is unpleasant to do, ^an4-115-3-10

doing it still proves beneficial.’ ^an4-115-3-11

They do that thing, ^an4-115-3-12

so that proves beneficial. ^an4-115-3-13

Next, take the thing that’s pleasant to do, ^an4-115-4-1

but doing it proves harmful. ^an4-115-4-2

It is here that you can tell who is foolish and who is astute in regard to human strength, energy, and vigor. ^an4-115-4-3

A fool doesn’t reflect: ^an4-115-4-4

‘Even though this thing is pleasant to do, ^an4-115-4-5

doing it still proves harmful.’ ^an4-115-4-6

They do that thing, ^an4-115-4-7

and so that proves harmful. ^an4-115-4-8

An astute person does reflect: ^an4-115-4-9

‘Despite the fact that this thing is pleasant to do, ^an4-115-4-10

doing it still proves harmful.’ ^an4-115-4-11

They don’t do that thing, ^an4-115-4-12

so that proves beneficial. ^an4-115-4-13

Next, take the thing that’s pleasant to do, and doing it proves beneficial. ^an4-115-5-1

This is regarded as a thing that should be done on both grounds: ^an4-115-5-2

because it’s pleasant, ^an4-115-5-3

and because doing it proves beneficial. ^an4-115-5-4

This is regarded as a thing that should be done on both grounds. ^an4-115-5-5

These are the four things.” ^an4-115-5-6

### Diligence (Numbered Discourses 4.116) ^an4-116-0

“Mendicants, you should be diligent in four situations. ^an4-116-1-1

What four? ^an4-116-1-2

Give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind; and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-116-1-3

Don’t neglect these things. ^an4-116-1-4

Give up wrong view; and develop right view. ^an4-116-1-9

Don’t neglect this. ^an4-116-1-10

A mendicant who has done these things does not fear death in lives to come.” ^an4-116-2-1

### Guarding (Numbered Discourses 4.117) ^an4-117-0

“Mendicants, in your own way you should practice diligence, mindfulness, and guarding of the mind in four situations. ^an4-117-1-1

What four? ^an4-117-1-2

‘May my mind not be aroused by things that arouse greed.’ In your own way you should practice diligence, mindfulness, and guarding of the mind. ^an4-117-1-3

‘May my mind not be angered by things that provoke hate.’ … ^an4-117-1-4

‘May my mind not be deluded by things that promote delusion.’ … ^an4-117-1-5

‘May my mind not be intoxicated by things that intoxicate.’ … ^an4-117-1-6

When a mendicant’s mind is no longer affected by greed, hate, delusion, or intoxication because they’ve got rid of these things, they don’t shake, tremble, quake, or get nervous, nor are they persuaded by the teachings of other ascetics.” ^an4-117-2-1

### Inspiring (Numbered Discourses 4.118) ^an4-118-0

“Mendicants, a faithful gentleman should go to see these four inspiring places. ^an4-118-1-1

What four? ^an4-118-1-2

Thinking: ‘Here the Realized One was born!’—that is an inspiring place. ^an4-118-1-3

Thinking: ‘Here the Realized One became awakened as a supreme fully awakened Buddha!’—that is an inspiring place. ^an4-118-1-4

Thinking: ‘Here the Realized One rolled forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma!’—that is an inspiring place. ^an4-118-1-5

Thinking: ‘Here the Realized One was fully quenched in the element of extinguishment, with no residue!’—that is an inspiring place. ^an4-118-1-6

These are the four inspiring places that a faithful gentleman should go to see.” ^an4-118-1-7

### Perils (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.119) ^an4-119-0

“Mendicants, there are these four perils. ^an4-119-1-1

What four? ^an4-119-1-2

The perils of rebirth, old age, sickness, and death. ^an4-119-1-3

These are the four perils.” ^an4-119-1-4

### Perils (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.120) ^an4-120-0

“Mendicants, there are these four perils. ^an4-120-1-1

What four? ^an4-120-1-2

The perils of fire, water, kings, and bandits. ^an4-120-1-3

These are the four perils.” ^an4-120-1-4

### Guilt (Numbered Discourses 4.121) ^an4-121-0

“Mendicants, there are these four fears. ^an4-121-1-1

What four? ^an4-121-1-2

The fears of guilt, shame, punishment, and going to a bad place. ^an4-121-1-3

And what, mendicants, is the fear of guilt? ^an4-121-2-1

It’s when someone reflects: ^an4-121-2-2

‘If I were to do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind, wouldn’t I blame myself for my conduct?’ ^an4-121-2-3

Being afraid of guilt, they give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, keeping themselves pure. ^an4-121-2-4

This is called the fear of guilt. ^an4-121-2-5

And what, mendicants, is the fear of shame? ^an4-121-3-1

It’s when someone reflects: ^an4-121-3-2

‘If I were to do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind, wouldn’t others blame me for my conduct?’ ^an4-121-3-3

Being afraid of shame, they give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, keeping themselves pure. ^an4-121-3-4

This is called the fear of shame. ^an4-121-3-5

And what, mendicants, is the fear of punishment? ^an4-121-4-1

It’s when someone sees that the kings have arrested a bandit, a criminal, and subjected them to various punishments— ^an4-121-4-2

whipping, caning, and clubbing; cutting off hands or feet, or both; cutting off ears or nose, or both; the ‘porridge pot’, the ‘shell-shave’, the ‘Rāhu’s mouth’, the ‘garland of fire’, the ‘burning hand’, the ‘bulrush twist’, the ‘bark dress’, the ‘antelope’, the ‘meat hook’, the ‘coins’, the ‘caustic pickle’, the ‘twisting bar’, the ‘straw mat’; being splashed with hot oil, being fed to the hounds, being impaled alive, and being beheaded. ^an4-121-4-3

They think: ^an4-121-5-1

‘If I were to do the same kind of bad deed, the kings would punish me in the same way.’ … ^an4-121-5-2

Being afraid of punishment, they don’t steal the belongings of others. ^an4-121-5-4

They give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, keeping themselves pure. ^an4-121-5-5

This is called the fear of punishment. ^an4-121-5-6

And what, mendicants, is the fear of rebirth in a bad place? ^an4-121-6-1

It’s when someone reflects: ^an4-121-6-2

‘Bad conduct of body, speech, or mind has a bad result in the next life. ^an4-121-6-3

If I were to do such bad things, when my body breaks up, after death, I’d be reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.’ ^an4-121-6-4

Being afraid of rebirth in a bad place, they give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, keeping themselves pure. ^an4-121-6-5

This is called the fear of rebirth in a bad place. ^an4-121-6-6

These are the four fears.” ^an4-121-6-7

### The Danger of Waves (Numbered Discourses 4.122) ^an4-122-0

“Mendicants, anyone who enters the water should anticipate four dangers. ^an4-122-1-1

What four? ^an4-122-1-2

The dangers of waves, gharials, whirlpools, and sharks. ^an4-122-1-3

These are the four dangers that anyone who enters the water should anticipate. ^an4-122-1-4

In the same way, a gentleman gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in this teaching and training should anticipate four dangers. ^an4-122-1-5

What four? ^an4-122-1-6

The dangers of waves, gharials, whirlpools, and sharks. ^an4-122-1-7

And what, mendicants, is the danger of waves? ^an4-122-2-1

It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, thinking: ^an4-122-2-2

‘I’m swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I’m swamped by suffering, mired in suffering. ^an4-122-2-3

Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.’ ^an4-122-2-4

When they’ve gone forth, their spiritual companions advise and instruct them: ^an4-122-2-5

‘You should go out like this, and come back like that. You should look to the front like this, and to the side like that. You should contract your limbs like this, and extend them like that. This is how you should bear your outer robe, bowl, and robes.’ ^an4-122-2-6

They think: ^an4-122-2-7

‘Formerly, as laypeople, we advised and instructed others. ^an4-122-2-8

And now these mendicants—who you’d think were our children or grandchildren—imagine they can advise and instruct us!’ ^an4-122-2-9

Angry and upset, they resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an4-122-2-10

This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of waves. ^an4-122-2-11

‘Danger of waves’ is a term for anger and distress. ^an4-122-2-12

This is called the danger of waves. ^an4-122-2-13

And what, mendicants, is the danger of gharials? ^an4-122-3-1

It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness … ^an4-122-3-2

When they’ve gone forth, their spiritual companions advise and instruct them: ^an4-122-3-5

‘You may eat, consume, taste, and drink these things, but not those. You may eat what’s allowable, but not what’s unallowable. You may eat at the right time, but not at the wrong time.’ ^an4-122-3-6

They think: ^an4-122-3-7

‘Formerly, as laypeople, we used to eat, consume, taste, and drink what we wanted, not what we didn’t want. ^an4-122-3-8

We ate and drank both allowable and unallowable things, at the right time and the wrong time. ^an4-122-3-12

And these faithful householders give us delicious fresh and cooked foods at the wrong time of day. But these guys imagine they can gag our mouths!’ ^an4-122-3-13

Angry and upset, they resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an4-122-3-14

This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of gharials. ^an4-122-3-15

‘Danger of gharials’ is a term for gluttony. ^an4-122-3-16

This is called the danger of gharials. ^an4-122-3-17

And what, mendicants, is the danger of whirlpools? ^an4-122-4-1

It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness … ^an4-122-4-2

When they’ve gone forth, they robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an4-122-4-5

There they see a householder or their child amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an4-122-4-6

They think: ^an4-122-4-7

‘Formerly, as laypeople, we amused ourselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an4-122-4-8

And it’s true that my family is wealthy. ^an4-122-4-9

I can both enjoy my wealth and make merit. ^an4-122-4-10

Why don’t I resign the training and return to a lesser life, so I can enjoy my wealth and make merit?’ ^an4-122-4-11

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an4-122-4-12

This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of whirlpools. ^an4-122-4-13
