‘Danger of whirlpools’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an4-122-4-14

This is called the danger of whirlpools. ^an4-122-4-15

And what, mendicants, is the danger of sharks? ^an4-122-5-1

It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness … ^an4-122-5-2

When they’ve gone forth, they robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an4-122-5-5

There they see a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^an4-122-5-6

Lust infects their mind, ^an4-122-5-7

so they resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an4-122-5-8

This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life because they’re afraid of the danger of sharks. ^an4-122-5-9

‘Danger of sharks’ is a term for ladies. ^an4-122-5-10

This is called the danger of sharks. ^an4-122-5-11

These are the four dangers that a gentleman gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in this teaching and training should anticipate.” ^an4-122-5-12

### Difference (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.123) ^an4-123-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-123-1-1

What four? ^an4-123-1-2

Take an individual who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an4-123-1-3

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-123-1-4

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the Divinity’s host. ^an4-123-1-5

The lifespan of the gods of the Divinity’s host is one eon. ^an4-123-1-6

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-123-1-7

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-123-1-8

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-123-1-9

Furthermore, take an individual who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an4-123-2-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-123-2-2

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of streaming radiance. ^an4-123-2-3

The lifespan of the gods of streaming radiance is two eons. ^an4-123-2-4

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-123-2-5

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-123-2-6

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-123-2-7

Furthermore, take an individual who, with the fading away of rapture, enters and remains in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an4-123-3-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-123-3-2

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of universal beauty. ^an4-123-3-3

The lifespan of the gods of universal beauty is four eons. ^an4-123-3-4

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-123-3-5

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-123-3-6

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-123-3-7

Furthermore, take an individual who, giving up pleasure and pain, and ending former happiness and sadness, enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an4-123-4-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-123-4-2

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of abundant fruit. ^an4-123-4-3

The lifespan of the gods of abundant fruit is five hundred eons. ^an4-123-4-4

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-123-4-5

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-123-4-6

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-123-4-7

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-123-4-8

### Difference (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.124) ^an4-124-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-124-1-1

What four? ^an4-124-1-2

Take an individual who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … ^an4-124-1-3

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an4-124-1-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the pure abodes. ^an4-124-1-5

This rebirth is distinct from that of ordinary people. ^an4-124-1-6

Furthermore, take an individual who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an4-124-2-1

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an4-124-2-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the pure abodes. ^an4-124-2-3

This rebirth is distinct from that of ordinary people. ^an4-124-2-4

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-124-2-5

### Love (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.125) ^an4-125-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-125-1-1

What four? ^an4-125-1-2

Firstly, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-125-1-3

They enjoy this and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-125-1-4

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the Divinity’s host. ^an4-125-1-5

The lifespan of the gods of the Divinity’s host is one eon. ^an4-125-1-6

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-125-1-7

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-125-1-8

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-125-1-9

Furthermore, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of compassion … They’re reborn in the company of the gods of streaming radiance. The lifespan of the gods of streaming radiance is two eons. ^an4-125-2-1

Furthermore, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of rejoicing … They’re reborn in the company of the gods of universal beauty. The lifespan of the gods of universal beauty is four eons. ^an4-125-2-2

Furthermore, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-125-2-3

They enjoy this and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-125-2-4

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die ^an4-125-2-5

they’re reborn in the company of the gods of abundant fruit. ^an4-125-2-7

The lifespan of the gods of abundant fruit is five hundred eons. ^an4-125-2-8

An ordinary person stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they go to hell or the animal realm or the ghost realm. ^an4-125-2-9

But a disciple of the Buddha stays there until the lifespan of those gods is spent, then they’re extinguished in that very life. ^an4-125-2-10

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person, that is, when there is a place of rebirth. ^an4-125-2-11

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-125-2-12

### Love (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.126) ^an4-126-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-126-1-1

What four? ^an4-126-1-2

Firstly, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-126-1-3

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an4-126-1-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the pure abodes. ^an4-126-1-5

This rebirth is distinct from that of ordinary people. ^an4-126-1-6

Furthermore, an individual meditates spreading a heart full of compassion … ^an4-126-2-1

rejoicing … ^an4-126-2-2

equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-126-2-3

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an4-126-2-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the pure abodes. ^an4-126-2-5

This rebirth is distinct from that of ordinary people. ^an4-126-2-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-126-2-7

### Incredible Things About the Realized One (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.127) ^an4-127-0

“Mendicants, with the appearance of a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, four incredible and amazing things appear. ^an4-127-1-1

What four? ^an4-127-1-2

When the being intent on awakening passes away from the host of joyful gods, he’s conceived in his mother’s womb, mindful and aware. And then—in this world with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—an immeasurable, magnificent light appears, surpassing the glory of the gods. ^an4-127-1-3

Even in the boundless gloom of interstellar space—so utterly dark that even the luminosity of the moon and the sun, so mighty and powerful, makes no impression—an immeasurable, magnificent light appears, surpassing the glory of the gods. ^an4-127-1-4

And the sentient beings reborn there recognize each other by that light: ^an4-127-1-5

‘So, it seems other sentient beings have been reborn here!’ ^an4-127-1-6

This is the first incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-127-1-7

Furthermore, the being intent on awakening emerges from his mother’s womb, mindful and aware. And then … an immeasurable, magnificent light appears … ^an4-127-2-1

even in the boundless gloom of interstellar space … ^an4-127-2-2

This is the second incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-127-2-5

Furthermore, the Realized One awakens to the supreme perfect awakening. And then … an immeasurable, magnificent light appears … ^an4-127-3-1

even in the boundless gloom of interstellar space … ^an4-127-3-2

This is the third incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-127-3-5

Furthermore, the Realized One rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma. And then … an immeasurable, magnificent light appears … ^an4-127-4-1

even in the boundless gloom of interstellar space … ^an4-127-4-2

This is the fourth incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-127-4-5

With the appearance of a Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, these four incredible and amazing things appear.” ^an4-127-4-6

### Incredible Things About the Realized One (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.128) ^an4-128-0

“Mendicants, with the appearance of a Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, four incredible and amazing things appear. ^an4-128-1-1

What four? ^an4-128-1-2

People like clinging, they love it and enjoy it. ^an4-128-1-3

Yet when a Realized One is teaching the Dhamma of non-clinging, they want to listen, they actively listen, and they try to understand. ^an4-128-1-4

This is the first incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-128-1-5

People like conceit, they love it and enjoy it. ^an4-128-2-1

Yet when a Realized One is teaching the Dhamma of removing conceit, they want to listen, they actively listen, and they try to understand. ^an4-128-2-2

This is the second incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-128-2-3

People like excitement, they love it and enjoy it. ^an4-128-3-1

Yet when a Realized One is teaching the Dhamma of peace, they want to listen, they actively listen, and they try to understand. ^an4-128-3-2

This is the third incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-128-3-3

This population is lost in ignorance, swaddled in their shells. ^an4-128-4-1

Yet when a Realized One is teaching the Dhamma of removing ignorance, they want to listen, they actively listen, and they try to understand. ^an4-128-4-2

This is the fourth incredible and amazing thing that appears with the appearance of a Realized One. ^an4-128-4-3

With the appearance of a Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, four incredible and amazing things appear.” ^an4-128-4-4

### Incredible Things About Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 4.129) ^an4-129-0

“Mendicants, there are these four incredible and amazing things about Ānanda. ^an4-129-1-1

What four? ^an4-129-1-2

If an assembly of monks goes to see Ānanda, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-129-1-3

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-129-1-4

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-129-1-5

If an assembly of nuns … ^an4-129-2-1

laymen … ^an4-129-3-1

or laywomen goes to see Ānanda, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-129-4-1

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-129-4-2

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-129-4-3

These are the four incredible and amazing things about Ānanda.” ^an4-129-4-4

### Incredible Things About the Wheel-Turning Monarch (Numbered Discourses 4.130) ^an4-130-0

“Mendicants, there are these four incredible and amazing things about a wheel-turning monarch. ^an4-130-1-1

What four? ^an4-130-1-2

If an assembly of aristocrats goes to see a wheel-turning monarch, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-130-1-3

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-130-1-4

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-130-1-5

If an assembly of brahmins … ^an4-130-2-1

householders … ^an4-130-3-1

or ascetics goes to see a wheel-turning monarch, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-130-4-1

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-130-4-2

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-130-4-3

These are the four incredible and amazing things about a wheel-turning monarch. ^an4-130-4-4

In the same way, there are these four incredible and amazing things about Ānanda. ^an4-130-5-1

What four? ^an4-130-5-2

If an assembly of monks goes to see Ānanda, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-130-5-3

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-130-5-4

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-130-5-5

If an assembly of nuns … laymen … or laywomen goes to see Ānanda, they’re uplifted by seeing him ^an4-130-6-1

and uplifted by hearing him speak. ^an4-130-6-2

And when he falls silent, they’ve never had enough. ^an4-130-6-3

These are the four incredible and amazing things about Ānanda.” ^an4-130-6-4

### Fetters (Numbered Discourses 4.131) ^an4-131-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-131-1-1

What four? ^an4-131-1-2

One individual hasn’t given up the lower fetters, the fetters for getting reborn, or the fetters for getting a continued existence. ^an4-131-1-3

One individual has given up the lower fetters, but not the fetters for getting reborn, or the fetters for getting a continued existence. ^an4-131-2-1

One individual has given up the lower fetters and the fetters for getting reborn, but not the fetters for getting a continued existence. ^an4-131-3-1

One individual has given up the lower fetters, the fetters for getting reborn, and the fetters for getting a continued existence. ^an4-131-4-1

What individual hasn’t given up the lower fetters, the fetters for getting reborn, or the fetters for getting a continued existence? ^an4-131-5-1

A once-returner. ^an4-131-5-2

This is the individual who hasn’t given up the lower fetters, the fetters for getting reborn, or the fetters for getting a continued existence. ^an4-131-5-3

What individual has given up the lower fetters, but not the fetters for getting reborn, or the fetters for getting a continued existence? ^an4-131-6-1

One heading upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an4-131-6-2

What individual has given up the lower fetters and the fetters for getting reborn, but not the fetters for getting a continued existence? ^an4-131-7-1

One extinguished between one life and the next. ^an4-131-7-2

What individual has given up the lower fetters, the fetters for getting reborn, and the fetters for getting a continued existence? ^an4-131-8-1

A perfected one. ^an4-131-8-2

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-131-8-4

### Eloquence (Numbered Discourses 4.132) ^an4-132-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-132-1-1

What four? ^an4-132-1-2

One whose elocution is on topic, but not fluent. ^an4-132-1-3

One whose elocution is fluent, but not on topic. ^an4-132-1-4

One whose elocution is both on topic and fluent. ^an4-132-1-5

One whose elocution is neither on topic nor fluent. ^an4-132-1-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-132-1-7

### One Who Understands Immediately (Numbered Discourses 4.133) ^an4-133-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-133-1-1

What four? ^an4-133-1-2

One who understands immediately, one who understands after detailed explanation, one who needs education, and one who merely learns by rote. ^an4-133-1-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-133-1-4

### The Fruits of Initiative (Numbered Discourses 4.134) ^an4-134-0

“These four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-134-1-1

What four? ^an4-134-1-2

One who lives off the fruit of initiative, but not deeds; ^an4-134-1-3

one who lives off the fruit of deeds, but not initiative; ^an4-134-1-4

one who lives off the fruit of both deeds and initiative; ^an4-134-1-5

one who lives off the fruit of neither initiative nor deeds. ^an4-134-1-6

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-134-1-7

### Blameworthy (Numbered Discourses 4.135) ^an4-135-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-135-1-1

What four? ^an4-135-1-2

The blameworthy, the mostly blameworthy, the slightly blameworthy, and the blameless. ^an4-135-1-3

And how is an individual blameworthy? ^an4-135-2-1

It’s when an individual does things by way of body, speech, and mind that are blameworthy. ^an4-135-2-2

That’s how an individual is blameworthy. ^an4-135-2-3

And how is an individual mostly blameworthy? ^an4-135-3-1

It’s when an individual does things by way of body, speech, and mind that are mostly blameworthy, but occasionally blameless. ^an4-135-3-2

That’s how an individual is mostly blameworthy. ^an4-135-3-5

And how is an individual slightly blameworthy? ^an4-135-4-1

It’s when an individual does things by way of body, speech, and mind that are mostly blameless, but occasionally blameworthy. ^an4-135-4-2

That’s how an individual is slightly blameworthy. ^an4-135-4-5

And how is an individual blameless? ^an4-135-5-1

It’s when an individual does things by way of body, speech, and mind that are blameless. ^an4-135-5-2

That’s how an individual is blameless. ^an4-135-5-3

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-135-5-4

### Ethics (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.136) ^an4-136-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-136-1-1

What four? ^an4-136-1-2

One individual has not fulfilled ethics, immersion, or wisdom. ^an4-136-1-3

One individual has fulfilled ethics, but not immersion or wisdom. ^an4-136-2-1

One individual has fulfilled ethics and immersion, but not wisdom. ^an4-136-3-1

One individual has fulfilled ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-136-4-1

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-136-4-2

### Ethics (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.137) ^an4-137-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-137-1-1

What four? ^an4-137-1-2

One individual doesn’t value or submit to ethics, immersion, or wisdom. ^an4-137-1-3

One individual values and submits to ethics, but not to immersion or wisdom. ^an4-137-2-1

One individual values and submits to ethics and immersion, but not wisdom. ^an4-137-3-1

One individual values and submits to ethics, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-137-4-1

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-137-4-2

### Retreat (Numbered Discourses 4.138) ^an4-138-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-138-1-1

What four? ^an4-138-1-2

One is on retreat in body, but not mind; ^an4-138-1-3

one is on retreat in mind, but not body; ^an4-138-1-4

one is on retreat in neither body nor mind; and ^an4-138-1-5

one is on retreat in both body and mind. ^an4-138-1-6

And how is an individual on retreat in body, but not mind? ^an4-138-2-1

It’s when an individual frequents remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-138-2-2

But they think sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^an4-138-2-3

That’s how an individual is on retreat in body, but not mind. ^an4-138-2-4

And how is an individual on retreat in mind, but not body? ^an4-138-3-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-138-3-2

But they think thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. ^an4-138-3-3

That’s how an individual is on retreat in mind, but not body. ^an4-138-3-4

And how is an individual on retreat in neither body nor mind? ^an4-138-4-1

It’s when an individual doesn’t frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-138-4-2

And they think sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^an4-138-4-3

That’s how an individual is on retreat in neither body nor mind. ^an4-138-4-4

And how is an individual on retreat in both body and mind? ^an4-138-5-1

It’s when an individual frequents remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-138-5-2

And they think thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. ^an4-138-5-3

That’s how an individual is on retreat in both body and mind. ^an4-138-5-4

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-138-5-5

### Dhamma Speakers (Numbered Discourses 4.139) ^an4-139-0

“Mendicants, there are these four Dhamma speakers. ^an4-139-1-1

What four? ^an4-139-1-2

One Dhamma speaker speaks little and off topic. ^an4-139-1-3

And their assembly can’t tell what’s on topic and what’s off topic. ^an4-139-1-4

Such an assembly regards such a Dhamma speaker simply as a Dhamma speaker. ^an4-139-1-5

One Dhamma speaker speaks little but stays on topic. ^an4-139-2-1

And their assembly can tell what’s on topic and what’s off topic. ^an4-139-2-2

Such an assembly regards such a Dhamma speaker simply as a Dhamma speaker. ^an4-139-2-3

One Dhamma speaker speaks much but off topic. ^an4-139-3-1

And their assembly can’t tell what’s on topic and what’s off topic. ^an4-139-3-2

Such an assembly regards such a Dhamma speaker simply as a Dhamma speaker. ^an4-139-3-3

One Dhamma speaker speaks much and stays on topic. ^an4-139-4-1

And their assembly can tell what’s on topic and what’s off topic. ^an4-139-4-2

Such an assembly regards such a Dhamma speaker simply as a Dhamma speaker. ^an4-139-4-3

These are the four Dhamma speakers.” ^an4-139-4-4

### Speaker (Numbered Discourses 4.140) ^an4-140-0

“Mendicants, there are these four speakers. ^an4-140-1-1

What four? ^an4-140-1-2

There’s a speaker who runs out of meaningful things to say, but not of ways of phrasing things. ^an4-140-1-3

There’s a speaker who runs out of ways of phrasing things, but not of meaningful things to say. ^an4-140-1-4

There’s a speaker who runs out of both meaningful things to say, and ways of phrasing things. ^an4-140-1-5

There’s a speaker who never runs out of meaningful things to say, or ways of phrasing things. ^an4-140-1-6

These are the four speakers. ^an4-140-1-7

It is impossible, it cannot happen that someone accomplished in the four kinds of textual analysis will ever run out of meaningful things to say, or ways of phrasing things.” ^an4-140-1-8

### Brightness (Numbered Discourses 4.141) ^an4-141-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of brightness. ^an4-141-1-1

What four? ^an4-141-1-2

The brightness of the moon, sun, fire, and wisdom. ^an4-141-1-3

These are the four kinds of brightness. ^an4-141-1-4

The best of these four kinds of brightness is the brightness of wisdom.” ^an4-141-1-5

### Radiance (Numbered Discourses 4.142) ^an4-142-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of radiance. ^an4-142-1-1

What four? ^an4-142-1-2

The radiance of the moon, sun, fire, and wisdom. ^an4-142-1-3

These are the four kinds of radiance. ^an4-142-1-4

The best of these four kinds of radiance is the radiance of wisdom.” ^an4-142-1-5

### Light (Numbered Discourses 4.143) ^an4-143-0

“Mendicants, there are these four lights. ^an4-143-1-1

What four? ^an4-143-1-2

The lights of the moon, sun, fire, and wisdom. ^an4-143-1-3

These are the four lights. ^an4-143-1-4

The best of these four lights is the light of wisdom.” ^an4-143-1-5

### Shining (Numbered Discourses 4.144) ^an4-144-0

“Mendicants, there are four kinds of shining. ^an4-144-1-1

What four? ^an4-144-1-2

The shining of the moon, sun, fire, and wisdom. ^an4-144-1-3

These are the four kinds of shining. ^an4-144-1-4

The best of these four kinds of shining is the shining of wisdom.” ^an4-144-1-5

### Lamps (Numbered Discourses 4.145) ^an4-145-0

“Mendicants, there are these four lamps. ^an4-145-1-1

What four? ^an4-145-1-2

The lamps of the moon, sun, fire, and wisdom. ^an4-145-1-3

These are the four lamps. ^an4-145-1-4

The best of these four lamps is the lamp of wisdom.” ^an4-145-1-5

### Times (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.146) ^an4-146-0

“Mendicants, there are these four times. ^an4-146-1-1

What four? ^an4-146-1-2

A time for listening to the teaching, a time for discussing the teaching, a time for serenity, and a time for discernment. ^an4-146-1-3

These are the four times.” ^an4-146-1-4

### Times (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.147) ^an4-147-0

“Mendicants, when these four times are rightly developed and reiterated, they gradually lead to the ending of defilements. ^an4-147-1-1

What four? ^an4-147-1-2

A time for listening to the teaching, a time for discussing the teaching, a time for serenity, and a time for discernment. ^an4-147-1-3

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an4-147-2-1

In the same way, when these four times are rightly developed and reiterated, they gradually lead to the ending of defilements.” ^an4-147-2-2

### Bad Conduct (Numbered Discourses 4.148) ^an4-148-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of bad conduct by way of speech. ^an4-148-1-1

What four? ^an4-148-1-2

Speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-148-1-3

These are the four kinds of bad conduct by way of speech.” ^an4-148-1-4

### Good Conduct (Numbered Discourses 4.149) ^an4-149-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of good conduct by way of speech. ^an4-149-1-1

What four? ^an4-149-1-2

Speech that’s true, harmonious, gentle, and thoughtful. ^an4-149-1-3

These are the four kinds of good conduct by way of speech.” ^an4-149-1-4

### Essentials (Numbered Discourses 4.150) ^an4-150-0

“Mendicants, there are these four essentials. ^an4-150-1-1

What four? ^an4-150-1-2

Ethics, immersion, wisdom, and freedom are essentials. ^an4-150-1-3

These are the four essentials.” ^an4-150-1-4

### Faculties (Numbered Discourses 4.151) ^an4-151-0

“Mendicants, there are these four faculties. ^an4-151-1-1

What four? ^an4-151-1-2

The faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, and immersion. ^an4-151-1-3

These are the four faculties.” ^an4-151-1-4

### The Power of Faith (Numbered Discourses 4.152) ^an4-152-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an4-152-1-1

What four? ^an4-152-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, and immersion. ^an4-152-1-3

These are the four powers.” ^an4-152-1-4

### The Power of Wisdom (Numbered Discourses 4.153) ^an4-153-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an4-153-1-1

What four? ^an4-153-1-2

The powers of wisdom, energy, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an4-153-1-3

These are the four powers.” ^an4-153-1-4

### The Power of Mindfulness (Numbered Discourses 4.154) ^an4-154-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an4-154-1-1

What four? ^an4-154-1-2

The powers of mindfulness, immersion, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an4-154-1-3

These are the four powers.” ^an4-154-1-4

### The Power of Reflection (Numbered Discourses 4.155) ^an4-155-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an4-155-1-1

What four? ^an4-155-1-2

The powers of reflection, development, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an4-155-1-3

These are the four powers.” ^an4-155-1-4

### Eons (Numbered Discourses 4.156) ^an4-156-0

“Mendicants, an eon contains four uncountable periods. ^an4-156-1-1

What four? ^an4-156-1-2

When an eon contracts, it’s not easy to calculate ^an4-156-1-3

how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it takes. ^an4-156-1-4

When an eon remains fully contracted, it’s not easy to calculate ^an4-156-2-1

how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it takes. ^an4-156-2-2

When an eon expands, it’s not easy to calculate ^an4-156-3-1

how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it takes. ^an4-156-3-2

When an eon remains fully expanded, it’s not easy to calculate ^an4-156-4-1

how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it takes. ^an4-156-4-2

These are the four uncountable periods of an eon.” ^an4-156-4-3

### Illness (Numbered Discourses 4.157) ^an4-157-0

“Mendicants, there are two kinds of illness. ^an4-157-1-1

What two? ^an4-157-1-2

Mental and physical. ^an4-157-1-3

Some sentient beings are seen who can claim to be free of physical illness for a year, or two, or three years … even up to a hundred years or more. ^an4-157-1-4

But it’s very hard to find any sentient beings in the world who can claim to be free of mental illness even for an hour, apart from those who have ended the defilements. ^an4-157-1-5

There are four kinds of illness for those gone forth. ^an4-157-2-1

What four? ^an4-157-2-2

To start with, a mendicant has many wishes, is frustrated, and is not content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an4-157-2-3

Because of this, they focus their corrupt wishes on status, and on getting material things, honor, and popularity. ^an4-157-2-4

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get these things. ^an4-157-2-5

They have an ulterior motive when they visit families. They have an ulterior motive when they sit down, when they speak on Dhamma, and even when they hold it in when they need to go to the toilet. ^an4-157-2-6

These are the four kinds of illness for those gone forth. ^an4-157-2-7

So you should train like this: ^an4-157-3-1

‘We will not have many wishes or be frustrated. We will be content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. We won’t focus our corrupt wishes on status, and on getting material things, honor, and popularity. We won’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get these things. We will endure cold, heat, hunger, and thirst. We will endure the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. We will endure rude and unwelcome criticism. We will put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening.’ ^an4-157-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an4-157-3-3

### Decline (Numbered Discourses 4.158) ^an4-158-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an4-158-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an4-158-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an4-158-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an4-158-1-4

“Reverends, any monk or nun who sees four things inside themselves should conclude: ^an4-158-2-1

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^an4-158-2-2

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^an4-158-2-3

What four? ^an4-158-2-4

They have much greed, much hate, and much delusion; and their wisdom eye doesn’t go into the many deep matters. ^an4-158-2-5

Any monk or nun who sees these four things inside themselves should conclude: ^an4-158-2-6

‘My skillful qualities are declining. ^an4-158-2-7

For this is what the Buddha calls decline.’ ^an4-158-2-8

Any monk or nun who sees four things inside themselves should conclude: ^an4-158-3-1

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^an4-158-3-2

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’ ^an4-158-3-3

What four? ^an4-158-3-4

Their greed, hate, and delusion grow less; and their wisdom eye goes into the many deep matters. ^an4-158-3-5

Any monk or nun who sees these four things inside themselves should conclude: ^an4-158-3-6

‘My skillful qualities are not declining. ^an4-158-3-7

For this is what the Buddha calls non-decline.’” ^an4-158-3-8

### Nun (Numbered Discourses 4.159) ^an4-159-0

So I have heard. ^an4-159-1-1

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an4-159-1-2

And then a certain nun addressed a man, ^an4-159-1-3

“Please, my man, go to Venerable Ānanda, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Say to him: ^an4-159-1-4

‘Sir, the nun named so-and-so is sick, suffering, and gravely ill. ^an4-159-1-5

She bows with her head to your feet.’ And then say: ^an4-159-1-6

‘Sir, please go to the nuns’ quarters to visit that nun out of sympathy.’” ^an4-159-1-7

“Yes, ma’am,” that man replied. He did as the nun asked. ^an4-159-1-8

Ānanda consented with silence. ^an4-159-2-4

Then Ānanda robed up and went to the nuns’ quarters to visit that nun, taking his bowl and robe. ^an4-159-3-1

That nun saw Ānanda coming off in the distance. ^an4-159-3-2

She wrapped herself up from head to foot and laid down on her cot. ^an4-159-3-3

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to her, and sat down on the seat spread out. Then Ānanda said to the nun: ^an4-159-3-4

“Sister, this body is produced by food. Relying on food, ^an4-159-4-1

you should give up food. ^an4-159-4-2

This body is produced by craving. Relying on craving, ^an4-159-4-3

you should give up craving. ^an4-159-4-4

This body is produced by conceit. Relying on conceit, ^an4-159-4-5

you should give up conceit. ^an4-159-4-6

This body is produced by sex. ^an4-159-4-7

The Buddha spoke of breaking off everything to do with sex. ^an4-159-4-8

‘This body is produced by food. Relying on food, ^an4-159-5-1

you should give up food.’ This is what I said, ^an4-159-5-2

but why did I say it? ^an4-159-5-3

Take a mendicant who reflects rationally on the food that they eat: ^an4-159-5-4

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^an4-159-5-5

After some time, relying on food, they give up food. ^an4-159-5-6

That’s why I said what I said. ^an4-159-5-8

‘This body is produced by craving. Relying on craving, ^an4-159-6-1

you should give up craving.’ This is what I said, ^an4-159-6-2

but why did I say it? ^an4-159-6-3

Take a mendicant who hears this: ^an4-159-6-4

‘They say that the mendicant named so-and-so has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an4-159-6-5

They think: ^an4-159-6-6

‘Oh, when will I too realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. …’ ^an4-159-6-7

After some time, relying on craving, they give up craving. ^an4-159-6-8

That’s why I said what I said. ^an4-159-6-10

‘This body is produced by conceit. Relying on conceit, ^an4-159-7-1

you should give up conceit.’ This is what I said, ^an4-159-7-2

but why did I say it? ^an4-159-7-3

Take a mendicant who hears this: ^an4-159-7-4

‘They say that the mendicant named so-and-so has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an4-159-7-5

They think: ^an4-159-7-6

‘Well, that venerable can realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. … ^an4-159-7-7

Why can’t I?’ ^an4-159-7-8

After some time, relying on conceit, they give up conceit. ^an4-159-7-9

That’s why I said what I said. ^an4-159-7-11

‘This body is produced by sex. ^an4-159-8-1

The Buddha spoke of breaking off everything to do with sex.’” ^an4-159-8-2

Then that nun rose from her cot, placed her robe over one shoulder, bowed with her head at Ānanda’s feet, and said, ^an4-159-9-1

“I made a mistake, sir. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of me to act in that way. ^an4-159-9-2

Please, sir, accept my mistake for what it is, so I can restrain myself in future.” ^an4-159-9-3

“Indeed, sister, you made a mistake. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of you to act in that way. ^an4-159-9-4

But since you have recognized your mistake for what it is, and have dealt with it properly, I accept it. ^an4-159-9-5

For it is growth in the training of the Noble One to recognize a mistake for what it is, deal with it properly, and commit to restraint in the future.” ^an4-159-9-6

### The Training of a Holy One (Numbered Discourses 4.160) ^an4-160-0

“Mendicants, a Holy One or a Holy One’s training remain in the world for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an4-160-1-1

And who is a Holy One? ^an4-160-2-1

It’s when a Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^an4-160-2-2

This is a Holy One. ^an4-160-2-3

And what is the training of a Holy One? ^an4-160-3-1

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an4-160-3-2

This is the training of a Holy One. ^an4-160-3-3

This is how a Holy One or a Holy One’s training remain in the world for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an4-160-3-4

These four things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-4-1

What four? ^an4-160-4-2

Firstly, the mendicants memorize discourses that they learned incorrectly, with misplaced words and phrases. ^an4-160-4-3

When the words and phrases are misplaced, the meaning is misinterpreted. ^an4-160-4-4

This is the first thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-4-5

Furthermore, the mendicants are hard to admonish, having qualities that make them hard to admonish. They’re impatient, and don’t take instruction respectfully. ^an4-160-5-1

This is the second thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-5-2

Furthermore, the mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—don’t carefully make others recite the discourses. ^an4-160-6-1

When they pass away, the discourses are cut off at the root, with no-one to preserve them. ^an4-160-6-2

This is the third thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-6-3

Furthermore, the senior mendicants are indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, not rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an4-160-7-1

Those who come after them follow their example. ^an4-160-7-2

They too become indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, not rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an4-160-7-3

This is the fourth thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-7-4

These are four things that lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an4-160-7-5

These four things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an4-160-8-1

What four? ^an4-160-8-2

Firstly, the mendicants memorize discourses that have been learned correctly, with well placed words and phrases. ^an4-160-8-3

When the words and phrases are well placed, the meaning is interpreted correctly. ^an4-160-8-4

This is the first thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an4-160-8-5

Furthermore, the mendicants are easy to admonish, having qualities that make them easy to admonish. They’re patient, and take instruction respectfully. ^an4-160-9-1

This is the second thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an4-160-9-2

Furthermore, the mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—carefully make others recite the discourses. ^an4-160-10-1

When they pass away, the discourses aren’t cut off at the root, and they have someone to preserve them. ^an4-160-10-2

This is the third thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an4-160-10-3

Furthermore, the senior mendicants are not indulgent or slack, nor are they backsliders; instead, they take the lead in seclusion, rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an4-160-11-1

Those who come after them follow their example. ^an4-160-11-2

They too aren’t indulgent or slack … ^an4-160-11-3

This is the fourth thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an4-160-11-4

These are four things that lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.” ^an4-160-11-5

### In Brief (Numbered Discourses 4.161) ^an4-161-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-161-1-1

What four? ^an4-161-1-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-161-1-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-161-1-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-161-1-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-161-1-6

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-161-1-7

### In Detail (Numbered Discourses 4.162) ^an4-162-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-162-1-1

What four? ^an4-162-1-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-162-1-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-162-1-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-162-1-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-162-1-6

And what’s the painful practice with slow insight? ^an4-162-2-1

It’s when someone is ordinarily full of acute greed, hate, and delusion. They often feel the pain and sadness that greed, hate, and delusion bring. ^an4-162-2-2

These five faculties manifest in them weakly: ^an4-162-2-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-162-2-6

Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-162-2-7

This is called the painful practice with slow insight. ^an4-162-2-8

And what’s the painful practice with swift insight? ^an4-162-3-1

It’s when someone is ordinarily full of acute greed, hate, and delusion. They often feel the pain and sadness that greed, hate, and delusion bring. ^an4-162-3-2

And these five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-162-3-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-162-3-6

Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-162-3-7

This is called the painful practice with swift insight. ^an4-162-3-8

And what’s pleasant practice with slow insight? ^an4-162-4-1

It’s when someone is not ordinarily full of acute greed, hate, and delusion. They rarely feel the pain and sadness that greed, hate, and delusion bring. ^an4-162-4-2

These five faculties manifest in them weakly: ^an4-162-4-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-162-4-6

Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-162-4-7

This is called the pleasant practice with slow insight. ^an4-162-4-8

And what’s the pleasant practice with swift insight? ^an4-162-5-1

It’s when someone is not ordinarily full of acute greed, hate, and delusion. They rarely feel the pain and sadness that greed, hate, and delusion bring. ^an4-162-5-2

These five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-162-5-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-162-5-6

Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-162-5-7

This is called the pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-162-5-8

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-162-5-9

### Ugly (Numbered Discourses 4.163) ^an4-163-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-163-1-1

What four? ^an4-163-1-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-163-1-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-163-1-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-163-1-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-163-1-6

And what’s the painful practice with slow insight? ^an4-163-2-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, ^an4-163-2-2

and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an4-163-2-3

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-163-2-4

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-163-2-5

But these five faculties manifest in them weakly: ^an4-163-2-6

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-163-2-7

Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-163-2-8

This is called the painful practice with slow insight. ^an4-163-2-9

And what’s the painful practice with swift insight? ^an4-163-3-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, ^an4-163-3-2

and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an4-163-3-3

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-163-3-4

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-163-3-5

And these five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-163-3-6

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-163-3-7

Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-163-3-8

This is called the painful practice with swift insight. ^an4-163-3-9

And what’s the pleasant practice with slow insight? ^an4-163-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an4-163-4-2

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an4-163-4-3

And with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an4-163-4-4

With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an4-163-4-5

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-163-4-6

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-163-4-7

But these five faculties manifest in them weakly: ^an4-163-4-8

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-163-4-9

Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-163-4-10

This is called the pleasant practice with slow insight. ^an4-163-4-11

And what’s the pleasant practice with swift insight? ^an4-163-5-1

It’s when a mendicant … enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an4-163-5-2

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-163-5-3

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-163-5-4

And these five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-163-5-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-163-5-6

Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life. ^an4-163-5-7

This is called the pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-163-5-8

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-163-5-9

### Patient (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.164) ^an4-164-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-164-1-1

What four? ^an4-164-1-2

Impatient practice, patient practice, taming practice, and calming practice. ^an4-164-1-3

And what’s the impatient practice? ^an4-164-1-4

It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with you, and you abuse, annoy, or argue right back at them. ^an4-164-1-5

This is called the impatient practice. ^an4-164-1-6

And what’s the patient practice? ^an4-164-2-1

It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with you, and you don’t abuse, annoy, or argue back at them. ^an4-164-2-2

This is called the patient practice. ^an4-164-2-3

And what’s the taming practice? ^an4-164-3-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-164-3-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-164-3-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an4-164-3-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an4-164-3-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an4-164-3-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an4-164-3-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-164-3-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-164-3-9

This is called the taming practice. ^an4-164-3-10

And what’s the calming practice? ^an4-164-4-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, calm them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an4-164-4-2

This is called the calming practice. ^an4-164-4-3

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-164-4-4

### Patience (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.165) ^an4-165-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-165-1-1

What four? ^an4-165-1-2

Impatient practice, patient practice, taming practice, and calming practice. ^an4-165-1-3

And what’s the impatient practice? ^an4-165-2-1

It’s when someone cannot endure cold, heat, hunger, and thirst. They cannot endure the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. They cannot endure rude and unwelcome criticism. And they cannot put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an4-165-2-2

This is called the impatient practice. ^an4-165-2-3

And what’s the patient practice? ^an4-165-3-1

It’s when someone endures cold, heat, hunger, and thirst. They endure the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. They endure rude and unwelcome criticism. And they put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an4-165-3-2

This is called the patient practice. ^an4-165-3-3

And what’s the taming practice? ^an4-165-4-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. … ^an4-165-4-2

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an4-165-4-3

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an4-165-4-4

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an4-165-4-5

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an4-165-4-6

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-165-4-7

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-165-4-8

This is called the taming practice. ^an4-165-4-9

And what’s the calming practice? ^an4-165-5-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, calm them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an4-165-5-2

This is called the calming practice. ^an4-165-5-3

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-165-5-4

### Both (Numbered Discourses 4.166) ^an4-166-0

“Mendicants, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-166-1-1

What four? ^an4-166-1-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-166-1-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-166-1-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-166-1-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-166-1-6

Of these, the painful practice with slow insight is said to be inferior in both ways: because it’s painful and because it’s slow. ^an4-166-2-1

This practice is said to be inferior in both ways. ^an4-166-2-2

The painful practice with swift insight is said to be inferior because it’s painful. ^an4-166-3-1

The pleasant practice with slow insight is said to be inferior because it’s slow. ^an4-166-4-1

The pleasant practice with swift insight is said to be superior in both ways: because it’s pleasant, and because it’s swift. ^an4-166-5-1

This practice is said to be superior in both ways. ^an4-166-5-2

These are the four ways of practice.” ^an4-166-5-3

### Moggallāna’s Practice (Numbered Discourses 4.167) ^an4-167-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-167-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Sāriputta sat down to one side and said to Mahāmoggallāna: ^an4-167-1-2

“Reverend Moggallāna, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-167-2-1

What four? ^an4-167-2-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-167-2-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-167-2-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-167-2-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-167-2-6

These are the four ways of practice. ^an4-167-2-7

Which one of these four ways of practice did you rely on to free your mind from defilements by not grasping?” ^an4-167-2-8

“Reverend Sāriputta … ^an4-167-3-1

I relied on the painful practice with swift insight to free my mind from defilements by not grasping.” ^an4-167-3-5

### Sāriputta’s Practice (Numbered Discourses 4.168) ^an4-168-0

Then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-168-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Mahāmoggallāna sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an4-168-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, there are four ways of practice. ^an4-168-2-1

What four? ^an4-168-2-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an4-168-2-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an4-168-2-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an4-168-2-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an4-168-2-6

These are the four ways of practice. ^an4-168-2-7

Which one of these four ways of practice did you rely on to free your mind from defilements by not grasping?” ^an4-168-2-8

“Reverend Moggallāna … ^an4-168-3-1

I relied on the pleasant practice with swift insight to free my mind from defilements by not grasping.” ^an4-168-3-5

### Extra Effort (Numbered Discourses 4.169) ^an4-169-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-169-1-1

What four? ^an4-169-1-2

One individual is fully extinguished in this very life by making extra effort. ^an4-169-1-3

One individual is fully extinguished when the body breaks up by making extra effort. ^an4-169-1-4

One individual is fully extinguished in this very life without making extra effort. ^an4-169-1-5

One individual is fully extinguished when the body breaks up without making extra effort. ^an4-169-1-6

And how is an individual fully extinguished in this very life by making extra effort? ^an4-169-2-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, ^an4-169-2-2

and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an4-169-2-3

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-169-2-4

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-169-2-5

And these five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-169-2-6

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-169-2-7

Because of the strength of the five faculties, they are fully extinguished in this very life by making extra effort. ^an4-169-2-8

That’s how an individual is fully extinguished in this very life by making extra effort. ^an4-169-2-9

How is an individual fully extinguished when the body breaks up by making extra effort? ^an4-169-3-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, ^an4-169-3-2

and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an4-169-3-3

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-169-3-4

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-169-3-5

But these five faculties manifest in them weakly: ^an4-169-3-6

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-169-3-7

Because of the weakness of the five faculties, they are fully extinguished when the body breaks up by making extra effort. ^an4-169-3-8

That’s how an individual is fully extinguished when the body breaks up by making extra effort. ^an4-169-3-9

And how is an individual fully extinguished in this very life without making extra effort? ^an4-169-4-1

It’s when a mendicant … enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an4-169-4-2

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-169-4-3

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-169-4-4

And these five faculties manifest in them strongly: ^an4-169-4-5

faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-169-4-6

Because of the strength of the five faculties, they are fully extinguished in this very life without making extra effort. ^an4-169-4-7

That’s how an individual is fully extinguished in this very life without making extra effort. ^an4-169-4-8

And how is an individual fully extinguished when the body breaks up without making extra effort? ^an4-169-5-1

It’s when a mendicant … enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an4-169-5-2

They rely on these five powers of a trainee: ^an4-169-5-3

faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an4-169-5-4

But these five faculties manifest in them weakly: faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-169-5-5

Because of the weakness of the five faculties, they are fully extinguished when the body breaks up without making extra effort. ^an4-169-5-6

That’s how an individual is fully extinguished when the body breaks up without making extra effort. ^an4-169-5-7

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-169-5-8

### In Conjunction (Numbered Discourses 4.170) ^an4-170-0

So I have heard. ^an4-170-1-1

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an4-170-1-2

There Ānanda addressed the mendicants: ^an4-170-1-3

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an4-170-1-4

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an4-170-1-5

Ānanda said this: ^an4-170-1-6

“Reverends, all of the monks and nuns who declare in my presence that they have attained perfection, did so by one or other of four paths. ^an4-170-2-1

What four? ^an4-170-3-1

Take a mendicant who develops serenity before discernment. ^an4-170-3-2

As they do so, the path is born in them. ^an4-170-3-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an4-170-3-4

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an4-170-3-5

Another mendicant develops discernment before serenity. ^an4-170-4-1

As they do so, the path is born in them. ^an4-170-4-2

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an4-170-4-3

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an4-170-4-4

Another mendicant develops serenity and discernment in conjunction. ^an4-170-5-1

As they do so, the path is born in them. ^an4-170-5-2

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an4-170-5-3

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an4-170-5-4

Another mendicant’s heart is seized by restlessness to realize the teaching. ^an4-170-6-1

But there comes a time when their mind is stilled internally; it settles, unifies, and becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an4-170-6-2

The path is born in them. ^an4-170-6-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an4-170-6-4

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an4-170-6-5

All of the monks and nuns who declare in my presence that they have attained perfection, did so by one or other of these four paths.” ^an4-170-7-1

### Intention (Numbered Discourses 4.171) ^an4-171-0

“Mendicants, as long as there’s a body, the intention that gives rise to bodily action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. ^an4-171-1-1

As long as there’s a voice, the intention that gives rise to verbal action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. ^an4-171-1-2

As long as there’s a mind, the intention that gives rise to mental action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. But this is only when dependent on ignorance. ^an4-171-1-3

By oneself one instigates the choice that gives rise to bodily, verbal, and mental action, conditioned by which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^an4-171-2-1

Or else others instigate the choice … ^an4-171-2-2

One instigates the choice after deliberation … ^an4-171-2-3

Or else one instigates the choice without deliberation … ^an4-171-2-4

Ignorance is included in all these things. ^an4-171-5-1

But when ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, that body, that voice, and that mind no longer exist dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself. ^an4-171-5-2

That field does not exist, that ground does not exist, that basis does not exist, that foundation does not exist, dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself.” ^an4-171-5-3

“Mendicants, there are four kinds of incarnation. ^an4-171-6-1

What four? ^an4-171-6-2

There is incarnation where one’s own intention is effective, not that of others. ^an4-171-6-3

There is incarnation where the intention of others is effective, not one’s own. ^an4-171-6-4

There is incarnation where both one’s own and others’ intentions are effective. ^an4-171-6-5

There is incarnation where neither one’s own nor others’ intentions are effective. ^an4-171-6-6

These are the four kinds of incarnation.” ^an4-171-6-7

When he said this, Venerable Sāriputta said to the Buddha: ^an4-171-7-1

“Sir, this is how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement. ^an4-171-7-2

Take the case of the incarnation where one’s own intention is effective, not that of others. Those sentient beings pass away from that realm due to their own intention. ^an4-171-7-3

Take the case of the incarnation where the intention of others is effective, not one’s own. Those sentient beings pass away from that realm due to the intention of others. ^an4-171-7-4

Take the case of the incarnation where both one’s own and others’ intentions are effective. Those sentient beings pass away from that realm due to both their own and others’ intentions. ^an4-171-7-5

But sir, in the case of the incarnation where neither one’s own nor others’ intentions are effective, what kind of gods does this refer to?” ^an4-171-7-6

“Sāriputta, it refers to the gods reborn in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.” ^an4-171-7-7

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why some sentient beings pass away from that realm as returners who come back to this place, ^an4-171-8-1

while others are non-returners who don’t come back?” ^an4-171-8-2

“Sāriputta, take an individual who hasn’t given up the lower fetters. In this very life they enter and abide in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-171-8-3

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-171-8-4

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-171-8-5

When they pass away from there, they’re a returner, who comes back to this place. ^an4-171-8-6

Sāriputta, take an individual who has given up the lower fetters. In this very life they enter and abide in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-171-9-1

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an4-171-9-2

If they abide in that, are committed to it, and meditate on it often without losing it, when they die they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-171-9-3

When they pass away from there, they’re a non-returner, not coming back to this place. ^an4-171-9-4

This is the cause, this is the reason why some sentient beings pass away from that realm as returners who come back to this place, ^an4-171-10-1

while others are non-returners who don’t come back.” ^an4-171-10-2

### Sāriputta’s Attainment of Textual Analysis (Numbered Discourses 4.172) ^an4-172-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an4-172-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an4-172-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an4-172-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an4-172-1-4

“Reverends, I realized the textual analysis of the meaning—piece by piece and expression by expression—a fortnight after I ordained. ^an4-172-2-1

In many ways I explain, teach, assert, establish, clarify, analyze, and reveal it. ^an4-172-2-2

Whoever has any doubt or uncertainty, let them ask me, ^an4-172-2-3

I will answer. Our teacher is present, he who is so very skilled in our teachings. ^an4-172-2-4

I realized the textual analysis of the text—piece by piece and expression by expression—a fortnight after I ordained. … ^an4-172-3-1

I realized the textual analysis of definition—piece by piece and expression by expression—a fortnight after I ordained. … ^an4-172-4-1

I realized the textual analysis of eloquence—piece by piece and expression by expression—a fortnight after I ordained. ^an4-172-5-1

In many ways I explain, teach, assert, establish, clarify, analyze, and reveal it. ^an4-172-5-2

If anyone has any doubt or uncertainty, let them ask me, ^an4-172-5-3

I will answer. Our teacher is present, he who is so very skilled in our teachings.” ^an4-172-5-4

### With Mahākoṭṭhita (Numbered Discourses 4.173) ^an4-173-0

Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-173-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Mahākoṭṭhita sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an4-173-1-2

“Reverend, when the six fields of contact have faded away and ceased with no residue left behind, does something else still exist?” ^an4-173-2-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-173-3-1

“Does something else no longer exist?” ^an4-173-4-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-173-5-1

“Does something else both still exist and no longer exist?” ^an4-173-6-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-173-7-1

“Does something else neither still exist nor no longer exist?” ^an4-173-8-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-173-9-1

“Reverend, when asked whether—when the six fields of contact have faded away and ceased with no residue left behind—something else still exists, you say ‘don’t put it like that’. ^an4-173-10-1

When asked whether something else no longer exists, ^an4-173-10-2

you say ‘don’t put it like that’. ^an4-173-10-3

When asked whether something else both still exists and no longer exists, ^an4-173-10-4

you say ‘don’t put it like that’. ^an4-173-10-5

When asked whether something else neither still exists nor no longer exists, ^an4-173-10-6

you say ‘don’t put it like that’. ^an4-173-10-7

How then should we see the meaning of this statement?” ^an4-173-10-8

“In saying that, ‘When the six fields of contact have faded away and ceased with no residue left behind, something else still exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-173-11-1

In saying that, ‘something else no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-173-11-2

In saying that, ‘something else both still exists and no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-173-11-3

In saying that, ‘something else neither still exists nor no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-173-11-4

The scope of proliferation extends as far as the scope of the six fields of contact. ^an4-173-11-5

The scope of the six fields of contact extends as far as the scope of proliferation. ^an4-173-11-6

When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with no residue left behind, proliferation stops and is stilled.” ^an4-173-11-7

### With Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 4.174) ^an4-174-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-174-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Ānanda sat down to one side, and said to Mahākoṭṭhita: ^an4-174-1-2

“Reverend, when these six fields of contact have faded away and ceased with no residue left behind, does something else exist?” ^an4-174-2-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-174-3-1

“Does something else no longer exist?” ^an4-174-4-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-174-5-1

“Does something else both still exist and no longer exist?” ^an4-174-6-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-174-7-1

“Does something else neither still exist nor no longer exist?” ^an4-174-8-1

“Don’t put it like that, reverend.” ^an4-174-9-1

“Reverend, when asked these questions, ^an4-174-10-1

you say ‘don’t put it like that’. ^an4-174-10-2

… How then should we see the meaning of this statement?” ^an4-174-10-9

“In saying that, ‘when the six fields of contact have faded away and ceased with no residue left behind, something else exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-174-11-1

In saying that, ‘something else no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-174-11-2

In saying that, ‘something else both still exists and no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-174-11-3

In saying that, ‘something else neither still exists nor no longer exists’, you’re proliferating the unproliferated. ^an4-174-11-4

The scope of proliferation extends as far as the scope of the six fields of contact. ^an4-174-11-5

The scope of the six fields of contact extends as far as the scope of proliferation. ^an4-174-11-6

When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with no residue left behind, proliferation stops and is stilled.” ^an4-174-11-7

### With Upavāṇa (Numbered Discourses 4.175) ^an4-175-0

Then Venerable Upavāṇa went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-175-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Upavāṇa sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an4-175-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, do you become a terminator because of knowledge?” ^an4-175-2-1

“That’s not it, reverend.” ^an4-175-3-1

“Do you become a terminator because of conduct?” ^an4-175-4-1

“That’s not it, reverend.” ^an4-175-5-1

“Do you become a terminator because of both knowledge and conduct?” ^an4-175-6-1

“That’s not it, reverend.” ^an4-175-7-1

“Do you become a terminator for some reason other than knowledge and conduct?” ^an4-175-8-1

“That’s not it, reverend.” ^an4-175-9-1

“Reverend Sāriputta, when asked whether you become a terminator because of knowledge or conduct or knowledge and conduct, or for some other reason, ^an4-175-10-1

you say ‘that’s not it’. ^an4-175-10-2

How then do you become a terminator?” ^an4-175-10-9

“Reverend, if you became a terminator because of knowledge, then even someone who still grasps fuel could be a terminator. ^an4-175-11-1

If you became a terminator because of conduct, then even someone who still grasps fuel could be a terminator. ^an4-175-11-2

If you became a terminator because of both knowledge and conduct, then even someone who still grasps fuel could be a terminator. ^an4-175-11-3

If you became a terminator for some reason other than knowledge and conduct, then even an ordinary person could be a terminator. ^an4-175-11-4

For an ordinary person lacks knowledge and conduct. ^an4-175-11-5

Reverend, someone lacking good conduct does not know and see things as they are. ^an4-175-11-6

Someone accomplished in good conduct knows and sees things as they are. ^an4-175-11-7

Knowing and seeing things as they are, one is a terminator.” ^an4-175-11-8

### Aspiration (Numbered Discourses 4.176) ^an4-176-0

“Mendicants, a faithful monk would rightly aspire: ^an4-176-1-1

‘May I be like Sāriputta and Moggallāna!’ ^an4-176-1-2

These are a standard and a measure for my monk disciples, that is, Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^an4-176-1-3

A faithful nun would rightly aspire: ^an4-176-2-1

‘May I be like the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā!’ ^an4-176-2-2

These are a standard and a measure for my nun disciples, that is, the nuns Khemā and Uppalavaṇṇā. ^an4-176-2-3

A faithful layman would rightly aspire: ^an4-176-3-1

‘May I be like the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī!’ ^an4-176-3-2

These are a standard and a measure for my male lay disciples, that is, the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī. ^an4-176-3-3

A faithful laywoman would rightly aspire: ^an4-176-4-1

‘May I be like the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother!’ ^an4-176-4-2

These are a standard and a measure for my female lay disciples, that is, the laywomen Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother.” ^an4-176-4-3

### With Rāhula (Numbered Discourses 4.177) ^an4-177-0

Then Venerable Rāhula went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an4-177-1-1

“Rāhula, the interior earth element and the exterior earth element are just the earth element. ^an4-177-2-1

This should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-177-2-2

When you truly see with right understanding, you grow disillusioned with the earth element, detaching the mind from the earth element. ^an4-177-2-3

The interior water element and the exterior water element are just the water element. ^an4-177-3-1

This should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-177-3-2

When you truly see with right understanding, you grow disillusioned with the water element, detaching the mind from the water element. ^an4-177-3-3

The interior fire element and the exterior fire element are just the fire element. ^an4-177-4-1

This should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-177-4-2

When you truly see with right understanding, you grow disillusioned with the fire element, detaching the mind from the fire element. ^an4-177-4-3
