The interior air element and the exterior air element are just the air element. ^an4-177-5-1

This should be truly seen with right understanding like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-177-5-2

When you truly see with right understanding, you grow disillusioned with the air element, detaching the mind from the air element. ^an4-177-5-3

When a mendicant sees these four elements as neither self nor belonging to self, they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an4-177-6-1

### Billabong (Numbered Discourses 4.178) ^an4-178-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-178-1-1

What four? ^an4-178-1-2

Take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-1-3

They focus on the cessation of substantial reality, ^an4-178-1-4

but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an4-178-1-5

You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality. ^an4-178-1-6

Suppose a person were to grab a branch with a glue-smeared hand. Their hand would stick, hold, and bind to it. ^an4-178-1-7

In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-1-8

They focus on the cessation of substantial reality, ^an4-178-1-9

but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an4-178-1-10

You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality. ^an4-178-1-11

Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-2-1

They focus on the cessation of substantial reality, ^an4-178-2-2

and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an4-178-2-3

You would expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality. ^an4-178-2-4

Suppose a person were to grab a branch with a clean hand. Their hand wouldn’t stick, hold, or bind to it. ^an4-178-2-5

In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-2-6

They focus on the cessation of substantial reality, ^an4-178-2-7

and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an4-178-2-8

You would expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality. ^an4-178-2-9

Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-3-1

They focus on smashing ignorance, ^an4-178-3-2

but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an4-178-3-3

You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to smash ignorance. ^an4-178-3-4

Suppose there was a billabong that had been stagnant for many years. ^an4-178-3-5

And someone was to close off the inlets and open up the drains, and the heavens didn’t provide enough rain. ^an4-178-3-6

You wouldn’t expect that billabong to break its banks. ^an4-178-3-7

In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a certain peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-3-8

They focus on smashing ignorance, ^an4-178-3-9

but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an4-178-3-10

You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to smash ignorance. ^an4-178-3-11

Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-4-1

They focus on smashing ignorance, ^an4-178-4-2

and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an4-178-4-3

You would expect that mendicant to smash ignorance. ^an4-178-4-4

Suppose there was a billabong that had been stagnant for many years. ^an4-178-4-5

And someone was to open up the inlets and close off the drains, and the heavens provided plenty of rain. ^an4-178-4-6

You would expect that billabong to break its banks. ^an4-178-4-7

In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a certain peaceful release of the heart. ^an4-178-4-8

They focus on smashing ignorance, ^an4-178-4-9

and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an4-178-4-10

You would expect that mendicant to smash ignorance. ^an4-178-4-11

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-178-4-12

### Extinguishment (Numbered Discourses 4.179) ^an4-179-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-179-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Ānanda sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an4-179-1-2

“What is the cause, Reverend Sāriputta, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life?” ^an4-179-1-3

“Reverend Ānanda, it’s because some sentient beings don’t really understand which perceptions make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration. ^an4-179-2-1

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life.” ^an4-179-2-2

“What is the cause, Reverend Sāriputta, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?” ^an4-179-3-1

“Reverend Ānanda, it’s because some sentient beings truly understand which perceptions make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration. ^an4-179-3-2

That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.” ^an4-179-3-3

### The Four Great References (Numbered Discourses 4.180) ^an4-180-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near the Bhoga City, at the Ānanda Shrine. ^an4-180-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an4-180-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an4-180-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an4-180-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an4-180-1-5

“Mendicants, I will teach you the four great references. ^an4-180-1-6

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an4-180-1-7

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an4-180-1-8

The Buddha said this: ^an4-180-1-9

“Mendicants, what are the four great references? ^an4-180-2-1

Take a mendicant who says: ^an4-180-2-2

‘Reverend, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an4-180-2-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-2-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-2-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-2-6

If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-2-7

‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-2-8

It has been incorrectly memorized by that mendicant.’ ^an4-180-2-9

And so you should discard it. ^an4-180-2-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-3-1

‘Reverend, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an4-180-3-2

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-3-3

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-3-4

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-3-5

If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-3-6

‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-3-7

It has been correctly memorized by that mendicant.’ ^an4-180-3-8

You should remember it. This is the first great reference. ^an4-180-3-9

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-4-1

‘In such-and-such monastery lives a Saṅgha with seniors and leaders. ^an4-180-4-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that Saṅgha: ^an4-180-4-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-4-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-4-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-4-6

If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-4-7

‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-4-8

It has been incorrectly memorized by that Saṅgha.’ ^an4-180-4-9

And so you should discard it. ^an4-180-4-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-5-1

‘In such-and-such monastery lives a Saṅgha with seniors and leaders. ^an4-180-5-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that Saṅgha: ^an4-180-5-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-5-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-5-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-5-6

If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-5-7

‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-5-8

It has been correctly memorized by that Saṅgha.’ ^an4-180-5-9

You should remember it. This is the second great reference. ^an4-180-5-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-6-1

‘In such-and-such monastery there are several senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who remember the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an4-180-6-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of those senior mendicants: ^an4-180-6-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-6-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-6-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-6-6

If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-6-7

‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-6-8

It has been incorrectly memorized by those senior mendicants.’ ^an4-180-6-9

And so you should discard it. ^an4-180-6-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-7-1

‘In such-and-such monastery there are several senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who remember the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an4-180-7-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of those senior mendicants: ^an4-180-7-3

this is the teaching, this is the monastic law, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-7-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-7-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-7-6

If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-7-7

‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-7-8

It has been correctly memorized by those senior mendicants.’ ^an4-180-7-9

You should remember it. This is the third great reference. ^an4-180-7-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-8-1

‘In such-and-such monastery there is a single senior mendicant who is very learned, an inheritor of the heritage, who has memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an4-180-8-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that senior mendicant: ^an4-180-8-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-8-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-8-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-8-6

If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-8-7

‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-8-8

It has been incorrectly memorized by that senior mendicant.’ ^an4-180-8-9

And so you should discard it. ^an4-180-8-10

Take another mendicant who says: ^an4-180-9-1

‘In such-and-such monastery there is a single senior mendicant who is very learned, an inheritor of the heritage, who has memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an4-180-9-2

I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that senior mendicant: ^an4-180-9-3

this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’ ^an4-180-9-4

You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement. ^an4-180-9-5

Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training. ^an4-180-9-6

If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion: ^an4-180-9-7

‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an4-180-9-8

It has been correctly memorized by that senior mendicant.’ ^an4-180-9-9

You should remember it. This is the fourth great reference. ^an4-180-9-10

These are the four great references.” ^an4-180-9-11

### A Warrior (Numbered Discourses 4.181) ^an4-181-0

“Mendicants, a warrior with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-181-1-1

What four? ^an4-181-1-2

He’s skilled in the basics, a long-distance shooter, a marksman, one who shatters large objects. ^an4-181-1-3

A warrior with these four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-181-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-181-1-5

What four? ^an4-181-1-6

He’s skilled in the basics, a long-distance shooter, a marksman, one who shatters large objects. ^an4-181-1-7

And how is a mendicant skilled in the basics? ^an4-181-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-181-2-2

That’s how a mendicant is skilled in the basics. ^an4-181-2-3

And how is a mendicant a long-distance shooter? ^an4-181-3-1

It’s when a mendicant truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-181-3-2

They truly see any kind of feeling … ^an4-181-3-3

perception … ^an4-181-3-4

choices … ^an4-181-3-5

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near, <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-181-3-6

That’s how a mendicant is a long-distance shooter. ^an4-181-3-7

And how is a mendicant a marksman? ^an4-181-4-1

It’s when they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-181-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is a marksman. ^an4-181-4-3

And how does a mendicant shatter large objects? ^an4-181-5-1

It’s when a mendicant shatters the great mass of ignorance. ^an4-181-5-2

That’s how a mendicant shatters large objects. ^an4-181-5-3

A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an4-181-5-4

### Guarantee (Numbered Discourses 4.182) ^an4-182-0

“There are four things that no-one can guarantee— ^an4-182-1-1

not an ascetic, a brahmin, a god, a Māra, a divinity, or anyone in the world. ^an4-182-1-2

What four? ^an4-182-2-1

No-one can guarantee that someone liable to old age will not grow old. ^an4-182-2-2

No-one can guarantee that someone liable to sickness will not get sick. ^an4-182-2-4

No-one can guarantee that someone liable to death will not die. ^an4-182-2-6

No-one can guarantee that the bad deeds done in past lives—corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death—will not produce their result. ^an4-182-2-8

These are the four things that no-one can guarantee— ^an4-182-3-1

not an ascetic, a brahmin, a god, a Māra, a divinity, or anyone in the world.” ^an4-182-3-2

### Vassakāra on What is Heard (Numbered Discourses 4.183) ^an4-183-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an4-183-1-1

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, a chief minister of Magadha, went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-183-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an4-183-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, this is my doctrine and view: ^an4-183-2-1

There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve seen, saying: ^an4-183-2-2

‘So I have seen.’ ^an4-183-2-3

There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve heard, saying: ^an4-183-2-4

‘So I have heard.’ ^an4-183-2-5

There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve thought, saying: ^an4-183-2-6

‘So I have thought.’ ^an4-183-2-7

There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve known, saying: ^an4-183-2-8

‘So I have known.’” ^an4-183-2-9

“Brahmin, I don’t say you should talk about everything you see, hear, think, and know. ^an4-183-3-1

But I also don’t say you should talk about nothing you see, hear, think, and know. ^an4-183-3-2

When talking about certain things you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t talk about those things. ^an4-183-4-1

When talking about other things you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should talk about those things.” ^an4-183-4-2

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, rose from his seat and left. ^an4-183-8-1

### Fearless (Numbered Discourses 4.184) ^an4-184-0

Then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-184-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an4-184-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, this is my doctrine and view: ^an4-184-2-1

‘All those liable to death are frightened and terrified of death.’” ^an4-184-2-2

“Brahmin, some of those liable to death are frightened and terrified of death. ^an4-184-2-3

But some of those liable to death are not frightened and terrified of death. ^an4-184-2-4

Who are those frightened of death? ^an4-184-3-1

It’s someone who isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an4-184-3-2

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-3-3

they think: ^an4-184-3-4

‘The sensual pleasures that I love so much will leave me, and I’ll leave them.’ ^an4-184-3-5

They sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-3-6

This is someone who is frightened of death. ^an4-184-3-7

Furthermore, it’s someone who isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for the body. ^an4-184-4-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-4-2

they think: ^an4-184-4-3

‘This body that I love so much will leave me, and I’ll leave it.’ ^an4-184-4-4

They sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-4-5

This, too, is someone who is frightened of death. ^an4-184-4-6

Furthermore, it’s someone who hasn’t done good and skillful things that keep them safe, but has done bad things, violence and sin. ^an4-184-5-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-5-2

they think: ^an4-184-5-3

‘Well, I haven’t done good and skillful things that keep me safe. ^an4-184-5-4

And I have done bad things, violence and sin. ^an4-184-5-5

When I depart, I’ll go to the place where people who’ve done such things go.’ ^an4-184-5-6

They sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-5-7

This, too, is someone who is frightened of death. ^an4-184-5-8

Furthermore, it’s someone who’s doubtful, uncertain, and undecided about the true teaching. ^an4-184-6-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-6-2

they think: ^an4-184-6-3

‘I’m doubtful, uncertain, and undecided about the true teaching.’ ^an4-184-6-4

They sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-6-5

This, too, is someone who is frightened of death. ^an4-184-6-6

These are the four individuals liable to death who are frightened and terrified of death. ^an4-184-6-7

Who are those not frightened of death? ^an4-184-7-1

It’s someone who is rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an4-184-7-2

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-7-3

they don’t think: ^an4-184-7-4

‘The sensual pleasures that I love so much will leave me, and I’ll leave them.’ ^an4-184-7-5

They don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-7-6

This is someone who’s not frightened of death. ^an4-184-7-7

Furthermore, it’s someone who is rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for the body. ^an4-184-8-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-8-2

they don’t think: ^an4-184-8-3

‘This body that I love so much will leave me, and I’ll leave it.’ ^an4-184-8-4

They don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-8-5

This, too, is someone who’s not frightened of death. ^an4-184-8-6

Furthermore, it’s someone who hasn’t done bad things, violence and sin, but has done good and skillful deeds that keep them safe. ^an4-184-9-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-9-2

they think: ^an4-184-9-3

‘Well, I haven’t done bad things, violence and sin. ^an4-184-9-4

And I have done good and skillful deeds that keep me safe. ^an4-184-9-5

When I depart, I’ll go to the place where people who’ve done such things go.’ ^an4-184-9-6

They don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-9-7

This, too, is someone who’s not frightened of death. ^an4-184-9-8

Furthermore, it’s someone who’s not doubtful, uncertain, or undecided about the true teaching. ^an4-184-10-1

When they fall seriously ill, ^an4-184-10-2

they think: ^an4-184-10-3

‘I’m not doubtful, uncertain, or undecided about the true teaching.’ ^an4-184-10-4

They don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-184-10-5

This, too, is someone who’s not frightened of death. ^an4-184-10-6

These are the four individuals liable to death who are not frightened and terrified of death.” ^an4-184-10-7

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! … From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-184-11-1

### Truths of the Brahmins (Numbered Discourses 4.185) ^an4-185-0

Once the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an4-185-1-1

Now at that time several very well-known wanderers were residing in the monastery of the wanderers on the bank of the Serpentine river. They included Annabhāra, Varadhara, Sakuludāyī, and other very well-known wanderers. ^an4-185-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the wanderer’s monastery on the bank of the Serpentine river. ^an4-185-1-3

Now at that time this discussion came up while those wanderers of other religions were sitting together, ^an4-185-2-1

“The truths of the brahmins are like this; the truths of the brahmins are like that.” ^an4-185-2-2

Then the Buddha went up to those wanderers, sat down on the seat spread out, and said to them, ^an4-185-2-3

“Wanderers, what were you sitting talking about just now? What conversation was left unfinished?” ^an4-185-3-1

“Well, worthy Gotama, this discussion came up among us while we were sitting together: ^an4-185-3-2

‘The truths of the brahmins are like this; the truths of the brahmins are like that.’” ^an4-185-3-3

“Wanderers, I declare these four truths of the brahmins, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-185-4-1

What four? ^an4-185-4-2

Take a brahmin who says: ^an4-185-4-3

‘No sentient beings should be killed.’ ^an4-185-4-4

In speaking like this, a brahmin speaks the truth, not lies. ^an4-185-4-5

But they don’t think of themselves as an ‘ascetic’ or ‘brahmin’ because of that. Nor do they think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. ^an4-185-4-6

Rather, they simply practice out of kindness and sympathy for living creatures, having had insight into the truth of that. ^an4-185-4-7

Take another brahmin who says: ^an4-185-5-1

‘All sensual pleasures are impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ ^an4-185-5-2

Saying this, a brahmin speaks the truth, not lies. ^an4-185-5-3

But they don’t think of themselves as an ‘ascetic’ or ‘brahmin’ because of that. Nor do they think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. ^an4-185-5-4

Rather, they simply practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding sensual pleasures, having had insight into the truth of that. ^an4-185-5-5

Take another brahmin who says: ^an4-185-6-1

‘All states of existence are impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ … They simply practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding future lives, having had insight into the truth of that. ^an4-185-6-2

Take another brahmin who says: ^an4-185-7-1

‘I don’t belong to anyone anywhere. And nothing belongs to me anywhere.’ ^an4-185-7-2

In speaking like this, a brahmin speaks the truth, not lies. ^an4-185-7-3

But they don’t think of themselves as an ‘ascetic’ or ‘brahmin’ because of that. Nor do they think ‘I’m better’ or ‘I’m equal’ or ‘I’m worse’. ^an4-185-7-4

Rather, they simply practice the path of nothingness, having had insight into the truth of that. ^an4-185-7-5

These are the four truths of the brahmins that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-185-7-6

### Approach (Numbered Discourses 4.186) ^an4-186-0

Then one of the mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-186-1-1

“Sir, what leads the world on? What drags it around? What arises and takes control?” ^an4-186-1-2

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an4-186-2-1

Your approach and eloquence are excellent, and it’s a good question. ^an4-186-2-2

For you asked: ^an4-186-2-3

‘What leads the world on? What drags it around? What arises and takes control?’” ^an4-186-2-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an4-186-2-5

“Mendicant, the mind leads the world on. The mind drags it around. When the mind arises, it takes control.” ^an4-186-2-6

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he asked another question: ^an4-186-3-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘a learned memorizer of the teaching’. ^an4-186-3-2

How is a learned memorizer of the teaching defined?” ^an4-186-3-3

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an4-186-4-1

Your approach and eloquence are excellent, and it’s a good question. … ^an4-186-4-2

I have taught many teachings: ^an4-186-4-7

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-186-4-8

But if anyone understands the meaning and the text of even a four-line verse, and if they practice in line with that teaching, they’re qualified to be called a ‘learned memorizer of the teaching’.” ^an4-186-4-9

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he asked another question: ^an4-186-5-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘a learned person with penetrating wisdom’. ^an4-186-5-2

How is a learned person with penetrating wisdom defined?” ^an4-186-5-3

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an4-186-6-1

Your approach and eloquence are excellent, and it’s a good question. … ^an4-186-6-2

Take a mendicant who has heard: ‘This is suffering.’ They see what it means with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-186-6-7

They’ve heard: ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ^an4-186-6-8

‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ^an4-186-6-9

‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering.’ They see what it means with penetrating wisdom. ^an4-186-6-10

That’s how an individual is learned, with penetrating wisdom.” ^an4-186-6-11

Saying “Good, sir”, that mendicant approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. Then he asked another question: ^an4-186-7-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘an astute person with great wisdom’. ^an4-186-7-2

How is an astute person with great wisdom defined?” ^an4-186-7-3

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an4-186-8-1

Your approach and eloquence are excellent, and it’s a good question. … ^an4-186-8-2

An astute person with great wisdom is one who has no intention to hurt themselves, or to hurt others, or to hurt both. When they think, they only think of the benefit for themselves, for others, for both, and for the whole world. ^an4-186-8-7

That’s how an individual is astute, with great wisdom.” ^an4-186-8-8

### With Vassakāra (Numbered Discourses 4.187) ^an4-187-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an4-187-1-1

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, a chief minister of Magadha, went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-187-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an4-187-1-3

“Worthy Gotama, could an untrue person know of an untrue person: ^an4-187-2-1

‘This worthy one is an untrue person’?” ^an4-187-2-2

“That’s impossible, brahmin, it can’t happen.” ^an4-187-2-3

“Could an untrue person know of a true person: ^an4-187-2-5

‘This worthy one is a true person’?” ^an4-187-2-6

“That too is impossible, it can’t happen.” ^an4-187-2-7

“Worthy Gotama, could a true person know of a true person: ^an4-187-2-9

‘This worthy one is a true person’?” ^an4-187-2-10

“That, brahmin, is possible.” ^an4-187-2-11

“Could a true person know of an untrue person: ^an4-187-2-13

‘This worthy one is an untrue person’?” ^an4-187-2-14

“That too is possible.” ^an4-187-2-15

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing, ^an4-187-3-1

how well said this was by the worthy Gotama: ^an4-187-3-2

‘It’s impossible, it can’t happen, that an untrue person could know … ^an4-187-3-3

But it is possible that a true person could know …’ ^an4-187-3-7

Once, members of the brahmin Todeyya’s assembly were going on complaining about others: ^an4-187-4-1

‘This King Eḷeyya is a fool to be so devoted to the ascetic Rāmaputta. He even shows him the utmost deference by bowing down to him, rising up for him, greeting him with cupped palms, and observing proper etiquette for him. ^an4-187-4-2

And these king’s men are fools too— ^an4-187-4-3

Yamaka, Moggalla, Ugga, Nāvindakī, Gandhabba, and Aggivessa—for they show the same kind of deference to Rāmaputta.’ ^an4-187-4-4

Then the brahmin Todeyya reasoned with them like this: ^an4-187-4-5

‘What do the good fellows think? ^an4-187-4-6

When it comes to the various duties and speeches, isn’t King Eḷeyya astute, even better than the experts?’ ^an4-187-4-7

‘That’s true, sir.’ ^an4-187-4-8

‘It’s because the ascetic Rāmaputta is even more astute and expert than King Eḷeyya that the king is so devoted to him. That’s why he even shows Rāmaputta the utmost deference by bowing down to him, rising up for him, greeting him with cupped palms, and observing proper etiquette for him. ^an4-187-5-1

‘What do the good fellows think? ^an4-187-6-1

When it comes to the various duties and speeches, aren’t the king’s men—Yamaka, Moggalla, Ugga, Nāvindakī, Gandhabba, and Aggivessa—astute, even better than the experts?’ ^an4-187-6-3

‘That’s true, sir.’ ^an4-187-6-4

‘It’s because Rāmaputta is even more astute and expert than the king’s men that they have such devotion to him. … ^an4-187-7-1

It’s because Rāmaputta is even more astute and expert than King Eḷeyya that the king is so devoted to him. That’s why he even shows Rāmaputta the utmost deference by bowing down to him, rising up for him, greeting him with cupped palms, and observing proper etiquette for him.’ ^an4-187-7-2

It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing, ^an4-187-8-1

how well said this was by the worthy Gotama: ^an4-187-8-2

‘It’s impossible, it can’t happen, that an untrue person could know … ^an4-187-8-3

But it is possible that a true person could know …’ ^an4-187-8-7

Well, now, worthy Gotama, I must go. ^an4-187-8-11

I have many duties, and much to do.” ^an4-187-8-12

“Please, brahmin, go at your convenience.” ^an4-187-8-13

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, rose from his seat and left. ^an4-187-9-1

### With Upaka (Numbered Discourses 4.188) ^an4-188-0

Once the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an4-188-1-1

Then Upaka the son of Maṇḍikā went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-188-1-2

“Sir, this is my doctrine and view: ^an4-188-2-1

‘Whoever goes on complaining about others without giving any reasons ^an4-188-2-2

is reprehensible and at fault.’” ^an4-188-2-3

“Upaka, if someone goes on complaining about others without giving a reason, they’re reprehensible and at fault. ^an4-188-2-4

But that’s what you do, so you’re reprehensible and at fault!” ^an4-188-2-5

“Sir, like a fish caught in a big trap just as it rises, ^an4-188-2-6

so the Buddha caught me in a big trap of words just as I rose up.” ^an4-188-2-7

“Upaka, I’ve declared: ‘This is unskillful.’ ^an4-188-3-1

And for that there are limitless words, phrasings, and teachings of the Realized One: ^an4-188-3-2

‘This is another way of saying that this is unskillful.’ ^an4-188-3-3

I’ve declared: ‘The unskillful should be given up.’ ^an4-188-3-4

And for that there are limitless words, phrasings, and teachings of the Realized One: ^an4-188-3-5

‘This is another way of saying that the unskillful should be given up.’ ^an4-188-3-6

I’ve declared that: ‘This is skillful.’ ^an4-188-4-1

And for that there are limitless words, phrasings, and teachings of the Realized One: ^an4-188-4-2

‘This is another way of saying that this is skillful.’ ^an4-188-4-3

I’ve declared: ‘The skillful should be developed.’ ^an4-188-4-4

And for that there are limitless words, phrasings, and teachings of the Realized One: ^an4-188-4-5

‘This is another way of saying that the skillful should be developed.’” ^an4-188-4-6

And then Upaka son of Maṇḍikā approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right. Then he went up to King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha. He told the King of all they had discussed. ^an4-188-5-1

But Ajātasattu became angry and upset, and said to Upaka, ^an4-188-5-2

“How rude of this salt-maker’s boy! How scurrilous and impudent of him to imagine he could attack the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha! ^an4-188-5-3

Get out, Upaka, go away! Don’t let me see you again.” ^an4-188-5-4

### Things to be Realized (Numbered Discourses 4.189) ^an4-189-0

“Mendicants, these four things should be realized. ^an4-189-1-1

What four? ^an4-189-1-2

There are things to be realized with direct meditative experience. ^an4-189-1-3

There are things to be realized with recollection. ^an4-189-1-4

There are things to be realized with vision. ^an4-189-1-5

There are things to be realized with wisdom. ^an4-189-1-6

What things are to be realized with direct meditative experience? ^an4-189-1-7

The eight liberations. ^an4-189-1-8

What things are to be realized with recollection? ^an4-189-2-1

Past lives. ^an4-189-2-2

What things are to be realized with vision? ^an4-189-3-1

The passing away and rebirth of sentient beings. ^an4-189-3-2

What things are to be realized with wisdom? ^an4-189-4-1

The ending of defilements. ^an4-189-4-2

These are the four things to be realized.” ^an4-189-4-3

### Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 4.190) ^an4-190-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an4-190-1-1

Now, at that time it was the sabbath, and the Buddha was sitting surrounded by the Saṅgha of monks. ^an4-190-1-2

Then the Buddha looked around the Saṅgha of mendicants, who were so very silent. He addressed them: ^an4-190-1-3

“This assembly has no chaff, mendicants, it is free of chaff, pure, and consolidated in the core. ^an4-190-2-1

Such is this Saṅgha of mendicants, such is this assembly! ^an4-190-2-2

An assembly such as this is rarely seen in the world. ^an4-190-2-3

An assembly such as this is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-190-2-4

For an assembly such as this, giving little becomes much, while giving much becomes even more. ^an4-190-2-5

An assembly such as this is worth traveling many leagues to see, even if you have to carry your own provisions in a knapsack. ^an4-190-2-6

There are mendicants staying in this Saṅgha who have attained to the gods. ^an4-190-3-1

There are mendicants staying in this Saṅgha who have attained to divinity. ^an4-190-3-2

There are mendicants staying in this Saṅgha who have attained to the imperturbable. ^an4-190-3-3

There are mendicants staying in this Saṅgha who have attained to nobility. ^an4-190-3-4

And how has a mendicant attained to the gods? ^an4-190-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … ^an4-190-4-2

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an4-190-4-3

That’s how a mendicant has attained to the gods. ^an4-190-4-4

And how has a mendicant attained to divinity? ^an4-190-5-1

Firstly, a mendicant meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-190-5-2

Furthermore, a mendicant meditates spreading a heart full of compassion … ^an4-190-5-3

rejoicing … ^an4-190-5-4

equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an4-190-5-5

That’s how a mendicant has attained to divinity. ^an4-190-5-6

And how has a mendicant attained to the imperturbable? ^an4-190-6-1

It’s when a mendicant—going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity—aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an4-190-6-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, he enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an4-190-6-3

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, he enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an4-190-6-4

Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, he enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an4-190-6-5

That’s how a mendicant has attained to the imperturbable. ^an4-190-6-6

And how has a mendicant attained to nobility? ^an4-190-7-1

It’s when they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-190-7-2

That’s how a mendicant has attained to nobility.” ^an4-190-7-3

### Followed by Ear (Numbered Discourses 4.191) ^an4-191-0

“Mendicants, you can expect four benefits when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically. ^an4-191-1-1

What four? ^an4-191-1-2

Take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an4-191-1-3

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-191-1-4

They’ve followed those teachings by ear, consolidated them by voice, examined them by the mind, and well comprehended them theoretically. ^an4-191-1-5

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. ^an4-191-1-6

Being happy there, passages of the teaching come back to them. ^an4-191-1-7

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-1-8

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-1-9

This is the first benefit you can expect when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically. ^an4-191-1-10

Furthermore, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an4-191-2-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-191-2-2

They’ve followed those teachings by ear, consolidated them by voice, examined them by the mind, and well comprehended them theoretically. ^an4-191-2-3

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. ^an4-191-2-4

Though they’re happy there, passages of the teaching don’t come back to them. ^an4-191-2-5

However, a mendicant with psychic powers, who has achieved mastery of the mind, teaches Dhamma to the assembly of gods. ^an4-191-2-6

They think: ^an4-191-2-7

‘I used to lead the spiritual life in this same teaching and training.’ ^an4-191-2-8

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-2-9

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-2-10

Suppose a person was skilled in the sound of drums. ^an4-191-2-11

While traveling along a road they hear the sound of drums. ^an4-191-2-12

They wouldn’t have any doubts or uncertainties about whether ^an4-191-2-13

that was the sound of drums or not. ^an4-191-2-14

They’d just conclude, ‘That’s the sound of drums.’ ^an4-191-2-15

In the same way, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching … ^an4-191-2-16

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. … ^an4-191-2-19

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-2-24

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-2-25

This is the second benefit you can expect when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically. ^an4-191-2-26

Furthermore, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an4-191-3-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-191-3-2

They’ve followed those teachings by ear, consolidated them by voice, examined them by the mind, and well comprehended them theoretically. ^an4-191-3-3

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. ^an4-191-3-4

But passages of the teaching don’t come back to them when they’re happy, nor does a mendicant with psychic powers … teach Dhamma to the assembly of gods. ^an4-191-3-5

However, a godling teaches Dhamma to the assembly of gods. ^an4-191-3-6

They think: ^an4-191-3-7

‘I used to lead the spiritual life in this same teaching and training.’ ^an4-191-3-8

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-3-9

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-3-10

Suppose a person was skilled in the sound of horns. ^an4-191-3-11

While traveling along a road they hear the sound of horns. ^an4-191-3-12

They wouldn’t have any doubt about whether ^an4-191-3-13

that was the sound of horns or not. ^an4-191-3-14

They’d just conclude, ‘That’s the sound of horns.’ ^an4-191-3-15

In the same way, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching … ^an4-191-3-16

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. … ^an4-191-3-19

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-3-24

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-3-25

This is the third benefit you can expect when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically. ^an4-191-3-26

Furthermore, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an4-191-4-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an4-191-4-2

They’ve followed those teachings by ear, consolidated them by voice, examined them by the mind, and well comprehended them theoretically. ^an4-191-4-3

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. ^an4-191-4-4

But passages of the teaching don’t come back to them when they’re happy, and neither a mendicant with psychic powers … nor a godling teaches Dhamma to the assembly of gods. ^an4-191-4-5

But a being who has been reborn spontaneously reminds another such being: ^an4-191-4-6

‘Do you remember, good fellow? Do you remember where we used to lead the spiritual life?’ ^an4-191-4-7

He says: ^an4-191-4-8

‘I remember, good fellow, I remember!’ ^an4-191-4-9

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-4-10

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-4-11

Suppose there were two friends who had played together in the sand. ^an4-191-4-12

Some time or other they’d meet. ^an4-191-4-13

And one friend would say to the other: ^an4-191-4-14

‘Do you remember this, dear? Do you remember that, dear?’ ^an4-191-4-15

They’d say: ^an4-191-4-16

‘I remember, dear, I remember!’ ^an4-191-4-17

In the same way, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching … ^an4-191-4-18

But they die unmindful and are reborn in one of the orders of gods. … ^an4-191-4-21

Memory comes up slowly, ^an4-191-4-27

but then that being quickly reaches distinction. ^an4-191-4-28

This is the fourth benefit you can expect when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically. ^an4-191-4-29

You can expect these four benefits when the teachings have been followed by ear, consolidated by voice, examined by the mind, and well comprehended theoretically.” ^an4-191-4-30

### Facts (Numbered Discourses 4.192) ^an4-192-0

“Mendicants, these four things can be known in four situations. ^an4-192-1-1

What four? ^an4-192-1-2

You can get to know a person’s ethics by living with them. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless. ^an4-192-1-3

You can get to know a person’s purity by dealing with them. … ^an4-192-1-4

You can get to know a person’s resilience in times of trouble. … ^an4-192-1-5

You can get to know a person’s wisdom by discussion. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless. ^an4-192-1-6

‘You can get to know a person’s ethics by living with them. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless.’ ^an4-192-2-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an4-192-2-2

Take an individual who’s living with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-2-3

‘For a long time this venerable’s deeds have been broken, tainted, spotty, and marred. Their deeds and behavior are inconsistent. ^an4-192-2-4

This venerable is unethical, not ethical.’ ^an4-192-2-5

Take another individual who’s living with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-3-1

‘For a long time this venerable’s deeds have been intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred. Their deeds and behavior are consistent. ^an4-192-3-2

This venerable is ethical, not unethical.’ ^an4-192-3-3

That’s why I said that you can get to know a person’s ethics by living with them. But only after a long time, not a short time; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless. ^an4-192-3-4

‘You can get to know a person’s purity by dealing with them. …’ ^an4-192-4-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an4-192-4-2

Take an individual who has dealings with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-4-3

‘This venerable deals with one person in one way. Then they deal with two, three, or many people each in different ways. ^an4-192-4-4

They’re not consistent from one deal to the next. ^an4-192-4-5

This venerable’s dealings are impure, not pure.’ ^an4-192-4-6

Take another individual who has dealings with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-5-1

‘This venerable deals with one person in one way. Then they deal with two, three, or many people each in the same way. ^an4-192-5-2

They’re consistent from one deal to the next. ^an4-192-5-3

This venerable’s dealings are pure, not impure.’ ^an4-192-5-4

That’s why I said that you can get to know a person’s purity by dealing with them. … ^an4-192-5-5

‘You can get to know a person’s resilience in times of trouble. …’ ^an4-192-6-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an4-192-6-2

Take someone who experiences loss of family, wealth, or health. But they don’t reflect: ^an4-192-6-3

‘Worldly life is like that. Incarnation is like that. That’s why the eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around the eight worldly conditions: ^an4-192-6-4

gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, pleasure and pain.’ ^an4-192-6-5

They sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-192-6-6

Take someone else who experiences loss of family, wealth, or health. But they reflect: ^an4-192-7-1

‘Worldly life is like that. Incarnation is like that. That’s why the eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around the eight worldly conditions: ^an4-192-7-2

gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, pleasure and pain.’ ^an4-192-7-3

They don’t sorrow or wail or lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an4-192-7-4

That’s why I said that you can get to know a person’s resilience in times of trouble. … ^an4-192-7-5

‘You can get to know a person’s wisdom by discussion. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless.’ ^an4-192-8-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an4-192-8-2

Take an individual who is discussing with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-8-3

‘Judging by this venerable’s approach, by what they’re getting at, and by how they discuss a question, they’re witless, not wise. ^an4-192-8-4

Why is that? ^an4-192-8-5

This venerable does not bring up a deep and meaningful saying that is peaceful, sublime, beyond the scope of logic, subtle, comprehensible to the astute. ^an4-192-8-6

When this venerable speaks on Dhamma they’re not able to explain the meaning, either briefly or in detail. They can’t teach it, assert it, establish it, clarify it, analyze it, or reveal it. ^an4-192-8-7

This venerable is witless, not wise.’ ^an4-192-8-8

Suppose a person with clear eyes was standing on the bank of a lake. They’d see a little fish rising, ^an4-192-9-1

and think: ^an4-192-9-2

‘Judging by this fish’s approach, by the ripples it makes, and by its force, it’s a little fish, not a big one.’ ^an4-192-9-3

In the same way, an individual who is discussing with someone else would come to know: ^an4-192-9-4

‘Judging by this venerable’s approach, by what they’re getting at, and by how they discuss a question, they’re witless, not wise. …’ ^an4-192-9-5

Take another individual who is discussing with someone else. They come to know: ^an4-192-10-1

‘Judging by this venerable’s approach, by what they’re getting at, and by how they discuss a question, they’re wise, not witless. ^an4-192-10-2

Why is that? ^an4-192-10-3

This venerable brings up a deep and meaningful saying that is peaceful, sublime, beyond the scope of logic, subtle, comprehensible to the astute. ^an4-192-10-4

When this venerable speaks on Dhamma they’re able to explain the meaning, either briefly or in detail. They teach it, assert it, establish it, clarify it, analyze it, and reveal it. ^an4-192-10-5

This venerable is wise, not witless.’ ^an4-192-10-6

Suppose a man with clear eyes was standing on the bank of a lake. He’d see a big fish rising, ^an4-192-11-1

and think: ^an4-192-11-2

‘Judging by this fish’s approach, by the ripples it makes, and by its force, it’s a big fish, not a little one.’ ^an4-192-11-3

In the same way, an individual who is discussing with someone else would come to know: ^an4-192-11-4

‘Judging by this venerable’s approach, by what they’re getting at, and by how they articulate a question, they’re wise, not witless. …’ ^an4-192-11-5

That’s why I said that you can get to know a person’s wisdom by discussion. But only after a long time, not casually; only when attentive, not when inattentive; and only by the wise, not the witless. ^an4-192-12-1

These are the four things that can be known in four situations.” ^an4-192-12-2

### With Bhaddiya (Numbered Discourses 4.193) ^an4-193-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an4-193-1-1

Then Bhaddiya the Licchavi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-193-1-2

“Sir, I have heard this: ^an4-193-2-1

‘The ascetic Gotama is deceitful. He knows a conversion trick, and uses it to convert the disciples of those of other religions.’ ^an4-193-2-2

I trust that those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and do not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?” ^an4-193-2-4

“Please, Bhaddiya, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think ‘The ascetic is our respected teacher.’ ^an4-193-3-1

But when you know for yourselves: ^an4-193-3-2

‘These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering’, then you should give them up. ^an4-193-3-3

What do you think, Bhaddiya? ^an4-193-4-1

Does greed come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an4-193-4-2

“Harm, sir.” ^an4-193-4-3

“A greedy individual—overcome by greed—kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an4-193-4-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an4-193-4-5

“What do you think, Bhaddiya? ^an4-193-5-1

Does hate … or delusion … or aggression come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an4-193-5-2

“Harm, sir.” ^an4-193-5-3

“An aggressive individual kills living creatures, steals, commits adultery, lies, and encourages others to do the same. Is that for their lasting harm and suffering?” ^an4-193-5-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an4-193-5-5

“What do you think, Bhaddiya, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an4-193-6-1

“Unskillful, sir.” ^an4-193-6-2

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an4-193-6-3

“Blameworthy, sir.” ^an4-193-6-4

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an4-193-6-5

“Criticized by sensible people, sir.” ^an4-193-6-6

“When you undertake them, do they lead to harm and suffering, or not? ^an4-193-6-7

Or how do you see this?” ^an4-193-6-8

“When you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering. ^an4-193-6-9

That’s how we see it.” ^an4-193-6-10

“So, Bhaddiya, when we said: ^an4-193-7-1

‘Please, Bhaddiya, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an4-193-7-2

But when you know for yourselves: ^an4-193-7-3

“These things are unskillful, blameworthy, criticized by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to harm and suffering”, then you should give them up.’ ^an4-193-7-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an4-193-7-5

Please, Bhaddiya, don’t rely on oral transmission … ^an4-193-8-1

But when you know for yourselves: ^an4-193-8-2

‘These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness’, then you should acquire them and keep them. ^an4-193-8-3

What do you think, Bhaddiya? ^an4-193-9-1

Does contentment … love … understanding … benevolence come up in a person for their welfare or harm?” ^an4-193-9-2

“Welfare, sir.” ^an4-193-10-2

“An individual who is benevolent—not overcome by aggression—doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit adultery, lie, or encourage others to do the same. Is that for their lasting welfare and happiness?” ^an4-193-10-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an4-193-10-4

“What do you think, Bhaddiya, are these things skillful or unskillful?” ^an4-193-11-1

“Skillful, sir.” ^an4-193-11-2

“Blameworthy or blameless?” ^an4-193-11-3

“Blameless, sir.” ^an4-193-11-4

“Criticized or praised by sensible people?” ^an4-193-11-5

“Praised by sensible people, sir.” ^an4-193-11-6

“When you undertake them, do they lead to welfare and happiness, or not? ^an4-193-11-7

Or how do you see this?” ^an4-193-11-8

“When you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness. ^an4-193-11-9

That’s how we see it.” ^an4-193-11-10

“So, Bhaddiya, when we said: ^an4-193-12-1

‘Please, Bhaddiya, don’t go by oral transmission, don’t go by lineage, don’t go by testament, don’t go by canonical authority, don’t rely on logic, don’t rely on inference, don’t go by reasoned train of thought, don’t go by the acceptance of a view after deliberation, don’t go by the appearance of competence, and don’t think “The ascetic is our respected teacher.” ^an4-193-12-2

But when you know for yourselves: ^an4-193-12-3

“These things are skillful, blameless, praised by sensible people, and when you undertake them, they lead to welfare and happiness”, then you should acquire them and keep them.’ ^an4-193-12-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an4-193-12-5

The good and true persons in the world encourage their disciples: ^an4-193-13-1

‘Please, my man, live rid of greed. ^an4-193-13-2

Then you won’t act out of greed by way of body, speech, or mind. ^an4-193-13-3

Live rid of hate … delusion … aggression. ^an4-193-13-4

Then you won’t act out of hate … delusion … aggression by way of body, speech, or mind.” ^an4-193-13-5

When he said this, Bhaddiya the Licchavi said to the Buddha, ^an4-193-14-1

“Excellent, sir! … From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-193-14-2

“Well, Bhaddiya, did I say to you: ^an4-193-15-1

‘Please, Bhaddiya, be my disciple, ^an4-193-15-2

and I will be your teacher’?” ^an4-193-15-3

“No, sir.” ^an4-193-15-4

“Though I state and assert this, certain ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with the incorrect, hollow, false, untruthful claim: ^an4-193-15-5

‘The ascetic Gotama is deceitful. He knows a conversion trick, and uses it to convert the disciples of those of other religions.’” ^an4-193-15-6

“Sir, this conversion trick is excellent. ^an4-193-15-7

This conversion trick is lovely! ^an4-193-15-8

If my loved ones—relatives and kin—were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an4-193-15-9

If all the aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an4-193-15-10

“That’s so true, Bhaddiya! That’s so true, Bhaddiya! ^an4-193-16-1

If all the aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an4-193-16-2

If the whole world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—were to be converted by this, for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an4-193-16-4

If these great sal trees were to be converted by this, for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness—if they were sentient. ^an4-193-16-5

How much more then a human being!” ^an4-193-16-6

### At Sāpūga (Numbered Discourses 4.194) ^an4-194-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Sāpūga. ^an4-194-1-1

Then several Koliyans from Sāpūga went up to Ānanda, bowed, and sat down to one side. Then Venerable Ānanda said to them: ^an4-194-1-2

“Byagghapajjas, these four factors of trying to be pure have been rightly explained by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment. ^an4-194-2-1

What four? ^an4-194-2-2

The factors of trying to be pure in ethics, mind, view, and freedom. ^an4-194-2-3

And what is the factor of trying to be pure in ethics? ^an4-194-3-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-194-3-2

This is called purity of ethics. ^an4-194-3-3

They think: ‘I will fulfill such purity of ethics, or, if it’s already fulfilled, I’ll support it in every situation by wisdom.’ Their enthusiasm for that—their effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness—is called the factor of trying to be pure in ethics. ^an4-194-3-4

And what is the factor of trying to be pure in mind? ^an4-194-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an4-194-4-2

This is called purity of mind. ^an4-194-4-3

They think: ‘I will fulfill such purity of mind, or, if it’s already fulfilled, I’ll support it in every situation by wisdom.’ Their enthusiasm for that—their effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness—is called the factor of trying to be pure in mind. ^an4-194-4-4

And what is the factor of trying to be pure in view? ^an4-194-5-1

Take a mendicant who truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-194-5-2

This is called purity of view. ^an4-194-5-3

They think: ‘I will fulfill such purity of view, or, if it’s already fulfilled, I’ll support it in every situation by wisdom.’ Their enthusiasm for that—their effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness—is called the factor of trying to be pure in view. ^an4-194-5-4

And what is the factor of trying to be pure in freedom? ^an4-194-6-1

That noble disciple—who has these factors of trying to be pure in ethics, mind, and view—detaches their mind from things that arouse greed, and frees their mind from things that it should be freed from. ^an4-194-6-2

Doing so, they experience perfect freedom. ^an4-194-6-3

This is called purity of freedom. ^an4-194-6-4

They think: ‘I will fulfill such purity of freedom, or, if it’s already fulfilled, I’ll support it in every situation by wisdom.’ Their enthusiasm for that—their effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness—is called the factor of trying to be pure in freedom. ^an4-194-6-5

These four factors of trying to be pure have been rightly explained by the Blessed One, who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.” ^an4-194-7-1

### With Vappa (Numbered Discourses 4.195) ^an4-195-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an4-195-1-1

Then Vappa of the Sakyans, a disciple of the Jains, went up to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, and sat down to one side. Mahāmoggallāna said to him: ^an4-195-1-2

“Vappa, take an individual who, due to the fading away of ignorance and the arising of knowledge, is restrained in body, speech, and mind. ^an4-195-2-1

Do you see any reason why defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life?” ^an4-195-2-2

“Sir, I do see such a case. ^an4-195-2-3

Take someone who did bad deeds in a past life. But the result of that has not yet ripened. ^an4-195-2-4

For this reason defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life.” ^an4-195-2-5

But this conversation between Mahāmoggallāna and Vappa was left unfinished. ^an4-195-2-6

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall. He sat down on the seat spread out, and said to Mahāmoggallāna, ^an4-195-3-1

“Moggallāna, what were you sitting talking about just now? What conversation was left unfinished?” ^an4-195-4-1

Moggallāna repeated the entire conversation to the Buddha, and concluded: ^an4-195-4-2

“This was my conversation with Vappa that was unfinished when the Buddha arrived.” ^an4-195-4-9

Then the Buddha said to Vappa, ^an4-195-5-1

“Vappa, we can discuss this. But only if you grant what should be granted, and reject what should be rejected. And if you ask me the meaning of anything you don’t understand, saying: ^an4-195-5-2

‘Sir, why is this? What’s the meaning of that?’” ^an4-195-5-3

“Sir, let us discuss this. I will do as you say.” ^an4-195-5-4

“What do you think, Vappa? ^an4-195-6-1

There are distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of instigating bodily activity. These don’t occur in someone who refrains from such bodily activity. ^an4-195-6-2

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an4-195-6-3

Do you see any reason why defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life?” ^an4-195-6-4

“No, sir.” ^an4-195-6-5

“What do you think, Vappa? ^an4-195-7-1

There are distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of instigating verbal activity. These don’t occur in someone who refrains from such verbal activity. ^an4-195-7-2

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an4-195-7-3

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an4-195-7-4

Do you see any reason why defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life?” ^an4-195-7-5

“No, sir.” ^an4-195-7-6

“What do you think, Vappa? ^an4-195-8-1

There are distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of instigating mental activity. These don’t occur in someone who refrains from such mental activity. ^an4-195-8-2

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an4-195-8-3

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an4-195-8-4

Do you see any reason why defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life?” ^an4-195-8-5

“No, sir.” ^an4-195-8-6

“What do you think, Vappa? ^an4-195-9-1

There are distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of ignorance. These don’t occur when ignorance fades away and knowledge arises. ^an4-195-9-2

They don’t perform any new deeds, and old deeds are eliminated by experiencing their results little by little. ^an4-195-9-3

This wearing away is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an4-195-9-4

Do you see any reason why defilements giving rise to painful feelings would defile that person in the next life?” ^an4-195-9-5

“No, sir.” ^an4-195-9-6

“A mendicant whose mind is rightly freed like this has achieved six consistent responses. ^an4-195-10-1

Seeing a sight with the eye, they’re neither happy nor sad, but remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an4-195-10-2

Hearing a sound with the ears … ^an4-195-10-3

Smelling an odor with the nose … ^an4-195-10-4

Tasting a flavor with the tongue … ^an4-195-10-5

Encountering a touch with the body … ^an4-195-10-6

Knowing an idea with the mind, they’re neither happy nor sad, but remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an4-195-10-7

Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^an4-195-10-8

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, being no longer relished, will become cool right here.’ ^an4-195-10-9

Suppose there was a shadow cast by a post. ^an4-195-11-1

Then along comes a person with a spade and basket. ^an4-195-11-2

They cut down the post at its base, ^an4-195-11-3

dig it up, ^an4-195-11-4

and pull it out by its roots, right down to the fibers and stems. ^an4-195-11-5

Then they split it apart, ^an4-195-11-6

cut up the parts, ^an4-195-11-7

and chop them into splinters. ^an4-195-11-8

Next they dry the splinters in the wind and sun, ^an4-195-11-9

burn them with fire, ^an4-195-11-10

and reduce them to ashes. ^an4-195-11-11

Then they whisk away the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream. ^an4-195-11-12

And so the shadow cast by the post is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an4-195-11-13

In the same way, a mendicant whose mind is rightly freed like this has achieved six consistent responses. ^an4-195-12-1

Seeing a sight with the eye, they’re neither happy nor sad, but remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an4-195-12-2

Hearing a sound with the ears … ^an4-195-12-3

Smelling an odor with the nose … ^an4-195-12-4

Tasting a flavor with the tongue … ^an4-195-12-5

Encountering a touch with the body … ^an4-195-12-6

Knowing an idea with the mind, they’re neither happy nor sad, but remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an4-195-12-7

Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’ ^an4-195-12-8

They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, being no longer relished, will become cool right here.’” ^an4-195-12-9

When he said this, Vappa the Sakyan, the disciple of the Jains, said to the Buddha: ^an4-195-13-1

“Sir, suppose there was a man who raised commercial horses for profit. ^an4-195-13-2

But he never made any profit, and instead just got weary and frustrated. ^an4-195-13-3

In the same way, I paid homage to those Jain fools for profit. ^an4-195-13-4

But I never made any profit, and instead just got weary and frustrated. ^an4-195-13-5

From this day forth, any confidence I had in those Jain fools I whisk away as in a strong wind, or glide away as down a swift stream. ^an4-195-13-6

Excellent, sir! … From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-195-13-7

### With Sāḷha (Numbered Discourses 4.196) ^an4-196-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an4-196-1-1

Then Sāḷha and Abhaya the Licchavis went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-196-1-2

“There are, sir, some ascetics and brahmins who advocate crossing the flood by means of two things: ^an4-196-2-1

purification of ethics, and mortification in disgust of sin. ^an4-196-2-2

What does the Buddha say about this?” ^an4-196-2-3

“Sāḷha, purification of ethics is one of the factors of the ascetic life, I say. ^an4-196-3-1

But those ascetics and brahmins who teach fervent mortification in disgust of sin—regarding it as essential and sticking to it—are incapable of crossing the flood. ^an4-196-3-2

And those ascetics and brahmins whose livelihood and behavior by way of body, speech, and mind, is not pure are also incapable of knowing and seeing, of supreme awakening. ^an4-196-3-3

Suppose a man who wanted to cross a river took a sharp axe into a wood. ^an4-196-4-1

There he’d see a large green sal tree, straight and young and grown unskewed. ^an4-196-4-2

He’d cut it down at the base, ^an4-196-4-3

cut off the top, ^an4-196-4-4

and completely strip off the branches and foliage. ^an4-196-4-5

Then he’d trim it with axes and ^an4-196-4-6

machetes, ^an4-196-4-7

plane it, ^an4-196-4-8

and sand it with a rock. ^an4-196-4-9

Finally, he’d launch out on the river. ^an4-196-4-10

What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an4-196-5-1

Is that man capable of crossing the river?” ^an4-196-5-2

“No, sir. ^an4-196-5-3

Why not? ^an4-196-5-4

Because that green sal tree is well worked on the outside, but inside it’s still not cleared out. ^an4-196-5-5

I’d expect that green sal tree to sink, and the man to come to ruin.” ^an4-196-5-6

“In the same way, Sāḷha, those ascetics and brahmins who teach fervent mortification in disgust of sin—regarding it as essential and sticking to it—are incapable of crossing the flood. ^an4-196-6-1

And those ascetics and brahmins whose livelihood and behavior by way of body, speech, and mind is not pure are also incapable of knowing and seeing, of supreme awakening. ^an4-196-6-2

But those ascetics and brahmins who don’t teach mortification in disgust of sin—not regarding it as essential or sticking to it—are capable of crossing the flood. ^an4-196-7-1

And those ascetics and brahmins whose behavior by way of body, speech, and mind is pure are also capable of knowing and seeing, of supreme awakening. ^an4-196-7-2

Suppose a man who wanted to cross a river took a sharp axe into a wood. ^an4-196-8-1

There he’d see a large green sal tree, straight and young and grown unskewed. ^an4-196-8-2

He’d cut it down at the base, ^an4-196-8-3

cut off the top, ^an4-196-8-4

and completely strip off the branches and foliage. ^an4-196-8-5

Then he’d trim it with axes and ^an4-196-8-6

machetes. ^an4-196-8-7

Then he’d take a chisel and completely clear it out inside. ^an4-196-8-8

Then he’d plane it, ^an4-196-8-9

sand it with a rock, ^an4-196-8-10

and make it into a boat. ^an4-196-8-11

Finally he’d fix it with oars and rudder, ^an4-196-8-12

and launch out on the river. ^an4-196-8-13

What do you think, Sāḷha? ^an4-196-9-1

Is that man capable of crossing the river?” ^an4-196-9-2

“Yes, sir. ^an4-196-9-3

Why is that? ^an4-196-9-4

Because that green sal tree is well worked on the outside, cleared out on the inside, made into a boat, and fixed with oars and rudder. ^an4-196-9-5

I’d expect ^an4-196-9-6

that boat to not sink, and the man to safely make it to the far shore.” ^an4-196-9-7

“In the same way, Sāḷha, those ascetics and brahmins who don’t teach fervent mortification in disgust of sin—not regarding it as essential or sticking to it—are capable of crossing the flood. ^an4-196-10-1

And those ascetics and brahmins whose behavior by way of body, speech, and mind is pure are also capable of knowing and seeing, of supreme awakening. ^an4-196-10-2

Suppose there was a warrior who knew lots of fancy archery tricks. ^an4-196-10-3

It is only with these three factors that he becomes worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-196-10-4

What three? ^an4-196-10-5

He’s a long-distance shooter, a marksman, and one who shatters large objects. ^an4-196-10-6

Just as a warrior is a long-distance shooter, ^an4-196-11-1

a noble disciple has right immersion. ^an4-196-11-2

A noble disciple with right immersion truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: <em>all</em> form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-196-11-3

They truly see any kind of feeling … ^an4-196-11-4

perception … ^an4-196-11-5

choices … ^an4-196-11-6

consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near, <em>all</em> consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’ ^an4-196-11-7

Just as a warrior is a marksman, ^an4-196-12-1

a noble disciple has right view. ^an4-196-12-2

A noble disciple with right view truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-196-12-3

Just as a warrior shatters large objects, ^an4-196-13-1

a noble disciple has right freedom. ^an4-196-13-2

A noble disciple with right freedom shatters the great mass of ignorance.” ^an4-196-13-3

### Queen Mallikā (Numbered Discourses 4.197) ^an4-197-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an4-197-1-1

Then Queen Mallikā went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-197-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why in this life some ladies are ugly, unattractive, and bad-looking; ^an4-197-2-1

and poor, with few assets and possessions; and insignificant? ^an4-197-2-2

And why are some ladies ugly, unattractive, and bad-looking; ^an4-197-3-1

but rich, affluent, wealthy, and illustrious? ^an4-197-3-2

And why are some ladies attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty; ^an4-197-4-1

but poor, with few assets and possessions; and insignificant? ^an4-197-4-2

And why are some ladies attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty; and rich, affluent, wealthy, and illustrious?” ^an4-197-5-1

“Take a lady who is irritable and bad-tempered. ^an4-197-6-1

Even when criticized a little bit she loses her temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and displaying annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an4-197-6-2
