She doesn’t give to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an4-197-6-3

And she’s jealous, ^an4-197-6-4

envying, resenting, and begrudging the possessions, honor, respect, reverence, homage, and veneration given to others. ^an4-197-6-5

If she comes back to this place after passing away, wherever she is reborn she’s ugly, unattractive, and bad-looking; ^an4-197-6-6

and poor, with few assets and possessions; and insignificant. ^an4-197-6-7

Take another lady who is irritable and bad-tempered. … ^an4-197-7-1

But she does give to ascetics or brahmins … ^an4-197-7-3

And she’s not jealous … ^an4-197-7-4

If she comes back to this place after passing away, wherever she is reborn she’s ugly, unattractive, and bad-looking; ^an4-197-7-6

but rich, affluent, wealthy, and illustrious. ^an4-197-7-7

Take another lady who isn’t irritable and bad-tempered. … ^an4-197-8-1

But she doesn’t give to ascetics or brahmins … ^an4-197-8-3

And she’s jealous … ^an4-197-8-4

If she comes back to this place after passing away, wherever she is reborn she’s attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty; ^an4-197-8-6

but poor, with few assets and possessions; and insignificant. ^an4-197-8-7

Take another lady who isn’t irritable and bad-tempered. … ^an4-197-9-1

She gives to ascetics and brahmins … ^an4-197-9-3

And she’s not jealous … ^an4-197-9-4

If she comes back to this place after passing away, wherever she is reborn she’s attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty; ^an4-197-9-6

and rich, affluent, wealthy, and illustrious. ^an4-197-9-7

This is why some ladies are ugly … ^an4-197-10-1

and poor … and insignificant. ^an4-197-10-2

And some ladies are ugly … ^an4-197-10-3

but rich … and illustrious. ^an4-197-10-4

And some ladies are attractive … ^an4-197-10-5

but poor … and insignificant. ^an4-197-10-6

And some ladies are attractive … ^an4-197-10-7

and rich … and illustrious.” ^an4-197-10-8

When this was said, Queen Mallikā said to the Buddha: ^an4-197-11-1

“Sir, in another life I must have been irritable and bad-tempered. ^an4-197-11-2

Even when lightly criticized I must have lost my temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and displaying annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an4-197-11-3

For now I am ugly, unattractive, and bad-looking. ^an4-197-11-4

In another life I must have given to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. For now I am rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an4-197-12-1

In another life, I must not have been jealous, envying, resenting, and begrudging the possessions, honor, respect, reverence, homage, and veneration given to others. For now I am illustrious. ^an4-197-13-1

In this royal court I command maidens of the aristocrats, brahmins, and householders. ^an4-197-13-2

So, sir, from this day forth I will not be irritable and bad-tempered. ^an4-197-13-3

Even when heavily criticized I won’t lose my temper, become annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, or display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an4-197-13-4

I will give to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an4-197-13-5

I will not be jealous, envying, resenting, and begrudging the possessions, honor, respect, reverence, homage, and veneration given to others. ^an4-197-13-6

Excellent, sir! … From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an4-197-13-7

### Fervent Mortification of Oneself (Numbered Discourses 4.198) ^an4-198-0

“Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world. ^an4-198-1-1

What four? ^an4-198-1-2

One individual mortifies themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves. ^an4-198-1-3

One individual mortifies others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others. ^an4-198-1-4

One individual mortifies themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others. ^an4-198-1-5

One individual neither mortifies themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others. ^an4-198-1-6

They live without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine. ^an4-198-1-7

And how does one individual mortify themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves? ^an4-198-2-1

It’s when someone goes naked, ignoring conventions. They lick their hands, and don’t come or wait when called. They don’t consent to food brought to them, or food prepared on their behalf, or an invitation for a meal. ^an4-198-2-2

They don’t receive anything from a pot or bowl; or from someone who keeps sheep, or who has a weapon or a shovel in their home; or where a couple is eating; or where there is a woman who is pregnant, breastfeeding, or who lives with a man; or where there’s a hound waiting or flies buzzing. They accept no fish or meat or beer or wine, and drink no fermented gruel. ^an4-198-2-3

They go to just one house for alms, taking just one mouthful, or two houses and two mouthfuls, up to seven houses and seven mouthfuls. ^an4-198-2-4

They feed on one saucer a day, two saucers a day, up to seven saucers a day. ^an4-198-2-5

They eat once a day, once every second day, up to once a week, and so on, even up to once a fortnight. They live pursuing the practice of eating food at set intervals. ^an4-198-2-6

They eat herbs, millet, wild rice, poor rice, water lettuce, rice bran, scum from boiling rice, sesame flour, grass, or cow dung. They survive on forest roots and fruits, or eating fallen fruit. ^an4-198-3-1

They wear robes of sunn hemp, mixed hemp, corpse-wrapping cloth, rags, lodh tree bark, antelope hide (whole or in strips), kusa grass, bark, wood-chips, human hair, horse-tail hair, or owls’ wings. ^an4-198-4-1

They tear out hair and beard, pursuing this practice. ^an4-198-4-2

They constantly stand, turning down seats. ^an4-198-4-3

They squat, committed to the endeavor of squatting. ^an4-198-4-4

They lie on a mat of thorns, making a mat of thorns their bed. ^an4-198-4-5

They’re devoted to ritual bathing three times a day, including at dusk. ^an4-198-4-6

And so they live pursuing these various ways of mortifying and tormenting the body. ^an4-198-4-7

That’s how one individual mortifies themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves. ^an4-198-4-8

And how does one individual mortify others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others? ^an4-198-5-1

It’s when an individual is a slaughterer of sheep, pigs, poultry, or deer, a hunter or fisher, a bandit, an executioner, a butcher of cattle, a jailer, or has some other cruel livelihood. ^an4-198-5-2

That’s how one individual mortifies others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others. ^an4-198-5-3

And how does one individual mortify themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others? ^an4-198-6-1

It’s when an individual is an anointed aristocratic king or a well-to-do brahmin. ^an4-198-6-2

He has a new ceremonial hall built to the east of the citadel. He shaves off his hair and beard, dresses in a rough antelope hide, and smears his body with ghee and oil. Scratching his back with antlers, he enters the hall with his chief queen and the brahmin high priest. ^an4-198-6-3

There he lies on the bare ground strewn with grass. ^an4-198-6-4

The king feeds on the milk from one teat of a cow that has a calf of the same color. The chief queen feeds on the milk from the second teat. The brahmin high priest feeds on the milk from the third teat. The milk from the fourth teat is served to the sacred flame. The calf feeds on the leftovers. ^an4-198-6-5

He says: ^an4-198-6-6

‘Slaughter this many bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats, rams, and horses for the sacrifice! Fell this many trees and reap this much grass for the sacrificial equipment!’ ^an4-198-6-7

His bondservants, servants, and workers do their jobs under threat of punishment and danger, weeping, with tearful faces. ^an4-198-6-8

That’s how one individual mortifies themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others. ^an4-198-6-9

And how does one individual neither mortify themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others, ^an4-198-7-1

living without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine? ^an4-198-7-2

It’s when a Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^an4-198-7-3

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. ^an4-198-7-4

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an4-198-7-5

A householder hears that teaching, or a householder’s child, or someone reborn in a good family. ^an4-198-7-6

They gain faith in the Realized One ^an4-198-7-7

and reflect: ^an4-198-7-8

‘Life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open. ^an4-198-7-9

It’s not easy for someone living at home to lead the spiritual life utterly full and pure, like a polished shell. ^an4-198-7-10

Why don’t I shave off my hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness?’ ^an4-198-7-11

After some time they give up a large or small fortune, and a large or small family circle. They shave off hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an4-198-7-12

Once they’ve gone forth, they take up the training and livelihood of the mendicants. They give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They’re scrupulous and kind, living full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an4-198-8-1

They give up stealing. They take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. They keep themselves clean by not thieving. ^an4-198-8-2

They give up unchastity. They are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an4-198-8-3

They give up lying. They speak the truth and stick to the truth. They’re honest and dependable, and don’t trick the world with their words. ^an4-198-8-4

They give up backbiting. They don’t repeat in one place what they heard in another so as to divide people against each other. Instead, they reconcile those who are divided and support those who are united, delighting in harmony, loving harmony, speaking words that promote harmony. ^an4-198-8-5

They give up harsh speech. They speak in a way that’s mellow, pleasing to the ear, endearing, going to the heart, polite, likable, and agreeable to the people. ^an4-198-8-6

They give up talking nonsense. Their words are timely, true, and meaningful, in line with the teaching and training. They say things at the right time which are valuable, reasonable, succinct, and beneficial. ^an4-198-8-7

They refrain from injuring plants and seeds. ^an4-198-9-1

They eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and food at the wrong time. ^an4-198-9-2

They refrain from seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music . ^an4-198-9-3

They refrain from attiring and adorning themselves with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an4-198-9-4

They refrain from high and luxurious beds. ^an4-198-9-5

They refrain from receiving gold and currency, ^an4-198-9-6

raw grains, ^an4-198-9-7

raw meat, ^an4-198-9-8

women and girls, ^an4-198-9-9

male and female bondservants, ^an4-198-9-10

goats and sheep, ^an4-198-9-11

chickens and pigs, ^an4-198-9-12

elephants, cows, horses, and mares, ^an4-198-9-13

and fields and land. ^an4-198-9-14

They refrain from running errands and messages; ^an4-198-9-15

buying and selling; ^an4-198-9-16

falsifying weights, metals, or measures; ^an4-198-9-17

bribery, fraud, cheating, and duplicity; ^an4-198-9-18

mutilation, murder, abduction, banditry, plunder, and violence. ^an4-198-9-19

They’re content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things. ^an4-198-10-1

They’re like a bird: wherever it flies, wings are its only burden. ^an4-198-10-2

In the same way, a mendicant is content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things. ^an4-198-10-3

When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, they experience a blameless happiness inside themselves. ^an4-198-10-4

Seeing a sight with the eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-198-11-1

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-198-11-2

Hearing a sound with the ears … ^an4-198-11-3

Smelling an odor with the nose … ^an4-198-11-4

Tasting a flavor with the tongue … ^an4-198-11-5

Encountering a touch with the body … ^an4-198-11-6

Knowing an idea with the mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an4-198-11-7

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an4-198-11-8

When they have this noble sense restraint, they experience an unsullied bliss inside themselves. ^an4-198-11-9

They act with situational awareness when going out and coming back; when looking ahead and aside; when bending and extending the limbs; when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes; when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; when urinating and defecating; when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent. ^an4-198-12-1

When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, this noble contentment, this noble sense restraint, and this noble mindfulness and situational awareness, ^an4-198-13-1

they frequent a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw. ^an4-198-13-2

After the meal, they return from almsround, sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. ^an4-198-13-3

Giving up covetousness for the world, they meditate with a heart rid of covetousness, cleansing the mind of covetousness. ^an4-198-13-4

Giving up ill will and malevolence, they meditate with a mind rid of ill will, full of sympathy for all living beings, cleansing the mind of ill will. ^an4-198-13-5

Giving up dullness and drowsiness, they meditate with a mind rid of dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, mindful and aware, cleansing the mind of dullness and drowsiness. ^an4-198-13-6

Giving up restlessness and remorse, they meditate without restlessness, their mind peaceful inside, cleansing the mind of restlessness and remorse. ^an4-198-13-7

Giving up doubt, they meditate having gone beyond doubt, not undecided about skillful qualities, cleansing the mind of doubt. ^an4-198-13-8

They give up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^an4-198-13-9

Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, they enter and remain in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an4-198-13-10

When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward recollection of past lives … ^an4-198-14-1

knowledge of the death and rebirth of sentient beings … ^an4-198-14-2

knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an4-198-14-3

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-198-14-4

They truly understand: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. ^an4-198-14-5

Knowing and seeing like this, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an4-198-15-1

When they’re freed, they know they’re freed. ^an4-198-15-2

They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an4-198-15-3

That’s how one individual neither mortifies themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others, ^an4-198-15-4

living without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine. ^an4-198-15-5

These are the four individuals found in the world.” ^an4-198-15-6

### Craving, the Weaver (Numbered Discourses 4.199) ^an4-199-0

The Buddha said this: ^an4-199-1-1

“Mendicants, I will teach you about craving—the weaver, the migrant, the creeping, the clinging. This world is choked by it, enveloped by it. It makes the world tangled like yarn, knotted like a ball of thread, and matted like rushes and reeds, not escaping the places of loss, the bad places, the underworld, transmigration. ^an4-199-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an4-199-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an4-199-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an4-199-1-5

“And what is that craving …? ^an4-199-2-1

There are eighteen currents of craving that derive from the interior, and eighteen that derive from the exterior. ^an4-199-2-2

What are the eighteen currents of craving that derive from the interior? ^an4-199-3-1

When there is the concept ‘I am’, there are the concepts ‘I am such’, ‘I am thus’, ‘I am otherwise’; ‘I am fleeting’, ‘I am lasting’; ‘mine’, ‘such is mine’, ‘thus is mine’, ‘otherwise is mine’; ‘also mine’, ‘such is also mine’, ‘thus is also mine’, ‘otherwise is also mine’; ‘I will be’, ‘I will be such’, ‘I will be thus’, ‘I will be otherwise’. ^an4-199-3-2

These are the eighteen currents of craving that derive from the interior. ^an4-199-3-3

What are the eighteen currents of craving that derive from the exterior? ^an4-199-4-1

When there is the concept ‘I am because of this’, there are the concepts ‘I am such because of this’, ‘I am thus because of this’, ‘I am otherwise because of this’; ‘I am fleeting because of this’, ‘I am lasting because of this’; ‘mine because of this’, ‘such is mine because of this’, ‘thus is mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is mine because of this’; ‘also mine because of this’, ‘such is also mine because of this’, ‘thus is also mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is also mine because of this’; ‘I will be because of this’, ‘I will be such because of this’, ‘I will be thus because of this’, ‘I will be otherwise because of this’. ^an4-199-4-2

These are the eighteen currents of craving that derive from the exterior. ^an4-199-4-3

So there are eighteen currents of craving that derive from the interior, and eighteen that derive from the exterior. ^an4-199-5-1

These are called the thirty-six currents of craving. ^an4-199-5-2

Each of these pertain to the past, future, and present, ^an4-199-5-3

making one hundred and eight currents of craving. ^an4-199-5-4

This is that craving—the weaver, the migrant, the creeping, the clinging. This world is choked by it, enveloped by it. It makes the world tangled like yarn, knotted like a ball of thread, and matted like rushes and reeds, not escaping the places of loss, the bad places, the underworld, transmigration.” ^an4-199-6-1

### Fondness (Numbered Discourses 4.200) ^an4-200-0

“Mendicants, these four things are born. ^an4-200-1-1

What four? ^an4-200-1-2

Fondness is born of fondness, ^an4-200-1-3

hate is born of fondness, ^an4-200-1-4

fondness is born of hate, and ^an4-200-1-5

hate is born of hate. ^an4-200-1-6

And how is fondness born of fondness? ^an4-200-2-1

It’s when someone likes, loves, and cares for an individual. ^an4-200-2-2

Others treat them with liking, love, and care. ^an4-200-2-3

They think: ^an4-200-2-4

‘These others like the individual I like.’ ^an4-200-2-5

And so fondness for them springs up. ^an4-200-2-6

That’s how fondness is born of fondness. ^an4-200-2-7

And how is hate born of fondness? ^an4-200-3-1

It’s when someone likes, loves, and cares for an individual. ^an4-200-3-2

Others treat them with disliking, loathing, and detestation. ^an4-200-3-3

They think: ^an4-200-3-4

‘These others dislike the individual I like.’ ^an4-200-3-5

And so hate for them springs up. ^an4-200-3-6

That’s how hate is born of fondness. ^an4-200-3-7

And how is fondness born of hate? ^an4-200-4-1

It’s when someone dislikes, loathes, and detests an individual. ^an4-200-4-2

Others treat them with disliking, loathing, and detestation. ^an4-200-4-3

They think: ^an4-200-4-4

‘These others dislike the individual I dislike.’ ^an4-200-4-5

And so fondness for them springs up. ^an4-200-4-6

That’s how fondness is born of hate. ^an4-200-4-7

And how is hate born of hate? ^an4-200-5-1

It’s when someone dislikes, loathes, and detests an individual. ^an4-200-5-2

Others treat them with liking, love, and care. ^an4-200-5-3

They think: ^an4-200-5-4

‘These others like the individual I dislike.’ ^an4-200-5-5

And so hate for them springs up. ^an4-200-5-6

That’s how hate is born of hate. ^an4-200-5-7

These are the four things that are born of fondness and hate. ^an4-200-5-8

A time comes when a mendicant … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an4-200-6-1

At that time they have no fondness born of fondness, hate born of fondness, fondness born of hate, or hate born of hate. ^an4-200-6-2

A time comes when a mendicant … enters and remains in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an4-200-7-1

At that time they have no fondness born of fondness, hate born of fondness, fondness born of hate, or hate born of hate. ^an4-200-7-2

A time comes when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-200-8-1

At that time any fondness born of fondness, hate born of fondness, fondness born of hate, or hate born of hate is given up, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an4-200-8-2

This is called a mendicant who doesn’t draw close or push back or fume or ignite or burn up. ^an4-200-8-3

And how does a mendicant draw close? ^an4-200-9-1

It’s when a mendicant regards form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^an4-200-9-2

They regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling. ^an4-200-9-3

They regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception. ^an4-200-9-4

They regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices. ^an4-200-9-5

They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^an4-200-9-6

That’s how a mendicant draws close. ^an4-200-9-7

And how does a mendicant not draw close? ^an4-200-10-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form. ^an4-200-10-2

They don’t regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling. ^an4-200-10-3

They don’t regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception. ^an4-200-10-4

They don’t regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices. ^an4-200-10-5

They don’t regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness. ^an4-200-10-6

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t draw close. ^an4-200-10-7

And how does a mendicant push back? ^an4-200-11-1

It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with a mendicant, and the mendicant abuses, annoys, or argues back at them. ^an4-200-11-2

That’s how a mendicant pushes back. ^an4-200-11-3

And how does a mendicant not push back? ^an4-200-12-1

It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with a mendicant, and the mendicant doesn’t abuse, annoy, or argue back at them. ^an4-200-12-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t push back. ^an4-200-12-3

And how does a mendicant fume? ^an4-200-13-1

When there is the concept ‘I am’, there are the concepts ‘I am such’, ‘I am thus’, ‘I am otherwise’; ‘I am fleeting’, ‘I am lasting’; ‘mine’, ‘such is mine’, ‘thus is mine’, ‘otherwise is mine’; ‘also mine’, ‘such is also mine’, ‘thus is also mine’, ‘otherwise is also mine’; ‘I will be’, ‘I will be such’, ‘I will be thus’, ‘I will be otherwise’. ^an4-200-13-2

That’s how a mendicant fumes. ^an4-200-13-3

And how does a mendicant not fume? ^an4-200-14-1

When there is no concept ‘I am’, there are no concepts ‘I am such’, ‘I am thus’, ‘I am otherwise’; ‘I am fleeting’, ‘I am lasting’; ‘mine’, ‘such is mine’, ‘thus is mine’, ‘otherwise is mine’; ‘also mine’, ‘such is also mine’, ‘thus is also mine’, ‘otherwise is also mine’; ‘I will be’, ‘I will be such’, ‘I will be thus’, ‘I will be otherwise’. ^an4-200-14-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t fume. ^an4-200-14-3

And how is a mendicant ignited? ^an4-200-15-1

When there is the concept ‘I am because of this’, there are the concepts ‘I am such because of this’, ‘I am thus because of this’, ‘I am otherwise because of this’; ‘I am fleeting because of this’, ‘I am lasting because of this’; ‘mine because of this’, ‘such is mine because of this’, ‘thus is mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is mine because of this’; ‘also mine because of this’, ‘such is also mine because of this’, ‘thus is also mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is also mine because of this’; ‘I will be because of this’, ‘I will be such because of this’, ‘I will be thus because of this’, ‘I will be otherwise because of this’. ^an4-200-15-2

That’s how a mendicant is ignited. ^an4-200-15-3

And how is a mendicant not ignited? ^an4-200-16-1

When there is no concept ‘I am because of this’, there are no concepts ‘I am such because of this’, ‘I am thus because of this’, ‘I am otherwise because of this’; ‘I am fleeting because of this’, ‘I am lasting because of this’; ‘mine because of this’, ‘such is mine because of this’, ‘thus is mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is mine because of this’; ‘also mine because of this’, ‘such is also mine because of this’, ‘thus is also mine because of this’, ‘otherwise is also mine because of this’; ‘I will be because of this’, ‘I will be such because of this’, ‘I will be thus because of this’, ‘I will be otherwise because of this’. ^an4-200-16-2

That’s how a mendicant is not ignited. ^an4-200-16-3

And how does a mendicant burn up? ^an4-200-17-1

It’s when a mendicant hasn’t given up the conceit ‘I am’, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an4-200-17-2

That’s how a mendicant is burned up. ^an4-200-17-3

And how does a mendicant not burn up? ^an4-200-18-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up the conceit ‘I am’, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an4-200-18-2

That’s how a mendicant is not burned up.” ^an4-200-18-3

### Training Rules (Numbered Discourses 4.201) ^an4-201-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-201-1-1

a true person and an even truer person. ^an4-201-1-2

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an4-201-1-3

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an4-201-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an4-201-1-5

“And what is an untrue person? ^an4-201-2-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an4-201-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-201-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-201-3-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-201-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-201-3-7

And what is a true person? ^an4-201-4-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an4-201-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-201-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-201-5-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. And they encourage others to refrain from these things. ^an4-201-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-201-5-7

### Faithless (Numbered Discourses 4.202) ^an4-202-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-202-1-1

a true person and an even truer person. ^an4-202-1-2

And what is an untrue person? ^an4-202-2-1

It’s someone who is faithless, shameless, imprudent, with little learning, lazy, unmindful, and witless. ^an4-202-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-202-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-202-3-1

It’s someone who is faithless, shameless, imprudent, with little learning, lazy, unmindful, and witless. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-202-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-202-3-9

And what is a true person? ^an4-202-4-1

It’s someone who is faithful, conscientious, prudent, learned, energetic, mindful, and wise. ^an4-202-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-202-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-202-5-1

It’s someone who is personally accomplished in faith, conscience, prudence, learning, energy, mindfulness, and wisdom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-202-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-202-5-9

### Seven Kinds of Deeds (Numbered Discourses 4.203) ^an4-203-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-203-1-1

a true person and a truer person. ^an4-203-1-2

And what is an untrue person? ^an4-203-2-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, and uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-203-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-203-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-203-3-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, and uses speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-203-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-203-3-9

And what is a true person? ^an4-203-4-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-203-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-203-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-203-5-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they encourage others to refrain from these things. ^an4-203-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-203-5-9

### Ten Kinds of Deeds (Numbered Discourses 4.204) ^an4-204-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-204-1-1

a true person and an even truer person. ^an4-204-1-2

And what is an untrue person? ^an4-204-2-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. ^an4-204-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-204-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-204-3-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-204-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-204-3-4

And what is a true person? ^an4-204-4-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. ^an4-204-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-204-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-204-5-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-204-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-204-5-5

### Eightfold (Numbered Discourses 4.205) ^an4-205-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-205-1-1

a true person and an even truer person. ^an4-205-1-2

And what is an untrue person? ^an4-205-2-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong immersion. ^an4-205-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-205-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-205-3-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong immersion. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-205-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-205-3-10

And what is a true person? ^an4-205-4-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an4-205-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-205-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-205-5-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-205-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-205-5-10

### The Path with Ten Factors (Numbered Discourses 4.206) ^an4-206-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an untrue person and an even more untrue person, ^an4-206-1-1

a true person and an even truer person. ^an4-206-1-2

And what is an untrue person? ^an4-206-2-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. ^an4-206-2-2

This is called an untrue person. ^an4-206-2-3

And what is an even more untrue person? ^an4-206-3-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-206-3-2

This is called an even more untrue person. ^an4-206-3-4

And what is a true person? ^an4-206-4-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an4-206-4-2

This is called a true person. ^an4-206-4-3

And what is an even truer person? ^an4-206-5-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-206-5-2

This is called an even truer person.” ^an4-206-5-4

### Bad Character (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.207) ^an4-207-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you who’s bad and who’s worse, ^an4-207-1-1

who’s good and who’s better. ^an4-207-1-2

And who’s bad? ^an4-207-2-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. ^an4-207-2-2

This is called bad. ^an4-207-2-3

And who’s worse? ^an4-207-3-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-207-3-2

This is called worse. ^an4-207-3-3

And who’s good? ^an4-207-4-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. ^an4-207-4-2

This is called good. ^an4-207-4-3

And who’s better? ^an4-207-5-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-207-5-2

This is called better.” ^an4-207-5-3

### Bad Character (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.208) ^an4-208-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you who’s bad and who’s worse, ^an4-208-1-1

who’s good and who’s better. ^an4-208-1-2

And who’s bad? ^an4-208-2-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. ^an4-208-2-2

This is called bad. ^an4-208-2-3

And who’s worse? ^an4-208-3-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-208-3-2

This is called worse. ^an4-208-3-4

And who’s good? ^an4-208-4-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an4-208-4-2

This is called good. ^an4-208-4-3

And who’s better? ^an4-208-5-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-208-5-2

This is called better.” ^an4-208-5-4

### Bad Character (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 4.209) ^an4-209-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you bad character and worse character, ^an4-209-1-1

good character and better character. ^an4-209-1-2

And who has bad character? ^an4-209-2-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. ^an4-209-2-2

This is called bad character. ^an4-209-2-3

And who has worse character? ^an4-209-3-1

It’s someone who kills living creatures, steals, and commits sexual misconduct. They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re covetous, malicious, with wrong view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-209-3-2

This is called worse character. ^an4-209-3-3

And who has good character? ^an4-209-4-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. ^an4-209-4-2

This is called good character. ^an4-209-4-3

And who has better character? ^an4-209-5-1

It’s someone who doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. They’re contented, kind-hearted, with right view. And they encourage others to do these things. ^an4-209-5-2

This is called better character.” ^an4-209-5-3

### Bad Character (4th) (Numbered Discourses 4.210) ^an4-210-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you bad character and worse character, ^an4-210-1-1

good character and better character. ^an4-210-1-2

And who has bad character? ^an4-210-2-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. ^an4-210-2-2

This is called bad character. ^an4-210-2-3

And who has worse character? ^an4-210-3-1

It’s someone who has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong immersion, wrong knowledge, and wrong freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-210-3-2

This is called worse character. ^an4-210-3-4

And who has good character? ^an4-210-4-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an4-210-4-2

This is called good character. ^an4-210-4-3

And who has better character? ^an4-210-5-1

It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. And they encourage others in these same qualities. ^an4-210-5-2

This is called better character.” ^an4-210-5-4

### Assembly (Numbered Discourses 4.211) ^an4-211-0

“Mendicants, these four corrupt an assembly. ^an4-211-1-1

What four? ^an4-211-1-2

A monk, nun, layman, or laywoman who is unethical, of bad character. ^an4-211-1-3

These are the four that corrupt an assembly. ^an4-211-1-7

Mendicants, these four grace an assembly. ^an4-211-2-1

What four? ^an4-211-2-2

A monk, nun, layman, or laywoman who is ethical, of good character. ^an4-211-2-3

These are the four that grace an assembly.” ^an4-211-2-7

### View (Numbered Discourses 4.212) ^an4-212-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-212-1-1

What four? ^an4-212-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and wrong view. ^an4-212-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-212-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-212-2-1

What four? ^an4-212-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and right view. ^an4-212-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-212-2-4

### Ungrateful (Numbered Discourses 4.213) ^an4-213-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-213-1-1

What four? ^an4-213-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and being ungrateful and thankless. ^an4-213-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-213-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-213-2-1

What four? ^an4-213-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and being grateful and thankful. ^an4-213-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-213-2-4

### Killing Living Creatures (Numbered Discourses 4.214) ^an4-214-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. … Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or lie. …” ^an4-214-1-1

### Path (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.215) ^an4-215-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action. … Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … right view, right purpose, right speech, right action. …” ^an4-215-1-1

### Path (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.216) ^an4-216-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong immersion. Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. …” ^an4-216-1-1

### Kinds of Expression (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.217) ^an4-217-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … They say they’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, but they haven’t. … Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … They say they haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and they haven’t. …” ^an4-217-1-1

### Kinds of Expression (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.218) ^an4-218-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … They say they haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, but they have. … Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … They say they’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, and they have. …” ^an4-218-1-1

### Lack of Conscience (Numbered Discourses 4.219) ^an4-219-0

“Someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. … They’re faithless, unethical, shameless, and imprudent. … Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … They’re faithful, ethical, conscientious, and prudent. …” ^an4-219-1-1

### Unethical (Numbered Discourses 4.220) ^an4-220-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-220-1-1

What four? ^an4-220-1-2

They’re faithless, unethical, lazy, and witless. ^an4-220-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-220-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-220-2-1

What four? ^an4-220-2-2

They’re faithful, ethical, energetic, and wise. ^an4-220-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-220-2-4

### Verbal Conduct (Numbered Discourses 4.221) ^an4-221-0

“Mendicants, there are these four kinds of bad conduct by way of speech. ^an4-221-1-1

What four? ^an4-221-1-2

Speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. ^an4-221-1-3

These are the four kinds of bad conduct by way of speech. ^an4-221-1-4

There are these four kinds of good conduct by way of speech. ^an4-221-1-5

What four? ^an4-221-1-6

Speech that’s true, harmonious, gentle, and thoughtful. ^an4-221-1-7

These are the four kinds of good conduct by way of speech.” ^an4-221-1-8

### View (Numbered Discourses 4.222) ^an4-222-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-222-1-1

What four? ^an4-222-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and wrong view. ^an4-222-1-3

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness. ^an4-222-1-4

When an astute, competent true person has four qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-222-2-1

What four? ^an4-222-2-2

Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and right view. ^an4-222-2-3

When an astute, competent true person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an4-222-2-4

### Ungrateful (Numbered Discourses 4.223) ^an4-223-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-223-1-1

What four? ^an4-223-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and being ungrateful and thankless. ^an4-223-1-3

An astute person … brims with much merit. … Good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and being grateful and thankful. …” ^an4-223-1-4

### Killing Living Creatures (Numbered Discourses 4.224) ^an4-224-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or lie. …” ^an4-224-1-1

### Path (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.225) ^an4-225-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … right view, right purpose, right speech, right action. …” ^an4-225-1-1

### Path (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.226) ^an4-226-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong immersion. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. …” ^an4-226-1-1

### Kinds of Expression (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.227) ^an4-227-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … They say they’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, but they haven’t. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … They say they haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and they haven’t. …” ^an4-227-1-1

### Kinds of Expression (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.228) ^an4-228-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … They say they haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, but they have. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … They say they’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, and they have. …” ^an4-228-1-1

### Lack of Conscience (Numbered Discourses 4.229) ^an4-229-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … They’re faithless, unethical, shameless, and imprudent. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … They’re faithful, ethical, conscientious, and prudent. …” ^an4-229-1-1

### Witless (Numbered Discourses 4.230) ^an4-230-0

“A foolish person … brims with much wickedness. … They’re faithless, unethical, lazy, and witless. … An astute person … brims with much merit. … They’re faithful, ethical, energetic, and wise. ^an4-230-1-1

When an astute, competent true person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an4-230-1-2

### Poets (Numbered Discourses 4.231) ^an4-231-0

“Mendicants, there are these four poets. ^an4-231-1-1

What four? ^an4-231-1-2

A poet who thoughtfully composes their own work, a poet who repeats the oral transmission, a poet who educates, and a poet who improvises. ^an4-231-1-3

These are the four poets.” ^an4-231-1-4

### Deeds In Brief (Numbered Discourses 4.232) ^an4-232-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-232-1-1

What four? ^an4-232-1-2

There are dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-232-1-3

There are bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-232-1-4

There are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-232-1-5

There are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-232-1-6

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-232-1-7

### Deeds in Detail (Numbered Discourses 4.233) ^an4-233-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-233-1-1

What four? ^an4-233-1-2

There are dark deeds with dark results; ^an4-233-1-3

bright deeds with bright results; ^an4-233-1-4

dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results; and ^an4-233-1-5

neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-233-1-6

And what are dark deeds with dark results? ^an4-233-2-1

It’s when someone makes hurtful choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-233-2-2

Having made these choices, they’re reborn in a hurtful world, ^an4-233-2-3

where hurtful contacts strike them. ^an4-233-2-4

Touched by hurtful contacts, they experience hurtful feelings that are exclusively painful—like the beings in hell. ^an4-233-2-5

These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-233-2-6

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-233-3-1

It’s when someone makes pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-233-3-2

Having made these choices, they’re reborn in a pleasing world, ^an4-233-3-3

where pleasing contacts strike them. ^an4-233-3-4

Touched by pleasing contacts, they experience pleasing feelings of perfect happiness—like the gods of universal beauty. ^an4-233-3-5

These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-233-3-6

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-233-4-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an4-233-4-2

Having made these choices, they are reborn in a world that is both hurtful and pleasing, ^an4-233-4-3

where hurtful and pleasing contacts strike them. ^an4-233-4-4

Touched by both hurtful and pleasing contacts, they experience both hurtful and pleasing feelings that are a mixture of pleasure and pain—like humans, some gods, and some beings in the underworld. ^an4-233-4-5

These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-233-4-6

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-233-5-1

It’s the intention to give up dark deeds with dark results, bright deeds with bright results, and both dark and bright deeds with both dark and bright results. ^an4-233-5-2

These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-233-5-3

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-233-5-4

### About Soṇakāyana (Numbered Discourses 4.234) ^an4-234-0

Then Sikhāmoggallāna the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an4-234-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Sikhāmoggallāna sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an4-234-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, a few days ago the student Soṇakāyana came to me and said: ^an4-234-2-1

‘The ascetic Gotama advocates not doing any deeds. So he teaches the annihilation of the world!’ ^an4-234-2-2

The world exists through deeds, and it remains because deeds are undertaken.” ^an4-234-2-3

“Brahmin, I can’t recall even seeing the student Soṇakāyana, ^an4-234-3-1

so how could we possibly have had such a discussion? ^an4-234-3-2

I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-234-3-3

What four? ^an4-234-3-4

There are dark deeds with dark results; ^an4-234-3-5

bright deeds with bright results; ^an4-234-3-6

dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results; and ^an4-234-3-7

neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-234-3-8

And what are the dark deeds with dark results? ^an4-234-4-1

It’s when someone makes hurtful choices by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an4-234-4-2

Touched by hurtful contacts, they experience hurtful feelings that are exclusively painful—like the beings in hell. ^an4-234-4-5

These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-234-4-6

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-234-5-1

It’s when someone makes pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an4-234-5-2

Touched by pleasing contacts, they experience pleasing feelings of perfect happiness—like the gods of universal beauty. ^an4-234-5-5

These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-234-5-6

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-234-6-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. … ^an4-234-6-2

Touched by both hurtful and pleasing contacts, they experience both hurtful and pleasing feelings that are a mixture of pleasure and pain—like humans, some gods, and some beings in the underworld. ^an4-234-6-5

These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-234-6-6

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-234-7-1

It’s the intention to give up dark deeds with dark results, bright deeds with bright results, and both dark and bright deeds with both dark and bright results. ^an4-234-7-2

These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-234-7-3

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-234-7-4

### Training Rules (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.235) ^an4-235-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-235-1-1

What four? ^an4-235-1-2

There are dark deeds with dark results; ^an4-235-1-3

bright deeds with bright results; ^an4-235-1-4

dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results; and ^an4-235-1-5

neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-235-1-6

And what are the dark deeds with dark results? ^an4-235-1-7

It’s when someone kills living creatures, steals, commits sexual misconduct, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an4-235-1-8

These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-235-1-9

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-235-2-1

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an4-235-2-2

These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-235-2-3

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-235-3-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-235-3-2

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-235-4-1

It’s the intention to give up dark deeds with dark results, bright deeds with bright results, and both dark and bright deeds with both dark and bright results. These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-235-4-2

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-235-4-3

### Training Rules (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.236) ^an4-236-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-236-1-1

What four? ^an4-236-1-2

There are dark deeds with dark results; ^an4-236-1-3

bright deeds with bright results; ^an4-236-1-4

dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results; and ^an4-236-1-5

neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-236-1-6

And what are dark deeds with dark results? ^an4-236-2-1

It’s when someone murders their mother or father or a perfected one. They maliciously shed the blood of a Realized One. Or they cause a schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-236-2-2

These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-236-2-3

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-236-3-1

It’s when someone doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, or commit sexual misconduct. They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical. And they’re content, kind-hearted, with right view. ^an4-236-3-2

These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-236-3-3

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-236-4-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-236-4-2

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-236-5-1

It’s the intention to give up dark deeds with dark results, bright deeds with bright results, and both dark and bright deeds with both dark and bright results. These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-236-5-2

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-236-5-3

### The Noble Path (Numbered Discourses 4.237) ^an4-237-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight. ^an4-237-1-1

What four? ^an4-237-1-2

There are dark deeds with dark results; ^an4-237-1-3

bright deeds with bright results; ^an4-237-1-4

dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results; and ^an4-237-1-5

neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-237-1-6

And what are dark deeds with dark results? ^an4-237-2-1

It’s when someone makes hurtful choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-237-2-2

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-237-3-1

It’s when someone makes pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-237-3-2

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-237-4-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-237-4-2

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-237-5-1

Right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an4-237-5-2

These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-237-5-3

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-237-5-4

### Awakening Factors (Numbered Discourses 4.238) ^an4-238-0

“Mendicants, I declare these four kinds of deeds, having realized them with my own insight…. ^an4-238-1-1

And what are dark deeds with dark results? It’s when someone makes hurtful choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark deeds with dark results. ^an4-238-1-2

And what are bright deeds with bright results? ^an4-238-2-1

It’s when someone makes pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called bright deeds with bright results. ^an4-238-2-2

And what are dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results? ^an4-238-3-1

It’s when someone makes both hurtful and pleasing choices by way of body, speech, and mind. These are called dark and bright deeds with dark and bright results. ^an4-238-3-2

And what are neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds? ^an4-238-4-1

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^an4-238-4-2

These are called neither dark nor bright deeds with neither dark nor bright results, which lead to the ending of deeds. ^an4-238-4-3

These are the four kinds of deeds that I declare, having realized them with my own insight.” ^an4-238-4-4

### Blameworthy (Numbered Discourses 4.239) ^an4-239-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-239-1-1

What four? ^an4-239-1-2

Blameworthy deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and blameworthy view. ^an4-239-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-239-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-239-2-1

What four? ^an4-239-2-2

Blameless deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and blameless view. ^an4-239-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-239-2-4

### Pleasing (Numbered Discourses 4.240) ^an4-240-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-240-1-1

What four? ^an4-240-1-2

Hurtful deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and hurtful view. ^an4-240-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-240-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-240-2-1

What four? ^an4-240-2-2

Pleasing deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and pleasing view. ^an4-240-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-240-2-4

### Ascetics (Numbered Discourses 4.241) ^an4-241-0

“‘Only here is there a first ascetic, here a second ascetic, here a third ascetic, and here a fourth ascetic. ^an4-241-1-1

Other sects are empty of ascetics.’ ^an4-241-1-2

This, mendicants, is how you should rightly roar your lion’s roar. ^an4-241-1-3

And who is the first ascetic? ^an4-241-2-1

It’s a mendicant who—with the ending of three fetters—is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^an4-241-2-2

This is the first ascetic. ^an4-241-2-3

And who is the second ascetic? ^an4-241-3-1

It’s a mendicant who—with the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion—is a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an4-241-3-2

This is the second ascetic. ^an4-241-3-3

And who is the third ascetic? ^an4-241-4-1

It’s a mendicant who—with the ending of the five lower fetters—is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an4-241-4-2

This is the third ascetic. ^an4-241-4-3

And who is the fourth ascetic? ^an4-241-5-1

It’s a mendicant who realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-241-5-2

This is the fourth ascetic. ^an4-241-5-3

‘Only here is there a first ascetic, here a second ascetic, here a third ascetic, and here a fourth ascetic. ^an4-241-6-1

Other sects are empty of ascetics.’ ^an4-241-6-2

This, mendicants, is how you should rightly roar your lion’s roar.” ^an4-241-6-3

### Benefits of Relying on a True Person (Numbered Discourses 4.242) ^an4-242-0

“Mendicants, you can expect four benefits from relying on a true person. ^an4-242-1-1

What four? ^an4-242-1-2

Growth in noble ethics, immersion, wisdom, and freedom. ^an4-242-1-3

You can expect these four benefits from relying on a true person.” ^an4-242-1-4

### Schism in the Saṅgha (Numbered Discourses 4.243) ^an4-243-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an4-243-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an4-243-1-2

“Well, Ānanda, has that disciplinary issue been settled yet?” ^an4-243-1-3

“How could it be, sir? ^an4-243-1-4

Venerable Anuruddha’s protégé Bāhiya remains entirely committed to creating a schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-1-5

But Anuruddha doesn’t think to say a single word about it.” ^an4-243-1-6

“But Ānanda, since when has Anuruddha been involved in disciplinary issues in the midst of the Saṅgha? ^an4-243-2-1

Shouldn’t you, together with Sāriputta and Moggallāna, settle all disciplinary issues that come up? ^an4-243-2-2

A bad monk sees four reasons to relish schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-3-1

What four? ^an4-243-3-2

Take an unethical monk, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved. ^an4-243-3-3

He thinks: ^an4-243-3-4

‘Suppose the monks know that I’m a bad monk … ^an4-243-3-5

If they’re in harmony, they’ll expel me, ^an4-243-3-6

but if they’re divided they won’t.’ ^an4-243-3-7

A bad monk sees this as the first reason to relish schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-3-8

Furthermore, a bad monk has wrong view, he’s attached to an extremist view. ^an4-243-4-1

He thinks: ^an4-243-4-2

‘Suppose the monks know that I have wrong view … ^an4-243-4-3

If they’re in harmony they’ll expel me, ^an4-243-4-4

but if they’re divided they won’t.’ ^an4-243-4-5

A bad monk sees this as the second reason to relish schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-4-6

Furthermore, a bad monk has wrong livelihood and earns a living by wrong livelihood. ^an4-243-5-1

He thinks: ^an4-243-5-2

‘Suppose the monks know that I have wrong livelihood … ^an4-243-5-3

If they’re in harmony they’ll expel me, ^an4-243-5-4

but if they’re divided they won’t.’ ^an4-243-5-5

A bad monk sees this as the third reason to relish schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-5-6

Furthermore, a bad monk desires material things, honor, and admiration. ^an4-243-6-1

He thinks: ^an4-243-6-2

‘Suppose the monks know that I desire material things, honor, and admiration. ^an4-243-6-3

If they’re in harmony they won’t honor, respect, revere, or venerate me, ^an4-243-6-4

but if they’re divided they will.’ ^an4-243-6-5

A bad monk sees this as the fourth reason to relish schism in the Saṅgha. ^an4-243-6-6

A bad monk sees these four reasons to relish schism in the Saṅgha.” ^an4-243-6-7

### Perils of Offenses (Numbered Discourses 4.244) ^an4-244-0

“Mendicants, there are these four perils of offenses. ^an4-244-1-1

What four? ^an4-244-1-2

Suppose they were to arrest a bandit, a criminal and present him to the king, saying: ^an4-244-1-3

‘Your Majesty, this is a bandit, a criminal. ^an4-244-1-4

May Your Majesty punish them!’ ^an4-244-1-5

The king would say: ^an4-244-1-6

‘Go, my men, and tie this man’s arms tightly behind his back with a strong rope. Shave his head and march him from street to street and square to square to the beating of a harsh drum. Then take him out the south gate and there, to the south of the city, chop off his head.’ ^an4-244-1-7

The king’s men would do as they were told. ^an4-244-1-8

Then a bystander might think: ^an4-244-1-9

‘This man must have done a truly bad and reprehensible deed, a capital offense. ^an4-244-1-10

There’s no way I’d ever do such a bad and reprehensible deed, a capital offense.’ ^an4-244-1-12

In the same way, take any monk or nun who has set up such an acute perception of peril regarding expulsion offenses. ^an4-244-1-13

It can be expected that if they haven’t committed an expulsion offense they won’t, and if they committed one they will deal with it properly. ^an4-244-1-14

Suppose a man was to put on a black cloth, mess up his hair, and put a club on his shoulder. Then he approaches a large crowd and says: ^an4-244-2-1

‘Sirs, I’ve done a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of clubbing. I submit to your pleasure.’ ^an4-244-2-2

Then a bystander might think: ^an4-244-2-3

‘This man must have done a truly bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of clubbing. … ^an4-244-2-4

There’s no way I’d ever do such a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of clubbing.’ ^an4-244-2-7

In the same way, take any monk or nun who has set up such an acute perception of peril regarding suspension offenses. It can be expected that if they haven’t committed a suspension offense they won’t, and if they committed one they will deal with it properly. ^an4-244-2-8

Suppose a man was to put on a black cloth, mess up his hair, and put a sack of ashes on his shoulder. Then he approaches a large crowd and says: ^an4-244-3-1

‘Sirs, I’ve done a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of a sack of ashes. ^an4-244-3-2

I submit to your pleasure.’ ^an4-244-3-3

Then a bystander might think: ^an4-244-3-4

‘This man must have done a truly bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of a sack of ashes. … ^an4-244-3-5

There’s no way I’d ever do such a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of a sack of ashes.’ ^an4-244-3-9

In the same way, take any monk or nun who has set up such an acute perception of peril regarding confessable offenses. It can be expected that if they haven’t committed a confessable offense they won’t, and if they committed one they will deal with it properly. ^an4-244-3-10

Suppose a man was to put on a black cloth and mess up his hair. Then he approaches a large crowd and says: ^an4-244-4-1

‘Sirs, I’ve done a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of criticism. ^an4-244-4-2

I submit to your pleasure.’ ^an4-244-4-3

Then a bystander might think: ^an4-244-4-4

‘This man must have done a truly bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of criticism. … ^an4-244-4-5

There’s no way I’d ever do such a bad and reprehensible deed, deserving of criticism.’ ^an4-244-4-9

In the same way, take any monk or nun who has set up such an acute perception of peril regarding confessable offenses. It can be expected that if they haven’t committed an confessable offense they won’t, and if they committed one they will deal with it properly. ^an4-244-4-10

These are the four perils of offenses.” ^an4-244-4-11

### The Benefits of Training (Numbered Discourses 4.245) ^an4-245-0

“Mendicants, this spiritual life is lived with training as its benefit, with wisdom as its overseer, with freedom as its core, and with mindfulness as its ruler. ^an4-245-1-1

And how is training its benefit? ^an4-245-1-2

Firstly, I laid down for my disciples the training that deals with supplementary regulations in order to inspire confidence in those without it and to increase confidence in those who have it. ^an4-245-1-3

They undertake whatever supplementary regulations I have laid down, keeping them intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred. ^an4-245-1-4

Furthermore, I laid down for my disciples the training that deals with the fundamentals of the spiritual life in order to rightly end suffering in every way. ^an4-245-2-1

They undertake whatever training that deals with the fundamentals of the spiritual life I have laid down, keeping it intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred. ^an4-245-2-2

That’s how training is its benefit. ^an4-245-2-3

And how is wisdom its overseer? ^an4-245-3-1

I taught the Dhamma to my disciples in order to rightly end suffering in every way. ^an4-245-3-2

They examine with wisdom any teachings I taught them. ^an4-245-3-3

That’s how wisdom is its overseer. ^an4-245-3-4

And how is freedom its core? ^an4-245-4-1

I taught the Dhamma to my disciples in order to rightly end suffering in every way. ^an4-245-4-2

They experience through freedom any teachings I taught them. ^an4-245-4-3

That’s how freedom is its core. ^an4-245-4-4

And how is mindfulness its ruler? ^an4-245-5-1

Mindfulness is well established in oneself: ‘In this way I’ll fulfill the training dealing with supplementary regulations, or support with wisdom in every situation the training dealing with supplementary regulations I’ve already fulfilled.’ ^an4-245-5-2

Mindfulness is well established in oneself: ‘In this way I’ll fulfill the training dealing with the fundamentals of the spiritual life, or support with wisdom in every situation the training dealing with the fundamentals of the spiritual life I’ve already fulfilled.’ ^an4-245-5-3

Mindfulness is well established in oneself: ‘In this way I’ll examine with wisdom the teaching that I haven’t yet examined, or support with wisdom in every situation the teaching I’ve already examined.’ ^an4-245-5-4

Mindfulness is well established in oneself: ‘In this way I’ll experience through freedom the teaching that I haven’t yet experienced, or support with wisdom in every situation the teaching I’ve already experienced.’ ^an4-245-5-5

That’s how mindfulness is its ruler. ^an4-245-5-6

‘This spiritual life is lived with training as its benefit, with wisdom as its overseer, with freedom as its core, and with mindfulness as its ruler.’ ^an4-245-5-7

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an4-245-5-8

### Lying Postures (Numbered Discourses 4.246) ^an4-246-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of lying down. ^an4-246-1-1

What four? ^an4-246-1-2

The ways a corpse, a pleasure seeker, a lion, and a Realized One lie down. ^an4-246-1-3

And how does a corpse lie down? ^an4-246-1-4

Corpses usually lie flat on their backs. ^an4-246-1-5

This is called the way a corpse lies down. ^an4-246-1-6

And how does a pleasure seeker lie down? ^an4-246-2-1

Pleasure seekers usually lie down on their left side. ^an4-246-2-2

This is called the way a pleasure seeker lies down. ^an4-246-2-3

And how does a lion lie down? ^an4-246-3-1

The lion, king of beasts, lies down on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other, with his tail tucked between his thighs. ^an4-246-3-2

When he wakes, he lifts his front quarters and checks his hind quarters. ^an4-246-3-3

If he sees that any part of his body is disordered or displaced, he is displeased. ^an4-246-3-4

But if he sees that no part of his body is disordered or displaced, he is pleased. ^an4-246-3-5

This is called the way a lion lies down. ^an4-246-3-6

And how does a Realized One lie down? ^an4-246-4-1

It’s when a Realized One, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an4-246-4-2

This is called the way a Realized One lies down. ^an4-246-4-3

These are the four ways of lying down.” ^an4-246-4-4

### Worthy of a Monument (Numbered Discourses 4.247) ^an4-247-0

“Mendicants, these four are worthy of a monument. ^an4-247-1-1

What four? ^an4-247-1-2

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha; an Independent Buddha; a disciple of a Realized One; and a wheel-turning monarch. ^an4-247-1-3

These four are worthy of a monument.” ^an4-247-1-4

### The Growth of Wisdom (Numbered Discourses 4.248) ^an4-248-0

“Mendicants, these four things lead to the growth of wisdom. ^an4-248-1-1

What four? ^an4-248-1-2

Associating with true persons, listening to the true teaching, rational application of mind, and practicing in line with the teaching. ^an4-248-1-3

These four things lead to the growth of wisdom.” ^an4-248-1-4

### Very Helpful (Numbered Discourses 4.249) ^an4-249-0

“Mendicants, these four things are very helpful to a human being. ^an4-249-1-1

What four? ^an4-249-1-2

Associating with true persons, listening to the true teaching, rational application of mind, and practicing in line with the teaching. ^an4-249-1-3

These four things are very helpful to a human being.” ^an4-249-1-4

### Expressions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.250) ^an4-250-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ignoble expressions. ^an4-250-1-1

What four? ^an4-250-1-2

Saying you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, but you haven’t. ^an4-250-1-3

These are the four ignoble expressions.” ^an4-250-1-4

### Expressions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.251) ^an4-251-0

“Mendicants, there are these four noble expressions. ^an4-251-1-1

What four? ^an4-251-1-2

Saying you haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you haven’t. ^an4-251-1-3

These are the four noble expressions.” ^an4-251-1-4

### Expressions (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 4.252) ^an4-252-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ignoble expressions. ^an4-252-1-1

What four? ^an4-252-1-2

Saying you haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you have. ^an4-252-1-3

These are the four ignoble expressions.” ^an4-252-1-4

### Expressions (4th) (Numbered Discourses 4.253) ^an4-253-0
