“Mendicants, there are these four noble expressions. ^an4-253-1-1

What four? ^an4-253-1-2

Saying you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you have. ^an4-253-1-3

These are the four noble expressions.” ^an4-253-1-4

### Insight (Numbered Discourses 4.254) ^an4-254-0

“Mendicants, there are these four things. ^an4-254-1-1

What four? ^an4-254-1-2

There are things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-1-3

There are things that should be given up by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-1-4

There are things that should be developed by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-1-5

There are things that should be realized by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-1-6

And what are the things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge? ^an4-254-2-1

The five grasping aggregates. ^an4-254-2-2

These are called the things that should be completely understood by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-2-3

And what are the things that should be given up by direct knowledge? ^an4-254-3-1

Ignorance and craving for continued existence. ^an4-254-3-2

These are called the things that should be given up by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-3-3

And what are the things that should be developed by direct knowledge? ^an4-254-4-1

Serenity and discernment. ^an4-254-4-2

These are called the things that should be developed by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-4-3

And what are the things that should be realized by direct knowledge? ^an4-254-5-1

Knowledge and freedom. ^an4-254-5-2

These are called the things that should be realized by direct knowledge. ^an4-254-5-3

These are the four things.” ^an4-254-5-4

### Searches (Numbered Discourses 4.255) ^an4-255-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ignoble searches. ^an4-255-1-1

What four? ^an4-255-1-2

Someone liable to old age searches only for what grows old. ^an4-255-1-3

Someone liable to sickness searches only for what gets sick. ^an4-255-1-4

Someone liable to death searches only for what dies. ^an4-255-1-5

Someone liable to become corrupted searches only for what is defiled. ^an4-255-1-6

These are the four ignoble searches. ^an4-255-1-7

There are these four noble searches. ^an4-255-2-1

What four? ^an4-255-2-2

Someone who is liable to grow old, knowing the drawback in what grows old, searches for that which is free of old age, the supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment. ^an4-255-2-3

Someone who is liable to get sick, knowing the drawback in what gets sick, searches for that which is free of sickness, the supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment. ^an4-255-2-4

Someone who is liable to die, knowing the drawback in what dies, searches for that which is free of death, the supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment. ^an4-255-2-5

Someone liable to become corrupted, knowing the drawback in what is defiled, searches for the undefiled supreme sanctuary from the yoke, extinguishment. ^an4-255-2-6

These are the four noble searches.” ^an4-255-2-7

### Ways of Being Inclusive (Numbered Discourses 4.256) ^an4-256-0

“Mendicants, there are these four ways of being inclusive. ^an4-256-1-1

What four? ^an4-256-1-2

Giving, kindly words, taking care, and equality. ^an4-256-1-3

These are the four ways of being inclusive.” ^an4-256-1-4

### With Māluṅkyaputta (Numbered Discourses 4.257) ^an4-257-0

Then Venerable Māluṅkyaputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an4-257-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^an4-257-2-1

“Well now, Māluṅkyaputta, what are we to say to the young monks, ^an4-257-2-2

when even an old man like you, elderly and senior, asks the Realized One for brief advice?” ^an4-257-2-3

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief! May the Holy One teach me the Dhamma in brief! Hopefully I can understand the meaning of what the Buddha says! Hopefully I can be an heir of the Buddha’s teaching!” ^an4-257-2-4

“Māluṅkyaputta, there are four things that give rise to craving in a mendicant. ^an4-257-3-1

What four? ^an4-257-3-2

For the sake of robes, ^an4-257-3-3

almsfood, ^an4-257-3-4

lodgings, ^an4-257-3-5

or rebirth in this or that state. ^an4-257-3-6

These are the four things that give rise to craving in a mendicant. ^an4-257-3-7

That craving is given up by a mendicant, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. Then they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an4-257-3-8

When Māluṅkyaputta had been given this advice by the Buddha, he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an4-257-4-1

Then Māluṅkyaputta, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an4-257-4-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an4-257-4-3

And Venerable Māluṅkyaputta became one of the perfected. ^an4-257-4-4

### Families (Numbered Discourses 4.258) ^an4-258-0

“Mendicants, when families don’t stay wealthy for long, it’s always for one or more of these four reasons. ^an4-258-1-1

What four? ^an4-258-1-2

They don’t look for what’s lost; they don’t repair old things; they eat and drink too much; or they put an unethical woman or man in charge. ^an4-258-1-3

When families don’t stay wealthy for long, it’s always for one or more of these four reasons. ^an4-258-1-4

When families do stay wealthy for long, it’s always for one or more of these four reasons. ^an4-258-2-1

What four? ^an4-258-2-2

They look for what’s lost; they repair old things; they eat and drink in moderation; and they put an ethical woman or man in charge. ^an4-258-2-3

When families do stay wealthy for long, it’s always for one or more of these four reasons.” ^an4-258-2-4

### A Thoroughbred (1st) (Numbered Discourses 4.259) ^an4-259-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-259-1-1

What four? ^an4-259-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, fast, and well-proportioned. ^an4-259-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these four factors is worthy of a king. … ^an4-259-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-259-2-1

What four? ^an4-259-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, fast, and well proportioned. ^an4-259-2-3

And how is a mendicant beautiful? ^an4-259-3-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-259-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is beautiful. ^an4-259-3-3

And how is a mendicant strong? ^an4-259-4-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an4-259-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is strong. ^an4-259-4-3

And how is a mendicant fast? ^an4-259-5-1

It’s when they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an4-259-5-2

That’s how a mendicant is fast. ^an4-259-5-3

And how is a mendicant well proportioned? ^an4-259-6-1

It’s when a mendicant receives robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an4-259-6-2

That’s how a mendicant is well proportioned. ^an4-259-6-3

A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an4-259-7-1

### A Thoroughbred (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 4.260) ^an4-260-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an4-260-1-1

What four? ^an4-260-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, fast, and well-proportioned. ^an4-260-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these four factors is worthy of a king. … ^an4-260-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an4-260-2-1

What four? ^an4-260-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, fast, and well proportioned. ^an4-260-2-3

And how is a mendicant beautiful? ^an4-260-3-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an4-260-3-2

That’s how a mendicant is beautiful. ^an4-260-3-3

And how is a mendicant strong? ^an4-260-4-1

It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an4-260-4-2

That’s how a mendicant is strong. ^an4-260-4-3

And how is a mendicant fast? ^an4-260-5-1

It’s when a mendicant realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an4-260-5-2

That’s how a mendicant is fast. ^an4-260-5-3

And how is a mendicant well proportioned? ^an4-260-6-1

It’s when a mendicant receives robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an4-260-6-2

That’s how a mendicant is well proportioned. ^an4-260-6-3

A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an4-260-7-1

### Powers (Numbered Discourses 4.261) ^an4-261-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an4-261-1-1

What four? ^an4-261-1-2

The powers of energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an4-261-1-3

These are the four powers.” ^an4-261-1-4

### Wilderness (Numbered Discourses 4.262) ^an4-262-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has four qualities they’re not ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-262-1-1

What four? ^an4-262-1-2

They have sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts; or they’re witless, dull, and idiotic. ^an4-262-1-3

When a mendicant possesses these four qualities they’re not ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-262-1-4

When a mendicant has four qualities they’re ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an4-262-2-1

What four? ^an4-262-2-2

They have thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness; and they’re wise, bright, and clever. ^an4-262-2-3

When a mendicant possesses these four qualities they’re ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.” ^an4-262-2-4

### Deeds (Numbered Discourses 4.263) ^an4-263-0

“When a foolish, incompetent untrue person has four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an4-263-1-1

What four? ^an4-263-1-2

Blameworthy deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and blameworthy view. ^an4-263-1-3

When a foolish, incompetent untrue person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness. ^an4-263-1-4

When an astute, competent true person has four qualities they keep themselves intact and unscathed. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much merit. ^an4-263-2-1

What four? ^an4-263-2-2

Blameless deeds by way of body, speech, and mind, and blameless view. ^an4-263-2-3

When an astute, competent true person possesses these four qualities they keep themselves healthy and whole. They don’t deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much merit.” ^an4-263-2-4

### Killing Living Creatures (Numbered Discourses 4.264) ^an4-264-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-264-1-1

What four? ^an4-264-1-2

They themselves kill living creatures; they encourage others to kill living creatures; they approve of killing living creatures; and they praise killing living creatures. ^an4-264-1-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-264-1-4

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an4-264-2-1

What four? ^an4-264-2-2

They don’t themselves kill living creatures; they encourage others to not kill living creatures; they approve of not killing living creatures; and they praise not killing living creatures. ^an4-264-2-3

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-264-2-4

### Stealing (Numbered Discourses 4.265) ^an4-265-0

“Mendicants, someone with four qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an4-265-1-1

What four? ^an4-265-1-2

They themselves steal … ^an4-265-1-3

Someone with four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. … ^an4-265-1-4

They don’t themselves steal … ” ^an4-265-2-1

### Misconduct (Numbered Discourses 4.266) ^an4-266-0

“… They themselves commit sexual misconduct … ^an4-266-1-1

They don’t themselves commit sexual misconduct …” ^an4-266-2-1

### Lying (Numbered Discourses 4.267) ^an4-267-0

“… They themselves lie … ^an4-267-1-1

… They don’t themselves lie …” ^an4-267-2-1

### Backbiting (Numbered Discourses 4.268) ^an4-268-0

“… They themselves backbite … ^an4-268-1-1

… They don’t themselves backbite …” ^an4-268-2-1

### Harsh Speech (Numbered Discourses 4.269) ^an4-269-0

“… They themselves speak harshly … ^an4-269-1-1

… They don’t themselves speak harshly …” ^an4-269-2-1

### Talking Nonsense (Numbered Discourses 4.270) ^an4-270-0

“… They themselves talk nonsense … ^an4-270-1-1

… They don’t themselves talk nonsense …” ^an4-270-2-1

### Covetousness (Numbered Discourses 4.271) ^an4-271-0

“… They themselves are covetous … ^an4-271-1-1

… They themselves are content …” ^an4-271-2-1

### Ill Will (Numbered Discourses 4.272) ^an4-272-0

“… They themselves have ill will … ^an4-272-1-1

… They themselves have good will …” ^an4-272-2-1

### Wrong View (Numbered Discourses 4.273) ^an4-273-0

“… They themselves have wrong view … ^an4-273-1-1

They themselves have right view; they encourage others to have right view; they approve of right view; and they praise right view. ^an4-273-2-1

Someone with these four qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an4-273-2-2

### Mindfulness Meditation (Numbered Discourses 4.274) ^an4-274-0

“For insight into greed, four things should be developed. ^an4-274-1-1

What four? ^an4-274-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an4-274-1-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … mind … principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an4-274-1-4

For insight into greed, these four things should be developed.” ^an4-274-1-5

### Right Efforts (Numbered Discourses 4.275) ^an4-275-0

“For insight into greed, four things should be developed. ^an4-275-1-1

What four? ^an4-275-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. ^an4-275-1-3

…so that unskillful qualities that have arisen are given up … so that skillful qualities arise … so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are fulfilled by development. ^an4-275-1-4

For insight into greed, these four things should be developed.” ^an4-275-1-5

### Bases of Psychic Power (Numbered Discourses 4.276) ^an4-276-0

“For insight into greed, four things should be developed. ^an4-276-1-1

What four? ^an4-276-1-2

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^an4-276-1-3

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy … ^an4-276-1-4

mental development … ^an4-276-1-5

inquiry, and active effort. ^an4-276-1-6

For insight into greed, these four things should be developed.” ^an4-276-1-7

### Complete Understanding, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 4.277–303) ^an4-277-303-0

“For the complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go of greed, four things should be developed.” ^an4-277-303-1-1

### Insight into Hate, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 4.304–783) ^an4-304-783-0

“Of hate … delusion … anger … acrimony … disdain … contempt … jealousy … stinginess … deceit … deviousness … obstinacy … aggression … conceit … arrogance … vanity … negligence … for insight … complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go … four things should be developed.” ^an4-304-783-1-1

The Book of the Fours is finished. ^an4-304-783-1-5

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 5 ^an5-0

### In Brief (Numbered Discourses 5.1) ^an5-1-0

So I have heard. ^an5-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an5-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an5-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an5-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an5-1-1-6

“Mendicants, there are these five powers of a trainee. ^an5-1-2-1

What five? ^an5-1-2-2

The powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an5-1-2-3

These are the five powers of a trainee. ^an5-1-2-4

So you should train like this: ^an5-1-3-1

‘We will have the trainee’s powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.’ ^an5-1-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-1-3-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^an5-1-3-4

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an5-1-3-5

### In Detail (Numbered Discourses 5.2) ^an5-2-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers of a trainee. ^an5-2-1-1

What five? ^an5-2-1-2

The powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an5-2-1-3

And what is the power of faith? ^an5-2-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an5-2-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-2-2-3

This is called the power of faith. ^an5-2-2-4

And what is the power of conscience? ^an5-2-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has a conscience. They’re conscientious about bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and conscientious about having any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an5-2-3-2

This is called the power of conscience. ^an5-2-3-3

And what is the power of prudence? ^an5-2-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple is prudent. They’re prudent when it comes to bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and prudent when it comes to acquiring any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an5-2-4-2

This is called the power of prudence. ^an5-2-4-3

And what is the power of energy? ^an5-2-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-2-5-2

This is called the power of energy. ^an5-2-5-3

And what is the power of wisdom? ^an5-2-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-2-6-2

This is called the power of wisdom. ^an5-2-6-3

These are the five powers of a trainee. ^an5-2-6-4

So you should train like this: ^an5-2-7-1

‘We will have the trainee’s powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.’ ^an5-2-7-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-2-7-3

### Suffering (Numbered Discourses 5.3) ^an5-3-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has five qualities they live unhappily in this very life—with distress, anguish, and fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a bad rebirth. ^an5-3-1-1

What five? ^an5-3-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithless, shameless, imprudent, lazy, and witless. ^an5-3-1-3

When a mendicant possesses these five qualities they live unhappily in this very life—with distress, anguish, and fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a bad rebirth. ^an5-3-1-4

When a mendicant has five qualities they live happily in this very life—without distress, distress, or fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a good rebirth. ^an5-3-2-1

What five? ^an5-3-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, conscientious, prudent, energetic, and wise. ^an5-3-2-3

When a mendicant possesses these five qualities they live happily in this very life—without distress, anguish, or fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a good rebirth.” ^an5-3-2-4

### Cast Down (Numbered Discourses 5.4) ^an5-4-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-4-1-1

What five? ^an5-4-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithless, shameless, imprudent, lazy, and witless. ^an5-4-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-4-1-4

A mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-4-2-1

What five? ^an5-4-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, conscientious, prudent, energetic, and wise. ^an5-4-2-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-4-2-4

### Disrobing (Numbered Discourses 5.5) ^an5-5-0

“Mendicants, any monk or nun who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life deserves rebuttal and criticism on five legitimate grounds in this very life. ^an5-5-1-1

What five? ^an5-5-1-2

‘You had no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, or wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an5-5-1-3

Any monk or nun who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life deserves rebuttal and criticism on these five legitimate grounds in this very life. ^an5-5-1-4

Any monk or nun who lives the full and pure spiritual life in pain and sadness, weeping, with tearful face, deserves praise on five legitimate grounds in this very life. ^an5-5-2-1

What five? ^an5-5-2-2

‘You had faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an5-5-2-3

Any monk or nun who lives the full and pure spiritual life in pain and sadness, weeping, with tearful face, deserves praise on these five legitimate grounds in this very life.” ^an5-5-2-4

### Becoming (Numbered Discourses 5.6) ^an5-6-0

“Mendicants, you don’t become unskillful as long as faith is established in skillful qualities. ^an5-6-1-1

But when faith vanishes and faithlessness takes over, ^an5-6-1-2

you become unskillful. ^an5-6-1-3

You don’t become unskillful as long as conscience … prudence … energy … wisdom is established in skillful qualities. ^an5-6-2-1

But when wisdom vanishes and witlessness takes over, ^an5-6-5-2

you become unskillful.” ^an5-6-5-3

### Sensual Pleasures (Numbered Discourses 5.7) ^an5-7-0

“Mendicants, sentient beings are mostly charmed by sensual pleasures. ^an5-7-1-1

When a gentleman has abandoned the scythe and flail and gone forth from the lay life to homelessness, they’re qualified to be called ‘a faithful renunciate from a good family’. ^an5-7-1-2

Why is that? ^an5-7-1-3

Because a youth can get sensual pleasures of this kind or that. ^an5-7-1-4

Now, all sensual pleasures are just reckoned as ‘sensual pleasures’, regardless of whether they’re inferior, average, or superior. ^an5-7-1-5

Suppose there was a little baby boy who, because of his nursemaid’s negligence, puts a stick or stone in his mouth. ^an5-7-1-6

The nursemaid would very quickly notice ^an5-7-1-7

and try to take it out. ^an5-7-1-8

If that didn’t work, she’d cradle his head with her left hand, and take it out using a hooked finger of her right hand, even if it drew blood. ^an5-7-1-9

Why is that? ^an5-7-1-10

I admit she’d know, ‘This will distress the child, ^an5-7-1-11

there’s no denying.’ ^an5-7-1-12

Still, it should be done by a nursemaid who wants what’s best for him, out of kindness and sympathy. ^an5-7-1-13

And when the boy has grown up and has enough sense, his nursemaid would not be concerned about him, thinking: ^an5-7-1-14

‘The boy can look after himself. He won’t be negligent.’ ^an5-7-1-15

In the same way, I still need to look after a mendicant who hasn’t finished developing faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities. ^an5-7-2-1

But when a mendicant has finished developing faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities, I need not be concerned, thinking: ^an5-7-2-2

‘They can look after themselves. They won’t be negligent.’” ^an5-7-2-3

### Failure (Numbered Discourses 5.8) ^an5-8-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-8-1-1

What five? ^an5-8-1-2

A mendicant who is faithless … ^an5-8-1-3

shameless … ^an5-8-1-4

imprudent … ^an5-8-1-5

lazy … ^an5-8-1-6

witless fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-8-1-7

A mendicant with these five qualities fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-8-1-8

A mendicant with five qualities doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-8-2-1

What five? ^an5-8-2-2

A mendicant who is faithful … ^an5-8-2-3

conscientious … ^an5-8-2-4

prudent … ^an5-8-2-5

energetic … ^an5-8-2-6

wise doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-8-2-7

A mendicant with these five qualities doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching.” ^an5-8-2-8

### Disrespect (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.9) ^an5-9-0

“Mendicants, a disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with five qualities fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-9-1-1

What five? ^an5-9-1-2

A disrespectful and irreverent mendicant who is faithless … ^an5-9-1-3

shameless … ^an5-9-1-4

imprudent … ^an5-9-1-5

lazy … ^an5-9-1-6

witless fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-9-1-7

A disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with these five qualities fails, and doesn’t establish themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-9-1-8

A respectful and reverent mendicant with five qualities doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-9-2-1

What five? ^an5-9-2-2

A respectful and reverent mendicant who is faithful … ^an5-9-2-3

conscientious … ^an5-9-2-4

prudent … ^an5-9-2-5

energetic … ^an5-9-2-6

wise doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching. ^an5-9-2-7

A respectful and reverent mendicant with these five qualities doesn’t fail, and establishes themselves in the true teaching.” ^an5-9-2-8

### Disrespect (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.10) ^an5-10-0

“Mendicants, a disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with five qualities can’t achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an5-10-1-1

What five? ^an5-10-1-2

A disrespectful and irreverent mendicant who is faithless … ^an5-10-1-3

shameless … ^an5-10-1-4

imprudent … ^an5-10-1-5

lazy … ^an5-10-1-6

witless can’t achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an5-10-1-7

A disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with these five qualities can’t achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an5-10-1-8

A respectful and reverent mendicant with five qualities can achieve growth, improvement, and maturity in this teaching and training. ^an5-10-2-1

What five? ^an5-10-2-2

A respectful and reverent mendicant who is faithful … ^an5-10-2-3

conscientious … prudent … energetic … wise can achieve growth, improvement, and maturity in this teaching and training. ^an5-10-2-4

A respectful and reverent mendicant with these five qualities can achieve growth, improvement, and maturity in this teaching and training.” ^an5-10-2-5

### Not Learned From Anyone Else (Numbered Discourses 5.11) ^an5-11-0

“I claim to have attained perfection and consummation of insight regarding principles not learned before from another. ^an5-11-1-1

The Realized One has five powers of a Realized One. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an5-11-1-2

What five? ^an5-11-1-3

The powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an5-11-1-4

These are the five powers of a Realized One. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel.” ^an5-11-1-5

### Peak (Numbered Discourses 5.12) ^an5-12-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers of a trainee. ^an5-12-1-1

What five? ^an5-12-1-2

The powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an5-12-1-3

These are the five powers of a trainee. ^an5-12-1-4

Of these five powers of a trainee, the power of wisdom is the chief. It holds and binds everything together. ^an5-12-1-5

It’s like a bungalow. The roof-peak is the chief point, which holds and binds everything together. ^an5-12-2-1

In the same way, of these five powers of a trainee, the power of wisdom is the chief. It holds and binds everything together. ^an5-12-2-2

So you should train like this: ^an5-12-3-1

‘We will have the trainee’s powers of faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.’ ^an5-12-3-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-12-3-3

### In Brief (Numbered Discourses 5.13) ^an5-13-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers. ^an5-13-1-1

What five? ^an5-13-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-13-1-3

These are the five powers.” ^an5-13-1-4

### In Detail (Numbered Discourses 5.14) ^an5-14-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers. ^an5-14-1-1

What five? ^an5-14-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-14-1-3

And what is the power of faith? ^an5-14-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an5-14-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-14-2-3

This is called the power of faith. ^an5-14-2-4

And what is the power of energy? ^an5-14-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-14-3-2

This is called the power of energy. ^an5-14-3-3

And what is the power of mindfulness? ^an5-14-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple is mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. ^an5-14-4-2

This is called the power of mindfulness. ^an5-14-4-3

And what is the power of immersion? ^an5-14-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an5-14-5-2

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an5-14-5-3

And with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an5-14-5-4

With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an5-14-5-5

This is called the power of immersion. ^an5-14-5-6

And what is the power of wisdom? ^an5-14-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-14-6-2

This is called the power of wisdom. ^an5-14-6-3

These are the five powers.” ^an5-14-6-4

### Should Be Seen (Numbered Discourses 5.15) ^an5-15-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers. ^an5-15-1-1

What five? ^an5-15-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-15-1-3

And where should the power of faith be seen? ^an5-15-1-4

In the four factors of stream-entry. ^an5-15-1-5

And where should the power of energy be seen? ^an5-15-1-7

In the four right efforts. ^an5-15-1-8

And where should the power of mindfulness be seen? ^an5-15-1-10

In the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an5-15-1-11

And where should the power of immersion be seen? ^an5-15-1-13

In the four absorptions. ^an5-15-1-14

And where should the power of wisdom be seen? ^an5-15-1-16

In the four noble truths. ^an5-15-1-17

These are the five powers.” ^an5-15-1-19

### The Peak, Again (Numbered Discourses 5.16) ^an5-16-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers. ^an5-16-1-1

What five? ^an5-16-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-16-1-3

These are the five powers. ^an5-16-1-4

Of these five powers, the power of wisdom is the chief. It holds and binds everything together. ^an5-16-1-5

It’s like a bungalow. The roof-peak is the chief point, which holds and binds everything together. ^an5-16-1-6

In the same way, of these five powers, the power of wisdom is the chief. It holds and binds everything together.” ^an5-16-1-7

### One’s Own Welfare (Numbered Discourses 5.17) ^an5-17-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is practicing for their own welfare, but not that of others. ^an5-17-1-1

What five? ^an5-17-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. But they don’t encourage others in these qualities. ^an5-17-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is practicing for their own welfare, but not that of others.” ^an5-17-1-8

### Welfare of Others (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.18) ^an5-18-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is practicing for the welfare of others, but not their own. ^an5-18-1-1

What five? ^an5-18-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not personally accomplished in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, or the knowledge and vision of freedom. But they encourage others in these qualities. ^an5-18-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is practicing for the welfare of others, but not their own.” ^an5-18-1-8

### The Welfare of Neither (Numbered Discourses 5.19) ^an5-19-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is practicing neither for their own welfare, nor that of others. ^an5-19-1-1

What five? ^an5-19-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not personally accomplished in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, or the knowledge and vision of freedom. Nor do they encourage others in these qualities. ^an5-19-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is practicing neither for their own welfare, nor that of others.” ^an5-19-1-8

### The Welfare of Both (Numbered Discourses 5.20) ^an5-20-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is practicing for both their own welfare and that of others. ^an5-20-1-1

What five? ^an5-20-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. And they encourage others in these qualities. ^an5-20-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is practicing both for their own welfare and that of others.” ^an5-20-1-7

### Disrespect (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.21) ^an5-21-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with incompatible lifestyle to fulfill the practice dealing with supplementary regulations regarding their spiritual companions. ^an5-21-1-1

Without fulfilling the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s impossible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an5-21-1-2

Without fulfilling the practice of a trainee, it’s impossible to fulfill ethics. ^an5-21-1-3

Without fulfilling ethics, it’s impossible to fulfill right view. ^an5-21-1-4

Without fulfilling right view, it’s impossible to fulfill right immersion. ^an5-21-1-5

But it is quite possible for a respectful and reverent mendicant with compatible lifestyle to fulfill the practice dealing with supplementary regulations regarding their spiritual companions. ^an5-21-2-1

Having fulfilled the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s possible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an5-21-2-2

Having fulfilled the practice of a trainee, it’s possible to fulfill ethics. ^an5-21-2-3

Having fulfilled ethics, it’s possible to fulfill right view. ^an5-21-2-4

Having fulfilled right view, it’s possible to fulfill right immersion.” ^an5-21-2-5

### Disrespect (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.22) ^an5-22-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a disrespectful and irreverent mendicant with incompatible lifestyle to fulfill the practice dealing with supplementary regulations regarding their spiritual companions. ^an5-22-1-1

Without fulfilling the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s impossible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an5-22-1-2

Without fulfilling the practice of a trainee, it’s impossible to fulfill the entire spectrum of ethics. ^an5-22-1-3

Without fulfilling the entire spectrum of ethics, it’s impossible to fulfill the entire spectrum of immersion. ^an5-22-1-4

Without fulfilling the entire spectrum of immersion, it’s impossible to fulfill the entire spectrum of wisdom. ^an5-22-1-5

But it is quite possible for a respectful and reverent mendicant with compatible lifestyle to fulfill the practice dealing with supplementary regulations regarding their spiritual companions. ^an5-22-2-1

Having fulfilled the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s possible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an5-22-2-2

Having fulfilled the practice of a trainee, it’s possible to fulfill the entire spectrum of ethics. ^an5-22-2-3

Having fulfilled the entire spectrum of ethics, it’s possible to fulfill the entire spectrum of immersion. ^an5-22-2-4

Having fulfilled the entire spectrum of immersion, it’s possible to fulfill the entire spectrum of wisdom.” ^an5-22-2-5

### Corruptions (Numbered Discourses 5.23) ^an5-23-0

“Mendicants, there are these five corruptions of native gold. When native gold is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working. ^an5-23-1-1

What five? ^an5-23-1-2

Iron, copper, tin, lead, and silver. ^an5-23-1-3

When native gold is corrupted by these five corruptions it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working. ^an5-23-1-4

But when native gold is free of these five corruptions it becomes pliable, workable, and radiant, not brittle, and ready to be worked. ^an5-23-1-5

Then the goldsmith can successfully create any kind of ornament they want, whether a ring, earrings, a necklace, or a golden garland. ^an5-23-1-6

In the same way, there are these five corruptions of the mind. When the mind is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant. It’s brittle, and not rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an5-23-2-1

What five? ^an5-23-2-2

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an5-23-2-3

These are the five corruptions of the mind. When the mind is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant. It’s brittle, and not rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an5-23-2-4

But when the mind is free of these five corruptions it’s pliable, workable, and radiant. It’s not brittle, and is rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements. ^an5-23-2-5

You become capable of realizing anything that can be realized by insight to which you extend the mind, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-2-6

If you wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power—multiplying myself and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful, controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.’ ^an5-23-3-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-3-2

If you wish: ‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.’ ^an5-23-4-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-4-2

If you wish: ‘May I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^an5-23-5-1

May I understand mind with greed as “mind with greed”, ^an5-23-5-2

and mind without greed as “mind without greed”; ^an5-23-5-3

mind with hate as “mind with hate”, ^an5-23-5-4

and mind without hate as “mind without hate”; ^an5-23-5-5

mind with delusion as “mind with delusion”, ^an5-23-5-6

and mind without delusion as “mind without delusion”; ^an5-23-5-7

constricted mind as “constricted mind”, ^an5-23-5-8

and scattered mind as “scattered mind”; ^an5-23-5-9

expansive mind as “expansive mind”, ^an5-23-5-10

and unexpansive mind as “unexpansive mind”; ^an5-23-5-11

mind that is not supreme as “mind that is not supreme”, ^an5-23-5-12

and mind that is supreme as “mind that is supreme”; ^an5-23-5-13

mind immersed in samādhi as “mind immersed in samādhi”, ^an5-23-5-14

and mind not immersed in samādhi as “mind not immersed in samādhi”; ^an5-23-5-15

freed mind as “freed mind”, ^an5-23-5-16

and unfreed mind as “unfreed mind”.’ ^an5-23-5-17

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-5-18

If you wish: ‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. May I remember: “There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.” May I recollect my many past lives, with features and details.’ ^an5-23-6-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-6-2

If you wish: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place—and understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds: “These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. And may I understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds.’ ^an5-23-7-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-23-7-2

If you wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an5-23-8-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.” ^an5-23-8-2

### Unethical (Numbered Discourses 5.24) ^an5-24-0

“Mendicants, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-24-1-1

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-24-1-2

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-24-1-3

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-24-1-4

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. ^an5-24-1-5

Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an5-24-1-6

In the same way, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-24-1-7

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-24-1-8

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-24-1-9

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-24-1-10

An ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-24-2-1

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-24-2-2

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-24-2-3

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-24-2-4

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would all grow to fullness. ^an5-24-2-5

In the same way, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-24-2-6

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-24-2-7

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-24-2-8

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an5-24-2-9

### Supported (Numbered Discourses 5.25) ^an5-25-0

“Mendicants, when right view is supported by five factors it has freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as its fruit and benefit. ^an5-25-1-1

What five? ^an5-25-2-1

It’s when right view is supported by ethics, learning, discussion, serenity, and discernment. ^an5-25-2-2

When right view is supported by these five factors it has freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as its fruit and benefit.” ^an5-25-2-3

### Opportunities for Freedom (Numbered Discourses 5.26) ^an5-26-0

“Mendicants, there are these five opportunities for freedom. If a mendicant stays diligent, keen, and resolute at these times, their mind is freed, their defilements are ended, and they arrive at the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an5-26-1-1

What five? ^an5-26-2-1

Firstly, the Teacher or a respected spiritual companion teaches Dhamma to a mendicant. ^an5-26-2-2

In whatever way the Teacher or a respected spiritual companion teaches Dhamma to that mendicant, they always feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an5-26-2-3

Feeling inspired, joy springs up. ^an5-26-2-4

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^an5-26-2-5

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^an5-26-2-6

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^an5-26-2-7

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an5-26-2-8

This is the first opportunity for freedom. If a mendicant stays diligent, keen, and resolute at this time, their mind is freed, their defilements are ended, and they arrive at the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an5-26-2-9

Furthermore, it may be that neither the Teacher nor a respected spiritual companion teaches Dhamma to a mendicant. But the mendicant teaches Dhamma in detail to others as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-26-3-1

In whatever way that mendicant teaches Dhamma in detail to others as they learned and memorized it, they always feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an5-26-3-2

Feeling inspired, joy springs up. ^an5-26-3-3

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^an5-26-3-4

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^an5-26-3-5

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^an5-26-3-6

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an5-26-3-7

This is the second opportunity for freedom. … ^an5-26-3-8

Furthermore, it may be that neither the Teacher nor … the mendicant teaches Dhamma. But the mendicant rehearses the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-26-4-1

In whatever way that mendicant rehearses the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it, they always feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an5-26-4-2

Feeling inspired, joy springs up. ^an5-26-4-3

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^an5-26-4-4

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^an5-26-4-5

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^an5-26-4-6

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an5-26-4-7

This is the third opportunity for freedom. … ^an5-26-4-8

Furthermore, it may be that neither the Teacher nor … the mendicant teaches Dhamma … nor does the mendicant rehearse the teaching. ^an5-26-5-1

But the mendicant thinks about and considers the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-26-5-2

In whatever way that mendicant thinks about and considers the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it, they always feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an5-26-5-3

Feeling inspired, joy springs up. ^an5-26-5-4

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^an5-26-5-5

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^an5-26-5-6

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^an5-26-5-7

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an5-26-5-8

This is the fourth opportunity for freedom. … ^an5-26-5-9

Furthermore, it may be that neither the Teacher nor … the mendicant teaches Dhamma … nor does the mendicant rehearse the teaching … or think about it. ^an5-26-6-1

But a meditation subject as a basis of immersion is properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom. ^an5-26-6-2

In whatever way a mendicant’s meditation subject as a basis of immersion is properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom, they always feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an5-26-6-3

Feeling inspired, joy springs up. ^an5-26-6-4

Being joyful, rapture springs up. ^an5-26-6-5

When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. ^an5-26-6-6

When the body is tranquil, one feels bliss. ^an5-26-6-7

And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an5-26-6-8

This is the fifth opportunity for freedom. … ^an5-26-6-9

These are the five opportunities for freedom. If a mendicant stays diligent, keen, and resolute at these times, their mind is freed, their defilements are ended, and they arrive at the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.” ^an5-26-7-1

### Immersion (Numbered Discourses 5.27) ^an5-27-0

“Mendicants, develop limitless immersion, alert and mindful. ^an5-27-1-1

When you develop limitless immersion, alert and mindful, five knowledges arise for you personally. ^an5-27-1-2

What five? ^an5-27-1-3

‘This immersion is blissful now, and results in bliss in the future.’ … ^an5-27-1-4

‘This immersion is noble and not of the flesh.’ … ^an5-27-1-5

‘This immersion is not cultivated by reprobates.’ … ^an5-27-1-6

‘This immersion is peaceful and sublime and tranquil and unified, not held in place by forceful suppression.’ … ^an5-27-1-7

‘I mindfully enter into and emerge from this immersion.’ … ^an5-27-1-8

Develop limitless immersion, alert and mindful. ^an5-27-2-1

When you develop limitless immersion, alert and mindful, these five knowledges arise for you personally.” ^an5-27-2-2

### With Five Factors (Numbered Discourses 5.28) ^an5-28-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you how to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an5-28-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an5-28-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an5-28-1-4

“And how do you develop noble right immersion with five factors? ^an5-28-2-1

Firstly, a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption. It has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an5-28-2-2

They drench, steep, fill, and spread their body with rapture and bliss born of seclusion. There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with rapture and bliss born of seclusion. ^an5-28-2-3

It’s like when a deft bathroom attendant or their apprentice pours bath powder into a bronze dish, sprinkling it little by little with water. They knead it until the ball of bath powder is soaked and saturated with moisture, spread through inside and out; yet no moisture oozes out. ^an5-28-2-4

In the same way, a mendicant drenches, steeps, fills, and spreads their body with rapture and bliss born of seclusion. ^an5-28-2-5

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with rapture and bliss born of seclusion. ^an5-28-2-6

This is the first way to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-2-7

Furthermore, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption. It has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an5-28-3-1

They drench, steep, fill, and spread their body with rapture and bliss born of immersion. ^an5-28-3-2

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with rapture and bliss born of immersion. ^an5-28-3-3

It’s like a deep lake fed by spring water. There’s no inlet to the east, west, north, or south, and the heavens would not properly bestow showers from time to time. ^an5-28-3-4

But the stream of cool water welling up in the lake drenches, steeps, fills, and spreads throughout the lake. There’s no part of the lake that’s not spread through with cool water. ^an5-28-3-5

In the same way, a mendicant drenches, steeps, fills, and spreads their body with rapture and bliss born of immersion. ^an5-28-3-6

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with rapture and bliss born of immersion. ^an5-28-3-7

This is the second way to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-3-8

Furthermore, with the fading away of rapture, a mendicant enters and remains in the third absorption. They meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an5-28-4-1

They drench, steep, fill, and spread their body with bliss free of rapture. ^an5-28-4-2

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with bliss free of rapture. ^an5-28-4-3

It’s like a pool with blue water lilies, or pink or white lotuses. Some of them sprout and grow in the water without rising above it, thriving underwater. ^an5-28-4-4

From the tip to the root they’re drenched, steeped, filled, and soaked with cool water. ^an5-28-4-5

There’s no part of them that’s not spread through with cool water. ^an5-28-4-6

In the same way, a mendicant drenches, steeps, fills, and spreads their body with bliss free of rapture. ^an5-28-4-7

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with bliss free of rapture. ^an5-28-4-8

This is the third way to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-4-9

Furthermore, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, a mendicant enters and remains in the fourth absorption. It is without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an5-28-5-1

They sit spreading their body through with pure bright mind. ^an5-28-5-2

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with pure bright mind. ^an5-28-5-3

It’s like someone sitting wrapped from head to foot with white cloth. ^an5-28-5-4

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread over with white cloth. ^an5-28-5-5

In the same way, they sit spreading their body through with pure bright mind. ^an5-28-5-6

There’s no part of the body that’s not spread with pure bright mind. ^an5-28-5-7

This is the fourth way to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-5-8

Furthermore, the meditation that is a basis for reviewing is properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom by a mendicant. ^an5-28-6-1

It’s like when someone views someone else. Someone standing might view someone sitting, or someone sitting might view someone lying down. ^an5-28-6-2

In the same way, the meditation that is a basis for reviewing is properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom by a mendicant. ^an5-28-6-3

This is the fifth way to develop noble right immersion with five factors. ^an5-28-6-4

When the noble right immersion with five factors is developed and cultivated in this way, a mendicant extends the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-7-1

Suppose a water jar was placed on a stand, full to the brim so a crow could drink from it. ^an5-28-8-1

If a strong man was to tip it any which way, would water pour out?” ^an5-28-8-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an5-28-8-3

“In the same way, when noble right immersion with five factors is developed and cultivated in this way, a mendicant extends the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-8-4

Suppose there was a square, walled lotus pond on level ground, full to the brim so a crow could drink from it. ^an5-28-9-1

If a strong man was to open the wall on any side, would water pour out?” ^an5-28-9-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an5-28-9-3

“In the same way, when noble right immersion with five factors is developed and cultivated in this way, a mendicant extends the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-9-4

Suppose a chariot stood harnessed to thoroughbreds at a level crossroads, with a goad ready. ^an5-28-10-1

A deft horse trainer, a master charioteer, might mount the chariot, taking the reins in his right hand and goad in the left. He’d drive out and back wherever he wishes, whenever he wishes. ^an5-28-10-2

In the same way, when noble right immersion with five factors is developed and cultivated in this way, ^an5-28-10-3

a mendicant extends the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-10-4

If you wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.’ ^an5-28-11-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-11-2

If you wish: ‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.’ ^an5-28-12-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-12-2

If you wish: ‘May I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^an5-28-13-1

May I understand mind with greed as “mind with greed”, ^an5-28-13-2

and mind without greed as “mind without greed”; ^an5-28-13-3

mind with hate as “mind with hate”, ^an5-28-13-4

and mind without hate as “mind without hate”; ^an5-28-13-5

mind with delusion as “mind with delusion”, ^an5-28-13-6

and mind without delusion as “mind without delusion”; ^an5-28-13-7

constricted mind as “constricted mind”, ^an5-28-13-8

and scattered mind as “scattered mind”; ^an5-28-13-9

expansive mind as “expansive mind”, ^an5-28-13-10

and unexpansive mind as “unexpansive mind”; ^an5-28-13-11

mind that is not supreme as “mind that is not supreme”, ^an5-28-13-12

and mind that is supreme as “mind that is supreme”; ^an5-28-13-13

mind immersed in samādhi as “mind immersed in samādhi”, ^an5-28-13-14

and mind not immersed in samādhi as “mind not immersed in samādhi”; ^an5-28-13-15

freed mind as “freed mind”, ^an5-28-13-16

and unfreed mind as “unfreed mind”.’ ^an5-28-13-17

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-13-18

If you wish: ‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details.’ ^an5-28-14-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-14-2

If you wish: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn according to their deeds.’ ^an5-28-15-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-28-15-2

If you wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an5-28-16-1

You’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.” ^an5-28-16-2

### Walking Meditation (Numbered Discourses 5.29) ^an5-29-0

“Mendicants, there are five benefits of walking meditation. ^an5-29-1-1

What five? ^an5-29-1-2

You get fit for traveling, fit for striving in meditation, and healthy. What’s eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted is properly digested. And immersion gained while walking lasts long. ^an5-29-1-3

These are the five benefits of walking meditation.” ^an5-29-1-4

### With Nāgita (Numbered Discourses 5.30) ^an5-30-0

So I have heard. ^an5-30-1-1

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a village of the Kosalan brahmins named Icchānaṅgala. ^an5-30-1-2

He stayed in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an5-30-1-3

The brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala heard: ^an5-30-1-4

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Icchānaṅgala. He is staying in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an5-30-1-5

He has this good reputation: ^an5-30-1-6

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-30-1-7

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. ^an5-30-1-8

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an5-30-1-9

It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^an5-30-1-10

Then, when the night had passed, they took many fresh and cooked foods and went to the forest near Icchānaṅgala, where they stood outside the gates making a colossal racket. ^an5-30-1-11

Now, at that time Venerable Nāgita was the Buddha’s attendant. ^an5-30-2-1

Then the Buddha said to Nāgita, ^an5-30-2-2

“Nāgita, who’s making that colossal racket? You’d think it was fishermen hauling in a catch!” ^an5-30-2-3

“Sir, it’s these brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala. They’ve brought many fresh and cooked foods, and they’re standing outside the gates wanting to offer it specially to the Buddha and the mendicant Saṅgha.” ^an5-30-2-4

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an5-30-2-5

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an5-30-2-6

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity.” ^an5-30-2-7

“Sir, may the Blessed One please relent now! May the Holy One relent! ^an5-30-3-1

Now is the time for the Buddha to relent. ^an5-30-3-2

Wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an5-30-3-3

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily and the water flows downhill. ^an5-30-3-4

In the same way, wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an5-30-3-5

Why is that? ^an5-30-3-6

Because of the Buddha’s ethics and wisdom.” ^an5-30-3-7

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an5-30-4-1

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an5-30-4-2

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity. ^an5-30-4-3

What you eat, drink, chew, and taste ends up as excrement and urine. ^an5-30-4-4

This is its outcome. ^an5-30-4-5

When loved ones decay and perish, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress arise. ^an5-30-4-6

This is its outcome. ^an5-30-4-7

When you pursue meditation on the feature of ugliness, revulsion at the feature of beauty becomes stabilized. ^an5-30-4-8

This is its outcome. ^an5-30-4-9

When you meditate observing impermanence in the six fields of contact, revulsion at contact becomes stabilized. ^an5-30-4-10

This is its outcome. ^an5-30-4-11

When you meditate observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates, revulsion at grasping becomes stabilized. ^an5-30-4-12

This is its outcome.” ^an5-30-4-13

### With Sumanā (Numbered Discourses 5.31) ^an5-31-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-31-1-1

Then Princess Sumanā, escorted by five hundred chariots and five hundred royal maidens, went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-31-1-2

“Sir, suppose there were two disciples equal in faith, ethics, and wisdom. ^an5-31-2-1

One is a giver, one is not. ^an5-31-2-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-31-2-3

When they have become gods, would there be any distinction or difference between them?” ^an5-31-2-4

“There would be, Sumanā,” said the Buddha. ^an5-31-3-1

“As a god, the one who was a giver surpasses the other in five respects: ^an5-31-3-2

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, and sovereignty. ^an5-31-3-3

As a god, the one who was a giver surpasses the other in these five respects.” ^an5-31-3-4

“But sir, if they pass away from there and come back to this place as human beings, would there still be any distinction or difference between them?” ^an5-31-4-1

“There would be, Sumanā,” said the Buddha. ^an5-31-4-2

“As a human being, the one who was a giver would surpass the other in five respects: ^an5-31-4-3

human lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, and sovereignty. ^an5-31-4-4

As a human being, the one who was a giver surpasses the other in these five respects.” ^an5-31-4-5

“But sir, if they both go forth from the lay life to homelessness, would there still be any distinction or difference between them?” ^an5-31-5-1

“There would be, Sumanā,” said the Buddha. ^an5-31-5-2

“As a renunciate, the one who was a giver surpasses the other in five respects. ^an5-31-5-3

They usually use only what they’ve been invited to accept—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—rarely using them without invitation. ^an5-31-5-4

When living with other spiritual practitioners, they usually find themselves treated agreeably by them by way of body, speech, and mind, and rarely disagreeably. And they are usually presented with agreeable things by them, rarely with disagreeable ones. ^an5-31-5-5

As a renunciate, the one who was a giver surpasses the other in these five respects.” ^an5-31-5-6

“But sir, if they both attain perfection, as perfected ones would there still be any distinction or difference between them?” ^an5-31-6-1

“In that case, I say there is no difference between the freedom of one and the freedom of the other.” ^an5-31-6-2

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an5-31-7-1

This is quite enough to justify giving gifts and making merit. ^an5-31-7-2

For merit is helpful for those who have become gods, human beings, and renunciates.” ^an5-31-7-3

“That’s so true, Sumanā. ^an5-31-7-4

It’s quite enough to justify giving gifts and making merit. ^an5-31-7-5

For merit is helpful for those who have become gods, human beings, and renunciates.” ^an5-31-7-6

That is what the Buddha said. ^an5-31-8-1

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^an5-31-8-2

“The moon so immaculate, ^an5-31-9-1

journeying across the dimension of space; ^an5-31-9-2

outshines with its radiance ^an5-31-9-3

all the world’s stars. ^an5-31-9-4

So too, a faithful individual, ^an5-31-10-1

perfect in ethics, ^an5-31-10-2

outshines with their generosity ^an5-31-10-3

all the world’s stingy people. ^an5-31-10-4

The thundering rain cloud, ^an5-31-11-1

its hundred peaks wreathed in lightning, ^an5-31-11-2

pours down over the rich earth, ^an5-31-11-3

soaking the uplands and valleys. ^an5-31-11-4

Even so, an astute person accomplished in vision, ^an5-31-12-1

a disciple of the fully awakened Buddha, ^an5-31-12-2

surpasses a stingy person ^an5-31-12-3

in five respects: ^an5-31-12-4

long life and fame, ^an5-31-13-1

beauty and happiness. ^an5-31-13-2

Lavished with riches, ^an5-31-13-3

they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an5-31-13-4

### With Cundī (Numbered Discourses 5.32) ^an5-32-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an5-32-1-1

Then Princess Cundī, escorted by five hundred chariots and five hundred royal maidens, went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-32-1-2

“Sir, my brother, Prince Cunda, says this: ^an5-32-2-1

‘Take a woman or man who goes for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. Only then do they get reborn in a good place, not a bad place, when their body breaks up, after death.’ ^an5-32-2-2

And so I ask the Buddha: ^an5-32-2-3

Sir, what kind of teacher should you have confidence in so as to be reborn in a good place, not a bad place, when the body breaks up, after death? ^an5-32-2-4

Sir, what kind of teaching should you have confidence in so as to be reborn in a good place, not a bad place, when the body breaks up, after death? ^an5-32-2-5

Sir, what kind of Saṅgha should you have confidence in so as to be reborn in a good place, not a bad place, when the body breaks up, after death? ^an5-32-2-6

Sir, what kind of ethics should you fulfill so as to be reborn in a good place, not a bad place, when the body breaks up, after death?” ^an5-32-2-7

“Cundī, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, is said to be the best of all sentient beings—be they footless, with two feet, four feet, or many feet; with form or formless; with perception or without perception or with neither perception nor non-perception. ^an5-32-3-1

Those who have confidence in the Buddha have confidence in the best. ^an5-32-3-2

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an5-32-3-3

The noble eightfold path is said to be the best of all conditioned things. ^an5-32-4-1

Those who have confidence in the noble eightfold path have confidence in the best. Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an5-32-4-2

Fading away is said to be the best of all things whether conditioned or unconditioned. ^an5-32-5-1

That is, the quelling of vanity, the removing of thirst, the uprooting of clinging, the breaking of the round, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^an5-32-5-2

Those who have confidence in the teaching of fading away have confidence in the best. ^an5-32-5-3

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an5-32-5-4

The Saṅgha of the Realized One’s disciples is said to be the best of all communities and groups. ^an5-32-6-1

It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-32-6-2

Those who have confidence in the Saṅgha have confidence in the best. ^an5-32-6-3

Having confidence in the best, the result is the best. ^an5-32-6-4

The ethical conduct loved by the noble ones is said to be the best of all ethics. ^an5-32-7-1

It is intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an5-32-7-2

Those who fulfill the ethics loved by the noble ones fulfill the best. ^an5-32-7-3

Fulfilling the best, the result is the best. ^an5-32-7-4

For those who, knowing the best teaching, ^an5-32-8-1

base their confidence on the best— ^an5-32-8-2

confident in the best Awakened One, ^an5-32-8-3

supremely worthy of a religious donation; ^an5-32-8-4

confident in the best teaching, ^an5-32-9-1

the bliss of fading and stilling; ^an5-32-9-2

confident in the best Saṅgha, ^an5-32-9-3

the supreme field of merit— ^an5-32-9-4

giving gifts to the best, ^an5-32-10-1

the best of merit grows: ^an5-32-10-2

the best lifespan, beauty, ^an5-32-10-3

fame, reputation, happiness, and strength. ^an5-32-10-4

An intelligent person gives to the best, ^an5-32-11-1

settled on the best teaching. ^an5-32-11-2

When they become a god or human, ^an5-32-11-3

they rejoice at reaching the best.” ^an5-32-11-4

### With Uggaha (Numbered Discourses 5.33) ^an5-33-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Bhaddiya, in Jātiyā Wood. ^an5-33-1-1

Then Uggaha, Meṇḍaka’s grandson, went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-33-1-2

“Sir, would the Buddha, together with three other monks, please accept tomorrow’s meal from me?” ^an5-33-2-1

The Buddha consented with silence. ^an5-33-2-2

Then, knowing that the Buddha had consented, Uggaha rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an5-33-2-3

Then when the night had passed, the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to Uggaha’s home, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an5-33-3-1

Then Uggaha served and satisfied the Buddha with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an5-33-3-2

When the Buddha had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, Uggaha sat down to one side, ^an5-33-3-3

and said to him, ^an5-33-3-4

“Sir, these girls of mine will be going to their husbands’ families. ^an5-33-3-5

May the Buddha please advise ^an5-33-3-6

and instruct them. It will be for their lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an5-33-3-7

Then the Buddha said to those girls: ^an5-33-4-1

“So, girls, you should train like this: ^an5-33-4-2

‘Our parents will give us to a husband wanting what’s best, out of kindness and sympathy. We will get up before him and go to bed after him, and be obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely.’ ^an5-33-4-3

That’s how you should train. ^an5-33-4-4

So, girls, you should train like this: ^an5-33-5-1

‘Those our husband respects—mother and father, ascetics and brahmins—we will honor, respect, revere, and venerate, and serve with a seat and a drink when they come as guests.’ ^an5-33-5-2

That’s how you should train. ^an5-33-5-3

So, girls, you should train like this: ^an5-33-6-1

‘We will be deft and tireless in doing domestic duties for our husband, such as knitting and sewing. We will have an understanding of how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work.’ ^an5-33-6-2

That’s how you should train. ^an5-33-6-3

So, girls, you should train like this: ^an5-33-7-1

‘We will know what work our husband’s domestic bondservants, servants, and workers have completed, and what they’ve left incomplete. We will know who is sick, and who is fit or unwell. We will distribute to each a fair portion of fresh and cooked foods.’ ^an5-33-7-2

That’s how you should train. ^an5-33-7-3

So, girls, you should train like this: ^an5-33-8-1

‘We will ensure that any income our husbands earn is guarded and protected, whether money, grain, silver, or gold. We will not overspend, steal, waste, or lose it.’ ^an5-33-8-2

That’s how you should train. ^an5-33-8-3

When they possess these five qualities, ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an5-33-8-4

She’d never look down on her husband, ^an5-33-9-1

who’s always eager to work hard, ^an5-33-9-2

always looking after her, ^an5-33-9-3

and bringing whatever she wants. ^an5-33-9-4

And a good woman never scolds her husband ^an5-33-10-1

with jealous words. ^an5-33-10-2

Being astute, she reveres ^an5-33-10-3

those respected by her husband. ^an5-33-10-4

She gets up early, works tirelessly, ^an5-33-11-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an5-33-11-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an5-33-11-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an5-33-11-4

A lady who fulfills these duties ^an5-33-12-1

according to her husband’s desire, ^an5-33-12-2

is reborn among the gods ^an5-33-12-3
