called ‘Agreeable’.” ^an5-33-12-4

### With General Sīha (Numbered Discourses 5.34) ^an5-34-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-34-1-1

Then General Sīha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and asked him, ^an5-34-1-2

“Sir, can you point out a fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life?” ^an5-34-1-3

“I can, Sīha,” said the Buddha. ^an5-34-2-1

“A giver, a donor is dear and beloved to many people. ^an5-34-2-2

This is a fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life. ^an5-34-2-3

Furthermore, good and true persons associate with a giver. ^an5-34-3-1

This is another fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life. ^an5-34-3-2

Furthermore, a giver gains a good reputation. ^an5-34-4-1

This is another fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life. ^an5-34-4-2

Furthermore, a giver enters any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics. ^an5-34-5-1

This is another fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life. ^an5-34-5-2

Furthermore, when a giver’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-34-6-1

This is a fruit of giving to do with lives to come.” ^an5-34-6-2

When he said this, General Sīha said to the Buddha, ^an5-34-7-1

“When it comes to those four fruits of giving that are apparent in the present life, I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha, for I know them too. ^an5-34-7-2

I’m a giver, a donor, and am dear and beloved to many people. ^an5-34-7-3

I’m a giver, and good and true persons associate with me. ^an5-34-7-4

I’m a giver, and I have this good reputation: ‘General Sīha gives, serves, and attends on the Saṅgha.’ ^an5-34-7-5

I’m a giver, and I enter any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics. ^an5-34-7-6

When it comes to these four fruits of giving that are apparent in the present life, I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha, for I know them too. ^an5-34-7-7

But when the Buddha says: ^an5-34-7-8

‘When a giver’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ I don’t know this, so I have to rely on faith in the Buddha.” ^an5-34-7-9

“That’s so true, Sīha! That’s so true! ^an5-34-7-10

When a giver’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-34-7-11

Giving, you’re loved and befriended by many people. ^an5-34-8-1

You get a good reputation, and your fame grows. ^an5-34-8-2

A generous man enters an assembly ^an5-34-8-3

bold and self-assured. ^an5-34-8-4

So an astute person, seeking happiness, <j>would give gifts, ^an5-34-9-1

having driven out the stain of stinginess. ^an5-34-9-2

They live long in the Third Heaven, ^an5-34-9-3

enjoying the company of the gods. ^an5-34-9-4

Having taken the opportunity to do good, <j>when they pass from here ^an5-34-10-1

they wander radiant in the Garden of Delight. ^an5-34-10-2

There they delight, rejoice, and enjoy themselves, ^an5-34-10-3

provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an5-34-10-4

Having practiced the word of the unattached, <j>the unaffected, ^an5-34-10-5

disciples of the Holy One rejoice in heaven.” ^an5-34-10-6

### The Benefits of Giving (Numbered Discourses 5.35) ^an5-35-0

“Mendicants, there are five benefits of giving. ^an5-35-1-1

What five? ^an5-35-1-2

A giver, a donor is dear and beloved by many people. ^an5-35-1-3

Good and true persons associate with them. ^an5-35-1-4

They get a good reputation. ^an5-35-1-5

They don’t neglect a layperson’s duties. ^an5-35-1-6

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-35-1-7

These are the five benefits of giving. ^an5-35-1-8

Giving, one is loved, ^an5-35-2-1

and follows the way of the good. ^an5-35-2-2

The good, disciplined spiritual practitioners ^an5-35-2-3

always associate with you. ^an5-35-2-4

They teach you the Dhamma ^an5-35-3-1

that casts aside all suffering. ^an5-35-3-2

Having understood this teaching, ^an5-35-3-3

the undefiled one is fully extinguished.” ^an5-35-3-4

### Timely Gifts (Numbered Discourses 5.36) ^an5-36-0

“Mendicants, there are these five timely gifts. ^an5-36-1-1

What five? ^an5-36-1-2

A gift to a visitor. ^an5-36-1-3

A gift to someone setting out on a journey. ^an5-36-1-4

A gift to someone who is sick. ^an5-36-1-5

A gift at a time of famine. ^an5-36-1-6

Presenting the freshly harvested grains and fruits first to those who are ethical. ^an5-36-1-7

These are the five timely gifts. ^an5-36-1-8

The wise give at the right time, ^an5-36-2-1

being bountiful and rid of stinginess. ^an5-36-2-2

A religious donation at the right time ^an5-36-2-3

to the noble ones, upright and unaffected, ^an5-36-2-4

given with a clear and confident mind, ^an5-36-3-1

is indeed abundant. ^an5-36-3-2

Those who rejoice at that, ^an5-36-3-3

or do other services, ^an5-36-3-4

don’t miss out on the offering; ^an5-36-3-5

they too have a share in the merit. ^an5-36-3-6

So you should give without holding back, ^an5-36-4-1

where a gift is very fruitful. ^an5-36-4-2

The good deeds of sentient beings ^an5-36-4-3

support them in the next world.” ^an5-36-4-4

### Food (Numbered Discourses 5.37) ^an5-37-0

“Mendicants, when a giver gives food, they give the recipients five things. ^an5-37-1-1

What five? ^an5-37-1-2

Long life, beauty, happiness, strength, and eloquence. ^an5-37-1-3

Giving long life, they have long life as a god or human. ^an5-37-1-4

Giving beauty, they have beauty as a god or human. ^an5-37-1-5

Giving happiness, they have happiness as a god or human. ^an5-37-1-6

Giving strength, they have strength as a god or human. ^an5-37-1-7

Giving eloquence, they are eloquent as a god or human. ^an5-37-1-8

When a giver gives food, they give the recipients five things. ^an5-37-1-9

Being attentive, one is a giver of life, strength, ^an5-37-2-1

beauty, and eloquence. ^an5-37-2-2

An intelligent giver of happiness ^an5-37-2-3

gains happiness in return. ^an5-37-2-4

Giving life, strength, beauty, ^an5-37-3-1

happiness, and eloquence, ^an5-37-3-2

they’re long-lived and famous ^an5-37-3-3

wherever they’re reborn.” ^an5-37-3-4

### Faith (Numbered Discourses 5.38) ^an5-38-0

“Mendicants, a faithful gentleman gets five benefits. ^an5-38-1-1

What five? ^an5-38-1-2

The true persons in the world show sympathy first to the faithful, not so much to the unfaithful. ^an5-38-1-3

They first approach the faithful, not so much the unfaithful. ^an5-38-1-4

They first receive alms from the faithful, not so much the unfaithful. ^an5-38-1-5

They first teach Dhamma to the faithful, not so much the unfaithful. ^an5-38-1-6

When their body breaks up, after death, the faithful are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-38-1-7

A faithful gentleman gets these five benefits. ^an5-38-1-8

Suppose there was a great banyan tree at a level crossroads. It would become a refuge for birds from all around. ^an5-38-2-1

In the same way, a faithful gentleman becomes a refuge for many people—monks, nuns, laywomen, and laymen. ^an5-38-2-2

With its branches, leaves, and fruit, ^an5-38-3-1

a great tree with its strong trunk, ^an5-38-3-2

firmly-rooted and fruit-bearing, ^an5-38-3-3

supports many birds. ^an5-38-3-4

It’s a lovely place, ^an5-38-4-1

frequented by the sky-soarers. ^an5-38-4-2

Those that need shade go in the shade, ^an5-38-4-3

those that need fruit enjoy the fruit. ^an5-38-4-4

So too, a faithful individual ^an5-38-5-1

is perfect in ethics, ^an5-38-5-2

placid in manner and amenable, ^an5-38-5-3

gentle, courteous, and tender. ^an5-38-5-4

Those free of greed, freed of hate, ^an5-38-6-1

free of delusion, undefiled, ^an5-38-6-2

fields of merit for the world, ^an5-38-6-3

associate with such a person. ^an5-38-6-4

They teach them the Dhamma, ^an5-38-7-1

that casts aside all suffering. ^an5-38-7-2

Having understood this teaching, ^an5-38-7-3

the undefiled one is fully extinguished.” ^an5-38-7-4

### A Child (Numbered Discourses 5.39) ^an5-39-0

“Mendicants, parents see five reasons to wish for the birth of a child in the family. ^an5-39-1-1

What five? ^an5-39-1-2

Since we looked after them, they’ll look after us. ^an5-39-1-3

They’ll do their duty for us. ^an5-39-1-4

The family traditions will last. ^an5-39-1-5

They’ll take care of the inheritance. ^an5-39-1-6

Or else when we have passed away they’ll give an offering on our behalf. ^an5-39-1-7

Parents see these five reasons to wish for the birth of a child in the family. ^an5-39-1-8

Seeing five reasons, ^an5-39-2-1

astute people wish for a child. ^an5-39-2-2

Since we looked after them, they’ll look after us. ^an5-39-2-3

They’ll do their duty for us. ^an5-39-2-4

The family traditions will last. ^an5-39-3-1

They’ll take care of the inheritance. ^an5-39-3-2

Or else when we have passed away ^an5-39-3-3

they’ll give an offering on our behalf. ^an5-39-3-4

Seeing these five reasons ^an5-39-4-1

astute people wish for a child. ^an5-39-4-2

And so true persons, ^an5-39-4-3

grateful and thankful, ^an5-39-4-4

look after their parents, ^an5-39-5-1

remembering past deeds. ^an5-39-5-2

They do for their parents, ^an5-39-5-3

as their parents did for them in the past. ^an5-39-5-4

Following their advice, <j>looking after those who raised them, ^an5-39-6-1

the family traditions are not lost. ^an5-39-6-2

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an5-39-6-3

such a child is praiseworthy.” ^an5-39-6-4

### Great Sal Trees (Numbered Discourses 5.40) ^an5-40-0

“Mendicants, great sal trees grow in five ways supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^an5-40-1-1

What five? ^an5-40-1-2

The branches, leaves, and foliage; ^an5-40-1-3

the bark; ^an5-40-1-4

the shoots; ^an5-40-1-5

the softwood; ^an5-40-1-6

and the hardwood. ^an5-40-1-7

Great sal trees grow in these five ways supported by the Himalayas, the king of mountains. ^an5-40-1-8

In the same way, a family grows in five ways supported by a family head with faith. ^an5-40-1-9

What five? ^an5-40-1-10

Faith, ^an5-40-1-11

ethics, ^an5-40-1-12

learning, ^an5-40-1-13

generosity, ^an5-40-1-14

and wisdom. ^an5-40-1-15

A family grows in these five ways supported by a family head with faith. ^an5-40-1-16

Supported by the mountain crags ^an5-40-2-1

in the wilds, the formidable forest, ^an5-40-2-2

the tree grows ^an5-40-2-3

to become lord of the forest. ^an5-40-2-4

So too, when the family head ^an5-40-3-1

is ethical and faithful, ^an5-40-3-2

supported by them, they grow: ^an5-40-3-3

children, partners, and kin, ^an5-40-3-4

colleagues, relatives, ^an5-40-3-5

and those dependent for their livelihood. ^an5-40-3-6

Seeing the ethical conduct of the virtuous, ^an5-40-4-1

the generosity and good deeds, ^an5-40-4-2

those who see clearly ^an5-40-4-3

do likewise. ^an5-40-4-4

Having practiced the teaching here, ^an5-40-5-1

the path that goes to a good place, ^an5-40-5-2

they delight in the heavenly realm, ^an5-40-5-3

enjoying all the pleasures they desire.” ^an5-40-5-4

### Getting Rich (Numbered Discourses 5.41) ^an5-41-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-41-1-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an5-41-1-2

“Householder, there are these five reasons to get rich. ^an5-41-1-3

What five? ^an5-41-1-4

Firstly, with his legitimate wealth—earned by his efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow—a noble disciple makes himself happy and pleased, keeping himself properly happy. ^an5-41-1-5

He makes his mother and father happy … ^an5-41-1-6

He makes his children, partners, bondservants, workers, and staff happy … ^an5-41-1-7

This is the first reason to get rich. ^an5-41-1-8

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he makes his friends and colleagues happy … ^an5-41-2-1

This is the second reason to get rich. ^an5-41-2-2

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he protects himself against losses from such things as fire, water, kings, bandits, or unloved heirs. ^an5-41-3-1

He keeps himself safe. ^an5-41-3-3

This is the third reason to get rich. ^an5-41-3-4

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he makes five spirit-offerings: ^an5-41-4-1

to relatives, guests, ancestors, king, and deities. ^an5-41-4-2

This is the fourth reason to get rich. ^an5-41-4-3

Furthermore, with his legitimate wealth he establishes an uplifting religious donation for ascetics and brahmins—those who refrain from intoxication and negligence, are settled in patience and gentleness, and who tame, calm, and extinguish themselves—that’s conducive to heaven, ripens in happiness, and leads to heaven. ^an5-41-5-1

This is the fifth reason to get rich. ^an5-41-5-2

These are the five reasons to get rich. ^an5-41-5-3

Now if the riches a noble disciple gets for these five reasons run out, he thinks: ^an5-41-6-1

‘So, the riches I have obtained for these reasons are running out.’ ^an5-41-6-2

And so he has no regrets. ^an5-41-6-3

But if the riches a noble disciple gets for these five reasons increase, he thinks: ^an5-41-6-4

‘So, the riches I have obtained for these reasons are increasing.’ ^an5-41-6-5

And so he has no regrets in both cases. ^an5-41-6-6

‘I’ve enjoyed my wealth, <j>supporting those who depend on me; ^an5-41-7-1

I’ve overcome losses; ^an5-41-7-2

I’ve given uplifting religious donations; ^an5-41-7-3

and made the five spirit-offerings. ^an5-41-7-4

I have looked after the ethical and ^an5-41-7-5

disciplined spiritual practitioners. ^an5-41-7-6

I’ve achieved the goal ^an5-41-8-1

for which an astute layperson ^an5-41-8-2

wishes to gain wealth. ^an5-41-8-3

I don’t regret what I’ve done.’ ^an5-41-8-4

A mortal man who recollects this ^an5-41-9-1

stands firm in the teaching of the noble ones. ^an5-41-9-2

They’re praised in this life by the astute, ^an5-41-9-3

and they depart to rejoice in heaven.” ^an5-41-9-4

### A True Person (Numbered Discourses 5.42) ^an5-42-0

“Mendicants, a true person is born in a family for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. ^an5-42-1-1

For the benefit, welfare, and happiness of mother and father; ^an5-42-1-2

children and partners; ^an5-42-1-3

bondservants, workers, and staff; ^an5-42-1-4

friends and colleagues; ^an5-42-1-5

and ascetics and brahmins. ^an5-42-1-6

It’s like a great rain cloud, which nourishes all the crops for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. ^an5-42-2-1

In the same way, a true person is born in a family for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. … ^an5-42-2-2

The gods protect one who is guarded by principle, ^an5-42-3-1

who uses their wealth for the welfare of the many. ^an5-42-3-2

One who is learned, <j>with precepts and observances intact, ^an5-42-3-3

and steady in principle, doesn’t lose their reputation. ^an5-42-3-4

Firm in principle, accomplished in ethical conduct, ^an5-42-4-1

truthful, conscientious; ^an5-42-4-2

like a pendant of Black Plum River gold, ^an5-42-4-3

who is worthy to criticize them? ^an5-42-4-4

Even the gods praise them, ^an5-42-4-5

and by the Divinity, too, they’re praised.” ^an5-42-4-6

### Likable (Numbered Discourses 5.43) ^an5-43-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an5-43-1-1

“Householder, these five things, which are likable, desirable, and agreeable are hard to get in the world. ^an5-43-2-1

What five? ^an5-43-2-2

Long life, ^an5-43-2-3

beauty, ^an5-43-2-4

happiness, ^an5-43-2-5

fame, ^an5-43-2-6

and heaven. ^an5-43-2-7

These are the five things, which are likable, desirable, and agreeable, but hard to get in the world. ^an5-43-2-8

And I say that these five things are not got by supplication or wishing for them. ^an5-43-3-1

If they were, who would lack them? ^an5-43-3-2

A noble disciple who wants to live long ought not pray for it, or hope for it, or pine for it. ^an5-43-4-1

Instead, they should practice the way that leads to long life. ^an5-43-4-2

For by practicing that way they gain long life ^an5-43-4-3

as a god or a human being. ^an5-43-4-4

A noble disciple who wants to be beautiful ought not pray for it, or hope for it, or pine for it. ^an5-43-5-1

Instead, they should practice the way that leads to beauty. ^an5-43-5-2

For by practicing that way they gain beauty ^an5-43-5-3

as a god or a human being. ^an5-43-5-4

A noble disciple who wants to be happy ought not pray for it, or hope for it, or pine for it. ^an5-43-6-1

Instead, they should practice the way that leads to happiness. ^an5-43-6-2

For by practicing that way they gain happiness ^an5-43-6-3

as a god or a human being. ^an5-43-6-4

A noble disciple who wants to be famous ought not pray for it, or hope for it, or pine for it. ^an5-43-7-1

Instead, they should practice the way that leads to fame. ^an5-43-7-2

For by practicing that way they gain fame ^an5-43-7-3

as a god or a human being. ^an5-43-7-4

A noble disciple who wants to go to heaven ought not pray for it, or hope for it, or pine for it. ^an5-43-8-1

Instead, they should practice the way that leads to heaven. ^an5-43-8-2

For by practicing that way they gain heaven, ^an5-43-8-3

they are one who gains the heavens. ^an5-43-8-4

For one who desires lofty delights ^an5-43-9-1

again and again— ^an5-43-9-2

long life, beauty, fame and reputation, ^an5-43-9-3

heaven, and an eminent family— ^an5-43-9-4

the astute praise diligence ^an5-43-10-1

in making merit. ^an5-43-10-2

Being diligent, an astute person ^an5-43-10-3

secures both benefits: ^an5-43-10-4

the benefit in this life, ^an5-43-11-1

and in lives to come. ^an5-43-11-2

An attentive one, comprehending the meaning, ^an5-43-11-3

is called ‘astute’.” ^an5-43-11-4

### Agreeable (Numbered Discourses 5.44) ^an5-44-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-44-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Ugga of Vesālī, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an5-44-1-2

Then Ugga went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-44-1-3

“Sir, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an5-44-2-1

‘The giver of the agreeable gets the agreeable.’ ^an5-44-2-2

My sal flower porridge is agreeable: ^an5-44-2-3

may the Buddha please accept it from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-2-4

So the Buddha accepted it out of sympathy. ^an5-44-2-5

“Sir, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an5-44-3-1

‘The giver of the agreeable gets the agreeable.’ ^an5-44-3-2

My pork with jujube is agreeable: ^an5-44-3-3

may the Buddha please accept it from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-3-4

So the Buddha accepted it out of sympathy. ^an5-44-3-5

“… My fried vegetable stalks are agreeable: ^an5-44-4-3

may the Buddha please accept them from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-4-4

So the Buddha accepted them out of sympathy. ^an5-44-4-5

“… My boiled fine rice with the dark grains picked out, served with many soups and sauces is agreeable: ^an5-44-5-3

may the Buddha please accept it from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-5-4

So the Buddha accepted it out of sympathy. ^an5-44-5-5

“… My cloths imported from Kāsi are agreeable: ^an5-44-6-3

may the Buddha please accept them from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-6-4

So the Buddha accepted them out of sympathy. ^an5-44-6-5

“… My couch spread with woolen covers—shag-piled or embroidered with flowers—and spread with a fine deer hide, with a canopy above and red pillows at both ends is agreeable. ^an5-44-7-3

But, sir, I know that ^an5-44-7-4

this is not proper for the Buddha. ^an5-44-7-5

However, this plank of sandalwood is worth over a thousand dollars. ^an5-44-7-6

May the Buddha please accept it from me out of sympathy.” ^an5-44-7-7

So the Buddha accepted it out of sympathy. ^an5-44-7-8

And then the Buddha rejoiced with Ugga with these verses of appreciation: ^an5-44-7-9

“The giver of the agreeable gets the agreeable, ^an5-44-8-1

enthusiastically giving clothing, bedding, ^an5-44-8-2

food and drink, and various requisites ^an5-44-8-3

to those of sincere conduct. ^an5-44-8-4

Knowing the perfected ones to be like a field ^an5-44-9-1

for what’s given, offered and not held back, ^an5-44-9-2

a true person gives what’s hard to give: ^an5-44-9-3

the giver of the agreeable gets the agreeable.” ^an5-44-9-4

And then the Buddha, having rejoiced with Ugga with these verses of appreciation, rose from his seat and left. ^an5-44-10-1

Then after some time Ugga passed away, ^an5-44-11-1

and was reborn in a host of mind-made gods. ^an5-44-11-2

At that time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-44-11-3

Then, late at night, the glorious godling Ugga, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an5-44-11-4

“Ugga, I trust it is all you wished?” ^an5-44-11-5

“Sir, it is indeed just as I wished.” ^an5-44-11-6

Then the Buddha addressed Ugga in verse: ^an5-44-11-7

“The giver of the agreeable gets the agreeable, ^an5-44-12-1

the giver of the foremost gets the foremost, ^an5-44-12-2

the giver of the excellent gets the excellent, ^an5-44-12-3

the giver of the best gets the best. ^an5-44-12-4

A person who gives the foremost, ^an5-44-13-1

the excellent, the best: ^an5-44-13-2

they’re long-lived and famous ^an5-44-13-3

wherever they’re reborn.” ^an5-44-13-4

### Overflowing Merit (Numbered Discourses 5.45) ^an5-45-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an5-45-1-1

What five? ^an5-45-2-1

When a mendicant enters and remains in a limitless immersion of heart while using a robe … ^an5-45-2-2

almsfood … lodging … bed and chair … ^an5-45-3-1

medicines and supplies for the sick, the overflowing of merit for the donor is limitless … ^an5-45-4-1

These are the five kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness, and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness, and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an5-45-4-2

When a noble disciple has these five kinds of overflowing merit and goodness, it’s not easy to grasp how much merit they have by saying that ^an5-45-5-1

this is the extent of their overflowing merit … that leads to happiness. ^an5-45-5-2

It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of merit. ^an5-45-5-3

It’s like trying to grasp how much water is in the ocean. It’s not easy to say: ^an5-45-6-1

‘This is how many gallons, how many hundreds, thousands, hundreds of thousands of gallons there are.’ ^an5-45-6-2

It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of water. ^an5-45-6-3

In the same way, when a noble disciple has these five kinds of overflowing merit and goodness, it’s not easy to grasp how much merit they have: ^an5-45-6-4

‘This is how much this overflowing merit … leads to happiness.’ ^an5-45-6-5

It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of merit. ^an5-45-6-6

Hosts of people use the rivers, ^an5-45-7-1

and though the rivers are many, ^an5-45-7-2

all reach the great deep, the boundless ocean, ^an5-45-7-3

the cruel sea that’s home to precious gems. ^an5-45-7-4

So too, when a person gives food, drink, and clothes; ^an5-45-8-1

and they’re a giver of beds, seats, and mats— ^an5-45-8-2

the streams of merit reach that astute person, ^an5-45-8-3

as the rivers bring their waters to the sea.” ^an5-45-8-4

### Success (Numbered Discourses 5.46) ^an5-46-0

“Mendicants, there are five accomplishments. ^an5-46-1-1

What five? ^an5-46-1-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an5-46-1-3

These are the five accomplishments.” ^an5-46-1-4

### Wealth (Numbered Discourses 5.47) ^an5-47-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of wealth. ^an5-47-1-1

What five? ^an5-47-1-2

The wealth of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an5-47-1-3

And what is the wealth of faith? ^an5-47-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an5-47-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-47-2-3

This is called the wealth of faith. ^an5-47-2-4

And what is the wealth of ethics? ^an5-47-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-47-3-2

This is called the wealth of ethics. ^an5-47-3-3

And what is the wealth of learning? ^an5-47-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-47-4-2

This is called the wealth of learning. ^an5-47-4-3

And what is the wealth of generosity? ^an5-47-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an5-47-5-2

This is called the wealth of generosity. ^an5-47-5-3

And what is the wealth of wisdom? ^an5-47-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-47-6-2

This is called the wealth of wisdom. ^an5-47-6-3

These are the five kinds of wealth. ^an5-47-6-4

Whoever has faith in the Realized One, ^an5-47-7-1

unwavering and well grounded; ^an5-47-7-2

whose ethical conduct is good, ^an5-47-7-3

praised and loved by the noble ones; ^an5-47-7-4

who has confidence in the Saṅgha, ^an5-47-8-1

and correct view: ^an5-47-8-2

they’re said to be prosperous, ^an5-47-8-3

their life is not in vain. ^an5-47-8-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^an5-47-9-1

to faith, ethical behavior, ^an5-47-9-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^an5-47-9-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an5-47-9-4

### Things That Cannot Be Had (Numbered Discourses 5.48) ^an5-48-0

“Mendicants, there are five things that cannot be had by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-48-1-1

What five? ^an5-48-1-2

That someone liable to old age should not grow old. ^an5-48-1-3

That someone liable to sickness should not get sick. … That someone liable to death should not die. … That someone liable to ending should not end. … That someone liable to perishing should not perish. … ^an5-48-1-4

An unlearned ordinary person has someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-48-2-1

But they don’t reflect on old age: ^an5-48-2-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to old age who grows old. For as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn, they all have someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-48-2-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to old age grows old, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-48-2-4

And so, when someone liable to old age grows old, they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-48-2-5

This is called ^an5-48-2-6

an unlearned ordinary person struck by sorrow’s poisoned arrow, who only mortifies themselves. ^an5-48-2-7

Furthermore, an unlearned ordinary person has someone liable to sickness … death … ending … perishing. ^an5-48-3-1

But they don’t reflect on perishing: ^an5-48-3-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to perishing who perishes. For as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn, they all have someone liable to perishing who perishes. ^an5-48-3-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to perishing perishes, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-48-3-4

And so, when someone liable to perishing perishes, they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-48-3-5

This is called ^an5-48-3-6

an unlearned ordinary person struck by sorrow’s poisoned arrow, who only mortifies themselves. ^an5-48-3-7

A learned noble disciple has someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-48-4-1

So they reflect on old age: ^an5-48-4-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to old age who grows old. For as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn, they all have someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-48-4-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to old age grows old, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-48-4-4

And so, when someone liable to old age grows old, they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-48-4-5

This is called ^an5-48-4-6

a learned noble disciple who has drawn out sorrow’s poisoned arrow, struck by which unlearned ordinary people only mortify themselves. ^an5-48-4-7

Sorrowless, free of thorns, that noble disciple only extinguishes themselves. ^an5-48-4-8

Furthermore, a learned noble disciple has someone liable to sickness … death … ending … perishing. ^an5-48-5-1

So they reflect on perishing: ^an5-48-5-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to perishing who perishes. For as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn, they all have someone liable to perishing who perishes. ^an5-48-5-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to perishing perishes, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-48-5-4

And so, when someone liable to perishing perishes, they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-48-5-5

This is called ^an5-48-5-6

a learned noble disciple who has drawn out sorrow’s poisoned arrow, struck by which unlearned ordinary people only mortify themselves. ^an5-48-5-7

Sorrowless, free of thorns, that noble disciple only extinguishes themselves. ^an5-48-5-8

These are the five things that cannot be had by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-48-6-1

Sorrowing and lamenting ^an5-48-7-1

doesn’t do even a little bit of good. ^an5-48-7-2

When they know that you’re sad, ^an5-48-7-3

your enemies are encouraged. ^an5-48-7-4

When an astute person doesn’t waver <j>in the face of adversity, ^an5-48-8-1

as they’re able to evaluate what’s beneficial, ^an5-48-8-2

their enemies suffer, ^an5-48-8-3

seeing that their normal expression doesn’t change. ^an5-48-8-4

Chants, recitations, fine sayings, ^an5-48-9-1

charity or traditions: ^an5-48-9-2

if by means of any such things you benefit, ^an5-48-9-3

then by all means keep doing them. ^an5-48-9-4

But if you understand that ‘this good thing ^an5-48-10-1

can’t be had by me or by anyone else’, ^an5-48-10-2

you should accept it without sorrowing, thinking: ^an5-48-10-3

‘The deed is powerful. What can I do now?’” ^an5-48-10-4

### The King of Kosala (Numbered Discourses 5.49) ^an5-49-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-49-1-1

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an5-49-1-2

Then a man went up to the king and whispered in his ear, ^an5-49-2-2

“Your Majesty, Queen Mallikā has passed away.” ^an5-49-2-3

When this was said, King Pasenadi was miserable and sad. He sat with his shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say. ^an5-49-2-4

Knowing this, the Buddha said to him, ^an5-49-3-1

“Great king, there are five things that cannot be had by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-49-3-2

What five? ^an5-49-3-3

That someone liable to old age should not grow old. … Sorrowing and lamenting doesn’t do even a little bit of good … ‘The deed is powerful. What can I do now?’” ^an5-49-3-4

### With Nārada (Numbered Discourses 5.50) ^an5-50-0

At one time Venerable Nārada was staying near Pāṭaliputta, in the Chicken Monastery. ^an5-50-1-1

Now at that time King Muṇḍa’s dear and beloved Queen Bhaddā had just passed away. ^an5-50-1-2

And since that time, the king did not bathe, anoint himself, eat his meals, or apply himself to his work. ^an5-50-1-3

Day and night he brooded over Queen Bhaddā’s corpse. ^an5-50-1-4

Then King Muṇḍa addressed Piyaka, the keeper of the treasury, ^an5-50-1-5

“So, my good Piyaka, please place Queen Bhaddā’s corpse in an iron casket filled with oil. Then close it up with another casket, so that we can view Queen Bhaddā’s body even longer.” ^an5-50-1-6

“Yes, Your Majesty,” replied Piyaka, and he did as the king instructed. ^an5-50-1-7

Then it occurred to Piyaka, ^an5-50-2-1

“King Muṇḍa’s dear and beloved Queen Bhaddā has passed away. ^an5-50-2-2

Since then the king does not bathe, anoint himself, eat his meals, or apply himself to his work. ^an5-50-2-3

Day and night he broods over Queen Bhaddā’s corpse. ^an5-50-2-4

Now, what ascetic or brahmin might the king pay homage to, whose teaching could help the king give up sorrow’s arrow?” ^an5-50-2-5

Then it occurred to Piyaka, ^an5-50-3-1

“This Venerable Nārada is staying in the Chicken Monastery at Pāṭaliputta. ^an5-50-3-2

He has this good reputation: ^an5-50-3-3

‘He is astute, competent, intelligent, learned, a brilliant speaker, eloquent, mature, a perfected one.’ ^an5-50-3-4

What if King Muṇḍa was to pay homage to Venerable Nārada? Hopefully when he hears Nārada’s teaching, the king could give up sorrow’s arrow.” ^an5-50-3-5

Then Piyaka went to the king and said to him, ^an5-50-4-1

“Sire, this Venerable Nārada is staying in the Chicken Monastery at Pāṭaliputta. ^an5-50-4-2

He has this good reputation: ^an5-50-4-3

‘He is astute, competent, intelligent, learned, a brilliant speaker, eloquent, mature, a perfected one.’ ^an5-50-4-4

What if Your Majesty was to pay homage to Venerable Nārada? Hopefully when you hear Nārada’s teaching, you could give up sorrow’s arrow.” ^an5-50-4-5

“Well then, my good Piyaka, let Nārada know. ^an5-50-4-6

For how on earth could one such as I presume to visit an ascetic or brahmin in my realm without letting them know beforehand?” ^an5-50-4-7

“Yes, Your Majesty,” replied Piyaka. He went to Nārada, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-50-4-8

“Sir, King Muṇḍa’s dear and beloved Queen Bhaddā has passed away. ^an5-50-5-1

And since she passed away, the king has not bathed, anointed himself, eaten his meals, or got his business done. ^an5-50-5-2

Day and night he broods over Queen Bhaddā’s corpse. ^an5-50-5-3

Sir, please teach the king so that, when he hears your teaching, he can give up sorrow’s arrow.” ^an5-50-5-4

“Please, Piyaka, let the king come when he likes.” ^an5-50-5-5

Then Piyaka rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled Venerable Nārada, keeping him on his right, before going to the king and saying, ^an5-50-6-1

“Sire, the request for an audience with Venerable Nārada has been granted. ^an5-50-6-2

Please, Your Majesty, go at your convenience.” ^an5-50-6-3

“Well then, my good Piyaka, harness the finest chariots.” ^an5-50-6-4

“Yes, Your Majesty,” replied Piyaka. He did so, then told the king: ^an5-50-6-5

“Sire, the finest chariots are harnessed. ^an5-50-6-6

Please, Your Majesty, go at your convenience.” ^an5-50-6-7

Then King Muṇḍa mounted a fine carriage and, along with other fine carriages, set out in full royal pomp to see Venerable Nārada at the Chicken Monastery. ^an5-50-7-1

He went by carriage as far as the terrain allowed, then descended and entered the monastery on foot. ^an5-50-7-2

Then the king went up to Nārada, bowed, and sat down to one side. Then Nārada said to him: ^an5-50-7-3

“Great king, there are five things that cannot be had by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-50-8-1

What five? ^an5-50-8-2

That someone liable to old age should not grow old. … ^an5-50-8-3

That someone liable to sickness should not get sick. … That someone liable to death should not die. … That someone liable to ending should not end. … That someone liable to perishing should not perish. … ^an5-50-8-4

An unlearned ordinary person has someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-50-9-1

But they don’t reflect on old age: ^an5-50-9-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to old age who grows old. For all sentient beings have someone liable to old age who grows old, as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn. ^an5-50-9-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to old age grows old, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-50-9-4

And so, when someone liable to old age grows old, they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-50-9-5

This is called ^an5-50-9-6

an unlearned ordinary person struck by sorrow’s poisoned arrow, who only mortifies themselves. ^an5-50-9-7

Furthermore, an unlearned ordinary person has someone liable to sickness … death … ending … perishing. ^an5-50-10-1

But they don’t reflect on perishing: ^an5-50-10-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to perishing who perishes. For all sentient beings have someone liable to perishing who perishes, as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn. ^an5-50-10-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to perishing perishes, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-50-10-4

And so, when someone liable to perishing perishes, they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-50-10-5

This is called ^an5-50-10-6

an unlearned ordinary person struck by sorrow’s poisoned arrow, who only mortifies themselves. ^an5-50-10-7

A learned noble disciple has someone liable to old age who grows old. ^an5-50-11-1

So they reflect on old age: ^an5-50-11-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to old age who grows old. For all sentient beings have someone liable to old age who grows old, as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn. ^an5-50-11-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to old age grows old, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-50-11-4

And so, when someone liable to old age grows old, they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-50-11-5

This is called ^an5-50-11-6

a learned noble disciple who has drawn out sorrow’s poisoned arrow, struck by which unlearned ordinary people only mortify themselves. ^an5-50-11-7

Sorrowless, free of thorns, that noble disciple only extinguishes themselves. ^an5-50-11-8

Furthermore, a learned noble disciple has someone liable to sickness … death … ending … perishing. ^an5-50-12-1

So they reflect on perishing: ^an5-50-12-2

‘It’s not just me who has someone liable to perishing who perishes. For all sentient beings have someone liable to perishing who perishes, as long as sentient beings come and go, pass away and are reborn. ^an5-50-12-3

If I were to sorrow and wail and lament, beating my breast and falling into confusion, just because someone liable to perishing perishes, I’d lose my appetite and my physical appearance would deteriorate. My work wouldn’t get done, my enemies would be encouraged, and my friends would be dispirited.’ ^an5-50-12-4

And so, when someone liable to perishing perishes, they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion. ^an5-50-12-5

This is called ^an5-50-12-6

a learned noble disciple who has drawn out sorrow’s poisoned arrow, struck by which unlearned ordinary people only mortify themselves. ^an5-50-12-7

Sorrowless, free of thorns, that noble disciple only extinguishes themselves. ^an5-50-12-8

These are the five things that cannot be had by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-50-13-1

Sorrowing and lamenting ^an5-50-14-1

doesn’t do even a little bit of good. ^an5-50-14-2

When they know that you’re sad, ^an5-50-14-3

your enemies are encouraged. ^an5-50-14-4

When an astute person doesn’t waver <j>in the face of adversity, ^an5-50-15-1

as they’re able to evaluate what’s beneficial, ^an5-50-15-2

their enemies suffer, ^an5-50-15-3

seeing that their normal expression doesn’t change. ^an5-50-15-4

Chants, recitations, fine sayings, ^an5-50-16-1

charity or traditions: ^an5-50-16-2

if by means of any such things you benefit, ^an5-50-16-3

then by all means keep doing them. ^an5-50-16-4

But if you understand that ‘this good thing ^an5-50-17-1

can’t be had by me or by anyone else’, ^an5-50-17-2

you should accept it without sorrowing, thinking: ^an5-50-17-3

‘The deed is powerful. What can I do now?’” ^an5-50-17-4

When he said this, King Muṇḍa said to Venerable Nārada, ^an5-50-18-1

“Sir, what is the name of this exposition of the teaching?” ^an5-50-18-2

“Great king, this exposition of the teaching is named ‘Pulling Out Sorrow’s Arrow’.” ^an5-50-18-3

“Indeed, sir, this is the pulling out of sorrow’s arrow! ^an5-50-18-4

Hearing this exposition of the teaching, I’ve given up sorrow’s arrow.” ^an5-50-18-5

Then King Muṇḍa addressed Piyaka, the keeper of the treasury, ^an5-50-19-1

“Well then, my good Piyaka, cremate Queen Bhaddā’s corpse and build a monument. ^an5-50-19-2

From this day forth, I will bathe, anoint myself, eat my meals, and apply myself to my work.” ^an5-50-19-3

### Obstacles (Numbered Discourses 5.51) ^an5-51-0

So I have heard. ^an5-51-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-51-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an5-51-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an5-51-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an5-51-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an5-51-1-6

“Mendicants, there are these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. ^an5-51-2-1

What five? ^an5-51-2-2

Sensual desire … ^an5-51-2-3

Ill will … ^an5-51-2-4

Dullness and drowsiness … ^an5-51-2-5

Restlessness and remorse … ^an5-51-2-6

Doubt … ^an5-51-2-7

These are the five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. ^an5-51-2-8

Take a mendicant who has feeble and weak wisdom, not having given up these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. It is quite impossible that they would know what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both; or that they would realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an5-51-3-1

Suppose there was a mountain river that flowed swiftly, going far, carrying all before it. ^an5-51-3-2

But then a man would open channels on both sides, ^an5-51-3-3

so the mid-river current would be dispersed, displaced, and separated. The river would no longer flow swiftly, going far, carrying all before it. ^an5-51-3-4

In the same way, take a mendicant who has feeble and weak wisdom, not having given up these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. It is not possible that they would know what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both; or that they would realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an5-51-3-5

Take a mendicant who has powerful wisdom, having given up these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. It’s quite possible that they would know what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both; or that they would realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an5-51-4-1

Suppose there was a mountain river that flowed swiftly, going far, carrying all before it. ^an5-51-4-2

But then a man would close up the channels on both sides, ^an5-51-4-3

so the mid-river current would not be dispersed, displaced, and separated. The river would keep flowing swiftly for a long way, carrying all before it. ^an5-51-4-4

In the same way, take a mendicant who has powerful wisdom, having given up these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom. It’s quite possible that they would know what’s for their own good, the good of another, or the good of both; or that they would realize any superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.” ^an5-51-4-5

### A Heap of the Unskillful (Numbered Discourses 5.52) ^an5-52-0

“Mendicants, rightly speaking, you’d call the five hindrances a ‘heap of the unskillful’. ^an5-52-1-1

For these five hindrances are entirely a heap of the unskillful. ^an5-52-1-2

What five? ^an5-52-1-3

The hindrances of sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an5-52-1-4

Rightly speaking, you’d call these five hindrances a ‘heap of the unskillful’. ^an5-52-1-5

For these five hindrances are entirely a heap of the unskillful.” ^an5-52-1-6

### Factors That Support Meditation (Numbered Discourses 5.53) ^an5-53-0

“Mendicants, there are these five factors that support meditation. ^an5-53-1-1

What five? ^an5-53-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an5-53-1-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-53-1-4

They are rarely ill or unwell. ^an5-53-1-5

Their stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an5-53-1-6

They’re not devious or deceitful. ^an5-53-1-7

They reveal themselves honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions. ^an5-53-1-8

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-53-1-9

They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-53-1-10

These are the five factors that support meditation.” ^an5-53-1-11

### Times Good for Meditation (Numbered Discourses 5.54) ^an5-54-0

“Mendicants, there are five times that are not good for meditation. ^an5-54-1-1

What five? ^an5-54-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant is old, overcome with old age. ^an5-54-1-3

This is the first time that’s not good for meditation. ^an5-54-1-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is sick, overcome by sickness. ^an5-54-2-1

This is the second time that’s not good for meditation. ^an5-54-2-2

Furthermore, there’s a famine, a bad harvest, so it’s hard to get almsfood, and not easy to keep going by collecting alms. ^an5-54-3-1

This is the third time that’s not good for meditation. ^an5-54-3-2

Furthermore, there’s peril due to turmoil in the wilds, so the countryfolk mount their vehicles and flee everywhere. ^an5-54-4-1

This is the fourth time that’s not good for meditation. ^an5-54-4-2

Furthermore, there’s a schism in the Saṅgha. ^an5-54-5-1

When the Saṅgha is split, they abuse, insult, block, and forsake each other. ^an5-54-5-2

This doesn’t inspire confidence in those without it, and it causes some with confidence to change their minds. ^an5-54-5-3

This is the fifth time that’s not good for meditation. ^an5-54-5-4

These are the five times that are not good for meditation. ^an5-54-5-5

There are five times that are good for meditation. ^an5-54-6-1

What five? ^an5-54-6-2

Firstly, a mendicant is a youth, young, with pristine black hair, blessed with youth, in the prime of life. ^an5-54-6-3

This is the first time that’s good for meditation. ^an5-54-6-4

Furthermore, they are rarely ill or unwell. Their stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an5-54-7-1

This is the second time that’s good for meditation. ^an5-54-7-2

Furthermore, there’s plenty of food, a good harvest, so it’s easy to get almsfood, and easy to keep going by collecting alms. ^an5-54-8-1

This is the third time that’s good for meditation. ^an5-54-8-2

Furthermore, people live in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, blending like milk and water, and regarding each other with kindly eyes. ^an5-54-9-1

This is the fourth time that’s good for meditation. ^an5-54-9-2

Furthermore, the Saṅgha lives comfortably, in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, with one recitation. ^an5-54-10-1

When the Saṅgha is in harmony, they don’t abuse, insult, block, or forsake each other. ^an5-54-10-2

This inspires confidence in those without it, and increases confidence in those who have it. ^an5-54-10-3

This is the fifth time that’s good for meditation. ^an5-54-10-4

These are the five times that are good for meditation.” ^an5-54-10-5

### Mother and Son (Numbered Discourses 5.55) ^an5-55-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-55-1-1

Now, at that time a mother and son had both entered the rainy season residence at Sāvatthī, ^an5-55-1-2

as a monk and a nun. ^an5-55-1-3

They wanted to see each other often. ^an5-55-1-4

The mother wanted to see her son often, ^an5-55-1-5

and the son his mother. ^an5-55-1-6

Seeing each other often, they became close. ^an5-55-1-7

Being so close, they became intimate. ^an5-55-1-8

And being intimate, lust overcame them. ^an5-55-1-9

With their minds swamped by lust, without resigning the training and declaring their inability to continue, they had sex. ^an5-55-1-10

Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an5-55-2-1

“Mendicants, how could that futile man imagine that ^an5-55-3-1

a mother cannot lust for her son, or that a son cannot lust for his mother? ^an5-55-3-2

Compared to the sight of a woman, I do not see a single sight that is so arousing, sensuous, intoxicating, captivating, and infatuating, and such an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an5-55-3-3

Sentient beings are lustful, greedy, tied, infatuated, and attached to the sight of a woman. ^an5-55-3-4

They sorrow for a long time under the sway of a woman’s sight. ^an5-55-3-5

Compared to the sound … smell … taste … touch of a woman, I do not see a single touch that is so arousing, sensuous, intoxicating, captivating, and infatuating, and such an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an5-55-4-1

Sentient beings are lustful, greedy, tied, infatuated, and attached to the touch of a woman. ^an5-55-4-2

They sorrow for a long time under the sway of a woman’s touch. ^an5-55-4-3

When a woman walks, she occupies a man’s mind. ^an5-55-5-1

When a woman stands … sits … lies down … laughs … speaks … sings … cries … is injured, she occupies a man’s mind. Even when a woman is dead, she occupies a man’s mind. ^an5-55-5-2

For if anyone should be rightly called ‘an all-round snare of Māra’, it’s ladies. ^an5-55-5-3

You might chat with someone who has knife in hand. ^an5-55-6-1

You might even chat with a goblin. ^an5-55-6-2

You might sit close by a viper, ^an5-55-6-3

whose bite would take your life. ^an5-55-6-4

But never should you chat ^an5-55-6-5

one on one with a lady. ^an5-55-6-6

They captivate the unmindful ^an5-55-7-1

with a glance and a smile. ^an5-55-7-2

Or scantily clad, ^an5-55-7-3

they speak charming words. ^an5-55-7-4

It’s not good to sit with such a person, ^an5-55-7-5

even if she’s injured or dead. ^an5-55-7-6

These five kinds of sensual stimulation ^an5-55-8-1

are apparent in a woman’s body: ^an5-55-8-2

sights, sounds, tastes, smells, ^an5-55-8-3

and touches so delightful. ^an5-55-8-4

Those swept away by the flood of sensual pleasures, ^an5-55-9-1

not comprehending them, ^an5-55-9-2

are governed by transmigration— ^an5-55-9-3

time and destination, and life after life. ^an5-55-9-4

But those who fully understand sensual pleasures ^an5-55-10-1

live fearing nothing from any quarter. ^an5-55-10-2

They are those in the world who’ve crossed over, ^an5-55-10-3

having reached the ending of defilements.” ^an5-55-10-4

### Mentor (Numbered Discourses 5.56) ^an5-56-0

Then a mendicant went up to his own mentor, and said, ^an5-56-1-1

“Now, sir, my body feels like it’s drugged. I’m disorientated, the teachings don’t spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness fill my mind. I lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, and have doubts about the teachings.” ^an5-56-1-2

Then that mendicant took his protégé to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-56-2-1

“Sir, this mendicant says this: ^an5-56-2-2

‘Now, sir, my body feels like it’s drugged. I’m disorientated, the teachings don’t spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness fill my mind. I lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, and have doubts about the teachings.’” ^an5-56-2-3

“That’s how it is, mendicant, when your sense doors are unguarded, you eat too much, you’re not dedicated to wakefulness, you’re unable to discern skillful qualities, and you don’t pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn. Your body feels like it’s drugged. You’re disorientated, the teachings don’t spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness fill your mind. You lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, and have doubts about the teachings. ^an5-56-3-1

So you should train like this: ^an5-56-3-2

‘I will guard my sense doors, eat in moderation, be dedicated to wakefulness, discern skillful qualities, and pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn.’ ^an5-56-3-3

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-56-3-4

When that mendicant had been given this advice by the Buddha, he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an5-56-4-1

Then that mendicant, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-56-4-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an5-56-4-3

And that mendicant became one of the perfected. ^an5-56-4-4

When that mendicant had attained perfection, he went up to his own mentor, and said, ^an5-56-5-1

“Now, sir, my body doesn’t feel like it’s drugged. I’m not disorientated, the teachings spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness don’t fill my mind. I lead the spiritual life satisfied, and have no doubts about the teachings.” ^an5-56-5-2

Then that mendicant took his protégé to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-56-5-3

“Sir, this mendicant says this: ^an5-56-5-4

‘Now, sir, my body doesn’t feel like it’s drugged. I’m not disorientated, the teachings spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness don’t fill my mind. I lead the spiritual life satisfied, and have no doubts about the teachings.’” ^an5-56-5-5

“That’s how it is, mendicant, when your sense doors are guarded, you’re moderate in eating, you’re dedicated to wakefulness, you’re able to discern skillful qualities, and you pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn. Your body doesn’t feel like it’s drugged. You’re not disorientated, the teachings spring to mind, and dullness and drowsiness don’t fill your mind. You lead the spiritual life satisfied, and have no doubts about the teachings. ^an5-56-6-1

So you should train like this: ^an5-56-6-2

‘We will guard our sense doors, eat in moderation, be dedicated to wakefulness, discern skillful qualities, and pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn.’ ^an5-56-6-3

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-56-6-4

### Subjects for Regular Reviewing (Numbered Discourses 5.57) ^an5-57-0

“Mendicants, a woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate should often review these five subjects. ^an5-57-1-1

What five? ^an5-57-1-2

‘I am liable to grow old, I am not exempt from old age.’ A woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate should often review this. ^an5-57-1-3

‘I am liable to get sick, I am not exempt from sickness.’ … ^an5-57-1-4

‘I am liable to die, I am not exempt from death.’ … ^an5-57-1-5

‘I must be parted and separated from all I hold dear and beloved.’ … ^an5-57-1-6

‘I am the owner of my deeds and heir to my deeds. Deeds are my womb, my relative, and my refuge. ^an5-57-1-7

I shall be the heir of whatever deeds I do, whether good or bad.’ ^an5-57-1-8

A woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate should often review this. ^an5-57-1-9

What is the advantage for a woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to grow old, I am not exempt from old age’? ^an5-57-2-1

There are sentient beings who, intoxicated with the vanity of youth, do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-57-2-2

Reviewing this subject often, they entirely give up the vanity of youth, or at least reduce it. ^an5-57-2-3

This is the advantage for a woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to grow old, I am not exempt from old age’. ^an5-57-2-4

What is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to get sick, I am not exempt from sickness’? ^an5-57-3-1

There are sentient beings who, drunk on the vanity of health, do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-57-3-2

Reviewing this subject often, they entirely give up the vanity of health, or at least reduce it. ^an5-57-3-3

This is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to get sick, I am not exempt from sickness’. ^an5-57-3-4

What is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to die, I am not exempt from death’? ^an5-57-4-1

There are sentient beings who, drunk on the vanity of life, do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-57-4-2

Reviewing this subject often, they entirely give up the vanity of life, or at least reduce it. ^an5-57-4-3

This is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I am liable to die, I am not exempt from death’. ^an5-57-4-4

What is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I must be parted and separated from all I hold dear and beloved’? ^an5-57-5-1

There are sentient beings who, aroused by desire and lust for their dear and beloved, do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-57-5-2

Reviewing this subject often, they entirely give up desire and lust for their dear and beloved, or at least reduce it. ^an5-57-5-3

This is the advantage of often reviewing this: ‘I must be parted and separated from all I hold dear and beloved’. ^an5-57-5-4

What is the advantage of often reviewing like this: ‘I am the owner of my deeds and heir to my deeds. Deeds are my womb, my relative, and my refuge. ^an5-57-6-1

I shall be the heir of whatever deeds I do, whether good or bad’? ^an5-57-6-2

There are sentient beings who do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-57-6-3

Reviewing this subject often, they entirely give up bad conduct, or at least reduce it. ^an5-57-6-4

This is the advantage for a woman or a man, a layperson or a renunciate of often reviewing like this: ‘I am the owner of my deeds and heir to my deeds. Deeds are my womb, my relative, and my refuge. ^an5-57-6-5

I shall be the heir of whatever deeds I do, whether good or bad.’ ^an5-57-6-6

Then that noble disciple reflects: ^an5-57-7-1

‘It’s not just me who is liable to grow old, not being exempt from old age. For all sentient beings grow old according to their nature, as long as they come and go, pass away and are reborn.’ ^an5-57-7-2

When they review this subject often, the path is born in them. ^an5-57-7-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an5-57-7-4

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an5-57-7-5

‘It’s not just me who is liable to get sick, not being exempt from sickness. For all sentient beings get sick according to their nature, as long as they come and go, pass away and are reborn.’ ^an5-57-8-1

When they review this subject often, the path is born in them. ^an5-57-8-2

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an5-57-8-3

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an5-57-8-4

‘It’s not just me who is liable to die, not being exempt from death. For all sentient beings die according to their nature, as long as they come and go, pass away and are reborn.’ ^an5-57-9-1

When they review this subject often, the path is born in them. ^an5-57-9-2

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an5-57-9-3

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an5-57-9-4

‘It’s not just me who must be parted and separated from all I hold dear and beloved. For all sentient beings must be parted and separated from all they hold dear and beloved, as long as they come and go, pass away and are reborn.’ ^an5-57-10-1

When they review this subject often, the path is born in them. ^an5-57-10-2

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an5-57-10-3

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an5-57-10-4

‘It’s not just me who shall be the owner of my deeds and heir to my deeds. ^an5-57-11-1

For all sentient beings shall be the owners of their deeds and heirs to their deeds, as long as they come and go, pass away and are reborn.’ ^an5-57-11-2

When they review this subject often, the path is born in them. ^an5-57-11-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of it. ^an5-57-11-4

By doing so, they give up the fetters and eliminate the underlying tendencies. ^an5-57-11-5

For others, sickness is natural, ^an5-57-12-1

and so are old age and death. ^an5-57-12-2

Though this is how their nature is, ^an5-57-12-3

ordinary people feel disgusted. ^an5-57-12-4

If I were to be disgusted ^an5-57-13-1

with creatures whose nature is such, ^an5-57-13-2

it would not be appropriate for me, ^an5-57-13-3

since my life is just the same. ^an5-57-13-4

Living in such a way, ^an5-57-14-1

I understood the truth without attachments. ^an5-57-14-2

I mastered all vanities— ^an5-57-14-3

of health, of youth, ^an5-57-14-4

and even of life— ^an5-57-15-1

seeing renunciation as sanctuary. ^an5-57-15-2

Zeal sprang up in me ^an5-57-15-3

as I looked to extinguishment. ^an5-57-15-4

Now I’m unable ^an5-57-16-1

to indulge in sensual pleasures; ^an5-57-16-2

there’s no turning back, ^an5-57-16-3

I’m committed to the spiritual life.” ^an5-57-16-4

### The Licchavi Youths (Numbered Discourses 5.58) ^an5-58-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-58-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Vesālī for alms. ^an5-58-1-2

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, he plunged deep into the Great Wood and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^an5-58-1-3

Now at that time several Licchavi youths took strung bows and, escorted by a pack of hounds, were going for a walk in the Great Wood when they saw the Buddha seated at the root of a tree. ^an5-58-2-1

When they saw him, they put down their strung bows, dispatched their hounds to one side, and went up to him. They bowed and silently paid homage to the Buddha with cupped palms. ^an5-58-2-2

Now at that time Mahānāma the Licchavi was going for a walk in the Great Wood when he saw those Licchavi youths silently paying homage to the Buddha with cupped palms. ^an5-58-3-1

Seeing this, he went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an5-58-3-2

and expressed this heartfelt sentiment, ^an5-58-3-3

“They will become Vajjis! They will become Vajjis!” ^an5-58-3-4

“But Mahānāma, why do you say that ^an5-58-4-1

they will become Vajjis?” ^an5-58-4-2

“Sir, these Licchavi youths are violent, harsh, and brash. ^an5-58-4-3

Whenever sweets are left out for families—sugar-cane, jujube fruits, pancakes, pies, or fritters—they filch them and eat them up. ^an5-58-4-4

And they hit women and girls of good families on their backs. ^an5-58-4-5

But now they’re silently paying homage to the Buddha with cupped palms.” ^an5-58-4-6

“Mahānāma, you can expect only growth, not decline, when you find five qualities in any gentleman—whether he’s an anointed aristocratic king, an appointed or hereditary official, an army general, a village chief, a guild chief, or a ruler of his own clan. ^an5-58-5-2

What five? ^an5-58-6-1

Firstly, a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth—earned by his efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow—to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate his mother and father. ^an5-58-6-2

Honored in this way, his mother and father love him with a good heart, wishing: ^an5-58-6-3

‘Live long! Stay alive for a long time!’ ^an5-58-6-4

When a gentleman is loved by his mother and father, you can expect only growth, not decline. ^an5-58-6-5

Furthermore, a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate his wives and children, bondservants, workers, and staff. ^an5-58-7-1

Honored in this way, his wives and children, bondservants, workers, and staff love him with a good heart, wishing: ^an5-58-7-2

‘Live long! Stay alive for a long time!’ ^an5-58-7-3

When a gentleman is loved by his wives and children, bondservants, workers, and staff, you can expect only growth, not decline. ^an5-58-7-4

Furthermore, a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate those who work the neighboring fields, and those he does business with. ^an5-58-8-1

Honored in this way, those who work the neighboring fields, and those he does business with love him with a good heart, wishing: ^an5-58-8-2

‘Live long! Stay alive for a long time!’ ^an5-58-8-3

When a gentleman is loved by those who work the neighboring fields, and those he does business with, you can expect only growth, not decline. ^an5-58-8-4

Furthermore, a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the deities who receive spirit-offerings. ^an5-58-9-1

Honored in this way, the deities who receive spirit-offerings love him with a good heart, wishing: ^an5-58-9-2

‘Live long! Stay alive for a long time!’ ^an5-58-9-3

When a gentleman is loved by the deities, you can expect only growth, not decline. ^an5-58-9-4

Furthermore, a gentleman uses his legitimate wealth to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate ascetics and brahmins. ^an5-58-10-1

Honored in this way, ascetics and brahmins love him with a good heart, wishing: ^an5-58-10-2

‘Live long! Stay alive for a long time!’ ^an5-58-10-3

When a gentleman is loved by ascetics and brahmins, you can expect only growth, not decline. ^an5-58-10-4

You can expect only growth, not decline, when you find these five qualities in any gentleman— ^an5-58-11-1

whether he’s an anointed aristocratic king, an appointed or hereditary official, an army general, a village chief, a guild chief, or a ruler of his own clan. ^an5-58-11-2

He’s always dutiful to his mother and father, ^an5-58-12-1

and for the good of his wives and children. ^an5-58-12-2

He looks after those in his household, ^an5-58-12-3

and those dependent on him for their livelihood. ^an5-58-12-4

A bountiful and ethical person ^an5-58-13-1

looks after the welfare of relatives— ^an5-58-13-2

both those who have passed away, ^an5-58-13-3

and those alive at present. ^an5-58-13-4

While living at home, an astute person ^an5-58-14-1

uses legitimate means to give rise to joy ^an5-58-14-2

for ascetics, brahmins, ^an5-58-14-3

and also the gods. ^an5-58-14-4

Having done good, ^an5-58-15-1

he’s venerable and praiseworthy. ^an5-58-15-2

They praise him in this life, ^an5-58-15-3

and he departs to rejoice in heaven.” ^an5-58-15-4

### Gone Forth When Old (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.59) ^an5-59-0

“Mendicants, it’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who has five qualities. ^an5-59-1-1

What five? ^an5-59-1-2

It’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who is sophisticated, well-presented, and learned, who can teach Dhamma, and has memorized the monastic law. ^an5-59-1-3

It’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who possesses these five qualities.” ^an5-59-1-4

### Gone Forth When Old (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.60) ^an5-60-0

“Mendicants, it’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who has five qualities. ^an5-60-1-1

What five? ^an5-60-1-2

It’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who is easy to admonish, retains what they learn, and learns respectfully, who can teach the Dhamma, and has memorized the monastic law. ^an5-60-1-3

It’s hard to find someone gone forth when old who possesses these five qualities.” ^an5-60-1-4

### Perceptions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.61) ^an5-61-0

“Mendicants, these five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an5-61-1-1

What five? ^an5-61-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, drawbacks, repulsiveness of food, and dissatisfaction with the whole world. ^an5-61-1-3

These five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination.” ^an5-61-1-4

### Perceptions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.62) ^an5-62-0

“Mendicants, these five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an5-62-1-1

What five? ^an5-62-1-2

The perceptions of impermanence, not-self, death, repulsiveness of food, and dissatisfaction with the whole world. ^an5-62-1-3

These five perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination.” ^an5-62-1-4

### Growth (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.63) ^an5-63-0

“Mendicants, a male noble disciple who grows in five ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an5-63-1-1

What five? ^an5-63-1-2

He grows in faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an5-63-1-3

A male noble disciple who grows in these five ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an5-63-1-4

He who grows in faith and ethics, ^an5-63-2-1

wisdom, and both generosity and learning— ^an5-63-2-2

a good man such as he sees clearly, ^an5-63-2-3

and takes on what is essential for himself in this life.” ^an5-63-2-4

### Growth (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.64) ^an5-64-0

“Mendicants, a female noble disciple who grows in five ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an5-64-1-1

What five? ^an5-64-1-2

She grows in faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an5-64-1-3

A female noble disciple who grows in these five ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an5-64-1-4

She who grows in faith and ethics, ^an5-64-2-1

wisdom, and both generosity and learning— ^an5-64-2-2

a virtuous laywoman such as she ^an5-64-2-3

takes on what is essential for herself in this life.” ^an5-64-2-4

### Discussion (Numbered Discourses 5.65) ^an5-65-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is fit to hold a discussion with their spiritual companions. ^an5-65-1-1

What five? ^an5-65-1-2

A mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, and answers questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in ethics. ^an5-65-1-3

They’re personally accomplished in immersion, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in immersion. ^an5-65-1-4

They’re personally accomplished in wisdom, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in wisdom. ^an5-65-1-5

They’re personally accomplished in freedom, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in freedom. ^an5-65-1-6

They’re personally accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-65-1-7

A mendicant with these five qualities is fit to hold a discussion with their spiritual companions.” ^an5-65-1-8

### Sharing Life (Numbered Discourses 5.66) ^an5-66-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is fit to share their life with their spiritual companions. ^an5-66-1-1

What five? ^an5-66-1-2

A mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, and answers questions posed when discussing accomplishment in ethics. ^an5-66-1-3

They’re personally accomplished in immersion, and they answer questions posed when discussing accomplishment in immersion. ^an5-66-1-4

They’re personally accomplished in wisdom, and they answer questions posed when discussing accomplishment in wisdom. ^an5-66-1-5

They’re personally accomplished in freedom, and they answer questions posed when discussing accomplishment in freedom. ^an5-66-1-6

They’re personally accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom, and they answer questions posed when discussing accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-66-1-7

A mendicant with these five qualities is fit to share their life with their spiritual companions.” ^an5-66-1-8

### Bases of Psychic Power (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.67) ^an5-67-0

“Mendicants, any monk or nun who develops and cultivates five qualities can expect one of two results: ^an5-67-1-1

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return. ^an5-67-1-2

What five? ^an5-67-2-1

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort … ^an5-67-2-2

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort … ^an5-67-2-3

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort … ^an5-67-2-4

A mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. ^an5-67-2-5

And the fifth is sheer vigor. ^an5-67-2-6

Any monk or nun who develops and cultivates these five qualities can expect one of two results: ^an5-67-2-7

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return.” ^an5-67-2-8

### Bases of Psychic Power (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.68) ^an5-68-0

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still not awake but intent on awakening—I developed and cultivated five things. ^an5-68-1-1

What five? ^an5-68-1-2

The basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort … ^an5-68-1-3

the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort … ^an5-68-1-4

the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort … ^an5-68-1-5

the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. ^an5-68-1-6

And the fifth is sheer vigor. ^an5-68-1-7

When I had developed and cultivated these five things, with vigor as fifth, ^an5-68-1-8

I extended the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and I was capable of realizing those things, since each and every one was within range. ^an5-68-1-9

If I wished: ‘May I multiply myself and become one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.’ ^an5-68-2-1

I was capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an5-68-2-2

If I wished: … ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an5-68-3-1

I was capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.” ^an5-68-3-2

### Disillusionment (Numbered Discourses 5.69) ^an5-69-0

“Mendicants, these five things, when developed and cultivated, lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an5-69-1-1

What five? ^an5-69-2-1

A mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an5-69-2-2

These five things, when developed and cultivated, lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment.” ^an5-69-2-3

### The Ending of Defilements (Numbered Discourses 5.70) ^an5-70-0

“Mendicants, these five things, when developed and cultivated, lead to the ending of defilements. ^an5-70-1-1

What five? ^an5-70-1-2

A mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an5-70-1-3

These five things, when developed and cultivated, lead to the ending of defilements.” ^an5-70-1-4

### Freedom of Heart is the Fruit (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.71) ^an5-71-0

“Mendicants, these five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit. ^an5-71-1-1

What five? ^an5-71-2-1

A mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an5-71-2-2

These five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit. ^an5-71-2-3

When a mendicant has freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom, ^an5-71-2-4
