such a mendicant is one who is called ‘one who has lifted the cross-bar’, ‘one who has filled in the moat’, ‘one who has pulled up the pillar’, ‘one who is unimpeded’, and also ‘a noble one with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached’. ^an5-71-2-5

And how has a mendicant lifted the cross-bar? ^an5-71-3-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up ignorance, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an5-71-3-2

That’s how a mendicant has lifted the cross-bar. ^an5-71-3-3

And how has a mendicant filled in the moat? ^an5-71-4-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up transmigrating through births in future lives, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an5-71-4-2

That’s how a mendicant has filled in the moat. ^an5-71-4-3

And how has a mendicant pulled up the pillar? ^an5-71-5-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up craving, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an5-71-5-2

That’s how a mendicant has pulled up the pillar. ^an5-71-5-3

And how is a mendicant unimpeded? ^an5-71-6-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up the five lower fetters, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an5-71-6-2

That’s how a mendicant is unimpeded. ^an5-71-6-3

And how is a mendicant a noble one with with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached? ^an5-71-7-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up the conceit ‘I am’, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future. ^an5-71-7-2

That’s how a mendicant is a noble one with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached.” ^an5-71-7-3

### Freedom of Heart is the Fruit (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.72) ^an5-72-0

“Mendicants, these five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit. ^an5-72-1-1

What five? ^an5-72-1-2

The perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of giving up, and the perception of fading away. ^an5-72-1-3

These five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit. ^an5-72-1-4

When a mendicant has freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom, ^an5-72-1-5

such a mendicant is one who is called ‘one who has lifted the cross-bar’, ‘one who has filled in the moat’, ‘one who has pulled up the pillar’, ‘one who is unimpeded’, and also ‘a noble one with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached’. …” ^an5-72-1-6

### One Who Lives by the Teaching (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.73) ^an5-73-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-73-1-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘one who lives by the teaching’. ^an5-73-1-2

How is a mendicant who lives by the teaching defined?” ^an5-73-1-3

“Mendicant, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an5-73-2-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-73-2-2

They spend too much time studying that teaching. They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-73-2-3

That mendicant is called one who studies a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-2-4

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches Dhamma in detail to others as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-73-3-1

They spend too much time advocating that teaching. They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-73-3-2

That mendicant is called one who advocates a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-3-3

Furthermore, a mendicant rehearses the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-73-4-1

They spend too much time reciting that teaching. They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-73-4-2

That mendicant is called one who rehearses a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant thinks about and considers the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-73-5-1

They spend too much time thinking about that teaching. They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-73-5-2

That mendicant is called one who thinks a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-5-3

Take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an5-73-6-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-73-6-2

They don’t spend too much time studying that teaching. They don’t neglect retreat, and they’re committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-73-6-3

That’s how a mendicant is one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-6-4

So, mendicant, I’ve taught you the one who studies a lot, the one who advocates a lot, the one who rehearses a lot, the one who thinks a lot, and the one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-73-7-1

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^an5-73-7-2

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicant! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^an5-73-7-3

### One Who Lives by the Teaching (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.74) ^an5-74-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-74-1-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘one who lives by the teaching’. ^an5-74-1-2

How is a mendicant who lives by the teaching defined?” ^an5-74-1-3

“Mendicant, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an5-74-2-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-74-2-2

But they don’t understand the higher meaning. ^an5-74-2-3

That mendicant is called one who studies a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-2-4

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches Dhamma in detail to others as they learned and memorized it. But they don’t understand the higher meaning. ^an5-74-3-1

That mendicant is called one who advocates a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-3-2

Furthermore, a mendicant rehearses the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. But they don’t understand the higher meaning. ^an5-74-4-1

That mendicant is called one who rehearses a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-4-2

Furthermore, a mendicant thinks about and considers the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it. But they don’t understand the higher meaning. ^an5-74-5-1

That mendicant is called one who thinks a lot, not one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-5-2

Take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an5-74-6-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-74-6-2

And they do understand the higher meaning. ^an5-74-6-3

That’s how a mendicant is one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-6-4

So, mendicant, I’ve taught you the one who studies a lot, the one who advocates a lot, the one who rehearses a lot, the one who thinks a lot, and the one who lives by the teaching. ^an5-74-7-1

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^an5-74-7-2

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicant! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^an5-74-7-3

### Warriors (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.75) ^an5-75-0

“Mendicants, these five warriors are found in the world. ^an5-75-1-1

What five? ^an5-75-1-2

Firstly, one warrior falters and founders at the mere sight of a cloud of dust. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-75-1-3

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-75-1-4

This is the first warrior found in the world. ^an5-75-1-5

Furthermore, one warrior can prevail over a cloud of dust, ^an5-75-2-1

but he falters and founders at the mere sight of a banner’s crest. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-75-2-2

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-75-2-3

This is the second warrior found in the world. ^an5-75-2-4

Furthermore, one warrior can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest, ^an5-75-3-1

but he falters and founders at the mere sound of turmoil. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-75-3-2

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-75-3-3

This is the third warrior found in the world. ^an5-75-3-4

Furthermore, one warrior can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil, ^an5-75-4-1

but he’s killed or injured when blows are struck. ^an5-75-4-2

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-75-4-3

This is the fourth warrior found in the world. ^an5-75-4-4

Furthermore, one warrior can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil and being struck. ^an5-75-5-1

He wins victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an5-75-5-2

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-75-5-3

This is the fifth warrior found in the world. ^an5-75-5-4

These are the five warriors found in the world. ^an5-75-5-5

In the same way, these five individuals similar to warriors are found among the monks. ^an5-75-6-1

What five? ^an5-75-6-2

Firstly, one monk falters and founders at the mere sight of a cloud of dust. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-6-3

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-6-4

What is his ‘cloud of dust’? ^an5-75-6-5

It’s when a monk hears: ^an5-75-6-6

‘In such and such a village or town there’s a women or a girl who is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty.’ ^an5-75-6-7

Hearing this, he falters and founders. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-6-8

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-6-9

This is his ‘cloud of dust’. ^an5-75-6-10

I say that this individual is like the warrior who falters and founders at the mere sight of a cloud of dust. ^an5-75-7-1

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-75-7-3

This is the first individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-75-7-4

Furthermore, one monk can prevail over a cloud of dust, ^an5-75-8-1

but at the mere sight of a banner’s crest he falters and founders. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-8-2

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-8-3

What is his ‘banner’s crest’? ^an5-75-8-4

It’s when a monk doesn’t hear: ^an5-75-8-5

‘In such and such a village or town there’s a women or a girl who is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty.’ ^an5-75-8-6

But he sees for himself a women or a girl who is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. ^an5-75-8-7

Seeing her, he falters and founders. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-8-8

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-8-9

This is his ‘banner’s crest’. ^an5-75-8-10

I say that this individual is like the warrior who can prevail over a cloud of dust, ^an5-75-9-1

but he falters and founders at the mere sight of a banner’s crest. ^an5-75-9-2

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-75-9-4

This is the second individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-75-9-5

Furthermore, one monk can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest, ^an5-75-10-1

but he falters and founders at the mere sound of turmoil. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-10-2

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-10-3

What is his ‘turmoil’? ^an5-75-10-4

It’s when a mendicant who has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut is approached by a lady. She smiles, chats, laughs, and teases him. ^an5-75-10-5

He falters and founders. He doesn’t stay firm, and fails to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-75-10-6

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-75-10-7

This is his ‘turmoil’. ^an5-75-10-8

I say that this individual is like the warrior who can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest, ^an5-75-11-1

but he falters and founders at the mere sound of turmoil. ^an5-75-11-2

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-75-11-4

This is the third individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-75-11-5

Furthermore, one monk can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil, ^an5-75-12-1

but he’s killed or injured when blows are struck. ^an5-75-12-2

What is his ‘blows are struck’? ^an5-75-12-3

It’s when a mendicant who has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut is approached by a lady. She sits right by him, lies down, or embraces him. ^an5-75-12-4

Without resigning the training and declaring his inability to continue, he indulges in sex. ^an5-75-12-5

This is his ‘blows are struck’. ^an5-75-12-6

I say that this individual is like the warrior who can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil, but is killed or injured when blows are struck. ^an5-75-13-1

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-75-13-3

This is the fourth individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-75-13-4

Furthermore, one monk can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil, and being struck. He wins victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an5-75-14-1

What is his ‘victory in battle’? ^an5-75-14-2

It’s when a mendicant who has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut is approached by a lady. She sits right by him, lies down, or embraces him. ^an5-75-14-3

But he disentangles and frees himself, and goes wherever he wants. ^an5-75-14-4

He frequents a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw. ^an5-75-14-5

Gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, he sits down cross-legged, sets his body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^an5-75-15-1

Giving up covetousness for the world, he meditates with a heart rid of covetousness, cleansing the mind of covetousness. ^an5-75-15-2

Giving up ill will and malevolence, he meditates with a mind rid of ill will, full of sympathy for all living beings, cleansing the mind of ill will. ^an5-75-15-3

Giving up dullness and drowsiness, he meditates with a mind rid of dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, mindful and aware, cleansing the mind of dullness and drowsiness. ^an5-75-15-4

Giving up restlessness and remorse, he meditates without restlessness, his mind peaceful inside, cleansing the mind of restlessness and remorse. ^an5-75-15-5

Giving up doubt, he meditates having gone beyond doubt, not undecided about skillful qualities, cleansing the mind of doubt. ^an5-75-15-6

He gives up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^an5-75-15-7

Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, he enters and remains in the first absorption … ^an5-75-15-8

second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an5-75-15-9

When his mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—he extends it toward knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an5-75-16-1

He truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an5-75-16-2

He truly understands: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. ^an5-75-16-3

Knowing and seeing like this, his mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an5-75-16-4

When it is freed, he knows it is freed. ^an5-75-16-5

He understands: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an5-75-16-6

This is his ‘victory in battle’. ^an5-75-16-7

I say that this individual is like the warrior who can prevail over a cloud of dust and a banner’s crest and turmoil and being struck. He wins victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an5-75-17-1

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-75-17-3

This is the fifth individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-75-17-4

These five individuals similar to warriors are found among the monks.” ^an5-75-17-5

### Warriors (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.76) ^an5-76-0

“Mendicants, these five warriors are found in the world. ^an5-76-1-1

What five? ^an5-76-1-2

Firstly, one warrior dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. ^an5-76-1-3

He strives and struggles in the battle, ^an5-76-1-4

but his foes kill him and finish him off. ^an5-76-1-5

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-76-1-6

This is the first warrior found in the world. ^an5-76-1-7

Furthermore, one warrior dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. ^an5-76-2-1

He strives and struggles in the battle, ^an5-76-2-2

but his foes wound him. ^an5-76-2-3

He’s carried off and taken to his relatives, ^an5-76-2-4

but he dies on the road before he reaches them. ^an5-76-2-5

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-76-2-6

This is the second warrior found in the world. ^an5-76-2-7

Furthermore, one warrior dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. ^an5-76-3-1

He strives and struggles in the battle, ^an5-76-3-2

but his foes wound him. ^an5-76-3-3

He’s carried off and taken to his relatives, ^an5-76-3-4

who nurse him and care for him. ^an5-76-3-5

But he dies of his injuries while in their care. ^an5-76-3-6

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-76-3-7

This is the third warrior found in the world. ^an5-76-3-8

Furthermore, one warrior dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. ^an5-76-4-1

He strives and struggles in the battle, ^an5-76-4-2

but his foes wound him. ^an5-76-4-3

He’s carried off and taken to his relatives, ^an5-76-4-4

who nurse him and care for him. ^an5-76-4-5

And while in their care, he recovers from his injuries. ^an5-76-4-6

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-76-4-7

This is the fourth warrior found in the world. ^an5-76-4-8

Furthermore, one warrior dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. ^an5-76-5-1

He wins victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an5-76-5-2

Some warriors are like that. ^an5-76-5-3

This is the fifth warrior found in the world. ^an5-76-5-4

These are the five warriors found in the world. ^an5-76-5-5

In the same way, these five individuals similar to warriors are found among the monks. ^an5-76-6-1

What five? ^an5-76-6-2

Firstly, a mendicant lives supported by a town or village. ^an5-76-6-3

He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an5-76-6-4

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^an5-76-6-5

Lust infects his mind, ^an5-76-6-6

and, without resigning the training and declaring his inability to continue, he indulges in sex. ^an5-76-6-7

I say that this individual is like the warrior who is killed and finished off by his foes. Some individuals are like that. ^an5-76-7-1

This is the first individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-76-7-2

Furthermore, a mendicant lives supported by a town or village. ^an5-76-8-1

He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an5-76-8-2

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^an5-76-8-3

Lust infects his mind, ^an5-76-8-4

and his body and mind burn with it. ^an5-76-8-5

He thinks: ^an5-76-8-6

‘Why don’t I visit the monastery and tell the monks: ^an5-76-8-7

“Reverends, I am overcome with lust, mired in lust. I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-76-8-8

I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.”’ ^an5-76-8-9

But while traveling on the road, before he reaches the monastery he declares his inability to continue training. He disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-76-8-10

I say that they are like the warrior who is taken to his relatives for care, but he dies on the road before he reaches them. Some individuals are like that. ^an5-76-9-1

This is the second individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-76-9-2

Furthermore, a mendicant lives supported by a town or village. ^an5-76-10-1

He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an5-76-10-2

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^an5-76-10-3

Lust infects his mind, ^an5-76-10-4

and his body and mind burn with it. ^an5-76-10-5

He thinks: ^an5-76-10-6

‘Why don’t I visit the monastery and tell the monks: ^an5-76-10-7

“Reverends, I am overcome with lust, mired in lust. I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-76-10-8

I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.”’ ^an5-76-10-9

He goes to the monastery and tells the monks: ^an5-76-10-10

‘Reverends, I am overcome with lust, mired in lust. I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-76-10-11

I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-10-12

His spiritual companions advise and instruct him: ^an5-76-11-1

‘Reverend, the Buddha says that sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. ^an5-76-11-2

With the similes of a skeleton … ^an5-76-11-3

a scrap of meat … ^an5-76-11-4

a grass torch … ^an5-76-11-5

a pit of glowing coals … ^an5-76-11-6

a dream … ^an5-76-11-7

borrowed goods … ^an5-76-11-8

fruit on a tree … ^an5-76-11-9

a butcher’s knife and chopping board … ^an5-76-11-10

swords and spears … ^an5-76-11-11

a snake’s head, the Buddha says that sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. ^an5-76-11-12

Be happy with the spiritual life. ^an5-76-11-13

Venerable, please don’t declare your inability to continue training, disavow it and return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-11-14

When thus advised and instructed by his spiritual companions, he says: ^an5-76-12-1

‘Reverends, even though the Buddha says that sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks, ^an5-76-12-2

I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-12-3

Declaring his inability to continue training, he disavows it and returns to a lesser life. ^an5-76-12-4

I say that they are like the warrior who dies of his injuries while in the care of his relatives. Some individuals are like that. ^an5-76-13-1

This is the third individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-76-13-2

Furthermore, a mendicant lives supported by a town or village. ^an5-76-14-1

He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties. ^an5-76-14-2

There he sees a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes. ^an5-76-14-3

Lust infects his mind, ^an5-76-14-4

and his body and mind burn with it. ^an5-76-14-5

He thinks: ^an5-76-14-6

‘Why don’t I visit the monastery and tell the monks: ^an5-76-14-7

“Reverends, I am overcome with lust, mired in lust. I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-76-14-8

I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.”’ ^an5-76-14-9

He goes to the monastery and tells the monks: ^an5-76-14-10

‘Reverends, I am overcome with lust, mired in lust. I am unable to keep up the spiritual life. ^an5-76-14-11

I declare my inability to continue training. I disavow it and will return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-14-12

His spiritual companions advise and instruct him: ^an5-76-15-1

‘Reverend, the Buddha says that sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. ^an5-76-15-2

With the simile of a skeleton … ^an5-76-15-3

a scrap of meat … a grass torch … a pit of glowing coals … a dream … borrowed goods … fruit on a tree … a butcher’s knife and chopping board … swords and spears … a snake’s head, the Buddha says that sensual pleasures give little gratification and much suffering and distress, and they are all the more full of drawbacks. ^an5-76-15-4

Be happy with the spiritual life. ^an5-76-15-5

Venerable, please don’t declare your inability to continue training, disavow it and return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-15-6

When thus advised and instructed by his spiritual companions, he says: ^an5-76-16-1

‘I’ll try, reverends, I’ll struggle, I’ll be happy. ^an5-76-16-2

I won’t now declare my inability to continue training, disavow it and return to a lesser life.’ ^an5-76-16-3

I say that this individual is like the warrior who recovers from his injuries while in the care of his relatives. ^an5-76-17-1

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-76-17-2

This is the fourth individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-76-17-3

Furthermore, a mendicant lives supported by a town or village. ^an5-76-18-1

He robes up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, enters a village or town, guarding body, speech, and mind, establishing mindfulness, and restraining the sense faculties. ^an5-76-18-2

Seeing a sight with his eyes, he doesn’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an5-76-18-3

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, he practices restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an5-76-18-4

Hearing a sound with his ears … ^an5-76-18-5

Smelling an odor with his nose … ^an5-76-18-6

Tasting a flavor with his tongue … ^an5-76-18-7

Encountering a touch with his body … ^an5-76-18-8

Knowing an idea with his mind, he doesn’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an5-76-18-9

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, he practices restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an5-76-18-10

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, ^an5-76-18-11

he frequents a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw. ^an5-76-18-12

Gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, he sits down cross-legged, sets his body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^an5-76-18-13

He gives up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^an5-76-18-14

Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, he enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an5-76-18-15

When his mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—he extends it toward knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an5-76-19-1

He truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. He truly understands: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. Knowing and seeing like this, his mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. When freed, he knows ‘it is freed’. ^an5-76-19-2

He understands: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an5-76-19-3

I say that this individual is like the warrior who dons his sword and shield, fastens his bow and arrows, and plunges into the thick of battle. He wins victory in battle, establishing himself as foremost in battle. ^an5-76-20-1

Some individuals are like that. ^an5-76-20-2

This is the fifth individual similar to a warrior found among the monks. ^an5-76-20-3

These five individuals similar to warriors are found among the monks.” ^an5-76-20-4

### Future Perils (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.77) ^an5-77-0

“Mendicants, seeing these five future perils is quite enough for a wilderness mendicant to meditate diligently, keenly, and resolutely for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-77-1-1

What five? ^an5-77-2-1

Firstly, a wilderness mendicant reflects: ^an5-77-2-2

‘Currently I’m living alone in a wilderness. ^an5-77-2-3

While living here alone ^an5-77-2-4

I might get bitten by a snake, a scorpion, or a centipede. ^an5-77-2-5

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an5-77-2-6

I’d better rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an5-77-2-7

This is the first future peril … ^an5-77-2-8

Furthermore, a wilderness mendicant reflects: ^an5-77-3-1

‘Currently I’m living alone in a wilderness. ^an5-77-3-2

While living here alone ^an5-77-3-3

I might stumble off a cliff, or get food poisoning, or suffer a disturbance of bile, phlegm, or piercing winds. ^an5-77-3-4

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an5-77-3-5

I’d better rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an5-77-3-6

This is the second future peril … ^an5-77-3-7

Furthermore, a wilderness mendicant reflects: ^an5-77-4-1

‘Currently I’m living alone in a wilderness. ^an5-77-4-2

While living here alone ^an5-77-4-3

I might encounter wild beasts—a lion, a tiger, a leopard, a bear, or a hyena—which might take my life. ^an5-77-4-4

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an5-77-4-5

I’d better rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an5-77-4-6

This is the third future peril … ^an5-77-4-7

Furthermore, a wilderness mendicant reflects: ^an5-77-5-1

‘Currently I’m living alone in a wilderness. ^an5-77-5-2

While living here alone ^an5-77-5-3

I might encounter youths escaping a crime or on their way to commit one, and they might take my life. ^an5-77-5-4

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an5-77-5-5

I’d better rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an5-77-5-6

This is the fourth future peril … ^an5-77-5-7

Furthermore, a wilderness mendicant reflects: ^an5-77-6-1

‘Currently I’m living alone in a wilderness. ^an5-77-6-2

But in a wilderness there are savage monsters who might take my life. ^an5-77-6-3

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an5-77-6-4

I’d better rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an5-77-6-5

This is the fifth future peril … ^an5-77-6-6

These are the five future perils, seeing which is quite enough for a wilderness mendicant to meditate diligently, keenly, and resolutely for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.” ^an5-77-7-1

### Future Perils (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.78) ^an5-78-0

“Mendicants, seeing these five future perils is quite enough for a mendicant to meditate diligently, keenly, and resolutely for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-78-1-1

What five? ^an5-78-1-2

A mendicant reflects: ^an5-78-1-3

‘Currently I’m a youth, young, with pristine black hair, blessed with youth, in the prime of life. ^an5-78-1-4

But there will come a time when this body is struck with old age. ^an5-78-1-5

When you’re old, overcome by old age, it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas, and it’s not easy to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-78-1-6

Before that unlikable, undesirable, and disagreeable thing happens, ^an5-78-1-7

I’d better preempt it by rousing up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. That way, when it happens, I’ll live comfortably even though I’m old.’ ^an5-78-1-8

This is the first future peril … ^an5-78-1-9

Furthermore, a mendicant reflects: ^an5-78-2-1

‘Currently, I’m rarely ill or unwell. My stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an5-78-2-2

But there will come a time when this body is struck with sickness. ^an5-78-2-3

When you’re sick, overcome by sickness, it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas, and it’s not easy to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-78-2-4

Before that unlikable, undesirable, and disagreeable thing happens, ^an5-78-2-5

I’d better preempt it by rousing up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. That way, when it happens, I’ll live comfortably even though I’m sick.’ ^an5-78-2-6

This is the second future peril … ^an5-78-2-7

Furthermore, a mendicant reflects: ^an5-78-3-1

‘Currently, there’s plenty of food, a good harvest, so it’s easy to get almsfood, and easy to keep going by collecting alms. ^an5-78-3-2

But there will come a time of famine, a bad harvest, when it’s hard to get almsfood, and not easy to keep going by collecting alms. ^an5-78-3-3

In a time of famine, people move to where there’s plenty of food, ^an5-78-3-4

where they live crowded and cramped together. ^an5-78-3-5

When you live crowded and cramped together, it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas, and it’s not easy to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-78-3-6

Before that unlikable, undesirable, and disagreeable thing happens, ^an5-78-3-7

I’d better preempt it by rousing up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. That way, when it happens, I’ll live comfortably even though there’s a famine.’ ^an5-78-3-8

This is the third future peril … ^an5-78-3-9

Furthermore, a mendicant reflects: ^an5-78-4-1

‘Currently, people live in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, blending like milk and water, and regarding each other with kindly eyes. ^an5-78-4-2

But there will come a time of peril due to turmoil in the wilds, so the countryfolk mount their vehicles and flee everywhere. ^an5-78-4-3

In a time of peril, people move to where there’s sanctuary, ^an5-78-4-4

where they live crowded and cramped together. ^an5-78-4-5

When you live crowded and cramped together, it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas, and it’s not easy to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-78-4-6

Before that unlikable, undesirable, and disagreeable thing happens, ^an5-78-4-7

I’d better preempt it by rousing up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. That way, when it happens, I’ll live comfortably even in a time of peril.’ ^an5-78-4-8

This is the fourth future peril … ^an5-78-4-9

Furthermore, a mendicant reflects: ^an5-78-5-1

‘Currently, the Saṅgha lives comfortably, in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, with one recitation. ^an5-78-5-2

But there will come a time of schism in the Saṅgha. ^an5-78-5-3

When there is schism in the Saṅgha, it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas, and it’s not easy to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-78-5-4

Before that unlikable, undesirable, and disagreeable thing happens, ^an5-78-5-5

I’d better preempt it by rousing up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. That way, when it happens, I’ll live comfortably even though there’s schism in the Saṅgha.’ ^an5-78-5-6

This is the fifth future peril … ^an5-78-5-7

These are the five future perils, seeing which is quite enough for a mendicant to meditate diligently, keenly, and resolutely for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.” ^an5-78-6-1

### Future Perils (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 5.79) ^an5-79-0

“Mendicants, these five future perils have not currently arisen, but they will arise in the future. ^an5-79-1-1

You should look out for them ^an5-79-1-2

and try to give them up. ^an5-79-1-3

What five? ^an5-79-2-1

In a future time there will be mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-2-2

They will ordain others, ^an5-79-2-3

but be unable to guide them in the higher ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-2-4

They too will not develop their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-2-5

They too will ordain others, ^an5-79-2-6

but be unable to guide them in the higher ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-2-7

They too will not develop their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-2-8

And that is how corrupt training comes from corrupt teachings, ^an5-79-2-9

and corrupt teachings come from corrupt training. ^an5-79-2-10

This is the first future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-79-2-11

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-3-1

They will give dependence to others, ^an5-79-3-2

but be unable to guide them in the higher ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-3-3

They too will not develop their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-3-4

They too will give dependence to others, ^an5-79-3-5

but be unable to guide them in the higher ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-3-6

They too will not develop their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-3-7

And that is how corrupt training comes from corrupt teachings, ^an5-79-3-8

and corrupt teachings come from corrupt training. ^an5-79-3-9

This is the second future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-79-3-10

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-4-1

In discussion about the teachings and elaborations they’ll fall into dark ideas without realizing it. ^an5-79-4-2

And that is how corrupt training comes from corrupt teachings, ^an5-79-4-3

and corrupt teachings come from corrupt training. ^an5-79-4-4

This is the third future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-79-4-5

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-5-1

When discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited they won’t want to listen. They won’t actively listen or try to understand, nor will they think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing. ^an5-79-5-2

But when discourses composed by poets—poetry, with fancy words and phrases, composed by outsiders or spoken by disciples—are being recited they will want to listen. They’ll actively listen and try to understand, and they’ll think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing. ^an5-79-5-3

And that is how corrupt training comes from corrupt teachings, ^an5-79-5-4

and corrupt teachings come from corrupt training. ^an5-79-5-5

This is the fourth future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-79-5-6

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who have not developed their physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom. ^an5-79-6-1

The senior mendicants will be indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, not rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-79-6-2

Those who come after them will follow their example. ^an5-79-6-3

They too will become indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, not rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-79-6-4

And that is how corrupt training comes from corrupt teachings, ^an5-79-6-5

and corrupt teachings come from corrupt training. ^an5-79-6-6

This is the fifth future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-79-6-7

These are the five future perils that have not currently arisen, but will arise in the future. ^an5-79-7-1

You should look out for them, ^an5-79-7-2

and try to give them up.” ^an5-79-7-3

### Future Perils (4th) (Numbered Discourses 5.80) ^an5-80-0

“Mendicants, these five future perils have not currently arisen, but they will arise in the future. ^an5-80-1-1

You should look out for them ^an5-80-1-2

and try to give them up. ^an5-80-1-3

What five? ^an5-80-2-1

In a future time there will be mendicants who like nice robes. ^an5-80-2-2

They will neglect the practice of wearing rag robes and the practice of frequenting remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-80-2-3

They will come down to the villages, towns, and capital cities and make their homes there. And they will employ many kinds of improper solicitation for the sake of a robe. ^an5-80-2-4

This is the first future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-80-2-5

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who like nice almsfood. ^an5-80-3-1

They will neglect the practice of walking for almsfood and the practice of frequenting remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-80-3-2

They will come down to the villages, towns, and capital cities and make their homes there. And they will employ many kinds of improper solicitation for the sake of almsfood. ^an5-80-3-3

This is the second future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-80-3-4

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who like nice lodgings. ^an5-80-4-1

They will neglect the practice of staying at the root of a tree and the practice of frequenting remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-80-4-2

They will come down to the villages, towns, and capital cities and make their homes there. And they will employ many kinds of improper solicitation for the sake of lodgings. ^an5-80-4-3

This is the third future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-80-4-4

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who mix closely with nuns, trainee nuns, and novice nuns. ^an5-80-5-1

In such conditions, it can be expected that ^an5-80-5-2

they will lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, or commit one of the corrupt offenses, or disavow the training and return to a lesser life. ^an5-80-5-3

This is the fourth future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-80-5-4

Furthermore, in a future time there will be mendicants who mix closely with monastery attendants and novices. ^an5-80-6-1

In such conditions it can be expected that ^an5-80-6-2

they will engage in storing up goods for their own use, and making obvious hints about digging the earth and cutting plants. ^an5-80-6-3

This is the fifth future peril that has not currently arisen, but will arise in the future … ^an5-80-6-4

These are the five future perils that have not currently arisen, but will arise in the future. ^an5-80-7-1

You should look out for them ^an5-80-7-2

and try to give them up.” ^an5-80-7-3

### Desirable (Numbered Discourses 5.81) ^an5-81-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-81-1-1

What five? ^an5-81-1-2

They desire the desirable, they hate the hateful, they’re deluded by the delusory, they’re annoyed by the annoying, and they’re intoxicated by the intoxicating. ^an5-81-1-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-81-1-4

A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-81-2-1

What five? ^an5-81-2-2

They don’t desire the desirable, they don’t hate the hateful, they’re not deluded by the delusory, they’re not annoyed by the annoying, and they’re not intoxicated by the intoxicating. ^an5-81-2-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-81-2-4

### Free of Greed (Numbered Discourses 5.82) ^an5-82-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-82-1-1

What five? ^an5-82-1-2

They’re not free of greed, hate, and delusion; they are offensive and contemptuous. ^an5-82-1-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-82-1-4

A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-82-2-1

What five? ^an5-82-2-2

They’re free of greed, hate, and delusion; they’re not offensive and contemptuous. ^an5-82-2-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-82-2-4

### Deceiver (Numbered Discourses 5.83) ^an5-83-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-83-1-1

What five? ^an5-83-1-2

They use deceit, flattery, hinting, and belittling, and they use material things to chase after other material things. ^an5-83-1-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-83-1-4

A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-83-2-1

What five? ^an5-83-2-2

They don’t use deceit, flattery, hinting, or belittling, and they don’t use material things to chase after other material things. ^an5-83-2-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-83-2-4

### Faithless (Numbered Discourses 5.84) ^an5-84-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-84-1-1

What five? ^an5-84-1-2

They’re faithless, shameless, imprudent, lazy, and witless. ^an5-84-1-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-84-1-4

A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-84-2-1

What five? ^an5-84-2-2

They’re faithful, conscientious, prudent, energetic, and wise. ^an5-84-2-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-84-2-4

### Cannot Endure (Numbered Discourses 5.85) ^an5-85-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-85-1-1

What five? ^an5-85-1-2

They can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-85-1-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is unlikable and disagreeable to their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an5-85-1-4

A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-85-2-1

What five? ^an5-85-2-2

They can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-85-2-3

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-85-2-4

### Attaining the Methods of Textual Analysis (Numbered Discourses 5.86) ^an5-86-0

“A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-86-1-1

What five? ^an5-86-1-2

They have attained the textual analysis of meaning, text, definition, and eloquence. ^an5-86-1-3

And they are deft and tireless in a diverse spectrum of duties for their spiritual companions, understanding how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an5-86-1-4

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-86-1-5

### Ethical (Numbered Discourses 5.87) ^an5-87-0

“A senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-87-1-1

What five? ^an5-87-1-2

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-87-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-87-1-4

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well, with a polished, clear, and articulate voice that expresses the meaning. ^an5-87-1-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-87-1-6

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-87-1-7

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-87-1-8

### Senior Mendicants (Numbered Discourses 5.88) ^an5-88-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant who has five qualities is acting for the detriment and suffering of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of of gods and humans. ^an5-88-1-1

What five? ^an5-88-2-1

They are long standing and have long gone forth. ^an5-88-2-2

They’re well-known, famous, with a large following that includes both laypeople and renunciates. ^an5-88-2-3

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-88-2-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-88-2-5

But they have wrong view and distorted perspective. They draw many people away from the true teaching and establish them in false teachings. ^an5-88-2-6

People follow their example, thinking that the senior mendicant is long standing and has long gone forth. Or that they’re well-known, famous, with a large following that includes both laypeople and renunciates. Or that they receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. Or that they’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. ^an5-88-2-7

A senior mendicant who possesses these five qualities is acting for the detriment and suffering of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of gods and humans. ^an5-88-2-8

A senior mendicant who has five qualities is acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an5-88-3-1

What five? ^an5-88-4-1

They are long standing and have long gone forth. ^an5-88-4-2

They’re well-known, famous, with a large following, including both laypeople and renunciates. ^an5-88-4-3

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-88-4-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-88-4-5

And they have right view and an undistorted perspective. They draw many people away from false teachings and establish them in the true teaching. ^an5-88-4-6

People follow their example, thinking that the senior mendicant is long standing and has long gone forth. Or that they’re well-known, famous, with a large following that includes both laypeople and renunciates. Or that they receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. Or that they’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. ^an5-88-4-7

A senior mendicant who possesses these five qualities is acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.” ^an5-88-4-8

### A Trainee (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.89) ^an5-89-0

“These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-89-1-1

What five? ^an5-89-1-2

They relish work, talk, sleep, and company. And they don’t review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-89-1-3

These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-89-1-4

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-89-2-1

What five? ^an5-89-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, sleep, and company. And they review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-89-2-3

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an5-89-2-4

### A Trainee (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.90) ^an5-90-0

“These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-1-1

What five? ^an5-90-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant trainee has many duties and responsibilities, and is competent in many tasks. ^an5-90-1-3

They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-1-4

This is the first thing that leads to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee spends too much time doing trivial work. ^an5-90-2-1

They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-2-2

This is the second thing that leads to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-2-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee mixes closely with laypeople and renunciates, socializing inappropriately like a layperson. ^an5-90-3-1

They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-3-2

This is the third thing that leads to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-3-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee enters the town at the wrong time, and returns too late in the day. ^an5-90-4-1

They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-4-2

This is the fourth thing that leads to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee doesn’t get to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when they want, without trouble or difficulty. That is, ^an5-90-5-1

talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-90-5-2

They neglect retreat, and are not committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-5-3

This is the fifth thing that leads to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-5-4

These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-5-5

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-6-1

What five? ^an5-90-6-2

Firstly, a mendicant trainee doesn’t have many duties and responsibilities, even though they are competent in many tasks. ^an5-90-6-3

They don’t neglect retreat, and are committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-6-4

This is the first thing that doesn’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-6-5

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee doesn’t waste their day doing trivial work. ^an5-90-7-1

They don’t neglect retreat, and are committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-7-2

This is the second thing that doesn’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-7-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee doesn’t mix closely with laypeople and renunciates, socializing inappropriately like a layperson. ^an5-90-8-1

They don’t neglect retreat, and are committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-8-2

This is the third thing that doesn’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-8-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee doesn’t enter the village too early or return too late in the day. ^an5-90-9-1

They don’t neglect retreat, and are committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-9-2

This is the fourth thing that doesn’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-9-3

Furthermore, a mendicant trainee gets to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when they want, without trouble or difficulty. That is, ^an5-90-10-1

talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-90-10-2

They don’t neglect retreat, and are committed to internal serenity of heart. ^an5-90-10-3

This is the fifth thing that doesn’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an5-90-10-4

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an5-90-10-5

### Accomplishments (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.91) ^an5-91-0

“Mendicants, there are five accomplishments. ^an5-91-1-1

What five? ^an5-91-1-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an5-91-1-3

These are the five accomplishments.” ^an5-91-1-4

### Accomplishment (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.92) ^an5-92-0

“Mendicants, there are five accomplishments. ^an5-92-1-1

What five? ^an5-92-1-2

Accomplishment in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-92-1-3

These are the five accomplishments.” ^an5-92-1-4

### Declarations (Numbered Discourses 5.93) ^an5-93-0

“Mendicants, there are five ways of declaring enlightenment. ^an5-93-1-1

What five? ^an5-93-1-2

One declares enlightenment out of stupidity and folly. ^an5-93-1-3

Or because of corrupt wishes, being of wishful temperament. ^an5-93-1-4

Or because of madness and mental disorder. ^an5-93-1-5

Or out of overestimation. ^an5-93-1-6

Or one declares enlightenment rightly. ^an5-93-1-7

These are the five ways of declaring enlightenment.” ^an5-93-1-8

### Living Comfortably (Numbered Discourses 5.94) ^an5-94-0

“Mendicants, there are these five ways of living comfortably. ^an5-94-1-1

What five? ^an5-94-1-2

It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an5-94-1-3

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … ^an5-94-1-4

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-94-1-5

These are the five ways of living comfortably.” ^an5-94-1-6

### Unshakable (Numbered Discourses 5.95) ^an5-95-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable. ^an5-95-1-1

What five? ^an5-95-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has attained the textual analysis of meaning, text, definition, and eloquence, and they review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-95-1-3

A mendicant who has these five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.” ^an5-95-1-4

### Remembering What You’ve Learned (Numbered Discourses 5.96) ^an5-96-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant cultivating mindfulness of breathing who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable. ^an5-96-1-1

What five? ^an5-96-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has few obligations and duties, and is unburdensome and contented with life’s necessities. ^an5-96-1-3

They eat little, not devoted to filling their stomach. ^an5-96-1-4

They are rarely drowsy, and are dedicated to wakefulness. ^an5-96-1-5

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-96-1-6

They review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-96-1-7

A mendicant cultivating mindfulness of breathing who has these five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.” ^an5-96-1-8

### Talk (Numbered Discourses 5.97) ^an5-97-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant developing mindfulness of breathing who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable. ^an5-97-1-1

What five? ^an5-97-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has few concerns and duties, and is unburdensome and contented with life’s necessities. ^an5-97-1-3

They eat little, not devoted to filling their stomach. ^an5-97-1-4

They are rarely drowsy, and are dedicated to wakefulness. ^an5-97-1-5

They get to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when they want, without trouble or difficulty. That is, ^an5-97-1-6

talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, keeping your distance, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-97-1-7

They review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-97-1-8

A mendicant developing mindfulness of breathing who has these five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.” ^an5-97-1-9

### In the Wilderness (Numbered Discourses 5.98) ^an5-98-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant practicing mindfulness of breathing who has five things will soon penetrate the unshakable. ^an5-98-1-1

What five? ^an5-98-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has few obligations and duties, and is unburdensome and contented with life’s necessities. ^an5-98-1-3

They eat little, not devoted to filling their stomach. ^an5-98-1-4

They are rarely drowsy, and are dedicated to wakefulness. ^an5-98-1-5

They live in the wilderness, in remote lodgings. ^an5-98-1-6

They review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-98-1-7

A mendicant practicing mindfulness of breathing who has these five things will soon penetrate the unshakable.” ^an5-98-1-8

### The Lion (Numbered Discourses 5.99) ^an5-99-0

“Mendicants, towards evening the lion, king of beasts, emerges from his den, ^an5-99-1-1

yawns, ^an5-99-1-2

surveys the four quarters, ^an5-99-1-3

and roars his lion’s roar three times. ^an5-99-1-4

Then he sets out on the hunt. ^an5-99-1-5

If he strikes an elephant, he does it carefully, not carelessly. ^an5-99-1-6

If he strikes a buffalo … ^an5-99-1-7

a cow … ^an5-99-1-8

a leopard … ^an5-99-1-9

or any smaller creatures—even a hare or a cat—he does it carefully, not carelessly. ^an5-99-1-10

Why is that? ^an5-99-1-11

Thinking: ‘May I not lose my way.’ ^an5-99-1-12

‘Lion’ is a term for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an5-99-2-1

When the Realized One teaches Dhamma to an assembly, this is his lion’s roar. ^an5-99-2-2

When the Realized One teaches the monks … ^an5-99-2-3

nuns … ^an5-99-2-4

laymen … ^an5-99-2-5

laywomen … ^an5-99-2-6

or ordinary people—even food-carriers and hunters—he teaches them carefully, not carelessly. ^an5-99-2-7

Why is that? ^an5-99-2-8

Because the Realized One has respect and reverence for the teaching.” ^an5-99-2-9

### With Kakudha (Numbered Discourses 5.100) ^an5-100-0

So I have heard. ^an5-100-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an5-100-1-2

At that time the Koliyan named Kakudha—Venerable Mahāmoggallāna’s supporter—had recently passed away and been reborn in a certain host of mind-made gods. ^an5-100-1-3

His incarnation was two or three times the size of a Magadhan village with its fields. ^an5-100-1-4

But with that incarnation he didn’t obstruct himself or others. ^an5-100-1-5

Then the godling Kakudha went up to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^an5-100-2-1

“Sir, this fixed desire arose in Devadatta: ^an5-100-2-2

‘I shall lead the mendicant Saṅgha.’ ^an5-100-2-3

And as that thought arose, Devadatta lost that psychic power.” ^an5-100-2-4

That’s what the godling Kakudha said. ^an5-100-2-5

Then he bowed and respectfully circled Mahāmoggallāna, keeping him on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an5-100-2-6

Then Mahāmoggallāna went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an5-100-3-1

“But Moggallāna, did you encompass the godling Kakudha’s mind, and know that ^an5-100-5-1

everything he says is correct and not otherwise?” ^an5-100-5-2

“Indeed I did, sir.” ^an5-100-5-3

“Mark these words, Moggallāna! ^an5-100-5-5

Mark these words! ^an5-100-5-6

Now that futile man Devadatta will expose himself by his own deeds. ^an5-100-5-7

Moggallāna, there are these five teachers found in the world. ^an5-100-6-1

What five? ^an5-100-6-2

Firstly, some teacher with impure conduct claims: ‘I am pure in ethics. My ethical conduct is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-6-3

But their disciples know: ^an5-100-6-4

‘This teacher has impure ethical conduct, but claims to be ethically pure. ^an5-100-6-5

They wouldn’t like it if we were to tell the laypeople. ^an5-100-6-6

And how could we treat them in a way that they don’t like? ^an5-100-6-7

But they consent to robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-100-6-8

They themselves will be recognized by their own deeds.’ ^an5-100-6-9

The disciples of such a teacher cover up their teacher’s conduct, ^an5-100-6-10

and the teacher expects them to do so. ^an5-100-6-11

Furthermore, some teacher with impure livelihood claims: ‘I am pure in livelihood. My livelihood is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-7-1

But their disciples know: ^an5-100-7-2

‘This teacher has impure livelihood, but claims to have pure livelihood. ^an5-100-7-3

They wouldn’t like it if we were to tell the laypeople. ^an5-100-7-4

And how could we treat them in a way that they don’t like? ^an5-100-7-5

But they consent to robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-100-7-6

They themselves will be recognized by their own deeds.’ ^an5-100-7-7

The disciples of such a teacher cover up their teacher’s livelihood, ^an5-100-7-8

and the teacher expects them to do so. ^an5-100-7-9

Furthermore, some teacher with impure teaching claims: ‘I am pure in teaching. My teaching is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-8-1

But their disciples know: ^an5-100-8-2

‘This teacher has impure teaching, but claims to have pure teaching. ^an5-100-8-3

They wouldn’t like it if we were to tell the laypeople. ^an5-100-8-4

And how could we treat them in a way that they don’t like? ^an5-100-8-5

But they consent to robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-100-8-6

They themselves will be recognized by their own deeds.’ ^an5-100-8-7

The disciples of such a teacher cover up their teacher’s teaching, ^an5-100-8-8

and the teacher expects them to do so. ^an5-100-8-9

Furthermore, some teacher with impure answers claims: ‘I am pure in how I answer. My answers are pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-9-1

But their disciples know: ^an5-100-9-2

‘This teacher has impure answers, but claims to have pure answers. ^an5-100-9-3

They wouldn’t like it if we were to tell the laypeople. ^an5-100-9-4

And how could we treat them in a way that they don’t like? ^an5-100-9-5

But they consent to robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-100-9-6

They themselves will be recognized by their own deeds.’ ^an5-100-9-7

The disciples of such a teacher cover up their teacher’s answers, ^an5-100-9-8

and the teacher expects them to do so. ^an5-100-9-9

Furthermore, some teacher with impure knowledge and vision claims: ‘I am pure in knowledge and vision. My knowledge and vision are pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-10-1

But their disciples know: ^an5-100-10-2

‘This teacher has impure knowledge and vision, but claims to have pure knowledge and vision. ^an5-100-10-3

They wouldn’t like it if we were to tell the laypeople. ^an5-100-10-4

And how could we treat them in a way that they don’t like? ^an5-100-10-5

But they consent to robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-100-10-6

They themselves will be recognized by their own deeds.’ ^an5-100-10-7

The disciples of such a teacher cover up their teacher’s knowledge and vision, ^an5-100-10-8

and the teacher expects them to do so. ^an5-100-10-9

These are the five teachers found in the world. ^an5-100-10-10

But Moggallāna, I have pure ethical conduct, and I claim: ‘I am pure in ethical conduct. My ethical conduct is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-11-1

My disciples don’t cover up my conduct, and I don’t expect them to. ^an5-100-11-2

I have pure livelihood, and I claim: ‘I am pure in livelihood. My livelihood is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-11-3

My disciples don’t cover up my livelihood, and I don’t expect them to. ^an5-100-11-4

I have pure teaching, and I claim: ‘I am pure in teaching. My teaching is pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-11-5

My disciples don’t cover up my teaching, and I don’t expect them to. ^an5-100-11-6

I have pure answers, and I claim: ‘I am pure in how I answer. My answers are pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-11-7

My disciples don’t cover up my answers, and I don’t expect them to. ^an5-100-11-8

I have pure knowledge and vision, and I claim: ‘I am pure in knowledge and vision. My knowledge and vision are pure, bright, uncorrupted.’ ^an5-100-11-9

My disciples don’t cover up my knowledge and vision, and I don’t expect them to.” ^an5-100-11-10

### Assurance (Numbered Discourses 5.101) ^an5-101-0

“Mendicants, these five qualities make a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-1-1

What five? ^an5-101-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, ethical, learned, energetic, and wise. ^an5-101-1-3

A person of faith doesn’t have the insecurities of someone who lacks faith. ^an5-101-2-1

So this quality makes a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-2-2

An ethical person doesn’t have the insecurities of someone who is unethical. ^an5-101-3-1

So this quality makes a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-3-2

A learned person doesn’t have the insecurities of a person of little learning. ^an5-101-4-1

So this quality makes a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-4-2

An energetic person doesn’t have the insecurities of a lazy person. ^an5-101-5-1

So this quality makes a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-5-2

A wise person doesn’t have the insecurities of someone who is witless. ^an5-101-6-1

So this quality makes a trainee self-assured. ^an5-101-6-2

These are the five qualities that make a trainee self-assured.” ^an5-101-6-3

### Suspected (Numbered Discourses 5.102) ^an5-102-0

“Mendicants, even if a monk is of impeccable character, he might be suspected and distrusted as a ‘bad monk’ for five reasons. ^an5-102-1-1

What five? ^an5-102-2-1

It’s when a monk resorts to prostitutes, widows, voluptuous girls, eunuchs, or nuns for alms. ^an5-102-2-2

Even if a monk is of impeccable character, he might be suspected and distrusted as a ‘bad monk’ for these five reasons.” ^an5-102-3-1

### A Master Thief (Numbered Discourses 5.103) ^an5-103-0

“Mendicants, a master thief with five factors breaks into houses, plunders wealth, steals from isolated buildings, and commits highway robbery. ^an5-103-1-1

What five? ^an5-103-1-2

A master thief relies on rough ground, on thick cover, and on the powerful; they pay bribes, and they act alone. ^an5-103-1-3

And how does a master thief rely on rough ground? ^an5-103-2-1

It’s when a master thief relies on inaccessible riverlands or rugged mountains. ^an5-103-2-2

That’s how a master thief relies on rough ground. ^an5-103-2-3

And how does a master thief rely on thick cover? ^an5-103-3-1

It’s when a master thief relies on thick grass, thick trees, a blind spot, or a large forest grove. ^an5-103-3-2

That’s how a master thief relies on thick cover. ^an5-103-3-3

And how does a master thief rely on the powerful? ^an5-103-4-1

It’s when a master thief relies on rulers or their chief ministers. ^an5-103-4-2

They think: ^an5-103-4-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, these rulers or their chief ministers will speak in my defense in the case.’ ^an5-103-4-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an5-103-4-5

That’s how a master thief relies on the powerful. ^an5-103-4-6

And how does a master thief pay bribes? ^an5-103-5-1

It’s when a master thief is rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an5-103-5-2

They think: ^an5-103-5-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, I’ll settle it with a bribe.’ ^an5-103-5-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an5-103-5-5

That’s how a master thief pays bribes. ^an5-103-5-6

And how does a master thief act alone? ^an5-103-6-1

It’s when a master thief carries out robbery all alone. ^an5-103-6-2

Why is that? ^an5-103-6-3

So that their secret plans are not leaked to others. ^an5-103-6-4

That’s how a master thief acts alone. ^an5-103-6-5

A master thief with these five factors breaks into houses, plunders wealth, steals from isolated buildings, and commits highway robbery. ^an5-103-7-1

In the same way, when a bad mendicant has five qualities, they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they brim with much wickedness. ^an5-103-8-1

What five? ^an5-103-8-2

A bad mendicant relies on rough ground, on thick cover, and on the powerful; they pay bribes, and they act alone. ^an5-103-8-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on rough ground? ^an5-103-9-1

It’s when a bad mendicant has unethical conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-103-9-2

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on rough ground. ^an5-103-9-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on thick cover? ^an5-103-10-1

It’s when a bad mendicant has wrong view, he’s attached to an extremist view. ^an5-103-10-2

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on thick cover. ^an5-103-10-3

And how does a bad mendicant rely on the powerful? ^an5-103-11-1

It’s when a bad mendicant relies on rulers or their chief ministers. ^an5-103-11-2

They think: ^an5-103-11-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, these rulers or their chief ministers will speak in my defense in the case.’ ^an5-103-11-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an5-103-11-5

That’s how a bad mendicant relies on the powerful. ^an5-103-11-6

And how does a bad mendicant pay bribes? ^an5-103-12-1

It’s when a bad mendicant receives robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-103-12-2

They think: ^an5-103-12-3

‘If anyone accuses me of anything, I’ll settle it with a bribe.’ ^an5-103-12-4

And that’s exactly what happens. ^an5-103-12-5

That’s how a bad mendicant pays bribes. ^an5-103-12-6

And how does a bad mendicant act alone? ^an5-103-13-1

It’s when a bad mendicant dwells alone in the borderlands. ^an5-103-13-2

They visit families there to acquire material things. ^an5-103-13-3

That’s how a bad mendicant acts alone. ^an5-103-13-4

When a bad mendicant possesses these five qualities, they keep themselves broken and damaged. They deserve to be blamed and criticized by sensible people, and they create much wickedness.” ^an5-103-14-1

### A Delicate Ascetic of Ascetics (Numbered Discourses 5.104) ^an5-104-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is a delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an5-104-1-1

What five? ^an5-104-2-1

It’s when a mendicant usually uses only what they’ve been invited to accept—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—rarely using them without invitation. ^an5-104-2-2

When living with other spiritual practitioners, they usually find themselves treated agreeably by them by way of body, speech, and mind, and rarely disagreeably. ^an5-104-2-3

And they are usually presented with agreeable things by them, rarely with disagreeable ones. ^an5-104-2-4

They’re healthy, so the various unpleasant feelings—stemming from disorders of bile, phlegm, wind, or their conjunction; or caused by change in weather, by not taking care of themselves, by overexertion, or as the result of past deeds—usually don’t come up. ^an5-104-2-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-104-2-6

And they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-104-2-7

A mendicant with these five qualities is a delicate ascetic of ascetics. ^an5-104-2-8

And if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me. ^an5-104-3-1

For I usually use only what I’ve been invited to accept. ^an5-104-3-2

When living with other mendicants, I am usually treated agreeably by them. ^an5-104-3-3

And I usually present them with agreeable things. ^an5-104-3-4

I’m healthy. ^an5-104-3-5

I get the four absorptions when I want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-104-3-6

And I’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. ^an5-104-3-7

So if anyone should be rightly called a delicate ascetic of ascetics, it’s me.” ^an5-104-4-1

### Living Comfortably (Numbered Discourses 5.105) ^an5-105-0

“Mendicants, there are these five ways of living comfortably. ^an5-105-1-1

What five? ^an5-105-1-2
