It’s when a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with kindness by way of body, speech, and mind, both in public and in private. ^an5-105-1-3

They live according to the precepts shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. Those precepts are intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an5-105-1-4

They live according to the view shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. That view is noble and emancipating, and brings one who practices it to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-105-1-5

These are the five ways of living comfortably.” ^an5-105-1-6

### With Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 5.106) ^an5-106-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an5-106-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an5-106-1-2

“Sir, how could a mendicant live comfortably while staying in a monastic community?” ^an5-106-2-1

“It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in their own ethical conduct, but they don’t urge others to be ethical. ^an5-106-2-2

That’s how a mendicant could live comfortably while staying in a monastic community.” ^an5-106-2-3

“But sir, could there be another way for a mendicant to live comfortably while staying in a monastic community?” ^an5-106-3-1

“There could, Ānanda. ^an5-106-3-2

It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in their own ethical conduct, but they don’t urge others to be ethical. ^an5-106-3-3

And they watch themselves, but don’t watch others. ^an5-106-3-4

That’s how a mendicant could live comfortably while staying in a monastic community.” ^an5-106-3-5

“But sir, could there be another way for a mendicant to live comfortably while staying in a monastic community?” ^an5-106-4-1

“There could, Ānanda. ^an5-106-4-2

It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in their own ethical conduct, but they don’t urge others to be ethical. ^an5-106-4-3

And they watch themselves, but don’t watch others. ^an5-106-4-4

And they’re not well-known, but aren’t bothered by that. ^an5-106-4-5

That’s how a mendicant could live comfortably while staying in a monastic community.” ^an5-106-4-6

“But sir, could there be another way for a mendicant to live comfortably while staying in a monastic community?” ^an5-106-5-1

“There could, Ānanda. ^an5-106-5-2

It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in their own ethical conduct, but they don’t urge others to be ethical. ^an5-106-5-3

And they watch themselves, but don’t watch others. ^an5-106-5-4

And they’re not well-known, but aren’t bothered by that. ^an5-106-5-5

And they get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-106-5-6

That’s how a mendicant could live comfortably while staying in a monastic community.” ^an5-106-5-7

“But sir, might there be another way for a mendicant to live comfortably while staying in a monastic community?” ^an5-106-6-1

“There could, Ānanda. ^an5-106-6-2

It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in their own ethical conduct, but they don’t urge others to be ethical. ^an5-106-6-3

And they watch themselves, but don’t watch others. ^an5-106-6-4

And they’re not well-known, but aren’t bothered by that. ^an5-106-6-5

And they get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-106-6-6

And they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-106-6-7

That’s how a mendicant could live comfortably while staying in a monastic community. ^an5-106-6-8

And I say that there is no better or finer way of living comfortably than this.” ^an5-106-7-1

### Ethics (Numbered Discourses 5.107) ^an5-107-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-107-1-1

What five? ^an5-107-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is accomplished in ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-107-2-2

A mendicant with these five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an5-107-3-1

### An adept (Numbered Discourses 5.108) ^an5-108-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-108-1-1

What five? ^an5-108-2-1

It’s when they have the entire spectrum of the adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-108-2-2

A mendicant with these five qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an5-108-2-3

### All Four Quarters (Numbered Discourses 5.109) ^an5-109-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is at ease in any quarter. ^an5-109-1-1

What five? ^an5-109-1-2

It’s when mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-109-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-109-1-4

They’re content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-109-1-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-109-1-6

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-109-1-7

A mendicant with these five qualities is at ease in any quarter.” ^an5-109-1-8

### Wilderness (Numbered Discourses 5.110) ^an5-110-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has five qualities they’re ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an5-110-1-1

What five? ^an5-110-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-110-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-110-1-4

They live with energy roused up. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-110-1-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-110-1-6

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-110-1-7

When a mendicant possesses these five qualities they’re ready to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.” ^an5-110-1-8

### Visiting Families (Numbered Discourses 5.111) ^an5-111-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities who visits families is unlikable and disagreeable, not respected or admired. ^an5-111-1-1

What five? ^an5-111-1-2

They act as though they’re close to people they hardly know. They give away things they don’t own. They over-associate with close friends. They whisper in the ear. And they ask for too much. ^an5-111-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities who visits families is unlikable and disagreeable, not respected or admired. ^an5-111-1-4

A mendicant with five qualities who visits families is dear and beloved, respected and admired. ^an5-111-2-1

What five? ^an5-111-2-2

They don’t act as though they’re close to people they hardly know. They don’t give away things they don’t own. They don’t over-associate with close friends. They don’t whisper in the ear. And they don’t ask for too much. ^an5-111-2-3

A mendicant with these five qualities who visits families is dear and beloved, respected and admired.” ^an5-111-2-4

### A Second Monk (Numbered Discourses 5.112) ^an5-112-0

“Mendicants, you shouldn’t take a second monk with five qualities. ^an5-112-1-1

What five? ^an5-112-1-2

They walk too far away or too close behind. They don’t take your bowl when it’s full. They don’t warn you when your speech is bordering on an offense. They keep on interrupting while you’re speaking. And they’re witless, dull, and idiotic. ^an5-112-1-3

You shouldn’t take a second monk these five qualities. ^an5-112-1-4

You should take a second monk with five qualities. ^an5-112-2-1

What five? ^an5-112-2-2

They don’t walk too far away or too close behind. They take your bowl when it is full. They warn you when your speech is bordering on an offense. They don’t interrupt while you’re speaking. And they’re wise, bright, and clever. ^an5-112-2-3

You should take a second monk with these five qualities.” ^an5-112-2-4

### Right Immersion (Numbered Discourses 5.113) ^an5-113-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has five qualities can’t enter and remain in right immersion. ^an5-113-1-1

What five? ^an5-113-1-2

It’s when a mendicant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-113-1-3

A mendicant who possesses these five qualities can’t enter and remain in right immersion. ^an5-113-1-4

A mendicant who has five qualities can enter and remain in right immersion. ^an5-113-2-1

What five? ^an5-113-2-2

It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-113-2-3

A mendicant who possesses these five qualities can enter and remain in right immersion.” ^an5-113-2-4

### At Andhakavinda (Numbered Discourses 5.114) ^an5-114-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Magadhans at Andhakavinda. ^an5-114-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an5-114-1-2

“Ānanda, those mendicants who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training should be encouraged, supported, and established in five things. ^an5-114-2-1

What five? ^an5-114-2-2

They should be encouraged, supported, and established in restraint in the monastic code: ‘Reverends, please be ethical. Live restrained in the monastic code, conducting yourselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, keep the rules you’ve undertaken.’ ^an5-114-2-3

They should be encouraged, supported, and established in sense restraint: ‘Reverends, please live with sense doors guarded, mindfully alert and on guard, with protected mind, having a heart protected by mindfulness.’ ^an5-114-3-1

They should be encouraged, supported, and established in limiting their speech: ‘Reverends, please speak little. Put a limit on your speech.’ ^an5-114-4-1

They should be encouraged, supported, and established in retreat: ‘Reverends, please live in the wilderness. Frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.’ ^an5-114-5-1

They should be encouraged, supported, and established in right perspective: ‘Reverends, please hold right view and have right perspective.’ ^an5-114-6-1

Those mendicants who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training should be encouraged, supported, and established by you in these five things.” ^an5-114-6-2

### Stingy (Numbered Discourses 5.115) ^an5-115-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-115-1-1

What five? ^an5-115-1-2

She is stingy with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teaching. ^an5-115-1-3

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-115-1-4

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-115-2-1

What five? ^an5-115-2-2

She is not stingy with dwellings, families, material things, praise, or the teaching. ^an5-115-2-3

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-115-2-4

### Praise (Numbered Discourses 5.116) ^an5-116-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-116-1-1

What five? ^an5-116-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, she praises those deserving of criticism, ^an5-116-1-3

and criticizes those deserving of praise. ^an5-116-1-4

She arouses faith in things that are dubious, ^an5-116-1-5

and doesn’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an5-116-1-6

And she wastes gifts given in faith. ^an5-116-1-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-116-1-8

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-116-2-1

What five? ^an5-116-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, she criticizes those deserving of criticism, ^an5-116-2-3

and praises those deserving of praise. ^an5-116-2-4

She doesn’t arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an5-116-2-5

and does arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an5-116-2-6

And she doesn’t waste gifts given in faith. ^an5-116-2-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-116-2-8

### Jealous (Numbered Discourses 5.117) ^an5-117-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-117-1-1

What five? ^an5-117-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, she praises those deserving of criticism, ^an5-117-1-3

and criticizes those deserving of praise. ^an5-117-1-4

She is jealous, ^an5-117-1-5

stingy, ^an5-117-1-6

and wastes gifts given in faith. ^an5-117-1-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-117-1-8

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-117-2-1

What five? ^an5-117-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, she criticizes those deserving of criticism, ^an5-117-2-3

and praises those deserving of praise. ^an5-117-2-4

She is not jealous, ^an5-117-2-5

or stingy, ^an5-117-2-6

and doesn’t waste gifts given in faith. ^an5-117-2-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-117-2-8

### Having Wrong View (Numbered Discourses 5.118) ^an5-118-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-118-1-1

What five? ^an5-118-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, she praises those deserving of criticism, ^an5-118-1-3

and criticizes those deserving of praise. ^an5-118-1-4

She has wrong view ^an5-118-1-5

and wrong purpose, ^an5-118-1-6

and wastes gifts given in faith. ^an5-118-1-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-118-1-8

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-118-2-1

What five? ^an5-118-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, she criticizes those deserving of criticism, ^an5-118-2-3

and praises those deserving of praise. ^an5-118-2-4

She has right view ^an5-118-2-5

and right purpose, ^an5-118-2-6

and doesn’t waste gifts given in faith. ^an5-118-2-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-118-2-8

### Wrong Speech (Numbered Discourses 5.119) ^an5-119-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-119-1-1

What five? ^an5-119-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, she praises those deserving of criticism, ^an5-119-1-3

and criticizes those deserving of praise. ^an5-119-1-4

She has wrong speech ^an5-119-1-5

and wrong action, ^an5-119-1-6

and wastes gifts given in faith. ^an5-119-1-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-119-1-8

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-119-2-1

What five? ^an5-119-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, she criticizes those deserving of criticism, ^an5-119-2-3

and praises those deserving of praise. ^an5-119-2-4

She has right speech ^an5-119-2-5

and right action, ^an5-119-2-6

and doesn’t waste gifts given in faith. ^an5-119-2-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-119-2-8

### Wrong Effort (Numbered Discourses 5.120) ^an5-120-0

“Mendicants, a nun with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-120-1-1

What five? ^an5-120-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, she praises those deserving of criticism, ^an5-120-1-3

and criticizes those deserving of praise. ^an5-120-1-4

She has wrong effort and ^an5-120-1-5

wrong mindfulness, ^an5-120-1-6

and wastes gifts given in faith. ^an5-120-1-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-120-1-8

A nun with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-120-2-1

What five? ^an5-120-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, she criticizes those deserving of criticism, ^an5-120-2-3

and praises those deserving of praise. ^an5-120-2-4

She has right effort and ^an5-120-2-5

right mindfulness, ^an5-120-2-6

and doesn’t waste gifts given in faith. ^an5-120-2-7

A nun with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-120-2-8

### Sick (Numbered Discourses 5.121) ^an5-121-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-121-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the infirmary, where ^an5-121-1-2

he saw a certain mendicant who was weak and sick. ^an5-121-1-3

He sat down on the seat spread out, ^an5-121-1-4

and addressed the mendicants: ^an5-121-1-5

“Mendicants, if a weak and sick mendicant does not neglect five things, it can be expected that ^an5-121-2-1

they will soon realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-121-2-2

What five? ^an5-121-3-1

It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, and has well established the perception of their own death. ^an5-121-3-2

If a weak and sick mendicant does not neglect these five things, it can be expected that ^an5-121-3-3

they will soon realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^an5-121-3-4

### Mindfulness Well Established (Numbered Discourses 5.122) ^an5-122-0

“Mendicants, any monk or nun who develops and cultivates five qualities can expect one of two results: ^an5-122-1-1

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return. ^an5-122-1-2

What five? ^an5-122-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has well established mindfulness inside themselves in order to understand the arising and passing away of phenomena, meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, and observes the impermanence of all conditions. ^an5-122-2-2

Any monk or nun who develops and cultivates these five qualities can expect one of two results: ^an5-122-2-3

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return.” ^an5-122-2-4

### A Carer (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.123) ^an5-123-0

“Mendicants, a patient with five qualities is hard to care for. ^an5-123-1-1

What five? ^an5-123-1-2

They do what is unsuitable. They don’t know moderation in what is suitable. They don’t take their medicine. Though their carer wants what’s best for them, they don’t accurately report their symptoms by saying when they’re getting worse, getting better, or staying the same. And they cannot endure physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an5-123-1-3

A patient with these five qualities is hard to care for. ^an5-123-1-4

A patient with five qualities is easy to care for. ^an5-123-2-1

What five? ^an5-123-2-2

They do what is suitable. They know moderation in what is suitable. They take their medicine. Because their carer wants what’s best for them, they accurately report their symptoms by saying when they’re getting worse, getting better, or staying the same. And they can endure physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an5-123-2-3

A patient with these five qualities is easy to care for.” ^an5-123-2-4

### A Carer (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.124) ^an5-124-0

“Mendicants, a carer with five qualities is not competent to care for a patient. ^an5-124-1-1

What five? ^an5-124-1-2

They’re unable to prepare medicine. ^an5-124-1-3

They don’t know what is suitable and unsuitable, so they supply what is unsuitable and remove what is suitable. ^an5-124-1-4

They care for the sick for the sake of material benefits, not out of love. ^an5-124-1-5

They’re disgusted to remove feces, urine, vomit, or spit. ^an5-124-1-6

They’re unable to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire the patient with a Dhamma talk from time to time. ^an5-124-1-7

A carer with these five qualities is not competent to care for a patient. ^an5-124-1-8

A carer with five qualities is competent to care for a patient. ^an5-124-2-1

What five? ^an5-124-2-2

They’re able to prepare medicine. ^an5-124-2-3

They know what is suitable and unsuitable, so they remove what is unsuitable and supply what is suitable. ^an5-124-2-4

They care for the sick out of love, not for the sake of material benefits. ^an5-124-2-5

They’re not disgusted to remove feces, urine, vomit, or spit. ^an5-124-2-6

They’re able to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire the patient with a Dhamma talk from time to time. ^an5-124-2-7

A carer with these five qualities is competent to care for a patient.” ^an5-124-2-8

### Longevity (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.125) ^an5-125-0

“Mendicants, these five things impede longevity. ^an5-125-1-1

What five? ^an5-125-1-2

Doing what is unsuitable, not knowing moderation in what is suitable, eating food unfit for consumption, activity at unsuitable times, and unchastity. ^an5-125-1-3

These are the five things that impede longevity. ^an5-125-1-4

These five things promote longevity. ^an5-125-2-1

What five? ^an5-125-2-2

Doing what is suitable, knowing moderation in what is suitable, eating food fit for consumption, activity at suitable times, and chastity. ^an5-125-2-3

These are the five things that promote longevity.” ^an5-125-2-4

### Longevity (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.126) ^an5-126-0

“Mendicants, these five things impede longevity. ^an5-126-1-1

What five? ^an5-126-1-2

Doing what is unsuitable, not knowing moderation in what is suitable, eating food unfit for consumption, unethical behavior, and bad friends. ^an5-126-1-3

These are the five things that impede longevity. ^an5-126-1-4

These five things promote longevity. ^an5-126-2-1

What five? ^an5-126-2-2

Doing what is suitable, knowing moderation in what is suitable, eating food fit for consumption, ethical conduct, and good friends. ^an5-126-2-3

These are the five things that promote longevity.” ^an5-126-2-4

### Living Apart (Numbered Discourses 5.127) ^an5-127-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is not fit to live apart from a Saṅgha community. ^an5-127-1-1

What five? ^an5-127-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not content with any kind of robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they have a lot of sensual thoughts. ^an5-127-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is not fit to live apart from a Saṅgha community. ^an5-127-1-4

A mendicant with five qualities is fit to live apart from a Saṅgha community. ^an5-127-2-1

What five? ^an5-127-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is content with any kind of robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they think a lot about renunciation. ^an5-127-2-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is fit to live apart from a Saṅgha community.” ^an5-127-2-4

### An Ascetic’s Happiness (Numbered Discourses 5.128) ^an5-128-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of suffering for an ascetic. ^an5-128-1-1

What five? ^an5-128-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not content with any kind of robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they lead the spiritual life dissatisfied. ^an5-128-1-3

These are five kinds of suffering for an ascetic. ^an5-128-1-4

There are these five kinds of happiness for an ascetic. ^an5-128-2-1

What five? ^an5-128-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is content with any kind of robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they lead the spiritual life satisfied. ^an5-128-2-3

These are five kinds of happiness for an ascetic.” ^an5-128-2-4

### Fatal Wounds (Numbered Discourses 5.129) ^an5-129-0

“Mendicants, these five fatal wounds lead to a place of loss, to hell. ^an5-129-1-1

What five? ^an5-129-1-2

Murdering your mother or father or a perfected one; maliciously shedding the blood of a Realized One; and causing a schism in the Saṅgha. ^an5-129-1-3

These five fatal wounds lead to a place of loss, to hell.” ^an5-129-1-4

### Loss (Numbered Discourses 5.130) ^an5-130-0

“Mendicants, there are these five losses. ^an5-130-1-1

What five? ^an5-130-1-2

Loss of relatives, wealth, health, ethics, and view. ^an5-130-1-3

It is not because of loss of relatives, wealth, or health that sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-130-1-4

It is because of loss of ethics or view that sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-130-1-5

These are the five losses. ^an5-130-1-6

There are these five endowments. ^an5-130-2-1

What five? ^an5-130-2-2

Endowment with relatives, wealth, health, ethics, and view. ^an5-130-2-3

It is not because of endowment with relatives, wealth, or health that sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-130-2-4

It is because of endowment with ethics or view that sentient beings, when their body breaks up, after death, are reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-130-2-5

These are the five endowments.” ^an5-130-2-6

### Wielding Power (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.131) ^an5-131-0

“Mendicants, possessing five factors a wheel-turning monarch wields power only in a principled manner. ^an5-131-1-1

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an5-131-1-2

What five? ^an5-131-2-1

A wheel-turning monarch knows what is right, knows principle, knows moderation, knows the right time, and knows the assembly. ^an5-131-2-2

A wheel-turning monarch who possesses these five factors wields power only in a principled manner. ^an5-131-2-3

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an5-131-2-4

In the same way, possessing five qualities a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma only in a principled manner. ^an5-131-3-1

And that wheel cannot be rolled back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-131-3-2

What five? ^an5-131-4-1

A Realized One knows what is right, knows principle, knows moderation, knows the right time, and knows the assembly. ^an5-131-4-2

Possessing these five qualities a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma only in a principled manner. ^an5-131-4-3

And that wheel cannot be rolled back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world.” ^an5-131-4-4

### Wielding Power (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.132) ^an5-132-0

“Mendicants, possessing five factors a wheel-turning monarch’s eldest son continues to wield the power set in motion by his father only in a principled manner. ^an5-132-1-1

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an5-132-1-2

What five? ^an5-132-2-1

A wheel-turning monarch’s oldest son knows what is right, knows principle, knows moderation, knows the right time, and knows the assembly. ^an5-132-2-2

A wheel-turning monarch’s oldest son who possesses these five factors continues to wield the power set in motion by his father only in a principled manner. ^an5-132-2-3

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an5-132-2-4

In the same way, possessing five qualities Sāriputta rightly keeps rolling the supreme Wheel of Dhamma that was rolled forth by the Realized One. ^an5-132-3-1

And that wheel cannot be turned back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world. ^an5-132-3-2

What five? ^an5-132-4-1

Sāriputta knows what is right, knows principle, knows moderation, knows the right time, and knows the assembly. ^an5-132-4-2

Possessing these five qualities Sāriputta rightly keeps rolling the supreme Wheel of Dhamma that was rolled forth by the Realized One. ^an5-132-4-3

And that wheel cannot be turned back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world.” ^an5-132-4-4

### A Principled King (Numbered Discourses 5.133) ^an5-133-0

“Mendicants, even a wheel-turning monarch, a just and principled king, does not wield power without having their own king.” ^an5-133-1-1

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha, ^an5-133-1-2

“But who is the king of the wheel-turning monarch, the just and principled king?” ^an5-133-1-3

“It is principle, monk,” said the Buddha. ^an5-133-1-4

“Monk, a wheel-turning monarch provides just protection and security for his court, relying only on principle—honoring, respecting, and venerating principle, having principle as his flag, banner, and authority. ^an5-133-2-1

He provides just protection and security for his aristocrats, vassals, troops, brahmins and householders, people of town and country, ascetics and brahmins, beasts and birds. ^an5-133-3-1

When he has done this, he wields power only in a principled manner. ^an5-133-3-2

And this power cannot be undermined by any human enemy. ^an5-133-3-3

In the same way, monk, a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, a just and principled king, provides just protection and security for the monks, relying only on principle—honoring, respecting, and venerating principle, having principle as his flag, banner, and authority. ^an5-133-4-1

‘This kind of bodily action should be cultivated. This kind of bodily action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-4-2

This kind of verbal action should be cultivated. This kind of verbal action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-4-3

This kind of mental action should be cultivated. This kind of mental action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-4-4

This kind of livelihood should be cultivated. This kind of livelihood should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-4-5

This kind of village or town should be cultivated. This kind of village or town should not be cultivated.’ ^an5-133-4-6

In the same way, monk, a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, a just and principled king, provides just protection and security for the nuns … laymen … laywomen, relying only on principle—honoring, respecting, and venerating principle, having principle as his flag, banner, and authority. ^an5-133-5-1

‘This kind of bodily action should be cultivated. This kind of bodily action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-5-2

This kind of verbal action should be cultivated. This kind of verbal action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-5-3

This kind of mental action should be cultivated. This kind of mental action should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-5-4

This kind of livelihood should be cultivated. This kind of livelihood should not be cultivated. ^an5-133-5-5

This kind of village or town should be cultivated. This kind of village or town should not be cultivated.’ ^an5-133-5-6

When a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has provided just protection and security for the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen, he rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma only in a principled manner. ^an5-133-6-1

And that wheel cannot be rolled back by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world.” ^an5-133-6-2

### In Whatever Region (Numbered Discourses 5.134) ^an5-134-0

“Mendicants, with five factors an anointed aristocratic king lives in his own realm, no matter what region he lives in. ^an5-134-1-1

What five? ^an5-134-2-1

An anointed aristocratic king is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-134-2-2

He is rich, affluent, and wealthy, with a full treasury and storehouses. ^an5-134-2-3

He is powerful, having an army of four divisions that is obedient and carries out instructions. ^an5-134-2-4

He has a commander who is astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present. ^an5-134-2-5

These four things bring his glory to fruition. ^an5-134-2-6

With these five qualities, including glory, an anointed aristocratic king lives in his own realm, no matter what direction he lives in. ^an5-134-2-7

Why is that? ^an5-134-2-8

Because that is how it is for victors. ^an5-134-2-9

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities lives with mind freed, no matter what region they live in. ^an5-134-3-1

What five? ^an5-134-3-2

It’s when mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-134-3-3

This is like the anointed aristocratic king’s impeccable birth. ^an5-134-3-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-134-3-5

This is like the anointed aristocratic king being rich, affluent, and wealthy, with full treasury and storehouses. ^an5-134-3-6

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-134-3-7

This is like the anointed aristocratic king having power. ^an5-134-3-8

They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-134-3-9

This is like the anointed aristocratic king having a commander. ^an5-134-3-10

These four qualities bring their freedom to fruition. ^an5-134-3-11

With these five qualities, including freedom, they live in their own realm, no matter what region they live in. ^an5-134-3-12

Why is that? ^an5-134-3-13

Because that is how it is for those whose mind is free.” ^an5-134-3-14

### Aspiration (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.135) ^an5-135-0

“Mendicants, an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with five factors aspires to kingship. ^an5-135-1-1

What five? ^an5-135-1-2

It’s when an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-135-1-3

He is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. ^an5-135-1-4

He is dear and beloved to his parents. ^an5-135-1-5

He is dear and beloved to the people of town and country. ^an5-135-1-6

He is trained and skilled in the arts of anointed aristocratic kings, such as elephant riding, horse riding, driving a chariot, archery, and swordsmanship. ^an5-135-1-7

He thinks: ^an5-135-2-1

‘I’m well born on both my mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-135-2-2

Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship? ^an5-135-2-3

I’m attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. ^an5-135-2-4

Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship? ^an5-135-2-5

I’m dear and beloved to my parents. ^an5-135-2-6

Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship? ^an5-135-2-7

I’m dear and beloved to the people of town and country. ^an5-135-2-8

Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship? ^an5-135-2-9

I’m trained and skilled in the arts of anointed aristocratic kings, such as elephant riding, horse riding, driving a chariot, archery, and swordsmanship. ^an5-135-2-10

Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?’ ^an5-135-2-11

An anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with these five factors aspires to kingship. ^an5-135-2-12

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities aspires to end the defilements. ^an5-135-3-1

What five? ^an5-135-3-2

It’s when a mendicant has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an5-135-3-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-135-3-4

They are rarely ill or unwell. Their stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an5-135-3-5

They’re not devious or deceitful. They reveal themselves honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions. ^an5-135-3-6

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-135-3-7

They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-135-3-8

They think: ^an5-135-4-1

‘I am a person of faith; I have faith in the Realized One’s awakening … ^an5-135-4-2

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements? ^an5-135-4-4

I’m rarely ill or unwell. My stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an5-135-4-5

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements? ^an5-135-4-6

I’m not devious or deceitful. I reveal myself honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions. ^an5-135-4-7

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements? ^an5-135-4-8

I live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. I’m strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-135-4-9

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements? ^an5-135-4-10

I’m wise. I have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-135-4-11

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?’ ^an5-135-4-12

A mendicant with these five qualities aspires to end the defilements.” ^an5-135-4-13

### Aspiration (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.136) ^an5-136-0

“Mendicants, an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with five factors aspires to become a viceroy. ^an5-136-1-1

What five? ^an5-136-1-2

It’s when an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-136-1-3

He is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. ^an5-136-1-4

He is dear and beloved to his parents. ^an5-136-1-5

He is dear and beloved to the armed forces. ^an5-136-1-6

He is astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present. ^an5-136-1-7

He thinks: ^an5-136-2-1

‘I’m well born … ^an5-136-2-2

attractive … ^an5-136-2-4

dear and beloved to my parents … ^an5-136-2-6

dear and beloved to the armed forces … ^an5-136-2-8

I’m astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present. ^an5-136-2-10

Why shouldn’t I aspire to become a viceroy?’ ^an5-136-2-11

An anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with these five factors aspires to become a viceroy. ^an5-136-2-12

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities aspires to end the defilements. ^an5-136-3-1

What five? ^an5-136-3-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-136-3-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-136-3-4

Their mind is firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an5-136-3-5

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an5-136-3-6

They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-136-3-7

They think: ^an5-136-4-1

‘I’m ethical … ^an5-136-4-2

learned … ^an5-136-4-4

mindful … ^an5-136-4-6

energetic … ^an5-136-4-8

wise. I have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an5-136-4-10

Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?’ ^an5-136-4-11

A mendicant with these five qualities aspires to end the defilements.” ^an5-136-4-12

### Little Sleep (Numbered Discourses 5.137) ^an5-137-0

“Mendicants, these five sleep little at night, staying mostly awake. ^an5-137-1-1

What five? ^an5-137-1-2

A woman longing for a man. ^an5-137-1-3

A man longing for a woman. ^an5-137-1-4

A thief longing for their loot. ^an5-137-1-5

A king busy with his duties. ^an5-137-1-6

A mendicant longing for freedom from the yoke. ^an5-137-1-7

These five sleep little at night, staying mostly awake.” ^an5-137-1-8

### Eating Food (Numbered Discourses 5.138) ^an5-138-0

“Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with five factors eats food, takes up space, drops dung, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a royal bull elephant. ^an5-138-1-1

What five? ^an5-138-1-2

It’s when a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-138-1-3

A royal bull elephant with these five factors eats food, takes up space, drops dung, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a royal bull elephant. ^an5-138-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities eats food, takes up space, tramples beds and chairs, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a mendicant. ^an5-138-2-1

What five? ^an5-138-2-2

It’s when a mendicant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-138-2-3

A mendicant with these five qualities eats food, takes up space, tramples beds and chairs, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a mendicant.” ^an5-138-2-4

### Cannot Endure (Numbered Discourses 5.139) ^an5-139-0

“Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with five factors is not worthy of a king, not fit to serve a king, and is not reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-139-1-1

What five? ^an5-139-1-2

It’s when a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-139-1-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights? ^an5-139-2-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders at the sight of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-139-2-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights. ^an5-139-2-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure sounds? ^an5-139-3-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders at the sound of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry, or the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-139-3-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure sounds. ^an5-139-3-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure smells? ^an5-139-4-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when it smells the odor of the feces and urine of battle-hardened, pedigree royal bull elephants. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-139-4-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure smells. ^an5-139-4-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure tastes? ^an5-139-5-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when it misses a meal of grass and water, or it misses two, three, four, or five meals. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-139-5-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure tastes. ^an5-139-5-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure touches? ^an5-139-6-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when struck by a swift arrow, or by two, three, four, or five swift arrows. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle. ^an5-139-6-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure touches. ^an5-139-6-3

A royal bull elephant with these five factors is not worthy of a king, not fit to serve a king, and is not reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-139-7-1

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is not worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, not worthy of hospitality, not worthy of a religious donation, not worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is not the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-139-8-1

What five? ^an5-139-8-2

It’s when a mendicant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-139-8-3

And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure sights? ^an5-139-9-1

It’s when a mendicant, seeing a sight with their eyes, is aroused by a desirable sight, so is not able to still the mind. ^an5-139-9-2

That’s how a mendicant can’t endure sights. ^an5-139-9-3

And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure sounds? ^an5-139-10-1

It’s when a mendicant, hearing a sound with their ears, is aroused by a desirable sound, so is not able to still the mind. ^an5-139-10-2

That’s how a mendicant can’t endure sounds. ^an5-139-10-3

And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure smells? ^an5-139-11-1

It’s when a mendicant, smelling an odor with their nose, is aroused by a desirable smell, so is not able to still the mind. ^an5-139-11-2

That’s how a mendicant can’t endure smells. ^an5-139-11-3

And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure tastes? ^an5-139-12-1

It’s when a mendicant, tasting a flavor with their tongue, is aroused by desirable tastes, so is not able to still the mind. ^an5-139-12-2

That’s how a mendicant can’t endure tastes. ^an5-139-12-3

And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure touches? ^an5-139-13-1

It’s when a mendicant, encountering a touch with their body, is aroused by a desirable touch, so is not able to still the mind. ^an5-139-13-2

That’s how a mendicant can’t endure touches. ^an5-139-13-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is not worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, not worthy of hospitality, not worthy of a religious donation, not worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is not the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-139-14-1

A royal bull elephant with five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-139-15-1

What five? ^an5-139-15-2

It’s when a royal bull elephant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-139-15-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure sights? ^an5-139-16-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder at the sight of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry. It stays firm, and plunges into battle. ^an5-139-16-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure sights. ^an5-139-16-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure sounds? ^an5-139-17-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant does not falter or founder at the sound of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry, or the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. It stays firm, and plunges into battle. ^an5-139-17-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure sounds. ^an5-139-17-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure smells? ^an5-139-18-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when it smells the odor of the feces and urine of battle-hardened, pedigree royal bull elephants. It stays firm, and plunges into battle. ^an5-139-18-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure smells. ^an5-139-18-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure tastes? ^an5-139-19-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when it misses a meal of grass and water, or it misses two, three, four, or five meals. It stays firm, and plunges into battle. ^an5-139-19-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure tastes. ^an5-139-19-3

And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure touches? ^an5-139-20-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when struck by a swift arrow, or by two, three, four, or five swift arrows. It stays firm, and plunges into battle. ^an5-139-20-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure touches. ^an5-139-20-3

A royal bull elephant with these five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-139-21-1

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-139-22-1

What five? ^an5-139-22-2

It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an5-139-22-3

And how is it that a mendicant can endure sights? ^an5-139-23-1

It’s when a mendicant, seeing a sight with their eyes, is not aroused by a desirable sight, so is able to still the mind. ^an5-139-23-2

That’s how a mendicant can endure sights. ^an5-139-23-3

And how is it that a mendicant can endure sounds? ^an5-139-24-1

It’s when a mendicant, hearing a sound with their ears, is not aroused by desirable sounds, so is able to still the mind. ^an5-139-24-2

That’s how a mendicant can endure sounds. ^an5-139-24-3

And how is it that a mendicant can endure smells? ^an5-139-25-1

It’s when a mendicant, smelling an odor with their nose, is not aroused by a desirable smell, so is able to still the mind. ^an5-139-25-2

That’s how a mendicant can endure smells. ^an5-139-25-3

And how is it that a mendicant can endure tastes? ^an5-139-26-1

It’s when a mendicant, tasting a flavor with their tongue, is not aroused by desirable tastes, so is able to still the mind. ^an5-139-26-2

That’s how a mendicant can endure tastes. ^an5-139-26-3

And how is it that a mendicant can endure touches? ^an5-139-27-1

It’s when a mendicant, encountering a touch with their body, is not aroused by a desirable touch, so is able to still the mind. ^an5-139-27-2

That’s how a mendicant can endure touches. ^an5-139-27-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an5-139-28-1

### A Listener (Numbered Discourses 5.140) ^an5-140-0

“Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-140-1-1

What five? ^an5-140-1-2

A royal bull elephant listens, destroys, protects, endures, and goes fast. ^an5-140-1-3

And how does a royal bull elephant listen? ^an5-140-2-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens to whatever task the elephant trainer has it do, ^an5-140-2-2

whether or not it has done it before. ^an5-140-2-3

That’s how a royal bull elephant listens. ^an5-140-2-5

And how does a royal bull elephant destroy? ^an5-140-3-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant in battle destroys elephants with their riders, horses with their riders, chariots and charioteers, and foot soldiers. ^an5-140-3-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant destroys. ^an5-140-3-3

And how does a royal bull elephant protect? ^an5-140-4-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant in battle protects its fore-quarters and hind-quarters, its fore-feet and hind-feet, and its head, ears, tusks, trunk, tail, and rider. ^an5-140-4-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant protects. ^an5-140-4-3

And how does a royal bull elephant endure? ^an5-140-5-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant in battle endures being struck by spears, swords, arrows, and axes; it endures the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. ^an5-140-5-2

That’s how a royal bull elephant endures. ^an5-140-5-3

And how does a royal bull elephant go fast? ^an5-140-6-1

It’s when a royal bull elephant swiftly goes in whatever direction the elephant trainer sends it, ^an5-140-6-2

whether or not it has been there before. ^an5-140-6-3

That’s how a royal bull elephant goes fast. ^an5-140-6-5

A royal bull elephant with these five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-140-7-1

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-140-8-1

What five? ^an5-140-8-2

A mendicant listens, destroys, protects, endures, and goes fast. ^an5-140-8-3

And how does a mendicant listen? ^an5-140-9-1

It’s when a mendicant pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens when the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One is being taught. ^an5-140-9-2

That’s how a mendicant listens. ^an5-140-9-3

And how does a mendicant destroy? ^an5-140-10-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, calm them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an5-140-10-2

That’s how a mendicant destroys. ^an5-140-10-3

And how does a mendicant protect? ^an5-140-11-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with the eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an5-140-11-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an5-140-11-3

Hearing a sound with the ears … ^an5-140-11-4

Smelling an odor with the nose … ^an5-140-11-5

Tasting a flavor with the tongue … ^an5-140-11-6

Encountering a touch with the body … ^an5-140-11-7

Knowing an idea with the mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an5-140-11-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it. ^an5-140-11-9

That’s how a mendicant protects. ^an5-140-11-10

And how does a mendicant endure? ^an5-140-12-1

It’s when a mendicant endures cold, heat, hunger, and thirst; the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles; rude and unwelcome criticism; and puts up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an5-140-12-2

That’s how a mendicant endures. ^an5-140-12-3

And how does a mendicant go fast? ^an5-140-13-1

It’s when a mendicant swiftly goes in the direction they’ve never gone before in all this long time; that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment. ^an5-140-13-2

That’s how a mendicant goes fast. ^an5-140-13-3

A mendicant with these five qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an5-140-14-1

### Scorn (Numbered Discourses 5.141) ^an5-141-0

“Mendicants, these five individuals are found in the world. ^an5-141-1-1

What five? ^an5-141-1-2

One gives then scorns, one lives together then scorns, one is gullible for gossip, one is impulsive, and one is dull and stupid. ^an5-141-1-3

And how does an individual give then scorn? ^an5-141-2-1

It’s when an individual gives an individual robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-141-2-2

They think: ^an5-141-2-3

‘I give; ^an5-141-2-4

this one receives.’ ^an5-141-2-5

They give to them, then they scorn them. ^an5-141-2-6

That’s how an individual gives then scorns. ^an5-141-2-7

And how does an individual live together then scorn? ^an5-141-3-1

It’s when an individual lives with someone else for two or three years. ^an5-141-3-2

They live together with them, then they scorn them. ^an5-141-3-3

That’s how an individual lives together then scorns. ^an5-141-3-4

And how is an individual gullible for gossip? ^an5-141-4-1

It’s when they’re very quick to believe what a certain individual says in praise or criticism of another. ^an5-141-4-2

That’s how an individual is gullible for gossip. ^an5-141-4-3

And how is an individual impulsive? ^an5-141-5-1

It’s when a certain individual is fickle in faith, devotion, fondness, and confidence. ^an5-141-5-2

That’s how an individual is impulsive. ^an5-141-5-3

And how is an individual dull and stupid? ^an5-141-6-1

It’s when they don’t know the difference between qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright. ^an5-141-6-2

That’s how an individual is dull and stupid. ^an5-141-6-3

These are the five individuals found in the world.” ^an5-141-6-4

### Transgression (Numbered Discourses 5.142) ^an5-142-0

“Mendicants, these five individuals are found in the world. ^an5-142-1-1

What five? ^an5-142-1-2

One individual transgresses and regrets it. ^an5-142-1-3

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^an5-142-1-4

One individual transgresses and doesn’t regret it. ^an5-142-2-1

And they don’t understand … ^an5-142-2-2

One individual doesn’t transgress yet still feels regret. ^an5-142-3-1

And they don’t understand … ^an5-142-3-2

One individual neither transgresses nor regrets. ^an5-142-4-1

But they don’t understand … ^an5-142-4-2

One individual neither transgresses nor regrets. ^an5-142-5-1

And they do understand where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^an5-142-5-2

Take the case of the individual who transgresses and regrets it. ^an5-142-6-1

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without remainder. ^an5-142-6-2

They should be told: ^an5-142-6-3

‘Venerable, the defilements born of transgression are found in you, and the defilements born of regret grow. You would do well to give up the defilements born of transgression and get rid of the defilements born of regret, and then develop the mind and wisdom. ^an5-142-6-4

In this way you’ll become just like the fifth individual.’ ^an5-142-6-5

Take the case of the individual who transgresses and doesn’t regret it. ^an5-142-7-1

And they don’t understand… ^an5-142-7-2

They should be told: ^an5-142-7-3

‘Venerable, the defilements born of transgression are found in you, but the defilements born of regret don’t grow. You would do well to give up the defilements born of transgression, and then develop the mind and wisdom. ^an5-142-7-4

In this way you’ll become just like the fifth individual.’ ^an5-142-7-5

Take the case of the individual who doesn’t transgress yet feels regret. ^an5-142-8-1

And they don’t understand… ^an5-142-8-2

They should be told: ^an5-142-8-3

‘Venerable, the defilements born of transgression are not found in you, yet the defilements born of regret grow. You would do well to get rid of the defilements born of regret, and then develop the mind and wisdom. ^an5-142-8-4

In this way you’ll become just like the fifth individual.’ ^an5-142-8-5

Take the case of the individual who neither transgresses nor regrets. ^an5-142-9-1

But they don’t understand… ^an5-142-9-2

They should be told: ^an5-142-9-3

‘Venerable, the defilements born of transgression are not found in you, and the defilements born of regret don’t grow. You would do well to develop the mind and wisdom. ^an5-142-9-4

In this way you’ll become just like the fifth individual.’ ^an5-142-9-5

And so, mendicants, when these four individuals are advised and instructed by comparison with the fifth individual, they gradually attain the ending of defilements.” ^an5-142-10-1

### At Sārandada (Numbered Discourses 5.143) ^an5-143-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-143-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Vesālī for alms. ^an5-143-1-2

Now at that time around five hundred Licchavis were sitting together at the Sārandada Shrine, and this discussion came up among them, ^an5-143-1-3

“The appearance of five treasures is rare in the world. ^an5-143-1-4

What five? ^an5-143-1-5

The elephant-treasure, the horse-treasure, the jewel-treasure, the woman-treasure, and the householder-treasure. ^an5-143-1-6

The appearance of these five treasures is rare in the world.” ^an5-143-1-7

Then those Licchavis sent a man out on to the road, saying, ^an5-143-2-1

“My man, please tell us when you see the Buddha.” ^an5-143-2-2

That man saw the Buddha coming off in the distance. ^an5-143-2-3

He went to the Licchavis and said, ^an5-143-2-4

“Sirs, the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha is coming. ^an5-143-2-5

Please go at your convenience.” ^an5-143-2-6

Then those Licchavis went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^an5-143-3-1

“Please go to the Sārandada Shrine, out of sympathy.” ^an5-143-4-1

The Buddha consented with silence. ^an5-143-4-2

Then the Buddha went up to the Sārandada Shrine, where he sat on the seat spread out, and said to the Licchavis, ^an5-143-4-3

“Licchavis, what were you sitting talking about just now? What conversation was left unfinished?” ^an5-143-4-4

“Well, worthy Gotama, this discussion came up among us while we were sitting together: ^an5-143-4-5

‘The appearance of five treasures is rare in the world. …’” ^an5-143-4-6

“You Licchavis are so fixated on sensual pleasures, that’s the only discussion that came up! ^an5-143-5-1

Licchavis, the appearance of five treasures is rare in the world. ^an5-143-5-2

What five? ^an5-143-5-3

The appearance of a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. ^an5-143-5-4

A teacher of the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. ^an5-143-5-5

An individual who understands the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. ^an5-143-5-6

An individual who practices in line with the teaching. ^an5-143-5-7

An individual who is grateful and thankful. ^an5-143-5-8

The appearance of these five treasures is rare in the world.” ^an5-143-5-9

### At Tikaṇḍakī (Numbered Discourses 5.144) ^an5-144-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāketa, in Tikaṇḍakī Wood. ^an5-144-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an5-144-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an5-144-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an5-144-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an5-144-1-5

“Mendicants, a mendicant would do well to meditate from time to time perceiving the following: the repulsive in the unrepulsive, ^an5-144-2-1

the unrepulsive in the repulsive, ^an5-144-2-2

the repulsive in both the unrepulsive and the repulsive, and ^an5-144-2-3

the unrepulsive in both the repulsive and the unrepulsive. ^an5-144-2-4

A mendicant would do well to meditate from time to time staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive. ^an5-144-2-5

For what reason should a mendicant meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive? ^an5-144-3-1

‘May greed not arise in me for things that arouse greed.’ ^an5-144-3-2

A mendicant should meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive for this reason. ^an5-144-3-3

For what reason should a mendicant meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive? ^an5-144-4-1

‘May hate not arise in me for things that provoke hate.’ … ^an5-144-4-2

For what reason should a mendicant meditate perceiving the repulsive in both the unrepulsive and the repulsive? ^an5-144-5-1

‘May greed not arise in me for things that arouse greed. May hate not arise in me for things that provoke hate.’ … ^an5-144-5-2

For what reason should a mendicant meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in both the repulsive and the unrepulsive? ^an5-144-6-1

‘May hate not arise in me for things that provoke hate. May greed not arise in me for things that arouse greed.’ … ^an5-144-6-2

For what reason should a mendicant meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive? ^an5-144-7-1

‘May no greed for things that arouse greed, hate for things that provoke hate, or delusion for things that promote delusion arise in me in any way at all.’ ^an5-144-7-2

For this reason a mendicant should meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive.” ^an5-144-7-3

### Hell (Numbered Discourses 5.145) ^an5-145-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-145-1-1

What five? ^an5-145-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-145-1-3

Someone with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-145-1-4

Someone with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there ^an5-145-2-1

What five? ^an5-145-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-145-2-3

Someone with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-145-2-4

### A Friend (Numbered Discourses 5.146) ^an5-146-0

“Mendicants, you shouldn’t associate with a mendicant friend who has five qualities. ^an5-146-1-1

What five? ^an5-146-1-2

They start up work projects. They take up disciplinary issues. They conflict with leading mendicants. They like long and aimless wandering. They’re unable to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire you from time to time with a Dhamma talk. ^an5-146-1-3

Mendicants, you shouldn’t associate with a mendicant friend who possesses these five qualities. ^an5-146-1-4

You should associate with a mendicant friend who has five qualities. ^an5-146-2-1

What five? ^an5-146-2-2

They don’t start up work projects. They don’t take up disciplinary issues. They don’t conflict with leading mendicants. They don’t like long and aimless wandering. They’re able to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire you from time to time with a Dhamma talk. ^an5-146-2-3

You should associate with a mendicant friend who possesses these five qualities.” ^an5-146-2-4

### Gifts of an Untrue Person (Numbered Discourses 5.147) ^an5-147-0

“Mendicants, there are these five gifts of an untrue person. ^an5-147-1-1

What five? ^an5-147-1-2

They give carelessly. They give thoughtlessly. They don’t give with their own hand. They give the dregs. They give without consideration for consequences. ^an5-147-1-3

These are the five gifts of an untrue person. ^an5-147-1-4

There are these five gifts of a true person. ^an5-147-2-1

What five? ^an5-147-2-2

They give carefully. They give thoughtfully. They give with their own hand. They don’t give the dregs. They give with consideration for consequences. ^an5-147-2-3

These are the five gifts of a true person.” ^an5-147-2-4

### Gifts of a True Person (Numbered Discourses 5.148) ^an5-148-0

“There are these five gifts of a true person. ^an5-148-1-1

What five? ^an5-148-1-2

They give a gift out of faith. They give a gift carefully. They give a gift at the right time. They give a gift with no strings attached. They give a gift without damaging themselves or others. ^an5-148-1-3

Having given a gift out of faith, in whatever place the result of that gift manifests they become rich, affluent, and wealthy. And they’re attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. ^an5-148-2-1

Having given a gift carefully, in whatever place the result of that gift manifests they become rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an5-148-3-1

And their children, wives, bondservants, servants, and workers want to listen. They actively listen and try to understand. ^an5-148-3-2

Having given a gift at the right time, in whatever place the result of that gift manifests they become rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an5-148-4-1

And when the time is right, their needs are amply satisfied. ^an5-148-4-2

Having given a gift with no strings attached, in whatever place the result of that gift manifests they become rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an5-148-5-1

And their mind tends to enjoy the five refined kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an5-148-5-2

Having given a gift without damaging themselves or others, in whatever place the result of that gift manifests they become rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an5-148-6-1

And no damage comes to their property from anywhere, whether fire, flood, rulers, bandits, or unloved heirs. ^an5-148-6-2

These are the five gifts of a true person.” ^an5-148-6-3

### Temporarily Free (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.149) ^an5-149-0

“Mendicants, these five things lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-149-1-1

What five? ^an5-149-1-2

They relish work, talk, sleep, and company. And they don’t review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-149-1-3

These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-149-1-4

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-149-2-1

What five? ^an5-149-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, sleep, and company. And they review the extent of their mind’s freedom. ^an5-149-2-3

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free.” ^an5-149-2-4

### Temporarily Free (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.150) ^an5-150-0

“Mendicants, these five things lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-150-1-1

What five? ^an5-150-1-2

They relish work, talk, and sleep. They don’t guard the sense doors and they eat too much. ^an5-150-1-3

These five things lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-150-1-4

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free. ^an5-150-2-1

What five? ^an5-150-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, and sleep. They guard the sense doors and they have moderation in eating. ^an5-150-2-3

These five things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant who is temporarily free.” ^an5-150-2-4

### The Sure Path (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.151) ^an5-151-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-151-1-1

What five? ^an5-151-1-2

They disparage the talk, the speaker, or themselves. They listen with scattered and disunified mind. They apply the mind irrationally. ^an5-151-1-3

Someone with these five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-151-1-4

Someone with five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-151-2-1

What five? ^an5-151-2-2

They don’t disparage the talk, the speaker, or themselves. They listen with unscattered and unified mind. They apply the mind rationally. ^an5-151-2-3

Someone with these five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an5-151-2-4

### The Sure Path (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.152) ^an5-152-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-152-1-1

What five? ^an5-152-1-2

They disparage the talk, the speaker, or themselves. They’re witless, dull, and idiotic. They think they know what they don’t know. ^an5-152-1-3

Someone with these five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-152-1-4

Someone with five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-152-2-1

What five? ^an5-152-2-2

They don’t disparage the talk, the speaker, or themselves. They’re wise, bright, and clever. They don’t think they know what they don’t know. ^an5-152-2-3

Someone with these five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an5-152-2-4

### The Sure Path (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 5.153) ^an5-153-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-153-1-1

What five? ^an5-153-1-2

They listen to the teaching bent only on putting it down. They listen to the teaching with a hostile, fault-finding mind. They’re antagonistic to the teacher, planning to attack them. They’re witless, dull, and idiotic. And they think they know what they don’t know. ^an5-153-1-3

Someone with these five qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-153-1-4

Someone with five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an5-153-2-1

What five? ^an5-153-2-2

They don’t listen to the teaching bent only on putting it down. They don’t listen to the teaching with a hostile, fault-finding mind. They’re not antagonistic to the teacher, and not planning to attack them. They’re wise, bright, and clever. And they don’t think they know what they don’t know. ^an5-153-2-3

Someone with these five qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an5-153-2-4

### The Decline of the True Teaching (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.154) ^an5-154-0

“Mendicants, these five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-154-1-1

What five? ^an5-154-1-2

It’s when mendicants don’t carefully listen to the teachings, memorize them, and remember them. They don’t carefully examine the meaning of teachings that they remember. And they don’t carefully practice in line with the meaning and the teaching they’ve understood. ^an5-154-1-3

These five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-154-1-4

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-154-2-1

What five? ^an5-154-2-2

It’s when mendicants carefully listen to the teachings, memorize them, and remember them. They carefully examine the meaning of teachings that they remember. And they carefully practice in line with the meaning and the teaching they’ve understood. ^an5-154-2-3

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.” ^an5-154-2-4

### The Decline of the True Teaching (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.155) ^an5-155-0

“Mendicants, these five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-155-1-1

What five? ^an5-155-1-2

It’s when the mendicants don’t memorize the teaching— ^an5-155-1-3

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-155-1-4

This is the first thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-155-1-5

Furthermore, the mendicants don’t explain the teaching in detail to others as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-2-1

This is the second thing … ^an5-155-2-2

Furthermore, the mendicants don’t make others recite the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-3-1

This is the third thing … ^an5-155-3-2

Furthermore, the mendicants don’t rehearse the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-4-1

This is the fourth thing … ^an5-155-4-2

Furthermore, the mendicants don’t think about and consider the teaching in their hearts, examining it with their minds as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-5-1

This is the fifth thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-155-5-2

These five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-155-5-3

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-155-6-1

What five? ^an5-155-6-2

It’s when the mendicants memorize the teaching— ^an5-155-6-3

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an5-155-6-4

This is the first thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-155-6-5

Furthermore, the mendicants explain the teaching in detail to others as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-7-1

This is the second thing … ^an5-155-7-2

Furthermore, the mendicants make others recite the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-8-1

This is the third thing … ^an5-155-8-2

Furthermore, the mendicants rehearse the teaching in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-9-1

This is the fourth thing … ^an5-155-9-2

Furthermore, the mendicants think about and consider the teaching in their hearts, examining it with their minds as they learned and memorized it. ^an5-155-10-1

This is the fifth thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-155-10-2

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.” ^an5-155-10-3
