### The Decline of the True Teaching (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 5.156) ^an5-156-0

“Mendicants, these five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-156-1-1

What five? ^an5-156-1-2

It’s when the mendicants memorize discourses that they learned incorrectly, with misplaced words and phrases. ^an5-156-1-3

When the words and phrases are misplaced, the meaning is misinterpreted. ^an5-156-1-4

This is the first thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-156-1-5

Furthermore, the mendicants are hard to admonish, having qualities that make them hard to admonish. They’re impatient, and don’t take instruction respectfully. ^an5-156-2-1

This is the second thing … ^an5-156-2-2

Furthermore, the mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—don’t carefully make others recite the discourses. ^an5-156-3-1

When they pass away, the discourses are cut off at the root, with no-one to preserve them. ^an5-156-3-2

This is the third thing … ^an5-156-3-3

Furthermore, the senior mendicants are indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, not rousing energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-156-4-1

Those who come after them follow their example. ^an5-156-4-2

They too are indulgent and slack … ^an5-156-4-3

This is the fourth thing … ^an5-156-4-4

Furthermore, there’s a schism in the Saṅgha. ^an5-156-5-1

When the Saṅgha is split, they abuse, insult, block, and forsake each other. ^an5-156-5-2

This doesn’t inspire confidence in those without it, and it causes some with confidence to change their minds. ^an5-156-5-3

This is the fifth thing that leads to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-156-5-4

These five things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching. ^an5-156-5-5

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-156-6-1

What five? ^an5-156-6-2

It’s when the mendicants memorize discourses that have been learned correctly, with well placed words and phrases. ^an5-156-6-3

When the words and phrases are well organized, the meaning is correctly interpreted. ^an5-156-6-4

This is the first thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-156-6-5

Furthermore, the mendicants are easy to admonish, having qualities that make them easy to admonish. They’re patient, and take instruction respectfully. ^an5-156-7-1

This is the second thing … ^an5-156-7-2

Furthermore, the mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—carefully make others recite the discourses. ^an5-156-8-1

When they pass away, the discourses aren’t cut off at the root, and they have someone to preserve them. ^an5-156-8-2

This is the third thing … ^an5-156-8-3

Furthermore, the senior mendicants are not indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion. They rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an5-156-9-1

Those who come after them follow their example. ^an5-156-9-2

They too are not indulgent or slack … ^an5-156-9-3

This is the fourth thing … ^an5-156-9-4

Furthermore, the Saṅgha lives comfortably, in harmony, appreciating each other, without dispute, with one recitation. ^an5-156-10-1

When the Saṅgha is in harmony, they don’t abuse, insult, block, or forsake each other. ^an5-156-10-2

This inspires confidence in those without it, and increases confidence in those who have it. ^an5-156-10-3

This is the fifth thing that leads to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching. ^an5-156-10-4

These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.” ^an5-156-10-5

### Inappropriate Talk (Numbered Discourses 5.157) ^an5-157-0

“Mendicants, it is inappropriate to speak to five individuals by comparing that individual with someone else. ^an5-157-1-1

What five? ^an5-157-1-2

It’s inappropriate to talk to an unfaithful person about faith. ^an5-157-1-3

It’s inappropriate to talk to an unethical person about ethics. ^an5-157-1-4

It’s inappropriate to talk to an unlearned person about learning. ^an5-157-1-5

It’s inappropriate to talk to a stingy person about generosity. ^an5-157-1-6

It’s inappropriate to talk to a witless person about wisdom. ^an5-157-1-7

And why is it inappropriate to talk to an unfaithful person about faith? ^an5-157-2-1

When an unfaithful person is spoken to about faith they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and displaying annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an5-157-2-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-2-3

Not seeing that faith in themselves, they don’t get the rapture and joy that faith brings. ^an5-157-2-4

That’s why it’s inappropriate to talk to an unfaithful person about faith. ^an5-157-2-5

And why is it inappropriate to talk to an unethical person about ethics? ^an5-157-3-1

When an unethical person is spoken to about ethics they lose their temper … ^an5-157-3-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-3-3

Not seeing that ethical conduct in themselves, they don’t get the rapture and joy that ethical conduct brings. ^an5-157-3-4

That’s why it’s inappropriate to talk to an unethical person about ethics. ^an5-157-3-5

And why is it inappropriate to talk to an unlearned person about learning? ^an5-157-4-1

When an unlearned person is spoken to about learning they lose their temper … ^an5-157-4-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-4-3

Not seeing that learning in themselves, they don’t get the rapture and joy that learning brings. ^an5-157-4-4

That’s why it’s inappropriate to talk to an unlearned person about learning. ^an5-157-4-5

And why is it inappropriate to talk to a stingy person about generosity? ^an5-157-5-1

When a stingy person is spoken to about generosity they lose their temper … ^an5-157-5-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-5-3

Not seeing that generosity in themselves, they don’t get the rapture and joy that generosity brings. ^an5-157-5-4

That’s why it’s inappropriate to talk to a stingy person about generosity. ^an5-157-5-5

And why is it inappropriate to talk to a witless person about wisdom? ^an5-157-6-1

When a witless person is spoken to about wisdom they lose their temper, becoming annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, and displaying annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an5-157-6-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-6-3

Not seeing that wisdom in themselves, they don’t get the rapture and joy that wisdom brings. ^an5-157-6-4

That’s why it’s inappropriate to talk to a witless person about wisdom. ^an5-157-6-5

It is inappropriate to speak to these five individuals by comparing that individual with someone else. ^an5-157-6-6

It is appropriate to speak to five individuals by comparing that individual with someone else. ^an5-157-7-1

What five? ^an5-157-7-2

It’s appropriate to talk to a faithful person about faith. ^an5-157-7-3

It’s appropriate to talk to an ethical person about ethical conduct. ^an5-157-7-4

It’s appropriate to talk to a learned person about learning. ^an5-157-7-5

It’s appropriate to talk to a generous person about generosity. ^an5-157-7-6

It’s appropriate to talk to a wise person about wisdom. ^an5-157-7-7

And why is it appropriate to talk to a faithful person about faith? ^an5-157-8-1

When a faithful person is spoken to about faith they don’t lose their temper, they don’t get annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, or display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an5-157-8-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-8-3

Seeing that faith in themselves, they get the rapture and joy that faith brings. ^an5-157-8-4

That’s why it’s appropriate to talk to a faithful person about faith. ^an5-157-8-5

And why is it appropriate to talk to an ethical person about ethical conduct? ^an5-157-9-1

When an ethical person is spoken to about ethical conduct they don’t lose their temper … ^an5-157-9-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-9-3

Seeing that ethical conduct in themselves, they get the rapture and joy that ethical conduct brings. ^an5-157-9-4

That’s why it’s appropriate to talk to an ethical person about ethical conduct. ^an5-157-9-5

And why is it appropriate to talk to a learned person about learning? ^an5-157-10-1

When a learned person is spoken to about learning they don’t lose their temper … ^an5-157-10-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-10-3

Seeing that learning in themselves, they get the rapture and joy that learning brings. ^an5-157-10-4

That’s why it’s appropriate to talk to a learned person about learning. ^an5-157-10-5

And why is it appropriate to talk to a generous person about generosity? ^an5-157-11-1

When a generous person is spoken to about generosity they don’t lose their temper … ^an5-157-11-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-11-3

Seeing that generosity in themselves, they get the rapture and joy that generosity brings. ^an5-157-11-4

That’s why it’s appropriate to talk to a generous person about generosity. ^an5-157-11-5

And why is it appropriate to talk to a wise person about wisdom? ^an5-157-12-1

When a wise person is spoken to about wisdom they don’t lose their temper, they don’t get annoyed, hostile, and hard-hearted, or display annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an5-157-12-2

Why is that? ^an5-157-12-3

Seeing that wisdom in themselves, they get the rapture and joy that wisdom brings. ^an5-157-12-4

That’s why it’s appropriate to talk to a wise person about wisdom. ^an5-157-12-5

It is appropriate to speak to these five individuals by comparing that individual with someone else.” ^an5-157-12-6

### Timidity (Numbered Discourses 5.158) ^an5-158-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five qualities is overcome by timidity. ^an5-158-1-1

What five? ^an5-158-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithless, unethical, with little learning, lazy, and witless. ^an5-158-1-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is overcome by timidity. ^an5-158-1-4

A mendicant with five qualities is self-assured. ^an5-158-2-1

What five? ^an5-158-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, ethical, learned, energetic, and wise. ^an5-158-2-3

A mendicant with these five qualities is self-assured.” ^an5-158-2-4

### With Udāyī (Numbered Discourses 5.159) ^an5-159-0

So I have heard. ^an5-159-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an5-159-1-2

Now, at that time Venerable Udāyī was sitting teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople. ^an5-159-1-3

Seeing this, Venerable Ānanda ^an5-159-1-4

went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-159-1-5

“Sir, Venerable Udāyī is teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople.” ^an5-159-1-6

“Ānanda, it’s not easy to teach Dhamma to others. ^an5-159-2-1

You should establish five things in yourself before teaching Dhamma to others. ^an5-159-2-2

What five? ^an5-159-2-3

You should teach Dhamma to others thinking: ‘I will teach step by step.’ … ^an5-159-2-4

‘I will teach showing my method.’ … ^an5-159-2-5

‘I will teach out of kindness.’ … ^an5-159-2-6

‘I will not teach while secretly hoping to profit.’ … ^an5-159-2-7

‘I will teach without damaging myself or others.’ ^an5-159-2-8

It’s not easy to teach Dhamma to others. ^an5-159-2-9

You should establish these five things in yourself before teaching Dhamma to others.” ^an5-159-2-10

### Hard to Get Rid Of (Numbered Discourses 5.160) ^an5-160-0

“Mendicants, these five things are hard to get rid of once they’ve arisen. ^an5-160-1-1

What five? ^an5-160-1-2

Greed, hate, delusion, the feeling of being inspired to speak out, and thoughts of traveling. ^an5-160-1-3

These five things are hard to get rid of once they’ve arisen.” ^an5-160-1-4

### Getting Rid of Resentment (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.161) ^an5-161-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant should use these five ways to completely get rid of resentment when it has arisen toward anyone. ^an5-161-1-1

What five? ^an5-161-1-2

You should develop love for an individual you resent. ^an5-161-1-3

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-161-1-4

You should develop compassion for an individual you resent. … ^an5-161-1-5

You should develop equanimity for an individual you resent. … ^an5-161-1-7

You should disregard an individual you resent, paying no attention to them. … ^an5-161-1-9

You should focus on the ownership of deeds regarding that individual: ^an5-161-1-11

‘This venerable is the owner of their deeds and heir to their deeds. Deeds are their womb, their relative, and their refuge. ^an5-161-1-12

They shall be the heir of whatever deeds they do, whether good or bad.’ ^an5-161-1-13

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-161-1-14

A mendicant should use these five ways to completely get rid of resentment when it has arisen toward anyone.” ^an5-161-1-15

### Getting Rid of Resentment (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.162) ^an5-162-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-162-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an5-162-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an5-162-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an5-162-1-4

“Reverends, a mendicant should use these five ways to completely get rid of resentment when it has arisen toward anyone. ^an5-162-2-1

What five? ^an5-162-2-2

In the case of an individual whose behavior by way of body is impure, but whose behavior by way of speech is pure, ^an5-162-2-3

you should get rid of resentment for that kind of individual. ^an5-162-2-4

In the case of an individual whose behavior by way of speech is impure, but whose behavior by way of body is pure, … ^an5-162-2-5

In the case of an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is impure, but who gets an openness and clarity of heart from time to time, … ^an5-162-2-7

In the case of an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is impure, and who doesn’t get an openness and clarity of heart from time to time, … ^an5-162-2-9

In the case of an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is pure, and who gets an openness and clarity of heart from time to time, ^an5-162-2-11

you should get rid of resentment for that kind of individual. ^an5-162-2-12

How should you get rid of resentment for an individual whose behavior by way of body is impure, but whose behavior by way of speech is pure? ^an5-162-3-1

Suppose a mendicant wearing rag robes sees a rag by the side of the road. They’d hold it down with the left foot, spread it out with the right foot, tear out what was intact, and take it away with them. ^an5-162-3-2

In the same way, at that time you should ignore that individual’s impure behavior by way of body and focus on their pure behavior by way of speech. ^an5-162-3-3

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-162-3-4

How should you get rid of resentment for an individual whose behavior by way of speech is impure, but whose behavior by way of body is pure? ^an5-162-4-1

Suppose there was a lotus pond enveloped with moss and aquatic plants. ^an5-162-4-2

Then along comes a person struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^an5-162-4-3

They’d plunge into the lotus pond, sweep apart the moss and aquatic plants, drink from their cupped hands, and be on their way. ^an5-162-4-4

In the same way, at that time you should ignore that individual’s impure behavior by way of speech and focus on their pure behavior by way of body. ^an5-162-4-5

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-162-4-6

How should you get rid of resentment for an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is impure, but who gets an openness and clarity of heart from time to time? ^an5-162-5-1

Suppose there was a little water in a cow’s hoofprint. ^an5-162-5-2

Then along comes a person struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^an5-162-5-3

They might think: ^an5-162-5-4

‘This little bit of water is in a cow’s hoofprint. ^an5-162-5-5

If I drink it with my cupped hands or a bowl, I’ll stir it and disturb it, making it undrinkable. ^an5-162-5-6

Why don’t I get down on all fours and drink it up like a cow, then be on my way?’ ^an5-162-5-7

So that’s what they do. ^an5-162-5-8

In the same way, at that time you should ignore that individual’s impure behavior by way of speech and body, ^an5-162-5-9

and focus on the fact that they get an openness and clarity of heart from time to time. ^an5-162-5-11

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-162-5-12

How should you get rid of resentment for an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is impure, and who doesn’t get an openness and clarity of heart from time to time? ^an5-162-6-1

Suppose a person was traveling along a road, and they were sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an5-162-6-2

And it was a long way to a village, whether ahead or behind. ^an5-162-6-3

And they didn’t have any suitable food or medicine, or a competent carer, or someone to bring them within a village. ^an5-162-6-4

Another person traveling along the road might see them, ^an5-162-6-5

and think of them with nothing but compassion, kindness, and sympathy: ^an5-162-6-6

‘Oh, may this person get suitable food or medicine, or a competent carer, or someone to bring them within a village. ^an5-162-6-7

Why is that? ^an5-162-6-8

So that they don’t come to ruin right here.’ ^an5-162-6-9

In the same way, at that time you should ignore that individual’s impure behavior by way of speech and body, and the fact that they don’t get an openness and clarity of heart from time to time, and think of them with nothing but compassion, kindness, and sympathy: ^an5-162-6-10

‘Oh, may this venerable give up bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and develop good conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-162-6-11

Why is that? ^an5-162-6-12

So that, when their body breaks up, after death, they’re not reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.’ ^an5-162-6-13

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-162-6-14

How should you get rid of resentment for an individual whose behavior by way of body and speech is pure, and who gets an openness and clarity of heart from time to time? ^an5-162-7-1

Suppose there was a lotus pond with clear, sweet, cool water, clean, with smooth banks, delightful, and shaded by many trees. ^an5-162-7-2

Then along comes a person struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^an5-162-7-3

They’d plunge into the lotus pond to bathe and drink. And after emerging they’d sit or lie down right there in the shade of the trees. ^an5-162-7-4

In the same way, at that time you should focus on that individual’s pure behavior by way of body and speech, and on the fact that they get an openness and clarity of heart from time to time. ^an5-162-8-1

That’s how to get rid of resentment for that individual. ^an5-162-8-4

Relying on an individual who is impressive all around, the mind becomes confident. ^an5-162-8-5

A mendicant should use these five ways to completely get rid of resentment when it has arisen toward anyone.” ^an5-162-9-1

### Discussions (Numbered Discourses 5.163) ^an5-163-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-163-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an5-163-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an5-163-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an5-163-1-4

“A mendicant with five qualities is fit to hold a discussion with their spiritual companions. ^an5-163-2-1

What five? ^an5-163-2-2

A mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, and answers questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in ethics. ^an5-163-2-3

They’re personally accomplished in immersion, … ^an5-163-2-4

They’re personally accomplished in wisdom, … ^an5-163-2-5

They’re personally accomplished in freedom, … ^an5-163-2-6

They’re personally accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-163-2-7

A mendicant with these five qualities is fit to hold a discussion with their spiritual companions.” ^an5-163-2-8

### Sharing a Way of Life (Numbered Discourses 5.164) ^an5-164-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-164-1-1

“A mendicant with five qualities is fit to share their life with their spiritual companions. ^an5-164-1-2

What five? ^an5-164-1-3

A mendicant is personally accomplished in ethics, and answers questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in ethics. ^an5-164-1-4

They’re personally accomplished in immersion, … ^an5-164-1-5

They’re personally accomplished in wisdom, … ^an5-164-1-6

They’re personally accomplished in freedom, … ^an5-164-1-7

They’re personally accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom, and they answer questions that come up when discussing accomplishment in the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-164-1-8

A mendicant with these five qualities is fit to share their life with their spiritual companions.” ^an5-164-1-9

### Asking Questions (Numbered Discourses 5.165) ^an5-165-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: … “Whoever asks a question of another, does so for one or other of these five reasons. ^an5-165-1-1

What five? ^an5-165-1-2

Someone asks a question of another from stupidity and folly. Or they ask from corrupt wishes, being of wishful temperament. Or they ask in order to disparage. Or they ask wanting to understand. Or they ask with the thought, ^an5-165-1-3

‘If they correctly answer the question I ask it’s good. If not, I’ll correctly answer it for them.’ ^an5-165-1-4

Whoever asks a question of another, does so for one or other of these five reasons. ^an5-165-1-5

As for myself, I ask with the thought, ^an5-165-1-6

‘If they correctly answer the question I ask it’s good. If not, I’ll correctly answer it for them.’” ^an5-165-1-7

### Cessation (Numbered Discourses 5.166) ^an5-166-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-166-1-1

“Reverends, take a mendicant who is accomplished in ethics, immersion, and wisdom. They might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-1-2

That is possible. ^an5-166-1-3

If they don’t reach enlightenment in this very life, then, surpassing the company of gods that consume edible food, they’re reborn in a certain host of mind-made gods. There they might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-1-4

That is possible.” ^an5-166-1-5

When he said this, Venerable Udāyī said to him, ^an5-166-2-1

“This is not possible, Reverend Sāriputta, it cannot happen!” ^an5-166-2-2

But for a second … and a third time Sāriputta repeated his statement. ^an5-166-3-1

And for a third time, Udāyī said to him, ^an5-166-4-1

“This is not possible, Reverend Sāriputta, it cannot happen!” ^an5-166-4-2

Then Venerable Sāriputta thought, ^an5-166-5-1

“Venerable Udāyī disagrees with me three times, and not one mendicant agrees with me. ^an5-166-5-2

Why don’t I go to see the Buddha?” ^an5-166-5-3

Then Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an5-166-5-4

and said to the mendicants: ^an5-166-5-5

“Reverends, take a mendicant who is accomplished in ethics, immersion, and wisdom. They might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-5-6

There is such a possibility. ^an5-166-5-7

If they don’t reach enlightenment in this very life, they’re reborn in the company of a certain host of mind-made gods, who surpass the gods that consume edible food. There they might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-5-8

That is possible.” ^an5-166-5-9

When he said this, Udāyī said to him, ^an5-166-6-1

“This is not possible, Reverend Sāriputta, it cannot happen!” ^an5-166-6-2

But for a second … and a third time Sāriputta repeated his statement. ^an5-166-7-1

And for a third time, Udāyī said to him, ^an5-166-8-1

“This is not possible, Reverend Sāriputta, it cannot happen!” ^an5-166-8-2

Then Venerable Sāriputta thought, ^an5-166-9-1

“Even in front of the Buddha Venerable Udāyī disagrees with me three times, and not one mendicant agrees with me. ^an5-166-9-2

I’d better stay silent.” ^an5-166-9-3

Then Sāriputta fell silent. ^an5-166-9-4

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Udāyī, ^an5-166-10-1

“But Udāyī, do you believe in a mind-made body?” ^an5-166-10-2

“For those gods, sir, who are formless, made of perception.” ^an5-166-10-3

“Udāyī, what has an incompetent fool like you got to say? ^an5-166-10-4

How on earth could you imagine you’ve got something worth saying!” ^an5-166-10-5

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Ānanda, ^an5-166-11-1

“Ānanda! How can you just watch while a senior mendicant is being harassed? ^an5-166-11-2

Don’t you have any compassion for a senior mendicant who is being harassed?” ^an5-166-11-3

Then the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an5-166-12-1

“Mendicants, take a mendicant who is accomplished in ethics, immersion, and wisdom. They might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-12-2

That is possible. ^an5-166-12-3

If they don’t reach enlightenment in this very life, they’re reborn in the company of a certain host of mind-made gods, who surpass the gods that consume edible food. There they might enter into and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an5-166-12-4

That is possible.” ^an5-166-12-5

That is what the Buddha said. ^an5-166-12-6

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an5-166-12-7

Then, not long after the Buddha had left, Venerable Ānanda went to Venerable Upavāṇa and said to him, ^an5-166-13-1

“Reverend Upavāṇa, they’ve been harassing other senior mendicants, ^an5-166-13-2

but I didn’t question them. ^an5-166-13-3

I wouldn’t be surprised if the Buddha brings this up when he comes out of retreat later this afternoon. He might even call upon Venerable Upavāṇa himself. ^an5-166-13-4

And right now I feel timid.” ^an5-166-13-5

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall, where he sat on the seat spread out, and said to Upavāṇa, ^an5-166-13-6

“Upavāṇa, how many qualities should a senior mendicant have to be dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired?” ^an5-166-14-1

“Sir, a senior mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-166-14-2

What five? ^an5-166-14-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-166-14-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-166-14-5

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well, with a polished, clear, and articulate voice that expresses the meaning. ^an5-166-14-6

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-166-14-7

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-166-14-8

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-166-14-9

“Good, good, Upavāṇa! ^an5-166-15-1

A senior mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-166-15-2

If these five qualities are not found in a senior mendicant, why would their spiritual companions honor, respect, revere, or venerate them? Because of their broken teeth, gray hair, and wrinkled skin? ^an5-166-15-3

But since these five qualities are found in a senior mendicant, their spiritual companions honor, respect, revere, or venerate them.” ^an5-166-15-4

### Accusation (Numbered Discourses 5.167) ^an5-167-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-167-1-1

“Reverends, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should first establish five things in themselves. ^an5-167-1-2

What five? ^an5-167-2-1

I will speak at the right time, not at the wrong time. ^an5-167-2-2

I will speak truthfully, not falsely. ^an5-167-2-3

I will speak gently, not harshly. ^an5-167-2-4

I will speak beneficially, not harmfully. ^an5-167-2-5

I will speak lovingly, not from secret hate. ^an5-167-2-6

A mendicant who wants to accuse another should first establish these five things in themselves. ^an5-167-2-7

Take a case where I see a certain individual being accused at the wrong time, not being disturbed at the right time. They’re accused falsely, not disturbed truthfully. They’re accused harshly, not disturbed gently. They’re accused harmfully, not disturbed beneficially. They’re accused with secret hate, not disturbed lovingly. ^an5-167-3-1

The mendicant who is accused improperly should be reassured in five ways. ^an5-167-4-1

‘Venerable, you were accused at the wrong time, not at the right time. There’s no need for you to feel remorse. ^an5-167-4-2

You were accused falsely, not truthfully. … ^an5-167-4-3

You were accused harshly, not gently. … ^an5-167-4-4

You were accused harmfully, not beneficially. … ^an5-167-4-5

You were accused with secret hate, not lovingly. There’s no need for you to feel remorse.’ ^an5-167-4-6

A mendicant who is accused improperly should be reassured in these five ways. ^an5-167-4-7

The mendicant who makes improper accusations should be chastened in five ways. ^an5-167-5-1

‘Reverend, you made an accusation at the wrong time, not at the right time. There’s a reason for you to feel remorse. ^an5-167-5-2

You made an accusation falsely, not truthfully. … ^an5-167-5-3

You made an accusation harshly, not gently. … ^an5-167-5-4

You made an accusation harmfully, not beneficially. … ^an5-167-5-5

You made an accusation with secret hate, not lovingly. There’s a reason for you to feel remorse.’ ^an5-167-5-6

The mendicant who makes improper accusations should be chastened in these five ways. ^an5-167-5-7

Why is that? ^an5-167-5-8

So that another mendicant wouldn’t think to make a false accusation. ^an5-167-5-9

Take a case where I see a certain individual being accused at the right time, not being disturbed at the wrong time. They’re accused truthfully, not disturbed falsely. They’re accused gently, not disturbed harshly. They’re accused beneficially, not disturbed harmfully. They’re accused lovingly, not disturbed with secret hate. ^an5-167-6-1

The mendicant who is accused properly should be chastened in five ways. ^an5-167-7-1

‘Venerable, you were accused at the right time, not at the wrong time. There’s a reason for you to feel remorse. ^an5-167-7-2

You were accused truthfully, not falsely. … ^an5-167-7-3

You were accused gently, not harshly. … ^an5-167-7-4

You were accused beneficially, not harmfully. … ^an5-167-7-5

You were accused lovingly, not with secret hate. There’s a reason for you to feel remorse.’ ^an5-167-7-6

The mendicant who is accused properly should be chastened in these five ways. ^an5-167-7-7

The mendicant who makes proper accusations should be reassured in five ways. ^an5-167-8-1

‘Reverend, you made an accusation at the right time, not at the wrong time. There’s no need for you to feel remorse. ^an5-167-8-2

You made an accusation truthfully, not falsely. … ^an5-167-8-3

You made an accusation gently, not harshly. … ^an5-167-8-4

You made an accusation beneficially, not harmfully. … ^an5-167-8-5

You made an accusation lovingly, not with secret hate. There’s no need for you to feel remorse.’ ^an5-167-8-6

The mendicant who makes proper accusations should be reassured in these five ways. ^an5-167-8-7

Why is that? ^an5-167-8-8

So that another mendicant would think to make a true accusation. ^an5-167-8-9

An individual who is accused should ground themselves in two things: ^an5-167-9-1

truth and an even temper. ^an5-167-9-2

Even if others accuse me—at the right time or the wrong time, truthfully or falsely, gently or harshly, beneficially or harmfully, lovingly or with secret hate—I will still ground myself in two things: ^an5-167-9-3

truth and an even temper. ^an5-167-9-4

If I know that ^an5-167-9-5

that quality is found in me, I will tell them that it is. ^an5-167-9-6

If I know that ^an5-167-9-8

that quality is not found in me, I will tell them that it is not.” ^an5-167-9-9

“Even when you speak like this, Sāriputta, there are still some futile people here who do not respectfully take it up.” ^an5-167-10-1

“Sir, there are those faithless individuals who went forth from the lay life to homelessness not out of faith but to earn a livelihood. They’re devious, deceitful, and sneaky. They’re restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, and loose-tongued. They do not guard their sense doors or eat in moderation, and they are not dedicated to wakefulness. They don’t care about the ascetic life, and don’t keenly respect the training. They’re indulgent and slack, leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion, lazy, and lacking energy. They’re unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds, witless and idiotic. When I speak to them like this, they don’t respectfully take it up. ^an5-167-11-1

Sir, there are those gentlemen who went forth from the lay life to homelessness out of faith. They’re not devious, deceitful, and sneaky. They’re not restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, and loose-tongued. They guard their sense doors and eat in moderation, and they are dedicated to wakefulness. They care about the ascetic life, and keenly respect the training. They’re not indulgent or slack, nor are they leaders in backsliding, neglecting seclusion. They’re energetic and determined. They’re mindful, with situational awareness, immersion, and unified minds; wise and clever. When I speak to them like this, they do respectfully take it up.” ^an5-167-12-1

“Sāriputta, those faithless individuals who went forth from the lay life to homelessness not out of faith but to earn a livelihood … Leave them be. ^an5-167-13-1

But those gentlemen who went forth from the lay life to homelessness out of faith … You should speak to them. ^an5-167-14-1

Sāriputta, you should advise your spiritual companions! ^an5-167-14-2

You should instruct your spiritual companions! ^an5-167-14-3

Thinking: ‘I will draw my spiritual companions away from false teachings and ground them in true teachings.’ ^an5-167-14-4

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-167-14-5

### Ethics (Numbered Discourses 5.168) ^an5-168-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an5-168-1-1

“Reverends, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-168-1-2

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-168-1-3

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-168-1-4

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-168-1-5

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. ^an5-168-1-6

Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an5-168-1-7

In the same way, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-168-1-8

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-168-1-9

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-168-1-10

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-168-1-11

An ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-168-2-1

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-168-2-2

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-168-2-3

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-168-2-4

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would grow to fullness. ^an5-168-2-5

In the same way, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an5-168-2-6

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an5-168-2-7

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an5-168-2-8

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an5-168-2-9

### Quick-witted (Numbered Discourses 5.169) ^an5-169-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an5-169-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him: ^an5-169-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, how are we to define a mendicant who is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings, who learns well, learns much, and does not forget what they’ve learned?” ^an5-169-2-1

“Well, Venerable Ānanda, you’re very learned. ^an5-169-2-2

Why don’t you clarify this yourself?” ^an5-169-2-3

“Well then, Reverend Sāriputta, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an5-169-2-4

“Yes, reverend,” Sāriputta replied. ^an5-169-2-5

Venerable Ānanda said this: ^an5-169-2-6

“It’s when a mendicant is skilled in the meaning, skilled in the teaching, skilled in definition, skilled in phrasing, and skilled in sequence. ^an5-169-3-1

That is how to define a mendicant who is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings, who learns well, learns much, and does not forget what they’ve learned.” ^an5-169-3-2

“It’s incredible, it’s amazing! ^an5-169-3-3

How well this was said by Venerable Ānanda! ^an5-169-3-4

And we will remember Venerable Ānanda as someone who possesses these five qualities: ^an5-169-3-5

‘Reverend Ānanda is skilled in the meaning, skilled in the teaching, skilled in definition, skilled in phrasing, and skilled in sequence.’” ^an5-169-3-6

### With Bhaddaji (Numbered Discourses 5.170) ^an5-170-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an5-170-1-1

Then Venerable Bhaddaji went up to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an5-170-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side, and Venerable Ānanda said to him: ^an5-170-1-3

“Reverend Bhaddaji, what is the best sight, the best sound, the best happiness, the best perception, and the best state of existence?” ^an5-170-1-4

“Reverend, there is this Divinity, the vanquisher, the unvanquished, the universal seer, the wielder of power. When you see the Divinity, that’s the best sight. ^an5-170-2-1

There are the gods called ‘of streaming radiance’, who are drenched and steeped in pleasure. ^an5-170-2-2

Every so often they feel inspired to exclaim: ^an5-170-2-3

‘Oh, what bliss! Oh, what bliss!’ ^an5-170-2-4

When you hear that, it’s the best sound. ^an5-170-2-5

There are the gods called ‘of universal beauty’. ^an5-170-2-6

Since they’re truly content, they experience pleasure. This is the best happiness. ^an5-170-2-7

There are the gods reborn in the dimension of nothingness. This is the best perception. ^an5-170-2-8

There are the gods reborn in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. This is the best state of existence.” ^an5-170-2-9

“So, Venerable Bhaddaji, do you agree with what most people say about this?” ^an5-170-2-10

“Well, Venerable Ānanda, you’re very learned. ^an5-170-3-1

Why don’t you clarify this yourself?” ^an5-170-3-2

“Well then, Reverend Bhaddaji, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an5-170-3-3

“Yes, reverend,” Bhaddaji replied. ^an5-170-3-4

Ānanda said this: ^an5-170-3-5

“What you see when the defilements are ended without delay is the best sight. ^an5-170-4-1

What you hear when the defilements are ended without delay is the best sound. ^an5-170-4-2

The happiness you feel when the defilements are ended without delay is the best happiness. ^an5-170-4-3

What you perceive when the defilements are ended without delay is the best perception. ^an5-170-4-4

The state of existence in which the defilements are ended without delay is the best state of existence.” ^an5-170-4-5

### Timidity (Numbered Discourses 5.171) ^an5-171-0

So I have heard. ^an5-171-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an5-171-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an5-171-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an5-171-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an5-171-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an5-171-1-6

“A lay follower with five qualities is overcome by timidity. ^an5-171-2-1

What five? ^an5-171-2-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-171-2-3

A lay follower with these five qualities is overcome by timidity. ^an5-171-2-4

A lay follower with five qualities is self-assured. ^an5-171-3-1

What five? ^an5-171-3-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-171-3-3

A lay follower with these five qualities is self-assured.” ^an5-171-3-4

### Assured (Numbered Discourses 5.172) ^an5-172-0

“A lay follower living at home with five qualities is not self-assured. ^an5-172-1-1

What five? ^an5-172-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-172-1-3

A lay follower living at home with these five qualities is not self-assured. ^an5-172-1-4

A lay follower living at home with these five qualities is self-assured. ^an5-172-2-1

What five? ^an5-172-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-172-2-3

A lay follower living at home with these five qualities is self-assured.” ^an5-172-2-4

### Hell (Numbered Discourses 5.173) ^an5-173-0

“Mendicants, a lay follower with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-173-1-1

What five? ^an5-173-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-173-1-3

A lay follower with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-173-1-4

A lay follower with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-173-2-1

What five? ^an5-173-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-173-2-3

A lay follower with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-173-2-4

### Threats (Numbered Discourses 5.174) ^an5-174-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an5-174-1-1

“Householder, unless these five fears and enmities are given up, one is said to be unethical, and is reborn in hell. ^an5-174-2-1

What five? ^an5-174-2-2

Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-174-2-3

Unless these five fears and enmities are given up, one is said to be unethical, and is reborn in hell. ^an5-174-2-4

Once these five fears and enmities are given up, one is said to be ethical, and is reborn in a good place. ^an5-174-3-1

What five? ^an5-174-3-2

Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-174-3-3

Once these five fears and enmities are given up, one is said to be ethical, and is reborn in a good place. ^an5-174-3-4

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from killing living creatures does not brim with fear and enmity either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an5-174-4-1

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^an5-174-4-2

Anyone who steals … ^an5-174-5-1

Anyone who commits sexual misconduct … ^an5-174-6-1

Anyone who lies … ^an5-174-7-1

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates no dangers and threats either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an5-174-8-1

So that danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-174-8-2

Take anyone in this world ^an5-174-9-1

who kills living creatures, ^an5-174-9-2

speaks falsely, steals, ^an5-174-9-3

commits adultery, ^an5-174-9-4

and indulges in drinking ^an5-174-9-5

beer and wine. ^an5-174-9-6

Unless they give up these five threats, ^an5-174-10-1

they’re said to be unethical. ^an5-174-10-2

When their body breaks up, that witless person ^an5-174-10-3

is reborn in hell. ^an5-174-10-4

A person in the world doesn’t kill living creatures, ^an5-174-11-1

speak falsely, ^an5-174-11-2

steal, ^an5-174-11-3

commit adultery, ^an5-174-11-4

or indulge in drinking ^an5-174-11-5

beer and wine. ^an5-174-11-6

Giving up these five threats, ^an5-174-12-1

they’re said to be ethical. ^an5-174-12-2

When their body breaks up, that wise person ^an5-174-12-3

is reborn in a good place.” ^an5-174-12-4

### Corpse-worker (Numbered Discourses 5.175) ^an5-175-0

“Mendicants, a lay follower with five qualities is a corpse-worker, a stain, and a reject among lay followers. ^an5-175-1-1

What five? ^an5-175-1-2

They’re faithless. ^an5-175-1-3

They’re unethical. ^an5-175-1-4

They follow boisterous, superstitious rites, believing in omens rather than deeds. ^an5-175-1-5

They seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an5-175-1-6

And they make offerings there first. ^an5-175-1-7

A lay follower with these five qualities is a corpse-worker, a stain, and a reject among lay followers. ^an5-175-1-8

A lay follower with five qualities is a gem, a pink lotus, and a white lotus among lay followers. ^an5-175-2-1

What five? ^an5-175-2-2

They’re faithful. ^an5-175-2-3

They’re ethical. ^an5-175-2-4

They don’t practice boisterous, superstitious rites, and believe in deeds rather than omens. ^an5-175-2-5

They don’t seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an5-175-2-6

And they don’t make offerings there first. ^an5-175-2-7

A lay follower with these five qualities is a gem, a pink lotus, and a white lotus among lay followers.” ^an5-175-2-8

### Rapture (Numbered Discourses 5.176) ^an5-176-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika, escorted by around five hundred lay followers, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an5-176-1-1

“Householders, you have supplied the mendicant Saṅgha with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-176-2-1

But you should not be content with just this much. ^an5-176-2-2

So you should train like this: ^an5-176-2-4

‘How can we, from time to time, enter and dwell in the rapture of seclusion?’ ^an5-176-2-5

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-176-2-6

When he said this, Venerable Sāriputta said to the Buddha, ^an5-176-3-1

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an5-176-3-2

How well said this was by the Buddha: ^an5-176-3-3

‘Householders, you have supplied the mendicant Saṅgha with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an5-176-3-4

But you should not be content with just this much. ^an5-176-3-5

So you should train like this: ^an5-176-3-7

“How can we, from time to time, enter and dwell in the rapture of seclusion?” ^an5-176-3-8

That’s how you should train.’ ^an5-176-3-9

At a time when a noble disciple enters and dwells in the rapture of seclusion, five things aren’t present in him. ^an5-176-3-10

The pain and sadness connected with sensual pleasures. ^an5-176-3-11

The pleasure and happiness connected with sensual pleasures. ^an5-176-3-12

The pain and sadness connected with the unskillful. ^an5-176-3-13

The pleasure and happiness connected with the unskillful. ^an5-176-3-14

The pain and sadness connected with the skillful. ^an5-176-3-15

At a time when a noble disciple enters and dwells in the rapture of seclusion, these five things aren’t present in him.” ^an5-176-3-16

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^an5-176-4-1

At a time when a noble disciple enters and dwells in the rapture of seclusion, five things aren’t present in him. ^an5-176-4-2

The pain and sadness connected with sensual pleasures. ^an5-176-4-3

The pleasure and happiness connected with sensual pleasures. ^an5-176-4-4

The pain and sadness connected with the unskillful. ^an5-176-4-5

The pleasure and happiness connected with the unskillful. ^an5-176-4-6

The pain and sadness connected with the skillful. ^an5-176-4-7

At a time when a noble disciple enters and dwells in the rapture of seclusion, these five things aren’t present in him.” ^an5-176-4-8

### Trades (Numbered Discourses 5.177) ^an5-177-0

“Mendicants, a lay follower should not engage in these five trades. ^an5-177-1-1

What five? ^an5-177-1-2

Trade in weapons, living creatures, meat, intoxicants, and poisons. ^an5-177-1-3

A lay follower should not engage in these five trades.” ^an5-177-1-4

### Kings (Numbered Discourses 5.178) ^an5-178-0

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an5-178-1-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an5-178-1-2

of a person who has given up killing living creatures, ^an5-178-1-3

and the kings have them arrested for that, and execute, imprison, or banish them, or do what the case requires?” ^an5-178-1-4

“No, sir.” ^an5-178-1-5

“Good, mendicants! ^an5-178-1-6

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an5-178-1-7

Rather, the kings are informed of someone’s bad deed: ^an5-178-1-10

‘This person has murdered a man or a woman.’ ^an5-178-1-11

The kings have them arrested for killing, and execute, imprison, or banish them, or do what the case requires. ^an5-178-1-12

Have you ever seen or heard of such a case?” ^an5-178-1-13

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^an5-178-1-14

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an5-178-2-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an5-178-2-2

of a person who has given up stealing, ^an5-178-2-3

and the kings have them arrested for that …?” ^an5-178-2-4

“No, sir.” ^an5-178-2-5

“Good, mendicants! ^an5-178-2-6

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an5-178-2-7

Rather, the kings are informed of someone’s bad deed: ^an5-178-2-10

‘This person took something from a village or wilderness, with the intention to commit theft.’ ^an5-178-2-11

The kings have them arrested for stealing … ^an5-178-2-12

Have you ever seen or heard of such a case?” ^an5-178-2-13

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^an5-178-2-14

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an5-178-3-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an5-178-3-2

of a person who has given up sexual misconduct, ^an5-178-3-3

and the kings have them arrested for that …?” ^an5-178-3-4

“No, sir.” ^an5-178-3-5

“Good, mendicants! ^an5-178-3-6

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an5-178-3-7

Rather, the kings are informed of someone’s bad deed: ^an5-178-3-10

‘This person had sexual relations with women or maidens under someone else’s protection.’ ^an5-178-3-11

The kings have them arrested for that … ^an5-178-3-12

Have you ever seen or heard of such a case?” ^an5-178-3-13

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^an5-178-3-14

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an5-178-4-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an5-178-4-2

of a person who has given up lying, ^an5-178-4-3

and the kings have them arrested for that …?” ^an5-178-4-4

“No, sir.” ^an5-178-4-5

“Good, mendicants! ^an5-178-4-6

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an5-178-4-7

Rather, the kings are informed of someone’s bad deed: ^an5-178-4-10

‘This person has ruined a householder or householder’s child by lying.’ ^an5-178-4-11

The kings have them arrested for that … ^an5-178-4-12

Have you ever seen or heard of such a case?” ^an5-178-4-13

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^an5-178-4-14

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an5-178-5-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an5-178-5-2

of a person who has given up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor, ^an5-178-5-3

and the kings have them arrested for that, and execute, imprison, or banish them, or do what the case requires?” ^an5-178-5-4

“No, sir.” ^an5-178-5-5

“Good, mendicants! ^an5-178-5-6

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an5-178-5-7

Rather, the kings are informed of someone’s bad deed: ^an5-178-5-10

‘While under the influence of intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor, this person murdered a woman or a man. ^an5-178-5-11

Or they stole something from a village or wilderness. ^an5-178-5-12

Or they had sexual relations with women or maidens under someone else’s protection. ^an5-178-5-13

Or they ruined a householder or householder’s child by lying.’ ^an5-178-5-14

The kings have them arrested for being under the influence of intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor, and execute, imprison, or banish them, or do what the case requires. ^an5-178-5-15

Have you ever seen or heard of such a case?” ^an5-178-5-16

“Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.” ^an5-178-5-17

### A Layperson (Numbered Discourses 5.179) ^an5-179-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika, escorted by around five hundred lay followers, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an5-179-1-1

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Sāriputta: ^an5-179-1-2

“You should know this, Sāriputta, about those white-clothed laypeople whose actions are restrained in the five precepts, and who get four blissful meditations in this life belonging to the higher mind when they want, without trouble or difficulty. They may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an5-179-1-3

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^an5-179-1-4

And what are the five precepts in which their actions are restrained? ^an5-179-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-179-2-2

These are the five precepts in which their actions are restrained. ^an5-179-2-3

And what are the four blissful meditations in this life belonging to the higher mind that they get when they want, without trouble or difficulty? ^an5-179-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^an5-179-3-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an5-179-3-3

This is the first blissful meditation in this life belonging to the higher mind, which they achieve in order to purify the unpurified mind and cleanse the unclean mind. ^an5-179-3-4

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the teaching: ^an5-179-4-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an5-179-4-2

This is the second blissful meditation … ^an5-179-4-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^an5-179-5-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an5-179-5-2

This is the third blissful meditation … ^an5-179-5-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an5-179-6-1

This is the fourth blissful meditation in this life belonging to the higher mind, which they achieve in order to purify the unpurified mind and cleanse the unclean mind. ^an5-179-6-2

These are the four blissful meditations in this life belonging to the higher mind that they get when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-179-6-3

You should know this, Sāriputta, about those white-clothed laypeople whose actions are restrained in the five precepts, and who get four blissful meditations in this life belonging to the higher mind when they want, without trouble or difficulty. They may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an5-179-7-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^an5-179-7-2

Seeing the peril in the hells, ^an5-179-8-1

you should shun bad deeds. ^an5-179-8-2

Taking up the teaching of the noble ones, ^an5-179-8-3

an astute person should shun them. ^an5-179-8-4

You shouldn’t harm living beings, ^an5-179-9-1

so long as strength is found. ^an5-179-9-2

Nor should you knowingly speak falsehood, ^an5-179-9-3

or take what is not given. ^an5-179-9-4

Content with your own partners, ^an5-179-10-1

you should stay away from the partners of others. ^an5-179-10-2

A personage shouldn’t drink wine or toddy, ^an5-179-10-3

as they confuse the mind. ^an5-179-10-4

You should recollect the Buddha, ^an5-179-11-1

and reflect on the teaching. ^an5-179-11-2

You should develop a harmless mind of welfare, ^an5-179-11-3

which leads to the realms of gods. ^an5-179-11-4

When suitable gifts to give are available ^an5-179-12-1

to someone who seeks and needs merit, ^an5-179-12-2

a religious donation is abundant ^an5-179-12-3

if given first to the peaceful ones. ^an5-179-12-4

I will tell of the peaceful ones, ^an5-179-13-1

Sāriputta, listen to me. ^an5-179-13-2

Cows may be black or white, ^an5-179-13-3

red or tawny, ^an5-179-13-4

mottled or uniform, ^an5-179-14-1

or pigeon-colored. ^an5-179-14-2

But when one is born among them, ^an5-179-14-3

the bull that’s tamed— ^an5-179-14-4

a behemoth, powerful, ^an5-179-15-1

well-paced in pulling forward— ^an5-179-15-2

they yoke the load just to him, ^an5-179-15-3

regardless of his color. ^an5-179-15-4

So it is for humans, ^an5-179-16-1

wherever they may be born— ^an5-179-16-2

among aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, ^an5-179-16-3

menials, or corpse-workers and scavengers— ^an5-179-16-4

but when one is born among them, ^an5-179-17-1

tamed, true to their vows. ^an5-179-17-2

Firm in principle, accomplished in ethical conduct, ^an5-179-17-3

truthful, conscientious, ^an5-179-17-4

they’ve given up birth and death, ^an5-179-18-1

and have completed the spiritual journey. ^an5-179-18-2

With burden put down, detached, ^an5-179-18-3

they’ve completed the task <j>and are free of defilements. ^an5-179-18-4

Gone beyond all things, ^an5-179-19-1

they’re quenched by not grasping. ^an5-179-19-2

In that flawless field, ^an5-179-19-3

a religious donation is abundant. ^an5-179-19-4

Fools who don’t understand— ^an5-179-20-1

unlearned simpletons— ^an5-179-20-2

give their gifts to those outside, ^an5-179-20-3

and don’t attend the peaceful ones. ^an5-179-20-4

But those who do attend the peaceful ones— ^an5-179-21-1

wise, esteemed as attentive— ^an5-179-21-2

and whose faith in the Holy One ^an5-179-21-3

has roots planted deep, ^an5-179-21-4

they go to the realm of the gods, ^an5-179-22-1

or are born here in a good family. ^an5-179-22-2

Gradually those astute ones ^an5-179-22-3

reach extinguishment.” ^an5-179-22-4

### About Gavesī (Numbered Discourses 5.180) ^an5-180-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^an5-180-1-1

While traveling along a road the Buddha saw a large sal grove in a certain spot. ^an5-180-1-2

He left the road, went to the sal grove, and plunged deep into it. And at a certain spot he smiled. ^an5-180-1-3

Then Venerable Ānanda thought, ^an5-180-2-1

“What is the cause, what is the reason why the Buddha smiled? ^an5-180-2-2

Realized Ones do not smile for no reason.” ^an5-180-2-3

So Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^an5-180-2-4

“What is the cause, what is the reason why the Buddha smiled? ^an5-180-2-5

Realized Ones do not smile for no reason.” ^an5-180-2-6

“Once upon a time, Ānanda, there was a city in this spot that was successful and prosperous, populous, full of people. ^an5-180-3-1

And Kassapa, a blessed one, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, lived supported by that city. ^an5-180-3-2

He had a lay follower named Gavesī who had not fulfilled all the precepts. ^an5-180-3-3

And the five hundred lay followers who were taught and advised by Gavesī also had not fulfilled all the precepts. ^an5-180-3-4

Then Gavesī thought: ^an5-180-3-5

‘I’m the helper, leader, and adviser of these five hundred lay followers, yet neither I nor they have fulfilled the precepts. ^an5-180-3-6

We’re the same, I’m in no way better. ^an5-180-3-7

So let me do better.’ ^an5-180-3-8

Then Gavesī went to those five hundred lay followers and said to them: ^an5-180-4-1

‘From this day forth may the venerables remember me as one who has fulfilled the precepts.’ ^an5-180-4-2

Then those five hundred lay followers thought: ^an5-180-4-3

‘Master Gavesī is our helper, leader, and adviser, ^an5-180-4-4

and now he will fulfill the precepts. ^an5-180-4-5

Why don’t we do the same?’ ^an5-180-4-6

Then those five hundred lay followers went to Gavesī and said to him: ^an5-180-4-7

‘From this day forth may Master Gavesī remember these five hundred lay followers as having fulfilled the precepts.’ ^an5-180-4-8

Then Gavesī thought: ^an5-180-4-9

‘I’m the helper, leader, and adviser of these five hundred lay followers, and both I and they have fulfilled the precepts. ^an5-180-4-10

We’re the same, I’m in no way better. ^an5-180-4-11

So let me do better.’ ^an5-180-4-12

Then Gavesī went to those five hundred lay followers and said to them: ^an5-180-5-1

‘From this day forth may the venerables remember me as one who is chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex.’ ^an5-180-5-2

Then those five hundred lay followers did the same. … ^an5-180-5-3

Then Gavesī thought: ^an5-180-5-9

‘These five hundred lay followers … ^an5-180-5-10

are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an5-180-5-14

We’re the same, I’m in no way better. ^an5-180-5-15

So let me do better.’ ^an5-180-5-16

Then Gavesī went to those five hundred lay followers and said to them: ^an5-180-6-1

‘From this day forth may the venerables remember me as one who eats in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night, and from food at the wrong time.’ ^an5-180-6-2

Then those five hundred lay followers did the same. … ^an5-180-6-3

Then Gavesī thought: ^an5-180-6-9

‘These five hundred lay followers … ^an5-180-6-10

eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night, and food at the wrong time. ^an5-180-6-16

We’re the same, I’m in no way better. ^an5-180-6-17

So let me do better.’ ^an5-180-6-18

Then the lay follower Gavesī went up to the blessed one Kassapa, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha and said to him: ^an5-180-7-1

‘Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?’ ^an5-180-7-2

And he received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^an5-180-7-3

Not long after his ordination, the mendicant Gavesī, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-180-7-4

He understood: ‘Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an5-180-7-5

And the mendicant Gavesī became one of the perfected. ^an5-180-7-6

Then those five hundred lay followers thought: ^an5-180-8-1

‘Master Gavesī is our helper, leader, and adviser, ^an5-180-8-2

He has shaved off his hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and gone forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-180-8-3

Why don’t we do the same?’ ^an5-180-8-4

Then those five hundred lay followers went up to the blessed one Kassapa, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha and said to him: ^an5-180-8-5

‘Sir, may we receive the going forth and ordination in the Buddha’s presence?’ ^an5-180-8-6

And they did receive the going forth and ordination in the Buddha’s presence. ^an5-180-8-7

Then the mendicant Gavesī thought: ^an5-180-9-1

‘I get the supreme bliss of freedom whenever I want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-180-9-2

Oh, may these five hundred mendicants do the same!’ ^an5-180-9-3

Then those five hundred mendicants, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. They lived having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-180-9-4

They understood: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an5-180-9-6

And so, Ānanda, those five hundred mendicants headed by Gavesī, trying to go higher and higher, better and better, realized the supreme bliss of freedom. ^an5-180-10-1

So you should train like this: ^an5-180-10-2

‘Trying to go higher and higher, better and better, we will realize the supreme bliss of freedom.’ ^an5-180-10-3

That’s how you should train.” ^an5-180-10-4

### Wilderness Dwellers (Numbered Discourses 5.181) ^an5-181-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of wilderness dwellers. ^an5-181-1-1

What five? ^an5-181-1-2

One may be a wilderness dweller because of stupidity and folly. Or because of corrupt wishes, being of wishful temperament. Or because of madness and mental disorder. Or because it is praised by the Buddhas and their disciples. Or for the sake of having few wishes, for the sake of contentment, self-effacement, seclusion, and simplicity. ^an5-181-1-3

These are the five kinds of wilderness dwellers. ^an5-181-1-4

But the one who dwells in the wilderness for the sake of having few wishes is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the five. ^an5-181-1-5

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^an5-181-2-1

In the same way, the one who dwells in the wilderness for the sake of having few wishes is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the five.” ^an5-181-2-2

### Robes (Numbered Discourses 5.182) ^an5-182-0

“Mendicants, these five wear rag robes. ^an5-182-1-1

What five? ^an5-182-1-2

One may wear rag robes because of stupidity … corrupt wishes … madness … because it is praised by the Buddhas … or for the sake of having few wishes … ^an5-182-1-3

These are the five who wear rag robes.” ^an5-182-1-4

### Dwelling at the Root of a Tree (Numbered Discourses 5.183) ^an5-183-0

“Mendicants, there are these five who dwell at the root of a tree. ^an5-183-1-1

What five? ^an5-183-1-2

One may dwell at the root of a tree because of stupidity … corrupt wishes … madness … because it is praised by the Buddhas … or for the sake of having few wishes … ^an5-183-1-3

These are the five who dwell at the root of a tree.” ^an5-183-1-4

### Charnel Ground Dwellers (Numbered Discourses 5.184) ^an5-184-0

“Mendicants, these five dwell in a charnel ground. ^an5-184-1-1

What five? ^an5-184-1-2

One may dwell in a charnel ground because of stupidity … corrupt wishes … madness … because it is praised by the Buddhas … or for the sake of having few wishes … ^an5-184-1-3

These are the five who dwell in a charnel ground.” ^an5-184-1-4

### Open Air Dwellers (Numbered Discourses 5.185) ^an5-185-0

“These five dwell in the open air. …” ^an5-185-1-1

### Those Who Never Lie Down (Numbered Discourses 5.186) ^an5-186-0

“These five never lie down. …” ^an5-186-1-1

### Those Who Sleep Wherever a Mat is Laid (Numbered Discourses 5.187) ^an5-187-0

“These five sleep wherever they lay their mat. …” ^an5-187-1-1

### Those Who Eat in One Sitting (Numbered Discourses 5.188) ^an5-188-0

“These five eat in one sitting per day. …” ^an5-188-1-1

### Refusers of Late Food (Numbered Discourses 5.189) ^an5-189-0

“These five refuse to accept food offered after the meal has begun. …” ^an5-189-1-1

### Those Who Eat Only From the Almsbowl (Numbered Discourses 5.190) ^an5-190-0

“Mendicants, these five eat only from the almsbowl. ^an5-190-1-1

What five? ^an5-190-1-2

One may eat only from the almsbowl because of stupidity and folly. Or because of corrupt wishes, being of wishful temperament. Or because of madness and mental disorder. Or because it is praised by the Buddhas and their disciples. Or for the sake of having few wishes, for the sake of contentment, self-effacement, seclusion, and simplicity. ^an5-190-1-3

These are the five who eat only from the almsbowl. ^an5-190-1-4

But the person who eats only from the almsbowl for the sake of having few wishes is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the five. ^an5-190-1-5

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^an5-190-2-1

In the same way, the one who eats only from the almsbowl for the sake of having few wishes is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the five.” ^an5-190-2-2

### Hounds (Numbered Discourses 5.191) ^an5-191-0

“Mendicants, these five ancient traditions of the brahmins are exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-1-1

What five? ^an5-191-1-2

In the past brahmins had sex only with brahmin women, not with others. ^an5-191-1-3

These days brahmins have sex with both brahmin women and others. ^an5-191-1-4

But these days hounds have sex only with female hounds, not with other species. ^an5-191-1-5

This is the first ancient tradition of the brahmins exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-1-6

In the past brahmins had sex only with brahmin women in the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle, not at other times. ^an5-191-2-1

These days brahmins have sex with brahmin women both in the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle and at other times. ^an5-191-2-2

But these days hounds have sex only with female hounds when they are in heat, not at other times. ^an5-191-2-3

This is the second ancient tradition of the brahmins exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-2-4

In the past brahmins neither bought nor sold brahmin women. They lived together because they loved each other and wanted their family line to continue. ^an5-191-3-1

These days brahmins both buy and sell brahmin women. They live together whether they love each other or not and they want their family line to continue. ^an5-191-3-2

But these days hounds neither buy nor sell female hounds. They live together because they’re attracted to each other and want their family line to continue. ^an5-191-3-3

This is the third ancient tradition of the brahmins exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-3-4

In the past brahmins did not store up money, grain, silver, or gold. ^an5-191-4-1

These days brahmins do store up money, grain, silver, and gold. ^an5-191-4-2

But these days hounds don’t store up money, grain, silver, or gold. ^an5-191-4-3

This is the fourth ancient tradition of the brahmins exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-4-4

In the past brahmins went looking for almsfood for dinner at dusk, and for breakfast at daybreak. ^an5-191-5-1

These days brahmins eat as much as they like until their bellies are full, then take away the leftovers. ^an5-191-5-2

But these days hounds go looking for dinner at dusk, and for breakfast at daybreak. ^an5-191-5-3

This is the fifth ancient tradition of the brahmins exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins. ^an5-191-5-4

These five ancient traditions of the brahmins are exhibited these days among hounds, but not among brahmins.” ^an5-191-5-5

### With the Brahmin Doṇa (Numbered Discourses 5.192) ^an5-192-0

Then Doṇa the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an5-192-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Doṇa sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an5-192-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, I have heard that ^an5-192-2-1

the ascetic Gotama doesn’t bow to old brahmins, the elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat. ^an5-192-2-2

And this is indeed the case, ^an5-192-2-3

for the worthy Gotama does not bow to old brahmins, elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat. ^an5-192-2-4

This is not appropriate, worthy Gotama.” ^an5-192-2-5

“Doṇa, do you too claim to be a brahmin?” ^an5-192-2-6

“Worthy Gotama, if anyone should be rightly called ^an5-192-2-7

a brahmin, it’s me. ^an5-192-2-8

For I am well born on both my mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. I recite and remember the hymns, and have mastered the three Vedas, together with their vocabularies and ritual performance, their phonology and word classification, and the testaments as fifth. I know them word-by-word, and their grammar. I am well versed in cosmology and the marks of a great man.” ^an5-192-2-11

“Doṇa, the ancient brahmin seers were Aṭṭhaka, Vāmaka, Vāmadeva, Vessāmitta, Yamadaggi, Aṅgīrasa, Bhāradvāja, Vāseṭṭha, Kassapa, and Bhagu. They were the authors and propagators of the hymns, whose hymnal was sung and propagated and compiled in ancient times. These days, brahmins continue to sing and chant it. They continue chanting what was chanted, rehearsing what was rehearsed, and teaching what was taught. ^an5-192-3-1

Those seers described five kinds of brahmins. ^an5-192-3-2

A brahmin who is equal to the Divinity, one who is equal to a god, one who toes the line, one who crosses the line, and the fifth is a brahmin corpse-worker. ^an5-192-3-3

Which one of these are you, Doṇa?” ^an5-192-3-4

“Worthy Gotama, we don’t know about these five kinds of brahmins. We just know the word ‘brahmin’. ^an5-192-4-1

Worthy Gotama, please teach me this matter so I can learn about these five brahmins.” ^an5-192-4-2

“Well then, brahmin, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an5-192-4-3

“Yes, worthy sir,” Doṇa replied. ^an5-192-4-4

The Buddha said this: ^an5-192-4-5

“Doṇa, how is a brahmin equal to the Divinity? ^an5-192-5-1

It’s when a brahmin is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-192-5-2

For forty-eight years he leads the boy’s spiritual life studying the hymns. ^an5-192-5-4

Then he seeks a fee for his tutor, but only by legitimate means, not illegitimate. ^an5-192-5-5

In this context, Doṇa, what is legitimate? ^an5-192-6-1

Not by farming, trade, raising cattle, archery, government service, or one of the professions, but solely by living on alms, not scorning the alms bowl. ^an5-192-6-2

Having offered the fee to his tutor, he shaves off his hair and beard, dresses in ocher robes, and goes forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-192-6-3

Then they meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an5-192-6-4

They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion … ^an5-192-6-5

rejoicing … ^an5-192-6-6

equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an5-192-6-7

Having developed these four divine meditations, when the body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a realm of divinity. ^an5-192-6-8

That’s how a brahmin is equal to the Divinity. ^an5-192-6-9

And how is a brahmin equal to a god? ^an5-192-7-1

It’s when a brahmin is well born on both the mother’s and the father’s sides … ^an5-192-7-2

Having offered the fee to his tutor, he seeks a wife, but only by legitimate means, not illegitimate. ^an5-192-7-8

In this context, Doṇa, what is legitimate? ^an5-192-8-1

Not by buying or selling, he only accepts a brahmin woman by the pouring of water. ^an5-192-8-2

He has sex only with a brahmin woman. He does not have sex with a woman from a family of aristocrats, peasants, menials, corpse-workers, hunters, bamboo-workers, chariot-makers, or scavengers. Nor does he have sex with women who are pregnant, breastfeeding, or outside the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle. ^an5-192-8-3

And why does the brahmin not have sex with a pregnant woman? ^an5-192-8-4

If a brahmin had sex with a pregnant woman, the boy or girl would be born in too much filth. ^an5-192-8-5

That’s why the brahmin doesn’t have sex with a pregnant woman. ^an5-192-8-6

And why does the brahmin not have sex with a breastfeeding woman? ^an5-192-8-7

If a brahmin had sex with a breastfeeding woman, the boy or girl would drink back the semen. ^an5-192-8-8
