That’s why the brahmin doesn’t have sex with a breastfeeding woman. ^an5-192-8-9

And why does the brahmin not have sex outside the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle? Because his brahmin wife is not there for sensual pleasure, fun, and enjoyment, but only for procreation. ^an5-192-8-10

Having ensured his progeny through sex, he shaves off his hair and beard, dresses in ocher robes, and goes forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-192-8-11

When he has gone forth, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, he enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an5-192-8-12

Having developed these four absorptions, when the body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-192-8-13

That’s how a brahmin is equal to god. ^an5-192-8-14

And how does a brahmin toe the line? ^an5-192-9-1

It’s when a brahmin is well born on both the mother’s and the father’s sides … ^an5-192-9-2

Not by buying or selling, he only accepts a brahmin woman by the pouring of water. ^an5-192-10-2

Having ensured his progeny through sex, his child makes him happy. Because of this attachment he stays in his family property, and does not go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an5-192-10-11

As far as the line of the ancient brahmins extends, he doesn’t cross over it. ^an5-192-10-12

That’s why he’s called a brahmin who toes the line. ^an5-192-10-13

That’s how a brahmin toes the line. ^an5-192-10-14

And how does a brahmin cross the line? ^an5-192-11-1

It’s when a brahmin is well born on both the mother’s and the father’s sides … ^an5-192-11-2

Having offered a fee for his tutor, he seeks a wife by both legitimate and illegitimate means. That is, by buying or selling, as well as accepting a brahmin woman by the pouring of water. ^an5-192-12-3

He has sex with a brahmin woman, as well as with a woman from a family of aristocrats, peasants, menials, corpse-workers, hunters, bamboo workers, chariot-makers, or scavengers. And he has sex with women who are pregnant, breastfeeding, or outside the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle. ^an5-192-12-4

His brahmin wife is there for sensual pleasure, fun, and enjoyment, as well as for procreation. ^an5-192-12-5

As far as the line of the ancient brahmins extends, he crosses over it. ^an5-192-12-6

That’s why he’s called a brahmin who crosses the line. ^an5-192-12-7

That’s how a brahmin crosses the line. ^an5-192-12-8

And how is a brahmin a brahmin corpse-worker? ^an5-192-13-1

It’s when a brahmin is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, stemming from a clean vessel, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation. ^an5-192-13-2

For forty-eight years he leads the virginal spiritual life studying the hymns. ^an5-192-13-4

Then he seeks a fee for his tutor by legitimate means and illegitimate means. ^an5-192-13-5

By farming, trade, raising cattle, archery, government service, or one of the professions, not solely by living on alms, not scorning the alms bowl. ^an5-192-13-6

Having offered a fee for his tutor, he seeks a wife by both legitimate and illegitimate means. That is, by buying or selling, as well as accepting a brahmin woman by the pouring of water. ^an5-192-14-1

He has sex with a brahmin woman, as well as with a woman from a family of aristocrats, peasants, menials, corpse-workers, hunters, bamboo workers, chariot-makers, or scavengers. And he has sex with women who are pregnant, breastfeeding, or outside the fertile phase of the menstrual cycle. ^an5-192-14-2

His brahmin wife is there for sensual pleasure, fun, and enjoyment, as well as for procreation. ^an5-192-14-3

He earns a living by any kind of work. ^an5-192-14-4

The brahmins say to him, ^an5-192-14-5

‘My good man, why is it that you claim to be a brahmin, but you earn a living by any kind of work?’ ^an5-192-14-6

He says, ^an5-192-14-7

‘It’s like a fire that burns both pure and filthy things, but doesn’t become corrupted by them. ^an5-192-14-8

In the same way, mister, if a brahmin earns a living by any kind of work, he is not corrupted by that.’ ^an5-192-14-9

A brahmin is called a brahmin corpse-worker because he earns a living by any kind of work. ^an5-192-14-10

That’s how a brahmin is a brahmin corpse-worker. ^an5-192-14-11

Doṇa, the ancient brahmin seers were Aṭṭhaka, Vāmaka, Vāmadeva, Vessāmitta, Yamadaggi, Aṅgīrasa, Bhāradvāja, Vāseṭṭha, Kassapa, and Bhagu. They were the authors and propagators of the hymns, whose hymnal was sung and propagated and compiled in ancient times. These days, brahmins continue to sing and chant it. They continue chanting what was chanted, rehearsing what was rehearsed, and teaching what was taught. ^an5-192-15-1

Those seers described five kinds of brahmins. ^an5-192-15-2

A brahmin who is equal to the Divinity, one who is equal to a god, one who toes the line, one who crosses the line, and the fifth is a brahmin corpse-worker. ^an5-192-15-3

Which one of these are you, Doṇa?” ^an5-192-15-4

“This being so, worthy Gotama, I don’t even qualify as a brahmin corpse-worker. ^an5-192-16-1

Excellent, worthy Gotama! … From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an5-192-16-2

### With Saṅgārava (Numbered Discourses 5.193) ^an5-193-0

Then Saṅgārava the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an5-193-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Saṅgārava sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an5-193-1-2

“What is the cause, Mister Gotama, what is the reason why sometimes even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed? ^an5-193-1-3

And why is it that sometimes even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed?” ^an5-193-1-4

“Brahmin, there’s a time when your heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire and you don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. At that time you don’t truly know or see what is good for yourself, good for another, or good for both. Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-2-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was mixed with dye such as red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. ^an5-193-2-2

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^an5-193-2-3

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-2-4

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-3-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was heated by fire, boiling and bubbling. ^an5-193-3-2

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^an5-193-3-3

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-3-4

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-4-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water overgrown with moss and aquatic plants. ^an5-193-4-2

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^an5-193-4-3

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-4-4

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-5-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water stirred by the wind, churning, swirling, and rippling. ^an5-193-5-2

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^an5-193-5-3

In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-5-4

Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in doubt … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-6-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was cloudy, murky, and muddy, hidden in the darkness. ^an5-193-6-2

Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it. ^an5-193-6-3

In the same way, there’s a time when your heart is overcome and mired in doubt and you don’t truly understand the escape from doubt that has arisen. At that time you don’t truly know or see what is good for yourself, good for another, or good for both. Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-6-4

There’s a time when your heart is not overcome and mired in sensual desire and you truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. At that time you truly know and see what is good for yourself, good for another, and good for both. Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^an5-193-7-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that was not mixed with dye such as red lac, turmeric, indigo, or rose madder. ^an5-193-7-2

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^an5-193-7-3

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in sensual desire … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-7-4

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-8-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that’s not heated by a fire, boiling and bubbling. ^an5-193-8-2

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^an5-193-8-3

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in ill will … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-8-4

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-9-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that’s not overgrown with moss and aquatic plants. ^an5-193-9-2

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^an5-193-9-3

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-9-4

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-10-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that’s not stirred by the wind, churning, swirling, and rippling. ^an5-193-10-2

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^an5-193-10-3

In the same way, when your heart is not overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … Even hymns that are long-unpracticed spring to mind, let alone those that are practiced. ^an5-193-10-4

Furthermore, when your heart is not overcome and mired in doubt … Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^an5-193-11-1

Suppose there was a bowl of water that’s transparent, clear, and unclouded, brought into the light. ^an5-193-11-2

A person with clear eyes checking their own reflection would truly know it and see it. ^an5-193-11-3

In the same way, there’s a time when your heart is not overcome and mired in doubt and you truly understand the escape from doubt that has arisen. At that time you truly know and see what is good for yourself, good for another, and good for both. Even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed. ^an5-193-11-4

This is the cause, brahmin, this is the reason why sometimes even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed. ^an5-193-12-1

And this is why sometimes even hymns that are long-unrehearsed spring to mind, let alone those that are rehearsed.” ^an5-193-12-2

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! … From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an5-193-13-1

### With Kāraṇapālī (Numbered Discourses 5.194) ^an5-194-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-194-1-1

Now at that time the brahmin Kāraṇapālī was working for the Licchavis. ^an5-194-1-2

He saw the brahmin Piṅgiyānī coming off in the distance ^an5-194-1-3

and said to him, ^an5-194-1-4

“So, Piṅgiyānī, where are you coming from in the middle of the day?” ^an5-194-2-1

“I’m coming, mister, from the presence of the ascetic Gotama.” ^an5-194-2-2

“What do you think of the ascetic Gotama’s lucidity of wisdom? Do you think he’s astute?” ^an5-194-2-3

“Mister, who am I to judge the ascetic Gotama’s lucidity of wisdom? ^an5-194-2-4

You’d really have to be on the same level to judge his lucidity of wisdom.” ^an5-194-2-5

“Mister Piṅgiyānī praises the ascetic Gotama with high praise indeed.” ^an5-194-2-6

“Who am I to praise the ascetic Gotama? ^an5-194-2-7

He is praised by the praised as the first among gods and humans.” ^an5-194-2-8

“But for what reason are you so devoted to the ascetic Gotama?” ^an5-194-2-9

“Suppose a person was completely satisfied by the best tasting food. They wouldn’t be attracted to anything that tasted inferior. ^an5-194-3-1

In the same way, when you hear the worthy Gotama’s teaching— ^an5-194-3-2

whatever it may be, whether statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, or amazing stories— ^an5-194-3-3

then you’re not attracted to the doctrines of the various ascetics and brahmins. ^an5-194-3-4

Suppose a person who was weak with hunger was to obtain a honey-cake. ^an5-194-4-1

Wherever they taste it, they would enjoy a sweet, delectable flavor. ^an5-194-4-2

In the same way, when you hear the worthy Gotama’s teaching— ^an5-194-4-3

whatever it may be, whether statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, or amazing stories— ^an5-194-4-4

then you get a sense of uplift, a confidence of the heart. ^an5-194-4-5

Suppose a person were to obtain a piece of sandalwood, ^an5-194-5-1

whether yellow or red. ^an5-194-5-2

Wherever they smelled it— ^an5-194-5-3

whether at the root, the middle, or the top— ^an5-194-5-4

they’d enjoy an delectable fragrance. ^an5-194-5-5

In the same way, when you hear the worthy Gotama’s teaching— ^an5-194-5-6

whatever it may be, whether statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, or amazing stories— ^an5-194-5-7

then you become filled with joy and happiness. ^an5-194-5-8

Suppose there was a person who was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an5-194-6-1

A good doctor would cure them on the spot. ^an5-194-6-2

In the same way, when you hear the worthy Gotama’s teaching— ^an5-194-6-3

whatever it may be, whether statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, or amazing stories— ^an5-194-6-4

then you make an end of sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^an5-194-6-5

Suppose there was a lotus pond with clear, sweet, cool water, clean, with smooth banks, delightful. ^an5-194-7-1

Then along comes a person struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched. ^an5-194-7-2

They’d plunge into the lotus pond to bathe and drink. And all their stress, weariness, and heat exhaustion would die down. ^an5-194-7-3

In the same way, when you hear the worthy Gotama’s teaching— ^an5-194-7-4

whatever it may be, whether statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, or amazing stories— ^an5-194-7-5

then all your stress, weariness, and exhaustion die down.” ^an5-194-7-6

When this was said, the brahmin Kāraṇapālī rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, knelt on his right knee, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and expressed this heartfelt sentiment three times: ^an5-194-8-1

“Homage to him, the blessed one, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha! ^an5-194-9-1

Homage to him, the blessed one, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha! ^an5-194-10-1

Homage to him, the blessed one, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha! ^an5-194-11-1

Excellent, worthy Piṅgiyānī! Excellent! ^an5-194-12-1

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the worthy Piṅgiyānī has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an5-194-12-2

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an5-194-12-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Piṅgiyānī remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an5-194-12-4

### Piṅgiyānī (Numbered Discourses 5.195) ^an5-195-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an5-195-1-1

Now at that time around five hundred Licchavis were visiting the Buddha. ^an5-195-1-2

Some of the Licchavis were in blue, of blue color, clad in blue, adorned with blue. And some were similarly colored in yellow, red, or white. ^an5-195-1-3

But the Buddha outshone them all in beauty and glory. ^an5-195-1-4

Then the brahmin Piṅgīyānī rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha, and said, ^an5-195-2-1

“I feel inspired to speak, Blessed One! I feel inspired to speak, Holy One!” ^an5-195-2-2

“Then speak as you feel inspired,” said the Buddha. ^an5-195-2-3

So the brahmin Piṅgīyānī extolled the Buddha in his presence with a fitting verse. ^an5-195-2-4

“Like a fragrant pink lotus ^an5-195-3-1

that blooms at daybreak, its fragrance unfaded— ^an5-195-3-2

see Aṅgīrasa shine, ^an5-195-3-3

bright as the sun in the sky!” ^an5-195-3-4

Then those Licchavis clothed Piṅgiyānī with five hundred upper robes. ^an5-195-4-1

And Piṅgiyānī clothed the Buddha with them. ^an5-195-4-2

Then the Buddha said to the Licchavis: ^an5-195-5-1

“Licchavis, the appearance of five treasures is rare in the world. ^an5-195-5-2

What five? ^an5-195-5-3

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. ^an5-195-5-4

A teacher of the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. ^an5-195-5-5

An individual who understands the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. ^an5-195-5-6

An individual who practices in line with the teaching. ^an5-195-5-7

An individual who is grateful and thankful. ^an5-195-5-8

The appearance of these five treasures is rare in the world.” ^an5-195-5-9

### The Great Dreams (Numbered Discourses 5.196) ^an5-196-0

“Mendicants, before his awakening five great dreams appeared to the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, when he was still not awake but intent on awakening. ^an5-196-1-1

What five? ^an5-196-1-2

This great earth was his bed. Himalaya, king of mountains, was his pillow. His left hand was laid down in the eastern sea. His right hand was laid down in the western sea. And both his feet were laid down in the southern sea. ^an5-196-1-3

This is the first great dream that appeared to the Realized One before his awakening. ^an5-196-1-4

Next, a kind of grass called ‘the crosser’ grew up from his navel and stood pressing against the cloudy sky. ^an5-196-2-1

This is the second great dream that appeared to the Realized One before his awakening. ^an5-196-2-2

Next, white caterpillars with black heads crawled up from his feet and covered his knees. ^an5-196-3-1

This is the third great dream that appeared to the Realized One before his awakening. ^an5-196-3-2

Next, four birds of different colors came from the four quarters. They fell at his feet, turning pure white. ^an5-196-4-1

This is the fourth great dream that appeared to the Realized One before his awakening. ^an5-196-4-2

Next, he walked back and forth on top of a huge mountain of filth while remaining unsoiled. ^an5-196-5-1

This is the fifth great dream that appeared to the Realized One before his awakening. ^an5-196-5-2

Now, as to when, before his awakening, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha was still not awake but intent on awakening. This great earth was his bed. Himalaya, king of mountains, was his pillow. His left hand was laid down in the eastern sea. His right hand was laid down in the western sea. And both his feet were laid down in the southern sea. ^an5-196-6-1

This was fulfilled when the Buddha awakened to the perfect awakening. ^an5-196-6-2

This was the first great dream that appeared to him while he was still not awakened. ^an5-196-6-3

As to when a kind of grass called ‘the crosser’ grew up from his navel and stood pressing against the cloudy sky. ^an5-196-7-1

This was fulfilled when, after the Buddha had awakened to the noble eightfold path, it was well proclaimed wherever there are gods and humans. ^an5-196-7-2

This was the second great dream that appeared to him while he was still not awakened. ^an5-196-7-3

As to when white caterpillars with black heads crawled up from his feet and covered his knees. ^an5-196-8-1

This was fulfilled when many white-clothed laypeople went for refuge to him for life. ^an5-196-8-2

This was the third great dream that appeared to him while he was still not awakened. ^an5-196-8-3

As to when four birds of different colors came from the four quarters. They fell at his feet, turning pure white. ^an5-196-9-1

This was fulfilled when members of the four classes—aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials—went forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One and realized supreme freedom. ^an5-196-9-2

This was the fourth great dream that appeared to him while he was still not awakened. ^an5-196-9-3

As to when he walked back and forth on top of a huge mountain of filth while remaining unsoiled. ^an5-196-10-1

This was fulfilled when the Realized One received robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And he used them untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. ^an5-196-10-2

This was the fifth great dream that appeared to him while he was still not awakened. ^an5-196-10-3

Before his awakening these five great dreams appeared to the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, when he was still not awake but intent on awakening.” ^an5-196-11-1

### Obstacles to Rain (Numbered Discourses 5.197) ^an5-197-0

“Mendicants, there are these five obstacles to rain, which the forecasters don’t know, and which their vision does not traverse. ^an5-197-1-1

What five? ^an5-197-1-2

In the upper atmosphere the fire element flares up, ^an5-197-1-3

which disperses the clouds. ^an5-197-1-4

This is the first obstacle to rain, which the forecasters don’t know, and which their vision does not traverse. ^an5-197-1-5

Furthermore, in the upper atmosphere the air element flares up, ^an5-197-2-1

which disperses the clouds. ^an5-197-2-2

This is the second obstacle to rain … ^an5-197-2-3

Furthermore, Rāhu, lord of titans, receives water in his hand and discards it in the ocean. ^an5-197-3-1

This is the third obstacle to rain … ^an5-197-3-2

Furthermore, the gods of the rain clouds become negligent. ^an5-197-4-1

This is the fourth obstacle to rain … ^an5-197-4-2

Furthermore, humans become unprincipled. ^an5-197-5-1

This is the fifth obstacle to rain, which the forecasters don’t know, and which their vision does not traverse. ^an5-197-5-2

These are the five obstacles to rain, which the forecasters don’t know, and which their vision does not traverse.” ^an5-197-5-3

### Well-Spoken Words (Numbered Discourses 5.198) ^an5-198-0

“Mendicants, speech that has five factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people. ^an5-198-1-1

What five? ^an5-198-1-2

It is speech that is timely, true, gentle, beneficial, and loving. ^an5-198-1-3

Speech with these five factors is well spoken, not poorly spoken. It’s blameless and is not criticized by sensible people.” ^an5-198-1-4

### Families (Numbered Discourses 5.199) ^an5-199-0

“When ethical renunciates come to a family, the people brim with much merit for five reasons. ^an5-199-1-1

What five? ^an5-199-1-2

When they see ethical renunciates coming to their family, the people bring up confidence in their hearts. At that time the family is practicing a path leading to heaven. ^an5-199-1-3

When ethical renunciates come to their family, the people rise from their seats, bow down, and offer them a seat. At that time the family is practicing a path leading to a birth in an eminent family. ^an5-199-2-1

When ethical renunciates come to their family, the people get rid of the stain of stinginess. At that time the family is practicing a path leading to being illustrious. ^an5-199-3-1

When ethical renunciates come to their family, the people share what they have as best they can. At that time the family is practicing a path leading to great wealth. ^an5-199-4-1

When ethical renunciates come to their family, the people ask questions and listen to the teachings. At that time the family is practicing a path leading to great wisdom. ^an5-199-5-1

When ethical renunciates come to a family, the people brim with much merit for these five reasons.” ^an5-199-5-2

### Elements of Escape (Numbered Discourses 5.200) ^an5-200-0

“Mendicants, there are these five elements of escape. ^an5-200-1-1

What five? ^an5-200-1-2

Take a case where a mendicant focuses on sensual pleasures, but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an5-200-1-3

But when they focus on renunciation, on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an5-200-1-4

Their mind is in a good state, well developed, well risen, well freed, and well detached from sensual pleasures. ^an5-200-1-5

They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of sensual pleasures, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling. ^an5-200-1-6

This is how the escape from sensual pleasures is explained. ^an5-200-1-7

Take another case where a mendicant focuses on ill will, but their mind does not leap forth … ^an5-200-2-1

But when they focus on good will, their mind leaps forth … ^an5-200-2-2

Their mind is in a good state … well detached from ill will. ^an5-200-2-3

They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of ill will, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling. ^an5-200-2-4

This is how the escape from ill will is explained. ^an5-200-2-5

Take another case where a mendicant focuses on harming, but their mind does not leap forth … ^an5-200-3-1

But when they focus on compassion, their mind leaps forth … ^an5-200-3-2

Their mind is in a good state … well detached from harming. ^an5-200-3-3

They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of harming, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling. ^an5-200-3-4

This is how the escape from harming is explained. ^an5-200-3-5

Take another case where a mendicant focuses on form, but their mind does not leap forth … ^an5-200-4-1

But when they focus on the formless, their mind leaps forth … ^an5-200-4-2

Their mind is in a good state … well detached from forms. ^an5-200-4-3

They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of form, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling. ^an5-200-4-4

This is how the escape from forms is explained. ^an5-200-4-5

Take a case where a mendicant focuses on substantial reality, but their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided. ^an5-200-5-1

But when they focus on the cessation of substantial reality, their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided. ^an5-200-5-2

Their mind is in a good state, well developed, well risen, well freed, and well detached from substantial reality. ^an5-200-5-3

They’re freed from the distressing and feverish defilements that arise because of substantial reality, so they don’t experience that kind of feeling. ^an5-200-5-4

This is how the escape from substantial reality is explained. ^an5-200-5-5

Delight in sensual pleasures, ill will, harming, form, and substantial reality don’t linger within them. ^an5-200-6-1

That’s why they’re called a mendicant who is without underlying tendencies, who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering. ^an5-200-6-2

These are the five elements of escape.” ^an5-200-6-3

### With Kimbila (Numbered Discourses 5.201) ^an5-201-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kimbilā in the Freshwater Mangrove Wood. ^an5-201-1-1

Then Venerable Kimbila went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an5-201-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an5-201-1-3

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen lack respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and each other after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an5-201-1-4

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an5-201-1-5

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an5-201-2-1

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen maintain respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and each other after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an5-201-2-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an5-201-2-3

### Listening to the Teaching (Numbered Discourses 5.202) ^an5-202-0

“Mendicants, there are these five benefits of listening to the teaching. ^an5-202-1-1

What five? ^an5-202-1-2

You learn new things, clarify what you’ve learned, get over uncertainty, correct your views, and inspire confidence in your mind. ^an5-202-1-3

These are the five benefits of listening to the teaching.” ^an5-202-1-4

### A Thoroughbred (Numbered Discourses 5.203) ^an5-203-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an5-203-1-1

What five? ^an5-203-2-1

Integrity, speed, tenderness, patience, and gentleness. ^an5-203-2-2

A fine royal thoroughbred with these five factors is worthy of a king. … ^an5-203-2-3

In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an5-203-2-4

What five? ^an5-203-3-1

Integrity, speed, tenderness, patience, and gentleness. ^an5-203-3-2

A mendicant with these five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an5-203-3-3

### Powers (Numbered Discourses 5.204) ^an5-204-0

“Mendicants, there are these five powers. ^an5-204-1-1

What five? ^an5-204-1-2

Faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom. ^an5-204-1-3

These are the five powers.” ^an5-204-1-4

### Hard-heartedness (Numbered Discourses 5.205) ^an5-205-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of hard-heartedness. ^an5-205-1-1

What five? ^an5-205-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant has doubts about the Teacher. They’re uncertain, undecided, and lacking confidence. ^an5-205-1-3

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an5-205-1-4

This is the first kind of hard-heartedness. ^an5-205-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has doubts about the teaching … ^an5-205-2-1

the Saṅgha … ^an5-205-2-2

the training … ^an5-205-2-3

A mendicant is angry and upset with their spiritual companions, resentful and closed off. ^an5-205-2-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an5-205-2-5

This is the fifth kind of hard-heartedness. ^an5-205-2-6

These are the five kinds of hard-heartedness.” ^an5-205-2-7

### Shackles (Numbered Discourses 5.206) ^an5-206-0

“Mendicants, there are these five shackles of the heart. ^an5-206-1-1

What five? ^an5-206-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an5-206-1-3

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an5-206-1-4

This is the first shackle of the heart. ^an5-206-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant isn’t free of greed for the body … ^an5-206-2-1

They’re not free of greed for form … ^an5-206-2-2

They eat as much as they like until their belly is full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing … ^an5-206-2-3

They lead the spiritual life hoping to be reborn in one of the orders of gods, thinking: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^an5-206-2-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an5-206-2-5

This is the fifth shackle of the heart. ^an5-206-2-6

These are the five shackles of the heart.” ^an5-206-2-7

### Porridge (Numbered Discourses 5.207) ^an5-207-0

“Mendicants, there are these five benefits of porridge. ^an5-207-1-1

What five? ^an5-207-1-2

It wards off hunger, quenches thirst, settles the wind, cleans the bladder, and helps digestion. ^an5-207-1-3

These are the five benefits of porridge.” ^an5-207-1-4

### Chew Sticks (Numbered Discourses 5.208) ^an5-208-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of not using chew sticks. ^an5-208-1-1

What five? ^an5-208-1-2

It’s not good for your eyes, you get bad breath, your taste-buds aren’t cleaned, bile and phlegm cover your food, and you lose your appetite. ^an5-208-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of not using chew sticks. ^an5-208-1-4

There are these five benefits of using chew sticks. ^an5-208-2-1

What five? ^an5-208-2-2

It’s good for your eyes, you don’t get bad breath, your taste-buds are cleaned, bile and phlegm don’t cover your food, and food agrees with you. ^an5-208-2-3

These are the five benefits of using chew sticks.” ^an5-208-2-4

### The Sound of Singing (Numbered Discourses 5.209) ^an5-209-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in reciting with a drawn-out singing voice. ^an5-209-1-1

What five? ^an5-209-1-2

You relish the sound of your own voice. Others relish the sound of your voice. Householders complain: ^an5-209-1-3

‘These ascetics who follow the Sakyan, sing just like us!’ When you’re enjoying the melody, your immersion breaks up. Those who come after follow your example. ^an5-209-1-4

These are the five drawbacks in reciting with a drawn-out singing voice.” ^an5-209-1-5

### Unmindful (Numbered Discourses 5.210) ^an5-210-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of falling asleep unmindful and unaware. ^an5-210-1-1

What five? ^an5-210-1-2

You sleep badly and wake miserably. You have bad dreams. The deities don’t protect you. And you emit semen. ^an5-210-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of falling asleep unmindful and unaware. ^an5-210-1-4

There are these five benefits of falling asleep mindful and aware. ^an5-210-2-1

What five? ^an5-210-2-2

You sleep at ease and wake happily. You don’t have bad dreams. The deities protect you. And you don’t emit semen. ^an5-210-2-3

These are the five benefits of falling asleep mindful and aware.” ^an5-210-2-4

### An Abuser (Numbered Discourses 5.211) ^an5-211-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, denouncing the noble ones, can expect these five drawbacks. ^an5-211-1-1

What five? ^an5-211-1-2

They’re expelled, cut off, blocked out; or they commit a corrupt offense; or they contract a severe illness. They feel lost when they die. And when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-211-1-3

A mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, denouncing the noble ones, can expect these five drawbacks.” ^an5-211-1-4

### Starting Fights (Numbered Discourses 5.212) ^an5-212-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who starts fights, quarrels, disputes, debates, and disciplinary issues in the Saṅgha can expect five drawbacks. ^an5-212-1-1

What five? ^an5-212-1-2

They don’t achieve the unachieved. What they have achieved falls away. They get a bad reputation. They feel lost when they die. And when their body breaks up, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-212-1-3

A mendicant who starts fights, quarrels, disputes, debates, and disciplinary issues in the Saṅgha can expect these five drawbacks.” ^an5-212-1-4

### Ethics (Numbered Discourses 5.213) ^an5-213-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for an unethical person because of their failure in ethics. ^an5-213-1-1

What five? ^an5-213-1-2

Firstly, an unethical person loses great wealth on account of negligence. ^an5-213-1-3

This is the first drawback. ^an5-213-1-4

Furthermore, an unethical person gets a bad reputation. ^an5-213-2-1

This is the second drawback. ^an5-213-2-2

Furthermore, an unethical person enters any kind of assembly timid and embarrassed, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics. ^an5-213-3-1

This is the third drawback. ^an5-213-3-2

Furthermore, an unethical person feels lost when they die. ^an5-213-4-1

This is the fourth drawback. ^an5-213-4-2

Furthermore, an unethical person, when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-213-5-1

This is the fifth drawback. ^an5-213-5-2

These are the five drawbacks for an unethical person because of their failure in ethics. ^an5-213-5-3

There are these five benefits for an ethical person because of their accomplishment in ethics. ^an5-213-6-1

What five? ^an5-213-6-2

Firstly, an ethical person gains great wealth on account of diligence. ^an5-213-6-3

This is the first benefit. ^an5-213-6-4

Furthermore, an ethical person gets a good reputation. ^an5-213-7-1

This is the second benefit. ^an5-213-7-2

Furthermore, an ethical person enters any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics. ^an5-213-8-1

This is the third benefit. ^an5-213-8-2

Furthermore, an ethical person dies not feeling lost. ^an5-213-9-1

This is the fourth benefit. ^an5-213-9-2

Furthermore, when an ethical person’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-213-10-1

This is the fifth benefit. ^an5-213-10-2

These are the five benefits for an ethical person because of their accomplishment in ethics.” ^an5-213-10-3

### Someone Who Talks a Lot (Numbered Discourses 5.214) ^an5-214-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for an individual who talks a lot. ^an5-214-1-1

What five? ^an5-214-1-2

They use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, and nonsensical. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-214-1-3

These are the five drawbacks for an individual who talks a lot. ^an5-214-1-4

There are these five benefits for an individual who talks thoughtfully. ^an5-214-2-1

What five? ^an5-214-2-2

They don’t use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, and nonsensical. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-214-2-3

These are the five benefits for an individual who talks thoughtfully.” ^an5-214-2-4

### Intolerance (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.215) ^an5-215-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of intolerance. ^an5-215-1-1

What five? ^an5-215-1-2

Most people find you unlikable and disagreeable. You have lots of enmity and many faults. You feel lost when you die. And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-215-1-3

These are the five drawbacks to intolerance. ^an5-215-1-4

There are these five benefits of tolerance. ^an5-215-2-1

What five? ^an5-215-2-2

Most people find you dear and agreeable. You have little enmity and few faults. You don’t feel lost when you die. And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-215-2-3

These are the five benefits of tolerance.” ^an5-215-2-4

### Intolerance (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.216) ^an5-216-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of intolerance. ^an5-216-1-1

What five? ^an5-216-1-2

Most people find you unlikable and disagreeable. You’re cruel and remorseful. You feel lost when you die. And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-216-1-3

These are the five drawbacks to intolerance. ^an5-216-1-4

There are these five benefits of tolerance. ^an5-216-2-1

What five? ^an5-216-2-2

Most people find you likable and agreeable. You’re neither cruel nor remorseful. You don’t feel lost when you die. And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-216-2-3

These are the five benefits of tolerance.” ^an5-216-2-4

### Unimpressive Conduct (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.217) ^an5-217-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of unimpressive conduct. ^an5-217-1-1

What five? ^an5-217-1-2

You blame yourself. ^an5-217-1-3

After examination, sensible people criticize you. ^an5-217-1-4

You get a bad reputation. ^an5-217-1-5

You feel lost when you die. ^an5-217-1-6

And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-217-1-7

These are the five drawbacks of unimpressive conduct. ^an5-217-1-8

There are these five benefits of impressive conduct. ^an5-217-2-1

What five? ^an5-217-2-2

You don’t blame yourself. ^an5-217-2-3

After examination, sensible people praise you. ^an5-217-2-4

You get a good reputation. ^an5-217-2-5

You don’t feel lost when you die. ^an5-217-2-6

And when the body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-217-2-7

These are the five benefits of impressive conduct.” ^an5-217-2-8

### Unimpressive Conduct (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.218) ^an5-218-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of unimpressive conduct. ^an5-218-1-1

What five? ^an5-218-1-2

You don’t inspire confidence in those without it. You cause some with confidence to change their minds. You don’t follow the Teacher’s instructions. Those who come after you follow your example. And your mind doesn’t become clear. ^an5-218-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of unimpressive conduct. ^an5-218-1-4

There are these five benefits of impressive conduct. ^an5-218-2-1

What five? ^an5-218-2-2

You inspire confidence in those without it. You increase confidence in those who have it. You follow the Teacher’s instructions. Those who come after you follow your example. And your mind becomes clear. ^an5-218-2-3

These are the five benefits of impressive conduct.” ^an5-218-2-4

### Fire (Numbered Discourses 5.219) ^an5-219-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of a fire. ^an5-219-1-1

What five? ^an5-219-1-2

It’s bad for your eyes. It’s bad for your complexion. It makes you weak. It draws in groups. And it encourages low talk. ^an5-219-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of a fire.” ^an5-219-1-4

### About Madhurā (Numbered Discourses 5.220) ^an5-220-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of Madhurā. ^an5-220-1-1

What five? ^an5-220-1-2

The ground is uneven and dusty, the hounds are fierce, the native spirits are vicious, and it’s hard to get almsfood. ^an5-220-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of Madhurā.” ^an5-220-1-4

### Long Wandering (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.221) ^an5-221-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for someone who likes long and aimless wandering. ^an5-221-1-1

What five? ^an5-221-1-2

You don’t learn new things. You don’t clarify what you’ve learned. You lack confidence in some things you have learned. You contract a severe illness. You don’t have any friends. ^an5-221-1-3

These are the five drawbacks for someone who likes long and aimless wandering. ^an5-221-1-4

There are these five benefits of a reasonable amount of wandering. ^an5-221-2-1

What five? ^an5-221-2-2

You learn new things. You clarify what you’ve learned. You have confidence in some things you have learned. You don’t contract severe illness. You have friends. ^an5-221-2-3

These are the five benefits of a reasonable amount of wandering.” ^an5-221-2-4

### Long Wandering (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.222) ^an5-222-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for someone who likes long and aimless wandering. ^an5-222-1-1

What five? ^an5-222-1-2

You don’t achieve the unachieved. What you have achieved falls away. You lose confidence in some things you’ve achieved. You contract a severe illness. You don’t have any friends. ^an5-222-1-3

These are the five drawbacks for someone who likes long and aimless wandering. ^an5-222-1-4

There are these five benefits of a reasonable amount of wandering. ^an5-222-2-1

What five? ^an5-222-2-2

You achieve the unachieved. What you have achieved doesn’t fall away. You’re confident in some things you’ve achieved. You don’t contract severe illness. You have friends. ^an5-222-2-3

These are the five benefits of a reasonable amount of wandering.” ^an5-222-2-4

### Overstaying (Numbered Discourses 5.223) ^an5-223-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of overstaying. ^an5-223-1-1

What five? ^an5-223-1-2

You have a lot of stuff and store it up. You have a lot of medicine and store it up. You have a lot of duties and responsibilities, and become capable in whatever needs to be done. You mix closely with laypeople and renunciates, socializing inappropriately like a layperson. And when you leave that monastery, you miss it. ^an5-223-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of overstaying. ^an5-223-1-4

There are these five benefits of staying for a reasonable length of time. ^an5-223-2-1

What five? ^an5-223-2-2

You don’t have a lot of stuff and store it up. You don’t have a lot of medicine and store it up. You don’t have a lot of duties and responsibilities, or become capable in whatever needs to be done. You don’t mix closely with laypeople and renunciates, socializing inappropriately like a layperson. And when you leave that monastery, you don’t miss it. ^an5-223-2-3

These are the five benefits of staying for a reasonable length of time.” ^an5-223-2-4

### Stingy (Numbered Discourses 5.224) ^an5-224-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of overstaying. ^an5-224-1-1

What five? ^an5-224-1-2

You become stingy with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teaching. ^an5-224-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of overstaying. ^an5-224-1-4

There are these five benefits of staying for a reasonable length of time. ^an5-224-2-1

What five? ^an5-224-2-2

You’re not stingy with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teaching. ^an5-224-2-3

These are the five benefits of staying for a reasonable length of time.” ^an5-224-2-4

### Visiting Families (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.225) ^an5-225-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of visiting families. ^an5-225-1-1

What five? ^an5-225-1-2

You fall into an offense for wandering without leave. You fall into an offense for sitting in a private place with someone of the opposite sex. You fall into an offense for sitting in a hidden place with someone of the opposite sex. You fall into an offense for teaching more than five or six sentences to someone of the opposite sex. You have a lot of sensual thoughts. ^an5-225-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of visiting families.” ^an5-225-1-4

### Visiting Families (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.226) ^an5-226-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for a mendicant who visits families for too long, mixing closely with them. ^an5-226-1-1

What five? ^an5-226-1-2

You often see members of the opposite sex. ^an5-226-1-3

Seeing them, you become close. ^an5-226-1-4

Being so close, you become intimate. ^an5-226-1-5

Being intimate, lust overcomes you. ^an5-226-1-6

When your mind is swamped by lust, you can expect that you will lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, or commit one of the corrupt offenses, or resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an5-226-1-7

These are the five drawbacks for a mendicant who visits families for too long, mixing closely with them.” ^an5-226-1-8

### Riches (Numbered Discourses 5.227) ^an5-227-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of riches. ^an5-227-1-1

What five? ^an5-227-1-2

Fire, water, kings, thieves, and unloved heirs all take a share. ^an5-227-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of riches. ^an5-227-1-4

There are these five benefits of riches. ^an5-227-2-1

What five? ^an5-227-2-2

Riches enable you to make yourself happy and pleased, to make your mother and father; your children, partners, bondservants, workers, and staff, and your friends and colleagues happy and pleased, and to keep them all happy. And they enable you to establish an uplifting religious donation for ascetics and brahmins that’s conducive to heaven, ripens in happiness, and leads to heaven. ^an5-227-2-3

These are the five benefits of riches.” ^an5-227-2-4

### Eating Late (Numbered Discourses 5.228) ^an5-228-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks for a family who takes their meals late in the day. ^an5-228-1-1

What five? ^an5-228-1-2

When guests visit, they are not served on time. ^an5-228-1-3

The deities who accept spirit-offerings are not served on time. ^an5-228-1-4

Ascetics and brahmins who eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night, and from food at the wrong time are not served on time. ^an5-228-1-5

Bondservants, workers, and staff do their duties neglectfully. ^an5-228-1-6

A meal eaten during the wrong period is not nutritious. ^an5-228-1-7

These are the five drawbacks for a family who takes their meals late in the day. ^an5-228-1-8

There are these five benefits for a family who takes their meals at a proper time. ^an5-228-2-1

What five? ^an5-228-2-2

When guests visit, they are served on time. ^an5-228-2-3

The deities who accept spirit-offerings are served on time. ^an5-228-2-4

Ascetics and brahmins who eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night, and from food at the wrong time are served on time. ^an5-228-2-5

Bondservants, workers, and staff do their duties attentively. ^an5-228-2-6

A meal eaten during the proper period is nutritious. ^an5-228-2-7

These are the five benefits for a family who takes their meals at a proper time.” ^an5-228-2-8

### Black Snakes (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.229) ^an5-229-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of a black snake. ^an5-229-1-1

What five? ^an5-229-1-2

It’s filthy, stinking, cowardly, frightening, and treacherous. ^an5-229-1-3

These are the five dangers of a black snake. ^an5-229-1-4

In the same way there are these five drawbacks of a lady. ^an5-229-2-1

What five? ^an5-229-2-2

She’s filthy, stinking, cowardly, frightening, and treacherous. ^an5-229-2-3

These are the five drawbacks of a lady.” ^an5-229-2-4

### Black Snakes (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.230) ^an5-230-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of a black snake. ^an5-230-1-1

What five? ^an5-230-1-2

It’s irritable, acrimonious, venomous, fork-tongued, and treacherous. ^an5-230-1-3

These are the five dangers of a black snake. ^an5-230-1-4

In the same way there are these five drawbacks of a lady. ^an5-230-2-1

What five? ^an5-230-2-2

She’s irritable, acrimonious, venomous, fork-tongued, and treacherous. ^an5-230-2-3

This is a lady’s venom: ^an5-230-2-4

usually she’s very lustful. ^an5-230-2-5

This is a lady’s forked tongue: ^an5-230-2-6

usually she backbites. ^an5-230-2-7

This is a lady’s treachery: ^an5-230-2-8

usually she’s an adulteress. ^an5-230-2-9

These are the five drawbacks of a lady.” ^an5-230-2-10

### A Resident Mendicant (Numbered Discourses 5.231) ^an5-231-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is not admirable. ^an5-231-1-1

What five? ^an5-231-1-2

They’re not accomplished in being well-presented and doing their duties. ^an5-231-1-3

They’re not very learned and don’t remember what they’ve learned. ^an5-231-1-4

They’re not self-effacing and don’t enjoy self-effacement. ^an5-231-1-5

They’re not a good speaker and do not enunciate well. ^an5-231-1-6

They’re witless, dull, and idiotic. ^an5-231-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is not admirable. ^an5-231-1-8

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is admirable. ^an5-231-2-1

What five? ^an5-231-2-2

They’re accomplished in being well-presented and doing their duties. ^an5-231-2-3

They’re very learned and remember what they’ve learned. ^an5-231-2-4

They’re self-effacing and enjoy self-effacement. ^an5-231-2-5

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. ^an5-231-2-6

They’re wise, bright, and clever. ^an5-231-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is admirable.” ^an5-231-2-8

### Liked (Numbered Discourses 5.232) ^an5-232-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an5-232-1-1

What five? ^an5-232-1-2

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-232-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-232-1-4

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well, with a polished, clear, and articulate voice that expresses the meaning. ^an5-232-1-5

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-232-1-6

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an5-232-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is dear and beloved to their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an5-232-1-8

### Beautification (Numbered Discourses 5.233) ^an5-233-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities graces the monastery. ^an5-233-1-1

What five? ^an5-233-1-2

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-233-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-233-1-4

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well, with a polished, clear, and articulate voice that expresses the meaning. ^an5-233-1-5

They’re able to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire those who approach them with a Dhamma talk. ^an5-233-1-6

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-233-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities graces the monastery.” ^an5-233-1-8

### Very Helpful (Numbered Discourses 5.234) ^an5-234-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is very helpful to the monastery. ^an5-234-1-1

What five? ^an5-234-1-2

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an5-234-1-3

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an5-234-1-4

They repair what is decayed and damaged. ^an5-234-1-5

When a large mendicant Saṅgha is arriving with mendicants from abroad, they go to the lay people and announce: ^an5-234-1-6

‘A large mendicant Saṅgha is arriving with mendicants from abroad. Make merit! Now is the time to make merit!’ ^an5-234-1-7

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an5-234-1-8

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is very helpful to the monastery.” ^an5-234-1-9

### A Sympathetic Mendicant (Numbered Discourses 5.235) ^an5-235-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities shows sympathy to the lay people. ^an5-235-1-1

What five? ^an5-235-1-2

They encourage them in higher ethics. ^an5-235-1-3

They equip them to see the truth of the teachings. ^an5-235-1-4

When they are sick, they go to them and prompt their mindfulness, saying: ^an5-235-1-5

‘Establish your mindfulness, good fellows, in what is worthy.’ ^an5-235-1-6

When a large mendicant Saṅgha is arriving with mendicants from abroad, they go to the lay people and announce: ^an5-235-1-7

‘A large mendicant Saṅgha is arriving with mendicants from abroad. Make merit! Now is the time to make merit!’ ^an5-235-1-8

And they eat whatever food they give them, coarse or fine, not wasting a gift given in faith. ^an5-235-1-9

A resident mendicant with these five qualities shows sympathy to the lay people.” ^an5-235-1-10

### Deserving Criticism (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.236) ^an5-236-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-236-1-1

What five? ^an5-236-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism, ^an5-236-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an5-236-1-4

Without examining or scrutinizing, they arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an5-236-1-5

and they don’t arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an5-236-1-6

And they waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-236-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-236-1-8

A resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-236-2-1

What five? ^an5-236-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism, ^an5-236-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an5-236-2-4

They don’t arouse faith in things that are dubious, ^an5-236-2-5

and they do arouse faith in things that are inspiring. ^an5-236-2-6

And they don’t waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-236-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-236-2-8

### Deserving Criticism (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.237) ^an5-237-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-237-1-1

What five? ^an5-237-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism, ^an5-237-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an5-237-1-4

They’re stingy and avaricious regarding monasteries. ^an5-237-1-5

They’re stingy and avaricious regarding families. ^an5-237-1-6

And they waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-237-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-237-1-8

A resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-237-2-1

What five? ^an5-237-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism, ^an5-237-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an5-237-2-4

They’re not stingy and avaricious regarding monasteries. ^an5-237-2-5

They’re not stingy and avaricious regarding families. ^an5-237-2-6

And they don’t waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-237-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-237-2-8

### Deserving Criticism (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 5.238) ^an5-238-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-238-1-1

What five? ^an5-238-1-2

Without examining or scrutinizing, they praise those deserving of criticism, ^an5-238-1-3

and they criticize those deserving of praise. ^an5-238-1-4

They’re stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-238-1-5

families, ^an5-238-1-6

and material things. ^an5-238-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-238-1-8

A resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-238-2-1

What five? ^an5-238-2-2

After examining and scrutinizing, they criticize those deserving of criticism, ^an5-238-2-3

and they praise those deserving of praise. ^an5-238-2-4

They’re not stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-238-2-5

families, ^an5-238-2-6

and material things. ^an5-238-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-238-2-8

### Stinginess (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.239) ^an5-239-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-239-1-1

What five? ^an5-239-1-2

They’re stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-239-1-3

families, ^an5-239-1-4

material things, ^an5-239-1-5

and praise. ^an5-239-1-6

And they waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-239-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-239-1-8

A resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-239-2-1

What five? ^an5-239-2-2

They’re not stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-239-2-3

families, ^an5-239-2-4

material things, ^an5-239-2-5

and praise. ^an5-239-2-6

And they don’t waste a gift given in faith. ^an5-239-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-239-2-8

### Stinginess (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.240) ^an5-240-0

“Mendicants, a resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-240-1-1

What five? ^an5-240-1-2

They’re stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-240-1-3

families, ^an5-240-1-4

material things, ^an5-240-1-5

praise, ^an5-240-1-6

and the teachings. ^an5-240-1-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-240-1-8

A resident mendicant with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-240-2-1

What five? ^an5-240-2-2

They’re not stingy regarding monasteries, ^an5-240-2-3

families, ^an5-240-2-4

material things, ^an5-240-2-5

praise, ^an5-240-2-6

and the teachings. ^an5-240-2-7

A resident mendicant with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-240-2-8

### Bad Conduct (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.241) ^an5-241-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of bad conduct. ^an5-241-1-1

What five? ^an5-241-1-2

You blame yourself. ^an5-241-1-3

After examination, sensible people criticize you. ^an5-241-1-4

You get a bad reputation. ^an5-241-1-5

You feel lost when you die. ^an5-241-1-6

And when your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an5-241-1-7

These are the five drawbacks of bad conduct. ^an5-241-1-8

There are these five benefits of good conduct. ^an5-241-2-1

What five? ^an5-241-2-2

You don’t blame yourself. ^an5-241-2-3

After examination, sensible people praise you. ^an5-241-2-4

You get a good reputation. ^an5-241-2-5

You don’t feel lost when you die. ^an5-241-2-6

When your body breaks up, after death, you’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an5-241-2-7

These are the five benefits of good conduct.” ^an5-241-2-8

### Bad Bodily Conduct (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.242) ^an5-242-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad bodily conduct … benefits in good bodily conduct …” ^an5-242-1-1

### Bad Verbal Conduct (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.243) ^an5-243-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad verbal conduct … benefits in good verbal conduct …” ^an5-243-1-1

### Bad Mental Conduct (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5.244) ^an5-244-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad mental conduct … benefits in good mental conduct …” ^an5-244-1-1

### Bad Conduct (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.245) ^an5-245-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of bad conduct. ^an5-245-1-1

What five? ^an5-245-1-2

You blame yourself. ^an5-245-1-3

After examination, sensible people criticize you. ^an5-245-1-4

You get a bad reputation. ^an5-245-1-5

You drift away from true teachings. ^an5-245-1-6

You settle on untrue teachings. ^an5-245-1-7

These are the five drawbacks of bad conduct. ^an5-245-1-8

There are these five benefits of good conduct. ^an5-245-2-1

What five? ^an5-245-2-2

You don’t blame yourself. ^an5-245-2-3

After examination, sensible people praise you. ^an5-245-2-4

You get a good reputation. ^an5-245-2-5

You drift away from untrue teachings. ^an5-245-2-6

You settle on true teachings. ^an5-245-2-7

These are the five benefits of good conduct.” ^an5-245-2-8

### Bad Bodily Conduct (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.246) ^an5-246-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad bodily conduct … benefits in good bodily conduct …” ^an5-246-1-1

### Bad Verbal Conduct (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.247) ^an5-247-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad verbal conduct … benefits in good verbal conduct …” ^an5-247-1-1

### Bad Mental Conduct (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5.248) ^an5-248-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks in bad mental conduct … benefits in good mental conduct …” ^an5-248-1-1

### A Charnel Ground (Numbered Discourses 5.249) ^an5-249-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks to a charnel ground. ^an5-249-1-1

What five? ^an5-249-1-2

It’s filthy, stinking, frightening, a gathering place for savage monsters, and a weeping place for many people. ^an5-249-1-3

These are the five drawbacks of a charnel ground. ^an5-249-1-4

In the same way there are five drawbacks of an individual like a charnel ground. ^an5-249-2-1

What five? ^an5-249-2-2

To start with, an individual has filthy conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an5-249-2-3

This is how they’re filthy, I say. ^an5-249-2-6

That individual is just as filthy as a charnel ground. ^an5-249-2-7

Because of their filthy conduct, they get a bad reputation. ^an5-249-3-1

This is how they’re stinky, I say. ^an5-249-3-2

That individual is just as stinky as a charnel ground. ^an5-249-3-3

Because of their filthy conduct, good-hearted spiritual companions avoid them from afar. ^an5-249-4-1

That’s how they’re frightening, I say. ^an5-249-4-2

That individual is just as frightening as a charnel ground. ^an5-249-4-3

Because of their filthy conduct, they live together with individuals of a similar character. ^an5-249-5-1

This is how they gather with savage monsters, I say. ^an5-249-5-2

That individual is just as much a gathering place of savage monsters as a charnel ground. ^an5-249-5-3

Because of their filthy conduct, when good-hearted spiritual companions see them they complain: ^an5-249-6-1

‘Oh, it’s so painful for us to have to live together with such as these.’ ^an5-249-6-2

This is how there’s weeping, I say. ^an5-249-6-3

This person is just as much a weeping place for many people as a charnel ground. ^an5-249-6-4

These are the five drawbacks of an individual like a charnel ground.” ^an5-249-6-6

### Faith in Individuals (Numbered Discourses 5.250) ^an5-250-0

“Mendicants, there are these five drawbacks of placing faith in an individual. ^an5-250-1-1

What five? ^an5-250-1-2

The individual to whom an individual is devoted falls into an offense such that the Saṅgha suspends them. ^an5-250-1-3

It occurs to them: ^an5-250-1-4

‘This individual dear and beloved to me has been suspended by the Saṅgha.’ ^an5-250-1-5

They lose much of their faith in mendicants. ^an5-250-1-6

So they don’t frequent other mendicants, ^an5-250-1-7

they don’t hear the true teaching, ^an5-250-1-8

and they fall away from the true teaching. ^an5-250-1-9

This is the first drawback in placing faith in an individual. ^an5-250-1-10

Furthermore, the individual to whom an individual is devoted falls into an offense such that the Saṅgha makes them sit at the end of the line. … ^an5-250-2-1

This is the second drawback in placing faith in an individual. ^an5-250-2-8

Furthermore, the individual to whom an individual is devoted leaves for another region … disrobes … passes away. ^an5-250-3-1

It occurs to them: ^an5-250-3-2

‘This individual dear and beloved to me has passed away.’ ^an5-250-3-3

So they don’t frequent other mendicants, ^an5-250-3-4

they don’t hear the true teaching, ^an5-250-3-5

and they fall away from the true teaching. ^an5-250-3-6

This is the fifth drawback in placing faith in an individual. ^an5-250-3-7

These are the five drawbacks of placing faith in an individual.” ^an5-250-3-8

### Who Should Give Ordination (Numbered Discourses 5.251) ^an5-251-0

“Mendicants, ordination should be given by a mendicant with five qualities. ^an5-251-1-1

What five? ^an5-251-1-2

It’s a mendicant who has the entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, ^an5-251-1-3

immersion, ^an5-251-1-4

wisdom, ^an5-251-1-5

freedom, ^an5-251-1-6

and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-251-1-7

Ordination should be given by a mendicant with these five qualities.” ^an5-251-1-8

### Who Should Give Dependence (Numbered Discourses 5.252) ^an5-252-0

“Mendicants, dependence should be given by a mendicant with five qualities. ^an5-252-1-1

What five? ^an5-252-1-2

It’s a mendicant who has the entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-252-1-3

Dependence should be given by a mendicant with these five qualities.” ^an5-252-1-4

### Who Should Have a Novice as Attendant (Numbered Discourses 5.253) ^an5-253-0

“Mendicants, a novice should attend on a mendicant with five qualities. ^an5-253-1-1

What five? ^an5-253-1-2

It’s a mendicant who has the entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an5-253-1-3

A novice should attend on a mendicant with these five qualities.” ^an5-253-1-4

### Five Kinds of Stinginess (Numbered Discourses 5.254) ^an5-254-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of stinginess. ^an5-254-1-1

What five? ^an5-254-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-254-1-3

These are the five kinds of stinginess. ^an5-254-1-4

The most contemptible of these five kinds of stinginess is stinginess with the teaching.” ^an5-254-1-5

### Giving Up Stinginess (Numbered Discourses 5.255) ^an5-255-0

“Mendicants, the spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these five kinds of stinginess. ^an5-255-1-1

What five? ^an5-255-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-255-1-3

The spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these five kinds of stinginess.” ^an5-255-1-5

### The First Absorption (Numbered Discourses 5.256) ^an5-256-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-256-1-1

What five? ^an5-256-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-256-1-3

Without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-256-1-4

But after giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-256-2-1

What five? ^an5-256-2-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-256-2-3

After giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption.” ^an5-256-2-4

### The Second Absorption, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 5.257–263) ^an5-257-263-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … or realize the fruit of stream-entry … the fruit of once-return … the fruit of non-return … perfection. ^an5-257-263-1-1

What five? ^an5-257-263-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-257-263-1-3

Without giving up these five qualities you can’t realize perfection. ^an5-257-263-1-4

But after giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … and realize the fruit of stream-entry … the fruit of once-return … the fruit of non-return … perfection. ^an5-257-263-2-1

What five? ^an5-257-263-2-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teachings. ^an5-257-263-2-3

After giving up these five qualities you can realize perfection.” ^an5-257-263-2-4

### Another Discourse on the First Absorption (Numbered Discourses 5.264) ^an5-264-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-264-1-1

What five? ^an5-264-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and lack of gratitude and thankfulness. ^an5-264-1-3

Without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-264-1-4

But after giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an5-264-2-1

What five? ^an5-264-2-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and lack of gratitude and thankfulness. ^an5-264-2-3

After giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption.” ^an5-264-2-4

### Another Discourse on the Second Absorption, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 5.265–271) ^an5-265-271-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these five qualities you can’t enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … or realize the fruit of stream-entry … the fruit of once-return … the fruit of non-return … perfection. ^an5-265-271-1-1

What five? ^an5-265-271-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and lack of gratitude and thankfulness. ^an5-265-271-1-3

Without giving up these five qualities you can’t realize perfection. ^an5-265-271-1-4

But after giving up these five qualities you can enter and remain in the second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption … and realize the fruit of stream-entry … the fruit of once-return … the fruit of non-return … perfection. ^an5-265-271-2-1

What five? ^an5-265-271-2-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and lack of gratitude and thankfulness. ^an5-265-271-2-3

After giving up these five qualities you can realize perfection.” ^an5-265-271-2-4

### A Meal Assigner (Numbered Discourses 5.272) ^an5-272-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities should not be appointed as meal assigner. ^an5-272-1-1

What five? ^an5-272-1-2

They make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. And they don’t know if a meal has been assigned or not. ^an5-272-1-3

Someone with these five qualities should not be appointed as meal assigner. ^an5-272-1-4

Someone with five qualities should be appointed as meal assigner. ^an5-272-2-1

What five? ^an5-272-2-2

They don’t make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. And they know if a meal has been assigned or not. ^an5-272-2-3

Someone with these five qualities should be appointed as meal assigner. ^an5-272-2-4

Someone with five qualities who has been appointed as meal assigner should not be called upon … should be called upon … should be known as a fool … should be known as astute … they keep themselves broken and damaged … they keep themselves unbroken and undamaged … is placed in hell as if delivered there … is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-272-3-1

What five? ^an5-272-3-2

They don’t make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. And they know if a meal has been assigned or not. ^an5-272-3-3

A meal assigner with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-272-3-4

### A Lodgings Allotter (Numbered Discourses 5.273–285) ^an5-273-285-0

“Mendicants, someone with five qualities should not be appointed as lodgings allotter … they don’t know if a lodging has been allotted or not … Someone with five qualities should be appointed as lodgings allotter … they know if a lodging has been allotted or not … ^an5-273-285-1-1

A person should not be appointed as lodgings allocator … they don’t know if a lodging has been allocated or not … A person should be appointed as lodgings allocator … they know if a lodging has been allocated or not … ^an5-273-285-2-1

A person should not be appointed as storeperson … they don’t know if stores are protected or not … A person should be appointed as storeperson … they know if stores are protected or not … ^an5-273-285-3-1

… robe receiver … ^an5-273-285-4-1

… robe distributor … ^an5-273-285-5-1

… porridge distributor … ^an5-273-285-6-1

… fruit distributor … ^an5-273-285-7-1

… cake distributor … ^an5-273-285-8-1

… dispenser of minor accessories … ^an5-273-285-9-1

… allocator of bathing cloths … ^an5-273-285-10-1

… bowl allocator … ^an5-273-285-11-1

… supervisor of monastery staff … ^an5-273-285-12-1

… supervisor of novices … ^an5-273-285-13-1

What five? ^an5-273-285-14-2

They don’t make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice. And they know if a novice has been supervised or not. ^an5-273-285-14-3

A supervisor of novices with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-273-285-14-4

### A Monk (Numbered Discourses 5.286) ^an5-286-0

“Mendicants, a monk with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-286-1-1

What five? ^an5-286-1-2

He kills living creatures, steals, has sex, lies, and consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-286-1-3

A monk with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-286-1-4

A monk with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-286-2-1
