What five? ^an5-286-2-2

He doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, have sex, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-286-2-3

A monk with these five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-286-2-4

### A Nun (Numbered Discourses 5.287–292) ^an5-287-292-0

“A nun … trainee nun … novice monk … novice nun … layman … laywoman … with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-287-292-1-1

What five? ^an5-287-292-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-287-292-1-3

With these five qualities they’re placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-287-292-1-4

A nun … trainee nun … novice monk … novice nun … layman … laywoman … with five qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an5-287-292-2-1

What five? ^an5-287-292-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-287-292-2-3

With these five qualities they’re placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an5-287-292-2-4

### An Ājīvaka (Numbered Discourses 5.293) ^an5-293-0

“Mendicants, an Ājīvaka ascetic with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-293-1-1

What five? ^an5-293-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, have sex, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-293-1-3

An Ājīvaka ascetic with these five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there.” ^an5-293-1-4

### A Jain Ascetic, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 5.294–302) ^an5-294-302-0

“A Jain ascetic … disciple of the shavelings … a matted-hair ascetic … a wanderer … a follower of Māgaṇḍiya … a trident-bearing ascetic … a follower of the unobstructed … a follower of Gotama … a performer of rituals for the gods … with five qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an5-294-302-1-1

What five? ^an5-294-302-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, and consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an5-294-302-1-3

With these five qualities they’re placed in hell as if delivered there.” ^an5-294-302-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (1st) (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-303-0

“For insight into greed, five things should be developed. ^an5-303-1-1

What five? ^an5-303-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, drawbacks, repulsiveness of food, and dissatisfaction with the whole world. ^an5-303-1-3

For insight into greed, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-303-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-304-0

“For insight into greed, five things should be developed. ^an5-304-1-1

What five? ^an5-304-1-2

The perceptions of impermanence, not-self, death, repulsiveness of food, and dissatisfaction with the whole world. ^an5-304-1-3

For insight into greed, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-304-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-305-0

“For insight into greed, five things should be developed. ^an5-305-1-1

What five? ^an5-305-1-2

The perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of giving up, and the perception of fading away. ^an5-305-1-3

For insight into greed, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-305-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (4th) (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-306-0

“For insight into greed, five things should be developed. ^an5-306-1-1

What five? ^an5-306-1-2

The faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-306-1-3

For insight into greed, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-306-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (5th) (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-307-0

“For insight into greed, five things should be developed. ^an5-307-1-1

What five? ^an5-307-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-307-1-3

For insight into greed, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-307-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 5) ^an5-308-1152-0

“For the complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go of greed, five things should be developed.” ^an5-308-1152-1-1

“Of hate … delusion … anger … acrimony … disdain … contempt … jealousy … stinginess … deceit … deviousness … obstinacy … aggression … conceit … arrogance … vanity … negligence … for insight … complete understanding … finishing … giving up … ending … vanishing … fading away … cessation … giving away … letting go … five things should be developed. ^an5-308-1152-1-2

What five? ^an5-308-1152-2-1

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an5-308-1152-2-2

For the letting go of negligence, these five things should be developed.” ^an5-308-1152-2-3

The Book of the Fives is finished. ^an5-308-1152-4-13

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 6 ^an6-0

### Worthy of Offerings (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.1) ^an6-1-0

So I have heard. ^an6-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an6-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an6-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an6-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an6-1-1-6

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-1-2-1

What six? ^an6-1-2-2

It’s a mendicant who, when they see a sight with their eyes, is neither happy nor sad. They remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an6-1-2-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an6-1-2-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an6-1-2-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an6-1-2-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an6-1-2-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they’re neither happy nor sad. They remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an6-1-2-8

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-1-2-9

That is what the Buddha said. ^an6-1-3-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an6-1-3-2

### Worthy of Offerings (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.2) ^an6-2-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-2-1-1

What six? ^an6-2-1-2

It’s a mendicant who wields the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. They control the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an6-2-1-3

With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, they hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far. ^an6-2-2-1

They understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with their own mind. ^an6-2-3-1

They understand mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’, ^an6-2-3-2

and mind without greed as ‘mind without greed’. ^an6-2-3-3

They understand mind with hate … ^an6-2-3-4

mind without hate … ^an6-2-3-5

mind with delusion … ^an6-2-3-6

mind without delusion … ^an6-2-3-7

constricted mind … ^an6-2-3-8

scattered mind … ^an6-2-3-9

expansive mind … ^an6-2-3-10

unexpansive mind … ^an6-2-3-11

mind that is not supreme … ^an6-2-3-12

mind that is supreme … ^an6-2-3-13

mind immersed in samādhi … ^an6-2-3-14

mind not immersed in samādhi … ^an6-2-3-15

freed mind … ^an6-2-3-16

They understand unfreed mind as ‘unfreed mind’. ^an6-2-3-17

They recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so they recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an6-2-4-1

With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds: ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an6-2-5-1

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an6-2-6-1

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-2-7-1

### Faculties (Numbered Discourses 6.3) ^an6-3-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-3-1-1

What six? ^an6-3-1-2

The faculties of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. And they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an6-3-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-3-1-4

### Powers (Numbered Discourses 6.4) ^an6-4-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-4-1-1

What six? ^an6-4-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. And they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an6-4-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-4-1-4

### The Thoroughbred (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.5) ^an6-5-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-5-1-1

What six? ^an6-5-2-1

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. And it’s beautiful. ^an6-5-2-2

A fine royal thoroughbred with these six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-5-2-3

In the same way, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-5-3-1

What six? ^an6-5-3-2

It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^an6-5-3-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-5-3-4

### The Thoroughbred (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.6) ^an6-6-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-6-1-1

What six? ^an6-6-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. And it’s strong. ^an6-6-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-6-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-6-2-1

What six? ^an6-6-2-2

It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^an6-6-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-6-2-4

### The Thoroughbred (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 6.7) ^an6-7-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-7-1-1

What six? ^an6-7-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. And it’s fast. ^an6-7-1-3

A fine royal thoroughbred with these six factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an6-7-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-7-2-1

What six? ^an6-7-2-2

It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^an6-7-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-7-2-4

### Unsurpassable (Numbered Discourses 6.8) ^an6-8-0

“Mendicants, these six things are unsurpassable. ^an6-8-1-1

What six? ^an6-8-1-2

The unsurpassable seeing, listening, acquisition, training, service, and recollection. ^an6-8-1-3

These are the six unsurpassable things.” ^an6-8-1-4

### Topics for Recollection (Numbered Discourses 6.9) ^an6-9-0

“Mendicants, there are these six topics for recollection. ^an6-9-1-1

What six? ^an6-9-1-2

The recollection of the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, ethics, generosity, and the deities. ^an6-9-1-3

These are the six topics for recollection.” ^an6-9-1-4

### With Mahānāma (Numbered Discourses 6.10) ^an6-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an6-10-1-1

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-10-1-2

“Sir, when a noble disciple has reached the fruit and understood the instructions, what kind of meditation do they frequently practice?” ^an6-10-1-3

“Mahānāma, when a noble disciple has reached the fruit and understood the instructions they frequently practice this kind of meditation. ^an6-10-2-1

Firstly, a noble disciple recollects the Realized One: ^an6-10-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an6-10-2-3

When a noble disciple recollects the Realized One their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-10-2-4

At that time their mind is quite unswerving, based on the Realized One. ^an6-10-2-5

A noble disciple whose mind is unswerving finds inspiration in the meaning and the teaching, and finds joy connected with the teaching. ^an6-10-2-6

When they’re joyful, rapture springs up. When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. When the body is tranquil, they feel bliss. And when they’re blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an6-10-2-7

This is called ^an6-10-2-8

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of the Buddha. ^an6-10-2-9

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the teaching: ^an6-10-3-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an6-10-3-2

When a noble disciple recollects the teaching their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-10-3-3

This is called ^an6-10-3-7

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of the teaching. ^an6-10-3-8

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha: ^an6-10-4-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an6-10-4-2

When a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-10-4-3

This is called ^an6-10-4-7

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of the Saṅgha. ^an6-10-4-8

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own ethical conduct, which is intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an6-10-5-1

When a noble disciple recollects their ethical conduct their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-10-5-2

This is called ^an6-10-5-6

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of ethics. ^an6-10-5-7

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own generosity: ^an6-10-6-1

‘I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate! ^an6-10-6-2

Among people full of the stain of stinginess I live at home rid of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.’ ^an6-10-6-3

When a noble disciple recollects their own generosity their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-10-6-4

This is called ^an6-10-6-8

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of generosity. ^an6-10-6-9

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the deities: ^an6-10-7-1

‘There are the gods of the four great kings, the gods of the thirty-three, the gods of Yama, the joyful gods, the gods who love to create, the gods who control what is created by others, the gods of the Divinity’s host, and gods even higher than these. ^an6-10-7-2

When those deities passed away from here, they were reborn there because of their faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. I, too, have the same kind of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.’ ^an6-10-7-3

When a noble disciple recollects the faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom of both themselves and the deities their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-10-7-8

At that time their mind is quite unswerving, based on the deities. ^an6-10-7-9

A noble disciple whose mind is unswerving finds inspiration in the meaning and the teaching, and finds joy connected with the teaching. ^an6-10-7-10

When you’re joyful, rapture springs up. When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil. When the body is tranquil, you feel bliss. And when you’re blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi. ^an6-10-7-11

This is called ^an6-10-7-12

a noble disciple who lives in balance among people who are unbalanced, and lives untroubled among people who are troubled. They’ve entered the stream of the teaching and develop the recollection of the deities. ^an6-10-7-13

When a noble disciple has reached the fruit and understood the instructions this is the kind of meditation they frequently practice.” ^an6-10-8-1

### Warm-hearted (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.11) ^an6-11-0

“Mendicants, there are these six warm-hearted qualities. ^an6-11-1-1

What six? ^an6-11-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with bodily kindness, both in public and in private. This is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-11-1-3

Furthermore, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with verbal kindness, both in public and in private. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-11-2-1

Furthermore, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with mental kindness … ^an6-11-3-1

Furthermore, a mendicant shares without reservation any material things they have gained by legitimate means, even the food placed in the alms-bowl, using them in common with their ethical spiritual companions. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-11-4-1

Furthermore, a mendicant lives according to the precepts shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. Those precepts are intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-11-5-1

Furthermore, a mendicant lives according to the view shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. That view is noble and emancipating, and delivers one who practices it to the complete ending of suffering. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-11-6-1

These are the six warm-hearted qualities.” ^an6-11-7-1

### Warm-hearted (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.12) ^an6-12-0

“Mendicants, these six warm-hearted qualities make for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute. ^an6-12-1-1

What six? ^an6-12-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with bodily kindness, both in public and in private. ^an6-12-1-3

This warm-hearted quality makes for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute. ^an6-12-1-4

Furthermore, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with verbal kindness … ^an6-12-2-1

Furthermore, a mendicant consistently treats their spiritual companions with mental kindness … ^an6-12-3-1

Furthermore, a mendicant shares without reservation any material things they have gained by legitimate means, even the food placed in the alms-bowl, using them in common with their ethical spiritual companions. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-12-4-1

Furthermore, a mendicant lives according to the precepts shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. Those precepts are intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. This too is a warm-hearted quality. ^an6-12-5-1

Furthermore, a mendicant lives according to the view shared with their spiritual companions, both in public and in private. That view is noble and emancipating, and delivers one who practices it to the complete ending of suffering. ^an6-12-6-1

This warm-hearted quality makes for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute. ^an6-12-6-2

These six warm-hearted qualities make for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without quarreling.” ^an6-12-7-1

### Elements of Escape (Numbered Discourses 6.13) ^an6-13-0

“Mendicants, there are these six elements of escape. ^an6-13-1-1

What six? ^an6-13-1-2

Take a mendicant who says: ^an6-13-1-3

‘I’ve developed the heart’s release by love. I’ve cultivated it, made it my vehicle and my basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. ^an6-13-1-4

Yet somehow ill will still occupies my mind.’ ^an6-13-1-5

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! Don’t say that. Don’t misrepresent the Buddha, for misrepresentation of the Buddha is not good. And the Buddha would not say that. ^an6-13-1-6

It’s impossible, reverend, it cannot happen that the heart’s release by love has been developed and properly implemented, ^an6-13-1-7

yet somehow ill will still occupies the mind. ^an6-13-1-8

For it is the heart’s release by love that is the escape from ill will.’ ^an6-13-1-9

Take another mendicant who says: ^an6-13-2-1

‘I’ve developed the heart’s release by compassion. I’ve cultivated it, made it my vehicle and my basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. ^an6-13-2-2

Yet somehow the thought of harming still occupies my mind.’ ^an6-13-2-3

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! … ^an6-13-2-4

For it is the heart’s release by compassion that is the escape from thoughts of harming.’ ^an6-13-2-7

Take another mendicant who says: ^an6-13-3-1

‘I’ve developed the heart’s release by rejoicing. I’ve cultivated it, made it my vehicle and my basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. ^an6-13-3-2

Yet somehow discontent still occupies my mind.’ ^an6-13-3-3

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! … ^an6-13-3-4

For it is the heart’s release by rejoicing that is the escape from discontent.’ ^an6-13-3-7

Take another mendicant who says: ^an6-13-4-1

‘I’ve developed the heart’s release by equanimity. I’ve cultivated it, made it my vehicle and my basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. ^an6-13-4-2

Yet somehow desire still occupies my mind.’ ^an6-13-4-3

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! … ^an6-13-4-4

For it is the heart’s release by equanimity that is the escape from desire.’ ^an6-13-4-7

Take another mendicant who says: ^an6-13-5-1

‘I’ve developed the signless release of the heart. I’ve cultivated it, made it my vehicle and my basis, kept it up, consolidated it, and properly implemented it. ^an6-13-5-2

Yet somehow my consciousness still follows after signs.’ ^an6-13-5-3

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! … ^an6-13-5-4

For it is the signless release of the heart that is the escape from all signs.’ ^an6-13-5-7

Take another mendicant who says: ^an6-13-6-1

‘I’m rid of the conceit “I am”. And I don’t regard anything as “I am this”. ^an6-13-6-2

Yet somehow the dart of doubt and indecision still occupies my mind.’ ^an6-13-6-3

They should be told, ‘Not so, venerable! Don’t say that. Don’t misrepresent the Buddha, for misrepresentation of the Buddha is not good. And the Buddha would not say that. ^an6-13-6-4

It’s impossible, reverend, it cannot happen that the conceit “I am” has been done away with, and nothing is regarded as “I am this”, ^an6-13-6-5

yet somehow the dart of doubt and indecision still occupies the mind. ^an6-13-6-6

For it is the uprooting of the conceit “I am” that is the escape from the dart of doubt and indecision.’ ^an6-13-6-7

These are the six elements of escape.” ^an6-13-7-1

### A Good Death (Numbered Discourses 6.14) ^an6-14-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an6-14-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an6-14-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an6-14-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an6-14-1-4

“A mendicant lives life so as to not have a good death. ^an6-14-2-1

And how do they live life so as to not have a good death? ^an6-14-2-2

Take a mendicant who relishes work, talk, sleep, company, closeness, and proliferation. They love these things and like to relish them. ^an6-14-3-1

A mendicant who lives life like this does not have a good death. ^an6-14-3-2

This is called ^an6-14-3-3

a mendicant who enjoys substantial reality, who hasn’t given up substantial reality to rightly make an end of suffering. ^an6-14-3-4

A mendicant lives life so as to have a good death. ^an6-14-4-1

And how do they live life so as to have a good death? ^an6-14-4-2

Take a mendicant who doesn’t relish work, talk, sleep, company, closeness, and proliferation. They don’t love these things or like to relish them. ^an6-14-5-1

A mendicant who lives life like this has a good death. ^an6-14-5-2

This is called ^an6-14-5-3

a mendicant who delights in extinguishment, who has given up substantial reality to rightly make an end of suffering. ^an6-14-5-4

A beast who likes to proliferate, ^an6-14-6-1

enjoying proliferation, ^an6-14-6-2

fails to win extinguishment, ^an6-14-6-3

the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an6-14-6-4

But one who gives up proliferation, ^an6-14-7-1

enjoying the state of non-proliferation, ^an6-14-7-2

wins extinguishment, ^an6-14-7-3

the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.” ^an6-14-7-4

### Regret (Numbered Discourses 6.15) ^an6-15-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an6-15-1-1

“As a mendicant makes their bed, so they must lie in it, and die tormented by regrets. ^an6-15-1-2

And how do they die tormented by regrets? ^an6-15-1-3

Take a mendicant who relishes work, talk, sleep, company, closeness, and proliferation. They love these things and like to relish them. ^an6-15-2-1

A mendicant who makes their bed like this must lie in it, and die tormented by regrets. ^an6-15-2-6

This is called ^an6-15-2-7

a mendicant who enjoys substantial reality, who hasn’t given up substantial reality to rightly make an end of suffering. ^an6-15-2-8

As a mendicant makes their bed, so they must lie in it, and die free of regrets. ^an6-15-3-1

And how do they die free of regrets? ^an6-15-3-2

Take a mendicant who doesn’t relish work, talk, sleep, company, closeness, and proliferation. They don’t love these things or like to relish them. ^an6-15-4-1

A mendicant who makes their bed like this must lie in it, and die free of regrets. ^an6-15-4-6

This is called ^an6-15-4-7

a mendicant who delights in extinguishment, who has given up substantial reality to rightly make an end of suffering. ^an6-15-4-8

A beast who likes to proliferate, ^an6-15-5-1

enjoying proliferation, ^an6-15-5-2

fails to win extinguishment, ^an6-15-5-3

the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. ^an6-15-5-4

But one who gives up proliferation, ^an6-15-6-1

enjoying the state of non-proliferation, ^an6-15-6-2

wins extinguishment, ^an6-15-6-3

the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.” ^an6-15-6-4

### Nakula’s Father (Numbered Discourses 6.16) ^an6-16-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^an6-16-1-1

Now at that time the householder Nakula’s father was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an6-16-1-2

Then the housewife Nakula’s mother said to him: ^an6-16-1-3

“Householder, don’t pass away with concerns. ^an6-16-2-1

Such concern is suffering, ^an6-16-2-2

and it’s criticized by the Buddha. ^an6-16-2-3

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-2-4

‘When I’ve gone, the housewife Nakula’s mother won’t be able to provide for the children and keep up the household carpets.’ ^an6-16-2-5

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-2-6

I’m skilled at spinning cotton and carding wool. ^an6-16-2-7

I’m able to provide for the children and keep up the household carpets. ^an6-16-2-8

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns … ^an6-16-2-9

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-3-1

‘When I’ve gone, the housewife Nakula’s mother will take another husband.’ ^an6-16-3-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-3-3

Both you and I know that we have remained chaste while at home for the past sixteen years. ^an6-16-3-4

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns … ^an6-16-3-5

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-4-1

‘When I’ve gone, the housewife Nakula’s mother won’t want to see the Buddha and his Saṅgha of mendicants.’ ^an6-16-4-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-4-3

When you’ve gone, I’ll want to see the Buddha and his mendicant Saṅgha even more. ^an6-16-4-4

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns … ^an6-16-4-5

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-5-1

‘The housewife Nakula’s mother won’t fulfill ethics.’ ^an6-16-5-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-5-3

I am one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who fulfills their ethics. ^an6-16-5-4

For whoever doubts this, that Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha is staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. Let them approach him and ask. ^an6-16-5-5

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns … ^an6-16-5-6

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-6-1

‘The housewife Nakula’s mother doesn’t have internal serenity of heart.’ ^an6-16-6-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-6-3

I am one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who has internal serenity of heart. ^an6-16-6-4

For whoever doubts this, that Blessed One is staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. Let them approach him and ask. ^an6-16-6-5

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns … ^an6-16-6-6

Householder, you might think: ^an6-16-7-1

‘The housewife Nakula’s mother has not found a footing, a firm footing, and solace in this teaching and training. She has not gone beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and gained assurance. And she’s not independent of others in the Teacher’s instructions.’ ^an6-16-7-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an6-16-7-3

I am one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who has found a footing, a firm footing, and solace in this teaching and training. I have gone beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and gained assurance. And I am independent of others in the Teacher’s instructions. ^an6-16-7-4

For whoever doubts this, that Blessed One is staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. Let them approach him and ask. ^an6-16-7-5

So householder, don’t pass away with concerns. ^an6-16-7-6

Such concern is suffering, ^an6-16-7-7

and it’s criticized by the Buddha.” ^an6-16-7-8

And then, as Nakula’s mother was giving this advice to Nakula’s father, his illness died down on the spot. ^an6-16-8-1

And that’s how Nakula’s father recovered from that illness. ^an6-16-8-2

Soon after recovering, leaning on a staff he went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an6-16-8-4

“You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate, ^an6-16-9-1

to have the housewife Nakula’s mother advise and instruct you out of kindness and sympathy. ^an6-16-9-2

She is one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who fulfills their ethics. ^an6-16-9-3

She is one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who has internal serenity of heart. ^an6-16-9-4

She is one of those white-robed disciples of the Buddha who has found a footing, a firm footing, and solace in this teaching and training. She has gone beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and gained assurance. And she is independent of others in the Teacher’s instructions. ^an6-16-9-5

You’re fortunate, householder, so very fortunate, ^an6-16-9-6

to have the housewife Nakula’s mother advise and instruct you out of kindness and sympathy.” ^an6-16-9-7

### Sleep (Numbered Discourses 6.17) ^an6-17-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-17-1-1

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat, went to the assembly hall, and sat down on the seat spread out. ^an6-17-1-2

Venerable Sāriputta also came out of retreat, went to the assembly hall, bowed to the Buddha and sat down to one side. ^an6-17-1-3

Venerables Mahāmoggallāna, ^an6-17-1-4

Mahākassapa, ^an6-17-1-5

Mahākaccāna, ^an6-17-1-6

Mahākoṭṭhita, ^an6-17-1-7

Mahācunda, ^an6-17-1-8

Mahākappina, ^an6-17-1-9

Anuruddha, ^an6-17-1-10

Revata, ^an6-17-1-11

and Ānanda did the same. ^an6-17-1-12

The Buddha spent much of the night sitting in meditation, then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an6-17-1-13

And soon after the Buddha left those venerables each went to their own dwelling. ^an6-17-1-14

But those mendicants who were junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training slept until the sun came up, snoring. ^an6-17-1-15

The Buddha saw them doing this, with his clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman. ^an6-17-1-16

He went to the assembly hall, sat down on the seat spread out, ^an6-17-1-17

and addressed the mendicants: ^an6-17-1-18

“Mendicants, where is Sāriputta? ^an6-17-2-1

Where are Mahāmoggallāna, ^an6-17-2-2

Mahākassapa, ^an6-17-2-3

Mahākaccāna, ^an6-17-2-4

Mahākoṭṭhita, ^an6-17-2-5

Mahācunda, ^an6-17-2-6

Mahākappina, ^an6-17-2-7

Anuruddha, ^an6-17-2-8

Revata, ^an6-17-2-9

and Ānanda? ^an6-17-2-10

Where have these senior disciples gone?” ^an6-17-2-11

“Soon after the Buddha left those venerables each went to their own dwelling.” ^an6-17-2-12

“So, mendicants, when the senior mendicants left, why did you sleep until the sun came up, snoring? ^an6-17-2-13

What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-17-2-14

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-17-2-15

of an anointed aristocratic king who rules his whole life, dear and beloved to the country, while indulging in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing as much as he likes?” ^an6-17-2-16

“No, sir.” ^an6-17-2-17

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-17-2-18

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-17-2-19

What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-17-3-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-17-3-2

of an appointed official … ^an6-17-3-3

a hereditary official … ^an6-17-3-4

a general … ^an6-17-3-5

a village chief … ^an6-17-3-6

or a guild head who runs the guild his whole life, dear and beloved to the guild, while indulging in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing as much as he likes?” ^an6-17-3-7

“No, sir.” ^an6-17-3-8

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-17-3-9

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-17-3-10

What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-17-4-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-17-4-2

of an ascetic or brahmin who indulges in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing as much as they like? Their sense doors are unguarded, they eat too much, they’re not dedicated to wakefulness, they’re unable to discern skillful qualities, and they don’t pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn. Yet they realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^an6-17-4-3

“No, sir.” ^an6-17-4-4

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-17-4-5

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-17-4-6

So you should train like this: ^an6-17-5-1

‘We will guard our sense doors, eat in moderation, be dedicated to wakefulness, discern skillful qualities, and pursue the development of the qualities on the side of awakening in the evening and toward dawn.’ ^an6-17-5-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an6-17-5-3

### A Fish Dealer (Numbered Discourses 6.18) ^an6-18-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^an6-18-1-1

While walking along the road he saw a fish dealer in a certain spot selling fish that he had killed himself. ^an6-18-1-2

Seeing this he left the road, sat at the root of a tree on the seat spread out, ^an6-18-1-3

and addressed the mendicants, ^an6-18-1-4

“Mendicants, do you see that fish dealer selling fish that he killed himself?” ^an6-18-1-5

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-18-1-6

“What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-18-2-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-18-2-2

of a fish dealer selling fish that he killed himself who, by means of that work and livelihood, got to travel by elephant, horse, chariot, or vehicle, or to enjoy wealth, or to live off a large fortune?” ^an6-18-2-3

“No, sir.” ^an6-18-2-4

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-18-2-5

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-18-2-6

Why is that? ^an6-18-2-8

Because when the fish are led to the slaughter he regards them with bad intentions. ^an6-18-2-9

What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-18-3-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-18-3-2

of a butcher of cattle selling cattle that he killed himself who, by means of that work and livelihood, got to travel by elephant, horse, chariot, or vehicle, or to enjoy wealth, or to live off a large fortune?” ^an6-18-3-3

“No, sir.” ^an6-18-3-4

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-18-3-5

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-18-3-6

Why is that? ^an6-18-3-8

Because when the cattle are led to the slaughter he regards them with bad intentions. ^an6-18-3-9

What do you think, mendicants? ^an6-18-4-1

Have you ever seen or heard ^an6-18-4-2

of a butcher of sheep … ^an6-18-4-3

a butcher of pigs … ^an6-18-4-4

a butcher of poultry … ^an6-18-4-5

or a deer-hunter selling deer which he killed himself who, by means of that work and livelihood, got to travel by elephant, horse, chariot, or vehicle, or to enjoy wealth, or to live off a large fortune?” ^an6-18-4-6

“No, sir.” ^an6-18-4-7

“Good, mendicants! ^an6-18-4-8

I too have never seen or heard of such a thing. ^an6-18-4-9

Why is that? ^an6-18-4-11

Because when the deer are led to the slaughter he regards them with bad intentions. ^an6-18-4-12

By regarding even animals led to the slaughter with bad intentions he did not get to travel by elephant, horse, chariot, or vehicle, or to enjoy wealth, or to live off a large fortune. ^an6-18-4-13

How much worse is someone who regards human beings brought to the slaughter with bad intentions! ^an6-18-4-14

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an6-18-4-15

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.” ^an6-18-4-16

### Mindfulness of Death (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.19) ^an6-19-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^an6-19-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an6-19-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an6-19-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an6-19-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an6-19-1-5

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of death is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination. ^an6-19-1-6

But do you develop mindfulness of death?” ^an6-19-1-7

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-2-1

“Sir, I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-2-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-2-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-2-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live for another day and night, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-2-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-2-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-3-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-3-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-3-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-3-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live for another day, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-3-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-3-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-4-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-4-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-4-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-4-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to eat a meal of almsfood, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-4-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-4-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-5-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-5-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-5-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-5-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-5-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-5-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-6-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-6-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-6-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-6-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to chew and swallow a single mouthful, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-6-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-6-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an6-19-7-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-7-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an6-19-7-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an6-19-7-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to breathe out after breathing in, or to breathe in after breathing out, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an6-19-7-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an6-19-7-6

When this was said, the Buddha said to those mendicants: ^an6-19-8-1

“As to the mendicants who develop mindfulness of death by wishing ^an6-19-8-2

to live for a day and night … ^an6-19-8-3

or to live for a day … ^an6-19-9-2

or to live as long as it takes to eat a meal of almsfood … ^an6-19-10-2

or to live as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls— ^an6-19-11-2

these are called mendicants who live negligently. They slackly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements. ^an6-19-12-1

But as to the mendicants who develop mindfulness of death by wishing ^an6-19-13-1

to live as long as it takes to chew and swallow a single mouthful … ^an6-19-13-2

or to live as long as it takes to breathe out after breathing in, or to breathe in after breathing out— ^an6-19-14-2

these are called mendicants who live diligently. They keenly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements. ^an6-19-15-1

So you should train like this: ^an6-19-16-1

‘We will live diligently. We will keenly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements.’ ^an6-19-16-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an6-19-16-3

### Mindfulness of Death (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.20) ^an6-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^an6-20-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an6-20-1-2

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of death is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination. ^an6-20-1-3

And how is mindfulness of death developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial, with freedom from death as its objective and culmination? ^an6-20-1-4

As day passes by and night draws close, a mendicant reflects: ^an6-20-2-1

‘I might die of many causes. ^an6-20-2-2

A snake might bite me, or a scorpion or centipede might sting me. ^an6-20-2-3

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to me. ^an6-20-2-4

Or I might stumble off a cliff, or get food poisoning, or suffer a disturbance of bile, phlegm, or piercing winds. ^an6-20-2-5

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ’ ^an6-20-2-6

That mendicant should reflect: ^an6-20-2-7

‘Are there any bad, unskillful qualities that I haven’t given up, which might be an obstacle to my progress if I die tonight?’ ^an6-20-2-8

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an6-20-3-1

there are such bad, unskillful qualities. Then in order to give them up they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an6-20-3-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an6-20-3-3

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an6-20-3-4

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an6-20-4-1

there are no such bad, unskillful qualities. Then that mendicant should meditate with rapture and joy, training day and night in skillful qualities. ^an6-20-4-2

Or else, as night passes by and day draws close, a mendicant reflects: ^an6-20-5-1

‘I might die of many causes. ^an6-20-5-2

A snake might bite me, or a scorpion or centipede might sting me. ^an6-20-5-3

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an6-20-5-4

Or I might stumble off a cliff, or get food poisoning, or suffer a disturbance of bile, phlegm, or piercing winds. ^an6-20-5-5

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ’ ^an6-20-5-6

That mendicant should reflect: ^an6-20-5-7

‘Are there any bad, unskillful qualities that I haven’t given up, which might be an obstacle to my progress if I die today?’ ^an6-20-5-8

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an6-20-6-1

there are such bad, unskillful qualities. Then in order to give them up they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an6-20-6-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an6-20-6-3

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an6-20-6-4

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an6-20-7-1

there are no such bad, unskillful qualities. Then that mendicant should meditate with rapture and joy, training day and night in skillful qualities. ^an6-20-7-2

Mindfulness of death, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.” ^an6-20-8-1

### At Sāma Village (Numbered Discourses 6.21) ^an6-21-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans near the little village of Sāma, by a lotus pond. ^an6-21-1-1

Then, late at night, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire lotus pond, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^an6-21-1-2

“Sir, three qualities lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an6-21-2-1

What three? ^an6-21-2-2

Relishing work, talk, and sleep. ^an6-21-2-3

These three qualities lead to the decline of a mendicant.” ^an6-21-2-4

That’s what that deity said, ^an6-21-2-5

and the teacher approved. ^an6-21-2-6

Then that deity, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^an6-21-2-7

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened, adding: ^an6-21-3-1

“It’s unfortunate for those of you who even the deities know are declining in skillful qualities. ^an6-21-3-9

I will teach you three more qualities that lead to decline. ^an6-21-4-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-21-4-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an6-21-4-3

The Buddha said this: ^an6-21-4-4

“And what, mendicants, are three qualities that lead to decline? ^an6-21-4-5

Enjoyment of company, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an6-21-4-6

These three qualities lead to decline. ^an6-21-4-7

Whether in the past, future, or present, all those who decline in skillful qualities do so because of these six qualities.” ^an6-21-5-1

### Non-decline (Numbered Discourses 6.22) ^an6-22-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you these six principles that prevent decline. … ^an6-22-1-1

And what, mendicants, are the six principles that prevent decline? ^an6-22-1-2

Not relishing work, talk, sleep, and company, being easy to admonish, and having good friends. ^an6-22-1-3

These six qualities prevent decline. ^an6-22-1-4

Whether in the past, future, or present, all those who have not declined in skillful qualities do so because of these six qualities.” ^an6-22-2-1

### Dangers (Numbered Discourses 6.23) ^an6-23-0

“‘Danger’, mendicants, is a term for sensual pleasures. ^an6-23-1-1

‘Suffering’, ^an6-23-1-2

‘disease’, ^an6-23-1-3

‘boil’, ^an6-23-1-4

‘chain’, ^an6-23-1-5

and ‘bog’ are terms for sensual pleasures. ^an6-23-1-6

And why is ‘danger’ a term for sensual pleasures? ^an6-23-2-1

Someone who is besotted by sensual greed and shackled by lustful desire is not freed from dangers in this life or in lives to come. That is why ‘danger’ is a term for sensual pleasures. ^an6-23-2-2

And why are ‘suffering’, ^an6-23-2-3

‘disease’, ^an6-23-2-4

‘boil’, ^an6-23-2-5

‘snare’, ^an6-23-2-6

and ‘bog’ terms for sensual pleasures? ^an6-23-2-7

Someone who is besotted by sensual greed and shackled by lustful desire is not freed from suffering, disease, boils, chains, or bogs in this life or in lives to come. That is why these are terms for sensual pleasures. ^an6-23-2-8

Danger, suffering, disease, boils, ^an6-23-3-1

and chains and bogs both. ^an6-23-3-2

These describe the sensual pleasures ^an6-23-3-3

to which ordinary people are attached. ^an6-23-3-4

Seeing the danger in grasping, ^an6-23-4-1

the origin of birth and death, ^an6-23-4-2

the unattached are freed ^an6-23-4-3

with the ending of birth and death. ^an6-23-4-4

Happy, they’ve come to a safe place, ^an6-23-5-1

quenched in this very life. ^an6-23-5-2

They’ve gone beyond all threats and dangers, ^an6-23-5-3

and risen above all suffering.” ^an6-23-5-4

### The Himalaya (Numbered Discourses 6.24) ^an6-24-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has six qualities could shatter Himalaya, the king of mountains, let alone this wretched ignorance! ^an6-24-1-1

What six? ^an6-24-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is skilled in entering immersion, skilled in remaining in immersion, skilled in emerging from immersion, skilled in positivity for immersion, skilled in the territory of immersion, and skilled in projecting the mind purified by immersion. ^an6-24-1-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities could shatter Himalaya, the king of mountains, let alone this wretched ignorance!” ^an6-24-1-4

### Topics for Recollection (Numbered Discourses 6.25) ^an6-25-0

“Mendicants, there are these six topics for recollection. ^an6-25-1-1

What six? ^an6-25-1-2

Firstly, a noble disciple recollects the Realized One: ^an6-25-1-3

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an6-25-1-4

When a noble disciple recollects the Realized One their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-25-1-5

At that time their mind is quite unswerving. They’ve left behind greed; they’re free of it and have risen above it. ^an6-25-1-6

‘Greed’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an6-25-1-7

Relying on this, some sentient beings are purified in this way. ^an6-25-1-8

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the teaching: ^an6-25-2-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an6-25-2-2

When a noble disciple recollects the teaching their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-25-2-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha: ^an6-25-3-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an6-25-3-2

When a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-25-3-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own ethical precepts, which are intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an6-25-4-1

When a noble disciple recollects their ethical precepts their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-25-4-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own generosity: ^an6-25-5-1

‘I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate! ^an6-25-5-2

Among people full of the stain of stinginess I live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.’ ^an6-25-5-3

When a noble disciple recollects their generosity their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-25-5-4

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the deities: ^an6-25-6-1

‘There are the gods of the four great kings, the gods of the thirty-three, the gods of Yama, the joyful gods, the gods who love to create, the gods who control what is created by others, the gods of the Divinity’s host, and gods even higher than these. ^an6-25-6-2

When those deities passed away from here, they were reborn there because of their faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. I, too, have the same kind of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.’ ^an6-25-6-3

When a noble disciple recollects the faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom of both themselves and the deities their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-25-7-1

At that time their mind is quite unswerving. They’ve left behind greed; they’re free of it and have risen above it. ^an6-25-7-2

‘Greed’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an6-25-7-3

Relying on this, some sentient beings are purified in this way. ^an6-25-7-4

These are the six topics for recollection.” ^an6-25-8-1

### With Mahākaccāna (Numbered Discourses 6.26) ^an6-26-0

There Mahākaccāna addressed the mendicants: ^an6-26-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an6-26-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an6-26-1-3

Venerable Mahākaccāna said this: ^an6-26-1-4

“It’s incredible, reverends, ^an6-26-1-5

it’s amazing! ^an6-26-1-6

How this Blessed One who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, has found an opening amid confinement; that is, the six topics for recollection. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment. ^an6-26-1-7

What six? ^an6-26-2-1

Firstly, a noble disciple recollects the Realized One: ^an6-26-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an6-26-2-3

When a noble disciple recollects the Realized One their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-26-2-4

At that time their mind is quite unswerving. They’ve left behind greed; they’re free of it and have risen above it. ^an6-26-2-5

‘Greed’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an6-26-2-6

That noble disciple meditates with a heart just like space, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an6-26-2-7

Relying on this, some sentient beings become pure in this way. ^an6-26-2-8

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the teaching: ^an6-26-3-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an6-26-3-2

When a noble disciple recollects the teaching their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-26-3-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha: ^an6-26-4-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an6-26-4-2

When a noble disciple recollects the Saṅgha their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-26-4-3

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own ethical precepts, which are intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an6-26-5-1

When a noble disciple recollects their ethical precepts their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-26-5-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects their own generosity: ^an6-26-6-1

‘I’m so fortunate, so very fortunate! ^an6-26-6-2

Among people full of the stain of stinginess I live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.’ ^an6-26-6-3

When a noble disciple recollects their own generosity their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. … ^an6-26-6-4

Furthermore, a noble disciple recollects the deities: ^an6-26-7-1

‘There are the gods of the four great kings, the gods of the thirty-three, the gods of Yama, the joyful gods, the gods who love to create, the gods who control what is created by others, the gods of the Divinity’s host, and gods even higher than these. ^an6-26-7-2

When those deities passed away from here, they were reborn there because of their faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. I, too, have the same kind of faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom.’ ^an6-26-7-3

When a noble disciple recollects the faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom of both themselves and the deities their mind is not full of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-26-7-8

At that time their mind is quite unswerving. They’ve left behind greed; they’re free of it and have risen above it. ^an6-26-7-9

‘Greed’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an6-26-7-10

That noble disciple meditates with a heart just like space, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an6-26-7-11

Relying on this, some sentient beings become pure in this way. ^an6-26-7-12

It’s incredible, reverends, it’s amazing! ^an6-26-8-1

How this Blessed One who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, has found an opening amid confinement; that is, the six topics for recollection. They are in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment.” ^an6-26-8-2

### Proper Occasions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.27) ^an6-27-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-27-1-1

“Sir, how many occasions are there for going to see an esteemed mendicant?” ^an6-27-1-2

“Mendicant, there are six occasions for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-1-3

What six? ^an6-27-2-1

Firstly, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, and they don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. On that occasion they should go to an esteemed mendicant and say: ^an6-27-2-2

‘My heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, and I don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. ^an6-27-2-3

Venerable, please teach me how to give up sensual desire.’ ^an6-27-2-4

Then that esteemed mendicant teaches them how to give up sensual desire. ^an6-27-2-5

This is the first occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-2-6

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in ill will … ^an6-27-3-1

This is the second occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-3-5

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … ^an6-27-4-1

This is the third occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-4-5

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in restlessness and remorse … ^an6-27-5-1

This is the fourth occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-5-5

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in doubt … ^an6-27-6-1

This is the fifth occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-6-5

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant doesn’t understand what kind of meditation they need to focus on in order to end the defilements without delay. On that occasion they should go to an esteemed mendicant and say: ^an6-27-7-1

‘I don’t understand what kind of meditation to focus on in order to end the defilements without delay. ^an6-27-7-2

Venerable, please teach me how to end the defilements.’ ^an6-27-7-3

Then that esteemed mendicant teaches them how to end the defilements. ^an6-27-7-4

This is the sixth occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-27-7-5

These are the six occasions for going to see an esteemed mendicant.” ^an6-27-8-1

### Proper Occasions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.28) ^an6-28-0

At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^an6-28-1-1

Then after the meal, on their return from almsround, this discussion came up among them while sitting together in the pavilion. ^an6-28-1-2

“Reverends, how many occasions are there for going to see an esteemed mendicant?” ^an6-28-1-3

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-28-2-1

“Reverends, there’s a time after an esteemed mendicant’s meal when they return from almsround. Having washed their feet they sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. That is the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant.” ^an6-28-2-2

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to that mendicant: ^an6-28-3-1

“Reverend, that’s not the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-28-3-2

For at that time the fatigue from walking and from eating has not faded away. ^an6-28-3-3

There’s a time late in the afternoon when an esteemed mendicant comes out of retreat. They sit cross-legged in the shade of their porch , set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. That is the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant.” ^an6-28-3-5

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to that mendicant: ^an6-28-4-1

“Reverend, that’s not the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-28-4-2

For at that time they are still practicing the same meditation subject as a basis of immersion that they focused on during the day. ^an6-28-4-3

There’s a time when an esteemed mendicant has risen at the crack of dawn. They sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. That is the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant.” ^an6-28-4-5

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to that mendicant: ^an6-28-5-1

“Reverend, that’s not the proper occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-28-5-2

For at that time their body is full of vitality and they find it easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an6-28-5-3

When this was said, Venerable Mahākaccāna said to those senior mendicants: ^an6-28-6-1

“Reverends, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an6-28-6-2

‘Mendicants, there are six occasions for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-28-6-3

What six? ^an6-28-7-1

Firstly, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, and they don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. On that occasion they should go to an esteemed mendicant and say: ^an6-28-7-2

“My heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, and I don’t truly understand the escape from sensual desire that has arisen. ^an6-28-7-3

Venerable, please teach me how to give up sensual desire.” ^an6-28-7-4

Then that esteemed mendicant teaches them how to give up sensual desire. ^an6-28-7-5

This is the first occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant. ^an6-28-7-6

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant’s heart is overcome and mired in ill will … ^an6-28-8-1

dullness and drowsiness … ^an6-28-9-1

restlessness and remorse … ^an6-28-10-1

doubt … ^an6-28-11-1

Furthermore, there’s a time when a mendicant doesn’t understand what kind of meditation they need to focus on in order to end the defilements without delay. On that occasion they should go to an esteemed mendicant and say, ^an6-28-12-1

“I don’t understand what kind of meditation to focus on in order to end the defilements without delay. ^an6-28-12-2

Venerable, please teach me how to end the defilements.” ^an6-28-12-3

Then that esteemed mendicant teaches them how to end the defilements. ^an6-28-12-4

This is the sixth occasion for going to see an esteemed mendicant.’ ^an6-28-12-5

Reverends, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an6-28-13-1

‘These are the six occasions for going to see an esteemed mendicant.’” ^an6-28-13-2

### With Udāyī (Numbered Discourses 6.29) ^an6-29-0

Then the Buddha said to Udāyī, ^an6-29-1-1

“Udāyī, how many topics for recollection are there?” ^an6-29-1-2

When he said this, Udāyī kept silent. ^an6-29-1-3

And a second time … ^an6-29-1-4

and a third time, the Buddha said to him, ^an6-29-1-7

“Udāyī, how many topics for recollection are there?” ^an6-29-1-8

And a second time and a third time Udāyī kept silent. ^an6-29-1-9

Then Venerable Ānanda said to Venerable Udāyī, ^an6-29-2-1

“Reverend Udāyī, the teacher is addressing you.” ^an6-29-2-2

“Reverend Ānanda, I hear the Buddha. ^an6-29-2-3

It’s when a mendicant recollects many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so they recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an6-29-2-4

This is a topic for recollection.” ^an6-29-2-5

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Ānanda: ^an6-29-3-1

“Ānanda, I know that ^an6-29-3-2

this futile man Udāyī is not committed to the higher mind. ^an6-29-3-3

Ānanda, how many topics for recollection are there?” ^an6-29-3-4

“Sir, there are five topics for recollection. ^an6-29-4-1

What five? ^an6-29-4-2

Firstly, a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption. ^an6-29-4-3

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to blissful meditation in this very life. ^an6-29-4-4

Furthermore, a mendicant focuses on the perception of light, focusing on the perception of day regardless of whether it’s night or day. ^an6-29-5-1

And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, they develop a mind that’s full of radiance. ^an6-29-5-2

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to knowledge and vision. ^an6-29-5-3

Furthermore, a mendicant examines their own body up from the soles of the feet and down from the tips of the hairs, wrapped in skin and full of many kinds of filth. ^an6-29-6-1

‘In this body there is head hair, body hair, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, undigested food, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, snot, synovial fluid, urine.’ ^an6-29-6-2

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to giving up sensual desire. ^an6-29-6-3

Furthermore, suppose a mendicant were to see a corpse thrown in a charnel ground. And it had been dead for one, two, or three days, bloated, livid, and festering. ^an6-29-7-1

They’d compare it with their own body: ^an6-29-7-2

‘This body is also of that same nature, that same kind, and cannot go beyond that.’ ^an6-29-7-3

Or suppose they were to see a corpse thrown in a charnel ground being devoured by crows, hawks, vultures, hounds, jackals, and many kinds of little creatures. ^an6-29-8-1

They’d compare it with their own body: ^an6-29-8-2

‘This body is also of that same nature, that same kind, and cannot go beyond that.’ ^an6-29-8-3

Furthermore, suppose they were to see a corpse thrown in a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together by sinews … ^an6-29-9-1

A skeleton without flesh but smeared with blood, and held together by sinews … ^an6-29-9-2

A skeleton rid of flesh and blood, held together by sinews … ^an6-29-9-3

Bones rid of sinews scattered in every direction. Here a hand-bone, there a foot-bone, here a shin-bone, there a thigh-bone, here a hip-bone, there a rib-bone, here a back-bone, there an arm-bone, here a neck-bone, there a jaw-bone, here a tooth, there the skull … White bones, the color of shells … Decrepit bones, heaped in a pile … Bones rotted and crumbled to powder. ^an6-29-9-4

They’d compare it with their own body: ^an6-29-9-5

‘This body is also of that same nature, that same kind, and cannot go beyond that.’ ^an6-29-9-6

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to uprooting the conceit ‘I am’. ^an6-29-9-7

Furthermore, a mendicant, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an6-29-10-1

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to the penetration of many elements. ^an6-29-10-2

These are the five topics for recollection.” ^an6-29-10-3

“Good, good, Ānanda. ^an6-29-11-1

Well then, Ānanda, you should also remember this sixth topic for recollection. ^an6-29-11-2

In this case, a mendicant goes out mindfully, returns mindfully, stands mindfully, sits mindfully, lies down mindfully, and focuses on work mindfully. ^an6-29-11-3

When this topic of recollection is developed and cultivated in this way it leads to mindfulness and situational awareness.” ^an6-29-11-4

### Unsurpassable (Numbered Discourses 6.30) ^an6-30-0

“Mendicants, these six things are unsurpassable. ^an6-30-1-1

What six? ^an6-30-1-2

The unsurpassable seeing, listening, acquisition, training, service, and recollection. ^an6-30-1-3

And what is the unsurpassable seeing? ^an6-30-2-1

Some people go to see an elephant-treasure, a horse-treasure, a jewel-treasure, or a diverse spectrum of sights; or ascetics and brahmins of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-2-2

There is such a seeing, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-2-3

That seeing is low, crude, ordinary, ignoble, and pointless. It doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an6-30-2-4

The unsurpassable seeing is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, goes to see a Realized One or their disciple. This is in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment. ^an6-30-2-5

This is called the unsurpassable seeing. ^an6-30-2-6

Such is the unsurpassable seeing. ^an6-30-2-7

But what of the unsurpassable hearing? ^an6-30-3-1

Some people go to hear the sound of drums, arched harps, singing, or a diverse spectrum of sounds; or ascetics and brahmins of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-3-2

There is such a hearing, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-3-3

That hearing … doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an6-30-3-4

The unsurpassable hearing is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, goes to hear the teaching of a Realized One or one of his disciples. … ^an6-30-3-5

This is called the unsurpassable hearing. ^an6-30-3-6

Such is the unsurpassable seeing and hearing. ^an6-30-3-7

But what of the unsurpassable acquisition? ^an6-30-4-1

Some people acquire a child, a wife, wealth, or a diverse spectrum of things; or they acquire faith in an ascetic or brahmin of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-4-2

There is such an acquisition, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-4-3

That acquisition … doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an6-30-4-4

The unsurpassable acquisition is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, acquires faith in a Realized One or their disciple. … ^an6-30-4-5

This is called the unsurpassable acquisition. ^an6-30-4-6

Such is the unsurpassable seeing, hearing, and acquisition. ^an6-30-4-7

But what of the unsurpassable training? ^an6-30-5-1

Some people train in elephant riding, horse riding, chariot driving, archery, swordsmanship, or a diverse spectrum of things; or they train under an ascetic or brahmin of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-5-2

There is such a training, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-5-3

That training … doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an6-30-5-4

The unsurpassable training is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, trains in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom in the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. … ^an6-30-5-5

This is called the unsurpassable training. ^an6-30-5-6

Such is the unsurpassable seeing, hearing, acquisition, and training. ^an6-30-5-7

But what of the unsurpassable service? ^an6-30-6-1

Some people serve an aristocrat, a brahmin, a householder, or a diverse spectrum of people; or they serve ascetics and brahmins of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-6-2

There is such service, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-6-3

That service … doesn’t lead to extinguishment. ^an6-30-6-4

The unsurpassable service is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, serves a Realized One or their disciple. … ^an6-30-6-5
