This is called the unsurpassable service. ^an6-30-6-6

Such is the unsurpassable seeing, listening, acquisition, training, and service. ^an6-30-6-7

But what of the unsurpassable recollection? ^an6-30-7-1

Some people recollect a child, a wife, wealth, or a diverse spectrum of things; or they recollect an ascetic or brahmin of wrong view and wrong practice. ^an6-30-7-2

There is such recollection, I don’t deny it. ^an6-30-7-3

That recollection is low, crude, ordinary, ignoble, and pointless. It doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an6-30-7-4

The unsurpassable recollection is when someone with settled faith and fondness, sure and devoted, recollects a Realized One or their disciple. … ^an6-30-7-5

This is called the unsurpassable recollection. ^an6-30-7-6

These are the six unsurpassable things. ^an6-30-8-1

They’ve gained the unsurpassed seeing, ^an6-30-9-1

the unsurpassed hearing, ^an6-30-9-2

and the unsurpassable acquisition. ^an6-30-9-3

They enjoy the unsurpassable training ^an6-30-9-4

and serve with care. ^an6-30-10-1

Then they develop recollection ^an6-30-10-2

connected with seclusion, ^an6-30-10-3

which is safe, and leads to freedom from death. ^an6-30-10-4

They rejoice in diligence, ^an6-30-11-1

alert and ethically restrained. ^an6-30-11-2

And in time they arrive ^an6-30-11-3

at the place where suffering ceases.” ^an6-30-11-4

### A Trainee (Numbered Discourses 6.31) ^an6-31-0

“These six things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an6-31-1-1

What six? ^an6-31-1-2

They relish work, talk, sleep, and company. They don’t guard the sense doors, and they eat too much. ^an6-31-1-3

These six things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an6-31-1-4

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an6-31-2-1

What six? ^an6-31-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, sleep, and company. They guard the sense doors, and they don’t eat too much. ^an6-31-2-3

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an6-31-2-4

### Non-decline (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.32) ^an6-32-0

Then, late at night, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an6-32-1-1

“Sir, these six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an6-32-2-1

What six? ^an6-32-2-2

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for diligence, and for hospitality. ^an6-32-2-3

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.” ^an6-32-2-4

That’s what that deity said, ^an6-32-2-5

and the teacher approved. ^an6-32-2-6

Then that deity, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^an6-32-2-7

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened, adding: ^an6-32-3-1

“Respect for the Teacher and the teaching, ^an6-32-4-1

and keen respect for the Saṅgha; ^an6-32-4-2

a mendicant who respects diligence ^an6-32-4-3

and hospitality ^an6-32-4-4

can’t decline, ^an6-32-4-5

and has drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an6-32-4-6

### Non-decline (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.33) ^an6-33-0

“Tonight, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an6-33-1-1

‘Sir, these six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an6-33-1-2

What six? ^an6-33-1-3

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for conscience, and for prudence. ^an6-33-1-4

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.’ ^an6-33-1-5

That is what that deity said. ^an6-33-1-6

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an6-33-1-7

One respectful of the Teacher and the teaching, ^an6-33-2-1

keenly respecting the Saṅgha; ^an6-33-2-2

endowed with conscience and prudence, ^an6-33-2-3

reverential and respectful— ^an6-33-2-4

it is impossible for them to decline; ^an6-33-2-5

they have drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an6-33-2-6

### With Mahāmoggallāna (Numbered Discourses 6.34) ^an6-34-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-34-1-1

Then as Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^an6-34-1-2

“Which gods know that they are ^an6-34-1-3

stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^an6-34-1-4

Now, at that time a monk named Tissa had recently passed away and been reborn in a realm of divinity. ^an6-34-1-5

There they knew that ^an6-34-1-6

The divinity Tissa was very mighty and powerful. ^an6-34-1-7

And then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared in that realm of divinity. ^an6-34-2-1

Tissa saw Moggallāna coming off in the distance, ^an6-34-2-2

and said to him, ^an6-34-2-3

“Come, my good fellow Moggallāna! Welcome, my good fellow! ^an6-34-2-4

It’s been a long time since you took the opportunity to come here. ^an6-34-2-5

Sit, my good Moggallāna, this seat is for you.” ^an6-34-2-6

Moggallāna sat down on the seat spread out. ^an6-34-2-7

Then Tissa bowed to Moggallāna and sat to one side. ^an6-34-2-8

Moggallāna said to him, ^an6-34-2-9

“Tissa, which gods know that they are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^an6-34-3-1

“The gods of the four great kings know this.” ^an6-34-3-2

“But do all of them know this?” ^an6-34-4-1

“No, my good Moggallāna, not all of them. ^an6-34-4-2

Those who lack experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and lack the ethics loved by the noble ones, do not know that they are stream-enterers. ^an6-34-4-3

But those who have experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and have the ethics loved by the noble ones, do know that they are stream-enterers.” ^an6-34-4-4

“But Tissa, is it only the gods of the four great kings who know that they are stream-enterers, or do the gods of the thirty-three … ^an6-34-5-1

the gods of Yama … ^an6-34-5-2

the joyful gods … ^an6-34-5-3

the gods who love to create … ^an6-34-5-4

and the gods who control what is created by others know that they are stream-enterers, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening?” ^an6-34-5-5

“The gods of these various classes know this.” ^an6-34-5-6

“But do all of them know this?” ^an6-34-6-1

“No, my good Moggallāna, not all of them. ^an6-34-6-2

Those who lack experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and lack the ethics loved by the noble ones, do not know that they are stream-enterers. ^an6-34-6-3

But those who have experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and have the ethics loved by the noble ones, do know that they are stream-enterers.” ^an6-34-6-4

Moggallāna approved and agreed with what the divinity Tissa said. Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from that realm of divinity and reappeared in Jeta’s Grove. ^an6-34-7-1

### Things That Play a Part in Realization (Numbered Discourses 6.35) ^an6-35-0

“These six things play a part in realization. ^an6-35-1-1

What six? ^an6-35-1-2

The perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of giving up, the perception of fading away, and the perception of cessation. ^an6-35-1-3

These are the six things that play a part in realization.” ^an6-35-1-4

### Roots of Dispute (Numbered Discourses 6.36) ^an6-36-0

“Mendicants, there are these six roots of dispute. ^an6-36-1-1

What six? ^an6-36-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant is irritable and acrimonious. ^an6-36-1-3

Such a mendicant lacks respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and they don’t fulfill the training. ^an6-36-1-4

They create a dispute in the Saṅgha, which is for the detriment and suffering of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of gods and humans. ^an6-36-1-5

If you see such a root of dispute in yourselves or others, you should try to give up this bad thing. ^an6-36-1-6

If you don’t see it, you should practice so that it doesn’t come up in the future. ^an6-36-1-7

That’s how to give up this bad root of dispute, so it doesn’t come up in the future. ^an6-36-1-8

Furthermore, a mendicant is offensive and contemptuous … ^an6-36-2-1

They’re jealous and stingy … ^an6-36-2-2

devious and deceitful … ^an6-36-2-3

with corrupt wishes and wrong view … ^an6-36-2-4

They’re attached to their own views, holding them tight, and refusing to let go. ^an6-36-2-5

Such a mendicant lacks respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and they don’t fulfill the training. ^an6-36-2-6

They create a dispute in the Saṅgha, which is for the detriment and unhappiness of the people, against the people, for the harm, detriment, and suffering of gods and humans. ^an6-36-2-7

If you see such a root of dispute in yourselves or others, you should try to give up this bad thing. ^an6-36-2-8

If you don’t see it, you should practice so that it doesn’t come up in the future. ^an6-36-2-9

That’s how to give up this bad root of dispute, so it doesn’t come up in the future. ^an6-36-2-10

These are the six roots of dispute.” ^an6-36-2-11

### A Gift With Six Factors (Numbered Discourses 6.37) ^an6-37-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-37-1-1

Now at that time Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother, was preparing a religious donation with six factors for the mendicant Saṅgha headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^an6-37-1-2

The Buddha saw her doing this, with his clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, ^an6-37-1-3

and he addressed the mendicants: ^an6-37-1-4

“This Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother, is preparing a religious donation with six factors for the mendicant Saṅgha headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^an6-37-1-5

And how does a religious donation have six factors? ^an6-37-2-1

Three factors apply to the donor and three to the recipients. ^an6-37-2-2

What three factors apply to the donor? ^an6-37-2-3

It’s when a donor is in a good mood before giving, while giving they feel confident, and after giving they’re uplifted. ^an6-37-2-4

These three factors apply to the donor. ^an6-37-2-5

What three factors apply to the recipients? ^an6-37-3-1

It’s when the recipients are free of greed, hate, and delusion, or practicing to be free of them. ^an6-37-3-2

These three factors apply to the recipients. ^an6-37-3-3

Thus three factors apply to the donor and three to the recipients. ^an6-37-3-4

That’s how a religious donation has six factors. ^an6-37-3-5

It’s not easy to grasp the merit of such a six-factored donation by saying that ^an6-37-4-1

this is the extent of their overflowing merit, overflowing goodness that nurtures happiness and is conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. And it leads to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an6-37-4-2

It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of merit. ^an6-37-4-3

It’s like trying to grasp how much water is in the ocean. It’s not easy to say ^an6-37-5-1

how many gallons, how many hundreds, thousands, hundreds of thousands of gallons there are. ^an6-37-5-2

It’s simply reckoned as an incalculable, immeasurable, great mass of water. ^an6-37-5-3

In the same way, it’s not easy to grasp the merit of such a six-factored donation … ^an6-37-5-4

A good mood before giving, ^an6-37-6-1

confidence while giving, ^an6-37-6-2

feeling uplifted after giving: ^an6-37-6-3

this is the perfect sacrifice. ^an6-37-6-4

Free of greed, free of hate, ^an6-37-7-1

free of delusion, undefiled; ^an6-37-7-2

this is the field for the perfect sacrifice, ^an6-37-7-3

the disciplined spiritual practitioners. ^an6-37-7-4

After rinsing, ^an6-37-8-1

you give with your own hands. ^an6-37-8-2

This sacrifice is very fruitful ^an6-37-8-3

for both yourself and others. ^an6-37-8-4

When an intelligent, faithful person, ^an6-37-9-1

sacrifices like this, with a mind of letting go, ^an6-37-9-2

that astute one is reborn ^an6-37-9-3

in a happy, pleasing world.” ^an6-37-9-4

### One’s Own Volition (Numbered Discourses 6.38) ^an6-38-0

Then a certain brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-38-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an6-38-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, this is my doctrine and view: ^an6-38-1-3

One does not act of one’s own volition, nor does one act of another’s volition.” ^an6-38-1-4

“Brahmin, may I never see or hear of anyone holding such a doctrine or view! ^an6-38-1-5

How on earth can someone who comes and goes on his own say that ^an6-38-1-6

one does not act of one’s own volition, nor does one act of another’s volition? ^an6-38-1-7

What do you think, brahmin, is there an element of initiative?” ^an6-38-2-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-38-2-2

“Since this is so, do we find sentient beings who initiate activity?” ^an6-38-2-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-38-2-4

“Since there is an element of initiative, and sentient beings who initiate activity are found, sentient beings act of their own volition or that of another. ^an6-38-2-5

What do you think, brahmin, is there an element of persistence … ^an6-38-3-1

exertion … ^an6-38-3-2

strength … ^an6-38-3-3

endurance … ^an6-38-3-4

energy?” ^an6-38-3-5

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-38-3-6

“Since this is so, do we find sentient beings who have energy?” ^an6-38-3-7

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-38-3-8

“Since there is an element of energy, and sentient beings who have energy are found, sentient beings act of their own volition or that of another. ^an6-38-3-9

Brahmin, may I never see or hear of anyone holding such a doctrine or view! ^an6-38-4-1

How on earth can someone who comes and goes on his own say that ^an6-38-4-2

one does not act of one’s own volition, nor does one act of another’s volition?” ^an6-38-4-3

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an6-38-5-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an6-38-5-2

### Sources (Numbered Discourses 6.39) ^an6-39-0

“Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an6-39-1-1

What three? ^an6-39-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an6-39-1-3

Greed doesn’t give rise to contentment. ^an6-39-1-4

Rather, greed just gives rise to greed. ^an6-39-1-5

Hate doesn’t give rise to love. ^an6-39-1-6

Rather, hate just gives rise to hate. ^an6-39-1-7

Delusion doesn’t give rise to understanding. ^an6-39-1-8

Rather, delusion just gives rise to delusion. ^an6-39-1-9

It’s not because of deeds born of greed, hate, and delusion that gods, humans, or those in any other good places are found. ^an6-39-1-10

Rather, it’s because of deeds born of greed, hate, and delusion that hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, or any other bad places are found. ^an6-39-1-11

These are three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an6-39-1-12

Mendicants, there are these three sources that give rise to deeds. ^an6-39-2-1

What three? ^an6-39-2-2

Contentment, love, and understanding are sources that give rise to deeds. ^an6-39-2-3

Contentment doesn’t give rise to greed. ^an6-39-2-4

Rather, contentment just gives rise to contentment. ^an6-39-2-5

Love doesn’t give rise to hate. ^an6-39-2-6

Rather, love just gives rise to love. ^an6-39-2-7

Understanding doesn’t give rise to delusion. ^an6-39-2-8

Rather, understanding just gives rise to understanding. ^an6-39-2-9

It’s not because of deeds born of contentment, love, and understanding that hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, or any other bad places are found. ^an6-39-2-10

Rather, it’s because of deeds born of contentment, love, and understanding that gods, humans, or those in any other good places are found. ^an6-39-2-11

These are three sources that give rise to deeds.” ^an6-39-2-12

### With Kimbila (Numbered Discourses 6.40) ^an6-40-0

So I have heard. ^an6-40-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kimbilā in the Freshwater Mangrove Wood. ^an6-40-1-2

Then Venerable Kimbila went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-40-1-3

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an6-40-1-4

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen lack respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, diligence, and hospitality after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an6-40-1-5

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an6-40-1-6

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an6-40-2-1

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen maintain respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, diligence, and hospitality after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an6-40-2-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an6-40-2-3

### A Tree Trunk (Numbered Discourses 6.41) ^an6-41-0

So I have heard. ^an6-41-1-1

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an6-41-1-2

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, descended the Vulture’s Peak together with several mendicants. At a certain spot he saw a large tree trunk, ^an6-41-1-3

and he addressed the mendicants, ^an6-41-1-4

“Reverends, do you see this large tree trunk?” ^an6-41-1-5

“Yes, reverend.” ^an6-41-1-6

“If they wanted to, a mendicant with psychic powers who has mastered their mind could determine this tree trunk to be nothing but earth. ^an6-41-2-1

Why is that? ^an6-41-2-2

Because the earth element exists in the tree trunk. Relying on that a mendicant with psychic powers could determine it to be nothing but earth. ^an6-41-2-3

If they wanted to, a mendicant with psychic powers who has mastered their mind could determine this tree trunk to be nothing but water. … ^an6-41-2-4

Or they could determine it to be nothing but fire … ^an6-41-2-5

Or they could determine it to be nothing but air … ^an6-41-2-6

Or they could determine it to be nothing but beautiful … ^an6-41-2-7

Or they could determine it to be nothing but ugly. ^an6-41-2-8

Why is that? ^an6-41-2-9

Because the element of ugliness exists in the tree trunk. Relying on that a mendicant with psychic powers could determine it to be nothing but ugly.” ^an6-41-2-10

### With Nāgita (Numbered Discourses 6.42) ^an6-42-0

So I have heard. ^an6-42-1-1

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a village of the Kosalan brahmins named Icchānaṅgala. ^an6-42-1-2

He stayed in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an6-42-1-3

The brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala heard: ^an6-42-1-4

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Icchānaṅgala. He is staying in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an6-42-1-5

He has this good reputation: ^an6-42-1-6

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an6-42-1-7

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased; and he explains a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^an6-42-1-8

Then, when the night had passed, they took many fresh and cooked foods and went to the forest near Icchānaṅgala, where they stood outside the gates making a colossal racket. ^an6-42-1-9

Now, at that time Venerable Nāgita was the Buddha’s attendant. ^an6-42-2-1

Then the Buddha said to Nāgita, ^an6-42-2-2

“Nāgita, who’s making that colossal racket? You’d think it was fishermen hauling in a catch!” ^an6-42-2-3

“Sir, it’s these brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala. They’ve brought many fresh and cooked foods, and they’re standing outside the gates wanting to offer it specially to the Buddha and the mendicant Saṅgha.” ^an6-42-2-4

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an6-42-2-5

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an6-42-2-6

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity.” ^an6-42-2-7

“Sir, may the Blessed One please relent now! May the Holy One relent! ^an6-42-3-1

Now is the time for the Buddha to relent. ^an6-42-3-2

Wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an6-42-3-3

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily and the water flows downhill. ^an6-42-3-4

In the same way, wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an6-42-3-5

Why is that? ^an6-42-3-6

Because of the Buddha’s ethics and wisdom.” ^an6-42-3-7

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an6-42-4-1

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an6-42-4-2

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity. ^an6-42-4-3

Take a mendicant living within a village who I see sitting immersed in samādhi. ^an6-42-5-1

I think to myself: ^an6-42-5-2

‘Now a monastery worker, a novice, or a fellow practitioner will make this venerable fall from immersion.’ ^an6-42-5-3

So I’m not pleased that that mendicant is living within a village. ^an6-42-5-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting nodding in meditation. ^an6-42-6-1

I think to myself: ^an6-42-6-2

‘Now this venerable, having dispelled that sleepiness and weariness, will focus just on the unified perception of wilderness.’ ^an6-42-6-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an6-42-6-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting without being immersed in samādhi. ^an6-42-7-1

I think to myself: ^an6-42-7-2

‘Now if this venerable’s mind is not immersed in samādhi they will immerse it, or if it is immersed in samādhi, they will preserve it.’ ^an6-42-7-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an6-42-7-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting immersed in samādhi. ^an6-42-8-1

I think to myself: ^an6-42-8-2

‘Now this venerable will free the unfreed mind or preserve the freed mind.’ ^an6-42-8-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an6-42-8-4

Take a mendicant who I see living within a village receiving robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an6-42-9-1

Enjoying possessions, honor, and popularity they neglect retreat, and they neglect remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an6-42-9-2

They come down to villages, towns, and capital cities and make their home there. ^an6-42-9-3

So I’m not pleased that that mendicant is living within a village. ^an6-42-9-4

Take a mendicant who I see in the wilderness receiving robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an6-42-10-1

Fending off possessions, honor, and popularity they don’t neglect retreat, and they don’t neglect remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an6-42-10-2

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an6-42-10-3

Nāgita, when I’m walking along a road and I don’t see anyone ahead or behind I feel relaxed, even if I need to urinate or defecate.” ^an6-42-11-1

### The Giant (Numbered Discourses 6.43) ^an6-43-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-43-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^an6-43-1-2

Then, after the meal, on his return from almsround, he addressed Venerable Ānanda, ^an6-43-1-3

“Come, Ānanda, let’s go to the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery for the day’s meditation.” ^an6-43-1-4

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an6-43-1-5

So the Buddha went with Ānanda to the Eastern Monastery. ^an6-43-2-1

In the late afternoon the Buddha came out of retreat and addressed Ānanda, ^an6-43-2-2

“Come, Ānanda, let’s go to the eastern gate to bathe.” ^an6-43-2-3

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an6-43-2-4

So the Buddha went with Ānanda to the eastern gate to bathe. ^an6-43-2-5

When he had bathed and emerged from the water he stood in one robe drying his limbs. ^an6-43-2-6

Now, at that time King Pasenadi had a giant bull elephant called “White”. It emerged from the eastern gate to the beating and playing of musical instruments. ^an6-43-3-1

When people saw it they said, ^an6-43-3-2

“The royal giant is so handsome! The royal giant is so good-looking! The royal giant is so lovely! The royal giant has such a huge body!” ^an6-43-3-3

When they said this, Venerable Udāyī said to the Buddha, ^an6-43-3-4

“Sir, is it only when they see elephants with such a huge, formidable body that people say: ^an6-43-3-5

‘A giant, such a giant’? Or do they say it when they see any other creatures with huge, formidable bodies?” ^an6-43-3-6

“Udāyī, when they see elephants with such a huge, formidable body people say: ^an6-43-3-7

‘A giant, such a giant!’ ^an6-43-3-8

And also when they see a horse with a huge, formidable body … ^an6-43-3-9

When they see a bull with a huge, formidable body … ^an6-43-3-10

When they see a serpent with a huge, formidable body … ^an6-43-3-11

When they see a tree with a huge, formidable body … ^an6-43-3-12

And when they see a human being with such a huge, formidable body people say: ^an6-43-3-13

‘A giant, such a giant!’ ^an6-43-3-14

But Udāyī, one who does nothing monstrous by way of body, speech, and mind is who I call a ‘giant’ in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans.” ^an6-43-3-15

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an6-43-4-1

How well said this was by the Buddha: ^an6-43-4-2

‘But Udāyī, one who does nothing monstrous by way of body, speech, and mind is who I call a “giant” in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans.’ ^an6-43-4-3

And I celebrate the well-spoken words of the Buddha with these verses: ^an6-43-4-4

Awakened as a human being, ^an6-43-5-1

self-tamed and immersed in samādhi, ^an6-43-5-2

following the spiritual path, ^an6-43-5-3

he loves peace of mind. ^an6-43-5-4

Revered by people, ^an6-43-6-1

gone beyond all things, ^an6-43-6-2

even the gods revere him; ^an6-43-6-3

so I’ve heard from the perfected one. ^an6-43-6-4

He has transcended all fetters ^an6-43-7-1

and escaped from entanglements. ^an6-43-7-2

Delighting to renounce sensual pleasures, ^an6-43-7-3

he’s freed like lustrous gold from stone. ^an6-43-7-4

That giant outshines all, ^an6-43-8-1

like the Himalaya beside other mountains. ^an6-43-8-2

Of all those named ‘giant’, ^an6-43-8-3

he is truly named, supreme. ^an6-43-8-4

I shall extol the giant for you, ^an6-43-9-1

for he does nothing monstrous. ^an6-43-9-2

Gentleness and harmlessness ^an6-43-9-3

are two feet of the giant. ^an6-43-9-4

Fervor and chastity ^an6-43-10-1

are his two other feet. ^an6-43-10-2

Faith is the giant’s trunk, ^an6-43-10-3

and equanimity his white tusks. ^an6-43-10-4

Mindfulness is his neck, his head is wisdom— ^an6-43-11-1

inquiry and thinking about principles. ^an6-43-11-2

His belly is the sacred hearth of the Dhamma, ^an6-43-11-3

and his tail is seclusion. ^an6-43-11-4

Practicing absorption, enjoying the breath, ^an6-43-12-1

he is serene within. ^an6-43-12-2

The giant is serene when walking, ^an6-43-12-3

the giant is serene when standing, ^an6-43-12-4

the giant is serene when lying down, ^an6-43-13-1

and when sitting, the giant is serene. ^an6-43-13-2

The giant is restrained everywhere: ^an6-43-13-3

this is the accomplishment of the giant. ^an6-43-13-4

He eats blameless things, ^an6-43-14-1

he doesn’t eat blameworthy things. ^an6-43-14-2

When he gets food and clothes, ^an6-43-14-3

he avoids storing them up. ^an6-43-14-4

Having severed all bonds, ^an6-43-15-1

fetters large and small, ^an6-43-15-2

wherever he goes, ^an6-43-15-3

he goes without concern. ^an6-43-15-4

A white lotus, ^an6-43-16-1

fragrant and delightful, ^an6-43-16-2

sprouts in water and grows there, ^an6-43-16-3

but water does not stick to it. ^an6-43-16-4

Just so the Buddha is born in the world, ^an6-43-17-1

and lives in the world, ^an6-43-17-2

but the world does not stick to him, ^an6-43-17-3

as water does not stick to the lotus. ^an6-43-17-4

A great blazing fire ^an6-43-18-1

dies down when the fuel runs out. ^an6-43-18-2

When the coals have gone out ^an6-43-18-3

it’s said to be ‘quenched’. ^an6-43-18-4

This simile is taught by the discerning ^an6-43-19-1

to express the meaning clearly. ^an6-43-19-2

Great giants will understand ^an6-43-19-3

what the giant taught the giant. ^an6-43-19-4

Free of greed, free of hate, ^an6-43-20-1

free of delusion, undefiled; ^an6-43-20-2

the giant, giving up his body, ^an6-43-20-3

being undefiled, will be fully quenched.” ^an6-43-20-4

### With Migasālā (Numbered Discourses 6.44) ^an6-44-0

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the laywoman Migasālā, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an6-44-1-1

Then the laywoman Migasālā went up to Ānanda, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an6-44-1-2

“Honorable Ānanda, how on earth are we supposed to understand the teaching taught by the Buddha, when the chaste and the unchaste are both reborn in exactly the same place in the next life? ^an6-44-2-1

My father Purāṇa was chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an6-44-2-2

When he passed away the Buddha declared that, since he was a once-returner, he was reborn in the host of joyful gods. ^an6-44-2-3

But my uncle Isidatta was not chaste; he lived content with his wife. ^an6-44-2-4

When he passed away the Buddha declared that, since he was also a once-returner, he was reborn in the host of joyful gods. ^an6-44-2-5

How on earth are we supposed to understand the teaching taught by the Buddha, when the chaste and the unchaste are both reborn in exactly the same place in the next life?” ^an6-44-2-6

“You’re right, sister, but that’s how the Buddha declared it.” ^an6-44-2-7

Then Ānanda, after receiving almsfood at Migasālā’s home, rose from his seat and left. ^an6-44-3-1

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, Ānanda went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an6-44-3-2

“Ānanda, who is this laywoman Migasālā, a foolish incompetent aunty, with an aunty’s wit? And who is it that knows how to assess individual persons? ^an6-44-5-1

These six individuals are found in the world. ^an6-44-5-2

What six? ^an6-44-6-1

Take a certain individual who is gentle and pleasant to be with. And spiritual companions enjoy living together with them. ^an6-44-6-2

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an6-44-6-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an6-44-6-4

Take another individual who is gentle and pleasant to be with. And spiritual companions enjoy living together with them. ^an6-44-7-1

And they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found temporary freedom. ^an6-44-7-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an6-44-7-3

Judgmental people compare them, saying: ^an6-44-8-1

‘This one has just the same qualities as the other, so why is one worse and one better?’ ^an6-44-8-2

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an6-44-8-3

In this case, the individual who is gentle … and has listened, learned, comprehended theoretically, and found temporary freedom ^an6-44-9-1

is better and finer than the other individual. ^an6-44-9-2

Why is that? ^an6-44-9-3

Because the stream of the teaching carries them along. ^an6-44-9-4

But who knows the difference between them except a Realized One? ^an6-44-9-5

So, Ānanda, don’t be judgmental about individuals. ^an6-44-9-6

Don’t pass judgment on individuals. ^an6-44-9-7

Those who pass judgment on individuals harm themselves. ^an6-44-9-8

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an6-44-9-9

Take another individual who is angry and conceited, and from time to time has greedy thoughts. ^an6-44-10-1

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an6-44-10-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an6-44-10-3

Take another individual who is angry and conceited, and from time to time has greedy thoughts. … ^an6-44-11-1

Because the stream of the teaching carries them along. … ^an6-44-11-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re headed for a higher place, not a lower. They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an6-44-11-3

Judgmental people compare them … ^an6-44-12-1

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on people. ^an6-44-12-2

Take another individual who is angry and conceited, and from time to time has the impulse to speak inappropriately. ^an6-44-13-1

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an6-44-13-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an6-44-13-3

Take another individual who is angry and conceited, and from time to time has the impulse to speak inappropriately. ^an6-44-14-1

But they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found temporary freedom. ^an6-44-14-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an6-44-14-3

Judgmental people compare them, saying: ^an6-44-15-1

‘This one has just the same qualities as the other, so why is one worse and one better?’ ^an6-44-15-2

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an6-44-15-3

In this case, the individual who is angry and conceited, but has listened, learned, comprehended theoretically, and found temporary freedom ^an6-44-16-1

is better and finer than the other individual. ^an6-44-16-2

Why is that? ^an6-44-16-3

Because the stream of the teaching carries them along. ^an6-44-16-4

But who knows the difference between them except a Realized One? ^an6-44-16-5

So, Ānanda, don’t be judgmental about individuals. ^an6-44-16-6

Don’t pass judgment on individuals. ^an6-44-16-7

Those who pass judgment on individuals harm themselves. ^an6-44-16-8

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an6-44-16-9

Who is this laywoman Migasālā, a foolish incompetent aunty, with an aunty’s wit? And who is it that knows how to assess individual persons? ^an6-44-17-1

These six individuals are found in the world. ^an6-44-17-2

If Isidatta had achieved Purāṇa’s level of ethical conduct, Purāṇa could not have even known Isidatta’s destination. ^an6-44-18-1

And if Purāṇa had achieved Isidatta’s level of wisdom, Isidatta could not have even known Purāṇa’s destination. ^an6-44-18-2

So both individuals were lacking in one respect.” ^an6-44-18-3

### Debt (Numbered Discourses 6.45) ^an6-45-0

“Mendicants, isn’t poverty suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-1-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-1-2

“When a poor, penniless person falls into debt, isn’t being in debt also suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-2-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-2-2

“When a poor person who has fallen into debt agrees to pay interest, isn’t the interest also suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-3-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-3-2

“When a poor person who has fallen into debt and agreed to pay interest fails to pay it when it falls due, they get a warning. ^an6-45-4-1

Isn’t being warned suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-4-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-4-3

“When a poor person fails to pay after getting a warning, they’re prosecuted. ^an6-45-5-1

Isn’t being prosecuted suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-5-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-5-3

“When a poor person fails to pay after being prosecuted, they’re imprisoned. ^an6-45-6-1

Isn’t being imprisoned suffering in the world for a person who enjoys sensual pleasures?” ^an6-45-6-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-45-6-3

“So mendicants, poverty, debt, interest, warnings, prosecution, and imprisonment are suffering in the world for those who enjoy sensual pleasures. ^an6-45-7-1

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an6-45-7-2

is called poor and penniless in the training of the Noble One. ^an6-45-7-3

Since they have no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, or wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities, they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-45-8-1

This is how they’re in debt, I say. ^an6-45-8-2

In order to conceal the bad things they do by way of body, speech, and mind they harbor corrupt wishes. ^an6-45-9-1

They wish, plan, speak, and act with the thought: ‘May no-one find me out!’ ^an6-45-9-2

This is how they pay interest, I say. ^an6-45-9-3

Good-hearted spiritual companions say this about them: ^an6-45-10-1

‘This venerable acts like this, and behaves like that.’ ^an6-45-10-2

This is how they’re warned, I say. ^an6-45-10-3

When they go to a wilderness, the root of a tree, or an empty hut, they’re beset by remorseful, unskillful thoughts. ^an6-45-11-1

This is how they’re prosecuted, I say. ^an6-45-11-2

That poor, penniless person has done bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re trapped in the prison of hell or the animal realm. ^an6-45-12-1

I don’t see a single prison that’s as grim, as vicious, and such an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke as the prison of hell or the animal realm. ^an6-45-12-2

Poverty is said to be suffering in the world, ^an6-45-13-1

and so is being in debt. ^an6-45-13-2

A poor person who has fallen into debt ^an6-45-13-3

frets even when spending the loan. ^an6-45-13-4

And then they’re prosecuted, ^an6-45-14-1

or even thrown in jail. ^an6-45-14-2

Such imprisonment is true suffering ^an6-45-14-3

for someone who prays for pleasure and possessions. ^an6-45-14-4

In the same way, in the training of the Noble One ^an6-45-15-1

whoever has no faith, ^an6-45-15-2

no conscience or prudence, ^an6-45-15-3

contemplates bad deeds. ^an6-45-15-4

After doing bad things ^an6-45-16-1

by way of body, ^an6-45-16-2

speech, and mind, ^an6-45-16-3

they wish, ‘May no-one find me out!’ ^an6-45-16-4

Their behavior is creepy ^an6-45-17-1

by body, speech, and mind. ^an6-45-17-2

They pile up bad deeds ^an6-45-17-3

on and on, life after life. ^an6-45-17-4

That simpleton evildoer, ^an6-45-18-1

knowing their own misdeeds, ^an6-45-18-2

is a poor person who has fallen into debt, ^an6-45-18-3

and frets even when spending the loan. ^an6-45-18-4

And when in village or wilderness ^an6-45-19-1

they’re prosecuted ^an6-45-19-2

by painful mental plans, ^an6-45-19-3

which are born of remorse. ^an6-45-19-4

That simpleton evildoer, ^an6-45-20-1

knowing their own misdeeds, ^an6-45-20-2

goes to one of the animal realms, ^an6-45-20-3

or is trapped in hell. ^an6-45-20-4

Such imprisonment is true suffering, ^an6-45-21-1

from which the attentive are released. ^an6-45-21-2

With confident heart, they give ^an6-45-21-3

with wealth that is properly earned. ^an6-45-21-4

That faithful householder of discernment ^an6-45-22-1

holds a winning hand on both counts: ^an6-45-22-2

welfare and benefit in this life, ^an6-45-22-3

and happiness in the next. ^an6-45-22-4

This is how, for a householder, ^an6-45-22-5

merit grows by generosity. ^an6-45-22-6

In the same way, in the training of the Noble One, ^an6-45-23-1

whoever is grounded in faith, ^an6-45-23-2

with conscience and prudence, ^an6-45-23-3

wise, and ethically restrained, ^an6-45-23-4

is said to live happily ^an6-45-24-1

in the training of the Noble One. ^an6-45-24-2

After gaining pleasure not of the flesh, ^an6-45-24-3

they concentrate on equanimity. ^an6-45-24-4

They give up the five hindrances, ^an6-45-25-1

constantly energetic, ^an6-45-25-2

and enter the absorptions, ^an6-45-25-3

unified, alert, and mindful. ^an6-45-25-4

Truly knowing in this way ^an6-45-26-1

regards the ending of all fetters, ^an6-45-26-2

by not grasping in any way, ^an6-45-26-3

their mind is rightly freed. ^an6-45-26-4

To that unaffected one, rightly freed ^an6-45-27-1

with the end of the fetters of continued existence, ^an6-45-27-2

the knowledge comes: ^an6-45-27-3

‘My freedom is unshakable.’ ^an6-45-27-4

This is the ultimate knowledge. ^an6-45-28-1

This is the supreme happiness. ^an6-45-28-2

Sorrowless, stainless, secure: ^an6-45-28-3

this is the highest freedom from debt.” ^an6-45-28-4

### By Mahācunda (Numbered Discourses 6.46) ^an6-46-0

So I have heard. ^an6-46-1-1

At one time Venerable Mahācunda was staying in the land of the Cetis at Sahajāti. ^an6-46-1-2

There he addressed the mendicants: ^an6-46-1-3

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an6-46-1-4

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an6-46-1-5

Venerable Mahācunda said this: ^an6-46-1-6

“Take a case where mendicants who practice discernment of principles rebuke mendicants who practice absorption: ^an6-46-2-1

‘They say, “We practice absorption meditation! We practice absorption meditation!” And they meditate and concentrate and contemplate and ruminate. ^an6-46-2-2

Why do they practice absorption meditation? In what way do they practice absorption meditation? How do they practice absorption meditation?’ ^an6-46-2-3

In this case the mendicants who practice discernment of principles are not inspired, and the mendicants who practice absorption are not inspired. And they’re not acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an6-46-2-4

Now, take a case where mendicants who practice absorption meditation rebuke mendicants who practice discernment of principles: ^an6-46-3-1

‘They say, “We practice discernment of principles! We practice discernment of principles!” But they’re restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties. ^an6-46-3-2

Why do they practice discernment of principles? In what way do they practice discernment of principles? How do they practice discernment of principles?’ ^an6-46-3-3

In this case the mendicants who practice absorption are not inspired, and the mendicants who practice discernment of principles are not inspired. And they’re not acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an6-46-3-4

Now, take a case where mendicants who practice discernment of principles praise only others like them, not mendicants who practice absorption. ^an6-46-4-1

In this case the mendicants who practice discernment of principles are not inspired, and the mendicants who practice absorption are not inspired. And they’re not acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an6-46-4-2

And take a case where mendicants who practice absorption praise only others like them, not mendicants who practice discernment of principles. ^an6-46-5-1

In this case the mendicants who practice absorption are not inspired, and the mendicants who practice discernment of principles are not inspired. And they’re not acting for the welfare and happiness of the people, for the people, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans. ^an6-46-5-2

So you should train like this: ^an6-46-6-1

‘As mendicants who practice discernment of principles, we will praise mendicants who practice absorption.’ ^an6-46-6-2

That’s how you should train. ^an6-46-6-3

Why is that? ^an6-46-6-4

Because it’s rare to find such incredible individuals in the world who have direct meditative experience of the element free of death. ^an6-46-6-5

So you should train like this: ^an6-46-7-1

‘As mendicants who practice absorption, we will praise mendicants who practice discernment of principles.’ ^an6-46-7-2

That’s how you should train. ^an6-46-7-3

Why is that? ^an6-46-7-4

Because it’s incredibly rare to find individuals in the world who see the meaning of a deep saying with penetrating wisdom.” ^an6-46-7-5

### Apparent in the Present Life (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.47) ^an6-47-0

And then the wanderer Moḷiyasīvaka went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-47-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an6-47-1-2

“Sir, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^an6-47-1-3

In what way is the teaching apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^an6-47-1-4

“Well then, Sīvaka, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an6-47-2-1

What do you think, Sīvaka? ^an6-47-2-2

When there’s greed in you, do you understand ‘I have greed in me’? And when there’s no greed in you, do you understand ‘I have no greed in me’?” ^an6-47-2-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-47-2-4

“Since you know this, ^an6-47-2-5

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an6-47-2-6

What do you think, Sīvaka? ^an6-47-3-1

When there’s hate … ^an6-47-3-2

delusion … ^an6-47-3-3

greedy ideas … ^an6-47-3-4

hateful ideas … ^an6-47-3-5

When there are delusional ideas in you, do you understand ‘I have delusional ideas in me’? And when there are no delusional ideas in you, do you understand ‘I have no delusional ideas in me’?” ^an6-47-3-6

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-47-3-7

“Since you know this, ^an6-47-3-8

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^an6-47-3-9

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^an6-47-4-1

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an6-47-4-2

### Apparent in the Present Life (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.48) ^an6-48-0

Then a certain brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-48-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an6-48-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^an6-48-1-3

In what way is the teaching apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves?” ^an6-48-1-4

“Well then, brahmin, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an6-48-2-1

What do you think, brahmin? ^an6-48-2-2

When there’s greed in you, do you understand ‘I have greed in me’? And when there’s no greed in you, do you understand ‘I have no greed in me’?” ^an6-48-2-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-48-2-4

“Since you know this, ^an6-48-2-5

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves. ^an6-48-2-6

What do you think, brahmin? ^an6-48-3-1

When there’s hate … ^an6-48-3-2

delusion … ^an6-48-3-3

corruption that leads to physical deeds … ^an6-48-3-4

corruption that leads to speech … ^an6-48-3-5

When there’s corruption that leads to mental deeds in you, do you understand ‘I have corruption that leads to mental deeds in me’? And when there’s no corruption that leads to mental deeds in you, do you understand ‘I have no corruption that leads to mental deeds in me’?” ^an6-48-3-6

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-48-3-7

“Since you know this, ^an6-48-3-8

this is how the teaching is apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.” ^an6-48-3-9

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an6-48-4-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an6-48-4-2

### With Khema (Numbered Discourses 6.49) ^an6-49-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an6-49-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Khema and Venerable Sumana were staying near Sāvatthī in the Dark Forest. ^an6-49-1-2

Then they went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Venerable Khema said to the Buddha: ^an6-49-1-3

“Sir, a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—does not think: ^an6-49-2-1

‘There is someone better than me, or equal to me, or worse than me.’” ^an6-49-2-2

That is what Khema said, ^an6-49-2-3

and the teacher approved. ^an6-49-2-4

Then Khema, knowing that the teacher approved, rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an6-49-2-5

And then, not long after Khema had left, Sumana said to the Buddha: ^an6-49-3-1

“Sir, a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—does not think: ^an6-49-3-2

‘There is no-one better than me, or equal to me, or worse than me.’” ^an6-49-3-3

That is what Sumana said, ^an6-49-3-4

and the teacher approved. ^an6-49-3-5

Then Sumana, knowing that the teacher approved, rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an6-49-3-6

And then, soon after Khema and Sumana had left, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an6-49-4-1

“Mendicants, this is how gentlemen declare enlightenment. ^an6-49-4-2

The goal is spoken of, but the self is not involved. ^an6-49-4-3

But it seems that there are some futile men here who declare enlightenment as a joke. ^an6-49-4-4

Later they will fall into distress. ^an6-49-4-5

They don’t represent themselves ^an6-49-5-1

as being among superiors, inferiors, or equals. ^an6-49-5-2

Rebirth is ended, <j>the spiritual journey has been completed. ^an6-49-5-3

They live freed from fetters.” ^an6-49-5-4

### Sense Restraint (Numbered Discourses 6.50) ^an6-50-0

“Mendicants, when there is no sense restraint, one who lacks sense restraint has destroyed a vital condition for ethical conduct. ^an6-50-1-1

When there is no ethical conduct, one who lacks ethics has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an6-50-1-2

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an6-50-1-3

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an6-50-1-4

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an6-50-1-5

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. ^an6-50-1-6

Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an6-50-1-7

In the same way, when there is no sense restraint, one who lacks sense restraint has destroyed a vital condition for ethical conduct. … ^an6-50-1-8

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an6-50-1-9

When there is sense restraint, one who has fulfilled sense restraint has fulfilled a vital condition for ethical conduct. ^an6-50-2-1

When there is ethical conduct, one who has fulfilled ethical conduct has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an6-50-2-2

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an6-50-2-3

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an6-50-2-4

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an6-50-2-5

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would all grow to fullness. ^an6-50-2-6

In the same way, when there is sense restraint, one who has fulfilled sense restraint has fulfilled a vital condition for ethical conduct. … ^an6-50-2-7

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an6-50-2-8

### With Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 6.51) ^an6-51-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-51-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Ānanda sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an6-51-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, how does a mendicant get to hear a teaching they haven’t heard before? How do they remember those teachings they have heard? How do they keep exercising the teachings with which they are already familiar? And how do they come to understand what they haven’t understood before?” ^an6-51-2-1

“Well, Venerable Ānanda, you’re very learned. ^an6-51-2-2

Why don’t you clarify this yourself?” ^an6-51-2-3

“Well then, Reverend Sāriputta, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-51-2-4

“Yes, reverend,” Sāriputta replied. ^an6-51-2-5

Ānanda said this: ^an6-51-2-6

“Reverend Sāriputta, take a mendicant who memorizes the teaching— ^an6-51-3-1

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an6-51-3-2

Then, just as they learned and memorized it, they teach others in detail, make them recite in detail, they rehearse it themselves in detail, and they think about and consider the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind. ^an6-51-3-3

They enter the rains retreat in a monastery with senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an6-51-3-4

From time to time they go up to those mendicants and ask them questions: ^an6-51-3-5

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an6-51-3-6

Those venerables reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an6-51-3-7

This is how a mendicant gets to hear a teaching they haven’t heard before. It’s how they remember those teachings they have heard. It’s how they keep exercising the teachings with which they are already familiar. And it’s how they come to understand what they haven’t understood before.” ^an6-51-3-8

“It’s incredible, reverend, it’s amazing! How well said this was by Venerable Ānanda! ^an6-51-4-1

And we will remember Venerable Ānanda as someone who possesses these six qualities. ^an6-51-4-2

For Ānanda memorizes the teaching … ^an6-51-4-3

statements, mixed prose & verse, discussions, verses, inspired exclamations, legends, stories of past lives, amazing stories, and elaborations. ^an6-51-4-4

Those venerables reveal to Ānanda what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters.” ^an6-51-4-9

### Aristocrats (Numbered Discourses 6.52) ^an6-52-0

And then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-52-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an6-52-1-2

“Aristocrats, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-2-1

“Aristocrats, brahmin, have wealth as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with wisdom. They’re fixated on power. They insist on territory. Their ultimate goal is authority.” ^an6-52-2-2

“Brahmins, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-3-1

“Brahmins have wealth as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with wisdom. They’re fixated on the hymns. They insist on sacrifice. Their ultimate goal is the realm of divinity.” ^an6-52-3-2

“Householders, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-4-1

“Householders have wealth as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with wisdom. They’re fixated on their profession. They insist on work. Their ultimate goal is to complete their work.” ^an6-52-4-2

“Women, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-5-1

“Women have a man as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with adornments. They’re fixated on their children. They insist on being without a co-wife. Their ultimate goal is authority.” ^an6-52-5-2

“Bandits, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-6-1

“Bandits have theft as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with a hiding place. They’re fixated on their sword. They insist on darkness. Their ultimate goal is invisibility.” ^an6-52-6-2

“Ascetics, worthy Gotama, have what as their ambition? What is their preoccupation? What are they fixated on? What do they insist on? What is their ultimate goal?” ^an6-52-7-1

“Ascetics have patience and gentleness as their ambition. They’re preoccupied with wisdom. They’re fixated on ethical conduct. They insist on owning nothing. Their culmination is extinguishment.” ^an6-52-7-2

“It’s incredible, worthy Gotama, it’s amazing! ^an6-52-8-1

The worthy Gotama knows the ambition, preoccupation, fixation, insistence, and ultimate goal of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, women, bandits, and ascetics. ^an6-52-8-2

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an6-52-8-3

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an6-52-8-4

### Diligence (Numbered Discourses 6.53) ^an6-53-0

Then a certain brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an6-53-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an6-53-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, is there one thing that, when developed and cultivated, secures benefits for both this life and lives to come?” ^an6-53-2-1

“There is, brahmin.” ^an6-53-2-2

“So what is it?” ^an6-53-3-1

“Diligence, brahmin, is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, secures benefits for both this life and lives to come. ^an6-53-3-2

The footprints of all creatures that walk can fit inside an elephant’s footprint. So an elephant’s footprint is said to be the biggest of them all. ^an6-53-4-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, secures benefits for both this life and lives to come. ^an6-53-4-2

The rafters of a bungalow all lean to the peak, slope to the peak, and meet at the peak, so the peak is said to be the topmost of them all. ^an6-53-5-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing … ^an6-53-5-2

A reed-cutter, having cut the reeds, grabs them at the top and shakes them down, shakes them about, and shakes them off. ^an6-53-6-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing … ^an6-53-6-2

When the stalk of a bunch of mangoes is cut, all the mangoes attached to the stalk will follow along. ^an6-53-7-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing … ^an6-53-7-2

All lesser rulers are vassals of a wheel-turning monarch, so the wheel-turning monarch is said to be the foremost of them all. ^an6-53-8-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing … ^an6-53-8-2

The radiance of all the stars is not worth a sixteenth part of the moon’s radiance, so the moon’s radiance is said to be the best of them all. ^an6-53-9-1

In the same way, diligence is one thing that, when developed and cultivated, secures benefits for both this life and lives to come. ^an6-53-9-2

This is the one thing that, when developed and cultivated, secures benefits for both this life and lives to come.” ^an6-53-10-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an6-53-11-1

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an6-53-11-2

### About Dhammika (Numbered Discourses 6.54) ^an6-54-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an6-54-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Dhammika was a resident in all seven monasteries of his native land. ^an6-54-1-2

There he abused visiting mendicants; he insulted, harmed, attacked, and harassed them. ^an6-54-1-3

The visiting mendicants who were treated in this way did not stay. They left, abandoning the monastery. ^an6-54-1-4

Then the local lay followers thought to themselves, ^an6-54-2-1

“We have supplied the mendicant Saṅgha with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an6-54-2-2

But the visiting mendicants don’t stay. They leave, abandoning the monastery. ^an6-54-2-3

What is the cause, what is the reason for this?” ^an6-54-2-4

Then the local lay followers thought to themselves, ^an6-54-2-5

“This Venerable Dhammika abuses visiting mendicants; he insults, harms, attacks, and harasses them. ^an6-54-2-6

The visiting mendicants who were treated in this way do not stay. They leave, abandoning the monastery. ^an6-54-2-7

Why don’t we banish Venerable Dhammika?” ^an6-54-2-8

Then the local lay followers went up to Venerable Dhammika and said to him, ^an6-54-3-1

“Sir, please leave this monastery. ^an6-54-3-2

You’ve stayed here long enough.” ^an6-54-3-3

Then Venerable Dhammika left and went to another monastery. ^an6-54-3-4

There he abused visiting mendicants; he insulted, harmed, attacked, and harassed them. ^an6-54-3-5

The visiting mendicants who were treated in this way did not stay. They left, abandoning the monastery. ^an6-54-3-6

Then the local lay followers thought to themselves: … ^an6-54-4-1

They said to Venerable Dhammika, ^an6-54-5-1

“Sir, please leave this monastery. ^an6-54-5-2

You’ve stayed here long enough.” ^an6-54-5-3

Then Venerable Dhammika left and went to another monastery. ^an6-54-5-4

There he abused visiting mendicants; he insulted, harmed, attacked, and harassed them. ^an6-54-5-5

The visiting mendicants who were treated in this way did not stay. They left, abandoning the monastery. ^an6-54-5-6

Then the local lay followers thought to themselves, ^an6-54-6-1

“Why don’t we banish Venerable Dhammika from all seven monasteries in our native land?” ^an6-54-6-6

Then the local lay followers went up to Venerable Dhammika and said to him, ^an6-54-6-7

“Sir, please leave all seven monasteries in our native land.” ^an6-54-6-8

Then Venerable Dhammika thought, ^an6-54-6-9

“I’ve been banished by the local lay followers from all seven monasteries in my native land. ^an6-54-6-10

Where am I to go now?” ^an6-54-6-11

He thought, ^an6-54-6-12

“Why don’t I go to see the Buddha?” ^an6-54-6-13

Then Venerable Dhammika took his bowl and robe and set out for Rājagaha. ^an6-54-7-1

Eventually he came to Rājagaha and the Vulture’s Peak. He went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an6-54-7-2

“So, Brahmin Dhammika, where have you come from?” ^an6-54-7-3

“Sir, I’ve been banished by the local lay followers from all seven monasteries in my native land.” ^an6-54-7-4

“Enough, Brahmin Dhammika, what’s that to you? Now that you’ve been banished from all of those places, you have come to me. ^an6-54-7-5

Once upon a time, some sea-merchants set sail for the ocean deeps, taking with them a land-spotting bird. ^an6-54-8-1

When their ship was out of sight of land, they released the bird. ^an6-54-8-2

It flew right away to the east, the west, the north, the south, upwards, and in-between. ^an6-54-8-3

If it saw land on any side, it went there and stayed. ^an6-54-8-4

But if it saw no land on any side it returned to the ship. ^an6-54-8-5

In the same way, now that you’ve been banished from all of those places, you have come to me. ^an6-54-8-6

Once upon a time, King Koravya had a royal banyan tree with five trunks called ‘Well Planted’. It was shady and lovely. ^an6-54-9-1

Its canopy spread over twelve leagues, while the network of roots spread for five leagues. ^an6-54-9-2

Its fruits were as large as ^an6-54-9-3

a rice pot. ^an6-54-9-4

And they were as sweet as ^an6-54-9-5

pure wild honey. ^an6-54-9-6

The king and harem made use of one trunk, the troops another, the people of town and country another, ascetics and brahmins another, and beasts and birds another. ^an6-54-9-7

No-one guarded the fruit, yet no-one damaged another’s fruits. ^an6-54-9-8

Then a certain person ate as much as he liked of the fruit, then broke off a branch and left. ^an6-54-10-1

Then the deity haunting the royal banyan tree thought, ^an6-54-10-2

‘Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^an6-54-10-3

How wicked this person is, to eat as much as they like, then break off a branch and leave! Why don’t I make sure that the royal banyan tree gives no fruit in future?’ ^an6-54-10-4

Then the royal banyan tree gave no more fruit. ^an6-54-10-5

Then King Koravya went up to Sakka, lord of gods, and said to him, ^an6-54-11-1

‘Please good fellow, you should know that the royal banyan tree called Well Planted gives no fruit.’ ^an6-54-11-2

Then Sakka used his psychic powers to will that a violent storm come. And it felled and uprooted the royal banyan tree. ^an6-54-11-3

Then the deity haunting the tree stood to one side, miserable and sad, weeping, with a tearful face. ^an6-54-11-4

Then Sakka went up to that deity, and said, ^an6-54-12-1

‘Why, god, are you standing to one side, miserable and sad, weeping, with a tearful face?’ ^an6-54-12-2

‘Because, my good fellow, a violent storm came and felled and uprooted my home.’ ^an6-54-12-3

‘Well, did you stand by your tree’s duty when the storm came?’ ^an6-54-12-4

‘But my good fellow, how does a tree stand by its duty?’ ^an6-54-12-5

‘It’s when those who need the tree’s roots, bark, leaves, flowers, or fruit take what they need. ^an6-54-12-6

Yet the deity is not displeased or upset because of this. ^an6-54-12-7

This is how a tree stands by its duty.’ ^an6-54-12-8

‘I was not standing by a tree’s duty when the storm came and felled and uprooted my home.’ ^an6-54-12-9

‘God, if you were to stand by a tree’s duty, your home may be as it was before.’ ^an6-54-12-10

‘I will stand by a tree’s duty! May my home be as it was before!’ ^an6-54-12-11

Then Sakka used his psychic power to will that a violent storm come. And it raised up that mighty banyan tree and the bark of the roots was healed. ^an6-54-13-1

In the same way, Brahmin Dhammika, were you standing by an ascetic’s duty when the local lay followers banished you from all seven of the monasteries in your native land?” ^an6-54-13-2

“But sir, how do I stand by an ascetic’s duty?” ^an6-54-13-3

“When someone abuses, annoys, or argues with an ascetic, the ascetic doesn’t abuse, annoy, or argue back at them. ^an6-54-13-4

That’s how an ascetic stands by an ascetic’s duty.” ^an6-54-13-5

“I was not standing by an ascetic’s duty when the local lay followers banished me from all seven of the monasteries in my native land.” ^an6-54-13-6

“Once upon a time, there was a teacher named Sunetta. He was a religious founder and was free of desire for sensual pleasures. ^an6-54-13-7

He had many hundreds of disciples. ^an6-54-13-8

He taught them the path to rebirth in the company of Divinity. ^an6-54-13-9

Those lacking confidence in Sunetta were—when their body broke up, after death—reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an6-54-13-10

Those full of confidence in Sunetta were—when their body broke up, after death—reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an6-54-13-11

Once upon a time there was a teacher named Mūgapakkha … ^an6-54-14-1

Aranemi … ^an6-54-15-1

Kuddālaka … ^an6-54-16-1

Hatthipāla … ^an6-54-17-1

Jotipāla. He was a religious founder and was free of desire for sensual pleasures. ^an6-54-18-1

He had many hundreds of disciples. ^an6-54-18-2

He taught them the way to rebirth in the company of Divinity. ^an6-54-18-3

Those lacking confidence in Jotipāla were—when their body broke up, after death—reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an6-54-18-4

Those full of confidence in Jotipāla were—when their body broke up, after death—reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an6-54-18-5

What do you think, Brahmin Dhammika? ^an6-54-19-1

If someone with malicious intent were to abuse and insult these six teachers with their hundreds of followers, would they not brim with much wickedness?” ^an6-54-19-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-54-19-3

“They would indeed. ^an6-54-19-4

But someone who abuses and insults a single individual accomplished in view with malicious intent brims even more with wickedness. ^an6-54-19-5

Why is that? ^an6-54-19-6

Brahmin Dhammika, I say that any injury done by those outside of the Buddhist community does not compare with what is done to one’s own spiritual companions. ^an6-54-19-7

So you should train like this: ^an6-54-19-8

‘We will have no malicious intent for those who we want to have as our spiritual companions.’ ^an6-54-19-9

That is how you should train. ^an6-54-19-10

Sunetta and Mūgapakkha, ^an6-54-20-1

and Aranemi the brahmin, ^an6-54-20-2

Hatthipāla the student, ^an6-54-20-3

and Kuddālaka were Teachers. ^an6-54-20-4

And Jotipāla Govinda ^an6-54-21-1

was priest for seven kings. ^an6-54-21-2

These six famous teachers, ^an6-54-21-3

harmless ones of the past, ^an6-54-21-4

were free of putrefaction, compassionate, ^an6-54-22-1

gone beyond the fetter of sensuality. ^an6-54-22-2

Detached from sensual desire, ^an6-54-22-3

they were reborn in the realm of divinity. ^an6-54-22-4

Many hundreds of ^an6-54-23-1

their disciples were also ^an6-54-23-2

free of putrefaction-stench, compassionate, ^an6-54-23-3

gone beyond the fetter of sensuality. ^an6-54-23-4

Detached from sensual desire, ^an6-54-23-5

they were reborn in the realm of divinity. ^an6-54-23-6

One who insults ^an6-54-24-1

with malicious intent ^an6-54-24-2

these non-Buddhist seers, ^an6-54-24-3

free of desire, immersed in samādhi; ^an6-54-24-4

such a man ^an6-54-24-5

brims with much wickedness. ^an6-54-24-6

But one who insults ^an6-54-25-1

with malicious intent ^an6-54-25-2

a single individual accomplished in view, ^an6-54-25-3

a mendicant disciple of the Buddha; ^an6-54-25-4

that man ^an6-54-25-5

brims even more with wickedness. ^an6-54-25-6

You shouldn’t attack a holy person, ^an6-54-26-1

who has given up the grounds for views. ^an6-54-26-2

This individual is called ^an6-54-26-3

the seventh of the noble Saṅgha. ^an6-54-26-4

They’re not free of desire for sensual pleasures, ^an6-54-27-1

and their faculties are still immature: ^an6-54-27-2

faith, mindfulness, and energy, ^an6-54-27-3

serenity and discernment. ^an6-54-27-4

If you attack such a mendicant, ^an6-54-28-1

you first hurt yourself. ^an6-54-28-2

Having hurt yourself, ^an6-54-28-3

you harm the other. ^an6-54-28-4

But if you protect yourself, ^an6-54-29-1

the other is also protected. ^an6-54-29-2

So you should protect yourself. ^an6-54-29-3

An astute person is always uninjured.” ^an6-54-29-4

### With Soṇa (Numbered Discourses 6.55) ^an6-55-0

So I have heard. ^an6-55-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an6-55-1-2

Now at that time Venerable Soṇa was staying near Rājagaha in the Cool Grove. ^an6-55-1-3

Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind, ^an6-55-1-4

“I am one of the Buddha’s most energetic disciples. ^an6-55-1-5

Yet my mind is not freed from defilements by not grasping. But my family has wealth. I could enjoy that wealth and make merit. ^an6-55-1-6

Why don’t I resign the training and return to a lesser life, so I can enjoy my wealth and make merit?” ^an6-55-1-7

Then the Buddha knew Venerable Soṇa’s train of thought. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the Vulture’s Peak and reappeared in the Cool Grove in front of Soṇa, ^an6-55-2-1

and sat on the seat spread out. ^an6-55-2-2

Soṇa bowed to the Buddha and sat down to one side. ^an6-55-2-3

The Buddha said to him, ^an6-55-2-4

“Soṇa, as you were in private retreat didn’t this thought come to your mind: ^an6-55-3-1

‘I am one of the Buddha’s most energetic disciples. ^an6-55-3-2

Yet my mind is not freed from defilements by not grasping. But my family has wealth. I could enjoy that wealth and make merit. ^an6-55-3-3

Why don’t I resign the training and return to a lesser life, so I can enjoy my wealth and make merit?’” ^an6-55-3-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-55-3-5

“What do you think, Soṇa? ^an6-55-4-1

When you were still a layman, weren’t you a good player of the arched harp?” ^an6-55-4-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-55-4-3

“When your harp’s strings were tuned too tight, was it resonant and playable?” ^an6-55-4-4

“No, sir.” ^an6-55-4-5

“When your harp’s strings were tuned too slack, was it resonant and playable?” ^an6-55-5-1

“No, sir.” ^an6-55-5-2

“But when your harp’s strings were tuned neither too tight nor too slack, but fixed at an even tension, was it resonant and playable?” ^an6-55-6-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-55-6-2

“In the same way, Soṇa, when energy is too forceful it leads to restlessness. When energy is too slack it leads to laziness. ^an6-55-7-1

So, Soṇa, you should focus on energy and serenity, find a balance of the faculties, and learn the character of this situation.” ^an6-55-7-2

“Yes, sir,” Soṇa replied. ^an6-55-7-3

After advising Soṇa like this, the Buddha, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from the Cool Grove and reappeared on the Vulture’s Peak. ^an6-55-7-4

After some time Soṇa focused on energy and serenity, found a balance of the faculties, and learned the pattern of this situation. ^an6-55-8-1

Then Soṇa, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an6-55-8-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an6-55-8-3

And Venerable Soṇa became one of the perfected. ^an6-55-8-4

Then, when Soṇa had attained perfection, he thought, ^an6-55-9-1

“Why don’t I go to the Buddha and declare my enlightenment in his presence?” ^an6-55-9-2

Then Soṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-55-9-3

“Sir, a mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—is dedicated to six things. ^an6-55-10-1

They are dedicated to renunciation, seclusion, kindness, the ending of craving, the ending of grasping, and mental clarity. ^an6-55-10-2

It may be, sir, that one of the venerables here thinks: ^an6-55-11-1

‘Maybe this venerable is dedicated to renunciation solely out of mere faith.’ ^an6-55-11-2

But it should not be seen like this. ^an6-55-11-3

A mendicant with defilements ended does not see in themselves anything more to do, or anything that needs improvement. They’re dedicated to renunciation because they’re free of greed, hate, and delusion with the end of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-55-11-4

It may be, sir, that one of the venerables here thinks: ^an6-55-12-1

‘Maybe this venerable is dedicated to seclusion because they enjoy possessions, honor, and popularity.’ ^an6-55-12-2

But it should not be seen like this. … ^an6-55-12-3

It may be, sir, that one of the venerables here thinks: ^an6-55-13-1

‘Maybe this venerable is dedicated to kindness because they believe that adhering to precepts and observances is essential.’ ^an6-55-13-2

But it should not be seen like this. … ^an6-55-13-3

They’re dedicated to the ending of craving because they’re free of greed, hate, and delusion with the end of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-55-14-1

They’re dedicated to the ending of grasping because they’re free of greed, hate, and delusion with the end of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-55-15-1

They’re dedicated to clarity of mind because they’re free of greed, hate, and delusion with the end of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-55-16-1

When a mendicant’s mind is rightly freed like this, even if compelling sights come into the range of vision they don’t overcome their mind. ^an6-55-17-1

Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance. ^an6-55-17-2

Even if compelling sounds … ^an6-55-17-3

smells … ^an6-55-17-4

tastes … ^an6-55-17-5

touches … ^an6-55-17-6

and ideas come into the range of the mind they don’t overcome the mind. ^an6-55-17-7

Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance. ^an6-55-17-8

Suppose there was a mountain that was one solid mass of rock, without cracks or holes. ^an6-55-17-9

Even if violent storms were to blow up out of the east, the west, the north, and the south, ^an6-55-17-10

they couldn’t make it shake or rock or tremble. ^an6-55-17-12

In the same way, when a mendicant’s mind is rightly freed like this, even if compelling sights come into the range of vision they don’t overcome their mind. … ^an6-55-17-13
