Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance. ^an6-55-17-20

When you’re dedicated to renunciation ^an6-55-18-1

and seclusion of the heart; ^an6-55-18-2

when you’re dedicated to kindness ^an6-55-18-3

and the end of grasping; ^an6-55-18-4

when you’re dedicated to the ending of craving ^an6-55-19-1

and clarity of heart; ^an6-55-19-2

and you’ve seen the arising of the senses, ^an6-55-19-3

your mind is rightly freed. ^an6-55-19-4

For that one, rightly freed, ^an6-55-20-1

a mendicant with peaceful mind, ^an6-55-20-2

there’s nothing to be improved, ^an6-55-20-3

and nothing more to do. ^an6-55-20-4

As the wind cannot stir ^an6-55-21-1

a solid mass of rock, ^an6-55-21-2

so too sights, tastes, sounds, ^an6-55-21-3

smells, and touches—the lot— ^an6-55-21-4

and ideas, whether liked or disliked, ^an6-55-22-1

don’t disturb the unaffected one. ^an6-55-22-2

Their mind is steady and free ^an6-55-22-3

as they observe fall.” ^an6-55-22-4

### With Phagguna (Numbered Discourses 6.56) ^an6-56-0

Now at that time Venerable Phagguna was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an6-56-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-56-1-2

“Sir, Venerable Phagguna is sick. ^an6-56-1-3

Sir, please go to Venerable Phagguna out of sympathy.” ^an6-56-1-4

The Buddha consented with silence. ^an6-56-1-5

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to Venerable Phagguna. ^an6-56-1-6

Venerable Phagguna saw the Buddha coming off in the distance ^an6-56-1-7

and stirred in his cot. ^an6-56-1-8

The Buddha said to him, ^an6-56-1-9

“It’s all right, Phagguna, don’t get up. ^an6-56-1-10

There are some seats spread out by others, I will sit there.” ^an6-56-1-11

He sat on the seat spread out ^an6-56-1-12

and said to Venerable Phagguna: ^an6-56-1-13

“I hope you’re keeping well, Phagguna; I hope you’re all right. And I hope the pain is fading, not growing, that its fading is evident, not its growing.” ^an6-56-2-1

“Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading. ^an6-56-2-2

The winds piercing my head are so severe, it feels like a strong man drilling into my head with a sharp point. ^an6-56-3-1

I’m not keeping well. ^an6-56-3-2

The pain in my head is so severe, it feels like a strong man tightening a strong leather strap around my head. ^an6-56-4-1

I’m not keeping well. ^an6-56-4-2

The winds slicing my belly are so severe, like a deft butcher or their apprentice were slicing open a cows’s belly with a sharp meat cleaver. ^an6-56-5-1

I’m not keeping well. ^an6-56-5-2

The burning in my body is so severe, it feels like two strong men grabbing a weaker man by the arms to burn and scorch him on a pit of glowing coals. ^an6-56-6-1

I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.” ^an6-56-6-2

Then the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired Venerable Phagguna with a Dhamma talk, after which he rose from his seat and left. ^an6-56-6-3

Not long after the Buddha left, Venerable Phagguna passed away. ^an6-56-7-1

At the time of his death, his faculties were bright and clear. ^an6-56-7-2

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an6-56-7-3

“Sir, soon after the Buddha left, Venerable Phagguna died. ^an6-56-7-4

At the time of his death, his faculties were bright and clear.” ^an6-56-7-5

“And why shouldn’t his faculties be bright and clear? ^an6-56-8-1

The mendicant Phagguna’s mind was not freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-8-2

But when he heard that teaching his mind was freed from them. ^an6-56-8-3

Ānanda, there are these six benefits to hearing the teaching at the right time and examining the meaning at the right time. ^an6-56-9-1

What six? ^an6-56-9-2

Firstly, take the case of a mendicant whose mind is not freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-9-3

At the time of death they get to see the Realized One. ^an6-56-9-4

The Realized One proclaims a teaching to them that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an6-56-9-5

When they hear that teaching their mind is freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-9-6

This is the first benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-9-7

Next, take the case of another mendicant whose mind is not freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-10-1

At the time of death they don’t get to see the Realized One, but they get to see a Realized One’s disciple. ^an6-56-10-2

The Realized One’s disciple teaches them Dhamma … ^an6-56-10-3

When they hear that teaching their mind is freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-10-4

This is the second benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-10-5

Next, take the case of another mendicant whose mind is not freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-11-1

At the time of death they don’t get to see the Realized One, or to see a Realized One’s disciple. ^an6-56-11-2

But they think about and consider the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it. ^an6-56-11-3

As they do so their mind is freed from the five lower fetters. ^an6-56-11-4

This is the third benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-11-5

Next, take the case of a mendicant whose mind is freed from the five lower fetters, but not with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-12-1

At the time of death they get to see the Realized One. ^an6-56-12-2

The Realized One teaches them Dhamma … ^an6-56-12-3

When they hear that teaching their mind is freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-12-4

This is the fourth benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-12-5

Next, take the case of another mendicant whose mind is freed from the five lower fetters, but not with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-13-1

At the time of death they don’t get to see the Realized One, but they get to see a Realized One’s disciple. ^an6-56-13-2

The Realized One’s disciple teaches them Dhamma … ^an6-56-13-3

When they hear that teaching their mind is freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-13-4

This is the fifth benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-13-5

Next, take the case of another mendicant whose mind is freed from the five lower fetters, but not with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-14-1

At the time of death they don’t get to see the Realized One, or to see a Realized One’s disciple. ^an6-56-14-2

But they think about and consider the teaching in their heart, examining it with the mind as they learned and memorized it. ^an6-56-14-3

As they do so their mind is freed with the supreme ending of attachments. ^an6-56-14-4

This is the sixth benefit of listening to the teaching. ^an6-56-14-5

These are the six benefits to hearing the teaching at the right time and examining the meaning at the right time.” ^an6-56-15-1

### The Six Classes of Rebirth (Numbered Discourses 6.57) ^an6-57-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an6-57-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an6-57-1-2

“Sir, Pūraṇa Kassapa describes six classes of rebirth: ^an6-57-1-3

black, blue, red, yellow, white, and ultimate white. ^an6-57-1-4

The black class of rebirth consists of slaughterers of sheep, pigs, poultry, or deer, hunters or fishers, bandits, executioners, butchers of cattle, jailers, and any others with a cruel livelihood. ^an6-57-2-1

The blue class of rebirth consists of mendicants whose life is thorny, and any others who teach the efficacy of deeds and action. ^an6-57-3-1

The red class of rebirth consists of the Jain ascetics who wear one cloth. ^an6-57-4-1

The yellow class of rebirth consists of the lay people dressed in white who are disciples of the naked ascetics. ^an6-57-5-1

The white class of rebirth consists of male and female Ājīvaka ascetics. ^an6-57-6-1

And the ultimate white class of rebirth consists of Nanda Vaccha, Kisa Saṅkicca, and the bamboo-staffed ascetic Gosāla. ^an6-57-7-1

These are the six classes of rebirth that Pūraṇa Kassapa describes.” ^an6-57-8-1

“But Ānanda, did the whole world authorize Pūraṇa Kassapa to describe these six classes of rebirth?” ^an6-57-9-1

“No, sir.” ^an6-57-9-2

“It’s as if they were to force a chop on a poor, penniless person, telling them, ‘Eat this meat and pay for it!’ ^an6-57-9-3

In the same way, Pūraṇa Kassapa has described these six classes of rebirth without the consent of those ascetics and brahmins. And he has done so in a foolish, incompetent, unskilled way, lacking common sense. ^an6-57-9-4

I, however, also describe six classes of rebirth. ^an6-57-10-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-57-10-2

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an6-57-10-3

The Buddha said this: ^an6-57-10-4

“And what, Ānanda, are the six classes of rebirth? ^an6-57-10-5

Someone born into a dark class gives rise to a dark result. ^an6-57-10-6

Someone born into a dark class gives rise to a bright result. ^an6-57-10-7

Someone born into a dark class gives rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-10-8

Someone born into a bright class gives rise to a dark result. ^an6-57-10-9

Someone born into a bright class gives rise to a bright result. ^an6-57-10-10

Someone born into a bright class gives rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-10-11

And how does someone born into a dark class give rise to a dark result? ^an6-57-11-1

It’s when someone is reborn in a low family—a family of corpse-workers, hunters, bamboo-workers, chariot-makers, or scavengers—poor, with little to eat or drink, where life is tough, and food and shelter are hard to find. ^an6-57-11-2

And they’re ugly, unsightly, deformed, sickly—one-eyed, crippled, lame, or half-paralyzed. They don’t get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; or bed, house, and lighting. ^an6-57-11-3

And they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-57-11-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an6-57-11-5

That’s how someone born into a dark class gives rise to a dark result. ^an6-57-11-6

And how does someone born into a dark class give rise to a bright result? ^an6-57-12-1

It’s when some person is reborn in a low family … ^an6-57-12-2

But they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-57-12-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an6-57-12-5

That’s how someone born into a dark class gives rise to a bright result. ^an6-57-12-6

And how does someone born into a dark class give rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright? ^an6-57-13-1

It’s when some person is reborn in a low family … ^an6-57-13-2

They shave off their hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an6-57-13-4

They give up the five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. They firmly establish their mind in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. They truly develop the seven awakening factors. And then they give rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-13-5

That’s how someone born in a dark class gives rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-13-6

And how does someone born into a bright class give rise to a dark result? ^an6-57-14-1

It’s when someone is reborn in an eminent family—a well-to-do family of aristocrats, brahmins, or householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. ^an6-57-14-2

And they’re attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty. They get to have food, drink, clothes, and vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an6-57-14-3

But they do bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-57-14-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an6-57-14-5

That’s how someone born into a bright class gives rise to a dark result. ^an6-57-14-6

And how does someone born into a bright class give rise to a bright result? ^an6-57-15-1

It’s when some person is reborn in an eminent family … ^an6-57-15-2

And they do good things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-57-15-4

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an6-57-15-5

That’s how someone born into a bright class give rise to a bright result. ^an6-57-15-6

And how does someone born into a bright class give rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright? ^an6-57-16-1

It’s when some person is reborn in an eminent family … ^an6-57-16-2

They shave off their hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an6-57-16-4

They give up the five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. They firmly establish their mind in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. They truly develop the seven awakening factors. And then they give rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-16-5

That’s how someone born into a bright class gives rise to extinguishment, which is neither dark nor bright. ^an6-57-16-6

These are the six classes of rebirth.” ^an6-57-17-1

### Defilements (Numbered Discourses 6.58) ^an6-58-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an6-58-1-1

What six? ^an6-58-2-1

It’s a mendicant who, by restraint, has given up the defilements that should be given up by restraint. ^an6-58-2-2

By using, they’ve given up the defilements that should be given up by using. ^an6-58-2-3

By enduring, they’ve given up the defilements that should be given up by enduring. ^an6-58-2-4

By avoiding, they’ve given up the defilements that should be given up by avoiding. ^an6-58-2-5

By getting rid, they’ve given up the defilements that should be given up by getting rid. ^an6-58-2-6

By developing, they’ve given up the defilements that should be given up by developing. ^an6-58-2-7

And what are the defilements that should be given up by restraint? ^an6-58-3-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, lives restraining the eye faculty. ^an6-58-3-2

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without restraint of the eye faculty do not arise when there is such restraint. ^an6-58-3-3

Reflecting rationally, they live restraining the ear faculty … ^an6-58-3-4

the nose faculty … ^an6-58-3-5

the tongue faculty … ^an6-58-3-6

the body faculty … ^an6-58-3-7

the mind faculty. ^an6-58-3-8

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without restraint of the mind faculty do not arise when there is such restraint. ^an6-58-3-9

These are called the defilements that should be given up by restraint. ^an6-58-3-10

And what are the defilements that should be given up by using? ^an6-58-4-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, makes use of robes: ^an6-58-4-2

‘Only for the sake of warding off cold and heat; for warding off the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles; and for covering up the private parts.’ ^an6-58-4-3

Reflecting rationally, they make use of almsfood: ^an6-58-4-4

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^an6-58-4-5

Reflecting rationally, they make use of lodgings: ^an6-58-4-6

‘Only for the sake of warding off cold and heat; for warding off the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles; to shelter from harsh weather and to enjoy retreat.’ ^an6-58-4-7

Reflecting rationally, they make use of medicines and supplies for the sick: ^an6-58-4-8

‘Only for the sake of warding off the pains of illness and to promote good health.’ ^an6-58-4-9

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without using these things do not arise when they are used. ^an6-58-4-10

These are called the defilements that should be given up by using. ^an6-58-4-11

And what are the defilements that should be given up by enduring? ^an6-58-5-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, endures cold, heat, hunger, and thirst. They endure the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. They endure rude and unwelcome criticism. And they put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening. ^an6-58-5-2

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without enduring these things do not arise when they are endured. ^an6-58-5-3

These are called the defilements that should be given up by enduring. ^an6-58-5-4

And what are the defilements that should be given up by avoiding? ^an6-58-6-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, avoids a wild elephant, a wild horse, a wild ox, a wild dog, a snake, a stump, thorny ground, a pit, a cliff, a swamp, and a sewer. Reflecting rationally, they avoid sitting on inappropriate seats, walking in inappropriate neighborhoods, and mixing with bad friends—whatever sensible spiritual companions would believe to be a bad setting. ^an6-58-6-2

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without avoiding these things do not arise when they are avoided. ^an6-58-6-3

These are called the defilements that should be given up by avoiding. ^an6-58-6-4

And what are the defilements that should be given up by getting rid? ^an6-58-7-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, doesn’t tolerate a sensual, ^an6-58-7-2

malicious, ^an6-58-7-3

or cruel thought that has arisen. ^an6-58-7-4

They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an6-58-7-5

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without getting rid of these things do not arise when they are gotten rid of. ^an6-58-7-6

These are called the defilements that should be given up by getting rid. ^an6-58-7-7

And what are the defilements that should be given up by developing? ^an6-58-8-1

Take a mendicant who, reflecting rationally, develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, ^an6-58-8-2

investigation of principles, ^an6-58-8-3

energy, ^an6-58-8-4

rapture, ^an6-58-8-5

tranquility, ^an6-58-8-6

immersion, ^an6-58-8-7

and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go. ^an6-58-8-8

For the distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without developing these things do not arise when they are developed. ^an6-58-8-9

These are called the defilements that should be given up by developing. ^an6-58-8-10

A mendicant with these six qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an6-58-9-1

### With Dārukammika (Numbered Discourses 6.59) ^an6-59-0

So I have heard. ^an6-59-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^an6-59-1-2

Then the householder Dārukammika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an6-59-1-3

“Householder, I wonder whether your family gives gifts?” ^an6-59-1-4

“It does, sir. ^an6-59-1-5

Gifts are given to those mendicants who are perfected or on the path to perfection; they live in the wilderness, eat only almsfood, and wear rag robes.” ^an6-59-1-6

“Householder, as a layman enjoying sensual pleasures, living at home with your children, using sandalwood imported from Kāsi, wearing garlands, fragrance, and makeup, and accepting gold and currency, it’s hard for you to know who is perfected or on the path to perfection. ^an6-59-2-1

If a mendicant living in the wilderness is restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties, ^an6-59-3-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-3-2

If a mendicant living in the wilderness is not restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, or loose-tongued, but has established mindfulness, situational awareness and immersion, with unified mind and restrained faculties, ^an6-59-3-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-3-4

If a mendicant who lives within a village is restless … ^an6-59-4-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-4-2

If a mendicant who lives within a village is not restless … ^an6-59-4-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-4-4

If a mendicant who eats only almsfood is restless … ^an6-59-5-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-5-2

If a mendicant who eats only almsfood is not restless … ^an6-59-5-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-5-4

If a mendicant who accepts invitations is restless … ^an6-59-6-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-6-2

If a mendicant who accepts invitations is not restless … ^an6-59-6-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-6-4

If a mendicant who wears rag robes is restless … ^an6-59-7-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-7-2

If a mendicant who wears rag robes is not restless … ^an6-59-7-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-7-4

If a mendicant who wears robes offered by householders is restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties, ^an6-59-8-1

then in this respect they’re reprehensible. ^an6-59-8-2

If a mendicant who wears robes offered by householders is not restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, or loose-tongued, but has established mindfulness, situational awareness and immersion, with unified mind and restrained faculties, ^an6-59-8-3

then in this respect they’re praiseworthy. ^an6-59-8-4

Go ahead, householder, give gifts to the Saṅgha. ^an6-59-9-1

Your mind will become bright and clear, ^an6-59-9-2

and when your body breaks up, after death, you’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^an6-59-9-3

“Sir, from this day forth I will give gifts to the Saṅgha.” ^an6-59-9-4

### With Hatthisāriputta (Numbered Discourses 6.60) ^an6-60-0

So I have heard. ^an6-60-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^an6-60-1-2

Now at that time several senior mendicants, after the meal, on their return from almsround, sat together in the pavilion talking about the teachings. ^an6-60-1-3

Venerable Citta Hatthisāriputta interrupted them while they were talking. ^an6-60-1-4

Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita said to Venerable Citta Hatthisāriputta, ^an6-60-1-5

“Venerable, please don’t interrupt the senior mendicants while they’re talking about the teachings. Wait until the end of the discussion.” ^an6-60-1-6

When he said this, Citta Hatthisāriputta’s companions said to Mahākoṭṭhita, ^an6-60-1-7

“Venerable, please don’t rebuke Citta Hatthisāriputta. He is astute, ^an6-60-1-8

and quite capable of talking about the teachings with the senior mendicants.” ^an6-60-1-9

“It’s not easy to know this, reverends, for those who don’t encompass another’s mind. ^an6-60-2-1

Take an individual who is the gentlest of the gentle, the most placid of the placid, the calmest of the calm, so long as they live relying on the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role. ^an6-60-2-2

But when they’re separated from the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role, they mix closely with monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; with rulers and their chief ministers, and with monastics of other religions and their disciples. ^an6-60-2-3

As they mix closely, they become intimate and loose, spending time chatting, and so lust infects their mind. ^an6-60-2-4

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-2-5

Suppose an ox fond of crops was tied up or shut in a pen. ^an6-60-3-1

Would it be right to say that that ox will never again invade the crops?” ^an6-60-3-2

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-3-3

For it’s quite possible that that ox will snap the ropes or break out of the pen, and then invade the crops.” ^an6-60-3-4

“In the same way, take an individual who is the gentlest of the gentle … ^an6-60-3-5

As they mix closely, they become intimate and loose, spending time chatting, and so lust infects their mind. ^an6-60-3-7

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-3-8

And take an individual who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an6-60-4-1

Thinking, ‘I get the first absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-4-2

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-4-3

Suppose the heavens were raining heavily at the crossroads so that the dust vanished and mud appeared. ^an6-60-4-4

Would it be right to say that now dust will never appear at this crossroad again?” ^an6-60-4-5

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-4-6

For it is quite possible that people or cattle and so on will cross over the crossroad, or that the wind and sun will evaporate the moisture so that the dust appears again.” ^an6-60-4-7

“In the same way, take an individual who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an6-60-4-8

Thinking, ‘I get the first absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-4-9

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-4-10

And take an individual who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … enters and remains in the second absorption. ^an6-60-5-1

Thinking, ‘I get the second absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-5-2

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-5-3

Suppose there was a large pond not far from a town or village. ^an6-60-5-4

After the heavens have rained heavily there, the clams and mussels, and pebbles and gravel would vanish. ^an6-60-5-5

Would it be right to say that now the clams and mussels, and pebbles and gravel will never appear here again?” ^an6-60-5-6

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-5-7

For it’s quite possible that people or cattle and so on will drink from the pond, or that the wind and sun will evaporate it so that the clams and mussels, and pebbles and gravel appear again.” ^an6-60-5-8

“In the same way, take an individual who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … enters and remains in the second absorption. ^an6-60-5-9

Thinking, ‘I get the second absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-5-10

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-5-11

And take an individual who, with the fading away of rapture … enters and remains in the third absorption. ^an6-60-6-1

Thinking, ‘I get the third absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-6-2

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-6-3

Suppose a person had finished a delicious meal. They’d have no appetite for leftovers. ^an6-60-6-4

Would it be right to say that now food will never appeal to this person again?” ^an6-60-6-5

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-6-6

For it’s quite possible that other food won’t appeal to that person as long as the nourishment is still present. ^an6-60-6-7

But when the nourishment vanishes food will appeal again.” ^an6-60-6-8

“In the same way, take an individual who, with the fading away of rapture … enters and remains in the third absorption. ^an6-60-6-9

Thinking, ‘I get the third absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-6-10

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-6-11

And take an individual who, giving up pleasure and pain … enters and remains in the fourth absorption. ^an6-60-7-1

Thinking, ‘I get the fourth absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-7-2

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-7-3

Suppose that in a mountain glen there was a lake, placid and free of waves. ^an6-60-7-4

Would it be right to say that now waves will never appear in this lake again?” ^an6-60-7-5

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-7-6

For it is quite possible that a violent storm could blow up out of the east, west, north, or south, and stir up waves in that lake.” ^an6-60-7-7

“In the same way, take an individual who, giving up pleasure and pain … enters and remains in the fourth absorption. ^an6-60-7-8

Thinking, ‘I get the fourth absorption!’ they mix closely with monks … ^an6-60-7-9

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-7-10

And take an individual who, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart. ^an6-60-8-1

Thinking, ‘I get the signless immersion of the heart!’ they mix closely with monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; with rulers and their chief ministers, and with monastics of other religions and their disciples. ^an6-60-8-2

As they mix closely, they become intimate and loose, spending time chatting, and so lust infects their mind. ^an6-60-8-3

They resign the training and return to a lesser life. ^an6-60-8-4

Suppose a ruler or their chief minister, while walking along the road with an army of four divisions, was to arrive at a forest grove where they set up camp for the night. ^an6-60-8-5

There, because of the noise of the elephants, horses, chariots, soldiers, and the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals, the chirping of crickets would vanish. ^an6-60-8-6

Would it be right to say that now the chirping of crickets will never be heard in this woodland grove again?” ^an6-60-8-7

“No it would not, reverend. ^an6-60-8-8

For it is quite possible that the ruler or their chief minister will leave that woodland grove so that the chirping of crickets will be heard there again.” ^an6-60-8-9

“In the same way, take an individual who, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart … ^an6-60-8-10

They resign the training and return to a lesser life.” ^an6-60-8-13

Then after some time Venerable Citta Hatthisāriputta resigned the training and returned to a lesser life. ^an6-60-9-1

Then the mendicants who were his companions went up to Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita and said, ^an6-60-9-2

“Did Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita encompass Citta Hatthisāriputta’s mind and know that ^an6-60-9-3

he had gained such and such meditative attainments, yet he would still resign the training and return to a lesser life? ^an6-60-9-4

Or did deities tell you about it?” ^an6-60-9-5

“Reverends, I encompassed his mind and knew this. ^an6-60-9-7

And deities also told me.” ^an6-60-9-9

Then the mendicants who were Citta Hatthisāriputta’s companions went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an6-60-10-1

“Sir, Citta Hatthisāriputta, who had gained such and such meditative attainments, has still resigned the training and returned to a lesser life.” ^an6-60-10-2

“Mendicants, soon Citta will remember renunciation.” ^an6-60-10-3

And not long after Citta Hatthisāriputta shaved off his hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an6-60-11-1

Then Citta Hatthisāriputta, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an6-60-11-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an6-60-11-3

And Venerable Citta Hatthisāriputta became one of the perfected. ^an6-60-11-4

### In the Middle (Numbered Discourses 6.61) ^an6-61-0

So I have heard. ^an6-61-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana. ^an6-61-1-2

Now at that time, after the meal, on return from almsround, several senior mendicants sat together in the pavilion and this discussion came up among them, ^an6-61-1-3

“Reverends, this was said by the Buddha in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Metteyya’: ^an6-61-1-4

‘The thoughtful one who has known both ends, ^an6-61-2-1

and is not stuck in the middle: ^an6-61-2-2

he is a great man, I declare, ^an6-61-2-3

he has escaped the seamstress here.’ ^an6-61-2-4

But what is one end? What’s the second end? What’s the middle? And who is the seamstress?” ^an6-61-3-1

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-3-2

“Contact, reverends, is one end. The origin of contact is the second end. The cessation of contact is the middle. And craving is the seamstress, ^an6-61-3-3

for craving weaves one to being regenerated in one state of existence or another. ^an6-61-3-4

That’s how a mendicant directly knows what should be directly known and completely understands what should be completely understood. Knowing and understanding thus they make an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-3-5

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-4-1

“The past, reverends, is one end. The future is the second end. The present is the middle. And craving is the seamstress … ^an6-61-4-2

That’s how a mendicant directly knows … an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-4-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-5-1

“Pleasant feeling, reverends, is one end. Painful feeling is the second end. Neutral feeling is the middle. And craving is the seamstress … ^an6-61-5-2

That’s how a mendicant directly knows … an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-5-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-6-1

“Name, reverends, is one end. Form is the second end. Consciousness is the middle. And craving is the seamstress … ^an6-61-6-2

That’s how a mendicant directly knows … an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-6-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-7-1

“The six interior sense fields, reverends, are one end. The six exterior sense fields are the second end. Consciousness is the middle. And craving is the seamstress … ^an6-61-7-2

That’s how a mendicant directly knows … an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-7-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-8-1

“Substantial reality, reverends, is one end. The origin of substantial reality is the second end. The cessation of substantial reality is the middle. And craving is the seamstress, ^an6-61-8-2

for craving weaves one to being regenerated in one state of existence or another. ^an6-61-8-3

That’s how a mendicant directly knows what should be directly known and completely understands what should be completely understood. Knowing and understanding thus they make an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-8-4

When this was said, one of the mendicants said to the senior mendicants: ^an6-61-9-1

“Each of us has spoken from the heart. ^an6-61-9-2

Come, reverends, let’s go to the Buddha, and inform him about this. ^an6-61-9-3

As he answers, so we’ll remember it.” ^an6-61-9-4

“Yes, reverend,” those senior mendicants replied. ^an6-61-10-1

Then those senior mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an6-61-10-2

and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. They asked, ^an6-61-10-3

“Sir, who has spoken well?” ^an6-61-10-4

“Mendicants, you’ve all spoken well in a way. However, this is what I was referring to in ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, in ‘The Questions of Metteyya’ when I said: ^an6-61-10-5

‘The sage has known both ends, ^an6-61-11-1

and is not stuck in the middle. ^an6-61-11-2

He is a great man, I declare, ^an6-61-11-3

he has escaped the seamstress here.’ ^an6-61-11-4

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-61-12-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an6-61-12-2

The Buddha said this: ^an6-61-12-3

“Contact, mendicants, is one end. The origin of contact is the second end. The cessation of contact is the middle. And craving is the seamstress, ^an6-61-12-4

for craving weaves one to being regenerated in one state of existence or another. ^an6-61-12-5

That’s how a mendicant directly knows what should be directly known and completely understands what should be completely understood. Knowing and understanding thus they make an end of suffering in this very life.” ^an6-61-12-6

### Knowledge of the Faculties of Persons (Numbered Discourses 6.62) ^an6-62-0

So I have heard. ^an6-62-1-1

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a town of the Kosalans named Daṇḍakappaka. ^an6-62-1-2

The Buddha left the road and sat at the root of a tree on the seat spread out. ^an6-62-1-3

The mendicants entered Daṇḍakappaka to look for a guest house. ^an6-62-1-4

Then Venerable Ānanda together with several mendicants went to the Aciravatī River to bathe. ^an6-62-2-1

When he had bathed and emerged from the water he stood in one robe drying his limbs. ^an6-62-2-2

Then a certain mendicant went up to Venerable Ānanda, and said to him, ^an6-62-2-3

“Reverend Ānanda, when the Buddha declared that Devadatta was ^an6-62-2-4

going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable, did he do so after wholeheartedly deliberating, or was this just a way of speaking?” ^an6-62-2-5

“You’re right, reverend, that’s how the Buddha declared it.” ^an6-62-2-6

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an6-62-3-1

“Ānanda, that mendicant must be junior, recently gone forth, or else a foolish, incompetent senior mendicant. ^an6-62-4-1

How on earth can he take something that I have declared categorically to be ambiguous? ^an6-62-4-2

I do not see a single other individual about whom I have given such whole-hearted deliberation before making a declaration as I did in the case of Devadatta. ^an6-62-4-3

As long as I saw even a fraction of a hair’s tip of goodness in Devadatta ^an6-62-4-4

I did not declare that he was ^an6-62-4-5

going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an6-62-4-6

But when I saw that there was not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of goodness in Devadatta ^an6-62-4-7

I declared that he was ^an6-62-4-8

going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an6-62-4-9

Suppose there was a sewer deeper than a man’s height, full to the brim with feces, ^an6-62-5-1

and someone was sunk into it over their head. ^an6-62-5-2

Then along comes a person who wants to help make them safe, who wants to lift them out of that sewer. ^an6-62-5-3

But circling all around the sewer they couldn’t see even a fraction of a hair’s tip of that person that was not smeared with feces. ^an6-62-5-4

In the same way, when I saw that there was not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of goodness in Devadatta ^an6-62-5-5

I declared that he was ^an6-62-5-6

going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an6-62-5-7

If you would all listen, Ānanda, I will analyze the Realized One’s knowledges of the faculties of persons.” ^an6-62-5-8

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^an6-62-6-1

Let the Buddha analyze the faculties of persons. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an6-62-6-2

“Well then, Ānanda, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-62-6-3

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an6-62-6-4

The Buddha said this: ^an6-62-6-5

“Ānanda, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-7-1

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-7-2

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-7-3

‘The skillful qualities of this individual have vanished, but the unskillful qualities are still present. ^an6-62-7-4

Nevertheless, their skillful root is unbroken, and from that the skillful will appear. ^an6-62-7-5

So this individual is not liable to decline in the future.’ ^an6-62-7-6

Suppose some seeds were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. They’re sown in a well-prepared, productive field. ^an6-62-7-7

Wouldn’t you know that those seeds would grow, increase, and mature?” ^an6-62-7-8

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-7-9

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-7-10

This individual is not liable to decline in the future … ^an6-62-7-15

This is how another individual is known to the Realized One by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-7-16

And this is how the Realized One knows a person’s faculties by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-7-17

And this is how the Realized One knows the future origination of a person’s qualities by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-7-18

When I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-8-1

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-8-2

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-8-3

‘The unskillful qualities of this individual have vanished, but the skillful qualities are still present. ^an6-62-8-4

Nevertheless, their unskillful root is unbroken, and from that the unskillful will appear. ^an6-62-8-5

So this individual is still liable to decline in the future.’ ^an6-62-8-6

Suppose some seeds were intact, unspoiled, not weather-damaged, fertile, and well-kept. And they were sown on a broad rock. ^an6-62-8-7

Wouldn’t you know that those seeds would not grow, increase, and mature?” ^an6-62-8-8

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-8-9

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-8-10

This individual is still liable to decline in the future … ^an6-62-8-15

This is how another individual is known to the Realized One … ^an6-62-8-16

When I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-9-1

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-9-2

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-9-3

‘This individual has not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of goodness. They have exclusively dark, unskillful qualities. When their body breaks up, after death, they will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.’ ^an6-62-9-4

Suppose some seeds were broken, spoiled, weather-damaged. They’re sown in a well-prepared, productive field. ^an6-62-9-5

Wouldn’t you know that those seeds would not grow, increase, and mature?” ^an6-62-9-6

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-9-7

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-9-8

‘This individual has not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of goodness. They have exclusively dark, unskillful qualities. When their body breaks up, after death, they will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.’ …” ^an6-62-9-11

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^an6-62-10-1

“Sir, can you describe three other individuals who share a portion of these three?” ^an6-62-10-2

“I can, Ānanda,” said the Buddha. ^an6-62-10-3

“Ānanda, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-10-4

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-10-5

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-10-6

‘The skillful qualities of this individual have vanished, but the unskillful qualities are still present. ^an6-62-10-7

Nevertheless, their skillful root is unbroken, but it’s about to be totally destroyed. ^an6-62-10-8

So this individual is still liable to decline in the future.’ ^an6-62-10-9

Suppose that there were some burning coals, blazing and glowing. And they were placed on a broad rock. ^an6-62-10-10

Wouldn’t you know that those coals would not grow, increase, and spread?” ^an6-62-10-11

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-10-12

“Or suppose it was the late afternoon and the sun was going down. Wouldn’t you know that the light was about to vanish and darkness appear?” ^an6-62-10-13

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-10-14

“Or suppose that it’s nearly time for the midnight meal. Wouldn’t you know that the light had vanished and the darkness appeared?” ^an6-62-10-15

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-10-16

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-10-17

This individual is still liable to decline in the future … ^an6-62-10-22

When I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-11-1

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-11-2

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-11-3

‘The unskillful qualities of this individual have vanished, but the skillful qualities are still present. ^an6-62-11-4

Nevertheless, their unskillful root is unbroken, but it’s about to be totally destroyed. ^an6-62-11-5

So this individual is not liable to decline in the future.’ ^an6-62-11-6

Suppose that there were some burning coals, blazing and glowing. They were placed on a pile of grass or timber. ^an6-62-11-7

Wouldn’t you know that those coals would grow, increase, and spread?” ^an6-62-11-8

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-11-9

“Suppose it’s the crack of dawn and the sun is rising. Wouldn’t you know that the dark will vanish and the light appear?” ^an6-62-11-10

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-11-11

“Or suppose that it’s nearly time for the midday meal. Wouldn’t you know that the dark had vanished and the light appeared?” ^an6-62-11-12

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-11-13

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-11-14

This individual is not liable to decline in the future … ^an6-62-11-19

When I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand: ^an6-62-12-1

‘Both skillful and unskillful qualities are found in this individual.’ ^an6-62-12-2

After some time I encompass their mind and understand: ^an6-62-12-3

‘This individual has not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of unskillful qualities. They have exclusively bright, blameless qualities. They will become extinguished in this very life.’ ^an6-62-12-4

Suppose that there were some cool, quenched coals. They were placed on a pile of grass or timber. ^an6-62-12-5

Wouldn’t you know that those coals would not grow, increase, and spread?” ^an6-62-12-6

“Yes, sir.” ^an6-62-12-7

“In the same way, when I’ve comprehended the mind of an individual, I understand … ^an6-62-12-8

‘This individual has not even a fraction of a hair’s tip of unskillful qualities. They have exclusively bright, blameless qualities. They will become extinguished in this very life.’ ^an6-62-12-11

This is how another individual is known to the Realized One by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-12-12

And this is how the Realized One knows a person’s faculties by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-12-13

And this is how the Realized One knows the future origination of a person’s qualities by encompassing their mind. ^an6-62-12-14

And so, Ānanda, of the first three individuals one is not liable to decline, one is liable to decline, and one is bound for a place of loss, hell. ^an6-62-13-1

And of the second three individuals, one is liable to decline, one is not liable to decline, and one is bound to become extinguished.” ^an6-62-13-2

### Penetrative (Numbered Discourses 6.63) ^an6-63-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you a penetrative exposition of the teaching. ^an6-63-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an6-63-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an6-63-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an6-63-1-4

“Mendicants, what is the penetrative exposition of the teaching? ^an6-63-2-1

Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known. ^an6-63-2-2

Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known. ^an6-63-3-1

Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known. ^an6-63-4-1

Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known. ^an6-63-5-1

Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known. ^an6-63-6-1

Suffering should be known. And its source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known. ^an6-63-7-1

‘Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ That’s what I said, ^an6-63-8-1

but why did I say it? ^an6-63-8-2

There are these five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an6-63-8-3

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an6-63-8-4

Sounds known by the ear … ^an6-63-8-5

Smells known by the nose … ^an6-63-8-6

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an6-63-8-7

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an6-63-8-8

However, these are not sensual pleasures. In the training of the Noble One they’re called ‘kinds of sensual stimulation’. ^an6-63-8-9

Greedy intention is a person’s sensual pleasure. ^an6-63-8-10

The world’s pretty things aren’t sensual pleasures. ^an6-63-8-11

Greedy intention is a person’s sensual pleasure. ^an6-63-8-12

The world’s pretty things stay just as they are, ^an6-63-8-13

but the attentive remove desire for them. ^an6-63-8-14

And what is the source of sensual pleasures? ^an6-63-9-1

Contact is their source. ^an6-63-10-2

And what is the diversity of sensual pleasures? ^an6-63-11-1

The sensual desire for sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches are all different. ^an6-63-11-2

This is called the diversity of sensual pleasures. ^an6-63-11-3

And what is the result of sensual pleasures? ^an6-63-12-1

When one who desires sensual pleasures generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds—this is called the result of sensual pleasures. ^an6-63-12-2

And what is the cessation of sensual pleasures? ^an6-63-13-1

When contact ceases, sensual pleasures cease. ^an6-63-13-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of sensual pleasures is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-13-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-13-4

When a noble disciple understands sensual pleasures in this way—and understands their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation—they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of sensual pleasures. ^an6-63-14-1

‘Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-14-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-14-3

‘Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-15-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an6-63-15-2

There are these three feelings: ^an6-63-15-3

pleasant, painful, and neutral. ^an6-63-15-4

And what is the source of feelings? ^an6-63-16-1

Contact is their source. ^an6-63-16-2

And what is the diversity of feelings? ^an6-63-17-1

There are pleasant feelings of the flesh, pleasant feelings not of the flesh, painful feelings of the flesh, painful feelings not of the flesh, neutral feelings of the flesh, and neutral feelings not of the flesh. ^an6-63-17-2

This is called the disparity of feelings. ^an6-63-17-3

And what is the result of feelings? ^an6-63-18-1

When one who feels generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds— ^an6-63-18-2

this is called the result of feelings. ^an6-63-18-3

And what is the cessation of feelings? ^an6-63-19-1

When contact ceases, feelings cease. ^an6-63-19-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-19-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-19-4

When a noble disciple understands feelings in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of feelings. ^an6-63-20-1

‘Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-20-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-20-3

‘Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-21-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an6-63-21-2

There are these six perceptions: ^an6-63-21-3

perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas. ^an6-63-21-4

And what is the source of perceptions? ^an6-63-22-1

Contact is their source. ^an6-63-22-2

And what is the diversity of perceptions? ^an6-63-23-1

The perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas are all different. ^an6-63-23-2

This is called the diversity of perceptions. ^an6-63-23-3

And what is the result of perceptions? ^an6-63-24-1

Communication is the result of perception, I say. ^an6-63-24-2

You communicate something in whatever manner you perceive it, saying ‘That’s what I perceived.’ ^an6-63-24-3

This is called the result of perceptions. ^an6-63-24-4

And what is the cessation of perception? ^an6-63-25-1

When contact ceases, perception ceases. ^an6-63-25-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-25-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-25-4

When a noble disciple understands perception in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of perception. ^an6-63-26-1

‘Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-26-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-26-3

‘Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-27-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an6-63-27-2

There are these three defilements: ^an6-63-27-3

the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an6-63-27-4

And what is the source of defilements? ^an6-63-28-1

Ignorance is the source of defilements. ^an6-63-28-2

And what is the disparity of defilements? ^an6-63-29-1

There are defilements that lead to rebirth in hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, the human world, and the world of the gods. ^an6-63-29-2

This is called the disparity of defilements. ^an6-63-29-3

And what is the result of defilements? ^an6-63-30-1

When one who is ignorant generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds—this is called the result of defilements. ^an6-63-30-2

And what is the cessation of defilements? ^an6-63-31-1

When ignorance ceases, defilements cease. ^an6-63-31-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of defilements is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-31-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-31-4

When a noble disciple understands defilements in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of defilements. ^an6-63-32-1

‘Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-32-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-32-3

‘Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ That’s what I said, ^an6-63-33-1

but why did I say it? ^an6-63-33-2

It is intention that I call deeds. ^an6-63-33-3

For after making a choice one acts ^an6-63-33-4

by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-63-33-5

And what is the source of deeds? ^an6-63-34-1

Contact is their source. ^an6-63-34-2

And what is the disparity of deeds? ^an6-63-35-1

There are deeds that lead to rebirth in hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, the human world, and the world of the gods. ^an6-63-35-2

This is called the disparity of deeds. ^an6-63-35-3

And what is the result of deeds? ^an6-63-36-1

The result of deeds is threefold, I say: ^an6-63-36-2

in this very life, on rebirth in the next life, or at some later time. ^an6-63-36-3

This is called the result of deeds. ^an6-63-36-4

And what is the cessation of deeds? ^an6-63-37-1

When contact ceases, deeds cease. ^an6-63-37-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of deeds is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-37-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-37-4

When a noble disciple understands deeds in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of deeds. ^an6-63-38-1

‘Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-38-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-38-3

‘Suffering should be known. And its source, disparity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-39-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an6-63-39-2

Rebirth is suffering; old age is suffering; illness is suffering; death is suffering; sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are suffering; not getting what you wish for is suffering. In brief, the five grasping aggregates are suffering. ^an6-63-39-3

And what is the source of suffering? ^an6-63-40-1

Craving is the source of suffering. ^an6-63-40-2

And what is the disparity of suffering? ^an6-63-41-1

There is suffering that is severe, mild, slow to fade, and quick to fade. ^an6-63-41-2

This is called the disparity of suffering. ^an6-63-41-3

And what is the result of suffering? ^an6-63-42-1

It’s when someone who is overcome and overwhelmed by suffering sorrows and wails and laments, beating their breast and falling into confusion. Or else, overcome by that suffering, they begin an external search, wondering: ^an6-63-42-2

‘Who knows one or two phrases to stop this suffering?’ ^an6-63-42-3

The result of suffering is either confusion or a search, I say. ^an6-63-42-4

This is called the result of suffering. ^an6-63-42-5

And what is the cessation of suffering? ^an6-63-43-1

When craving ceases, suffering ceases. ^an6-63-43-2

The practice that leads to the cessation of suffering is simply this noble eightfold path, that is: ^an6-63-43-3

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an6-63-43-4

When a noble disciple understands suffering in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of suffering. ^an6-63-44-1

‘Suffering should be known. And its source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known.’ ^an6-63-44-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an6-63-44-3

This is the penetrative exposition of the teaching.” ^an6-63-45-1

### The Lion’s Roar (Numbered Discourses 6.64) ^an6-64-0

“Mendicants, the Realized One possesses six powers of a Realized One. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an6-64-1-1

What six? ^an6-64-1-2

Firstly, the Realized One truly understands the possible as possible and the impossible as impossible. ^an6-64-1-3

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. Relying on this he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an6-64-1-4

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes. ^an6-64-2-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an6-64-2-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments. ^an6-64-3-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an6-64-3-2

Furthermore, the Realized One recollects many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. He remembers: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so he recollects his many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an6-64-4-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an6-64-4-2

Furthermore, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, the Realized One sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an6-64-5-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an6-64-5-2

Furthermore, the Realized One has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And he lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an6-64-6-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. Relying on this he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an6-64-6-2

These are the six powers of a Realized One that the Realized One possesses. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an6-64-6-3

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of the possible as possible and the impossible as impossible, ^an6-64-7-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-7-2

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes, ^an6-64-8-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-8-2

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments, ^an6-64-9-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-9-2

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of recollection of past lives, ^an6-64-10-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-10-2

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of passing away and rebirth of sentient beings, ^an6-64-11-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-11-2

If others come to the Realized One and ask questions about his true knowledge of the ending of defilements, ^an6-64-12-1

the Realized One answers them in whatever manner he has truly known it. ^an6-64-12-2

And I say that true knowledge of the possible as possible and the impossible as impossible is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-1

And true knowledge of the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-2

And true knowledge of corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-3

And true knowledge of the recollection of past lives is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-4

And true knowledge of the passing away and rebirth of sentient beings is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-5

And true knowledge of the ending of defilements is for those with immersion, not for those without immersion. ^an6-64-13-6

And so, mendicants, immersion is the path. No immersion is the wrong path.” ^an6-64-13-7

### The Fruit of Non-Return (Numbered Discourses 6.65) ^an6-65-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six things you can’t realize the fruit of non-return. ^an6-65-1-1

What six? ^an6-65-1-2

Lack of faith, conscience, and prudence; laziness, unmindfulness, and witlessness. ^an6-65-1-3

Without giving up these six things you can’t realize the fruit of non-return. ^an6-65-1-4

After giving up six things you can realize the fruit of non-return. ^an6-65-2-1

What six? ^an6-65-2-2

Lack of faith, conscience, and prudence; laziness, unmindfulness, and witlessness. ^an6-65-2-3

After giving up these six things you can realize the fruit of non-return.” ^an6-65-2-4

### Perfection (Numbered Discourses 6.66) ^an6-66-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six things you can’t realize perfection. ^an6-66-1-1

What six? ^an6-66-1-2

Dullness, drowsiness, restlessness, remorse, lack of faith, and negligence. ^an6-66-1-3

Without giving up these six things you can’t realize perfection. ^an6-66-1-4

After giving up six things you can realize perfection. ^an6-66-2-1

What six? ^an6-66-2-2

Dullness, drowsiness, restlessness, remorse, lack of faith, and negligence. ^an6-66-2-3

After giving up these six things you can realize perfection.” ^an6-66-2-4

### Friends (Numbered Discourses 6.67) ^an6-67-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible that a mendicant with bad friends, companions, and associates, while frequenting, accompanying, and attending, and following their example, will fulfill the practice dealing with the supplementary regulations. ^an6-67-1-1

Without fulfilling the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s impossible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an6-67-1-2

Without fulfilling the practice of a trainee, it’s impossible to fulfill ethics. ^an6-67-1-3

Without fulfilling ethics, it’s impossible to give up sensual desire, or desire to be reborn in the realm of luminous form or in the formless realm. ^an6-67-1-4

It’s quite possible that a mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates, while frequenting, accompanying, and attending, and following their example, will fulfill the practice dealing with the supplementary regulations. ^an6-67-2-1

Having fulfilled the practice dealing with supplementary regulations, it’s possible to fulfill the practice of a trainee. ^an6-67-2-2

Having fulfilled the practice of a trainee, it’s possible to fulfill ethics. ^an6-67-2-3

Having fulfilled ethics, it’s possible to give up sensual desire, and desire to be reborn in the realm of luminous form and in the formless realm.” ^an6-67-2-4

### Enjoying Company (Numbered Discourses 6.68) ^an6-68-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible that a mendicant who enjoys company and groups, who loves them and likes to enjoy them, should take pleasure in being alone in seclusion. ^an6-68-1-1

Without taking pleasure in being alone in seclusion, it’s impossible to learn the patterns of the mind. ^an6-68-1-2

Without learning the mind’s character, it’s impossible to fulfill right view. ^an6-68-1-3

Without fulfilling right view, it’s impossible to fulfill right immersion. ^an6-68-1-4

Without fulfilling right immersion, it’s impossible to give up the fetters. ^an6-68-1-5

Without giving up the fetters, it’s impossible to realize extinguishment. ^an6-68-1-6

It’s quite possible that a mendicant who doesn’t enjoy company and groups, who doesn’t love them and like to enjoy them, should take pleasure in being alone in seclusion. ^an6-68-2-1

For someone who takes pleasure in being alone in seclusion, it’s possible to learn the mind’s character. ^an6-68-2-2

For someone who learns the mind’s character, it’s possible to fulfill right view. ^an6-68-2-3

Having fulfilled right view, it’s possible to fulfill right immersion. ^an6-68-2-4

Having fulfilled right immersion, it’s possible to give up the fetters. ^an6-68-2-5

Having given up the fetters, it’s possible to realize extinguishment.” ^an6-68-2-6

### A God (Numbered Discourses 6.69) ^an6-69-0

Then, late at night, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an6-69-1-1

“Sir, these six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an6-69-1-2

What six? ^an6-69-1-3

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training; being easy to admonish, and good friendship. ^an6-69-1-4

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.” ^an6-69-1-5

That’s what that deity said, ^an6-69-1-6

and the teacher approved. ^an6-69-1-7

Then that deity, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^an6-69-1-8

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an6-69-2-1

“Tonight, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an6-69-2-2

‘Sir, these six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an6-69-2-3

What six? ^an6-69-2-4

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training; being easy to admonish, and good friendship. ^an6-69-2-5

These six things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.’ ^an6-69-2-6

That is what that deity said. ^an6-69-2-7

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there.” ^an6-69-2-8

When he said this, Venerable Sāriputta said to the Buddha: ^an6-69-3-1

“Sir, this is how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement. ^an6-69-3-2

It’s when a mendicant personally respects the Teacher and praises such respect. ^an6-69-3-3

And they encourage other mendicants who lack such respect to respect the Teacher. ^an6-69-3-4

And they praise other mendicants who respect the Teacher at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an6-69-3-5

They personally respect the teaching … ^an6-69-3-6

They personally respect the Saṅgha … ^an6-69-3-7

They personally respect the training … ^an6-69-3-8

They are personally easy to admonish … ^an6-69-3-9

They personally have good friends, and praise such friendship. ^an6-69-3-10

And they encourage other mendicants who lack good friends to develop good friendship. ^an6-69-3-11

And they praise other mendicants who have good friends at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an6-69-3-12

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^an6-69-3-13

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^an6-69-4-1

It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this. ^an6-69-4-2

It’s when a mendicant personally respects the Teacher … ^an6-69-4-3

They personally respect the teaching … ^an6-69-4-6

They personally respect the Saṅgha … ^an6-69-4-7

They personally respect the training … ^an6-69-4-8

They are personally easy to admonish … ^an6-69-4-9

They personally have good friends, and praise such friendship. ^an6-69-4-10

And they encourage other mendicants who lack good friends to develop good friendship. ^an6-69-4-11

And they praise other mendicants who have good friends at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an6-69-4-12

This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” ^an6-69-4-13

### Immersion (Numbered Discourses 6.70) ^an6-70-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible that a mendicant without immersion that is peaceful, refined, tranquil, and unified will wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. They control the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an6-70-1-1

It’s impossible that with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, they’ll hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far. ^an6-70-1-2

It’s impossible that they’ll understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with their own mind, ^an6-70-1-3

understanding mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’ … ^an6-70-1-4

and freed mind as ‘freed mind’. ^an6-70-1-5

It’s impossible that they’ll recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an6-70-1-6

It’s impossible that with clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, they’ll understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an6-70-1-7

It’s impossible that they’ll realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an6-70-1-8

But it’s quite possible that a mendicant who has immersion that is peaceful, refined, tranquil, and unified will wield the many kinds of psychic power … ^an6-70-2-1

It’s possible that with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, they’ll hear both kinds of sounds … ^an6-70-2-2

It’s possible that they’ll understand the minds of other beings … ^an6-70-2-3

It’s possible that they’ll recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an6-70-2-6

It’s possible that with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they’ll understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an6-70-2-7

It’s possible that they’ll realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^an6-70-2-8

### Capable of Realizing (Numbered Discourses 6.71) ^an6-71-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is incapable of realizing anything that can be realized, since each and every one is within range. ^an6-71-1-1

What six? ^an6-71-1-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t truly understand which qualities make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration. And they don’t practice carefully or do what’s suitable. ^an6-71-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is incapable of realizing anything that can be realized, since each and every one is within range. ^an6-71-1-4

A mendicant with six qualities is capable of realizing anything that can be realized, since each and every one is within range. ^an6-71-2-1

What six? ^an6-71-2-2

It’s when a mendicant truly understands which qualities make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration. And they practice carefully and do what’s suitable. ^an6-71-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities is capable of realizing anything that can be realized, since each and every one is within range.” ^an6-71-2-4

### Strength (Numbered Discourses 6.72) ^an6-72-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has six qualities can’t attain strength in immersion. ^an6-72-1-1

What six? ^an6-72-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not skilled in entering immersion, skilled in remaining in immersion, or skilled in emerging from immersion. And they don’t practice carefully and persistently, and they don’t do what’s suitable. ^an6-72-1-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities can’t attain strength in immersion. ^an6-72-1-4

A mendicant who has six qualities can attain strength in immersion. ^an6-72-2-1

What six? ^an6-72-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is skilled in entering immersion, skilled in remaining in immersion, and skilled in emerging from immersion. And they practice carefully and persistently, and do what’s suitable. ^an6-72-2-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities can attain strength in immersion.” ^an6-72-2-4

### First Absorption (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.73) ^an6-73-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these six qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-73-1-1

What six? ^an6-73-1-2

Desire for sensual pleasures, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an6-73-1-3

And the drawbacks of sensual pleasures have not been truly seen clearly with right wisdom. ^an6-73-1-4

Without giving up these six qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-73-1-5

But after giving up these six qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-73-2-1

What six? ^an6-73-2-2

Desire for sensual pleasures, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an6-73-2-3

And the drawbacks of sensual pleasures have been truly seen clearly with right wisdom. ^an6-73-2-4

After giving up these six qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption.” ^an6-73-2-5

### First Absorption (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.74) ^an6-74-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these six qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-74-1-1

What six? ^an6-74-1-2

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts; and sensual, malicious, and cruel perceptions. ^an6-74-1-3

Without giving up these six qualities you can’t enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-74-1-4

But after giving up these six qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an6-74-2-1

What six? ^an6-74-2-2

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts; and sensual, malicious, and cruel perceptions. ^an6-74-2-3

After giving up these six qualities you can enter and remain in the first absorption.” ^an6-74-2-4

### Suffering (Numbered Discourses 6.75) ^an6-75-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has six qualities they live unhappily in this very life—with distress, anguish, and fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a bad rebirth. ^an6-75-1-1

What six? ^an6-75-1-2

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts; and sensual, malicious, and cruel perceptions. ^an6-75-1-3
