When a mendicant possesses these six qualities they live unhappily in this very life—with distress, anguish, and fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a bad rebirth. ^an6-75-1-4

When a mendicant has six qualities they live happily in this very life—without distress, anguish, or fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a good rebirth. ^an6-75-2-1

What six? ^an6-75-2-2

Thoughts of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. And perceptions of renunciation, good will, and harmlessness. ^an6-75-2-3

When a mendicant possesses these six qualities they live happily in this very life—without distress, anguish, or fever—and when the body breaks up, after death, they can expect a good rebirth.” ^an6-75-2-4

### Perfection (Numbered Discourses 6.76) ^an6-76-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six things you can’t realize perfection. ^an6-76-1-1

What six? ^an6-76-1-2

Conceit, inferiority complex, superiority complex, overestimation, obstinacy, and groveling. ^an6-76-1-3

Without giving up these six qualities you can’t realize perfection. ^an6-76-1-4

After giving up six things you can realize perfection. ^an6-76-2-1

What six? ^an6-76-2-2

Conceit, inferiority complex, superiority complex, overestimation, obstinacy, and groveling. ^an6-76-2-3

After giving up these six things you can realize perfection.” ^an6-76-2-4

### Superhuman States (Numbered Discourses 6.77) ^an6-77-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six qualities you can’t realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an6-77-1-1

What six? ^an6-77-1-2

Lack of mindfulness and situational awareness, not guarding the sense doors, eating too much, fawning, and flattery. ^an6-77-1-3

Without giving up these six qualities you can’t realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an6-77-1-4

But after giving up six qualities you can realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones. ^an6-77-2-1

What six? ^an6-77-2-2

Lack of mindfulness and situational awareness, not guarding the sense doors, eating too much, fawning, and flattery. ^an6-77-2-3

After giving up these six qualities you can realize a superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.” ^an6-77-2-4

### Joy and Happiness (Numbered Discourses 6.78) ^an6-78-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant has six things they’re full of joy and happiness in this very life, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements. ^an6-78-1-1

What six? ^an6-78-1-2

It’s when a mendicant enjoys the teaching, meditation, giving up, seclusion, kindness, and non-proliferation. ^an6-78-1-3

When a mendicant possesses these six things they’re full of joy and happiness in this very life, and they have laid the groundwork for ending the defilements.” ^an6-78-1-4

### Achievement (Numbered Discourses 6.79) ^an6-79-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has six qualities is unable to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an6-79-1-1

What six? ^an6-79-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is not skilled in progress, skilled in regress, or skilled in means. They don’t generate enthusiasm to achieve skillful qualities not yet achieved. They don’t protect skillful qualities they have achieved. And they don’t try to persist in the task. ^an6-79-1-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities is unable to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an6-79-1-4

A mendicant who has six qualities is able to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired. ^an6-79-2-1

What six? ^an6-79-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is skilled in progress, skilled in regress, and skilled in means. They generate enthusiasm to achieve skillful qualities not yet achieved. They protect skillful qualities they have achieved. And they try to persist in the task. ^an6-79-2-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities is able to acquire more skillful qualities or to increase the skillful qualities they’ve already acquired.” ^an6-79-2-4

### Greatness (Numbered Discourses 6.80) ^an6-80-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities soon acquires great and abundant good qualities. ^an6-80-1-1

What six? ^an6-80-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is full of light, full of practice, full of inspiration, and full of eagerness. They don’t slack off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. They reach further. ^an6-80-1-3

A mendicant who possesses these six qualities soon acquires great and abundant good qualities.” ^an6-80-1-4

### Hell (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.81) ^an6-81-0

“Mendicants, someone with six qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an6-81-1-1

What six? ^an6-81-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. And they have corrupt wishes and wrong view. ^an6-81-1-3

Someone with these six qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an6-81-1-4

Someone with six qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an6-81-2-1

What six? ^an6-81-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or lie. And they have few desires and right view. ^an6-81-2-3

Someone with these six qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an6-81-2-4

### Hell (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.82) ^an6-82-0

“Mendicants, someone with six qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an6-82-1-1

What six? ^an6-82-1-2

They kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, and lie. And they’re greedy and rude. ^an6-82-1-3

Someone with these six qualities is placed in hell as if delivered there. ^an6-82-1-4

Someone with six qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there. ^an6-82-2-1

What six? ^an6-82-2-2

They don’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, or lie. And they’re not greedy or rude. ^an6-82-2-3

Someone with these six qualities is placed in heaven as if delivered there.” ^an6-82-2-4

### The Best Thing (Numbered Discourses 6.83) ^an6-83-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities can’t realize the best thing, perfection. ^an6-83-1-1

What six? ^an6-83-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithless, shameless, imprudent, lazy, and witless. And they’re concerned with their body and their life. ^an6-83-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can’t realize the best thing, perfection. ^an6-83-1-4

A mendicant with six qualities can realize the best thing, perfection. ^an6-83-2-1

What six? ^an6-83-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, conscientious, prudent, energetic, and wise. And they have no concern for their body and their life. ^an6-83-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can realize the best thing, perfection.” ^an6-83-2-4

### Day and Night (Numbered Discourses 6.84) ^an6-84-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an6-84-1-1

What six? ^an6-84-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has many desires—they’re frustrated and not content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they’re faithless, unethical, unmindful, and witless. ^an6-84-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an6-84-1-4

A mendicant with six qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an6-84-2-1

What six? ^an6-84-2-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t have many desires—they’re not frustrated but content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. And they’re faithful, ethical, mindful, and wise. ^an6-84-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night.” ^an6-84-2-4

### Coolness (Numbered Discourses 6.85) ^an6-85-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities can’t realize supreme coolness. ^an6-85-1-1

What six? ^an6-85-1-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t keep their mind in check when they should. They don’t exert their mind when they should. They don’t encourage the mind when they should. They don’t watch over the mind with equanimity when they should. They have inferior convictions. They delight in substantial reality. ^an6-85-1-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can’t realize supreme coolness. ^an6-85-1-4

A mendicant with six qualities can realize supreme coolness. ^an6-85-2-1

What six? ^an6-85-2-2

It’s when a mendicant keeps their mind in check when they should. They exert their mind when they should. They encourage the mind when they should. They watch over the mind with equanimity when they should. They have excellent convictions. They delight in extinguishment. ^an6-85-2-3

A mendicant with these six qualities can realize supreme coolness.” ^an6-85-2-4

### Obstacles (Numbered Discourses 6.86) ^an6-86-0

“Mendicants, someone with six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-86-1-1

What six? ^an6-86-1-2

They’re obstructed by deeds, defilements, or results. And they’re faithless, unenthusiastic, and witless. ^an6-86-1-3

Someone with these six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-86-1-4

Someone with six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-86-2-1

What six? ^an6-86-2-2

They’re not obstructed by deeds, defilements, or results. And they’re faithful, enthusiastic, and wise. ^an6-86-2-3

Someone with these six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an6-86-2-4

### A Murderer (Numbered Discourses 6.87) ^an6-87-0

“Mendicants, someone with six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-87-1-1

What six? ^an6-87-1-2

They murder their mother or father or a perfected one. They maliciously shed the blood of a Realized One. They cause a schism in the Saṅgha. They’re witless, dull, and idiotic. ^an6-87-1-3

Someone with these six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-87-1-4

Someone with six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-87-2-1

What six? ^an6-87-2-2

They don’t murder their mother or father or a perfected one. They don’t maliciously shed the blood of a Realized One. They don’t cause a schism in the Saṅgha. They’re wise, bright, and clever. ^an6-87-2-3

Someone with these six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an6-87-2-4

### Wanting to Listen (Numbered Discourses 6.88) ^an6-88-0

“Mendicants, someone with six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-88-1-1

What six? ^an6-88-1-2

When the teaching and practice proclaimed by the Realized One is being taught they don’t want to listen. They don’t actively listen or try to understand. They learn the incorrect meaning and neglect the correct meaning. They accept views that contradict the teaching. ^an6-88-1-3

Someone with these six qualities is unable to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities, even when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-88-1-4

Someone with six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching. ^an6-88-2-1

What six? ^an6-88-2-2

When the teaching and practice proclaimed by the Realized One is being taught they want to listen. They actively listen and try to understand. They learn the correct meaning and neglect the incorrect meaning. They accept views that conform with the teaching. ^an6-88-2-3

Someone with these six qualities is able to step into the sure path with regards to skillful qualities when listening to the true teaching.” ^an6-88-2-4

### Not Giving Up (Numbered Discourses 6.89) ^an6-89-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six things you can’t become accomplished in view. ^an6-89-1-1

What six? ^an6-89-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, and forms of greed, hate, and delusion that lead to rebirth in places of loss. ^an6-89-1-3

Without giving up these six things you can’t become accomplished in view. ^an6-89-1-4

After giving up six things you can become accomplished in view. ^an6-89-2-1

What six? ^an6-89-2-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, and forms of greed, hate, and delusion that lead to rebirth in places of loss. ^an6-89-2-3

After giving up these six things you can become accomplished in view.” ^an6-89-2-4

### Given Up (Numbered Discourses 6.90) ^an6-90-0

“Mendicants, an individual accomplished in view has given up these six things. ^an6-90-1-1

What six? ^an6-90-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, and forms of greed, hate, and delusion that lead to rebirth in places of loss. ^an6-90-1-3

An individual accomplished in view has given up these six things.” ^an6-90-1-4

### Can’t Give Rise (Numbered Discourses 6.91) ^an6-91-0

“Mendicants, an individual accomplished in view can’t give rise to six things. ^an6-91-1-1

What six? ^an6-91-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, and forms of greed, hate, and delusion that lead to rebirth in places of loss. ^an6-91-1-3

An individual accomplished in view can’t give rise to these six things.” ^an6-91-1-4

### Things That Can’t Be Done (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6.92) ^an6-92-0

“Mendicants, these six things can’t be done. ^an6-92-1-1

What six? ^an6-92-1-2

An individual accomplished in view can’t live disrespectful and irreverent toward the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, or the training. They can’t establish their belief on unreliable grounds. And they can’t generate an eighth rebirth. ^an6-92-1-3

These are the six things that can’t be done.” ^an6-92-1-4

### Things That Can’t Be Done (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6.93) ^an6-93-0

“Mendicants, these six things can’t be done. ^an6-93-1-1

What six? ^an6-93-1-2

An individual accomplished in view can’t take conditions to be permanent. They cannot take conditions to be happiness. They cannot take anything to be the self. They can’t do deeds with fixed result in the next life. They can’t fall back on purification through boisterous, superstitious rites. They can’t seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an6-93-1-3

These are the six things that can’t be done.” ^an6-93-1-4

### Things That Can’t Be Done (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 6.94) ^an6-94-0

“Mendicants, these six things can’t be done. ^an6-94-1-1

What six? ^an6-94-1-2

An individual accomplished in view can’t murder their mother or father or a perfected one. They can’t maliciously shed the blood of the Realized One. They can’t cause a schism in the Saṅgha. They can’t dedicate themselves to another teacher. ^an6-94-1-3

These are the six things that can’t be done.” ^an6-94-1-4

### Things That Can’t Be Done (4th) (Numbered Discourses 6.95) ^an6-95-0

“Mendicants, these six things can’t be done. ^an6-95-1-1

What six? ^an6-95-1-2

An individual accomplished in view can’t fall back on the idea that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, or that they’re made by another, or that they’re made by both. Nor can they fall back on the idea that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself, by another, or by both. ^an6-95-1-3

Why is that? ^an6-95-1-4

It is because an individual accomplished in view has clearly seen causes and the phenomena that arise from causes. ^an6-95-1-5

These are the six things that can’t be done.” ^an6-95-1-6

### Appearance (Numbered Discourses 6.96) ^an6-96-0

“Mendicants, the appearance of six things is rare in the world. ^an6-96-1-1

What six? ^an6-96-1-2

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. A teacher of the teaching and training proclaimed by a Realized One. Rebirth in a civilized region. Unimpaired sense faculties. Being bright and clever. Enthusiasm for skillful qualities. ^an6-96-1-3

The appearance of these six things is rare in the world.” ^an6-96-1-4

### Benefit (Numbered Discourses 6.97) ^an6-97-0

“Mendicants, these are the six benefits of realizing the fruit of stream-entry. ^an6-97-1-1

What six? ^an6-97-1-2

You’re bound for the true teaching. You’re not liable to decline. You suffer only for a limited period. You have distinct knowledge. You’ve clearly seen causes and the phenomena that arise from causes. ^an6-97-1-3

These are the six benefits of realizing the fruit of stream-entry.” ^an6-97-1-4

### Impermanence (Numbered Discourses 6.98) ^an6-98-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a mendicant who regards any condition as permanent to accept views that conform with the teaching. ^an6-98-1-1

Without accepting views that conform with the teaching, it’s impossible to step into the sure path. ^an6-98-1-2

Without stepping into the sure path, it’s impossible to realize the fruit of stream-entry, once-return, non-return, or perfection. ^an6-98-1-3

It’s quite possible for a mendicant who regards all conditions as impermanent to accept views that conform with the teaching. ^an6-98-2-1

Having accepted views that conform with the teaching, it’s possible to step into the sure path. ^an6-98-2-2

Having stepped into the sure path, it’s possible to realize the fruit of stream-entry, once-return, non-return, or perfection.” ^an6-98-2-3

### Suffering (Numbered Discourses 6.99) ^an6-99-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a mendicant who regards any condition as pleasurable to accept views that agree with the teaching. … ^an6-99-1-1

It’s quite possible for a mendicant who regards all conditions as suffering to accept views that agree with the teaching. …” ^an6-99-1-2

### Not-Self (Numbered Discourses 6.100) ^an6-100-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a mendicant who regards any condition as self to accept views that agree with the teaching. … ^an6-100-1-1

It’s quite possible for a mendicant who regards all things as not-self to accept views that agree with the teaching. …” ^an6-100-1-2

### Extinguished (Numbered Discourses 6.101) ^an6-101-0

“Mendicants, it is quite impossible for a mendicant who regards extinguishment as suffering to accept views that conform with the teaching. … ^an6-101-1-1

It’s quite possible for a mendicant who regards extinguishment as pleasurable to accept views that conform with the teaching. …” ^an6-101-2-1

### Transience (Numbered Discourses 6.102) ^an6-102-0

“Mendicants, seeing six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of impermanence in all conditions without qualification. ^an6-102-1-1

What six? ^an6-102-1-2

‘All conditions will appear to me as transient.’ ‘My mind will not delight anywhere in the world.’ ‘My mind will rise above the whole world.’ ‘My heart will incline to extinguishment.’ ‘My fetters will be given up.’ ‘I will achieve the ultimate goal of the ascetic life.’ ^an6-102-1-3

Seeing these six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of impermanence in all conditions without qualification.” ^an6-102-1-4

### With a Drawn Sword (Numbered Discourses 6.103) ^an6-103-0

“Mendicants, seeing six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of suffering in all conditions without qualification. ^an6-103-1-1

What six? ^an6-103-1-2

‘Perception of disillusionment will be established in me for all conditions, like a killer with a drawn sword.’ ^an6-103-1-3

‘My mind will rise above the whole world.’ ‘I will see extinguishment as peaceful.’ ‘My underlying tendencies will be uprooted.’ ‘I will fulfill my duty.’ ‘I will have served my Teacher with love.’ ^an6-103-1-4

Seeing these six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of suffering in all conditions without qualification.” ^an6-103-1-5

### Not Determined by Anything (Numbered Discourses 6.104) ^an6-104-0

“Mendicants, seeing six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of not-self in all things without qualification. ^an6-104-1-1

What six? ^an6-104-1-2

‘I will not be determined by anything in the world.’ ‘My I-making will stop.’ ‘My mine-making will stop.’ ‘I will have distinct knowledge.’ ‘I will clearly see causes and the phenomena that arise from causes.’ ^an6-104-1-3

Seeing these six benefits is quite enough to establish the perception of not-self in all things without qualification.” ^an6-104-1-4

### States of Existence (Numbered Discourses 6.105) ^an6-105-0

“Mendicants, you should give up these three states of existence. And you should train in three trainings. ^an6-105-1-1

What are the three states of existence you should give up? ^an6-105-1-2

Existence in the sensual realm, the realm of luminous form, and the formless realm. ^an6-105-1-3

These are the three states of existence you should give up. ^an6-105-1-4

What are the three trainings you should train in? ^an6-105-1-5

The training in the higher ethics, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom. ^an6-105-1-6

These are the three trainings you should train in. ^an6-105-1-7

When a mendicant has given up these three states of existence and has trained in these three trainings ^an6-105-1-8

they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an6-105-1-9

### Craving (Numbered Discourses 6.106) ^an6-106-0

“Mendicants, you should give up these three cravings and three conceits. ^an6-106-1-1

What three cravings should you give up? ^an6-106-1-2

Craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence. ^an6-106-1-3

These are the three cravings you should give up. ^an6-106-1-4

What three conceits should you give up? ^an6-106-1-5

Conceit, inferiority complex, and superiority complex. ^an6-106-1-6

These are the three conceits you should give up. ^an6-106-1-7

When a mendicant has given up these three cravings and these three conceits ^an6-106-1-8

they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an6-106-1-9

### Greed (Numbered Discourses 6.107) ^an6-107-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-107-1-1

What three? ^an6-107-1-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an6-107-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-107-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-107-1-5

What three? ^an6-107-1-6

You should develop the perception of ugliness to give up greed, love to give up hate, and wisdom to give up delusion. ^an6-107-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-107-1-8

### Bad Conduct (Numbered Discourses 6.108) ^an6-108-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-108-1-1

What three? ^an6-108-1-2

Bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an6-108-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-108-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-108-1-5

What three? ^an6-108-1-6

You should develop good bodily conduct to give up bad bodily conduct, good verbal conduct to give up bad verbal conduct, and good mental conduct to give up bad mental conduct. ^an6-108-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-108-1-8

### Thoughts (Numbered Discourses 6.109) ^an6-109-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-109-1-1

What three? ^an6-109-1-2

Sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^an6-109-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-109-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-109-1-5

What three? ^an6-109-1-6

You should develop thoughts of renunciation to give up sensual thoughts, thoughts of good will to give up malicious thoughts, and thoughts of harmlessness to give up cruel thoughts. ^an6-109-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-109-1-8

### Perceptions (Numbered Discourses 6.110) ^an6-110-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-110-1-1

What three? ^an6-110-1-2

Sensual, malicious, and cruel perceptions. ^an6-110-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-110-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-110-1-5

What three? ^an6-110-1-6

You should develop perceptions of renunciation to give up sensual perceptions, perceptions of good will to give up malicious perceptions, and perceptions of harmlessness to give up cruel perceptions. ^an6-110-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-110-1-8

### Elements (Numbered Discourses 6.111) ^an6-111-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-111-1-1

What three? ^an6-111-1-2

The elements of sensuality, malice, and cruelty. ^an6-111-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-111-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-111-1-5

What three? ^an6-111-1-6

You should develop the element of renunciation to give up the element of sensuality, the element of good will to give up the element of malice, and the element of harmlessness to give up the element of cruelty. ^an6-111-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-111-1-8

### Gratification (Numbered Discourses 6.112) ^an6-112-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-112-1-1

What three? ^an6-112-1-2

The view that things are gratifying, the theory of self, and wrong view. ^an6-112-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-112-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-112-1-5

What three? ^an6-112-1-6

You should develop the perception of impermanence to give up the view that things are gratifying; the perception of not-self to give up the theory of self; and right view to give up wrong view. ^an6-112-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-112-1-8

### Discontent (Numbered Discourses 6.113) ^an6-113-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-113-1-1

What three? ^an6-113-1-2

Discontent, cruelty, and unprincipled conduct. ^an6-113-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-113-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-113-1-5

What three? ^an6-113-1-6

You should develop rejoicing to give up discontent, harmlessness to give up cruelty, and principled conduct to give up unprincipled conduct. ^an6-113-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-113-1-8

### Contentment (Numbered Discourses 6.114) ^an6-114-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-114-1-1

What three? ^an6-114-1-2

Lack of contentment, lack of situational awareness, and having many wishes. ^an6-114-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-114-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-114-1-5

What three? ^an6-114-1-6

You should develop contentment to give up lack of contentment, situational awareness to give up lack of situational awareness, and having few wishes to give up having many wishes. ^an6-114-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-114-1-8

### Hard to Admonish (Numbered Discourses 6.115) ^an6-115-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-115-1-1

What three? ^an6-115-1-2

Being hard to admonish, bad friendship, and a scattered mind. ^an6-115-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-115-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-115-1-5

What three? ^an6-115-1-6

You should develop being easy to admonish to give up being hard to admonish, good friendship to give up bad friendship, and mindfulness of breathing to give up a scattered mind. ^an6-115-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-115-1-8

### Restlessness (Numbered Discourses 6.116) ^an6-116-0

“Mendicants, there are these three things. ^an6-116-1-1

What three? ^an6-116-1-2

Restlessness, lack of restraint, and negligence. ^an6-116-1-3

These are the three things. ^an6-116-1-4

To give up these three things you should develop three things. ^an6-116-1-5

What three? ^an6-116-1-6

You should develop serenity to give up restlessness, restraint to give up lack of restraint, and diligence to give up negligence. ^an6-116-1-7

These are the three things you should develop to give up those three things.” ^an6-116-1-8

### Observing the Body (Numbered Discourses 6.117) ^an6-117-0

“Mendicants, without giving up these six qualities you can’t meditate observing an aspect of the body. ^an6-117-1-1

What six? ^an6-117-1-2

Relishing work, talk, sleep, and company, not guarding the sense doors, and eating too much. ^an6-117-1-3

Without giving up these six qualities you can’t meditate observing an aspect of the body. ^an6-117-1-4

But after giving up these six qualities you can meditate observing an aspect of the body. ^an6-117-2-1

What six? ^an6-117-2-2

Relishing work, talk, sleep, and company, not guarding the sense doors, and eating too much. ^an6-117-2-3

After giving up these six qualities you can meditate observing an aspect of the body.” ^an6-117-2-4

### Observing Principles, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 6.118) ^an6-118-0

“Mendicants, without giving up six things you can’t meditate observing an aspect of the body internally … ^an6-118-1-1

body externally … ^an6-118-1-2

body internally and externally … ^an6-118-1-3

feelings internally … ^an6-118-1-4

feelings externally … ^an6-118-1-5

feelings internally and externally … ^an6-118-1-6

mind internally … ^an6-118-1-7

mind externally … ^an6-118-1-8

mind internally and externally … ^an6-118-1-9

principles internally … ^an6-118-1-10

principles externally … ^an6-118-1-11

principles internally and externally. ^an6-118-1-12

What six? ^an6-118-1-13

Relishing work, talk, sleep, and company, not guarding the sense doors, and eating too much. ^an6-118-1-14

After giving up these six qualities you can meditate observing an aspect of principles internally and externally.” ^an6-118-1-15

### About Tapussa (Numbered Discourses 6.119) ^an6-119-0

“Mendicants, having six qualities the householder Tapussa is certain about the Realized One, sees freedom from death  and lives having realized freedom from death. ^an6-119-1-1

What six? ^an6-119-1-2

Experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and noble ethics, knowledge, and freedom. ^an6-119-1-3

Having these six qualities the householder Tapussa is certain about the Realized One, sees freedom from death and lives having realized freedom from death.” ^an6-119-1-4

### About Bhallika, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 6.120–139) ^an6-120-139-0

“Mendicants, having six qualities the householders Bhallika … ^an6-120-139-1-1

Sudatta Anāthapiṇḍika … ^an6-120-139-1-2

Citta of Macchikāsaṇḍa … ^an6-120-139-1-3

Hatthaka of Āḷavī … ^an6-120-139-1-4

Mahānāma the Sakyan … ^an6-120-139-1-5

Ugga of Vesālī … ^an6-120-139-1-6

Uggata … ^an6-120-139-1-7

Sūrambaṭṭha … ^an6-120-139-1-8

Jīvaka Komārabhacca … ^an6-120-139-1-9

Nakula’s father … ^an6-120-139-1-10

Tavakaṇṇika … ^an6-120-139-1-11

Pūraṇa … ^an6-120-139-1-12

Isidatta … ^an6-120-139-1-13

Sandhāna … ^an6-120-139-1-14

Vijaya … ^an6-120-139-1-15

Vijayamāhita … ^an6-120-139-1-16

Meṇḍaka … ^an6-120-139-1-17

the lay followers Vāseṭṭha … ^an6-120-139-1-18

Ariṭṭha … ^an6-120-139-1-19

and Sāragga are certain about the Realized One, see freedom from death and live having realized freedom from death. ^an6-120-139-1-20

What six? ^an6-120-139-1-21

Experiential confidence in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and noble ethics, knowledge, and freedom. ^an6-120-139-1-22

Having these six qualities the lay follower Sāragga is certain about the Realized One, sees freedom from death and lives having realized freedom from death.” ^an6-120-139-1-23

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (1st) (Numbered Discourses 6) ^an6-140-0

“For insight into greed, six things should be developed. ^an6-140-1-1

What six? ^an6-140-1-2

The unsurpassable seeing, listening, acquisition, training, service, and recollection. ^an6-140-1-3

For insight into greed, these six things should be developed.” ^an6-140-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 6) ^an6-141-0

“For insight into greed, six things should be developed. ^an6-141-1-1

What six? ^an6-141-1-2

The recollection of the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, ethics, generosity, and the deities. ^an6-141-1-3

For insight into greed, these six things should be developed.” ^an6-141-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 6) ^an6-142-0

“For insight into greed, six things should be developed. ^an6-142-1-1

What six? ^an6-142-1-2

The perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of giving up, the perception of fading away, and the perception of cessation. ^an6-142-1-3

For insight into greed, these six things should be developed.” ^an6-142-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 6) ^an6-143-169-0

“For the complete understanding of greed … ^an6-143-169-1-1

complete ending … ^an6-143-169-1-2

giving up … ^an6-143-169-1-3

ending … ^an6-143-169-1-4

vanishing … ^an6-143-169-1-5

fading away … ^an6-143-169-1-6

cessation … ^an6-143-169-1-7

giving away … ^an6-143-169-1-8

letting go of greed these six things should be developed.” ^an6-143-169-1-9

### Untitled Discourses on Hate, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 6) ^an6-170-649-0

“Of hate … ^an6-170-649-1-1

delusion … ^an6-170-649-1-2

anger … ^an6-170-649-1-3

acrimony … ^an6-170-649-1-4

disdain … ^an6-170-649-1-5

contempt … ^an6-170-649-1-6

jealousy … ^an6-170-649-1-7

stinginess … ^an6-170-649-1-8

deceitfulness … ^an6-170-649-1-9

deviousness … ^an6-170-649-1-10

obstinacy … ^an6-170-649-1-11

aggression … ^an6-170-649-1-12

conceit … ^an6-170-649-1-13

arrogance … ^an6-170-649-1-14

vanity … ^an6-170-649-1-15

for insight into negligence … ^an6-170-649-1-16

complete understanding … ^an6-170-649-1-17

complete ending … ^an6-170-649-1-18

giving up … ^an6-170-649-1-19

ending … ^an6-170-649-1-20

vanishing … ^an6-170-649-1-21

fading away … ^an6-170-649-1-22

cessation … ^an6-170-649-1-23

giving away … ^an6-170-649-1-24

letting go of negligence ^an6-170-649-1-25

these six things should be developed.” ^an6-170-649-1-26

That is what the Buddha said. ^an6-170-649-1-27

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an6-170-649-1-28

The Book of the Sixes is finished. ^an6-170-649-1-30

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 7 ^an7-0

### Pleasing (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.1) ^an7-1-0

So I have heard. ^an7-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an7-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an7-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an7-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an7-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an7-1-1-6

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an7-1-2-1

What seven? ^an7-1-2-2

It’s when a mendicant desires material things, honor, and status. They lack conscience and prudence. They have corrupt wishes and wrong view. ^an7-1-2-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an7-1-2-4

A mendicant with seven qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an7-1-3-1

What seven? ^an7-1-3-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t desire material things, honor, and status. They have conscience and prudence. They have few desires and right view. ^an7-1-3-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an7-1-3-4

### Pleasing (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.2) ^an7-2-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an7-2-1-1

What seven? ^an7-2-1-2

It’s when a mendicant desires material things, honor, and status. They lack conscience and prudence. They’re jealous and stingy. ^an7-2-1-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an7-2-1-4

A mendicant with seven qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, respected and admired. ^an7-2-2-1

What seven? ^an7-2-2-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t desire material things, honor, and status. They have conscience and prudence. They’re not jealous or stingy. ^an7-2-2-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, respected and admired.” ^an7-2-2-4

### Powers in Brief (Numbered Discourses 7.3) ^an7-3-0

So I have heard. ^an7-3-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. … ^an7-3-1-2

“Mendicants, there are these seven powers. ^an7-3-1-3

What seven? ^an7-3-1-4

The powers of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an7-3-1-5

These are the seven powers. ^an7-3-1-6

The powers are faith and energy, ^an7-3-2-1

conscience and prudence, ^an7-3-2-2

mindfulness and immersion, ^an7-3-2-3

and wisdom as the seventh power. ^an7-3-2-4

Empowered by these, ^an7-3-2-5

an astute mendicant lives happily. ^an7-3-2-6

They should examine the teaching rationally, ^an7-3-3-1

discerning the meaning with wisdom. ^an7-3-3-2

The liberation of their heart ^an7-3-3-3

is like a lamp going out.” ^an7-3-3-4

### Powers in Detail (Numbered Discourses 7.4) ^an7-4-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven powers. ^an7-4-1-1

What seven? ^an7-4-1-2

The powers of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom. ^an7-4-1-3

And what is the power of faith? ^an7-4-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an7-4-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an7-4-2-3

This is called the power of faith. ^an7-4-2-4

And what is the power of energy? ^an7-4-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an7-4-3-2

This is called the power of energy. ^an7-4-3-3

And what is the power of conscience? ^an7-4-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple has a conscience. They’re conscientious about bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and conscientious about acquiring any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an7-4-4-2

This is called the power of conscience. ^an7-4-4-3

And what is the power of prudence? ^an7-4-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple is prudent. They’re prudent when it comes to bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and prudent when it comes to the acquiring of any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an7-4-5-2

This is called the power of prudence. ^an7-4-5-3

And what is the power of mindfulness? ^an7-4-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple is mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. ^an7-4-6-2

This is called the power of mindfulness. ^an7-4-6-3

And what is the power of immersion? ^an7-4-7-1

It’s when a noble disciple, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. … ^an7-4-7-2

With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an7-4-7-4

This is called the power of immersion. ^an7-4-7-5

And what is the power of wisdom? ^an7-4-8-1

It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an7-4-8-2

This is called the power of wisdom. ^an7-4-8-3

These are the seven powers. ^an7-4-9-1

The powers are faith and energy, ^an7-4-10-1

conscience and prudence, ^an7-4-10-2

mindfulness and immersion, ^an7-4-10-3

and wisdom as the seventh power. ^an7-4-10-4

Empowered by these, ^an7-4-10-5

an astute mendicant lives happily. ^an7-4-10-6

They should examine the teaching rationally, ^an7-4-11-1

discerning the meaning with wisdom. ^an7-4-11-2

The liberation of their heart ^an7-4-11-3

is like a lamp going out.” ^an7-4-11-4

### Wealth in Brief (Numbered Discourses 7.5) ^an7-5-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven kinds of wealth. ^an7-5-1-1

What seven? ^an7-5-1-2

The wealth of faith, ethics, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an7-5-1-3

These are the seven kinds of wealth. ^an7-5-1-4

Faith and ethical conduct are kinds of wealth, ^an7-5-2-1

as are conscience and prudence, ^an7-5-2-2

learning and generosity, ^an7-5-2-3

and wisdom is the seventh kind of wealth. ^an7-5-2-4

When a woman or man ^an7-5-3-1

has these kinds of wealth, ^an7-5-3-2

they’re said to be prosperous, ^an7-5-3-3

their life is not in vain. ^an7-5-3-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^an7-5-4-1

to faith, ethical behavior, ^an7-5-4-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^an7-5-4-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an7-5-4-4

### Wealth in Detail (Numbered Discourses 7.6) ^an7-6-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven kinds of wealth. ^an7-6-1-1

What seven? ^an7-6-1-2

The wealth of faith, ethics, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an7-6-1-3

And what is the wealth of faith? ^an7-6-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening … ^an7-6-2-2

This is called the wealth of faith. ^an7-6-2-4

And what is the wealth of ethical conduct? ^an7-6-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, use speech that’s false, backbiting, harsh, or nonsensical, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an7-6-3-2

This is called the wealth of ethical conduct. ^an7-6-3-3

And what is the wealth of conscience? ^an7-6-4-1

It’s when a noble disciple has a conscience. They’re conscientious about bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and conscientious about having any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an7-6-4-2

This is called the wealth of conscience. ^an7-6-4-3

And what is the wealth of prudence? ^an7-6-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple is prudent. They’re prudent when it comes to bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and prudent when it comes to the acquiring of any bad, unskillful qualities. ^an7-6-5-2

This is called the wealth of prudence. ^an7-6-5-3

And what is the wealth of learning? ^an7-6-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an7-6-6-2

This is called the wealth of learning. ^an7-6-6-3

And what is the wealth of generosity? ^an7-6-7-1

It’s when a noble disciple lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an7-6-7-2

This is called the wealth of generosity. ^an7-6-7-3

And what is the wealth of wisdom? ^an7-6-8-1

It’s when a noble disciple is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an7-6-8-2

This is called the wealth of wisdom. ^an7-6-8-3

These are the seven kinds of wealth. ^an7-6-9-1

Faith and ethical conduct are kinds of wealth, ^an7-6-10-1

as are conscience and prudence, ^an7-6-10-2

learning and generosity, ^an7-6-10-3

and wisdom is the seventh kind of wealth. ^an7-6-10-4

When a woman or man ^an7-6-11-1

has these kinds of wealth, ^an7-6-11-2

they’re said to be prosperous, ^an7-6-11-3

their life is not in vain. ^an7-6-11-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^an7-6-12-1

to faith, ethical behavior, ^an7-6-12-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^an7-6-12-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an7-6-12-4

### With Ugga (Numbered Discourses 7.7) ^an7-7-0

Then Ugga the government chief minister went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an7-7-1-1

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an7-7-2-1

Migāra of Rohaṇa is so rich, so very wealthy.” ^an7-7-2-2

“But Ugga, how rich is he?” ^an7-7-2-3

“He has a hundred thousand gold coins, not to mention the silver coins!” ^an7-7-2-4

“Well, Ugga, that is wealth, I can’t deny it. ^an7-7-2-5

But fire, water, rulers, thieves, and unloved heirs all take a share of that wealth. ^an7-7-2-6

There are these seven kinds of wealth that they can’t take a share of. ^an7-7-2-7

What seven? ^an7-7-2-8

The wealth of faith, ethics, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an7-7-2-9

There are these seven kinds of wealth that fire, water, rulers, thieves, and unloved heirs can’t take a share of. ^an7-7-2-10

Faith and ethical conduct are kinds of wealth, ^an7-7-3-1

as are conscience and prudence, ^an7-7-3-2

learning and generosity, ^an7-7-3-3

and wisdom is the seventh kind of wealth. ^an7-7-3-4

When a woman or man ^an7-7-4-1

has these kinds of wealth, ^an7-7-4-2

they’re really rich in the world, ^an7-7-4-3

invincible among gods and humans. ^an7-7-4-4

So let the wise devote themselves ^an7-7-5-1

to faith, ethical behavior, ^an7-7-5-2

confidence, and insight into the teaching, ^an7-7-5-3

remembering the instructions of the Buddhas.” ^an7-7-5-4

### Fetters (Numbered Discourses 7.8) ^an7-8-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven fetters. ^an7-8-1-1

What seven? ^an7-8-1-2

The fetters of attraction, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-8-1-3

These are the seven fetters.” ^an7-8-1-4

### Giving Up (Numbered Discourses 7.9) ^an7-9-0

“Mendicants, the spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these seven fetters. ^an7-9-1-1

What seven? ^an7-9-1-2

The fetters of attraction, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an7-9-1-3

The spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these seven fetters. ^an7-9-1-9

When a mendicant has given up the fetters of attraction, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance—cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future— ^an7-9-1-10

they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an7-9-1-17

### Stinginess (Numbered Discourses 7.10) ^an7-10-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven fetters. ^an7-10-1-1

What seven? ^an7-10-1-2

The fetters of attraction, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, jealousy, and stinginess. ^an7-10-1-3

These are the seven fetters.” ^an7-10-1-4

### Underlying Tendencies (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.11) ^an7-11-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven underlying tendencies. ^an7-11-1-1

What seven? ^an7-11-1-2

The underlying tendencies of sensual desire, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-11-1-3

These are the seven underlying tendencies.” ^an7-11-1-4

### Underlying Tendencies (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.12) ^an7-12-0

“Mendicants, the spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these seven underlying tendencies. ^an7-12-1-1

What seven? ^an7-12-1-2

The underlying tendencies of sensual desire, aversion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an7-12-1-3

The spiritual life is lived to give up and cut out these seven underlying tendencies. ^an7-12-1-9

When a mendicant has given up the underlying tendencies of sensual desire, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance—cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future— ^an7-12-2-1

they’re called a mendicant who has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.” ^an7-12-2-8

### A Family (Numbered Discourses 7.13) ^an7-13-0

“Mendicants, visiting a family with seven factors is not worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is not worthwhile. ^an7-13-1-1

What seven? ^an7-13-1-2

They don’t politely rise, bow, or offer a seat. They hide what they have. Even when they have much they give little. Even when they have fine things they give coarse things. They give carelessly, not carefully. ^an7-13-1-3

Visiting a family with these seven factors is not worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is not worthwhile. ^an7-13-1-4

Visiting a family with seven factors is worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is worthwhile. ^an7-13-2-1

What seven? ^an7-13-2-2

They politely rise, bow, and offer a seat. They don’t hide what they have. When they have much they give much. When they have refined things they give refined things. They give carefully, not carelessly. ^an7-13-2-3

Visiting a family with these seven factors is worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is worthwhile.” ^an7-13-2-4

### Individuals (Numbered Discourses 7.14) ^an7-14-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an7-14-1-1

What seven? ^an7-14-1-2

The one freed both ways, the one freed by wisdom, the direct witness, the one attained to view, the one freed by faith, the follower of teachings, and the follower by faith. ^an7-14-1-3

These are the seven individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an7-14-1-4

### A Simile With Water (Numbered Discourses 7.15) ^an7-15-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals found in the world are like those in water. ^an7-15-1-1

What seven? ^an7-15-1-2

One individual sinks under once and stays under. ^an7-15-1-3

One individual rises up then sinks under. ^an7-15-1-4

One individual rises up then stays put. ^an7-15-1-5

One individual rises up then sees and discerns. ^an7-15-1-6

One individual rises up then crosses over. ^an7-15-1-7

One individual rises up then finds a foothold. ^an7-15-1-8

One individual has risen up, crossed over, and gone to the far shore, a brahmin who stands on solid ground. ^an7-15-1-9

And what kind of individual sinks under once and stays under? ^an7-15-2-1

It’s the kind of individual who has exclusively dark, unskillful qualities. ^an7-15-2-2

This kind of individual sinks under once and stays under. ^an7-15-2-3

And what kind of individual rises up then sinks under? ^an7-15-3-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-3-2

However their faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom don’t last or grow, but dwindle away. ^an7-15-3-6

This kind of individual rises up then sinks under. ^an7-15-3-10

And what kind of individual rises up then stays put? ^an7-15-4-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-4-2

And their faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom lasts, neither dwindling nor growing. ^an7-15-4-6

This kind of individual rises up then stays put. ^an7-15-4-11

And what kind of individual rises up then sees and discerns? ^an7-15-5-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-5-2

With the ending of three fetters they’re a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, assured, destined for awakening. ^an7-15-5-6

This kind of individual rises up then sees and discerns. ^an7-15-5-7

And what kind of individual rises up then crosses over? ^an7-15-6-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-6-2

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an7-15-6-6

This kind of individual rises up then crosses over. ^an7-15-6-7

And what kind of individual rises up then finds a foothold? ^an7-15-7-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-7-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an7-15-7-6

This kind of individual rises up then finds a foothold. ^an7-15-7-7

And what kind of individual has risen up, crossed over, and gone to the far shore, a brahmin who stands on solid ground? ^an7-15-8-1

It’s the kind of individual who, rising up, thinks: ‘It’s good to have faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom regarding skillful qualities.’ ^an7-15-8-2

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-15-8-6

This kind of individual has risen up, crossed over, and gone to the far shore, a brahmin who stands on solid ground. ^an7-15-8-7

These seven individuals found in the world are like those in water.” ^an7-15-9-1

### Observing Impermanence (Numbered Discourses 7.16) ^an7-16-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an7-16-1-1

What seven? ^an7-16-1-2

First, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in all conditions. They perceive impermanence and experience impermanence. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. ^an7-16-1-3

They’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-16-1-4

This is the first individual. ^an7-16-1-5

Next, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in all conditions. ^an7-16-2-1

Their defilements and their life come to an end at exactly the same time. ^an7-16-2-2

This is the second individual. ^an7-16-2-3

Next, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in all conditions. ^an7-16-3-1

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. … ^an7-16-3-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. … ^an7-16-3-3

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. … ^an7-16-3-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. … ^an7-16-3-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an7-16-3-6

This is the seventh individual. ^an7-16-3-7

These are the seven individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an7-16-3-8

### Observing Suffering (Numbered Discourses 7.17) ^an7-17-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an7-17-1-1

What seven? ^an7-17-1-2

First, take an individual who meditates observing suffering in all conditions. They perceive suffering and experience suffering. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. …” ^an7-17-1-3

### Observing Not-self (Numbered Discourses 7.18) ^an7-18-0

“First, take an individual who meditates observing not-self in all things. They perceive not-self and experience not-self. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. …” ^an7-18-1-1

### Extinguishment (Numbered Discourses 7.19) ^an7-19-0

“One meditates observing the happiness in extinguishment. They perceive happiness and experience happiness. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. ^an7-19-1-1

They’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-19-1-2

This is the first individual worthy of offerings. ^an7-19-1-3

Next, take an individual who meditates observing the happiness in extinguishment. They perceive happiness and experience happiness. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. ^an7-19-2-1

Their defilements and their life come to an end at exactly the same time. ^an7-19-2-2

This is the second individual. ^an7-19-2-3

Next, take an individual who meditates observing the happiness in extinguishment. They perceive happiness and experience happiness. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. ^an7-19-3-1

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. … ^an7-19-3-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. … ^an7-19-3-3

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. … ^an7-19-3-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. … ^an7-19-3-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an7-19-3-6

This is the seventh individual. ^an7-19-3-7

These are the seven individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an7-19-3-8

### Qualifications for Graduation (Numbered Discourses 7.20) ^an7-20-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven qualifications for graduation. ^an7-20-1-1

What seven? ^an7-20-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has a keen enthusiasm to undertake the training … ^an7-20-1-3

to examine the teachings … ^an7-20-1-4

to get rid of desires … ^an7-20-1-5

for retreat … ^an7-20-1-6

to rouse up energy … ^an7-20-1-7

for mindfulness and alertness … ^an7-20-1-8

to penetrate theoretically. And they don’t lose these desires in the future. ^an7-20-1-9

These are the seven qualifications for graduation.” ^an7-20-1-10

### At Sārandada (Numbered Discourses 7.21) ^an7-21-0

So I have heard. ^an7-21-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Sārandada Shrine. ^an7-21-1-2

Then several Licchavis went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and the Buddha said to these Licchavis: ^an7-21-1-3

“Licchavis, I will teach you these seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-21-1-4

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an7-21-1-5

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an7-21-1-6

The Buddha said this: ^an7-21-1-7

“And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-21-2-1

As long as the Vajjis meet frequently and have many meetings, ^an7-21-2-2

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-2-3

As long as the Vajjis meet in harmony, leave in harmony, and carry on their business in harmony, ^an7-21-3-1

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-3-2

As long as the Vajjis don’t make new decrees or abolish existing decrees, but undertake and follow the ancient Vajjian traditions as they have been decreed, ^an7-21-4-1

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-4-2

As long as the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate Vajjian elders, and think them worth listening to, ^an7-21-5-1

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-5-2

As long as the Vajjis don’t forcibly abduct the women or girls of the clans and make them live with them, ^an7-21-6-1

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-6-2

As long as the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Vajjian shrines, whether inner or outer, not neglecting the proper spirit-offerings that were given and made in the past, ^an7-21-7-1

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-7-2

As long as the Vajjis organize proper protection, shelter, and security for perfected ones, so that ^an7-21-8-1

more perfected ones might come to the realm and those already here may live in comfort, ^an7-21-8-2

they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-21-8-3

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the Vajjis, and as long as the Vajjis are seen following them, ^an7-21-9-1

they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-21-9-2

### With Vassakāra (Numbered Discourses 7.22) ^an7-22-0

So I have heard. ^an7-22-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an7-22-1-2

Now at that time King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, wanted to invade the Vajjis. ^an7-22-1-3

He declared: ^an7-22-1-4

“I shall wipe out these Vajjis, so mighty and powerful! I shall destroy them, and lay ruin and devastation upon them!” ^an7-22-1-5

And then King Ajātasattu addressed Vassakāra the brahmin minister of Magadha, ^an7-22-2-1

“Please, brahmin, go to the Buddha, and in my name bow with your head to his feet. Ask him if he is healthy and well, nimble, strong, and living comfortably. ^an7-22-2-2

And then say: ^an7-22-2-4

‘Sir, King Ajātasattu of Magadha, son of the princess of Videha, wants to invade the Vajjis. ^an7-22-2-5

He has declared: ^an7-22-2-6

“I shall wipe out these Vajjis, so mighty and powerful! I shall destroy them, and lay ruin and devastation upon them!”’ ^an7-22-2-7

Remember well how the Buddha answers and tell it to me. ^an7-22-2-8

For Realized Ones say nothing that is not so.” ^an7-22-2-9

“Yes, worthy sir,” Vassakāra replied. He went to the Buddha and exchanged greetings with him. ^an7-22-3-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an7-22-3-2

“Worthy Gotama, King Ajātasattu bows with his head to your feet. He asks if you are healthy and well, nimble, strong, and living comfortably. ^an7-22-3-3

King Ajātasattu wants to invade the Vajjis. ^an7-22-3-4

He has declared: ^an7-22-3-5

‘I shall wipe out these Vajjis, so mighty and powerful! I shall destroy them, and lay ruin and devastation upon them!’” ^an7-22-3-6

Now at that time Venerable Ānanda was standing behind the Buddha fanning him. ^an7-22-4-1

Then the Buddha said to him: ^an7-22-4-2

“Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis meet frequently and have many meetings?” ^an7-22-4-3

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-4-4

“As long as the Vajjis meet frequently and have many meetings, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-4-5

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis meet in harmony, leave in harmony, and carry on their business in harmony?” ^an7-22-5-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-5-2

“As long as the Vajjis meet in harmony, leave in harmony, and carry on their business in harmony, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-5-3

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis don’t make new decrees or abolish existing decrees, but proceed having undertaken the ancient Vajjian principles as they have been decreed?” ^an7-22-6-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-6-2

“As long as the Vajjis don’t make new decrees or abolish existing decrees, but proceed having undertaken the ancient Vajjian traditions as they have been decreed, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-6-3

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate Vajjian elders, and think them worth listening to?” ^an7-22-7-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-7-2

“As long as the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate Vajjian elders, and think them worth listening to, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-7-3

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis don’t forcibly abduct the women or girls of the clans and make them live with them?” ^an7-22-8-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-8-2

“As long as the Vajjis don’t forcibly abduct the women or girls of the clans and make them live with them, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-8-3

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Vajjian shrines, whether inner or outer, not neglecting the proper spirit-offerings that were given and made in the past?” ^an7-22-9-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-9-2

“As long as the Vajjis honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Vajjian shrines, whether inner or outer, not neglecting the proper spirit-offerings that were given and made in the past, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-9-3

Ānanda, have you heard that the Vajjis organize proper protection, shelter, and security for perfected ones, so that more perfected ones might come to the realm and those already here may live in comfort?” ^an7-22-10-1

“I have heard that, sir.” ^an7-22-10-2

“As long as the Vajjis organize proper protection, shelter, and security for perfected ones, so that more perfected ones might come to the realm and those already here may live in comfort, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-22-10-3

Then the Buddha said to Vassakāra: ^an7-22-11-1

“Brahmin, this one time I was staying near Vesālī at the Sārandada Shrine. ^an7-22-11-2

There I taught the Vajjis these principles that prevent decline. ^an7-22-11-3

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the Vajjis, and as long as the Vajjis are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-22-11-4

When the Buddha had spoken, Vassakāra said to him: “Worthy Gotama, if the Vajjis follow even a single one of these principles they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-22-12-1

How much more so all seven! ^an7-22-12-2

King Ajātasattu cannot defeat the Vajjis in war, unless by bribery or by sowing dissension. ^an7-22-12-3

Well, now, worthy Gotama, I must go. I have many duties, and much to do.” ^an7-22-12-4

“Please, brahmin, go at your convenience.” ^an7-22-12-5

Then Vassakāra the brahmin, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, rose from his seat and left. ^an7-22-12-6

### Non-Decline for Mendicants (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.23) ^an7-23-0

So I have heard. ^an7-23-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an7-23-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an7-23-1-3

“Mendicants, I will teach you these seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-23-1-4

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an7-23-1-5

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an7-23-1-6

The Buddha said this: ^an7-23-1-7

“What are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-23-2-1

As long as the mendicants meet frequently and have many meetings, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-2-2

As long as the mendicants meet in harmony, leave in harmony, and carry on their business in harmony, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-3-1

As long as the mendicants don’t make new decrees or abolish existing decrees, but undertake and follow the training rules as they have been decreed, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-4-1

As long as the mendicants honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the senior mendicants—of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha—and think them worth listening to, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-5-1

As long as the mendicants don’t fall under the sway of arisen craving for future lives, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-6-1

As long as the mendicants take care to live in wilderness lodgings, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-7-1

As long as the mendicants individually establish mindfulness, so that more good-hearted spiritual companions might come, and those that have already come may live comfortably, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-23-8-1

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the mendicants, and as long as the mendicants are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-23-9-1

### Non-Decline for Mendicants (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.24) ^an7-24-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-24-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well … ^an7-24-1-2

And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-24-1-3

As long as the mendicants don’t relish work, loving it and liking to relish it, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-24-2-1

As long as they don’t enjoy talk … ^an7-24-3-1

sleep … ^an7-24-3-2

company … ^an7-24-3-3

they don’t have corrupt wishes, falling under the sway of corrupt wishes … ^an7-24-3-4

they don’t have bad friends, companions, and associates … ^an7-24-3-5

they don’t stop half-way after achieving some trifling distinction, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-24-3-6

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the mendicants, and as long as the mendicants are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-24-4-1

### Non-Decline for Mendicants (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 7.25) ^an7-25-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-25-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well … ^an7-25-1-2

And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-25-1-3

As long as the mendicants are faithful … ^an7-25-1-4

conscientious … ^an7-25-2-1

prudent … ^an7-25-2-2

learned … ^an7-25-2-3

energetic … ^an7-25-2-4

mindful … ^an7-25-2-5

wise, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-25-2-6

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the mendicants, and as long as the mendicants are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-25-3-1

### Awakening Factors (Numbered Discourses 7.26) ^an7-26-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-26-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well … ^an7-26-1-2

And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-26-1-3

As long as the mendicants develop the awakening factor of mindfulness … ^an7-26-1-4

investigation of principles … ^an7-26-2-1

energy … ^an7-26-2-2

rapture … ^an7-26-2-3

tranquility … ^an7-26-2-4

immersion … ^an7-26-2-5

equanimity, they can expect growth, not decline. ^an7-26-2-6

As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the mendicants, and as long as the mendicants are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-26-3-1

### Perceptions (Numbered Discourses 7.27) ^an7-27-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline. ^an7-27-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well … ^an7-27-1-2

And what are the seven principles that prevent decline? ^an7-27-1-3

As long as the mendicants develop the perception of impermanence … ^an7-27-1-4

not-self … ^an7-27-2-1

ugliness … ^an7-27-2-2

drawbacks … ^an7-27-2-3

giving up … ^an7-27-2-4

fading away … ^an7-27-2-5

cessation, ^an7-27-2-6

they can expect growth, not decline. As long as these seven principles that prevent decline last among the mendicants, and as long as the mendicants are seen following them, they can expect growth, not decline.” ^an7-27-3-1

### Non-decline for a Mendicant Trainee (Numbered Discourses 7.28) ^an7-28-0

So I have heard. ^an7-28-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an7-28-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an7-28-1-3

“These seven things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an7-28-1-4

What seven? ^an7-28-1-5

They relish work, talk, sleep, and company. They don’t guard the sense doors and they eat too much. And when there is Saṅgha business to be carried out, they don’t reflect: ^an7-28-1-6

‘There are senior mendicants in the Saṅgha of long standing, long gone forth, responsible. They’ll be known for taking care of this.’ So they get involved in that. ^an7-28-1-7

These seven things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an7-28-1-8

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an7-28-2-1

What seven? ^an7-28-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, sleep, and company. They guard the sense doors and don’t eat too much. And when there is Saṅgha business to be carried out, they reflect: ^an7-28-2-3

‘There are senior mendicants in the Saṅgha of long standing, long gone forth, responsible. They’ll be known for taking care of this.’ So they don’t get involved in that. ^an7-28-2-4

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an7-28-2-5

### Non-decline for a Lay Follower (Numbered Discourses 7.29) ^an7-29-0

“These seven things lead to the decline of a lay follower. ^an7-29-1-1

What seven? ^an7-29-1-2

They miss out on seeing the mendicants. ^an7-29-1-3

They neglect listening to the true teaching. ^an7-29-1-4

They don’t train in higher ethical conduct. ^an7-29-1-5

They’re very suspicious about mendicants, whether senior, junior, or middle. ^an7-29-1-6

They listen to the teaching with a hostile, fault-finding mind. ^an7-29-1-7

They seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an7-29-1-8

And they serve them first. ^an7-29-1-9

These seven things lead to the decline of a lay follower. ^an7-29-1-10

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a lay follower. ^an7-29-2-1

What seven? ^an7-29-2-2

They don’t miss out on seeing the mendicants. ^an7-29-2-3

They don’t neglect listening to the true teaching. ^an7-29-2-4

They train in higher ethical conduct. ^an7-29-2-5

They’re very confident about mendicants, whether senior, junior, or middle. ^an7-29-2-6

They don’t listen to the teaching with a hostile, fault-finding mind. ^an7-29-2-7

They don’t seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an7-29-2-8

And they serve the Buddhist community first. ^an7-29-2-9

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a lay follower.” ^an7-29-2-10

That is what the Buddha said. ^an7-29-2-11

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^an7-29-2-12

“A lay follower misses out on seeing ^an7-29-3-1

those who are evolved ^an7-29-3-2

and listening to the teachings of the Noble One. ^an7-29-3-3

They don’t train in higher ethical conduct, ^an7-29-3-4

and their suspicion about mendicants ^an7-29-4-1

just grows and grows. ^an7-29-4-2

They want to listen to the true teaching ^an7-29-4-3

with a fault-finding mind. ^an7-29-4-4

They seek outside the Buddhist community ^an7-29-5-1

for someone else worthy of religious donations, ^an7-29-5-2

and that lay follower ^an7-29-5-3

serves them first. ^an7-29-5-4

These seven principles leading to decline ^an7-29-6-1

have been well taught. ^an7-29-6-2

A lay follower who practices them ^an7-29-6-3

falls away from the true teaching. ^an7-29-6-4

A lay follower doesn’t miss out on seeing ^an7-29-7-1

those who are evolved ^an7-29-7-2

and listening to the teachings of the Noble One. ^an7-29-7-3

They train in higher ethical conduct, ^an7-29-7-4

and their confidence in mendicants ^an7-29-8-1

just grows and grows. ^an7-29-8-2

They want to listen to the true teaching ^an7-29-8-3

without a fault-finding mind. ^an7-29-8-4

They don’t seek outside the Buddhist community ^an7-29-9-1

for someone else worthy of religious donations, ^an7-29-9-2

and that lay follower ^an7-29-9-3

serves the Buddhist community first. ^an7-29-9-4

These seven principles that prevent decline ^an7-29-10-1

have been well taught. ^an7-29-10-2

A lay follower who practices them ^an7-29-10-3

doesn’t fall away from the true teaching.” ^an7-29-10-4

### Failures for a Lay Follower (Numbered Discourses 7.30) ^an7-30-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven failures for a lay follower … ^an7-30-1-1

There are these seven accomplishments for a lay follower …” ^an7-30-1-2

### Downfalls for a Lay Follower (Numbered Discourses 7.31) ^an7-31-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven downfalls for a lay follower … ^an7-31-1-1

There are these seven successes for a lay follower. ^an7-31-1-2

What seven? ^an7-31-1-3

They don’t miss out on seeing the mendicants. ^an7-31-1-4

They don’t neglect listening to the true teaching. ^an7-31-1-5

They train in higher ethical conduct. ^an7-31-1-6

They’re very confident about mendicants, whether senior, junior, or middle. ^an7-31-1-7

They don’t listen to the teaching with a hostile, fault-finding mind. ^an7-31-1-8

They don’t seek outside of the Buddhist community for those worthy of religious donations. ^an7-31-1-9

And they serve the Buddhist community first. ^an7-31-1-10

These are the seven successes for a lay follower. ^an7-31-1-11

A lay follower misses out on seeing ^an7-31-2-1

those who are evolved ^an7-31-2-2

and listening to the teachings of the Noble One. ^an7-31-2-3

They don’t train in higher ethical conduct, ^an7-31-2-4

and their suspicion about mendicants ^an7-31-3-1

just grows and grows. ^an7-31-3-2

They want to listen to the true teaching ^an7-31-3-3

with a fault-finding mind. ^an7-31-3-4

They seek outside the Buddhist community ^an7-31-4-1

for someone else worthy of religious donations, ^an7-31-4-2

and that lay follower ^an7-31-4-3

serves them first. ^an7-31-4-4

These seven principles leading to decline ^an7-31-5-1

have been well taught. ^an7-31-5-2

A lay follower who practices these ^an7-31-5-3

falls away from the true teaching. ^an7-31-5-4

A lay follower doesn’t miss out on seeing ^an7-31-6-1

those who are evolved ^an7-31-6-2

and listening to the teachings of the Noble One. ^an7-31-6-3

They train in higher ethical conduct, ^an7-31-6-4

and their confidence in mendicants ^an7-31-7-1

just grows and grows. ^an7-31-7-2

They want to listen to the true teaching ^an7-31-7-3

without a fault-finding mind. ^an7-31-7-4

They don’t seek outside the Buddhist community ^an7-31-8-1

for someone else worthy of religious donations, ^an7-31-8-2

and that lay follower ^an7-31-8-3

serves the Buddhist community first. ^an7-31-8-4

These seven principles that prevent decline ^an7-31-9-1

have been well taught. ^an7-31-9-2

A lay follower who practices them ^an7-31-9-3

doesn’t fall away from the true teaching.” ^an7-31-9-4

### Respect for Diligence (Numbered Discourses 7.32) ^an7-32-0

Then, late at night, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an7-32-1-1
