“Sir, these seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an7-32-2-1

What seven? ^an7-32-2-2

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for immersion, for diligence, and for hospitality. ^an7-32-2-3

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.” ^an7-32-2-4

That’s what that deity said, ^an7-32-2-5

and the teacher approved. ^an7-32-2-6

Then that deity, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^an7-32-2-7

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened, adding: ^an7-32-3-1

“Respect for the Teacher and the teaching, ^an7-32-4-1

and keen respect for the Saṅgha; ^an7-32-4-2

respect for immersion, being energetic, ^an7-32-4-3

and keen respect for the training. ^an7-32-4-4

A mendicant who respects diligence ^an7-32-5-1

and hospitality ^an7-32-5-2

can’t decline, ^an7-32-5-3

and has drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an7-32-5-4

### Respect for Conscience (Numbered Discourses 7.33) ^an7-33-0

“Mendicants, tonight, a glorious deity, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an7-33-1-1

‘Sir, these seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an7-33-1-2

What seven? ^an7-33-1-3

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for immersion, for conscience, and for prudence. ^an7-33-1-4

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.’ ^an7-33-1-5

That is what that deity said. ^an7-33-1-6

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an7-33-1-7

One respectful of the Teacher and the teaching, ^an7-33-2-1

keenly respecting the Saṅgha; ^an7-33-2-2

respecting immersion, being energetic, ^an7-33-2-3

and keenly respecting the training; ^an7-33-2-4

endowed with conscience and prudence, ^an7-33-3-1

reverential and respectful— ^an7-33-3-2

it is impossible for them to decline; ^an7-33-3-3

they have drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an7-33-3-4

### Easy to Admonish (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.34) ^an7-34-0

“Mendicants, tonight a deity … said to me: ^an7-34-1-1

‘Sir, these seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an7-34-1-2

What seven? ^an7-34-1-3

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for immersion; being easy to admonish, and good friendship. ^an7-34-1-4

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.’ ^an7-34-1-5

That is what that deity said. ^an7-34-1-6

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an7-34-1-7

Respect for the Teacher and the teaching, ^an7-34-2-1

and keen respect for the Saṅgha; ^an7-34-2-2

respect for immersion, being energetic, ^an7-34-2-3

and keen respect for the training. ^an7-34-2-4

One with good friends, easy to admonish, ^an7-34-3-1

reverential and respectful, ^an7-34-3-2

can’t decline, ^an7-34-3-3

and has drawn near to extinguishment.” ^an7-34-3-4

### Easy to Admonish (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.35) ^an7-35-0

“Mendicants, tonight a deity … said to me: ^an7-35-1-1

‘Sir, these seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant. ^an7-35-1-2

What seven? ^an7-35-1-3

Respect for the Teacher, for the teaching, for the Saṅgha, for the training, for immersion; being easy to admonish, and good friendship. ^an7-35-1-4

These seven things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant.’ ^an7-35-1-5

That is what that deity said. ^an7-35-1-6

Then he bowed and respectfully circled me, keeping me on his right side, before vanishing right there.” ^an7-35-1-7

When he said this, Venerable Sāriputta said to the Buddha: ^an7-35-2-1

“Sir, this is how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement. ^an7-35-2-2

It’s when a mendicant personally respects the Teacher and praises such respect. ^an7-35-2-3

And they encourage other mendicants who lack such respect to respect the Teacher. ^an7-35-2-4

And they praise other mendicants who respect the Teacher at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an7-35-2-5

They personally respect the teaching … ^an7-35-2-6

They personally respect the Saṅgha … ^an7-35-2-7

They personally respect the training … ^an7-35-2-8

They personally respect immersion … ^an7-35-2-9

They are personally easy to admonish … ^an7-35-2-10

They personally have good friends, and praise such friendship. ^an7-35-2-11

And they encourage other mendicants who lack good friends to develop good friendship. ^an7-35-2-12

And they praise other mendicants who have good friends at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an7-35-2-13

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.” ^an7-35-2-14

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^an7-35-3-1

It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this. ^an7-35-3-2

It’s when a mendicant personally respects the Teacher … ^an7-35-3-3

And they encourage other mendicants who lack such respect to respect the Teacher. ^an7-35-3-4

And they praise other mendicants who respect the Teacher at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an7-35-3-5

They personally respect the teaching … ^an7-35-3-6

They personally respect the Saṅgha … ^an7-35-3-7

They personally respect the training … ^an7-35-3-8

They personally respect immersion … ^an7-35-3-9

They are personally easy to admonish … ^an7-35-3-10

They personally have good friends, and praise such friendship. ^an7-35-3-11

And they encourage other mendicants who lack good friends to develop good friendship. ^an7-35-3-12

And they praise other mendicants who have good friends at the right time, truthfully and correctly. ^an7-35-3-13

This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” ^an7-35-3-14

### A Friend (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.36) ^an7-36-0

“Mendicants, you should associate with a friend who has seven factors. ^an7-36-1-1

What seven? ^an7-36-1-2

They give what is hard to give. They do what is hard to do. They endure what is hard to endure. They reveal their secrets to you. They keep your secrets. They don’t abandon you in times of trouble. They don’t look down on you in times of loss. ^an7-36-1-3

You should associate with a friend who has these seven factors. ^an7-36-1-4

A friend gives what is hard to give, ^an7-36-2-1

and does what’s hard to do. ^an7-36-2-2

They put up with your harsh words, ^an7-36-2-3

and with things hard to endure. ^an7-36-2-4

They tell you their secrets, ^an7-36-3-1

and keep your secrets for you. ^an7-36-3-2

They don’t abandon you in times of trouble, ^an7-36-3-3

or look down on you in times of loss. ^an7-36-3-4

The individual in whom ^an7-36-4-1

these things are found is your friend. ^an7-36-4-2

If you want to have a friend, ^an7-36-4-3

you should keep company with that sort.” ^an7-36-4-4

### A Friend (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.37) ^an7-37-0

“Mendicants, when a friend has seven qualities you should associate with, accompany, and attend them, even if they drive you away. ^an7-37-1-1

What seven? ^an7-37-1-2

They’re likable, agreeable, respected, and admired. They admonish you and they accept admonishment. They speak on deep matters. And they don’t urge you to do bad things. ^an7-37-1-3

When a friend has these seven qualities you should associate with, accompany, and attend them, even if they drive you away. ^an7-37-1-4

They’re liked, respected, and admired, ^an7-37-2-1

an admonisher who accepts admonishment, ^an7-37-2-2

speaks on deep matters, ^an7-37-2-3

and doesn’t urge you to do bad. ^an7-37-2-4

The individual in whom ^an7-37-3-1

these things are found is your friend. ^an7-37-3-2

If you want to have a friend, ^an7-37-3-3

benevolent and sympathetic, ^an7-37-3-4

then even if they chase you away, ^an7-37-3-5

you should keep company with that sort.” ^an7-37-3-6

### Textual Analysis (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.38) ^an7-38-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities will soon realize the four kinds of textual analysis and live having achieved them with their own insight. ^an7-38-1-1

What seven? ^an7-38-1-2

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is mental sluggishness’. ^an7-38-1-3

They truly understand internally constricted mind as ‘internally constricted mind’. ^an7-38-1-4

They truly understand externally scattered mind as ‘externally scattered mind’. ^an7-38-1-5

They know feelings as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an7-38-1-6

They know perceptions as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an7-38-1-7

They know thoughts as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an7-38-1-8

The patterns of qualities—suitable or unsuitable, inferior or superior, or those with a portion of dark or bright—are properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom. ^an7-38-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities will soon realize the four kinds of textual analysis and live having achieved them with their own insight.” ^an7-38-1-10

### Textual Analysis (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.39) ^an7-39-0

“Mendicants, having seven qualities, Sāriputta realized the four kinds of textual analysis and lives having achieved them with his own insight. ^an7-39-1-1

What seven? ^an7-39-1-2

It’s when Sāriputta truly understood: ‘This is mental sluggishness’. ^an7-39-1-3

He truly understood internally constricted mind as ‘internally constricted mind’. ^an7-39-1-4

He truly understood externally scattered mind as ‘externally scattered mind’. ^an7-39-1-5

He knew feelings, perceptions, and thoughts as they arose, as they remained, and as they went away. ^an7-39-1-6

The patterns of qualities—suitable or unsuitable, inferior or superior, or those with a portion of dark or bright—were properly grasped, focused on, borne in mind, and penetrated with wisdom. ^an7-39-1-9

Having these seven qualities, Sāriputta realized the four kinds of textual analysis and lives having achieved them with his own insight.” ^an7-39-1-10

### Mastery of the Mind (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.40) ^an7-40-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities masters their mind and is not mastered by it. ^an7-40-1-1

What seven? ^an7-40-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is skilled at immersion, skilled in entering immersion, skilled in remaining in immersion, skilled in emerging from immersion, skilled in positivity for immersion, skilled in the territory of immersion, and skilled in projecting the mind purified by immersion. ^an7-40-1-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities masters their mind and is not mastered by it.” ^an7-40-1-4

### Mastery of the Mind (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.41) ^an7-41-0

“Mendicants, having seven qualities Sāriputta has mastered his mind and is not mastered by it. ^an7-41-1-1

What seven? ^an7-41-1-2

Sāriputta is skilled at immersion, skilled in entering immersion, skilled in remaining in immersion, skilled in emerging from immersion, skilled in positivity for immersion, skilled in the territory of immersion, and skilled in projecting the mind purified by immersion. ^an7-41-1-3

Having these seven qualities Sāriputta has mastered his mind and is not mastered by it.” ^an7-41-1-4

### Graduation (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.42) ^an7-42-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^an7-42-1-1

Then it occurred to him, ^an7-42-1-2

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī. ^an7-42-1-3

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an7-42-1-4

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^an7-42-1-5

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. ^an7-42-1-6

Now at that time while those wanderers of other religions were sitting together this discussion came up among them, ^an7-42-1-7

“Reverends, anyone who lives the full and pure spiritual life for twelve years is qualified to be called a ‘graduate mendicant’.” ^an7-42-1-8

Sāriputta neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^an7-42-2-1

He rose from his seat, thinking, ^an7-42-2-2

“I will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^an7-42-2-3

Then Sāriputta wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened, adding: ^an7-42-2-4

“Sir, in this teaching and training can we describe a mendicant as a ‘graduate’ solely because they have completed a certain number of years?” ^an7-42-3-12

“No, Sāriputta, we cannot. ^an7-42-4-1

I make known these seven qualifications for graduation after realizing them with my own insight. ^an7-42-4-2

What seven? ^an7-42-5-1

It’s when a mendicant has a keen enthusiasm to undertake the training … to examine the teachings … to get rid of desires … for retreat … to rouse up energy … for mindfulness and alertness … to penetrate theoretically. And they don’t lose these desires in the future. ^an7-42-5-2

These are the seven qualifications for graduation that I make known after realizing them with my own insight. ^an7-42-5-3

A mendicant who has these seven qualifications for graduation is qualified to be called a ‘graduate mendicant’. This is so whether they have lived the full and pure spiritual life for twelve years, ^an7-42-5-4

twenty-four years, ^an7-42-5-5

thirty-six years, or forty-eight years.” ^an7-42-5-6

### Graduation (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.43) ^an7-43-0

So I have heard. ^an7-43-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an7-43-1-2

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Kosambī for alms. ^an7-43-1-3

Then it occurred to him, ^an7-43-1-4

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Kosambī. ^an7-43-1-5

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an7-43-1-6

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^an7-43-1-7

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. ^an7-43-1-8

Now at that time while those wanderers of other religions were sitting together this discussion came up among them, ^an7-43-2-1

“Reverends, anyone who lives the full and pure spiritual life for twelve years is qualified to be called a ‘graduate mendicant’.” ^an7-43-2-2

Ānanda neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^an7-43-3-1

He rose from his seat, thinking, ^an7-43-3-2

“I will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^an7-43-3-3

Then Ānanda wandered for alms in Kosambī. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened, adding: ^an7-43-3-4

“Sir, in this teaching and training can we describe a mendicant as a ‘graduate’ solely because they have completed a certain number of years?” ^an7-43-5-6

“No, Ānanda, we cannot. ^an7-43-6-1

These are the seven qualifications for graduation that I make known after realizing them with my own insight. ^an7-43-6-2

What seven? ^an7-43-7-1

It’s when someone is faithful, conscientious, prudent, learned, energetic, mindful, and wise. ^an7-43-7-2

These are the seven qualifications for graduation that I make known after realizing them with my own insight. ^an7-43-7-3

A mendicant who possesses these seven qualifications for graduation is qualified to be called a ‘graduate mendicant’. This is so whether they have lived the full and pure spiritual life for twelve years, ^an7-43-7-4

twenty-four years, ^an7-43-7-5

thirty-six years, or forty-eight years.” ^an7-43-7-6

### Planes of Consciousness (Numbered Discourses 7.44) ^an7-44-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven planes of consciousness. ^an7-44-1-1

What seven? ^an7-44-1-2

There are sentient beings that are diverse in body and diverse in perception, such as human beings, some gods, and some beings in the underworld. ^an7-44-1-3

This is the first plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-1-4

There are sentient beings that are diverse in body and unified in perception, such as the gods reborn in the Divinity’s host through the first absorption. ^an7-44-2-1

This is the second plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-2-2

There are sentient beings that are unified in body and diverse in perception, such as the gods of streaming radiance. ^an7-44-3-1

This is the third plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-3-2

There are sentient beings that are unified in body and unified in perception, such as the gods of universal beauty. ^an7-44-4-1

This is the fourth plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-4-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond perceptions of form. With the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they have been reborn in the dimension of infinite space. ^an7-44-5-1

This is the fifth plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-5-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond the dimension of infinite space. Aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they have been reborn in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an7-44-6-1

This is the sixth plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-6-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness. Aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they have been reborn in the dimension of nothingness. ^an7-44-7-1

This is the seventh plane of consciousness. ^an7-44-7-2

These are the seven planes of consciousness.” ^an7-44-8-1

### Prerequisites for Immersion (Numbered Discourses 7.45) ^an7-45-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven prerequisites for immersion. ^an7-45-1-1

What seven? ^an7-45-1-2

Right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, and right mindfulness. ^an7-45-1-3

Unification of mind with these seven factors as prerequisites is what is called noble right immersion ‘with its vital conditions’ and also ‘with its prerequisites’.” ^an7-45-1-4

### Fires (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.46) ^an7-46-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven fires. ^an7-46-1-1

What seven? ^an7-46-1-2

The fires of greed, hate, delusion. The fire of those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods. A householder’s fire. The fire of those worthy of a religious donation. And a wood fire. ^an7-46-1-3

These are the seven fires.” ^an7-46-1-4

### Fires (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.47) ^an7-47-0

Now at that time the brahmin Uggatasarīra had prepared a large sacrifice. ^an7-47-1-1

Bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats and rams—five hundred of each—had been led to the post for the sacrifice. ^an7-47-1-2

Then the brahmin Uggatasarīra went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an7-47-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha, ^an7-47-1-4

“Worthy Gotama, I have heard that kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^an7-47-2-1

“I’ve also heard this, brahmin.” ^an7-47-2-2

For a second time … ^an7-47-2-3

and third time Uggatasarīra said to the Buddha, ^an7-47-2-4

“Worthy Gotama, I have heard that kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^an7-47-2-5

“I’ve also heard this, brahmin.” ^an7-47-2-6

“Then the worthy Gotama and I are in total agreement in this matter.” ^an7-47-2-7

When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to Uggatasarīra, ^an7-47-3-1

“Brahmin, you shouldn’t ask the Buddha in this way. ^an7-47-3-2

You should ask in this way: ^an7-47-3-4

‘Sir, I want to kindle the sacrificial fire and raise the sacrificial post. ^an7-47-3-5

May the Buddha please advise ^an7-47-3-6

and instruct me. It will be for my lasting welfare and happiness.’” ^an7-47-3-7

Then Uggatasarīra said to the Buddha, ^an7-47-4-1

“Worthy Gotama, I want to kindle the sacrificial fire and raise the sacrificial post. ^an7-47-4-2

May the worthy Gotama please advise ^an7-47-4-3

and instruct me. It will be for my lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an7-47-4-4

“Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post, one raises three unskillful knives which ripen and result in suffering. ^an7-47-5-1

What three? ^an7-47-5-2

The knives of the body, speech, and mind. ^an7-47-5-3

Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post one gives rise to the thought: ^an7-47-5-4

‘May this many bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats, and rams be slaughtered for the sacrifice!’ ^an7-47-5-5

Thinking, ‘May I make goodness’, one makes wickedness. Thinking, ‘May I do good’, one does bad. Thinking, ‘May I seek the path to a good rebirth’, one seeks the path to a bad rebirth. ^an7-47-5-6

Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post one raises this first unskillful mental knife which ripens and results in suffering. ^an7-47-5-7

Furthermore, even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post, one says such things as: ^an7-47-6-1

‘May this many bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats, and rams be slaughtered for the sacrifice!’ ^an7-47-6-2

Thinking, ‘May I make goodness’, one makes wickedness. Thinking, ‘May I do good’, one does bad. Thinking, ‘May I seek the path to a good rebirth’, one seeks the path to a bad rebirth. ^an7-47-6-3

Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post one raises this second unskillful verbal knife which ripens and results in suffering. ^an7-47-6-4

Furthermore, even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post one first personally undertakes preparations for the sacrificial slaughter of bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats, and rams. ^an7-47-7-1

Thinking, ‘May I make goodness’, one makes wickedness. Thinking, ‘May I do good’, one does bad. Thinking, ‘May I seek the path to a good rebirth’, one seeks the path to a bad rebirth. ^an7-47-7-2

Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post, one raises this third unskillful bodily knife which ripens and results in suffering. ^an7-47-7-3

Even before kindling the sacrificial fire and raising the sacrificial post, one raises these three unskillful knives which ripen and result in suffering. ^an7-47-7-4

Brahmin, these three fires should be given up and shunned, not cultivated. ^an7-47-8-1

What three? ^an7-47-8-2

The fires of greed, hate, and delusion. ^an7-47-8-3

And why should the fire of greed be given up and shunned, not cultivated? ^an7-47-9-1

A greedy person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an7-47-9-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an7-47-9-3

That’s why the fire of greed should be given up and shunned, not cultivated. ^an7-47-9-4

And why should the fire of hate be given up and shunned, not cultivated? ^an7-47-10-1

A hateful person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an7-47-10-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an7-47-10-3

That’s why the fire of hate should be given up and shunned, not cultivated. ^an7-47-10-4

And why should the fire of delusion be given up and shunned, not cultivated? ^an7-47-11-1

A deluded person does bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an7-47-11-2

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an7-47-11-3

That’s why the fire of delusion should be given up and shunned, not cultivated. ^an7-47-11-4

These three fires should be given up and shunned, not cultivated. ^an7-47-11-5

Brahmin, you should properly and happily take care of three fires, honoring, respecting, esteeming, and venerating them. ^an7-47-12-1

What three? ^an7-47-12-2

The fire of those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods. The fire of a householder. And the fire of those worthy of a religious donation. ^an7-47-12-3

And what is the fire of those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods? ^an7-47-13-1

Your mother and father are called the fire of those worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods. ^an7-47-13-2

Why is that? ^an7-47-13-3

Since it is from them that you’ve been incubated and produced. So you should properly and happily take care of this fire, honoring, respecting, esteeming, and venerating it. ^an7-47-13-4

And what is the fire of a householder? ^an7-47-14-1

Your children, partners, bondservants, servants, and workers are called a householder’s fire. ^an7-47-14-2

So you should properly and happily take care of this fire, honoring, respecting, esteeming, and venerating it. ^an7-47-14-3

And what is the fire of those worthy of a religious donation? ^an7-47-15-1

The ascetics and brahmins who refrain from intoxication and negligence, are settled in patience and gentleness, and who tame, calm, and extinguish themselves are called the fire of those worthy of a religious donation. ^an7-47-15-2

So you should properly and happily take care of this fire, honoring, respecting, esteeming, and venerating it. ^an7-47-15-3

You should properly and happily take care of these three fires, honoring, respecting, esteeming, and venerating them. ^an7-47-15-4

But the wood fire, brahmin, should, from time to time, be fanned, watched over with equanimity, extinguished, or put aside.” ^an7-47-16-1

When he said this, the brahmin Uggatasarīra said to the Buddha, ^an7-47-17-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an7-47-17-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life. ^an7-47-17-3

Worthy Gotama, I now set free these five hundred bulls, five hundred bullocks, five hundred heifers, five hundred goats, and five hundred rams. I give them life! ^an7-47-17-4

May they eat grass and drink cool water and enjoy a cool breeze!” ^an7-47-17-5

### Perceptions in Brief (Numbered Discourses 7.48) ^an7-48-0

“Mendicants, these seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an7-48-1-1

What seven? ^an7-48-2-1

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, and not-self in suffering. ^an7-48-2-2

These seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination.” ^an7-48-2-3

### Perceptions in Detail (Numbered Discourses 7.49) ^an7-49-0

“Mendicants, these seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an7-49-1-1

What seven? ^an7-49-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, and not-self in suffering. ^an7-49-1-3

These seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an7-49-1-4

‘When the perception of ugliness is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ ^an7-49-2-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-2-2

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of ugliness, their mind draws back from sexual intercourse. They shrink away, turn aside, and don’t get drawn into it. And either equanimity or revulsion become stabilized. ^an7-49-2-3

It’s like a chicken’s feather or a scrap of sinew thrown in a fire. It shrivels up, shrinks, rolls up, and doesn’t stretch out. ^an7-49-2-4

In the same way, when a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of ugliness, their mind draws back from sexual intercourse. … ^an7-49-2-5

If a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of ugliness, but their mind is drawn to sexual intercourse, and not repulsed, ^an7-49-3-1

they should know: ‘My perception of ugliness is undeveloped. I don’t have any distinction higher than before. I haven’t attained a fruit of development.’ ^an7-49-3-2

In this way they are aware of the situation. ^an7-49-3-3

But if a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of ugliness, their mind draws back from sexual intercourse … ^an7-49-3-4

they should know: ‘My perception of ugliness is well developed. I have realized a distinction higher than before. I have attained a fruit of development.’ ^an7-49-3-5

In this way they are aware of the situation. ^an7-49-3-6

‘When the perception of ugliness is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ ^an7-49-3-7

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-3-8

‘When the perception of death is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-4-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of death, their mind draws back from desire to be reborn. … ^an7-49-4-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-5-8

‘When the perception of the repulsiveness of food is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-6-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of the repulsiveness of food, their mind draws back from craving for tastes. … ^an7-49-6-2

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-7-8

‘When the perception of dissatisfaction with the whole world is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-8-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of dissatisfaction with the whole world, their mind draws back from the world’s shiny things. … ^an7-49-8-3

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-9-8

‘When the perception of impermanence is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-10-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of impermanence, their mind draws back from material things, honors, and fame. … ^an7-49-10-3

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-11-8

‘When the perception of suffering in impermanence is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-12-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of suffering in impermanence, they establish a keen perception of the danger of sloth, laziness, slackness, negligence, lack of commitment, and failure to review, like a killer with a drawn sword. … ^an7-49-12-3

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-13-8

‘When the perception of not-self in suffering is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an7-49-14-1

When a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of not-self in suffering, their heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs. It has gone beyond discrimination, and is peaceful and well freed. ^an7-49-14-3

If a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of not-self in suffering, but their heart is not rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; nor has it gone beyond discrimination, and is not peaceful or well freed, ^an7-49-15-1

they should know: ‘My perception of not-self in suffering is undeveloped. I don’t have any distinction higher than before. I haven’t attained a fruit of development.’ ^an7-49-15-2

In this way they are aware of the situation. ^an7-49-15-3

But if a mendicant often meditates with a mind reinforced with the perception of not-self in suffering, and their heart is rid of I-making, mine-making, and conceit for this conscious body and externally for all signs; and it has gone beyond discrimination, and is peaceful and well freed, ^an7-49-16-1

they should know: ‘My perception of not-self in suffering is well developed. I have realized a distinction higher than before. I have attained a fruit of development.’ ^an7-49-16-2

In this way they are aware of the situation. ^an7-49-16-3

‘When the perception of not-self in suffering is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.’ ^an7-49-16-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an7-49-16-5

These seven perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. Their objective and culmination is freedom from death.” ^an7-49-17-1

### Sex (Numbered Discourses 7.50) ^an7-50-0

Then the brahmin Jānussoṇi went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an7-50-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha, ^an7-50-1-2

“Does Mister Gotama claim to be chaste?” ^an7-50-1-3

“Brahmin, if anyone should be rightly said to ^an7-50-1-4

live the chaste life intact, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, full and pure, it’s me.” ^an7-50-1-5

“But what, Mister Gotama, is a break, taint, stain, or mar in chastity?” ^an7-50-1-7

“Firstly, an ascetic or brahmin who claims to be perfectly chaste does not mutually engage in sex with a lady. ^an7-50-2-1

However, they consent to being anointed, massaged, bathed, and rubbed by a lady. ^an7-50-2-2

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an7-50-2-3

This is a break, taint, stain, or mar in chastity. ^an7-50-2-4

This is called one who lives the chaste life impurely, yoked by the yoke of sex. They’re not freed from rebirth, old age, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^an7-50-2-5

Furthermore, an ascetic or brahmin who claims to be perfectly chaste does not mutually engage in sex with a lady. Nor do they consent to massage and bathing. ^an7-50-3-1

However, they giggle and play and have fun with ladies. … ^an7-50-3-2

they gaze into a lady’s eyes. … ^an7-50-4-2

they listen through a wall or rampart to the sound of ladies laughing or chatting or singing or crying. … ^an7-50-5-2

they recall when they used to laugh, chat, and have fun with ladies … ^an7-50-6-2

they see a householder or their child amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. … ^an7-50-7-2

They don’t see a householder or their child amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an7-50-8-1

However, they live the chaste life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods. They think: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^an7-50-8-2

They enjoy it and like it and find it rewarding. ^an7-50-8-3

This is a break, taint, stain, or mar in chastity. ^an7-50-8-4

This is called one who lives the chaste life impurely, yoked by the yoke of sex. They’re not free from rebirth, old age, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re not free from suffering, I say. ^an7-50-8-5

As long as I saw that these seven sexual yokes—or even one of them—had not been given up in me, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an7-50-9-1

But when I saw that these seven sexual yokes—every one of them—had been given up in me, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an7-50-10-1

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^an7-50-10-2

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^an7-50-10-3

When he said this, the brahmin Jānussoṇi said to the Buddha, ^an7-50-11-1

“Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! … ^an7-50-11-2

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an7-50-11-3

### Yoking and Unyoking (Numbered Discourses 7.51) ^an7-51-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you an exposition of the teaching on yoking and unyoking. ^an7-51-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. … ^an7-51-1-2

And what is the exposition of the teaching on yoking and unyoking? ^an7-51-1-3

A woman focuses on her own femininity: ^an7-51-2-1

her feminine moves, feminine appearance, feminine ways, feminine desires, feminine voice, and feminine adornment. ^an7-51-2-2

She’s stimulated by this and takes pleasure in it. ^an7-51-2-3

So she focuses on the masculinity of others: ^an7-51-2-4

masculine moves, masculine appearance, masculine ways, masculine desires, masculine voice, and masculine adornment. ^an7-51-2-5

She’s stimulated by this and takes pleasure in it. ^an7-51-2-6

So she desires to yoke herself to another. ^an7-51-2-7

And she desires the pleasure and happiness that comes from such yoking. ^an7-51-2-8

Sentient beings who relish their femininity are yoked to men. ^an7-51-2-9

This is how a woman does not transcend her femininity. ^an7-51-2-10

A man focuses on his own masculinity: ^an7-51-3-1

his masculine moves, masculine appearance, masculine ways, masculine desires, masculine voice, and masculine adornment. ^an7-51-3-2

He’s stimulated by this and takes pleasure in it. ^an7-51-3-3

So he focuses on the femininity of others: ^an7-51-3-4

feminine moves, feminine appearance, feminine ways, feminine desires, feminine voice, and feminine adornment. ^an7-51-3-5

He’s stimulated by this and takes pleasure in it. ^an7-51-3-6

So he desires to yoke himself to another. ^an7-51-3-7

And he desires the pleasure and happiness that comes from such yoking. ^an7-51-3-8

Sentient beings who relish their masculinity are yoked to women. ^an7-51-3-9

This is how a man does not transcend his masculinity. ^an7-51-3-10

This is how yoking comes about. ^an7-51-3-11

And how does unyoking come about? ^an7-51-4-1

A woman doesn’t focus on her own femininity: ^an7-51-4-2

her feminine moves, feminine appearance, feminine ways, feminine desires, feminine voice, and feminine adornment. ^an7-51-4-3

She isn’t stimulated by this and takes no pleasure in it. ^an7-51-4-4

So she doesn’t focus on the masculinity of others: ^an7-51-4-5

masculine moves, masculine appearance, masculine ways, masculine desires, masculine voice, and masculine adornment. ^an7-51-4-6

She isn’t stimulated by this and takes no pleasure in it. ^an7-51-4-7

So she doesn’t desire to yoke herself to another. ^an7-51-4-8

Nor does she desire the pleasure and happiness that comes from such yoking. ^an7-51-4-9

Sentient beings who do not relish their femininity are not yoked to men. ^an7-51-4-10

This is how a woman transcends her femininity. ^an7-51-4-11

A man doesn’t focus on his own masculinity: ^an7-51-5-1

masculine moves, masculine appearance, masculine ways, masculine desires, masculine voice, and masculine adornment. ^an7-51-5-2

He isn’t stimulated by this and takes no pleasure in it. ^an7-51-5-3

So he doesn’t focus on the femininity of others: ^an7-51-5-4

feminine moves, feminine appearance, feminine ways, feminine desires, feminine voice, and feminine adornment. ^an7-51-5-5

He isn’t stimulated by this and takes no pleasure in it. ^an7-51-5-6

So he doesn’t desire to yoke himself to another. ^an7-51-5-7

Nor does he desire the pleasure and happiness that comes from such yoking. ^an7-51-5-8

Sentient beings who do not relish their masculinity are not yoked to women. ^an7-51-5-9

This is how a man transcends his masculinity. ^an7-51-5-10

This is how unyoking comes about. ^an7-51-5-11

This is the exposition of the teaching on yoking and unyoking.” ^an7-51-5-12

### A Very Fruitful Gift (Numbered Discourses 7.52) ^an7-52-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond. ^an7-52-1-1

Then several lay followers of Campā went to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an7-52-1-2

“Sir, it’s been a long time since we’ve heard a Dhamma talk from the Buddha. ^an7-52-1-3

It would be good if we got to hear a Dhamma talk from the Buddha.” ^an7-52-1-4

“Well then, reverends, come on the next sabbath day. ^an7-52-1-5

Hopefully you’ll get to hear a Dhamma talk from the Buddha.” ^an7-52-1-6

“Yes, sir” they replied. Then they rose from their seats, bowed to Sāriputta, and respectfully circled him before leaving. ^an7-52-1-7

Then on the next sabbath the lay followers of Campā went to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, and stood to one side. ^an7-52-2-1

Then they went together with Sāriputta to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Sāriputta said to the Buddha: ^an7-52-2-2

“Sir, could it be that someone gives a gift and it is not very fruitful or beneficial, ^an7-52-3-1

while someone else gives exactly the same gift and it is very fruitful and beneficial?” ^an7-52-3-2

“Indeed it could, Sāriputta.” ^an7-52-3-3

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason for this?” ^an7-52-3-5

“Sāriputta, take the case of a someone who gives a gift as an investment, their mind tied to it, expecting to keep it, thinking ‘I’ll enjoy this in my next life’. ^an7-52-4-1

They give to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an7-52-4-2

What do you think, Sāriputta, don’t some people give gifts in this way?” ^an7-52-4-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an7-52-4-4

“Sāriputta, someone who gives a gift as an investment, ^an7-52-5-1

when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an7-52-5-2

When that deed, success, glory, and sovereignty is spent they return to this place. ^an7-52-5-3

Next, take the case of a someone who gives a gift not as an investment, their mind not tied to it, not expecting to keep it, and not thinking, ‘I’ll enjoy this in my next life’. ^an7-52-6-1

But they give a gift thinking, ‘It’s good to give’ … ^an7-52-6-2

They give a gift thinking, ‘Giving was practiced by my father and my father’s father. It would not be right for me to abandon this family tradition.’ … ^an7-52-7-2

They give a gift thinking, ‘I cook, they don’t. It wouldn’t be right for me to not give to them.’ … ^an7-52-8-2

They give a gift thinking, ‘The ancient brahmin seers were Aṭṭhaka, Vāmaka, Vāmadeva, Vessāmitta, Yamadaggi, Aṅgīrasa, Bhāradvāja, Vāseṭṭha, Kassapa, and Bhagu. ^an7-52-9-2

Just as they performed great sacrifices, I will share a gift.’ … ^an7-52-9-3

They give a gift thinking, ‘When giving this gift my mind becomes clear, and I become happy and joyful.’ … ^an7-52-10-3

They don’t give a gift thinking, ‘When giving this gift my mind becomes clear, and I become happy and joyful.’ ^an7-52-11-1

But they give a gift thinking, ‘This is an adornment and requisite for the mind.’ ^an7-52-11-2

They give to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an7-52-11-3

What do you think, Sāriputta, don’t some people give gifts in this way?” ^an7-52-11-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an7-52-11-5

“Sāriputta, someone who gives gifts, not for any other reason, ^an7-52-12-1

but thinking, ‘This is an adornment and requisite for the mind’, ^an7-52-12-11

when their body breaks up, after death, is reborn among the gods of the Divinity’s host. ^an7-52-12-12

When that deed, success, glory, and sovereignty is spent they are a non-returner; they do not return to this place. ^an7-52-12-13

This is the cause, this is the reason why someone gives a gift and it is not very fruitful or beneficial, ^an7-52-13-1

while someone else gives exactly the same gift and it is very fruitful and beneficial.” ^an7-52-13-2

### Nanda’s Mother (Numbered Discourses 7.53) ^an7-53-0

So I have heard. ^an7-53-1-1

At one time the venerables Sāriputta and Mahāmoggallāna were wandering in the Southern Hills together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants. ^an7-53-1-2

Now at that time the laywoman Veḷukaṇṭakī, Nanda’s mother, rose at the crack of dawn and recited the verses of “The Way to the Far Shore”. ^an7-53-1-3

And at that time the great king Vessavaṇa was on his way from the north to the south on some business. ^an7-53-2-1

He heard Nanda’s Mother reciting, and stood waiting for her to finish. ^an7-53-2-2

Then when her recital was over she fell silent. ^an7-53-3-1

Then, knowing she had finished, Vessavaṇa applauded, saying, ^an7-53-3-2

“Good, sister! Good, sister!” ^an7-53-3-3

“But who might you be, dearest?” ^an7-53-3-4

“Sister, I am your brother Vessavaṇa, the great king.” ^an7-53-3-5

“Good, dearest! Then may my recital of the teaching be my offering to you as my guest.” ^an7-53-3-6

“Good, sister! And let this also be your offering to me as your guest. ^an7-53-3-7

Tomorrow, the mendicant Saṅgha headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna will arrive at Veḷukaṇṭa before breakfast. When you’ve served the Saṅgha, please dedicate the religious donation to me. ^an7-53-3-8

Then that will also be your offering to me as your guest.” ^an7-53-3-9

And when the night had passed the lay woman Nanda’s Mother had delicious fresh and cooked foods prepared in her own home. ^an7-53-4-1

Then the Saṅgha of mendicants headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna arrived at Veḷukaṇṭa. ^an7-53-4-2

Then Nanda’s Mother addressed a man, ^an7-53-4-3

“Please, my man, go to the monastery and announce the time to the Saṅgha, saying: ^an7-53-4-4

‘Sirs, it’s time. The meal is ready in the home of the lady Nanda’s Mother.’” ^an7-53-4-5

“Yes, Ma’am,” that man replied, and he did as she said. ^an7-53-4-6

And then the Saṅgha of mendicants headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, went to the home of Nanda’s Mother, where they sat on the seats spread out. ^an7-53-4-8

Then Nanda’s Mother served and satisfied them with her own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an7-53-4-9

When Sāriputta had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, Nanda’s Mother sat down to one side. ^an7-53-5-1

Sāriputta said to her, ^an7-53-5-2

“Nanda’s Mother, who told you that the Saṅgha of mendicants was about to arrive?” ^an7-53-5-3

“Sir, last night I rose at the crack of dawn and recited the verses of ‘The Way to the Far Shore’, and then I fell silent. ^an7-53-6-1

Then the great king Vessavaṇa, knowing I had finished, applauded me, ^an7-53-6-2

‘Good, sister! Good, sister!’ ^an7-53-6-3

I asked: ‘But who might you be, dearest?’ ^an7-53-6-4

‘Sister, I am your brother Vessavaṇa, the great king.’ ^an7-53-6-5

‘Good, dearest! Then may my recital of the teaching be my offering to you as my guest.’ ^an7-53-6-6

‘Good, sister! And let this also be your offering to me as your guest. ^an7-53-6-7

Tomorrow, the mendicant Saṅgha headed by Sāriputta and Moggallāna will arrive at Veḷukaṇṭa before breakfast. When you’ve served the Saṅgha, please dedicate the religious donation to me. ^an7-53-6-8

Then that will also be your offering to me as your guest.’ ^an7-53-6-9

And so, sir, may the merit and the flourishing of merit of this gift be for the happiness of the great king Vessavaṇa.” ^an7-53-6-10

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing ^an7-53-7-1

that you converse face to face with a mighty and illustrious godling like the great king Vessavaṇa.” ^an7-53-7-2

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-8-1

there is another. ^an7-53-8-2

I had an only son named Nanda who I loved dearly. ^an7-53-8-3

The rulers forcibly abducted him on some pretext and had him executed. ^an7-53-8-4

But I can’t recall getting upset when my boy was under arrest or being arrested, imprisoned or being put in prison, killed or being killed.” ^an7-53-8-5

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing ^an7-53-8-6

that you purify even the arising of a thought.” ^an7-53-8-7

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-9-1

there is another. ^an7-53-9-2

When my husband passed away he was reborn in one of the realms of spirits. ^an7-53-9-3

He revealed to me his previous incarnation. ^an7-53-9-4

But I can’t recall getting upset on that account.” ^an7-53-9-5

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing ^an7-53-9-6

that you purify even the arising of a thought.” ^an7-53-9-7

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-10-1

there is another. ^an7-53-10-2

Ever since we were both young, and I was given in marriage to my husband, I can’t recall betraying him even in thought, still less in deed.” ^an7-53-10-3

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing ^an7-53-10-4

that you purify even the arising of a thought.” ^an7-53-10-5

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-11-1

there is another. ^an7-53-11-2

Ever since I declared myself a lay follower, I can’t recall deliberately breaking any precept.” ^an7-53-11-3

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing!” ^an7-53-11-4

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-12-1

there is another. ^an7-53-12-2

Whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an7-53-12-3

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, I enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an7-53-12-4

And with the fading away of rapture, I enter and remain in the third absorption, where I meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an7-53-12-5

With the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, I enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.” ^an7-53-12-6

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing!” ^an7-53-12-7

“Sir, this is not my only incredible and amazing quality; ^an7-53-13-1

there is another. ^an7-53-13-2

Of the five lower fetters taught by the Buddha, I don’t see any that I haven’t given up.” ^an7-53-13-3

“It’s incredible, Nanda’s Mother, it’s amazing!” ^an7-53-13-4

Then Venerable Sāriputta educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired Nanda’s Mother with a Dhamma talk, after which he rose from his seat and left. ^an7-53-14-1

### The Undeclared Points (Numbered Discourses 7.54) ^an7-54-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an7-54-1-1

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why a learned noble disciple has no doubts regarding the undeclared points?” ^an7-54-1-2

“Mendicant, it’s due to the cessation of views that a learned noble disciple has no doubts regarding the undeclared points. ^an7-54-2-1

‘A realized one still exists after death’: this is a misconception. ^an7-54-2-2

‘A realized one no longer exists after death’: this is a misconception. ^an7-54-2-3

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death’: this is a misconception. ^an7-54-2-4

‘A realized one neither still exists nor no longer exists after death’: this is a misconception. ^an7-54-2-5

An unlearned ordinary person doesn’t understand views, their origin, their cessation, or the practice that leads to their cessation. ^an7-54-2-6

And so their views grow. They’re not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^an7-54-2-7

A learned noble disciple does understand views, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^an7-54-3-1

And so their views cease. They’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re freed from suffering, I say. ^an7-54-3-2

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple does not answer: ‘A realized one still exists after death’, ^an7-54-3-3

‘A realized one no longer exists after death’, ^an7-54-3-4

‘a realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death’, ^an7-54-3-5

‘a realized one neither still exists nor no longer exists after death.’ ^an7-54-3-6

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple does not declare the undeclared points. ^an7-54-3-7

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple doesn’t shake, tremble, quake, or get nervous regarding the undeclared points. ^an7-54-3-8

‘A realized one still exists after death’: this is just about craving. … ^an7-54-4-1

it’s just about perception … ^an7-54-4-2

it’s a conceiving … ^an7-54-4-3

it’s a proliferation … ^an7-54-4-4

it’s just about grasping … ^an7-54-4-5

‘A realized one still exists after death’: this is a regret. ^an7-54-4-6

‘A realized one no longer exists after death’: this is a regret. ^an7-54-4-7

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death’: this is a regret. ^an7-54-4-8

‘A realized one neither still exists nor no longer exists after death’: this is a regret. ^an7-54-4-9

An unlearned ordinary person doesn’t understand regrets, their origin, their cessation, or the practice that leads to their cessation. ^an7-54-4-10

And so their regrets grow. They’re not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^an7-54-4-11

A learned noble disciple does understand regrets, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation. ^an7-54-5-1

And so their regrets cease. They’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re freed from suffering, I say. ^an7-54-5-2

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple does not answer: ‘A realized one still exists after death’ … ^an7-54-5-3

‘a realized one neither still exists nor no longer exists after death.’ ^an7-54-5-4

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple does not declare the undeclared points. ^an7-54-5-5

Knowing and seeing this, a learned noble disciple doesn’t shake, tremble, quake, or get nervous regarding the undeclared points. ^an7-54-5-6

This is the cause, this is the reason why a learned noble disciple has no doubts regarding the undeclared points.” ^an7-54-5-7

### Places People Are Reborn (Numbered Discourses 7.55) ^an7-55-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you seven places people are reborn, and extinguishment by not grasping. ^an7-55-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an7-55-1-2

“Yes, sir,” the mendicants replied. ^an7-55-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an7-55-1-4

“And what are the seven places people are reborn? ^an7-55-1-5

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-2-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine. I am giving up what exists, what has come to be.’ They gain equanimity. ^an7-55-2-2

They’re not attached to life, or to creating a new life. And they see with right wisdom that there is a peaceful state beyond. ^an7-55-2-3

But they haven’t completely realized that state. They haven’t totally given up the underlying tendencies of conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-55-2-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-2-5

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off would be extinguished. ^an7-55-2-6

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-2-7

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-2-11

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-3-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine. I am giving up what exists, what has come to be.’ They gain equanimity. ^an7-55-3-2

They’re not attached to life, or to creating a new life. And they see with right wisdom that there is a peaceful state beyond. ^an7-55-3-3

But they haven’t totally realized that state. They haven’t completely given up the underlying tendencies of conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-55-3-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-3-5

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would be extinguished. ^an7-55-3-6

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-3-7

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-3-9

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-4-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-4-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-4-3

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would be extinguished before landing. ^an7-55-4-4

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-4-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an7-55-4-7

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-5-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-5-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. ^an7-55-5-3

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would be extinguished upon landing. ^an7-55-5-4

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-5-5

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-5-6

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. ^an7-55-5-7

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-6-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-6-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^an7-55-6-3

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would fall on a little heap of grass or twigs. ^an7-55-6-4

There it would ignite a fire and produce smoke. But the fire would consume the grass or twigs and become extinguished due to not being fed. ^an7-55-6-5

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-6-6

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-6-7

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^an7-55-6-8

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-7-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-7-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^an7-55-7-3

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would fall on a large heap of grass or twigs. ^an7-55-7-4

There it would ignite a fire and produce smoke. But the fire would consume the grass or twigs and become extinguished due to not being fed. ^an7-55-7-5

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-7-6

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-7-7

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^an7-55-7-8

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-8-1

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine. I am giving up what exists, what has come to be.’ They gain equanimity. ^an7-55-8-2

They’re not attached to life, or to creating a new life. And they see with right wisdom that there is a peaceful state beyond. ^an7-55-8-3

But they haven’t totally realized that state. They haven’t completely given up the underlying tendencies of conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-55-8-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an7-55-8-5

Suppose you struck an iron pot that had been heated all day. Any spark that flew off and floated away would fall on a huge heap of grass or twigs. ^an7-55-8-6

There it would ignite a fire and produce smoke. And after consuming the grass and twigs, the fire would burn up plants and trees until it reached a green field, a roadside, a cliff’s edge, a body of water, or cleared parkland, where it would be extinguished due to not being fed. ^an7-55-8-7

In the same way, a mendicant who practices like this … ^an7-55-8-8

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. …’ ^an7-55-8-9

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an7-55-8-10

These are the seven places people are reborn. ^an7-55-8-11

And what is complete extinguishment with no fuel for grasping? ^an7-55-9-1

Take a mendicant who practices like this: ^an7-55-9-2

‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine. I am giving up what exists, what has come to be.’ They gain equanimity. ^an7-55-9-3

They’re not attached to life, or to creating a new life. And they see with right wisdom that there is a peaceful state beyond. ^an7-55-9-4

And they have totally realized that state. They’ve completely given up the underlying tendencies of conceit, desire for continued existence, and ignorance. ^an7-55-9-5

They’ve realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-55-9-6

This is called complete extinguishment with no fuel for grasping. ^an7-55-9-7

These are the seven places people are reborn, and extinguishment by not grasping.” ^an7-55-9-8

### Tissa the Divinity (Numbered Discourses 7.56) ^an7-56-0

So I have heard. ^an7-56-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an7-56-1-2

Then, late at night, two glorious deities, lighting up the entire Vulture’s Peak, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. One deity said to him, ^an7-56-1-3

“Sir, these nuns are freed!” ^an7-56-1-4

The other deity said to him, ^an7-56-1-5

“Sir, these nuns are well freed with no residue!” ^an7-56-1-6

This is what those deities said, ^an7-56-1-7

and the teacher approved. ^an7-56-1-8

Then those deities, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there. ^an7-56-1-9

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened. ^an7-56-2-1

Now at that time Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was sitting not far from the Buddha. ^an7-56-3-1

He thought, ^an7-56-3-2

“Which gods know whether a person has remainder or not?” ^an7-56-3-3

Now, at that time a monk named Tissa had recently passed away and been reborn in a realm of divinity. ^an7-56-3-5

There they knew that ^an7-56-3-6

Tissa the Divinity was very mighty and powerful. ^an7-56-3-7

And then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from the Vulture’s Peak and reappeared in that realm of divinity. ^an7-56-4-1

Tissa saw Moggallāna coming off in the distance, ^an7-56-4-2

and said to him, ^an7-56-4-3

“Come, my good Moggallāna! ^an7-56-4-4

Welcome, my good Moggallāna! ^an7-56-4-5

It’s been a long time since you took the opportunity to come here. ^an7-56-4-6

Sit, my good Moggallāna, here, a seat is ready.” ^an7-56-4-7

Moggallāna sat down on the seat spread out. ^an7-56-4-8

Then Tissa bowed to Moggallāna and sat to one side. ^an7-56-4-9

Moggallāna said to him, ^an7-56-4-10

“Which gods know whether a person has remainder or not?” ^an7-56-4-11

“The gods of the Divinity’s host know this.” ^an7-56-4-13

“But do all of them know this?” ^an7-56-5-1

“No, my good Moggallāna, not all of them. ^an7-56-5-3

Those gods of the Divinity’s host who are content with the lifespan of the Divinity, with the beauty, happiness, glory, and sovereignty of the Divinity, and who don’t truly understand any escape beyond: ^an7-56-6-1

they don’t know this. ^an7-56-6-2

But those gods of the Divinity’s host who are not content with the lifespan of the Divinity, with the beauty, happiness, glory, and sovereignty of the Divinity, and who do truly understand any escape beyond: ^an7-56-6-4

they do know this. ^an7-56-6-5

Take a mendicant who is freed both ways. ^an7-56-7-1

The gods know of them: ^an7-56-7-2

‘This venerable is freed both ways. ^an7-56-7-3

As long as their body remains they will be seen by gods and humans. ^an7-56-7-4

But when their body breaks up gods and humans will see them no more.’ ^an7-56-7-5

This too is how those gods know whether a person has remainder or not. ^an7-56-7-6

Take a mendicant who is freed by wisdom. ^an7-56-8-1

The gods know of them: ^an7-56-8-2

‘This venerable is freed by wisdom. ^an7-56-8-3

As long as their body remains they will be seen by gods and humans. ^an7-56-8-4

But when their body breaks up gods and humans will see them no more.’ ^an7-56-8-5

This too is how those gods know whether a person has remainder or not. ^an7-56-8-6

Take a mendicant who is a direct witness. ^an7-56-9-1

The gods know of them: ^an7-56-9-2

‘This venerable is a direct witness. ^an7-56-9-3

Hopefully this venerable will frequent appropriate lodgings, associate with good friends, and control their faculties. ^an7-56-9-4

Then they might realize the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life, and live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.’ ^an7-56-9-5

This too is how those gods know whether a person has remainder or not. ^an7-56-9-6

Take a mendicant who is attained to view. … ^an7-56-10-1

freed by faith … ^an7-56-10-2

a follower of teachings. ^an7-56-10-3

The gods know of them: ^an7-56-10-4

‘This venerable is a follower of teachings. ^an7-56-10-5

Hopefully this venerable will frequent appropriate lodgings, associate with good friends, and control their faculties. ^an7-56-10-6

Then they might realize the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life, and live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.’ ^an7-56-10-7

This too is how those gods know whether a person has remainder or not.” ^an7-56-10-8

Moggallāna approved and agreed with what Tissa the Divinity said. Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared on the Vulture’s Peak. ^an7-56-11-1

Then Mahāmoggallāna went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an7-56-11-2

and told him what had happened. ^an7-56-11-3

“But Moggallāna, Tissa the Divinity didn’t teach the seventh individual, the signless meditator.” ^an7-56-12-1

“Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One! ^an7-56-12-2

May the Buddha teach the seventh individual, the signless meditator. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an7-56-12-3

“Well then, Moggallāna, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an7-56-12-4

“Yes, sir,” Mahāmoggallāna replied. ^an7-56-12-5

The Buddha said this: ^an7-56-12-6

“Moggallāna, take the case of a mendicant who, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart. ^an7-56-13-1

The gods know of them: ^an7-56-13-2

‘This venerable, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart. ^an7-56-13-3

Hopefully this venerable will frequent appropriate lodgings, associate with good friends, and control their faculties. ^an7-56-13-4

Then they might realize the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life, and live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.’ ^an7-56-13-5

This too is how those gods know whether a person has remainder or not.” ^an7-56-13-6

### General Sīha (Numbered Discourses 7.57) ^an7-57-0

So I have heard. ^an7-57-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an7-57-1-2

Then General Sīha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an7-57-1-3

“Sir, can you point out a fruit of giving that’s apparent in the present life?” ^an7-57-1-4

“Well then, Sīha, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like. ^an7-57-2-1

What do you think, Sīha? ^an7-57-2-2

Consider two people. ^an7-57-2-3

One is faithless, stingy, miserly, and abusive. One is a faithful donor who loves charity. ^an7-57-2-4

Which do you think the perfected ones will show sympathy for first?” ^an7-57-2-5

“Why would the perfected ones first show sympathy for the person who is faithless, stingy, miserly, and abusive? ^an7-57-3-1

They’d show sympathy first for the faithful donor who loves charity.” ^an7-57-3-2

“Which do you think the perfected ones will first approach?” ^an7-57-4-1

“They’d first approach the faithful donor who loves charity.” ^an7-57-4-4

“Which do you think the perfected ones will receive alms from first?” ^an7-57-5-1

“They’d receive alms first from the faithful donor who loves charity.” ^an7-57-5-4

“Which do you think the perfected ones will teach the Dhamma to first?” ^an7-57-6-1

“They’d first teach the Dhamma to the faithful donor who loves charity.” ^an7-57-6-4

“Which do you think would get a good reputation?” ^an7-57-7-1

“The faithful donor who loves charity would get a good reputation.” ^an7-57-7-4

“Which do you think would enter any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics?” ^an7-57-8-1

“How could the person who is faithless, stingy, miserly, and abusive enter any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics? ^an7-57-8-3

The faithful donor who loves charity would enter any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics.” ^an7-57-8-4

“When their body breaks up, after death, which do you think would be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm?” ^an7-57-9-1

“Why would the person who is faithless, stingy, miserly, and abusive be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm? ^an7-57-9-3

The faithful donor who loves charity would, when their body breaks up, after death, be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm. ^an7-57-9-4

When it comes to these fruits of giving that are apparent in the present life, I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha, ^an7-57-10-1

for I know them too. ^an7-57-10-2

I’m a giver, a donor, and the perfected ones show sympathy for me first. ^an7-57-10-3

I’m a giver, and the perfected ones approach me first. ^an7-57-10-4

I’m a giver, and the perfected ones receive alms from me first. ^an7-57-10-5

I’m a giver, and the perfected ones teach me Dhamma first. ^an7-57-10-6

I’m a giver, and I have this good reputation: ^an7-57-10-7

‘General Sīha gives, serves, and attends on the Saṅgha.’ ^an7-57-10-8

I’m a giver, I enter any kind of assembly bold and self-assured, whether it’s an assembly of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, or ascetics. ^an7-57-10-9

When it comes to these fruits of giving that are apparent in the present life, I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha, ^an7-57-10-10

for I know them too. ^an7-57-10-11

But when the Buddha says: ^an7-57-10-12

‘When a giver’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ I don’t know this, ^an7-57-10-13

so I rely on faith in the Buddha.” ^an7-57-10-14

“That’s so true, Sīha! That’s so true! ^an7-57-10-15

When a giver’s body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.” ^an7-57-10-16

### Nothing to Hide (Numbered Discourses 7.58) ^an7-58-0

“Mendicants, there are four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide, and three ways he is irreproachable. ^an7-58-1-1

What are the four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide? ^an7-58-1-2

His bodily behavior is pure. ^an7-58-1-3

So the Realized One has no bodily misconduct to hide, thinking: ^an7-58-1-4

‘Don’t let others find this out about me!’ ^an7-58-1-5

His verbal behavior is pure. ^an7-58-2-1

So the Realized One has no verbal misconduct to hide, thinking: ^an7-58-2-2

‘Don’t let others find this out about me!’ ^an7-58-2-3

His mental behavior is pure. ^an7-58-3-1

So the Realized One has no mental misconduct to hide, thinking: ^an7-58-3-2

‘Don’t let others find this out about me!’ ^an7-58-3-3

His livelihood is pure. ^an7-58-4-1

So the Realized One has no wrong livelihood to hide, thinking: ^an7-58-4-2

‘Don’t let others find this out about me!’ ^an7-58-4-3

These are the four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide. ^an7-58-5-1

What are the three ways the Realized One is irreproachable? ^an7-58-6-1

The Realized One has explained the teaching well. ^an7-58-6-2

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ^an7-58-6-3

‘For such and such reasons you haven’t explained the teaching well.’ ^an7-58-6-4

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an7-58-6-6

I have clearly described the practice that leads to extinguishment for my disciples. ^an7-58-7-1

Practicing in accordance with this, my disciples realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-58-7-2

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ^an7-58-7-3

‘For such and such reasons you haven’t clearly described the practice that leads to extinguishment for your disciples.’ ^an7-58-7-4

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an7-58-7-6

Many hundreds in my assembly of disciples have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-58-8-1

I see no reason for anyone—whether ascetic, brahmin, god, Māra, or the Divinity, or anyone else in the world—to legitimately scold me, saying: ^an7-58-8-2

‘For such and such reasons you don’t have many hundreds of disciples in your following who have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and who live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an7-58-8-3

Since I see no such reason, I live secure, fearless, and self-assured. ^an7-58-8-5

These are the three ways the Realized One is irreproachable. ^an7-58-9-1

These are the four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide, and the three ways he is irreproachable.” ^an7-58-10-1

### With Kimbila (Numbered Discourses 7.59) ^an7-59-0

So I have heard. ^an7-59-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kimbilā in the Freshwater Mangrove Wood. ^an7-59-1-2

Then Venerable Kimbila went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an7-59-1-3

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an7-59-1-4

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen lack respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, immersion, diligence, and hospitality after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an7-59-2-1

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does not last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an7-59-2-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One?” ^an7-59-3-1

“Kimbila, it’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen maintain respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, immersion, diligence, and hospitality after the final quenching of the Realized One. ^an7-59-3-2

This is the cause, this is the reason why the true teaching does last long after the final quenching of the Realized One.” ^an7-59-3-3

### Seven Qualities (Numbered Discourses 7.60) ^an7-60-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities soon realizes the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. They live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an7-60-1-1

What seven? ^an7-60-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is faithful, ethical, learned, secluded, energetic, mindful, and wise. ^an7-60-1-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities soon realizes the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. They live having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.” ^an7-60-1-4

### Nodding Off (Numbered Discourses 7.61) ^an7-61-0

So I have heard. ^an7-61-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^an7-61-1-2

Now at that time, in the land of the Magadhans near Kallavāḷamutta Village, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was nodding off while meditating. ^an7-61-1-3

The Buddha saw him with his clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman. ^an7-61-1-4

Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood in the land of the Bhaggas and reappeared in front of Mahāmoggallāna near Kallavāḷamutta Village in the land of the Magadhans. ^an7-61-1-6

He sat on the seat spread out ^an7-61-1-7

and said to Mahāmoggallāna, ^an7-61-1-8

“Are you nodding off, Moggallāna? Are you nodding off?” ^an7-61-2-1

“Yes, sir.” ^an7-61-2-2

“So, Moggallāna, don’t focus on or cultivate the perception that you were meditating on when you fell drowsy. ^an7-61-2-3

It’s possible that you’ll give up drowsiness in this way. ^an7-61-2-4

But what if that doesn’t work? Then think about and consider the teaching as you’ve learned and memorized it, examining it with your mind. ^an7-61-3-1

It’s possible that you’ll give up drowsiness in this way. ^an7-61-3-2

But what if that doesn’t work? Then rehearse in detail the teaching as you’ve learned and memorized it. ^an7-61-4-1

It’s possible that you’ll give up drowsiness in this way. ^an7-61-4-2

But what if that doesn’t work? Then pinch your ears and rub your limbs. ^an7-61-5-1

It’s possible that you’ll give up drowsiness in this way. ^an7-61-5-2

But what if that doesn’t work? Then get up from your seat, flush your eyes with water, look around in every direction, and look up at the stars and constellations. ^an7-61-6-1

It’s possible that you’ll give up drowsiness in this way. ^an7-61-6-2
