a hundred each of the winter, summer, and rains. ^an7-74-10-5

Living for three hundred seasons, there are just twelve hundred months, ^an7-74-10-6

four hundred in each of the winter, summer, and rains. ^an7-74-10-7

Living for twelve hundred months, there are just twenty-four hundred fortnights, ^an7-74-10-8

eight hundred in each of the winter, summer, and rains. ^an7-74-10-9

Living for 2,400 fortnights, there are just 36,000 days, ^an7-74-10-10

12,000 in each of the summer, winter, and rains. ^an7-74-10-11

Living for 36,000 days, you just eat 72,000 meals, ^an7-74-10-12

24,000 in each of the summer, winter, and rains, including when you’re suckling at the breast, and when you’re prevented from eating. ^an7-74-10-13

Things that prevent you from eating include anger, pain, sickness, sabbath, or being unable to get food. ^an7-74-11-1

So mendicants, for a human being with a hundred years lifespan I have counted the lifespan, the limit of the lifespan, the seasons, the years, the months, the fortnights, the nights, the days, the meals, and the things that prevent them from eating. ^an7-74-11-2

Out of sympathy, I’ve done what a teacher should do who wants what’s best for their disciples. ^an7-74-11-3

Here are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later! This is my instruction to you.” ^an7-74-11-4

### An Expert in the Monastic Law (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.75) ^an7-75-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-75-1-1

What seven? ^an7-75-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-75-1-3

They know what is not an offense. ^an7-75-1-4

They know what is a light offense. ^an7-75-1-5

They know what is a serious offense. ^an7-75-1-6

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an7-75-1-7

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an7-75-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-75-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-75-1-10

### An Expert in the Monastic Law (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7.76) ^an7-76-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-76-1-1

What seven? ^an7-76-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-76-1-3

They know what is not an offense. ^an7-76-1-4

They know what is a light offense. ^an7-76-1-5

They know what is a serious offense. ^an7-76-1-6

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an7-76-1-7

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an7-76-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-76-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-76-1-10

### An Expert in the Monastic Law (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 7.77) ^an7-77-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-77-1-1

What seven? ^an7-77-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-77-1-3

They know what is not an offense. ^an7-77-1-4

They know what is a light offense. ^an7-77-1-5

They know what is a serious offense. ^an7-77-1-6

They’re firm and unfaltering in the training. ^an7-77-1-7

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an7-77-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-77-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-77-1-10

### An Expert in the Monastic Law (4th) (Numbered Discourses 7.78) ^an7-78-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-78-1-1

What seven? ^an7-78-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-78-1-3

They know what is not an offense. ^an7-78-1-4

They know what is a light offense. ^an7-78-1-5

They know what is a serious offense. ^an7-78-1-6

They recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an7-78-1-7

With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an7-78-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-78-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities is an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-78-1-10

### Shines as an Expert in the Monastic Law (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7.79) ^an7-79-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities shines as an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-79-1-1

What seven? ^an7-79-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-79-1-3

They know what is not an offense. ^an7-79-1-4

They know what is a light offense. ^an7-79-1-5

They know what is a serious offense. ^an7-79-1-6

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places; seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an7-79-1-7

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in the present life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an7-79-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-79-1-9

A mendicant with these seven qualities shines as an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-79-1-10

They know what is an offense. ^an7-80-1-3

A mendicant with these seven qualities shines as an expert in the monastic law.” ^an7-80-1-10

“Mendicants, a mendicant with seven qualities shines as an expert in the monastic law. ^an7-82-1-1

What seven? ^an7-82-1-2

They know what is an offense. ^an7-82-1-3

### The Teacher’s Instructions (Numbered Discourses 7.83) ^an7-83-0

Then Venerable Upāli went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an7-83-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^an7-83-2-1

“Upāli, you might know that ^an7-83-2-2

certain things don’t lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an7-83-2-3

Categorically, you should remember these things as ^an7-83-2-4

not the teaching, not the training, and not the Teacher’s instructions. ^an7-83-2-5

You might know that ^an7-83-2-6

certain things do lead solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an7-83-2-7

Categorically, you should remember these things as ^an7-83-2-8

the teaching, the training, and the Teacher’s instructions.” ^an7-83-2-9

### Settlement of Disciplinary Issues (Numbered Discourses 7.84) ^an7-84-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven principles for the settlement of any disciplinary issues that might arise. ^an7-84-1-1

What seven? ^an7-84-1-2

Resolution face-to-face to be applied. Resolution through recollection to be granted. Resolution because of past insanity to be granted. Acting according to what has been admitted. Majority decision. Further penalty. Covering over as if with grass. ^an7-84-1-3

These are the seven principles for the settlement of any disciplinary issues that might arise.” ^an7-84-1-4

### A Mendicant (Numbered Discourses 7.85) ^an7-85-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of breaking seven things that you become a mendicant. ^an7-85-1-1

What seven? ^an7-85-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, greed, hate, delusion, and conceit. ^an7-85-1-3

It’s because of breaking these seven things that you become a mendicant.” ^an7-85-1-4

### An Ascetic (Numbered Discourses 7.86) ^an7-86-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of quelling seven things that you become an ascetic …” ^an7-86-1-1

### Brahmin (Numbered Discourses 7.87) ^an7-87-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of barring out seven things that you become a brahmin …” ^an7-87-1-1

### Scholar (Numbered Discourses 7.88) ^an7-88-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of scouring off seven things that you become a scholar …” ^an7-88-1-1

### Bathed (Numbered Discourses 7.89) ^an7-89-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of bathing off seven things that you become a bathed initiate …” ^an7-89-1-1

### A Knowledge Master (Numbered Discourses 7.90) ^an7-90-0

“Mendicants, it’s because of knowing seven things that you become a knowledge master …” ^an7-90-1-1

### A Noble One (Numbered Discourses 7.91) ^an7-91-0

“Mendicants, it’s because seven foes have been nobbled that you become a noble one …” ^an7-91-1-1

### A Perfected One (Numbered Discourses 7.92) ^an7-92-0

“Mendicants, it’s by being impeccably remote from seven things that you become a perfected one. ^an7-92-1-1

What seven? ^an7-92-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, greed, hate, delusion, and conceit. ^an7-92-1-3

It’s because of being impeccably remote from these seven things that you become a perfected one.” ^an7-92-1-4

### Bad Qualities (Numbered Discourses 7.93) ^an7-93-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven bad qualities. ^an7-93-1-1

What seven? ^an7-93-1-2

Someone is faithless, shameless, imprudent, unlearned, lazy, unmindful, and witless. ^an7-93-1-3

These are the seven bad qualities.” ^an7-93-1-4

### Good Qualities (Numbered Discourses 7.94) ^an7-94-0

“Mendicants, there are these seven good qualities. ^an7-94-1-1

What seven? ^an7-94-1-2

Someone is faithful, conscientious, prudent, learned, energetic, mindful, and wise. ^an7-94-1-3

These are the seven good qualities.” ^an7-94-1-4

### Observing Impermanence in the Eye (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-95-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an7-95-1-1

What seven? ^an7-95-1-2

First, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in the eye. They perceive impermanence and experience impermanence. Constantly, continually, and without interruption, they apply the mind and fathom with wisdom. ^an7-95-1-3

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an7-95-1-4

This is the first individual who is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an7-95-1-5

Next, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in the eye. … ^an7-95-2-1

Their defilements and their life come to an end at exactly the same time. ^an7-95-2-2

This is the second individual who is worthy of offerings … ^an7-95-2-3

Next, take an individual who meditates observing impermanence in the eye. … ^an7-95-3-1

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. … ^an7-95-3-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. … ^an7-95-3-3

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. … ^an7-95-3-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. … ^an7-95-3-5

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. … ^an7-95-3-6

This is the seventh individual. ^an7-95-3-7

These are the seven individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an7-95-3-8

### Observing Suffering in the Eye, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-96-614-0

“Mendicants, these seven individuals are worthy of offerings … ^an7-96-614-1-1

What seven? ^an7-96-614-1-2

First, take an individual who meditates observing suffering in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-3

observing not-self in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-4

observing ending in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-5

observing vanishing in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-6

observing fading away in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-7

observing cessation in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-8

observing letting go in the eye. … ^an7-96-614-1-9

ear … ^an7-96-614-2-1

nose … ^an7-96-614-2-2

tongue … ^an7-96-614-2-3

body … ^an7-96-614-2-4

mind … ^an7-96-614-2-5

sights … ^an7-96-614-3-1

sounds … ^an7-96-614-3-2

smells … ^an7-96-614-3-3

tastes … ^an7-96-614-3-4

touches … ^an7-96-614-3-5

ideas … ^an7-96-614-3-6

eye consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-1

ear consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-2

nose consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-3

tongue consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-4

body consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-5

mind consciousness … ^an7-96-614-4-6

eye contact … ^an7-96-614-5-1

ear contact … ^an7-96-614-5-2

nose contact … ^an7-96-614-5-3

tongue contact … ^an7-96-614-5-4

body contact … ^an7-96-614-5-5

mind contact … ^an7-96-614-5-6

feeling born of eye contact … ^an7-96-614-6-1

feeling born of ear contact … ^an7-96-614-6-2

feeling born of nose contact … ^an7-96-614-6-3

feeling born of tongue contact … ^an7-96-614-6-4

feeling born of body contact … ^an7-96-614-6-5

feeling born of mind contact … ^an7-96-614-6-6

perception of sights … ^an7-96-614-7-1

perception of sounds … ^an7-96-614-7-2

perception of smells … ^an7-96-614-7-3

perception of tastes … ^an7-96-614-7-4

perception of touches … ^an7-96-614-7-5

perception of ideas … ^an7-96-614-7-6

intention regarding sights … ^an7-96-614-8-1

intention regarding sounds … ^an7-96-614-8-2

considerations regarding tastes … ^an7-96-614-11-4

considerations regarding touches … ^an7-96-614-11-5

meditates observing impermanence in the five aggregates … ^an7-96-614-12-1

the aggregate of form … ^an7-96-614-12-2

not-self … ^an7-96-614-12-8

ending … ^an7-96-614-12-9

vanishing … ^an7-96-614-12-10

fading away … ^an7-96-614-12-11

cessation … ^an7-96-614-12-12

letting go …” ^an7-96-614-12-13

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (1st) (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-615-0

“Mendicants, for insight into greed, seven things should be developed. ^an7-615-1-1

What seven? ^an7-615-1-2

The awakening factor of mindfulness … ^an7-615-1-3

the awakening factor of equanimity. ^an7-615-1-4

These seven things should be developed for insight into greed.” ^an7-615-1-5

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-616-0

“Mendicants, for insight into greed, seven things should be developed. ^an7-616-1-1

What seven? ^an7-616-1-2

The perception of impermanence, the perception of not-self, the perception of ugliness, the perception of drawbacks, the perception of giving up, the perception of fading away, and the perception of cessation. ^an7-616-1-3

These seven things should be developed for insight into greed.” ^an7-616-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-617-0

“Mendicants, for insight into greed, seven things should be developed. ^an7-617-1-1

What seven? ^an7-617-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, and not-self in suffering. ^an7-617-1-3

These seven things should be developed for insight into greed.” ^an7-617-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-618-644-0

“For the complete understanding of greed … ^an7-618-644-1-1

complete ending … ^an7-618-644-1-2

giving up … ^an7-618-644-1-3

ending … ^an7-618-644-1-4

vanishing … ^an7-618-644-1-5

fading away … ^an7-618-644-1-6

cessation … ^an7-618-644-1-7

giving away … ^an7-618-644-1-8

For the letting go of greed, these seven things should be developed.” ^an7-618-644-1-9

### Untitled Discourses on Hate, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 7) ^an7-645-1124-0

“Of hate … ^an7-645-1124-1-1

delusion … ^an7-645-1124-1-2

anger … ^an7-645-1124-1-3

acrimony … ^an7-645-1124-1-4

disdain … ^an7-645-1124-1-5

contempt … ^an7-645-1124-1-6

jealousy … ^an7-645-1124-1-7

stinginess … ^an7-645-1124-1-8

deceitfulness … ^an7-645-1124-1-9

deviousness … ^an7-645-1124-1-10

obstinacy … ^an7-645-1124-1-11

aggression … ^an7-645-1124-1-12

conceit … ^an7-645-1124-1-13

arrogance … ^an7-645-1124-1-14

vanity … ^an7-645-1124-1-15

for insight into negligence … ^an7-645-1124-1-16

complete understanding … ^an7-645-1124-1-17

complete ending … ^an7-645-1124-1-18

giving up … ^an7-645-1124-1-19

ending … ^an7-645-1124-1-20

vanishing … ^an7-645-1124-1-21

fading away … ^an7-645-1124-1-22

cessation … ^an7-645-1124-1-23

giving away … ^an7-645-1124-1-24

letting go of negligence ^an7-645-1124-1-25

these seven things should be developed.” ^an7-645-1124-1-26

That is what the Buddha said. ^an7-645-1124-2-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an7-645-1124-2-2

The Book of the Sevens is finished. ^an7-645-1124-2-4

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 8 ^an8-0

### The Benefits of Love (Numbered Discourses 8.1) ^an8-1-0

So I have heard. ^an8-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an8-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an8-1-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an8-1-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an8-1-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an8-1-1-6

“Mendicants, you can expect eight benefits when the heart’s release by love has been cultivated, developed, and practiced, made a vehicle and a basis, kept up, consolidated, and properly implemented. ^an8-1-2-1

What eight? ^an8-1-2-2

You sleep at ease. You wake happily. You don’t have bad dreams. Humans love you. Non-humans love you. Deities protect you. You can’t be harmed by fire, poison, or blade. If you don’t reach any higher, you’ll be reborn in a realm of divinity. ^an8-1-2-3

You can expect these eight benefits when the heart’s release by love has been cultivated, developed, and practiced, made a vehicle and a basis, kept up, consolidated, and properly implemented. ^an8-1-2-4

A mindful one who develops ^an8-1-3-1

limitless love ^an8-1-3-2

weakens the fetters, ^an8-1-3-3

seeing the ending of attachments. ^an8-1-3-4

Loving just one creature with a hateless heart ^an8-1-4-1

makes you a true person. ^an8-1-4-2

Sympathetic for all creatures, ^an8-1-4-3

a noble one creates abundant merit. ^an8-1-4-4

The royal potentates conquered this land ^an8-1-5-1

and traveled around sponsoring sacrifices— ^an8-1-5-2

horse sacrifice, human sacrifice, ^an8-1-5-3

the sacrifices of the ‘casting of the yoke-pin’, <j>the ‘royal soma drinking’, and the ‘unimpeded’. ^an8-1-5-4

These are not worth a sixteenth part ^an8-1-6-1

of the mind developed with love, ^an8-1-6-2

as all the constellations of stars ^an8-1-6-3

aren’t worth a sixteenth part of the moon’s light. ^an8-1-6-4

Don’t kill or cause others to kill, ^an8-1-7-1

don’t conquer or encourage others to conquer, ^an8-1-7-2

with love for all sentient beings, ^an8-1-7-3

you’ll have no enmity for anyone.” ^an8-1-7-4

### Wisdom (Numbered Discourses 8.2) ^an8-2-0

“Mendicants, there are eight causes and reasons that lead to acquiring the wisdom fundamental to the spiritual life, and to its increase, growth, and full development once it has been acquired. ^an8-2-1-1

What eight? ^an8-2-1-2

It’s when a mendicant lives relying on the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role. And they set up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for them, with warmth and respect. ^an8-2-1-3

This is the first cause. ^an8-2-1-4

When a mendicant lives relying on the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role—with a keen sense of conscience and prudence for them, with warmth and respect—from time to time they go and ask them questions: ^an8-2-2-1

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an8-2-2-2

Those venerables reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an8-2-2-3

This is the second cause. ^an8-2-2-4

After hearing that teaching they perfect withdrawal of both body and mind. ^an8-2-3-1

This is the third cause. ^an8-2-3-2

A mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an8-2-4-1

This is the fourth cause. ^an8-2-4-2

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an8-2-5-1

This is the fifth cause. ^an8-2-5-2

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an8-2-6-1

This is the sixth cause. ^an8-2-6-2

When in the Saṅgha they don’t engage in motley talk or low talk. ^an8-2-7-1

Either they talk on Dhamma, or they invite someone else to do so, or they respect noble silence. ^an8-2-7-2

This is the seventh cause. ^an8-2-7-3

They meditate observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. ^an8-2-8-1

‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the disappearance of form. ^an8-2-8-2

Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the disappearance of feeling. ^an8-2-8-3

Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the ending of perception. ^an8-2-8-4

Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the ending of choices. ^an8-2-8-5

Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.’ ^an8-2-8-6

This is the eighth cause. ^an8-2-8-7

Their spiritual companions esteem them: ^an8-2-9-1

‘This venerable lives relying on the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role. They set up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for them, with warmth and respect. ^an8-2-9-2

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-9-3

This quality leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-9-4

‘This venerable lives relying on the Teacher or a spiritual companion in a teacher’s role, and from time to time they go and ask them questions … ^an8-2-10-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-10-4

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-10-5

‘After hearing that teaching they perfect withdrawal of both body and mind. ^an8-2-11-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-11-2

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-11-3

‘This venerable is ethical … ^an8-2-12-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-12-2

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-12-3

‘This venerable is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. … ^an8-2-13-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-13-2

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-13-3

‘This venerable lives with energy roused up … ^an8-2-14-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-14-2

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-14-3

‘When in the Saṅgha they don’t engage in motley talk or low talk. ^an8-2-15-1

Either they talk on Dhamma, or they invite someone else to do so, or they respect noble silence. ^an8-2-15-2

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-15-3

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-15-4

‘They meditate observing rise and fall in the five grasping aggregates. … ^an8-2-16-1

Clearly this venerable knows and sees.’ ^an8-2-16-7

This quality also leads to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an8-2-16-8

These are the eight causes and reasons that lead to acquiring the wisdom fundamental to the spiritual life, and to its increase, growth, and full development once it has been acquired.” ^an8-2-17-1

### Disliked (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.3) ^an8-3-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with eight qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an8-3-1-1

What eight? ^an8-3-1-2

It’s when a mendicant praises the disliked and criticizes the liked. They desire material things and honor. They lack conscience and prudence. They have corrupt wishes and wrong view. ^an8-3-1-3

A mendicant with these eight qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an8-3-1-4

A mendicant with eight qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, and respected and admired. ^an8-3-2-1

What eight? ^an8-3-2-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t praise the disliked and criticize the liked. They don’t desire material things and honor. They have conscience and prudence. They have few desires and right view. ^an8-3-2-3

A mendicant with these eight qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, and respected and admired.” ^an8-3-2-4

### Disliked (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.4) ^an8-4-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with eight qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an8-4-1-1

What eight? ^an8-4-1-2

It’s when a mendicant desires material things, honor, and status. They know neither moderation nor the proper time. Their conduct is impure, they talk a lot, and they insult and abuse their spiritual companions. ^an8-4-1-3

A mendicant with these eight qualities is disliked and disapproved by their spiritual companions, not respected or admired. ^an8-4-1-4

A mendicant with eight qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, and respected and admired. ^an8-4-2-1

What eight? ^an8-4-2-2

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t desire material things, honor, and status. They know moderation and the proper time. Their conduct is pure, they don’t talk a lot, and they don’t insult and abuse their spiritual companions. ^an8-4-2-3

A mendicant with these eight qualities is liked and approved by their spiritual companions, and respected and admired.” ^an8-4-2-4

### Worldly Conditions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.5) ^an8-5-0

“Mendicants, the eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around the eight worldly conditions. ^an8-5-1-1

What eight? ^an8-5-1-2

Gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, pleasure and pain. ^an8-5-1-3

These eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around these eight worldly conditions. ^an8-5-1-4

Gain and loss, fame and disgrace, ^an8-5-2-1

blame and praise, and pleasure and pain. ^an8-5-2-2

These qualities among mankind are impermanent, ^an8-5-2-3

transient, and perishable. ^an8-5-2-4

An intelligent and mindful person knows these things, ^an8-5-3-1

seeing that they’re perishable. ^an8-5-3-2

Desirable things don’t disturb their mind, ^an8-5-3-3

nor are they repelled by the undesirable. ^an8-5-3-4

Both favoring and opposing ^an8-5-4-1

are cleared and disappeared, they are no more. ^an8-5-4-2

Knowing the stainless, sorrowless state, ^an8-5-4-3

they who have gone beyond rebirth <j>understand rightly.” ^an8-5-4-4

### Worldly Conditions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.6) ^an8-6-0

“Mendicants, the eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around the eight worldly conditions. ^an8-6-1-1

What eight? ^an8-6-1-2

Gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, pleasure and pain. ^an8-6-1-3

These eight worldly conditions revolve around the world, and the world revolves around these eight worldly conditions. ^an8-6-1-4

An unlearned ordinary person encounters gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, and pleasure and pain. ^an8-6-2-1

And so does a learned noble disciple. ^an8-6-2-2

What, then, is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an ordinary unlearned person?” ^an8-6-2-3

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an8-6-2-4

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an8-6-3-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an8-6-3-2

The Buddha said this: ^an8-6-3-3

“Mendicants, an unlearned ordinary person encounters gain. ^an8-6-3-4

They don’t reflect: ^an8-6-3-5

‘I’ve encountered this gain. ^an8-6-3-6

It’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ They don’t truly understand it. ^an8-6-3-7

They encounter loss … ^an8-6-3-8

fame … ^an8-6-3-9

disgrace … ^an8-6-3-10

blame … ^an8-6-3-11

praise … ^an8-6-3-12

pleasure … ^an8-6-3-13

pain. ^an8-6-3-14

They don’t reflect: ^an8-6-3-15

‘I’ve encountered this pain. ^an8-6-3-16

It’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ They don’t truly understand it. ^an8-6-3-17

So gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, and pleasure and pain occupy their mind. ^an8-6-4-1

They favor gain and oppose loss. ^an8-6-4-2

They favor fame and oppose disgrace. ^an8-6-4-3

They favor praise and oppose blame. ^an8-6-4-4

They favor pleasure and oppose pain. ^an8-6-4-5

Being so full of favoring and opposing, they’re not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^an8-6-4-6

They’re not freed from suffering, I say. ^an8-6-4-7

A learned noble disciple encounters gain. ^an8-6-5-1

They reflect: ^an8-6-5-2

‘I’ve encountered this gain. ^an8-6-5-3

It’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ They truly understand it. ^an8-6-5-4

They encounter loss … ^an8-6-5-5

fame … ^an8-6-5-6

disgrace … ^an8-6-5-7

blame … ^an8-6-5-8

praise … ^an8-6-5-9

pleasure … ^an8-6-5-10

pain. ^an8-6-5-11

They reflect: ^an8-6-5-12

‘I’ve encountered this pain. ^an8-6-5-13

It’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable.’ They truly understand it. ^an8-6-5-14

So gain and loss, fame and disgrace, blame and praise, and pleasure and pain don’t occupy their mind. ^an8-6-6-1

They don’t favor gain or oppose loss. ^an8-6-6-2

They don’t favor fame or oppose disgrace. ^an8-6-6-3

They don’t favor praise or oppose blame. ^an8-6-6-4

They don’t favor pleasure or oppose pain. ^an8-6-6-5

Having given up favoring and opposing, they’re freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. ^an8-6-6-6

They’re freed from suffering, I say. ^an8-6-6-7

This is the difference between a learned noble disciple and an unlearned ordinary person. ^an8-6-6-8

Gain and loss, fame and disgrace, ^an8-6-7-1

blame and praise, and pleasure and pain. ^an8-6-7-2

These qualities among mankind are impermanent, ^an8-6-7-3

transient, and perishable. ^an8-6-7-4

An intelligent and mindful person knows these things, ^an8-6-8-1

seeing that they’re perishable. ^an8-6-8-2

Desirable things don’t disturb their mind, ^an8-6-8-3

nor are they repelled by the undesirable. ^an8-6-8-4

Both favoring and opposing ^an8-6-9-1

are cleared and disappeared, they are no more. ^an8-6-9-2

Knowing the stainless, sorrowless state, ^an8-6-9-3

they who have gone beyond rebirth <j>understand rightly.” ^an8-6-9-4

### Devadatta’s Failure (Numbered Discourses 8.7) ^an8-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had left. ^an8-7-1-1

There the Buddha spoke to the mendicants about Devadatta: ^an8-7-1-2

“Mendicants, it’s good for a mendicant to check their own failings from time to time. ^an8-7-1-3

It’s good for a mendicant to check the failings of others from time to time. ^an8-7-1-4

It’s good for a mendicant to check their own successes from time to time. ^an8-7-1-5

It’s good for a mendicant to check the successes of others from time to time. ^an8-7-1-6

Overcome and overwhelmed by eight things that oppose the true teaching, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-7-1-7

What eight? ^an8-7-2-1

Overcome and overwhelmed by gain … ^an8-7-2-2

loss … ^an8-7-2-3

fame … ^an8-7-2-4

disgrace … ^an8-7-2-5

honor … ^an8-7-2-6

dishonor … ^an8-7-2-7

corrupt wishes … ^an8-7-2-8

bad friendship, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-7-2-9

Overcome and overwhelmed by these eight things that oppose the true teaching, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-7-2-10

It’s good for a mendicant, whenever they encounter it, to overcome gain … ^an8-7-3-1

loss … ^an8-7-3-2

fame … ^an8-7-3-3

disgrace … ^an8-7-3-4

honor … ^an8-7-3-5

dishonor … ^an8-7-3-6

corrupt wishes … ^an8-7-3-7

bad friendship. ^an8-7-3-8

What advantage does a mendicant gain by overcoming these eight things? ^an8-7-4-1

The distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without overcoming these eight things do not arise when they have overcome them. ^an8-7-5-1

This is the advantage that a mendicant gains by overcoming these eight things. ^an8-7-5-9

So, mendicants, you should train like this: ^an8-7-6-1

‘Whenever we encounter it, we will overcome gain … ^an8-7-6-2

loss … ^an8-7-6-3

fame … ^an8-7-6-4

disgrace … ^an8-7-6-5

honor … ^an8-7-6-6

dishonor … ^an8-7-6-7

corrupt wishes … ^an8-7-6-8

bad friendship.’ ^an8-7-6-9

That’s how you should train.” ^an8-7-6-10

### Uttara on Failure (Numbered Discourses 8.8) ^an8-8-0

At one time Venerable Uttara was staying on the Saṅkheyyaka Mountain in the Mahisa region near Dhavajālikā. ^an8-8-1-1

There Uttara addressed the mendicants: ^an8-8-1-2

“Mendicants, it’s good for a mendicant to check their own failings from time to time. ^an8-8-1-3

It’s good for a mendicant to check the failings of others from time to time. ^an8-8-1-4

It’s good for a mendicant to check their own successes from time to time. ^an8-8-1-5

It’s good for a mendicant to check the successes of others from time to time.” ^an8-8-1-6

Now at that time the great king Vessavaṇa was on his way from the north to the south on some business. ^an8-8-2-1

He heard Venerable Uttara teaching this to the mendicants on Saṅkheyyaka Mountain. ^an8-8-2-2

Then Vessavaṇa vanished from Saṅkheyyaka Mountain and appeared among the gods of the thirty-three, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm. ^an8-8-3-1

Then he went up to Sakka, lord of gods, and said to him: ^an8-8-3-2

“Please good fellow, you should know this. ^an8-8-3-3

Venerable Uttara is teaching the mendicants on Saṅkheyyaka Mountain in this way: ^an8-8-3-4

‘It’s good for a mendicant from time to time to check their own failings. … ^an8-8-3-5

the failings of others … ^an8-8-3-6

their own successes … ^an8-8-3-7

the successes of others.’” ^an8-8-3-8

Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, Sakka vanished from the gods of the thirty-three and reappeared on Saṅkheyyaka Mountain in front of Venerable Uttara. ^an8-8-4-1

Then Sakka went up to Venerable Uttara, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an8-8-4-2

“Is it really true, sir, that you teach the mendicants in this way: ^an8-8-5-1

‘It’s good for a mendicant from time to time to check their own failings … ^an8-8-5-2

the failings of others … ^an8-8-5-3

their own successes … ^an8-8-5-4

the successes of others’?” ^an8-8-5-5

“Indeed, lord of gods.” ^an8-8-5-6

“Sir, did this teaching come to you from your own inspiration, or was it spoken by the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha?” ^an8-8-5-7

“Well then, lord of gods, I shall give you a simile. ^an8-8-5-8

For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said. ^an8-8-5-9

Suppose there was a large heap of grain not far from a town or village. ^an8-8-6-1

And a large crowd were to take away grain ^an8-8-6-2

with carrying poles, baskets, hip sacks, or their cupped hands. ^an8-8-6-3

If someone were to go to that crowd and ask them ^an8-8-6-4

where they got the grain from, how should that crowd rightly reply?” ^an8-8-6-5

“Sir, they should reply that they took it from the large heap of grain.” ^an8-8-6-6

“In the same way, lord of gods, whatever is well spoken is spoken by the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an8-8-6-7

Both myself and others rely completely on that when we speak.” ^an8-8-6-8

“It’s incredible, sir, it’s amazing! ^an8-8-7-1

How well this was said by Venerable Uttara! ^an8-8-7-2

‘Whatever is well spoken is spoken by the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an8-8-7-3

Both myself and others rely completely on that when we speak.’ ^an8-8-7-4

At one time, Honorable Uttara, the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had left. ^an8-8-7-5

There the Buddha spoke to the mendicants about Devadatta: ^an8-8-7-6

‘Mendicants, it’s good for a mendicant from time to time to check their own failings … ^an8-8-8-1

the failings of others … ^an8-8-8-2

their own successes … ^an8-8-8-3

the successes of others. ^an8-8-8-4

Overcome and overwhelmed by eight things that oppose the true teaching, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-8-8-5

What eight? ^an8-8-8-6

Overcome and overwhelmed by gain … ^an8-8-8-7

loss … ^an8-8-8-8

fame … ^an8-8-8-9

disgrace … ^an8-8-8-10

honor … ^an8-8-8-11

dishonor … ^an8-8-8-12

corrupt wishes … ^an8-8-8-13

bad friendship, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-8-8-14

Overcome and overwhelmed by these eight things that oppose the true teaching, Devadatta is going to a place of loss, to hell, there to remain for an eon, irredeemable. ^an8-8-8-15

It’s good for a mendicant, whenever they encounter it, to overcome gain … ^an8-8-9-1

loss … ^an8-8-9-2

fame … ^an8-8-9-3

disgrace … ^an8-8-9-4

honor … ^an8-8-9-5

dishonor … ^an8-8-9-6

corrupt wishes … ^an8-8-9-7

bad friendship. ^an8-8-9-8

What advantage does a mendicant gain by overcoming these eight things? ^an8-8-10-1

The distressing and feverish defilements that might arise in someone who lives without overcoming these eight things do not arise when they have overcome them. ^an8-8-11-1

This is the advantage that a mendicant gains by overcoming these eight things. ^an8-8-11-9

So you should train like this: ^an8-8-12-1

“Whenever we encounter it, we will overcome gain … ^an8-8-12-2

loss … ^an8-8-12-3

fame … ^an8-8-12-4

disgrace … ^an8-8-12-5

honor … ^an8-8-12-6

dishonor … ^an8-8-12-7

corrupt wishes … ^an8-8-12-8

bad friendship.” ^an8-8-12-9

That’s how you should train.’ ^an8-8-12-10

Honorable Uttara, this exposition of the teaching is not established anywhere in the four assemblies— ^an8-8-13-1

monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen. ^an8-8-13-2

Sir, learn this exposition of the teaching! ^an8-8-13-4

Memorize this exposition of the teaching! ^an8-8-13-5

Remember this exposition of the teaching! ^an8-8-13-6

Sir, this exposition of the teaching is beneficial and relevant to the fundamentals of the spiritual life.” ^an8-8-13-7

### Nanda (Numbered Discourses 8.9) ^an8-9-0

“Mendicants, you could rightly call Nanda ‘Gentleman’, ^an8-9-1-1

‘strong’, ^an8-9-1-2

‘lovely’, ^an8-9-1-3

and ‘lustful’. ^an8-9-1-4

How could he live the full and pure spiritual life unless he guards the sense doors, eats in moderation, is dedicated to wakefulness, and has mindfulness and situational awareness? ^an8-9-1-5

This is how Nanda guards the sense doors. ^an8-9-1-6

If he has to look to the east, he wholeheartedly concentrates before looking, thinking: ^an8-9-1-7

‘When I look to the east, bad, unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure will not overwhelm me.’ ^an8-9-1-8

In this way he’s aware of the situation. ^an8-9-1-9

If he has to look to the west … ^an8-9-2-1

north … ^an8-9-2-2

south … ^an8-9-2-3

up … ^an8-9-2-4

down … ^an8-9-2-5

If he has to survey the intermediate directions, he wholeheartedly concentrates before looking, thinking: ^an8-9-2-6

‘When I survey the intermediate directions, bad, unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure will not overwhelm me.’ ^an8-9-2-7

In this way he’s aware of the situation. ^an8-9-2-8

This is how Nanda guards the sense doors. ^an8-9-2-9

This is how Nanda eats in moderation. ^an8-9-3-1

Nanda reflects rationally on the food he eats: ^an8-9-3-2

‘Not for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration, but only to sustain this body, to avoid harm, and to support spiritual practice. In this way, I shall put an end to old discomfort and not give rise to new discomfort, and I will have the means to keep going, blamelessness, and a comfortable abiding.’ ^an8-9-3-3

This is how Nanda eats in moderation. ^an8-9-3-4

This is how Nanda is committed to wakefulness. ^an8-9-4-1

Nanda practices walking and sitting meditation by day, purifying his mind from obstacles. ^an8-9-4-2

In the first watch of the night, he continues to practice walking and sitting meditation. ^an8-9-4-3

In the middle watch, he lies down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^an8-9-4-4

In the last watch, he gets up and continues to practice walking and sitting meditation, purifying his mind from obstacles. ^an8-9-4-5

This is how Nanda is committed to wakefulness. ^an8-9-4-6

This is how Nanda has mindfulness and situational awareness. ^an8-9-5-1

Nanda knows feelings as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an8-9-5-2

He knows perceptions as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an8-9-5-3

He knows thoughts as they arise, as they remain, and as they go away. ^an8-9-5-4

This is how Nanda has mindfulness and situational awareness. ^an8-9-5-5

How could Nanda live the full and pure spiritual life unless he guards the sense doors, eats in moderation, is dedicated to wakefulness, and has mindfulness and situational awareness?” ^an8-9-6-1

### Trash (Numbered Discourses 8.10) ^an8-10-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond. ^an8-10-1-1

Now at that time the mendicants accused a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-10-1-2

The accused mendicant dodged the issue, distracted the discussion with irrelevant points, and displayed annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an8-10-1-3

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants, ^an8-10-2-1

“Mendicants, throw this individual out! ^an8-10-2-2

Throw this individual out! ^an8-10-2-3

This individual should be shown the door. ^an8-10-2-4

Why should you be vexed by an outsider? ^an8-10-2-5

Take a case where a certain individual looks just the same as other good-natured mendicants when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an8-10-2-6

That is, so long as the mendicants don’t notice his offense. ^an8-10-2-7

But when the mendicants notice the offense, they know that he’s ^an8-10-2-8

a corrupt ascetic, just useless trash. ^an8-10-2-9

When they realize this they send him away. ^an8-10-2-10

Why is that? ^an8-10-2-11

So that he doesn’t corrupt good-natured mendicants. ^an8-10-2-12

Suppose in a growing field of barley some bad barley appeared, just useless trash. ^an8-10-3-1

Its roots, stem, and leaves would look just the same as the healthy barley. ^an8-10-3-2

That is, so long as the head doesn’t appear. ^an8-10-3-5

But when the head appears, they know that it’s ^an8-10-3-6

bad barley, just useless trash. ^an8-10-3-7

When they realize this they pull it up by the roots and throw it outside the field. ^an8-10-3-8

Why is that? ^an8-10-3-9

So that it doesn’t spoil the good barley. ^an8-10-3-10

In the same way, take a case where a certain individual looks just the same as other good-natured mendicants when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an8-10-4-1

That is, so long as the mendicants don’t notice his offense. ^an8-10-4-2

But when the mendicants notice the offense, they know that he’s ^an8-10-4-3

a corrupt ascetic, just useless trash. ^an8-10-4-4

When they realize this they send him away. ^an8-10-4-5

Why is that? ^an8-10-4-6

So that he doesn’t corrupt good-natured mendicants. ^an8-10-4-7

Suppose that a large heap of grain is being winnowed. The grains that are firm and solid form a heap on one side. And the grains that are flimsy and insubstantial are blown over to the other side. ^an8-10-5-1

The owners take a broom and sweep them even further away. ^an8-10-5-2

Why is that? ^an8-10-5-3

So that it doesn’t spoil the good grain. ^an8-10-5-4

In the same way, take a case where a certain individual looks just the same as other good-natured mendicants when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an8-10-5-5

That is, so long as the mendicants don’t notice his offense. ^an8-10-5-6

But when the mendicants notice the offense, they know that he’s ^an8-10-5-7

a corrupt ascetic, just useless trash. ^an8-10-5-8

When they realize this they send him away. ^an8-10-5-9

Why is that? ^an8-10-5-10

So that he doesn’t corrupt good-natured mendicants. ^an8-10-5-11

Suppose a man needs an irrigation gutter for a well. He’d take a sharp axe and enter the wood, ^an8-10-6-1

where he’d knock various trees with the axe. The trees that were firm and solid made a cracking sound. ^an8-10-6-2

But the trees that were rotten inside, decomposing and decayed, made a thud. ^an8-10-6-3

He’d cut down such a tree at the root, lop off the crown, and thoroughly clear out the insides. Then he’d use it as an irrigation gutter for the well. ^an8-10-6-4

In the same way, take a case where a certain individual looks just the same as other good-natured mendicants when going out and coming back, when looking ahead and aside, when bending and extending the limbs, and when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes. ^an8-10-6-5

That is, so long as the mendicants don’t notice his offense. ^an8-10-6-6

But when the mendicants notice the offense, they know that he’s ^an8-10-6-7

a corrupt ascetic, just useless trash. ^an8-10-6-8

When they realize this they send him away. ^an8-10-6-9

Why is that? ^an8-10-6-10

So that he doesn’t corrupt good-natured mendicants. ^an8-10-6-11

By living together, know that ^an8-10-7-1

they’re irritable, with corrupt wishes, ^an8-10-7-2

offensive, stubborn, and contemptuous, ^an8-10-7-3

jealous, stingy, and devious. ^an8-10-7-4

They speak to people with a voice ^an8-10-8-1

so smooth, just like an ascetic. ^an8-10-8-2

But they act in secret, with their bad views ^an8-10-8-3

and their lack of regard for others. ^an8-10-8-4

You should recognize them for what they are: ^an8-10-9-1

a creep and liar. ^an8-10-9-2

Then having gathered in harmony, ^an8-10-9-3

you should expel them. ^an8-10-9-4

Throw out the trash! ^an8-10-10-1

Get rid of the rubbish! ^an8-10-10-2

And sweep away the scraps— ^an8-10-10-3

they’re not ascetics, they just think they are. ^an8-10-10-4

When you’ve thrown out those of corrupt wishes, ^an8-10-11-1

of bad behavior and alms-resort, ^an8-10-11-2

dwell in communion, ever mindful, ^an8-10-11-3

the pure with the pure. ^an8-10-11-4

Then in harmony, alert, ^an8-10-11-5

you’ll make an end of suffering.” ^an8-10-11-6

### At Verañjā (Numbered Discourses 8.11) ^an8-11-0

So I have heard. ^an8-11-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying in Verañjā at the root of a neem tree dedicated to Naḷeru. ^an8-11-1-2

Then the brahmin Verañja went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an8-11-1-3

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an8-11-1-4

“Worthy Gotama, I have heard that ^an8-11-2-1

the ascetic Gotama doesn’t bow to old brahmins, the elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat. ^an8-11-2-2

And this is indeed the case, ^an8-11-2-3

for the worthy Gotama does not bow to old brahmins, elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; nor does he rise in their presence or offer them a seat. ^an8-11-2-4

This is not appropriate, worthy Gotama.” ^an8-11-2-5

“Brahmin, I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—for whom I should bow down or rise up or offer a seat. ^an8-11-2-6

If the Realized One bowed down or rose up or offered a seat to anyone, their head would explode!” ^an8-11-2-7

“Worthy Gotama lacks taste.” ^an8-11-3-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say ^an8-11-3-2

that I lack taste. ^an8-11-3-3

For the Realized One has given up taste for sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-3-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I lack taste. ^an8-11-3-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-3-6

“Worthy Gotama is indelicate.” ^an8-11-4-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say ^an8-11-4-2

that I’m indelicate. ^an8-11-4-3

For the Realized One has given up delight in sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. It’s cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-4-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m indelicate. ^an8-11-4-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-4-6

“Worthy Gotama is a teacher of inaction.” ^an8-11-5-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-5-2

I’m a teacher of inaction. ^an8-11-5-3

For I teach inaction regarding bad bodily, verbal, and mental conduct, and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-11-5-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m a teacher of inaction. ^an8-11-5-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-5-6

“Worthy Gotama is a teacher of annihilationism.” ^an8-11-6-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-6-2

I’m a teacher of annihilationism. ^an8-11-6-3

For I teach the annihilation of greed, hate, and delusion, and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-11-6-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m a teacher of annihilationism. ^an8-11-6-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-6-6

“Worthy Gotama is disgusted.” ^an8-11-7-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-7-2

I’m disgusted. ^an8-11-7-3

For I’m disgusted by bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, and by attainment of the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-11-7-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m disgusted. ^an8-11-7-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-7-6

“Worthy Gotama is an exterminator.” ^an8-11-8-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-8-2

I’m an exterminator. ^an8-11-8-3

For I teach the extermination of greed, hate, and delusion, and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-11-8-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m an exterminator. ^an8-11-8-5

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-8-6

“Worthy Gotama is a mortifier.” ^an8-11-9-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-9-2

I’m a mortifier. ^an8-11-9-3

For I say that bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind should be mortified. ^an8-11-9-4

I say that a mortifier is someone who has given up unskillful qualities that should be mortified. They’ve cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-9-5

The Realized One is someone who has given up unskillful qualities that should be mortified. He has cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-9-6

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m a mortifier. ^an8-11-9-7

But that’s not what you’re talking about.” ^an8-11-9-8

“Worthy Gotama is immature.” ^an8-11-10-1

“There is, brahmin, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-11-10-2

I’m immature. ^an8-11-10-3

I say that an immature person is someone who has given up future wombs and regeneration into a new state of existence. They’ve cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-10-4

The Realized One has given up future wombs and regeneration into a new state of existence. He has cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-11-10-5

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m immature. ^an8-11-10-6

But that’s not what you’re talking about. ^an8-11-10-7

Suppose, brahmin, there was a chicken with eight or ten or twelve eggs. ^an8-11-11-1

And she properly sat on them to keep them warm and incubated. ^an8-11-11-2

Now, the chick that is first to break out of the eggshell with its claws and beak and hatch safely: should that be called the eldest or the youngest?” ^an8-11-11-3

“Worthy Gotama, that should be called the eldest. For it is the eldest among them.” ^an8-11-11-4

“In the same way, in this population lost in ignorance, swaddled in their shells, I alone have broken open the egg of ignorance and realized the supreme perfect awakening. ^an8-11-12-1

So, brahmin, I am the eldest and the first in the world. ^an8-11-12-2

My energy was roused up and unflagging, my mindfulness was established and lucid, my body was tranquil and undisturbed, and my mind was immersed in samādhi. ^an8-11-13-1

Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I entered and remained in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an8-11-13-2

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I entered and remained in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an8-11-13-3

And with the fading away of rapture, I entered and remained in the third absorption, where I meditated with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an8-11-13-4

With the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, I entered and remained in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an8-11-13-5

When my mind had immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—I extended it toward recollection of past lives. ^an8-11-14-1

I recollected many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. I remembered: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so I recollected my many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an8-11-14-2

This was the first knowledge, which I achieved in the first watch of the night. ^an8-11-15-1

Ignorance was banished and knowledge arose; darkness was banished and light arose, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an8-11-15-2

This was my first breaking out, like a chick breaking out of the eggshell. ^an8-11-15-3

When my mind had immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—I extended it toward knowledge of the death and rebirth of sentient beings. ^an8-11-16-1

With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, I saw sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. I understood how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds: ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, I saw sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. I understood how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an8-11-16-2

This was the second knowledge, which I achieved in the middle watch of the night. ^an8-11-17-1

Ignorance was banished and knowledge arose; darkness was banished and light arose, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an8-11-17-2

This was my second breaking out, like a chick breaking out of the eggshell. ^an8-11-17-3

When my mind had immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—I extended it toward knowledge of the ending of defilements. ^an8-11-18-1

I truly understood: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an8-11-18-2

I truly understood: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’. ^an8-11-18-3

Knowing and seeing like this, my mind was freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance. ^an8-11-18-4

When it was freed, I knew it was freed. ^an8-11-18-5

I understood: ‘Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.’ ^an8-11-18-6

This was the third knowledge, which I achieved in the last watch of the night. ^an8-11-19-1

Ignorance was banished and knowledge arose; darkness was banished and light arose, as happens for a meditator who is diligent, keen, and resolute. ^an8-11-19-2

This was my third breaking out, like a chick breaking out of the eggshell.” ^an8-11-19-3

When he said this, the brahmin Verañja said to the Buddha: ^an8-11-20-1

“The worthy Gotama is the eldest! The worthy Gotama is the best! ^an8-11-20-2

Excellent, worthy Gotama! Excellent! ^an8-11-20-3

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways. ^an8-11-20-4

I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an8-11-20-5

From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an8-11-20-6

### With Sīha (Numbered Discourses 8.12) ^an8-12-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-12-1-1

Now at that time several very prominent Licchavis were sitting together at the town hall, praising the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha in many ways. ^an8-12-1-2

Now at that time General Sīha, a disciple of the Jains, was sitting in that assembly. ^an8-12-2-1

He thought, ^an8-12-2-2

“That Blessed One must without a doubt be a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. For several very prominent Licchavis are praising the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha in many ways. ^an8-12-2-3

Why don’t I go to see that Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha!” ^an8-12-2-4

Then General Sīha went to the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan and said to him, ^an8-12-2-5

“Sir, I’d like to go to see the ascetic Gotama.” ^an8-12-2-6

“But Sīha, you believe in the doctrine of action. Why should you go to see the ascetic Gotama, who teaches a doctrine of inaction? ^an8-12-3-1

For the ascetic Gotama believes in a doctrine of inaction, he teaches inaction, and he guides his disciples in that way.” ^an8-12-3-2

Then Sīha’s determination to go and see the Buddha died down. ^an8-12-3-3

For a second time, several prominent Licchavis were sitting together at the town hall, praising the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha in many ways. ^an8-12-4-1

And for a second time Sīha thought: ^an8-12-4-2

“Why don’t I go to see that Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha!” ^an8-12-4-4

Then General Sīha went to the Jain Ñātika … ^an8-12-4-5

Then for a second time Sīha’s determination to go and see the Buddha died down. ^an8-12-5-3

For a third time, several prominent Licchavis were sitting together at the town hall, praising the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha in many ways. ^an8-12-6-1

And for a third time Sīha thought, ^an8-12-6-2

“That Blessed One must without a doubt be a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. For several very prominent Licchavis are praising the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha in many ways. ^an8-12-6-3

What can these Jains do to me, whether I take leave of them or not? ^an8-12-6-4

Why don’t I, without taking leave of them, go to see that Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha!” ^an8-12-6-5

Then Sīha, with around five hundred chariots, set out from Vesālī in the middle of the day to see the Buddha. ^an8-12-7-1

He went by carriage as far as the terrain allowed, then descended and went by foot. ^an8-12-7-2

Then General Sīha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an8-12-7-3

“Sir, I have heard this: ^an8-12-8-1

‘The ascetic Gotama believes in a doctrine of inaction, he teaches inaction, and he guides his disciples in that way.’ ^an8-12-8-2

I trust those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and do not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism? ^an8-12-8-3

For we don’t want to misrepresent the Blessed One.” ^an8-12-8-4

“There is, Sīha, a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-9-1

I believe in inaction, I teach inaction, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-9-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-10-1

I believe in action, I teach action, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-10-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-11-1

I believe in annihilationism, I teach annihilation, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-11-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-12-1

I’m disgusted, I teach disgust, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-12-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-13-1

I’m an exterminator, I teach extermination, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-13-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-14-1

I’m a mortifier, I teach mortification, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-14-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-15-1

I’m immature, I teach immaturity, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-15-2

And there is a sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-16-1

I’m ambitious, I teach ambition, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-16-2

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-17-1

I believe in inaction, I teach inaction, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-17-2

I teach inaction regarding bad bodily, verbal, and mental conduct, ^an8-12-17-3

and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-12-17-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I teach inaction. ^an8-12-17-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-18-1

I believe in action, I teach action, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-18-2

I teach action regarding good bodily, verbal, and mental conduct, ^an8-12-18-3

and the many kinds of skillful things. ^an8-12-18-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I teach action. ^an8-12-18-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-19-1

I believe in annihilationism, I teach annihilation, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-19-2

I teach the annihilation of greed, hate, and delusion, ^an8-12-19-3

and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-12-19-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I teach annihilationism. ^an8-12-19-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-20-1

I’m disgusted, I teach disgust, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-20-2

I’m disgusted by bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind, ^an8-12-20-3

and by attainment of the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-12-20-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m disgusted. ^an8-12-20-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-21-1

I’m an exterminator, I teach extermination, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-21-2

I teach the extermination of greed, hate, and delusion, ^an8-12-21-3

and the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-12-21-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m an exterminator. ^an8-12-21-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-22-1

I’m a mortifier, I teach mortification, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-22-2

I say that bad conduct by way of body, speech, and mind should be mortified. ^an8-12-22-3

I say that a mortifier is someone who has given up unskillful qualities that should be mortified. They’ve cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-12-22-4

The Realized One is someone who has given up unskillful qualities that should be mortified. He has cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-12-22-5

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m a mortifier. ^an8-12-22-6

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-23-1

I’m immature, I teach immaturity, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-23-2

I say that an immature person is someone who has given up future wombs and regeneration into a new state of existence. They’ve cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-12-23-3

The Realized One has given up future wombs and regeneration into a new state of existence. He has cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so that they’re unable to arise in the future. ^an8-12-23-4

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m immature. ^an8-12-23-5

And what’s the sense in which you could rightly say that ^an8-12-24-1

I’m ambitious, I teach ambition, and I guide my disciples in that way? ^an8-12-24-2

I’m ambitious to offer solace, the highest solace, I teach solace, and I guide my disciples in that way. ^an8-12-24-3

In this sense you could rightly say that I’m ambitious.” ^an8-12-24-4

When he said this, General Sīha said to the Buddha, ^an8-12-25-1

“Excellent, sir! Excellent! ^an8-12-25-2

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an8-12-25-3

“Sīha, you should act after careful consideration. It’s good for well-known people such as yourself to act after careful consideration.” ^an8-12-26-1

“Now I’m even more delighted and satisfied with the Buddha, since he tells me to act after careful consideration. ^an8-12-26-2

For if the followers of other religions were to gain me as a disciple, they’d carry a banner all over Vesālī, saying: ^an8-12-26-3

‘General Sīha has become our disciple!’ ^an8-12-26-4

And yet the Buddha tells me to act after careful consideration. ^an8-12-26-5

For a second time, I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an8-12-26-6

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an8-12-26-7

“For a long time now, Sīha, your family has been a well-spring of support for the Jain ascetics. You should consider giving to them when they come.” ^an8-12-27-1

“Now I’m even more delighted and satisfied with the Buddha, since he tells me to ^an8-12-27-2

consider giving to the Jain ascetics when they come. ^an8-12-27-3

Sir, I have heard that ^an8-12-27-4

the ascetic Gotama says this: ^an8-12-27-5

‘Gifts should only be given to me, and to my disciples. ^an8-12-27-6

Only what is given to me is very fruitful, not what is given to others. ^an8-12-27-7

Only what is given to my disciples is very fruitful, not what is given to the disciples of others.’ ^an8-12-27-8

Yet the Buddha encourages me to give to the Jain ascetics. ^an8-12-27-9

Well, sir, we’ll know the proper time for that. ^an8-12-27-10

For a third time, I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha. ^an8-12-27-11

From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.” ^an8-12-27-12

Then the Buddha taught Sīha step by step, with ^an8-12-28-1

a talk on giving, ethical conduct, and heaven. He explained the drawbacks of sensual pleasures, so sordid and corrupt, and the benefit of renunciation. ^an8-12-28-2

And when the Buddha knew that Sīha’s mind was ready, pliable, rid of hindrances, elated, and confident he explained the special teaching of the Buddhas: ^an8-12-28-3

suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. ^an8-12-28-4

Just as a clean cloth rid of stains would properly absorb dye, ^an8-12-28-5

in that very seat the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in General Sīha: ^an8-12-28-6

“Everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease.” ^an8-12-28-7

Then Sīha saw, attained, understood, and fathomed the Dhamma. He went beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and became self-assured and independent of others regarding the Teacher’s instructions. He said to the Buddha, ^an8-12-29-1

“Sir, would the Buddha together with the mendicant Saṅgha please accept tomorrow’s meal from me?” ^an8-12-29-2

The Buddha consented with silence. ^an8-12-29-3

Then, knowing that the Buddha had consented, Sīha rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an8-12-29-4

Then Sīha addressed a certain man, ^an8-12-30-1

“My man, please find out if there is any meat ready for sale.” ^an8-12-30-2

And when the night had passed General Sīha had delicious fresh and cooked foods prepared in his own home. Then he had the Buddha informed of the time, saying, ^an8-12-30-3

“Sir, it’s time. The meal is ready.” ^an8-12-30-4

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to Sīha’s home, where he sat on the seat spread out, together with the Saṅgha of mendicants. ^an8-12-31-1

Now at that time many Jain ascetics in Vesālī went from street to street and from square to square, calling out with raised arms: ^an8-12-31-2

“Today General Sīha has slaughtered a fat calf for the ascetic Gotama’s meal. ^an8-12-31-3

The ascetic Gotama knowingly eats meat prepared specially for him: this is a deed he caused.” ^an8-12-31-4

Then a certain person went up to Sīha and whispered in his ear, ^an8-12-32-1

“Please sir, you should know this. ^an8-12-32-2

Many Jain ascetics in Vesālī are going from street to street and square to square, calling out with raised arms: ^an8-12-32-3

‘Today General Sīha has slaughtered a fat calf for the ascetic Gotama’s meal. ^an8-12-32-4

The ascetic Gotama knowingly eats meat prepared specially for him: this is a deed he caused.’” ^an8-12-32-5

“Enough, master. For a long time those venerables have wanted to discredit the Buddha, his teaching, and his Saṅgha. ^an8-12-32-6

They’ll never stop misrepresenting the Buddha with their incorrect, hollow, false, untruthful claims. ^an8-12-32-7

We would never deliberately take the life of a living creature, not even for life’s sake.” ^an8-12-32-8

Then Sīha served and satisfied the mendicant Saṅgha headed by the Buddha with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods. ^an8-12-33-1

When the Buddha had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, Sīha sat down to one side. ^an8-12-33-2

Then the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired him with a Dhamma talk, after which he rose from his seat and left. ^an8-12-33-3

### A Thoroughbred (Numbered Discourses 8.13) ^an8-13-0

“Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with eight factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned a factor of kingship. ^an8-13-1-1

What eight? ^an8-13-1-2

It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is well born on both ^an8-13-1-3

the mother’s and the father’s sides. ^an8-13-1-4

He’s bred in the region fine thoroughbreds come from. ^an8-13-1-5

Whatever food he’s given, ^an8-13-1-6

fresh or dry, ^an8-13-1-7

he eats carefully, without making a mess. ^an8-13-1-8

He’s disgusted by sitting or lying down in excrement or urine. ^an8-13-1-9

He’s gentle and pleasant to live with, and he doesn’t upset the other horses. ^an8-13-1-10

He openly shows his tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints to his trainer, ^an8-13-1-11

so the trainer can try to subdue them. ^an8-13-1-12

He carries his load, determining: ^an8-13-1-13

‘Whether or not the other horses carry their loads, I’ll carry mine.’ ^an8-13-1-14

He always walks in a straight path. ^an8-13-1-15

He’s strong, and stays strong even until death. ^an8-13-1-16

A fine royal thoroughbred with these eight factors is worthy of a king. … ^an8-13-1-17

In the same way, a mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-13-2-1

What eight? ^an8-13-2-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an8-13-2-3

Whatever food they’re given, ^an8-13-2-4

coarse or fine, ^an8-13-2-5

they eat carefully, without bother. ^an8-13-2-6

They’re disgusted with bad conduct by way of body, speech, or mind, ^an8-13-2-7

and by attainment of the many kinds of unskillful things. ^an8-13-2-8

They're gentle and pleasant to live with, and they don’t upset the other mendicants. ^an8-13-2-9

They openly show their tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints to their sensible spiritual companions, ^an8-13-2-10

so they can try to subdue them. ^an8-13-2-11

They do their training, determining: ^an8-13-2-12

‘Whether or not the other mendicants do their training, I’ll do mine.’ ^an8-13-2-13

They always walk in a straight path. ^an8-13-2-14

And here the straight path is ^an8-13-2-15

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an8-13-2-16

They’re energetic: ^an8-13-2-17

‘Gladly, let my skin, sinews, and bones remain! Let the blood and flesh waste away in my body! I will not stop trying until I have achieved what is possible by human strength, energy, and vigor.’ ^an8-13-2-18

A mendicant with these eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an8-13-2-19

### A Wild Colt (Numbered Discourses 8.14) ^an8-14-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you about eight wild colts and eight defects in horses, and about eight wild people and eight defects in people. ^an8-14-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an8-14-1-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an8-14-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an8-14-1-4

“And what, mendicants, are the eight wild colts and eight defects in horses? ^an8-14-2-1

Firstly, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts back right up and spin the chariot behind them. ^an8-14-2-2

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-2-3

This is the first defect of a horse. ^an8-14-2-4

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts jump back, wreck the hub, and break the triple rod. ^an8-14-3-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-3-2

This is the second defect of a horse. ^an8-14-3-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts shake the draft-pole off their thigh and trample it. ^an8-14-4-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-4-2

This is the third defect of a horse. ^an8-14-4-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts take a wrong turn, sending the chariot off track. ^an8-14-5-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-5-2

This is the fourth defect of a horse. ^an8-14-5-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts rear up and strike out with their fore-legs. ^an8-14-6-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-6-2

This is the fifth defect of a horse. ^an8-14-6-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts ignore the trainer and the goad, spit out the bit, and go wherever they want. ^an8-14-7-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-7-2

This is the sixth defect of a horse. ^an8-14-7-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts don’t step forward or turn back but stand right there still as a post. ^an8-14-8-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-8-2

This is the seventh defect of a horse. ^an8-14-8-3

Furthermore, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, some wild colts tuck in their fore-legs and hind-legs, and sit right down on their four legs. ^an8-14-9-1

Some wild colts are like that. ^an8-14-9-2

This is the eighth defect of a horse. ^an8-14-9-3

These are the eight wild colts and the eight defects in horses. ^an8-14-9-4
