And what are the eight wild people and eight defects in people? ^an8-14-10-1

Firstly, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-10-2

But the accused mendicant evades it by saying they don’t remember. ^an8-14-10-3

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, back right up and spin the chariot behind them. ^an8-14-10-4

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-10-6

This is the first defect of a person. ^an8-14-10-7

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-11-1

But the accused mendicant objects to the accuser: ^an8-14-11-2

‘What has an incompetent fool like you got to say? ^an8-14-11-3

How on earth could you imagine you’ve got something worth saying!’ ^an8-14-11-4

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, jump back, wreck the hub, and break the triple rod. ^an8-14-11-5

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-11-7

This is the second defect of a person. ^an8-14-11-8

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-12-1

But the accused mendicant retorts to the accuser: ^an8-14-12-2

‘Well, you’ve fallen into such-and-such an offense. You should deal with that first.’ ^an8-14-12-3

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, shake the draft-pole off their thigh and trample it. ^an8-14-12-4

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-12-6

This is the third defect of a person. ^an8-14-12-7

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-13-1

But the accused mendicant dodges the issue, distracts the discussion with irrelevant points, and displays annoyance, hate, and bitterness. ^an8-14-13-2

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, take a wrong turn, sending the chariot off track. ^an8-14-13-3

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-13-5

This is the fourth defect of a person. ^an8-14-13-6

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-14-1

But the accused mendicant gesticulates while speaking in the middle of the Saṅgha. ^an8-14-14-2

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, rear up and strike out with their fore-legs. ^an8-14-14-3

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-14-5

This is the fifth defect of a person. ^an8-14-14-6

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-15-1

But the accused mendicant ignores the Saṅgha and the accusation and, though still guilty of the offense, they go wherever they want. ^an8-14-15-2

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, ignore the trainer and the goad, spit out the bit, and go wherever they want. ^an8-14-15-3

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-15-5

This is the sixth defect of a person. ^an8-14-15-6

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-16-1

But the accused mendicant neither confesses to the offense nor denies it, but frustrates the Saṅgha by staying silent. ^an8-14-16-2

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, don’t step forward or turn back but stand right there still as a post. ^an8-14-16-3

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-16-5

This is the seventh defect of a person. ^an8-14-16-6

Furthermore, the mendicants accuse a mendicant of an offense. ^an8-14-17-1

But the accused mendicant says this: ^an8-14-17-2

‘Why are you venerables making so much of an issue over me? Now I’ll resign the training and return to a lesser life.’ ^an8-14-17-3

When they have resigned the training, they say: ^an8-14-17-4

‘Well, venerables, are you happy now?’ ^an8-14-17-5

I say that this individual is comparable to the wild colts who, when the trainer says ‘giddyup!’ and spurs and goads them on, tuck in their fore-legs and hind-legs, and sit right down on their four legs. ^an8-14-17-6

Some wild people are like that. ^an8-14-17-8

This is the eighth defect of a person. ^an8-14-17-9

These are the eight wild people and eight defects in people.” ^an8-14-17-10

### Stains (Numbered Discourses 8.15) ^an8-15-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight stains. ^an8-15-1-1

What eight? ^an8-15-1-2

Not rehearsing is the stain of hymns. ^an8-15-1-3

Neglect is the stain of houses. ^an8-15-1-4

Laziness is the stain of beauty. ^an8-15-1-5

Negligence is a guard’s stain. ^an8-15-1-6

Misconduct is a woman’s stain. ^an8-15-1-7

Stinginess is a giver’s stain. ^an8-15-1-8

Bad, unskillful qualities are a stain in this world and the next. ^an8-15-1-9

Worse than any of these is ignorance, the worst stain of all. ^an8-15-1-10

These are the eight stains. ^an8-15-1-11

Not rehearsing is the stain of hymns. ^an8-15-2-1

The stain of houses is neglect. ^an8-15-2-2

Laziness is the stain of beauty. ^an8-15-2-3

A guard’s stain is negligence. ^an8-15-2-4

Misconduct is a woman’s stain. ^an8-15-3-1

A giver’s stain is stinginess. ^an8-15-3-2

Bad qualities are a stain ^an8-15-3-3

in this world and the next. ^an8-15-3-4

But a worse stain than these ^an8-15-3-5

is ignorance, the worst stain of all.” ^an8-15-3-6

### Going on a Mission (Numbered Discourses 8.16) ^an8-16-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of going on a mission. ^an8-16-1-1

What eight? ^an8-16-1-2

It’s a mendicant who learns and educates others. They memorize and remember. They understand and help others understand. They’re skilled at knowing what’s on topic and what isn’t. And they don’t cause quarrels. ^an8-16-1-3

A mendicant with these eight qualities is worthy of going on a mission. ^an8-16-1-4

Having eight qualities Sāriputta is worthy of going on a mission. ^an8-16-1-5

What eight? ^an8-16-1-6

He learns and educates others. He memorizes and remembers. He understands and helps others understand. He’s skilled at knowing what’s on topic and what isn’t. And he doesn’t cause quarrels. ^an8-16-1-7

Having these eight qualities Sāriputta is worthy of going on a mission. ^an8-16-1-8

They don’t tremble when arriving ^an8-16-2-1

at an assembly of fierce debaters. ^an8-16-2-2

They don’t miss out any words, ^an8-16-2-3

or conceal the instructions. ^an8-16-2-4

Their words aren’t poisoned, ^an8-16-3-1

and they don’t tremble when questioned. ^an8-16-3-2

Such a mendicant ^an8-16-3-3

is worthy of going on a mission.” ^an8-16-3-4

### Catching (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.17) ^an8-17-0

“Mendicants, a woman catches a man using eight features. ^an8-17-1-1

What eight? ^an8-17-1-2

With weeping, ^an8-17-1-3

laughing, ^an8-17-1-4

speaking, ^an8-17-1-5

appearance, ^an8-17-1-6

gifts of wildflowers, ^an8-17-1-7

scents, ^an8-17-1-8

tastes, ^an8-17-1-9

and touches. ^an8-17-1-10

A woman catches a man using these eight features. ^an8-17-1-11

But those beings who are caught by touch are well and truly caught.” ^an8-17-1-12

### Catching (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.18) ^an8-18-0

“Mendicants, a man catches a woman using eight features. ^an8-18-1-1

What eight? ^an8-18-1-2

With weeping, ^an8-18-1-3

laughing, ^an8-18-1-4

speaking, ^an8-18-1-5

appearance, ^an8-18-1-6

gifts of wildflowers, ^an8-18-1-7

scents, ^an8-18-1-8

tastes, ^an8-18-1-9

and touches. ^an8-18-1-10

A man catches a woman using these eight features. ^an8-18-1-11

But those beings who are caught by touch are well and truly caught.” ^an8-18-1-12

### With Pahārāda (Numbered Discourses 8.19) ^an8-19-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in Verañjā at the root of a neem tree dedicated to Naḷeru. ^an8-19-1-1

Then Pahārāda, lord of titans, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an8-19-1-2

“Well, Pahārāda, do the titans love the ocean?” ^an8-19-2-1

“Sir, they do indeed.” ^an8-19-2-2

“But seeing what incredible and amazing things do the titans love the ocean?” ^an8-19-2-3

“Sir, seeing eight incredible and amazing things the titans love the ocean. ^an8-19-2-4

What eight? ^an8-19-2-5

The ocean gradually slants, slopes, and inclines, with no abrupt precipice. ^an8-19-2-6

This is the first thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-2-8

Furthermore, the ocean is consistent and doesn’t overflow its boundaries. ^an8-19-3-1

This is the second thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-3-3

Furthermore, the ocean doesn’t accommodate a carcass, but quickly carries it to the shore and strands it on the beach. ^an8-19-4-1

This is the third thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-4-3

Furthermore, when they reach the ocean, all the great rivers—that is, ^an8-19-5-1

the Ganges, Yamunā, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—lose their names and clans and are simply reckoned as ‘the ocean’. ^an8-19-5-2

This is the fourth thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-5-4

Furthermore, for all the world’s streams that reach it, and the showers that fall from the sky, the ocean never empties or fills up. ^an8-19-6-1

This is the fifth thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-6-3

Furthermore, the ocean has just one taste, the taste of salt. ^an8-19-7-1

This is the sixth thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-7-3

Furthermore, the ocean is full of many kinds of treasures, such as pearls, gems, beryl, conch, quartz, coral, silver, native gold, rubies, and emeralds. ^an8-19-8-1

This is the seventh thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-8-3

Furthermore, many great beings live in the ocean, such as leviathans, leviathan-gulpers, leviathan-gulper-gulpers, titans, dragons, and centaurs. In the ocean there are life-forms a hundred leagues long, or even two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, or five hundred leagues long. ^an8-19-9-1

This is the eighth thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-19-9-3

Seeing these eight incredible and amazing things the titans love the ocean. ^an8-19-9-4

Well, sir, do the mendicants love this teaching and training?” ^an8-19-10-1

“They do indeed, Pahārāda.” ^an8-19-10-2

“But seeing how many incredible and amazing things do the mendicants love this teaching and training?” ^an8-19-10-3

“Seeing eight incredible and amazing things, Pahārāda, the mendicants love this teaching and training. ^an8-19-10-4

What eight? ^an8-19-10-5

The ocean gradually slants, slopes, and inclines, with no abrupt precipice. ^an8-19-10-6

In the same way in this teaching and training the penetration to enlightenment comes from gradual training, progress, and practice, not abruptly. ^an8-19-10-7

This is the first thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-10-9

The ocean is consistent and doesn’t overflow its boundaries. ^an8-19-11-1

In the same way, when a training rule is laid down for my disciples they wouldn’t break it even for the sake of their own life. ^an8-19-11-2

This is the second thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-11-4

The ocean doesn’t accommodate a carcass, but quickly carries it to the shore and strands it on the beach. ^an8-19-12-1

In the same way, the Saṅgha doesn’t accommodate an individual who is unethical, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved. But they quickly gather and expel them. Even if they are sitting in the middle of the Saṅgha, they’re far from the Saṅgha, and the Saṅgha is far from them. ^an8-19-13-1

This is the third thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-13-3

When they reach the ocean, all the great rivers—that is, the Ganges, Yamunā, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—lose their names and clans and are simply reckoned as ‘the ocean’. ^an8-19-14-1

In the same way, when they go forth from the lay life to homelessness, all four classes—aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials—lose their former names and clans and are simply reckoned as ‘ascetics who follow the Sakyan’. ^an8-19-14-2

This is the fourth thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-14-4

For all the world’s streams that reach it, and the showers that fall from the sky, the ocean never empties or fills up. ^an8-19-15-1

In the same way, though several mendicants become fully extinguished in the element of extinguishment with no residue, the element of extinguishment never empties or fills up. ^an8-19-15-2

This is the fifth thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-15-4

The ocean has just one taste, the taste of salt. ^an8-19-16-1

In the same way, this teaching and training has one taste, the taste of freedom. ^an8-19-16-2

This is the sixth thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-16-4

The ocean is full of many kinds of treasures, such as pearls, gems, beryl, conch, quartz, coral, silver, native gold, rubies, and emeralds. ^an8-19-17-1

In the same way, this teaching and training is full of many kinds of treasures, such as the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, the four right efforts, the four bases of psychic power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven awakening factors, and the noble eightfold path. ^an8-19-17-2

This is the seventh thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-17-4

Many great beings live in the ocean, such as leviathans, leviathan-gulpers, leviathan-gulper-gulpers, titans, dragons, and centaurs. In the ocean there are life-forms a hundred leagues long, or even two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, or five hundred leagues long. ^an8-19-18-1

In the same way, great beings live in this teaching and training, and these are those beings. The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The perfected one, and the one practicing for perfection. ^an8-19-18-2

This is the eighth thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-19-18-4

Seeing these eight incredible and amazing things, Pahārāda, the mendicants love this teaching and training.” ^an8-19-19-1

### Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 8.20) ^an8-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an8-20-1-1

Now, at that time it was the sabbath, and the Buddha was sitting surrounded by the Saṅgha of monks. ^an8-20-1-2

And then, as the night was getting late, in the first watch of the night, Venerable Ānanda rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha and said, ^an8-20-1-3

“Sir, the night is getting late. It is the first watch of the night, and the Saṅgha of monks has been sitting long. ^an8-20-1-4

Please, sir, may the Buddha recite the monastic code to the monks.” ^an8-20-1-5

But when he said this, the Buddha kept silent. ^an8-20-2-1

For a second time, as the night was getting late, in the middle watch of the night, Ānanda rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha and said, ^an8-20-2-2

“Sir, the night is getting late. It is the middle watch of the night, and the Saṅgha of monks has been sitting long. ^an8-20-2-3

Please, sir, may the Buddha recite the monastic code to the monks.” ^an8-20-2-4

But for a second time the Buddha kept silent. ^an8-20-2-5

For a third time, as the night was getting late, in the last watch of the night, as dawn stirred, bringing joy to the night, Ānanda rose from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms toward the Buddha and said, ^an8-20-2-6

“Sir, the night is getting late. It is the last watch of the night and dawn stirs, bringing joy to the night. ^an8-20-2-7

And the Saṅgha has been sitting long. ^an8-20-2-8

Please, sir, may the Buddha recite the monastic code to the monks.” ^an8-20-2-9

“Ānanda, the assembly is not pure.” ^an8-20-2-10

Then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna thought, ^an8-20-3-1

“Who is the Buddha talking about?” ^an8-20-3-2

Then he focused on encompassing the minds of everyone in the Saṅgha. ^an8-20-3-4

He saw that unethical individual, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved, sitting in the middle of the Saṅgha. ^an8-20-3-5

When he saw him he rose from his seat, went up to him and said, ^an8-20-3-6

“Get up, reverend. The Buddha has seen you. ^an8-20-3-7

You can’t live in communion with the monks.” ^an8-20-3-8

But when he said this, that individual kept silent. ^an8-20-4-1

For a second time ^an8-20-4-2

and a third time, ^an8-20-4-6

he asked that monk to leave. ^an8-20-4-7

But for a third time that individual kept silent. ^an8-20-4-9

Then Venerable Mahāmoggallāna took that individual by the arm, ejected him out the gate, and bolted the door. Then he went up to the Buddha, and said to him, ^an8-20-5-1

“I have ejected that individual. ^an8-20-5-2

The assembly is pure. ^an8-20-5-3

Please, sir, may the Buddha recite the monastic code to the monks.” ^an8-20-5-4

“It’s incredible, Moggallāna, it’s amazing, ^an8-20-5-5

how that futile man waited to be taken by the arm!” ^an8-20-5-6

Then the Buddha said to the monks: ^an8-20-6-1

“Now, monks, you should perform the sabbath and recite the monastic code. ^an8-20-6-2

From this day forth, I will not perform the sabbath or recite the monastic code. ^an8-20-6-3

It’s impossible, monks, it can’t happen that a Realized One could recite the monastic code in an impure assembly. ^an8-20-6-4

Seeing these eight incredible and amazing things the titans love the ocean. ^an8-20-7-1

What eight? ^an8-20-7-2

The ocean gradually slants, slopes, and inclines, with no abrupt precipice. ^an8-20-7-3

This is the first thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-20-7-5

(Tell in full as in the previous discourse.) ^an8-20-7-6

Furthermore, many great beings live in the ocean, such as leviathans, leviathan-gulpers, leviathan-gulper-gulpers, titans, dragons, and centaurs. In the ocean there are life-forms a hundred leagues long, or even two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, or five hundred leagues long. ^an8-20-8-1

This is the eighth thing the titans love about the ocean. ^an8-20-8-3

Seeing these eight incredible and amazing things the titans love the ocean. ^an8-20-8-4

In the same way, seeing eight incredible and amazing things, mendicants, the mendicants love this teaching and training. ^an8-20-9-1

What eight? ^an8-20-9-2

The ocean gradually slants, slopes, and inclines, with no abrupt precipice. ^an8-20-9-3

In the same way in this teaching and training the penetration to enlightenment comes from gradual training, progress, and practice, not abruptly. ^an8-20-9-4

This is the first thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. … ^an8-20-9-6

Many great beings live in the ocean, such as leviathans, leviathan-gulpers, leviathan-gulper-gulpers, titans, dragons, and centaurs. In the ocean there are life-forms a hundred leagues long, or even two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, or five hundred leagues long. ^an8-20-9-7

In the same way, great beings live in this teaching and training, and these are those beings. The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The perfected one, and the one practicing for perfection. ^an8-20-9-8

This is the eighth thing the mendicants love about this teaching and training. ^an8-20-9-10

Seeing these eight incredible and amazing things, the mendicants love this teaching and training.” ^an8-20-9-11

### With Ugga of Vesālī (Numbered Discourses 8.21) ^an8-21-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-21-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-21-1-2

“Mendicants, you should remember the householder Ugga of Vesālī as someone who has eight amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-21-1-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^an8-21-1-4

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an8-21-1-5

Then a certain mendicant robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Ugga of Vesālī, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an8-21-2-1

Then Ugga of Vesālī went up to that mendicant, bowed, and sat down to one side. The mendicant said to him: ^an8-21-2-2

“Householder, the Buddha declared that you have eight amazing and incredible qualities. ^an8-21-3-1

What are the eight qualities that he spoke of?” ^an8-21-3-2

“Sir, I don’t know ^an8-21-3-3

what eight amazing and incredible qualities the Buddha was referring to. ^an8-21-3-4

But these eight amazing and incredible qualities are found in me. ^an8-21-3-5

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an8-21-3-6

“Yes, householder,” replied the mendicant. ^an8-21-3-7

Ugga of Vesālī said this: ^an8-21-3-8

“Sir, when I first saw the Buddha off in the distance, ^an8-21-3-9

my heart was inspired as soon as I saw him. ^an8-21-3-10

This is the first incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-3-11

With confident heart I paid homage to the Buddha. ^an8-21-4-1

The Buddha taught me step by step, ^an8-21-4-2

with a talk on giving, ethical conduct, and heaven. He explained the drawbacks of sensual pleasures, so sordid and corrupt, and the benefit of renunciation. ^an8-21-4-3

And when he knew that my mind was ready, pliable, rid of hindrances, elated, and confident he explained the special teaching of the Buddhas: ^an8-21-4-4

suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. ^an8-21-4-5

Just as a clean cloth rid of stains would properly absorb dye, ^an8-21-4-6

in that very seat the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in me: ^an8-21-4-7

‘Everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease.’ ^an8-21-4-8

I saw, attained, understood, and fathomed the Dhamma. I went beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and became self-assured and independent of others regarding the Teacher’s instructions. ^an8-21-4-9

Right there I went for refuge to the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha. And I undertook the five training rules with chastity as the fifth. ^an8-21-4-10

This is the second incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-4-11

I had four teenage wives. ^an8-21-5-1

And I went to them and said: ^an8-21-5-2

‘Sisters, I’ve undertaken the five training rules with chastity as fifth. ^an8-21-5-3

If you wish, you may stay here, enjoy my wealth, and do good deeds. Or you can return to your own families. ^an8-21-5-4

Or would you prefer if I gave you to another man?’ ^an8-21-5-5

When I said this, my eldest wife said to me: ^an8-21-5-6

‘Master, please give me to such-and-such a man.’ ^an8-21-5-7

Then I summoned that man. Taking my wife with my left hand and a ceremonial vase with my right, I presented her to that man with the pouring of water. ^an8-21-5-8

But I can’t recall getting upset while giving away my teenage wife. ^an8-21-5-9

This is the third incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-5-10

And though my family has wealth, ^an8-21-6-1

it’s shared without reserve with ethical people of good character. ^an8-21-6-2

This is the fourth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-6-3

When I pay homage to a mendicant, ^an8-21-7-1

I do so carefully, not carelessly. ^an8-21-7-2

This is the fifth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-7-3

If that venerable teaches me the Dhamma, ^an8-21-8-1

I listen carefully, not carelessly. ^an8-21-8-2

But if they don’t teach me the Dhamma, I teach them. ^an8-21-8-3

This is the sixth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-8-4

It’s not unusual for deities to come to me and announce: ^an8-21-9-1

‘Householder, the Buddha’s teaching is well explained!’ ^an8-21-9-2

When they say this I say to them: ^an8-21-9-3

‘The Buddha’s teaching is well explained, regardless of whether or not you deities say so!’ ^an8-21-9-4

But I don’t recall getting too excited by the fact that ^an8-21-9-5

the deities come to me, and I have a conversation with them. ^an8-21-9-6

This is the seventh incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-9-7

Of the five lower fetters taught by the Buddha, I don’t see any that I haven’t given up. ^an8-21-10-1

This is the eighth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-21-10-2

These eight amazing and incredible qualities are found in me. ^an8-21-11-1

But I don’t know ^an8-21-11-2

what eight amazing and incredible qualities the Buddha was referring to.” ^an8-21-11-3

Then that mendicant, after taking almsfood in Ugga of Vesālī’s home, rose from his seat and left. ^an8-21-12-1

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an8-21-12-2

He informed the Buddha of all he had discussed with the householder Ugga of Vesālī. The Buddha said: ^an8-21-12-3

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an8-21-13-1

When I declared that the householder Ugga of Vesālī was someone who has eight amazing and incredible qualities, I was referring to the same eight qualities that he rightly explained to you. ^an8-21-13-2

You should remember the householder Ugga of Vesālī as someone who possesses these eight amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-21-13-3

### With Uggata of Elephant Village (Numbered Discourses 8.22) ^an8-22-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Vajjis at Elephant Village. ^an8-22-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-22-1-2

“Mendicants, you should remember the householder Uggata of Elephant Village as someone who has eight amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-22-1-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^an8-22-1-4

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an8-22-1-5

Then a certain mendicant robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Uggata of Elephant Village, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an8-22-2-1

Then Uggata of Elephant Village went up to that mendicant, bowed, and sat down to one side. The mendicant said to him: ^an8-22-2-2

“Householder, the Buddha declared that you have eight amazing and incredible qualities. ^an8-22-2-3

What are the eight qualities that he spoke of?” ^an8-22-2-4

“Sir, I don’t know ^an8-22-3-1

what eight amazing and incredible qualities the Buddha was referring to. ^an8-22-3-2

But these eight amazing and incredible qualities are found in me. ^an8-22-3-3

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an8-22-3-4

“Yes, householder,” replied the mendicant. ^an8-22-3-5

Uggata of Elephant Village said this: ^an8-22-3-6

“Sir, when I first saw the Buddha off in the distance I was partying in the Dragon’s Park. ^an8-22-3-7

My heart was inspired as soon as I saw him, and I sobered up. ^an8-22-3-8

This is the first incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-3-9

With confident heart I paid homage to the Buddha. ^an8-22-4-1

The Buddha taught me step by step, with ^an8-22-4-2

a talk on giving, ethical conduct, and heaven. He explained the drawbacks of sensual pleasures, so sordid and corrupt, and the benefit of renunciation. ^an8-22-4-3

And when he knew that my mind was ready, pliable, rid of hindrances, elated, and confident he explained the special teaching of the Buddhas: ^an8-22-4-4

suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. ^an8-22-4-5

Just as a clean cloth rid of stains would properly absorb dye, ^an8-22-4-6

in that very seat the stainless, immaculate vision of the Dhamma arose in me: ^an8-22-4-7

‘Everything that is liable to arise is liable to cease.’ ^an8-22-4-8

I saw, attained, understood, and fathomed the Dhamma. I went beyond doubt, got rid of indecision, and became self-assured and independent of others regarding the Teacher’s instructions. Right there I went for refuge to the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha. And I undertook the five training rules with chastity as the fifth. ^an8-22-4-9

This is the second incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-4-10

I had four teenage wives. ^an8-22-5-1

And I went to them and said: ^an8-22-5-2

‘Sisters, I’ve undertaken the five training rules with chastity as fifth. ^an8-22-5-3

If you wish, you may stay here, enjoy my wealth, and do good deeds. Or you can return to your own families. ^an8-22-5-4

Or would you prefer if I gave you to another man?’ ^an8-22-5-5

When I said this, my eldest wife said to me: ^an8-22-5-6

‘Master, please give me to such-and-such a man.’ ^an8-22-5-7

Then I summoned that man. Taking my wife with my left hand and a ceremonial vase with my right, I presented her to that man with the pouring of water. ^an8-22-5-8

But I can’t recall getting upset while giving away my teenage wife. ^an8-22-5-9

This is the third incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-5-10

And though my family has wealth, ^an8-22-6-1

it’s shared without reserve with ethical people of good character. ^an8-22-6-2

This is the fourth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-6-3

When I pay homage to a mendicant, ^an8-22-7-1

I do so carefully, not carelessly. ^an8-22-7-2

If that venerable teaches me the Dhamma, ^an8-22-7-3

I listen carefully, not carelessly. ^an8-22-7-4

But if they don’t teach me the Dhamma, I teach them. ^an8-22-7-5

This is the fifth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-7-6

It’s not unusual for deities to come to me when the Saṅgha has been invited and announce: ^an8-22-8-1

‘Householder, that mendicant is freed both ways. That one is freed by wisdom. That one is a direct witness. That one is attained to view. That one is freed by faith. That one is a follower of teachings. That one is a follower by faith. That one is ethical, of good character. That one is unethical, of bad character.’ ^an8-22-8-2

But while I’m serving the Saṅgha I don’t recall thinking: ^an8-22-8-3

‘Let me give this one just a little, and that one a lot.’ ^an8-22-8-4

Rather, I give impartially. ^an8-22-8-5

This is the sixth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-8-6

It’s not unusual for deities to come to me and announce: ^an8-22-9-1

‘Householder, the Buddha’s teaching is well explained!’ ^an8-22-9-2

When they say this I say to them: ^an8-22-9-3

‘The Buddha’s teaching is well explained, regardless of whether or not you deities say so!’ ^an8-22-9-4

But I don’t recall getting too excited by the fact that ^an8-22-9-5

the deities come to me, and I have a conversation with them. ^an8-22-9-6

This is the seventh incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-9-7

If I pass away before the Buddha, it would be no wonder if the Buddha declares of me: ^an8-22-10-1

‘The householder Uggata of Elephant Village is bound by no fetter that might return him to this world.’ ^an8-22-10-2

This is the eighth incredible and amazing quality found in me. ^an8-22-10-3

These eight amazing and incredible qualities are found in me. ^an8-22-11-1

But I don’t know ^an8-22-11-2

what eight amazing and incredible qualities the Buddha was referring to.” ^an8-22-11-3

Then that mendicant, after taking almsfood in Uggata of Elephant Village’s home, rose from his seat and left. ^an8-22-12-1

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an8-22-12-2

He informed the Buddha of all he had discussed with the householder Uggata of Elephant Village. The Buddha said: ^an8-22-12-3

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an8-22-13-1

When I declared that the householder Uggata of Elephant Village was someone who has eight amazing and incredible qualities, I was referring to the same eight qualities that he rightly explained to you. ^an8-22-13-2

You should remember the householder Uggata of Elephant Village as someone who has these eight amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-22-13-3

### With Hatthaka (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.23) ^an8-23-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine. ^an8-23-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-23-1-2

“Mendicants, you should remember the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī as someone who has seven amazing and incredible qualities. ^an8-23-1-3

What seven? ^an8-23-1-4

He’s faithful, ^an8-23-1-5

ethical, ^an8-23-1-6

conscientious, ^an8-23-1-7

prudent, ^an8-23-1-8

learned, ^an8-23-1-9

generous, ^an8-23-1-10

and wise. ^an8-23-1-11

You should remember the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī as someone who possesses these seven amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-23-1-12

That is what the Buddha said. ^an8-23-1-13

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an8-23-1-14

Then a certain mendicant robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an8-23-2-1

Then Hatthaka went up to that mendicant, bowed, and sat down to one side. The mendicant said to Hatthaka: ^an8-23-2-2

“Householder, the Buddha declared that you have seven amazing and incredible qualities. ^an8-23-3-1

What seven? ^an8-23-3-2

He said that you’re faithful, ^an8-23-3-3

ethical, ^an8-23-3-4

conscientious, ^an8-23-3-5

prudent, ^an8-23-3-6

learned, ^an8-23-3-7

generous, ^an8-23-3-8

and wise. ^an8-23-3-9

The Buddha declared that you possess these seven amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-23-3-10

“But sir, I trust that no white-clothed lay people were present?” ^an8-23-3-11

“No, there weren’t any white-clothed lay people present.” ^an8-23-3-12

“That’s good, sir.” ^an8-23-3-13

Then that mendicant, after taking almsfood in Hatthaka of Āḷavī’s home, rose from his seat and left. ^an8-23-4-1

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him of what he had discussed with the householder Hatthaka. The Buddha said: ^an8-23-4-2

“Good, good, mendicant! ^an8-23-7-1

That gentleman has few wishes. ^an8-23-7-2

He doesn’t want his own good qualities to be made known to others. ^an8-23-7-3

Well then, mendicant, you should remember the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī as someone who has this eighth amazing and incredible quality, that is, fewness of wishes.” ^an8-23-7-4

### With Hatthaka (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.24) ^an8-24-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine. ^an8-24-1-1

Then the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī, escorted by around five hundred lay followers, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to Hatthaka: ^an8-24-1-2

“Hatthaka, you have a large congregation. ^an8-24-1-3

How do you bring together such a large congregation?” ^an8-24-1-4

“Sir, I bring together such a large congregation by using the four ways of being inclusive as taught by the Buddha. ^an8-24-1-5

When I know that they ^an8-24-1-6

can be included by a gift, I include them by giving a gift. ^an8-24-1-7

When I know that they ^an8-24-1-8

can be included by kindly words, I include them by kindly words. ^an8-24-1-9

When I know that they ^an8-24-1-10

can be included by taking care of them, I include them by caring for them. ^an8-24-1-11

When I know that they ^an8-24-1-12

can be included by equality, I include them by treating them equally. ^an8-24-1-13

But also, worthy sir, my family is wealthy. ^an8-24-1-14

They wouldn’t think that a poor person was worth listening to in the same way.” ^an8-24-1-15

“Good, good, Hatthaka! ^an8-24-1-16

This is the right way to bring together a large congregation. ^an8-24-1-17

Whether in the past, future, or present, all those who have brought together a large congregation have done so by using these four ways of being inclusive.” ^an8-24-1-18

Then the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired Hatthaka of Āḷavī with a Dhamma talk, after which he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha before leaving. ^an8-24-2-1

Then, not long after Hatthaka had left, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-24-2-2

“Mendicants, you should remember the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī as someone who has eight amazing and incredible qualities. ^an8-24-2-3

What eight? ^an8-24-2-4

He’s faithful, ^an8-24-2-5

ethical, ^an8-24-2-6

conscientious, ^an8-24-2-7

prudent, ^an8-24-2-8

learned, ^an8-24-2-9

generous, ^an8-24-2-10

wise, ^an8-24-2-11

and has few wishes. ^an8-24-2-12

You should remember the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī as someone who possesses these eight amazing and incredible qualities.” ^an8-24-2-13

### With Mahānāma (Numbered Discourses 8.25) ^an8-25-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an8-25-1-1

Then Mahānāma the Sakyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an8-25-1-2

“Sir, how is a lay follower defined?” ^an8-25-1-3

“Mahānāma, when you’ve gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, ^an8-25-1-4

you’re considered to be a lay follower.” ^an8-25-1-5

“But how is an ethical lay follower defined?” ^an8-25-2-1

“When a lay follower doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor, ^an8-25-2-2

they’re considered to be an ethical lay follower.” ^an8-25-2-3

“But how do we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit themselves, not others?” ^an8-25-3-1

“A lay follower is accomplished in faith, but doesn’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-2

They’re accomplished in ethical conduct, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-3

They’re accomplished in generosity, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-4

They like to see the mendicants, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-5

They like to hear the true teaching, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-6

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-7

They examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve memorized, but don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-8

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching, but they don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-3-9

That’s how we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit themselves, not others.” ^an8-25-3-10

“But how do we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit both themselves and others?” ^an8-25-4-1

“A lay follower is accomplished in faith and encourages others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-2

They’re accomplished in ethical conduct and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-3

They’re accomplished in generosity and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-4

They like to see the mendicants and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-5

They like to hear the true teaching and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-6

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-7

They examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve memorized and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-8

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching and they encourage others to do the same. ^an8-25-4-9

That’s how we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit both themselves and others.” ^an8-25-4-10

### With Jīvaka (Numbered Discourses 8.26) ^an8-26-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha in Jīvaka’s Mango Grove. ^an8-26-1-1

Then Jīvaka Komārabhacca went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an8-26-1-2

“Sir, how is a lay follower defined?” ^an8-26-1-3

“Jīvaka, when you’ve gone for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, ^an8-26-1-4

you’re considered to be a lay follower.” ^an8-26-1-5

“But how is an ethical lay follower defined?” ^an8-26-2-1

“When a lay follower doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor, ^an8-26-2-2

they’re considered to be an ethical lay follower.” ^an8-26-2-3

“But how do we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit themselves, not others?” ^an8-26-3-1

“A lay follower is accomplished in faith, but doesn’t encourage others to do the same. They’re accomplished in ethical conduct … they’re accomplished in generosity … they like to see the mendicants … they like to hear the true teaching … they memorize the teachings … they examine the meaning … ^an8-26-3-2

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching, but they don’t encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-3-3

That’s how we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit themselves, not others.” ^an8-26-3-4

“But how do we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit both themselves and others?” ^an8-26-4-1

“A lay follower is accomplished in faith and encourages others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-2

They’re accomplished in ethical conduct and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-3

They’re accomplished in generosity and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-4

They like to see the mendicants and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-5

They like to hear the true teaching and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-6

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-7

They examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve memorized and encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-8

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching and they encourage others to do the same. ^an8-26-4-9

That’s how we define a lay follower who is practicing to benefit both themselves and others.” ^an8-26-4-10

### Powers (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.27) ^an8-27-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight powers. ^an8-27-1-1

What eight? ^an8-27-1-2

Crying is the power of babies. Anger is the power of ladies. Weapons are the power of bandits. Authority is the power of rulers. Complaining is the power of fools. Reason is the power of the astute. Reflection is the power of the learned. Patience is the power of ascetics and brahmins. ^an8-27-1-3

These are the eight powers.” ^an8-27-1-4

### Powers (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.28) ^an8-28-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an8-28-1-1

“Sāriputta, how many powers does a mendicant who has ended the defilements have that qualify them to claim: ^an8-28-1-2

‘My defilements have ended’?” ^an8-28-1-3

“Sir, a mendicant who has ended the defilements has eight powers that qualify them to claim: ^an8-28-1-4

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-1-5

What eight? ^an8-28-2-1

Firstly, a mendicant with defilements ended has clearly seen with right wisdom all conditions as truly impermanent. ^an8-28-2-2

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an8-28-2-3

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-2-4

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has clearly seen with right wisdom that sensual pleasures are truly like a pit of glowing coals. ^an8-28-3-1

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an8-28-3-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-3-3

Furthermore, the mind of a mendicant with defilements ended slants, slopes, and inclines to seclusion. They’re withdrawn, loving renunciation, and have totally eliminated defiling influences. ^an8-28-4-1

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an8-28-4-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has well developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an8-28-5-1

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an8-28-5-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-5-3

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has well developed the four bases of psychic power … ^an8-28-6-1

the five faculties … ^an8-28-6-2

the seven awakening factors … ^an8-28-6-3

the noble eightfold path. ^an8-28-6-4

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an8-28-6-5

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an8-28-6-6

A mendicant who has ended the defilements possesses these eight powers that qualify them to claim: ^an8-28-7-1

‘My defilements have ended.’” ^an8-28-7-2

### Lost Opportunities (Numbered Discourses 8.29) ^an8-29-0

“‘Now is the time! Now is the time!’ So says an unlearned ordinary person. But they don’t know whether it’s time or not. ^an8-29-1-1

Mendicants, there are eight lost opportunities for spiritual practice. ^an8-29-1-2

What eight? ^an8-29-1-3

Firstly, a Realized One has arisen in the world—perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. He teaches the Dhamma leading to peace, extinguishment, awakening, as proclaimed by the Holy One. ^an8-29-1-4

But an individual has been reborn in hell. ^an8-29-1-5

This is the first lost opportunity for spiritual practice. ^an8-29-1-6

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. ^an8-29-2-1

But an individual has been reborn in the animal realm. This is the second lost opportunity. ^an8-29-2-2

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. ^an8-29-3-1

But an individual has been reborn in the ghost realm. This is the third lost opportunity. ^an8-29-3-2

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. ^an8-29-4-1

But an individual has been reborn in one of the long-lived orders of gods. This is the fourth lost opportunity. ^an8-29-4-2

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. ^an8-29-5-1

But an individual has been reborn in the borderlands, among uneducated foreigners, where monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen do not go. This is the fifth lost opportunity … ^an8-29-5-2

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. And an individual is reborn in a central country. But they have wrong view and distorted perspective: ^an8-29-6-1

‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’ This is the sixth lost opportunity … ^an8-29-6-2

Furthermore, a Realized One has arisen in the world. ^an8-29-7-1

And an individual is reborn in a central country. But they’re witless, dull, idiotic, and unable to distinguish what is well said from what is poorly said. ^an8-29-7-2

This is the seventh lost opportunity … ^an8-29-7-3

Furthermore, a Realized One has not arisen in the world … ^an8-29-8-1

So he doesn’t teach the Dhamma leading to peace, extinguishment, awakening, as proclaimed by the Holy One. And an individual is reborn in a central country. And they’re wise, bright, clever, and able to distinguish what is well said from what is poorly said. ^an8-29-8-2

This is the eighth lost opportunity … ^an8-29-8-3

There are these eight lost opportunities for spiritual practice. ^an8-29-9-1

Mendicants, there is just one opportunity for spiritual practice. ^an8-29-10-1

What is that one? ^an8-29-10-2

It’s when a Realized One has arisen in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^an8-29-10-3

He teaches the Dhamma leading to peace, extinguishment, awakening, as proclaimed by the Holy One. And an individual is reborn in a central country. And they’re wise, bright, clever, and able to distinguish what is well said from what is poorly said. ^an8-29-10-4

This is the one opportunity for spiritual practice. ^an8-29-10-5

When you’ve gained the human state, ^an8-29-11-1

and the true teaching has been so well proclaimed, ^an8-29-11-2

those who don’t seize the moment ^an8-29-11-3

are wasting their time. ^an8-29-11-4

For many wrong times are spoken of, ^an8-29-12-1

which obstruct the path. ^an8-29-12-2

Only on rare occasions ^an8-29-12-3

do Realized Ones arise. ^an8-29-12-4

If you find yourself in their presence, ^an8-29-13-1

so hard to find in the world, ^an8-29-13-2

and if you’ve gained a human birth, ^an8-29-13-3

and the teaching of the Dhamma; ^an8-29-13-4

that’s enough to make an effort, ^an8-29-13-5

for a personage who desires self-knowledge. ^an8-29-13-6

How is the true teaching to be understood ^an8-29-14-1

so that the moment doesn’t pass you by? ^an8-29-14-2

For if you miss your moment ^an8-29-14-3

you’ll grieve when sent to hell. ^an8-29-14-4

If you fail to achieve ^an8-29-15-1

certainty regarding the true teaching ^an8-29-15-2

you’ll regret it for a long time, ^an8-29-15-3

like the merchant in the story of the past. ^an8-29-15-4

A man shrouded by ignorance, ^an8-29-16-1

a failure in the true teaching, ^an8-29-16-2

will long undergo ^an8-29-16-3

transmigration through birth and death. ^an8-29-16-4

Those who’ve gained the human state ^an8-29-17-1

when the true teaching has been so well proclaimed, ^an8-29-17-2

and have completed what the Teacher taught— ^an8-29-17-3

or will do so, or are doing so now— ^an8-29-17-4

have realized the right time in the world ^an8-29-18-1

for the supreme spiritual life. ^an8-29-18-2

You should live guarded, ever mindful, ^an8-29-18-3

not festering with defilements, ^an8-29-18-4

among those restrained ones ^an8-29-19-1

who have practiced the path ^an8-29-19-2

proclaimed by the Realized One, the Clear-eyed One, ^an8-29-19-3

and taught by the Kinsman of the Sun. ^an8-29-19-4

Having cut off all underlying tendencies ^an8-29-20-1

that follow those drifting in Māra’s dominion, ^an8-29-20-2

they’re the ones in this world <j>who’ve truly crossed over, ^an8-29-20-3

having reached the ending of defilements.” ^an8-29-20-4

### Anuruddha and the Great Thoughts (Numbered Discourses 8.30) ^an8-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^an8-30-1-1

And at that time Venerable Anuruddha was staying in the land of the Cetīs in the Park of the East-Raftered Hall. ^an8-30-1-2

Then as Anuruddha was in private retreat this thought came to his mind: ^an8-30-1-3

“This teaching is for those of few wishes, not those of many wishes. ^an8-30-1-4

It’s for the contented, not those who lack contentment. ^an8-30-1-5

It’s for the secluded, not those who enjoy company. ^an8-30-1-6

It’s for the energetic, not the lazy. ^an8-30-1-7

It’s for the mindful, not the unmindful. ^an8-30-1-8

It’s for those with immersion, not those without immersion. ^an8-30-1-9

It’s for the wise, not the witless.” ^an8-30-1-10

Then the Buddha knew Anuruddha’s train of thought. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood in the land of the Bhaggas and reappeared in front of Anuruddha in the Park of the East-Raftered Hall in the land of the Cetīs, ^an8-30-2-1

and sat on the seat spread out. ^an8-30-2-2

Anuruddha bowed to the Buddha and sat down to one side. ^an8-30-2-3

The Buddha said to him: ^an8-30-2-4

“Good, good, Anuruddha! ^an8-30-3-1

It’s good that you reflect on these thoughts of a great man: ^an8-30-3-2

‘This teaching is for those of few wishes, not those of many wishes. ^an8-30-3-3

It’s for the contented, not those who lack contentment. ^an8-30-3-4

It’s for the secluded, not those who enjoy company. ^an8-30-3-5

It’s for the energetic, not the lazy. ^an8-30-3-6

It’s for the mindful, not the unmindful. ^an8-30-3-7

It’s for those with immersion, not those without immersion. ^an8-30-3-8

It’s for the wise, not the witless.’ ^an8-30-3-9

Well then, Anuruddha, you should also reflect on the following eighth thought of a great man: ^an8-30-3-10

‘This teaching is for those who don’t enjoy proliferating and don’t like to proliferate, not for those who enjoy proliferating and like to proliferate.’ ^an8-30-3-11

First you’ll reflect on these eight thoughts of a great man. Then as far as you want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, you’ll enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an8-30-4-1

You’ll enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an8-30-5-1

You’ll enter and remain in the third absorption, where you’ll meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an8-30-6-1

With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, you’ll enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an8-30-7-1

First you’ll reflect on these eight thoughts of a great man, and you’ll get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when you want, without trouble or difficulty. Then as you live contented, your rag robe will seem to you like a chest full of garments of different colors seems to a householder or householder’s child. ^an8-30-8-1

It will be for your enjoyment, relief, and comfort, and for alighting upon extinguishment. ^an8-30-8-2

As you live contented, your scraps of almsfood will seem to you like boiled fine rice with the dark grains picked out, served with many soups and sauces seems to a householder or householder’s child. ^an8-30-9-1

It will be for your enjoyment, relief, and comfort, and for alighting upon extinguishment. ^an8-30-9-2

As you live contented, your lodging at the root of a tree will seem to you like a bungalow, plastered inside and out, draft-free, with door fastened and window shuttered seems to a householder or householder’s child. ^an8-30-10-1

It will be for your enjoyment, relief, and comfort, and for alighting upon extinguishment. ^an8-30-10-2

As you live contented, your grass mat for lodging will seem to you like a couch spread with woolen covers—shag-piled, pure white, or embroidered with flowers—and spread with a fine deer hide, with a canopy above and red pillows at both ends seems to a householder or householder’s child. ^an8-30-11-1

It will be for your enjoyment, relief, and comfort, and for alighting upon extinguishment. ^an8-30-11-2

As you live contented your rancid urine as medicine will seem to you like various medicines—ghee, butter, oil, honey, and molasses—seem to a householder or householder’s child. ^an8-30-12-1

It will be for your enjoyment, relief, and comfort, and for alighting upon extinguishment. ^an8-30-12-2

Well then, Anuruddha, for the next rainy season residence you should stay right here in the land of the Cetīs in the Park of the East-Raftered Hall.” ^an8-30-12-3

“Yes, sir,” Anuruddha replied. ^an8-30-12-4

After advising Anuruddha like this, the Buddha—as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from the Park of the East-Raftered Hall in the land of the Cetīs and reappeared in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow. ^an8-30-13-1

He sat on the seat spread out ^an8-30-13-2

and addressed the mendicants: ^an8-30-13-3

“Mendicants, I will teach you the eight thoughts of a great man. Listen … ^an8-30-13-4

And what are the eight thoughts of a great man? ^an8-30-13-5

This teaching is for those of few wishes, not those of many wishes. ^an8-30-13-6

It’s for the contented, not those who lack contentment. ^an8-30-13-7

It’s for the secluded, not those who enjoy company. ^an8-30-13-8

It’s for the energetic, not the lazy. ^an8-30-13-9

It’s for the mindful, not the unmindful. ^an8-30-13-10

It’s for those with immersion, not those without immersion. ^an8-30-13-11

It’s for the wise, not the witless. ^an8-30-13-12

It’s for those who don’t enjoy proliferating and don’t like to proliferate, not for those who enjoy proliferating and like to proliferate. ^an8-30-13-13

‘This teaching is for those of few wishes, not those of many wishes.’ ^an8-30-14-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-14-2

A mendicant with few wishes doesn’t wish: ‘May they know me as having few wishes!’ When contented, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as contented!’ When secluded, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as secluded!’ When energetic, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as energetic!’ When mindful, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as mindful!’ When immersed, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as immersed!’ When wise, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as wise!’ When not enjoying proliferation, they don’t wish: ‘May they know me as one who doesn’t enjoy proliferating!’ ^an8-30-14-3

‘This teaching is for those of few wishes, not those of many wishes.’ ^an8-30-14-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-14-5

‘This teaching is for the contented, not those who lack contentment.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-15-1

It’s for a mendicant who’s content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an8-30-15-2

‘This teaching is for the contented, not those who lack contentment.’ ^an8-30-15-3

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-15-4

‘This teaching is for the secluded, not those who enjoy company.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-16-1

It’s for a mendicant who lives secluded. But monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen, rulers and their chief ministers, monastics of other religions and their disciples go to visit them. ^an8-30-16-2

With a mind slanting, sloping, and inclining to seclusion, withdrawn, and loving renunciation, that mendicant invariably gives a talk concerned only with sending them away. ^an8-30-16-3

‘This teaching is for the secluded, not those who enjoy company.’ ^an8-30-16-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-16-5

‘This teaching is for the energetic, not the lazy.’ That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-17-1

It’s for a mendicant who lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an8-30-17-2

‘This teaching is for the energetic, not the lazy.’ ^an8-30-17-3

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-17-4

‘This teaching is for the mindful, not the unmindful.’ ^an8-30-18-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-18-2

It’s for a mendicant who’s mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. ^an8-30-18-3

‘This teaching is for the mindful, not the unmindful.’ ^an8-30-18-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-18-5

‘This teaching is for those with immersion, not those without immersion.’ ^an8-30-19-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-19-2

It’s for a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption. ^an8-30-19-3

‘This teaching is for those with immersion, not those without immersion.’ ^an8-30-19-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-19-5

‘This teaching is for the wise, not the witless.’ ^an8-30-20-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-20-2

It’s for a mendicant who’s wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-30-20-3

‘This teaching is for the wise, not the witless.’ ^an8-30-20-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an8-30-20-5

‘This teaching is for those who don’t enjoy proliferating and don’t like to proliferate, not for those who enjoy proliferating and like to proliferate.’ ^an8-30-21-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an8-30-21-2

It’s for a mendicant whose mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided regarding the cessation of proliferation. ^an8-30-21-3

‘This teaching is for those who don’t enjoy proliferating and don’t like to proliferate, not for those who enjoy proliferating and like to proliferate.’ ^an8-30-21-4

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an8-30-21-5

Then Anuruddha stayed the next rainy season residence right there in the land of the Cetīs in the Park of the East-Raftered Hall. ^an8-30-22-1

And Anuruddha, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an8-30-22-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an8-30-22-3

And Venerable Anuruddha became one of the perfected. ^an8-30-22-4

And on the occasion of attaining perfection he recited these verses: ^an8-30-22-5

“Knowing my thoughts, ^an8-30-23-1

the supreme Teacher in the world ^an8-30-23-2

came to me in a mind-made body, ^an8-30-23-3

using his psychic power. ^an8-30-23-4

He taught me more ^an8-30-24-1

than I had thought of. ^an8-30-24-2

The Buddha who loves non-proliferation ^an8-30-24-3

taught me non-proliferation. ^an8-30-24-4

Understanding that teaching, ^an8-30-25-1

I happily did his bidding. ^an8-30-25-2

I’ve attained the three knowledges, ^an8-30-25-3

and have fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.” ^an8-30-25-4

### Giving (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.31) ^an8-31-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight gifts. ^an8-31-1-1

What eight? ^an8-31-1-2

Someone might give a gift after insulting the recipient. ^an8-31-1-3

Or they give out of fear. ^an8-31-1-4

Or they give thinking, ‘They gave to me.’ ^an8-31-1-5

Or they give thinking, ‘They’ll give to me.’ ^an8-31-1-6

Or they give thinking, ‘It’s good to give.’ ^an8-31-1-7

Or they give thinking, ‘I cook, they don’t. It wouldn’t be right for me to not give to them.’ ^an8-31-1-8

Or they give thinking, ‘By giving this gift I’ll get a good reputation.’ ^an8-31-1-9

Or they give thinking, ‘This is an adornment and requisite for the mind.’ ^an8-31-1-10

These are the eight gifts.” ^an8-31-1-11

### Giving (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.32) ^an8-32-0

“Faith, conscience, and skillful giving ^an8-32-1-1

are qualities true persons follow. ^an8-32-1-2

For this, they say, is the path of the gods, ^an8-32-1-3

which leads to the heavenly realm.” ^an8-32-1-4

### Reasons to Give (Numbered Discourses 8.33) ^an8-33-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight grounds for giving. ^an8-33-1-1

What eight? ^an8-33-1-2

Someone might give a gift out of favoritism or hostility or stupidity or cowardice. Or they give thinking, ‘Giving was practiced by my father and my father’s father. It would not be right for me to abandon this family tradition.’ Or they give thinking, ‘After I’ve given this gift, when my body breaks up, after death, I’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ Or they give thinking, ‘When giving this gift my mind becomes clear, and I become happy and joyful.’ Or they give a gift thinking, ‘This is an adornment and requisite for the mind.’ ^an8-33-1-3

These are the eight grounds for giving.” ^an8-33-1-4

### A Field (Numbered Discourses 8.34) ^an8-34-0

“Mendicants, when a field has eight factors a seed sown in it is not very fruitful or rewarding or productive. ^an8-34-1-1

What eight factors does it have? ^an8-34-1-2

It’s when a field has mounds and ditches. It has stones and gravel. It’s salty. It doesn’t have deep furrows. And it’s not equipped with water inlets, water outlets, irrigation channels, and boundaries. ^an8-34-1-3

When a field has these eight factors a seed sown in it is not fruitful or rewarding or productive. ^an8-34-1-4

In the same way, when an ascetic or brahmin has eight factors a gift given to them is not very fruitful or beneficial or brilliant or bountiful. ^an8-34-2-1

What eight factors do they have? ^an8-34-2-2

It’s when an ascetic or brahmin has wrong view, wrong purpose, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong immersion. ^an8-34-2-3

When an ascetic or brahmin has these eight factors a gift given to them is not very fruitful or beneficial or brilliant or bountiful. ^an8-34-2-4

When a field has eight factors a seed sown in it is very fruitful and rewarding and productive. ^an8-34-3-1

What eight factors does it have? ^an8-34-3-2

It’s when a field doesn’t have mounds and ditches. It doesn’t have stones and gravel. It’s not salty. It has deep furrows. And it’s equipped with water inlets, water outlets, irrigation channels, and boundaries. ^an8-34-3-3

When a field has these eight factors a seed sown in it is very fruitful and rewarding and productive. ^an8-34-3-4

In the same way, when an ascetic or brahmin has eight factors a gift given to them is very fruitful and beneficial and brilliant and bountiful. ^an8-34-4-1

What eight factors do they have? ^an8-34-4-2

It’s when an ascetic or brahmin has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an8-34-4-3

When an ascetic or brahmin has these eight factors a gift given to them is very fruitful and beneficial and brilliant and bountiful. ^an8-34-4-4

When the field is excellent, ^an8-34-5-1

and the seed sown in it is excellent, ^an8-34-5-2

and the rainfall is excellent, ^an8-34-5-3

the crop of grain will be excellent. ^an8-34-5-4

The absence of crop loss is excellent, ^an8-34-6-1

its growth is excellent, ^an8-34-6-2

its maturation is excellent, ^an8-34-6-3

and its fruit is excellent. ^an8-34-6-4

So too, when you give excellent food ^an8-34-7-1

to those of excellent ethics, ^an8-34-7-2

it leads to many excellences, ^an8-34-7-3

for what you did was excellent. ^an8-34-7-4

So if a person wants excellence, ^an8-34-8-1

let them excel in this. ^an8-34-8-2

You should frequent those with excellent wisdom, ^an8-34-8-3

so that your own excellence will flourish. ^an8-34-8-4

Excelling in knowledge and conduct, ^an8-34-9-1

and having excellence of mind, ^an8-34-9-2

you perform excellent deeds, ^an8-34-9-3

and gain excellent benefits. ^an8-34-9-4

Truly knowing the world, ^an8-34-10-1

and having attained excellence of view, ^an8-34-10-2

one who excels in mind proceeds, ^an8-34-10-3

relying on excellence in the path. ^an8-34-10-4

Shaking off all stains, ^an8-34-11-1

and attaining the excellence of extinguishment, ^an8-34-11-2

you’re freed from all sufferings: ^an8-34-11-3

this is complete excellence.” ^an8-34-11-4

### Rebirth by Giving (Numbered Discourses 8.35) ^an8-35-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight rebirths by giving. ^an8-35-1-1

What eight? ^an8-35-1-2

First, someone gives to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an8-35-1-3

Whatever they give they expect back. ^an8-35-1-4

They see a well-to-do aristocrat or brahmin or householder amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an8-35-1-5

It occurs to them: ^an8-35-1-6

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of well-to-do aristocrats or brahmins or householders!’ ^an8-35-1-7

They settle on that thought, stabilize it, and develop it. ^an8-35-1-8

As they’ve settled for less and not developed further, their thought leads to rebirth there. ^an8-35-1-9

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of well-to-do aristocrats or brahmins or householders. ^an8-35-1-10

But I say that this is only for those of ethical conduct, not for the unethical. ^an8-35-1-11

The heart’s wish of an ethical person succeeds because of their purity. ^an8-35-1-12

Next, someone gives to ascetics or brahmins … ^an8-35-2-1

Whatever they give they expect back. ^an8-35-2-2

And they’ve heard: ^an8-35-2-3

‘The gods of the four great kings are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^an8-35-2-4

It occurs to them: ^an8-35-2-5

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings!’ … ^an8-35-2-6

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-35-2-9

But I say that this is only for those of ethical conduct, not for the unethical. ^an8-35-2-10

The heart’s wish of an ethical person succeeds because of their purity. ^an8-35-2-11

Next, someone gives to ascetics or brahmins … ^an8-35-3-1

Whatever they give they expect back. ^an8-35-3-2

And they’ve heard: ^an8-35-3-3

‘The gods of the thirty-three …’ ^an8-35-3-4

‘The gods of Yama …’ ^an8-35-3-5

‘The joyful gods …’ ^an8-35-3-6

‘The gods who love to create …’ ^an8-35-3-7

‘The gods who control what is created by others are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^an8-35-3-8

It occurs to them: ^an8-35-3-9

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others!’ ^an8-35-3-10

They settle on that thought, stabilize it, and develop it. ^an8-35-3-11

As they’ve settled for less and not developed further, their thought leads to rebirth there. ^an8-35-3-12

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-35-3-13

But I say that this is only for those of ethical conduct, not for the unethical. ^an8-35-3-14

The heart’s wish of an ethical person succeeds because of their purity. ^an8-35-3-15

Next, someone gives to ascetics or brahmins such things as food, drink, clothing, vehicles; garlands, fragrance, and makeup; and bed, house, and lighting. ^an8-35-4-1

Whatever they give they expect back. ^an8-35-4-2

And they’ve heard: ^an8-35-4-3

‘The gods of the Divinity’s host are long-lived, beautiful, and very happy.’ ^an8-35-4-4

It occurs to them: ^an8-35-4-5

‘If only, when my body breaks up, after death, I would be reborn in the company of the gods of the Divinity’s host!’ ^an8-35-4-6

They settle on that thought, stabilize it, and develop it. ^an8-35-4-7

As they’ve settled for less and not developed further, their thought leads to rebirth there. ^an8-35-4-8

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the Divinity’s host. ^an8-35-4-9

But I say that this is only for those of ethical conduct, not for the unethical. ^an8-35-4-10

And for those free of desire, not those with desire. ^an8-35-4-11

The heart’s wish of an ethical person succeeds because of their freedom from desire. ^an8-35-4-12

These are the eight rebirths by giving.” ^an8-35-4-13

### Grounds for Making Merit (Numbered Discourses 8.36) ^an8-36-0

“Mendicants, there are these three grounds for making merit. ^an8-36-1-1

What three? ^an8-36-1-2

Giving, ethical conduct, and meditation are all grounds for making merit. ^an8-36-1-3

First, someone has practiced a little giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-2-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn among disadvantaged humans. ^an8-36-2-2

Next, someone has practiced a moderate amount of giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-3-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn among well-off humans. ^an8-36-3-2

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-4-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-36-4-2

There, the four great kings themselves have practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So they surpass them in ten respects: ^an8-36-4-3

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, sovereignty, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an8-36-4-4

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-5-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of the thirty-three. ^an8-36-5-2

There, Sakka, lord of gods, has practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So he surpasses them in ten respects … ^an8-36-5-3

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-6-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods of Yama. ^an8-36-6-2

There, the godling Suyāma has practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So he surpasses them in ten respects … ^an8-36-6-3

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-7-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the joyful gods. ^an8-36-7-2

There, the godling Santusita has practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So he surpasses them in ten respects … ^an8-36-7-3

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-8-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods who love to create. ^an8-36-8-2

There, the godling Sunimmita has practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So he surpasses them in ten respects … ^an8-36-8-3

Next, someone has practiced to an extraordinary degree giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit, but they haven’t got as far as meditation as a ground for making merit. ^an8-36-9-1

When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-36-9-2

There, the godling Vasavattī has practiced giving and ethical conduct as grounds for making merit to a greater degree than the other gods. So he surpasses them in ten respects: ^an8-36-9-3

heavenly lifespan, beauty, happiness, glory, sovereignty, sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches. ^an8-36-9-4

These are the three grounds for making merit.” ^an8-36-9-5

### Gifts of a True Person (Numbered Discourses 8.37) ^an8-37-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight gifts of a true person. ^an8-37-1-1

What eight? ^an8-37-1-2

Their gift is pure, good quality, timely, appropriate, intelligent, and regular. While giving their heart is confident, and afterwards they’re uplifted. ^an8-37-1-3

These are the eight gifts of a true person. ^an8-37-1-4

He gives pure, good quality, and timely gifts ^an8-37-2-1

of appropriate food and drinks ^an8-37-2-2

regularly to spiritual practitioners ^an8-37-2-3

who are fertile fields of merit. ^an8-37-2-4

They never regret ^an8-37-3-1

giving away many things of the flesh. ^an8-37-3-2

Discerning people praise ^an8-37-3-3

giving such gifts. ^an8-37-3-4

An intelligent person sacrifices like this, ^an8-37-4-1

faithful, with a mind of letting go. ^an8-37-4-2

Such an astute person is reborn ^an8-37-4-3

in a happy, pleasing world.” ^an8-37-4-4

### A True Person (Numbered Discourses 8.38) ^an8-38-0

“Mendicants, a true person is born in a family for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. ^an8-38-1-1

For the benefit, welfare, and happiness of mother and father; children and partners; bondservants, workers, and staff; friends and colleagues; departed ancestors; the king; the deities; and ascetics and brahmins. ^an8-38-1-2

It’s like a great rain cloud, which nourishes all the crops for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. ^an8-38-2-1

In the same way, a true person is born in a family for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of the people. … ^an8-38-2-2

A wise person living at home ^an8-38-3-1

benefits many people. ^an8-38-3-2

Neither by day or at night do they neglect ^an8-38-3-3

their mother, father, and ancestors. ^an8-38-3-4

They venerate them in accord with the teaching, ^an8-38-4-1

remembering what they have done. ^an8-38-4-2

One of settled faith and good nature ^an8-38-4-3

venerates the homeless renunciates, ^an8-38-4-4

the mendicant spiritual practitioners, ^an8-38-5-1

knowing their good-hearted qualities. ^an8-38-5-2

Good for the king, good for the gods, ^an8-38-5-3

and good for relatives and comrades. ^an8-38-5-4

In fact, they’re good for everyone, ^an8-38-6-1

well grounded in the true teaching. ^an8-38-6-2

Rid of the stain of stinginess, ^an8-38-6-3

they’ll enjoy a world of grace.” ^an8-38-6-4

### Overflowing Merit (Numbered Discourses 8.39) ^an8-39-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness. ^an8-39-1-1

What eight? ^an8-39-1-2

Firstly, a noble disciple has gone for refuge to the Buddha. ^an8-39-1-3

This is the first kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-1-4

Furthermore, a noble disciple has gone for refuge to the teaching. ^an8-39-2-1

This is the second kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-2-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple has gone for refuge to the Saṅgha. ^an8-39-3-1

This is the third kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-3-2

Mendicants, these five gifts are great, primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. They are uncorrupted, as they have been since the beginning. They’re not being corrupted now nor will they be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on them. ^an8-39-3-3

What five? ^an8-39-3-4

Firstly, a noble disciple gives up killing living creatures. ^an8-39-3-5

By so doing they give to countless sentient beings the gift of freedom from fear, enmity, and ill will. ^an8-39-3-6

And they themselves also enjoy unlimited freedom from fear, enmity, and ill will. ^an8-39-3-7

This is the first gift that is a great offering, primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. It is uncorrupted, as it has been since the beginning. It’s not being corrupted now nor will it be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on it. ^an8-39-3-8
