This is the fourth kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-3-9

Furthermore, a noble disciple gives up stealing. … ^an8-39-4-1

This is the fifth kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-4-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple gives up sexual misconduct. … ^an8-39-5-1

This is the sixth kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-5-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple gives up lying. … ^an8-39-6-1

This is the seventh kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-6-2

Furthermore, a noble disciple gives up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-39-7-1

By so doing they give to countless sentient beings the gift of freedom from fear, enmity, and ill will. ^an8-39-7-2

And they themselves also enjoy unlimited freedom from fear, enmity, and ill will. ^an8-39-7-3

This is the fifth gift that is a great offering, primordial, long-standing, traditional, and ancient. It is uncorrupted, as it has been since the beginning. It’s not being corrupted now nor will it be. Sensible ascetics and brahmins don’t look down on it. ^an8-39-7-4

This is the eighth kind of overflowing merit … ^an8-39-7-5

These are the eight kinds of overflowing merit, overflowing goodness. They nurture happiness and are conducive to heaven, ripening in happiness and leading to heaven. They lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, to welfare and happiness.” ^an8-39-8-1

### The Results of Misconduct (Numbered Discourses 8.40) ^an8-40-0

“Mendicants, the killing of living creatures, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-1-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is a short lifespan. ^an8-40-1-2

Stealing, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-2-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is loss of wealth. ^an8-40-2-2

Sexual misconduct, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-3-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is rivalry and enmity. ^an8-40-3-2

Lying, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-4-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is false accusations. ^an8-40-4-2

Backbiting, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-5-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is being divided against friends. ^an8-40-5-2

Harsh speech, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-6-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is hearing disagreeable things. ^an8-40-6-2

Talking nonsense, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-7-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is that no-one takes what you say seriously. ^an8-40-7-2

Drinking beer and wine, when cultivated, developed, and practiced, leads to hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. ^an8-40-8-1

The minimum result it leads to for a human being is madness.” ^an8-40-8-2

### The Sabbath With Eight Factors, In Brief (Numbered Discourses 8.41) ^an8-41-0

So I have heard. ^an8-41-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an8-41-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an8-41-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an8-41-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an8-41-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an8-41-1-6

“Mendicants, the observance of the sabbath with its eight factors is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-41-2-1

And how should it be observed? ^an8-41-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an8-41-2-3

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-41-2-4

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-41-2-5

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-2-6

This is its first factor. ^an8-41-2-7

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up stealing. They take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. They keep themselves clean by not thieving. ^an8-41-3-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up stealing. I’ll take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. I’ll keep myself clean by not thieving. ^an8-41-3-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-3-3

This is its second factor. ^an8-41-3-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up unchastity. They are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an8-41-4-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up unchastity. I will be chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an8-41-4-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-4-3

This is its third factor. ^an8-41-4-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up lying. They speak the truth and stick to the truth. They’re honest and dependable, and don’t trick the world with their words. ^an8-41-5-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up lying. I’ll speak the truth and stick to the truth. I’ll be honest and dependable, and won’t trick the world with my words. ^an8-41-5-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-5-3

This is its fourth factor. ^an8-41-5-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-41-6-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-41-6-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-6-3

This is its fifth factor. ^an8-41-6-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and from food at the wrong time. ^an8-41-7-1

I, too, for this day and night will eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and food at the wrong time. ^an8-41-7-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-7-3

This is its sixth factor. ^an8-41-7-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music ; and attiring and adorning themselves with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an8-41-8-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music ; and attiring and adorning myself with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an8-41-8-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-8-3

This is its seventh factor. ^an8-41-8-4

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-41-9-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-41-9-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-41-9-3

This is its eighth factor. ^an8-41-9-4

The observance of the sabbath with its eight factors in this way is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful.” ^an8-41-10-1

### The Sabbath With Eight Factors, In Detail (Numbered Discourses 8.42) ^an8-42-0

“Mendicants, the observance of the sabbath with its eight factors is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-42-1-1

And how should it be observed? ^an8-42-1-2

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an8-42-1-3

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-42-1-4

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-42-1-5

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-42-1-6

This is its first factor. … ^an8-42-1-7

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-42-2-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-42-2-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-42-2-3

This is its eighth factor. ^an8-42-2-4

The observance of the sabbath with its eight factors in this way is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-42-2-5

How much so? ^an8-42-3-1

Suppose you were to rule as sovereign lord over these sixteen great countries—Aṅga, Magadha, Kāsi, Kosala, Vajji, Malla, Cetī, Vaccha, Kuru, Pañcāla, Maccha, Sūrasena, Assaka, Avanti, Gandhāra, and Kamboja—full of the seven kinds of precious things. ^an8-42-3-2

This wouldn’t be worth a sixteenth part of the sabbath with its eight factors. ^an8-42-3-3

Why is that? ^an8-42-3-4

Because human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods. ^an8-42-3-5

Fifty years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-42-4-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-4-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-4-3

The lifespan of the gods of the four great kings is five hundred of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-4-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-42-4-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-4-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-4-7

A hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the thirty-three. ^an8-42-5-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-5-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-5-3

The lifespan of the gods of the thirty-three is a thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-5-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the thirty-three. ^an8-42-5-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-5-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-5-7

Two hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of Yama. ^an8-42-6-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-6-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-6-3

The lifespan of the gods of Yama is two thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-6-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of Yama. ^an8-42-6-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-6-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-6-7

Four hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the joyful gods. ^an8-42-7-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-7-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-7-3

The lifespan of the joyful gods is four thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-7-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the joyful gods. ^an8-42-7-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-7-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-7-7

Eight hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who love to create. ^an8-42-8-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-8-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-8-3

The life span of the gods who love to create is eight thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-8-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who love to create. ^an8-42-8-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-8-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-8-7

Sixteen hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-42-9-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-42-9-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-42-9-3

The life span of the gods who control what is created by others is sixteen thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-42-9-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-42-9-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-42-9-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-42-9-7

You shouldn’t kill living creatures, or steal, ^an8-42-10-1

or lie, or drink liquor. ^an8-42-10-2

Be chaste, refraining from sex, ^an8-42-10-3

and don’t eat at night, the wrong time. ^an8-42-10-4

Not wearing garlands or applying fragrance, ^an8-42-11-1

you should sleep on a low bed, <j>or a mat on the ground. ^an8-42-11-2

This is the eight-factored sabbath, they say, ^an8-42-11-3

explained by the Buddha, <j>who has gone to suffering’s end. ^an8-42-11-4

The moon and sun are both fair to see, ^an8-42-12-1

radiating as far as they revolve. ^an8-42-12-2

Those dispellers of darkness, travellers in midair, ^an8-42-12-3

shine in the firmament, lighting up the quarters. ^an8-42-12-4

All of the wealth that’s found in this realm— ^an8-42-13-1

pearls, gems, fine beryl too, ^an8-42-13-2

mountain gold or lustrous gold, ^an8-42-13-3

or the native gold called ‘coruscant’— ^an8-42-13-4

they’re not worth a sixteenth part ^an8-42-14-1

of the sabbath with its eight factors, ^an8-42-14-2

as starlight cannot rival the moon. ^an8-42-14-3

So an ethical woman or man, ^an8-42-15-1

who has observed the eight-factored sabbath, ^an8-42-15-2

having made merit whose outcome is happiness, ^an8-42-15-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an8-42-15-4

### With Visākhā on the Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 8.43) ^an8-43-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an8-43-1-1

Then Visākhā, Migāra’s mother, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to her: ^an8-43-1-2

“Visākhā, the observance of the sabbath with its eight factors is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-43-1-3

And how should it be observed? ^an8-43-1-4

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an8-43-1-5

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-43-1-6

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-43-1-7

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-43-1-8

This is its first factor. … ^an8-43-1-9

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-43-2-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-43-2-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-43-2-3

This is its eighth factor. ^an8-43-2-4

The observance of the sabbath with its eight factors in this way is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-43-2-5

How much so? ^an8-43-3-1

Suppose you were to rule as sovereign lord over these sixteen great countries—Aṅga, Magadha, Kāsi, Kosala, Vajji, Malla, Cetī, Vaccha, Kuru, Pañcāla, Maccha, Sūrasena, Assaka, Avanti, Gandhāra, and Kamboja—full of the seven kinds of precious things. ^an8-43-3-2

This wouldn’t be worth a sixteenth part of the sabbath with its eight factors. ^an8-43-3-3

Why is that? ^an8-43-3-4

Because human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods. ^an8-43-3-5

Fifty years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-43-4-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-43-4-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-43-4-3

The lifespan of the gods of the four great kings is five hundred of these heavenly years. ^an8-43-4-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-43-4-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-43-4-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-43-4-7

A hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the thirty-three. ^an8-43-5-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-43-5-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-43-5-3

The lifespan of the gods of the thirty-three is a thousand of these divine years. ^an8-43-5-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the thirty-three. ^an8-43-5-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-43-5-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-43-5-7

Two hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-43-6-1

Four hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-43-6-2

Eight hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-43-6-3

Sixteen hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-43-6-4

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-43-6-5

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-43-6-6

The life span of the gods who control what is created by others is sixteen thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-43-6-7

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-43-6-8

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-43-6-9

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-43-6-10

You shouldn’t kill living creatures, or steal, ^an8-43-7-1

or lie, or drink liquor. ^an8-43-7-2

Be chaste, refraining from sex, ^an8-43-7-3

and don’t eat at night, the wrong time. ^an8-43-7-4

Not wearing garlands or applying fragrance, ^an8-43-8-1

you should sleep on a low bed, <j>or a mat on the ground. ^an8-43-8-2

This is the eight-factored sabbath, they say, ^an8-43-8-3

explained by the Buddha, <j>who has gone to suffering’s end. ^an8-43-8-4

The moon and sun are both fair to see, ^an8-43-9-1

radiating as far as they revolve. ^an8-43-9-2

Those dispellers of darkness, travellers in midair, ^an8-43-9-3

shine in the firmament, lighting up the quarters. ^an8-43-9-4

All of the wealth that’s found in this realm— ^an8-43-10-1

pearls, gems, fine beryl too, ^an8-43-10-2

mountain gold or lustrous gold, ^an8-43-10-3

or the native gold called ‘coruscant’— ^an8-43-10-4

they’re not worth a sixteenth part ^an8-43-11-1

of the sabbath with its eight factors, ^an8-43-11-2

as starlight cannot rival the moon. ^an8-43-11-3

So an ethical woman or man, ^an8-43-12-1

who has observed the eight-factored sabbath, ^an8-43-12-2

having made merit whose outcome is happiness, ^an8-43-12-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an8-43-12-4

### With Vāseṭṭha on the Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 8.44) ^an8-44-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-44-1-1

Then the layman Vāseṭṭha went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an8-44-1-2

“Vāseṭṭha, the observance of the sabbath with its eight factors is very fruitful and beneficial and brilliant and bountiful … ^an8-44-1-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an8-44-1-4

When he said this, Vāseṭṭha said to the Buddha: ^an8-44-2-1

“If my loved ones—relatives and kin—were to observe this sabbath with its eight factors, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an8-44-2-2

If all the aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials were to observe this sabbath with its eight factors, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.” ^an8-44-2-3

“That’s so true, Vāseṭṭha! That’s so true, Vāseṭṭha! ^an8-44-3-1

If all the aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials were to observe this sabbath with its eight factors, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an8-44-3-2

If the whole world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—were to observe this sabbath with its eight factors, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness. ^an8-44-3-3

If these great sal trees were to observe this sabbath with its eight factors, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness—if they were sentient. ^an8-44-3-4

How much more then a human being!” ^an8-44-3-5

### With Bojjhā on the Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 8.45) ^an8-45-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an8-45-1-1

Then the laywoman Bojjhā went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to her: ^an8-45-1-2

“Bojjhā, the observance of the sabbath with its eight factors is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-45-2-1

And how should it be observed? ^an8-45-2-2

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an8-45-2-3

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-45-2-4

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an8-45-2-5

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-45-2-6

This is its first factor. … ^an8-45-2-7

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-45-3-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an8-45-3-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an8-45-3-3

This is its eighth factor. ^an8-45-3-4

The observance of the sabbath with its eight factors in this way is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an8-45-3-5

How much so? ^an8-45-4-1

Suppose you were to rule as sovereign lord over these sixteen great countries—Aṅga, Magadha, Kāsi, Kosala, Vajji, Malla, Cetī, Vaccha, Kuru, Pañcāla, Maccha, Sūrasena, Assaka, Avanti, Gandhāra, and Kamboja—full of the seven kinds of precious things. ^an8-45-4-2

This wouldn’t be worth a sixteenth part of the sabbath with its eight factors. ^an8-45-4-3

Why is that? ^an8-45-4-4

Because human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods. ^an8-45-4-5

Fifty years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-45-5-1

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-45-5-2

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-45-5-3

The lifespan of the gods of the four great kings is five hundred of these heavenly years. ^an8-45-5-4

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods of the four great kings. ^an8-45-5-5

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-45-5-6

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-45-5-7

A hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-45-6-1

Two hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-45-6-2

Four hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-45-6-3

Eight hundred years in the human realm … ^an8-45-6-4

Sixteen hundred years in the human realm is one day and night for the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-45-6-5

Thirty such days make up a month. ^an8-45-6-6

Twelve such months make up a year. ^an8-45-6-7

The life span of the gods who Control what is created by Others is sixteen thousand of these heavenly years. ^an8-45-6-8

It’s possible that some woman or man who has observed the eight-factored sabbath will—when their body breaks up, after death—be reborn in the company of the gods who control what is created by others. ^an8-45-6-9

This is what I was referring to when I said: ^an8-45-6-10

‘Human kingship is a poor thing compared to the happiness of the gods.’ ^an8-45-6-11

You shouldn’t kill living creatures, or steal, ^an8-45-7-1

or lie, or drink liquor. ^an8-45-7-2

Be chaste, refraining from sex, ^an8-45-7-3

and don’t eat at night, the wrong time. ^an8-45-7-4

Not wearing garlands or applying fragrance, ^an8-45-8-1

you should sleep on a low bed, <j>or a mat on the ground. ^an8-45-8-2

This is the eight-factored sabbath, they say, ^an8-45-8-3

explained by the Buddha, <j>who has gone to suffering’s end. ^an8-45-8-4

The moon and sun are both fair to see, ^an8-45-9-1

radiating as far as they revolve. ^an8-45-9-2

Those dispellers of darkness, travellers in midair, ^an8-45-9-3

shine in the firmament, lighting up the quarters. ^an8-45-9-4

All of the wealth that’s found in this realm— ^an8-45-10-1

pearls, gems, fine beryl too, ^an8-45-10-2

mountain gold or pure gold, ^an8-45-10-3

or the native gold called ‘coruscant’— ^an8-45-10-4

they’re not worth a sixteenth part ^an8-45-11-1

of the mind developed with love, ^an8-45-11-2

as starlight cannot rival the moon. ^an8-45-11-3

So an ethical woman or man, ^an8-45-12-1

who has observed the eight-factored sabbath, ^an8-45-12-2

having made merit whose outcome is happiness, ^an8-45-12-3

blameless, they go to a heavenly place.” ^an8-45-12-4

### Anuruddha and the Agreeable Deities (Numbered Discourses 8.46) ^an8-46-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an8-46-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Anuruddha had gone into retreat for the day’s meditation. ^an8-46-1-2

Then several deities of the Agreeable Host went up to Venerable Anuruddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an8-46-1-3

“Honorable Anuruddha, we are the deities called ‘Agreeable’. We wield authority and control over three things. ^an8-46-1-4

We can turn any color we want on the spot. ^an8-46-1-5

We can get any voice that we want on the spot. ^an8-46-1-6

We can get any pleasure that we want on the spot. ^an8-46-1-7

We are the deities called ‘Agreeable’. We wield authority and control over these three things.” ^an8-46-1-8

Then Venerable Anuruddha thought, ^an8-46-2-1

“If only these deities would all turn blue, of blue color, clad in blue, adorned with blue!” ^an8-46-2-2

Then those deities, knowing Anuruddha’s thought, all turned blue. ^an8-46-2-3

Then Venerable Anuruddha thought, ^an8-46-3-1

“If only these deities would all turn yellow …” ^an8-46-3-2

“If only these gods would all turn red …” ^an8-46-3-3

“If only these gods would all turn white …” ^an8-46-3-4

Then those deities, knowing Anuruddha’s thought, all turned white. ^an8-46-3-5

Then one of those deities sang, one danced, and one snapped her fingers. ^an8-46-4-1

Suppose there was a quintet made up of skilled musicians who had practiced well and kept excellent rhythm. They’d sound graceful, tantalizing, sensuous, endearing, and intoxicating. ^an8-46-4-2

In the same way the performance by those deities sounded graceful, tantalizing, sensuous, endearing, and intoxicating. ^an8-46-4-3

But Venerable Anuruddha averted his senses. ^an8-46-4-4

Then those deities, thinking “Master Anuruddha isn’t enjoying this,” vanished right there. ^an8-46-5-1

Then in the late afternoon, Anuruddha came out of retreat and went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened, adding: ^an8-46-5-2

“How many qualities do ladies have so that—when their body breaks up, after death—they are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host?” ^an8-46-9-2

“Anuruddha, when they have eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-46-10-1

What eight? ^an8-46-10-2

Take the case of a lady whose mother and father give her to a husband wanting what’s best for her, out of kindness and sympathy. She would get up before him and go to bed after him, and be obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely. ^an8-46-10-3

She honors, respects, esteems, and venerates those her husband respects, such as ^an8-46-11-1

mother and father, and ascetics and brahmins. ^an8-46-11-2

And when they arrive she serves them with a seat and water. ^an8-46-11-3

She’s deft and tireless in her husband’s household duties, such as knitting and sewing. She understands how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-46-12-1

She knows what work her husband’s domestic bondservants, servants, and workers have completed, and what they’ve left incomplete. ^an8-46-13-1

She knows who is sick, and who is fit or unwell. She distributes to each a fair portion of fresh and cooked foods. ^an8-46-13-2

She ensures that any income her husband earns is guarded and protected, whether money, grain, silver, or gold. She doesn’t overspend, steal, waste, or lose it. ^an8-46-14-1

She’s a lay follower who has gone for refuge to the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an8-46-15-1

She’s ethical. ^an8-46-16-1

She doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-46-16-2

She’s generous. ^an8-46-17-1

She lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-46-17-2

When they possess these eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-46-18-1

She’d never look down on her husband, ^an8-46-19-1

who’s always eager to work hard, ^an8-46-19-2

always looking after her, ^an8-46-19-3

and bringing whatever she wants. ^an8-46-19-4

And a good woman never scolds her husband ^an8-46-20-1

with jealous words. ^an8-46-20-2

Being astute, she reveres ^an8-46-20-3

those respected by her husband. ^an8-46-20-4

She gets up early, works tirelessly, ^an8-46-21-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an8-46-21-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an8-46-21-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an8-46-21-4

A lady who fulfills these duties ^an8-46-22-1

according to her husband’s desire, ^an8-46-22-2

is reborn among the gods ^an8-46-22-3

called ‘Agreeable’.” ^an8-46-22-4

### With Visākhā on the Agreeable Gods (Numbered Discourses 8.47) ^an8-47-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an8-47-1-1

Then Visākhā, Migāra’s mother, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an8-47-1-2

The Buddha said to her: ^an8-47-1-3

“Visākhā, when they have eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-47-2-1

What eight? ^an8-47-2-2

Take the case of a lady whose mother and father give her to a husband wanting what’s best for her, out of kindness and sympathy. She would get up before him and go to bed after him, and be obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely. … ^an8-47-2-3

She’s generous. ^an8-47-3-1

She lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-47-3-2

When they possess these eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-47-3-3

She’d never look down on her husband, ^an8-47-4-1

who’s always eager to work hard, ^an8-47-4-2

always looking after her, ^an8-47-4-3

and bringing whatever she wants. ^an8-47-4-4

And a good woman never scolds her husband ^an8-47-5-1

with jealous words. ^an8-47-5-2

Being astute, she reveres ^an8-47-5-3

those respected by her husband. ^an8-47-5-4

She gets up early, works tirelessly, ^an8-47-6-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an8-47-6-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an8-47-6-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an8-47-6-4

A lady who fulfills these duties ^an8-47-7-1

according to her husband’s desire, ^an8-47-7-2

is reborn among the gods ^an8-47-7-3

called ‘Agreeable’.” ^an8-47-7-4

### With Nakula’s Mother on the Agreeable Gods (Numbered Discourses 8.48) ^an8-48-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood. ^an8-48-1-1

Then the housewife Nakula’s mother went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to her: ^an8-48-1-2

“Nakula’s mother, when they have eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-48-2-1

What eight? ^an8-48-2-2

Take the case of a lady whose mother and father give her to a husband wanting what’s best for her, out of kindness and sympathy. She would get up before him and go to bed after him, and be obliging, behaving nicely and speaking politely. ^an8-48-2-3

She honors, respects, esteems, and venerates those her husband respects, such as ^an8-48-3-1

mother and father, and ascetics and brahmins. ^an8-48-3-2

And when they arrive she serves them with a seat and water. ^an8-48-3-3

She’s deft and tireless in her husband’s household duties, such as knitting and sewing. She understands how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-48-4-1

She knows what work her husband’s domestic bondservants, servants, and workers have completed, and what they’ve left incomplete. ^an8-48-5-1

She knows who is sick, and who is fit or unwell. She distributes to each a fair portion of fresh and cooked foods. ^an8-48-5-2

She ensures that any income her husband earns is guarded and protected, whether money, grain, silver, or gold. She doesn’t overspend, steal, waste, or lose it. ^an8-48-6-1

She’s a lay follower who has gone for refuge to the Buddha, his teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an8-48-7-1

She’s ethical. ^an8-48-8-1

She doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-48-8-2

She’s generous. She lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-48-9-1

When they possess these eight qualities ladies—when their body breaks up, after death—are reborn in company with the Gods of the Agreeable Host. ^an8-48-10-1

She’d never look down on her husband, ^an8-48-11-1

who’s always eager to work hard, ^an8-48-11-2

always looking after her, ^an8-48-11-3

and bringing whatever she wants. ^an8-48-11-4

And a good woman never scolds her husband ^an8-48-12-1

with jealous words. ^an8-48-12-2

Being astute, she reveres ^an8-48-12-3

those respected by her husband. ^an8-48-12-4

She gets up early, works tirelessly, ^an8-48-13-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an8-48-13-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an8-48-13-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an8-48-13-4

A lady who fulfills these duties ^an8-48-14-1

according to her husband’s desire, ^an8-48-14-2

is reborn among the gods ^an8-48-14-3

called ‘Agreeable’.” ^an8-48-14-4

### Winning in This Life (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.49) ^an8-49-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the stilt longhouse of Migāra’s mother in the Eastern Monastery. ^an8-49-1-1

Then Visākhā, Migāra’s mother, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. ^an8-49-1-2

The Buddha said to her: ^an8-49-1-3

“Visākhā, a lady who has four qualities is practicing to win in this life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-49-2-1

What four? ^an8-49-2-2

It’s when a lady is well-organized at work, manages the domestic help, acts lovingly toward her husband, and preserves his earnings. ^an8-49-2-3

And how is a lady well-organized at work? ^an8-49-3-1

It’s when she’s deft and tireless in doing domestic duties for her husband, such as knitting and sewing. She understands how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-49-3-2

That’s how a lady is well-organized at work. ^an8-49-3-3

And how does a lady manage the domestic help? ^an8-49-4-1

It’s when she knows what work her husband’s domestic bondservants, servants, and workers have completed, and what they’ve left incomplete. ^an8-49-4-2

She knows who is sick, and who is fit or unwell. She distributes to each a fair portion of fresh and cooked foods. ^an8-49-4-3

That’s how a lady manages the domestic help. ^an8-49-4-4

And how does a lady act lovingly toward her husband? ^an8-49-5-1

It’s when a lady would not transgress in any way that her husband would not consider agreeable, even for the sake of her own life. ^an8-49-5-2

That’s how a lady acts lovingly toward her husband. ^an8-49-5-3

And how does a lady preserve his earnings? ^an8-49-6-1

It’s when she ensures that any income her husband earns is guarded and protected, whether money, grain, silver, or gold. She doesn’t overspend, steal, waste, or lose it. ^an8-49-6-2

That’s how a lady preserves his earnings. ^an8-49-6-3

A lady who possesses these four qualities is practicing to win in this life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-49-6-4

A lady who has four qualities is practicing to win in the next life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-49-7-1

What four? ^an8-49-7-2

It’s when a lady is accomplished in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-49-7-3

And how is a lady accomplished in faith? ^an8-49-8-1

It’s when a lady has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an8-49-8-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an8-49-8-3

That’s how a lady is accomplished in faith. ^an8-49-8-4

And how is a lady accomplished in ethics? ^an8-49-9-1

It’s when a lady doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-49-9-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in ethics. ^an8-49-9-3

And how is a lady accomplished in generosity? ^an8-49-10-1

It’s when she lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-49-10-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in generosity. ^an8-49-10-3

And how is a lady accomplished in wisdom? ^an8-49-11-1

It’s when a lady is wise. She has the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-49-11-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in wisdom. ^an8-49-11-3

A lady who possesses these four qualities is practicing to win in the next life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-49-12-1

She’s organized at work, ^an8-49-13-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an8-49-13-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an8-49-13-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an8-49-13-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-49-14-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an8-49-14-2

she always purifies the path ^an8-49-14-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-49-14-4

And so, a lady in whom ^an8-49-15-1

these eight qualities are found ^an8-49-15-2

is known as virtuous, ^an8-49-15-3

firmly principled, and truthful. ^an8-49-15-4

Accomplished in sixteen respects, ^an8-49-16-1

complete with the eight factors, ^an8-49-16-2

a virtuous laywoman such as she ^an8-49-16-3

is reborn in the realm of the Agreeable Gods.” ^an8-49-16-4

### Winning in This Life (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.50) ^an8-50-0

“Mendicants, a lady who has four qualities is practicing to win in this life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-50-1-1

What four? ^an8-50-1-2

It’s when a lady is well-organized at work, manages the domestic help, acts lovingly toward her husband, and preserves his earnings. ^an8-50-1-3

And how is a lady well-organized at work? ^an8-50-2-1

It’s when she’s skilled and tireless in doing domestic duties for her husband … ^an8-50-2-2

That’s how a lady is well-organized at work. ^an8-50-2-3

And how does a lady manage the domestic help? ^an8-50-3-1

It’s when she knows what work her husband’s domestic bondservants, servants, and workers have completed, and what they’ve left incomplete. She knows who is sick, and who is fit or unwell. She distributes to each a fair portion of fresh and cooked foods. ^an8-50-3-2

That’s how a lady manages the domestic help. ^an8-50-3-3

And how does a lady act lovingly toward her husband? ^an8-50-4-1

It’s when a lady would not transgress in any way that her husband would not consider agreeable, even for the sake of her own life. ^an8-50-4-2

That’s how a lady acts lovingly toward her husband. ^an8-50-4-3

And how does a lady preserve his earnings? ^an8-50-5-1

It’s when she tries to guard and protect any income her husband earns … ^an8-50-5-2

That’s how a lady preserves his earnings. ^an8-50-5-3

A lady who possesses these four qualities is practicing to win in this life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-50-5-4

A lady who has four qualities is practicing to win in the next life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-50-6-1

What four? ^an8-50-6-2

It’s when a lady is accomplished in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-50-6-3

And how is a lady accomplished in faith? ^an8-50-7-1

It’s when a lady has faith in the Realized One’s awakening … ^an8-50-7-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in faith. ^an8-50-7-3

And how is a lady accomplished in ethics? ^an8-50-8-1

It’s when a lady doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, ^an8-50-8-2

or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-50-8-3

That’s how a lady is accomplished in ethics. ^an8-50-8-4

And how is a lady accomplished in generosity? ^an8-50-9-1

It’s when she lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-50-9-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in generosity. ^an8-50-9-3

And how is a lady accomplished in wisdom? ^an8-50-10-1

It’s when a lady is wise. She has the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-50-10-2

That’s how a lady is accomplished in wisdom. ^an8-50-10-3

A lady who possesses these four qualities is practicing to win in the next life, and she succeeds at it. ^an8-50-10-4

She’s organized at work, ^an8-50-11-1

and manages the domestic help. ^an8-50-11-2

She’s agreeable to her husband, ^an8-50-11-3

and preserves his wealth. ^an8-50-11-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-50-12-1

being bountiful and rid of stinginess. ^an8-50-12-2

She always purifies the path ^an8-50-12-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-50-12-4

And so, a lady in whom ^an8-50-13-1

these eight qualities are found ^an8-50-13-2

is known as virtuous, ^an8-50-13-3

firmly principled, and truthful. ^an8-50-13-4

Accomplished in sixteen respects, ^an8-50-14-1

complete with the eight factors, ^an8-50-14-2

a virtuous laywoman such as she ^an8-50-14-3

is reborn in the realm of the Agreeable Gods.” ^an8-50-14-4

### With Gotamī (Numbered Discourses 8.51) ^an8-51-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an8-51-1-1

Then Mahāpajāpati Gotamī went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an8-51-1-2

“Sir, please let ladies gain the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-1-3

“Enough, Gotamī. ^an8-51-1-4

Don’t endorse the going forth for ladies from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-1-5

For a second time … ^an8-51-2-1

For a third time, Mahāpajāpati Gotamī said to the Buddha: ^an8-51-3-1

“Sir, please let ladies gain the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-3-2

“Enough, Gotamī. ^an8-51-3-3

Don’t endorse the going forth for ladies from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-3-4

Then Mahāpajāpati Gotamī thought, “The Buddha does not permit ladies to go forth.” Miserable and sad, weeping, with a tearful face, she bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on her right, before leaving. ^an8-51-4-1

When the Buddha had stayed in Kapilavatthu as long as he pleased, he set out for Vesālī. ^an8-51-5-1

Traveling stage by stage, he arrived at Vesālī, ^an8-51-5-2

where he stayed at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-51-5-3

Then Mahāpajāpati Gotamī had her hair shaved, and dressed in ocher robes. Together with several Sakyan ladies she set out for Vesālī. ^an8-51-5-4

Traveling stage by stage, she arrived at Vesālī and went to the Great Wood, the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-51-5-5

Then Mahāpajāpati Gotamī stood crying outside the gate, her feet swollen, her limbs covered with dust, miserable and sad, with tearful face. ^an8-51-5-6

Venerable Ānanda saw her standing there, ^an8-51-6-1

and said to her, ^an8-51-6-2

“Gotamī, why do you stand crying outside the gate, your feet swollen, your limbs covered with dust, miserable and sad, with tearful face?” ^an8-51-6-3

“Honorable Ānanda, it’s because the Buddha does not permit ladies to go forth in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-6-4

“Well then, Gotamī, wait here an hour while I ask the Buddha to grant the going forth for ladies.” ^an8-51-6-5

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an8-51-7-1

“Sir, Mahāpajāpati Gotamī is standing crying outside the gate, her feet swollen, her limbs covered with dust, miserable and sad, with tearful face. ^an8-51-7-2

She says that it’s because the Buddha does not permit ladies to go forth. ^an8-51-7-3

Sir, please let ladies gain the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-7-4

“Enough, Ānanda. ^an8-51-7-5

Don’t endorse the going forth for ladies from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-7-6

For a second time … ^an8-51-8-1

For a third time, Ānanda said to the Buddha: ^an8-51-8-2

“Sir, please let ladies gain the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-8-3

“Enough, Ānanda. ^an8-51-8-4

Don’t endorse the going forth for ladies from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-8-5

Then Venerable Ānanda thought, ^an8-51-9-1

“The Buddha does not permit ladies to go forth. ^an8-51-9-2

Why don’t I try another approach?” ^an8-51-9-3

Then Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha, ^an8-51-9-4

“Sir, is a lady able to realize the fruits of stream-entry, once-return, non-return, and perfection once she has gone forth?” ^an8-51-9-5

“She is able, Ānanda.” ^an8-51-9-6

“If a lady is able to realize the fruits of stream-entry, once-return, non-return, and perfection once she has gone forth. ^an8-51-9-7

Sir, Mahāpajāpati has been very helpful to the Buddha. As his aunt, she raised him, nurtured him, and gave him her milk. ^an8-51-9-8

When the Buddha’s birth mother passed away, she nurtured him at her own breast. ^an8-51-9-9

Sir, please let ladies gain the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.” ^an8-51-9-10

“Ānanda, if Mahāpajāpati Gotamī accepts these eight principles of respect, that will be her ordination. ^an8-51-10-1

A nun, even if she has been ordained for a hundred years, should bow down to a monk who was ordained that very day. She should rise up for him, greet him with cupped palms, and observe proper etiquette toward him. ^an8-51-11-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-11-2

A nun should not commence the rainy season residence in a monastery without monks. ^an8-51-12-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-12-2

Each fortnight the nuns should expect two things from the community of monks: ^an8-51-13-1

the date of the sabbath, and visiting for advice. ^an8-51-13-2

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-13-3

After completing the rainy season residence the nuns should invite admonition from the communities of both monks and nuns in regard to anything that was ^an8-51-14-1

seen, heard, or suspected. ^an8-51-14-2

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-14-3

A nun who has committed a grave offense should undergo penance in the communities of both monks and nuns for a fortnight. ^an8-51-15-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-15-2

A trainee nun who has trained in the six rules for two years should seek ordination from the communities of both monks and nuns. ^an8-51-16-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-16-2

A nun should not abuse or insult a monk in any way. ^an8-51-17-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-17-2

From this day forth it is forbidden for nuns to criticize monks, but it is not forbidden for monks to criticize nuns. ^an8-51-18-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-18-2

If Mahāpajāpati Gotamī accepts these eight principles of respect, that will be her ordination.” ^an8-51-19-1

Then Ānanda, having learned these eight principles of respect from the Buddha himself, went to Mahāpajāpati Gotamī and said: ^an8-51-20-1

“Gotamī, if you accept eight principles of respect, that will be your ordination. ^an8-51-21-1

A nun, even if she has been ordained for a hundred years, should bow down to a monk who was ordained that very day. She should rise up for him, greet him with cupped palms, and observe proper etiquette toward him. ^an8-51-22-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. … ^an8-51-22-2

From this day forth it is forbidden for nuns to criticize monks, but it is not forbidden for monks to criticize nuns. ^an8-51-23-1

This principle should be honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated, and not transgressed so long as life lasts. ^an8-51-23-2

If you accept these eight principles of respect, that will be your ordination.” ^an8-51-23-3

“Ānanda, suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and had bathed their head. After getting a garland of lotuses, jasmine, or liana flowers, they would take them in both hands and place them on the crown of the head. ^an8-51-24-1

In the same way, sir, I accept these eight principles of respect as not to be transgressed so long as life lasts.” ^an8-51-24-2

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an8-51-25-1

“Sir, Mahāpajāpati Gotamī has accepted the eight principles of respect as not to be transgressed so long as life lasts.” ^an8-51-25-2

“Ānanda, if ladies had not gained the going forth from the lay life to homelessness in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One, the spiritual life would have lasted long. The true teaching would have remained for a thousand years. ^an8-51-26-1

But since they have gained the going forth, now the spiritual life will not last long. ^an8-51-26-2

The true teaching will remain only five hundred years. ^an8-51-26-3

It’s like those families with many women and few men. They’re easy prey for bandits and grain-jar thieves. ^an8-51-27-1

In the same way, the spiritual life does not last long in a teaching and training where ladies gain the going forth. ^an8-51-27-2

It’s like a field full of rice. Once the disease called ‘white bones’ attacks, it doesn’t last long. ^an8-51-28-1

In the same way, the spiritual life does not last long in a teaching and training where ladies gain the going forth. ^an8-51-28-2

It’s like a field full of sugar cane. Once the disease called ‘red rot’ attacks, it doesn’t last long. ^an8-51-29-1

In the same way, the spiritual life does not last long in a teaching and training where ladies gain the going forth. ^an8-51-29-2

As a man might build a dyke around a large lake as a precaution against the water overflowing, ^an8-51-30-1

in the same way as a precaution I’ve prescribed the eight principles of respect as not to be transgressed so long as life lasts.” ^an8-51-30-2

### An Adviser for Nuns (Numbered Discourses 8.52) ^an8-52-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-52-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an8-52-1-2

“Sir, how many qualities should a monk have to be deemed an adviser for nuns?” ^an8-52-1-3

“Ānanda, a monk with eight qualities may be deemed an adviser for nuns. ^an8-52-2-1

What eight? ^an8-52-2-2

Firstly, a monk is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an8-52-2-3

They’re learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an8-52-2-4

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an8-52-2-5

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-52-2-6

They’re able to educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire the community of nuns. ^an8-52-2-7

They’re likable and agreeable to most of the nuns. ^an8-52-2-8

They have never previously sexually harassed any woman wearing the ocher robe who has gone forth in the Buddha’s name. ^an8-52-2-9

They have been ordained for twenty years or more. ^an8-52-2-10

A monk with these eight qualities may be deemed an adviser for nuns.” ^an8-52-2-11

### Brief Advice to Gotamī (Numbered Discourses 8.53) ^an8-53-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-53-1-1

Then Mahāpajāpati Gotamī went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him: ^an8-53-1-2

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^an8-53-2-1

“Gotamī, you might know that certain things ^an8-53-2-2

lead to passion, not dispassion; ^an8-53-2-3

to yoking, not to unyoking; ^an8-53-2-4

to accumulation, not dispersal; ^an8-53-2-5

to more desires, not fewer; ^an8-53-2-6

to lack of contentment, not contentment; ^an8-53-2-7

to crowding, not seclusion; ^an8-53-2-8

to laziness, not energy; ^an8-53-2-9

to being burdensome, not being unburdensome. ^an8-53-2-10

Categorically, you should remember these things as ^an8-53-2-11

not the teaching, not the training, and not the Teacher’s instructions. ^an8-53-2-12

You might know that certain things ^an8-53-3-1

lead to dispassion, not passion; ^an8-53-3-2

to unyoking, not to yoking; ^an8-53-3-3

to dispersal, not accumulation; ^an8-53-3-4

to fewer desires, not more; ^an8-53-3-5

to contentment, not lack of contentment; ^an8-53-3-6

to seclusion, not crowding; ^an8-53-3-7

to energy, not laziness; ^an8-53-3-8

to being unburdensome, not being burdensome. ^an8-53-3-9

Categorically, you should remember these things as ^an8-53-3-10

the teaching, the training, and the Teacher’s instructions.” ^an8-53-3-11

### With Dīghajāṇu (Numbered Discourses 8.54) ^an8-54-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Kakkarapatta. ^an8-54-1-1

Then Dīghajāṇu the Koliyan went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an8-54-1-2

“Sir, we are laypeople who enjoy sensual pleasures and living at home with our children. We use sandalwood imported from Kāsi, we wear garlands, fragrance, and makeup, and we accept gold and currency. ^an8-54-1-3

May the Buddha please teach us the Dhamma in a way that leads to our welfare and happiness in this life and in future lives.” ^an8-54-1-4

“Byagghapajja, these four things lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in this life. ^an8-54-2-1

What four? ^an8-54-2-2

Accomplishment in initiative, protection, good friendship, and balanced finances. ^an8-54-2-3

And what is accomplishment in initiative? ^an8-54-2-4

It’s when a gentleman earns a living by means such as ^an8-54-2-5

farming, trade, raising cattle, archery, government service, or one of the professions. ^an8-54-2-6

He understands how to go about these things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-54-2-7

This is called accomplishment in initiative. ^an8-54-2-8

And what is accomplishment in protection? ^an8-54-3-1

It’s when a gentleman owns legitimate wealth that he has earned by his own efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. ^an8-54-3-2

He ensures it is guarded and protected, thinking: ^an8-54-3-3

‘How can I prevent my wealth from being taken by rulers or bandits, consumed by fire, swept away by flood, or taken by unloved heirs?’ ^an8-54-3-4

This is called accomplishment in protection. ^an8-54-3-5

And what is accomplishment in good friendship? ^an8-54-4-1

It’s when a gentleman resides in a town or village. And in that place there are householders or their children who may be young or old, but are mature in conduct, accomplished in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. He associates with them, converses and engages in discussion. And he emulates the same kind of accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-54-4-2

This is called accomplishment in good friendship. ^an8-54-4-3

And what is accomplishment in balanced finances? ^an8-54-5-1

It’s when a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-54-5-2

It’s like an appraiser or their apprentice who, holding up the scales, knows that it’s low by this much or high by this much. ^an8-54-5-3

In the same way, a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-54-5-4

If a gentleman has little income but an opulent life, people will say: ‘This gentleman eats their wealth like a fig-eater!’ ^an8-54-5-5

If a gentleman has a large income but a spartan life, people will say: ‘This gentleman is starving themselves to death!’ ^an8-54-5-6

But a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, leads a balanced life, neither too extravagant nor too frugal, thinking, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-54-5-7

This is called accomplishment in balanced finances. ^an8-54-5-8

There are four drains on wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-54-6-1

Womanizing, drinking, gambling, and having bad friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-54-6-2

Suppose there was a large reservoir with four inlets and four drains. ^an8-54-6-3

And someone was to open up the drains and close off the inlets, ^an8-54-6-4

and the heavens don’t provide enough rain. ^an8-54-6-5

You’d expect that large reservoir to dwindle, not expand. ^an8-54-6-6

In the same way, there are four drains on wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-54-6-7

Womanizing, drinking, gambling, and having bad friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-54-6-8

There are four inlets for wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-54-7-1

Not womanizing, drinking, or gambling, and having good friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-54-7-2

Suppose there was a large reservoir with four inlets and four drains. ^an8-54-7-3

And someone was to open up the inlets and close off the drains, ^an8-54-7-4

and the heavens provide plenty of rain. ^an8-54-7-5

You’d expect that large reservoir to expand, not dwindle. ^an8-54-7-6

In the same way, there are four inlets for wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-54-7-7

Not womanizing, drinking, or gambling, and having good friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-54-7-8

These are the four things that lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in this life. ^an8-54-8-1

These four things lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in future lives. ^an8-54-9-1

What four? ^an8-54-9-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-54-9-3

And what is accomplishment in faith? ^an8-54-10-1

It’s when a gentleman has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an8-54-10-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an8-54-10-3

This is called accomplishment in faith. ^an8-54-10-4

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an8-54-11-1

It’s when a gentleman doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-54-11-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an8-54-11-3

And what is accomplishment in generosity? ^an8-54-12-1

It’s when a gentleman lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-54-12-2

This is called accomplishment in generosity. ^an8-54-12-3

And what is accomplishment in wisdom? ^an8-54-13-1

It’s when a gentleman is wise. He has the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-54-13-2

This is called accomplishment in wisdom. ^an8-54-13-3

These are the four things that lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in future lives. ^an8-54-14-1

They’re enterprising in the workplace, ^an8-54-15-1

diligent in managing things, ^an8-54-15-2

they balance their finances, ^an8-54-15-3

and preserve their wealth. ^an8-54-15-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-54-16-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an8-54-16-2

they always purify the path ^an8-54-16-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-54-16-4

And so these eight qualities ^an8-54-17-1

of a faithful householder of discernment ^an8-54-17-2

are declared by the one who is truly named ^an8-54-17-3

to lead to happiness in both spheres, ^an8-54-17-4

welfare and benefit in this life, ^an8-54-18-1

and happiness in the future lives. ^an8-54-18-2

This is how, for a householder, ^an8-54-18-3

merit grows by generosity.” ^an8-54-18-4

### With Ujjaya (Numbered Discourses 8.55) ^an8-55-0

Then Ujjaya the brahmin went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an8-55-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an8-55-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, we wish to travel abroad. ^an8-55-1-3

May the Buddha please teach us the Dhamma in a way that leads to our welfare and happiness in this life and in future lives.” ^an8-55-1-4

“Brahmin, these four things lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in this life. ^an8-55-2-1

What four? ^an8-55-2-2

Accomplishment in initiative, protection, good friendship, and balanced finances. ^an8-55-2-3

And what is accomplishment in initiative? ^an8-55-3-1

A gentleman may earn a living by means such as ^an8-55-3-2

farming, trade, raising cattle, archery, government service, or one of the professions. ^an8-55-3-3

He understands how to go about these things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-55-3-4

This is called accomplishment in initiative. ^an8-55-3-5

And what is accomplishment in protection? ^an8-55-4-1

It’s when a gentleman owns legitimate wealth that he has earned by his own efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. ^an8-55-4-2

He ensures it is guarded and protected, thinking: ^an8-55-4-3

‘How can I prevent my wealth from being taken by rulers or bandits, consumed by fire, swept away by flood, or taken by unloved heirs?’ ^an8-55-4-4

This is called accomplishment in protection. ^an8-55-4-5

And what is accomplishment in good friendship? ^an8-55-5-1

It’s when a gentleman resides in a town or village. And in that place there are householders or their children who may be young or old, but are mature in conduct, accomplished in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. He associates with them, converses and engages in discussion. And he emulates the same kind of accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-55-5-2

This is called accomplishment in good friendship. ^an8-55-5-3

And what is accomplishment in balanced finances? ^an8-55-6-1

It’s when a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-55-6-2

It’s like an appraiser or their apprentice who, holding up the scales, knows that it’s low by this much or high by this much. ^an8-55-6-3

In the same way, a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-55-6-4

If a gentleman has little income but an opulent life, people will say: ‘This gentleman eats their wealth like a fig-eater!’ ^an8-55-6-5

If a gentleman has a large income but a spartan life, people will say: ‘This gentleman is starving themselves to death!’ ^an8-55-6-6

But a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, leads a balanced life, neither too extravagant nor too frugal, thinking, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-55-6-7

This is called accomplishment in balanced finances. ^an8-55-6-8

There are four drains on wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-55-7-1

Womanizing, drinking, gambling, and having bad friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-55-7-2

Suppose there was a large reservoir with four inlets and four drains. And someone was to open up the drains and close off the inlets, and the heavens don’t provide enough rain. You’d expect that large reservoir to dwindle, not expand. ^an8-55-7-3

In the same way, there are four drains on wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-55-7-4

Womanizing, drinking, gambling, and having bad friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-55-7-5

There are four inlets for wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-55-8-1

Not womanizing, drinking, or gambling, and having good friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-55-8-2

Suppose there was a large reservoir with four inlets and four drains. And someone was to open up the inlets and close off the drains, and the heavens provide plenty of rain. You’d expect that large reservoir to expand, not dwindle. ^an8-55-8-3

In the same way, there are four inlets for wealth that has been gathered in this way. ^an8-55-8-4

Not womanizing, drinking, or gambling, and having good friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-55-8-5

These are the four things that lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in this life. ^an8-55-9-1

These four things lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in future lives. ^an8-55-10-1

What four? ^an8-55-10-2

Accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-55-10-3

And what is accomplishment in faith? ^an8-55-10-4

It’s when a gentleman has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an8-55-10-5

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an8-55-10-6

This is called accomplishment in faith. ^an8-55-10-7

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an8-55-11-1

It’s when a gentleman doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-55-11-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an8-55-11-3

And what is accomplishment in generosity? ^an8-55-12-1

It’s when a gentleman lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-55-12-2

This is called accomplishment in generosity. ^an8-55-12-3

And what is accomplishment in wisdom? ^an8-55-13-1

It’s when a gentleman is wise. He has the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-55-13-2

This is called accomplishment in wisdom. ^an8-55-13-3

These are the four things that lead to the welfare and happiness of a gentleman in future lives. ^an8-55-14-1

They’re enterprising in the workplace, ^an8-55-15-1

diligent in managing things, ^an8-55-15-2

they balance their finances, ^an8-55-15-3

and preserve their wealth. ^an8-55-15-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-55-16-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an8-55-16-2

they always purify the path ^an8-55-16-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-55-16-4

And so these eight qualities ^an8-55-17-1

of a faithful householder of discernment ^an8-55-17-2

are declared by the one who is truly named ^an8-55-17-3

to lead to happiness in both spheres, ^an8-55-17-4

welfare and benefit in this life, ^an8-55-18-1

and happiness in the next. ^an8-55-18-2

This is how, for a householder, ^an8-55-18-3

merit grows by generosity.” ^an8-55-18-4

### Danger (Numbered Discourses 8.56) ^an8-56-0

“Mendicants, ‘danger’ is a term for sensual pleasures. ^an8-56-1-1

‘Suffering’, ^an8-56-1-2

‘disease’, ^an8-56-1-3

‘boil’, ^an8-56-1-4

‘dart’, ^an8-56-1-5

‘chain’, ^an8-56-1-6

‘bog’, ^an8-56-1-7

and ‘womb’ are terms for sensual pleasures. ^an8-56-1-8

And why is ‘danger’ a term for sensual pleasures? ^an8-56-1-9

Someone who is besotted by sensual greed and shackled by lustful desire is not freed from dangers in this life or in lives to come. That is why ‘danger’ is a term for sensual pleasures. ^an8-56-1-10

And why are ‘suffering’, ^an8-56-1-11

‘disease’, ^an8-56-1-12

‘boil’, ^an8-56-1-13

‘dart’, ^an8-56-1-14

‘chain’, ^an8-56-1-15

‘bog’, ^an8-56-1-16

and ‘womb’ terms for sensual pleasures? ^an8-56-1-17

Someone who is besotted by sensual greed and shackled by lustful desire is not freed from wombs in this life or in lives to come. That is why ‘womb’ is a term for sensual pleasures. ^an8-56-1-18

Danger, suffering, and disease, ^an8-56-2-1

boil, dart, and chain, ^an8-56-2-2

and bogs and wombs both. ^an8-56-2-3

These describe the sensual pleasures ^an8-56-2-4

to which ordinary people are attached. ^an8-56-2-5

Swamped by things that seem pleasant, ^an8-56-3-1

you go to another womb. ^an8-56-3-2

But when a mendicant is keen, ^an8-56-3-3

and doesn’t forget awareness, ^an8-56-3-4

in this way that sort transcend ^an8-56-4-1

this grueling swamp. ^an8-56-4-2

They watch this population flounder, ^an8-56-4-3

fallen into rebirth and old age.” ^an8-56-4-4

### Worthy of Offerings Dedicated to the Gods (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.57) ^an8-57-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-57-1-1

What eight? ^an8-57-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an8-57-1-3

They’re learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an8-57-1-4

They have good friends, companions, and associates. ^an8-57-1-5

They have right view, possessing right perspective. ^an8-57-1-6

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an8-57-1-7

They recollect many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an8-57-1-8

With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, they see how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an8-57-1-9

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an8-57-1-10

A mendicant with these eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an8-57-1-11

### Worthy of Offerings Dedicated to the Gods (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.58) ^an8-58-0

“A mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-58-1-1

What eight? ^an8-58-1-2

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an8-58-1-3

They’re learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an8-58-1-4

They live with energy roused up. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an8-58-1-5

They live in the wilderness, in remote lodgings. ^an8-58-1-6

They prevail over desire and discontent, and live having mastered desire and discontent whenever they arose. ^an8-58-1-7

They prevail over fear and dread, and live having mastered fear and dread whenever they arose. ^an8-58-1-8

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an8-58-1-9

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an8-58-1-10

A mendicant with these eight qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an8-58-1-11

### Eight Individuals (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.59) ^an8-59-0

“Mendicants, these eight individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-59-1-1

What eight? ^an8-59-1-2

The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The perfected one, and the one practicing for perfection. ^an8-59-1-3

These are the eight individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-59-1-4

Four practicing the path, ^an8-59-2-1

and four established in the fruit. ^an8-59-2-2

This is the upright Saṅgha, ^an8-59-2-3

with wisdom, ethics, and immersion. ^an8-59-2-4

For humans, those merit-seeking creatures, ^an8-59-3-1

who sponsor sacrifices, ^an8-59-3-2

making worldly merit, ^an8-59-3-3

what is given to the Saṅgha is very fruitful.” ^an8-59-3-4

### Eight Individuals (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.60) ^an8-60-0

“Mendicants, these eight individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-60-1-1

What eight? ^an8-60-1-2

The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The perfected one, and the one practicing for perfection. ^an8-60-1-3

These are the eight individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an8-60-1-4

Four practicing the path, ^an8-60-2-1

and four established in the fruit. ^an8-60-2-2

This is the exalted Saṅgha, ^an8-60-2-3

the eight individuals among sentient beings. ^an8-60-2-4

For humans, those merit-seeking creatures, ^an8-60-3-1

who sponsor sacrifices, ^an8-60-3-2

making worldly merit, ^an8-60-3-3

what’s given here is very fruitful.” ^an8-60-3-4

### Desire (Numbered Discourses 8.61) ^an8-61-0

“Mendicants, these eight individuals are found in the world. ^an8-61-1-1

What eight? ^an8-61-1-2

First, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-1-3

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-1-4

But material things don’t come to them. ^an8-61-1-5

And so they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-61-1-6

This is called ^an8-61-1-7
