a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they do not acquire material things, they sorrow and lament. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-1-8

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-2-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-2-2

And material things do come to them. ^an8-61-2-3

And so they become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-61-2-4

This is called ^an8-61-2-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they acquire material things, they become intoxicated and negligent. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-2-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-3-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-3-2

And material things don’t come to them. ^an8-61-3-3

And so they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-61-3-4

This is called ^an8-61-3-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they do not acquire material things, they sorrow and lament. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-3-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-4-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-4-2

But material things do come to them. ^an8-61-4-3

And so they become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-61-4-4

This is called ^an8-61-4-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they acquire material things, they become intoxicated and negligent. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-4-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-5-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-5-2

But material things don’t come to them. ^an8-61-5-3

But they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-61-5-4

This is called ^an8-61-5-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they do not acquire material things, they don’t sorrow and lament. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-5-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-6-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-6-2

And material things do come to them. ^an8-61-6-3

But they don’t become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-61-6-4

This is called ^an8-61-6-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they acquire material things, they don’t become intoxicated and negligent. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-6-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-7-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-7-2

And material things don’t come to them. ^an8-61-7-3

But they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-61-7-4

This is called ^an8-61-7-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they do not acquire material things, they don’t sorrow and lament. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-7-6

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-61-8-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-61-8-2

But material things do come to them. ^an8-61-8-3

But they don’t become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-61-8-4

This is called ^an8-61-8-5

a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they acquire material things, they don’t become intoxicated and negligent. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-61-8-6

These are the eight individuals found in the world.” ^an8-61-9-1

### Good Enough (Numbered Discourses 8.62) ^an8-62-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with six qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-62-1-1

What six? ^an8-62-1-2

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-1-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-1-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-1-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-1-6

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-62-1-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-1-8

A mendicant with these six qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-62-1-9

A mendicant with five qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-62-2-1

What five? ^an8-62-2-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-2-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-2-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-2-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-2-6

They’re a good speaker. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-62-2-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-2-8

A mendicant with these five qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-62-2-9

A mendicant with four qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-3-1

What four? ^an8-62-3-2

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-3-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-3-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-3-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-3-6

But they’re not a good speaker and do not enunciate well. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-62-3-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-3-8

A mendicant with these four qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-3-9

A mendicant with four qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-62-4-1

What four? ^an8-62-4-2

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-4-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-4-4

But they don’t examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-4-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-4-6

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-62-4-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-4-8

A mendicant with these four qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-62-4-9

A mendicant with three qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-5-1

What three? ^an8-62-5-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-5-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-5-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-5-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-5-6

But they’re not a good speaker and do not enunciate well. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-62-5-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-5-8

A mendicant with these three qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-5-9

A mendicant with three qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-62-6-1

What three? ^an8-62-6-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-6-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-6-4

But they don’t examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-6-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-6-6

They’re a good speaker. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-62-6-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-6-8

A mendicant with these three qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-62-6-9

A mendicant with two qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-7-1

What two? ^an8-62-7-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-7-3

And they don’t readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-7-4

But they examine the meaning of teachings they have memorized. ^an8-62-7-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-7-6

They’re not a good speaker. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-62-7-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-7-8

A mendicant with these two qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-62-7-9

A mendicant with two qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-62-8-1

What two? ^an8-62-8-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-62-8-3

And they don’t readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-62-8-4

Nor do they examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-62-8-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-62-8-6

But they’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-62-8-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-62-8-8

A mendicant with these two qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves.” ^an8-62-8-9

### A Teaching in Brief (Numbered Discourses 8.63) ^an8-63-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an8-63-1-1

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.” ^an8-63-1-2

“This is exactly how some futile people ask me for something. ^an8-63-1-3

But when the teaching has been explained they think only of following me around.” ^an8-63-1-4

“Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief! May the Holy One teach me the Dhamma in brief! Hopefully I can understand the meaning of what the Buddha says! Hopefully I can be an heir of the Buddha’s teaching!” ^an8-63-1-5

“Well then, mendicant, you should train like this: ^an8-63-1-6

‘My mind will be steady and well settled internally. And bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen will not occupy my mind.’ ^an8-63-1-7

That’s how you should train. ^an8-63-1-8

When your mind is steady and well settled internally, and bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen don’t occupy your mind, then you should train like this: ^an8-63-2-1

‘I will develop the heart’s release by love. I’ll cultivate it, make it my vehicle and my basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^an8-63-2-2

That’s how you should train. ^an8-63-2-3

When this immersion is developed and cultivated in this way, you should develop it while placing the mind and keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind or keeping it connected. You should develop it with rapture. You should develop it without rapture. You should develop it with pleasure. You should develop it with equanimity. ^an8-63-3-1

When this immersion is well developed in this way, you should train like this: ^an8-63-4-1

‘I will develop the heart’s release by compassion …’ … ^an8-63-4-2

‘I will develop the heart’s release by rejoicing …’ … ^an8-63-4-3

‘I will develop the heart’s release by equanimity. I’ll cultivate it, make it my vehicle and my basis, keep it up, consolidate it, and properly implement it.’ ^an8-63-4-4

That’s how you should train. ^an8-63-4-5

When this immersion is well developed in this way, you should develop it while placing the mind and keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind or keeping it connected. You should develop it with rapture. You should develop it without rapture. You should develop it with pleasure. You should develop it with equanimity. ^an8-63-5-1

When this immersion is well developed in this way, you should train like this: ^an8-63-6-1

‘I’ll meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.’ ^an8-63-6-2

That’s how you should train. ^an8-63-6-3

When this immersion is developed and cultivated in this way, you should develop it while placing the mind and keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind or keeping it connected. You should develop it with rapture. You should develop it without rapture. You should develop it with pleasure. You should develop it with equanimity. ^an8-63-7-1

When this immersion is well developed in this way, you should train like this: ^an8-63-8-1

‘I’ll meditate on an aspect of feelings …’ … ^an8-63-8-2

‘I’ll meditate on an aspect of the mind …’ … ^an8-63-8-3

‘I’ll meditate on an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.’ ^an8-63-8-4

That’s how you should train. ^an8-63-8-5

When this immersion is developed and cultivated in this way, you should develop it while placing the mind and keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind, merely keeping it connected. You should develop it without placing the mind or keeping it connected. You should develop it with rapture. You should develop it without rapture. You should develop it with pleasure. You should develop it with equanimity. ^an8-63-9-1

When this immersion is well developed in this way, wherever you walk, you’ll walk comfortably. Wherever you stand, you’ll stand comfortably. Wherever you sit, you’ll sit comfortably. Wherever you lie down, you’ll lie down comfortably.” ^an8-63-10-1

When that mendicant had been given this advice by the Buddha, he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an8-63-11-1

Then that mendicant, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme culmination of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an8-63-11-2

He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.” ^an8-63-11-3

And that mendicant became one of the perfected. ^an8-63-11-4

### At Gayā Head (Numbered Discourses 8.64) ^an8-64-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Gayā on Gayā Head. ^an8-64-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-64-1-2

“Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still not awake but intent on awakening—I perceived light but did not see forms. ^an8-64-1-3

Then it occurred to me, ^an8-64-2-1

‘What if I were to both perceive light and see forms? ^an8-64-2-2

Then my knowledge and vision would become even more purified.’ ^an8-64-2-3

So after some time, living diligent, keen, and resolute, I perceived light and saw forms. ^an8-64-3-1

But I didn’t associate with those deities, converse, or engage in discussion. ^an8-64-3-2

Then it occurred to me, ^an8-64-4-1

‘What if I were to perceive light and see forms; and associate with those deities, converse, and engage in discussion? ^an8-64-4-2

Then my knowledge and vision would become even more purified.’ ^an8-64-4-3

So after some time … I perceived light and saw forms. And I associated with those deities, conversed, and engaged in discussion. ^an8-64-5-1

But I didn’t know which ^an8-64-5-2

orders of gods those deities came from. ^an8-64-5-3

Then it occurred to me, ^an8-64-6-1

‘What if I were to perceive light and see forms; and associate with those deities, converse, and engage in discussion; ^an8-64-6-2

and find out which orders of gods those deities come from? ^an8-64-6-3

Then my knowledge and vision would become even more purified.’ ^an8-64-6-4

So after some time … I perceived light and saw forms. And I associated with those deities … And I found out which orders of gods those deities came from. ^an8-64-7-1

But I didn’t know what ^an8-64-7-3

deeds caused those deities to be reborn there after passing away from here. ^an8-64-7-4

So after some time … I found out what ^an8-64-7-5

deeds caused those deities to be reborn there after passing away from here. ^an8-64-7-6

But I didn’t know what ^an8-64-7-7

deeds caused those deities to have such food and such an experience of pleasure and pain. ^an8-64-7-8

So after some time … I found out what ^an8-64-7-9

deeds caused those deities to have such food and such an experience of pleasure and pain. ^an8-64-7-10

But I didn’t know that ^an8-64-7-11

these deities have a lifespan of such a length. ^an8-64-7-12

So after some time … I found out that ^an8-64-7-13

these deities have a lifespan of such a length. ^an8-64-7-14

But I didn’t know whether or not I had previously lived together with those deities. ^an8-64-7-15

Then it occurred to me, ^an8-64-8-1

‘What if I were to perceive light and see forms; and associate with those deities, converse, and engage in discussion; ^an8-64-8-2

and find out which orders of gods those deities come from; ^an8-64-8-3

and what deeds caused those deities to be reborn there after passing away from here; ^an8-64-8-4

and what deeds caused those deities to have such food and such an experience of pleasure and pain; ^an8-64-8-5

and that these deities have a lifespan of such a length; and whether or not I have previously lived together with those deities? ^an8-64-8-6

Then my knowledge and vision would become even more purified.’ ^an8-64-8-7

So after some time … ^an8-64-9-1

I found out whether or not I have previously lived together with those deities. ^an8-64-9-5

As long as my knowledge and vision about the deities was not fully purified in these eight rounds, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an8-64-10-1

But when my knowledge and vision about the deities was fully purified in these eight rounds, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an8-64-10-2

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^an8-64-10-3

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^an8-64-10-4

### Dimensions of Mastery (Numbered Discourses 8.65) ^an8-65-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight dimensions of mastery. ^an8-65-1-1

What eight? ^an8-65-1-2

Perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. ^an8-65-1-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-1-4

This is the first dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-1-5

Perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. ^an8-65-2-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-2-2

This is the second dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-2-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. ^an8-65-3-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-3-2

This is the third dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-3-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. ^an8-65-4-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-4-2

This is the fourth dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-4-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, blue, with blue color and blue appearance. ^an8-65-5-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-5-2

This is the fifth dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-5-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, yellow, with yellow color and yellow appearance. ^an8-65-6-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-6-2

This is the sixth dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-6-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, red, with red color and red appearance. ^an8-65-7-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-7-2

This is the seventh dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-7-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an8-65-8-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-65-8-2

This is the eighth dimension of mastery. ^an8-65-8-3

These are the eight dimensions of mastery.” ^an8-65-8-4

### Liberations (Numbered Discourses 8.66) ^an8-66-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight liberations. ^an8-66-1-1

What eight? ^an8-66-1-2

Having physical form, they see forms. ^an8-66-1-3

This is the first liberation. ^an8-66-1-4

Not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally. ^an8-66-2-1

This is the second liberation. ^an8-66-2-2

They’re focused only on beauty. ^an8-66-3-1

This is the third liberation. ^an8-66-3-2

Going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space. ^an8-66-4-1

This is the fourth liberation. ^an8-66-4-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an8-66-5-1

This is the fifth liberation. ^an8-66-5-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. ^an8-66-6-1

This is the sixth liberation. ^an8-66-6-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an8-66-7-1

This is the seventh liberation. ^an8-66-7-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an8-66-8-1

This is the eighth liberation. ^an8-66-8-2

These are the eight liberations.” ^an8-66-8-3

### Ignoble Expressions (Numbered Discourses 8.67) ^an8-67-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight ignoble expressions. ^an8-67-1-1

What eight? ^an8-67-1-2

Saying you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, but you haven’t. And saying you haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you have. ^an8-67-1-3

These are the eight ignoble expressions.” ^an8-67-1-4

### Noble Expressions (Numbered Discourses 8.68) ^an8-68-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight noble expressions. ^an8-68-1-1

What eight? ^an8-68-1-2

Saying you haven’t seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you haven’t. And saying you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known something, and you have. ^an8-68-1-3

These are the eight noble expressions.” ^an8-68-1-4

### Assemblies (Numbered Discourses 8.69) ^an8-69-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight assemblies. ^an8-69-1-1

What eight? ^an8-69-1-2

The assemblies of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, and ascetics. An assembly of the gods of the four great kings. An assembly of the gods of the thirty-three. An assembly of Māras. An assembly of divinities. ^an8-69-1-3

I recall having approached an assembly of hundreds of aristocrats. ^an8-69-1-4

There I used to sit with them, converse, and engage in discussion. ^an8-69-1-5

And my appearance and voice became just like theirs. ^an8-69-1-6

I educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired them with a Dhamma talk. ^an8-69-1-7

But when I spoke they didn’t know: ^an8-69-1-8

‘Who is this that speaks? Is it a god or a human?’ ^an8-69-1-9

And when my Dhamma talk was finished I vanished. ^an8-69-1-10

But when I vanished they didn’t know: ^an8-69-1-11

‘Who was that who vanished? Was it a god or a human?’ ^an8-69-1-12

I recall having approached an assembly of hundreds of brahmins … ^an8-69-2-1

householders … ^an8-69-2-2

ascetics … ^an8-69-2-3

the gods of the four great kings … ^an8-69-2-4

the gods of the thirty-three … ^an8-69-2-5

Māras … ^an8-69-2-6

divinities. ^an8-69-2-7

There too I used to sit with them, converse, and engage in discussion. ^an8-69-2-8

And my appearance and voice became just like theirs. ^an8-69-2-9

I educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired them with a Dhamma talk. ^an8-69-2-10

But when I spoke they didn’t know: ^an8-69-2-11

‘Who is this that speaks? Is it a god or a human?’ ^an8-69-2-12

And when my Dhamma talk was finished I vanished. ^an8-69-2-13

But when I vanished they didn’t know: ^an8-69-2-14

‘Who was that who vanished? Was it a god or a human?’ ^an8-69-2-15

These are the eight assemblies.” ^an8-69-2-16

### Earthquakes (Numbered Discourses 8.70) ^an8-70-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an8-70-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Vesālī for alms. ^an8-70-1-2

Then, after the meal, on his return from almsround, he addressed Venerable Ānanda, ^an8-70-1-3

“Ānanda, get your sitting cloth. ^an8-70-1-4

Let’s go to the Cāpāla Shrine for the day’s meditation.” ^an8-70-1-5

“Yes, sir,” replied Ānanda. Taking his sitting cloth he followed behind the Buddha. ^an8-70-1-6

Then the Buddha went up to the Cāpāla Shrine, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an8-70-2-1

When he was seated he said to Venerable Ānanda: ^an8-70-2-2

“Ānanda, Vesālī is lovely. And the Udena, Gotamaka, Seven Maidens, Many Sons, Sārandada, and Cāpāla Shrines are all lovely. ^an8-70-3-1

Whoever has developed and cultivated the four bases of psychic power—made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them—may, if they wish, live for the proper lifespan or what’s left of it. ^an8-70-3-2

The Realized One has developed and cultivated the four bases of psychic power, made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them. If he wished, the Realized One could live for the proper lifespan or what’s left of it.” ^an8-70-3-3

But Ānanda didn’t get it, even though the Buddha dropped such an obvious hint, such a clear sign. ^an8-70-3-4

He didn’t beg the Buddha, ^an8-70-3-5

“Sir, may the Blessed One please remain for the proper lifespan! May the Holy One please remain for the proper lifespan! That would be for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.” For his mind was as if possessed by Māra. ^an8-70-3-6

For a second time … ^an8-70-4-1

And for a third time, the Buddha said to him: ^an8-70-4-2

“Ānanda, Vesālī is lovely. And the Udena, Gotamaka, Seven Maidens, Many Sons, Sārandada, and Cāpāla Shrines are all lovely. ^an8-70-4-3

Whoever has developed and cultivated the four bases of psychic power—made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them—may, if they wish, live for the proper lifespan or what’s left of it. ^an8-70-4-4

The Realized One has developed and cultivated the four bases of psychic power, made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them. If he wished, the Realized One could live for the proper lifespan or what’s left of it.” ^an8-70-4-5

But Ānanda didn’t get it, even though the Buddha dropped such an obvious hint, such a clear sign. ^an8-70-4-6

He didn’t beg the Buddha, ^an8-70-4-7

“Sir, may the Blessed One please remain for the proper lifespan! May the Holy One please remain for the proper lifespan! That would be for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.” For his mind was as if possessed by Māra. ^an8-70-4-8

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Ānanda, ^an8-70-5-1

“Go now, Ānanda, at your convenience.” ^an8-70-5-2

“Yes, sir,” replied Ānanda. He rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before sitting at the root of a tree close by. ^an8-70-5-3

And then, not long after Ānanda had left, Māra the Wicked said to the Buddha: ^an8-70-5-4

“Sir, may the Blessed One now be fully extinguished! May the Holy One now be fully extinguished! Now is the time for the full extinguishment of the Buddha. ^an8-70-6-1

Sir, you once made this statement: ^an8-70-6-2

‘Wicked One, I shall not be fully extinguished until I have monk disciples who are competent, educated, self-assured, learned, have memorized the teachings, and practice in line with the teaching; not until they practice appropriately, living in line with the teaching; not until they’ve learned their tradition, and explain, teach, assert, establish, clarify, analyze, and reveal; not until they can legitimately and completely refute the doctrines of others that come up, and teach with a demonstrable basis.’ ^an8-70-6-3

Today you do have such monk disciples. ^an8-70-6-4

May the Blessed One now be fully extinguished! May the Holy One now be fully extinguished! Now is the time for the full extinguishment of the Buddha. ^an8-70-7-1

Sir, you once made this statement: ^an8-70-7-2

‘Wicked One, I shall not be fully extinguished until I have nun disciples who are competent, educated, self-assured, learned …’ … ^an8-70-7-3

‘Wicked One, I shall not be fully extinguished until I have layman disciples who are competent, educated, self-assured, learned …’ … ^an8-70-7-4

‘Wicked One, I shall not be fully extinguished until I have laywoman disciples who are competent, educated, self-assured, learned …’ … ^an8-70-7-5

Today you do have such laywoman disciples. ^an8-70-7-6

Sir, may the Blessed One now be fully extinguished! May the Holy One be fully extinguished! Now is the time for the full extinguishment of the Buddha. ^an8-70-8-1

Sir, you once made this statement: ^an8-70-8-2

‘Wicked One, I shall not be fully extinguished until my spiritual path is successful and prosperous, extensive, popular, widespread, and well proclaimed wherever there are gods and humans.’ ^an8-70-8-3

Today your spiritual path is successful and prosperous, extensive, popular, widespread, and well proclaimed wherever there are gods and humans. ^an8-70-8-4

Sir, may the Blessed One now be fully extinguished! May the Holy One be fully extinguished! Now is the time for the full extinguishment of the Buddha.” ^an8-70-9-1

“Relax, Wicked One. The full extinguishment of the Realized One will be soon. ^an8-70-9-2

Three months from now the Realized One will be fully extinguished.” ^an8-70-9-3

So at the Cāpāla Tree-shrine the Buddha, mindful and aware, surrendered the life force. ^an8-70-10-1

When he did so there was a great earthquake, awe-inspiring and hair-raising, and thunder cracked the sky. ^an8-70-10-2

Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment: ^an8-70-10-3

“Comparing the incomparable <j>with the creation of prolonged life, ^an8-70-10-4

the sage surrendered the life force. ^an8-70-10-5

Happy inside, serene, ^an8-70-10-6

he shattered self-creation like a suit of armor.” ^an8-70-10-7

Then Venerable Ānanda thought, ^an8-70-11-1

“That was a really big earthquake! ^an8-70-12-2

That was really a very big earthquake; awe-inspiring and hair-raising, and thunder cracked the sky! ^an8-70-12-3

What’s the cause, what’s the reason for a great earthquake?” ^an8-70-12-4

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an8-70-13-1

“Sir, that was a really big earthquake! ^an8-70-13-2

That was really a very big earthquake; awe-inspiring and hair-raising, and thunder cracked the sky! ^an8-70-13-3

What’s the cause, what’s the reason for a great earthquake?” ^an8-70-13-4

“Ānanda, there are these eight causes and reasons for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-14-1

What eight? ^an8-70-14-2

This great earth is established on water, the water is established on air, and the air stands in space. At a time when a great wind blows, it stirs the water, and the water stirs the earth. ^an8-70-14-3

This is the first cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-14-4

Furthermore, there is an ascetic or brahmin with psychic power who has achieved mastery of the mind, or a god who is mighty and powerful. They’ve developed a limited perception of earth and a limitless perception of water. They make the earth shake and rock and tremble. ^an8-70-15-1

This is the second cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-15-2

Furthermore, when the being intent on awakening passes away from the host of joyful gods, he’s conceived in his mother’s womb, mindful and aware. Then the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-16-1

This is the third cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-16-2

Furthermore, when the being intent on awakening comes out of his mother’s womb mindful and aware, the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-17-1

This is the fourth cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-17-2

Furthermore, when the Realized One awakens to the supreme perfect awakening, the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-18-1

This is the fifth cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-18-2

Furthermore, when the Realized One rolls forth the supreme Wheel of Dhamma, the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-19-1

This is the sixth cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-19-2

Furthermore, when the Realized One, mindful and aware, surrenders the life force, the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-20-1

This is the seventh cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-20-2

Furthermore, when the Realized One becomes fully extinguished in the element of extinguishment with no residue, the earth shakes and rocks and trembles. ^an8-70-21-1

This is the eighth cause and reason for a great earthquake. ^an8-70-21-2

These are the eight causes and reasons for a great earthquake.” ^an8-70-21-3

### Inspiring All Around (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.71) ^an8-71-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant is faithful but not ethical. ^an8-71-1-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an8-71-1-2

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an8-71-1-3

‘How can I become faithful and ethical?’ ^an8-71-1-4

When the mendicant is faithful and ethical, ^an8-71-1-5

they’re complete in that respect. ^an8-71-1-6

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but not learned. ^an8-71-2-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an8-71-2-2

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an8-71-2-3

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned?’ ^an8-71-2-4

When the mendicant is faithful, ethical, and learned, ^an8-71-2-5

they’re complete in that respect. ^an8-71-2-6

A mendicant is faithful, ethical, and learned, but not a Dhamma speaker. … ^an8-71-3-1

they don’t frequent assemblies … ^an8-71-3-2

they don’t teach Dhamma to the assembly with assurance … ^an8-71-3-3

they don’t get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty … ^an8-71-3-4

they don’t realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an8-71-3-5

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an8-71-3-6

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an8-71-3-7

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, one who gets the four absorptions when they want, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements?’ ^an8-71-3-8

When they’re faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, one who gets the four absorptions when they want, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements, ^an8-71-4-1

they’re complete in that respect. ^an8-71-4-2

A mendicant who possesses these eight qualities is impressive all around, and is complete in every respect.” ^an8-71-4-3

### Inspiring All Around (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.72) ^an8-72-0

“A mendicant is faithful, but not ethical. ^an8-72-1-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an8-72-1-2

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an8-72-1-3

‘How can I become faithful and ethical?’ ^an8-72-1-4

When the mendicant is faithful and ethical, ^an8-72-1-5

they’re complete in that respect. ^an8-72-1-6

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but not learned. … ^an8-72-2-1

they’re not a Dhamma speaker … ^an8-72-2-2

they don’t frequent assemblies … ^an8-72-2-3

they don’t teach Dhamma to the assembly with assurance … ^an8-72-2-4

they don’t have direct meditative experience of the peaceful liberations that are formless, transcending form … ^an8-72-2-5

they don’t realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an8-72-2-6

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an8-72-2-7

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an8-72-2-8

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, one who gets the formless liberations, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements?’ ^an8-72-2-9

When they’re faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, one who gets the formless liberations, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements, ^an8-72-3-1

they’re complete in that respect. ^an8-72-3-2

A mendicant who possesses these eight qualities is impressive all around, and is complete in every respect.” ^an8-72-3-3

### Mindfulness of Death (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.73) ^an8-73-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^an8-73-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an8-73-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an8-73-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an8-73-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an8-73-1-5

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of death is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination. ^an8-73-1-6

But do you develop mindfulness of death?” ^an8-73-1-7

When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-2-1

“Sir, I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-2-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-2-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-2-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live for another day and night, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-2-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-2-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-3-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-3-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-3-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-3-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live for another day, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-3-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-3-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-4-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-4-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-4-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-4-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live for half a day, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-4-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-4-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-5-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-5-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-5-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-5-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to eat a single almsmeal, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-5-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-5-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-6-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-6-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-6-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-6-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to eat half an almsmeal, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-6-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-6-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-7-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-7-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-7-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-7-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-7-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-7-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-8-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-8-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-8-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-8-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to chew and swallow a single mouthful, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-8-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-8-6

Another mendicant said to the Buddha, ^an8-73-9-1

“Sir, I too develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-9-2

“But mendicant, how do you develop it?” ^an8-73-9-3

“In this case, sir, I think: ^an8-73-9-4

‘Oh, if I’d only live as long as it takes to breathe out after breathing in, or to breathe in after breathing out, I’d focus on the Buddha’s instructions and I could really achieve a lot.’ ^an8-73-9-5

That’s how I develop mindfulness of death.” ^an8-73-9-6

When this was said, the Buddha said to those mendicants: ^an8-73-10-1

“The mendicants who develop mindfulness of death by wishing ^an8-73-10-2

to live for a day and night … ^an8-73-10-3

or to live for a day … ^an8-73-10-5

or to live for half a day … ^an8-73-10-7

or to live as long as it takes to eat a meal of almsfood … ^an8-73-10-9

or to live as long as it takes to eat half a meal of almsfood … ^an8-73-10-11

or to live as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls … ^an8-73-10-13

These are called mendicants who live negligently. They slackly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements. ^an8-73-10-14

But the mendicants who develop mindfulness of death by wishing ^an8-73-11-1

to live as long as it takes to chew and swallow a single mouthful … ^an8-73-11-2

or to live as long as it takes to breathe out after breathing in, or to breathe in after breathing out … ^an8-73-11-4

These are called mendicants who live diligently. They keenly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements. ^an8-73-11-5

So you should train like this: ^an8-73-12-1

‘We will live diligently. We will keenly develop mindfulness of death for the ending of defilements.’ ^an8-73-12-2

That’s how you should train.” ^an8-73-12-3

### Mindfulness of Death (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.74) ^an8-74-0

At one time the Buddha was staying at Ñātika in the brick house. ^an8-74-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an8-74-1-2

“Mendicants, when mindfulness of death is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination. ^an8-74-1-3

And how is mindfulness of death developed and cultivated to be very fruitful and beneficial, with freedom from death as its objective and culmination? ^an8-74-2-1

As day passes by and night draws close, a mendicant reflects: ^an8-74-2-2

‘I might die of many causes. ^an8-74-2-3

A snake might bite me, or a scorpion or centipede might sting me. ^an8-74-2-4

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an8-74-2-5

Or I might stumble off a cliff, or get food poisoning, or suffer a disturbance of bile, phlegm, or piercing winds. Or I might be attacked by humans or non-humans. ^an8-74-2-6

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress.’ ^an8-74-2-7

That mendicant should reflect: ^an8-74-2-8

‘Are there any bad, unskillful qualities that I haven’t given up, which might be an obstacle to my progress if I die tonight?’ ^an8-74-2-9

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an8-74-3-1

there are such bad, unskillful qualities. Then in order to give them up they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an8-74-3-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an8-74-4-1

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an8-74-4-2

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an8-74-5-1

there are no such bad, unskillful qualities. Then that mendicant should meditate with rapture and joy, training day and night in skillful qualities. ^an8-74-5-2

Or else, as night passes by and day draws close, a mendicant reflects: ^an8-74-6-1

‘I might die of many causes. ^an8-74-6-2

A snake might bite me, or a scorpion or centipede might sting me. ^an8-74-6-3

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress. ^an8-74-6-4

Or I might stumble off a cliff, or get food poisoning, or suffer a disturbance of bile, phlegm, or piercing winds. Or I might be attacked by humans or non-humans. ^an8-74-6-5

And if I died from that it would be an obstacle to my progress.’ ^an8-74-6-6

That mendicant should reflect: ^an8-74-6-7

‘Are there any bad, unskillful qualities that I haven’t given up, which might be an obstacle to my progress if I die today?’ ^an8-74-6-8

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an8-74-7-1

there are such bad, unskillful qualities. Then in order to give them up they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an8-74-7-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an8-74-8-1

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an8-74-8-2

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows that ^an8-74-9-1

there are no such bad, unskillful qualities. Then that mendicant should meditate with rapture and joy, training day and night in skillful qualities. ^an8-74-9-2

Mindfulness of death, when developed and cultivated in this way, is very fruitful and beneficial. It has freedom from death as its objective and culmination.” ^an8-74-9-3

### Accomplishments (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8.75) ^an8-75-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight accomplishments. ^an8-75-1-1

What eight? ^an8-75-1-2

Accomplishment in initiative, protection, good friendship, and balanced finances. And accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-75-1-3

These are the eight accomplishments. ^an8-75-1-4

They’re enterprising in the workplace, ^an8-75-2-1

diligent in managing things, ^an8-75-2-2

they balance their finances, ^an8-75-2-3

and preserve their wealth. ^an8-75-2-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-75-3-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an8-75-3-2

they always purify the path ^an8-75-3-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-75-3-4

And so these eight qualities ^an8-75-4-1

of a faithful householder of discernment ^an8-75-4-2

are declared by the one who is truly named ^an8-75-4-3

to lead to happiness in both spheres, ^an8-75-4-4

welfare and benefit in this life, ^an8-75-5-1

and happiness in lives to come. ^an8-75-5-2

This is how, for a householder, ^an8-75-5-3

merit grows by generosity.” ^an8-75-5-4

### Accomplishments (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8.76) ^an8-76-0

“Mendicants, there are these eight accomplishments. ^an8-76-1-1

What eight? ^an8-76-1-2

Accomplishment in initiative, protection, good friendship, and balanced finances. And accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-76-1-3

And what is accomplishment in initiative? ^an8-76-1-4

It’s when a gentleman earns a living by means such as ^an8-76-1-5

farming, trade, raising cattle, archery, government service, or one of the professions. ^an8-76-1-6

He understands how to go about these things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an8-76-1-7

This is called accomplishment in initiative. ^an8-76-1-8

And what is accomplishment in protection? ^an8-76-2-1

It’s when a gentleman owns legitimate wealth that he has earned by his own efforts and initiative, built up with his own hands, gathered by the sweat of the brow. He ensures it is guarded and protected, thinking: ^an8-76-2-2

‘How can I prevent my wealth from being taken by rulers or bandits, consumed by fire, swept away by flood, or taken by unloved heirs?’ ^an8-76-2-3

This is called accomplishment in protection. ^an8-76-2-4

And what is accomplishment in good friendship? ^an8-76-3-1

It’s when a gentleman resides in a town or village. And in that place there are householders or their children who may be young or old, but are mature in conduct, accomplished in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. He associates with them, converses and engages in discussion. And he emulates the same kind of accomplishment in faith, ethics, generosity, and wisdom. ^an8-76-3-2

This is called accomplishment in good friendship. ^an8-76-3-3

And what is accomplishment in balanced finances? ^an8-76-4-1

It’s when a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-76-4-2

It’s like an appraiser or their apprentice who, holding up the scales, knows that it’s low by this much or high by this much. ^an8-76-4-3

In the same way, a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, balances his finances, being neither too extravagant nor too frugal. He thinks, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-76-4-4

If a gentleman has little income but an opulent life, people will say: ‘This gentleman eats their wealth like a fig-eater!’ ^an8-76-4-5

If a gentleman has a large income but a spartan life, people will say: ‘This gentleman is starving themselves to death!’ ^an8-76-4-6

But a gentleman, knowing his income and expenditure, leads a balanced life, neither too extravagant nor too frugal, thinking, ‘In this way my income will exceed my expenditure, not the reverse.’ ^an8-76-4-7

This is called accomplishment in balanced finances. ^an8-76-4-8

And what is accomplishment in faith? ^an8-76-5-1

It’s when a gentleman has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an8-76-5-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha … teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an8-76-5-3

This is called accomplishment in faith. ^an8-76-5-4

And what is accomplishment in ethics? ^an8-76-6-1

It’s when a gentleman doesn’t kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an8-76-6-2

This is called accomplishment in ethics. ^an8-76-6-3

And what is accomplishment in generosity? ^an8-76-7-1

It’s when a gentleman lives at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share. ^an8-76-7-2

This is called accomplishment in generosity. ^an8-76-7-3

And what is accomplishment in wisdom? ^an8-76-8-1

It’s when a gentleman is wise. He has the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an8-76-8-2

This is called accomplishment in wisdom. ^an8-76-8-3

These are the eight accomplishments. ^an8-76-9-1

They’re enterprising in the workplace, ^an8-76-10-1

diligent in managing things, ^an8-76-10-2

they balance their finances, ^an8-76-10-3

and preserve their wealth. ^an8-76-10-4

Faithful, accomplished in ethics, ^an8-76-11-1

bountiful, rid of stinginess, ^an8-76-11-2

they always purify the path ^an8-76-11-3

to well-being in lives to come. ^an8-76-11-4

And so these eight qualities ^an8-76-12-1

of a faithful householder of discernment ^an8-76-12-2

are declared by the one who is truly named ^an8-76-12-3

to lead to happiness in both spheres, ^an8-76-12-4

welfare and benefit in this life, ^an8-76-13-1

and happiness in the next. ^an8-76-13-2

This is how, for a householder, ^an8-76-13-3

merit grows by generosity.” ^an8-76-13-4

### Desires (Numbered Discourses 8.77) ^an8-77-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an8-77-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an8-77-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an8-77-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an8-77-1-4

“Reverends, these eight individuals are found in the world. ^an8-77-2-1

What eight? ^an8-77-2-2

First, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-2-3

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-2-4

But material things don’t come to them. ^an8-77-2-5

And so they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-77-2-6

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they do not acquire material things, they sorrow and lament. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-2-7

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-3-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-3-2

And material things do come to them. ^an8-77-3-3

And so they become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-77-3-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they acquire material things, they become intoxicated and negligent. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-3-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-4-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-4-2

And material things don’t come to them. ^an8-77-4-3

And so they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-77-4-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they do not acquire material things, they sorrow and lament. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-4-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-5-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-5-2

But material things do come to them. ^an8-77-5-3

And so they become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-77-5-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they acquire material things, they become intoxicated and negligent. They’ve fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-5-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-6-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-6-2

But material things don’t come to them. ^an8-77-6-3

But they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-77-6-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they do not acquire material things, they don’t sorrow and lament. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-6-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-7-1

They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-7-2

And material things do come to them. ^an8-77-7-3

But they don’t become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-77-7-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. But when they acquire material things, they don’t become intoxicated and negligent. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-7-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-8-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-8-2

And material things don’t come to them. ^an8-77-8-3

But they don’t sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion because they don’t get those material things. ^an8-77-8-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they do not acquire material things, they don’t sorrow and lament. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-8-5

Next, when a mendicant stays secluded, living independently, a desire arises for material things. ^an8-77-9-1

They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. ^an8-77-9-2

But material things do come to them. ^an8-77-9-3

But they don’t become indulgent and fall into negligence regarding those material things. ^an8-77-9-4

This is called a mendicant who lives desiring material things. They don’t try hard, strive, and make an effort to get them. And when they acquire material things, they don’t become intoxicated and negligent. They haven’t fallen from the true teaching. ^an8-77-9-5

These eight individuals are found in the world.” ^an8-77-9-6

### Good Enough (Numbered Discourses 8.78) ^an8-78-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an8-78-1-1

“Reverends, a mendicant with six qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-78-1-2

What six? ^an8-78-1-3

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-1-4

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-1-5

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-1-6

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-1-7

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-78-1-8

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-1-9

A mendicant with these six qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-78-1-10

A mendicant with five qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-78-2-1

What five? ^an8-78-2-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-2-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-2-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-2-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-2-6

They’re a good speaker. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-78-2-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-2-8

A mendicant with these five qualities is good enough for themselves and others. ^an8-78-2-9

A mendicant with four qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-3-1

What four? ^an8-78-3-2

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-3-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-3-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-3-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-3-6

They’re not a good speaker. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-78-3-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-3-8

A mendicant with these four qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-3-9

A mendicant with four qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-78-4-1

What four? ^an8-78-4-2

A mendicant is quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-4-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-4-4

But they don’t examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-4-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-4-6

They’re a good speaker. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-78-4-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-4-8

A mendicant with these four qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-78-4-9

A mendicant with three qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-5-1

What three? ^an8-78-5-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-5-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-5-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-5-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-5-6

They’re not a good speaker. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-78-5-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-5-8

A mendicant with these three qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-5-9

A mendicant with three qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-78-6-1

What three? ^an8-78-6-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-6-3

They readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-6-4

But they don’t examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-6-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-6-6

They’re a good speaker. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-78-6-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-6-8

A mendicant with these three qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-78-6-9

A mendicant with two qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-7-1

What two? ^an8-78-7-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-7-3

And they don’t readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-7-4

They examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-7-5

Understanding the meaning and the teaching, they practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-7-6

They’re not a good speaker. Their voice isn’t polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning. ^an8-78-7-7

They don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-7-8

A mendicant with these two qualities is good enough for themselves but not for others. ^an8-78-7-9

A mendicant with two qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves. ^an8-78-8-1

What two? ^an8-78-8-2

A mendicant is not quick-witted when it comes to skillful teachings. ^an8-78-8-3

And they don’t readily memorize the teachings they’ve heard. ^an8-78-8-4

Nor do they examine the meaning of teachings they’ve memorized. ^an8-78-8-5

Not understanding the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an8-78-8-6

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well. Their voice is polished, clear, articulate, and expresses the meaning. ^an8-78-8-7

They educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. ^an8-78-8-8

A mendicant with these two qualities is good enough for others but not for themselves.” ^an8-78-8-9

### Decline (Numbered Discourses 8.79) ^an8-79-0

“These eight things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an8-79-1-1

What eight? ^an8-79-1-2

They relish work, talk, sleep, and company. They don’t guard the sense doors and they eat too much. They relish closeness and proliferation. ^an8-79-1-3

These eight things lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an8-79-1-4

These eight things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee. ^an8-79-2-1

What eight? ^an8-79-2-2

They don’t relish work, talk, sleep and company. They guard the sense doors, and they don’t eat too much. They don’t relish closeness and proliferation. ^an8-79-2-3

These eight things don’t lead to the decline of a mendicant trainee.” ^an8-79-2-4

### Grounds for Laziness and Arousing Energy (Numbered Discourses 8.80) ^an8-80-0

“Mendicants, there are eight grounds for laziness. ^an8-80-1-1

What eight? ^an8-80-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant has some work to do. ^an8-80-1-3

They think: ^an8-80-1-4

‘I have some work to do. But while doing it my body will get tired. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-1-5

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-1-6

This is the first ground for laziness. ^an8-80-1-7

Furthermore, a mendicant has done some work. ^an8-80-2-1

They think: ^an8-80-2-2

‘I’ve done some work. But while working my body got tired. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-2-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-2-4

This is the second ground for laziness. ^an8-80-2-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has to go on a journey. ^an8-80-3-1

They think: ^an8-80-3-2

‘I have to go on a journey. But while walking my body will get tired. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-3-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-3-4

This is the third ground for laziness. ^an8-80-3-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has gone on a journey. ^an8-80-4-1

They think: ^an8-80-4-2

‘I’ve gone on a journey. But while walking my body got tired. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-4-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-4-4

This is the fourth ground for laziness. ^an8-80-4-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has wandered for alms, but they didn’t get to fill up on as much food as they like, coarse or fine. ^an8-80-5-1

They think: ^an8-80-5-2

‘I’ve wandered for alms, but I didn’t get to fill up on as much food as I like, coarse or fine. My body is tired and unfit for work. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-5-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for achieving the unachieved, attaining the unattained, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-5-4

This is the fifth ground for laziness. ^an8-80-5-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has wandered for alms, and they got to fill up on as much food as they like, coarse or fine. ^an8-80-6-1

They think: ^an8-80-6-2

‘I’ve wandered for alms, and I got to fill up on as much food as I like, coarse or fine. My body is heavy, unfit for work, like I’ve just eaten a load of beans. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-6-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for achieving the unachieved, attaining the unattained, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-6-4

This is the sixth ground for laziness. ^an8-80-6-5

Furthermore, a mendicant feels a little sick. ^an8-80-7-1

They think: ^an8-80-7-2

‘I feel a little sick. Lying down would be good for me. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-7-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for achieving the unachieved, attaining the unattained, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-7-4

This is the seventh ground for laziness. ^an8-80-7-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has recently recovered from illness. ^an8-80-8-1

They think: ^an8-80-8-2

‘I’ve recently recovered from illness. My body is weak and unfit for work. I’d better have a lie down.’ ^an8-80-8-3

They lie down, and don’t rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-8-4

This is the eighth ground for laziness. ^an8-80-8-5

These are the eight grounds for laziness. ^an8-80-8-6

There are eight grounds for arousing energy. ^an8-80-9-1

What eight? ^an8-80-9-2

Firstly, a mendicant has some work to do. ^an8-80-9-3

They think: ^an8-80-9-4

‘I have some work to do. While working it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an8-80-9-5

They rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-9-6

This is the first ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-9-7

Furthermore, a mendicant has done some work. ^an8-80-10-1

They think: ^an8-80-10-2

‘I’ve done some work. While I was working I wasn’t able to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an8-80-10-3

They rouse up energy … ^an8-80-10-4

This is the second ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-10-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has to go on a journey. ^an8-80-11-1

They think: ^an8-80-11-2

‘I have to go on a journey. While walking it’s not easy to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy …’ … ^an8-80-11-3

This is the third ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-11-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has gone on a journey. ^an8-80-12-1

They think: ^an8-80-12-2

‘I’ve gone on a journey. While I was walking I wasn’t able to focus on the instructions of the Buddhas. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy …’ … ^an8-80-12-3

This is the fourth ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-12-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has wandered for alms, but they didn’t get to fill up on as much food as they like, coarse or fine. ^an8-80-13-1

They think: ^an8-80-13-2

‘I’ve wandered for alms, but I didn’t get to fill up on as much food as I like, rough or fine. My body is light and fit for work. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy …’ … ^an8-80-13-3

This is the fifth ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-13-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has wandered for alms, and they got to fill up on as much food as they like, coarse or fine. ^an8-80-14-1

They think: ^an8-80-14-2

‘I’ve wandered for alms, and I got to fill up on as much food as I like, coarse or fine. My body is strong and fit for work. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy …’ … ^an8-80-14-3

This is the sixth ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-14-4

Furthermore, a mendicant feels a little sick. ^an8-80-15-1

They think: ^an8-80-15-2

‘I feel a little sick. It’s possible this illness will worsen. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy …’ … ^an8-80-15-3

This is the seventh ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-15-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has recently recovered from illness. ^an8-80-16-1

They think: ^an8-80-16-2

‘I’ve recently recovered from illness. It’s possible the illness will come back. I’d better preemptively rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized.’ ^an8-80-16-3

They rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an8-80-16-4

This is the eighth ground for arousing energy. ^an8-80-16-5

These are the eight grounds for arousing energy.” ^an8-80-17-1

### Mindfulness and Situational Awareness (Numbered Discourses 8.81) ^an8-81-0

“Mendicants, when there is no mindfulness and situational awareness, one who lacks mindfulness and situational awareness has destroyed a vital condition for conscience and prudence. ^an8-81-1-1

When there is no conscience and prudence, one who lacks conscience and prudence has destroyed a vital condition for sense restraint. ^an8-81-1-2

When there is no sense restraint, one who lacks sense restraint has destroyed a vital condition for ethical conduct. ^an8-81-1-3

When there is no ethical conduct, one who lacks ethics has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an8-81-1-4

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an8-81-1-5

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an8-81-1-6

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an8-81-1-7

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an8-81-1-8

In the same way, when there is no mindfulness and situational awareness, one who lacks mindfulness and situational awareness has destroyed a vital condition for conscience and prudence. ^an8-81-1-9

When there is no conscience and prudence … ^an8-81-1-10

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an8-81-1-11

When there is mindfulness and situational awareness, one who has fulfilled mindfulness and situational awareness has fulfilled a vital condition for conscience and prudence. ^an8-81-2-1

When there is conscience and prudence, one who has fulfilled conscience and prudence has fulfilled a vital condition for sense restraint. ^an8-81-2-2

When there is sense restraint, one who has fulfilled sense restraint has fulfilled a vital condition for ethical conduct. ^an8-81-2-3

When there is ethical conduct, one who has fulfilled ethical conduct has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an8-81-2-4

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an8-81-2-5

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an8-81-2-6

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an8-81-2-7

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would grow to fullness. ^an8-81-2-8

In the same way, when there is mindfulness and situational awareness, one who has fulfilled mindfulness and situational awareness has fulfilled a vital condition for conscience and prudence. ^an8-81-2-9

When there is conscience and prudence … ^an8-81-2-10

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an8-81-2-11

### With Puṇṇiya (Numbered Discourses 8.82) ^an8-82-0

Then Venerable Puṇṇiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an8-82-1-1

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes the Realized One feels inspired to teach, and other times not?” ^an8-82-1-2

“Puṇṇiya, when a mendicant has faith but doesn’t approach, ^an8-82-1-3

the Realized One doesn’t feel inspired to teach. ^an8-82-1-4

But when a mendicant has faith and approaches, ^an8-82-1-5

the Realized One feels inspired to teach. ^an8-82-1-6

When a mendicant has faith and approaches, but doesn’t pay homage … ^an8-82-1-7

they pay homage, but don’t ask questions … ^an8-82-1-8

they ask questions, but don’t actively listen to the teaching … ^an8-82-1-9

they actively listen to the teaching, but don’t remember the teaching they’ve heard … ^an8-82-1-10

they remember the teaching they’ve heard, but don’t examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve remembered … ^an8-82-1-11

they examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve remembered, but, not having understood the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the principle of the teaching. ^an8-82-1-12

The Realized One doesn’t feel inspired to teach. ^an8-82-1-13

But when a mendicant has faith, approaches, pays homage, asks questions, actively listens to the teachings, remembers the teachings, examines the meaning, and practices in line with the principle of the teaching, ^an8-82-2-1

the Realized One feels inspired to teach. ^an8-82-2-2

When someone possesses these eight qualities, the Realized One feels totally inspired to teach.” ^an8-82-2-3

### Rooted (Numbered Discourses 8.83) ^an8-83-0

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an8-83-1-1

‘Reverends, all things have what as their root? What produces them? What is their origin? What is their meeting place? What is their chief? What is their ruler? What is their overseer? What is their core?’ How would you answer them?” ^an8-83-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an8-83-1-3

“Well then, mendicants, I will teach it. ^an8-83-2-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an8-83-2-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an8-83-2-3

The Buddha said this: ^an8-83-2-4

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an8-83-2-5

‘Reverends, all things have what as their root? What produces them? What is their origin? What is their meeting place? What is their chief? What is their ruler? What is their overseer? What is their core?’ ^an8-83-2-6

You should answer them: ^an8-83-2-7

‘Reverends, all things are rooted in desire. They are produced by application of mind. Contact is their origin. Feeling is their meeting place. Immersion is their chief. Mindfulness is their ruler. Wisdom is their overseer. Freedom is their core.’ ^an8-83-2-8

When questioned by wanderers of other religions, that’s how you should answer them.” ^an8-83-2-9

### A Master Thief (Numbered Discourses 8.84) ^an8-84-0

“Mendicants, a master thief with eight factors is soon executed, and doesn’t have long to live. ^an8-84-1-1

What eight? ^an8-84-1-2

He attacks unprovoked. He steals everything without exception. He kills a woman. He rapes a girl. He robs a monk. He robs the royal treasury. He works close to home. He’s not skilled at hiding his booty. ^an8-84-1-3

A master thief with these eight factors is soon executed, and doesn’t have long to live. ^an8-84-1-4
