A master thief with eight factors is not soon executed, and lives long. ^an8-84-2-1

What eight? ^an8-84-2-2

He doesn’t attack unprovoked. He doesn’t steal everything without exception. He doesn’t kill a woman. He doesn’t rape a girl. He doesn’t rob a monk. He doesn’t rob the royal treasury. He doesn’t work close to home. He’s skilled at hiding his booty. ^an8-84-2-3

A master thief with these eight factors is not soon executed, and lives long.” ^an8-84-2-4

### Terms for the Realized One (Numbered Discourses 8.85) ^an8-85-0

“‘Ascetic’ is a term for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an8-85-1-1

‘Brahmin’, ^an8-85-1-2

‘Knowledge Master’, ^an8-85-1-3

‘Healer’, ^an8-85-1-4

‘Unstained’, ^an8-85-1-5

‘Immaculate’, ^an8-85-1-6

‘Knower’, ^an8-85-1-7

and ‘Freed’ are terms for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an8-85-1-8

The supreme should be attained by an ascetic, ^an8-85-2-1

a brahmin who has lived the life; ^an8-85-2-2

it should be attained by a knowledge master, ^an8-85-2-3

a healer. ^an8-85-2-4

The supreme should be attained by the unstained, ^an8-85-3-1

stainless and pure; ^an8-85-3-2

it should be attained by a knower, ^an8-85-3-3

who is free. ^an8-85-3-4

I am victorious in battle! ^an8-85-4-1

Released, I release others from their chains. ^an8-85-4-2

I am a dragon completely tamed, ^an8-85-4-3

an adept, I am quenched.” ^an8-85-4-4

### With Nāgita (Numbered Discourses 8.86) ^an8-86-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a village of the Kosalan brahmins named Icchānaṅgala. ^an8-86-1-1

He stayed in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an8-86-1-2

The brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala heard: ^an8-86-1-3

“It seems the ascetic Gotama—a Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family—has arrived at Icchānaṅgala. He is staying in a forest near Icchānaṅgala. ^an8-86-1-4

He has this good reputation: ^an8-86-1-5

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ … ^an8-86-1-6

It’s good to see such perfected ones.” ^an8-86-1-7

Then, when the night had passed, they took abundant fresh and cooked foods and went to the forest near Icchānaṅgala, where they stood outside the gates making a colossal racket. ^an8-86-2-1

Now, at that time Venerable Nāgita was the Buddha’s attendant. ^an8-86-2-2

Then the Buddha said to Nāgita, ^an8-86-2-3

“Nāgita, who’s making that colossal racket? You’d think it was fishermen hauling in a catch!” ^an8-86-2-4

“Sir, it’s these brahmins and householders of Icchānaṅgala. They’ve brought abundant fresh and cooked foods, and they’re standing outside the gates wanting to offer it specially to the Buddha and the mendicant Saṅgha.” ^an8-86-2-5

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an8-86-2-6

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-2-7

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity.” ^an8-86-2-8

“Sir, may the Blessed One please relent now! May the Holy One relent! ^an8-86-3-1

Now is the time for the Buddha to relent. ^an8-86-3-2

Wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an8-86-3-3

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily and the water flows downhill. ^an8-86-3-4

In the same way, wherever the Buddha now goes, the brahmins and householders, and people of town and country will incline the same way. ^an8-86-3-5

Why is that? ^an8-86-3-6

Because of the Buddha’s ethics and wisdom.” ^an8-86-3-7

“Nāgita, may I never become famous. May fame not come to me. ^an8-86-4-1

There are those who can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-4-2

Let them enjoy the filthy, lazy pleasure of possessions, honor, and popularity. ^an8-86-4-3

Even some of the deities can’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-5-1

When you all come together to enjoy each other’s company, I think: ^an8-86-5-2

‘These venerables mustn’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-5-3

That must be because they come together to enjoy each other’s company.’ ^an8-86-5-4

Take mendicants I see poking each other with their fingers, giggling and playing together. ^an8-86-6-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-6-2

‘These venerables mustn’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-6-3

That must be because they’re poking each other with their fingers, giggling and playing together.’ ^an8-86-6-4

Take mendicants I see eat as much as they like until their bellies are full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing. ^an8-86-7-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-7-2

‘These venerables mustn’t get the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of awakening when they want, without trouble or difficulty like I can. ^an8-86-7-3

That must be because they eat as much as they like until their bellies are full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing.’ ^an8-86-7-4

Take a mendicant living within a village who I see sitting immersed in samādhi. ^an8-86-8-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-8-2

‘Now a monastery worker, a novice, or a fellow practitioner will make this venerable fall from immersion.’ ^an8-86-8-3

So I’m not pleased that that mendicant is living within a village. ^an8-86-8-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting nodding in meditation. ^an8-86-9-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-9-2

‘Now this venerable, having dispelled that sleepiness and weariness, will focus just on the unified perception of wilderness.’ ^an8-86-9-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an8-86-9-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting without being immersed in samādhi. ^an8-86-10-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-10-2

‘Now if this venerable’s mind is not immersed in samādhi they will immerse it; or if it is immersed in samādhi, they will preserve it.’ ^an8-86-10-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an8-86-10-4

Take a mendicant in the wilderness who I see sitting immersed in samādhi. ^an8-86-11-1

I think to myself: ^an8-86-11-2

‘Now this venerable will free the unfreed mind or preserve the freed mind.’ ^an8-86-11-3

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an8-86-11-4

Take a mendicant who I see living within a village receiving robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an8-86-12-1

Enjoying possessions, honor, and popularity they neglect retreat, and they neglect remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an8-86-12-2

They come down to villages, towns and capital cities and make their homes there. ^an8-86-12-3

So I’m not pleased that that mendicant is living within a village. ^an8-86-12-4

Take a mendicant who I see in the wilderness receiving robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an8-86-13-1

Fending off possessions, honor, and popularity they don’t neglect retreat, and they don’t neglect remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an8-86-13-2

So I’m pleased that that mendicant is living in the wilderness. ^an8-86-13-3

Nāgita, when I’m walking along a road and I don’t see anyone ahead or behind I feel relaxed, even if I need to urinate or defecate.” ^an8-86-14-1

### Turning the Bowl Upside Down (Numbered Discourses 8.87) ^an8-87-0

“Mendicants, the Saṅgha may, if it wishes, turn the bowl upside down for a lay follower on eight grounds. ^an8-87-1-1

What eight? ^an8-87-1-2

They try to prevent the mendicants from getting material things. They try to harm mendicants. They try to drive mendicants from a monastery. They insult and abuse mendicants. They divide mendicants against each other. They criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an8-87-1-3

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, turn the bowl upside down for a lay follower on these eight grounds. ^an8-87-1-4

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, turn the bowl upright for a lay follower on eight grounds. ^an8-87-2-1

What eight? ^an8-87-2-2

They don’t try to prevent the mendicants from getting material things. They don’t try to harm mendicants. They don’t try to drive mendicants from a monastery. They don’t insult and abuse mendicants. They don’t divide mendicants against each other. They don’t criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. ^an8-87-2-3

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, turn the bowl upright for a lay follower on these eight grounds.” ^an8-87-2-4

### A Proclamation of No Confidence (Numbered Discourses 8.88) ^an8-88-0

“Mendicants, the lay followers may, if they wish, make a proclamation of no confidence in a mendicant who has eight qualities. ^an8-88-1-1

What eight? ^an8-88-1-2

They try to prevent laypeople from getting material things. They try to harm laypeople. They insult and abuse laypeople. They divide lay people against each other. They criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. They’re seen at an inappropriate place for collecting alms. ^an8-88-1-3

The lay followers may, if they wish, make a proclamation of no confidence in a mendicant who possesses these eight qualities. ^an8-88-1-4

The lay followers may, if they wish, make a proclamation of confidence in a mendicant who has eight qualities. ^an8-88-2-1

What eight? ^an8-88-2-2

They don’t try to prevent laypeople from getting material things. They don’t try to harm laypeople. They don’t insult and abuse laypeople. They don’t divide laypeople against each other. They don’t criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. They’re not seen at an inappropriate place for collecting alms. ^an8-88-2-3

The lay followers may, if they wish, make a proclamation of confidence in a mendicant who possesses these eight qualities.” ^an8-88-2-4

### Reconciliation (Numbered Discourses 8.89) ^an8-89-0

“Mendicants, the Saṅgha may, if it wishes, perform an act requiring that a mendicant who has eight qualities should pursue reconciliation. ^an8-89-1-1

What eight? ^an8-89-1-2

They try to prevent laypeople from getting material things. They try to harm laypeople. They insult and abuse lay people. They divide laypeople against each other. They criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. They don’t keep a legitimate promise made to a layperson. ^an8-89-1-3

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, perform an act requiring that a mendicant who has eight qualities should pursue reconciliation. ^an8-89-1-4

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, revoke the act requiring that a mendicant who has eight qualities should pursue reconciliation. ^an8-89-2-1

What eight? ^an8-89-2-2

They don’t try to prevent the laypeople from getting material things. They don’t try to harm laypeople. They don’t insult and abuse laypeople. They don’t divide laypeople against each other. They don’t criticize the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha. They keep a legitimate promise made to a layperson. ^an8-89-2-3

The Saṅgha may, if it wishes, revoke the act requiring that a mendicant who has eight qualities should pursue reconciliation.” ^an8-89-2-4

### Proper Behavior in a Case of Aggravated Misconduct (Numbered Discourses 8.90) ^an8-90-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant who has been convicted of aggravated misconduct must behave themselves properly in eight respects. ^an8-90-1-1

They must not perform an ordination, give dependence, or be attended by a novice. They must not consent to being appointed as adviser for nuns, and if they are appointed they should not give such advice. They must not consent to any Saṅgha appointment. They must not be put in a position of seniority. They must not resolve others from any offense similar to that which they have transgressed. ^an8-90-1-2

A mendicant who has been convicted of aggravated misconduct must behave themselves properly in these eight respects.” ^an8-90-1-3

### Untitled Discourses With Various Laywomen on the Sabbath (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-91-117-0

And then the lay woman Bojjhā … Sirīmā … Padumā … Sutanā … Manujā … Uttarā … Muttā … Khemā … Somā … Rucī … Cundī … Bimbī … Sumanā … Mallikā … Tissā … Tissamātā … Soṇā … Soṇā’s mother … Kāṇā … Kāṇamātā … Uttarā, Nanda’s mother … Visākhā Migāra’s mother … the lay woman Khujjuttarā … the lay woman Sāmāvatī … Suppavāsā the Koliyan … the lay woman Suppiyā … the housewife Nakula’s mother … ^an8-91-117-1-1

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (1st) (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-118-0

“For insight into greed, eight things should be developed. ^an8-118-1-1

What eight? ^an8-118-1-2

Right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion. ^an8-118-1-3

For insight into greed, these eight things should be developed.” ^an8-118-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-119-0

“For insight into greed, eight things should be developed. ^an8-119-1-1

What eight? ^an8-119-1-2

Perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. ^an8-119-1-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-119-1-4

Perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. … ^an8-119-1-5

Not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. … ^an8-119-1-6

Not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. … ^an8-119-1-7

Not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, blue, with blue color and blue appearance. … ^an8-119-1-8

yellow … ^an8-119-1-9

red … ^an8-119-1-10

Not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an8-119-1-11

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an8-119-1-12

For insight into greed, these eight things should be developed.” ^an8-119-1-13

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-120-0

“For insight into greed, eight things should be developed. ^an8-120-1-1

What eight? ^an8-120-1-2

Having physical form, they see forms … not perceiving form internally, they see forms externally … they’re focused only on beauty … going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space … going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness … going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness … going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception … going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling … ^an8-120-1-3

For insight into greed, these eight things should be developed.” ^an8-120-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-121-147-0

“For the complete understanding of greed … ^an8-121-147-1-1

complete ending … ^an8-121-147-1-2

giving up … ^an8-121-147-1-3

ending … ^an8-121-147-1-4

vanishing … ^an8-121-147-1-5

fading away … ^an8-121-147-1-6

cessation … ^an8-121-147-1-7

giving away … ^an8-121-147-1-8

letting go … ^an8-121-147-1-9

these eight things should be developed.” ^an8-121-147-1-10

### Untitled Discourses on Hate, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 8) ^an8-148-627-0

“Of hate … ^an8-148-627-1-1

delusion … ^an8-148-627-1-2

anger … ^an8-148-627-1-3

acrimony … ^an8-148-627-1-4

disdain … ^an8-148-627-1-5

contempt … ^an8-148-627-1-6

jealousy … ^an8-148-627-1-7

stinginess … ^an8-148-627-1-8

deceitfulness … ^an8-148-627-1-9

deviousness … ^an8-148-627-1-10

obstinacy … ^an8-148-627-1-11

aggression … ^an8-148-627-1-12

conceit … ^an8-148-627-1-13

arrogance … ^an8-148-627-1-14

vanity … ^an8-148-627-1-15

for insight into negligence … ^an8-148-627-1-16

complete understanding … ^an8-148-627-1-17

complete ending … ^an8-148-627-1-18

giving up … ^an8-148-627-1-19

ending … ^an8-148-627-1-20

vanishing … ^an8-148-627-1-21

fading away … ^an8-148-627-1-22

cessation … ^an8-148-627-1-23

giving away … ^an8-148-627-1-24

letting go of negligence ^an8-148-627-1-25

these eight things should be developed.” ^an8-148-627-1-26

The Book of the Eights is finished. ^an8-148-627-1-28

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 9 ^an9-0

### Awakening (Numbered Discourses 9.1) ^an9-1-0

So I have heard. ^an9-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an9-1-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an9-1-1-3

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an9-1-2-1

‘Reverends, what is the vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening?’ How would you answer them?” ^an9-1-2-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. … The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an9-1-2-3

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an9-1-3-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an9-1-3-2

The Buddha said this: ^an9-1-3-3

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an9-1-4-1

‘Reverends, what is the vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening?’ You should answer them: ^an9-1-4-2

‘It’s when a mendicant has good friends, companions, and associates. ^an9-1-5-1

This is the first vital condition for the development of the awakening factors. ^an9-1-5-2

Furthermore, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an9-1-6-1

This is the second vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening. ^an9-1-6-2

Furthermore, a mendicant gets to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when they want, without trouble or difficulty. That is, talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an9-1-7-1

This is the third vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening. ^an9-1-7-2

Furthermore, a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an9-1-8-1

This is the fourth vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening. ^an9-1-8-2

Furthermore, a mendicant is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an9-1-9-1

This is the fifth vital condition for the development of the qualities on the same side as awakening.’ ^an9-1-9-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-1-10-1

be ethical … ^an9-1-10-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-1-11-1

take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart … ^an9-1-11-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-1-12-1

live with energy roused up … ^an9-1-12-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-1-13-1

be wise … ^an9-1-13-2

But then, a mendicant grounded on these five things should develop four further things. ^an9-1-14-1

They should develop the perception of ugliness to give up greed, love to give up hate, mindfulness of breathing to cut off thinking, and perception of impermanence to uproot the conceit ‘I am’. ^an9-1-14-2

When you perceive impermanence, the perception of not-self becomes stabilized. ^an9-1-14-3

Perceiving not-self, you uproot the conceit ‘I am’ and attain extinguishment in this very life.” ^an9-1-14-4

### Supported (Numbered Discourses 9.2) ^an9-2-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an9-2-1-1

“Sir, they speak of being ‘supported’. ^an9-2-1-2

How is a mendicant who is supported defined?” ^an9-2-1-3

“Mendicant, if a mendicant supported by faith gives up the unskillful and develops the skillful, the unskillful is actually given up by them. ^an9-2-1-4

If a mendicant supported by conscience … ^an9-2-1-5

If a mendicant supported by prudence … ^an9-2-1-6

If a mendicant supported by energy … ^an9-2-1-7

If a mendicant supported by wisdom gives up the unskillful and develops the skillful, the unskillful is actually given up by them. ^an9-2-1-8

What’s been given up is completely given up when it has been given up by seeing with noble wisdom. ^an9-2-1-9

But then, a mendicant grounded on these five things should rely on four things. ^an9-2-2-1

What four? ^an9-2-2-2

After appraisal, a mendicant uses some things, endures some things, avoids some things, and gets rid of some things. ^an9-2-2-3

That’s how a mendicant is supported.” ^an9-2-2-4

### With Meghiya (Numbered Discourses 9.3) ^an9-3-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Cālikā, on the Cālikā mountain. ^an9-3-1-1

Now, at that time Venerable Meghiya was the Buddha’s attendant. ^an9-3-1-2

Then Venerable Meghiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^an9-3-1-3

“Sir, I’d like to enter Jantu village for alms.” ^an9-3-1-4

“Please, Meghiya, go at your convenience.” ^an9-3-1-5

Then Meghiya robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Jantu village for alms. ^an9-3-2-1

After the meal, on his return from almsround in Jantu village, he went to the shore of Kimikālā river. ^an9-3-2-2

As he was going for a walk along the shore of the river he saw a lovely and delightful mango grove. ^an9-3-2-3

It occurred to him, ^an9-3-2-4

“Oh, this mango grove is lovely and delightful! This is good enough for striving for a gentleman wanting to strive. ^an9-3-2-5

If the Buddha allows me, I’ll come back to this mango grove to meditate.” ^an9-3-2-6

Then Venerable Meghiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened, adding, ^an9-3-3-1

“If the Buddha allows me, I’ll go back to that mango grove to meditate.” ^an9-3-4-8

“We’re alone, Meghiya. Wait until another mendicant comes.” ^an9-3-4-9

For a second time Meghiya said to the Buddha, ^an9-3-5-1

“Sir, the Buddha has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement. ^an9-3-5-2

But I have. ^an9-3-5-3

If you allow me, I’ll go back to that mango grove to meditate.” ^an9-3-5-4

“We’re alone, Meghiya. Wait until another mendicant comes.” ^an9-3-5-5

For a third time Meghiya said to the Buddha, ^an9-3-6-1

“Sir, the Buddha has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement. ^an9-3-6-2

But I have. ^an9-3-6-3

If you allow me, I’ll go back to that mango grove to meditate.” ^an9-3-6-4

“Meghiya, since you speak of striving in meditation, what can I say? ^an9-3-6-5

Please, Meghiya, go at your convenience.” ^an9-3-6-6

Then Meghiya rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right. Then he went to that mango grove, and, having plunged deep into it, sat at the root of a certain tree for the day’s meditation. ^an9-3-7-1

But while Meghiya was meditating in that mango grove he was beset mostly by three kinds of bad, unskillful thoughts, namely, ^an9-3-7-2

sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts. ^an9-3-7-3

Then he thought, ^an9-3-7-4

“Oh lord, how incredible, how amazing! ^an9-3-7-5

I’ve gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, ^an9-3-7-6

but I’m still harassed by these three kinds of bad, unskillful thoughts: ^an9-3-7-7

sensual, malicious, and cruel thoughts.” ^an9-3-7-8

Then Venerable Meghiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an9-3-8-1

“Meghiya, when the heart’s release is not ripe, five things help it ripen. ^an9-3-10-1

What five? ^an9-3-10-2

Firstly, a mendicant has good friends, companions, and associates. ^an9-3-10-3

This is the first thing … ^an9-3-10-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an9-3-11-1

This is the second thing … ^an9-3-11-2

Furthermore, a mendicant gets to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when they want, without trouble or difficulty. That is, talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an9-3-12-1

This is the third thing … ^an9-3-12-2

Furthermore, a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an9-3-13-1

This is the fourth thing … ^an9-3-13-2

Furthermore, a mendicant is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an9-3-14-1

This is the fifth thing that, when the heart’s release is not ripe, helps it ripen. ^an9-3-14-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-3-15-1

be ethical … ^an9-3-15-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to ^an9-3-16-1

take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart … ^an9-3-16-2

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to be energetic … ^an9-3-17-1

A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to be wise … ^an9-3-18-1

But then, a mendicant grounded on these five things should develop four further things. ^an9-3-19-1

They should develop the perception of ugliness to give up greed, love to give up hate, mindfulness of breathing to cut off thinking, and perception of impermanence to uproot the conceit ‘I am’. ^an9-3-19-2

When you perceive impermanence, the perception of not-self becomes stabilized. ^an9-3-19-3

Perceiving not-self, you uproot the conceit ‘I am’ and attain extinguishment in this very life.” ^an9-3-19-4

### With Nandaka (Numbered Discourses 9.4) ^an9-4-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an9-4-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Nandaka was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk. ^an9-4-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall. He stood outside the door waiting for the talk to end. ^an9-4-1-3

When he knew the talk had ended he cleared his throat and knocked on the door-panel. ^an9-4-1-4

The mendicants opened the door for the Buddha, ^an9-4-1-5

and he entered the assembly hall, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an9-4-2-1

He said to Nandaka, ^an9-4-2-2

“Nandaka, that was a long exposition of the teaching you gave to the mendicants. ^an9-4-2-3

My back was aching while I stood outside the door waiting for the talk to end.” ^an9-4-2-4

When he said this, Nandaka felt embarrassed and said to the Buddha, ^an9-4-3-1

“Sir, we didn’t know that the Buddha was standing outside the door. ^an9-4-3-2

If we’d known, I wouldn’t have said so much.” ^an9-4-3-3

Then the Buddha, knowing that Nandaka was embarrassed, said to him, ^an9-4-4-1

“Good, good, Nandaka! ^an9-4-4-2

It’s appropriate for gentlemen like you, who have gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, to sit together for a Dhamma talk. ^an9-4-4-3

When you’re sitting together you should do one of two things: ^an9-4-4-4

discuss the teachings or keep noble silence. ^an9-4-4-5

Nandaka, a mendicant is faithful but not ethical. ^an9-4-4-6

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an9-4-4-7

and should fulfill it, thinking, ^an9-4-4-8

‘How can I become faithful and ethical?’ ^an9-4-4-9

When a mendicant is faithful and ethical, they’re complete in that respect. ^an9-4-4-10

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but does not get internal serenity of heart. ^an9-4-5-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an9-4-5-2

and should fulfill it, thinking, ^an9-4-5-3

‘How can I become faithful and ethical and get internal serenity of heart?’ ^an9-4-5-4

When a mendicant is faithful and ethical and gets internal serenity of heart, they’re complete in that respect. ^an9-4-5-5

A mendicant is faithful, ethical, and gets internal serenity of heart, but they don’t get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an9-4-6-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect. ^an9-4-6-2

Suppose, Nandaka, there was a four-footed animal ^an9-4-6-3

that was lame and disabled. ^an9-4-6-4

It would be incomplete in that respect. ^an9-4-6-5

In the same way, a mendicant is faithful, ethical, and gets internal serenity of heart, but they don’t get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an9-4-6-6

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an9-4-6-7

and should fulfill it, thinking, ^an9-4-6-8

‘How can I become faithful and ethical and get internal serenity of heart and get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles?’ ^an9-4-6-9

When a mendicant is faithful and ethical and gets internal serenity of heart and gets the higher wisdom of discernment of principles, they’re complete in that respect.” ^an9-4-7-1

That is what the Buddha said. ^an9-4-7-2

When he had spoken, the Holy One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling. ^an9-4-7-3

Then soon after the Buddha left, Venerable Nandaka said to the mendicants, ^an9-4-8-1

“Just now, reverends, the Buddha explained a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure in four statements, before getting up from his seat and entering his dwelling: ^an9-4-8-2

‘Nandaka, a mendicant is faithful but not ethical. ^an9-4-8-3

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an9-4-8-4

and should fulfill it, thinking, ^an9-4-8-5

“How can I become faithful and ethical?” ^an9-4-8-6

When a mendicant is faithful and ethical, they’re complete in that respect. ^an9-4-8-7

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but does not get internal serenity of heart. … ^an9-4-8-8

They get internal serenity of heart, but they don’t get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an9-4-8-9

So they’re incomplete in that respect. ^an9-4-8-10

Suppose, Nandaka, there was a four-footed animal that was lame and disabled. ^an9-4-8-11

It would be incomplete in that respect. ^an9-4-8-12

In the same way, a mendicant is faithful, ethical, and gets internal serenity of heart, but they don’t get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles. ^an9-4-8-13

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an9-4-8-14

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an9-4-8-15

“How can I become faithful and ethical and get internal serenity of heart and get the higher wisdom of discernment of principles?” ^an9-4-8-16

When a mendicant is faithful and ethical and gets internal serenity of heart and gets the higher wisdom of discernment of principles, ^an9-4-8-17

they’re complete in that respect.’ ^an9-4-8-18

Reverends, there are these five benefits of listening to the teachings at the right time and discussing the teachings at the right time. ^an9-4-9-1

What five? ^an9-4-9-2

Firstly, a mendicant proclaims a teaching to the mendicants that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And they reveal a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an9-4-9-3

Whenever they do this, they become liked and approved by the Teacher, respected and admired. ^an9-4-9-4

This is the first benefit … ^an9-4-9-5

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches the mendicants the Dhamma … ^an9-4-10-1

Whenever they do this, they feel inspired by the meaning and the teaching in that Dhamma. ^an9-4-10-2

This is the second benefit … ^an9-4-10-3

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches the mendicants the Dhamma … ^an9-4-11-1

Whenever they do this, they see the meaning of a deep saying in that Dhamma with penetrating wisdom. ^an9-4-11-2

This is the third benefit … ^an9-4-11-3

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches the mendicants the Dhamma … ^an9-4-12-1

Whenever they do this, their spiritual companions esteem them more highly, thinking, ^an9-4-12-2

‘For sure this venerable has attained or will attain.’ ^an9-4-12-3

This is the fourth benefit … ^an9-4-12-4

Furthermore, a mendicant teaches the mendicants the Dhamma … ^an9-4-13-1

Whenever they do this, there may be trainee mendicants present, who haven’t achieved their heart’s desire, but live aspiring to the supreme sanctuary from the yoke. Hearing that teaching, they rouse energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized. ^an9-4-13-2

There may be perfected mendicants present, who have ended the defilements, completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and are rightly freed through enlightenment. Hearing that teaching, they simply live happily in this life. ^an9-4-13-3

This is the fifth benefit … ^an9-4-13-4

These are the five benefits of listening to the teachings at the right time and discussing the teachings at the right time.” ^an9-4-13-5

### Powers (Numbered Discourses 9.5) ^an9-5-0

“Mendicants, there are these four powers. ^an9-5-1-1

What four? ^an9-5-1-2

The powers of wisdom, energy, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an9-5-1-3

And what is the power of wisdom? ^an9-5-1-4

One has clearly seen and clearly contemplated with wisdom those qualities that are skillful and considered to be skillful; those that are unskillful … blameworthy … blameless … dark … bright … to be cultivated … not to be cultivated … not worthy of the noble ones … worthy of the noble ones and considered to be worthy of the noble ones. ^an9-5-1-5

This is called the power of wisdom. ^an9-5-1-6

And what is the power of energy? ^an9-5-2-1

One generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives to give up those qualities that are unskillful and considered to be unskillful; those that are blameworthy … dark … not to be cultivated … not worthy of the noble ones and considered to be not worthy of the noble ones. ^an9-5-2-2

One generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives to gain those qualities that are skillful and considered to be skillful; those that are blameless … bright … to be cultivated … worthy of the noble ones and considered to be worthy of the noble ones. ^an9-5-2-3

This is called the power of energy. ^an9-5-2-4

And what is the power of blamelessness? ^an9-5-3-1

It’s when a noble disciple has blameless conduct by way of body, speech, and mind. ^an9-5-3-2

This is called the power of blamelessness. ^an9-5-3-3

And what is the power of inclusiveness? ^an9-5-4-1

There are these four ways of being inclusive. ^an9-5-4-2

Giving, kindly words, taking care, and equality. ^an9-5-4-3

The best of gifts is the gift of the teaching. ^an9-5-4-4

The best sort of kindly speech is to teach the Dhamma again and again to someone who is engaged and who actively listens. ^an9-5-4-5

The best way of taking care is to encourage, settle, and ground the unfaithful in faith, the unethical in ethics, the stingy in generosity, and the ignorant in wisdom. ^an9-5-4-6

The best kind of equality is the equality of a stream-enterer with another stream-enterer, a once-returner with another once-returner, a non-returner with another non-returner, and a perfected one with another perfected one. ^an9-5-4-7

This is called the power of inclusiveness. ^an9-5-4-8

These are the four powers. ^an9-5-4-9

A noble disciple who has these four powers has got past five fears. ^an9-5-5-1

What five? ^an9-5-5-2

Fear regarding livelihood, disrepute, feeling insecure in an assembly, death, and bad rebirth. ^an9-5-5-3

Then that noble disciple reflects: ^an9-5-5-4

‘I have no fear regarding livelihood. ^an9-5-5-5

Why would I be afraid of that? ^an9-5-5-6

I have these four powers: ^an9-5-5-7

the powers of wisdom, energy, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an9-5-5-8

A witless person might fear for their livelihood. ^an9-5-5-9

A lazy person might fear for their livelihood. ^an9-5-5-10

Someone who does blameworthy things by way of body, speech, and mind might fear for their livelihood. ^an9-5-5-11

Someone who does not include others might fear for their livelihood. ^an9-5-5-12

I have no fear of disrepute … ^an9-5-5-13

I have no fear about feeling insecure in an assembly … ^an9-5-5-14

I have no fear of death … ^an9-5-5-15

I have no fear of a bad rebirth. ^an9-5-5-16

Why would I be afraid of that? ^an9-5-5-17

I have these four powers: ^an9-5-5-18

the powers of wisdom, energy, blamelessness, and inclusiveness. ^an9-5-5-19

A witless person might be afraid of a bad rebirth. ^an9-5-5-20

A lazy person might be afraid of a bad rebirth. ^an9-5-5-21

Someone who does blameworthy things by way of body, speech, and mind might be afraid of a bad rebirth. ^an9-5-5-22

Someone who does not include others might be afraid of a bad rebirth.’ ^an9-5-5-23

A noble disciple who has these four powers has got past these five fears.” ^an9-5-5-24

### Association (Numbered Discourses 9.6) ^an9-6-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an9-6-1-1

“Reverends, you should distinguish two two kinds of individual: ^an9-6-2-1

those you should associate with, and those you shouldn’t associate with. ^an9-6-2-2

You should distinguish two kinds of robes: ^an9-6-2-3

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear. ^an9-6-2-4

You should distinguish two kinds of almsfood: ^an9-6-2-5

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat. ^an9-6-2-6

You should distinguish two kinds of lodging: ^an9-6-2-7

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an9-6-2-8

You should distinguish two kinds of village or town: ^an9-6-2-9

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an9-6-2-10

You should distinguish two kinds of country: ^an9-6-2-11

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an9-6-2-12

You should distinguish two two kinds of individual: ^an9-6-3-1

those you should associate with, and those you shouldn’t associate with.’ ^an9-6-3-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-3-3

Well, should you know of an individual: ^an9-6-3-4

‘When I associate with this individual, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline. ^an9-6-3-5

And the necessities of life that a renunciate requires—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—are hard to come by. ^an9-6-3-6

And the goal of the ascetic life for which I went forth from the lay life to homelessness is not being fully developed.’ In this case you should leave that individual at that very time of the day or night, without asking. You shouldn’t follow them. ^an9-6-3-7

Whereas, should you know of an individual: ^an9-6-4-1

‘When I associate with this individual, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline. ^an9-6-4-2

But the necessities of life that a renunciate requires—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—are easy to come by. ^an9-6-4-3

However, the goal of the ascetic life for which I went forth from the lay life to homelessness is not being fully developed.’ In this case you should leave that individual after reflecting, without asking. You shouldn’t follow them. ^an9-6-4-4

Well, should you know of an individual: ^an9-6-5-1

‘When I associate with this individual, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow. ^an9-6-5-2

And the necessities of life that a renunciate requires—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—are hard to come by. ^an9-6-5-3

But the goal of the ascetic life for which I went forth from the lay life to homelessness is being fully developed.’ In this case you should follow that individual after appraisal. You shouldn’t leave them. ^an9-6-5-4

Whereas, should you know of an individual: ^an9-6-6-1

‘When I associate with this individual, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow. ^an9-6-6-2

And the necessities of life that a renunciate requires—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—are easy to come by. ^an9-6-6-3

And the goal of the ascetic life for which I went forth from the lay life to homelessness is being fully developed.’ In this case you should follow that individual. You shouldn’t leave them, even if they send you away. ^an9-6-6-4

‘You should distinguish two kinds of individual: ^an9-6-6-5

those you should associate with, and those you shouldn’t associate with.’ ^an9-6-6-6

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-6-6-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of robes: ^an9-6-7-1

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear.’ ^an9-6-7-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-7-3

Well, should you know of a robe: ^an9-6-7-4

‘When I wear this robe, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not wear that kind of robe. ^an9-6-7-5

Whereas, should you know of a robe: ^an9-6-7-6

‘When I wear this robe, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should wear that kind of robe. ^an9-6-7-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of robes: ^an9-6-7-8

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear.’ ^an9-6-7-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-6-7-10

‘You should distinguish two kinds of almsfood: ^an9-6-8-1

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat.’ ^an9-6-8-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-8-3

Well, should you know of almsfood: ^an9-6-8-4

‘When I eat this almsfood, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not eat that kind of almsfood. ^an9-6-8-5

Whereas, should you know of almsfood: ^an9-6-8-6

‘When I eat this almsfood, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should eat that kind of almsfood. ^an9-6-8-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of almsfood: ^an9-6-8-8

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat.’ ^an9-6-8-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-6-8-10

‘You should distinguish two kinds of lodging: ^an9-6-9-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-9-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-9-3

Well, should you know of a lodging: ^an9-6-9-4

‘When I frequent this lodging, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of lodging. ^an9-6-9-5

Whereas, should you know of a lodging: ^an9-6-9-6

‘When I frequent this lodging, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of lodging. ^an9-6-9-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of lodging: ^an9-6-9-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-9-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-6-9-10

‘You should distinguish two kinds of village or town: ^an9-6-10-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-10-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-10-3

Well, should you know of a village or town: ^an9-6-10-4

‘When I frequent this village or town, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of village or town. ^an9-6-10-5

Whereas, should you know of a village or town: ^an9-6-10-6

‘When I frequent this village or town, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of village or town. ^an9-6-10-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of village or town: ^an9-6-10-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-10-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-6-10-10

‘You should distinguish two kinds of country: ^an9-6-11-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-11-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-6-11-3

Well, should you know of a country: ^an9-6-11-4

‘When I frequent this country, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of country. ^an9-6-11-5

Whereas, should you know of a country: ^an9-6-11-6

‘When I frequent this country, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of country. ^an9-6-11-7

‘You should distinguish two kinds of country: ^an9-6-11-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an9-6-11-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an9-6-11-10

### With Sutavā the Wanderer (Numbered Discourses 9.7) ^an9-7-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an9-7-1-1

Then the wanderer Sutavā went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an9-7-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an9-7-1-3

“Sir, this one time the Buddha was staying right here in Rājagaha, the Mountainfold. ^an9-7-2-1

There I heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an9-7-2-2

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in five respects. ^an9-7-2-3

A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately take the life of a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they did as a layperson.’ ^an9-7-2-4

I trust I properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that from the Buddha?” ^an9-7-2-5

“Indeed, Sutavā, you properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that. ^an9-7-3-1

In the past, as today, I say this: ^an9-7-3-2

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in nine respects. ^an9-7-3-3

A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately take the life of a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they did as a layperson. And they can’t make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, or cowardice.’ ^an9-7-3-4

In the past, as today, I say this: ^an9-7-3-5

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in these nine respects.’” ^an9-7-3-6

### With the Wanderer Sajjha (Numbered Discourses 9.8) ^an9-8-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain. ^an9-8-1-1

Then the wanderer Sajjha went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an9-8-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an9-8-1-3

“Sir, this one time the Buddha was staying right here in Rājagaha, the Mountainfold. ^an9-8-2-1

There I heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha: ^an9-8-2-2

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in five respects. ^an9-8-2-3

A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately take the life of a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they did as a layperson.’ ^an9-8-2-4

I trust I properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that from the Buddha?” ^an9-8-2-5

“Indeed, Sajjha, you properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that. ^an9-8-3-1

In the past, as today, I say this: ^an9-8-3-2

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in nine respects. ^an9-8-3-3

A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately kill a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they used to as a layperson. And they can’t abandon the Buddha, the teaching, the Saṅgha, or the training.’ ^an9-8-3-4

In the past, as today, I say this: ^an9-8-3-5

‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in these nine respects.’” ^an9-8-3-6

### Individuals (Numbered Discourses 9.9) ^an9-9-0

“Mendicants, these nine individuals are found in the world. ^an9-9-1-1

What nine? ^an9-9-1-2

The perfected one and the one practicing for perfection. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. And the ordinary person. ^an9-9-1-3

These are the nine individuals found in the world.” ^an9-9-1-4

### Worthy of Offerings Dedicated to the Gods (Numbered Discourses 9.10) ^an9-10-0

“Mendicants, these nine individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an9-10-1-1

What nine? ^an9-10-1-2

The perfected one and the one practicing for perfection. The non-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of non-return. The once-returner and the one practicing to realize the fruit of once-return. The stream-enterer and the one practicing to realize the fruit of stream-entry. And a lamb of the flock. ^an9-10-1-3

These are the nine individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an9-10-1-4

### Sāriputta’s Lion’s Roar (Numbered Discourses 9.11) ^an9-11-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an9-11-1-1

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an9-11-1-2

“Sir, I have completed the rainy season residence at Sāvatthī. ^an9-11-1-3

I wish to leave to wander the countryside.” ^an9-11-1-4

“Please, Sāriputta, go at your convenience.” ^an9-11-1-5

Then Sāriputta rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an9-11-1-6

And then, not long after Sāriputta had left, a certain monk said to the Buddha, ^an9-11-1-7

“Sir, Venerable Sāriputta attacked me and left without saying sorry.” ^an9-11-1-8

So the Buddha addressed one of the monks, ^an9-11-1-9

“Please, monk, in my name tell Sāriputta that ^an9-11-1-10

the teacher summons him.” ^an9-11-1-11

“Yes, sir,” that monk replied. He went to Sāriputta and said to him, ^an9-11-1-12

“Reverend Sāriputta, the teacher summons you.” ^an9-11-1-13

“Yes, reverend,” Sāriputta replied. ^an9-11-1-14

Now at that time the Venerables Mahāmoggallāna and Ānanda, taking a latchkey, went from dwelling to dwelling, saying: ^an9-11-2-1

“Come forth, venerables! Come forth, venerables! ^an9-11-2-2

Now Venerable Sāriputta will roar his lion’s roar in the presence of the Buddha!” ^an9-11-2-3

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an9-11-2-4

“Sāriputta, one of your spiritual companions has made this complaint: ^an9-11-2-5

‘Venerable Sāriputta attacked me and left without saying sorry.’” ^an9-11-2-6

“Sir, someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-3-1

Suppose they were to toss both clean and unclean things on the earth, like feces, urine, spit, pus, and blood. The earth isn’t horrified, repelled, and disgusted because of this. ^an9-11-4-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like the earth, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-4-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-4-3

Suppose they were to wash both clean and unclean things in water, like feces, urine, spit, pus, and blood. The water isn’t horrified, repelled, and disgusted because of this. ^an9-11-5-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like water, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-5-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-5-3

Suppose a fire were to burn both clean and unclean things, like feces, urine, spit, pus, and blood. The fire isn’t horrified, repelled, and disgusted because of this. ^an9-11-6-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like fire, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-6-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-6-3

Suppose the wind was to blow on both clean and unclean things, like feces, urine, spit, pus, and blood. The wind isn’t horrified, repelled, and disgusted because of this. ^an9-11-7-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like the wind, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-7-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-7-3

Suppose a rag was to wipe up both clean and unclean things, like feces, urine, spit, pus, and blood. The rag isn’t horrified, repelled, and disgusted because of this. ^an9-11-8-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like a rag, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-8-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-8-3

Suppose a boy or girl of a corpse-worker tribe, holding a pot and clad in rags, were to enter a town or village. They’d enter with a humble mind. ^an9-11-9-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like a boy or girl of a corpse-worker tribe, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-9-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-9-3

Suppose there was a bull with his horns cut, gentle, well tamed and well trained. He’d wander from street to street and square to square without hurting anyone with his feet or horns. ^an9-11-10-1

In the same way, I live with a heart like a bull with horns cut, abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-11-10-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-10-3

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and had bathed their head. If the carcass of a snake or a dog or a human were hung around their neck, they’d be horrified, repelled, and disgusted. ^an9-11-11-1

In the same way, I’m horrified, repelled, and disgusted by this rotting body. ^an9-11-11-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry. ^an9-11-11-3

Suppose someone was to carry around a bowl of fat that was leaking and oozing from holes and cracks. ^an9-11-12-1

In the same way, I carry around this body that’s leaking and oozing from holes and cracks. ^an9-11-12-2

Someone who had not established mindfulness of the body might well attack one of their spiritual companions and leave without saying sorry.” ^an9-11-12-3

Then that monk rose from his seat, placed his robe over one shoulder, bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet, and said, ^an9-11-13-1

“I made a mistake, sir. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of me to speak ill of Venerable Sāriputta with an incorrect, hollow, false, untruthful claim. ^an9-11-13-2

Please, sir, accept my mistake for what it is, so I will restrain myself in future.” ^an9-11-13-3

“Indeed, monk, you made a mistake. It was foolish, stupid, and unskillful of you to act in that way. ^an9-11-13-4

But since you have recognized your mistake for what it is, and have dealt with it properly, I accept it. ^an9-11-13-5

For it is growth in the training of the Noble One to recognize a mistake for what it is, deal with it properly, and commit to restraint in the future.” ^an9-11-13-6

Then the Buddha said to Venerable Sāriputta, ^an9-11-14-1

“Sāriputta, forgive that futile man before his head explodes into seven pieces right here.” ^an9-11-14-2

“I will pardon that venerable if he asks me: ^an9-11-14-3

‘May the venerable please pardon me too.’” ^an9-11-14-4

### With Residue (Numbered Discourses 9.12) ^an9-12-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an9-12-1-1

Then Venerable Sāriputta robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^an9-12-1-2

Then it occurred to him, ^an9-12-1-3

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī. ^an9-12-1-4

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an9-12-1-5

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^an9-12-1-6

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. ^an9-12-1-7

Now at that time while those wanderers of other religions were sitting together this discussion came up among them: ^an9-12-2-1

“Reverends, no-one who dies with residue is exempt from hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm. They’re not exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld.” ^an9-12-2-2

Sāriputta neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^an9-12-2-3

He rose from his seat, thinking, ^an9-12-2-4

“I will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^an9-12-2-5

Then Sāriputta wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an9-12-2-6

“Sāriputta, these foolish, incompetent wanderers following other religions: who are they to know whether someone has residue or not? ^an9-12-4-1

There are these nine individuals who, dying with residue, are exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^an9-12-5-1

What nine? ^an9-12-5-2

There’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics and immersion, but has limited wisdom. ^an9-12-5-3

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished between one life and the next. ^an9-12-5-4

This is the first individual … ^an9-12-5-5

Furthermore, there’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics and immersion, but has limited wisdom. ^an9-12-6-1

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished upon landing. This is the second individual … ^an9-12-6-2

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort. ^an9-12-6-3

This is the third individual … ^an9-12-6-4

With the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished with extra effort. ^an9-12-6-5

This is the fourth individual … ^an9-12-6-6

With the ending of the five lower fetters they head upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an9-12-6-7

This is the fifth individual … ^an9-12-6-8

Furthermore, there’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an9-12-7-1

With the ending of three fetters, and the weakening of greed, hate, and delusion, they’re a once-returner. They come back to this world once only, then make an end of suffering. ^an9-12-7-2

This is the sixth individual … ^an9-12-7-3

Furthermore, there’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an9-12-8-1

With the ending of three fetters, they’re a one-seeder. They will be reborn just one time in a human existence, then make an end of suffering. ^an9-12-8-2

This is the seventh individual … ^an9-12-8-3

Furthermore, there’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an9-12-9-1

With the ending of three fetters, they go from family to family. They will transmigrate between two or three families and then make an end of suffering. ^an9-12-9-2

This is the eighth individual … ^an9-12-9-3

Furthermore, there’s an individual who has fulfilled ethics, but has limited immersion and wisdom. ^an9-12-10-1

With the ending of three fetters, they have at most seven rebirths. They will transmigrate at most seven times among gods and humans and then make an end of suffering. ^an9-12-10-2

This is the ninth individual … ^an9-12-10-3

These foolish, incompetent wanderers following other religions: who are they to know whether someone has residue or not? ^an9-12-11-1

These are the nine individuals who, dying with residue, are exempt from hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. They’re exempt from places of loss, bad places, the underworld. ^an9-12-11-2

Up until now, Sāriputta, I have not felt the need to give this exposition of the teaching to the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen. ^an9-12-11-3

Why is that? ^an9-12-11-4

For I didn’t want those who heard it to introduce negligence. ^an9-12-11-5

However, I have spoken it in order to answer your question.” ^an9-12-11-6

### With Koṭṭhita (Numbered Discourses 9.13) ^an9-13-0

Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an9-13-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^an9-13-1-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced in this life be experienced by me in lives to come’?” ^an9-13-1-3

“Certainly not, reverend.” ^an9-13-1-4

“Then is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced in lives to come be experienced by me in this life’?” ^an9-13-2-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-2-2

“Is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced as pleasant be experienced by me as painful’?” ^an9-13-3-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-3-2

“Then is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced as painful be experienced by me as pleasant’?” ^an9-13-4-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-4-2

“Is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced when ripe be experienced by me when unripe’?” ^an9-13-5-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-5-2

“Then is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced when unripe be experienced by me when ripe’?” ^an9-13-6-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-6-2

“Is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced a lot be experienced by me a little’?” ^an9-13-7-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-7-2

“Then is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced a little be experienced by me a lot’?” ^an9-13-8-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-8-2

“Is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds to be experienced by me be not experienced’?” ^an9-13-9-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-9-2

“Then is the spiritual life led under the Buddha for this goal: ‘May deeds not to be experienced be experienced’?” ^an9-13-10-1

“Certainly not.” ^an9-13-10-2

“Reverend Sāriputta, when you were asked whether the spiritual life was lived under the Buddha so that deeds to be experienced in this life are experienced in lives to come, you said, ‘Certainly not’. ^an9-13-11-1

When you were asked whether the spiritual life was lived under the Buddha so that deeds to be experienced in lives to come are experienced in this life … ^an9-13-12-1

deeds to be experienced as pleasant are experienced as painful … ^an9-13-13-1

deeds to be experienced as painful are experienced as pleasant … ^an9-13-14-1

deeds to be experienced when ripe are experienced when unripe … ^an9-13-15-1

deeds to be experienced when unripe are experienced when ripe … ^an9-13-16-1

deeds to be experienced a lot are experienced a little … ^an9-13-17-1

deeds to be experienced a little are experienced a lot … ^an9-13-18-1

deeds to be experienced are not experienced … ^an9-13-19-1

When you were asked whether the spiritual life was lived under the Buddha so that deeds not to be experienced are experienced, you said, ‘Certainly not.’ ^an9-13-20-1

Then what exactly is the goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha?” ^an9-13-20-2

“Reverend, the spiritual life is lived under the Buddha to know, see, attain, realize, and comprehend that which is unknown, unseen, unattained, unrealized, and uncomprehended.” ^an9-13-21-1

“But what is the unknown, unseen, unattained, unrealized, and uncomprehended?” ^an9-13-21-2

“‘This is suffering.’ … ^an9-13-21-3

‘This is the origin of suffering.’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering.’ … ^an9-13-21-5

‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering.’ … ^an9-13-21-6

This is the unknown, unseen, unattained, unrealized, and uncomprehended. ^an9-13-21-8

The spiritual life is lived under the Buddha to know, see, attain, realize, and comprehend this.” ^an9-13-21-9

### With Samiddhi (Numbered Discourses 9.14) ^an9-14-0

Then Venerable Samiddhi went up to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, and sat to one side. Venerable Sāriputta said to him: ^an9-14-1-1

“Samiddhi, based on what do thoughts arise in a person?” ^an9-14-2-1

“Based on name and form, sir.” ^an9-14-2-2

“Where do they become diversified?” ^an9-14-3-1

“In the elements.” ^an9-14-3-2

“What is their origin?” ^an9-14-4-1

“Contact is their origin.” ^an9-14-4-2

“What is their meeting place?” ^an9-14-5-1

“Feeling is their meeting place.” ^an9-14-5-2

“What is their chief?” ^an9-14-6-1

“Immersion is their chief.” ^an9-14-6-2

“What is their ruler?” ^an9-14-7-1

“Mindfulness is their ruler.” ^an9-14-7-2

“What is their overseer?” ^an9-14-8-1

“Wisdom is their overseer.” ^an9-14-8-2

“What is their core?” ^an9-14-9-1

“Freedom is their core.” ^an9-14-9-2

“What is their objective?” ^an9-14-10-1

“Their objective is freedom from death.” ^an9-14-10-2

“Samiddhi, when you were asked what is the basis on which thoughts arise in a person, you answered ‘name and form’. ^an9-14-11-1

When you were asked … ^an9-14-11-2

what is their objective, you answered ‘freedom from death’. ^an9-14-11-9

Good, good, Samiddhi! ^an9-14-11-10

It’s good that you answered each question. But don’t get conceited because of that.” ^an9-14-11-11

### The Simile of the Boil (Numbered Discourses 9.15) ^an9-15-0

“Mendicants, suppose there was a boil that was many years old. ^an9-15-1-1

And that boil had nine orifices that were continually open wounds. ^an9-15-1-2

Whatever oozed out of them ^an9-15-1-3

would be filthy, stinking, and disgusting. ^an9-15-1-4

Whatever exuded from them ^an9-15-1-5

would be filthy, stinking, and disgusting. ^an9-15-1-6

‘Boil’ is a term for this body made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction. ^an9-15-2-1

And that boil has nine orifices that are continually open wounds. ^an9-15-2-2

Whatever oozes out of them ^an9-15-2-3

is filthy, stinking, and disgusting. ^an9-15-2-4

Whatever exudes from them ^an9-15-2-5

is filthy, stinking, and disgusting. ^an9-15-2-6

So, mendicants, have no illusion about this body.” ^an9-15-2-7

### Perceptions (Numbered Discourses 9.16) ^an9-16-0

“Mendicants, these nine perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. Their objective and culmination is freedom from death. ^an9-16-1-1

What nine? ^an9-16-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, not-self in suffering, giving up, and fading away. ^an9-16-1-3

These nine perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. Their objective and culmination is freedom from death.” ^an9-16-1-4

### Families (Numbered Discourses 9.17) ^an9-17-0

“Mendicants, visiting a family with nine factors is not worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is not worthwhile. ^an9-17-1-1

What nine? ^an9-17-1-2

They don’t politely rise, bow, or offer a seat. They hide what they have. Even when they have much they give little. Even when they have fine things they give coarse things. They give carelessly, not carefully. They don’t sit nearby to listen to the teachings. When you’re speaking, they don’t listen well. ^an9-17-1-3

Visiting a family with these nine factors is not worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is not worthwhile. ^an9-17-1-4

Visiting a family with nine factors is worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is worthwhile. ^an9-17-2-1

What nine? ^an9-17-2-2

They politely rise, bow, and offer a seat. They don’t hide what they have. When they have much they give much. When they have refined things they give refined things. They give carefully, not carelessly. They sit nearby to listen to the teachings. When you’re speaking, they listen well. ^an9-17-2-3

Visiting a family with these nine factors is worthwhile, or if you’ve already arrived, sitting down is worthwhile.” ^an9-17-2-4

### The Sabbath with Nine Factors (Numbered Discourses 9.18) ^an9-18-0

“Mendicants, the observance of the sabbath with its nine factors is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful. ^an9-18-1-1

And how should it be observed? ^an9-18-1-2

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an9-18-1-3

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They are scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an9-18-1-4

I, too, for this day and night will give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. I’ll be scrupulous and kind, and live full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an9-18-1-5

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an9-18-1-6

This is its first factor. … ^an9-18-1-7

‘As long as they live, the perfected ones give up high and luxurious beds. They sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an9-18-2-1

I, too, for this day and night will give up high and luxurious beds. I’ll sleep in a low place, either a cot or a grass mat. ^an9-18-2-2

I will observe the sabbath by doing as the perfected ones do in this respect.’ ^an9-18-2-3

This is its eighth factor. ^an9-18-2-4

They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will. ^an9-18-3-1

This is its ninth factor. ^an9-18-3-2

The observance of the sabbath with its nine factors in this way is very fruitful and beneficial and dazzling and bountiful.” ^an9-18-4-1

### A Deity (Numbered Discourses 9.19) ^an9-19-0

“Mendicants, tonight, several glorious deities, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, came to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an9-19-1-1

‘Sir, formerly when we were human beings, renunciates came to our homes. ^an9-19-1-2

We politely rose for them, but we didn’t bow. ^an9-19-1-3

And so, having not fulfilled our duty, full of remorse and regret, we were reborn in a lesser realm.’ ^an9-19-1-4

Then several other deities came to me and said: ^an9-19-2-1

‘Sir, formerly when we were human beings, renunciates came to our homes. ^an9-19-2-2

We politely rose for them and bowed, but we didn’t offer a seat. ^an9-19-2-3

And so, having not fulfilled our duty, full of remorse and regret, we were reborn in a lesser realm.’ ^an9-19-2-4

Then several other deities came to me and said: ^an9-19-3-1

‘Sir, formerly when we were human beings, renunciates came to our homes. ^an9-19-3-2

We politely rose for them, bowed, and offered a seat, but we didn’t share as best we could. …’ ^an9-19-3-3

‘… we didn’t sit nearby to listen to the teachings. …’ ^an9-19-3-4

‘… we didn’t actively listen to the teachings. …’ ^an9-19-3-5

‘… we didn’t memorize the teachings. …’ ^an9-19-3-6

‘… we didn’t examine the meaning of teachings we’d memorized. …’ ^an9-19-3-7

‘… not having understood the meaning and the teaching, we didn’t practice in line with the teaching. ^an9-19-3-8

And so, having not fulfilled our duty, full of remorse and regret, we were reborn in a lesser realm.’ ^an9-19-3-9

Then several other deities came to me and said: ^an9-19-4-1

‘Sir, formerly when we were human beings, renunciates came to our homes. ^an9-19-4-2

We politely rose, bowed, and offered them a seat. We shared as best we could. We sat nearby to listen to the teachings, lent an ear, memorized them, and examined their meaning. Understanding the teaching and the meaning we practiced in line with the teaching. ^an9-19-4-3

And so, having fulfilled our duty, free of remorse and regret, we were reborn in a superior realm.’ ^an9-19-4-4

Here, mendicants, are these roots of trees, and here are these empty huts. Practice absorption, mendicants! Don’t be negligent! Don’t regret it later, like those former deities.” ^an9-19-4-5

### About Velāma (Numbered Discourses 9.20) ^an9-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an9-20-1-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him, ^an9-20-1-2

“Householder, I wonder whether your family gives gifts?” ^an9-20-2-1

“It does, sir. ^an9-20-2-2

But only coarse gruel with false black pepper.” ^an9-20-2-3

“Householder, someone might give a gift that’s either coarse or fine. ^an9-20-2-4

But they give it carelessly, thoughtlessly, not with their own hand. They give the dregs, and they give without consideration for consequences. ^an9-20-2-5

Then wherever the result of any such gift manifests, their mind doesn’t incline toward enjoyment of nice food, clothes, vehicles, or the five refined kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-20-2-6

And their children, wives, bondservants, servants, and workers don’t want to listen to them. They don’t actively listen or try to understand. ^an9-20-2-7

Why is that? ^an9-20-2-8

Because that is the result of deeds done carelessly. ^an9-20-2-9

Someone might give a gift that’s either coarse or fine. ^an9-20-3-1

And they give it carefully, thoughtfully, with their own hand. They don’t give the dregs, and they give with consideration for consequences. ^an9-20-3-2

Then wherever the result of any such gift manifests, their mind inclines toward enjoyment of nice food, clothes, vehicles, or the five refined kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-20-3-3

And their children, wives, bondservants, servants, and workers want to listen. They actively listen and try to understand. ^an9-20-3-4

Why is that? ^an9-20-3-5

Because that is the result of deeds done carefully. ^an9-20-3-6

Once upon a time, householder, there was a brahmin named Velāma. ^an9-20-4-1

He gave the following gift, a great offering. ^an9-20-4-2

84,000 gold cups filled with silver. 84,000 silver cups filled with gold. 84,000 bronze cups filled with gold coins. 84,000 elephants with gold adornments and banners, covered with snow gold netting. 84,000 chariots upholstered with the hide of lions, tigers, and leopards, and cream rugs, with gold adornments and banners, covered with snow gold netting. 84,000 milk cows with muslin halter and bronze pails. 84,000 maidens bedecked with jeweled earrings. 84,000 couches spread with woolen covers—shag-piled, pure white, or embroidered with flowers—and spread with a fine deer hide, with canopies above and red pillows at both ends. 8,400,000,000 delicate cloths of linen, cotton, silk, and wool. And who can say how much food, drink, snacks, meals, refreshments, and beverages? It seemed like an overflowing river. ^an9-20-4-3

Householder, you might think: ^an9-20-5-1

‘Surely the brahmin Velāma must have been someone else at that time?’ ^an9-20-5-2

But you should not see it like this. ^an9-20-5-3

I myself was the brahmin Velāma at that time. ^an9-20-5-4

I gave that gift, a great offering. ^an9-20-5-5

But at that event there was no-one worthy of a religious donation, and no-one to purify the religious donation. ^an9-20-5-6

It would be more fruitful to feed one individual accomplished in view than that great offering of Velāma. ^an9-20-6-1

It would be more fruitful to feed one once-returner than a hundred of those accomplished in view. ^an9-20-7-1

It would be more fruitful to feed one non-returner than a hundred once-returners. ^an9-20-8-1

It would be more fruitful to feed one perfected one than a hundred non-returners. ^an9-20-8-2

It would be more fruitful to feed one Independent Buddha than a hundred perfected ones. ^an9-20-8-3

It would be more fruitful to feed one Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha than a hundred Independent Buddhas. ^an9-20-8-4

It would be more fruitful to feed the mendicant Saṅgha headed by the Buddha than to feed one Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha. ^an9-20-8-5

It would be more fruitful to build a dwelling especially for the Saṅgha of the four quarters than to feed the mendicant Saṅgha headed by the Buddha. ^an9-20-8-6
