It would be more fruitful to go for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha with a confident heart than to build a dwelling for the Saṅgha of the four quarters. ^an9-20-8-7

It would be more fruitful to undertake the training rules—not to kill living creatures, steal, commit sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor—than to go for refuge to the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha with a confident heart. ^an9-20-8-8

It would be more fruitful to develop a heart of love—even just as long as it takes to pull a cow’s udder—than to undertake the training rules. ^an9-20-8-9

It would be more fruitful to develop the perception of impermanence—even for as long as a finger-snap—than to do all of these things, including developing a heart of love for as long as it takes to pull a cow’s udder.” ^an9-20-9-1

### In Three Particulars (Numbered Discourses 9.21) ^an9-21-0

“The humans of the land north of Kuru surpass the gods of the thirty-three and the humans of the Black Plum Tree Land in three particulars. ^an9-21-1-1

What three? ^an9-21-1-2

They’re selfless and not possessive. They have a fixed lifespan. They have a distinctive nature. ^an9-21-1-3

The humans of the land north of Kuru surpass the gods of the thirty-three and the humans of the Black Plum Tree Land in these three particulars. ^an9-21-1-4

The gods of the thirty-three surpass the humans of the land north of Kuru and the Black Plum Tree Land in three particulars. ^an9-21-2-1

What three? ^an9-21-2-2

Heavenly lifespan, beauty, and happiness. ^an9-21-2-3

The gods of the thirty-three surpass the humans of the land north of Kuru and the Black Plum Tree Land in these three particulars. ^an9-21-2-4

The humans of the Black Plum Tree Land surpass the humans of the land north of Kuru and the gods of the thirty-three in three particulars. ^an9-21-3-1

What three? ^an9-21-3-2

Bravery, mindfulness, and the spiritual life is lived here. ^an9-21-3-3

The humans of the Black Plum Tree Land surpass the humans of the land north of Kuru and the gods of the thirty-three in these three particulars.” ^an9-21-3-4

### A Wild Colt (Numbered Discourses 9.22) ^an9-22-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you about three wild colts and three wild people; three excellent horses and three excellent people; and three fine thoroughbred horses and three fine thoroughbred people. ^an9-22-1-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. ^an9-22-1-2

And what are the three wild colts? ^an9-22-2-1

One wild colt is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an9-22-2-2

Another wild colt is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an9-22-2-3

While another wild colt is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-2-4

These are the three wild colts. ^an9-22-2-5

And what are the three wild people? ^an9-22-3-1

One wild person is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an9-22-3-2

Another wild person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an9-22-3-3

While another wild person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-3-4

And how is a wild person fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned? ^an9-22-4-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an9-22-4-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an9-22-4-3

But when asked a question about the teaching or training, they falter without answering. ^an9-22-4-4

This is how they’re not beautiful, I say. ^an9-22-4-5

And they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an9-22-4-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. ^an9-22-4-7

This is how a wild person is fast, but not beautiful or well proportioned. ^an9-22-4-8

And how is a wild person fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned? ^an9-22-5-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an9-22-5-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an9-22-5-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an9-22-5-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an9-22-5-5

But they don’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an9-22-5-6

This is how they’re not well proportioned, I say. ^an9-22-5-7

This is how a wild person is fast and beautiful, but not well proportioned. ^an9-22-5-8

And how is a wild person fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an9-22-6-1

They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’. ^an9-22-6-2

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an9-22-6-3

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an9-22-6-4

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an9-22-6-5

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an9-22-6-6

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an9-22-6-7

This is how a wild person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-6-8

These are the three wild people. ^an9-22-6-9

And what are the three excellent horses? ^an9-22-7-1

One excellent horse … ^an9-22-7-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-7-3

These are the three excellent horses. ^an9-22-7-4

What are the three excellent people? ^an9-22-8-1

One excellent person … ^an9-22-8-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-8-3

And how is an excellent person … ^an9-22-9-1

fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an9-22-9-2

It’s when a mendicant, with the ending of the five lower fetters, is reborn spontaneously. They’re extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-22-9-3

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an9-22-9-4

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an9-22-9-5

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an9-22-9-6

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an9-22-9-7

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an9-22-9-8

This is how an excellent person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-9-9

These are the three excellent people. ^an9-22-9-10

And what are the three fine thoroughbred horses? ^an9-22-10-1

One fine thoroughbred horse … ^an9-22-10-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-10-3

These are the three fine thoroughbred horses. ^an9-22-10-4

And what are the three fine thoroughbred people? ^an9-22-11-1

One fine thoroughbred person … ^an9-22-11-2

is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-11-3

And how is a fine thoroughbred person … ^an9-22-12-1

fast, beautiful, and well proportioned? ^an9-22-12-2

It’s a mendicant who realizes the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an9-22-12-3

This is how they’re fast, I say. ^an9-22-12-4

When asked a question about the teaching or training, they answer without faltering. ^an9-22-12-5

This is how they’re beautiful, I say. ^an9-22-12-6

They receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an9-22-12-7

This is how they’re well proportioned, I say. ^an9-22-12-8

This is how a fine thoroughbred person is fast, beautiful, and well proportioned. ^an9-22-12-9

These are the three fine thoroughbred people.” ^an9-22-12-10

### Rooted in Craving (Numbered Discourses 9.23) ^an9-23-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you about nine things rooted in craving. ^an9-23-1-1

And what are the nine things rooted in craving? ^an9-23-1-2

Craving gives rise to searching. Searching gives rise to gaining material things. Gaining material things gives rise to evaluation. Evaluation gives rise to desire and lust. Desire and lust gives rise to attachment. Attachment gives rise to ownership. Ownership gives rise to stinginess. Stinginess gives rise to safeguarding. Owing to safeguarding, many bad, unskillful things come to be: taking up the rod and the sword, quarrels, arguments, and disputes, accusations, backbiting, and lies. ^an9-23-1-3

These are the nine things rooted in craving.” ^an9-23-1-4

### Abodes of Sentient Beings (Numbered Discourses 9.24) ^an9-24-0

“Mendicants, there are nine abodes of sentient beings. ^an9-24-1-1

What nine? ^an9-24-1-2

There are sentient beings that are diverse in body and diverse in perception, such as human beings, some gods, and some beings in the underworld. ^an9-24-1-3

This is the first abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-1-4

There are sentient beings that are diverse in body and unified in perception, such as the gods reborn in the Divinity’s host through the first absorption. ^an9-24-2-1

This is the second abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-2-2

There are sentient beings that are unified in body and diverse in perception, such as the gods of streaming radiance. ^an9-24-3-1

This is the third abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-3-2

There are sentient beings that are unified in body and unified in perception, such as the gods of universal beauty. ^an9-24-4-1

This is the fourth abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-4-2

There are sentient beings that are non-percipient and do not experience anything, such as the gods who are non-percipient beings. ^an9-24-5-1

This is the fifth abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-5-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond perceptions of form. With the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they have been reborn in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-24-6-1

This is the sixth abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-6-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond the dimension of infinite space. Aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they have been reborn in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-24-7-1

This is the seventh abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-7-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness. Aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they have been reborn in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-24-8-1

This is the eighth abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-8-2

There are sentient beings that have gone totally beyond the dimension of nothingness. They have been reborn in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-24-9-1

This is the ninth abode of sentient beings. ^an9-24-9-2

These are the nine abodes of sentient beings.” ^an9-24-10-1

### Consolidated by Wisdom (Numbered Discourses 9.25) ^an9-25-0

“Mendicants, when a mendicant’s mind has been well consolidated with wisdom it’s appropriate for them to say: ^an9-25-1-1

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^an9-25-1-2

And how is a mendicant’s mind well consolidated with wisdom? ^an9-25-2-1

The mind is well consolidated with wisdom when they know: ‘My mind is without greed.’ ^an9-25-2-2

… ‘My mind is without hate.’ ^an9-25-2-3

… ‘My mind is without delusion.’ ^an9-25-2-4

… ‘My mind is not liable to become greedy.’ ^an9-25-2-5

… ‘My mind is not liable to become hateful.’ ^an9-25-2-6

… ‘My mind is not liable to become deluded.’ ^an9-25-2-7

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the sensual realm.’ ^an9-25-2-8

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the realm of luminous form.’ ^an9-25-2-9

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the formless realm.’ ^an9-25-2-10

When a mendicant’s mind has been well consolidated with wisdom it’s appropriate for them to say: ^an9-25-2-11

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’” ^an9-25-2-12

### The Simile of the Stone Post (Numbered Discourses 9.26) ^an9-26-0

So I have heard. ^an9-26-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an9-26-1-2

There Venerable Candikāputta addressed the mendicants, ^an9-26-1-3

“Reverends, Devadatta teaches the mendicants like this: ^an9-26-1-4

‘When a mendicant’s mind is solidified by heart, it’s appropriate for them to say: ^an9-26-1-5

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’” ^an9-26-1-6

When he said this, Venerable Sāriputta said to him, ^an9-26-2-1

“Reverend Candikāputta, Devadatta does not teach the mendicants like that. ^an9-26-2-2

He teaches like this: ^an9-26-2-5

‘When a mendicant’s mind is well consolidated by heart, it’s appropriate for them to say: ^an9-26-2-6

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’” ^an9-26-2-7

For a second time … ^an9-26-3-1

And for a third time Venerable Candikāputta addressed the mendicants … ^an9-26-4-1

And for a third time, Sāriputta said to him, ^an9-26-4-5

“Reverend Candikāputta, Devadatta does not teach the mendicants like that. ^an9-26-4-6

He teaches like this: ^an9-26-4-9

‘When a mendicant’s mind is well consolidated by heart, it’s appropriate for them to say: ^an9-26-4-10

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’ ^an9-26-4-11

And how is a mendicant’s mind well consolidated by heart? ^an9-26-5-1

The mind is well consolidated by heart when they know: ‘My mind is without greed.’ ^an9-26-5-2

… ‘My mind is without hate.’ ^an9-26-5-3

… ‘My mind is without delusion.’ ^an9-26-5-4

… ‘My mind is not liable to become greedy.’ ^an9-26-5-5

… ‘My mind is not liable to become hateful.’ ^an9-26-5-6

… ‘My mind is not liable to become deluded.’ ^an9-26-5-7

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the sensual realm.’ ^an9-26-5-8

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the realm of luminous form.’ ^an9-26-5-9

… ‘My mind is not liable to return to rebirth in the formless realm.’ ^an9-26-5-10

When a mendicant’s mind is rightly freed like this, even if compelling sights come into the range of vision they don’t occupy their mind. ^an9-26-5-11

Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance. ^an9-26-5-12

Suppose there was a stone post, sixteen feet long. ^an9-26-6-1

Eight feet were buried underground, and eight above ground. ^an9-26-6-2

And violent storms were to blow up out of the east, the west, the north, and the south. ^an9-26-6-3

They couldn’t make it tremor and tremble and quake. ^an9-26-6-6

Why is that? ^an9-26-6-7

It’s because that stone post is firmly embedded, with deep foundations. ^an9-26-6-8

In the same way, when a mendicant’s mind is rightly freed like this, even if compelling sights come into the range of vision they don’t occupy their mind. ^an9-26-6-9

Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance. ^an9-26-6-10

If even compelling sounds … ^an9-26-7-1

smells … ^an9-26-7-2

tastes … ^an9-26-7-3

touches … ^an9-26-7-4

and ideas come into the range of the mind they don’t occupy the mind. ^an9-26-7-5

Their mind is quite untainted. It is steady, imperturbable, observing disappearance.” ^an9-26-7-6

### Fears and Enmities (1st) (Numbered Discourses 9.27) ^an9-27-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an9-27-1-1

“Householder, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, and possesses the four factors of stream-entry, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an9-27-2-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^an9-27-2-2

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^an9-27-3-1

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from killing living creatures does not brim with fear and enmity either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an9-27-3-2

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^an9-27-3-3

Anyone who steals … ^an9-27-4-1

Anyone who commits sexual misconduct … ^an9-27-4-2

Anyone who lies … ^an9-27-4-3

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates no dangers and threats either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an9-27-4-4

So that danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an9-27-4-5

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^an9-27-5-1

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^an9-27-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^an9-27-6-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an9-27-6-3

They have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^an9-27-7-1

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an9-27-7-2

They have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^an9-27-8-1

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an9-27-8-2

And a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an9-27-9-1

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^an9-27-9-2

When a noble disciple has quelled these five fears and enmities, and possesses these four factors of stream-entry, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an9-27-10-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^an9-27-10-2

### Fears and Enmities (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 9.28) ^an9-28-0

“Mendicants, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, and possesses the four factors of stream-entry, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an9-28-1-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^an9-28-1-2

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^an9-28-2-1

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from killing living creatures does not brim with fear and enmity either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an9-28-2-2

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^an9-28-2-3

Anyone who steals … commits sexual misconduct … lies … ^an9-28-3-1

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates no dangers and threats either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an9-28-3-2

So that danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an9-28-3-3

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^an9-28-3-4

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^an9-28-4-1

When a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha … ^an9-28-4-2

the teaching … ^an9-28-4-4

the Saṅgha … ^an9-28-4-5

And a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an9-28-4-6

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^an9-28-4-7

When a noble disciple has quelled these five fears and enmities, and possesses these four factors of stream-entry, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an9-28-5-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^an9-28-5-2

### Grounds for Resentment (Numbered Discourses 9.29) ^an9-29-0

“Mendicants, there are nine grounds for resentment. ^an9-29-1-1

What nine? ^an9-29-1-2

Thinking: ‘They did wrong to me,’ you harbor resentment. ^an9-29-1-3

Thinking: ‘They are doing wrong to me’ … ^an9-29-1-4

‘They will do wrong to me’ … ^an9-29-1-5

‘They did wrong to someone I love’ … ^an9-29-1-6

‘They are doing wrong to someone I love’ … ^an9-29-1-7

‘They will do wrong to someone I love’ … ^an9-29-1-8

‘They helped someone I dislike’ … ^an9-29-1-9

‘They are helping someone I dislike’ … ^an9-29-1-10

Thinking: ‘They will help someone I dislike,’ you harbor resentment. ^an9-29-1-11

These are the nine grounds for resentment.” ^an9-29-1-12

### Getting Rid of Resentment (Numbered Discourses 9.30) ^an9-30-0

“Mendicants, there are these nine ways to get rid of resentment. ^an9-30-1-1

What nine? ^an9-30-1-2

Thinking: ‘They harmed me, but what can I possibly do?’ you get rid of resentment. ^an9-30-1-3

Thinking: ‘They are harming me …’ … ^an9-30-1-4

‘They will harm me …’ … ^an9-30-1-5

‘They harmed someone I love …’ … ^an9-30-1-6

‘They are harming someone I love …’ ^an9-30-1-7

‘They will harm someone I love …’ … ^an9-30-1-8

‘They helped someone I dislike …’ … ^an9-30-1-9

‘They are helping someone I dislike …’ … ^an9-30-1-10

Thinking: ‘They will help someone I dislike, but what can I possibly do?’ you get rid of resentment. ^an9-30-1-11

These are the nine ways to get rid of resentment.” ^an9-30-1-12

### Progressive Cessations (Numbered Discourses 9.31) ^an9-31-0

“Mendicants, there are these nine progressive cessations. ^an9-31-1-1

What nine? ^an9-31-1-2

For someone who has attained the first absorption, sensual perceptions have ceased. ^an9-31-1-3

For someone who has attained the second absorption, the placing of the mind and keeping it connected have ceased. ^an9-31-1-4

For someone who has attained the third absorption, rapture has ceased. ^an9-31-1-5

For someone who has attained the fourth absorption, breathing has ceased. ^an9-31-1-6

For someone who has attained the dimension of infinite space, the perception of form has ceased. ^an9-31-1-7

For someone who has attained the dimension of infinite consciousness, the perception of the dimension of infinite space has ceased. ^an9-31-1-8

For someone who has attained the dimension of nothingness, the perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness has ceased. ^an9-31-1-9

For someone who has attained the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, the perception of the dimension of nothingness has ceased. ^an9-31-1-10

For someone who has attained the cessation of perception and feeling, perception and feeling have ceased. ^an9-31-1-11

These are the nine progressive cessations.” ^an9-31-1-12

### Progressive Meditations (Numbered Discourses 9.32) ^an9-32-0

“Mendicants, there are these nine progressive meditations. ^an9-32-1-1

What nine? ^an9-32-1-2

The first absorption, the second absorption, the third absorption, the fourth absorption, the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness, the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an9-32-1-3

These are the nine progressive meditations.” ^an9-32-1-4

### The Nine Progressive Meditative Attainments (Numbered Discourses 9.33) ^an9-33-0

“Mendicants, I will teach you the nine progressive meditative attainments … ^an9-33-1-1

And what are the nine progressive meditative attainments? ^an9-33-1-2

Where sensual pleasures cease, and those who have thoroughly ended sensual pleasures meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-1-3

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where sensual pleasures cease’, ^an9-33-1-4

they should be told: ^an9-33-1-5

‘Reverend, it’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-33-1-6

That’s where sensual pleasures cease.’ ^an9-33-1-7

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-1-8

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-1-9

Where the placing of the mind and keeping it connected cease, and those who have thoroughly ended the placing of the mind and keeping it connected meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-2-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where the placing of the mind and keeping it connected cease’, ^an9-33-2-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-2-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-33-2-4

That’s where the placing of the mind and keeping it connected cease.’ ^an9-33-2-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-2-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-2-7

Where rapture ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended rapture meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-3-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where rapture ceases’, ^an9-33-3-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-3-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, with the fading away of rapture, enters and remains in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss”. ^an9-33-3-4

That’s where rapture ceases.’ ^an9-33-3-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-3-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-3-7

Where bliss with equanimity ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended bliss with equanimity meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-4-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where bliss with equanimity ceases’, ^an9-33-4-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-4-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an9-33-4-4

That’s where bliss with equanimity ceases.’ ^an9-33-4-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-4-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-4-7

Where perceptions of form ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended perceptions of form meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-5-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where perceptions of form ceases’, ^an9-33-5-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-5-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that “space is infinite”, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-33-5-4

That’s where perceptions of form cease.’ ^an9-33-5-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-5-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-5-7

Where the perception of the dimension of infinite space ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended the perception of the dimension of infinite space meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-6-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where the perception of the dimension of infinite space ceases’, ^an9-33-6-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-6-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-33-6-4

That’s where the perception of the dimension of infinite space ceases.’ ^an9-33-6-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-6-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-6-7

Where the perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended the perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-7-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where the perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness ceases’, ^an9-33-7-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-7-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-33-7-4

That’s where the perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness ceases.’ ^an9-33-7-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-7-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-7-7

Where the perception of the dimension of nothingness ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended the perception of the dimension of nothingness meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-8-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where the perception of the dimension of nothingness ceases’, ^an9-33-8-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-8-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-33-8-4

That’s where the perception of the dimension of nothingness ceases.’ ^an9-33-8-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-8-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-8-7

Where the perception of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception ceases, and those who have thoroughly ended the perception of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception meditate, I say: ‘Clearly those venerables are desireless, quenched, crossed over, and gone beyond in that respect.’ ^an9-33-9-1

If someone should say, ‘I do not know or see where the perception of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception ceases’, ^an9-33-9-2

they should be told: ^an9-33-9-3

‘It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an9-33-9-4

That’s where the perception of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception ceases.’ ^an9-33-9-5

Clearly someone who is not devious or deceitful would approve and agree with that statement. ^an9-33-9-6

They’d say ‘Good!’ and bowing down, they’d pay homage with cupped palms. ^an9-33-9-7

These are the nine progressive meditative attainments.” ^an9-33-10-1

### Extinguishment is Bliss (Numbered Discourses 9.34) ^an9-34-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an9-34-1-1

There he addressed the mendicants: ^an9-34-1-2

“Reverends, extinguishment is bliss! ^an9-34-1-3

Extinguishment is bliss!” ^an9-34-1-4

When he said this, Venerable Udāyī said to him, ^an9-34-1-5

“But Reverend Sāriputta, what’s blissful about it, since nothing is felt?” ^an9-34-1-6

“The fact that nothing is felt is precisely what’s blissful about it. ^an9-34-1-7

Reverend, there are these five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-34-1-8

What five? ^an9-34-1-9

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-34-1-10

Sounds known by the ear … ^an9-34-1-11

Smells known by the nose … ^an9-34-1-12

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an9-34-1-13

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-34-1-14

These are the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-34-1-15

The pleasure and happiness that arise from these five kinds of sensual stimulation is called sensual pleasure. ^an9-34-1-16

First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-34-2-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by sensual pleasures beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-2-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-2-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by sensual pleasures beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-2-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-2-5

That’s the way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-2-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption. ^an9-34-3-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by placing of the mind beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-3-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-3-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by placing of the mind beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-3-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-3-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-3-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, with the fading away of rapture, enters and remains in the third absorption. ^an9-34-4-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by rapture beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-4-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-4-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by rapture beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-4-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-4-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-4-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, enters and remains in the fourth absorption. ^an9-34-5-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by bliss with equanimity beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-5-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-5-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by bliss with equanimity beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-5-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-5-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-5-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-34-6-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by form beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-6-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-6-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by form beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-6-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-6-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-6-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-34-7-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite space beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-7-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-7-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite space beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-7-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-7-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-7-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-34-8-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite consciousness beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-8-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-8-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite consciousness beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-8-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-8-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-8-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-34-9-1

While a mendicant is practicing such a meditation, if perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of nothingness beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-9-2

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-34-9-3

In the same way, should perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of nothingness beset them, that’s an affliction for them. ^an9-34-9-4

And affliction has been called suffering by the Buddha. ^an9-34-9-5

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss. ^an9-34-9-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-34-10-1

That too is a way to understand how extinguishment is bliss.” ^an9-34-11-1

### The Simile of the Cow (Numbered Discourses 9.35) ^an9-35-0

“Mendicants, suppose there was a mountain cow who was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacked common sense when roaming on rugged mountains. ^an9-35-1-1

She might think, ^an9-35-1-2

‘Why don’t I go somewhere I’ve never been before? I could eat grass and drink water that I’ve never tried before.’ ^an9-35-1-3

She’d take a step with a fore-hoof; but before it was properly set down, she’d lift up a hind-hoof. ^an9-35-1-4

She wouldn’t go somewhere she’d never been before, or eat grass and drink water that she’d never tried before. ^an9-35-1-5

And she’d never return safely to the place she had started from. ^an9-35-1-6

Why is that? ^an9-35-1-8

Because that mountain cow was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacked common sense when roaming on rugged mountains. ^an9-35-1-9

In the same way, some foolish, incompetent, unskillful mendicant, lacking common sense, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-35-1-10

But they don’t cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis; they don’t ensure it is properly stabilized. ^an9-35-1-11

They think, ^an9-35-2-1

‘Why don’t I, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.’ ^an9-35-2-2

But they’re not able to enter and remain in the second absorption. ^an9-35-2-3

They think, ^an9-35-2-4

‘Why don’t I, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.’ ^an9-35-2-5

But they’re not able to enter and remain in the first absorption. ^an9-35-2-6

This is called a mendicant who has slipped and fallen from both sides. They’re like the mountain cow who was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacking in common sense when roaming on rugged mountains. ^an9-35-2-7

Suppose there was a mountain cow who was astute, competent, skillful, and used common sense when roaming on rugged mountains. ^an9-35-3-1

She might think, ^an9-35-3-2

‘Why don’t I go somewhere I’ve never been before? I could eat grass and drink water that I’ve never tried before.’ ^an9-35-3-3

She’d take a step with a fore-hoof; and after it was properly set down, she’d lift up a hind-hoof. ^an9-35-3-4

She’d go somewhere she’d never been before, and eat grass and drink water that she’d never tried before. ^an9-35-3-5

And she’d return safely to the place she had started from. ^an9-35-3-6

Why is that? ^an9-35-3-8

Because that mountain cow was astute, competent, skillful, and used common sense when roaming on rugged mountains. ^an9-35-3-9

In the same way, some astute, competent, skillful mendicant, using common sense, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-35-3-10

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-3-11

They think, ^an9-35-4-1

‘Why don’t I, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.’ ^an9-35-4-2

Without charging at the second absorption, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption. ^an9-35-4-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-4-4

They think, ^an9-35-5-1

‘Why don’t I, with the fading away of rapture, enter and remain in the third absorption, where I will meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.”’ ^an9-35-5-2

Without charging at the third absorption, with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption. ^an9-35-5-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-5-4

They think, ^an9-35-6-1

‘Why don’t I, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.’ ^an9-35-6-2

Without charging at the fourth absorption, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption. ^an9-35-6-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-6-4

They think, ^an9-35-7-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that “space is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space.’ ^an9-35-7-2

Without charging at the dimension of infinite space, with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-35-7-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-7-4

They think, ^an9-35-8-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness.’ ^an9-35-8-2

Without charging at the dimension of infinite consciousness, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-35-8-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-8-4

They think, ^an9-35-9-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness.’ ^an9-35-9-2

Without charging at the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-35-9-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-9-4

They think, ^an9-35-10-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’ ^an9-35-10-2

Without charging at the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-35-10-3

They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized. ^an9-35-10-4

They think, ^an9-35-11-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^an9-35-11-2

Without charging at the cessation of perception and feeling, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an9-35-11-3

When a mendicant enters and emerges from all these attainments, their mind becomes pliable and workable. ^an9-35-12-1

With a pliable and workable mind, their immersion becomes limitless and well developed. ^an9-35-12-2

They extend the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-12-3

They might wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again … controlling my body as far as the realm of divinity.’ ^an9-35-13-1

They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-13-2

They might wish: ‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.’ ^an9-35-14-1

They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-14-2

They might wish: ‘May I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind. ^an9-35-15-1

May I understand mind with greed as “mind with greed”, ^an9-35-15-2

and mind without greed as “mind without greed”; ^an9-35-15-3

mind with hate as “mind with hate”, ^an9-35-15-4

and mind without hate as “mind without hate”; ^an9-35-15-5

mind with delusion as “mind with delusion”, ^an9-35-15-6

and mind without delusion as “mind without delusion”; ^an9-35-15-7

constricted mind … ^an9-35-15-8

scattered mind … ^an9-35-15-9

expansive mind … ^an9-35-15-10

unexpansive mind … ^an9-35-15-11

mind that is not supreme … ^an9-35-15-12

mind that is supreme … ^an9-35-15-13

mind immersed in samādhi … ^an9-35-15-14

mind not immersed in samādhi … ^an9-35-15-15

freed mind … ^an9-35-15-16

and unfreed mind as “unfreed mind”.’ ^an9-35-15-17

They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-15-18

They might wish: ‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: “There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.” May I recollect my many past lives, with features and details.’ ^an9-35-16-1

They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-16-2

They might wish: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place—and understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds.’ ^an9-35-17-1

They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range. ^an9-35-17-2

They might wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ ^an9-35-18-1

They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.” ^an9-35-18-2

### Depending on Absorption (Numbered Discourses 9.36) ^an9-36-0

“Mendicants, I say that the first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-1

The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-2

The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-3

The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-4

The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-5

The dimension of infinite consciousness is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-6

The dimension of nothingness is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-7

The dimension of neither perception nor non-perception is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-8

The cessation of perception and feeling is also a basis for ending the defilements. ^an9-36-1-9

‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-2-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-36-2-2

Take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-36-2-3

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an9-36-2-4

They turn their mind away from those things, ^an9-36-2-5

and apply it to freedom from death: ^an9-36-2-6

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an9-36-2-7

Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements. ^an9-36-2-8

If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-36-2-9

It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects. ^an9-36-3-1

In the same way a noble disciple, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-36-3-2

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an9-36-3-3

They turn their mind away from those things, ^an9-36-3-4

and apply it to freedom from death: ^an9-36-3-5

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an9-36-3-6

Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements. ^an9-36-3-7

If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-36-3-8

‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-3-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-36-3-10

‘The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ … ^an9-36-4-1

‘The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ … ^an9-36-4-2

‘The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ … ^an9-36-4-3

‘The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-6-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-36-6-2

Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-36-6-3

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an9-36-6-4

They turn their mind away from those things, ^an9-36-6-5

and apply it to freedom from death: ^an9-36-6-6

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an9-36-6-7

Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements. ^an9-36-6-8

If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-36-6-9

It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects. ^an9-36-7-1

In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. … ^an9-36-7-2

‘The dimension of infinite space is a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-7-5

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-36-7-6

‘The dimension of infinite consciousness is a basis for ending the defilements.’ … ^an9-36-8-1

‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-8-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an9-36-8-3

Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-36-8-4

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an9-36-8-5

They turn their mind away from those things, ^an9-36-8-6

and apply it to freedom from death: ^an9-36-8-7

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an9-36-8-8

Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements. ^an9-36-8-9

If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-36-8-10

It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects. ^an9-36-9-1

In the same way, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-36-9-2

They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self. ^an9-36-9-3

They turn their mind away from those things, ^an9-36-9-4

and apply it to freedom from death: ^an9-36-9-5

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an9-36-9-6

Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements. ^an9-36-9-7

If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world. ^an9-36-9-8

‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’ ^an9-36-9-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an9-36-9-10

And so, mendicants, penetration to enlightenment extends as far as attainments with perception. ^an9-36-10-1

But the two dimensions that depend on these— ^an9-36-10-2

the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the cessation of perception and feeling—are properly explained by meditators who are skilled in these attainments and skilled in emerging from them, after they’ve entered them and emerged from them.” ^an9-36-10-3

### By Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 9.37) ^an9-37-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an9-37-1-1

There Ānanda addressed the mendicants: ^an9-37-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an9-37-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an9-37-1-4

Ānanda said this: ^an9-37-1-5

“It’s incredible, reverends, it’s amazing! ^an9-37-2-1

How this Blessed One who knows and sees, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, has found an opening amid confinement. It’s in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to discover the system, and to realize extinguishment. ^an9-37-2-2

The eye itself is actually present, and so are those sights. Yet one will not experience that sense-field. ^an9-37-2-3

The ear itself is actually present, and so are those sounds. Yet one will not experience that sense-field. ^an9-37-2-4

The nose itself is actually present, and so are those smells. Yet one will not experience that sense-field. ^an9-37-2-5

The tongue itself is actually present, and so are those tastes. Yet one will not experience that sense-field. ^an9-37-2-6

The body itself is actually present, and so are those touches. Yet one will not experience that sense-field.” ^an9-37-2-7

When he said this, Venerable Udāyī said to Venerable Ānanda: ^an9-37-3-1

“Reverend Ānanda, is one who doesn’t experience that sense-field actually percipient or not?” ^an9-37-3-2

“Reverend, one who doesn’t experience that sense-field is actually percipient, not non-percipient.” ^an9-37-3-3

“But what does one who doesn’t experience that sense-field perceive?” ^an9-37-4-1

“It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-37-4-2

One who doesn’t experience that sense-field perceives in this way. ^an9-37-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-37-5-1

One who doesn’t experience that sense-field perceives in this way. ^an9-37-5-2

Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-37-6-1

One who doesn’t experience that sense-field perceives in this way. ^an9-37-6-2

Reverend, this one time I was staying near Sāketa in the deer park in Añjana Wood. ^an9-37-7-1

Then the nun Jaṭilagāhikā came up to me, bowed, stood to one side, and said to me: ^an9-37-7-2

‘Honorable Ānanda, regarding the immersion that does not lean forward or pull back, and is not held in place by forceful suppression. ^an9-37-7-3

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, one is not anxious. ^an9-37-7-4

What did the Buddha say was the fruit of this immersion?’ ^an9-37-7-5

When she said this, I said to her: ^an9-37-8-1

‘Sister, regarding the immersion that does not lean forward or pull back, and is not held in place by forceful suppression. ^an9-37-8-2

Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, one is not anxious. ^an9-37-8-3

The Buddha said that the fruit of this immersion is enlightenment.’ ^an9-37-8-4

One who doesn’t experience that sense-field perceives in this way, too.” ^an9-37-8-5

### Brahmin Cosmologists (Numbered Discourses 9.38) ^an9-38-0

Then two brahmin cosmologists went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an9-38-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to the Buddha: ^an9-38-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, Pūraṇa Kassapa claims to be all-knowing and all-seeing, to know and see everything without exception, thus: ^an9-38-2-1

‘Knowledge and vision are constantly and continually present to me, while walking, standing, sleeping, and waking.’ ^an9-38-2-2

He says: ^an9-38-2-3

‘With infinite knowledge I know and see that the cosmos is infinite.’ ^an9-38-2-4

And the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan also claims to be all-knowing and all-seeing, to know and see everything without exception, thus: ^an9-38-2-5

‘Knowledge and vision are constantly and continually present to me, while walking, standing, sleeping, and waking.’ ^an9-38-2-6

He says: ^an9-38-2-7

‘With infinite knowledge I know and see that the cosmos is finite.’ ^an9-38-2-8

These two claim to speak from knowledge, but they directly contradict each other. Which one of them speaks the truth, and which falsehood?” ^an9-38-2-9

“Enough, brahmins, let that be. ^an9-38-3-1

I will teach you the Dhamma. ^an9-38-3-4

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an9-38-3-5

“Yes, worthy sir,” those brahmins replied. ^an9-38-3-6

The Buddha said this: ^an9-38-3-7

“Suppose there were four men standing in the four quarters. Each of them was extremely fast, with an extremely mighty stride. ^an9-38-4-1

They’re as fast as a light arrow easily shot across the shadow of a palm tree by a well-trained expert archer with a strong bow. ^an9-38-4-2

Their stride was such that it spanned from the eastern ocean to the western ocean. ^an9-38-4-3

Then the man standing in the east would say: ^an9-38-4-4

‘I will reach the end of the world by traveling.’ ^an9-38-4-5

Though he’d travel for his whole lifespan of a hundred years—pausing only to eat and drink, go to the toilet, and sleep to dispel weariness—he’d die along the way, never reaching the end of the world. ^an9-38-4-6

Then the man standing in the west … ^an9-38-4-7

Then the man standing in the north … ^an9-38-4-8

Then the man standing in the south would say: ^an9-38-4-9

‘I will reach the end of the world by traveling.’ ^an9-38-4-10

Though he’d travel for his whole lifespan of a hundred years—pausing only to eat and drink, go to the toilet, and sleep to dispel weariness—he’d die along the way, never reaching the end of the world. ^an9-38-4-11

Why is that? ^an9-38-4-12

I say it’s not possible to know or see or reach the end of the world by running like this. ^an9-38-4-13

But I also say there’s no making an end of suffering without reaching the end of the world. ^an9-38-4-14

These five kinds of sensual stimulation are called the world in the training of the Noble One. ^an9-38-5-1

What five? ^an9-38-5-2

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-38-5-3

Sounds known by the ear … ^an9-38-5-4

Smells known by the nose … ^an9-38-5-5

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an9-38-5-6

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-38-5-7

These five kinds of sensual stimulation are called the world in the training of the Noble One. ^an9-38-5-8

Take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-38-6-1

This is called a mendicant who, having gone to the end of the world, meditates at the end of the world. ^an9-38-6-2

Others say of them: ^an9-38-6-3

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-6-4

And I also say this: ^an9-38-6-5

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-6-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption … ^an9-38-7-1

third absorption … ^an9-38-7-2

fourth absorption. ^an9-38-7-3

This is called a mendicant who, having gone to the end of the world, meditates at the end of the world. ^an9-38-7-4

Others say of them: ^an9-38-7-5

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-7-6

And I also say this: ^an9-38-7-7

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-7-8

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-38-8-1

This is called a mendicant who, having gone to the end of the world, meditates at the end of the world. ^an9-38-8-2

Others say of them: ^an9-38-8-3

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-8-4

And I also say this: ^an9-38-8-5

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-8-6

Furthermore, take a mendicant who enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. … ^an9-38-9-1

the dimension of nothingness … ^an9-38-9-2

the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-38-9-3

This is called a mendicant who, having gone to the end of the world, meditates at the end of the world. ^an9-38-9-4

Others say of them: ^an9-38-9-5

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-9-6

And I also say this: ^an9-38-9-7

‘They’re included in the world, and haven’t yet left the world.’ ^an9-38-9-8

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-38-10-1

This is called a mendicant who, having gone to the end of the world, meditates at the end of the world. And they’ve crossed over clinging to the world.” ^an9-38-10-2

### The War Between the Gods and the Titans (Numbered Discourses 9.39) ^an9-39-0

“Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-1-1

In that battle the titans won and the gods lost. ^an9-39-1-2

Defeated, the gods fled north with the titans in pursuit. ^an9-39-1-3

Then the gods thought, ^an9-39-1-4

‘The titans are still in pursuit. ^an9-39-1-5

Why don’t we engage them in battle a second time?’ ^an9-39-1-6

And so a second battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-1-7

And for a second time the titans won and the gods lost. ^an9-39-1-8

Defeated, the gods fled north with the titans in pursuit. ^an9-39-1-9

Then the gods thought, ^an9-39-2-1

‘The titans are still in pursuit. ^an9-39-2-2

Why don’t we engage them in battle a third time?’ ^an9-39-2-3

And so a third battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-2-4

And for a third time the titans won and the gods lost. ^an9-39-2-5

Defeated and terrified, the gods fled right into the castle of the gods. ^an9-39-2-6

When they had entered their castle, they thought, ^an9-39-2-7

‘Now we’re in a secure location and the titans can’t do anything to us.’ ^an9-39-2-8

The titans also thought, ^an9-39-2-9

‘Now the gods are in a secure location and we can’t do anything to them.’ ^an9-39-2-10

Once upon a time, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-3-1

In that battle the gods won and the titans lost. ^an9-39-3-2

Defeated, the titans fled south with the gods in pursuit. ^an9-39-3-3

Then the titans thought, ^an9-39-3-4

‘The gods are still in pursuit. ^an9-39-3-5

Why don’t we engage them in battle a second time?’ ^an9-39-3-6

And so a second battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-3-7

And for a second time the gods won and the titans lost. ^an9-39-3-8

Defeated, the titans fled south with the gods in pursuit. ^an9-39-3-9

Then the titans thought, ^an9-39-4-1

‘The gods are still in pursuit. ^an9-39-4-2

Why don’t we engage them in battle a third time?’ ^an9-39-4-3

And so a third battle was fought between the gods and the titans. ^an9-39-4-4

And for a third time the gods won and the titans lost. ^an9-39-4-5

Defeated and terrified, the titans fled right into the citadel of the titans. ^an9-39-4-6

When they had entered their citadel, they thought, ^an9-39-4-7

‘Now we’re in a secure location and the gods can’t do anything to us.’ ^an9-39-4-8

And the gods also thought, ^an9-39-4-9

‘Now the titans are in a secure location and we can’t do anything to them.’ ^an9-39-4-10

In the same way, there’s a time when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-39-5-1

At that time the mendicant thinks, ^an9-39-5-2

‘Now I’m in a secure location and Māra can’t do anything to me.’ ^an9-39-5-3

And Māra the Wicked also thinks, ^an9-39-5-4

‘Now the mendicant is in a secure location and we can’t do anything to them.’ ^an9-39-5-5

There’s a time when, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption … ^an9-39-6-1

third absorption … ^an9-39-6-2

fourth absorption. ^an9-39-6-3

At that time the mendicant thinks, ^an9-39-6-4

‘Now I’m in a secure location and Māra can’t do anything to me.’ ^an9-39-6-5

And Māra the Wicked also thinks, ^an9-39-6-6

‘Now the mendicant is in a secure location and we can’t do anything to them.’ ^an9-39-6-7

There’s a time when a mendicant, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-39-7-1

This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra, put out his eyes without a trace, and gone where the Wicked One cannot see. ^an9-39-7-2

There’s a time when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. … ^an9-39-8-1

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. … ^an9-39-8-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. … ^an9-39-8-3

Going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-39-8-4

This is called a mendicant who has blinded Māra, put out his eyes without a trace, and gone where the Wicked One cannot see. And they’ve crossed over clinging to the world.” ^an9-39-8-5

### The Simile of the Bull Elephant in the Forest (Numbered Discourses 9.40) ^an9-40-0

“Mendicants, when a wild bull elephant is engrossed in the pasture, but other elephants—males, females, younglings, or cubs—got there first and trampled the grass, the wild bull elephant is horrified, repelled, and disgusted by that. ^an9-40-1-1

When the wild bull elephant is engrossed in the pasture, but other elephants—males, females, younglings, or cubs—eat the broken branches that he has dragged down, the wild bull elephant is horrified, repelled, and disgusted by that. ^an9-40-1-2

When a wild bull elephant has plunged into the pool, but other elephants—males, females, younglings, or cubs—got there first and stirred up the water with their trunks, the wild bull elephant is horrified, repelled, and disgusted by that. ^an9-40-1-3

When a wild bull elephant has come out of the pool and the female elephants bump into him, the wild bull elephant is horrified, repelled, and disgusted by that. ^an9-40-1-4

At that time the wild bull elephant thinks: ^an9-40-2-1

‘These days I live crowded by other males, females, younglings, and cubs. ^an9-40-2-2

I eat the grass they’ve trampled, and they eat the broken branches I’ve dragged down. I drink muddy water, and after my bath the female elephants bump into me. ^an9-40-2-3

Why don’t I live alone, withdrawn from the herd?’ ^an9-40-2-4

After some time he lives alone, withdrawn from the herd, and he eats untrampled grass, and other elephants don’t eat the broken branches he has dragged down. He doesn’t drink muddy water, and the female elephants don’t bump into him after his bath. ^an9-40-2-5

At that time the wild bull elephant thinks: ^an9-40-3-1

‘Formerly I lived crowded by other males, females, younglings, and cubs. I ate the grass they’d trampled, and they ate the broken branches I’d dragged down. I drank muddy water, and after my bath the female elephants bumped into me. Now I live alone, and I’m free of all these things.’ He breaks off a branch and scratches his body, happily relieving his itches. ^an9-40-3-2

In the same way, when a mendicant lives crowded by monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; by rulers and their chief ministers, and by monastics of other religions and their disciples, they think: ^an9-40-4-1

‘These days I live crowded by monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; by rulers and their chief ministers, and monastics of other religions and their disciples. ^an9-40-4-2

Why don’t I live alone, withdrawn from the group?’ ^an9-40-4-3

They frequent a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw. ^an9-40-4-4

Gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, they sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. ^an9-40-4-5

Giving up covetousness for the world, they meditate with a heart rid of covetousness, cleansing the mind of covetousness. ^an9-40-5-1

Giving up ill will and malevolence, they meditate with a mind rid of ill will, full of sympathy for all living beings, cleansing the mind of ill will. ^an9-40-5-2

Giving up dullness and drowsiness, they meditate with a mind rid of dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, mindful and aware, cleansing the mind of dullness and drowsiness. ^an9-40-5-3

Giving up restlessness and remorse, they meditate without restlessness, their mind peaceful inside, cleansing the mind of restlessness and remorse. ^an9-40-5-4

Giving up doubt, they meditate having gone beyond doubt, not undecided about skillful qualities, cleansing the mind of doubt. ^an9-40-5-5

They give up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^an9-40-5-6

Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, they enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-40-5-7

They happily relieve their itches. ^an9-40-5-8

As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption … ^an9-40-5-9

third absorption … ^an9-40-5-10

fourth absorption. ^an9-40-5-11

They happily relieve their itches. ^an9-40-5-12

Going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-40-6-1

They happily relieve their itches. ^an9-40-6-2

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness. … ^an9-40-6-3

Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. … ^an9-40-6-4

Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. … ^an9-40-6-5
