Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-40-6-6

They happily relieve their itches.” ^an9-40-6-7

### With the Householder Tapussa (Numbered Discourses 9.41) ^an9-41-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Mallas, near the Mallian town named Uruvelakappa. ^an9-41-1-1

Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Uruvelakappa for alms. ^an9-41-1-2

Then, after the meal, on his return from almsround, he addressed Venerable Ānanda, ^an9-41-1-3

“Ānanda, you stay right here, while I plunge deep into the Great Wood for the day’s meditation.” ^an9-41-1-4

“Yes, sir,” Ānanda replied. ^an9-41-1-5

Then the Buddha plunged deep into the Great Wood and sat at the root of a tree for the day’s meditation. ^an9-41-1-6

The householder Tapussa went up to Venerable Ānanda, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an9-41-2-1

“Honorable Ānanda, we are laypeople who enjoy sensual pleasures. We like sensual pleasures, we love them and take joy in them. ^an9-41-3-1

But renunciation seems like an abyss. ^an9-41-3-2

I have heard that in this teaching and training there are very young mendicants whose minds leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in renunciation. They see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-3-3

Renunciation is the dividing line between the multitude and the mendicants in this teaching and training.” ^an9-41-3-4

“Householder, we should see the Buddha about this matter. ^an9-41-4-1

Come, let’s go to the Buddha and inform him about this. ^an9-41-4-2

As he answers, so we’ll remember it.” ^an9-41-4-3

“Yes, sir,” replied Tapussa. ^an9-41-5-1

Then Ānanda together with Tapussa went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Ānanda told him what had happened. ^an9-41-5-2

“That’s so true, Ānanda! That’s so true! ^an9-41-7-1

Before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I too thought, ^an9-41-7-2

‘Renunciation is good! Seclusion is good!’ ^an9-41-7-3

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in renunciation. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-7-4

Then I thought, ^an9-41-7-5

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in renunciation? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-7-6

Then I thought, ^an9-41-7-7

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of sensual pleasures, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of renunciation, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-7-8

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in renunciation. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-7-9

Then I thought, ^an9-41-7-10

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of sensual pleasures, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of renunciation, I were to develop that. ^an9-41-7-11

It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in renunciation. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-7-12

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of sensual pleasures and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of renunciation and developed that. ^an9-41-7-13

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in renunciation. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-7-14

And so, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I entered and remained in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an9-41-7-15

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by sensual pleasures beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-7-16

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-7-17

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by sensual pleasures beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-7-18

Then I thought, ^an9-41-8-1

‘Why don’t I, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … enter and remain in the second absorption?’ ^an9-41-8-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in not placing the mind. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-8-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-8-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in not placing the mind? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-8-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-8-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of placing the mind, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of not placing the mind, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-8-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in not placing the mind. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-8-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-8-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of placing the mind, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of not placing the mind, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in not placing the mind. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-8-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of placing the mind and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of not placing the mind and developed that. ^an9-41-8-11

Then my mind did leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in not placing the mind. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-8-12

And so, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled … I entered and remained in the second absorption. ^an9-41-8-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by placing the mind beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-8-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-8-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by placing the mind and keeping it connected beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-8-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-9-1

‘Why don’t I, with the fading away of rapture, enter and remain in the third absorption, where I will meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss”?’ ^an9-41-9-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in freedom from rapture. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-9-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-9-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in freedom from rapture? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-9-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-9-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of rapture, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of freedom from rapture, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-9-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in freedom from rapture. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-9-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-9-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of rapture, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of freedom from rapture, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being free from rapture. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-9-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of rapture and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of freedom from rapture and developed that. ^an9-41-9-11

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in freedom from rapture. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-9-12

And so, with the fading away of rapture … I entered and remained in the third absorption. ^an9-41-9-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by rapture beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-9-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-9-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by rapture beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-9-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-10-1

‘Why don’t I, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness?’ ^an9-41-10-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being without pleasure and pain. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-10-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-10-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being without pleasure and pain? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-10-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-10-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of bliss with equanimity, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of being without pleasure and pain, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-10-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being without pleasure and pain. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-10-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-10-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of bliss with equanimity, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of being without pleasure and pain, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being without pleasure and pain. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-10-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of bliss with equanimity and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of being without pleasure and pain and developed that. ^an9-41-10-11

Then my mind did leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in being without pleasure and pain. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-10-12

And so, giving up pleasure and pain … I entered and remained in the fourth absorption. ^an9-41-10-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by bliss with equanimity beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-10-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-10-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by bliss with equanimity beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-10-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-11-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that “space is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space?’ ^an9-41-11-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite space. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-11-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-11-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite space? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-11-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-11-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of forms, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of the dimension of infinite space, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-11-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite space. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-11-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-11-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of forms, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of the dimension of infinite space, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite space. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-11-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of forms and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of the dimension of infinite space and developed that. ^an9-41-11-11

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in the dimension of infinite space. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-11-12

And so, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-41-11-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by forms beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-11-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-11-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by forms beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-11-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-12-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness?’ ^an9-41-12-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite consciousness. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-12-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-12-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite consciousness? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-12-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-12-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite space, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of the dimension of infinite consciousness, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-12-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite consciousness. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-12-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-12-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite space, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of the dimension of infinite consciousness, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of infinite consciousness. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-12-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite space and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of the dimension of infinite consciousness and developed that. ^an9-41-12-11

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in the dimension of infinite consciousness. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-12-12

And so, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, I entered and remained in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-41-12-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite space beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-12-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-12-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite space beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-12-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-13-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness?’ ^an9-41-13-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of nothingness. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-13-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-13-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of nothingness? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-13-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-13-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite consciousness, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of the dimension of nothingness, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-13-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of nothingness. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-13-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-13-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite consciousness, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of the dimension of nothingness, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of nothingness. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-13-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of the dimension of infinite consciousness and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of the dimension of nothingness and developed that. ^an9-41-13-11

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in the dimension of nothingness. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-13-12

And so, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, I entered and remained in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-41-13-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite consciousness beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-13-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-13-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of infinite consciousness beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-13-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-14-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception?’ ^an9-41-14-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-14-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-14-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-14-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-14-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of the dimension of nothingness, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-14-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-14-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-14-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of the dimension of nothingness, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-14-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of the dimension of nothingness and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception and developed that. ^an9-41-14-11

Then my mind leapt forth, gained confidence, settled down, and became decided in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-14-12

And so, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, I entered and remained in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-41-14-13

While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of nothingness beset me, and that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-14-14

Suppose a happy person were to experience pain; that would be an affliction for them. ^an9-41-14-15

In the same way, when perception and focus accompanied by the dimension of nothingness beset me, that was an affliction for me. ^an9-41-14-16

Then I thought, ^an9-41-15-1

‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling?’ ^an9-41-15-2

But my mind did not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the cessation of perception and feeling. I didn’t see it as peaceful. ^an9-41-15-3

Then I thought, ^an9-41-15-4

‘What is the cause, what is the reason why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the cessation of perception and feeling? Why don’t I see it as peaceful?’ ^an9-41-15-5

Then I thought, ^an9-41-15-6

‘I haven’t seen the drawbacks of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and so I haven’t cultivated that. I haven’t realized the benefits of the cessation of perception and feeling, and so I haven’t developed that. ^an9-41-15-7

That’s why my mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the cessation of perception and feeling. And it’s why I don’t see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-15-8

Then I thought, ^an9-41-15-9

‘Suppose that, seeing the drawbacks of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, I were to cultivate that. And suppose that, realizing the benefits of the cessation of perception and feeling, I were to develop that. It’s possible that my mind would leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the cessation of perception and feeling. And I would see it as peaceful.’ ^an9-41-15-10

And so, after some time, I saw the drawbacks of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception and cultivated that, and I realized the benefits of the cessation of perception and feeling and developed that. ^an9-41-15-11

Then my mind did leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided in the cessation of perception and feeling. I saw it as peaceful. ^an9-41-15-12

And so, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, I entered and remained in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, my defilements were ended. ^an9-41-15-13

As long as I hadn’t entered into and withdrawn from these nine progressive meditative attainments in both forward and reverse order, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an9-41-16-1

But when I had entered into and withdrawn from these nine progressive meditative attainments in both forward and reverse order, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans. ^an9-41-16-2

Knowledge and vision arose in me: ^an9-41-16-3

‘My freedom is unshakable; this is my last rebirth; now there’ll be no more future lives.’” ^an9-41-16-4

### Cramped (Numbered Discourses 9.42) ^an9-42-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Kosambī, in Ghosita’s Monastery. ^an9-42-1-1

Then Venerable Udāyī went up to Venerable Ānanda and exchanged greetings with him. ^an9-42-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda, ^an9-42-1-3

“Reverend, this was said by the godling Pañcālacaṇḍa: ^an9-42-1-4

‘The opening amid confinement ^an9-42-2-1

was discovered by the Buddha of vast intelligence, ^an9-42-2-2

who awakened to absorption, ^an9-42-2-3

the sage, the solitary boss bull.’ ^an9-42-2-4

But what is confinement, and what is the opening amid confinement that the Buddha spoke of?” ^an9-42-3-1

“Reverend, these five kinds of sensual stimulation are called ‘confinement’ by the Buddha. ^an9-42-3-2

What five? ^an9-42-3-3

Sights known by the eye that are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-42-3-4

Sounds known by the ear … ^an9-42-3-5

Smells known by the nose … ^an9-42-3-6

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an9-42-3-7

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-42-3-8

These are the five kinds of sensual stimulation that are called ‘confinement’ by the Buddha. ^an9-42-3-9

Now, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-42-4-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-4-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-4-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-4-4

Whatever placing of the mind and keeping it connected has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-4-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … enters and remains in the second absorption. ^an9-42-5-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-5-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-5-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-5-4

Whatever rapture has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-5-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, with the fading away of rapture … enters and remains in the third absorption. ^an9-42-6-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of creating an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-6-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-6-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-6-4

Whatever bliss with equanimity has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-6-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, giving up pleasure and pain … enters and remains in the fourth absorption. ^an9-42-7-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-7-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-7-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-7-4

Whatever perception of form has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-7-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-42-8-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-8-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-8-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-8-4

Whatever perception of the dimension of infinite space has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-8-5

Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. ^an9-42-9-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-9-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-9-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-9-4

Whatever perception of the dimension of infinite consciousness has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-9-5

Furthermore, a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness. ^an9-42-10-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-10-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-10-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-10-4

Whatever perception of the dimension of nothingness has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-10-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an9-42-11-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a qualified sense. ^an9-42-11-2

But it is still confined. ^an9-42-11-3

Confined by what? ^an9-42-11-4

Whatever perception of neither perception nor non-perception has not ceased is the confinement there. ^an9-42-11-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-42-12-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of an opening amid confinement in a definitive sense.” ^an9-42-12-2

### A Direct Witness (Numbered Discourses 9.43) ^an9-43-0

“Reverend, they speak of the ‘direct witness’. ^an9-43-1-1

What is the direct witness that the Buddha spoke of?” ^an9-43-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-43-2-1

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. ^an9-43-2-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the direct witness in a qualified sense. ^an9-43-2-3

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enters and remains in the second absorption … ^an9-43-3-1

third absorption … ^an9-43-3-2

fourth absorption. ^an9-43-3-3

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. ^an9-43-3-4

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the direct witness in a qualified sense. ^an9-43-3-5

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an9-43-4-1

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. ^an9-43-4-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the direct witness in a qualified sense. Furthermore, take a mendicant who enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness … the dimension of nothingness … the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception … ^an9-43-4-3

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-43-5-1

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. ^an9-43-5-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the direct witness in a definitive sense.” ^an9-43-5-3

### Freed by Wisdom (Numbered Discourses 9.44) ^an9-44-0

“Reverend, they speak of one ‘freed by wisdom’. ^an9-44-1-1

What is the one freed by wisdom that the Buddha spoke of?” ^an9-44-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-44-2-1

And they understand that with wisdom. ^an9-44-2-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the one freed by wisdom in a qualified sense. … ^an9-44-2-3

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-44-3-1

And they understand that with wisdom. ^an9-44-3-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the one freed by wisdom in a definitive sense.” ^an9-44-3-3

### Freed Both Ways (Numbered Discourses 9.45) ^an9-45-0

“Reverend, they speak of one ‘freed both ways’. ^an9-45-1-1

What is the one freed both ways that the Buddha spoke of?” ^an9-45-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-45-2-1

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. And they understand that with wisdom. ^an9-45-2-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the one freed both ways in a qualified sense. … ^an9-45-2-3

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-45-3-1

They meditate directly experiencing that dimension in every way. And they understand that with wisdom. ^an9-45-3-2

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the one freed both ways in a definitive sense.” ^an9-45-3-3

### In the Present Life (Numbered Discourses 9.46) ^an9-46-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘a teaching apparent in the present life’. ^an9-46-1-1

In what way did the Buddha speak of a teaching apparent in the present life?” ^an9-46-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-46-2-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the teaching apparent in the present life in a qualified sense. … ^an9-46-2-2

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-46-3-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of the teaching apparent in the present life in a definitive sense.” ^an9-46-3-2

### Extinguishment is Apparent in the Present Life (Numbered Discourses 9.47) ^an9-47-0

“Reverend, they say that ‘extinguishment is apparent in the present life’. ^an9-47-1-1

In what way did the Buddha say extinguishment is apparent in the present life?” ^an9-47-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-47-2-1

To this extent the Buddha said that extinguishment is apparent in the present life in a qualified sense. … ^an9-47-2-2

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-47-3-1

To this extent the Buddha said that extinguishment is apparent in the present life in a definitive sense.” ^an9-47-3-2

### Extinguishment (Numbered Discourses 9.48) ^an9-48-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘extinguishment’. …” ^an9-48-1-1

### Full Extinguishment (Numbered Discourses 9.49) ^an9-49-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘full extinguishment’. …” ^an9-49-1-1

### Extinguishment in a Certain Respect (Numbered Discourses 9.50) ^an9-50-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘extinguishment in a certain respect’. …” ^an9-50-1-1

### Extinguishment in This Life (Numbered Discourses 9.51) ^an9-51-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘extinguishment in this life’. ^an9-51-1-1

In what way did the Buddha speak of extinguishment in this life?” ^an9-51-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-51-2-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of extinguishment in this life in a qualified sense. … ^an9-51-2-2

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-51-3-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of extinguishment in this life in a definitive sense.” ^an9-51-3-2

### A Safe Place (Numbered Discourses 9.52) ^an9-52-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘a safe place’. ^an9-52-1-1

In what way did the Buddha speak of a safe place?” ^an9-52-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-52-2-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of a safe place in a qualified sense. … ^an9-52-2-2

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-52-3-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of a safe place in a definitive sense.” ^an9-52-3-2

### Reaching a Safe Place (Numbered Discourses 9.53) ^an9-53-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘reaching a safe place’. …” ^an9-53-1-1

### Freedom From Death (Numbered Discourses 9.54) ^an9-54-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘freedom from death’. …” ^an9-54-1-1

### Reaching Freedom From Death (Numbered Discourses 9.55) ^an9-55-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘reaching freedom from death’. …” ^an9-55-1-1

### A Place Without Fear (Numbered Discourses 9.56) ^an9-56-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘a place without fear’. …” ^an9-56-1-1

### Reaching a Place Without Fear (Numbered Discourses 9.57) ^an9-57-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘reaching a place without fear’. …” ^an9-57-1-1

### Tranquility (Numbered Discourses 9.58) ^an9-58-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘tranquility’. …” ^an9-58-1-1

### Progressive Tranquility (Numbered Discourses 9.59) ^an9-59-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘progressive tranquility’. …” ^an9-59-1-1

### Cessation (Numbered Discourses 9.60) ^an9-60-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘cessation’. …” ^an9-60-1-1

### Progressive Cessation (Numbered Discourses 9.61) ^an9-61-0

“Reverend, they speak of ‘progressive cessation’. ^an9-61-1-1

What is the progressive cessation that the Buddha spoke of?” ^an9-61-1-2

“First, take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and remains in the first absorption. ^an9-61-2-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of progressive cessation in a qualified sense. … ^an9-61-2-2

Furthermore, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enters and remains in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an9-61-3-1

To this extent the Buddha spoke of progressive cessation in a definitive sense.” ^an9-61-3-2

### Impossible (Numbered Discourses 9.62) ^an9-62-0

“Mendicants, without giving up nine things you can’t realize perfection. ^an9-62-1-1

What nine? ^an9-62-1-2

Greed, hate, delusion, anger, acrimony, disdain, contempt, jealousy, and stinginess. ^an9-62-1-3

Without giving up these nine things you can’t realize perfection. ^an9-62-1-4

After giving up nine things you can realize perfection. ^an9-62-2-1

What nine? ^an9-62-2-2

Greed, hate, delusion, anger, acrimony, disdain, contempt, jealousy, and stinginess. ^an9-62-2-3

After giving up these nine things you can realize the fruit of perfection.” ^an9-62-2-4

### Weaknesses in Training and Mindfulness Meditation (Numbered Discourses 9.63) ^an9-63-0

“Mendicants, there are these five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-63-1-1

What five? ^an9-63-1-2

Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an9-63-1-3

These are the five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-63-1-4

To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an9-63-2-1

What four? ^an9-63-2-2

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-63-2-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^an9-63-2-4

They meditate observing an aspect of the mind … ^an9-63-2-5

They meditate observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-63-2-6

To give up those five weaknesses in your training you should develop these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^an9-63-2-7

### Hindrances (Numbered Discourses 9.64) ^an9-64-0

“Mendicants, there are these five hindrances. ^an9-64-1-1

What five? ^an9-64-1-2

Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an9-64-1-3

These are the five hindrances. ^an9-64-1-4

To give up these five hindrances you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an9-64-2-1

What four? ^an9-64-2-2

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-64-2-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^an9-64-2-4

They meditate observing an aspect of the mind … ^an9-64-2-5

They meditate observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-64-2-6

To give up those five hindrances you should develop these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^an9-64-2-7

### Kinds of Sensual Stimulation (Numbered Discourses 9.65) ^an9-65-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-65-1-1

What five? ^an9-65-1-2

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-65-1-3

Sounds known by the ear … ^an9-65-1-4

Smells known by the nose … ^an9-65-1-5

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an9-65-1-6

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an9-65-1-7

These are the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an9-65-1-8

To give up these five kinds of sensual stimulation you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-65-2-1

### Grasping Aggregates (Numbered Discourses 9.66) ^an9-66-0

“Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates. ^an9-66-1-1

What five? ^an9-66-1-2

The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness. ^an9-66-1-3

These are the five grasping aggregates. ^an9-66-1-4

To give up these five grasping aggregates you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-66-2-1

### Lower Fetters (Numbered Discourses 9.67) ^an9-67-0

“Mendicants, there are five lower fetters. ^an9-67-1-1

What five? ^an9-67-1-2

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, sensual desire, and ill will. ^an9-67-1-3

These are the five lower fetters. ^an9-67-1-4

To give up these five lower fetters you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-67-2-1

### Places of Rebirth (Numbered Discourses 9.68) ^an9-68-0

“Mendicants, there are five destinations. ^an9-68-1-1

What five? ^an9-68-1-2

Hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, humanity, and the gods. ^an9-68-1-3

These are the five destinations. ^an9-68-1-4

To give up these five destinations you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-68-2-1

### Stinginess (Numbered Discourses 9.69) ^an9-69-0

“Mendicants, there are these five kinds of stinginess. ^an9-69-1-1

What five? ^an9-69-1-2

Stinginess with dwellings, families, material things, praise, and the teaching. ^an9-69-1-3

These are the five kinds of stinginess. ^an9-69-1-4

To give up these five kinds of stinginess you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-69-2-1

### Higher Fetters (Numbered Discourses 9.70) ^an9-70-0

“Mendicants, there are five higher fetters. ^an9-70-1-1

What five? ^an9-70-1-2

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^an9-70-1-3

These are the five higher fetters. ^an9-70-1-4

To give up these five higher fetters you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-70-2-1

### Hard-heartedness (Numbered Discourses 9.71) ^an9-71-0

“Mendicants, there are five kinds of hard-heartedness. ^an9-71-1-1

What five? ^an9-71-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant has doubts about the Teacher. They’re uncertain, undecided, and lacking confidence. ^an9-71-1-3

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an9-71-1-4

This is the first kind of hard-heartedness. ^an9-71-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant has doubts about the teaching … ^an9-71-2-1

the Saṅgha … ^an9-71-2-2

the training … ^an9-71-2-3

A mendicant is angry and upset with their spiritual companions, resentful and closed off. ^an9-71-2-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an9-71-2-5

This is the fifth kind of hard-heartedness. ^an9-71-2-6

To give up these five kinds of hard-heartedness you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. …” ^an9-71-3-1

### Shackles of the Heart (Numbered Discourses 9.72) ^an9-72-0

“Mendicants, there are these five shackles of the heart. ^an9-72-1-1

What five? ^an9-72-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an9-72-1-3

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an9-72-1-4

This is the first shackle of the heart. ^an9-72-1-5

Furthermore, a mendicant isn’t free of greed for the body … ^an9-72-2-1

They’re not free of greed for form … ^an9-72-2-2

They eat as much as they like until their belly is full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing … ^an9-72-2-3

They lead the spiritual life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^an9-72-2-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an9-72-2-5

This is the fifth shackle of the heart. ^an9-72-2-6

These are the five shackles of the heart. ^an9-72-2-7

To give up these five shackles of the heart you should develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an9-72-3-1

What four? ^an9-72-3-2

It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-72-3-3

They meditate observing an aspect of feelings … ^an9-72-3-4

They meditate observing an aspect of the mind … ^an9-72-3-5

They meditate observing an aspect of principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world. ^an9-72-3-6

To give up these five shackles of the heart you should develop these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.” ^an9-72-3-7

### Weaknesses in Training and Effort (Numbered Discourses 9.73) ^an9-73-0

“Mendicants, there are these five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-73-1-1

What five? ^an9-73-1-2

Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an9-73-1-3

These are the five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-73-1-4

To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop the four right efforts. ^an9-73-2-1

What four? ^an9-73-2-2

It’s when a mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. ^an9-73-2-3

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen are given up. ^an9-73-2-4

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities arise. ^an9-73-2-5

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are completed by development. ^an9-73-2-6

To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop these four right efforts.” ^an9-73-2-7

### Hindrances, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 9.74–81) ^an9-74-81-0

(Tell in full as in the chapter on mindfulness meditation.) ^an9-74-81-1-1

### Shackles of the Heart (Numbered Discourses 9.82) ^an9-82-0

“Mendicants, there are these five shackles of the heart. ^an9-82-1-1

What five? ^an9-82-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. … ^an9-82-1-3

These are the five shackles of the heart. ^an9-82-1-4

To give up these five shackles of the heart you should develop the four right efforts. ^an9-82-2-1

What four? ^an9-82-2-2

It’s when a mendicant generates enthusiasm, tries, makes an effort, exerts the mind, and strives so that bad, unskillful qualities don’t arise. … ^an9-82-2-3

so that unskillful qualities are given up … ^an9-82-2-4

so that skillful qualities arise … ^an9-82-2-5

They generate enthusiasm, try, make an effort, exert the mind, and strive so that skillful qualities that have arisen remain, are not lost, but increase, mature, and are completed by development. ^an9-82-2-6

To give up these five shackles of the hearts you should develop these four right efforts.” ^an9-82-2-7

### Weaknesses in Training and the Bases of Psychic Power (Numbered Discourses 9.83) ^an9-83-0

“Mendicants, there are these five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-83-1-1

What five? ^an9-83-1-2

Killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an9-83-1-3

These are the five weaknesses when you’re training. ^an9-83-1-4

To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop the four bases of psychic power. ^an9-83-2-1

What four? ^an9-83-2-2

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^an9-83-2-3

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort. ^an9-83-2-4

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort. ^an9-83-2-5

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. ^an9-83-2-6

To give up these five weaknesses in your training you should develop these four bases of psychic power.” ^an9-83-2-7

### Hindrances, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 9.84–91) ^an9-84-91-0

(Tell in full as in the chapter on mindfulness meditation.) ^an9-84-91-1-1

### Shackles of the Heart (Numbered Discourses 9.92) ^an9-92-0

“Mendicants, there are these five shackles of the heart. ^an9-92-1-1

What five? ^an9-92-1-2

Firstly, a mendicant isn’t free of greed for sensual pleasures. … ^an9-92-1-3

These are the five shackles of the heart. ^an9-92-1-4

To give up these five shackles of the heart you should develop the four bases of psychic power. ^an9-92-2-1

What four? ^an9-92-2-2

It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort. ^an9-92-2-3

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to energy, and active effort. ^an9-92-2-4

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to mental development, and active effort. ^an9-92-2-5

They develop the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to inquiry, and active effort. ^an9-92-2-6

To give up these five shackles of the heart you should develop these four bases of psychic power.” ^an9-92-2-7

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (1st) (Numbered Discourses 9) ^an9-93-0

“For insight into greed, nine things should be developed. ^an9-93-1-1

What nine? ^an9-93-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, not-self in suffering, giving up, and fading away. ^an9-93-1-3

For insight into greed, these nine things should be developed.” ^an9-93-1-4

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 9) ^an9-94-0

“For insight into greed, nine things should be developed. ^an9-94-1-1

What nine? ^an9-94-1-2

The first absorption, the second absorption, the third absorption, the fourth absorption, the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness, the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the cessation of perception and feeling. ^an9-94-1-3

For insight into greed, these nine things should be developed.” ^an9-94-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed (Numbered Discourses 9) ^an9-95-112-0

“For the complete understanding of greed … ^an9-95-112-1-1

complete ending … ^an9-95-112-1-2

giving up … ^an9-95-112-1-3

ending … ^an9-95-112-1-4

vanishing … ^an9-95-112-1-5

fading away … ^an9-95-112-1-6

cessation … ^an9-95-112-1-7

giving away … ^an9-95-112-1-8

letting go of greed … ^an9-95-112-1-9

these nine things should be developed.” ^an9-95-112-1-10

### Untitled Discourses on Hate, Etc. (Numbered Discourses 9) ^an9-113-432-0

“For insight into hate … ^an9-113-432-1-1

delusion … ^an9-113-432-1-2

anger … ^an9-113-432-1-3

acrimony … ^an9-113-432-1-4

disdain … ^an9-113-432-1-5

contempt … ^an9-113-432-1-6

jealousy … ^an9-113-432-1-7

stinginess … ^an9-113-432-1-8

deceitfulness … ^an9-113-432-1-9

deviousness … ^an9-113-432-1-10

obstinacy … ^an9-113-432-1-11

aggression … ^an9-113-432-1-12

conceit … ^an9-113-432-1-13

arrogance … ^an9-113-432-1-14

vanity … ^an9-113-432-1-15

for insight into negligence … ^an9-113-432-1-16

complete understanding … ^an9-113-432-1-17

complete ending … ^an9-113-432-1-18

giving up … ^an9-113-432-1-19

ending … ^an9-113-432-1-20

vanishing … ^an9-113-432-1-21

fading away … ^an9-113-432-1-22

ceasing … ^an9-113-432-1-23

giving away … ^an9-113-432-1-24

letting go of negligence … ^an9-113-432-1-25

these nine things should be developed.” ^an9-113-432-1-26

The Book of the Nines is finished. ^an9-113-432-1-28

## Aṅguttara Nipāta 10 ^an10-0

### What’s the Goal? (Numbered Discourses 10.1) ^an10-1-0

So I have heard. ^an10-1-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-1-1-2

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-1-1-3

“Sir, what is the goal and benefit of skillful ethics?” ^an10-1-2-1

“Ānanda, having no regrets is the goal and benefit of skillful ethics.” ^an10-1-2-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of having no regrets?” ^an10-1-3-1

“Joy is the goal and benefit of having no regrets.” ^an10-1-3-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of joy?” ^an10-1-4-1

“Rapture …” ^an10-1-4-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of rapture?” ^an10-1-5-1

“Tranquility …” ^an10-1-5-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of tranquility?” ^an10-1-6-1

“Bliss …” ^an10-1-6-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of bliss?” ^an10-1-7-1

“Immersion …” ^an10-1-7-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of immersion?” ^an10-1-8-1

“Truly knowing and seeing …” ^an10-1-8-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of truly knowing and seeing?” ^an10-1-9-1

“Disillusionment and dispassion …” ^an10-1-9-2

“But what is the goal and benefit of disillusionment and dispassion?” ^an10-1-10-1

“Knowledge and vision of freedom is the goal and benefit of disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-1-10-2

So, Ānanda, the goal and benefit of skillful ethics is not having regrets. ^an10-1-11-1

Joy is the goal and benefit of not having regrets. ^an10-1-11-2

Rapture is the goal and benefit of joy. ^an10-1-11-3

Tranquility is the goal and benefit of rapture. ^an10-1-11-4

Bliss is the goal and benefit of tranquility. ^an10-1-11-5

Immersion is the goal and benefit of bliss. ^an10-1-11-6

Truly knowing and seeing is the goal and benefit of immersion. ^an10-1-11-7

Disillusionment and dispassion is the goal and benefit of truly knowing and seeing. ^an10-1-11-8

Knowledge and vision of freedom is the goal and benefit of disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-1-11-9

So, Ānanda, skillful ethics progressively lead up to the highest.” ^an10-1-11-10

### Making a Wish (Numbered Discourses 10.2) ^an10-2-0

“Mendicants, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethical conduct, need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-1

‘May I have no regrets!’ ^an10-2-1-2

It’s only natural that an ethical person has no regrets. ^an10-2-1-3

When you have no regrets you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-4

‘May I feel joy!’ ^an10-2-1-5

It’s only natural that joy springs up when you have no regrets. ^an10-2-1-6

When you feel joy you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-7

‘May I experience rapture!’ ^an10-2-1-8

It’s only natural that rapture arises when you’re joyful. ^an10-2-1-9

When your mind is full of rapture you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-10

‘May my body become tranquil!’ ^an10-2-1-11

It’s only natural that your body becomes tranquil when your mind is full of rapture. ^an10-2-1-12

When your body is tranquil you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-13

‘May I feel bliss!’ ^an10-2-1-14

It’s only natural to feel bliss when your body is tranquil. ^an10-2-1-15

When you feel bliss you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-16

‘May my mind be immersed in samādhi!’ ^an10-2-1-17

It’s only natural for the mind to be immersed in samādhi when you feel bliss. ^an10-2-1-18

When your mind is immersed in samādhi you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-19

‘May I truly know and see!’ ^an10-2-1-20

It’s only natural to truly know and see when your mind is immersed in samādhi. ^an10-2-1-21

When you truly know and see you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-22

‘May I become disillusioned and dispassionate!’ ^an10-2-1-23

It’s only natural to become disillusioned and dispassionate when you truly know and see. ^an10-2-1-24

When you’re disillusioned and dispassionate you need not make a wish: ^an10-2-1-25

‘May I realize the knowledge and vision of freedom!’ ^an10-2-1-26

It’s only natural to realize the knowledge and vision of freedom when you’re disillusioned and dispassionate. ^an10-2-1-27

And so, mendicants, the knowledge and vision of freedom is the goal and benefit of disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-2-2-1

Disillusionment and dispassion is the goal and benefit of truly knowing and seeing. ^an10-2-2-2

Truly knowing and seeing is the goal and benefit of immersion. ^an10-2-2-3

Immersion is the goal and benefit of bliss. ^an10-2-2-4

Bliss is the goal and benefit of tranquility. ^an10-2-2-5

Tranquility is the goal and benefit of rapture. ^an10-2-2-6

Rapture is the goal and benefit of joy. ^an10-2-2-7

Joy is the goal and benefit of not having regrets. ^an10-2-2-8

Not having regrets is the goal and benefit of skillful ethics. ^an10-2-2-9

And so, mendicants, good qualities flow on and fill up from one to the other, for going from the near shore to the far shore.” ^an10-2-2-10

### Vital Conditions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.3) ^an10-3-0

“Mendicants, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-3-1-1

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. ^an10-3-1-2

When there is no joy, one who lacks joy has destroyed a vital condition for rapture. ^an10-3-1-3

When there is no rapture, one who lacks rapture has destroyed a vital condition for tranquility. ^an10-3-1-4

When there is no tranquility, one who lacks tranquility has destroyed a vital condition for bliss. ^an10-3-1-5

When there is no bliss, one who lacks bliss has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an10-3-1-6

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an10-3-1-7

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-3-1-8

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-3-1-9

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an10-3-1-10

In the same way, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-3-1-11

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-3-1-12

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-3-1-13

An ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-3-2-1

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. ^an10-3-2-2

When there is joy, one who has fulfilled joy has fulfilled a vital condition for rapture. ^an10-3-2-3

When there is rapture, one who has fulfilled rapture has fulfilled a vital condition for tranquility. ^an10-3-2-4

When there is tranquility, one who has fulfilled tranquility has fulfilled a vital condition for bliss. ^an10-3-2-5

When there is bliss, one who has fulfilled bliss has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an10-3-2-6

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an10-3-2-7

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-3-2-8

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-3-2-9

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would grow to fullness. ^an10-3-2-10

In the same way, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-3-2-11

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-3-2-12

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an10-3-2-13

### Vital Conditions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.4) ^an10-4-0

There Venerable Sāriputta addressed the mendicants … ^an10-4-1-1

“Reverends, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-4-1-2

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-4-1-3

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-4-1-4

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an10-4-1-5

In the same way, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-4-1-6

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-4-1-7

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-4-1-8

An ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-4-2-1

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-4-2-2

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-4-2-3

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would grow to fullness. ^an10-4-2-4

In the same way, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-4-2-5

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-4-2-6

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an10-4-2-7

### Vital Conditions (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 10.5) ^an10-5-0

There Venerable Ānanda addressed the mendicants … ^an10-5-1-1

“Reverends, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-5-1-2

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. ^an10-5-1-3

When there is no joy, one who lacks joy has destroyed a vital condition for rapture. ^an10-5-1-4

When there is no rapture, one who lacks rapture has destroyed a vital condition for tranquility. ^an10-5-1-5

When there is no tranquility, one who lacks tranquility has destroyed a vital condition for bliss. ^an10-5-1-6

When there is no bliss, one who lacks bliss has destroyed a vital condition for right immersion. ^an10-5-1-7

When there is no right immersion, one who lacks right immersion has destroyed a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an10-5-1-8

When there is no true knowledge and vision, one who lacks true knowledge and vision has destroyed a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-5-1-9

When there is no disillusionment and dispassion, one who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-5-1-10

Suppose there was a tree that lacked branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would not grow to fullness. ^an10-5-1-11

In the same way, an unethical person, who lacks ethics, has destroyed a vital condition for having no regrets. ^an10-5-1-12

When there are regrets, one who has regrets has destroyed a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-5-1-13

One who lacks disillusionment and dispassion has destroyed a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-5-1-14

An ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-5-2-1

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. ^an10-5-2-2

When there is joy, one who has fulfilled joy has fulfilled a vital condition for rapture. ^an10-5-2-3

When there is rapture, one who has fulfilled rapture has fulfilled a vital condition for tranquility. ^an10-5-2-4

When there is tranquility, one who has fulfilled tranquility has fulfilled a vital condition for bliss. ^an10-5-2-5

When there is bliss, one who has fulfilled bliss has fulfilled a vital condition for right immersion. ^an10-5-2-6

When there is right immersion, one who has fulfilled right immersion has fulfilled a vital condition for true knowledge and vision. ^an10-5-2-7

When there is true knowledge and vision, one who has fulfilled true knowledge and vision has fulfilled a vital condition for disillusionment and dispassion. ^an10-5-2-8

When there is disillusionment and dispassion, one who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-5-2-9

Suppose there was a tree that was complete with branches and foliage. Its shoots, bark, softwood, and heartwood would grow to fullness. ^an10-5-2-10

In the same way, an ethical person, who has fulfilled ethics, has fulfilled a vital condition for not having regrets. ^an10-5-2-11

When there are no regrets, one who has no regrets has fulfilled a vital condition for joy. … ^an10-5-2-12

One who has fulfilled disillusionment and dispassion has fulfilled a vital condition for knowledge and vision of freedom.” ^an10-5-2-13

### Immersion (Numbered Discourses 10.6) ^an10-6-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-6-1-1

“Could it be, sir, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And they wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an10-6-1-2

“It could be, Ānanda, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And they wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an10-6-1-3

“But how could this be, sir?” ^an10-6-2-1

“Ānanda, it’s when a mendicant perceives: ^an10-6-3-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an10-6-3-2

That’s how a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And they wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an10-6-3-3

### Sāriputta (Numbered Discourses 10.7) ^an10-7-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an10-7-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^an10-7-1-2

“Could it be, reverend Sāriputta, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And they wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an10-7-2-1

“It could be, Reverend Ānanda.” ^an10-7-3-1

“But how could this be?” ^an10-7-4-1

“Reverend Ānanda, this one time I was staying right here at Sāvatthī in the Dark Forest. ^an10-7-4-2

There I gained a state of immersion like this. I didn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And I didn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. And I didn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And yet I still perceived.” ^an10-7-4-3

“But at that time what did Reverend Sāriputta perceive?” ^an10-7-5-1

“One perception arose in me and another perception ceased: ‘The cessation of continued existence is extinguishment. The cessation of continued existence is extinguishment.’ ^an10-7-5-2

Suppose there was a burning pile of twigs. One flame would arise and another would cease. ^an10-7-5-3

In the same way, one perception arose in me and another perception ceased: ‘The cessation of continued existence is extinguishment. The cessation of continued existence is extinguishment.’ ^an10-7-5-4

At that time I perceived that the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment.” ^an10-7-5-5

### Inspiring All Around: the Absorptions (Numbered Discourses 10.8) ^an10-8-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant is faithful but not ethical. ^an10-8-1-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an10-8-1-2

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an10-8-1-3

‘How can I become faithful and ethical?’ ^an10-8-1-4

When the mendicant is faithful and ethical, they’re complete in that respect. ^an10-8-1-5

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but not learned. … ^an10-8-2-1

they’re not a Dhamma speaker … ^an10-8-2-2

they don’t frequent assemblies … ^an10-8-2-3

they don’t teach Dhamma to the assembly with assurance … ^an10-8-2-4

they’re not an expert in the monastic law … ^an10-8-2-5

they don’t stay in the wilderness, in remote lodgings … ^an10-8-2-6

they don’t get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty … ^an10-8-2-7

they don’t realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-8-2-8

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an10-8-2-9

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an10-8-2-10

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the training, one who lives in the wilderness, in remote lodgings, one who gets the four absorptions when they want, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements?’ ^an10-8-2-11

When they’re faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the training, one who lives in the wilderness, in remote lodgings, one who gets the four absorptions when they want, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements, ^an10-8-3-1

they’re complete in that respect. ^an10-8-3-2

A mendicant who possesses these ten qualities is impressive all around, and is complete in every respect.” ^an10-8-3-3

### Inspiring All Around: the Peaceful Liberations (Numbered Discourses 10.9) ^an10-9-0

“A mendicant is faithful, but not ethical. … ^an10-9-1-1

they’re not learned. … ^an10-9-1-2

they’re not a Dhamma speaker … ^an10-9-1-3

they don’t frequent assemblies … ^an10-9-1-4

they don’t teach Dhamma to the assembly with assurance … ^an10-9-1-5

they’re not an expert in the training … ^an10-9-1-6

they don’t stay in the wilderness, in remote lodgings … ^an10-9-1-7

they don’t have direct meditative experience of the peaceful liberations that are formless, transcending form … ^an10-9-1-8

they don’t realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-9-1-9

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an10-9-1-10

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an10-9-1-11

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the training, one who lives in the wilderness, in remote lodgings, one who gets the formless liberations, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements?’ ^an10-9-1-12

When they’re faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the monastic law, one who lives in the wilderness, in remote lodgings, one who gets the formless liberations, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements, ^an10-9-2-1

they’re complete in that respect. ^an10-9-2-2

A mendicant who possesses these ten qualities is impressive all around, and is complete in every respect.” ^an10-9-2-3

### Inspiring All Around: the Three Knowledges (Numbered Discourses 10.10) ^an10-10-0

“A mendicant is faithful, but not ethical. ^an10-10-1-1

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an10-10-1-2

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an10-10-1-3

‘How can I become faithful and ethical?’ ^an10-10-1-4

When the mendicant is faithful and ethical, they’re complete in that respect. ^an10-10-1-5

A mendicant is faithful and ethical, but not learned … ^an10-10-2-1

they’re not a Dhamma speaker … ^an10-10-2-2

they don’t frequent assemblies … ^an10-10-2-3

they don’t teach Dhamma to the assembly with assurance … ^an10-10-2-4

they’re not an expert in the monastic law … ^an10-10-2-5

they don’t recollect their many kinds of past lives … ^an10-10-2-6

they don’t, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, see sentient beings passing away and being reborn … ^an10-10-2-7

they don’t realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-10-2-8

So they’re incomplete in that respect, ^an10-10-2-9

and should fulfill it, thinking: ^an10-10-2-10

‘How can I become faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the training, one who recollects their many kinds of past lives, one who with clairvoyance that surpasses the human sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements?’ ^an10-10-2-11

When they are faithful, ethical, and learned, a Dhamma speaker, one who frequents assemblies, one who teaches Dhamma to the assembly with assurance, an expert in the training, one who recollects their many kinds of past lives, one who with clairvoyance that surpasses the human sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn, and one who lives having realized the ending of defilements, ^an10-10-3-1

they’re complete in that respect. ^an10-10-3-2

A mendicant who possesses these ten qualities is impressive all around, and is complete in every respect.” ^an10-10-3-3

### Lodgings (Numbered Discourses 10.11) ^an10-11-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with five factors, using and frequenting lodgings with five factors, will soon realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-11-1-1

And how does a mendicant have five factors? ^an10-11-2-1

It’s when a noble disciple has faith in the Realized One’s awakening: ^an10-11-2-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an10-11-2-3

They are rarely ill or unwell. Their stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation. ^an10-11-2-4

They’re not devious or deceitful. They reveal themselves honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions. ^an10-11-2-5

They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an10-11-2-6

They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an10-11-2-7

That’s how a mendicant has five factors. ^an10-11-2-8

And how does a lodging have five factors? ^an10-11-3-1

It’s when a lodging is neither too far nor too near, but convenient for coming and going. ^an10-11-3-2

It’s not bothered by people by day, and at night it’s quiet and still. ^an10-11-3-3

There’s little disturbance from flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. ^an10-11-3-4

While staying in that lodging the necessities of life—robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick—are easy to come by. ^an10-11-3-5

And in that lodging there are several senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines. ^an10-11-3-6

From time to time they go up to those mendicants and ask them questions: ^an10-11-3-7

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an10-11-3-8

Those venerables reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an10-11-3-9

That’s how a lodging has five factors. ^an10-11-3-10
