A mendicant with five factors, using and frequenting lodgings with five factors, will soon realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements.” ^an10-11-3-11

### Five Factors (Numbered Discourses 10.12) ^an10-12-0

“Mendicants, in this teaching and training a mendicant who has given up five factors and possesses five factors is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person. ^an10-12-1-1

And how has a mendicant given up five factors? ^an10-12-1-2

It’s when a mendicant has given up sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an10-12-1-3

That’s how a mendicant has given up five factors. ^an10-12-1-4

And how does a mendicant have five factors? ^an10-12-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has the entire spectrum of an adept’s ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-12-2-2

That’s how a mendicant has five factors. ^an10-12-2-3

In this teaching and training a mendicant who has given up five factors and possesses five factors is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person. ^an10-12-3-1

Sensual desire, ill will, ^an10-12-4-1

dullness and drowsiness, ^an10-12-4-2

restlessness, and doubt ^an10-12-4-3

are not found in a mendicant at all. ^an10-12-4-4

That sort are accomplished ^an10-12-5-1

in an adept’s ethics, ^an10-12-5-2

an adept’s immersion, ^an10-12-5-3

and freedom and knowledge. ^an10-12-5-4

Possessing these five factors, ^an10-12-6-1

and rid of five factors, ^an10-12-6-2

in this teaching and training ^an10-12-6-3

they’re called ‘consummate’.” ^an10-12-6-4

### Fetters (Numbered Discourses 10.13) ^an10-13-0

“Mendicants, there are ten fetters. ^an10-13-1-1

What ten? ^an10-13-1-2

The five lower fetters and the five higher fetters. ^an10-13-1-3

What are the five lower fetters? ^an10-13-1-4

Substantialist view, doubt, misapprehension of precepts and observances, sensual desire, and ill will. ^an10-13-1-5

These are the five lower fetters. ^an10-13-1-6

What are the five higher fetters? ^an10-13-2-1

Desire for rebirth in the realm of luminous form, desire for rebirth in the formless realm, conceit, restlessness, and ignorance. ^an10-13-2-2

These are the five higher fetters. ^an10-13-2-3

These are the ten fetters.” ^an10-13-2-4

### Hard-heartedness (Numbered Discourses 10.14) ^an10-14-0

“Mendicants, a monk or nun who has not given up five kinds of hard-heartedness and has not severed five shackles of the heart can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-14-1-1

What are the five kinds of hard-heartedness they haven’t given up? ^an10-14-2-1

Firstly, a mendicant has doubts about the Teacher. They’re uncertain, undecided, and lacking confidence. ^an10-14-2-2

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-2-3

This is the first kind of hard-heartedness they haven’t given up. ^an10-14-2-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has doubts about the teaching … ^an10-14-3-1

the Saṅgha … ^an10-14-3-2

the training … ^an10-14-3-3

A mendicant is angry and upset with their spiritual companions, resentful and closed off. ^an10-14-3-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-3-5

This is the fifth kind of hard-heartedness they haven’t given up. ^an10-14-3-6

These are the five kinds of hard-heartedness they haven’t given up. ^an10-14-3-7

What are the five shackles of the heart they haven’t severed? ^an10-14-4-1

Firstly, a mendicant isn’t free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an10-14-4-2

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-4-3

This is the first shackle of the heart they haven’t severed. ^an10-14-4-4

Furthermore, a mendicant isn’t free of greed for the body … ^an10-14-5-1

They’re not free of greed for form … ^an10-14-5-2

They eat as much as they like until their belly is full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing … ^an10-14-5-3

They lead the spiritual life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^an10-14-5-4

This being so, their mind doesn’t incline toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-5-5

This is the fifth shackle of the heart they haven’t severed. ^an10-14-5-6

These are the five shackles of the heart they haven’t severed. ^an10-14-5-7

A monk or nun who has not given up these five kinds of hard-heartedness and has not severed these five shackles of the heart can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-14-6-1

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^an10-14-7-1

In the same way, monk or nun who has not given up these five kinds of hard-heartedness and has not severed these five shackles of the heart can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-14-7-2

A monk or nun who has given up five kinds of hard-heartedness and has severed five shackles of the heart can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-14-8-1

What are the five kinds of hard-heartedness they’ve given up? ^an10-14-9-1

Firstly, a mendicant has no doubts about the Teacher. They’re not uncertain, undecided, or lacking confidence. ^an10-14-9-2

This being so, their mind inclines toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-9-3

This is the first kind of hard-heartedness they’ve given up. ^an10-14-9-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has no doubts about the teaching … ^an10-14-10-1

the Saṅgha … ^an10-14-10-2

the training … ^an10-14-10-3

A mendicant is not angry and upset with their spiritual companions, not resentful or closed off. ^an10-14-10-4

This being so, their mind inclines toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-10-5

This is the fifth kind of hard-heartedness they’ve given up. ^an10-14-10-6

These are the five kinds of hard-heartedness they’ve given up. ^an10-14-10-7

What are the five shackles of the heart they’ve severed? ^an10-14-11-1

Firstly, a mendicant is rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures. ^an10-14-11-2

This being so, their mind inclines toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-11-3

This is the first shackle of the heart they’ve severed. ^an10-14-11-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is rid of greed for the body … ^an10-14-12-1

They’re rid of greed for form … ^an10-14-12-2

They don’t eat as much as they like until their belly is full, then indulge in the pleasures of sleeping, lying down, and drowsing … ^an10-14-12-3

They don’t lead the spiritual life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’ ^an10-14-12-4

This being so, their mind inclines toward keenness, commitment, persistence, and striving. ^an10-14-12-5

This is the fifth shackle of the heart they’ve severed. ^an10-14-12-6

These are the five shackles of the heart they’ve severed. ^an10-14-12-7

A monk or nun who has given up these five kinds of hard-heartedness and has severed these five shackles of the heart can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-14-13-1

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^an10-14-14-1

In the same way, a monk or nun who has given up these five kinds of hard-heartedness and has severed these five shackles of the heart can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night.” ^an10-14-14-2

### Diligence (Numbered Discourses 10.15) ^an10-15-0

“Mendicants, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha, is said to be the best of all sentient beings—be they footless, with two feet, four feet, or many feet; with form or formless; with perception or without perception or with neither perception nor non-perception. ^an10-15-1-1

In the same way, all skillful qualities are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them. ^an10-15-1-2

The footprints of all creatures that walk can fit inside an elephant’s footprint, so an elephant’s footprint is said to be the biggest of them all. ^an10-15-2-1

In the same way, all skillful qualities are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them. ^an10-15-2-2

The rafters of a bungalow all lean to the peak, slope to the peak, and meet at the peak, so the peak is said to be the topmost of them all. ^an10-15-3-1

In the same way, all skillful qualities are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them. ^an10-15-3-2

Of all kinds of fragrant root, spikenard is said to be the best. ^an10-15-4-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-4-2

Of all kinds of fragrant heartwood, red sandalwood is said to be the best. ^an10-15-5-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-5-2

Of all kinds of fragrant flower, jasmine is said to be the best. ^an10-15-6-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-6-2

All lesser kings are vassals of a wheel-turning monarch, so the wheel-turning monarch is said to be the foremost of them all. ^an10-15-7-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-7-2

The radiance of all the stars is not worth a sixteenth part of the moon’s radiance, so the moon’s radiance is said to be the best of them all. ^an10-15-8-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-8-2

In the autumn the heavens are sunny and cloudless. And as the sun is rising to the firmament, having dispelled all the darkness of space, it shines and glows and radiates. ^an10-15-9-1

In the same way … ^an10-15-9-2

All the great rivers—that is, the Ganges, Yamunā, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—flow, slant, slope, and incline towards the ocean, and the ocean is said to be the greatest of them. ^an10-15-10-1

In the same way, all skillful qualities are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them.” ^an10-15-10-2

### Worthy of Offerings Dedicated to the Gods (Numbered Discourses 10.16) ^an10-16-0

“Mendicants, these ten individuals are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an10-16-1-1

What ten? ^an10-16-1-2

A Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha; an Independent Buddha; one freed both ways; one freed by wisdom; a direct witness; one attained to view; one freed by faith; a follower by faith; a follower of teachings; a lamb of the flock. ^an10-16-1-3

These are the ten individuals who are worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and are the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an10-16-1-4

### A Protector (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.17) ^an10-17-0

“Mendicants, you should live with a protector, not without one. ^an10-17-1-1

Living without a protector is suffering. ^an10-17-1-2

There are ten qualities that serve as protector. ^an10-17-1-3

What ten? ^an10-17-1-4

Firstly, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-17-1-5

This is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-1-7

Furthermore, a mendicant is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-17-2-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-2-3

Furthermore, a mendicant has good friends, companions, and associates. ^an10-17-3-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-3-3

Furthermore, a mendicant is easy to admonish, having qualities that make them easy to admonish. They’re patient, and take instruction respectfully. ^an10-17-4-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant is deft and tireless in a diverse spectrum of duties for their spiritual companions, understanding how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an10-17-5-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-5-3

Furthermore, a mendicant loves the teachings and is a delight to converse with, being full of joy in the teaching and training. ^an10-17-6-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-6-3

Furthermore, a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an10-17-7-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-7-3

Furthermore, a mendicant is content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an10-17-8-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-8-3

Furthermore, a mendicant is mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. ^an10-17-9-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-9-3

Furthermore, a mendicant is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an10-17-10-1

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-17-10-3

You should live with a protector, not without one. ^an10-17-11-1

Living without a protector is suffering. ^an10-17-11-2

These are the ten qualities that serve as protector.” ^an10-17-11-3

### A Protector (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.18) ^an10-18-0

So I have heard. ^an10-18-1-1

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-18-1-2

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an10-18-1-3

“Mendicants!” ^an10-18-1-4

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an10-18-1-5

The Buddha said this: ^an10-18-1-6

“Mendicants, you should live with a protector, not without one. ^an10-18-2-1

Living without a protector is suffering. ^an10-18-2-2

There are ten qualities that serve as protector. ^an10-18-2-3

What ten? ^an10-18-2-4

Firstly, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-18-2-5

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-2-6

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-2-7

This is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-2-8

Furthermore, a mendicant is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reinforcing them by recitation, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-18-3-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-3-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-3-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-3-4

Furthermore, a mendicant has good friends, companions, and associates. ^an10-18-4-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-4-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-4-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-4-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is easy to admonish, having qualities that make them easy to admonish. They’re patient, and take instruction respectfully. ^an10-18-5-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-5-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-5-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-5-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is deft and tireless in a diverse spectrum of duties for their spiritual companions, understanding how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. ^an10-18-6-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-6-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-6-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-6-4

Furthermore, a mendicant loves the teachings and is a delight to converse with, being full of joy in the teaching and training. ^an10-18-7-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-7-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-7-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-7-4

Furthermore, a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. ^an10-18-8-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-8-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-8-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-8-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an10-18-9-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-9-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-9-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-9-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. ^an10-18-10-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-10-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-10-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-10-4

Furthermore, a mendicant is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an10-18-11-1

Knowing this, the mendicants—whether senior, middle, or junior—think that mendicant is worth advising and instructing. ^an10-18-11-2

Being treated with such kindness by the senior, middle, and junior mendicants, that mendicant can expect only growth, not decline. ^an10-18-11-3

This too is a quality that serves as protector. ^an10-18-11-4

You should live with a protector, not without one. ^an10-18-12-1

Living without a protector is suffering. ^an10-18-12-2

These are the ten qualities that serve as protector.” ^an10-18-12-3

That is what the Buddha said. ^an10-18-12-4

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an10-18-12-5

### Abodes of the Noble Ones (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.19) ^an10-19-0

“There are these ten abodes of the noble ones in which the noble ones of the past, present, and future abide. ^an10-19-1-1

What ten? ^an10-19-1-2

A mendicant has given up five factors, is endowed with six factors, has a single guard, has four supports, has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth, has totally given up searching, has pure intentions, has stilled the physical process, and is well freed in mind and well freed by wisdom. ^an10-19-1-3

These are the ten abodes of the noble ones in which the noble ones of the past, present, and future abide.” ^an10-19-1-4

### Abodes of the Noble Ones (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.20) ^an10-20-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kurus, near the Kuru town named Kammāsadamma. ^an10-20-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an10-20-1-2

“There are these ten abodes of the noble ones in which the noble ones of the past, present, and future abide. ^an10-20-2-1

What ten? ^an10-20-2-2

A mendicant has given up five factors, possesses six factors, has a single guard, has four supports, has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth, has totally given up searching, has unsullied intentions, has stilled the physical process, and is well freed in mind and well freed by wisdom. ^an10-20-2-3

And how has a mendicant given up five factors? ^an10-20-3-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt. ^an10-20-3-2

That’s how a mendicant has given up five factors. ^an10-20-3-3

And how does a mendicant possess six factors? ^an10-20-4-1

It’s when a mendicant, seeing a sight with their eyes, is neither happy nor sad. They remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an10-20-4-2

Hearing a sound with their ears … ^an10-20-4-3

Smelling an odor with their nose … ^an10-20-4-4

Tasting a flavor with their tongue … ^an10-20-4-5

Encountering a touch with their body … ^an10-20-4-6

Knowing an idea with their mind, they’re neither happy nor sad. They remain equanimous, mindful and aware. ^an10-20-4-7

That’s how a mendicant possesses six factors. ^an10-20-4-8

And how does a mendicant have a single guard? ^an10-20-5-1

It’s when a mendicant’s heart is guarded by mindfulness. ^an10-20-5-2

That’s how a mendicant has a single guard. ^an10-20-5-3

And how does a mendicant have four supports? ^an10-20-6-1

After appraisal, a mendicant uses some things, endures some things, avoids some things, and gets rid of some things. ^an10-20-6-2

That’s how a mendicant has four supports. ^an10-20-6-3

And how has a mendicant cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth? ^an10-20-7-1

Different ascetics and brahmins have different idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth. For example: ^an10-20-7-2

the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists. ^an10-20-7-3

A mendicant has cast out, cast aside, thrown out, discarded, let go of, given up, and relinquished all these. ^an10-20-7-4

That’s how a mendicant has cast aside idiosyncratic interpretations of the truth. ^an10-20-7-5

And how has a mendicant totally given up searching? ^an10-20-8-1

It’s when they’ve given up searching for sensual pleasures, for continued existence, and for a spiritual life. ^an10-20-8-2

That’s how a mendicant has totally given up searching. ^an10-20-8-3

And how does a mendicant have unsullied intentions? ^an10-20-9-1

It’s when a mendicant has given up intentions of sensuality, malice, and cruelty. ^an10-20-9-2

That’s how a mendicant has unsullied intentions. ^an10-20-9-3

And how has a mendicant stilled the physical process? ^an10-20-10-1

It’s when, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. ^an10-20-10-2

That’s how a mendicant has stilled the physical process. ^an10-20-10-3

And how is a mendicant well freed in mind? ^an10-20-11-1

It’s when a mendicant’s mind is freed from greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-20-11-2

That’s how a mendicant is well freed in mind. ^an10-20-11-3

And how is a mendicant well freed by wisdom? ^an10-20-12-1

It’s when a mendicant understands: ‘I’ve given up greed, hate, and delusion, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they’re unable to arise in the future.’ ^an10-20-12-2

That’s how a mendicant’s mind is well freed by wisdom. ^an10-20-12-3

Mendicants, whether in the past, future, or present, all noble ones abide in these same ten abodes of the noble ones. ^an10-20-13-1

These are the ten abodes of the noble ones in which the noble ones of the past, present, and future abide.” ^an10-20-13-4

### The Lion’s Roar (Numbered Discourses 10.21) ^an10-21-0

“Mendicants, towards evening the lion, king of beasts, emerges from his den, ^an10-21-1-1

yawns, ^an10-21-1-2

surveys the four quarters, ^an10-21-1-3

and roars his lion’s roar three times. ^an10-21-1-4

Then he sets out on the hunt. ^an10-21-1-5

Why is that? ^an10-21-1-6

‘May I not injure any little creatures on unclear ground.’ ^an10-21-1-7

‘Lion’ is a term for the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha. ^an10-21-2-1

When the Realized One teaches Dhamma to an assembly, this is his lion’s roar. ^an10-21-2-2

The Realized One possesses ten powers of a Realized One. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an10-21-3-1

What ten? ^an10-21-3-2

Firstly, the Realized One truly understands the possible as possible and the impossible as impossible. ^an10-21-3-3

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. Relying on this he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an10-21-3-4

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes. ^an10-21-4-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-4-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands where all paths of practice lead. ^an10-21-5-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-5-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the world with its many and diverse elements. ^an10-21-6-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-6-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the diverse convictions of sentient beings. ^an10-21-7-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-7-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the faculties of other sentient beings and other individuals after encompassing them with his mind. ^an10-21-8-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-8-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments. ^an10-21-9-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-9-2

Furthermore, the Realized One recollects many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. He remembers: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ Thus he recollects his many past lives, with features and details. ^an10-21-10-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-10-2

Furthermore, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, the Realized One sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, he sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an10-21-11-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-11-2

Furthermore, the Realized One has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-21-12-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-21-12-2

These are the ten powers of a Realized One that the Realized One possesses. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel.” ^an10-21-13-1

### Hypotheses (Numbered Discourses 10.22) ^an10-22-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an10-22-1-1

“Ānanda, I claim to be self-assured regarding the teachings that lead to realizing by insight the various different hypotheses. ^an10-22-2-1

So I am able to teach the Dhamma in appropriate ways to different people. Practicing accordingly, when something exists they’ll know it exists. When it doesn’t exist they’ll know it doesn’t exist. When something is inferior they’ll know it’s inferior. When it’s superior they’ll know it’s superior. When something is not supreme they’ll know it’s not supreme. When it is supreme they’ll know it’s supreme. ^an10-22-2-2

And they will know or see or realize it in whatever way it should be known or seen or realized. This is possible. ^an10-22-2-3

But this is the unsurpassable knowledge, that is: truly knowing each and every case. ^an10-22-2-4

And Ānanda, I say that there is no other knowledge better or finer than this. ^an10-22-2-5

The Realized One possesses ten powers of a Realized One. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an10-22-3-1

What ten? ^an10-22-3-2

Firstly, the Realized One truly understands the possible as possible, and the impossible as impossible. ^an10-22-3-3

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. Relying on this he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel. ^an10-22-3-4

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the result of deeds undertaken in the past, future, and present in terms of grounds and causes. ^an10-22-4-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-4-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands where all paths of practice lead. ^an10-22-5-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-5-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the world with its many and diverse elements. ^an10-22-6-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-6-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the diverse convictions of sentient beings. ^an10-22-7-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-7-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands the faculties of other sentient beings and other individuals after encompassing them with his mind. ^an10-22-8-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-8-2

Furthermore, the Realized One truly understands corruption, cleansing, and emergence regarding the absorptions, liberations, immersions, and attainments. ^an10-22-9-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-9-2

Furthermore, the Realized One recollects many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an10-22-10-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-10-2

Furthermore, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, the Realized One sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an10-22-11-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-11-2

Furthermore, the Realized One has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-22-12-1

Since he truly understands this, this is a power of the Realized One. … ^an10-22-12-2

These are the ten powers of a Realized One that the Realized One possesses. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel.” ^an10-22-13-1

### Body (Numbered Discourses 10.23) ^an10-23-0

“Mendicants, there are things that should be given up by the body, not by speech. ^an10-23-1-1

There are things that should be given up by speech, not by the body. ^an10-23-1-2

There are things that should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom. ^an10-23-1-3

And what are the things that should be given up by the body, not by speech? ^an10-23-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has committed a certain unskillful offense by way of body. ^an10-23-2-2

After examination, sensible spiritual companions say this to them: ^an10-23-2-3

‘Venerable, you’ve committed a certain unskillful offense by way of body. ^an10-23-2-4

Please give up that bad bodily conduct and develop good bodily conduct.’ ^an10-23-2-5

When spoken to by their sensible spiritual companions they give up that bad bodily conduct and develop good bodily conduct. ^an10-23-2-6

These are the things that should be given up by the body, not by speech. ^an10-23-2-7

And what are the things that should be given up by speech, not by the body? ^an10-23-3-1

It’s when a mendicant has committed a certain unskillful offense by way of speech. ^an10-23-3-2

After examination, sensible spiritual companions say this to them: ^an10-23-3-3

‘Venerable, you’ve committed a certain unskillful offense by way of speech. ^an10-23-3-4

Please give up that bad verbal conduct and develop good verbal conduct.’ ^an10-23-3-5

When spoken to by their sensible spiritual companions they give up that bad verbal conduct and develop good verbal conduct. ^an10-23-3-6

These are the things that should be given up by speech, not by the body. ^an10-23-3-7

And what are the things that should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom? ^an10-23-4-1

Greed … ^an10-23-4-2

hate … ^an10-23-4-3

delusion … ^an10-23-4-4

anger … ^an10-23-4-5

acrimony … ^an10-23-4-6

disdain … ^an10-23-4-7

contempt … ^an10-23-4-8

and stinginess are things that should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom. ^an10-23-4-9

Nasty jealousy should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom. ^an10-23-5-1

And what is nasty jealousy? ^an10-23-5-2

It’s when a householder or their child is prospering in money, grain, silver, or gold. ^an10-23-5-3

And a bondservant or dependent thinks: ^an10-23-5-4

‘Oh, may that householder or their child not prosper in money, grain, silver, or gold!’ ^an10-23-5-5

Or an ascetic or brahmin receives robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an10-23-5-6

And some other ascetic or brahmin thinks: ^an10-23-5-7

‘Oh, may that venerable not receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.’ ^an10-23-5-8

This is called nasty jealousy. ^an10-23-5-9

Corrupt wishes should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom. ^an10-23-6-1

And what are corrupt wishes? ^an10-23-6-2

It’s when a faithless person wishes to be known as faithful. ^an10-23-6-3

An unethical person wishes to be known as ethical. ^an10-23-6-4

An unlearned person wishes to be known as learned. ^an10-23-6-5

A lover of company wishes to be known as secluded. ^an10-23-6-6

A lazy person wishes to be known as energetic. ^an10-23-6-7

An unmindful person wishes to be known as mindful. ^an10-23-6-8

A person without immersion wishes to be known as having immersion. ^an10-23-6-9

A witless person wishes to be known as wise. ^an10-23-6-10

Someone who has not ended the defilements wishes to be known as having ended the defilements. ^an10-23-6-11

These are called corrupt wishes. ^an10-23-6-12

Corrupt wishes should be given up neither by the body, nor by speech, but by seeing again and again with wisdom. ^an10-23-6-13

Suppose that greed masters that mendicant and keeps going. ^an10-23-7-1

Or that hate … ^an10-23-7-2

delusion … ^an10-23-7-3

anger … ^an10-23-7-4

acrimony … ^an10-23-7-5

disdain … ^an10-23-7-6

contempt … ^an10-23-7-7

stinginess … ^an10-23-7-8

nasty jealousy … ^an10-23-7-9

or corrupt wishes master them and keep going. ^an10-23-7-10

You should know of them: ^an10-23-7-11

‘This venerable does not have the understanding that would eliminate greed, so greed masters them and keeps going. ^an10-23-7-12

They don’t have the understanding that would eliminate hate … ^an10-23-7-13

delusion … ^an10-23-7-14

anger … ^an10-23-7-15

acrimony … ^an10-23-7-16

disdain … ^an10-23-7-17

contempt … ^an10-23-7-18

stinginess … ^an10-23-7-19

nasty jealousy … ^an10-23-7-20

or corrupt wishes, so corrupt wishes master them and keep going.’ ^an10-23-7-21

Suppose that greed does not master that mendicant and keep going. ^an10-23-8-1

Or that hate … ^an10-23-8-2

delusion … ^an10-23-8-3

anger … ^an10-23-8-4

acrimony … ^an10-23-8-5

disdain … ^an10-23-8-6

contempt … ^an10-23-8-7

stinginess … ^an10-23-8-8

nasty jealousy … ^an10-23-8-9

or corrupt wishes don’t master that mendicant and keep going. ^an10-23-8-10

You should know of them: ^an10-23-8-11

‘This venerable has the understanding that eliminates greed, so greed doesn’t master them and keep going. ^an10-23-8-12

They have the understanding that eliminates hate … ^an10-23-8-13

delusion … ^an10-23-8-14

anger … ^an10-23-8-15

acrimony … ^an10-23-8-16

disdain … ^an10-23-8-17

contempt … ^an10-23-8-18

stinginess … ^an10-23-8-19

nasty jealousy … ^an10-23-8-20

and corrupt wishes, so corrupt wishes don’t master them and keep going.’” ^an10-23-8-21

### By Mahācunda (Numbered Discourses 10.24) ^an10-24-0

At one time Venerable Mahācunda was staying in the land of the Cetīs at Sahajāti. ^an10-24-1-1

There he addressed the mendicants: ^an10-24-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-24-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-24-1-4

Venerable Mahācunda said this: ^an10-24-1-5

“Reverends, a mendicant who makes a declaration of knowledge says: ^an10-24-2-1

‘I know this teaching, I see this teaching.’ ^an10-24-2-2

Suppose that greed masters that mendicant and keeps going. ^an10-24-2-3

Or that hate … ^an10-24-2-4

delusion … ^an10-24-2-5

anger … ^an10-24-2-6

acrimony … ^an10-24-2-7

disdain … ^an10-24-2-8

contempt … ^an10-24-2-9

stinginess … ^an10-24-2-10

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-2-11

or corrupt wishes master that mendicant and keep going. You should know of them: ^an10-24-2-12

‘This venerable does not have the understanding that would eliminate greed, so greed masters them and keeps going. ^an10-24-2-13

They don’t have the understanding that would eliminate hate … ^an10-24-2-14

delusion … ^an10-24-2-15

anger … ^an10-24-2-16

acrimony … ^an10-24-2-17

disdain … ^an10-24-2-18

contempt … ^an10-24-2-19

stinginess … ^an10-24-2-20

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-2-21

or corrupt wishes, so corrupt wishes master them and keep going.’ ^an10-24-2-22

A mendicant who makes a declaration of development says: ^an10-24-3-1

‘I am developed in physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom.’ ^an10-24-3-2

Suppose that greed masters that mendicant and keeps going. ^an10-24-3-3

Or that hate … ^an10-24-3-4

delusion … ^an10-24-3-5

anger … ^an10-24-3-6

disdain … ^an10-24-3-8

contempt … ^an10-24-3-9

stinginess … ^an10-24-3-10

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-3-11

or corrupt wishes master that mendicant and keep going. You should know of them: ^an10-24-3-12

‘This venerable does not have the understanding that would eliminate greed, so greed masters them and keeps going. ^an10-24-3-13

They don’t have the understanding that would eliminate hate … ^an10-24-3-14

delusion … ^an10-24-3-15

anger … ^an10-24-3-16

acrimony … ^an10-24-3-17

disdain … ^an10-24-3-18

contempt … ^an10-24-3-19

stinginess … ^an10-24-3-20

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-3-21

or corrupt wishes, so corrupt wishes master them and keep going.’ ^an10-24-3-22

A mendicant who makes a declaration of both knowledge and development says: ^an10-24-4-1

‘I know this teaching, I see this teaching. And I am developed in physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom.’ ^an10-24-4-2

Suppose that greed masters that mendicant and keeps going. ^an10-24-4-3

Or that hate … ^an10-24-4-4

delusion … ^an10-24-4-5

anger … ^an10-24-4-6

acrimony … ^an10-24-4-7

disdain … ^an10-24-4-8

contempt … ^an10-24-4-9

stinginess … ^an10-24-4-10

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-4-11

or corrupt wishes master that mendicant and keep going. You should know of them: ^an10-24-4-12

‘This venerable does not have the understanding that would eliminate greed, so greed masters them and keeps going. ^an10-24-4-13

They don’t have the understanding that would eliminate hate … ^an10-24-4-14

delusion … ^an10-24-4-15

anger … ^an10-24-4-16

acrimony … ^an10-24-4-17

disdain … ^an10-24-4-18

contempt … ^an10-24-4-19

stinginess … ^an10-24-4-20

nasty jealousy … ^an10-24-4-21

or corrupt wishes, so corrupt wishes master them and keep going.’ ^an10-24-4-22

Suppose a poor, needy, and penniless person was to declare themselves to be rich, affluent, and wealthy. ^an10-24-5-1

But when it came time to make a payment they weren’t able to come up with any money, grain, silver, or gold. ^an10-24-5-2

They would know of them: ^an10-24-5-3

‘This person declares themselves to be rich, affluent, and wealthy, but they are in fact poor, penniless, and needy.’ ^an10-24-5-4

Why is that? ^an10-24-5-5

Because when it came time to make a payment they weren’t able to come up with any money, grain, silver, or gold. ^an10-24-5-6

In the same way, a mendicant who makes a declaration of knowledge and development says: ^an10-24-6-1

‘I know this teaching, I see this teaching. And I am developed in physical endurance, ethics, mind, and wisdom.’ ^an10-24-6-2

### Meditation on Universals (Numbered Discourses 10.25) ^an10-25-0

“Mendicants, there are these ten universal dimensions of meditation. ^an10-25-1-1

What ten? ^an10-25-1-2

Someone perceives the meditation on universal earth above, below, across, undivided and limitless. ^an10-25-1-3

They perceive the meditation on universal water … ^an10-25-1-4

the meditation on universal fire … ^an10-25-1-5

the meditation on universal air … ^an10-25-1-6

the meditation on universal blue … ^an10-25-1-7

the meditation on universal yellow … ^an10-25-1-8

the meditation on universal red … ^an10-25-1-9

the meditation on universal white … ^an10-25-1-10

the meditation on universal space … ^an10-25-1-11

They perceive the meditation on universal consciousness above, below, across, undivided and limitless. ^an10-25-1-12

These are the ten universal dimensions of meditation.” ^an10-25-1-13

### With Kāḷī (Numbered Discourses 10.26) ^an10-26-0

At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain. ^an10-26-1-1

Then the laywoman Kāḷī of Kurughara went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an10-26-1-2

“Sir, this was said by the Buddha in ‘The Maidens’ Questions’: ^an10-26-1-3

‘I’ve reached the goal, peace of heart. ^an10-26-2-1

Having conquered the army <j>of the likable and pleasant, ^an10-26-2-2

alone, practicing absorption, I awakened to bliss. ^an10-26-2-3

That’s why I don’t get too close to people, ^an10-26-2-4

and no-one gets too close to me.’ ^an10-26-2-5

How should we see the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement?” ^an10-26-3-1

“Sister, some ascetics and brahmins regard the attainment of the meditation on universal earth to be the ultimate. Thinking ‘this is the goal’, they are reborn. ^an10-26-4-1

The Buddha directly knew the extent to which the attainment of the meditation on universal earth was the ultimate. ^an10-26-4-2

Directly knowing this he saw the beginning, the drawback, and the escape. And he saw the knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path. ^an10-26-4-3

Because he saw the beginning, the drawback, and the escape, and he saw the knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path, he knew that he had reached the goal, peace of heart. ^an10-26-4-4

Some ascetics and brahmins regard the attainment of the meditation on universal water to be the ultimate. Thinking ‘this is the goal’, they are reborn. … ^an10-26-5-1

Some ascetics and brahmins regard the attainment of the meditation on universal fire … ^an10-26-5-2

universal air … ^an10-26-5-3

universal blue … ^an10-26-5-4

universal yellow … ^an10-26-5-5

universal red … ^an10-26-5-6

universal white … ^an10-26-5-7

universal space … ^an10-26-5-8

universal consciousness to be the ultimate. Thinking ‘this is the goal’, they are reborn. ^an10-26-5-9

The Buddha directly knew the extent to which the attainment of the meditation on universal consciousness was the ultimate. ^an10-26-5-10

Directly knowing this he saw the beginning, the drawback, and the escape. And he saw the knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path. ^an10-26-5-11

Because he saw the beginning, the drawback, and the escape, and he saw the knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path, he knew that he had reached the goal, peace of heart. ^an10-26-5-12

So, sister, that’s how to understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief in ‘The Maidens’ Questions’: ^an10-26-5-13

‘I’ve reached the goal, peace of heart. ^an10-26-6-1

Having conquered the army <j>of the likable and pleasant, ^an10-26-6-2

alone, practicing absorption, I awakened to bliss. ^an10-26-6-3

That’s why I don’t get too close to people, ^an10-26-6-4

and no-one gets too close to me.’” ^an10-26-6-5

### The Great Questions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.27) ^an10-27-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-27-1-1

Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms. ^an10-27-1-2

Then it occurred to him, ^an10-27-1-3

“It’s too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī. ^an10-27-1-4

Why don’t we visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an10-27-1-5

Then they went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^an10-27-2-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side. The wanderers said to them: ^an10-27-2-2

“Reverends, the ascetic Gotama teaches his disciples like this: ^an10-27-3-1

‘Please, mendicants, directly know all things. Meditate having directly known all things.’ ^an10-27-3-2

We too teach our disciples: ^an10-27-3-3

‘Please, reverends, directly know all things. Live having directly known all things.’ ^an10-27-3-4

What, then, is the difference between the ascetic Gotama’s teaching and instruction and ours?” ^an10-27-3-5

Those mendicants neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions. ^an10-27-4-1

They rose from their seat, thinking, ^an10-27-4-2

“We will learn the meaning of this statement from the Buddha himself.” ^an10-27-4-3

Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an10-27-5-1

“Mendicants, when wanderers of other religions say this, you should say to them: ^an10-27-9-1

‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer. Two … Three … Four … Five … Six … Seven … Eight … Nine … Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-27-9-2

Questioned like this, the wanderers of other religions would be stumped, and, in addition, would get frustrated. ^an10-27-9-3

Why is that? ^an10-27-9-4

Because they’re out of their element. ^an10-27-9-5

I don’t see anyone in this world—with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans—who could provide a satisfying answer to these questions except for the Realized One or his disciple or someone who has heard it from them. ^an10-27-9-6

‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-27-10-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-27-10-2

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding one thing, seeing its limits and fully comprehending its meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-27-10-3

What one thing? ^an10-27-10-4

‘All sentient beings are sustained by food.’ ^an10-27-10-5

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding this one thing, seeing its limits and fully comprehending its meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-27-10-6

‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-27-10-7

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-27-10-8

What two? ^an10-27-11-4

Name and form. … ^an10-27-11-5

What three? ^an10-27-12-4

Three feelings. … ^an10-27-12-5

What four? ^an10-27-13-4

The four foods. … ^an10-27-13-5

What five? ^an10-27-14-4

The five grasping aggregates. … ^an10-27-14-5

What six? ^an10-27-15-4

The six interior sense fields. … ^an10-27-15-5

What seven? ^an10-27-16-4

The seven planes of consciousness. … ^an10-27-16-5

What eight? ^an10-27-17-4

The eight worldly conditions. … ^an10-27-17-5

What nine? ^an10-27-18-4

The nine abodes of sentient beings. … ^an10-27-18-5

‘Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-27-19-1

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-27-19-2

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding ten things, seeing their limits and fully comprehending their meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-27-19-3

What ten? ^an10-27-19-4

The ten ways of performing unskillful deeds. ^an10-27-19-5

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding these ten things, seeing their limits and fully comprehending their meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-27-19-6

‘Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-27-19-7

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an10-27-19-8

### The Great Questions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.28) ^an10-28-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kajaṅgalā in a bamboo grove. ^an10-28-1-1

Then several lay followers of Kajaṅgalā went to the nun Kajaṅgalikā, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to her: ^an10-28-1-2

“Ma’am, this was said by the Buddha in ‘The Great Questions’: ^an10-28-2-1

‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer. Two … Three … Four … Five … Six … Seven … Eight … Nine … Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ ^an10-28-2-2

How should we see the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement?” ^an10-28-2-3

“Good people, I haven’t heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha or from esteemed mendicants. ^an10-28-3-1

But as to how it seems to me, ^an10-28-3-2

listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an10-28-3-3

“Yes, ma’am,” replied the lay followers. ^an10-28-3-4

The nun Kajaṅgalikā said this: ^an10-28-3-5

“‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer.’ That’s what the Buddha said, ^an10-28-4-1

but why did he say it? ^an10-28-4-2

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding one thing, seeing its limits and fully comprehending its meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-4-3

What one thing? ^an10-28-4-4

‘All sentient beings are sustained by food.’ ^an10-28-4-5

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding this one thing, seeing its limits and fully comprehending its meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-4-6

‘One thing: question, summary recital, and answer.’ That’s what the Buddha said, and this is why he said it. ^an10-28-4-7

What two? ^an10-28-5-4

Name and form. … ^an10-28-5-5

What three? ^an10-28-5-6

Three feelings. … ^an10-28-5-7

With a mind well developed in four things—seeing their limits and fully comprehending their meaning—a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-6-3

What four? ^an10-28-6-4

The four kinds of mindfulness meditation. … ^an10-28-6-5

With a mind well developed in these four things—seeing their limits and fully fathoming their meaning—a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. … ^an10-28-6-6

What five? ^an10-28-7-4

The five faculties. … ^an10-28-7-5

What six? ^an10-28-7-6

The six elements of escape. … ^an10-28-7-7

What seven? ^an10-28-7-8

The seven awakening factors. … ^an10-28-7-9

What eight? ^an10-28-7-10

The noble eightfold path. … ^an10-28-7-11

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding nine things, seeing their limits and fully comprehending their meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-8-3

What nine? ^an10-28-8-4

The nine abodes of sentient beings. ^an10-28-8-5

Becoming completely disillusioned, dispassionate, and freed regarding these nine things, seeing their limits and fully comprehending their meaning, a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-8-6

‘Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ That’s what the Buddha said, ^an10-28-9-1

but why did he say it? ^an10-28-9-2

Becoming well developed in ten things—seeing their limits and fully fathoming their meaning—a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-9-3

What ten? ^an10-28-9-4

The ten ways of performing skillful deeds. ^an10-28-9-5

With a mind well developed in these ten things—seeing their limits and fully fathoming their meaning—a mendicant makes an end of suffering in this very life. ^an10-28-9-6

‘Ten things: question, summary recital, and answer.’ That’s what the Buddha said, and this is why he said it. ^an10-28-9-7

That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief in ‘The Great Questions’. ^an10-28-10-1

If you wish, you may go to the Buddha and ask him about this. ^an10-28-10-4

You should remember it in line with the Buddha’s answer.” ^an10-28-10-5

“Yes, ma’am,” replied those lay followers, approving and agreeing with what the nun Kajaṅgalikā said. Then they rose from their seat, bowed, and respectfully circled her, keeping her on their right. Then they went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an10-28-10-6

and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^an10-28-10-7

“Good, good, householders. ^an10-28-11-1

The nun Kajaṅgalikā is astute, she has great wisdom. ^an10-28-11-2

If you came to me and asked this question, I would answer it in exactly the same way as the nun Kajaṅgalikā. ^an10-28-11-3

That is what it means, and that’s how you should remember it.” ^an10-28-11-4

### Kosala (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.29) ^an10-29-0

“As far as Kāsi and Kosala extend, and as far as the dominion of King Pasenadi of Kosala extends, King Pasenadi is said to be the foremost. ^an10-29-1-1

But even King Pasenadi decays and perishes. ^an10-29-1-2

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-1-3

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-1-4

A galaxy extends a thousand times as far as the moon and sun revolve and the shining ones light up the quarters. ^an10-29-2-1

In that galaxy there are a thousand moons, a thousand suns, a thousand Sinerus king of mountains, a thousand Black Plum Tree Lands, a thousand lands west of Godāvarī, a thousand lands north of Kuru, a thousand lands east of Videha, four thousand oceans, four thousand great kings, a thousand realms of the gods of the four great kings, a thousand realms of the gods of the thirty-three, of the gods of Yama, of the joyful gods, of the gods who love to create, of the gods who control what is created by others, and a thousand realms of divinity. ^an10-29-2-2

As far as the galaxy extends, the Great Divinity is said to be the foremost. ^an10-29-2-3

But even the Great Divinity decays and perishes. ^an10-29-2-4

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-2-5

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-2-6

There comes a time when this cosmos contracts. ^an10-29-3-1

As it contracts, most sentient beings migrate to the realm of streaming radiance. ^an10-29-3-2

There they are mind-made, feeding on rapture, self-luminous, wandering in midair, steadily glorious, and they remain like that for a very long time. ^an10-29-3-3

When the cosmos is contracting, the gods of streaming radiance are said to be the foremost. ^an10-29-3-4

But even the gods of streaming radiance decay and perish. ^an10-29-3-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-3-6

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-3-7

There are these ten universal dimensions of meditation. ^an10-29-4-1

What ten? ^an10-29-4-2

Someone perceives the meditation on universal earth above, below, across, undivided and limitless. ^an10-29-4-3

They perceive the meditation on universal water … ^an10-29-4-4

the meditation on universal fire … ^an10-29-4-5

the meditation on universal air … ^an10-29-4-6

the meditation on universal blue … ^an10-29-4-7

the meditation on universal yellow … ^an10-29-4-8

the meditation on universal red … ^an10-29-4-9

the meditation on universal white … ^an10-29-4-10

the meditation on universal space … ^an10-29-4-11

They perceive the meditation on universal consciousness above, below, across, undivided and limitless. ^an10-29-4-12

These are the ten universal dimensions of meditation. ^an10-29-4-13

The best of these ten universal dimensions of meditation is when someone perceives the meditation on universal consciousness above, below, across, undivided and limitless. ^an10-29-5-1

Some sentient beings perceive like this. ^an10-29-5-2

But even the sentient beings who perceive like this decay and perish. ^an10-29-5-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-5-4

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-5-5

There are these eight dimensions of mastery. ^an10-29-6-1

What eight? ^an10-29-6-2

Perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. ^an10-29-6-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-6-4

This is the first dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-6-5

Perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. ^an10-29-7-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-7-2

This is the second dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-7-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limited, both pretty and ugly. ^an10-29-8-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-8-2

This is the third dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-8-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, limitless, both pretty and ugly. ^an10-29-9-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-9-2

This is the fourth dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-9-3

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, blue, with blue color and blue appearance. ^an10-29-10-1

They’re like a flax flower that’s blue, with blue color and blue appearance. Or a cloth from Varanasi that’s smoothed on both sides, blue, with blue color and blue appearance. ^an10-29-10-2

In the same way, not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, blue, with blue color and blue appearance. ^an10-29-10-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-10-4

This is the fifth dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-10-5

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, yellow, with yellow color and yellow appearance. ^an10-29-11-1

They’re like a champak flower that’s yellow, with yellow color and yellow appearance. Or a cloth from Varanasi that’s smoothed on both sides, yellow, with yellow color and yellow appearance. ^an10-29-11-2

In the same way, not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, yellow, with yellow color and yellow appearance. ^an10-29-11-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-11-4

This is the sixth dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-11-5

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, red, with red color and red appearance. ^an10-29-12-1

They’re like a scarlet mallow flower that’s red, with red color and red appearance. Or a cloth from Varanasi that’s smoothed on both sides, red, with red color and red appearance. ^an10-29-12-2

In the same way, not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, red, with red color and red appearance. ^an10-29-12-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-12-4

This is the seventh dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-12-5

Not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an10-29-13-1

They’re like the morning star that’s white, with white color and white appearance. Or a cloth from Varanasi that’s smoothed on both sides, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an10-29-13-2

In the same way, not perceiving form internally, someone sees forms externally, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an10-29-13-3

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-13-4

This is the eighth dimension of mastery. ^an10-29-13-5

These are the eight dimensions of mastery. ^an10-29-13-6

The best of these dimensions of mastery is when someone, not perceiving form internally, sees forms externally, white, with white color and white appearance. ^an10-29-14-1

Mastering them, they perceive: ‘I know and see.’ ^an10-29-14-2

Some sentient beings perceive like this. ^an10-29-14-3

But even the sentient beings who perceive like this decay and perish. ^an10-29-14-4

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-14-5

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-14-6

There are four ways of practice. ^an10-29-15-1

What four? ^an10-29-15-2

Painful practice with slow insight, ^an10-29-15-3

painful practice with swift insight, ^an10-29-15-4

pleasant practice with slow insight, and ^an10-29-15-5

pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an10-29-15-6

These are the four ways of practice. ^an10-29-15-7

The best of these four ways of practice is the pleasant practice with swift insight. ^an10-29-16-1

Some sentient beings practice like this. ^an10-29-16-2

But even the sentient beings who practice like this decay and perish. ^an10-29-16-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-16-4

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-16-5

There are these four perceptions. ^an10-29-17-1

What four? ^an10-29-17-2

One individual perceives the limited. ^an10-29-17-3

One individual perceives the expansive. ^an10-29-17-4

One individual perceives the limitless. ^an10-29-17-5

One individual, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, perceives the dimension of nothingness. ^an10-29-17-6

These are the four perceptions. ^an10-29-17-7

The best of these four perceptions is when someone, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, perceives the dimension of nothingness. ^an10-29-18-1

Some sentient beings perceive like this. ^an10-29-18-2

But even the sentient beings who perceive like this decay and perish. ^an10-29-18-3

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-18-4

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-18-5

This is the best of the convictions of outsiders, that is: ‘I might not be, and it might not be mine. I will not be, and it will not be mine.’ ^an10-29-19-1

When someone has such a view, you can expect ^an10-29-19-2

that they will not be attracted to continued existence, ^an10-29-19-3

and they will not be repulsed by the cessation of continued existence. ^an10-29-19-4

Some sentient beings have such a view. ^an10-29-19-5

But even the sentient beings who have views like this decay and perish. ^an10-29-19-6

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-19-7

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-19-8

There are some ascetics and brahmins who advocate the ultimate purity of the spirit. ^an10-29-20-1

This is the best of the advocates of the ultimate purity of the spirit, that is, when someone, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an10-29-20-2

They teach Dhamma in order to directly know and realize this. ^an10-29-20-3

Some sentient beings have such a doctrine. ^an10-29-20-4

But even the sentient beings who have such a doctrine decay and perish. ^an10-29-20-5

Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with it. ^an10-29-20-6

Their desire fades away even for the foremost, let alone the inferior. ^an10-29-20-7

There are some ascetics and brahmins who advocate ultimate extinguishment in this very life. ^an10-29-21-1

This is the best of those who advocate extinguishment in this very life, that is, liberation by not grasping after truly understanding the origin, disappearance, gratification, drawback, and escape of the six fields of contact. ^an10-29-21-2

Though I state and assert this, certain ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with the incorrect, hollow, false, untruthful claim: ^an10-29-21-3

‘The ascetic Gotama doesn’t advocate the complete understanding of sensual pleasures, forms, or feelings.’ ^an10-29-21-4

But I do advocate the complete understanding of sensual pleasures, forms, and feelings. And I advocate full extinguishment by not grasping in this very life, wishless, quenched, and cooled.” ^an10-29-21-5

### Kosala (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.30) ^an10-30-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-30-1-1

Now at that time King Pasenadi of Kosala returned from combat after winning a battle and succeeding in his strategy. ^an10-30-1-2

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went to the monastery. ^an10-30-1-3

He went by carriage as far as the terrain allowed, then descended and entered the monastery on foot. ^an10-30-1-4

At that time several mendicants were walking mindfully in the open air. ^an10-30-1-5

Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to them and said, ^an10-30-1-6

“Sirs, where is the Blessed One at present, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha? ^an10-30-1-7

For I want to see the Buddha.” ^an10-30-1-8

“Great king, that’s his dwelling, with the door closed. Approach it quietly, without hurrying; go onto the porch, clear your throat, and knock on the door-panel. The Buddha will open the door.” ^an10-30-1-9

So the king approached the Buddha’s dwelling, cleared his throat and knocked on the door-panel, and the Buddha opened the door. ^an10-30-2-1

Then King Pasenadi entered the Buddha’s dwelling. He bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet, caressing them and covering them with kisses, and pronounced his name: ^an10-30-2-2

“Sir, I am Pasenadi, king of Kosala! ^an10-30-2-3

I am Pasenadi, king of Kosala!” ^an10-30-2-4

“But great king, for what reason do you act with such utmost deference for this body, conveying your manifest love?” ^an10-30-3-1

“Sir, it’s because of my gratitude and thanks for the Buddha that I act with such utmost deference, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-3-2

The Buddha is practicing for the welfare and happiness of the people. He has established many people in the noble system, that is, the principles of goodness and skillfulness. ^an10-30-4-1

This is a reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-4-2

Furthermore, the Buddha is ethical, possessing ethical conduct that is mature, noble, and skillful. ^an10-30-5-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-5-2

Furthermore, the Buddha lives in the wilderness, frequenting remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. ^an10-30-6-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-6-2

Furthermore, the Buddha is content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an10-30-7-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-7-2

Furthermore, the Buddha is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an10-30-8-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-8-2

Furthermore, the Buddha gets to take part in talk about self-effacement that helps open the heart, when he wants, without trouble or difficulty. That is, talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-30-9-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-9-2

Furthermore, the Buddha gets the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when he wants, without trouble or difficulty. ^an10-30-10-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-10-2

Furthermore, the Buddha recollects many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. He remembers: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so he recollects his many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an10-30-11-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-11-2

Furthermore, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, the Buddha sees sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ He understands how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an10-30-12-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-12-2

Furthermore, the Buddha has realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and lives having realized it with his own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-30-13-1

This is another reason that I act with such utmost deference for the Buddha, conveying my manifest love. ^an10-30-13-2

Well, now, sir, I must go. ^an10-30-14-1

I have many duties, and much to do.” ^an10-30-14-2

“Please, great king, go at your convenience.” ^an10-30-14-3

Then King Pasenadi rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an10-30-14-4

### With Upāli (Numbered Discourses 10.31) ^an10-31-0

Then Venerable Upāli went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-31-1-1

“Sir, for how many reasons did the Realized One lay down training rules for his disciples and recite the monastic code?” ^an10-31-1-2

“Upāli, the Realized One laid down training rules for his disciples and recited the monastic code for ten reasons. ^an10-31-2-1

What ten? ^an10-31-2-2

For the well-being of the Saṅgha and for the comfort of the Saṅgha. For keeping difficult individuals in check and for the comfort of good-hearted mendicants. For restraining defilements that affect this life and protecting against defilements that affect lives to come. For inspiring confidence in those without it, and increasing confidence in those who have it. For the continuation of the true teaching and the support of the training. ^an10-31-2-3

The Realized One laid down training rules for his disciples and recited the monastic code for these ten reasons.” ^an10-31-2-4

### Suspending the Recitation of the Monastic Code (Numbered Discourses 10.32) ^an10-32-0

“Sir, how many grounds are there to suspend the recitation of the monastic code?” ^an10-32-1-1

“Upāli, there are ten grounds to suspend the recitation of the monastic code. ^an10-32-1-2

What ten? ^an10-32-1-3

A mendicant who has committed an expulsion offense is sitting in the assembly. A discussion about whether someone has committed an expulsion offense is unfinished. Someone who is not fully ordained is sitting in the assembly. A discussion about whether someone is fully ordained or not is unfinished. Someone who has resigned the training is sitting in the assembly. A discussion about whether someone has disavowed the training or not is unfinished. A eunuch is sitting in the assembly. A discussion about whether someone is a eunuch is unfinished. A raper of nuns is sitting in the assembly. A discussion about whether or not someone is a raper of nuns is unfinished. ^an10-32-1-4

These are the ten grounds to suspend the recitation of the monastic code.” ^an10-32-1-5

### A Judge (Numbered Discourses 10.33) ^an10-33-0

“Sir, how many qualities should a mendicant have to be deemed a judge?” ^an10-33-1-1

“Upāli, a mendicant should have ten qualities to be deemed a judge. ^an10-33-1-2

What ten? ^an10-33-1-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-33-1-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-33-1-5

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an10-33-1-6

They’re firm and unfaltering in the training. ^an10-33-1-7

When there are opposing parties, they’re able to persuade, advocate, and convince them, make them see the other side and trust each other. ^an10-33-1-8

They’re skilled in the instigation and settlement of disciplinary issues. ^an10-33-1-9

They know what a disciplinary issue is. ^an10-33-1-10

They know how a disciplinary issue originates. ^an10-33-1-11

They know how a disciplinary issue ceases. ^an10-33-1-12

They know the practical way leading to the cessation of a disciplinary issue. ^an10-33-1-13

A mendicant should possess these ten qualities to be deemed a judge.” ^an10-33-1-14

### Ordination (Numbered Discourses 10.34) ^an10-34-0

“Sir, how many qualities should a mendicant have to give ordination?” ^an10-34-1-1

“Upāli, a mendicant should have ten qualities to give ordination. ^an10-34-1-2

What ten? ^an10-34-1-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-34-1-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-34-1-5

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an10-34-1-6

They’re able to care for the sick or get someone else to do so. ^an10-34-1-7

They’re able to settle dissatisfaction or get someone else to do so. ^an10-34-1-8
