They’re able to dispel remorse when it has come up. ^an10-34-1-9

They’re able to rationally dissuade someone from misconceptions that come up. ^an10-34-1-10

They’re able to encourage someone in the higher ethics, ^an10-34-1-11

the higher mind, ^an10-34-1-12

and the higher wisdom. ^an10-34-1-13

A mendicant should possess these ten qualities to give ordination.” ^an10-34-1-14

### Dependence (Numbered Discourses 10.35) ^an10-35-0

“Sir, how many qualities should a mendicant have to give dependence?” ^an10-35-1-1

“Upāli, a mendicant should have ten qualities to give dependence. ^an10-35-1-2

What ten? ^an10-35-1-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical … ^an10-35-1-4

They’re learned … ^an10-35-1-5

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an10-35-1-6

They’re able to care for the sick or get someone else to do so. ^an10-35-1-7

They’re able to settle dissatisfaction or get someone else to do so. ^an10-35-1-8

They’re able to dispel remorse when it has come up. ^an10-35-1-9

They’re able to rationally dissuade someone from misconceptions that come up. ^an10-35-1-10

They’re able to encourage someone in the higher ethics, ^an10-35-1-11

the higher mind, ^an10-35-1-12

and the higher wisdom. ^an10-35-1-13

A mendicant should possess these ten qualities to give dependence.” ^an10-35-1-14

### A Novice (Numbered Discourses 10.36) ^an10-36-0

“Sir, how many qualities should a mendicant have to be attended on by a novice?” ^an10-36-1-1

“Upāli, a mendicant should have ten qualities to be attended on by a novice. ^an10-36-1-2

What ten? ^an10-36-1-3

It’s when a mendicant is ethical … ^an10-36-1-4

They’re learned … ^an10-36-1-5

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an10-36-1-6

They’re able to care for the sick or get someone else to do so. ^an10-36-1-7

They’re able to settle dissatisfaction or get someone else to do so. ^an10-36-1-8

They’re able to dispel remorse when it has come up. ^an10-36-1-9

They’re able to rationally dissuade someone from misconceptions that come up. ^an10-36-1-10

They’re able to encourage someone in the higher ethics, ^an10-36-1-11

the higher mind, ^an10-36-1-12

and the higher wisdom. ^an10-36-1-13

A mendicant should possess these ten qualities to be attended on by a novice.” ^an10-36-1-14

### Schism in the Saṅgha (Numbered Discourses 10.37) ^an10-37-0

“Sir, they speak of ‘schism in the Saṅgha’. ^an10-37-1-1

How is schism in the Saṅgha defined?” ^an10-37-1-2

“Upāli, it’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as the teaching, and what is the teaching as not the teaching. They explain what is not the training as the training, and what is the training as not the training. They explain what was not spoken and stated by the Realized One as spoken and stated by the Realized One, and what was spoken and stated by the Realized One as not spoken and stated by the Realized One. They explain what was not practiced by the Realized One as practiced by the Realized One, and what was practiced by the Realized One as not practiced by the Realized One. They explain what was not prescribed by the Realized One as prescribed by the Realized One, and what was prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-37-1-3

On these ten grounds they split off and go their own way. They perform legal acts autonomously and recite the monastic code autonomously. ^an10-37-1-4

That is how schism in the Saṅgha is defined.” ^an10-37-1-5

### Harmony in the Saṅgha (Numbered Discourses 10.38) ^an10-38-0

“Sir, they speak of ‘harmony in the Saṅgha’. ^an10-38-1-1

How is harmony in the Saṅgha defined?” ^an10-38-1-2

“Upāli, it’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as not the teaching, and what is the teaching as the teaching. They explain what is not the training as not the training, and what is the training as the training. They explain what was not spoken and stated by the Realized One as not spoken and stated by the Realized One, and what was spoken and stated by the Realized One as spoken and stated by the Realized One. They explain what was not practiced by the Realized One as not practiced by the Realized One, and what was practiced by the Realized One as practiced by the Realized One. They explain what was not prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One, and what was prescribed by the Realized One as prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-38-1-3

On these ten grounds they don’t split off and go their own way. They don’t perform legal acts autonomously or recite the monastic code autonomously. ^an10-38-1-4

That is how harmony in the Saṅgha is defined.” ^an10-38-1-5

### With Ānanda (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.39) ^an10-39-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha: ^an10-39-1-1

“Sir, they speak of ‘schism in the Saṅgha’. ^an10-39-1-2

How is schism in the Saṅgha defined?” ^an10-39-1-3

“Ānanda, it’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as the teaching … ^an10-39-1-4

and what was prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-39-1-5

On these ten grounds they split off and go their own way. They perform legal acts autonomously and recite the monastic code autonomously. ^an10-39-1-6

That is how schism in the Saṅgha is defined.” ^an10-39-1-7

“But sir, what does someone who has split a harmonious Saṅgha brim with?” ^an10-39-2-1

“They brim with sin that lasts for an eon.” ^an10-39-2-2

“But sir, what is the sin that lasts for an eon?” ^an10-39-2-3

“They burn in hell for an eon, Ānanda. ^an10-39-2-4

A schismatic remains for the eon ^an10-39-3-1

in a place of loss, in hell. ^an10-39-3-2

Taking a stand against the teaching, ^an10-39-3-3

favoring factions, they ruin their sanctuary. ^an10-39-3-4

After causing schism in a harmonious Saṅgha, ^an10-39-3-5

they burn in hell for an eon.” ^an10-39-3-6

### With Ānanda (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.40) ^an10-40-0

“Sir, they speak of ‘harmony in the Saṅgha’. ^an10-40-1-1

How is harmony in the Saṅgha defined?” ^an10-40-1-2

“Ānanda, it’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as not the teaching, and what is the teaching as the teaching. They explain what is not the training as not the training, and what is the training as the training. They explain what was not spoken and stated by the Realized One as not spoken and stated by the Realized One, and what was spoken and stated by the Realized One as spoken and stated by the Realized One. They explain what was not practiced by the Realized One as not practiced by the Realized One, and what was practiced by the Realized One as practiced by the Realized One. They explain what was not prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One, and what was prescribed by the Realized One as prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-40-1-3

On these ten grounds they don’t split off and go their own way. They don’t perform legal acts autonomously or recite the monastic code autonomously. ^an10-40-1-4

That is how harmony in the Saṅgha is defined.” ^an10-40-1-5

“But sir, what does someone who has forged harmony in a schismatic Saṅgha brim with?” ^an10-40-2-1

“They brim with divine merit.” ^an10-40-2-2

“But what is divine merit?” ^an10-40-2-3

“They rejoice in heaven for an eon, Ānanda. ^an10-40-2-4

A Saṅgha in harmony is happy, ^an10-40-3-1

as is support for those in harmony. ^an10-40-3-2

Taking a stand on the teaching, ^an10-40-3-3

favoring harmony, they ruin no sanctuary. ^an10-40-3-4

After forging harmony in the Saṅgha, ^an10-40-3-5

they rejoice in heaven for an eon.” ^an10-40-3-6

### Dispute (Numbered Discourses 10.41) ^an10-41-0

Then Venerable Upāli went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-41-1-1

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason, why fights, quarrels, arguments, and disputes arise in the Saṅgha, and the mendicants don’t live happily?” ^an10-41-1-2

“Upāli, it’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as the teaching, and what is the teaching as not the teaching. They explain what is not the training as the training, and what is the training as not the training. They explain what was not spoken and stated by the Realized One as spoken and stated by the Realized One, and what was spoken and stated by the Realized One as not spoken and stated by the Realized One. They explain what was not practiced by the Realized One as practiced by the Realized One, and what was practiced by the Realized One as not practiced by the Realized One. They explain what was not prescribed by the Realized One as prescribed by the Realized One, and what was prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-41-1-3

This is the cause, this is the reason why fights, quarrels, arguments, and disputes arise in the Saṅgha, and the mendicants don’t live happily.” ^an10-41-1-4

### Roots of Dispute (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.42) ^an10-42-0

“Sir, how many roots of dispute are there?” ^an10-42-1-1

“Upāli, there are ten roots of dispute. ^an10-42-1-2

What ten? ^an10-42-1-3

It’s when a mendicant explains what is not the teaching as the teaching, and what is the teaching as not the teaching. They explain what is not the training as the training, and what is the training as not the training. They explain what was not spoken and stated by the Realized One as spoken and stated by the Realized One, and what was spoken and stated by the Realized One as not spoken and stated by the Realized One. They explain what was not practiced by the Realized One as practiced by the Realized One, and what was practiced by the Realized One as not practiced by the Realized One. They explain what was not prescribed by the Realized One as prescribed by the Realized One, and what was prescribed by the Realized One as not prescribed by the Realized One. ^an10-42-1-4

These are the ten roots of dispute.” ^an10-42-1-5

### Roots of Dispute (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.43) ^an10-43-0

“Sir, how many roots of dispute are there?” ^an10-43-1-1

“Upāli, there are ten roots of disputes. ^an10-43-1-2

What ten? ^an10-43-1-3

It’s when a mendicant explains what is not an offense as an offense, and what is an offense as not an offense. They explain a light offense as a serious offense, and a serious offense as a light offense. They explain an offense committed with corrupt intention as an offense not committed with corrupt intention, and an offense not committed with corrupt intention as an offense committed with corrupt intention. They explain an offense requiring rehabilitation as an offense not requiring rehabilitation, and an offense not requiring rehabilitation as an offense requiring rehabilitation. They explain an offense with redress as an offense without redress, and an offense without redress as an offense with redress. ^an10-43-1-4

These are the ten roots of dispute.” ^an10-43-1-5

### At Kusinārā (Numbered Discourses 10.44) ^an10-44-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Kusinārā, in the Forest of Offerings. ^an10-44-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an10-44-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an10-44-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an10-44-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an10-44-1-5

“Mendicants, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should first check five things in themselves and establish five things in themselves. ^an10-44-2-1

What five things should they check in themselves? ^an10-44-2-2

A mendicant who wants to accuse another should check this: ^an10-44-2-3

‘Is my bodily behavior pure? Do I have pure bodily behavior that is impeccable and irreproachable? ^an10-44-2-4

Is this thing found in me or not?’ ^an10-44-2-5

If it’s not, there will be people who say: ^an10-44-2-6

‘Come on, venerable, train your own bodily behavior first.’ ^an10-44-2-7

Furthermore, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should check this: ^an10-44-3-1

‘Is my verbal behavior pure? Do I have pure verbal behavior that is impeccable and irreproachable? ^an10-44-3-2

Is this thing found in me or not?’ ^an10-44-3-3

If it’s not, there will be people who say: ^an10-44-3-4

‘Come on, venerable, train your own verbal behavior first.’ ^an10-44-3-5

Furthermore, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should check this: ^an10-44-4-1

‘Is my heart established in love for my spiritual companions, without resentment? ^an10-44-4-2

Is this thing found in me or not?’ ^an10-44-4-3

If it’s not, there will be people who say: ^an10-44-4-4

‘Come on, venerable, establish your heart in love for your spiritual companions first.’ ^an10-44-4-5

Furthermore, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should check this: ^an10-44-5-1

‘Am I very learned, remembering and keeping what I’ve learned? These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. Am I very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically? ^an10-44-5-2

Is this thing found in me or not?’ ^an10-44-5-3

If it’s not, there will be people who say: ^an10-44-5-4

‘Come on, venerable, memorize the scriptures first.’ ^an10-44-5-5

Furthermore, a mendicant who wants to accuse another should check this: ^an10-44-6-1

‘Have both monastic codes been passed down to me in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, and well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material? ^an10-44-6-2

Is this thing found in me or not?’ ^an10-44-6-3

If it’s not, and if they are unable to respond when asked “Venerable, where was this spoken by the Buddha?” there will be people who say: ^an10-44-6-4

‘Come on, venerable, train in the monastic code first.’ ^an10-44-6-6

These are the five things they should check in themselves. ^an10-44-6-7

What five things should they establish in themselves? ^an10-44-7-1

‘I will speak at the right time, not at the wrong time. ^an10-44-7-2

I will speak truthfully, not falsely. ^an10-44-7-3

I will speak gently, not harshly. ^an10-44-7-4

I will speak beneficially, not harmfully. ^an10-44-7-5

I will speak lovingly, not from secret hate.’ ^an10-44-7-6

These are the five things they should establish in themselves. ^an10-44-7-7

A mendicant who wants to accuse another should first check these five things in themselves and establish these five things in themselves.” ^an10-44-7-8

### Entering a Royal Compound (Numbered Discourses 10.45) ^an10-45-0

“Mendicants, there are ten drawbacks to entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-1-1

What ten? ^an10-45-1-2

Firstly, a king is sitting with his chief queen ^an10-45-1-3

when a monk enters. ^an10-45-1-4

When the queen sees the monk she smiles, or when the monk sees the queen he smiles. ^an10-45-1-5

So the king thinks: ^an10-45-1-6

‘They’ve done it for sure, or they will do it.’ ^an10-45-1-7

This is the first drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-1-8

Furthermore, a king has many duties, and much to do. He has sex with one of the women but doesn’t remember. ^an10-45-2-1

She gets pregnant from that. ^an10-45-2-2

So the king thinks: ^an10-45-2-3

‘No-one else has entered here, except for that monk. ^an10-45-2-4

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-2-5

This is the second drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-2-6

Furthermore, a gem is lost somewhere in the royal compound. ^an10-45-3-1

So the king thinks: ^an10-45-3-2

‘No-one else has entered here, except for that monk. ^an10-45-3-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-3-4

This is the third drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-3-5

Furthermore, secret deliberations in the royal compound are leaked outside. ^an10-45-4-1

So the king thinks: ^an10-45-4-2

‘No-one else has entered here, except for that monk. ^an10-45-4-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-4-4

This is the fourth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-4-5

Furthermore, in a royal compound, a father longs for their son, or a son longs for his father. ^an10-45-5-1

They think: ^an10-45-5-2

‘No-one else has entered here, except for that monk. ^an10-45-5-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-5-4

This is the fifth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-5-5

Furthermore, the king promotes someone to a higher position. ^an10-45-6-1

Those who are upset by this think: ^an10-45-6-2

‘The king is close to that monk. ^an10-45-6-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-6-4

This is the sixth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-6-5

Furthermore, the king demotes someone to a lower position. ^an10-45-7-1

Those who are upset by this think: ^an10-45-7-2

‘The king is close to that monk. ^an10-45-7-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-7-4

This is the seventh drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-7-5

Furthermore, the king dispatches the army at the wrong time. ^an10-45-8-1

Those who are upset by this think: ^an10-45-8-2

‘The king is close to that monk. ^an10-45-8-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-8-4

This is the eighth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-8-5

Furthermore, the king dispatches the army at the right time, but orders it to turn back while still on the road. ^an10-45-9-1

Those who are upset by this think: ^an10-45-9-2

‘The king is close to that monk. ^an10-45-9-3

Could this be the monk’s doing?’ ^an10-45-9-4

This is the ninth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-9-5

Furthermore, in the royal compound there is a trampling of elephants, horses, and chariots, as well as arousing sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches that do not befit a monk. ^an10-45-10-1

This is the tenth drawback of entering a royal compound. ^an10-45-10-2

These are the ten drawbacks of entering a royal compound.” ^an10-45-10-3

### With the Sakyans (Numbered Discourses 10.46) ^an10-46-0

At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery. ^an10-46-1-1

Then on the sabbath several Sakyan lay followers went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them: ^an10-46-1-2

“Sakyans, do you observe the sabbath with its eight factors?” ^an10-46-1-3

“Sir, sometimes we do, sometimes we don’t.” ^an10-46-1-4

“That’s your loss, Sakyans, it’s your misfortune. In this life with its fear of sorrow and death, you sometimes keep the sabbath and you sometimes don’t. ^an10-46-1-5

What do you think, Sakyans? ^an10-46-2-1

Take a man who earns half a dollar for an honest day’s work. ^an10-46-2-2

Is this enough to call him a deft and industrious man?” ^an10-46-2-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-46-2-4

“What do you think, Sakyans? ^an10-46-3-1

Take a man who earns a dollar for an honest day’s work. ^an10-46-3-2

Is this enough to call him a deft and industrious man?” ^an10-46-3-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-46-3-4

“What do you think, Sakyans? ^an10-46-4-1

Take a man who, for an honest day’s work, earns two dollars … ^an10-46-4-2

three dollars … ^an10-46-4-3

four dollars … ^an10-46-4-4

five dollars … ^an10-46-4-5

six dollars … ^an10-46-4-6

seven dollars … ^an10-46-4-7

eight dollars … ^an10-46-4-8

nine dollars … ^an10-46-4-9

ten dollars … ^an10-46-4-10

twenty dollars … ^an10-46-4-11

thirty dollars … ^an10-46-4-12

forty dollars … ^an10-46-4-13

fifty dollars … ^an10-46-4-14

a hundred dollars. ^an10-46-4-15

Is this enough to call him a deft and industrious man?” ^an10-46-4-16

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-46-4-17

“What do you think, Sakyans? ^an10-46-5-1

Suppose that man earned a hundred or a thousand dollars every day and saved it all up. If he lived for a hundred years, would he not accumulate a large mass of wealth?” ^an10-46-5-2

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-46-5-3

“What do you think, Sakyans? ^an10-46-6-1

Would that man, on account of that wealth, experience perfect happiness for a single day or night, or even half a day or night?” ^an10-46-6-2

“No, sir.” ^an10-46-6-3

“Why is that?” ^an10-46-6-4

“Because sensual pleasures, sir, are impermanent, hollow, false, and deceptive.” ^an10-46-6-5

“But take one of my disciples who lives diligent, keen, and resolute for ten years, practicing in line with my instructions. They can experience perfect happiness for a hundred years, ten thousand years, or a hundred thousand years. ^an10-46-7-1

And they could become a once-returner or a non-returner, or, as a sure bet, a stream-enterer. ^an10-46-7-2

Let alone ten years, ^an10-46-7-3

take one of my disciples who lives diligent, keen, and resolute for nine years … ^an10-46-8-1

eight years … ^an10-46-8-2

seven years … ^an10-46-8-3

six years … ^an10-46-8-4

five years … ^an10-46-8-5

four years … ^an10-46-8-6

three years … ^an10-46-8-7

two years … ^an10-46-8-8

one year … ^an10-46-8-9

ten months … ^an10-46-9-1

nine months … ^an10-46-10-1

eight months … ^an10-46-10-2

seven months … ^an10-46-10-3

six months … ^an10-46-10-4

five months … ^an10-46-10-5

four months … ^an10-46-10-6

three months … ^an10-46-10-7

two months … ^an10-46-10-8

one month … ^an10-46-10-9

a fortnight … ^an10-46-10-10

ten days … ^an10-46-11-1

nine days … ^an10-46-12-1

eight days … ^an10-46-12-2

seven days … ^an10-46-12-3

six days … ^an10-46-12-4

five days … ^an10-46-12-5

four days … ^an10-46-12-6

three days … ^an10-46-12-7

two days … ^an10-46-12-8

Let alone two days, take one of my disciples who lives diligent, keen, and resolute for one day, practicing in line with my instructions. They can experience perfect happiness for a hundred years, ten thousand years, or a hundred thousand years. And they could become a once-returner or a non-returner, or, as a sure bet, a stream-enterer. ^an10-46-12-9

It’s your loss, Sakyans, it’s your misfortune. In this life with its fear of sorrow and death, you sometimes keep the sabbath and you sometimes don’t.” ^an10-46-12-10

“Well, sir, from this day forth we will observe the sabbath with its eight factors.” ^an10-46-12-11

### With Mahāli (Numbered Discourses 10.47) ^an10-47-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof. ^an10-47-1-1

Then Mahāli the Licchavi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-47-1-2

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason for doing bad deeds, for performing bad deeds?” ^an10-47-1-3

“Greed is a cause, Mahāli, greed is a reason for doing bad deeds, for performing bad deeds. ^an10-47-1-4

Hate is a cause of bad deeds … ^an10-47-1-5

Delusion is a cause of bad deeds … ^an10-47-1-6

Irrational application of mind gives rise to bad deeds … ^an10-47-1-7

A wrongly directed mind is a cause of bad deeds … ^an10-47-1-8

This is the cause, Mahāli, this is the reason for doing bad deeds, for performing bad deeds.” ^an10-47-1-9

“What is the cause, sir, what is the reason for doing good deeds, for performing good deeds?” ^an10-47-2-1

“Contentment is a cause, Mahāli, contentment is a reason for doing good deeds, for performing good deeds. ^an10-47-2-2

Love is a cause of good deeds … ^an10-47-2-3

Understanding is a cause of good deeds … ^an10-47-2-4

Rational application of mind gives rise to good deeds … ^an10-47-2-5

A rightly directed mind is a cause of good deeds … ^an10-47-2-6

This is the cause, Mahāli, this is the reason for doing good deeds, for performing good deeds. ^an10-47-2-7

If these ten things were not found in the world, we wouldn’t see either unprincipled and immoral conduct, or principled and moral conduct. ^an10-47-2-8

But since these ten things are found in the world, we see both unprincipled and immoral conduct, and principled and moral conduct.” ^an10-47-2-9

### Ten Regular Reflections for a Renunciate (Numbered Discourses 10.48) ^an10-48-0

“Mendicants, one who has gone forth should often review these ten things. ^an10-48-1-1

What ten? ^an10-48-1-2

One who has gone forth should often review this: ‘I have secured freedom from class.’ ^an10-48-2-1

‘My livelihood is tied up with others.’ ^an10-48-3-1

‘My behavior should be different.’ ^an10-48-4-1

‘I hope there’s no reason to blame myself when it comes to ethical conduct?’ ^an10-48-5-1

‘I hope that, after examination, sensible spiritual companions don’t reproach any aspect of my ethics?’ ^an10-48-6-1

‘I must be parted and separated from all I hold dear and beloved.’ ^an10-48-7-1

‘I am the owner of my deeds and heir to my deeds. Deeds are my womb, my relative, and my refuge. ^an10-48-8-1

I shall be the heir of whatever deeds I do, whether good or bad.’ ^an10-48-9-1

‘As the days and nights pass by, what sort of person am I becoming?’ ^an10-48-10-1

‘Do I love to stay in empty huts?’ ^an10-48-11-1

‘Do I have any superhuman distinctions in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones, so that when my spiritual companions question me on my deathbed I will not be embarrassed?’ ^an10-48-12-1

One who has gone forth should often review these ten things.” ^an10-48-13-1

### Existing Because of the Body (Numbered Discourses 10.49) ^an10-49-0

“Mendicants, these ten things exist because of the body. ^an10-49-1-1

What ten? ^an10-49-1-2

Cold, heat, hunger, thirst, feces, urine, restraint of body, speech, and livelihood, and the will to live that leads to future lives. ^an10-49-1-3

These ten things exist because of the body.” ^an10-49-1-4

### Fights (Numbered Discourses 10.50) ^an10-50-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-50-1-1

Now at that time, after the meal, on return from almsround, several mendicants sat together in the assembly hall. They were fighting, quarreling, and disputing, wounding each other with barbed words. ^an10-50-1-2

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall. He sat down on the seat spread out, ^an10-50-2-1

and addressed the mendicants: ^an10-50-2-2

“Mendicants, what were you sitting talking about just now? What conversation was left unfinished?” ^an10-50-2-3

“Sir, after the meal, on return from almsround, we sat together in the assembly hall, fighting, quarreling, and disputing, wounding each other with barbed words.” ^an10-50-3-1

“Mendicants, this is not appropriate for you gentlemen who have gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness. ^an10-50-3-2

There are ten warm-hearted qualities that make for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute. ^an10-50-4-1

What ten? ^an10-50-4-2

Firstly, a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-50-4-3

When a mendicant is ethical, ^an10-50-4-4

this warm-hearted quality makes for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute. ^an10-50-4-5

Furthermore, a mendicant is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. … ^an10-50-5-1

Furthermore, a mendicant has good friends, companions, and associates. … ^an10-50-6-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is easy to admonish, having qualities that make them easy to admonish. They’re patient, and take instruction respectfully. … ^an10-50-7-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is deft and tireless in a diverse spectrum of duties for their spiritual companions, understanding how to go about things in order to complete and organize the work. … ^an10-50-8-1

Furthermore, a mendicant loves the teachings and is a delight to converse with, being full of joy in the teaching and training. … ^an10-50-9-1

Furthermore, a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities. … ^an10-50-10-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. … ^an10-50-11-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is mindful. They have utmost mindfulness and alertness, and can remember and recall what was said and done long ago. … ^an10-50-12-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering. ^an10-50-13-1

When a mendicant is wise, ^an10-50-13-2

this warm-hearted quality makes for fondness and respect, helping the Saṅgha to live in harmony and unity, without quarreling. ^an10-50-13-3

These ten warm-hearted qualities make for fondness and respect, conducing to inclusion, harmony, and unity, without dispute.” ^an10-50-14-1

### Your Own Mind (Numbered Discourses 10.51) ^an10-51-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-51-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an10-51-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an10-51-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an10-51-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an10-51-1-5

“Mendicants, if a mendicant isn’t skilled in encompassing another’s mind, then they should train themselves: ‘I will be skilled in encompassing my own mind.’ ^an10-51-2-1

And how is a mendicant skilled in encompassing their own mind? ^an10-51-3-1

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. If they see any dirt or blemish there, they’d try to remove it. ^an10-51-3-2

But if they don’t see any dirt or blemish there, they’re happy with that, as they’ve got all they wished for: ^an10-51-3-3

‘How fortunate that I’m clean!’ ^an10-51-3-4

In the same way, checking is very helpful for a mendicant’s skillful qualities. ^an10-51-3-5

‘Am I often covetous or not? Am I often malicious or not? Am I often overcome with dullness and drowsiness or not? Am I often restless or not? Am I often doubtful or not? Am I often irritable or not? Am I often corrupted in mind or not? Am I often disturbed in body or not? Am I often energetic or not? Am I often immersed in samādhi or not?’ ^an10-51-3-6

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-51-4-1

‘I am often covetous, malicious, overcome with dullness and drowsiness, restless, doubtful, irritable, corrupted in mind, disturbed in body, lazy, and not immersed in samādhi.’ In order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-51-4-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-51-4-3

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an10-51-4-4

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-51-5-1

‘I am often content, kind-hearted, rid of dullness and drowsiness, calm, confident, loving, pure in mind, undisturbed in body, energetic, and immersed in samādhi.’ Grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements.” ^an10-51-5-2

### With Sāriputta (Numbered Discourses 10.52) ^an10-52-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an10-52-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-52-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-52-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an10-52-1-4

“Reverends, if a mendicant isn’t skilled in encompassing another’s mind, then they should train themselves: ‘I will be skilled in encompassing my own mind.’ ^an10-52-2-1

And how is a mendicant skilled in encompassing their own mind? ^an10-52-3-1

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. If they see any dirt or blemish there, they’d try to remove it. ^an10-52-3-2

But if they don’t see any dirt or blemish there, they’re happy with that, as they’ve got all they wished for: ^an10-52-3-3

‘How fortunate that I’m clean!’ ^an10-52-3-4

In the same way, checking is very helpful for a mendicant’s skillful qualities. ^an10-52-4-1

‘Am I often covetous or not? Am I often malicious or not? Am I often overcome with dullness and drowsiness or not? Am I often restless or not? Am I often doubtful or not? Am I often irritable or not? Am I often corrupted in mind or not? Am I often disturbed in body or not? Am I often energetic or not? Am I often immersed in samādhi or not?’ ^an10-52-4-2

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-52-5-1

‘I am often covetous, malicious, overcome with dullness and drowsiness, restless, doubtful, irritable, defiled in mind, disturbed in body, lazy, and not immersed in samādhi.’ In order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-52-5-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-52-5-3

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an10-52-5-4

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-52-6-1

‘I am often content, kind-hearted, rid of dullness and drowsiness, calm, confident, loving, pure in mind, undisturbed in body, energetic, and immersed in samādhi.’ Grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements.” ^an10-52-6-2

### Stagnation (Numbered Discourses 10.53) ^an10-53-0

“Mendicants, I don’t praise stagnation in skillful qualities, let alone decline. ^an10-53-1-1

I praise growth in skillful qualities, not stagnation or decline. ^an10-53-1-2

And how is there decline in skillful qualities, not stagnation or growth? ^an10-53-2-1

It’s when a mendicant has a certain degree of faith, ethics, generosity, wisdom, and eloquence. Those qualities neither stagnate nor grow in them. ^an10-53-2-2

I call this decline in skillful qualities, not stagnation or growth. ^an10-53-2-3

This is how there’s decline in skillful qualities, not stagnation or growth. ^an10-53-2-4

And how is there stagnation in skillful qualities, not decline or growth? ^an10-53-3-1

It’s when a mendicant has a certain degree of faith, ethics, generosity, wisdom, and eloquence. Those qualities neither decline nor grow in them. ^an10-53-3-2

I call this stagnation in skillful qualities, not decline or growth. ^an10-53-3-3

This is how there’s stagnation in skillful qualities, not decline or growth. ^an10-53-3-4

And how is there growth in skillful qualities, not stagnation or decline? ^an10-53-4-1

It’s when a mendicant has a certain degree of faith, ethics, generosity, wisdom, and eloquence. Those qualities neither stagnate nor decline in them. ^an10-53-4-2

I call this growth in skillful qualities, not stagnation or decline. ^an10-53-4-3

This is how there’s growth in skillful qualities, not stagnation or decline. ^an10-53-4-4

If a mendicant isn’t skilled in encompassing another’s mind, then they should train themselves: ‘I will be skilled in encompassing my own mind.’ ^an10-53-5-1

And how is a mendicant skilled in encompassing their own mind? ^an10-53-6-1

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. If they see any dirt or blemish there, they’d try to remove it. ^an10-53-6-2

But if they don’t see any dirt or blemish there, they’re happy with that, as they’ve got all they wished for: ^an10-53-6-3

‘How fortunate that I’m clean!’ ^an10-53-6-4

In the same way, checking is very helpful for a mendicant’s skillful qualities. ^an10-53-6-5

‘Am I often covetous or not? Am I often malicious or not? Am I often overcome with dullness and drowsiness or not? Am I often restless or not? Am I often doubtful or not? Am I often irritable or not? Am I often corrupted in mind or not? Am I often disturbed in body or not? Am I often energetic or not? Am I often immersed in samādhi or not?’ ^an10-53-6-6

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-53-7-1

‘I am often covetous, malicious, overcome with dullness and drowsiness, restless, doubtful, irritable, corrupted in mind, disturbed in body, lazy, and not immersed in samādhi.’ In order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-53-7-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-53-7-3

In the same way, in order to give up those bad, unskillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an10-53-7-5

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-53-8-1

‘I am often content, kind-hearted, rid of dullness and drowsiness, calm, confident, loving, pure in mind, undisturbed in body, energetic, and immersed in samādhi.’ Grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements.” ^an10-53-8-2

### Serenity (Numbered Discourses 10.54) ^an10-54-0

“Mendicants, if a mendicant isn’t skilled in encompassing another’s mind, then they should train themselves: ‘I will be skilled in encompassing my own mind.’ ^an10-54-1-1

And how is a mendicant skilled in encompassing their own mind? ^an10-54-2-1

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. If they see any dirt or blemish there, they’d try to remove it. ^an10-54-2-2

But if they don’t see any dirt or blemish there, they’re happy with that, as they’ve got all they wished for: ^an10-54-2-3

‘How fortunate that I’m clean!’ ^an10-54-2-4

In the same way, checking is very helpful for a mendicant’s skillful qualities. ^an10-54-2-5

‘Do I have internal serenity of heart or not? Do I have the higher wisdom of discernment of principles or not?’ ^an10-54-2-6

Suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-54-3-1

‘I have serenity but not discernment.’ Grounded on serenity, they should practice meditation to get discernment. ^an10-54-3-2

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an10-54-3-3

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-54-4-1

‘I have discernment but not serenity.’ Grounded on discernment, they should practice meditation to get serenity. ^an10-54-4-2

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an10-54-4-3

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-54-5-1

‘I have neither serenity nor discernment.’ In order to get those skillful qualities, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-54-5-2

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-54-5-3

In the same way, in order to get those skillful qualities, that mendicant should apply extraordinary enthusiasm … ^an10-54-5-5

After some time they have both serenity and discernment. ^an10-54-5-6

But suppose that, upon checking, a mendicant knows this: ^an10-54-6-1

‘I have both serenity and discernment.’ Grounded on those skillful qualities, they should practice meditation further to end the defilements. ^an10-54-6-2

I say that there are two kinds of robes: ^an10-54-7-1

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear. ^an10-54-7-2

I say that there are two kinds of almsfood: ^an10-54-7-3

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat. ^an10-54-7-4

I say that there are two kinds of lodging: ^an10-54-7-5

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an10-54-7-6

I say that there are two kinds of village or town: ^an10-54-7-7

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an10-54-7-8

I say that there are two kinds of country: ^an10-54-7-9

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an10-54-7-10

I say that there are two kinds of individual: ^an10-54-7-11

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent. ^an10-54-7-12

‘I say that there are two kinds of robes: ^an10-54-8-1

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear.’ ^an10-54-8-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-8-3

Well, should you know of a robe: ^an10-54-8-4

‘When I wear this robe, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not wear that kind of robe. ^an10-54-8-5

Whereas, should you know of a robe: ^an10-54-8-6

‘When I wear this robe, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should wear that kind of robe. ^an10-54-8-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of robes: ^an10-54-8-8

those you should wear, and those you shouldn’t wear.’ ^an10-54-8-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-54-8-10

‘I say that there are two kinds of almsfood: ^an10-54-9-1

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat.’ ^an10-54-9-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-9-3

Well, should you know of almsfood: ^an10-54-9-4

‘When I eat this almsfood, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not eat that kind of almsfood. ^an10-54-9-5

Whereas, should you know of almsfood: ^an10-54-9-6

‘When I eat this almsfood, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should eat that kind of almsfood. ^an10-54-9-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of almsfood: ^an10-54-9-8

that which you should eat, and that which you shouldn’t eat.’ ^an10-54-9-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-54-9-10

‘I say that there are two kinds of lodging: ^an10-54-10-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-10-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-10-3

Well, should you know of a lodging: ^an10-54-10-4

‘When I frequent this lodging, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of lodging. ^an10-54-10-5

Whereas, should you know of a lodging: ^an10-54-10-6

‘When I frequent this lodging, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of lodging. ^an10-54-10-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of lodging: ^an10-54-10-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-10-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-54-10-10

‘I say that there are two kinds of village or town: ^an10-54-11-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-11-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-11-3

Well, should you know of a village or town: ^an10-54-11-4

‘When I frequent this village or town, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of village or town. ^an10-54-11-5

Whereas, should you know of a village or town: ^an10-54-11-6

‘When I frequent this village or town, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of village or town. ^an10-54-11-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of village or town: ^an10-54-11-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-11-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-54-11-10

‘I say that there are two kinds of country: ^an10-54-12-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-12-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-12-3

Well, should you know of a country: ^an10-54-12-4

‘When I frequent this country, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of country. ^an10-54-12-5

Whereas, should you know of a country: ^an10-54-12-6

‘When I frequent this country, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of country. ^an10-54-12-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of country: ^an10-54-12-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-12-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it. ^an10-54-12-10

‘I say that there are two two kinds of individual: ^an10-54-13-1

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-13-2

That’s what I said, but why did I say it? ^an10-54-13-3

Well, should you know of an individual: ^an10-54-13-4

‘When I frequent this individual, unskillful qualities grow, and skillful qualities decline.’ You should not frequent that kind of individual. ^an10-54-13-5

Whereas, should you know of an individual: ^an10-54-13-6

‘When I frequent this individual, unskillful qualities decline, and skillful qualities grow.’ You should frequent that kind of individual. ^an10-54-13-7

‘I say that there are two kinds of individual: ^an10-54-13-8

those you should frequent, and those you shouldn’t frequent.’ ^an10-54-13-9

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an10-54-13-10

### Decline (Numbered Discourses 10.55) ^an10-55-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an10-55-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-55-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-55-1-3

Sāriputta said this: ^an10-55-1-4

“Reverends, they speak of an individual liable to decline, ^an10-55-2-1

and one not liable to decline. ^an10-55-2-2

But how did the Buddha define an individual liable to decline, and one not liable to decline?” ^an10-55-2-3

“Reverend, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta. ^an10-55-2-4

May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this. ^an10-55-2-5

The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an10-55-2-6

“Then listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an10-55-3-1

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^an10-55-3-2

Sāriputta said this: ^an10-55-3-3

“How did the Buddha define an individual liable to decline? ^an10-55-4-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t get to hear a teaching they haven’t heard before. They forget those teachings they have heard. They don’t keep exercising the teachings with which they are already familiar. And they don’t come to understand what they haven’t understood before. ^an10-55-4-2

That’s how the Buddha defined an individual liable to decline. ^an10-55-4-3

And how did the Buddha define an individual not liable to decline? ^an10-55-5-1

It’s when a mendicant gets to hear a teaching they haven’t heard before. They remember those teachings they have heard. They keep exercising the teachings with which they are already familiar. And they come to understand what they haven’t understood before. ^an10-55-5-2

That’s how the Buddha defined an individual not liable to decline. ^an10-55-5-3

If a mendicant isn’t skilled in encompassing another’s mind, then they should train themselves: ‘I will be skilled in encompassing my own mind.’ ^an10-55-6-1

And how is a mendicant skilled in encompassing their own mind? ^an10-55-7-1

Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. If they see any dirt or blemish there, they’d try to remove it. ^an10-55-7-2

But if they don’t see any dirt or blemish there, they’re happy with that, as they’ve got all they wished for: ^an10-55-7-3

‘How fortunate that I’m clean!’ ^an10-55-7-4

In the same way, checking is very helpful for a mendicant’s skillful qualities. ^an10-55-7-5

‘Is contentment often found in me or not? Is kind-heartedness often found in me or not? Is freedom from dullness and drowsiness often found in me or not? Is calm often found in me or not? Is confidence often found in me or not? Is love often found in me or not? Is purity of mind often found in me or not? Is internal joy with the teaching found in me or not? Is internal serenity of heart found in me or not? Is the higher wisdom of discernment of principles found in me or not?’ ^an10-55-7-6

Suppose a mendicant, while checking, doesn’t see any of these skillful qualities in themselves. In order to get them they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-55-8-1

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-55-8-2

In the same way, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm to get those skillful qualities … ^an10-55-8-4

Suppose a mendicant, while checking, sees some of these skillful qualities in themselves, but doesn’t see others. Grounded on the skillful qualities they see, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness in order to get the skillful qualities they don’t see. ^an10-55-9-1

Suppose your clothes or head were on fire. In order to extinguish it, you’d apply extraordinary enthusiasm, effort, zeal, vigor, perseverance, mindfulness, and situational awareness. ^an10-55-9-2

In the same way, grounded on the skillful qualities they see, they should apply extraordinary enthusiasm to get those skillful qualities they don’t see. ^an10-55-9-4

But suppose a mendicant, while checking, sees all of these skillful qualities in themselves. Grounded on all these skillful qualities they should practice meditation further to end the defilements.” ^an10-55-10-1

### Perceptions (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.56) ^an10-56-0

“Mendicants, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an10-56-1-1

What ten? ^an10-56-1-2

The perceptions of ugliness, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence, suffering in impermanence, and not-self in suffering, giving up, fading away, and cessation. ^an10-56-1-3

These ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination.” ^an10-56-1-4

### Perceptions (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.57) ^an10-57-0

“Mendicants, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination. ^an10-57-1-1

What ten? ^an10-57-1-2

The perceptions of impermanence, not-self, death, repulsiveness of food, dissatisfaction with the whole world, a skeleton, a worm-infested corpse, a livid corpse, a split open corpse, and a bloated corpse. ^an10-57-1-3

These ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are very fruitful and beneficial. They have freedom from death as their objective and culmination.” ^an10-57-1-4

### Rooted (Numbered Discourses 10.58) ^an10-58-0

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an10-58-1-1

‘Reverends, all things have what as their root? What produces them? What is their origin? What is their meeting place? What is their chief? What is their ruler? What is their overseer? What is their core? What is their objective? What is their culmination?’ How would you answer them?” ^an10-58-1-2

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an10-58-1-3

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an10-58-2-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an10-58-2-2

The Buddha said this: ^an10-58-2-3

“Mendicants, if wanderers of other religions were to ask: ^an10-58-3-1

‘Reverends, all things have what as their root? What produces them? What is their origin? What is their meeting place? What is their chief? What is their ruler? What is their overseer? What is their core? What is their objective? What is their culmination?’ ^an10-58-3-2

You should answer them: ^an10-58-3-3

‘Reverends, all things are rooted in desire. They are produced by application of mind. Contact is their origin. Feeling is their meeting place. Immersion is their chief. Mindfulness is their ruler. Wisdom is their overseer. Freedom is their core. Their objective is freedom from death. And extinguishment is their culmination.’ ^an10-58-3-4

When questioned by wanderers of other religions, that’s how you should answer them.” ^an10-58-3-5

### Going Forth (Numbered Discourses 10.59) ^an10-59-0

“So you should train like this: ^an10-59-1-1

‘Our minds will be consolidated as they were when we went forth, and arisen bad unskillful qualities will not occupy our minds. ^an10-59-1-2

Our minds will be consolidated in the perceptions of impermanence, not-self, ugliness, and drawbacks. Knowing what is fair and unfair in the world, our minds will be consolidated in that perception. Knowing  existence and nonexistence in the world, our minds will be consolidated in that perception. Knowing the origin and disappearance of the world, our minds will be consolidated in that perception. Our minds will be consolidated in the perceptions of giving up, fading away, and cessation.’ ^an10-59-1-3

That’s how you should train. ^an10-59-1-4

When your minds are consolidated in these ten perceptions, you can expect one of two results: ^an10-59-2-1

enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s residue left behind, non-return.” ^an10-59-2-2

### With Girimānanda (Numbered Discourses 10.60) ^an10-60-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-60-1-1

Now at that time Venerable Girimānanda was sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an10-60-1-2

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-60-1-3

“Sir, Venerable Girimānanda is sick, suffering, gravely ill. ^an10-60-2-1

Sir, please go to Venerable Girimānanda out of sympathy.” ^an10-60-2-2

“Ānanda, if you were to recite to the mendicant Girimānanda these ten perceptions, it’s possible that after hearing them his illness will die down on the spot. ^an10-60-2-3

What ten? ^an10-60-3-1

The perceptions of impermanence, not-self, ugliness, drawbacks, giving up, fading away, cessation, dissatisfaction with the whole world, impermanence of all conditions, and mindfulness of breathing. ^an10-60-3-2

And what is the perception of impermanence? ^an10-60-4-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an10-60-4-2

‘Form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness are impermanent.’ ^an10-60-4-3

And so they meditate observing impermanence in the five grasping aggregates. ^an10-60-4-4

This is called the perception of impermanence. ^an10-60-4-5

And what is the perception of not-self? ^an10-60-5-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an10-60-5-2

‘The eye and sights, ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, and mind and ideas are not-self.’ ^an10-60-5-3

And so they meditate observing not-self in the six interior and exterior sense fields. ^an10-60-5-4

This is called the perception of not-self. ^an10-60-5-5

And what is the perception of ugliness? ^an10-60-6-1

It’s when a mendicant examines their own body up from the soles of the feet and down from the tips of the hairs, wrapped in skin and full of many kinds of filth. ^an10-60-6-2

‘In this body there is head hair, body hair, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, undigested food, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, snot, synovial fluid, urine.’ ^an10-60-6-3

And so they meditate observing ugliness in this body. ^an10-60-6-4

This is called the perception of ugliness. ^an10-60-6-5

And what is the perception of drawbacks? ^an10-60-7-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an10-60-7-2

‘This body has much suffering and many drawbacks. For this body is beset with many kinds of affliction, such as the following. ^an10-60-7-3

Diseases of the eye, inner ear, nose, tongue, body, head, outer ear, mouth, teeth, and lips. Cough, asthma, catarrh, inflammation, fever, stomach ache, fainting, dysentery, gastric pain, cholera, leprosy, boils, eczema, tuberculosis, epilepsy, herpes, itch, scabs, smallpox, scabies, hemorrhage, diabetes, piles, pimples, and ulcers. Afflictions stemming from disorders of bile, phlegm, wind, or their conjunction. Afflictions caused by change in weather, by not taking care of yourself, by overexertion, or as the result of past deeds. Cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, and urination.’ ^an10-60-7-4

And so they meditate observing drawbacks in this body. ^an10-60-7-5

This is called the perception of drawbacks. ^an10-60-7-6

And what is the perception of giving up? ^an10-60-8-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought that has arisen, and they don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an10-60-8-2

This is called the perception of giving up. ^an10-60-8-3

And what is the perception of fading away? ^an10-60-9-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an10-60-9-2

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, extinguishment.’ ^an10-60-9-3

This is called the perception of fading away. ^an10-60-9-4

And what is the perception of cessation? ^an10-60-10-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this: ^an10-60-10-2

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an10-60-10-3

This is called the perception of cessation. ^an10-60-10-4

And what is the perception of dissatisfaction with the whole world? ^an10-60-11-1

It’s when a mendicant lives giving up and not grasping on to the attraction and grasping to the world, the mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. ^an10-60-11-2

This is called the perception of dissatisfaction with the whole world. ^an10-60-11-3

And what is the perception of the impermanence of all conditions? ^an10-60-12-1

It’s when a mendicant is horrified, repelled, and disgusted with all conditions. ^an10-60-12-2

This is called the perception of the impermanence of all conditions. ^an10-60-12-3

And what is mindfulness of breathing? ^an10-60-13-1

It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, sits down cross-legged, sets their body straight, and brings mindfulness to the present. ^an10-60-13-2

Just mindful, they breathe in. Mindful, they breathe out. ^an10-60-13-3

Breathing in heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing in heavily.’ Breathing out heavily they know: ‘I’m breathing out heavily.’ ^an10-60-13-4

When breathing in lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing in lightly.’ Breathing out lightly they know: ‘I’m breathing out lightly.’ ^an10-60-13-5

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the whole body.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the whole body.’ ^an10-60-13-6

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the physical process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the physical process.’ ^an10-60-13-7

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing rapture.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing rapture.’ ^an10-60-13-8

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing bliss.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing bliss.’ ^an10-60-13-9

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mental process.’ ^an10-60-13-10

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in stilling the mental process.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out stilling the mental process.’ ^an10-60-13-11

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in experiencing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out experiencing the mind.’ ^an10-60-13-12

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in gladdening the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out gladdening the mind.’ ^an10-60-13-13

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in immersing the mind in samādhi.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out immersing the mind in samādhi.’ ^an10-60-13-14

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in freeing the mind.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out freeing the mind.’ ^an10-60-13-15

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing impermanence.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing impermanence.’ ^an10-60-13-16

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing fading away.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing fading away.’ ^an10-60-13-17

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing cessation.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing cessation.’ ^an10-60-13-18

They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe in observing letting go.’ They practice like this: ‘I’ll breathe out observing letting go.’ ^an10-60-13-19

This is called mindfulness of breathing. ^an10-60-13-20

If you were to recite to the mendicant Girimānanda these ten perceptions, it’s possible that after hearing them his illness will die down on the spot.” ^an10-60-14-1

Then Ānanda, having learned these ten perceptions from the Buddha himself, went to Girimānanda and recited them. ^an10-60-15-1

Then after Girimānanda heard these ten perceptions his illness died down on the spot. ^an10-60-15-2

And that’s how he recovered from that illness. ^an10-60-15-3

### Ignorance (Numbered Discourses 10.61) ^an10-61-0

“Mendicants, it is said that no prior point of ignorance is evident, before which there was no ignorance, and afterwards it came to be. ^an10-61-1-1

And yet it is evident that there is a specific condition for ignorance. ^an10-61-1-2

I say that ignorance is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-1

And what is the fuel for ignorance? ^an10-61-2-2

You should say: ‘The five hindrances.’ ^an10-61-2-3

I say that the five hindrances are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-4

And what is the fuel for the five hindrances? ^an10-61-2-5

You should say: ‘The three kinds of misconduct.’ ^an10-61-2-6

I say that the three kinds of misconduct are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-7

And what is the fuel for the three kinds of misconduct? ^an10-61-2-8

You should say: ‘Lack of sense restraint.’ ^an10-61-2-9

I say that lack of sense restraint is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-10

And what is the fuel for lack of sense restraint? ^an10-61-2-11

You should say: ‘Lack of mindfulness and situational awareness.’ ^an10-61-2-12

I say that lack of mindfulness and situational awareness is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-13

And what is the fuel for lack of mindfulness and situational awareness? ^an10-61-2-14

You should say: ‘Irrational application of mind.’ ^an10-61-2-15

I say that irrational application of mind is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-16

And what is the fuel for irrational application of mind? ^an10-61-2-17

You should say: ‘Lack of faith.’ ^an10-61-2-18

I say that lack of faith is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-19

And what is the fuel for lack of faith? ^an10-61-2-20

You should say: ‘Listening to an untrue teaching.’ ^an10-61-2-21

I say that listening to an untrue teaching is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-2-22

And what is the fuel for listening to an untrue teaching? ^an10-61-2-23

You should say: ‘Associating with untrue persons.’ ^an10-61-2-24

In this way, when the factor of associating with untrue persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to an untrue teaching. When the factor of listening to an untrue teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of lack of faith … irrational application of mind … lack of mindfulness and situational awareness … lack of sense restraint …the three kinds of misconduct … the five hindrances. When the five hindrances are fulfilled, they fulfill ignorance. ^an10-61-3-1

That’s the fuel for ignorance, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-61-3-2

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an10-61-4-1

That’s the fuel for the ocean, and that’s how it’s filled up. ^an10-61-4-2

In the same way, when the factor of associating with untrue persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to an untrue teaching. When the factor of listening to an untrue teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of lack of faith … irrational application of mind … lack of mindfulness and situational awareness … lack of sense restraint …the three kinds of misconduct … the five hindrances. When the five hindrances are fulfilled, they fulfill ignorance. ^an10-61-5-1

That’s the fuel for ignorance, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-61-5-2

I say that knowledge and freedom are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-1

And what is the fuel for knowledge and freedom? ^an10-61-6-2

You should say: ‘The seven awakening factors.’ ^an10-61-6-3

I say that the seven awakening factors are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-4

And what is the fuel for the seven awakening factors? ^an10-61-6-5

You should say: ‘The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.’ ^an10-61-6-6

I say that the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-7

And what is the fuel for the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^an10-61-6-8

You should say: ‘The three kinds of good conduct.’ ^an10-61-6-9

I say that the three kinds of good conduct are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-10

And what is the fuel for the three kinds of good conduct? ^an10-61-6-11

You should say: ‘Sense restraint.’ ^an10-61-6-12

I say that sense restraint is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-13

And what is the fuel for sense restraint? ^an10-61-6-14

You should say: ‘Mindfulness and situational awareness.’ ^an10-61-6-15

I say that mindfulness and situational awareness is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-16

And what is the fuel for mindfulness and situational awareness? ^an10-61-6-17

You should say: ‘Rational application of mind.’ ^an10-61-6-18

I say that rational application of mind is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-19

And what is the fuel for rational application of mind? ^an10-61-6-20

You should say: ‘Faith.’ ^an10-61-6-21

I say that faith is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-22

And what is the fuel for faith? ^an10-61-6-23

You should say: ‘Listening to the true teaching.’ ^an10-61-6-24

I say that listening to the true teaching is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-61-6-25

And what is the fuel for listening to the true teaching? ^an10-61-6-26

You should say: ‘Associating with true persons.’ ^an10-61-6-27

In this way, when the factor of associating with true persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to the true teaching. When the factor of listening to the true teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of faith … rational application of mind … mindfulness and situational awareness … sense restraint …the three kinds of good conduct … the four kinds of mindfulness meditation … the seven awakening factors. When the seven awakening factors are fulfilled, they fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^an10-61-7-1

That’s the fuel for knowledge and freedom, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-61-7-2

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an10-61-8-1

That’s the fuel for the ocean, and that’s how it’s filled up. ^an10-61-8-2

In the same way, when the factor of associating with true persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to the true teaching. When the factor of listening to the true teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of faith … rational application of mind … mindfulness and situational awareness … sense restraint …the three kinds of good conduct … the four kinds of mindfulness meditation … the seven awakening factors. When the seven awakening factors are fulfilled, they fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^an10-61-9-1

That’s the fuel for knowledge and freedom, and that’s how it’s fulfilled.” ^an10-61-9-2

### Craving (Numbered Discourses 10.62) ^an10-62-0

“Mendicants, it is said that no prior point of craving for continued existence is evident, before which there was no craving for continued existence, and afterwards it came to be. ^an10-62-1-1

And yet it is evident that there is a specific condition for craving for continued existence. ^an10-62-1-2

I say that craving for continued existence is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-1

And what is the fuel for craving for continued existence? ^an10-62-2-2

You should say: ‘Ignorance.’ ^an10-62-2-3

I say that ignorance is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-4

And what is the fuel for ignorance? ^an10-62-2-5

You should say: ‘The five hindrances.’ ^an10-62-2-6

I say that the five hindrances are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-7

And what is the fuel for the five hindrances? ^an10-62-2-8

You should say: ‘The three kinds of misconduct.’ ^an10-62-2-9

I say that the three kinds of misconduct are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-10

And what is the fuel for the three kinds of misconduct? ^an10-62-2-11

You should say: ‘Lack of sense restraint.’ ^an10-62-2-12

I say that lack of sense restraint is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-13

And what is the fuel for lack of sense restraint? ^an10-62-2-14

You should say: ‘Lack of mindfulness and situational awareness.’ ^an10-62-2-15

I say that lack of mindfulness and situational awareness is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-16

And what is the fuel for lack of mindfulness and situational awareness? ^an10-62-2-17

You should say: ‘Irrational application of mind.’ ^an10-62-2-18

I say that irrational application of mind is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-19

And what is the fuel for irrational application of mind? ^an10-62-2-20

You should say: ‘Lack of faith.’ ^an10-62-2-21

I say that lack of faith is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-22

And what is the fuel for lack of faith? ^an10-62-2-23

You should say: ‘Listening to an untrue teaching.’ ^an10-62-2-24

I say that listening to an untrue teaching is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-2-25

And what is the fuel for listening to an untrue teaching? ^an10-62-2-26

You should say: ‘Associating with untrue persons.’ ^an10-62-2-27

In this way, when the factor of associating with untrue persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to an untrue teaching. When the factor of listening to an untrue teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of lack of faith … irrational application of mind … lack of mindfulness and situational awareness … lack of sense restraint …the three kinds of misconduct … the five hindrances … ignorance. When ignorance is fulfilled, it fulfills craving for continued existence. ^an10-62-3-1

That’s the fuel for craving for continued existence, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-62-3-2

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an10-62-4-1

That’s the fuel for the ocean, and that’s how it’s filled up. ^an10-62-4-2

In the same way, when the factor of associating with untrue persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to an untrue teaching. When the factor of listening to an untrue teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of lack of faith … irrational application of mind … lack of mindfulness and situational awareness … lack of sense restraint …the three kinds of misconduct … the five hindrances … ignorance. When ignorance is fulfilled, it fulfills craving for continued existence. ^an10-62-5-1

That’s the fuel for craving for continued existence, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-62-5-2

I say that knowledge and freedom are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-1

And what is the fuel for knowledge and freedom? ^an10-62-6-2

You should say: ‘The seven awakening factors.’ ^an10-62-6-3

I say that the seven awakening factors are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-4

And what is the fuel for the seven awakening factors? ^an10-62-6-5

You should say: ‘The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.’ ^an10-62-6-6

I say that the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-7

And what is the fuel for the four kinds of mindfulness meditation? ^an10-62-6-8

You should say: ‘The three kinds of good conduct.’ ^an10-62-6-9

I say that the three kinds of good conduct are fueled by something, they’re not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-10

And what is the fuel for the three kinds of good conduct? ^an10-62-6-11

You should say: ‘Sense restraint.’ ^an10-62-6-12

I say that sense restraint is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-13

And what is the fuel for sense restraint? ^an10-62-6-14

You should say: ‘Mindfulness and situational awareness.’ ^an10-62-6-15

I say that mindfulness and situational awareness is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-16

And what is the fuel for mindfulness and situational awareness? ^an10-62-6-17

You should say: ‘Rational application of mind.’ ^an10-62-6-18

I say that rational application of mind is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-19

And what is the fuel for rational application of mind? ^an10-62-6-20

You should say: ‘Faith.’ ^an10-62-6-21

I say that faith is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-22

And what is the fuel for faith? ^an10-62-6-23

You should say: ‘Listening to the true teaching.’ ^an10-62-6-24

I say that listening to the true teaching is fueled by something, it’s not unfueled. ^an10-62-6-25

And what is the fuel for listening to the true teaching? ^an10-62-6-26

You should say: ‘Associating with true persons.’ ^an10-62-6-27

In this way, when the factor of associating with true persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to the true teaching. When the factor of listening to the true teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of faith … rational application of mind … mindfulness and situational awareness … sense restraint …the three kinds of good conduct … the four kinds of mindfulness meditation … the seven awakening factors. When the seven awakening factors are fulfilled, they fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^an10-62-7-1

That’s the fuel for knowledge and freedom, and that’s how it’s fulfilled. ^an10-62-7-2

It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean. ^an10-62-8-1

That’s the fuel for the ocean, and that’s how it’s filled up. ^an10-62-8-2

In this way, when the factor of associating with true persons is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of listening to the true teaching. When the factor of listening to the true teaching is fulfilled, it fulfills the factor of faith … rational application of mind … mindfulness and situational awareness … sense restraint …the three kinds of good conduct … the four kinds of mindfulness meditation … the seven awakening factors. When the seven awakening factors are fulfilled, they fulfill knowledge and freedom. ^an10-62-8-3

That’s the fuel for knowledge and freedom, and that’s how it’s fulfilled.” ^an10-62-8-4

### Come to a Conclusion (Numbered Discourses 10.63) ^an10-63-0

“Mendicants, all those who have come to a conclusion about me are accomplished in view. ^an10-63-1-1

Of those who are accomplished in view, five conclude their path in this realm, and five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind. ^an10-63-1-2

Which five conclude their path in this realm? ^an10-63-1-3

The one who has seven rebirths at most, the one who goes from family to family, the one-seeder, the once returner, and the one who is perfected in this very life. ^an10-63-1-4

These five conclude their path in this realm. ^an10-63-1-5

Which five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind? ^an10-63-1-6

The one who is extinguished between one life and the next, the one who is extinguished upon landing, the one who is extinguished without extra effort, the one who is extinguished with extra effort, and the one who heads upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an10-63-1-7

These five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind. ^an10-63-1-8

All those who have come to a conclusion about me are accomplished in view. ^an10-63-1-9

Of those who are accomplished in view, these five conclude their path in this realm, and these five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind.” ^an10-63-1-10

### Experiential Confidence (Numbered Discourses 10.64) ^an10-64-0

“Mendicants, all those who have experiential confidence in me have entered the stream. ^an10-64-1-1

Of those who have entered the stream, five conclude their path in this realm, and five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind. ^an10-64-1-2

Which five conclude their path in this realm? ^an10-64-1-3

The one who has seven rebirths at most, the one who goes from family to family, the one-seeder, the once returner, and the one who is perfected in this very life. ^an10-64-1-4

These five conclude their path in this realm. ^an10-64-1-5

Which five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind? ^an10-64-1-6
