The one who is extinguished between one life and the next, the one who is extinguished upon landing, the one who is extinguished without extra effort, the one who is extinguished with extra effort, and the one who heads upstream, going to the Akaniṭṭha realm. ^an10-64-1-7

These five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind. ^an10-64-1-8

All those who have experiential confidence in me have entered the stream. ^an10-64-1-9

Of those who have entered the stream, these five conclude their path in this realm, and these five conclude their path after leaving this realm behind.” ^an10-64-1-10

### Happiness (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.65) ^an10-65-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying in the land of the Magadhans near the little village of Nālaka. ^an10-65-1-1

Then the wanderer Sāmaṇḍakāni went up to Venerable Sāriputta and exchanged greetings with him. ^an10-65-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Sāmaṇḍakāni sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta: ^an10-65-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, what is happiness and what is suffering?” ^an10-65-2-1

“Rebirth is suffering, reverend, no rebirth is happiness. ^an10-65-2-2

When there is rebirth, you can expect this kind of suffering. ^an10-65-2-3

Cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, and urination. Contact with fire, clubs, and knives. And relatives and friends get together and annoy you. ^an10-65-2-4

When there is rebirth, this is the kind of suffering you can expect. ^an10-65-2-5

When there is no rebirth, you can expect this kind of happiness. ^an10-65-2-6

No cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, or urination. No contact with fire, clubs, or knives. And relatives and friends don’t get together and annoy you. ^an10-65-2-7

When there is no rebirth, this is the kind of happiness you can expect.” ^an10-65-2-8

### Happiness (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.66) ^an10-66-0

At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying in the land of the Magadhans near the little village of Nālaka. ^an10-66-1-1

Then the wanderer Sāmaṇḍakāni went up to Venerable Sāriputta and exchanged greetings with him. ^an10-66-1-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Sāmaṇḍakāni sat down to one side and said to Sāriputta: ^an10-66-1-3

“Reverend Sāriputta, in this teaching and training, what is happiness and what is suffering?” ^an10-66-2-1

“Reverend, in this teaching and training dissatisfaction is suffering and satisfaction is happiness. ^an10-66-2-2

When you’re dissatisfied, you can expect this kind of suffering. ^an10-66-2-3

You find no happiness or pleasure while walking … ^an10-66-2-4

standing … ^an10-66-2-5

sitting … ^an10-66-2-6

or lying down … ^an10-66-2-7

or when in a village … ^an10-66-2-8

a wilderness … ^an10-66-2-9

at the root of a tree … ^an10-66-2-10

an empty hut … ^an10-66-2-11

the open air … ^an10-66-2-12

or when among the mendicants. ^an10-66-2-13

When you’re dissatisfied, this is the kind of suffering you can expect. ^an10-66-2-14

When you’re satisfied, you can expect this kind of happiness. ^an10-66-3-1

You find happiness or pleasure while walking … ^an10-66-3-2

standing … ^an10-66-3-3

sitting … ^an10-66-3-4

or lying down … ^an10-66-3-5

or when in a village … ^an10-66-3-6

a wilderness … ^an10-66-3-7

at the root of a tree … ^an10-66-3-8

an empty hut … ^an10-66-3-9

the open air … ^an10-66-3-10

or when among the mendicants. ^an10-66-3-11

When you’re satisfied, this is the kind of happiness you can expect.” ^an10-66-3-12

### At Naḷakapāna (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.67) ^an10-67-0

At one time the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Kosalans together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants when he arrived at a town of the Kosalans named Naḷakapāna. ^an10-67-1-1

There the Buddha stayed near Naḷakapāna in the grove of flame-of-the-forest trees. ^an10-67-1-2

Now, at that time it was the sabbath, and the Buddha was sitting surrounded by a Saṅgha of monks. ^an10-67-1-3

The Buddha spent much of the night educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk. Then he looked around the Saṅgha of mendicants, who were so very silent. He addressed Venerable Sāriputta: ^an10-67-1-4

“Sāriputta, the Saṅgha of mendicants is rid of dullness and drowsiness. ^an10-67-2-1

Give them some Dhamma talk as you feel inspired. ^an10-67-2-2

My back is sore, ^an10-67-2-3

I’ll stretch it.” ^an10-67-2-4

“Yes, sir,” Sāriputta replied. ^an10-67-2-5

And then the Buddha spread out his outer robe folded in four and laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^an10-67-3-1

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an10-67-3-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-67-3-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-67-3-4

Sāriputta said this: ^an10-67-3-5

“Reverends, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-4-1

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-4-4

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^an10-67-4-5

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-4-6

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-4-9

A faithless individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-1

An individual with no conscience is in decline. ^an10-67-5-2

An imprudent individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-3

A lazy individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-4

A witless individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-5

An irritable individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-6

An acrimonious individual is in decline. ^an10-67-5-7

An individual with corrupt wishes is in decline. ^an10-67-5-8

An individual with bad friends is in decline. ^an10-67-5-9

An individual with wrong view is in decline. ^an10-67-5-10

Whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-6-1

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-6-3

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^an10-67-6-4

In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-6-5

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-6-8

A faithful individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-1

An individual with a conscience doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-2

A prudent individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-3

An energetic individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-4

A wise individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-5

A loving individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-6

A kind individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-7

An individual with few desires doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-8

An individual with good friends doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-7-9

An individual with right view doesn’t decline.” ^an10-67-7-10

Then the Buddha got up and said to Venerable Sāriputta: ^an10-67-8-1

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^an10-67-8-2

Whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-8-3

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-8-6

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^an10-67-8-7

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-8-8

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-8-9

A faithless individual is in decline. ^an10-67-9-1

An individual with no conscience … ^an10-67-9-2

imprudent … ^an10-67-9-3

lazy … ^an10-67-9-4

witless … ^an10-67-9-5

irritable … ^an10-67-9-6

acrimonious … ^an10-67-9-7

with corrupt wishes … ^an10-67-9-8

bad friends … ^an10-67-9-9

An individual with wrong view is in decline. ^an10-67-9-10

Whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-10-1

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-10-4

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^an10-67-10-5

In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-67-10-6

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-67-10-9

A faithful individual doesn’t decline. ^an10-67-11-1

A conscientious individual … ^an10-67-11-2

prudent … ^an10-67-11-3

energetic … ^an10-67-11-4

wise … ^an10-67-11-5

loving … ^an10-67-11-6

kind … ^an10-67-11-7

with few desires … ^an10-67-11-8

good friends … ^an10-67-11-9

An individual with right view doesn’t decline.” ^an10-67-11-10

### At Naḷakapāna (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.68) ^an10-68-0

At one time the Buddha stayed near Naḷakapāna in the grove of flame-of-the-forest trees. ^an10-68-1-1

Now, at that time it was the sabbath, and the Buddha was sitting surrounded by a Saṅgha of monks. ^an10-68-1-2

The Buddha spent much of the night educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk. Then he looked around the Saṅgha of mendicants, who were so very silent. He addressed Venerable Sāriputta, ^an10-68-1-3

“Sāriputta, the Saṅgha of mendicants is rid of dullness and drowsiness. ^an10-68-2-1

Give them some Dhamma talk as you feel inspired. ^an10-68-2-2

My back is sore, ^an10-68-2-3

I’ll stretch it.” ^an10-68-2-4

“Yes, sir,” Sāriputta replied. ^an10-68-2-5

And then the Buddha spread out his outer robe folded in four and laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up. ^an10-68-3-1

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an10-68-3-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-68-3-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-68-3-4

Sāriputta said this: ^an10-68-3-5

“Reverends, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who doesn’t want to listen, doesn’t memorize the teachings, examine their meaning, or practice accordingly, and is not diligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-4-1

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-4-9

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^an10-68-4-10

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who doesn’t want to listen, doesn’t memorize the teachings, examine their meaning, or practice accordingly, and is negligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-4-11

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-4-19

Whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who wants to listen, memorizes the teachings, examines their meaning, and practices accordingly, and is diligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-5-1

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-5-9

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^an10-68-5-10

In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who wants to listen, memorizes the teachings, examines their meaning, and practices accordingly, and is diligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-5-11

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night.” ^an10-68-5-12

Then the Buddha got up and said to Venerable Sāriputta: ^an10-68-6-1

“Good, good, Sāriputta! ^an10-68-6-2

Whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who doesn’t want to listen, doesn’t memorize the teachings, examine their meaning, or practice accordingly, and is negligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-6-3

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-6-11

It’s like the moon in the waning fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only decline. ^an10-68-6-12

In the same way, whoever has no faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who doesn’t want to listen, doesn’t memorize the teachings, examine their meaning, or practice accordingly, and is negligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-6-13

can expect decline, not growth, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-6-14

Whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who wants to listen, memorizes the teachings, examines their meaning, and practices accordingly, and is diligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-7-1

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night. ^an10-68-7-9

It’s like the moon in the waxing fortnight. Whether by day or by night, its beauty, roundness, luminosity, and diameter and circumference only grow. ^an10-68-7-10

In the same way, whoever has faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom; who wants to listen, memorizes the teachings, examines their meaning, and practices accordingly, and is diligent when it comes to skillful qualities ^an10-68-7-11

can expect growth, not decline, in skillful qualities, whether by day or by night.” ^an10-68-7-12

### Topics of Discussion (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.69) ^an10-69-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-69-1-1

Now at that time, after the meal, on return from almsround, several mendicants sat together in the assembly hall. They engaged in all kinds of low talk, such as ^an10-69-1-2

talk about kings, bandits, and ministers; talk about armies, threats, and wars; talk about food, drink, clothes, and beds; talk about garlands and fragrances; talk about family, vehicles, villages, towns, cities, and countries; talk about women and heroes; street talk and well talk; talk about the departed; motley talk; tales of land and sea; and talk about being reborn in this or that place. ^an10-69-1-3

Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the assembly hall, where he sat on the seat spread out ^an10-69-2-1

and addressed the mendicants: ^an10-69-2-2

“Mendicants, what were you sitting talking about just now? What conversation was left unfinished?” ^an10-69-2-3

And they told him what had happened. ^an10-69-3-1

“Mendicants, it is not appropriate for you gentlemen who have gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness to engage in these kinds of low talk. ^an10-69-3-3

There are, mendicants, these ten topics of discussion. ^an10-69-4-1

What ten? ^an10-69-4-2

Talk about fewness of wishes, contentment, seclusion, aloofness, arousing energy, ethics, immersion, wisdom, freedom, and the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-69-4-3

These are the ten topics of discussion. ^an10-69-4-4

Mendicants, if you bring up these topics of conversation again and again then your glory could surpass even the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful, let alone the wanderers of other religions.” ^an10-69-5-1

### Topics of Discussion (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.70) ^an10-70-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-70-1-1

Now at that time, after the meal, on return from almsround, several mendicants sat together in the assembly hall. ^an10-70-1-2

They engaged in all kinds of low talk, such as talk about kings, bandits, and chief ministers; talk about armies, threats, and wars; talk about food, drink, clothes, and beds; talk about garlands and fragrances; talk about family, vehicles, villages, towns, cities, and nations; talk about women and heroes; street talk and well talk; talk about the departed; miscellaneous talk; tales of land and sea; and talk about being reborn in this or that state of existence. ^an10-70-1-3

“Mendicants, there are ten grounds for praise. ^an10-70-2-1

What ten? ^an10-70-2-2

It’s when a mendicant personally has few wishes, and speaks to the mendicants on having few wishes. ^an10-70-2-3

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-2-4

A mendicant personally is content, and speaks to the mendicants on contentment. ^an10-70-3-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-3-2

A mendicant personally is secluded, and speaks to the mendicants on seclusion. ^an10-70-4-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-4-2

A mendicant personally doesn’t mix closely with others, and speaks to the mendicants on not mixing closely with others. ^an10-70-5-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-5-2

A mendicant personally is energetic, and speaks to the mendicants on rousing energy. ^an10-70-6-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-6-2

A mendicant personally is accomplished in ethics, and speaks to the mendicants on being accomplished in ethics. ^an10-70-7-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-7-2

A mendicant personally is accomplished in immersion, and speaks to the mendicants on being accomplished in immersion. ^an10-70-8-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-8-2

A mendicant personally is accomplished in wisdom, and speaks to the mendicants on being accomplished in wisdom. ^an10-70-9-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-9-2

A mendicant personally is accomplished in freedom, and speaks to the mendicants on being accomplished in freedom. ^an10-70-10-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-10-2

A mendicant personally is accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom, and speaks to the mendicants on being accomplished in the knowledge and vision of freedom. ^an10-70-11-1

This is a ground for praise. ^an10-70-11-2

These are the ten grounds for praise.” ^an10-70-12-1

### One Might Wish (Numbered Discourses 10.71) ^an10-71-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-71-1-1

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an10-71-1-2

“Mendicants!” ^an10-71-1-3

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an10-71-1-4

The Buddha said this: ^an10-71-1-5

“Mendicants, live by the ethical precepts and the monastic code. Live restrained in the monastic code, conducting yourselves well and seeking alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, keep the rules you’ve undertaken. ^an10-71-2-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I be liked and approved by my spiritual companions, respected and admired.’ So let them fulfill their precepts, be committed to inner serenity of the heart, not neglect absorption, be endowed with discernment, and frequent empty huts. ^an10-71-3-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.’ So let them fulfill their precepts, be committed to inner serenity of the heart, not neglect absorption, be endowed with discernment, and frequent empty huts. ^an10-71-4-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May the services of those whose robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick I enjoy be very fruitful and beneficial for them.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-5-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘When deceased family and relatives who have passed away recollect me with a confident mind, may this be very fruitful and beneficial for them.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-6-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I be content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-7-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I endure cold, heat, hunger, and thirst. May I endure the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles. May I endure rude and unwelcome criticism. And may I put up with physical pain—sharp, severe, acute, unpleasant, disagreeable, and life-threatening.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-8-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I prevail over desire and discontent, and may desire and discontent not prevail over me. May I live having mastered desire and discontent whenever they have arisen.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-9-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I prevail over fear and dread, and may fear and dread not prevail over me. May I live having mastered fear and dread whenever they arise.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-10-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when I want, without trouble or difficulty.’ So let them fulfill their precepts … ^an10-71-11-1

A mendicant might wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’ So let them fulfill their precepts, be committed to inner serenity of the heart, not neglect absorption, be endowed with discernment, and frequent empty huts. ^an10-71-12-1

‘Live by the ethical precepts and the monastic code. Live restrained in the monastic code, conducting yourselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, keep the rules you’ve undertaken.’ ^an10-71-13-1

That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.” ^an10-71-13-2

### Thorns (Numbered Discourses 10.72) ^an10-72-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof, together with several well-known senior disciples. ^an10-72-1-1

They included Venerables Cāla, Upacāla, Kakkaṭa, Kaṭimbha, Kaṭa, Kaṭissaṅga, ^an10-72-1-2

and other well-known senior disciples. ^an10-72-1-3

Now at that time several well-known Licchavis plunged deep into the Great Wood to see the Buddha. Driving a succession of fine carriages, they made a colossal racket. ^an10-72-2-1

Then those venerables thought: ^an10-72-2-2

“These several well-known Licchavis have plunged deep into the Great Wood to see the Buddha. Driving a succession of fine carriages, they’re making a colossal racket. ^an10-72-2-3

But the Buddha has said that sound is a thorn to absorption. ^an10-72-2-4

Let’s go to the Gosiṅga Sal Wood. ^an10-72-2-5

There we can meditate comfortably, free of noise and crowds.” ^an10-72-2-6

Then those venerables went to the Gosiṅga Sal Wood, ^an10-72-2-7

where they meditated comfortably, free of noise and crowds. ^an10-72-2-8

Then the Buddha said to the mendicants: ^an10-72-3-1

“Mendicants, where are Cāla, Upacāla, Kakkaṭa, Kaṭimbha, Kaṭa, and Kaṭissaṅga? ^an10-72-3-2

Where have these senior disciples gone?” ^an10-72-3-3

And the mendicants told him what had happened. ^an10-72-4-1

“Good, good, mendicants! It’s just as those great disciples have so rightly explained. I have said that sound is a thorn to absorption. ^an10-72-5-1

Mendicants, there are these ten thorns. ^an10-72-6-1

What ten? ^an10-72-6-2

Relishing company is a thorn for someone who loves seclusion. Focusing on the beautiful feature of things is a thorn for someone pursuing the meditation on ugliness. Seeing shows is a thorn to someone restraining the senses. Lingering in the neighborhood of ladies is a thorn to chastity. Sound is a thorn to the first absorption. Placing the mind and keeping it connected are a thorn to the second absorption. Rapture is a thorn to the third absorption. Breathing is a thorn to the fourth absorption. Perception and feeling are a thorn to the attainment of the cessation of perception and feeling. Greed, hate, and delusion are thorns. ^an10-72-6-3

Mendicants, live free of thorns! ^an10-72-7-1

Live rid of thorns! ^an10-72-7-2

Mendicants, live free of thorns and rid of thorns! ^an10-72-7-3

The perfected ones live free of thorns, ^an10-72-7-4

rid of thorns, ^an10-72-7-5

free and rid of thorns.” ^an10-72-7-6

### Likable (Numbered Discourses 10.73) ^an10-73-0

“Mendicants, these ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things are rare in the world. ^an10-73-1-1

What ten? ^an10-73-1-2

Wealth, ^an10-73-1-3

beauty, ^an10-73-1-4

health, ^an10-73-1-5

ethical conduct, ^an10-73-1-6

the spiritual life, ^an10-73-1-7

friends, ^an10-73-1-8

learning, ^an10-73-1-9

wisdom, ^an10-73-1-10

good qualities, ^an10-73-1-11

and heaven are likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world. ^an10-73-1-12

Ten things are roadblocks for these ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world. ^an10-73-2-1

Sloth and lack of initiative are a roadblock for wealth. Lack of adornment and decoration are a roadblock for beauty. Unsuitable activity is a roadblock for health. Bad friendship is a roadblock for ethical conduct. Lack of sense restraint is a roadblock for the spiritual life. Dishonesty is a roadblock for friends. Not rehearsing is a roadblock for learning. Not wanting to listen and ask questions are roadblocks for wisdom. Lack of commitment and reviewing are roadblocks for good qualities. Wrong practice hinders heaven. ^an10-73-2-2

These ten things are roadblocks for these ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world. ^an10-73-2-3

Ten things nourish these ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world. ^an10-73-3-1

Application and initiative nourish wealth. Adornment and decoration nourish beauty. Suitable activity nourishes health. Good friendship nourishes ethical conduct. Sense restraint nourishes the spiritual life. Honesty nourishes friends. Rehearsing nourishes learning. Eagerness to listen and ask questions nourishes wisdom. Commitment and reviewing nourish good qualities. Right practice nourishes heaven. ^an10-73-3-2

These ten things nourish these ten likable, desirable, and agreeable things that are rare in the world.” ^an10-73-3-3

### Growth (Numbered Discourses 10.74) ^an10-74-0

“Mendicants, a noble disciple who grows in ten ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an10-74-1-1

What ten? ^an10-74-1-2

He grows in fields and lands, money and grain, wives and children, in bondservants, workers, and staff, and in livestock. And he grows in faith, ethics, learning, generosity, and wisdom. ^an10-74-1-3

A noble disciple who grows in ten ways grows nobly, taking on what is essential and excellent in this life. ^an10-74-1-4

Someone who grows in money and grain, ^an10-74-2-1

in wives, children, and livestock, ^an10-74-2-2

is wealthy, famous, and respected ^an10-74-2-3

by relatives and friends, and even by royals. ^an10-74-2-4

When someone grows in faith and ethics, ^an10-74-3-1

wisdom, and both generosity and learning— ^an10-74-3-2

a good man such as he sees clearly, ^an10-74-3-3

and in this very life he grows in both ways.” ^an10-74-3-4

### With Migasālā (Numbered Discourses 10.75) ^an10-75-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-75-1-1

Then Venerable Ānanda robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to the home of the laywoman Migasālā, where he sat on the seat spread out. ^an10-75-1-2

Then the laywoman Migasālā went up to Ānanda, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-75-1-3

“Honorable Ānanda, how on earth are we supposed to understand the teaching taught by the Buddha, when the chaste and the unchaste are both reborn in exactly the same place in the next life? ^an10-75-2-1

My father Purāṇa was chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an10-75-2-2

When he passed away the Buddha declared that, since he was a once-returner, he was reborn in the host of joyful gods. ^an10-75-2-3

But my uncle Isidatta was not chaste; he lived content with his wife. ^an10-75-2-4

When he passed away the Buddha also declared that, since he was a once-returner, he was reborn in the host of joyful gods. ^an10-75-2-5

How on earth are we supposed to understand the teaching taught by the Buddha, when the chaste and the unchaste are both reborn in exactly the same place in the next life?” ^an10-75-3-1

“You’re right, sister, but that’s how the Buddha declared it.” ^an10-75-3-2

Then Ānanda, after receiving almsfood at Migasālā’s home, rose from his seat and left. ^an10-75-4-1

Then after the meal, on his return from almsround, Ānanda went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. ^an10-75-4-2

“Ānanda, who is this laywoman Migasālā, a foolish incompetent aunty, with an aunty’s wit? And who is it that knows how to assess individual persons? ^an10-75-8-1

These ten individuals are found in the world. ^an10-75-9-1

What ten? ^an10-75-9-2

Take a certain individual who is unethical. ^an10-75-9-3

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that unethical conduct ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-9-4

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an10-75-9-5

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-9-6

They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-9-7

Take a certain individual who is unethical. ^an10-75-10-1

But they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that unethical conduct ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-10-2

And they have listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom. ^an10-75-10-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-10-4

They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-10-5

Judgmental people compare them, saying: ^an10-75-11-1

‘This one has just the same qualities as the other, so why is one worse and one better?’ ^an10-75-11-2

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an10-75-11-3

In this case, the individual who is unethical, ^an10-75-12-1

but truly understands the freedom of heart … ^an10-75-12-2

and has listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom ^an10-75-12-3

is better and finer than the other individual. ^an10-75-12-4

Why is that? ^an10-75-12-5

Because the stream of the teaching carries them along. ^an10-75-12-6

But who knows the difference between them except a Realized One? ^an10-75-12-7

So, Ānanda, don’t be judgmental about individuals. ^an10-75-12-8

Don’t pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-12-9

Those who pass judgment on individuals harm themselves. ^an10-75-12-10

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on people. ^an10-75-12-11

Take a certain individual who is ethical. ^an10-75-13-1

But they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that ethical conduct ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-13-2

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an10-75-13-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-13-4

They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-13-5

Take a certain individual who is ethical. ^an10-75-14-1

And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that ethical conduct ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-14-2

And they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom. ^an10-75-14-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-14-4

They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-14-5

Judgmental people compare them … ^an10-75-15-1

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-15-2

Take a certain individual who is very lustful. ^an10-75-16-1

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that lust ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-16-2

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an10-75-16-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-16-4

They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-16-5

Take a certain individual who is very lustful. ^an10-75-17-1

But they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that lust ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-17-2

And they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom. ^an10-75-17-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-17-4

They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-17-5

Judgmental people compare them … ^an10-75-18-1

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-18-2

Take a certain individual who is irritable. ^an10-75-19-1

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that anger ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-19-2

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an10-75-19-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-19-4

They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-19-5

Take a certain individual who is irritable. ^an10-75-20-1

But they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that anger ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-20-2

And they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom. ^an10-75-20-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-20-4

They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-20-5

Judgmental people compare them … ^an10-75-21-1

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-21-2

Take a certain individual who is restless. ^an10-75-22-1

And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that restlessness ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-22-2

And they’ve not listened or learned or penetrated theoretically or found even temporary freedom. ^an10-75-22-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-22-4

They’re going to a lower place, not a higher. ^an10-75-22-5

Take a certain individual who is restless. ^an10-75-23-1

But they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where that restlessness ceases without remainder. ^an10-75-23-2

And they’ve listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom. ^an10-75-23-3

When their body breaks up, after death, they depart for a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-23-4

They’re going to a higher place, not a lower. ^an10-75-23-5

Judgmental people compare them, saying: ^an10-75-24-1

‘This one has just the same qualities as the other, so why is one worse and one better?’ ^an10-75-24-2

This will be for their lasting harm and suffering. ^an10-75-24-3

In this case the individual who is restless, but truly understands the freedom of heart … ^an10-75-25-1

and has listened and learned and penetrated theoretically and found at least temporary freedom ^an10-75-25-2

is better and finer than the other individual. ^an10-75-25-3

Why is that? ^an10-75-25-4

Because the stream of the teaching carries them along. ^an10-75-25-5

But who knows the difference between them except a Realized One? ^an10-75-25-6

So, Ānanda, don’t be judgmental about individuals. ^an10-75-25-7

Don’t pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-25-8

Those who pass judgment on individuals harm themselves. ^an10-75-25-9

I, or someone like me, may pass judgment on individuals. ^an10-75-25-10

Who is this laywoman Migasālā, a foolish incompetent aunty, with an aunty’s wit? And who is it that knows how to assess individual persons? ^an10-75-26-1

These ten individuals are found in the world. ^an10-75-26-2

If Isidatta had achieved Purāṇa’s level of ethical conduct, Purāṇa could not have even known Isidatta’s destination. ^an10-75-27-1

And if Purāṇa had achieved Isidatta’s level of wisdom, Isidatta could not have even known Purāṇa’s destination. ^an10-75-27-2

So both individuals were lacking in one respect.” ^an10-75-27-3

### Three Things (Numbered Discourses 10.76) ^an10-76-0

“Mendicants, if three things were not found, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha would not arise in the world, and the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One would not shine in the world. ^an10-76-1-1

What three? ^an10-76-1-2

Rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-1-3

If these three things were not found, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha would not arise in the world, and the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One would not shine in the world. ^an10-76-1-4

But since these three things are found, the Realized One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha arises in the world, and the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One shines in the world. ^an10-76-1-5

Without giving up three things you can’t give up rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-2-1

What three? ^an10-76-2-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-2-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-2-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-3-1

What three? ^an10-76-3-2

Substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-3-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-3-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-4-1

What three? ^an10-76-4-2

Irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-4-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-4-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-5-1

What three? ^an10-76-5-2

Unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and scattered mind. ^an10-76-5-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-5-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and scattered mind. ^an10-76-6-1

What three? ^an10-76-6-2

Not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-6-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and scattered mind. ^an10-76-6-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-7-1

What three? ^an10-76-7-2

Restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-7-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-7-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-8-1

What three? ^an10-76-8-2

Faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-8-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-8-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-9-1

What three? ^an10-76-9-2

Disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-9-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-9-4

Without giving up three things you can’t give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-10-1

What three? ^an10-76-10-2

Lack of conscience, imprudence, and negligence. ^an10-76-10-3

Without giving up these three things you can’t give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-10-4

Mendicants, someone who lacks conscience and prudence is negligent. ^an10-76-11-1

When you’re negligent you can’t give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-11-2

When you’ve got bad friends you can’t give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-11-3

When you’re lazy you can’t give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-11-4

When you’re unethical you can’t give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-11-5

When you’ve got a fault-finding mind you can’t give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and a scattered mind. ^an10-76-11-6

When your mind is scattered you can’t give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-11-7

When your mind is sluggish you can’t give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-11-8

When you have doubts you can’t give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-11-9

Without giving up greed, hate, and delusion you can’t give up rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-11-10

After giving up three things you can give up rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-12-1

What three? ^an10-76-12-2

Greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-12-3

After giving up these three things you can give up rebirth, old age, and death. ^an10-76-12-4

After giving up three things you can give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-13-1

What three? ^an10-76-13-2

Substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-13-3

After giving up these three things you can give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-13-4

After giving up three things you can give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-14-1

What three? ^an10-76-14-2

Irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-14-3

After giving up these three things you can give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-14-4

After giving up three things you can give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-15-1

What three? ^an10-76-15-2

Unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and a scattered mind. ^an10-76-15-3

After giving up these three things you can give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-15-4

After giving up three things you can give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and scattered mind. ^an10-76-16-1

What three? ^an10-76-16-2

Not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-16-3

After giving up these three things you can give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and scattered mind. ^an10-76-16-4

After giving up three things you can give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-17-1

What three? ^an10-76-17-2

Restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-17-3

After giving up these three things you can give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-17-4

After giving up three things you can give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-18-1

What three? ^an10-76-18-2

Faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-18-3

After giving up these three things you can give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-18-4

After giving up three things you can give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-19-1

What three? ^an10-76-19-2

Disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-19-3

After giving up these three things you can give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-19-4

After giving up three things you can give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-20-1

What three? ^an10-76-20-2

Lack of conscience, imprudence, and negligence. ^an10-76-20-3

After giving up these three things you can give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-20-4

Mendicants, someone who has conscience and prudence is diligent. ^an10-76-21-1

When you’re diligent you can give up disregard, being hard to admonish, and having bad friends. ^an10-76-21-2

When you’ve got good friends you can give up faithlessness, uncharitableness, and laziness. ^an10-76-21-3

When you’re energetic you can give up restlessness, lack of restraint, and unethical conduct. ^an10-76-21-4

When you’re ethical you can give up not wanting to see the noble ones, not wanting to hear the teaching of the noble ones, and a fault-finding mind. ^an10-76-21-5

When you don’t have a fault-finding mind you can give up unmindfulness, lack of situational awareness, and a scattered mind. ^an10-76-21-6

When your mind isn’t scattered you can give up irrational application of mind, following a wrong path, and mental sluggishness. ^an10-76-21-7

When your mind isn’t sluggish you can give up substantialist view, doubt, and misapprehension of precepts and observances. ^an10-76-21-8

When you have no doubts you can give up greed, hate, and delusion. ^an10-76-21-9

After giving up greed, hate, and delusion you can give up rebirth, old age, and death.” ^an10-76-21-10

### A Crow (Numbered Discourses 10.77) ^an10-77-0

“Mendicants, a crow has ten bad qualities. ^an10-77-1-1

What ten? ^an10-77-1-2

They’re rude and impudent, gluttonous and voracious, cruel and pitiless, weak and raucous, unmindful and acquisitive. ^an10-77-1-3

A crow possesses these ten bad qualities. ^an10-77-1-4

In the same way, a bad mendicant possesses these ten bad qualities. ^an10-77-1-5

What ten? ^an10-77-1-6

They’re rude and impudent, gluttonous and voracious, cruel and pitiless, weak and raucous, unmindful and acquisitive. ^an10-77-1-7

A bad mendicant possesses these ten bad qualities.” ^an10-77-1-8

### Jains (Numbered Discourses 10.78) ^an10-78-0

“Mendicants, Jain ascetics have ten bad qualities. ^an10-78-1-1

What ten? ^an10-78-1-2

They’re faithless and ^an10-78-1-3

unethical, ^an10-78-1-4

without conscience ^an10-78-1-5

or prudence, ^an10-78-1-6

and devoted to untrue persons. ^an10-78-1-7

They glorify themselves and put others down. ^an10-78-1-8

They’re attached to their own views, holding them tight, and refusing to let go. ^an10-78-1-9

They’re deceptive, ^an10-78-1-10

with corrupt wishes ^an10-78-1-11

and bad friends. ^an10-78-1-12

Jain ascetics possess these ten bad qualities.” ^an10-78-1-13

### Grounds for Resentment (Numbered Discourses 10.79) ^an10-79-0

“Mendicants, there are ten grounds for resentment. ^an10-79-1-1

What ten? ^an10-79-1-2

Thinking: ‘They did wrong to me,’ you harbor resentment. ^an10-79-1-3

Thinking: ‘They are doing wrong to me’ … ^an10-79-1-4

‘They will do wrong to me’ … ^an10-79-1-5

‘They did wrong by someone I love’ … ^an10-79-1-6

‘They are doing wrong by someone I love’ … ^an10-79-1-7

‘They will do wrong by someone I love’ … ^an10-79-1-8

‘They helped someone I dislike’ … ^an10-79-1-9

‘They are helping someone I dislike’ … ^an10-79-1-10

Thinking: ‘They will help someone I dislike,’ you harbor resentment. ^an10-79-1-11

You get angry for no reason. ^an10-79-1-12

These are the ten grounds for resentment.” ^an10-79-1-13

### Getting Rid of Resentment (Numbered Discourses 10.80) ^an10-80-0

“Mendicants, there are these ten ways to get rid of resentment. ^an10-80-1-1

What ten? ^an10-80-1-2

Thinking: ‘They harmed me, but what can I possibly do?’ you get rid of resentment. ^an10-80-1-3

Thinking: ‘They are harming me …’ … ^an10-80-1-4

‘They will harm me …’ … ^an10-80-1-5

‘They harmed someone I love …’ … ^an10-80-1-6

‘They are harming someone I love …’ ^an10-80-1-7

‘They will harm someone I love …’ … They helped someone I dislike …’ … ^an10-80-1-8

‘They are helping someone I dislike …’ … ^an10-80-1-9

Thinking: ‘They will help someone I dislike, but what can I possibly do?’ you get rid of resentment. ^an10-80-1-10

And you don’t get angry for no reason. ^an10-80-1-11

These are the ten ways of getting rid of resentment.” ^an10-80-1-12

### With Bāhuna (Numbered Discourses 10.81) ^an10-81-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond. ^an10-81-1-1

Then Venerable Bāhuna went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-81-1-2

“Sir, how many things has the Realized One escaped from, so that he lives detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries?” ^an10-81-1-3

“Bāhuna, the Realized One has escaped from ten things, so that he lives detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries. ^an10-81-2-1

What ten? ^an10-81-2-2

Form … ^an10-81-2-3

feeling … ^an10-81-2-4

perception … ^an10-81-2-5

choices … ^an10-81-2-6

consciousness … ^an10-81-2-7

rebirth … ^an10-81-2-8

old age … ^an10-81-2-9

death … ^an10-81-2-10

suffering … ^an10-81-2-11

defilements … ^an10-81-2-12

Suppose there was a blue water lily, or a pink or white lotus. Though it sprouted and grew in the water, it would rise up above the water and stand with no water clinging to it. ^an10-81-2-13

In the same way, the Realized One has escaped from ten things, so that he lives detached, liberated, with mind free of boundaries.” ^an10-81-2-14

### With Ānanda (Numbered Discourses 10.82) ^an10-82-0

Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an10-82-1-1

“Ānanda, it is quite impossible for a faithless mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an10-82-2-1

It is quite impossible for a mendicant who is unethical … ^an10-82-3-1

unlearned … ^an10-82-4-1

hard to admonish … ^an10-82-5-1

with bad friends … ^an10-82-6-1

lazy … ^an10-82-7-1

unmindful … ^an10-82-8-1

lacking contentment … ^an10-82-9-1

of corrupt wishes … ^an10-82-10-1

of wrong view to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an10-82-11-1

It is quite impossible for a mendicant with these ten qualities to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an10-82-12-1

It is quite possible for a faithful mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an10-82-13-1

It is quite possible for a mendicant who is ethical … ^an10-82-14-1

a learned memorizer … ^an10-82-15-1

easy to admonish … ^an10-82-16-1

with good friends … ^an10-82-17-1

energetic … ^an10-82-18-1

mindful … ^an10-82-19-1

contented … ^an10-82-20-1

of few desires … ^an10-82-21-1

of right view to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an10-82-22-1

It is quite possible for a mendicant with these ten qualities to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training.” ^an10-82-23-1

### With Puṇṇiya (Numbered Discourses 10.83) ^an10-83-0

Then Venerable Puṇṇiya went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-83-1-1

“Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why sometimes the Realized One feels inspired to teach, and other times not?” ^an10-83-1-2

“Puṇṇiya, when a mendicant has faith but doesn’t approach, the Realized One doesn’t feel inspired to teach. ^an10-83-2-1

But when a mendicant has faith and approaches, the Realized One feels inspired to teach. ^an10-83-2-2

When a mendicant has faith and approaches, but doesn’t pay homage … ^an10-83-3-1

they pay homage, but don’t ask questions … ^an10-83-3-2

they ask questions, but don’t actively listen to the teaching … ^an10-83-3-3

they actively listen to the teaching, but don’t remember the teaching they’ve heard … ^an10-83-3-4

they remember the teaching they’ve heard, but don’t examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve remembered … ^an10-83-3-5

they examine the meaning of the teachings they’ve remembered, but, not having understood the meaning and the teaching, they don’t practice in line with the principle of the teaching … ^an10-83-3-6

they practice in line with the teaching, but they’re not a good speaker and do not enunciate well. Their voice is not polished, clear, articulate, and doesn’t express the meaning … ^an10-83-3-7

They’re a good speaker who enunciates well, but they don’t educate, encourage, fire up, and inspire their spiritual companions. The Realized One doesn’t feel inspired to teach. ^an10-83-3-8

But when a mendicant has faith, approaches, pays homage, asks questions, actively listens to the teachings, remembers the teachings, examines the meaning, practices in line with the principle of the teaching, has a good voice, and encourages their spiritual companions, ^an10-83-4-1

the Realized One feels inspired to teach. ^an10-83-4-2

When someone possesses these ten qualities, the Realized One feels totally inspired to teach.” ^an10-83-4-3

### Declaration (Numbered Discourses 10.84) ^an10-84-0

There Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the mendicants: ^an10-84-1-1

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-84-1-2

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-84-1-3

Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this: ^an10-84-1-4

“Take a mendicant who declares enlightenment: ^an10-84-2-1

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^an10-84-2-2

They’re pursued, pressed, and grilled by the Realized One, or by one of his disciples who is a meditator skilled in attainments, in the minds of others, and in encompassing another’s mind. ^an10-84-2-3

Grilled in this way they get stuck or lose their way. They fall to ruin and disaster. ^an10-84-2-4

The Realized One or one of his disciples encompasss their mind and investigates: ^an10-84-3-1

‘Why does this venerable declare enlightenment, saying: ^an10-84-3-2

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’?”’ ^an10-84-3-3

They understand: ^an10-84-4-1

‘This venerable gets irritable, ^an10-84-5-1

and often lives with a heart full of anger. ^an10-84-5-2

But being full of anger means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One. ^an10-84-5-3

This venerable is acrimonious … ^an10-84-6-1

prone to disdain … ^an10-84-7-1

contemptuous … ^an10-84-8-1

jealous … ^an10-84-9-1

stingy … ^an10-84-10-1

devious … ^an10-84-11-1

deceitful … ^an10-84-12-1

This venerable has corrupt wishes, ^an10-84-13-1

and often lives with a heart full of desire. ^an10-84-13-2

But being full of desire means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One. ^an10-84-13-3

When there is still more to be done, this venerable stopped half-way after achieving some trifling distinction. ^an10-84-14-1

But stopping half-way means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.’ ^an10-84-14-2

It is quite impossible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training without giving up these ten qualities. ^an10-84-15-1

It is quite possible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training after giving up these ten qualities.” ^an10-84-15-2

### A Boaster (Numbered Discourses 10.85) ^an10-85-0

At one time Venerable Mahācunda was staying in the land of the Cetīs at Sahajāti. ^an10-85-1-1

There he addressed the mendicants: ^an10-85-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-85-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-85-1-4

Venerable Mahācunda said this: ^an10-85-1-5

“Take a mendicant who boasts and brags about their achievements: ^an10-85-2-1

‘I enter and emerge from the first absorption, the second absorption, the third absorption, and the fourth absorption. And I enter and emerge from the dimensions of infinite space, infinite consciousness, nothingness, and neither perception nor non-perception. And I enter and emerge from the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^an10-85-2-2

They’re pursued, pressed, and grilled by the Realized One, or by one of his disciples who has the absorptions, and is skilled in attainments, in the minds of others, and in encompassing another’s mind. ^an10-85-3-1

Grilled in this way they get stuck or lose their way. They fall to ruin and disaster. ^an10-85-3-2

The Realized One or one of his disciples encompasses their mind and investigates: ^an10-85-4-1

‘Why does this venerable boast and brag about their achievements, saying, ^an10-85-4-2

“I enter and emerge from the first absorption … ^an10-85-4-3

and the cessation of perception and feeling.”’ ^an10-85-4-4

They understand, ^an10-85-5-1

‘For a long time this venerable’s deeds have been broken, tainted, spotty, and marred. Their deeds and behavior are inconsistent. ^an10-85-6-1

This venerable is unethical, ^an10-85-6-2

and unethical conduct means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One. ^an10-85-6-3

This venerable is unfaithful, ^an10-85-7-1

and lack of faith means decline … ^an10-85-7-2

This venerable is unlearned and unpracticed, ^an10-85-8-1

and lack of learning means decline … ^an10-85-8-2

This venerable is hard to admonish, ^an10-85-9-1

and being hard to admonish means decline … ^an10-85-9-2

This venerable has bad friends, ^an10-85-10-1

and bad friends mean decline … ^an10-85-10-2

This venerable is lazy, ^an10-85-11-1

and laziness means decline … ^an10-85-11-2

This venerable is unmindful, ^an10-85-12-1

and unmindfulness means decline … ^an10-85-12-2

This venerable is deceptive, ^an10-85-13-1

and deceitfulness means decline … ^an10-85-13-2

This venerable is burdensome, ^an10-85-14-1

and being burdensome means decline … ^an10-85-14-2

This venerable is witless, ^an10-85-15-1

and lack of wisdom means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.’ ^an10-85-15-2

Suppose one friend was to say to another: ^an10-85-16-1

‘My dear, when you need money for some payment, just ask me and I’ll give it.’ ^an10-85-16-2

Then when some payment falls due, that friend says to their friend: ‘I need some money, my dear. Give me some.’ ^an10-85-16-3

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-4

‘Well then, my dear, dig here.’ So they dig there, but don’t find anything. ^an10-85-16-5

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-6

‘You lied to me, my dear, you spoke hollow words when you told me to dig here.’ ^an10-85-16-7

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-8

‘My dear, I didn’t lie or speak hollow words. Well then, dig here.’ ^an10-85-16-9

So they dig there as well, but don’t find anything. ^an10-85-16-10

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-11

‘You lied to me, my dear, you spoke hollow words when you said dig here.’ ^an10-85-16-12

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-13

‘My dear, I didn’t lie or speak hollow words. Well then, dig here.’ ^an10-85-16-14

So they dig there as well, but don’t find anything. ^an10-85-16-15

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-16

‘You lied to me, my dear, you spoke hollow words when you said dig here.’ ^an10-85-16-17

They’d say: ^an10-85-16-18

‘My dear, I didn’t lie or speak hollow words. But I had gone mad, I was out of my mind.’ ^an10-85-16-19

In the same way, take a mendicant who boasts and brags about their achievements: ^an10-85-17-1

‘I enter and emerge from the first absorption … and the cessation of perception and feeling.’ ^an10-85-17-2

They’re pursued, pressed, and grilled by the Realized One, or by one of his disciples … ^an10-85-18-1

Grilled in this way they get stuck or lose their way. They fall to ruin and disaster. ^an10-85-18-2

The Realized One or one of his disciples encompasses their mind and investigates: ^an10-85-19-1

‘Why does this venerable boast and brag about their achievements, saying, ^an10-85-19-2

“I enter and emerge from the first absorption … ^an10-85-19-3

and the cessation of perception and feeling.”’ ^an10-85-19-4

They understand, ^an10-85-20-1

‘For a long time this venerable’s deeds have been broken, tainted, spotty, and marred. Their deeds and behavior are inconsistent. ^an10-85-21-1

This venerable is unethical, ^an10-85-21-2

and unethical conduct means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One. ^an10-85-21-3

This venerable is unfaithful … ^an10-85-22-1

unlearned and unpracticed … ^an10-85-23-1

hard to admonish … ^an10-85-24-1

with bad friends … ^an10-85-25-1

lazy … ^an10-85-26-1

unmindful … ^an10-85-27-1

deceptive … ^an10-85-28-1

burdensome … ^an10-85-29-1

This venerable is witless, ^an10-85-30-1

and lack of wisdom means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.’ ^an10-85-30-2

It is quite impossible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training without giving up these ten qualities. ^an10-85-31-1

It is quite possible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training after giving up these ten qualities.” ^an10-85-31-2

### Overestimation (Numbered Discourses 10.86) ^an10-86-0

At one time Venerable Mahākassapa was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. ^an10-86-1-1

There he addressed the mendicants: ^an10-86-1-2

“Reverends, mendicants!” ^an10-86-1-3

“Reverend,” they replied. ^an10-86-1-4

Venerable Mahākassapa said this: ^an10-86-1-5

“Take a mendicant who declares enlightenment: ^an10-86-2-1

‘I understand: “Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.”’ ^an10-86-2-2

They’re pursued, pressed, and grilled by the Realized One, or by one of his disciples who has the absorptions, and is skilled in attainments, in the minds of others, and in encompassing another’s mind. ^an10-86-2-3

Grilled in this way they get stuck or lose their way. They fall to ruin and disaster. ^an10-86-2-4

The Realized One or one of his disciples encompasses their mind and investigates: ^an10-86-3-1

‘Why does this venerable declare enlightenment, saying, ^an10-86-3-2

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’?”’ ^an10-86-3-3

They understand, ^an10-86-4-1

‘This venerable overestimates themselves and takes that to be the truth. They perceive that they’ve attained what they haven’t attained, done what they haven’t done, and achieved what they haven’t achieved. ^an10-86-5-1

And they declare enlightenment out of overestimation: ^an10-86-5-2

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’ ^an10-86-5-3

The Realized One or one of his disciples encompasses their mind and investigates: ^an10-86-6-1

‘Why does this venerable overestimate themselves and take that to be the truth? Why do they perceive that they’ve attained what they haven’t attained, done what they haven’t done, and achieved what they haven’t achieved? ^an10-86-6-2

And why do they declare enlightenment out of overestimation: ^an10-86-6-3

“I understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”’ ^an10-86-6-4

They understand, ^an10-86-7-1

‘This venerable is very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-86-8-1

Therefore this venerable overestimates themselves and takes that to be the truth. …’ ^an10-86-8-2

They understand, ^an10-86-9-1

‘This venerable is covetous, ^an10-86-10-1

and often lives with a heart full covetousness. ^an10-86-10-2

Being full of covetousness means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One. ^an10-86-10-3

This venerable has ill will … ^an10-86-11-1

dullness and drowsiness … ^an10-86-12-1

restlessness … ^an10-86-13-1

doubt … ^an10-86-14-1

This venerable relishes work. They love it and like to relish it … ^an10-86-15-1

This venerable relishes talk … ^an10-86-16-1

sleep … ^an10-86-17-1

company … ^an10-86-18-1

When there is still more to be done, this venerable stopped half-way after achieving some trifling distinction. ^an10-86-19-1

Stopping half-way means decline in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One.’ ^an10-86-19-2

It is quite impossible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training without giving up these ten qualities. ^an10-86-20-1

It is quite possible for a mendicant to achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training after giving up these ten qualities.” ^an10-86-20-2

### Disciplinary Issues (Numbered Discourses 10.87) ^an10-87-0

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants concerning the mendicant Kalandaka: ^an10-87-1-1

“Mendicants!” ^an10-87-1-2

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an10-87-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an10-87-1-4

“Firstly, a mendicant raises disciplinary issues and doesn’t praise the settlement of disciplinary issues. ^an10-87-2-1

This quality doesn’t conduce to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an10-87-2-2

Furthermore, a mendicant doesn’t want to train, and doesn’t praise taking up the training. … ^an10-87-3-1

Furthermore, a mendicant has corrupt wishes, and doesn’t praise getting rid of wishes. … ^an10-87-4-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is irritable, and doesn’t praise getting rid of anger. … ^an10-87-5-1

Furthermore, a mendicant denigrates others, and doesn’t praise getting rid of denigration. … ^an10-87-6-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is devious, and doesn’t praise getting rid of deviousness. … ^an10-87-7-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is deceitful, and doesn’t praise getting rid of deceitfulness. … ^an10-87-8-1

Furthermore, a mendicant doesn’t pay attention to the teachings, and doesn’t praise attending to the teachings. … ^an10-87-9-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is not in retreat, and doesn’t praise retreat. … ^an10-87-10-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is inhospitable to their spiritual companions, and doesn’t praise hospitality. ^an10-87-11-1

This quality doesn’t conduce to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an10-87-11-2

Even though a mendicant such as this might wish: ^an10-87-12-1

‘If only my spiritual companions would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me!’ Still they don’t honor, respect, esteem, and venerate them. ^an10-87-12-2

Why is that? ^an10-87-12-3

Because their sensible spiritual companions see that they haven’t given up those bad unskillful qualities. ^an10-87-12-4

Suppose a wild colt was to wish: ^an10-87-13-1

‘If only the humans would put me in a thoroughbred’s place, feed me a thoroughbred’s food, and give me a thoroughbred’s grooming.’ Still the humans wouldn’t put them in a thoroughbred’s place, feed them a thoroughbred’s food, or give them a thoroughbred’s grooming. ^an10-87-13-2

Why is that? ^an10-87-13-3

Because sensible humans see that they haven’t given up their tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints. ^an10-87-13-4

In the same way, even though a mendicant such as this might wish: ^an10-87-13-5

‘If only my spiritual companions would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me!’ Still they don’t honor, respect, esteem, and venerate them. ^an10-87-13-6

Why is that? ^an10-87-13-7

Because their sensible spiritual companions see that they haven’t given up those bad unskillful qualities. ^an10-87-13-8

Next, a mendicant doesn’t raise disciplinary issues and praises the settlement of disciplinary issues. ^an10-87-14-1

This quality conduces to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an10-87-14-2

Furthermore, a mendicant wants to train, and praises taking up the training. … ^an10-87-15-1

Furthermore, a mendicant has few desires, and praises getting rid of desires. … ^an10-87-16-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is not irritable, and praises getting rid of anger. … ^an10-87-17-1

Furthermore, a mendicant doesn’t denigrate others, and praises getting rid of denigration. … ^an10-87-18-1

Furthermore, a mendicant isn’t devious, and praises getting rid of deviousness. … ^an10-87-19-1

Furthermore, a mendicant isn’t deceitful, and praises getting rid of deceitfulness. … ^an10-87-20-1

Furthermore, a mendicant pays attention to the teachings, and praises attending to the teachings. … ^an10-87-21-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is in retreat, and praises retreat. … ^an10-87-22-1

Furthermore, a mendicant is hospitable to their spiritual companions, and praises hospitality. ^an10-87-23-1

This quality conduces to fondness, respect, esteem, harmony, and unity. ^an10-87-23-2

Even though a mendicant such as this might never wish: ^an10-87-24-1

‘If only my spiritual companions would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me!’ Still they honor, respect, esteem, and venerate them. ^an10-87-24-2

Why is that? ^an10-87-24-3

Because their sensible spiritual companions see that they’ve given up those bad unskillful qualities. ^an10-87-24-4

Suppose a fine thoroughbred never wished: ^an10-87-25-1

‘If only the humans would put me in a thoroughbred’s place, feed me a thoroughbred’s food, and give me a thoroughbred’s grooming.’ Still the humans would put them in a thoroughbred’s place, feed them a thoroughbred’s food, and give them a thoroughbred’s grooming. ^an10-87-25-2

Why is that? ^an10-87-25-3

Because sensible humans see that they’ve given up their tricks, bluffs, ruses, and feints. ^an10-87-25-4

In the same way, even though a mendicant such as this might never wish: ^an10-87-26-1

‘If only my spiritual companions would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me!’ Still they honor, respect, esteem, and venerate them. ^an10-87-26-2

Why is that? ^an10-87-26-3

Because their sensible spiritual companions see that they’ve given up those bad unskillful qualities.” ^an10-87-26-4

### An Abuser (Numbered Discourses 10.88) ^an10-88-0

“Mendicants, any mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, denouncing the noble ones, will, without a doubt, fall into one or other of these ten disasters. ^an10-88-1-1

What ten? ^an10-88-1-2

They don’t achieve the unachieved. What they have achieved falls away. They don’t refine their good qualities. They overestimate their good qualities, or lead the spiritual life dissatisfied, or commit a corrupt offense, or contract a severe illness, or go mad and lose their mind. They feel lost when they die. And when their body breaks up, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. ^an10-88-1-3

Any mendicant who abuses and insults their spiritual companions, denouncing the noble ones, will, without a doubt, fall into one or other of these ten disasters.” ^an10-88-1-4

### With Kokālika (Numbered Discourses 10.89) ^an10-89-0

Then the mendicant Kokālika went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an10-89-1-1

“Sir, Sāriputta and Moggallāna have corrupt wishes. They’ve fallen under the sway of corrupt wishes.” ^an10-89-1-2

“Don’t say that, Kokālika! Don’t say that, Kokālika! ^an10-89-1-3

Have confidence in Sāriputta and Moggallāna, ^an10-89-1-4

they’re good monks.” ^an10-89-1-5

For a second time Kokālika said to the Buddha, ^an10-89-2-1

“Despite my faith and trust in the Buddha, Sāriputta and Moggallāna have corrupt wishes. They’ve fallen under the sway of corrupt wishes.” ^an10-89-2-2

“Don’t say that, Kokālika! Don’t say that, Kokālika! ^an10-89-2-3

Have confidence in Sāriputta and Moggallāna, ^an10-89-2-4

they’re good monks.” ^an10-89-2-5

For a third time Kokālika said to the Buddha, ^an10-89-3-1

“Despite my faith and trust in the Buddha, Sāriputta and Moggallāna have corrupt wishes. They’ve fallen under the sway of corrupt wishes.” ^an10-89-3-2

“Don’t say that, Kokālika! Don’t say that, Kokālika! ^an10-89-3-3

Have confidence in Sāriputta and Moggallāna, ^an10-89-3-4

they’re good monks.” ^an10-89-3-5

Then Kokālika rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving. ^an10-89-4-1

Not long after he left his body erupted with boils the size of mustard seeds. ^an10-89-4-2

The boils grew to the size of mung beans, then chickpeas, then jujube seeds, then jujubes, then myrobalans, then unripe wood-apples, then ripe wood-apples. Finally they burst open, and pus and blood oozed out. ^an10-89-4-3

He just laid down on banana leaves like a poisoned fish. ^an10-89-4-4

Then Tudu the independent divinity went to Kokālika, and standing in the air he said to him, ^an10-89-5-1

“Kokālika, have confidence in Sāriputta and Moggallāna, ^an10-89-5-2

they’re good monks.” ^an10-89-5-3

“Who are you, reverend?” ^an10-89-5-4

“I am Tudu the independent divinity.” ^an10-89-5-5

“Didn’t the Buddha declare you a non-returner? So what then are you doing back here? ^an10-89-5-6

See how far you have strayed!” ^an10-89-5-7

Then Tudu addressed Kokālika in verse: ^an10-89-6-1

“A person is born ^an10-89-7-1

with an axe in their mouth. ^an10-89-7-2

A fool cuts themselves with it ^an10-89-7-3

when they say bad words. ^an10-89-7-4

When you praise someone worthy of criticism, ^an10-89-8-1

or criticize someone worthy of praise, ^an10-89-8-2

you choose a losing hand with your own mouth: ^an10-89-8-3

you’ll never find happiness that way. ^an10-89-8-4

A losing hand at dice is a trivial thing, ^an10-89-9-1

if all you lose is your money ^an10-89-9-2

and all you own, even yourself. ^an10-89-9-3

What’s a really terrible hand ^an10-89-9-4

is to hate the holy ones. ^an10-89-9-5

For a hundred thousand times a hundred million ^an10-89-10-1

times five hundred and thirty-six times <j>a thousand times ten million years ^an10-89-10-2

a slanderer of noble ones goes to hell, ^an10-89-10-3

having aimed bad words and thoughts at them.” ^an10-89-10-4

Then the mendicant Kokālika died of that illness. ^an10-89-11-1

He was reborn in the Pink Lotus hell because of his resentment for Sāriputta and Moggallāna. ^an10-89-11-2

Then, late at night, the glorious divinity Sahampati, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side, and said to him, ^an10-89-12-1

“Sir, the mendicant Kokālika has passed away. ^an10-89-12-2

He was reborn in the pink lotus hell because of his resentment for Sāriputta and Moggallāna.” ^an10-89-12-3

That’s what the divinity Sahampati said. ^an10-89-12-4

Then he bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right side, before vanishing right there. ^an10-89-12-5

Then, when the night had passed, the Buddha told the mendicants all that had happened. ^an10-89-13-1

When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha, ^an10-89-14-1

“Sir, how long is the lifespan in the Pink Lotus hell?” ^an10-89-14-2

“It’s long, mendicant. ^an10-89-14-3

It’s not easy to calculate ^an10-89-14-4

how many years, how many hundreds or thousands or hundreds of thousands of years it lasts.” ^an10-89-14-5

“But sir, is it possible to give a simile?” ^an10-89-15-1

“It’s possible,” said the Buddha. ^an10-89-15-2

“Suppose there was a Kosalan load of twenty packs of sesame seed. And at the end of every hundred years someone would remove a single seed from it. ^an10-89-15-3

By this means the Kosalan load of twenty packs of sesame seed would run out faster than a single lifetime in the Abbuda hell. ^an10-89-15-4

Now, twenty lifetimes in the Abbuda hell equal one lifetime in the Nirabbuda hell. ^an10-89-15-5

Twenty lifetimes in the Nirabbuda hell equal one lifetime in the Ababa hell. ^an10-89-15-6

Twenty lifetimes in the Ababa hell equal one lifetime in the Aṭaṭa hell. ^an10-89-15-7

Twenty lifetimes in the Aṭaṭa hell equal one lifetime in the Ahaha hell. ^an10-89-15-8

Twenty lifetimes in the Ahaha hell equal one lifetime in the Yellow Lotus hell. ^an10-89-15-9

Twenty lifetimes in the Yellow Lotus hell equal one lifetime in the Fragrant Water Lily hell. ^an10-89-15-10

Twenty lifetimes in the Fragrant Water Lily hell equal one lifetime in the Blue Water Lily hell. ^an10-89-15-11

Twenty lifetimes in the Blue Water Lily hell equal one lifetime in the White Lotus hell. ^an10-89-15-12

Twenty lifetimes in the White Lotus hell equal one lifetime in the Pink Lotus hell. ^an10-89-15-13

The mendicant Kokālika has been reborn in the Pink Lotus hell because of his resentment for Sāriputta and Moggallāna.” ^an10-89-15-14

That is what the Buddha said. ^an10-89-15-15

Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say: ^an10-89-15-16

“A person is born ^an10-89-16-1

with an axe in their mouth. ^an10-89-16-2

A fool cuts themselves with it ^an10-89-16-3

when they say bad words. ^an10-89-16-4

When you praise someone worthy of criticism, ^an10-89-17-1

or criticize someone worthy of praise, ^an10-89-17-2

you choose a losing hand with your own mouth: ^an10-89-17-3

you’ll never find happiness that way. ^an10-89-17-4

A losing hand at dice is a trivial thing, ^an10-89-18-1

if all you lose is your money ^an10-89-18-2

and all you own, even yourself. ^an10-89-18-3

What’s a really terrible hand ^an10-89-18-4

is to hate the holy ones. ^an10-89-18-5

For a hundred thousand times a hundred million, ^an10-89-19-1

times five hundred and thirty-six times <j>a thousand times ten million years ^an10-89-19-2

a slanderer of noble ones goes to hell, ^an10-89-19-3

having aimed bad words and thoughts at them.” ^an10-89-19-4

### The Powers of One Who has Ended Defilements (Numbered Discourses 10.90) ^an10-90-0

Then Venerable Sāriputta went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an10-90-1-1

“Sāriputta, how many powers does a mendicant who has ended the defilements have that qualify them to claim: ^an10-90-1-2

‘My defilements have ended.’” ^an10-90-1-3

“Sir, a mendicant who has ended the defilements has ten powers that qualify them to claim: ^an10-90-2-1

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-2-2

What ten? ^an10-90-2-3

Firstly, a mendicant with defilements ended has clearly seen with right wisdom all conditions as truly impermanent. ^an10-90-2-4

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an10-90-2-5

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-2-6

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has clearly seen with right wisdom that sensual pleasures are truly like a pit of glowing coals. ^an10-90-3-1

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an10-90-3-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-3-3

Furthermore, the mind of a mendicant with defilements ended slants, slopes, and inclines to seclusion. They’re withdrawn, loving renunciation, and have totally eliminated defiling influences. ^an10-90-4-1

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an10-90-4-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-4-3

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has well developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an10-90-5-1
