This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an10-90-5-2

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-5-3

Furthermore, a mendicant with defilements ended has well developed the four right efforts. … ^an10-90-6-1

the four bases of psychic power … ^an10-90-6-2

the five faculties … ^an10-90-6-3

the five powers … ^an10-90-6-4

the seven awakening factors … ^an10-90-6-5

the noble eightfold path. ^an10-90-6-6

This is a power that a mendicant who has ended the defilements relies on to claim: ^an10-90-6-7

‘My defilements have ended.’ ^an10-90-6-8

A mendicant who has ended the defilements possesses these ten powers that qualify them to claim: ^an10-90-7-1

‘My defilements have ended.’” ^an10-90-7-2

### Pleasure Seekers (Numbered Discourses 10.91) ^an10-91-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-91-1-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the Buddha said to the householder Anāthapiṇḍika: ^an10-91-1-2

“These ten pleasure seekers are found in the world. ^an10-91-2-1

What ten? ^an10-91-2-2

First, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. ^an10-91-2-3

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. ^an10-91-2-4

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. ^an10-91-3-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, but don’t share it and make merit. ^an10-91-3-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. ^an10-91-4-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^an10-91-4-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^an10-91-5-1

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. ^an10-91-5-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^an10-91-6-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, but don’t share it and make merit. ^an10-91-6-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive. ^an10-91-7-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^an10-91-7-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^an10-91-8-1

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. ^an10-91-8-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^an10-91-9-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, but don’t share it and make merit. ^an10-91-9-2

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^an10-91-10-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^an10-91-10-2

But they enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. ^an10-91-10-3

Next, a pleasure seeker seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. ^an10-91-11-1

They make themselves happy and pleased, and they share it and make merit. ^an10-91-11-2

And they enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. ^an10-91-11-3

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. They may be criticized on three grounds. ^an10-91-12-1

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^an10-91-12-2

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for criticism. ^an10-91-12-3

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the third ground for criticism. ^an10-91-12-4

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on these three grounds. ^an10-91-12-5

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. They may be criticized on two grounds, and praised on one. ^an10-91-13-1

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^an10-91-13-2

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the one ground for praise. ^an10-91-13-3

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^an10-91-13-4

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on these two grounds, and praised on this one. ^an10-91-13-5

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using illegitimate, coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. They may be criticized on one ground, and praised on two. ^an10-91-14-1

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the one ground for criticism. ^an10-91-14-2

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-14-3

They share it and make merit. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-14-4

This pleasure seeker may be criticized on this one ground, and praised on these two. ^an10-91-14-5

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. They may be praised on one ground, and criticized on three. ^an10-91-15-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the one ground for praise. ^an10-91-15-2

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^an10-91-15-3

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for criticism. ^an10-91-15-4

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the third ground for criticism. ^an10-91-15-5

This pleasure seeker may be praised on this one ground, and criticized on these three. ^an10-91-15-6

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. They may be praised on two grounds, and criticized on two. ^an10-91-16-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-16-2

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the first ground for criticism. ^an10-91-16-3

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-16-4

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^an10-91-16-5

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these two grounds, and criticized on these two. ^an10-91-16-6

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using means both legitimate and illegitimate, and coercive and non-coercive, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. They may be praised on three grounds, and criticized on one. ^an10-91-17-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-17-2

They seek for wealth using illegitimate, coercive means. This is the one ground for criticism. ^an10-91-17-3

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-17-4

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^an10-91-17-5

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these three grounds, and criticized on this one. ^an10-91-17-6

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who doesn’t make themselves happy and pleased, nor share it and make merit. They may be praised on one ground and criticized on two. ^an10-91-18-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the one ground for praise. ^an10-91-18-2

They don’t make themselves happy and pleased. This is the first ground for criticism. ^an10-91-18-3

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the second ground for criticism. ^an10-91-18-4

This pleasure seeker may be praised on this one ground, and criticized on these two. ^an10-91-18-5

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, but doesn’t share it and make merit. They may be praised on two grounds and criticized on one. ^an10-91-19-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-19-2

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-19-3

They don’t share it and make merit. This is the one ground for criticism. ^an10-91-19-4

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these two grounds, and criticized on this one. ^an10-91-19-5

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. But they enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. They may be praised on three grounds and criticized on one. ^an10-91-20-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-20-2

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-20-3

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^an10-91-20-4

They enjoy that wealth tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, and not understanding the escape. This is the one ground for criticism. ^an10-91-20-5

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these three grounds, and criticized on this one. ^an10-91-20-6

Now, consider the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, and who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit. And they enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. They may be praised on four grounds. ^an10-91-21-1

They seek for wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means. This is the first ground for praise. ^an10-91-21-2

They make themselves happy and pleased. This is the second ground for praise. ^an10-91-21-3

They share it and make merit. This is the third ground for praise. ^an10-91-21-4

They enjoy that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape. This is the fourth ground for praise. ^an10-91-21-5

This pleasure seeker may be praised on these four grounds. ^an10-91-21-6

These are the ten pleasure seekers found in the world. ^an10-91-22-1

The pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit, and who uses that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the ten. ^an10-91-22-2

From a cow comes milk, from milk comes curds, from curds come butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream of ghee. And the cream of ghee is said to be the best of these. ^an10-91-22-3

In the same way, the pleasure seeker who seeks wealth using legitimate, non-coercive means, who makes themselves happy and pleased, and shares it and makes merit, and who uses that wealth untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape is the foremost, best, chief, highest, and finest of the ten.” ^an10-91-23-1

### Dangers (Numbered Discourses 10.92) ^an10-92-0

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him: ^an10-92-1-1

“Householder, when a noble disciple has quelled five fears and enmities, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and penetrated the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an10-92-2-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’ ^an10-92-2-2

What are the five fears and enmities they have quelled? ^an10-92-3-1

Anyone who kills living creatures brims with fear and enmity both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from killing living creatures does not brim with fear and enmity either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an10-92-3-2

So that fear and enmity is quelled for anyone who refrains from killing living creatures. ^an10-92-3-3

Anyone who steals … ^an10-92-4-1

Anyone who commits sexual misconduct … ^an10-92-4-2

Anyone who lies … ^an10-92-4-3

Anyone who consumes intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates dangers and threats both in this life and in lives to come, and experiences mental pain and sadness. Anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor creates no dangers and threats either in this life or in lives to come, and doesn’t experience mental pain and sadness. ^an10-92-4-4

So that danger and threat is quelled for anyone who refrains from intoxicants of beer, wine, and liquor. ^an10-92-4-5

These are the five fears and enmities they have quelled. ^an10-92-4-6

What are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess? ^an10-92-5-1

It’s when a noble disciple has experiential confidence in the Buddha: ^an10-92-5-2

‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’ ^an10-92-5-3

They have experiential confidence in the teaching: ^an10-92-5-4

‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’ ^an10-92-5-5

They have experiential confidence in the Saṅgha: ^an10-92-5-6

‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’ ^an10-92-5-7

And a noble disciple’s ethical conduct is loved by the noble ones, unbroken, impeccable, spotless, and unmarred, liberating, praised by sensible people, not mistaken, and leading to immersion. ^an10-92-5-8

These are the four factors of stream-entry that they possess. ^an10-92-5-9

And what is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom? ^an10-92-6-1

It’s when a noble disciple reflects: ^an10-92-6-2

‘When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises. ^an10-92-6-3

When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is: ^an10-92-6-4

Ignorance is a requirement for choices. ^an10-92-6-5

Choices are a requirement for consciousness. ^an10-92-6-6

Consciousness is a requirement for name and form. ^an10-92-6-7

Name and form are requirements for the six sense fields. ^an10-92-6-8

The six sense fields are requirements for contact. ^an10-92-6-9

Contact is a requirement for feeling. ^an10-92-6-10

Feeling is a requirement for craving. ^an10-92-6-11

Craving is a requirement for grasping. ^an10-92-6-12

Grasping is a requirement for continued existence. ^an10-92-6-13

Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth. ^an10-92-6-14

Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be. ^an10-92-6-15

That is how this entire mass of suffering originates. ^an10-92-6-16

When ignorance fades away and ceases no residue left behind, choices cease. When choices cease, consciousness ceases. When consciousness ceases, name and form cease. When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease. When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases. When contact ceases, feeling ceases. When feeling ceases, craving ceases. When craving ceases, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases. When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases. When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease. ^an10-92-6-17

That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’ ^an10-92-6-18

This is the noble system that they have clearly seen and penetrated with wisdom. ^an10-92-6-19

When a noble disciple has quelled five dangers and threats, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and has clearly seen and comprehended the noble system with wisdom, they may, if they wish, declare of themselves: ^an10-92-7-1

‘I’ve finished with rebirth in hell, the animal realm, and the ghost realm. I’ve finished with all places of loss, bad places, the underworld. I am a stream-enterer! I’m not liable to be reborn in the underworld, and am assured, destined for awakening.’” ^an10-92-7-2

### What is Your View? (Numbered Discourses 10.93) ^an10-93-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. ^an10-93-1-1

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika left Sāvatthī in the middle of the day to see the Buddha. ^an10-93-1-2

Then it occurred to him, ^an10-93-1-3

“It’s the wrong time to see the Buddha, ^an10-93-1-4

as he’s in retreat. ^an10-93-1-5

And it’s the wrong time to see the esteemed mendicants, ^an10-93-1-6

as they’re in retreat. ^an10-93-1-7

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an10-93-1-8

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions. ^an10-93-2-1

Now at that time, the wanderers of other religions had come together, making an uproar, a colossal racket as they sat and talked about all kinds of low topics. ^an10-93-2-2

They saw Anāthapiṇḍika coming off in the distance, ^an10-93-2-3

and stopped each other, saying, ^an10-93-2-4

“Be quiet, good fellows, don’t make a sound. ^an10-93-2-5

The householder Anāthapiṇḍika, a disciple of the ascetic Gotama, is coming into our monastery. ^an10-93-2-6

He is included among the white-clothed lay disciples of the ascetic Gotama, who is residing in Sāvatthī. ^an10-93-2-7

Such venerables like the quiet, are educated to be quiet, and praise the quiet. ^an10-93-2-8

Hopefully if he sees that our assembly is quiet he’ll see fit to approach.” ^an10-93-2-9

Then those wanderers of other religions fell silent. ^an10-93-3-1

Then Anāthapiṇḍika went up to them, and exchanged greetings with those wanderers. ^an10-93-3-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The wanderers said to him, ^an10-93-3-3

“Tell us, householder, what is the view of the ascetic Gotama?” ^an10-93-3-4

“Sirs, I don’t know all his views.” ^an10-93-3-5

“Well then, since it seems you don’t know all the views of the ascetic Gotama, ^an10-93-4-1

tell us, what are the views of the mendicants?” ^an10-93-4-2

“Sirs, I don’t know all the mendicants’ views.” ^an10-93-4-3

“Well then, since it seems you don’t know all the views of the ascetic Gotama or of the mendicants, ^an10-93-5-1

tell us, householder, what is your view?” ^an10-93-5-2

“Sirs, it’s not hard for me to explain what my views are. ^an10-93-5-3

But please, let the venerables explain their own convictions first. Afterwards it won’t be hard for me to explain my views.” ^an10-93-5-4

When he said this, one of the wanderers said to him, ^an10-93-6-1

“The cosmos is eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-6-2

That’s my view, householder.” ^an10-93-6-3

Another wanderer said, ^an10-93-7-1

“The cosmos is not eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-7-2

That’s my view, householder.” ^an10-93-7-3

Another wanderer said, ^an10-93-8-1

“The cosmos is finite …” … ^an10-93-8-2

“The cosmos is infinite …” … ^an10-93-8-3

“The soul and the body are one and the same …” … ^an10-93-8-4

“The soul is one thing, the body another …” … ^an10-93-8-5

“A realized one still exists after death …” … ^an10-93-8-6

“A realized one no longer exists after death …” … ^an10-93-8-7

“A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death …” … ^an10-93-8-8

“A Realized One neither exists nor doesn’t exist after death. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-8-9

That’s my view, householder.” ^an10-93-8-10

When this was said, Anāthapiṇḍika said this, ^an10-93-9-1

“Sirs, regarding the venerable who said this: ^an10-93-9-2

‘The cosmos is eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-9-3

That’s my view, householder.’ ^an10-93-9-4

This view of his has either arisen from his own irrational application of mind, or is conditioned by what someone else says. ^an10-93-9-5

But that view is created, conditioned, chosen, dependently originated. ^an10-93-9-6

Whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-9-7

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-9-8

What he clings to and holds to is just suffering. ^an10-93-9-9

Regarding the venerable who said this: ^an10-93-10-1

‘The cosmos is not eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-10-2

That’s my view, householder.’ ^an10-93-10-3

This view of his has either arisen from his own irrational application of mind, or is conditioned by what someone else says. ^an10-93-10-4

But that view is created, conditioned, chosen, dependently originated. ^an10-93-10-5

Whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-10-6

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-10-7

What he clings to and holds to is just suffering. ^an10-93-10-8

Regarding the venerable who said this: ^an10-93-11-1

‘The cosmos is finite …’ … ^an10-93-11-2

‘The cosmos is infinite …’ … ^an10-93-11-3

‘The soul and the body are one and the same …’ … ^an10-93-11-4

‘The soul is one thing, the body another …’ … ^an10-93-11-5

‘A realized one still exists after death …’ … ^an10-93-11-6

‘A realized one no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-93-11-7

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-93-11-8

‘A Realized One neither exists nor doesn’t exist after death. This is the only truth, anything else is futile. ^an10-93-11-9

That’s my view, householder.’ ^an10-93-11-10

This view of his has either arisen from his own irrational application of mind, or is conditioned by what someone else says. ^an10-93-11-11

But that view is created, conditioned, chosen, dependently originated. ^an10-93-11-12

Whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-11-13

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-11-14

What he clings to and holds to is just suffering.” ^an10-93-11-15

When he said this the wanderers said to him, ^an10-93-12-1

“Householder, we’ve each explained our own convictions. ^an10-93-12-2

Tell us, householder, what is your view?” ^an10-93-12-3

“Sirs, whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-12-4

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-12-5

And what’s suffering is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self. ^an10-93-12-6

That’s my view, sirs.” ^an10-93-12-7

“Householder, whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-13-1

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-13-2

What you cling to and hold to is just suffering.” ^an10-93-13-3

“Sirs, whatever is created, conditioned, chosen, and dependently originated is impermanent. ^an10-93-14-1

And what’s impermanent is suffering. ^an10-93-14-2

And I’ve truly seen clearly with right wisdom that what’s suffering is not mine, I am not this, it’s not my self. ^an10-93-14-3

And I truly understand the escape beyond that.” ^an10-93-14-4

When this was said, those wanderers sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say. ^an10-93-15-1

Seeing this, Anāthapiṇḍika rose from his seat. He went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^an10-93-15-2

“Good, good, householder! ^an10-93-15-3

That’s how you should legitimately and completely refute those futile men from time to time.” ^an10-93-15-4

Then the Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired the householder Anāthapiṇḍika with a Dhamma talk, ^an10-93-16-1

after which Anāthapiṇḍika rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha before leaving. ^an10-93-16-2

Then, not long after Anāthapiṇḍika had left, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an10-93-17-1

“Mendicants, even a mendicant who has ordained for a hundred years in this teaching and training would legitimately and completely refute those wanderers of other religions just as the householder Anāthapiṇḍika did.” ^an10-93-17-2

### With Vajjiyamāhita (Numbered Discourses 10.94) ^an10-94-0

At one time the Buddha was staying near Campā on the banks of the Gaggarā Lotus Pond. ^an10-94-1-1

Then the householder Vajjiyamāhita left Sāvatthī in the middle of the day to see the Buddha. ^an10-94-1-2

Then it occurred to him, ^an10-94-1-3

“It’s the wrong time to see the Buddha, ^an10-94-1-4

as he’s in retreat. ^an10-94-1-5

And it’s the wrong time to see the esteemed mendicants, ^an10-94-1-6

as they’re in retreat. ^an10-94-1-7

Why don’t I visit the monastery of the wanderers of other religions?” ^an10-94-1-8

Then he went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions. ^an10-94-2-1

Now at that time, the wanderers of other religions had come together, making an uproar, a colossal racket as they sat and talked about all kinds of low topics. ^an10-94-2-2

They saw Vajjiyamāhita coming off in the distance, and stopped each other, saying, ^an10-94-3-1

“Be quiet, good fellows, don’t make a sound. ^an10-94-3-2

The householder Vajjiyamāhita, a disciple of the ascetic Gotama, is coming into our monastery. ^an10-94-3-3

He is included among the white-clothed lay disciples of the ascetic Gotama, who is residing near Campā. ^an10-94-3-4

Such venerables like the quiet, are educated to be quiet, and praise the quiet. ^an10-94-3-5

Hopefully if he sees that our assembly is quiet he’ll see fit to approach.” ^an10-94-3-6

Then those wanderers of other religions fell silent. ^an10-94-4-1

Then Vajjiyamāhita went up to them, and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there. ^an10-94-4-2

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The wanderers said to him: ^an10-94-4-3

“Is it really true, householder? Does the ascetic Gotama criticize all forms of mortification? Does he categorically condemn and denounce those fervent mortifiers who live rough?” ^an10-94-4-4

“No, sirs, the Buddha does not criticize all forms of mortification. Nor does he categorically condemn and denounce those fervent mortifiers who live rough. ^an10-94-4-5

The Buddha criticizes where it is due, and praises where it is due. ^an10-94-4-6

In doing so he is one who speaks after analyzing the question, ^an10-94-4-7

and is not one-sided on this point.” ^an10-94-4-8

When he said this, one of the wanderers said to him, ^an10-94-5-1

“Hold on, householder! That ascetic Gotama who you praise is an exterminator who refrains from making statements.” ^an10-94-5-2

“On this point, also, I reasonably respond to the venerables. ^an10-94-5-3

The Buddha has stated ‘This is skillful’ and ^an10-94-5-4

‘This is unskillful’. ^an10-94-5-5

So when it comes to what is skillful and unskillful the Buddha makes a statement. ^an10-94-5-6

He is not an exterminator who refrains from making statements.” ^an10-94-5-7

When this was said, those wanderers sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say. ^an10-94-6-1

Seeing this, Vajjiyamāhita rose from his seat. He went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, ^an10-94-6-2

and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed. ^an10-94-6-3

“Good, good, householder! ^an10-94-7-1

That’s how you should legitimately and completely refute those futile men from time to time. ^an10-94-7-2

Householder, I don’t say that all mortifications should be undergone. ^an10-94-7-3

But I don’t say that no mortifications should be undergone. ^an10-94-7-4

I don’t say that all observances should be undertaken. ^an10-94-7-5

But I don’t say that no observances should be undertaken. ^an10-94-7-6

I don’t say that all efforts should be tried. ^an10-94-7-7

But I don’t say that no efforts should be tried. ^an10-94-7-8

I don’t say that everything should be given up. ^an10-94-7-9

But I don’t say that nothing should be given up. ^an10-94-7-10

I don’t say that you should be liberated with all kinds of freedom. ^an10-94-7-11

But I don’t say that you should not be liberated with any kind of freedom. ^an10-94-7-12

When undergoing certain mortifications, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t undergo those mortifications. ^an10-94-8-1

When undergoing certain mortifications, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should undergo those mortifications. ^an10-94-8-2

When undertaking certain observances, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t undertake those observances. ^an10-94-9-1

When undertaking certain observances, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should undertake those observances. ^an10-94-9-2

When trying certain efforts, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t try those efforts. ^an10-94-10-1

When trying certain efforts, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should try those efforts. ^an10-94-10-2

When giving up certain things, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t give up those things. ^an10-94-11-1

When giving up certain things, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should give up those things. ^an10-94-11-2

When being liberated with certain kinds of freedom, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t be liberated with those kinds of freedom. ^an10-94-12-1

When being liberated with certain kinds of freedom, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should be liberated with those kinds of freedom.” ^an10-94-12-2

After Vajjiyamāhita had been educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired with a Dhamma talk by the Buddha, he rose from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha before leaving. ^an10-94-13-1

Then, not long after Vajjiyamāhita had left, the Buddha addressed the mendicants: ^an10-94-14-1

“Mendicants, even a mendicant who for a long time has had little dust in their eye in this teaching and training would legitimately and completely refute those wanderers of other religions just as the householder Vajjiyamāhita did.” ^an10-94-14-2

### With Uttiya (Numbered Discourses 10.95) ^an10-95-0

Then the wanderer Uttiya went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an10-95-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha, ^an10-95-1-2

“Worthy Gotama, is this right: ‘The cosmos is eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-95-1-3

“This has not been declared by me, Uttiya.” ^an10-95-1-4

“Then is this right: ‘The cosmos is not eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-95-2-1

“This has not been declared by me, Uttiya.” ^an10-95-2-2

“Then is this right: ‘The cosmos is finite …’ … ^an10-95-3-1

‘The cosmos is infinite …’ … ^an10-95-3-2

‘The soul and the body are one and the same …’ … ^an10-95-3-3

‘The soul is one thing, the body another …’ … ^an10-95-3-4

‘A realized one still exists after death …’ … ^an10-95-3-5

‘A realized one no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-95-3-6

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-95-3-7

‘A Realized One neither exists nor doesn’t exist after death. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-95-3-8

“This has not been declared by me, Uttiya.” ^an10-95-3-9

“When asked about all these points, worthy Gotama says that they have not been declared by him. ^an10-95-4-1

So what then has been declared by Mister Gotama?” ^an10-95-6-11

“Uttiya, I teach my disciples from my own insight in order to purify sentient beings, to get past sorrow and crying, to make an end of pain and sadness, to end the cycle of suffering, and to realize extinguishment.” ^an10-95-7-1

“But when the worthy Gotama teaches in this way, is the whole world saved, or half, or a third?” ^an10-95-8-1

But when he said this, the Buddha kept silent. ^an10-95-8-2

Then Venerable Ānanda thought, ^an10-95-9-1

“The wanderer Uttiya must not get the harmful misconception: ^an10-95-9-2

‘When the ascetic Gotama was asked this all-important question he falters without answering. He just can’t do it!’ ^an10-95-9-3

That would be for his lasting harm and suffering.” ^an10-95-9-4

Then Ānanda said to the wanderer Uttiya, ^an10-95-10-1

“Well then, Reverend Uttiya, I shall give you a simile. ^an10-95-10-2

For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said. ^an10-95-10-3

Suppose there was a king’s frontier citadel with fortified embankments, ramparts, and arches, and a single gate. ^an10-95-10-4

And it has a gatekeeper who is astute, competent, and clever. He keeps strangers out and lets known people in. ^an10-95-10-5

As he walks around the patrol path, he doesn’t see a hole or cleft in the wall, not even one big enough for a cat to slip out. ^an10-95-10-6

He doesn’t know how many creatures enter or leave the citadel. ^an10-95-10-7

But he does know that whatever sizable creatures enter or leave the citadel, all of them do so via this gate. ^an10-95-10-8

In the same way, it’s not the Realized One’s concern whether the whole world is saved by this, or half, or a third. ^an10-95-11-1

But the Realized One knows that whoever is saved from the world—whether in the past, the future, or the present—all have given up the five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. They have firmly established their mind in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. And they have truly developed the seven awakening factors. ^an10-95-11-2

That’s how they’re saved from the world, in the past, future, or present. ^an10-95-11-3

Uttiya, you were just asking the Buddha the same question as before in a different way. ^an10-95-11-4

That’s why he didn’t answer.” ^an10-95-11-5

### With Kokanada (Numbered Discourses 10.96) ^an10-96-0

At one time Venerable Ānanda was staying near Rājagaha in the Hot Springs Monastery. ^an10-96-1-1

Then Ānanda rose at the crack of dawn and went to the hot springs to bathe. ^an10-96-1-2

When he had bathed and emerged from the water he stood in one robe drying his limbs. ^an10-96-1-3

The wanderer Kokanada also rose at the crack of dawn and went to the hot springs to bathe. ^an10-96-1-4

He saw Ānanda coming off in the distance ^an10-96-2-1

and said to him, ^an10-96-2-2

“Who’s here, reverend?” ^an10-96-2-3

“I’m a mendicant, reverend.” ^an10-96-2-4

“Of which mendicants?” ^an10-96-3-1

“Of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan.” ^an10-96-3-2

“I’d like to ask the venerable about a certain point, if you’d take the time to answer.” ^an10-96-4-1

“Ask, reverend. When I’ve heard it I’ll know.” ^an10-96-4-2

“Is this your view: ‘The cosmos is eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-96-5-1

“That’s not my view, reverend.” ^an10-96-5-2

“Then is this your view: ‘The cosmos is not eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-96-6-1

“That’s not my view, reverend.” ^an10-96-6-2

“Then is this your view: ‘The cosmos is finite …’ … ^an10-96-7-1

‘The cosmos is infinite …’ … ^an10-96-7-2

‘The soul and the body are one and the same …’ … ^an10-96-7-3

‘The soul is one thing, the body another …’ … ^an10-96-7-4

‘A realized one still exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-7-5

‘A realized one no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-7-6

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-7-7

‘A Realized One neither exists nor doesn’t exist after death. This is the only truth, anything else is futile’?” ^an10-96-7-8

“That’s not my view, reverend.” ^an10-96-7-9

“Then, sir, do you neither know nor see?” ^an10-96-8-1

“That’s not so, reverend. ^an10-96-8-2

I do know and see.” ^an10-96-8-3

“When asked about all these points, you say that’s not your view. ^an10-96-9-1

Yet when asked whether you neither know nor see, ^an10-96-12-1

you say, ‘That’s not so, reverend. ^an10-96-12-2

I do know and see.’ ^an10-96-12-3

How then should we see the meaning of this statement?” ^an10-96-12-4

“‘The cosmos is eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile:’ that’s a misconception. ^an10-96-13-1

‘The cosmos is not eternal. This is the only truth, anything else is futile:’ that’s a misconception. ^an10-96-13-2

‘The cosmos is finite …’ … ^an10-96-13-3

‘The cosmos is infinite …’ … ^an10-96-13-4

‘The soul and the body are one and the same …’ … ^an10-96-13-5

‘The soul is one thing, the body another …’ … ^an10-96-13-6

‘A realized one still exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-13-7

‘A realized one no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-13-8

‘A realized one both still exists and no longer exists after death …’ … ^an10-96-13-9

‘A Realized One neither exists nor doesn’t exist after death. This is the only truth, anything else is futile:’ that’s a misconception. ^an10-96-13-10

I know and see the scope of convictions, the scope of grounds for views, fixation on views, obsession with views, the origin of views, and the uprooting of views. ^an10-96-14-1

Knowing and seeing thus, why should I say: ^an10-96-14-2

‘I neither know nor see?’ ^an10-96-14-3

I do know and see.” ^an10-96-14-4

“What is the venerable’s name? And how are you known among your spiritual companions?” ^an10-96-15-1

“Reverend, my name is Ānanda. ^an10-96-15-2

And that’s how I’m known among my spiritual companions.” ^an10-96-15-3

“Goodness! I had no idea I was consulting such a great tutor as ^an10-96-15-4

Venerable Ānanda! ^an10-96-15-5

If I had known who ^an10-96-15-6

you were, I wouldn’t have said so much. ^an10-96-15-7

May Venerable Ānanda please forgive me.” ^an10-96-15-8

### Worthy of Offerings Dedicated to the Gods (Numbered Discourses 10.97) ^an10-97-0

“Mendicants, a mendicant with ten qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world. ^an10-97-1-1

What ten? ^an10-97-2-1

It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-97-2-2

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-97-3-1

They have good friends, companions, and associates. ^an10-97-4-1

They have right view, possessing right perspective. ^an10-97-5-1

They wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. They control the body as far as the realm of divinity. ^an10-97-6-1

With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, they hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far. ^an10-97-7-1

They understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with their own mind. ^an10-97-8-1

They understand mind with greed as ‘mind with greed’, ^an10-97-8-2

and mind without greed as ‘mind without greed’. ^an10-97-8-3

They understand mind with hate … ^an10-97-8-4

mind without hate … ^an10-97-8-5

mind with delusion … ^an10-97-8-6

mind without delusion … ^an10-97-8-7

constricted mind … ^an10-97-8-8

scattered mind … ^an10-97-8-9

expansive mind … ^an10-97-8-10

unexpansive mind … ^an10-97-8-11

mind that is not supreme … ^an10-97-8-12

mind that is supreme … ^an10-97-8-13

mind immersed in samādhi … ^an10-97-8-14

mind not immersed in samādhi … ^an10-97-8-15

freed mind … ^an10-97-8-16

They understand unfreed mind as ‘unfreed mind’. ^an10-97-8-17

They recollect many kinds of past lives, that is, one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ Thus they recollect their many past lives, with features and details. ^an10-97-9-1

With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ‘These dear beings did bad things by way of body, speech, and mind. They denounced the noble ones; they had wrong view; and they chose to act out of that wrong view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell. These dear beings, however, did good things by way of body, speech, and mind. They never denounced the noble ones; they had right view; and they chose to act out of that right view. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’ And so, with clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, they see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place. They understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an10-97-10-1

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-97-11-1

A mendicant with these ten qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.” ^an10-97-12-1

### A Senior Mendicant (Numbered Discourses 10.98) ^an10-98-0

“Mendicants, a senior mendicant with ten qualities lives comfortably in whatever region they live. ^an10-98-1-1

What ten? ^an10-98-1-2

They are long standing and have long gone forth. ^an10-98-1-3

They’re ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken. ^an10-98-1-4

They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reinforcing them by recitation, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically. ^an10-98-1-5

Both monastic codes have been passed down to them in detail, well analyzed, well mastered, well evaluated in both the rules and accompanying material. ^an10-98-1-6

They’re skilled in the instigation and settlement of disciplinary issues. ^an10-98-1-7

They love the teachings and are a delight to converse with, being full of joy in the teaching and training. ^an10-98-1-8

They’re content with any kind of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an10-98-1-9

They look impressive when going out and coming back, and are well restrained when sitting in an inhabited area. ^an10-98-1-10

They get the four absorptions—blissful meditations in this life that belong to the higher mind—when they want, without trouble or difficulty. ^an10-98-1-11

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and they live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-98-1-12

A senior mendicant with these ten qualities lives comfortably in whatever region they live.” ^an10-98-1-13

### With Upāli (Numbered Discourses 10.99) ^an10-99-0

Then Venerable Upāli went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him, ^an10-99-1-1

“Sir, I wish to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.” ^an10-99-1-2

“Upāli, remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest are challenging. ^an10-99-2-1

It’s hard to maintain seclusion and hard to find joy in it. ^an10-99-2-2

Staying alone, the forests seem to rob the mind of a mendicant who isn’t immersed in samādhi. ^an10-99-2-3

If someone should say this, ^an10-99-2-4

‘Though I don’t have immersion, I’m going to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.’ You can expect that ^an10-99-2-5

they’ll sink down or float away. ^an10-99-2-6

Suppose there was a large lake, ^an10-99-3-1

and along comes a bull elephant that’s three or three and a half meters tall. ^an10-99-3-2

He’d think, ^an10-99-3-3

‘Why don’t I plunge into this lake and play around while washing my ears and back? ^an10-99-3-4

When I’ve bathed and drunk, I’ll emerge from the water and go wherever I want.’ ^an10-99-3-5

And that’s just what he does. ^an10-99-3-6

Why is that? ^an10-99-3-8

Because his large incarnation finds a footing in the depths. ^an10-99-3-9

Then along comes a rabbit or a cat. ^an10-99-4-1

They’d think, ^an10-99-4-2

‘What difference is there between me and a bull elephant? ^an10-99-4-3

Why don’t I plunge into this lake and play around while washing my ears and back? ^an10-99-4-4

When I’ve bathed and drunk, I’ll emerge from the water and go wherever I want.’ ^an10-99-4-5

They jump into the lake rashly, without thinking. ^an10-99-4-6

You can expect that ^an10-99-4-7

they’ll sink down or float away. ^an10-99-4-8

Why is that? ^an10-99-4-9

Because their little incarnation finds no footing in the depths. ^an10-99-4-10

If someone should say this, ^an10-99-4-11

‘Though I don’t have immersion, I’m going to frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest.’ You can expect that ^an10-99-4-12

they’ll sink down or float away. ^an10-99-4-13

Suppose there was a little baby boy playing in his own urine and feces. ^an10-99-5-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-5-2

Isn’t that a totally foolish game?” ^an10-99-5-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-5-4

“After some time that boy grows up and his faculties mature. ^an10-99-6-1

He accordingly plays childish games such as toy plows, tip-cat, somersaults, pinwheels, toy measures, toy carts, and toy bows. ^an10-99-6-2

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-6-3

Aren’t such games better than what he did before?” ^an10-99-6-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-6-5

“After some time that boy grows up and his faculties mature further. ^an10-99-7-1

He accordingly amuses himself, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation. ^an10-99-7-2

Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an10-99-7-3

Sounds known by the ear … ^an10-99-7-4

Smells known by the nose … ^an10-99-7-5

Tastes known by the tongue … ^an10-99-7-6

Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing. ^an10-99-7-7

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-7-8

Aren’t such games better than what he did before?” ^an10-99-7-9

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-7-10

“Now in this regard, Upāli, a Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed. ^an10-99-8-1

He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others. ^an10-99-8-2

He proclaims a teaching that’s good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. ^an10-99-8-3

A householder hears that teaching, or a householder’s child, or someone reborn in a good family. ^an10-99-9-1

They gain faith in the Realized One ^an10-99-9-2

and reflect, ^an10-99-9-3

‘Living in a house is cramped and dirty, but the life of one gone forth is wide open. ^an10-99-9-4

It’s not easy for someone living at home to lead the spiritual life utterly full and pure, like a polished shell. ^an10-99-9-5

Why don’t I shave off my hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness?’ ^an10-99-9-6

After some time they give up a large or small fortune, and a large or small family circle. They shave off hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness. ^an10-99-10-1

Once they’ve gone forth, they take up the training and livelihood of the mendicants. They give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They’re scrupulous and kind, living full of sympathy for all living beings. ^an10-99-11-1

They give up stealing. They take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. They keep themselves clean by not thieving. ^an10-99-12-1

They give up unchastity. They are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex. ^an10-99-13-1

They give up lying. They speak the truth and stick to the truth. They’re honest and dependable, and don’t trick the world with their words. ^an10-99-14-1

They give up backbiting. They don’t repeat in one place what they heard in another so as to divide people against each other. Instead, they reconcile those who are divided and support those who are united, delighting in harmony, loving harmony, speaking words that promote harmony. ^an10-99-15-1

They give up harsh speech. They speak in a way that’s mellow, pleasing to the ear, endearing, going to the heart, polite, likable and agreeable to the people. ^an10-99-16-1

They give up talking nonsense. Their words are timely, true, and meaningful, in line with the teaching and training. They say things at the right time which are valuable, reasonable, succinct, and beneficial. ^an10-99-17-1

They refrain from injuring plants and seeds. ^an10-99-18-1

They eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and food at the wrong time. ^an10-99-18-2

They refrain from seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music . ^an10-99-18-3

They refrain from attiring and adorning themselves with garlands, fragrance, and makeup. ^an10-99-18-4

They refrain from high and luxurious beds. ^an10-99-18-5

They refrain from receiving gold and currency, ^an10-99-18-6

raw grains, ^an10-99-18-7

raw meat, ^an10-99-18-8

women and girls, ^an10-99-18-9

male and female bondservants, ^an10-99-18-10

goats and sheep, ^an10-99-18-11

chickens and pigs, ^an10-99-18-12

elephants, cows, horses, and mares, ^an10-99-18-13

and fields and land. ^an10-99-18-14

They refrain from running errands and messages; ^an10-99-18-15

buying and selling; ^an10-99-18-16

falsifying weights, metals, or measures; ^an10-99-18-17

bribery, fraud, cheating, and duplicity; ^an10-99-18-18

mutilation, murder, abduction, banditry, plunder, and violence. ^an10-99-18-19

They’re content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. ^an10-99-19-1

Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things. ^an10-99-19-2

They’re like a bird: wherever it flies, wings are its only burden. ^an10-99-19-3

In the same way, a mendicant is content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things. ^an10-99-19-4

When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, they experience a blameless happiness inside themselves. ^an10-99-19-5

When they see a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an10-99-20-1

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it. ^an10-99-20-2

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an10-99-20-3

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an10-99-20-4

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an10-99-20-5

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an10-99-20-6

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an10-99-20-7

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^an10-99-20-8

When they have this noble sense restraint, they experience an unsullied bliss inside themselves. ^an10-99-20-9

They act with situational awareness when going out and coming back; when looking ahead and aside; when bending and extending the limbs; when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes; when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; when urinating and defecating; when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent. ^an10-99-21-1

When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, this noble sense restraint, and this noble mindfulness and situational awareness, ^an10-99-22-1

they frequent a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw. ^an10-99-22-2

Gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, they sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and bring mindfulness to the present. ^an10-99-22-3

Giving up covetousness for the world, they meditate with a heart rid of covetousness, cleansing the mind of covetousness. ^an10-99-23-1

Giving up ill will and malevolence, they meditate with a mind rid of ill will, full of sympathy for all living beings, cleansing the mind of ill will. ^an10-99-23-2

Giving up dullness and drowsiness, they meditate with a mind rid of dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, mindful and aware, cleansing the mind of dullness and drowsiness. ^an10-99-23-3

Giving up restlessness and remorse, they meditate without restlessness, their mind peaceful inside, cleansing the mind of restlessness and remorse. ^an10-99-23-4

Giving up doubt, they meditate having gone beyond doubt, not undecided about skillful qualities, cleansing the mind of doubt. ^an10-99-23-5

They give up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom. ^an10-99-24-1

Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, they enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an10-99-24-2

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-24-3

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-24-4

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-24-5

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. But so far they haven’t achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-25-1

Furthermore, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected. ^an10-99-26-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-26-2

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-26-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-26-4

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. But so far they haven’t achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-27-1

Furthermore, with the fading away of rapture, a mendicant enters and remains in the third absorption. They meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’ ^an10-99-28-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-28-2

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-28-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-28-4

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. But so far they haven’t achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-29-1

Furthermore, with the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, a mendicant enters and remains in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness. …” … ^an10-99-30-1

“Furthermore, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, a mendicant enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. ^an10-99-31-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-31-2

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-31-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-31-4

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. But so far they haven’t achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-32-1

Furthermore, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, a mendicant enters and remains in the dimension of infinite consciousness. …” … ^an10-99-33-1

“Going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness. …” … ^an10-99-34-1

“Going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, aware that ‘this is peaceful, this is sublime’, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. ^an10-99-35-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-35-2

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-35-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-35-4

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. But so far they haven’t achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-36-1

Furthermore, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling. And, having seen with wisdom, their defilements come to an end. ^an10-99-37-1

What do you think, Upāli? ^an10-99-37-2

Isn’t this state better than what they had before?” ^an10-99-37-3

“Yes, sir.” ^an10-99-37-4

“When my disciples see this quality inside themselves they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest. And they have achieved their heart’s goal. ^an10-99-38-1

Come on, Upāli, stay with the Saṅgha. ^an10-99-38-2

If you stay with the Saṅgha you’ll be comfortable.” ^an10-99-38-3

### Cannot (Numbered Discourses 10.100) ^an10-100-0

“Mendicants, without giving up ten things you can’t realize perfection. ^an10-100-1-1

What ten? ^an10-100-1-2

Greed, hate, delusion, anger, acrimony, disdain, contempt, jealousy, stinginess, and conceit. ^an10-100-1-3

Without giving up these ten things you can’t realize perfection. ^an10-100-1-4

After giving up ten things you can realize perfection. ^an10-100-2-1

What ten? ^an10-100-2-2

Greed, hate, delusion, anger, acrimony, disdain, contempt, jealousy, stinginess, and conceit. ^an10-100-2-3

After giving up these ten things you can realize perfection.” ^an10-100-2-4

### Perceptions for Ascetics (Numbered Discourses 10.101) ^an10-101-0

“Mendicants, when these three perceptions for ascetics are developed and cultivated they fulfill seven things. ^an10-101-1-1

What three? ^an10-101-1-2

‘I have secured freedom from class.’ ‘My livelihood is tied up with others.’ ‘My behavior should be different.’ ^an10-101-1-3

When these three perceptions for ascetics are developed and cultivated they fulfill seven things. ^an10-101-1-4

What seven? ^an10-101-2-1

Their deeds and behavior are always consistent with the precepts. They’re content, kind-hearted, and humble. They want to train. They use the necessities of life after reflecting on their purpose. They’re energetic. ^an10-101-2-2

When those three perceptions for ascetics are developed and cultivated they fulfill these seven things.” ^an10-101-2-3

### Awakening Factors (Numbered Discourses 10.102) ^an10-102-0

“Mendicants, when the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they fulfill three knowledges. ^an10-102-1-1

What seven? ^an10-102-1-2

The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity. ^an10-102-1-3

When these seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they fulfill three knowledges. ^an10-102-1-4

What three? ^an10-102-1-5

It’s when a mendicant recollects their many kinds of past lives. ^an10-102-1-6

That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details. ^an10-102-1-7

With clairvoyance that is purified and surpasses the human, they understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds. ^an10-102-1-8

They realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with their own insight due to the ending of defilements. ^an10-102-1-9

When those seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated they fulfill these three knowledges.” ^an10-102-1-10

### The Wrong Way (Numbered Discourses 10.103) ^an10-103-0

“Mendicants, relying on the wrong way leads to failure, not success. ^an10-103-1-1

And how does relying on the wrong way lead to failure, not success? ^an10-103-1-2

Wrong view produces wrong purpose. Wrong purpose produces wrong speech. Wrong speech produces wrong action. Wrong action produces wrong livelihood. Wrong livelihood produces wrong effort. Wrong effort produces wrong mindfulness. Wrong mindfulness produces wrong immersion. Wrong immersion produces wrong knowledge. Wrong knowledge produces wrong freedom. ^an10-103-1-3

That’s how relying on the wrong way leads to failure, not success. ^an10-103-1-4

Relying on the right way leads to success, not failure. ^an10-103-2-1

And how does relying on the right way lead to success, not failure? ^an10-103-2-2

Right view produces right purpose. Right purpose produces right speech. Right speech produces right action. Right action produces right livelihood. Right livelihood produces right effort. Right effort produces right mindfulness. Right mindfulness produces right immersion. Right immersion produces right knowledge. Right knowledge produces right freedom. ^an10-103-2-3

That’s how relying on the right way leads to success, not failure.” ^an10-103-2-4

### A Seed (Numbered Discourses 10.104) ^an10-104-0

“Mendicants, consider an individual who has wrong view, purpose, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, immersion, knowledge, and freedom. ^an10-104-1-1

Whatever bodily, verbal, or mental deeds they undertake in line with that view, their intentions, aims, wishes, and choices all lead to what is unlikable, undesirable, disagreeable, harmful, and suffering. ^an10-104-1-2

Why is that? ^an10-104-1-3

Because their view is bad. ^an10-104-1-4

Suppose a seed of neem, angled gourd, or bitter gourd was planted in moist earth. Whatever nutrients it takes up from the earth and water would lead to its bitter, acerbic, and unpleasant taste. ^an10-104-2-1

Why is that? ^an10-104-2-2

Because the seed is bad. ^an10-104-2-3

In the same way, consider an individual who has wrong view, purpose, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, immersion, knowledge, and freedom. ^an10-104-2-4

Whatever bodily, verbal, or mental deeds they undertake in line with that view, their intentions, aims, wishes, and choices all lead to what is unlikable, undesirable, disagreeable, harmful, and suffering. ^an10-104-2-5

Why is that? ^an10-104-2-6

Because their view is bad. ^an10-104-2-7

Consider an individual who has right view, purpose, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, immersion, knowledge, and freedom. Whatever bodily, verbal, or mental deeds they undertake in line with that view, their intentions, aims, wishes, and choices all lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, beneficial, and pleasant. ^an10-104-3-1

Why is that? ^an10-104-3-2

Because their view is good. ^an10-104-3-3

Suppose a seed of sugar cane, fine rice, or grape was planted in moist earth. Whatever nutrients it takes up from the earth and water would lead to its sweet, pleasant, delectable flavor. ^an10-104-4-1

Why is that? ^an10-104-4-2

Because the seed is fine. ^an10-104-4-3

In the same way, consider an individual who has right view, thought, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, immersion, knowledge, and freedom. ^an10-104-4-4

Whatever bodily, verbal, or mental deeds they undertake in line with that view, their intentions, aims, wishes, and choices all lead to what is likable, desirable, agreeable, beneficial, and pleasant. ^an10-104-4-5

Why is that? ^an10-104-4-6

Because their view is good.” ^an10-104-4-7

### Knowledge (Numbered Discourses 10.105) ^an10-105-0

“Mendicants, ignorance precedes the attainment of unskillful qualities, with lack of conscience and prudence following along. ^an10-105-1-1

An ignoramus, sunk in ignorance, produces wrong view. Wrong view produces wrong purpose. Wrong purpose produces wrong speech. Wrong speech produces wrong action. Wrong action produces wrong livelihood. Wrong livelihood produces wrong effort. Wrong effort produces wrong mindfulness. Wrong mindfulness produces wrong immersion. Wrong immersion produces wrong knowledge. Wrong knowledge produces wrong freedom. ^an10-105-1-2

Knowledge precedes the attainment of skillful qualities, with conscience and prudence following along. ^an10-105-2-1

A knowing one, firm in knowledge, produces right view. Right view produces right purpose. Right purpose produces right speech. Right speech produces right action. Right action produces right livelihood. Right livelihood produces right effort. Right effort produces right mindfulness. Right mindfulness produces right immersion. Right immersion produces right knowledge. Right knowledge produces right freedom.” ^an10-105-2-2

### Wearing Away (Numbered Discourses 10.106) ^an10-106-0

“Mendicants, there are these ten grounds for wearing away. ^an10-106-1-1

What ten? ^an10-106-1-2

For one of right view, wrong view is worn away. ^an10-106-1-3

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong view are worn away. ^an10-106-1-4

And because of right view, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-1-5

For one of right purpose, wrong purpose is worn away. ^an10-106-2-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong purpose are worn away. ^an10-106-2-2

And because of right purpose, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-2-3

For one of right speech, wrong speech is worn away. ^an10-106-3-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong speech are worn away. ^an10-106-3-2

And because of right speech, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-3-3

For one of right action, wrong action is worn away. ^an10-106-4-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong action are worn away. ^an10-106-4-2

And because of right action, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-4-3

For one of right livelihood, wrong livelihood is worn away. ^an10-106-5-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong livelihood are worn away. ^an10-106-5-2

And because of right livelihood, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-5-3

For one of right effort, wrong effort is worn away. ^an10-106-6-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong effort are worn away. ^an10-106-6-2

And because of right effort, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-6-3

For one of right mindfulness, wrong mindfulness is worn away. ^an10-106-7-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong mindfulness are worn away. ^an10-106-7-2

And because of right mindfulness, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-7-3

For one of right immersion, wrong immersion is worn away. ^an10-106-8-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong immersion are worn away. ^an10-106-8-2

And because of right immersion, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-8-3

For one of right knowledge, wrong knowledge is worn away. ^an10-106-9-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong knowledge are worn away. ^an10-106-9-2

And because of right knowledge, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-9-3

For one of right freedom, wrong freedom is worn away. ^an10-106-10-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong freedom are worn away. ^an10-106-10-2

And because of right freedom, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-106-10-3

These are the ten grounds for wearing away.” ^an10-106-11-1

### The Bone-Washing Ceremony (Numbered Discourses 10.107) ^an10-107-0

“Mendicants, in the southern lands there is a ceremony named ‘bone-washing’. ^an10-107-1-1

There they have food, drink, snacks, meals, refreshments, and beverages, as well as dancing, singing, and music. ^an10-107-1-2

There is such a washing, I don’t deny it. ^an10-107-1-3

But that washing is low, crude, ordinary, ignoble, and pointless. It doesn’t lead to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. ^an10-107-1-4

I will teach a noble washing that leads solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. Relying on that washing, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things. ^an10-107-2-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an10-107-2-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an10-107-2-3

The Buddha said this: ^an10-107-2-4

“And what is that noble washing? ^an10-107-3-1

For one of right view, wrong view is washed away. ^an10-107-4-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong view are washed away. ^an10-107-4-2

And because of right view, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-107-4-3

For one of right purpose, wrong purpose is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-1

For one of right speech, wrong speech is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-2

For one of right action, wrong action is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-3

For one of right livelihood, wrong livelihood is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-4

For one of right effort, wrong effort is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-5

For one of right mindfulness, wrong mindfulness is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-6

For one of right immersion, wrong immersion is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-7

For one of right knowledge, wrong knowledge is washed away. … ^an10-107-5-8

For one of right freedom, wrong freedom is washed away. ^an10-107-6-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities that arise because of wrong freedom are washed away. ^an10-107-6-2

And because of right freedom, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-107-6-3

This is the noble washing that leads solely to disillusionment, dispassion, cessation, peace, insight, awakening, and extinguishment. Relying on this washing, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things.” ^an10-107-6-4

### Doctors (Numbered Discourses 10.108) ^an10-108-0

“Mendicants, doctors prescribe a purgative for eliminating illnesses stemming from disorders of bile, phlegm, and wind. ^an10-108-1-1

There is such a purgative, I don’t deny it. ^an10-108-1-2

But this kind of purgative sometimes works and sometimes fails. ^an10-108-1-3

I will teach a noble purgative that works without fail. Relying on that purgative, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things. ^an10-108-2-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an10-108-2-2

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an10-108-2-3

The Buddha said this: ^an10-108-2-4

“And what is the noble purgative that works without fail? ^an10-108-3-1

For one of right view, wrong view is purged. ^an10-108-4-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong view are purged. ^an10-108-4-2

And because of right view, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-108-4-3

For one of right purpose, wrong purpose is purged. … ^an10-108-5-1

For one of right speech, wrong speech is purged. … ^an10-108-5-2

For one of right action, wrong action is purged. … ^an10-108-5-3

For one of right livelihood, wrong livelihood is purged. … ^an10-108-5-4

For one of right effort, wrong effort is purged. ^an10-108-5-5

For one of right mindfulness, wrong mindfulness is purged. … ^an10-108-5-6

For one of right immersion, wrong immersion is purged. … ^an10-108-5-7

For one of right knowledge, wrong knowledge is purged. … ^an10-108-5-8

For one of right freedom, wrong freedom is purged. ^an10-108-6-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong freedom are purged. ^an10-108-6-2

And because of right freedom, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-108-6-3

This is the noble purgative that works without fail. Relying on this purgative, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things.” ^an10-108-6-4

### Emetic (Numbered Discourses 10.109) ^an10-109-0

“Mendicants, doctors prescribe an emetic for eliminating illnesses stemming from disorders of bile, phlegm, and wind. ^an10-109-1-1

There is such an emetic, I don’t deny it. ^an10-109-1-2

But this kind of emetic sometimes works and sometimes fails. ^an10-109-1-3

I will teach a noble emetic that works without fail. Relying on that emetic, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things. ^an10-109-2-1

Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak. … ^an10-109-2-2

And what is that noble emetic that works without fail? ^an10-109-3-1

For one of right view, wrong view is vomited up. ^an10-109-4-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong view are vomited up. ^an10-109-4-2

And because of right view, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-109-4-3

For one of right purpose, wrong purpose is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-1

For one of right speech, wrong speech is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-2

For one of right action, wrong action is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-3

For one of right livelihood, wrong livelihood is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-4

For one of right effort, wrong effort is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-5

For one of right mindfulness, wrong mindfulness is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-6

For one of right immersion, wrong immersion is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-7

For one of right knowledge, wrong knowledge is vomited up. … ^an10-109-5-8

For one of right freedom, wrong freedom is vomited up. ^an10-109-6-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong freedom are vomited up. ^an10-109-6-2

And because of right freedom, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-109-6-3

This is the noble emetic that works without fail. Relying on this emetic, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things.” ^an10-109-6-4

### Blown Away (Numbered Discourses 10.110) ^an10-110-0

“Mendicants, these ten qualities should be blown away. ^an10-110-1-1

What ten? ^an10-110-1-2

For one of right view, wrong view is blown away. ^an10-110-1-3

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong view are blown away. ^an10-110-1-4

And because of right view, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-110-1-5

For one of right purpose, wrong purpose is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-1

For one of right speech, wrong speech is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-2

For one of right action, wrong action is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-3

For one of right livelihood, wrong livelihood is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-4

For one of right effort, wrong effort is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-5

For one of right mindfulness, wrong mindfulness is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-6

For one of right immersion, wrong immersion is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-7

For one of right knowledge, wrong knowledge is blown away. … ^an10-110-2-8

For one of right freedom, wrong freedom is blown away. ^an10-110-3-1

And the many bad, unskillful qualities produced by wrong freedom are blown away. ^an10-110-3-2

And because of right freedom, many skillful qualities are fully developed. ^an10-110-3-3

These are the ten qualities that should be blown away.” ^an10-110-3-4

### An Adept (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.111) ^an10-111-0

Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an10-111-1-1

“Sir, they speak of the one called ‘an adept’. ^an10-111-2-1

How is an adept mendicant defined?” ^an10-111-2-2

“Mendicant, it’s when a mendicant has an adept’s right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an10-111-2-3

That’s how a mendicant is an adept.” ^an10-111-2-4

### An Adept (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 10.112) ^an10-112-0

“Mendicants, there are ten qualities of an adept. ^an10-112-1-1

What ten? ^an10-112-1-2

An adept’s right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom. ^an10-112-1-3

These are the ten qualities of an adept.” ^an10-112-1-4

### Bad Principles (1st) (Numbered Discourses 10.113) ^an10-113-0

“Mendicants, you should know bad principles with bad results. ^an10-113-1-1

And you should know good principles with good results. ^an10-113-1-2
