Since the faculty of sight is left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure become overwhelming. They don’t practice restraint, they don’t protect the faculty of sight, and they don’t achieve its restraint. ^an11-17-6-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an11-17-6-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an11-17-6-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an11-17-6-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an11-17-6-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they get caught up in the features and details. ^an11-17-6-8

Since the faculty of the mind is left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure become overwhelming. They don’t practice restraint, they don’t protect the faculty of the mind, and they don’t achieve its restraint. ^an11-17-6-9

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t dress wounds. ^an11-17-6-10

And how does a mendicant not spread smoke? ^an11-17-7-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t teach others the Dhamma in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an11-17-7-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t spread smoke. ^an11-17-7-3

And how does a mendicant not know the ford? ^an11-17-8-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t from time to time go up to those mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—and ask them questions: ^an11-17-8-2

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an11-17-8-3

Those venerables don’t reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an11-17-8-4

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t know the ford. ^an11-17-8-5

And how does a mendicant not know satisfaction? ^an11-17-9-1

It’s when a mendicant, when the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One are being taught, finds no inspiration in the meaning and the teaching, and finds no joy connected with the teaching. ^an11-17-9-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t know satisfaction. ^an11-17-9-3

And how does a mendicant not know the trail? ^an11-17-10-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t truly understand the noble eightfold path. ^an11-17-10-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t know the trail. ^an11-17-10-3

And how is a mendicant not skilled in pastures? ^an11-17-11-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t truly understand the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an11-17-11-2

That’s how a mendicant is not skilled in pastures. ^an11-17-11-3

And how does a mendicant milk dry? ^an11-17-12-1

It’s when a mendicant is invited by a householder to accept robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. But they don’t know moderation in accepting. ^an11-17-12-2

That’s how a mendicant milks dry. ^an11-17-12-3

And how does a mendicant not show extra respect to senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha? ^an11-17-13-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t consistently treat senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha with kindness by way of body, speech, and mind, both in public and in private. ^an11-17-13-2

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t show extra respect to senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha. ^an11-17-13-3

A mendicant with these eleven qualities can’t achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training. ^an11-17-14-1

A cowherd with eleven factors can maintain and expand a herd of cattle. ^an11-17-15-1

What eleven? ^an11-17-15-2

It’s when a cowherd knows form, is skilled in characteristics, picks out flies’ eggs, dresses wounds, spreads smoke, knows the ford, knows satisfaction, knows the trail, is skilled in pastures, doesn’t milk dry, and shows extra respect to the bulls who are fathers and captains of the herd. ^an11-17-15-3

A cowherd with these eleven factors can maintain and expand a herd of cattle. ^an11-17-15-4

In the same way, a mendicant with eleven qualities can achieve growth, improvement, and maturity in this teaching and training. ^an11-17-16-1

What eleven? ^an11-17-16-2

It’s when a mendicant knows form, is skilled in characteristics, picks out flies’ eggs, dresses wounds, spreads smoke, knows the ford, knows satisfaction, knows the trail, is skilled in pastures, doesn’t milk dry, and shows extra respect to senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha. ^an11-17-16-3

And how does a mendicant know form? ^an11-17-17-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands that all form is the four principal states, or form derived from the four principal states. ^an11-17-17-2

That’s how a mendicant knows form. ^an11-17-17-3

And how is a mendicant skilled in characteristics? ^an11-17-18-1

It’s when a mendicant understands that a fool is characterized by their deeds, and an astute person is characterized by their deeds. ^an11-17-18-2

That’s how a mendicant is skilled in characteristics. ^an11-17-18-3

And how does a mendicant pick out flies’ eggs? ^an11-17-19-1

It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought that has arisen, but gives it up, gets rid of it, eliminates it, and exterminates it. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, eliminate them, and obliterate them. ^an11-17-19-2

That’s how a mendicant picks out flies’ eggs. ^an11-17-19-3

And how does a mendicant dress wounds? ^an11-17-20-1

When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an11-17-20-2

If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint. ^an11-17-20-3

When they hear a sound with their ears … ^an11-17-20-4

When they smell an odor with their nose … ^an11-17-20-5

When they taste a flavor with their tongue … ^an11-17-20-6

When they feel a touch with their body … ^an11-17-20-7

When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details. ^an11-17-20-8

If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint. ^an11-17-20-9

That’s how a mendicant dresses wounds. ^an11-17-20-10

And how does a mendicant spread smoke? ^an11-17-21-1

It’s when a mendicant teaches others the Dhamma in detail as they learned and memorized it. ^an11-17-21-2

That’s how a mendicant spreads smoke. ^an11-17-21-3

And how does a mendicant know the ford? ^an11-17-22-1

It’s when from time to time a mendicant goes up to those mendicants who are very learned—inheritors of the heritage, who have memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines—and asks them questions: ^an11-17-22-2

‘Why, sir, does it say this? What does that mean?’ ^an11-17-22-3

Those venerables reveal what is hidden, clarify what is unclear, and dispel doubt regarding the many doubtful matters. ^an11-17-22-4

That’s how a mendicant knows the ford. ^an11-17-22-5

And how does a mendicant know satisfaction? ^an11-17-23-1

It’s when a mendicant, when the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One are being taught, finds inspiration in the meaning and the teaching, and finds joy connected with the teaching. ^an11-17-23-2

That’s how a mendicant knows satisfaction. ^an11-17-23-3

And how does a mendicant know the trail? ^an11-17-24-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands the noble eightfold path. ^an11-17-24-2

That’s how a mendicant knows the trail. ^an11-17-24-3

And how is a mendicant skilled in pastures? ^an11-17-25-1

It’s when a mendicant truly understands the four kinds of mindfulness meditation. ^an11-17-25-2

That’s how a mendicant is skilled in pastures. ^an11-17-25-3

And how does a mendicant not milk dry? ^an11-17-26-1

It’s when a mendicant is invited by a householder to accept robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick. ^an11-17-26-2

And that mendicant knows moderation in accepting. ^an11-17-26-3

That’s how a mendicant doesn’t milk dry. ^an11-17-26-4

And how does a mendicant show extra respect to senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha? ^an11-17-27-1

It’s when a mendicant consistently treats senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha with kindness by way of body, speech, and mind, both in public and in private. ^an11-17-27-2

That’s how a mendicant shows extra respect to senior mendicants of long standing, long gone forth, fathers and leaders of the Saṅgha. ^an11-17-27-3

A mendicant with these eleven qualities can achieve growth, improvement, or maturity in this teaching and training.” ^an11-17-28-1

### Immersion (1st) (Numbered Discourses 11.18) ^an11-18-0

And then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him: ^an11-18-1-1

“Could it be, sir, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-18-2-1

“It could be, mendicants.” ^an11-18-3-1

“But how could this be?” ^an11-18-4-1

“It’s when a mendicant perceives: ^an11-18-5-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an11-18-5-2

That’s how a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-18-5-3

### Immersion (2nd) (Numbered Discourses 11.19) ^an11-19-0

There the Buddha addressed the mendicants, ^an11-19-1-1

“Mendicants!” ^an11-19-1-2

“Venerable sir,” they replied. ^an11-19-1-3

The Buddha said this: ^an11-19-1-4

“Could it be, mendicants, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-19-2-1

“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an11-19-2-2

“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an11-19-3-1

“Yes, sir,” they replied. ^an11-19-3-2

The Buddha said this: ^an11-19-3-3

“A mendicant could gain such a state of immersion.” ^an11-19-4-1

“But how could this be?” ^an11-19-5-1

“It’s when a mendicant perceives: ^an11-19-6-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an11-19-6-2

That’s how a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-19-6-3

### Immersion (3rd) (Numbered Discourses 11.20) ^an11-20-0

And then several mendicants went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him. ^an11-20-1-1

When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him: ^an11-20-1-2

“Could it be, reverend, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-20-2-1

“It could be, reverends.” ^an11-20-2-2

“But how could this be?” ^an11-20-3-1

“It’s when a mendicant perceives: ^an11-20-4-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an11-20-4-2

That’s how a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-20-4-3

### Immersion (4th) (Numbered Discourses 11.21) ^an11-21-0

There Sāriputta addressed the mendicants: ^an11-21-1-1

“Could it be, reverends, that a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this? They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-21-1-2

“Reverend, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta. ^an11-21-2-1

May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this. ^an11-21-2-2

The mendicants will listen and remember it.” ^an11-21-2-3

“Then listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.” ^an11-21-3-1

“Yes, reverend,” they replied. ^an11-21-3-2

Sāriputta said this: ^an11-21-3-3

“A mendicant could gain such a state of immersion.” ^an11-21-4-1

“But how could this be?” ^an11-21-5-1

“It’s when a mendicant perceives: ^an11-21-6-1

‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’ ^an11-21-6-2

That’s how a mendicant might gain a state of immersion like this. They wouldn’t perceive earth in earth, water in water, fire in fire, or air in air. And they wouldn’t perceive the dimension of infinite space in the dimension of infinite space, the dimension of infinite consciousness in the dimension of infinite consciousness, the dimension of nothingness in the dimension of nothingness, or the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. They wouldn’t perceive this world in this world, or the other world in the other world. And they wouldn’t perceive what is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, or explored by the mind. And yet they would still perceive.” ^an11-21-6-3

### Untitled Discourses on the Eye (Numbered Discourses 11.22–29) ^an11-22-29-0

“Mendicants, a cowherd with eleven factors can’t maintain and expand a herd of cattle. ^an11-22-1-1

What eleven? ^an11-22-1-2

It’s when a cowherd doesn’t know form, is unskilled in characteristics, doesn’t pick out flies’ eggs, doesn’t dress wounds, doesn’t spread smoke, doesn’t know the ford, doesn’t know satisfaction, doesn’t know the trail, is not skilled in pastures, milks dry, and doesn’t show extra respect to the bulls who are fathers and captains of the herd. ^an11-22-1-3

A cowherd with these eleven factors can’t maintain and expand a herd of cattle. ^an11-22-1-4

In the same way, a mendicant with eleven qualities can’t meditate observing impermanence in the eye … ^an11-22-2-1

suffering … ^an11-23-1-1

not-self … ^an11-24-1-1

ending … ^an11-25-1-1

vanishing … ^an11-26-1-1

fading away … ^an11-27-1-1

cessation … ^an11-28-1-1

letting go …” ^an11-29-1-1

### Untitled Discourse on Greed (Numbered Discourses 11.982) ^an11-982-0

“For insight into greed, eleven things should be developed. ^an11-982-1-1

What eleven? ^an11-982-1-2

The first, second, third, and fourth absorptions; the heart’s releases by love, compassion, rejoicing, and equanimity; the dimensions of infinite space, infinite consciousness, and nothingness. ^an11-982-1-3

For insight into greed, these eleven things should be developed.” ^an11-982-1-4

### Untitled Discourses on Greed (Numbered Discourses 11.983–991) ^an11-983-991-0

“For the complete understanding of greed … ^an11-983-991-1-1

complete ending … ^an11-983-991-1-2

giving up … ^an11-983-991-1-3

ending … ^an11-983-991-1-4

vanishing … ^an11-983-991-1-5

fading away … ^an11-983-991-1-6

cessation … ^an11-983-991-1-7

giving away … ^an11-983-991-1-8

letting go … ^an11-983-991-1-9

these eleven things should be developed.” ^an11-983-991-1-10

complete understanding … ^an11-992-1151-1-17

giving away … ^an11-992-1151-1-24

For the letting go of negligence, these eleven things should be developed.” ^an11-992-1151-1-25

That is what the Buddha said. ^an11-992-1151-2-1

Satisfied, the mendicants approved what the Buddha said. ^an11-992-1151-2-2

The Book of the Elevens is finished. ^an11-992-1151-3-5

The Numbered Discourses are complete. ^an11-992-1151-3-6
